input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED] | Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitted Diagnosis: Atrial fibrillation with features of LVH and SARS-CoV-2 positive infection

Major Procedures: None

Hospital Course:

The patient presented to the ED with malaise and palpitations, and a confirmed diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2. Upon admission, he was noted to be febrile with left lower lobe infiltrates and patchy perihilar and right middle lobe ground-glass airspace disease on chest x-ray. The patient's vital signs were monitored closely, and he was started on intravenous diltiazem for atrial fibrillation control. The patient showed an improvement in his PR after treatment and was then started on intravenous diltiazem drip at 5mg/hour. The patient received symptomatic treatment for fever given the presence of a fever on admission. The patient was maintained in droplet and contact isolation with eye protection.

Treatment:

The patient was treated intravenous diltiazem (10 mg bolus) and a 500 ml normal saline intravenous fluid bolus, which led to an improvement in his PR to approximately 100 bpm. After this, he was started on intravenous diltiazem drip at 5 mg/hour for atrial fibrillation control and admitted for further monitoring and workup.

Outcome:

The final diagnosis was atrial fibrillation with features of LVH and SARS-CoV-2 positive infection. The patient's tachycardia and fever subsided during his hospitalization course. He was discharged in a stable condition for further follow-up.

Recommendations:

The patient was advised to continue home isolation and complete the 14-day quarantine period. The patient was provided with a list of symptoms that he should report to his primary care physician if they occur. A follow-up appointment to his primary care physician was scheduled for further management after completing the quarantine period.

Overarching Risk Factors:

The patient had a past medical history of untreated essential hypertension.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED] | Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitted Diagnosis: Irregular heartbeat with thickening of the heart muscle and COVID-19 infection

Major Procedures: None

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the emergency room feeling unwell with a racing heart and tested positive for COVID-19. When admitted, the patient had a fever and chest x-rays showed signs of lung infection. The care team watched the patient's vital signs closely. The patient received diltiazem through an IV to help control the irregular heartbeat. The patient's heart rate got better after treatment. The patient then received a slow, steady dose of diltiazem through the IV at 5mg per hour. The patient received medicine to treat the fever. The patient was kept in a special room with safety measures to prevent spreading the virus.

Treatment:

The patient received diltiazem medicine through an IV and fluids through an IV. This helped lower the heart rate to about 100 beats per minute. After this, the patient received a slow, steady dose of diltiazem at 5 mg per hour to control the irregular heartbeat. The patient stayed in the hospital for more monitoring and tests.

Outcome:

The final diagnosis was irregular heartbeat with thickening of the heart muscle and COVID-19 infection. The patient's fast heart rate and fever went away during the hospital stay. The patient left the hospital in stable condition for follow-up care.

Recommendations:

The patient was told to stay home in isolation and complete 14 days of quarantine. The patient received a list of warning signs to report to the regular doctor if they happen. A follow-up visit with the regular doctor was scheduled after the quarantine period ends.

Overarching Risk Factors:

The patient had a history of high blood pressure that was not being treated.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED] 
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED] 
Date of Admission: [REDACTED] 
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED] 

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 78-year-old male, was admitted to the emergency room with complaints of dry cough, fever and malaise persisting for 1 month. He was previously treated with amoxicillin with no improvement. His medical history included diabetes and arterial blood hypertension, for which he was on metformin and amlodipine for 7 years. The patient presented with mildly lethargic orientation, febrile with mild tachycardia, hypoxemia, and crackles in the mid- and lower-lung fields on physical examination. Laboratory tests revealed elevated WBC counts, NLR, ESR, and CRP. The chest radiograph showed diffuse bilateral interstitial infiltrates. The HRCT showed diffuse interstitial lung involvement with a ground glass pattern. Blood and sputum cultures were negative for bacteria, urine antigen tests for Legionella pneumonia and Streptococcus pneumoniae were negative. Serum immunology tests for autoimmune diseases such as antinuclear antibodies, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies were all negative. A bronchoscopy was then performed, BAL and TBLB were negative for malignancy, bacteria, nocardia, fungi, and mycobacteria. BAL increased cellularity and had a mixed cellularity pattern. In view of the above findings, a diagnosis of Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia (COP) was considered. Treatment with antibiotics was ceased, and the patient was started on prednisone 20mg daily. The patient showed immediate improvement, blood inflammatory markers decreased, and he was discharged after 5 days on prednisone. He was advised to taper off corticosteroids for a total duration of treatment of 6-12 months.

Medical Condition:
Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia (COP)

Treatment:
Prednisone 20mg daily

Follow-up:
The patient has had no recurrence of symptoms at follow-up visits after complete clinical and radiological resolution of his condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 78-year-old male, came to the emergency room with a dry cough, fever, and feeling unwell for 1 month. He was treated before with an antibiotic called amoxicillin, but it did not help. His health history included diabetes and high blood pressure. He had been taking metformin and amlodipine for these conditions for 7 years. When examined, the patient was slightly drowsy, had a fever, a slightly fast heartbeat, low oxygen levels, and crackling sounds in the middle and lower parts of his lungs. Blood tests showed signs of infection and swelling in the body. A chest x-ray showed cloudy patches in both lungs. A detailed lung scan showed widespread lung tissue damage with a hazy pattern. Tests of blood and mucus samples found no bacteria. Urine tests for two types of lung infection bacteria were negative. Blood tests for diseases where the body attacks itself were all negative. A camera was used to look inside the airways. Fluid and tissue samples from the lungs showed no cancer, bacteria, or fungus. The fluid samples did show more cells than normal with a mixed pattern. Based on all these results, the doctors believed the patient had a lung condition called Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia. This is a type of lung swelling with no known cause. The antibiotics were stopped. The patient was started on a steroid medicine called prednisone at 20mg once a day. The patient got better right away. The signs of swelling in the blood went down. The patient left the hospital after 5 days on prednisone. He was told to slowly lower his steroid dose over 6 to 12 months total.

Medical Condition:
Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia, a type of lung swelling with no known cause

Treatment:
Prednisone 20mg once a day

Follow-up:
The patient has had no return of symptoms at follow-up visits. His condition has fully cleared up based on exams and scans.

input:
Hospital Course:
The 78-year-old male patient was admitted with complaints of right-sided pain and numbness in the ear, submandibular and temporal regions, and right side of the tongue root, as well as right-sided facial weakness. The patient reported paroxysms of odynophagia triggered by swallowing which lasted throughout his mealtime. The patient's pain was rated a 9-10 on the numerical rating scale, and he had difficulty taking food for several days due to the intensity of his pain. Medical therapy was initiated with the application of ipsilateral lidocaine spray to the tonsillar branch of the glossopharyngeal nerve before mealtime. 

Diagnostic findings:
The patient's peripheral facial paralysis was classified as House-Brackmann grade III, and the results of the audiogram were normal. Laboratory investigations and brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) were unremarkable. The delivery of pulsed radiofrequency (PRF) to the Gasserian ganglion was recommended to alleviate the pain of the mandibular nerve division of the trigeminal nerve.

Treatment:
The patient received pulsed radiofrequency treatment with a radiofrequency generator, which produced some pain relief, and a second application of PRF was performed a week later. Sensory testing at 50 Hz and motor testing at 2 Hz were performed to ensure the needle was on the sensory target. Infrared thermography was done to follow up lesion changes and provide effective assessment of combined therapy. The peripheral facial paralysis was treated with linear-polarized near-infrared light irradiation. The patient's treatment regimen was adjusted according to response, and gabapentin was initiated at a dose of 300 mg once per day, which was increased by 300 mg every day until a therapeutic dose (600 mg three times daily) was reached.

Outcome:
After ten sessions of linear-polarized near-infrared light irradiation, the House-Brackmann grade indicated that the patient was healthy, and the patient was discharged from the hospital. The patient was followed up with thermography to monitor the progress of therapy. At three months, the patient missed a dose of gabapentin, and infrared thermography of the pain area showed a higher temperature than that of the contralateral side. However, after gabapentin administration, the thermographic findings showed a lower temperature image.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The 78-year-old male patient came to the hospital with pain and numbness on the right side of his face. This included his ear, the area under his jaw, the side of his head, and the right side of his tongue. He also had weakness on the right side of his face. The patient had sudden bursts of sharp pain when swallowing that lasted during his entire meal. The patient rated his pain as 9-10 out of 10. He had trouble eating for several days because the pain was so bad. Doctors started treatment by spraying a numbing medicine on the right side of his throat before meals. This helped block the nerve that was causing pain when he swallowed.

Diagnostic findings:
The patient's facial weakness was rated as moderate. His hearing test results were normal. Blood tests and a brain scan showed no problems. Doctors suggested a treatment that uses electrical pulses to calm the nerve causing jaw pain.

Treatment:
The patient received a treatment that uses electrical pulses to calm the nerves. This gave some pain relief. A second treatment was done one week later. Doctors tested the nerves to make sure the needle was in the right spot. Heat imaging was used to check how well the treatment was working. The facial weakness was treated with a special light therapy. The patient's treatment plan was changed based on how he responded. He was also started on a pain medicine called gabapentin. He began with a low dose and slowly increased it until he reached the full dose of 600 mg three times a day.

Outcome:
After ten sessions of light therapy, the patient's facial weakness was fully healed. The patient was sent home from the hospital. Heat imaging was used to check how well the treatment was working over time. At three months, the patient missed a dose of gabapentin. Heat imaging showed the painful area was warmer than the other side of his face. After taking gabapentin again, the heat imaging showed the temperature went back down.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 27-year-old pregnant female with a history of congenital heart disease, was admitted to the hospital at 34 weeks of gestation due to pain abdomen for 2 days. On examination, the patient was found to be cyanotic with clubbed fingers and raised Jugular venous pressure, and there was a pansystolic murmur and bilateral basal crepitations present. The patient was diagnosed to have a large perimembranous ventricular septal defect (VSD) with bidirectional shunt and severe pulmonary hypertension (PAH), tricuspid regurgitation, and a dilated right atrium seen on echocardiography.

Treatment:

The patient had been on irregular treatment with sildenafil 25 mg twice a day since 4 months, and given her previous obstetric history, lower segment cesarean section (LSCS) was planned. All risks were explained, and written informed consent was obtained. Prophylaxis against bacterial endocarditis was provided, and an epidural catheter was inserted for pain management during the surgery. Ampicillin and gentamicin were administered as per standard prophylaxis guidelines.

Procedure:

Over a period of 30 min, a total of 7cc of lignocaine with adrenaline solution and 8 cc of bupivacaine with 25 μg of fentanyl produced a block height of T8 tested to touch with blunt forceps. CVP was maintained between10-12 mm throughout surgery. The procedure was uneventful, and after delivery of the baby, furosemide 10 mg was given intravenously. A total of 450 cc of colloid was given.

Postoperative:

After surgery, the patient was shifted to the intensive care unit (ICU) for monitoring and was discharged on the 10th day. Epidural analgesia was maintained with 0.125% bupivacaine and 20 μg of fentanyl every 8 hourly for 72 h.

Summary:

The patient was diagnosed to have a large perimembranous ventricular septal defect (VSD) with bidirectional shunt and severe pulmonary hypertension (PAH), tricuspid regurgitation, and a dilated right atrium seen on echocardiography. She had a history of a stillborn delivery in her previous pregnancy and was advised against conceiving again, but she refused. She had been on irregular treatment with sildenafil, and given her previous obstetric history, LSCS was planned. The procedure was uneventful, and the patient was discharged on the 10th day. Epidural analgesia was maintained for pain management after surgery.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 27-year-old pregnant woman with a heart problem she was born with. She came to the hospital at 34 weeks of pregnancy because of stomach pain for 2 days. During the exam, the patient had bluish skin, curved fingernails, and swollen neck veins. The doctor heard a heart murmur and crackling sounds in both lungs. A heart ultrasound showed the patient has a large hole between the lower chambers of the heart. Blood was flowing in both directions through this hole. The patient also has very high blood pressure in the lungs, a leaky heart valve, and an enlarged upper right heart chamber.

Treatment:

The patient had been taking sildenafil 25 mg twice a day for 4 months, but not regularly. Because of her past pregnancy history, doctors planned a cesarean section to deliver the baby. The medical team explained all the risks to the patient. The patient signed a consent form agreeing to the surgery. The patient received medicine to prevent heart infection. A thin tube was placed in the lower back to control pain during surgery. The patient was given two antibiotics called ampicillin and gentamicin to prevent infection.

Procedure:

Over 30 minutes, the doctors gave numbing medicine through the tube in the lower back. This blocked pain from the chest down to the belly button area. The pressure in the large vein near the heart was kept at a safe level during surgery. The surgery went well with no problems. After the baby was born, the patient received furosemide 10 mg through a vein to remove extra fluid. The patient also received fluids through a vein during surgery.

Postoperative:

After surgery, the patient was moved to the intensive care unit for close watching. Pain medicine was given through the tube in the lower back every 8 hours for 3 days. The patient left the hospital on the 10th day after surgery.

Summary:

The patient has a large hole between the lower chambers of the heart she was born with. Blood flows in both directions through this hole. She also has very high blood pressure in the lungs, a leaky heart valve, and an enlarged upper right heart chamber. In her last pregnancy, the baby did not survive. Doctors told her not to get pregnant again, but she chose to anyway. She had been taking sildenafil, but not regularly. Doctors planned a cesarean section because of her past pregnancy. The surgery went well with no problems. Pain medicine was given through a tube in the lower back after surgery. The patient left the hospital on the 10th day.

input:
Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Diagnosis: Diffuse Large B-cell Lymphoma

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with fever and cervical pain for which she had received antibiotics in the past. Physical examination showed multiple swollen lymph nodes in the left axillary region. CT imaging revealed axillary lymphadenopathy and bone destruction in the axis. The PET scan showed multiple lesions of the bone and bone marrow. Bone marrow aspiration revealed no large lymphoid cells. Biopsy of the left axillary lymph nodes showed absence of follicular structures and diffusely proliferative large lymphoid cells. Immunohistochemical staining confirmed that the large lymphoid cells were positive for CD20, bcl-2, and MIB-1, but negative for CD3, CD30, and EBER. The patient was diagnosed with clinical stage IV DLBCL, with low-to-intermediate risk according to the international prognostic index.

The patient received radiotherapy for the cervical lesions and declined conventional chemotherapy due to the risk of recurrent NTM infection. She was started on CAM and PSL, and her fever improved within a few days. The PSL dosage was initially 20 mg/day for 2 weeks and subsequently 15 mg/day for 2 weeks. The maintenance dosage was 10 mg/day. The patient experienced slightly reduced taste sensation, considered a side effect of long-term CAM use.

The follow-up imaging studies after 6 months showed complete remission with improvements in lymphadenopathy and lesions of the bone and bone marrow. The patient remains in complete remission 7 months after initiation of treatment.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted with fever and cervical pain. Multiple swollen lymph nodes were found in the left axillary region. Imaging studies showed axillary lymphadenopathy and bone destruction in the axis, with multiple lesions of the bone and bone marrow. Biopsy confirmed the diagnosis of clinical stage IV DLBCL. The patient received radiotherapy for cervical lesions and subsequently started on CAM and PSL. Follow-up imaging after 6 months showed complete remission, and the patient remains in complete remission 7 months after initiation of treatment.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Diagnosis: Diffuse Large B-cell Lymphoma (a type of blood cancer that affects white blood cells)

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with fever and neck pain. She had taken antibiotics for this before. The doctor found several swollen lymph nodes under the left arm. A CT scan showed swollen lymph nodes under the arm and bone damage in the spine. A PET scan showed the cancer had spread to many areas of the bone and bone marrow. A bone marrow test did not show large cancer cells. A small piece of tissue was taken from the lymph nodes under the left arm. The test showed cancer cells had spread throughout the tissue. Special lab tests confirmed the type of cancer. The patient was diagnosed with stage IV cancer that had spread to many parts of the body. The risk level was low to medium based on a standard scoring system.

The patient received radiation treatment for the neck area. She chose not to have regular chemotherapy because of the risk of a past lung infection coming back. She was started on two medicines called CAM and PSL. Her fever got better within a few days. The PSL dose started at 20 mg per day for 2 weeks. Then it was lowered to 15 mg per day for 2 weeks. The long-term dose was 10 mg per day. The patient noticed food tasted a little different. This was likely caused by taking CAM for a long time.

Scans taken 6 months later showed the cancer was gone. The swollen lymph nodes and bone problems had improved. The patient still has no signs of cancer 7 months after starting treatment.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came to the hospital with fever and neck pain. Doctors found several swollen lymph nodes under the left arm. Scans showed swollen lymph nodes, bone damage in the spine, and cancer in the bone and bone marrow. Tests confirmed stage IV blood cancer. The patient received radiation to the neck and started taking CAM and PSL. Scans after 6 months showed the cancer was gone. The patient still has no signs of cancer 7 months after starting treatment.

input:
Hospital Course

This 51-year-old male weighing 131 kg presented to the ED with altered mental status and slurred speech after undergoing cervical epidural injection. He became unresponsive during the injection with subsequent brief convulsive activity. Emergency medical services was called and found him obtunded with shallow breathing and low oxygen saturations requiring ventilation assistance. On arrival to the ED his breathing was erratic with low oxygen saturations. He remained somnolent with slurred speech, unable to answer questions appropriately or follow commands. On cardiopulmonary monitor, he was noted to have an irregularly irregular heart rhythm. Electrocardiogram showed atrial fibrillation with a rate of 82 beats per minute. Hemodynamically he was stable. Within 60 minutes of ED arrival, the patient’s mental status was back to baseline without recollection of the events that had occurred after the start of the procedure. Imaging studies obtained were all negative. 

Hospital Course: Diagnosis and Treatment

The patient was observed overnight in the hospital and remained asymptomatic and without any further dysrhythmia. He only had chest wall pain, possibly from sternal rub or from any bystander chest compressions that may have been performed when he became unresponsive. Serum/plasma levels of lidocaine and its primary active metabolite, monoethylglycinexylidide (MEGX), were obtained and returned undetectable. Aspirin 325 mg daily was prescribed and a referral to outpatient cardiology was made. 

Condition at Discharge

The patient was discharged with no symptoms and normal mental status.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course

This 51-year-old male weighing 131 kg came to the emergency room confused and with slurred speech. This happened after getting a pain injection in his neck. During the injection, he passed out and had a brief seizure. Emergency workers were called and found him barely awake with shallow breathing and low oxygen levels. He needed help breathing. When he arrived at the emergency room, his breathing was uneven and his oxygen levels were low. He stayed very drowsy with slurred speech. He could not answer questions correctly or follow directions. Heart monitors showed his heart was beating in an uneven pattern. A heart test showed he had a condition called atrial fibrillation, which means the heart beats in an irregular way. His heart rate was 82 beats per minute. His blood pressure and blood flow stayed normal. Within 60 minutes of arriving at the emergency room, the patient was thinking clearly again. He did not remember anything that happened after the procedure started. All imaging tests came back normal.

Hospital Course: Diagnosis and Treatment

The patient stayed in the hospital overnight for watching. He had no symptoms and no more heart rhythm problems. He only had chest wall pain. This was possibly from rubbing on his chest bone or from chest pushes that may have been done when he passed out. Blood tests checked for lidocaine and a related substance called MEGX. Lidocaine is a numbing medicine. These tests showed none of these substances in his blood. The patient was given aspirin 325 mg to take once a day. He was also given a referral to see a heart doctor as an outpatient.

Condition at Discharge

The patient was sent home with no symptoms and normal thinking.
perturbed:
Hospital Course

This 51-year-old male weighing 131 kg came to the emergency room confused and with slurred speech. This happened after getting a pain injection in his neck. During the injection, he passed out and had a brief seizure. Emergency workers were called and found him barely awake with shallow breathing and low oxygen levels. He needed help breathing. When he arrived at the emergency room, his breathing was uneven and his oxygen levels were low. He stayed very drowsy with slurred speech. He could not answer questions correctly or follow directions. Heart monitors showed his heart was beating in an uneven pattern. A heart test showed he had a condition called atrial fibrillation, which means the heart beats in an irregular way. His heart rate was 82 beats per minute. His blood pressure and blood flow stayed normal. Within 60 minutes of arriving at the emergency room, the patient was thinking clearly again. He did not remember anything that happened after the procedure started. All imaging tests came back normal.

Hospital Course: Diagnosis and Treatment

The patient stayed in the hospital overnight for watching. He had no symptoms and no more heart rhythm problems. He only had chest wall pain. This was possibly from rubbing on his chest bone or from chest pushes that may have been done when he passed out. Blood tests checked for lidocaine and a related substance called MEGX. Lidocaine is a numbing medicine. These tests showed none of these substances in his blood. The patient was given aspirin 3250 mg to take once a day. He was also given a referral to see a heart doctor as an outpatient.

Condition at Discharge

The patient was sent home with no symptoms and normal thinking.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: [Patient's Name]

Gender: Male

Age: 76 years

Medical Record Number: [Medical Record Number]

Admission Date: [Admission Date]

Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Elevated creatinine levels, elevated IgE level, proteinuria, and glucosuria

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the rheumatology clinic with severe fatigue, unintentional weight loss, memory loss, and ambulatory dysfunction. The patient had a 120 pack-year smoking history and also smoked marijuana. The patient had a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, COPD, and diabetes mellitus.

The labs showed an elevated creatinine level, elevated IgE level, proteinuria, and glucosuria. An ultrasound image of the Kidneys showed echogenic foci in the right kidney. The CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast showed a scar in the interpolar region of the right kidney. CT scan of the chest with contrast showed a 10 mm low-density area in the right thyroid lobe. Thyroid ultrasound showed bilateral thyroid nodules, including a 3.5 cm hypoechoic nodule in the lower pole. Fine-needle aspiration cytology of the nodule showed benign follicular nodule.

The patient had a repeat CT chest with contrast, which showed a 1.4 cm right lower lobe lung nodule, bilateral interlobular septal thickening with lower lobe predominance, multiple bilateral ground-glass opacities, cystic changes, and multiple enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes. PET scanning was done, which showed right lower lobe hypermetabolic nodule, highly suspicious for malignancy with extensive smoking history and significant weight loss.

The patient underwent right lower lobe basilar segmentectomy and mediastinal lymph node dissection. Histology was negative for malignancy and showed numerous positive IgG4 plasma cells, along with obliterative phlebitis, significant fibrosis, and arteritis as lymphoplasmacytic infiltration. IgG4:IgG ratio on the lung biopsy was greater than 40%. The lung biopsy was highly suggestive of IgG4-related disease. The lymph node also showed lymphoplasmacytic infiltration, IgG4-positive cells, irregular fibrous bands, but no obliterative phlebitis or arteritis.

Treatment and Procedures:

The patient was started on 40 mg of prednisone, which resolved his fatigue and had significant weight gain, and his prednisone was tapered down and stopped in 4 months. He was not placed on any immunosuppression and was monitored for any further relapse. He is in remission for the last one year off any medications without any relapses.

Diagnosis on Discharge:

IgG4-related disease.

Condition at Discharge:

The patient is asymptomatic with no need for further follow-up.

Follow-up Recommendations:

The patient is advised follow-up with the rheumatology clinic.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: [Patient's Name]

Gender: Male

Age: 76 years

Medical Record Number: [Medical Record Number]

Admission Date: [Admission Date]

Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: High kidney waste levels in blood, high allergy-related protein level, protein in urine, and sugar in urine

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the joint and immune disease clinic with severe tiredness, weight loss without trying, memory problems, and trouble walking. The patient had smoked heavily for many years (equal to 6 packs a day for 20 years) and also smoked marijuana. The patient had a history of high blood pressure, high cholesterol, lung disease from smoking, and diabetes.

Blood tests showed high kidney waste levels, high allergy-related protein levels, protein in urine, and sugar in urine. An ultrasound of the kidneys showed bright spots in the right kidney. A detailed scan of the belly area showed scar tissue in the right kidney. A chest scan showed a small area of concern in the right side of the thyroid gland in the neck. A thyroid ultrasound showed lumps on both sides, including a 3.5 cm lump in the lower part. A needle test of the lump showed it was not cancer.

The patient had another chest scan. It showed a 1.4 cm lump in the right lower lung, thickening between lung sections mostly in the lower lungs, hazy patches in both lungs, small air pockets, and several swollen lymph nodes in the chest. A special scan to look for cancer was done. It showed the right lower lung lump was very active and likely cancer given the heavy smoking history and weight loss.

The patient had surgery to remove part of the right lower lung and lymph nodes in the chest. Lab testing of the tissue showed no cancer. Instead, it showed many immune cells called IgG4, blocked veins, heavy scarring, and swollen blood vessels. The ratio of IgG4 cells was very high at over 40 percent. The lung tissue strongly pointed to a rare immune disease called IgG4-related disease. The lymph node also showed similar immune cells and scarring but no blocked veins or swollen blood vessels.

Treatment and Procedures:

The patient started taking 40 mg of prednisone, a steroid medicine. This helped the tiredness go away and the patient gained back weight. The steroid dose was slowly lowered and stopped after 4 months. The patient did not need other immune-lowering medicines and was watched for signs of the disease coming back. The patient has been free of disease for the last one year without taking any medicines and without the disease returning.

Diagnosis on Discharge:

IgG4-related disease (a rare condition where the immune system causes swelling and scarring in different body parts).

Condition at Discharge:

The patient has no symptoms and does not need further follow-up at this time.

Follow-up Recommendations:

The patient is advised to follow up with the joint and immune disease clinic.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: _______

Gender: Female

Age: 56 years

Admission Date: _______

Discharge Date: _______

Diagnosis: Primary Osteoarthritis of the Left Knee 

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 56-year-old female with a body mass index of 23.2, was admitted to our facility as a diagnosed case of primary osteoarthritis of the left knee. The osteoarthritis of the left knee was evaluated as grade 3 based on the Kellgren-Lawrence Scale. The patient had received an intra-articular injection of high molecular weight hyaluronic acid 2 years back which transiently improved pain symptoms. Significant improvement was not noted in the pain symptoms despite the administration of 4 injections of platelet-rich plasma (PRP) a month apart each. The patient also performed prescribed exercises that included strengthening of quadriceps and hamstrings, and stretching of the hamstrings and took precautionary measures in the activities of daily living which provided little relief in symptoms. An ultrasound of the knee joint was done to look for structural changes, and suitable changes for treatment were recommended. 

Treatment:

In view of inadequate response after 8 months of the last PRP injection, intra-articular injection of adalimumab was planned, and 10 mg of Adalimumab was administered under ultrasonography-guidance. The procedure was uneventful, and the patient did not have any transient discomfort or complications following injection adalimumab. The patient was put on Tablet Paracetamol 650 mg (as per need) as a rescue medication, and post adalimumab injection pill count was on an average 2 tablets per week. 

Outcome:

The patient reported noticeable reduction in pain beginning approximately 2 weeks from the date of injection. The 11-point visual analog scale (VAS) for pain intensity was used to evaluate the patient. VAS reduced from 8 (pre-treatment) to 3 at the 1 month follow-up, remained at 3 at the 3 months follow-up and became 4 at the 6 months follow-up. Ultrasonographic examination was repeated at the end of 12 weeks and 6 months, and pre- and post-intervention ultrasonographic findings did not show any change despite significant improvement in symptoms. The patient's knee injury & osteoarthritis outcome score (KOOS) improved from 32.20 (pre-treatment) to 61.20 at the 1 month follow-up, 60.90 at the 3 months follow-up and 57.50 at the 6 months follow-up. The patient was discharged and is under routine care in our outpatient department, and has not complained of any deterioration in symptoms. A formal evaluation of VAS, KOOS, and ultrasound examination is scheduled after 6 months. 

Recommendation:

The patient has been advised to follow the preventive measures to avoid any pain, inflammation, or stiffness in the joint. The patient has also been advised to follow-up with the treating physician regularly to avoid any complications or further injury.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: _______

Gender: Female

Age: 56 years

Admission Date: _______

Discharge Date: _______

Diagnosis: Wear and tear arthritis of the left knee

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 56-year-old female with a healthy body weight. She was admitted with wear and tear arthritis of the left knee. The arthritis was rated as moderate to severe on a standard scale. Two years ago, the patient received a joint injection with a lubricating gel. This helped with pain for a short time. The patient then received 4 injections of a blood-based healing treatment, given one month apart. These did not help much with the pain. The patient also did exercises to strengthen and stretch the thigh muscles. She made changes to her daily activities to protect the knee. These steps gave only a little relief. An imaging test using sound waves was done to look at the knee joint. Based on the results, a treatment plan was made.

Treatment:

After 8 months, the blood-based healing treatment was still not working well enough. The care team decided to try a different medicine injected into the joint. A 10 mg dose of adalimumab was given using sound wave imaging to guide the needle. The procedure went smoothly with no problems or discomfort after the injection. The patient was given 650 mg paracetamol tablets to take when needed for pain. After the injection, the patient took about 2 tablets per week on average.

Outcome:

The patient noticed less pain about 2 weeks after the injection. Pain was measured on a scale from 0 to 10. The pain score dropped from 8 before treatment to 3 at the 1 month checkup. It stayed at 3 at the 3 month checkup and rose slightly to 4 at the 6 month checkup. Sound wave imaging was repeated at 12 weeks and 6 months. The images showed no change in the joint, even though the patient felt much better. A score measuring knee function and quality of life improved from 32.20 before treatment to 61.20 at 1 month. It was 60.90 at 3 months and 57.50 at 6 months. The patient was sent home and is being seen at regular clinic visits. The patient has not reported any worsening of symptoms. A full checkup with pain scores, knee function scores, and imaging is planned in 6 months.

Recommendation:

The patient has been told to take steps to prevent pain, swelling, or stiffness in the joint. The patient has also been told to see the treating doctor regularly. This will help avoid any problems or further injury.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 61-year-old male with oral cavity SCC and previous surgical treatment of stage I disease in 2011, presented with cervical and left axillary lymphadenopathy. The patient had been lost to follow-up for over 4 years prior to this presentation. Biopsy of the lymph nodes revealed metastatic SCC. The patient was enrolled in a trial and treated with carboplatin, 5-FU, and pembrolizumab at a dose of 200 mg. After 4 cycles, the patient showed signs of partial response, but presented with fatigue and hypotension to systolic blood pressure of 70 seconds. The patient was diagnosed with immune-related central adrenal insufficiency and was started on high-dose dexamethasone and later transitioned to hydrocortisone. Brain MRI did not show any inflammation of the pituitary stalk and was without metastatic lesions. The patient's fatigue was resolved ten days later and was restarted on pembrolizumab. The patient continues to take hydrocortisone.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 61-year-old man with mouth cancer. He had surgery for early-stage cancer in 2011. He came back after not seeing doctors for over 4 years. He now had swollen lymph nodes in his neck and left armpit. A small tissue sample showed the cancer had spread to these lymph nodes. The patient joined a research study. He received three cancer drugs called carboplatin, 5-FU, and pembrolizumab at a dose of 200 mg. After 4 rounds of treatment, the cancer showed some shrinking. However, he came in feeling very tired and had low blood pressure with the top number at 70. Doctors found that his immune treatment caused his adrenal glands to stop working properly. These glands sit above the kidneys and make important hormones. He was given a strong steroid medicine called dexamethasone. Later, he switched to another steroid called hydrocortisone. A scan of his brain showed no swelling near the pituitary gland. The scan also showed no cancer spread to the brain. His tiredness went away after ten days. He then started taking pembrolizumab again. The patient continues to take hydrocortisone.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 40
Gender: Female
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
The patient was referred for an evaluation of right periorbital pain, right eye ptosis, and diplopia that had first appeared 20 days earlier. She had restricted adduction of the right eyeball. Initial MRIs showed enlargement of the right medial rectus muscle with mild contrast enhancement, and additional lesions in the right retrobulbar optic nerve sheath and preseptal area.

MEDICAL MANAGEMENT:
The initial diagnosis was orbital IIP, and the patient was started on 60 mg of prednisone daily for 10 days, which greatly improved her symptoms. The dosage was gradually reduced over the next 8 weeks, but her symptoms relapsed when prednisone was reduced to below 20 mg per day. Azathioprine and mycophenolate mofetil also could not be administered because of potential direct hepatotoxicity. The patient was started on 3 mg of tacrolimus while discontinuing prednisolone over 8 weeks. After 6 months of tacrolimus administration, the right periorbital pain had completely disappeared, and the restriction of right eye abduction had resolved. The second follow-up MRIs were obtained at 16 months after the onset, by which time the lesions had nearly resolved. Tacrolimus was gradually reduced over a period of 7 months before being discontinued, and the patient had no further relapse during the 1-year follow-up.

CLINICAL FINDINGS:
The patient had painful tenderness of the right medial orbital region. Routine serological tests, autoimmune screening tests, and CSF studies all produced normal findings. However, her hepatitis C virus RNA level was 1,724,673 IU/mL in the quantification test, indicating active hepatitis C.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient is advised to follow up with their healthcare provider for further management of hepatitis C. If any orbital IIP concerns arise, they are to contact their healthcare provider immediately.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 40
Gender: Female
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
The patient came in because of pain around the right eye, a drooping right eyelid, and double vision. These problems started 20 days before the visit. The patient had trouble moving the right eye toward the nose. Brain scans showed that a muscle controlling the right eye was swollen. The scans also showed other problem areas near the eye and behind it.

MEDICAL MANAGEMENT:
Doctors first thought the patient had swelling in the eye socket area. The patient started taking 60 mg of prednisone, a steroid medicine, every day for 10 days. This helped a lot. Doctors slowly lowered the dose over the next 8 weeks. But when the dose dropped below 20 mg per day, the symptoms came back. Two other medicines could not be used because they might harm the liver. The patient then started taking 3 mg of tacrolimus, a different medicine. The steroid was slowly stopped over 8 weeks. After 6 months on tacrolimus, the eye pain was completely gone. The patient could also move the right eye normally again. New brain scans done 16 months after symptoms first started showed the problem areas were almost gone. Doctors slowly reduced tacrolimus over 7 months and then stopped it. The patient had no more problems during the next year of checkups.

CLINICAL FINDINGS:
The patient had pain when the inner part of the right eye area was touched. Blood tests and tests for immune system problems came back normal. Fluid from around the spine was also tested and was normal. But a blood test showed the patient has an active hepatitis C infection. This is a virus that affects the liver.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should see their doctor to treat the hepatitis C. If any eye swelling problems come back, the patient should contact their doctor right away.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 50 years
Sex: Female
Race: South Indian

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the outpatient department with a productive cough with hemoptysis for the past 2 months. She had a history of pulmonary tuberculosis, which was discontinued within 3 weeks of starting the treatment. She also suffered from diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy for the past 8 years and was taking oral hypoglycemic agents for the same. The patient was diagnosed with an indwelling aspergilloma and was treated with an oral loading dose of Voriconazole followed by an oral maintenance dose. The patient responded well to the treatment, and the hemoptysis resolved after a lobectomy. After 3 weeks, the sputum became negative for acid-fast bacilli. The patient is currently in the 4th month of treatment and is doing well.

Laboratory Results:
Admission baseline investigations showed anemia, and the renal and liver function tests were within normal limits.

Diagnostic Study:
Computed tomogram of the thorax revealed a thick-walled cavity in the right lung upper lobe with an indwelling aspergilloma. 3 sputum samples were tested positive for acid-fast bacilli by Ziehl Neelsen's staining technique. Conventional method of culture on Lowenstein Jensen's medium yielded growth of M. tuberculosis. The anti-tuberculosis drug susceptibility test showed resistance to Isoniazid and Rifampicin but sensitive to Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin in their critical concentrations.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with pulmonary tuberculosis and an indwelling aspergilloma.

Treatment:
The patient was initially started on a daily dose of Isoniazid, Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin. However, after culture demonstrated MDR-TB, Isoniazid was stopped, and oral Levofloxacin and Ethionamide were included in the treatment regimen. Rifampicin was not included due to its interactions with Voriconazole. The patient was given an oral loading dose of Voriconazole 400 mg 12th hourly for 2 doses, which was followed by an oral maintenance dose of 200 mg 12th hourly for 6 weeks.

Follow-up:
After the treatment, the patient was counseled on the importance of adhering to medication and follow-up visits. She was advised to return to the hospital for follow-up visits and monitoring of symptoms.

Summary:
The 50-year-old South Indian woman presented with complaints of productive cough with hemoptysis for the past 2 months. She suffered from diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy and had a history of pulmonary tuberculosis. The patient was diagnosed with an indwelling aspergilloma and treated with an oral loading dose of Voriconazole followed by an oral maintenance dose. Hemoptysis resolved after lobectomy, and the sputum became negative for acid-fast bacilli after 3 weeks. The patient responded well to the treatment and is currently in the 4th month of treatment. The patient was advised to adhere to medication and follow-up visits.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 50 years
Sex: Female
Race: South Indian

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the clinic with a cough that brought up mucus and blood for the past 2 months. She had lung tuberculosis before but stopped taking her medicine within 3 weeks of starting it. She also has diabetes with nerve damage in her hands and feet for the past 8 years. She takes pills to control her blood sugar. The patient was found to have a fungus ball growing in her lung. She was treated with an antifungal medicine called Voriconazole, first at a higher dose, then at a lower dose. The patient got better with treatment. The coughing up of blood stopped after surgery to remove part of her lung. After 3 weeks, tests showed no more tuberculosis germs in her mucus. The patient is now in month 4 of treatment and is doing well.

Laboratory Results:
Tests done when the patient arrived showed low red blood cells. Her kidney and liver were working normally.

Diagnostic Study:
A CT scan of the chest showed a hollow space with thick walls in the upper part of the right lung. A fungus ball was growing inside this space. Three mucus samples tested positive for tuberculosis germs using a special staining test. Lab growth tests confirmed the germ was tuberculosis. Drug testing showed the tuberculosis was resistant to two main medicines, Isoniazid and Rifampicin. The tuberculosis could still be treated with Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with lung tuberculosis and a fungus ball in the lung.

Treatment:
The patient first started taking daily doses of Isoniazid, Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin. After tests showed drug-resistant tuberculosis, Isoniazid was stopped. Two new medicines, Levofloxacin and Ethionamide, were added. Rifampicin was not used because it does not work well with the antifungal medicine. For the fungus ball, the patient took Voriconazole 400 mg twice for the first day. Then she took 200 mg twice a day for 6 weeks.

Follow-up:
After treatment, the patient was told how important it is to take all medicines as directed. She was also told to come back to the hospital for check-up visits. The doctors will watch for any returning symptoms.

Summary:
The 50-year-old South Indian woman came in with a cough that brought up mucus and blood for 2 months. She has diabetes with nerve damage and had lung tuberculosis before. The patient was found to have a fungus ball in her lung. She was treated with the antifungal medicine Voriconazole, first at a higher dose, then at a lower dose. The coughing up of blood stopped after surgery to remove part of her lung. After 3 weeks, tests showed no more tuberculosis germs. The patient responded well to treatment and is now in month 4. She was told to keep taking her medicines and come back for follow-up visits.

For the fungus ball, the patient took Voriconazole 400 mg twice for the first day. Then she took 2000 mg twice a day for 6 weeks.

Follow-up:
After treatment, the patient was told how important it is to take all medicines as directed. She was also told to come back to the hospital for check-up visits. The doctors will watch for any returning symptoms.

Summary:
The 50-year-old South Indian woman came in with a cough that brought up mucus and blood for 2 months. She has diabetes with nerve damage and had lung tuberculosis before. The patient was found to have a fungus ball in her lung. She was treated with the antifungal medicine Voriconazole, first at a higher dose, then at a lower dose. The coughing up of blood stopped after surgery to remove part of her lung. After 3 weeks, tests showed no more tuberculosis germs. The patient responded well to treatment and is now in month 4. She was told to keep taking her medicines and come back for follow-up visits.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Sex: Male
Diagnosis: Mild Intellectual Disability with Paranoid Schizophrenia

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 28-year-old male, presented with irritable behavior, muttering to self, and hearing unreal voices of songs for 12 years. On MSE, delusion of reference, with 2nd person auditory hallucinations were present. IQ was found to be 54. Based on clinical evaluation, the patient was diagnosed with mild ID with Paranoid Schizophrenia. The patient was started on tablet olanzapine 10 mg and tablet sodium valproate 1000 mg with good response.

Patient Progress:
During the hospital stay, the patient showed significant improvement in symptoms of irritable behavior and auditory hallucinations. His sleep and appetite were also found to be improving. The patient was discharged after the treating physician deemed him stable and fit for discharge. The patient was advised to continue the prescribed medications, attend follow-up psychiatric visits, and adhere to the prescribed treatment plan.

Discharge Instructions:
The following discharge instructions were provided to the patient:
• Continue taking the prescribed medications regularly as per the dosage mentioned in the prescription.
• Attend scheduled follow-up psychiatric visits to monitor symptoms and progress.
• Notify the treating physician if any new symptoms or side effects occur.
• Adhere to the prescribed treatment plan including diet, exercise, and lifestyle modifications.
• In case of any emergency, contact the emergency services or visit the nearest hospital for medical assistance.

Follow-up Care:
The patient was advised to follow up with the treating psychiatrist as per the scheduled appointments. The patient and patient's family were informed about the importance of regular follow-up psychiatric visits to ensure the effectiveness of the treatment plan and to address any concerns or issues related to the patient's condition.

Summary:
The patient, a 28-year-old male with mild Intellectual Disability with Paranoid Schizophrenia, was discharged with significant improvement in symptoms. The treating physician advised the patient to continue taking the prescribed medications and attend follow-up psychiatric visits for continued monitoring. The patient and patient's family were educated about the importance of adhering to the prescribed treatment plan and following up with the treating physician regularly.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

DOB: [REDACTED]
Sex: Male
Diagnosis: Mild Learning Disability with a Mental Health Condition Causing False Beliefs and Hearing Things That Are Not Real

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 28-year-old male. He came to the hospital because he had been acting irritable and talking to himself for 12 years. He also heard songs that were not real. During the mental health exam, the patient believed that random events had special meaning about him. He also heard voices talking to him that were not real. Testing showed his thinking and learning skills are below average. Based on these findings, the doctors found he has a mild learning disability and a mental health condition. The patient was given olanzapine 10 mg pills and sodium valproate 1000 mg pills. These medicines worked well for him.

Patient Progress:
While in the hospital, the patient got much better. His irritable behavior and hearing things that are not real improved a lot. He also started sleeping and eating better. The doctor decided the patient was stable enough to go home. The patient was told to keep taking his medicines. He was also told to go to his follow-up mental health visits. He should follow the treatment plan given to him.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was given these instructions before leaving the hospital:
Keep taking the medicines as written on the prescription.
Go to all scheduled mental health visits so the doctor can check on symptoms and progress.
Tell the doctor if any new symptoms or side effects happen.
Follow the treatment plan including eating well, exercising, and making healthy lifestyle changes.
If there is an emergency, call emergency services or go to the nearest hospital for help.

Follow-up Care:
The patient should see his mental health doctor at the scheduled times. The patient and his family were told why regular mental health visits are important. These visits help make sure the treatment is working. They also help address any worries or problems with the patient's condition.

Summary:
The patient is a 28-year-old male with a mild learning disability and a mental health condition. He left the hospital feeling much better. The doctor told him to keep taking his medicines and go to his mental health visits. The patient and his family learned why it is important to follow the treatment plan. They also learned why regular visits with the doctor are needed.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Not specified
Medical Record Number: Not specified

Admission Date: Not specified
Discharge Date: Not specified

Admitting Diagnosis: Exudative AMD with Subfoveal PED and an Associated MH

Hospital Course: The 73-year-old Caucasian female was admitted with decreased vision and metamorphopsia for 6 months in her left eye. Initial examination showed best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) of 20/80 left eye (LE) and 20/25 right eye (RE). Fundus biomicroscopy revealed the presence of exudative AMD with a subfoveal PED and an associated MH. Spectral domain optical coherence tomography (SD-OCT) confirmed a full-thickness MH with attached edges and a partially detached posterior hyaloid on top of the prominent PED. Intravitreal anti-VEGF-therapy with three loading doses of a monthly bevacizumab (1.25 mg)-injections was started. After 4 weeks, the first injection resulted in the resolution of the PED with an approximation of the MH edges. However, BCVA decreased to 20/160. In the follow-up, the second injection was administered after four weeks, and SD-OCT showed complete closure of the MH along with marked visual improvement to 20/50. During further follow-up, PED recurred, but no reopening of the MH was observed. Continued intravitreal drug therapy with bevacizumab according to Pro Re Nata regimen led to resolution of PED and visual stabilization.

Discharge Diagnosis: Exudative AMD with Subfoveal PED and an Associated MH

Discharge Education: The patient was counseled on the importance of continued ophthalmic checkups and adherence to medication regimen. The potential side effects of the medication and the importance of promptly reporting any new symptoms were also discussed.

Follow-Up Care: The patient will follow-up with her ophthalmologist in three months.

Signed,

[Physician Name]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Not specified
Medical Record Number: Not specified

Admission Date: Not specified
Discharge Date: Not specified

Admitting Diagnosis: Wet age-related macular degeneration with a fluid-filled blister under the center of the retina and a related hole in the macula

Hospital Course: The 73-year-old Caucasian female was admitted with decreased vision and wavy or distorted vision for 6 months in her left eye. Initial examination showed the sharpest vision with glasses was 20/80 in the left eye and 20/25 in the right eye. A detailed eye exam showed wet age-related macular degeneration with a fluid-filled blister under the center of the retina and a related hole in the macula. A special imaging scan of the retina confirmed a hole going all the way through the macula with attached edges. It also showed a partly detached gel layer on top of the raised blister. Treatment with eye injections to stop abnormal blood vessel growth was started. The patient received three monthly injections of bevacizumab (1.25 mg) into the eye. After 4 weeks, the first injection made the fluid-filled blister go away and brought the edges of the hole closer together. However, vision got worse to 20/160. At the follow-up visit, the second injection was given after four weeks. The imaging scan showed the hole had closed completely and vision improved greatly to 20/50. During later follow-up visits, the fluid-filled blister came back, but the hole in the macula stayed closed. Ongoing eye injections with bevacizumab given as needed made the blister go away and kept vision stable.

Discharge Diagnosis: Wet age-related macular degeneration with a fluid-filled blister under the center of the retina and a related hole in the macula

Discharge Education: The patient was told about the importance of continued eye checkups and taking medication as directed. The possible side effects of the medication were explained. The patient was also told to report any new symptoms right away.

Follow-Up Care: The patient will follow-up with her eye doctor in three months.

Signed,

[Physician Name]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Patient: 15-year-old girl
- Diagnosis: Congenital Hyperinsulinism (CHI)
- Treatment: Diazoxide, Octreotide, Lanreotide

Hospital Course Summary:
- Patient with CHI was admitted electively to our pediatric inpatient unit to decide on an alternative medical treatment due to the troublesome hypertrichosis caused by diazoxide.
- Trial off diazoxide was performed whereby, diazoxide was gradually weaned and stopped for 72 hours. However, this led to subsequent development of recurrent symptomatic hypoglycaemia.
- Octreotide (a somatostatin analogue) was then commenced as four times daily subcutaneous injection.
- A good glycemic response was noted at this dose and she was able to tolerate a fast for a period of 24 hours with no evidence of hypoglycaemia.
- Lanreotide (long-acting somatostatin analogue) was commenced as a subcutaneous injection 30 mg once monthly and octreotide was gradually weaned and stopped.
- Continuous blood glucose monitoring system following the administration of lanreotide revealed good glycemic control with no episodes of hypoglycemia.
- Patient developed recurrent symptomatic hypoglycemic episodes after starting fluoxetine. Fluoxetine was discontinued and hypoglycemic episodes resolved within a week.
- Two weeks after the discontinuation of fluoxetine, the patient's depressive symptoms slowly improved over time with the help of counselling.

Final Diagnosis:
- Congenital Hyperinsulinism (CHI)

Follow-up Plan:
- No further specific follow-up needed at this time.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Patient: 15-year-old girl
Diagnosis: Congenital Hyperinsulinism (CHI), a condition where the body makes too much insulin, causing low blood sugar
Treatment: Diazoxide, Octreotide, Lanreotide

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient with CHI came to the children's hospital unit for a planned stay. The goal was to find a different medicine because diazoxide was causing unwanted hair growth.
The care team slowly lowered and then stopped diazoxide for 72 hours to see what would happen. However, this caused repeated episodes of low blood sugar with symptoms.
Octreotide was then started as a shot under the skin four times a day. This medicine helps control blood sugar.
The patient responded well to this dose. She was able to go without food for 24 hours with no low blood sugar.
Lanreotide was then started as a shot under the skin, 30 mg once a month. This is a long-acting medicine similar to octreotide. Octreotide was slowly lowered and then stopped.
A device that checks blood sugar all the time showed good blood sugar control after starting lanreotide. There were no episodes of low blood sugar.
The patient started having repeated low blood sugar episodes with symptoms after starting fluoxetine. Fluoxetine was stopped and the low blood sugar episodes went away within a week.
Two weeks after stopping fluoxetine, the patient's sad mood slowly got better over time with the help of talk therapy.

Final Diagnosis:
Congenital Hyperinsulinism (CHI)

Follow-up Plan:
No further specific follow-up needed at this time.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:
Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 67 years
Sex: Female

Chief Complaint:
Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon. Right hemicolectomy was performed, and the pathology confirmed stage III (pT3N1) adenocarcinoma. Adjuvant chemotherapy was initiated but was discontinued after four of eight cycles due to severe neuropathy. After completion of adjuvant treatment, the patient underwent active surveillance. In November 2012, a recurrence near the right psoas muscle was identified and excised in December. Immunohistochemical workup showed an abnormal mismatch repair profile with loss of MLH1 protein. The patient underwent radiotherapy concurrent with capecitabine, but again, significant neutropenia necessitated a capecitabine dose reduction. In October 2013, the patient relapsed with disease in the L3 spinous process and received 42 Gy of stereotactic radiotherapy in 10 fractions. In June 2014, the tumor recurred at the same site, and the patient underwent palliative resection of the mass.

Enter Personalized OncoGenomics (POG) initiative:
The patient consented to genomic analysis of the tumor resected from the L3 spinous process by the POG initiative at the BC Cancer Agency. The genomic analysis revealed overexpression of FOS and JUN genes that encode the AP-1 transcriptional complex. Immunohistochemical workup confirmed robust expression of c-JUN protein. It was suggested that irbesartan could be a potential option due to the blockade of the renin–angiotensin pathway.

Treatment:
The patient had a pretreatment baseline PET/CT scan and started irbesartan at a dose of 150 mg daily in December 2014. After 5 weeks of therapy, a follow-up PET/CT was performed, and there was virtually a complete functional radiological resolution of her disease. CEA levels decreased from 18 to 3.1.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has been discharged, and her condition is stable. Follow-up visits have been scheduled, and medication and dietary instructions have been provided.

Medications:
Irbesartan 150 mg daily.

Follow-up:
Follow-up appointments have been scheduled. The patient has been advised to adhere to a healthy diet and lifestyle and continue medication as prescribed.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:
Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 67 years
Sex: Female

Chief Complaint:
Medium-grade colon cancer in the right side of the colon.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with medium-grade cancer in the right side of the colon. Surgery was done to remove the right part of the colon. Lab tests confirmed stage III cancer that had spread to nearby lymph nodes. The patient started cancer-killing drug treatment but had to stop after four of eight rounds. This was because of serious nerve damage in the hands and feet. After finishing treatment, the patient had regular check-ups to watch for cancer return. In November 2012, the cancer came back near a muscle in the lower back area. It was removed by surgery in December. Special lab tests showed a problem with how the body fixes damaged genes. The patient received radiation treatment along with a cancer pill called capecitabine. But the patient's white blood cell count dropped too low. The dose of capecitabine had to be lowered. In October 2013, the cancer came back in a bone in the lower spine. The patient received focused radiation treatment in 10 sessions. In June 2014, the cancer returned in the same spot. The patient had surgery to remove the growth to help with symptoms.

Enter Personalized OncoGenomics (POG) initiative:
The patient agreed to have the tumor from the spine tested for gene changes. This was done by the POG program at the BC Cancer Agency. The gene tests showed that certain genes called FOS and JUN were too active. These genes control cell growth. Lab tests confirmed high levels of a protein called c-JUN. Doctors suggested trying a blood pressure drug called irbesartan. This drug might help by blocking a body system that could be helping the cancer grow.

Treatment:
The patient had a full body scan before starting treatment. The patient began taking irbesartan at 150 mg once a day in December 2014. After 5 weeks of treatment, another full body scan was done. The scan showed the cancer was almost completely gone. A blood test that measures cancer activity dropped from 18 to 3.1.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Medium-grade colon cancer in the right side of the colon.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has been discharged, and her condition is stable. Follow-up visits have been scheduled. Instructions for medicine and diet have been given.

Medications:
Irbesartan 150 mg once a day.

Follow-up:
Follow-up appointments have been scheduled. The patient has been told to eat healthy foods and live a healthy lifestyle. The patient should keep taking medicine as directed.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Patient Name]
Medical Record Number: [Medical Record Number]
Date of Admission: [Date of Admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital complaining of abdominal pain for 2 days duration that was sharp, intermittent, and radiating to the left abdomen, back and groin. Physical examination revealed mild tenderness at the epigastrium. Laboratory tests showed a white blood cell count of 6.2 × 103/µL, hemoglobin of 13.4 g/dL, hematocrit of 41%, and platelet count of 168 × 103/µL. The patient underwent a CT scan of abdomen and pelvis which showed an 8.2 × 2.4 × 3.6 cm fluid collection adjacent to the tail of the pancreas suspicious for a pseudocyst. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed a complex cystic mass lesion that could not be separated from the pancreatic tail. The patient underwent an upper endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) with fine-needle aspiration (FNA). FNA revealed a muddy yellow fluid aspirate, drained until the cyst collapsed. Immunohistopathology revealed numerous mature squamous cells, morphologically consistent with a pancreatic dermoid cyst.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was admitted to the hospital presenting with abdominal pain of 2 days duration. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed a fluid collection adjacent to the tail of the pancreas. Subsequent imaging with MRI showed a complex cystic mass lesion that could not be separated from the pancreatic tail. An upper endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) with fine-needle aspiration (FNA) was performed, and immunohistopathology revealed numerous mature squamous cells consistent with a pancreatic dermoid cyst. The cyst was successfully drained until it collapsed.

Diagnoses:

- Pancreatic Dermoid Cyst

Medical Management:

- None required.

Follow-Up:

- The patient is to follow-up with their primary care physician in 2-4 weeks for further evaluation.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Patient Name]
Medical Record Number: [Medical Record Number]
Date of Admission: [Date of Admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with belly pain that had lasted 2 days. The pain was sharp, came and went, and spread to the left side of the belly, back, and groin area. The doctor pressed on the belly and found mild soreness in the upper middle area. Blood tests showed normal results for white blood cells at 6.2 × 103/µL, red blood cell protein at 13.4 g/dL, red blood cell volume at 41%, and clotting cells at 168 × 103/µL. The patient had a CT scan of the belly and lower body area. The scan showed a pocket of fluid measuring 8.2 × 2.4 × 3.6 cm next to the tail end of the pancreas. Doctors thought it might be a false cyst. The patient also had an MRI scan. This showed a complex sac filled with fluid that could not be told apart from the tail of the pancreas. The patient had a special scope test called an upper endoscopic ultrasound with a needle to take a sample. The needle pulled out muddy yellow fluid until the sac emptied and shrank down. Lab tests on the fluid showed many normal flat skin cells. This meant the patient had a type of harmless growth called a dermoid cyst on the pancreas.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient came to the hospital with belly pain that had lasted 2 days. A CT scan of the belly and lower body showed a pocket of fluid next to the tail of the pancreas. An MRI scan showed a complex sac filled with fluid that could not be told apart from the tail of the pancreas. A special scope test with a needle was done to take a sample. Lab tests showed many normal flat skin cells that matched a dermoid cyst on the pancreas. The sac was drained until it emptied and shrank down.

Diagnoses:

Dermoid Cyst on the Pancreas (a harmless growth containing skin-like tissue)

Medical Management:

None needed.

Follow-Up:

The patient should see their regular doctor in 2 to 4 weeks for a checkup.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient name: [insert name]
Admission date: [insert date]
Discharge date: [insert date]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 58-year-old male laborer, presented with a rough scaly plaque on the right lower back. He had a history of a small annular lesion that spread peripherally to a larger plaque, which remained static for seven years before suddenly spreading in the last two years. The patient often worked with bare upper body for long hours and experienced itching and burning sensation in high mercury conditions.

Investigations:
The patients were thoroughly investigated, with all results within normal limits. The patients were negative for HIV and immunocompetent. The provisional diagnosis of porokeratosis was confirmed with biopsy specimens revealing cornoid lamellae, absent granular, and chronic inflammatory cells in papillary dermis.

Treatment:
The patients were advised to wear protective clothing and were prescribed capsule isotretinoin 20mg and halobetasol 0.05% for topical application at night. The patient who experienced itching and burning under the sun underwent partial excision, cryotherapy, and subsequently applied 0.05% tretinoin cream and 5% fluorouracil cream alternate nights. The patient experienced side effects of erythema, pruritus, oozing, crustation, and edema, after every 3-4 weeks, but ultimately showed moderate improvement over five months of treatment.

Follow-up:
One of the three patients was lost to follow up and the other two did not show any further signs of the condition. The patient who experienced side effects from treatment was lost to follow up due to the severity and frequency of side effects.

Diagnosis:
Porokeratosis

Instructions:
The patient is advised to wear protective clothing to avoid direct sunlight exposure. The patient should continue taking capsule isotretinoin 20mg and applying halobetasol 0.05% as prescribed.

Discharge Summary

Patient name: [insert name]
Admission date: [insert date]
Discharge date: [insert date]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 58-year-old male worker. He came in with a rough, scaly patch of skin on his right lower back. The patch started as a small ring-shaped spot. It grew outward into a larger patch. The patch stayed the same size for seven years. Then it started spreading again in the last two years. The patient often worked without a shirt for long hours. He felt itching and burning in hot weather.

Investigations:
The patients had many tests done. All test results came back normal. The patients tested negative for HIV. Their immune systems were working well. Doctors thought the skin problem was porokeratosis. A skin sample test confirmed this. The sample showed special features in the skin layers that matched this condition.

Treatment:
The patients were told to wear clothes that protect from the sun. They were given isotretinoin 20mg pills to take by mouth. They were also given halobetasol 0.05% cream to put on the skin at night. The patient with itching and burning in the sun had part of the patch removed. He also had freezing treatment. After that, he put on tretinoin 0.05% cream and fluorouracil 5% cream on different nights. This patient had side effects every 3-4 weeks. These included redness, itching, oozing, crusting, and swelling. After five months, the skin showed fair improvement.

Follow-up:
One of the three patients stopped coming to appointments. The other two patients showed no more signs of the skin problem. The patient who had side effects from treatment stopped coming because the side effects were bad and happened often.

Diagnosis:
Porokeratosis (a skin condition that causes rough, scaly patches)

Instructions:
The patient should wear clothes that protect the skin from direct sunlight. The patient should keep taking isotretinoin 20mg pills. The patient should keep using halobetasol 0.05% cream as directed.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital with painful bilateral ecchymoses, fatigue, shortness of breath with exertion, and lightheadedness. Laboratory values revealed anemia with hyponatremia, hypokalemia, and hypochloremia. Elevated LDH and total bilirubin were observed. In addition, the patient presented with corkscrew hairs and perifollicular petechiae. The patient was diagnosed with vitamin C deficiency and received 1 g IV vitamin C for three days. His bleeding, leg pain, and bruising improved. He was discharged on oral supplementation of vitamin C 250 mg daily and received nutritional education. Follow-up care was recommended.

Diagnosis:

Vitamin C deficiency

Stable

Routine follow-up care was recommended.

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with painful bruises on both legs, tiredness, trouble breathing during activity, and dizziness. Blood tests showed low red blood cells, low sodium, low potassium, and low chloride levels. Tests also showed signs that red blood cells were breaking down. The patient also had coiled hairs and tiny red spots around hair roots. The patient was found to have low vitamin C levels. The patient received vitamin C through a vein for three days. The bleeding, leg pain, and bruising got better. The patient was sent home with vitamin C pills to take by mouth, 250 mg once a day. The patient also learned about eating healthy foods. A follow-up visit was suggested.
