input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital following a stab wound to the left chest. He was transferred to the emergency department with hypotension, tachycardia, tachypnea, and Glasgow Coma Scale score of 14. The initial FAST revealed a pleural effusion in the left thorax. A chest drain was inserted, and more than 700 mL of blood was drained. Subsequent FAST had revealed a pericardial effusion.

A Clamshell thoracotomy was started due to the patient's unstable hemodynamic status, and surgical exploration showed a massive hemothorax in the left thorax, left lung posterior lobe injury, pericardial injury, and hemopericardium with blood clots. A 10-mm defect was found in the right ventricle near the left anterior descending coronary artery. Chemical cardiac arrest was induced using ATP, and the cardiac wall was repaired surgically using a horizontal mattress suture. The patient recovered after the surgery and received intensive care in the hospital for 13 days.

Discharge Summary:

The patient was discharged after a 13-day hospital stay without complications. The postoperative echocardiographic check-ups showed a septal injury, but the patient did not exhibit any symptoms of heart failure.

The patient should avoid strenuous activities and get plenty of rest for at least two weeks. It is essential to monitor for signs of infection, such as fever, inflammation, and excessive wound discharge. The patient should report chest pain, shortness of breath, or lightheadedness immediately.

Follow-up care should include a visit with a primary care provider or specialist, depending on the patient's condition, within one week. They should return to the hospital immediately if new symptoms appear or existing symptoms worsen.

The patient's condition is stable currently, and they have been discharged home with follow-up treatment as suggested above.
clean_ref:
Hospital Stay:

The patient came to the hospital after being stabbed in the left side of the chest. When he arrived at the emergency room, his blood pressure was very low. His heart was beating too fast, and he was breathing too fast. He was also slightly less alert than normal. A quick scan showed fluid had collected around his left lung. Doctors put in a tube to drain the fluid, and more than 700 mL of blood came out. Another scan showed fluid had also collected around his heart.

Doctors had to open the patient's chest because his condition was not stable. During surgery, they found a large amount of blood in the left chest area. They also found damage to the back part of the left lung and the sac around the heart. Blood and clots had built up around the heart. There was a small hole in the right side of the heart near a major blood vessel. Doctors used medicine to temporarily stop the heart so they could fix the hole with stitches. The patient recovered after surgery and stayed in the intensive care unit for 13 days.

Discharge Summary:

The patient left the hospital after 13 days with no problems. Heart scans after surgery showed some damage to the wall between the heart chambers. However, the patient did not show any signs that the heart was not pumping well.

The patient should not do hard physical activities and should rest for at least two weeks. It is important to watch for signs of infection, such as fever, redness or swelling, and fluid leaking from the wound. The patient should tell a doctor right away about any chest pain, trouble breathing, or feeling dizzy.

The patient should see a regular doctor or heart doctor within one week, depending on how he is doing. He should go back to the hospital right away if new problems start or current problems get worse.

The patient is doing well right now and has gone home with the care plan described above.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission:
A 43-year-old male with primary hypothyroidism was admitted for evaluation of progressive fatigue, weakness, muscle and joint pain in the setting of recurrent fractures.

History of Present Illness:
The patient had been experiencing musculoskeletal pain in several locations including bilateral knees, ankles, left shoulder, left arm, and lower back which affected his overall functional status. He struggled to climb a flight of stairs, often grabbing the railing to pull himself up. A nuclear medicine bone scan showed abnormal uptake in bilateral ribs, vertebrae, right knee, hips, ankles, and feet. A lower extremity MRI showed stress fractures in left fibula, fifth metatarsal, and right tibia.

Course in Hospital:
The patient was started on calcitriol and phosphate supplements which significantly improved muscle weakness. Due to concern for TIO, a positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) scan with 68Gallium (68Ga)-DOTATATE was performed which showed multiple lytic osseous lesions with increased somatostatin positive uptake seen throughout pelvis, ribs, ankles, and feet. However, after re-review with radiology, multiple areas of increased uptake were considered to be secondary to fractures, and left posterior acetabular lesion was presumed to be the tumor of interest given a remarkably higher standard uptake values (SUVmax) in this lesion compared to other lesions (SUVmax 20 vs. 4–5).

Procedure:
The left acetabular lesion was biopsied and pathology demonstrated a 1.4 cm phosphaturic mesenchymal tumor of the left acetabulum with uninvolved margins. The patient underwent radical resection of the tumor of the posterior left acetabulum with reconstruction of the posterior wall and posterior column of the acetabulum through a Kocher-Langenbach approach. His hip joint was able to be spared utilizing a 3D-printed custom bone cutting guide (CG) and intra-operative O-arm guided surgical navigation (Medtronic© Stealth) utilizing a navigated Sonopet Ultrasonic Bone Cutting Bovie (Stryker). The posterior wall and column of his acetabulum were constructed with a tantalum augment (Zimmer) and a pelvic reconstruction plate and screw construct (Synthes) after tumor removal.

Discharge Summary:
The patient was discharged with normal phosphorus and FGF23 levels, pain-free left hip, no evidence of hardware complication, back to hiking, and walking on the treadmill. Clinical and radiographic follow-up at 6 months postoperatively found him to be pain-free in his left hip, with almost complete resolution of bone pain, and no recurrence of fractures. Phosphorus level also remained normal at 3.4 mg/dL 6 months after surgery.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission:
A 43-year-old male with an underactive thyroid was admitted. Doctors needed to find out why he had ongoing tiredness, weakness, muscle and joint pain, and broken bones that kept happening.

History of Present Illness:
The patient had muscle and bone pain in many areas. These included both knees, both ankles, left shoulder, left arm, and lower back. This pain made it hard for him to do daily activities. He had trouble walking up stairs and had to pull himself up using the railing. A bone scan showed problem areas in both sides of his ribs, spine, right knee, hips, ankles, and feet. An imaging test of his lower legs showed small cracks in the left calf bone, a foot bone, and right shin bone.

Course in Hospital:
The patient was given vitamin D and phosphorus supplements. These greatly improved his muscle weakness. Doctors thought a rare tumor might be causing his problems. A special full-body scan was done to look for it. The scan showed many bone spots with holes that were picking up the scan marker. These spots were in the pelvis, ribs, ankles, and feet. After doctors looked at the scan again, most of these spots seemed to be from broken bones. One spot on the left hip socket stood out. It picked up much more of the scan marker than the other spots. This spot was most likely the tumor causing his problems.

Procedure:
Doctors took a small sample from the left hip socket spot. Testing showed a small tumor measuring 1.4 cm. This type of tumor causes the body to lose phosphorus. The edges of the sample were free of tumor cells. The patient had surgery to fully remove the tumor from the back of his left hip socket. Surgeons rebuilt the back wall and back support of the hip socket. They used a special approach from behind the hip. The hip joint itself was saved. Surgeons used a custom three-dimensional printed guide to cut the bone precisely. They also used a computer system to guide them during surgery. A special ultrasonic bone-cutting tool was used. After removing the tumor, the back wall and support were rebuilt. Surgeons used a metal support piece and a plate with screws made for pelvis repair.

Discharge Summary:
The patient went home with normal phosphorus levels. His hormone levels that control phosphorus were also normal. His left hip had no pain. The metal parts in his hip showed no problems. He was back to hiking and walking on a treadmill. At a checkup six months after surgery, he had no left hip pain. Almost all of his bone pain was gone. He had no new broken bones. His phosphorus level stayed normal at 3.4 mg per deciliter six months after surgery.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [omitted]
Age: 60
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: Brachial plexopathy
Length of Stay: [omitted]

HOSPITAL COURSE

This patient was admitted to our facility for an intense pruritus on her right arm, which had been ongoing for six months. She also complained of right brachial paresis.

COMORBIDITIES

The patient has a known history of multiple myeloma.

DIAGNOSTIC TESTS AND PROCEDURES

Computed tomography of the cervical spine, magnetic resonance imaging, and electromyography of the brachial plexus were performed, which showed right cervical disc herniation with nerve root compression of C6-C7 and right brachial plexus inflammation (Parsonage-Turner syndrome).

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS

The patient was treated with methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and oral hydroxyzine 25mg every 8 hours, which resulted in little improvement of the pruritus. Gabapentin was introduced at 900mg/day, which achieved optimal control after three months of follow-up.

FOLLOW-UP INSTRUCTIONS

The patient was referred to neurosurgery and oncology consultation for additional therapeutic orientation.

SUMMARY

The patient presented with brachial plexopathy, which is attributed to neoplastic infiltration of multiple myeloma. The treatment plan included the application of methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and oral hydroxyzine 25mg every 8 hours, along with the introduction of gabapentin at 900mg/day. The patient was referred to neurosurgery and oncology for further management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [omitted]
Age: 60
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: Nerve damage in the shoulder and arm area
Length of Stay: [omitted]

HOSPITAL COURSE

This patient was admitted to our facility for severe itching on her right arm. This problem had been going on for six months. She also had weakness in her right arm.

COMORBIDITIES

The patient has a known history of multiple myeloma. This is a type of blood cancer.

DIAGNOSTIC TESTS AND PROCEDURES

A CT scan of the neck, an MRI, and a nerve test of the shoulder and arm area were performed. These tests showed a bulging disc in the right side of the neck. This disc was pressing on nerves at the C6-C7 level. The tests also showed swelling of the nerves in the right shoulder and arm area. This condition is called Parsonage-Turner syndrome.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS

The patient was treated with methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1%. This is a steroid cream. She also took hydroxyzine 25mg by mouth every 8 hours. This helped only a little with the itching. Gabapentin was added at 900mg per day. After three months, this medicine controlled the itching well.

FOLLOW-UP INSTRUCTIONS

The patient was sent to see a brain and spine surgeon. She was also sent to see a cancer doctor for more treatment advice.

SUMMARY

The patient had nerve damage in the shoulder and arm area. This was caused by the spread of her blood cancer. The treatment plan included methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and hydroxyzine 25mg by mouth every 8 hours. Gabapentin at 900mg per day was also added. The patient was sent to a brain and spine surgeon and a cancer doctor for more care.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, an 81-year-old female with a significant history of prediabetes and end stage renal disease on hemodialysis, underwent coronary artery bypass graft surgery and replacement of mitral valve with postoperative complications. The patient presented with a dehiscence of the sternal wound, which required wound vacuum assisted closure, as well as a sacral decubitus ulcer, and multiple episodes of sepsis and respiratory failure.

Assessment and Plan:

During the first 45 days of admission, the patient's blood glucose was well-controlled with only 2 units total of sliding scale insulin required. However, soon after the start of intravenous ascorbic acid to aid in sternal wound healing, the patient experienced multiple high FSBG levels over 200 mg/dL, resulting in a total of 9 units of insulin treatment in 24 hours and subsequent FSBG levels remaining high over the next few days. Further investigation revealed a marked discrepancy of greater than 100 mg/dL between simultaneous FSBG and BGMP readings, with BGMP readings ranging from 72 to 146 mg/dL. Given the suspicion for falsely high FSBG readings caused by intravenous ascorbic acid, it was discontinued and FSBG values returned to normal. The patient did not experience hypoglycemia while receiving unnecessary insulin treatment.

Summary:

This discharge summary documents the hospital course of an elderly female with a history of renal disease and prediabetes who underwent coronary artery bypass graft surgery and replacement of mitral valve, complicated by postoperative complications. The patient had high FSBG levels after starting intravenous ascorbic acid, which were believed to be falsely elevated, resulting in unnecessary insulin treatment. The FSBG values returned to normal after discontinuation of intravenous ascorbic acid.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is an 81-year-old woman with a history of prediabetes and kidney failure requiring dialysis. She had heart bypass surgery and a heart valve replacement. After surgery, she had several problems. Her chest wound opened up and needed special vacuum bandages to help it heal. She also had a pressure sore on her lower back. She had several infections that spread to her blood and had trouble breathing on her own.

Assessment and Plan:

For the first 45 days in the hospital, the patient's blood sugar was well controlled. She only needed 2 units of insulin total during that time. However, soon after starting vitamin C through an IV to help heal her chest wound, her blood sugar readings became very high. The readings went above 200. She needed 9 units of insulin in one day, and her blood sugar stayed high for several days. Doctors then found a big difference between two ways of measuring blood sugar. The difference was more than 100 points. One method showed high readings. The other method showed normal readings between 72 and 146. Doctors believed the vitamin C was causing false high readings on one type of test. They stopped the vitamin C, and her blood sugar readings went back to normal. The patient did not have low blood sugar from getting insulin she did not need.

Summary:

This discharge summary describes the hospital stay of an elderly woman with kidney disease and prediabetes. She had heart bypass surgery and a heart valve replacement with problems after surgery. Her blood sugar readings became high after starting vitamin C through an IV. Doctors believe these readings were falsely high. This led to insulin treatment she did not need. Her blood sugar readings returned to normal after stopping the vitamin C.

input:
Discharge Summary
Patient: 39-year-old male

Medical History:
The patient had a history of allergic rhinitis. He reported many episodes of lightheadedness and patches of urticaria, with chest tightness that happened either with or shortly after exercise over the previous 3 months. Over time, the episodes had become more frequent and severe and required several treatments to relieve his symptoms. The patient was diagnosed with eosinophilic esophagitis via biopsy. 

Hospital Course:
The patient was given several medications to treat his throat and chest tightness with exercise, but they did not completely resolve his symptoms. He later tried to avoid wheat and all food for up to 12 hours before exercise but to no avail.

To manage his condition, the patient elected to start a trial of omalizumab, which was given in doses of 300 mg every 2 weeks. No food restrictions were given during this time. The patient reported marked improvement, and he was able to exercise three times a week without any symptoms. He had only one episode of mild urticaria, but it happened when he had missed one of his omalizumab injections. The patient continued to exercise normally, reporting any symptoms that he had and was followed up monthly.

The patient later experienced episodes of urticaria, markedly increased chest tightness, and periorbital edema after at least 15 minutes of exercise. He ultimately had an episode of anaphylaxis with urticaria, difficulty breathing, and facial swelling and was admitted to the emergency department, where he received several treatments to relieve his symptoms. After this episode, omalizumab was restarted at the same doses and intervals.

Follow-up and Outcome:
The patient continued on omalizumab with no further episodes of anaphylaxis and no restrictions on his activity level. He is expected to continue being followed up monthly and continue exercising normally.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary
Patient: 39-year-old male

Medical History:
The patient had a history of hay fever. He reported many episodes of dizziness and patches of hives, with chest tightness that happened either with or shortly after exercise over the previous 3 months. Over time, the episodes had become more frequent and severe and required several treatments to relieve his symptoms. The patient was diagnosed with a condition that causes swelling in the food pipe due to a type of white blood cell. This was confirmed through a small tissue sample.

Hospital Course:
The patient was given several medications to treat his throat and chest tightness with exercise, but they did not completely resolve his symptoms. He later tried to avoid wheat and all food for up to 12 hours before exercise but to no avail.

To manage his condition, the patient chose to start a trial of omalizumab, which was given in doses of 300 mg every 2 weeks. No food restrictions were given during this time. The patient reported major improvement, and he was able to exercise three times a week without any symptoms. He had only one episode of mild hives, but it happened when he had missed one of his omalizumab injections. The patient continued to exercise normally, reporting any symptoms that he had and was followed up monthly.

The patient later experienced episodes of hives, greatly increased chest tightness, and swelling around the eyes after at least 15 minutes of exercise. He ultimately had an episode of a severe allergic reaction with hives, difficulty breathing, and facial swelling and was admitted to the emergency room, where he received several treatments to relieve his symptoms. After this episode, omalizumab was restarted at the same doses and intervals.

Follow-up and Outcome:
The patient continued on omalizumab with no further episodes of severe allergic reactions and no restrictions on his activity level. He is expected to continue being followed up monthly and continue exercising normally.

Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]

Discharge Date: [Date]

Patient Name: [Name]

Medical Record Number: [Number]

Age: 32

Sex: Female

Date of Birth: [Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Second-stage uterine prolapse

Chief Complaint: Intermittent vaginal blood spotting

Hospital Course: 

The patient was admitted at 28+0 weeks of gestation with complaints of intermittent vaginal blood spotting and diagnosed with second-stage uterine prolapse. Pelvic examination revealed point C as the leading edge according to the pelvic organ prolapse quantification staging system. Sonographic examination revealed a short cervix with 17 mm length and a T-shape. A progesterone capsule was administered vaginally daily.

Two weeks later, at 30+0 weeks of gestation, the patient was admitted again because of regular uterine contractions. She was diagnosed with second-degree uterine prolapse. A more shortened cervix with 10-mm length and a T-shape was noted on sonographic examination. To control preterm uterine contractions, tocolytic therapy was initiated with a favorable outcome. Corticosteroids were also administered. The patient was advised to rest in bed with a suggestion of a slight Trendelenburg position. Vaginal culture revealed mycoplasma infection, which was treated with a single dose of azithromycin 1,000 mg.

At 34+0 weeks of gestation, uterine contractions disappeared without tocolytics, and the patient was discharged. However, she was readmitted at 37+0 weeks of gestation due to regular uterine contractions. The cervix was still prolapsed at the same level and the cervical orifice was opened to about 3 cm width. After a latency period, a healthy male neonate was delivered with an estimated blood loss of about 400 mL, which was not different from that of a normal pregnancy. Apgar scores were 10 at 1 minute and 10 at 5 minutes. 

The prolapsed uterus was manually reduced, and the postnatal period was uneventful. Two days after delivery, the patient was discharged in good health with complete resolution of the cervical prolapse. Follow-up examination 1 and 6 months postpartum revealed no evidence of uterine prolapse.

Discharge Diagnosis: Second-stage uterine prolapse

Condition at Discharge: Good

Discharge Instructions:

- Follow up with obstetrician as recommended for postpartum care.
- Avoid heavy lifting for at least 6 weeks.
- Resume normal activities gradually as tolerated.
- Review vaginal hygiene practices with a healthcare provider.
- Seek medical attention immediately if vaginal bleeding or abnormal discharge occurs.
- Take prescribed medications as directed.
- Follow a healthy diet and maintain a regular exercise routine.

Primary Care Provider: [Name]

Follow-Up Appointment: [Date and Time]

Follow-Up Phone Number: [Number]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]

Discharge Date: [Date]

Patient Name: [Name]

Medical Record Number: [Number]

Age: 32

Sex: Female

Date of Birth: [Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Uterus slipping down into the vagina (second stage)

Chief Complaint: Occasional vaginal bleeding

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital at 28 weeks of pregnancy. She had occasional vaginal bleeding. Doctors found that her uterus had slipped down into the vagina (second stage). An exam showed the lowest part of the uterus had dropped down. An ultrasound showed her cervix was short at 17 mm and shaped like a T. She was given a hormone capsule to place in the vagina each day.

Two weeks later, at 30 weeks of pregnancy, the patient came back. She was having regular tightening of the uterus. Her cervix was now even shorter at 10 mm. Doctors gave her medicine to stop early labor, and it worked well. She also received medicine to help the baby's lungs develop faster. The patient was told to stay in bed with her feet slightly higher than her head. A test found a vaginal infection. It was treated with a single dose of azithromycin 1,000 mg.

At 34 weeks of pregnancy, the tightening stopped without medicine. The patient went home. She came back at 37 weeks because the tightening started again. Her cervix was still slipping down and had opened to about 3 cm. After a waiting period, she gave birth to a healthy baby boy. She lost about 400 mL of blood, which is normal for childbirth. The baby scored 10 out of 10 on health tests at 1 minute and 5 minutes after birth.

The doctor gently pushed the uterus back into place by hand. Recovery after birth went smoothly. Two days after giving birth, the patient went home in good health. Her cervix was no longer slipping down. Checkups at 1 month and 6 months after birth showed no signs of the uterus slipping down.

Discharge Diagnosis: Uterus slipping down into the vagina (second stage)

Condition at Discharge: Good

Discharge Instructions:

Follow up with the pregnancy doctor as recommended for care after birth.
Do not lift heavy things for at least 6 weeks.
Slowly return to normal activities as the patient feels able.
Talk to a healthcare provider about keeping the vaginal area clean.
Get medical help right away if vaginal bleeding or unusual discharge happens.
Take all prescribed medicines as directed.
Eat healthy foods and exercise regularly.

Primary Care Provider: [Name]

Follow-Up Appointment: [Date and Time]

Follow-Up Phone Number: [Number]

Hospital Course Summary:

This is a hospital course summary for a 15-year-old girl who presented with oliguria and gross hematuria of 20 days' duration after catching a cold. She was diagnosed with ANCA-associated CGN with immune complex deposits, and the origin of thrombocytosis was unknown.

Treatment:
During her hospitalization, the patient received intermittent hemodialysis 3 times a week, pulse therapy with Methylprednisolone and Cyclophosphamide, and plasma exchange in conjunction with other supportive treatments such as blood pressure control and gastric mucosa protection.

Outcome:
When the patient was discharged, her serum creatinine fell to 313.8 μmol/L with a platelet count of 439 × 10^9 L. The patient continued pulse therapy with Cyclophosphamide at regular intervals for 3 times and until now, her platelet count has not gone beyond 500 × 10^9 L.

Diagnosis:
The final diagnosis was RT. 

Follow-up:
The patient underwent a follow-up of 6 months, and her platelet count remained within the normal range.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

This is a hospital course summary for a 15-year-old girl who came to the hospital because she had very little urine output and blood in her urine for 20 days after having a cold. She was found to have a type of kidney disease where the body's immune system attacks the small blood vessels in the kidneys. The cause of her high platelet count was not known.

Treatment:
During her hospital stay, the patient had a treatment to clean her blood 3 times a week. She also received high doses of two medicines through an IV to calm her immune system. These medicines were Methylprednisolone and Cyclophosphamide. She also had a treatment to filter and replace part of her blood. Other treatments helped control her blood pressure and protect her stomach lining.

Outcome:
When the patient left the hospital, her kidney waste levels had dropped to 313.8 units per liter. Her platelet count was 439 billion per liter. The patient kept getting high-dose Cyclophosphamide treatments at regular times for 3 more rounds. Up until now, her platelet count has stayed below 500 billion per liter.

Diagnosis:
The final diagnosis was RT.

Follow-up:
The patient was checked on for 6 months after leaving the hospital. Her platelet count stayed within the normal range during this time.

Discharge Summary

Patient name: [Name]
Medical record number: [Number]
Date of admission: [Date]
Date of discharge: [Date]

Hospital Course:
[Name], a 48-year-old female with polycystic liver and kidney diseases, was admitted to our hospital for surgical treatment. She had been followed up for 5 years at another institution and complained of progressive distension of the abdomen and abdominal fullness leading to gradual decrease in body weight without shortness of breath.

Upon admission, the patient was observed regularly without considering palliative surgery or liver transplantation. During the outpatient clinic follow-up for 1 year, the patient's condition worsened with increasing fatigue and dyspnea on exertion. Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status worsened from 2 to 3.

Medical Treatment:
Medical treatment with somatostatin analogue was not considered primarily due to the high medical cost as well as the undetermined therapeutic efficacy. After obtaining consent from the patient on the uncertain effect of fenestration treatment as well as high risk of disease recurrence, the patient underwent surgical fenestration treatment. Multiple cysts in the enlarged liver were opened, and the cyst walls were excised with electrocautery.

At the end of the fenestration procedure, the liver appeared to be markedly shrunken, and three Jackson-Pratt type drains were inserted to evacuate the ascitic fluid. After performing follow-up CT at 1 week, the drains were removed. The total liver volume by using CT volumetry was 3,870 ml before surgery and 3,125 ml at 1 week after surgery, showing a volume reduction of 19.3%.

Clinical Outcome:
After the surgery, ECOG performance status improved to 1 at 1 month and to 0 at 1 year. Gastrointestinal Quality of Life Index (GIQLI) score was significantly increased to 122 after 1 month. During regular follow-up at an interval of 6 months, follow-up CT showed no progression in cyst size and flat abdomen was observed. At 18 months after surgery, the patient is doing well and performing her normal activities with no evidence of disease recurrence.

Conclusion:
In summary, the patient underwent surgical fenestration treatment for polycystic liver and kidney diseases, resulting in significant improvement in clinical outcomes with no evidence of disease recurrence.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient name: [Name]
Medical record number: [Number]
Date of admission: [Date]
Date of discharge: [Date]

Hospital Course:
[Name] is a 48-year-old woman with a condition that causes many cysts to grow in the liver and kidneys. The patient was admitted to our hospital for surgery. The patient had been seen at another hospital for 5 years. The patient had a swelling belly that felt full. This caused the patient to slowly lose weight. The patient did not have trouble breathing.

When the patient first came in, the doctors watched the patient closely. They did not plan for surgery to ease symptoms or a liver transplant at that time. Over the next year of clinic visits, the patient got worse. The patient felt more tired and had trouble breathing during activity. The patient's ability to do daily tasks dropped from being able to do some things to being able to do very little.

Medical Treatment:
A medicine that slows cyst growth was not used. This was because it costs a lot and may not work well. The doctors told the patient that the surgery might not work and that cysts could come back. The patient agreed to have surgery. During surgery, the doctors opened many cysts in the large liver. They cut away the cyst walls using a tool that burns tissue.

After opening the cysts, the liver looked much smaller. The doctors put in three tubes to drain fluid from the belly. One week after surgery, the patient had a scan. Then the tubes were removed. The liver was measured before and after surgery. Before surgery, the liver was 3,870 ml. One week after surgery, it was 3,125 ml. This means the liver got 19.3% smaller.

Clinical Outcome:
After surgery, the patient got much better at doing daily tasks. At 1 month, the patient could do most things. At 1 year, the patient could do everything normally. The patient's quality of life score went up a lot to 122 after 1 month. The patient came back every 6 months for check-ups. Scans showed the cysts did not grow back. The patient's belly stayed flat. At 18 months after surgery, the patient is doing well. The patient does normal activities with no signs that the cysts have come back.

Conclusion:
In summary, the patient had surgery to open and drain cysts caused by a condition affecting the liver and kidneys. The patient got much better after surgery. There are no signs that the cysts have come back.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 68
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Diagnosis: 
- Aphakic bullous keratopathy 
- Corneal scarring

Hospital Course: 
The patient underwent penetrating keratoplasty for aphakic bullous keratopathy and corneal scarring. The donor cornea was replaced due to a large dendritic-geographic epithelial ulcer noted immediately after placement. Perioperative administration of intravenous aciclovir was given. Postoperatively, the patient was started on systemic and local antiviral therapy, and local antibiotic treatment. The graft was clear and re-epithelialized at 1 week. Long term prophylactic acyclovir 400 mg was started. However, six months later the patient developed a geographic corneal ulcer involving the graft-host interface with keratic precipitates and corneal edema. The patient was treated with acyclovir 400 mg and topical aciclovir both five times a day for 2 weeks with resolution of the ulcer and residual scarring.

Summary: 
The patient who had aphakic bullous keratopathy and corneal scarring underwent a successful penetrating keratoplasty with the use of a donor corneoscleral disc. The initial donor corneoscleral disc was removed and replaced with a second donor corneoscleral disc which was available. The patient was treated with antiviral and antibiotic medications following the surgery. The patient developed a geographic corneal ulcer, which was treated successfully with antiviral medications. The patient was discharged with instructions for a follow-up within a week.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 68
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Diagnosis:
The patient has swelling and blisters on the clear front part of the eye after lens removal. The patient also has scarring on the clear front part of the eye.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery to replace the damaged clear front part of the eye with tissue from a donor. The first donor tissue showed a large sore right after it was placed. It was removed and replaced with new donor tissue. The patient received antiviral medicine through a vein during surgery. After surgery, the patient was given antiviral pills and eye drops. The patient also received antibiotic eye drops to prevent infection. After one week, the new tissue was clear and the surface had healed. The patient started taking acyclovir 400 mg pills for long term protection. Six months later, a sore appeared on the new tissue where it meets the original eye tissue. There was also clouding and small spots on the clear front part of the eye. The patient took acyclovir 400 mg pills and used antiviral eye drops five times a day for two weeks. The sore healed but left some scarring.

Summary:
The patient had swelling and scarring on the clear front part of the eye. The patient had surgery to replace this damaged tissue with donor tissue. The first donor tissue had to be replaced with a second one. After surgery, the patient took antiviral and antibiotic medicines. Later, a sore developed on the clear front part of the eye. This was treated successfully with antiviral medicines. The patient was sent home with instructions to return for a check-up within one week.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Gender: Male
Age: 48 years
Diagnosis: Biliary Infection

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 48-year-old male with a history of a total gastrectomy with D2 lymphadenectomy 1 month ago and one course of postoperative chemotherapy, was admitted due to persistent fever and chills a day after completing the first course of chemotherapy. On admission, he presented with a high fever, elevated white cell count, and mild jaundice with moderate yellowing of the sclera. Abdominal examination was unremarkable except for the presence of right upper abdominal structures disorders as identified through an ultrasound. He undertook CT guided puncture positioning technology and a three-channel tube was set to drain around 400 ml of foul smell hazel turbid liquid. Conservative treatment, which comprised banning diet, total parenteral nutrition, and intravenous antibiotics, was complemented with daily monitoring of the drainage quantity. The patient's temperature dropped from 40 degrees to 37 degrees, and his jaundice significantly improved by the first day of conservative treatment. Blood picture normalised and drainage quantity gradually reduced over the next few days. He was switched to a liquid diet by day 5 and continued to recover well.

Hospital Course Summary:
A 48-year-old male was admitted to the hospital due to persistent fever and chills a day after completing the first course of chemotherapy. The patient had a history of a total gastrectomy with D2 lymphadenectomy 1 month ago and one course of postoperative chemotherapy. The patient's clinical presentation included high fever, elevated white blood cell count, and mild jaundice with moderate yellowing of the sclera. Upon admission, an ultrasound was conducted, which revealed the presence of right upper abdominal structures disorders. After an emergency CT guided puncture positioning technology, about 400 ml of foul smell hazel turbid liquid was drained. Conservative treatment was complemented with daily monitoring of the drainage quantity. The patient's temperature and jaundice significantly improved by the first day of conservative treatment, and blood picture normalised and drainage quantity gradually reduced over the next few days. The patient was switched to a liquid diet by day 5 and continued to recover well.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Gender: Male
Age: 48 years
Diagnosis: Infection in the bile ducts

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 48-year-old male. One month ago, he had surgery to remove his entire stomach and nearby lymph nodes. He also had one round of cancer treatment after surgery. He came to the hospital because he had a fever and chills that would not go away. This happened one day after finishing his first round of cancer treatment. When he arrived, he had a high fever and his white blood cell count was high. He also had mild yellowing of the skin and moderate yellowing of the whites of his eyes. A belly exam was normal except for problems in the upper right area seen on an ultrasound. Doctors used a CT scan to guide a needle and placed a three-way tube to drain fluid. About 400 ml of cloudy brown fluid with a bad smell was removed. Treatment included no food by mouth, nutrition given through an IV, and antibiotics through an IV. The amount of fluid draining was checked every day. By the first day of treatment, his temperature dropped from 40 degrees to 37 degrees. His skin yellowing also got much better. Over the next few days, his blood tests returned to normal and less fluid drained each day. By day 5, he started drinking liquids and kept getting better.

Hospital Course Summary:
A 48-year-old male came to the hospital with a fever and chills that would not go away. This happened one day after finishing his first round of cancer treatment. input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 28-year-old female who presented to the Emergency Department with sudden onset chest pain and recent fall. She complained of intermittent chest pain, substernal, 8/10 in intensity, and it resolved spontaneously. The patient also reported loss of consciousness followed by a fall that resulted in minor trauma to the head and leg. She denied any history of heart failure or any symptoms suggestive of heart failure. Additionally, the patient reported marijuana use two days prior. On examination, mild epigastric tenderness was detected on abdominal examination. Laboratories revealed a mild leukocytosis and mild hypokalemia. The troponin level was elevated indicating the possibility of non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI). During hospitalization, the patient was diagnosed with Non-Ischemic Cardiomyopathy with severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction. Urgent cardiac catheterization was consistent with echocardiogram results, and severe left ventricular dysfunction was present. Upon repeat echocardiogram, there was a significant improvement in the ejection fraction (EF) with a sound resolution of mitral regurgitation upon discharge. 



Discharge Diagnoses:
-Non-Ischemic Cardiomyopathy



Discharge Medications:
-The patient was discharged on the same doses of Enalapril, and Carvedilol, in addition to, low dose oral furosemide (20mg PO daily). Counseling for abstinence of toxic substances was conducted. The patient was recommended to undergo Cardiac MRI for further evaluation to rule out etiologies but opted for secondary consultation.



Discharge Condition:
The patient's condition improved while in the hospital. She was discharged with a diagnosis of Non-Ischemic Cardiomyopathy and recommended to continue medication and abstinence from toxic substances. Cardiac MRI follow up was recommended but was scheduled to undergo consultation with a specialist.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 28-year-old female who came to the Emergency Room with sudden chest pain and a recent fall. She had chest pain that came and went, located behind the breastbone, rated 8 out of 10 in pain level. The pain went away on its own. The patient also passed out and fell, which caused minor injuries to the head and leg. She had no history of heart problems or signs of heart trouble. The patient also reported using marijuana two days before. During the physical exam, the doctor found mild pain in the upper belly area. Blood tests showed a slightly high white blood cell count and low potassium levels. A heart damage marker in the blood was high, which suggested a possible mild heart attack. During the hospital stay, the patient was found to have a weak heart muscle not caused by blocked arteries. The heart was pumping much less blood than normal. A test using a thin tube in the heart matched earlier heart scan results and showed the heart was very weak. A follow-up heart scan before discharge showed the heart was pumping much better. A leaky heart valve had also healed well.

Discharge Diagnoses:
Weak heart muscle not caused by blocked arteries

Discharge Medications:
The patient went home with the same doses of Enalapril and Carvedilol, plus a low dose water pill called furosemide at 20mg by mouth daily. The patient received advice to stop using drugs and other harmful substances. The doctor suggested a special heart scan called an MRI to find the cause of the heart problem. The patient chose to see a specialist instead.

Discharge Condition:
The patient got better while in the hospital. She went home with a diagnosis of weak heart muscle not caused by blocked arteries. The patient should keep taking the prescribed medicines and stay away from drugs and harmful substances. A heart MRI was suggested, but the patient will first meet with a heart specialist.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

This is a hospital course summary for a 40-year-old female patient who was admitted due to ventricular arrhythmias during neoadjuvant chemotherapy for left breast ductal cancer. The patient had a history of LQTS type 2, a mutation of NM_000238.3(KCNH2):c.2593-2A>G, and had an internal defibrillator implanted in 2005. The patient was treated with slow-release Metoprolol for over 15 years with a prolonged QTc of approximately 480 ms. The patient reported no prior symptomatic arrhythmia episodes.

During the neoadjuvant therapy, the patient was shifted from Metoprolol 75mg to Propranolol slow-release 160mg due to ventricular arrhythmias associated with increased QTc. The patient was monitored by continuous telemetry during the second treatment, and no ventricular arrhythmias were observed. The course of neoadjuvant therapy was interrupted, and the patient underwent surgical resection. Post-operative, the patient received adjuvant treatment with four pulses of docetaxel 80mg/m2 under telemetry monitoring, and no arrhythmias were observed. 

One year after the diagnosis, the patient is doing well, and the QTc has been recorded at 496 ms. No clinical arrhythmias have occurred, and Holter recording 8 months after the first cytostatic treatment showed no premature ventricular complexes. There has been no reoccurrence of ventricular arrhythmias post-switching back to Metoprolol, according to the patient's own wish. 

The patient displayed no complications throughout the admission and was discharged in a stable condition. Follow-ups were scheduled, and the patient was educated regarding managing medications and monitoring of ventricular arrhythmias and QTc intervals.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

This is a hospital course summary for a 40-year-old female patient who was admitted due to abnormal heart rhythms during early cancer treatment for left breast cancer. The patient had a history of a heart condition called long QT syndrome type 2, which is caused by a change in one of her genes. The patient had a small device placed in her chest in 2005 to shock the heart back to normal rhythm if needed. The patient was treated with a heart medicine called slow-release Metoprolol for over 15 years. The patient had a longer than normal time between heartbeats, measured at about 480 ms. The patient reported no prior episodes of abnormal heart rhythms with symptoms.

During the early cancer treatment, the patient was switched from Metoprolol 75mg to another heart medicine called slow-release Propranolol 160mg. This change was made because of abnormal heart rhythms linked to a longer time between heartbeats. The patient was watched with a heart monitor during the second treatment. No abnormal heart rhythms were seen. The early cancer treatment was stopped, and the patient had surgery to remove the cancer. After surgery, the patient received four rounds of a cancer drug called docetaxel 80mg/m2 while being watched on a heart monitor. No abnormal heart rhythms were seen.

One year after finding the cancer, the patient is doing well. The time between heartbeats has been measured at 496 ms. No abnormal heart rhythms have happened. A 24-hour heart monitor test done 8 months after the first cancer drug showed no extra heartbeats. The patient has not had any abnormal heart rhythms since switching back to Metoprolol. This switch was done because the patient asked for it.

The patient had no problems during the hospital stay and was sent home in stable condition. Follow-up visits were scheduled. The patient was taught how to manage medicines and watch for abnormal heart rhythms and changes in the time between heartbeats.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [patient name]

Gender: [patient gender]

Date of Admission: [date of admission]

Reason for Hospitalization:

The patient was admitted at 22 weeks’ gestation to the Emergency Department due to miscarriage. Upon admission, the patient presented shaking chills, fever, stomatitis, sore throat, abdominal pain, and minimal vaginal bleeding for the previous three days.

Hospital Course:

The laboratory findings upon admission showed a low white blood cell count and high C-reactive protein levels. After induction of labour, the patient delivered a stillborn female fetus. The patient was treated with meropenem, followed by piperacillin/tazobactam for 6 days.

Medical History:

The patient had a complex medical history of monoarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis completely resolved at 15 years of age. The patient reported bilateral gonalgia six years before the current hospitalization. Laboratory results showed severe neutropenia, but no ANCA titers. The patient had undergone a bone marrow biopsy, which was reported within normal limits. The patient presented with low neutrophil counts in the past six years but had never contracted opportunistic infections. One year before admission, the patient had delivered a male infant with severe mastitis, followed by two episodes of bilateral knee arthritis.

Postmortem Examination:

The postmortem examination on the nonmacerated female fetus revealed intra-alveolar, gastric, and intestinal neutrophils with oedema, microhemorrhages, and acute tubular necrosis (NTA) of the renal parenchyma. The placenta showed necrotizing AC corresponding to a maternal inflammatory response stage 3/3 and grade 2/2. Funisitis was noted with neutrophilic infiltrate of the umbilical vein and the two arteries with extension to Wharton’s jelly consistent with fetal inflammatory response stage 2/3 and grade 2/2. Initial thrombosis was detected in few spiral arteries of the decidua.

Microbiological Analysis:

Autoptical samples were cultured on different media and identified as K. kingae using MALDI-ToF technology. The microorganism grew in pure culture in all the samples.

Medical Treatment:

The patient was treated with Granulocyte-Colony Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) and cyclosporine (200 mg a day) in addition to hydroxychloroquine due to low neutrophil count and recent intrauterine infection.

Discharge Condition:

The patient fully recovered and was discharged after 10 days.

Follow-Up Plan:

The patient will have regular follow-up appointments with her primary care physician to monitor her medical condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [patient name]

Gender: [patient gender]

Date of Admission: [date of admission]

Reason for Hospitalization:

The patient came to the Emergency Room at 22 weeks of pregnancy because of a miscarriage. When the patient arrived, she had shaking chills, fever, mouth sores, sore throat, belly pain, and light vaginal bleeding for the past three days.

Hospital Course:

Blood tests at arrival showed a low count of white blood cells that fight infection. The tests also showed high levels of a protein that signals swelling in the body. After medicine was given to start labor, the patient gave birth to a baby girl who was not alive. The patient was given strong antibiotics called meropenem, then piperacillin/tazobactam for 6 days.

Medical History:

The patient had a history of joint swelling in one joint as a child. This cleared up completely by age 15. Six years before this hospital stay, the patient reported pain in both knees. Blood tests showed a very low count of infection-fighting white blood cells. Tests for certain immune system problems were negative. The patient had a bone marrow test, which came back normal. The patient has had low counts of these white blood cells for six years. However, the patient never got infections that people with weak immune systems often get. One year before this hospital stay, the patient gave birth to a baby boy. After that birth, the patient had a serious breast infection. This was followed by two episodes of swelling in both knees.

Postmortem Examination:

The exam of the baby girl showed infection-fighting cells in the lungs, stomach, and intestines. There was also fluid buildup, small areas of bleeding, and kidney damage. The placenta showed severe infection and swelling from the mother at the highest levels. The umbilical cord also showed signs of infection. Infection-fighting cells were found in the cord vein and two arteries. The infection spread to the jelly-like tissue in the cord. This showed a strong response from the baby at high levels. A few blood vessels in the uterus lining showed early signs of blood clots.

Microbiological Analysis:

Samples taken during the exam were tested and grown in the lab. A germ called K. kingae was found using special testing technology. This same germ grew in all the samples tested.

Medical Treatment:

The patient was given medicine called G-CSF to help make more infection-fighting white blood cells. The patient also took cyclosporine (200 mg a day) and hydroxychloroquine. These medicines were given because of the low white blood cell count and the recent infection during pregnancy.

Discharge Condition:

The patient got better and left the hospital after 10 days.

Follow-Up Plan:

The patient will see her regular doctor for check-ups to watch her health condition.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Age: N/A
Gender: Male

History of Present Illness:

The patient, a 63-year-old male, has a 15-year history of recurrent depression. In the fall of 2020, his mental state deteriorated with symptoms of lowered mood, apathy, anxiety, agitation, and restlessness. Additionally, he experienced decreased appetite, weight loss, and significant problems with falling asleep and waking up earlier than usual. The severity of depressive symptoms was measured by the HDRS-17 scale and was found to be 18 points.

Hospital Course:

The patient received escitalopram at a dose of 10 mg, but after 5 weeks, the intensity of depression did not change (HDRS-17 17 points). At that time, the drug was changed to venlafaxine at the target dose of 150 mg; however, after another 6 weeks, the mental state did not improve. Then, the treatment was changed to mirtazapine at a dose of 30 mg at night. His condition improved after approximately 5 weeks of treatment, and at the visit 7 weeks after the initiation of mirtazapine, the severity of depression on the HDRS-17 was 9 points. After another 6 weeks, the remission of depression was maintained (HDRS-17 7 points). The patient tolerated the treatment well.

Social History:

N/A

Discharge Planning:

The patient has been discharged from the hospital in a stable condition. Follow-up care and periodic monitoring to assess his mental health status are recommended.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Age: N/A
Gender: Male

History of Present Illness:

The patient is a 63-year-old male. He has had depression that comes and goes for 15 years. In the fall of 2020, his mental health got worse. He felt sad, had no interest in things, and felt worried. He also felt restless and could not stay calm. He did not want to eat and lost weight. He had trouble falling asleep and woke up too early. A test that measures how bad depression is showed a score of 18 points. This means his depression was moderate to severe.

Hospital Course:

The patient first took a medicine called escitalopram at 10 mg. After 5 weeks, his depression did not get better. The test score was still 17 points. The doctor changed his medicine to venlafaxine at 150 mg. After 6 more weeks, he still did not feel better. Then the doctor switched him to a medicine called mirtazapine at 30 mg taken at night. After about 5 weeks on this medicine, he started to feel better. At a visit 7 weeks after starting mirtazapine, his test score dropped to 9 points. After 6 more weeks, his depression stayed under control. His test score was 7 points. The patient handled the medicine well with no problems.

Social History:

N/A

Discharge Planning:

The patient has left the hospital in stable condition. He should have follow-up visits with his doctor. Regular check-ups are needed to watch his mental health.

input:
Hospital Course:
This 70-year-old female patient presented with active rheumatoid arthritis and was under treatment with salazosulfapyridine 1,000 mg/day. The patient was diagnosed with BRAF wild-type primary mucosal melanoma, which was resected, and adjuvant treatment was administered in the form of 60-Gy/30-Fr radiotherapy. One year after the initial presentation, the patient experienced relapse in her left sixth rib and left iliac fossa, along with duodenal metastasis that resulted in ulceration with gastrointestinal bleeding. The patient required transfusion at least three times per week due to anemia.

Treatment:
The patient underwent treatment with nivolumab (2 mg/kg) and showed no substantial adverse effects. After 19 days, the tumor in the left sixth rib started to decrease in size, and after 26 days, it could no longer be visualized on chest radiography. Computed tomography (CT) performed 1 month after therapy initiation showed the absence of the costal lesion and an acceptable reduction of more than 60 % of the ileal lesion, and the ulcerated duodenal lesion on the endoscopy performed 4 months after treatment showed cicatrization. After the second week, anemia due to bleeding from the tumor was reduced and subsequently blood transfusion was discontinued. The patient received four courses of nivolumab therapy and slight changes to the laboratory data were observed, but no adverse events were noted, and the patient did not experience any worsening of joint pain or DAS28ESR scores. The patient maintained a complete response for 9 months after receiving six courses of nivolumab therapy, without rheumatic exacerbation or drug-related adverse events.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition, and there was no evidence of any acute exacerbation of rheumatoid arthritis or adverse effects related to nivolumab therapy.

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was advised to follow-up with her physicians for routine check-ups and keep a record of any unexpected side effects. The patient was also advised to adhere to a healthy lifestyle and exercise regimen.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
This 70-year-old female patient came to the hospital with active joint disease called rheumatoid arthritis. The patient was taking a medicine called salazosulfapyridine at 1,000 mg per day for this condition. The patient was found to have a type of skin cancer called melanoma that started in a moist body lining. This cancer did not have a certain gene change called BRAF. The cancer was removed with surgery. The patient then received radiation treatment to kill any remaining cancer cells. This treatment used 60 units of radiation given over 30 sessions. One year later, the cancer came back. It spread to the left sixth rib and the left lower belly area near the hip bone. The cancer also spread to the first part of the small intestine. This caused an open sore that led to bleeding in the gut. The patient needed blood given through a vein at least three times per week because of low blood count.

Treatment:
The patient received a cancer drug called nivolumab at a dose of 2 mg per kg of body weight. The patient did not have any major bad reactions to this medicine. After 19 days, the tumor in the left sixth rib began to get smaller. After 26 days, the tumor could no longer be seen on chest x-rays. A special scan called a CT scan was done one month after starting treatment. This scan showed the rib tumor was gone. The tumor near the hip bone had shrunk by more than 60 percent. A camera test looking inside the gut was done four months after treatment. This test showed the open sore in the small intestine had healed. After two weeks of treatment, the bleeding from the tumor slowed down. The patient no longer needed blood given through a vein. The patient received four rounds of nivolumab treatment. Blood tests showed small changes but nothing concerning. The patient had no bad reactions to the medicine. The patient did not have worse joint pain. A score that measures joint disease activity stayed the same. The cancer stayed completely gone for nine months after six rounds of nivolumab treatment. The patient had no flare-ups of joint disease or bad reactions to the medicine.

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition. There were no signs of worsening joint disease. There were no bad effects from the nivolumab treatment.

Discharge Medications:
None listed.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was told to see doctors for regular check-ups. The patient should write down any unexpected side effects. The patient was also told to eat well and exercise regularly.

input:
Hospital Course:
The 13-year-old Asian girl weighing 24 kg was admitted with a progressive thoracolumbar kyphoscoliosis deformity since 2 years. She underwent tracheostomy 6 years before, related to severe bronchopneumonia management. Since then, she continued to require ventilatory support at night. Genetic study confirmed PD and she was started on ERT.

Physical Exam:
At admission, the patient had thoracolumbar kyphoscoliosis extending from D1 to L4 level, varus deformity of the left foot, bilateral fixed flexor deformity of lower limbs, and wasting of the lower limb and paraspinal muscles with 3/5 motor power. Oxygen saturation was 95% on room air, and chest X-ray showed normal lung fields. Echocardiography showed normal ventricles with ejection fraction of 54% with no evidence of hypertrophy or obstructive cardiomyopathy. Serum creatinine kinase was raised.

Procedure:
General anaesthesia was planned for motor evoked potential (MEP) monitoring during the correction of kyphoscoliosis. The child was induced with dexmedetomidine infusion, ketamine, and morphine, and anaesthesia was maintained with dexmedetomidine infusion, oxygen, and sevoflurane. A total of 450 ml of blood was lost, and 2.3 L of Ringers lactate were infused. The surgery was successful.

Follow-up:
On the 2nd post-operative day, she developed bilateral mild pleural effusion which was treated with diuretics and antibiotics. On the 5th post-operative day, patient was weaned off from ventilator. However, she continued to require nocturnal ventilatory support. Rest of the hospital stay was uneventful, and she was discharged on the 10th day.

Plan:
The patient will continue with ERT and nocturnal ventilatory support. Follow-up appointments will be made with the appropriate specialists.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The 13-year-old Asian girl weighing 24 kg was admitted with a curved and twisted spine that had been getting worse for 2 years. She had a breathing tube placed in her neck 6 years before because of a serious lung infection. Since then, she needed a breathing machine at night. A gene test confirmed Pompe disease and she was started on enzyme replacement therapy.

Physical Exam:
At admission, the patient had a curved and twisted spine from the upper back to the lower back. She had an inward turning of the left foot. Both legs were bent and could not fully straighten. The muscles in her legs and along her spine were weak and thin with reduced strength. Her blood oxygen level was 95 percent on room air. A chest X-ray showed her lungs looked normal. A heart ultrasound showed her heart was working normally with 54 percent pumping strength. There were no signs of thickened or blocked heart muscle. A blood test showed high levels of a muscle enzyme.

Procedure:
The child was put to sleep with general anesthesia. Doctors watched her nerve signals during surgery to fix her curved spine. She was given a mix of medicines to keep her asleep and comfortable. She lost about 450 ml of blood during surgery. Doctors gave her 2.3 liters of fluid through an IV. The surgery went well.

Follow-up:
On day 2 after surgery, fluid built up around both lungs. This was treated with water pills and antibiotics. On day 5 after surgery, the patient was taken off the breathing machine. She still needed the breathing machine at night as before. The rest of her hospital stay went smoothly. She went home on day 10.

Plan:
The patient will keep getting enzyme replacement therapy and using the breathing machine at night. Follow-up visits will be scheduled with the right doctors.

She still needed the breathing machine at night as before. The rest of her hospital stay went smoothly. She went home on day 10.

Plan:
The patient will keep getting enzyme replacement therapy and using the breathing machine at night. Follow-up visits will be scheduled with the right doctors.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient was referred to our pain clinic due to an 8-day history of postural headache, which occurred following acupuncture treatment to manage chronic lower back pain caused by postlaminectomy syndrome. The patient had undergone L4-5 laminectomy 7 years prior. The physical and neurological examinations were normal. The patient was diagnosed with a postdural puncture headache (PDPH) and was placed on bed rest. An epidural blood patch (EBP) was performed, which failed to relieve the symptoms. A brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was unremarkable, while an MRI myelogram showed an abnormal fluid signal intensity in the left lumbar area, along with the left paraspinal muscle, and soft tissues at the L3-4-5 level, probably due to CSF leakage.

After conservative care, which included bed rest, hydration, and taking acetaminophen, the patient's postural headaches persisted. A repeat fluoroscopically guided interlaminar EBP was performed with minor relief. A transforaminal EBP at the L3-4 and L4-5 levels on the left side was performed. After the transforaminal EBP, the patient's headache and other symptoms subsided without complications.

At the 2-week follow-up, the patient reported being headache-free, and the activities had returned to normal.

Diagnosis:

Postdural puncture headache (PDPH)

Treatment:

Conservative care, including bed rest, hydration, and acetaminophen, was recommended. Epidural blood patches were performed, including an interlaminar approach using autologous blood at the L2-3 level with 15ml, and a repeat of this procedure using the same volume of blood at the L4-5 level. Finally, a transforaminal EBP with 3ml autologous blood was given at each level at L3-4 and L4-5 on the left side.

Follow-up Care:

The patient was discharged after 4 days. At the 2-week follow-up, the patient reported being headache-free, and the activities had returned to normal. The patient was advised to follow-up with their primary care physician if there were any changes in the symptoms.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient came to our pain clinic with a headache that started 8 days earlier. The headache got worse when sitting or standing up. It happened after acupuncture treatment for long-term lower back pain. The back pain was caused by problems after a spine surgery done 7 years ago. That surgery removed part of the bone in the lower spine at the L4-5 level. The physical exam and brain and nerve tests were normal. The doctors found that the headache was caused by spinal fluid leaking. The patient was told to stay in bed. A treatment was done where the patient's own blood was put into the spine to seal the leak. This did not help. A brain scan was normal. Another scan showed fluid leaking in the lower left back area at the L3-4-5 level of the spine.

The patient rested, drank plenty of fluids, and took acetaminophen for pain. The headache did not go away. The blood patch treatment was done again with some relief. Then a different type of blood patch was done on the left side at the L3-4 and L4-5 levels. After this treatment, the headache and other symptoms went away without any problems.

At the 2-week checkup, the patient had no headache. The patient was back to normal activities.

Diagnosis:

Headache caused by spinal fluid leak

Treatment:

The patient was told to rest in bed, drink plenty of fluids, and take acetaminophen for pain. Blood patch treatments were done to seal the leak. The first was done at the L2-3 level using 15ml of the patient's own blood. This was repeated at the L4-5 level with the same amount of blood. Finally, a different type of blood patch was done at L3-4 and L4-5 on the left side using 3ml of blood at each level.

Follow-up Care:

The patient left the hospital after 4 days. At the 2-week checkup, the patient had no headache and was back to normal activities. The patient was told to see their regular doctor if any symptoms came back.

input:
Hospital Course:
This 46-year-old male army colonel presented with blunt injury to the right eye sustained from direct contact with a tennis ball to the closed eyelid. Upon examination, the patient was noted to have central corneal abrasion, mild anterior chamber cells, flare, and a localized gray-white oval discoloration in the macula of the right eye. The patient was immediately started on topical corticosteroid ointment and systemic corticosteroids (50 mg prednisone daily for 5 days). 

Follow-Up:
Twenty-four hours following admission, high-definition SD-OCT and OCTA were obtained through clear cornea. The patient was observed throughout the hospital stay and exhibited no further complications. Coordinated follow-up with the patient's ophthalmologist was scheduled. 

Discharge Summary:
The 46-year-old male army colonel was hospitalized with blunt injury to the right eye. The patient was treated with topical corticosteroid ointment and systemic corticosteroids and exhibited no further complications. Coordinated follow-up with the patient's ophthalmologist was scheduled.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
This 46-year-old male army colonel came in with an injury to the right eye. A tennis ball hit the closed eyelid directly. The exam showed a scratch on the clear front part of the eye. There were also signs of mild swelling inside the eye. The doctor found a small gray-white spot in the back of the eye. The patient was given a steroid eye ointment right away. The patient also took steroid pills by mouth at 50 mg once a day for 5 days.

Follow-Up:
One day after being admitted, the patient had detailed eye scans done. These scans checked the eye structure and blood flow. The patient was watched during the hospital stay. No other problems occurred. A follow-up visit with the patient's eye doctor was set up.

Discharge Summary:
The 46-year-old male army colonel was in the hospital for an eye injury. A tennis ball hit the right eye. The patient received steroid eye ointment and steroid pills. No other problems occurred. A follow-up visit with the patient's eye doctor was set up.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [patient name]
Medical Record Number: [medical record number]

Admission Date: [admission date]
Discharge Date: [discharge date]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 43-year-old female admitted with acute hypoxic respiratory failure, requiring 15 litres per minute of oxygen. Physical exam was significant for wheezing in lungs bilaterally. Electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm. Troponin and electrolytes were in normal range. She had normocytic anemia with hemoglobin 10.8 g/dl, leukocytes 7.8 X 109/L, serum procalcitonin at 0.10 ng/mL. Arterial blood gases showed respiratory acidosis. Chest X-ray showed extensive bilateral lung infiltrates.

The patient was managed empirically for community acquired pneumonia with broad-spectrum antibiotics. On day 3 of hospital stay, her symptoms and oxygen requirement improved. However, she was noted to have hyperkalemia (potassium: 5.8 mmol/L) and elevated CK at 10,000 U/L. She denied any history of myalgias, muscle weakness, trauma, recent exercise, illicit drug use, and statins. An impression of rhabdomyolysis was made and aggressively hydrated. Surprisingly, the CK level increased despite resuscitation, raising diagnostic curiosity.

Detailed inflammatory workup was negative. Her course of hospitalization was complicated by volume overload requiring aggressive diuresis. A detailed discussion with the patient was held explaining enzyme abnormality and risk versus benefits of invasive workup. She refused further investigations including biopsy and imaging. A CK electrophoresis was ordered and the patient was discharged to follow up as an outpatient. Surprisingly, her isoenzyme panel showed CK MM with type 1 macroCK-macroCKemia.

On post-hospital follow up, subsequent normalization of enzyme levels were seen.

Discharge Diagnosis:

1. Acute hypoxic respiratory failure.
2. Community-acquired pneumonia.
3. Rhabdomyolysis.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient should follow up with her primary care physician. She should monitor for any recurrence of symptoms related to acute hypoxic respiratory failure or pneumonia and report them immediately if they do occur.

The patient should maintain her hydration level, follow a healthy diet and a normal lifestyle.

She should avoid strenuous physical activity until medical clearance is obtained. Emergency medical services should be contacted in case of any emergency issues.

The patient should be advised to go through regular lab tests to address any abnormality issues. 

Follow-up plan:

The patient should be followed up in the outpatient clinic within 2 weeks.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [patient name]
Medical Record Number: [medical record number]

Admission Date: [admission date]
Discharge Date: [discharge date]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 43-year-old female. She was admitted because her body was not getting enough oxygen. She needed 15 litres of oxygen per minute. Her lungs had wheezing sounds on both sides. Her heart test showed a normal heartbeat. Her heart enzyme and body salts were normal. Her blood test showed mild low red blood cells at 10.8 g/dl. Her white blood cells were normal at 7.8 X 109/L. Her infection marker was low at 0.10 ng/mL. Her blood gas test showed too much carbon dioxide in her blood. Her chest X-ray showed fluid or infection spread across both lungs.

The patient was treated for a lung infection caught outside the hospital. She was given strong antibiotics that work on many types of germs. On day 3, her breathing got better and she needed less oxygen. However, her potassium level was too high at 5.8 mmol/L. Her muscle enzyme was very high at 10,000 U/L. She said she had no muscle pain, weakness, injury, recent hard exercise, drug use, or cholesterol medicines. The doctors thought her muscles were breaking down. She was given lots of fluids through an IV. The muscle enzyme kept rising even with treatment. This was unexpected and needed more looking into.

Tests for body-wide swelling and immune problems were normal. Her hospital stay became harder because she had too much fluid in her body. She needed strong medicines to remove the extra fluid. The doctors talked with the patient about her unusual test results. They explained the good and bad sides of more testing. She did not want more tests like tissue samples or scans. A special test was ordered to look at the type of muscle enzyme. The patient went home and would get results later. The test showed a rare harmless condition called macroCK-macroCKemia. This means the enzyme clumps together and looks high but is not dangerous.

At her visit after leaving the hospital, her enzyme levels went back to normal.

Discharge Diagnosis:

1. Sudden breathing failure from low oxygen.
2. Lung infection caught outside the hospital.
3. Muscle breakdown.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient should see her regular doctor. She should watch for any return of breathing problems or lung infection signs. She should tell her doctor right away if these happen.

The patient should drink plenty of fluids. She should eat healthy foods and live a normal lifestyle.

She should not do hard exercise until her doctor says it is safe. She should call emergency services if any urgent problems happen.

The patient should get regular blood tests to check for any problems.

Follow-up plan:

The patient should come back to the clinic within 2 weeks.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
The patient is a 38-year-old male who presented with a painful arthritis of the left wrist.

Admission and Hospital Course:
Upon admission, the patient's left wrist was examined and MSU crystals were observed upon aspiration. The patient had a past history of gout affecting the ankles and left knee and had a previous road traffic accident with a fracture of the left wrist. The initial treatment with Colchicine (2 mg/day) was not effective in controlling the gout symptoms. Anakinra was administered and the patient had a rapid clinical response. On follow-up at day 7 after the first injection, the left wrist was still slightly swollen and tender, but the ankle and knee were asymptomatic. The patient was subsequently started on allopurinol.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with gout.

Treatment:
The patient was treated with Anakinra and allopurinol.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's left wrist had improved, showing only slight swelling and tenderness upon discharge. The patient was advised to continue taking allopurinol and was instructed to maintain a follow-up appointment with the treating physician.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient was advised to consult with the treating physician regarding further management and treatment of his gout condition if needed.

Prognosis:
The patient's prognosis was considered to be favorable given the positive response to Anakinra and the asymptomatic ankles and left knee.

Other:
The patient's medical history was reviewed and is relevant to his current condition. The patient did not report any drug or food allergies. Medication reconciliation was carried out to ensure accuracy of prescribed medication upon discharge.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
The patient is a 38-year-old man who came to the hospital with painful joint swelling in his left wrist.

Admission and Hospital Course:
When the patient arrived, doctors checked his left wrist. They removed fluid from the joint and found tiny crystals that cause gout. The patient had gout before in his ankles and left knee. He also broke his left wrist in a car accident in the past. Doctors first tried a medicine called Colchicine at 2 mg per day. This did not help control his gout symptoms. Then doctors gave him a medicine called Anakinra. The patient got better quickly after this treatment. At a checkup 7 days after the first shot, his left wrist was still a little swollen and sore. His ankle and knee felt fine with no symptoms. Doctors then started him on a medicine called allopurinol.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with gout, a type of joint swelling caused by crystal buildup.

Treatment:
The patient was treated with Anakinra and allopurinol.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's left wrist got better. It had only a little swelling and soreness when he left the hospital. The patient was told to keep taking allopurinol. He was also told to go to his follow-up visit with his doctor.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient was told to see his doctor about further care and treatment for his gout if needed.

Prognosis:
The patient's outlook is good. He responded well to Anakinra. His ankles and left knee have no symptoms.

Other:
Doctors reviewed the patient's health history. It relates to his current condition. The patient said he has no allergies to medicines or foods. The care team checked all medicines to make sure the prescriptions were correct when the patient left the hospital.
