input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 64-year-old male
Medical History: Postthoracotomy pain syndrome

Presenting Symptoms:
- Severe pain (9/10 on NRS) over the anterior and posterior right chest area
- Burning, tingling, and current-like sensation around the thoracotomy scar at the T8-T9 level
- Despite high dosage of oral analgesics, significant pain persisted

Treatment:
- Right ESP block performed with 20 mL of 0.375% ropivacaine and 40 mg of triamcinolone at the T8 level
- Pain immediately relieved (0 on 10 on NRS) with sensory blockade from T5 to T12 on the right side
- NRS score monitored for 24 hours
- Discharged with tapered oral medications

Outcomes:
- Patient remained pain-free for 1.5 months without consumption of any other oral analgesic
- A second ESP block performed with 20 mL of 0.25% ropivacaine and 40 mg triamcinolone, which produced significant analgesia lasting 2 weeks
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 64-year-old male
Medical History: Long-term pain after chest surgery

Presenting Symptoms:
The patient had very bad pain, rated 9 out of 10, in the front and back of the right chest area. The patient felt burning, tingling, and electric-like feelings around the surgery scar between the 8th and 9th ribs. The pain stayed bad even with high doses of pain pills taken by mouth.

Treatment:
The patient received a nerve block injection on the right side of the back. The doctor injected 20 mL of numbing medicine called ropivacaine and 40 mg of a steroid called triamcinolone near the 8th rib. The pain went away right away, dropping to 0 out of 10. The numbing spread from the 5th to 12th rib area on the right side. The care team checked the pain level for 24 hours. The patient went home with a slow reduction in pain pills.

Outcomes:
The patient had no pain for 1.5 months and did not need any pain pills by mouth. The patient received a second nerve block injection with 20 mL of a weaker numbing medicine and 40 mg of steroid. This second injection gave good pain relief that lasted 2 weeks.

input:
Hospital Course:

The 68-year-old female patient presented with sudden onset of skin rash, redness, and itching all over her body, along with shortness of breath and a generalized uncomfortable feeling. The symptoms occurred after administering her daily subcutaneous humulin 70/30 insulin injection. The patient was found to have an erythematous, pruritic urticarial rash spread over the body and mild dyspnea, consistent with a type-1 hypersensitivity reaction. The patient was treated with antihistamines and the symptoms were resolved without complications.

Past Medical History:

The patient had a past medical history of T2DM, hypertension, and grade-2 diastolic dysfunction. She was compliant with her prescribed medications until she started developing severe allergic reactions to insulin a week after acquiring the COVID-19 infection.

Course of Treatment:

The patient was diagnosed with COVID-19 infection eight days ago, after which she was self-quarantined in her home. To avoid further allergic reactions, switching to a different insulin formulation was recommended. The patient refused treatment with a different insulin regimen despite extensive counseling. She was prescribed oral antihistamines to be taken before her regular insulin injections, and also an EpiPen (epinephrine injection, USP) 0.3 mg, with the instructions to visit the ER if she develops symptoms again.

Outcome:

The patient completely recovered from the COVID-19 infection, but continued having insulin allergies with her humulin 70/30 insulin injections. Eventually, the patient agreed to switch to different insulin formulations after about two weeks of her initial ER presentation. The new insulin formulations brought the glucose levels down to her usual glycemic range without any further allergic reactions.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The 68-year-old female patient came in with a sudden skin rash, redness, and itching all over her body. She also had trouble breathing and felt unwell overall. These symptoms started after she gave herself her daily insulin shot under the skin. The patient had a red, itchy, hive-like rash all over her body and mild trouble breathing. This was caused by a severe allergic reaction. The patient was given allergy medicine and her symptoms went away without any problems.

Past Medical History:

The patient had type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, and a heart problem where the heart does not relax well between beats. She took all her medicines as directed until she started having bad allergic reactions to insulin. These reactions began about a week after she got COVID-19.

Course of Treatment:

The patient was found to have COVID-19 eight days before this visit. After that, she stayed home to avoid spreading the illness. To prevent more allergic reactions, doctors suggested she try a different type of insulin. The patient did not want to change her insulin even after doctors talked with her about it many times. She was given allergy pills to take before her regular insulin shots. She was also given an EpiPen, which is an emergency shot with 0.3 mg of medicine to stop severe allergic reactions. She was told to go to the emergency room if her symptoms come back.

Outcome:

The patient fully recovered from COVID-19. However, she kept having allergic reactions to her insulin shots. About two weeks after her first emergency room visit, the patient agreed to try different types of insulin. The new insulin brought her blood sugar levels back to her normal range. She did not have any more allergic reactions.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: Female, 72 years old
Date of Admission: (Insert date of admission)
Date of Discharge: (Insert date of discharge)

Chief Complaint:
Low back pain and radicular pain in right leg leading to walking disability.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with a history of low back pain and radicular pain in her right leg causing difficulty in walking. She underwent posterior spinal fusion from T9 to the sacrum using intrasacral screws and interbody fusion at L3/4, L4/5, and L5/S disc levels. The duration of surgery was 7 hours and 51 minutes with an estimated blood loss of 1271 mL. Autologous transfusion was performed, and allogenic transfusion was not required. There were no major intraoperative complications.

Consultations:
The patient was evaluated by the surgical team preoperatively, and follow-up evaluations were done postoperatively by the same team.

Diagnostic and Therapeutic Intervention:
Posterior spinal fusion was performed using intrasacral screws coupled with interbody fusion at L3/4, L4/5, and L5/S levels. The patient received standing rehabilitation on a tilt table from the fifth day after surgery, and walking was allowed with the use of a thoracolumbosacral orthosis beginning on the twentieth postoperative day.

Clinical Progress and Condition:
The patient’s symptoms of low back pain and radicular pain improved after the surgery. The patient could walk for more than 10 minutes with the use of a cane 1 year after the surgery. Spinal fusion was confirmed, but slight loosening of the intrasacral screws was observed by computed tomography at follow-up.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition with recommendations for follow-up visits as prescribed by the surgical team. 

Instructions and Medications:
N/A

Disposition:
The patient was discharged home.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: Female, 72 years old
Date of Admission: (Insert date of admission)
Date of Discharge: (Insert date of discharge)

Main Health Problem:
Lower back pain and shooting pain down the right leg that made walking very hard.

What Happened During the Hospital Stay:
The patient came to the hospital because of lower back pain and shooting pain in her right leg. This pain made it hard for her to walk. She had surgery to join bones in her spine together. The surgery connected bones from the middle back down to the base of the spine. Metal screws were placed in the lower spine. Bone spacers were also placed between the bones in the lower back. The surgery took 7 hours and 51 minutes. The patient lost about 1271 mL of blood during surgery. The patient received her own blood back during the surgery. She did not need blood from a donor. There were no major problems during the surgery.

Doctor Visits:
The surgery team checked on the patient before the surgery. The same team also checked on her after the surgery.

Treatments and Therapy:
The patient had spine surgery to join bones together using metal screws and bone spacers. On the fifth day after surgery, the patient started standing up using a tilting table. On the twentieth day after surgery, the patient was allowed to start walking. She wore a back brace that supported her middle and lower back.

How the Patient Got Better:
The lower back pain and shooting leg pain got better after surgery. One year after surgery, the patient could walk for more than 10 minutes using a cane. Tests showed the spine bones had joined together. However, the scans also showed the screws at the base of the spine had loosened a little.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition. She should go to follow-up visits as the surgery team told her.

Instructions and Medications:
None given.

Where the Patient Went:
The patient went home.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]      
Date of Admission: [redacted]       
Date of Discharge: [redacted]        

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 47-year-old woman, was admitted to the hospital with bilateral groin pain, inability to urinate, and bloody vaginal discharge. Clinical vaginal examination revealed the presence of a large necrotic mass filling the whole vaginal tract. Transabdominal ultrasound examination showed a cervico-vaginal mass measuring 112 × 114 mm that protruded from the uterine cavity with a distorted view of the uterus. After consultation and an MRI examination, a large heterogeneous signal intensity of a well-defined submucosal fundal fibroid mass was confirmed. Uterine anatomy was distorted as seen as “V shape” fundus in sagittal plane due to traction of the mass. 

Laboratory tests showed a decreased hemoglobin level of 10.1 g/dL, hematocrit of 30.9%, and elevated CRP level of 37.5 mg/L. The patient's fasting glucose level, AST and ALT liver enzymes were within normal range, but the CA125 level was 152 U/mL. 

At laparotomy, a large uterine mass with complete uterine inversion was confirmed. The mass was taken out via transverse uterine incision after depicting the myoma’s pedicle. Total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy was performed due to the patient's request before surgery. The excised specimen revealed a giant mass with extensive necrosis and multiple hemorrhagic foci. Pathology report confirmed 12 × 12 × 10 cm sized leiomyoma including large ischemic necrotic areas. There was no evidence of malignancy. 

The patient was discharged without any complications five days after the surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Uterine inversion with uterine fibroid.

Discharge Condition:
Stable. 

Discharge Medication:
N/A. 

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was instructed to follow up with her gynecologist and primary care physician. She was advised to avoid heavy lifting and intercourse for six weeks to allow for full recovery. She was also instructed to monitor for any signs of vaginal bleeding, pain or fever. If any of these symptoms appeared, she was advised to contact her physician immediately. 

Follow-Up:
The patient was advised to schedule a follow-up appointment with her gynecologist and primary care physician for recovery monitoring.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Date of Admission: [redacted]
Date of Discharge: [redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 47-year-old woman, came to the hospital with pain in both sides of her groin, not being able to urinate, and bloody discharge from her vagina. A physical exam showed a large dying tissue mass filling the entire vaginal canal. An ultrasound of the belly showed a mass in the cervix and vagina area measuring about 4.4 by 4.5 inches. The mass was coming out from inside the uterus, and the uterus looked twisted out of shape. After more tests and an MRI scan, doctors confirmed a large non-cancerous growth called a fibroid attached to the inside top of the uterus. The uterus was pulled into a V-shape because the mass was dragging on it.

Blood tests showed the patient had low red blood cell levels at 10.1 g/dL and 30.9 percent, which means mild blood loss. The patient also had high inflammation markers at 37.5 mg/L. Blood sugar and liver tests were normal. A test called CA125, which checks for certain problems, was high at 152 U/mL.

During surgery through the belly, doctors found a large growth in the uterus that had caused the uterus to turn inside out. The growth was removed by cutting into the uterus after finding where it was attached. The uterus, both fallopian tubes, and both ovaries were then removed because the patient asked for this before surgery. The removed tissue showed a very large mass with dead tissue and areas of bleeding. Lab tests on the tissue confirmed a fibroid measuring about 4.7 by 4.7 by 4 inches with large areas of dead tissue. There were no signs of cancer.

The patient left the hospital five days after surgery with no problems.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Uterus turned inside out with a non-cancerous uterine growth called a fibroid.

Discharge Condition:
Stable.

Discharge Medication:
None.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was told to follow up with her women's health doctor and regular doctor. She was told not to lift heavy things or have sex for six weeks so her body can fully heal. She was also told to watch for any signs of vaginal bleeding, pain, or fever. If any of these happen, she should call her doctor right away.

Follow-Up:
The patient was told to make an appointment with her women's health doctor and regular doctor to check on her healing.

Hospital Course:

This 60-year-old male patient presented to our outpatient clinic with a 1-year history of generalized yellowish ovoid skin-colored papular eruption located on the trunk, groin, and extremities. The patient reported that he first noticed pruritic papules, and 2–3 months later many yellow and ovoid pruritic papules emerged. On skin physical examination, firm, well-defined ovoid skin-colored to yellow papules were seen on the distal and proximal of upper and lower extremities, trunk, groins, and abdomen.

Diagnostic laboratory tests such as full blood cell count with differential, peripheral blood smear, renal, and liver function tests all were normal. Histopathological examination of the skin biopsy from one of the lesion on the trunk revealed proliferation of mast cells with ovoid and spindle nuclei with distinct cytoplasm borders around capillaries, which was compatible with mastocytosis. Based on concurrent clinical and pathological features, we diagnosed our case as xanthelasmoid mastocytosis.

Treatment with H1 antihistamine, cetirizine 10 mg orally nightly and fexofenadine 120 mg for every morning was prescribed for pruritus control which was successful, but eruptions were persistent without any changes. Treatment with phototherapy also recommended to the patient, but despite a 1-year course, no significant improvement was seen in the lesions.

The patient refused further investigations such as bone marrow biopsy or alternative treatments and decided to end the hospital course. The patient was advised to monitor the lesions and seek medical attention if he noticed any changes or deterioration. The patient was discharged with no complications.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

This 60-year-old male patient came to our outpatient clinic with skin bumps that had lasted for 1 year. The bumps were yellow, oval-shaped, and skin-colored. They appeared on the chest, back, groin area, arms, and legs. The patient said he first noticed itchy bumps. Then 2 to 3 months later, many more yellow and oval itchy bumps appeared. During the skin exam, doctors found firm, clear-edged, oval bumps. These bumps were skin-colored to yellow. They were located on the upper and lower arms and legs, chest, back, groin area, and belly.

Lab tests checked the blood cells, kidney function, and liver function. All test results came back normal. Doctors took a small skin sample from one bump on the trunk. The sample showed too many mast cells, which are a type of immune cell. These cells had oval and long-shaped centers with clear edges. They were found around tiny blood vessels. This finding matched a condition called mastocytosis. Based on the skin exam and lab results together, doctors determined the patient had a rare type called xanthelasmoid mastocytosis.

The patient received two allergy medicines to control the itching. Cetirizine 10 mg was taken by mouth each night. Fexofenadine 120 mg was taken each morning. These medicines helped stop the itching. However, the skin bumps stayed the same without any changes. Doctors also suggested light therapy treatment. The patient tried this for 1 year, but the bumps did not get much better.

The patient chose not to have more tests like a bone marrow sample. The patient also chose not to try other treatments. The patient decided to end care at this time. The patient was told to watch the bumps and see a doctor if they change or get worse. The patient left the hospital with no problems.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient: 11-year-old female child of South-Asian Indian origin

Date of Discharge: [Date]

Admission Date: [Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Septic leg infection

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the orthopaedics department with complaints of fever, non-traumatic pain and swelling in the right leg for about 3 days. The patient was diagnosed with a septic leg infection. Blood investigations showed a raised white cell count and marginally increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Blood sample was sent for culture and sensitivity which later showed gram negative bacilli.

Incision and drainage was performed the following day. Thick purulent discharge was drained and about 5 ml of the discharge was sent for culture and sensitivity. The wound was left open and dressed appropriately. The organism was identified as C. indologenes.

The patient was empirically started on parenteral cefotaxime, metronidazole, and gentamicin, was changed over to intravenous ceftazidime and metronidazole infusion based on the sensitivity report following which significant clinical improvement was noted. Subsequently secondary closure was done 10 days later. The wound healed well and the patient was discharged on oral antibiotics with follow-up.

Discharge Diagnosis: Septic leg infection

Course of Hospitalization:

The patient presented with a septic leg infection. Blood investigations showed a raised white cell count and marginally increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Blood sample was sent for culture and sensitivity which showed gram negative bacilli.

The patient underwent incision and drainage the following day. The organism was identified as C. indologenes. The patient was empirically started on parenteral cefotaxime, metronidazole, and gentamicin, was changed over to intravenous ceftazidime and metronidazole infusion based on the sensitivity report. Subsequently secondary closure was done 10 days later. The wound healed well and the patient was discharged on oral antibiotics with follow-up.

Summary:

The patient was diagnosed with a septic leg infection and underwent incision and drainage. The organism was identified as C. indologenes. The patient received appropriate treatment and showed significant clinical improvement. The secondary closure was done 10 days later, and the wound healed well. The patient was discharged on oral antibiotics with follow-up.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient: 11-year-old female child of South-Asian Indian origin

Date of Discharge: [Date]

Admission Date: [Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Serious leg infection caused by germs

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the bone and joint department with fever, pain, and swelling in the right leg for about 3 days. The leg pain was not caused by an injury. The patient was found to have a serious leg infection caused by germs. Blood tests showed a high number of white blood cells. The blood tests also showed the body was fighting an infection. A blood sample was sent to the lab to find out what germ was causing the infection. The lab found a type of bacteria in the blood.

The next day, doctors made a cut to let the infected fluid drain out. Thick yellow-green fluid came out of the leg. About 5 ml of this fluid was sent to the lab for testing. The wound was left open and covered with clean bandages. The lab found the germ was a type of bacteria called C. indologenes.

The patient was first given three germ-fighting medicines through a tube in the vein. These medicines were cefotaxime, metronidazole, and gentamicin. After the lab showed which medicines would work best, the doctors changed to ceftazidime and metronidazole given through the vein. The patient got much better after this change. Ten days later, doctors closed the wound with stitches. The wound healed well. The patient went home with germ-fighting pills to take by mouth and a follow-up visit planned.

After the lab showed which medicines would work best, doctors changed to ceftazidime and metronidazole given through the vein. Ten days later, doctors closed the wound with stitches. The wound healed well. The patient went home with germ-fighting pills to take by mouth and a follow-up visit planned.

Summary:

The patient was found to have a serious leg infection caused by germs. Doctors made a cut to let the infected fluid drain out. The lab found the germ was a type of bacteria called C. indologenes. DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient: 68-year-old female of Afghan descent.

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath, high-grade fever, and a cough productive of thick whitish sputum. The patient was diagnosed with acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia and was treated with methyl prednisolone. The patient was discharged after 10 days on oral prednisolone 40 mg daily.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia.

Condition at Discharge:

The patient was discharged in stable condition.

Disposition:

Discharged to home.

Follow-up:

Patient to follow up with neurosurgeon for correction of spinal stenosis.

Summary:

The 68-year-old female of Afghan descent was admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath, high-grade fever, and a cough productive of thick whitish sputum. The patient was diagnosed with acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia and was treated with methyl prednisolone. The patient was discharged after 10 days on oral prednisolone 40 mg daily. The patient was discharged in stable condition and advised to follow up with a neurosurgeon for correction of spinal stenosis.

The patient came to the hospital with trouble breathing, a high fever, and a cough with thick white mucus. Acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia, a type of lung swelling and scarring.

The patient was in stable condition when leaving the hospital.

The patient went home.

The patient should see a spine surgeon to treat the narrowing of the spinal canal.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient 4, an 8-month-old female, was admitted due to episodic binocular upward gaze for 7 months. She also had delayed growth development and was unable to sit alone steadily until the present. The patient's physical examination showed paroxysmal slanting neck, and the Children's Developmental Center of China scores were 78/81, indicating a deviation from the normal reference. 

No specific perinatal and family history was reported. Episodic upward gaze started when the patient was 1 month old, occurring daily after waking up and resolved in about 10 seconds without physical movements. The recurrent episodes lasted up to several hours with no apparent relationship to fatigue or infection. 

The patient underwent several examinations, including a brain MRI and an EEG. Both tests showed no abnormalities during the episodes, but slow waves in the posterior brain were observed during the interictal period. Topiramate 2.5 mg was found to be ineffective in treating the episodes.

After careful evaluation and consideration, the patient was discharged and advised to follow up with a pediatric neurologist for further evaluation and management.

Hospital Course Summary

Patient 4, an 8-month-old female, was admitted due to episodic binocular upward gaze for 7 months. The patient underwent several examinations, including a brain MRI and an EEG. Both tests showed no abnormalities during the episodes, but slow waves in the posterior brain were observed during the interictal period.

The patient received several medications, including valproic acid, methocarbamol, and topiramate. However, the patient discontinued these medications due to ineffectiveness or exacerbation of episodes.

The patient's general state was observed to be good during the interictal period, and no evidence showed the episodes were related to fatigue or infection. Upon careful evaluation and consideration, the patient was advised to follow up with a pediatric neurologist for further evaluation and management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient 4 is an 8-month-old baby girl. She was admitted to the hospital because her eyes kept rolling upward. This has been happening for 7 months. She also has slower growth than expected. She cannot sit up on her own yet. During the physical exam, doctors noticed her neck sometimes tilts to one side. Her development test scores were 78 out of 81. This is lower than what is normal for her age.

There were no problems during pregnancy or birth. There is no family history of this condition. The eye-rolling episodes started when she was 1 month old. They happen every day after she wakes up. Each episode lasts about 10 seconds. Her body does not move during these episodes. The episodes can repeat for several hours. Being tired or sick does not seem to cause these episodes.

The patient had several tests done. These included a brain scan and a test to measure brain waves. Both tests looked normal during the episodes. Between episodes, the brain wave test showed slower activity in the back of the brain. A medicine called Topiramate at 2.5 mg did not help stop the episodes.

After careful review, the patient was sent home. She should see a children's brain and nerve doctor for more tests and care.

Hospital Course Summary

Patient 4 is an 8-month-old baby girl. She was admitted because her eyes kept rolling upward for 7 months. She had a brain scan and a brain wave test. Both tests looked normal during the episodes. Between episodes, the brain wave test showed slower activity in the back of the brain.

The patient was given several medicines. These included valproic acid, methocarbamol, and topiramate. She stopped taking all of these medicines. They either did not work or made the episodes worse.

Between episodes, the patient seemed healthy overall. Being tired or sick did not seem to cause the episodes. After careful review, she should see a children's brain and nerve doctor for more tests and care.

input:
Discharge Summary

Identification: 26-year-old female diagnosed with asthma at the age of 16

Admission Date: October 2018

Discharge Date: July 2019

Chief Complaint: Asthma and nasal polyposis

Hospital Course:

- The patient was admitted with a history of nasal polyposis, which had been surgically treated eight years before but had occurred again in the past two years.
- The patient was treated with inhaled corticosteroids and reported anosmia. Tests for common inhalant allergens were negative.
- The patient displayed sour breath with diffuse obstruction.
- Blood eosinophil count was 1200/mmc; total IgE 126 IU/L; FEV1 by spirometry 70%; ACT 14.
- Treatment with ICS/LABA, combined with LAMA since few months, and daily oral corticosteroids (prednisone 25 mg/die) in the past 3 months.
- In October 2018, benralizumab therapy was started.
- On day 28, no blood eosinophils were detected, IgE value was 38 IU/L and both lung function and symptom control were improved.
- Notably, the use of oral corticosteroids had been reduced.
- At the last follow-up, in July 2019, blood eosinophils were still undetectable, IgE was 26 IU/L and a further clinical and functional improvement was observed.
- Oral corticosteroid therapy had been discontinued three months before.
- The patient did not experience any exacerbation, hospitalization or ER access throughout benralizumab therapy.

Discharge Diagnosis: Asthma with nasal polyposis

Disposition: Stable

Plan: Recommend follow-up with primary care physician. No other specific therapeutic interventions needed.

Discharge Summary

Identification: 26-year-old female found to have asthma at the age of 16

Admission Date: October 2018

Discharge Date: July 2019

Main Problem: Asthma and growths in the nose called polyps

Hospital Stay:

The patient came in with a history of nose polyps. These had been removed by surgery eight years before but came back in the past two years.
The patient was treated with steroid medicine breathed into the lungs. The patient reported losing the sense of smell. Tests for common things that cause allergies were negative.
The patient had sour breath with blocked airways throughout the lungs.
Blood tests showed high levels of a white blood cell type called eosinophils at 1200 per unit. A marker for allergies called IgE was 126 units. A breathing test showed the lungs were working at 70 percent. A score for asthma control was 14.
Treatment included inhaled steroids with a long-acting inhaler to open airways. A second type of long-acting inhaler was added a few months before. The patient also took steroid pills called prednisone at 25 mg daily for the past 3 months.
In October 2018, a new medicine called benralizumab was started.
On day 28, no eosinophils were found in the blood. The allergy marker dropped to 38 units. Lung function and symptom control both got better.
The amount of steroid pills had been lowered.
At the last visit in July 2019, eosinophils were still not found in the blood. The allergy marker was down to 26 units. The patient continued to improve in symptoms and lung function.
Steroid pills had been stopped three months before this visit.
The patient had no asthma flare-ups, hospital stays, or emergency room visits while on benralizumab.

Discharge Diagnosis: Asthma with nose polyps

Condition at Discharge: Stable

Plan: The patient should follow up with their regular doctor. No other specific treatments are needed.

input:
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Medical History:
The patient is a 32-year-old woman who presented with a mass in the vulva and intermenstrual bleeding. She had a history of unsuccessful sexual intercourse due to fear of pain, and the mass had gradually enlarged over the past 3 years.

Clinical Findings:
A physical examination revealed a nontender tubular neoplasm outside the intact hymen, with an opening at the distal end. A canal approximately 5 cm long was found in the mass, from which white mucus was secreted. Ultrasonographic evaluation of the pelvis showed a 4.5- × 1-cm stalk originating from the cervix and protruding into the vagina. A mini-hysteroscopy was performed to excise the mass.

Diagnostic Testing:
Hemoglobin level was 9.4 g/dL. The levels of estradiol and progesterone in the luteal phase were 138 pg/mL and 13.77 ng/mL, respectively. Liver and renal functional tests and thyroid hormone levels were all normal.

Treatment:
The patient underwent a mini-hysteroscopy to excise the mass while avoiding damage to the hymen. The mass was found to originate from the squamocolumnar junction at the 3 o’clock position of the left lip of the ectocervix, and it had no communication with the endocervix. The cervical mucosal fold of approximately 0.3 to 0.7 cm in thickness was found inside the canal by mini-hysteroscopy and it had a blind end proximally. The mass was totally excised after suturing of the pedicle. Deep endocervical and endometrial regions were found to be normal.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient's postoperative course was uneventful without bleeding or any other complications. Sexual intercourse was successful for this couple.

Follow-up:
Five weeks after surgery, the patient visited again in the outpatient clinic and was doing well. Written informed consent was obtained from the patient and her husband for publication of this case report and accompanying images. Institutional review board approval was not required for publication of this case report.
clean_ref:
Hospital Stay Summary:

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Medical History:
The patient is a 32-year-old woman who came to the hospital with a growth in the outer private area and bleeding between periods. She had not been able to have sex because she was afraid it would hurt. The growth had slowly gotten bigger over the past 3 years.

Clinical Findings:
A physical exam showed a painless tube-shaped growth outside the thin skin that covers the vaginal opening. The growth had an opening at the end. There was a canal about 2 inches long inside the growth that released white fluid. An ultrasound of the lower belly area showed a 1.8-inch by 0.4-inch stem starting from the lower part of the womb and sticking into the birth canal. A small camera procedure was done to remove the growth.

Diagnostic Testing:
The blood count showed low iron levels at 9.4 g/dL. Hormone levels for the monthly cycle were 138 pg/mL and 13.77 ng/mL. Liver tests, kidney tests, and thyroid hormone levels were all normal.

Treatment:
The patient had a small camera procedure to remove the growth without damaging the thin skin covering the vaginal opening. The growth started from the area where two types of skin meet on the left side of the lower womb opening. It did not connect to the inside of the womb opening. A fold of skin about 0.1 to 0.3 inches thick was found inside the canal using the small camera. This fold had a closed end. The growth was completely removed after stitching the base. The deeper parts of the womb opening and womb lining were normal.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient healed well after surgery with no bleeding or other problems. The couple was able to have sex successfully.

Follow-up:
Five weeks after surgery, the patient came back to the clinic and was doing well. The patient and her husband gave written permission to share this medical story and pictures. No special approval was needed to share this medical story.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 53-year-old male nurse diagnosed with HCV infection 

Hospital Course:
In January 2011, the patient was evaluated at the Outpatient Infectious Diseases clinic for abnormal liver function tests. His HCV RNA was 660,000 IU/mL, genotype 3a. A liver biopsy showed chronic active hepatitis with Metavir A2 necroinflammatory grading and F2 fibrosis. The patient was considered eligible for antiviral treatment and started therapy with PEG-IFN-α-2a and ribavirin in March 2011. Treatment was well tolerated, and HCV RNA became negative by the fourth week and remained undetectable during the entire 24-week treatment regimen. ALT normalized within the first 3 weeks of treatment. The patient underwent post-treatment follow-up until September 2012, and a new biopsy revealed a mild reduction in Metavir grading and staging (A1 and F1, respectively).

Hospitalization:
Nine weeks after finishing the antiviral therapy, the patient developed symmetrical polyarthritis with joint pain and edema in the wrists, knees, shoulders, and metacarpophalangeal joints, associated with prolonged morning stiffness. MRI detected bone erosions with juxta-articular osteopenia in the wrist, knee, and hand joints, revealing a diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody testing were positive. 

Treatment:
The patient was treated with prednisone and NSAIDs for two months, followed by methotrexate and sulfasalazine with a quick and favorable response to treatment. 

Discharge:
The patient was discharged home in satisfactory condition with a prescription for methotrexate and sulfasalazine. Follow-up with Rheumatologist and Gastroenterologist was advised.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 53-year-old male nurse found to have hepatitis C virus infection

Hospital Course:
In January 2011, the patient was seen at the Outpatient Infectious Diseases clinic because of abnormal liver test results. His hepatitis C virus level was 660,000 units per milliliter, type 3a. A small sample of liver tissue showed ongoing liver swelling with moderate damage and mild to moderate scarring. The patient was a good fit for treatment and began taking two medicines called PEG-IFN-α-2a and ribavirin in March 2011. The patient handled the treatment well. The virus was no longer found in the blood by week four. It stayed gone during the full 24 weeks of treatment. Liver test results returned to normal within the first 3 weeks. The patient had follow-up visits until September 2012. A new liver tissue sample showed less damage and less scarring than before.

Hospitalization:
Nine weeks after finishing the antiviral treatment, the patient developed joint swelling on both sides of the body. The patient had pain and swelling in the wrists, knees, shoulders, and finger joints. The patient also had stiffness in the morning that lasted a long time. An MRI scan showed bone damage and bone thinning near the joints in the wrist, knee, and hand. These findings led to a diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis, a disease where the body attacks its own joints. Blood tests for rheumatoid factor and another marker for this disease were both positive.

Treatment:
The patient was treated with prednisone, a steroid, and pain relievers for two months. After that, the patient took methotrexate and sulfasalazine. The patient responded quickly and well to this treatment.

Discharge:
The patient went home in good condition with prescriptions for methotrexate and sulfasalazine. The patient should follow up with a joint specialist and a stomach and liver specialist.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The 62-year-old male was admitted to the hospital with a complaint of abdominal pain, vomiting, and ambulatory dysfunction. The initial examination showed no abnormalities. The patient was suspected to have viral gastroenteritis, and hence he was hospitalized for dehydration management. The patient's nausea and vomiting improved after admission, but it was noted that he was leaning backwards and to the right when standing or attempting to ambulate on the first day of hospitalization.

Diagnostic Evaluations:
The neurology service was consulted after assessing the patient's condition on his second day of admission. A CT angiogram of the head and neck showed a congenitally hypoplastic left vertebral artery and no other abnormality was observed in the vertebrobasilar system. MRI of the brain showed acute/subacute infarct in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The echocardiogram showed moderate diastolic dysfunction with trace aortic regurgitation.

Clinical Impression:
The patient was diagnosed with acute/subacute infarct in the right cerebellar hemisphere secondary to chronic atherosclerotic disease that had extended and caused APS. The left-sided dysmetria was observed, which further indicated his neurological impairment.

Hospital Treatment:
The patient was monitored on telemetry for several days without any events or abnormalities noted. He was started on a scheduled antiemetic regimen of metoclopramide 10 mg four times daily and ondansetron 8 mg every eight hours that yielded positive results. Within one day, the patient's nausea and vomiting were controlled, and he was able to eat without difficulty. The patient was successfully discharged from the hospital to rehabilitation.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient is recommended to follow up with the neurology service as an outpatient and undergo regular checkups to monitor his neurological function and the progression of his cerebrovascular disease. He is also advised to maintain strict blood glucose control and hypertension management. The family members are recommended to observe the patient's activities, as he is more vulnerable to falls.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The 62-year-old male came to the hospital with belly pain, vomiting, and trouble walking. The first exam showed nothing wrong. Doctors thought he had a stomach bug and kept him in the hospital to treat fluid loss. His upset stomach and vomiting got better after he was admitted. On the first day, staff noticed he was leaning backward and to the right when standing or trying to walk.

Diagnostic Evaluations:
The brain and nerve doctors were called in on the second day. A scan of the blood vessels in his head and neck showed he was born with a smaller than normal artery on the left side of his neck. No other problems were found in those blood vessels. A brain scan showed he had a recent stroke in the right side of the back part of his brain. A heart test showed the heart does not relax well between beats. It also showed a small amount of blood leaking back through one heart valve.

Clinical Impression:
The patient was found to have a recent stroke in the right side of the back part of his brain. This was caused by long-term hardening of the arteries that had spread and blocked blood flow. The left side of his body showed trouble with coordination. This pointed to damage in his brain function.

Hospital Treatment:
The patient's heart rhythm was watched for several days with no problems found. He was given medicine to stop nausea. He took metoclopramide 10 mg four times a day and ondansetron 8 mg every eight hours. This helped him feel better. Within one day, his upset stomach and vomiting stopped. He was able to eat without problems. The patient left the hospital and went to a rehab center.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient should see the brain and nerve doctors for regular visits. These visits will check how his brain is working and watch for changes in his blood vessel disease. He should also keep his blood sugar and high blood pressure under control. Family members should watch him closely during activities because he has a higher chance of falling.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 4-year-old girl who was admitted to the hospital with the presentation of snoring, restless sleep, and increasing daytime sleepiness. The patient had no difficulty falling asleep, but she tended to awaken 4-5 times during the nighttime and hardly feel refreshed after sleep. She was diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) and underwent tonsillectomy and adenoidectomy, which resulted in the elimination of snoring and decreased daytime somnolence. However, her mother was still worried about her persistent hypersomnia and underwent a follow-up sleep test that showed no sleep apnea, with a mean sleep latency of 8 minutes and two sleep onset REM periods (SOREMPs). The diagnosis of OSA and narcolepsy without cataplexy was confirmed, and the patient was started on methylphenidate 10 mg per day, which significantly improved her daytime somnolence and sleep disturbance. She has since been discharged to follow-up with her primary care physician for further management.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) and narcolepsy without cataplexy.

Treatment:
The patient underwent tonsillectomy and adenoidectomy, which resolved the OSA. She was started on methylphenidate 10 mg per day for the narcolepsy symptoms.

Follow-Up:
The patient has been discharged and will follow-up with her primary care physician to manage her condition.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 4-year-old girl who came to the hospital because of snoring, restless sleep, and feeling very sleepy during the day. The patient had no trouble falling asleep, but she woke up 4-5 times each night and did not feel rested after sleeping. She was found to have a condition where her airway gets blocked during sleep. She had surgery to remove her tonsils and adenoids. This stopped her snoring and helped her feel less sleepy during the day. However, her mother was still worried because the patient kept sleeping too much during the day. A follow-up sleep test showed her airway was no longer blocked during sleep. The test also showed she fell asleep in about 8 minutes on average and entered dream sleep very quickly two times. The patient was found to have two sleep problems. The first is blocked airway during sleep. The second is a condition that causes sudden, uncontrollable sleepiness but without muscle weakness episodes. The patient was given a medicine called methylphenidate at 10 mg per day. This medicine helped her feel much less sleepy during the day and sleep better at night. She has now left the hospital and will see her regular doctor for ongoing care.

Diagnosis:
The patient was found to have two conditions. The first is a sleep problem where the airway gets blocked during sleep. The second is a condition that causes sudden sleepiness during the day but without episodes of muscle weakness.

Treatment:
The patient had surgery to remove her tonsils and adenoids. This fixed her blocked airway problem. She was also given a medicine called methylphenidate at 10 mg per day to help with her sudden sleepiness.

Follow-Up:
The patient has left the hospital. She will see her regular doctor to keep managing her condition.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the community hospital with a complaint of syncope. The patient reported experiencing numbness in her arms while kayaking, followed by a loss of consciousness. The patient had a history of dizziness two weeks prior to the current episode. Initial laboratory tests showed low serum potassium and elevated troponin levels. An electrocardiogram revealed sinus tachycardia with monomorphic premature ventricular complexes (PVCs), and a corrected QT (QTc) interval of 520 ms. The QTc interval was normalized with potassium repletion. Echocardiography and cardiac catheterization findings were normal. A treadmill test showed multifocal PVCs and the QT interval shortened with exertion. The patient was discharged on nadolol and a LifeVest® wearable cardioverter-defibrillator.

Later, the patient presented with tachycardia for which the WCD alarmed. The patient pressed the response button to suppress a shock for more than nine minutes but eventually received two shocks. An MRI scan with gadolinium enhancement showed delayed myocardial enhancement in the midmyocardium and epicardium, indicating a nonischemic origin such as myocarditis, amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, or some other form of nonischemic cardiomyopathy. The patient was diagnosed with a phenomenon of residual fibrosis from a past viral myocarditis.

Treatment:

A dual-chamber implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) was placed, and sotalol was initiated at 80 mg twice daily and later increased to 120 mg twice daily. The patient was found to be noninducible via noninvasive programmed stimulation. The patient has been arrhythmia- and symptom-free and is scheduled for further follow-up.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the community hospital after fainting. The patient reported feeling numbness in her arms while kayaking, then passed out. The patient had felt dizzy two weeks before this happened. First blood tests showed low potassium and high levels of a heart protein called troponin. A heart tracing test showed the heart was beating fast with extra beats from the lower heart chambers. The test also showed the heart took too long to reset between beats. This timing problem went back to normal after the patient received potassium. An ultrasound of the heart and a test to look at the heart blood vessels were normal. A walking test on a treadmill showed extra heartbeats coming from different spots. The heart reset time got better during exercise. The patient went home taking nadolol and wearing a special vest that can shock the heart if needed.

Later, the patient came back because the heart was beating too fast and the vest gave a warning. The patient pressed a button to stop the vest from giving a shock for more than nine minutes. The patient then received two shocks from the vest. A special scan of the heart showed scarring in the heart muscle walls. This scarring was not caused by blocked blood vessels. It could be from heart swelling, protein buildup, or other heart muscle diseases. The doctors found that the patient had scarring left over from a past infection that caused heart swelling.

Treatment:

Doctors placed a small device inside the chest that can shock the heart back to a normal rhythm if needed. The patient started taking sotalol at 80 mg two times a day. This was later raised to 120 mg two times a day. Doctors tested the heart and could not make it go into a dangerous rhythm. The patient has had no irregular heartbeats or symptoms since then. The patient has a follow-up visit scheduled.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient presented with high-grade intermittent fever for three days with spontaneous gum bleeding and mild headache. He subsequently developed gradually worsening drowsiness, and was found to have thrombocytopenia with microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA). A diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenia purpura (TTP) was made. He was promptly commenced on therapeutic plasma exchange and 1 mg/kg of oral prednisolone. 

On the fifth day of illness, the patient developed two episodes of generalized tonic-clonic convulsions which progressed into a non-convulsive status epilepticus, requiring elective ventilation in the intensive care unit. On day 18, he developed flaccid paraparesis with sphincter dysfunction. Magnetic resonance imaging showed haemorrhage at multiple spinal levels.

Despite 16 plasma exchanges and high dose steroids, his platelet count remained persistently low, and he was investigated for alternative etiologies. Polymerase chain reaction of serum revealed clinically significant levels of cytomegalovirus (CMV). He was commenced on oral valganciclovir 450 mg/daily for 21 days. After about 6 days of valganciclovir treatment, his platelet count increased, and the MAHA resolved. 

The patient subsequently had regained normal haematological and biochemical parameters with a negative PCR quantification of CMV. He had regained ambulatory status with support and had normal sphincter function. The patient was transferred to a rehabilitation facility for further care.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came in with a high fever that came and went for three days. He also had bleeding gums and a mild headache. He then became more and more sleepy over time. Tests showed he had a very low platelet count and his red blood cells were breaking apart. The doctors found he had a rare blood disorder called TTP that causes these problems. He was quickly started on a treatment to replace his blood plasma. He also began taking a steroid medicine called prednisolone by mouth.

On the fifth day of being sick, the patient had two seizures where his whole body shook. These seizures then turned into ongoing brain activity problems. He needed to be put on a breathing machine in the intensive care unit. On day 18, he lost strength in both legs and could not control his bladder or bowels. A special scan showed bleeding at several spots along his spine.

The patient had 16 plasma replacement treatments and high doses of steroids. But his platelet count stayed very low. The doctors then looked for other causes. A blood test found high levels of a virus called CMV. He was started on an antiviral medicine called valganciclovir, 450 mg once a day for 21 days. About 6 days after starting this medicine, his platelet count went up. His red blood cells also stopped breaking apart.

The patient's blood tests later returned to normal. The virus was no longer found in his blood. He could walk again with some help. He could also control his bladder and bowels normally again. The patient was moved to a rehab center for more care.

The patient's blood tests later returned to normal. The virus was no longer found in his blood. He could walk again with some help. He could also control his bladder and bowels normally again. The patient was moved to a rehab center for more care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 6 years

Reason for Admission:
Frequent ulcers in the mouth.

Past Medical History:
Recurrent spontaneous erosions all over the body, healed by hypo- to depigmented scars, hoarseness of voice, and pruritus.

Family History:
Nonconsanguineous marriage. No family history of a similar condition.

Physical Examination:
On extraoral examination, well-defined crusted types of erosion over dorsum of hands and elbows and hyper-, hypo-, or depigmented scars were also present over back, arms, elbows, and dorsum of hands. Beaded papules present on eyelids of both eyes.
On intraoral examination, soft tissue showed irregular elevations and depressions with pearly nodular deposits throughout. Small isolated vesicular ulcerations were visible on oral mucosa. Lips were thickened and infiltrated. Tongue was stiff and frenulum thickened with inability to protrude out the tongue completely. The lateral border showed clear demarcations of dentition, indicating macroglossia.

Diagnosis:
Lipoid proteinosis

Treatment:
Started with a second-generation retinoid, Acitretin 10 mg/day.
Referred to ear, nose, and throat and dermatology department where he was put on speech therapy and emollients were prescribed.

Hospital Course:
Mild improvement in occurrence of erosions and reduction in scarring was observed. Patient was referred to other departments for further management.

Summary:
The 6-year-old male patient was admitted to the Department of Dentistry with complaints of frequent ulcers in the mouth. The patient had a history of recurrent spontaneous erosions all over the body and hoarseness of voice. On examination, well-defined crusted types of erosion over dorsum of hands and elbows, hyper-, hypo-, or depigmented scars over back, arms, elbows, and dorsum of hands, and beaded papules on eyelids of both eyes were seen. Intraoral examination revealed irregular elevations and depressions with pearly nodular deposits, small isolated vesicular ulcerations, macroglossia, stiff tongue, and thickened/lumpy lips. The patient was diagnosed with lipoid proteinosis and treated with a second-generation retinoid. Referral to other departments was made for further management of the patient.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 6 years

Reason for Admission:
Frequent sores in the mouth.

Past Medical History:
The patient had sores that kept appearing on their own all over the body. These sores healed but left lighter or darker patches on the skin. The patient also had a rough, hoarse voice and itchy skin.

Family History:
The parents are not related by blood. No one else in the family has the same condition.

Physical Examination:
On the outside of the body, the doctor found crusty sores on the backs of the hands and elbows. There were also lighter, darker, or pale scars on the back, arms, elbows, and backs of the hands. Small bumps that look like beads were found on the eyelids of both eyes.
Inside the mouth, the soft tissue had bumpy surfaces with small shiny deposits throughout. Small blister-like sores were seen on the inside of the mouth. The lips were thick and firm. The tongue was stiff and the tissue under the tongue was thick. The patient could not stick out the tongue all the way. Teeth marks on the sides of the tongue showed the tongue was larger than normal.

Diagnosis:
Lipoid proteinosis (a rare condition where a fatty protein builds up in the skin and other body parts)

Treatment:
The patient was started on Acitretin 10 mg per day. This is a medicine that helps with skin problems.
The patient was sent to the ear, nose, and throat doctors and skin doctors. They started speech therapy. Skin moisturizers were also given.

Hospital Course:
The sores happened less often and left fewer scars. The patient was sent to other doctors for more care.

Summary:
The 6-year-old male patient came to the Dental Department because of frequent mouth sores. The patient had a history of sores appearing on their own all over the body and a hoarse voice. The doctor found crusty sores on the backs of the hands and elbows. There were lighter, darker, or pale scars on the back, arms, elbows, and backs of the hands. Small bead-like bumps were on the eyelids of both eyes. Inside the mouth, there were bumpy surfaces with shiny deposits and small blister-like sores. The tongue was large, stiff, and could not stick out fully. The lips were thick and firm. The patient was found to have lipoid proteinosis and was given a skin medicine. The patient was sent to other doctors for more care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information: Mr. X, 25-year-old male

Reason for Admission: High levels of Hb, management of gout

Hospital Course: Mr. X was admitted to the hospital for evaluation of high levels of Hb and management of gout. A complete blood count showed a Hb value of 20.4 g/dL and hematocrit value of 58%. Bone marrow biopsy indicated no evidence of myeloproliferative features. No abnormalities were found on Hb electrophoresis. Congenital (familial) erythrocytosis was suspected. After mutations were not found in JAK2 V617 or exon 12, MPL W515K/L, CALR exon 9, we performed alpha 1-globin gene (HBA1)/alpha 2-globin gene (HBA2), beta-globin gene (HBB), and Von Hippel Lindau (VHL) gene mutation analysis to identify the cause of congenital erythrocytosis. In HBB gene mutation analysis, we identified a mutation in exon 2 at nucleotide 310, known as Hb Heathrow.

Diagnosis: Hb Heathrow

Medical Management: The patient's case of Hb Heathrow was managed with pharmacological agents. 

Follow-up: Follow-up appointments were scheduled for monitoring and management of the patient's condition. 

Prognosis: The prognosis for Hb Heathrow is favorable, with proper management and monitoring.

Provider: Hospital staff

Date of Visit: [date]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information: Mr. X, 25-year-old male

Reason for Admission: High levels of hemoglobin (a protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen) and treatment of gout (a type of joint pain caused by too much uric acid)

Hospital Course: Mr. X was admitted to the hospital because his hemoglobin levels were too high and he needed treatment for gout. Blood tests showed his hemoglobin was 20.4 g/dL and his red blood cell level was 58%. Both numbers were higher than normal. A bone marrow test (where a small sample is taken from inside the bone) showed no signs of blood cell diseases. A test of his hemoglobin type showed no problems. Doctors thought the patient might have been born with a condition that causes too many red blood cells. Several gene tests were done to find the cause. These tests looked at genes that control how red blood cells are made. The tests found a change in one gene called the beta-globin gene. This change is known as Hemoglobin Heathrow.

Diagnosis: Hemoglobin Heathrow (a rare inherited condition that causes the body to make too many red blood cells)

Medical Management: The patient was treated with medicines to manage the Hemoglobin Heathrow condition.

Follow-up: The patient has follow-up appointments scheduled so doctors can check on and manage the condition.

Prognosis: The outlook for Hemoglobin Heathrow is good with proper treatment and regular check-ups.

Provider: Hospital staff

Date of Visit: [date]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Age: 74
Date of Admission: [Date of Admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]
Diagnosis: Cerebral Infarction and Subarachnoid Hemorrhage

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with cerebral infarction and subarachnoid hemorrhage. During hospital stay, the patient experienced massive fresh hematochezia which resulted in a drop of hemoglobin level from 14.7 to 8.6 g/dL. Multiple ulcers with gush-out hemorrhage on the lower rectum were revealed during sigmoidoscopy. Two hemoclips and hypertonic saline-epinephrine (HSE) injection were attempted but resulted in mechanical mucosal injury that caused another gush-out hemorrhage. Compression hemostasis under endoscopic observation was used to achieve hemostasis, which was achieved after 5 minutes. The postoperative course has been uneventful for 1 month after the achievement of hemostasis. 

Hospital Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged from the hospital in a stable condition with no active symptoms or complaints.

Medications:
The patient was discharged on the current medication regimen.

Future Follow-up:
The patient should schedule a follow-up appointment with their primary care physician for ongoing monitoring and management of their condition.

Instructions and Recommendations:
The patient was advised to follow a healthy and balanced diet that is low in fat, cholesterol, and sodium. The patient was also advised to engage in healthy lifestyle practices, including regular exercise and adequate sleep. Any changes in the patient's condition or new symptoms should be reported to their healthcare provider immediately. 

Summary:
The patient was admitted with cerebral infarction and subarachnoid hemorrhage and experienced massive fresh hematochezia. Hemostasis was achieved after endoscopic observation under compression. The patient was discharged in a stable condition and is advised to follow a healthy lifestyle and schedule a follow-up appointment with their primary care physician.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Age: 74
Date of Admission: [Date of Admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]
Diagnosis: Stroke and Bleeding in the Brain

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with a stroke and bleeding in the brain. During the hospital stay, the patient had heavy bleeding from the rectum with bright red blood. This caused the blood count to drop from 14.7 to 8.6 g/dL. A camera test of the lower bowel found several open sores that were bleeding heavily. Doctors tried to stop the bleeding using small metal clips and a salt solution with medicine. However, this hurt the bowel lining and caused more heavy bleeding. Doctors then used pressure while watching with a camera to stop the bleeding. The bleeding stopped after 5 minutes. The patient had no problems for 1 month after the bleeding was stopped.

Hospital Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition with no ongoing symptoms or concerns.

Medications:
The patient was sent home with the same medicines they were already taking.

Future Follow-up:
The patient should make an appointment with their regular doctor. The doctor will check on the patient and help manage their health.

Instructions and Recommendations:
The patient was told to eat healthy foods that are low in fat, cholesterol, and salt. The patient was also told to stay active with regular exercise and get enough sleep. The patient should tell their doctor right away if they notice any changes or new symptoms.

Summary:
The patient was admitted with a stroke and bleeding in the brain. The patient also had heavy bleeding from the rectum. Doctors stopped the bleeding using pressure while watching with a camera. The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient should eat healthy, stay active, and see their regular doctor for a follow-up visit.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: Unmarried Muslim male patient
Gender: Male
Age: 21 years old
Admitting Diagnosis: Bipolar I disorder

Hospital Course:
The patient, an unmarried Muslim male businessman, was admitted to our psychiatry department with symptoms of recent aggressive behavior, over talkativeness, inappropriate behavior (such as using abusive language, absconding tendencies), elated mood, spending excess money, dressing in unusual clothes and a history of grandiose ideas stating that he is the king of Kashmir. The patient also had an irregular sleep pattern, sleeping only two hours a day, and not attending work. 

The medical team conducted a series of investigations including metabolic profiles, renal and liver function, inflammatory and infectious conditions, and blood investigations. MRI results showed bilateral basal ganglia foci of calcification and subcortical regions of bilateral cerebral hemispheres, thalami, and cerebellar parenchyma. 

The patient was provisionally diagnosed with Bipolar I disorder. He was treated with mood stabilizer sodium valproate (500 mg twice daily) and olanzapine (10 mg at bedtime). During the treatment, the patient showed marked improvement in his abnormal behaviors and symptoms.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Bipolar I disorder

Discharge Plan:
The patient showed remarkable improvement with the prescribed medication. The medical team advised the patient to continue the medication. The patient received counseling and education on the importance of sleep hygiene, stress management, and healthy lifestyle habits. The patient was also instructed to follow-up with his primary care physician, and mental wellness checks were scheduled, as well as referrals to counseling or psychiatry services if needed.

Follow-Up:
The patient was advised to have regular follow-up appointments and to maintain communication with his primary care physician and mental health providers. 

Impression:
Bipolar I disorder with marked improvement with medication treatment. 

Signed,
[Physician Name]
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: Unmarried Muslim male patient
Gender: Male
Age: 21 years old
Admitting Diagnosis: Bipolar I disorder, a mental health condition that causes extreme mood swings

Hospital Course:
The patient, an unmarried Muslim male businessman, was admitted to our mental health department. He showed signs of recent aggressive behavior and talking much more than usual. He also acted in ways that were not appropriate, such as using bad language and trying to leave without permission. The patient had a very happy and excited mood and was spending too much money. He was wearing unusual clothes and believed he was the king of Kashmir. The patient was only sleeping about two hours a day and was not going to work.

The care team ran several tests. These included tests for body chemistry, kidney and liver health, signs of swelling or infection, and blood tests. Brain scans showed small calcium deposits in certain deep parts of the brain. These deposits were found in areas called the basal ganglia, outer brain regions on both sides, the thalami, and the cerebellum.

The patient was diagnosed with Bipolar I disorder. He was given sodium valproate, a mood-stabilizing medicine, at 500 mg two times a day. He was also given olanzapine, 10 mg at bedtime. During treatment, the patient got much better. His unusual behaviors and symptoms improved a lot.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Bipolar I disorder, a mental health condition that causes extreme mood swings

Discharge Plan:
The patient got much better with the medicines he was given. The care team told the patient to keep taking his medicines. The patient received advice and teaching about getting good sleep, managing stress, and living a healthy life. The patient was told to see his regular doctor for follow-up visits. Appointments to check on his mental health were also set up. He was given information about counseling or mental health services if needed.

Follow-Up:
The patient was told to keep his follow-up appointments. He should stay in touch with his regular doctor and mental health care providers.

Impression:
Bipolar I disorder with great improvement after taking medicines.

Signed,
[Physician Name]

input:
Hospital Course:
-The patient was admitted to the cardiac catheterization laboratory with sudden onset chest pain associated with anterior ST-segment elevation on the electrocardiogram. The chest pain occurred during a bowel movement and he was found to be in cardiogenic shock upon arrival.
-Emergency diagnostic coronary angiography was performed, revealing severe proximal stenosis in the left anterior descending (LAD) artery. An extravasation of contrast media from the LAD artery into the pericardium was noted, with no flow in the distal LAD artery, resulting in a suspected dehiscence of the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) graft.
-Injection of contrast media discovered that the LIMA graft's flow of contrast only reached the mid-segment and stopped, while the saphenous vein grafts (SVGs) in the right coronary artery (RCA) and circumflex (LCx) artery were patent.
-After diagnostic coronary angiography, the patient experienced pulseless electrical activity cardiac arrest, but spontaneous circulation was achieved with CPR, and an intra-aortic balloon pump was inserted.
-Urgent echocardiography revealed a large global pericardial effusion with right atrial and right ventricular collapse. Emergency pericardiocentesis was performed to aspirate 700 mL of haemorrhagic fluid that contained an organized thrombus.
-Due to on-going haemodynamic compromise and non-availability of cardiac surgery on-site, it was decided to perform percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to treat the LAD artery stenosis. Following PCI, thrombolysis in myocardial infarction (TIMI) 3 flow was achieved, and no further extravasation was seen at the dehiscence site.
-Assessment of the LIMA graft showed a sluggish flow with extravasation of contrast media into the pericardium, requiring four coils (Tornado Embolisation Microcoils. Cook Medical, Bloomington, IN, USA) for embolization to halt the flow.
-The patient was then transferred to the local cardiac surgery unit and underwent emergency re-sternotomy and evacuation of the blood clots. His haemoglobin level post CABG (4 weeks prior to this admission) was measured at 100 g/L and dropped to 79 g/L following LIMA avulsion. He received 2 units of blood transfusion.
-The echocardiogram post-PCI revealed residual blood clots in the pericardial space that were difficult to extract percutaneously.
-The patient made a full recovery with no neurological sequelae and was discharged after a few days.
-Outpatient review at 3 months confirmed an excellent recovery without report of cardiovascular symptoms. Control coronary angiography was performed at 5 months, and the patient underwent successful PCI to mid and proximal LAD into the left main (LM) artery with LCx ostium optimization.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to the heart procedure room with sudden chest pain. The heart tracing showed signs of a heart attack affecting the front wall of the heart. The chest pain started while having a bowel movement. When the patient arrived, the heart was not pumping well enough to support the body.

Doctors did an emergency test to look at the heart arteries. They found a major blockage in the main artery on the front of the heart. Dye leaked from this artery into the sac around the heart. Blood was not flowing past the blockage. Doctors suspected that the bypass graft from the chest wall artery had come loose.

Doctors injected dye and found that blood flow in the chest wall artery graft stopped halfway through. The vein grafts to the other two heart arteries were open and working.

After the test, the patient's heart stopped but still showed electrical activity. The medical team did chest compressions and got the heart beating again on its own. They placed a balloon pump to help the heart.

An urgent heart ultrasound showed a large buildup of fluid all around the heart. This fluid was pressing on the right side of the heart. Doctors drained 700 mL of bloody fluid that contained a blood clot using a needle.

The patient was still not stable and heart surgery was not available at this hospital. Doctors decided to open the blocked artery using a tube threaded through the blood vessels. After this procedure, blood flow returned to normal. No more dye leaked at the loose graft site.

Checking the chest wall artery graft showed slow flow with dye still leaking into the sac around the heart. Doctors placed four small metal coils to block this graft and stop the bleeding.

The patient was then moved to a hospital with heart surgery. Doctors opened the chest again in an emergency operation to remove the blood clots. The patient's blood count was 100 four weeks earlier after bypass surgery. It dropped to 79 after the graft came loose. The patient received two bags of donated blood.

A heart ultrasound after the artery-opening procedure showed leftover blood clots around the heart. These clots were hard to remove with a needle.

The patient fully recovered with no brain damage. The patient left the hospital after a few days.

At a checkup three months later, the patient had an excellent recovery with no heart symptoms. Five months later, doctors did another test to look at the heart arteries. The patient had a successful procedure to open blockages in the main heart artery and the branch to the side of the heart.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: Patient 2
Age: 35 years
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: 11β-OHD, metabolic syndrome, class I obesity, and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)

Chief Complaint:
Patient 2 was presented with spaniomenorrhea along with hirsutism and alopecia, which led to the diagnosis of PCOS. 

History of Present Illness:
Patient 2 was diagnosed with 11β-OHD at a neonatal age. Surgical correction was done to address the virilization of the external genitalia, after which she showed normal blood pressure levels. The patient was on hydrocortisone 30 mg b.i.d treatment, which was later reduced to 20 mg b.i.d when she gained some weight. She was diagnosed with metabolic syndrome, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), and class I obesity at the age of 28, after which her weight started increasing. At the age of 32, she weighed 94 Kg and complained of persistent hypokalemia, which led to the introduction of spironolactone in her treatment.

Hospital Course:
During her regular checkups, Patient 2 did not have any acute adrenal deficiency. She lost weight after her dose of hydrocortisone was reduced, and her bone density was found to be normal during her last checkup.

Summary:
Patient 2 was found to have 11β-OHD during her neonatal age, which led to surgical correction of virilization of external genitalia. She was diagnosed with PCOS, metabolic syndrome, and class I obesity at the age of 28. She had persistent hypokalemia, for which spironolactone was introduced, and her dose of hydrocortisone was reduced. Patient 2 did not have any acute adrenal deficiency during her hospital course, and her bone density levels were normal.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: Patient 2
Age: 35 years
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: A rare condition affecting hormone production called 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency, metabolic syndrome, mild obesity, and polycystic ovary syndrome

Chief Complaint:
Patient 2 came in with irregular periods that happen less often than normal. The patient also had extra hair growth on the face and body and hair loss on the head. These signs led to a diagnosis of polycystic ovary syndrome.

History of Present Illness:
Patient 2 was diagnosed with 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency as a newborn. The patient had surgery to correct changes to the outer private parts caused by extra male hormones. After surgery, the patient had normal blood pressure levels. The patient was taking hydrocortisone 30 mg twice a day. This dose was later lowered to 20 mg twice a day when the patient gained some weight. At age 28, the patient was diagnosed with metabolic syndrome, polycystic ovary syndrome, and mild obesity. After this, the patient's weight kept going up. At age 32, the patient weighed 94 kg and had ongoing low potassium levels in the blood. This led doctors to add spironolactone to the treatment plan.

Hospital Course:
During regular checkups, Patient 2 did not have any sudden drop in adrenal hormone levels. The patient lost weight after the hydrocortisone dose was lowered. The patient's bone strength was found to be normal at the last checkup.

Summary:
Patient 2 was found to have 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency as a newborn. This led to surgery to correct changes to the outer private parts. At age 28, the patient was diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome, metabolic syndrome, and mild obesity. The patient had ongoing low potassium levels, so spironolactone was added to the treatment. The hydrocortisone dose was also lowered. Patient 2 did not have any sudden drop in adrenal hormone levels during care. The patient's bone strength was normal.
