Name: Patient 2
Age: 35 years
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: A rare condition affecting hormone production called 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency, metabolic syndrome, mild obesity, and polycystic ovary syndrome

Chief Complaint:
Patient 2 came in with irregular periods that happen less often than normal. The patient also had extra hair growth on the face and body and hair loss on the head. These signs led to a diagnosis of polycystic ovary syndrome.

History of Present Illness:
Patient 2 was diagnosed with 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency as a newborn. The patient had surgery to correct changes to the outer private parts caused by extra male hormones. After surgery, the patient had normal blood pressure levels. The patient was taking hydrocortisone 300 mg twice a day. This dose was later lowered to 20 mg twice a day when the patient gained some weight. At age 28, the patient was diagnosed with metabolic syndrome, polycystic ovary syndrome, and mild obesity. After this, the patient's weight kept going up. At age 32, the patient weighed 94 kg and had ongoing low potassium levels in the blood. This led doctors to add spironolactone to the treatment plan.

Hospital Course:
During regular checkups, Patient 2 did not have any sudden drop in adrenal hormone levels. The patient lost weight after the hydrocortisone dose was lowered. The patient's bone strength was found to be normal at the last checkup.

Summary:
Patient 2 was found to have 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency as a newborn. This led to surgery to correct changes to the outer private parts. At age 28, the patient was diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome, metabolic syndrome, and mild obesity. The patient had ongoing low potassium levels, so spironolactone was added to the treatment. The hydrocortisone dose was also lowered. Patient 2 did not have any sudden drop in adrenal hormone levels during care. The patient's bone strength was normal.

input:
Hospital Admission: Gastrointestinal complaints

History: A 70-year-old female with a history of asthma, eosinophilic esophagitis, and eosinophilic sinusitis presented with postprandial abdominal pain, bloating, and dyspepsia, which had been ongoing intermittently for 2 years.

Significant Findings: Laboratory results revealed elevated eosinophils, lipase, ALT, AST, and alkaline phosphatase. Initial EGD showed a hypertrophic major papilla, antral gastritis, and superficial gastric ulcers. MRI/MRCP revealed CBD dilatation without choledocholithiasis but with tapering of the distal CBD. ERCP revealed an inflamed major ampulla with benign papillary stenosis.

Treatment: The patient underwent sphincterotomy and insertion of a plastic stent into the bile duct, which was followed by sphincteroplasty and dilation of the papilla and distal CBD. Repeat ERCP was performed with stent removal, and biopsies of the stomach and the ampulla showed benign glandular tissue with no eosinophils. Additionally, the patient was started on prednisone and demonstrated significant improvement with systemic steroid therapy.

Follow-Up: Over six months, the patient was monitored and demonstrated controlled symptoms with normal LFTs. The patient was discharged on 40 mg of pantoprazole twice a day and 2 mg of budesonide daily. 

Prognosis: The patient's prognosis is good at this time, with continued surveillance and management of her gastrointestinal symptoms recommended.
clean_ref:
Hospital Admission: Stomach and digestive problems

History: A 70-year-old woman has asthma and a condition where too many white blood cells cause swelling in her food pipe and sinuses. She came to the hospital with stomach pain after eating, a bloated feeling, and upset stomach. These problems had been coming and going for 2 years.

Significant Findings: Blood tests showed high levels of certain white blood cells and liver enzymes. A camera test looking into the stomach showed swelling at the opening where bile enters, irritation in the lower stomach, and shallow stomach sores. An imaging scan showed the main bile tube was wider than normal but had no stones. The end of the bile tube was narrower than it should be. Another test with a camera showed the bile duct opening was swollen and partly blocked but not cancerous.

Treatment: The patient had a procedure to cut open the tight muscle at the bile duct opening. A small plastic tube was placed in the bile duct to keep it open. Later, the opening was stretched wider to improve flow. A follow-up procedure removed the plastic tube. Tissue samples from the stomach and bile duct opening showed normal tissue with no extra white blood cells. The patient also started taking a steroid medicine called prednisone. The patient got much better with this steroid treatment.

Follow-Up: Over six months, the patient was checked regularly. Her symptoms were under control and her liver test results were normal. The patient went home with instructions to take 40 mg of pantoprazole twice a day for stomach acid and 2 mg of budesonide once a day for swelling.

Prognosis: The patient is expected to do well. She should continue regular check-ups and treatment for her stomach and digestive problems.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Admitting Diagnosis: Suspected Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 36-year-old man with a history of bronchial asthma and smoking, presented with sudden onset right upper limb weakness which spread to his lower limb along with slurring of speech and altered consciousness. He was initially admitted to an outside facility where CT scan and carotid ultrasound came out to be normal. His CBC with differential showed low hemoglobin and platelets with elevated bilirubin, and peripheral blood smear (PBS) indicated fragmented red blood cells. TTP was suspected, and fresh frozen plasma (FFP) infusion started. On transfer to our facility, he received PLEX and pulse steroids for 18 sessions which resulted in the stabilization of the patient's LDH and platelet count. During his hospital stay, he was also diagnosed with eosinophilia. A bone marrow aspirate and core biopsy showed trilineage hematopoiesis with 2% blasts and 30% eosinophils. FISH for FIP1L1-PDGFRA (4q12) rearrangement was positive.
After 18 sessions of PLEX, the patient's LDH and platelet counts normalized. However, as weaning off PLEX was attempted, schistocytes re-emerged with a subsequent drop in platelet count. Consequently, a four-dose course of rituximab and Imatinib 100 mg daily were started. Thirty sessions of PLEX were needed to treat the TTP, and the last session was 4 days after the initiation of Imatinib. After more than 28 months of follow-up, the patient's eosinophil count has normalized, and there has been no recurrence of TTP.

Discharge Diagnosis: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura and Eosinophilia

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted with suspected TTP, and after receiving 18 sessions of PLEX and methylprednisolone, his platelet count and LDH stabilized. The patient was also found to have eosinophilia with trilineage hematopoiesis, which was treated with a four-dose course of rituximab and Imatinib 100 mg daily. After 30 sessions of PLEX, the TTP was resolved, and the patient's eosinophil count had normalized. After more than 28 months of follow-up, there has been no recurrence of TTP.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admitting Diagnosis: Suspected TTP, a rare blood disorder that causes small blood clots to form throughout the body

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 36-year-old man with a history of asthma and smoking. He came to the hospital with sudden weakness in his right arm. The weakness spread to his leg. He also had slurred speech and confusion. He was first seen at another hospital. A brain scan and neck blood vessel test were normal. Blood tests showed low red blood cells and low platelets. A liver waste product was high. A blood sample showed broken red blood cells. Doctors thought he might have TTP. He was given a blood product called frozen plasma. He was then moved to our hospital. He received 18 treatments to filter and clean his blood. He also got high-dose steroids through his vein. These treatments helped his platelet count and a blood waste product return to normal levels. During his stay, doctors found he had too many of a type of white blood cell. A sample of bone marrow was taken and tested. The test showed his bone marrow was making blood cells. It also showed high levels of one type of white blood cell. A gene test came back positive for a certain gene change. After 18 blood filtering treatments, his blood levels became normal. But when doctors tried to stop the treatments, broken red blood cells came back. His platelet count dropped again. So doctors started two new medicines. One was given in four doses. The other was Imatinib, 100 mg taken daily by mouth. He needed 30 blood filtering treatments in total to treat the TTP. The last treatment was 4 days after starting Imatinib. After more than 28 months of checkups, his white blood cell count is normal. The TTP has not come back.

Discharge Diagnosis: TTP, a rare blood clotting disorder, and too many of one type of white blood cell

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came in with suspected TTP. After 18 blood filtering treatments and high-dose steroids, his platelet count and blood levels got better. Doctors also found he had too many white blood cells of one type. This was treated with two medicines. One was given in four doses. The other was Imatinib, 100 mg daily. After 30 blood filtering treatments, the TTP was gone. His white blood cell count became normal. After more than 28 months of checkups, the TTP has not come back.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 24
Sex: Male

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to our hospital with a painful nodular lesion on his left temple which worsened from a month ago. He had a history of chronic rhinosinusitis and was using a fluticasone/salmeterol inhaler for 6 months. Blood tests revealed eosinophilia, and further evaluation confirmed the diagnosis of concurrent JTA and HES. The patient underwent excisional biopsy of bilateral superficial temporal arteries for his JTA and received intravenous steroid for HES.

Diagnostic Findings:
- Physical examination: Tender nodular lesions on both temples and palpable lymph node on the right inguinal area
- Laboratory findings: Elevated total leukocyte, eosinophil, and hemoglobin levels. ESR was slightly elevated
- Imaging studies: CT scan revealed several LN enlargements at bilateral axillary areas, wedge-shaped nonenhancing portion at the spleen, and enlarged LN at right inguinal area. CT angiography and 3-dimensional-brain showed fusiform dilatation in both superficial temporal arteries with wall thickening.
- Histopathological findings: Marked eosinophilic infiltration in arterial wall and perivascular tissue with eosinophilic abscess, focal destruction of vascular wall, and organizing thrombi in the left superficial temporal artery. The right inguinal LN showed reactive follicles and massive eosinophil infiltration, but no evidence of malignancy.

Diagnosis:
Concurrent JTA and HES

Treatment:
- Excisional biopsy of bilateral superficial temporal arteries for JTA
- Intravenous steroid (methylprednisolone) 90 mg per day for HES
- Low-dose steroid (prednisone 10 mg/day) for maintenance after discharge

Outcome:
The patient did not experience a recurrence of JTA after surgical excision during 2 years of follow-up. He was well-maintained without any symptoms due to eosinophilia with low-dose steroid.

Recommendations:
The patient should continue low-dose steroid for maintenance and follow-up with a primary care physician regularly. Further evaluation and management are needed for chronic rhinosinusitis.

Discharge Condition:
Stable and in improved condition.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 24
Sex: Male

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital with a painful bump on his left temple. The bump had gotten worse over the past month. He had a history of long-term sinus problems. He had been using a breathing inhaler for 6 months. Blood tests showed too many white blood cells called eosinophils. More tests confirmed two conditions at the same time. The patient had swelling of the arteries near his temples and a blood disorder with too many eosinophils. Doctors removed parts of the swollen arteries on both sides of his head through surgery. The patient also received steroids through an IV to treat the blood disorder.

Diagnostic Findings:
Physical exam showed painful bumps on both temples. There was also a swollen gland in the right groin area. Blood tests showed high levels of white blood cells, eosinophils, and red blood cells. A marker for swelling in the body was slightly high. A CT scan showed swollen glands in both armpit areas. The scan also showed a damaged area in the spleen and a swollen gland in the right groin. Special imaging of blood vessels showed the arteries on both sides of the head were widened and had thickened walls. Tissue samples showed many eosinophils had built up in the artery walls and surrounding tissue. There was also a pocket of eosinophils, some damage to the blood vessel wall, and blood clots forming in the left temple artery. The groin gland showed signs of the body fighting something and many eosinophils, but no cancer.

Diagnosis:
Swelling of the temple arteries and a blood disorder with too many eosinophils happening at the same time.

Treatment:
Doctors surgically removed parts of the swollen arteries on both sides of the head. The patient received 90 mg of steroid medicine through an IV each day for the blood disorder. After leaving the hospital, the patient takes a low dose of steroid medicine at 10 mg per day to keep the condition under control.

Outcome:
The artery swelling did not come back after surgery during 2 years of checkups. The patient stayed healthy without any problems from the blood disorder while taking low-dose steroid medicine.

Recommendations:
The patient should keep taking low-dose steroid medicine to stay healthy. The patient should see a regular doctor for checkups on a regular basis. The patient needs more care and treatment for his long-term sinus problems.

Discharge Condition:
The patient is stable and doing better.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: [REDACTED]

Medical History
The patient presented with a 6-month history of joint swelling, deformity, and movement limitation in the small and large joints. Additionally, she had poor weight gain since age one year and was born with bilateral congenital talipes equinovarus deformity. Anthropometric measurements revealed a weight of 8 kg (<5 SD), height of 75.5 cm (<3 SD), and head circumference of 44.6 cm (<3 SD). Physical examination showed mildly coarse facial features, increased body hair, and breast enlargement. Radiological findings presented the loss of bone mineralization of the involved joints, decrease in joint space and resorption of phalanges. The immune and hematological reports were normal.

Hospital Course
The patient was diagnosed with Torg syndrome after a complete medical examination and molecular analysis. The molecular analysis showed a novel base variant of MMP2:c.538G>A (p.D180N) in the homozygous state. She stayed in the hospital for [REDACTED] days and received appropriate care and treatment according to her diagnosis.

Follow-up
The patient was discharged in stable condition and advised regular follow-up appointments with the doctor. Additionally, the patient was prescribed appropriate medications and instructed to continue with ongoing physical therapy. Also, advised to maintain a healthy lifestyle and avoid any kind of physical activities that would lead to joint strain.

Significant Findings
- Torg syndrome
- Novel base variant of MMP2:c.538G>A (p.D180N)
- Joint swelling and deformity
- Decrease in joint space and resorption of phalanges
- Mildly coarse facial features, increased body hair, and breast enlargement
- Radiological findings showed loss of bone mineralization of involved joints

Discharge Medications
[REDACTED]

Discharge Diagnosis
Torg syndrome
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: [REDACTED]

Medical History
The patient came in with 6 months of swollen joints, joints that changed shape, and trouble moving. This affected both small and large joints. The patient also had poor weight gain since age one year. The patient was born with both feet turned inward. Body measurements showed a weight of 8 kg (very low for age), height of 75.5 cm (very low for age), and head size of 44.6 cm (very low for age). A physical exam showed slightly thick facial features, more body hair than usual, and breast growth. X-rays showed weak bones in the affected joints, less space between the joints, and breakdown of finger and toe bones. Blood tests and tests of the body's defense system were normal.

Hospital Course
After a full medical exam and gene testing, the patient was found to have Torg syndrome. The gene test found a new change in a gene called MMP2:c.538G>A (p.D180N). Both copies of this gene had the same change. The patient stayed in the hospital for [REDACTED] days and received the right care and treatment for this condition.

Follow-up
The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient should see the doctor for regular checkups. The patient was given medicines to take and should keep doing exercises with a physical therapist. The patient should also live a healthy lifestyle and stay away from activities that put stress on the joints.

Significant Findings
Torg syndrome
New gene change called MMP2:c.538G>A (p.D180N)
Swollen joints and joints that changed shape
Less space between joints and breakdown of finger and toe bones
Slightly thick facial features, more body hair than usual, and breast growth
X-rays showed weak bones in the affected joints

Discharge Medications
[REDACTED]

Discharge Diagnosis
Torg syndrome

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Date of Birth: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Reason for Admission: Shortness of breath

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the emergency department with shortness of breath and increasing dyspnea. She was found to have a large goiter that was compressing the trachea and causing respiratory distress. The patient was quickly transferred to the intensive care unit (ICU) where she was intubated using rapid sequence induction to relieve respiratory distress. Subsequent cerebral and thoracic computed tomography (CT) scans revealed a large goiter plunging into the upper mediastinal orifice, which was removed successfully by thyroidectomy 48 hours after admission. Prior to reversal of the neuromuscular blockage, the tube balloon was deflated, and the diagnosis of tracheomalacia was almost confirmed. The endotracheal tube was left in place for 24 hours, and then removed the next day without incident.

Course of Treatment:
The patient received corticotherapy (dexamethasone at 12 mg per day for two days) prior to thyroidectomy to accelerate fetal lung maturation. Surgery was completed without incident, as no intraoperative tracheostomy was necessary. Patient was treated with levothyroxin sodium tablets 75mcg/day post-surgery.

Follow-Up Plan:
Patient was discharged from the hospital on the 5th postoperative day without any complications. Pathology showed multinodular goiter with areas of atypical hyperplasia, but no sign of malignancy. Patient gave birth to a healthy newborn four weeks later.

Recommendations:
Patient should continue taking levothyroxin sodium tablets 75mcg/day as prescribed. She should follow up with her primary care physician for monitoring of her thyroid function and any potential complications related to the goiter removal.

clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Date of Birth: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Reason for Admission: Shortness of breath

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room with shortness of breath that was getting worse. Doctors found a large growth on her thyroid gland. This growth was pressing on her windpipe and making it hard to breathe. The patient was moved quickly to the intensive care unit. There, doctors placed a breathing tube to help her breathe. Brain and chest scans showed the thyroid growth was very large and reaching into the upper chest area. Doctors removed the thyroid gland in surgery 48 hours after the patient arrived. Before waking the patient fully, doctors tested the windpipe. They found the windpipe walls were weak and floppy. The breathing tube stayed in place for 24 hours. It was removed the next day without any problems.

Course of Treatment:
The patient received a steroid medicine called dexamethasone before surgery. She took 12 mg per day for two days. This helped her baby's lungs develop faster. The surgery went well. Doctors did not need to make an opening in her windpipe. After surgery, the patient started taking thyroid hormone pills. The medicine is levothyroxin sodium tablets at 75 mcg per day.

Follow-Up Plan:
The patient left the hospital five days after surgery with no problems. Lab tests on the removed tissue showed a thyroid growth with many lumps. Some cells were growing in an unusual way. However, there was no cancer. The patient gave birth to a healthy baby four weeks later.

Recommendations:
The patient should keep taking levothyroxin sodium tablets at 75 mcg per day as directed. She should see her regular doctor for checkups. The doctor will monitor how well her thyroid medicine is working. The doctor will also watch for any problems from the thyroid removal surgery.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 59-year-old Japanese male with a history of mid-ventricular obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) presented with an asymptomatic thrombus in the apical aneurysm. The patient declined hospital admission and elected to receive treatment at an ambulatory clinic, where anti-coagulation therapy with dabigatran was initiated. The patient showed an approximately two-fold increase in activated partial thromboplastin time and an increased internationalized ratio of the prothrombin time. However, there were no significant alterations in the levels of D-dimer, thrombin-anti-thrombin III complex, and fibrinogen. During the course of treatment, weekly echocardiographic examinations demonstrated a gradual decrease in thrombus size, from 15.0mm×17.0mm to 6.8mm×6.0mm after two weeks. No thrombus was detected after three and four weeks of treatment. No systemic thromboembolic events were reported during the four-week treatment period. Dabigatran administration was continued even after the resolution of the thrombus. 

Diagnosis:

The patient was diagnosed with an asymptomatic thrombus in the apical aneurysm. 

Treatment:

The patient elected to receive treatment at an ambulatory clinic and was initiated on anti-coagulation therapy with dabigatran administration (150mg b.i.d.). Weekly echocardiographic examinations showed a gradual decrease in thrombus size, and after four weeks of treatment, the thrombus appeared to have been resolved. No systemic thromboembolic events were reported during the four-week treatment period. 

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to continue with dabigatran administration. Follow-up appointments were recommended to monitor the patient's condition. 

Discharge Medications:

Dabigatran (150mg b.i.d.) was continued even after the resolution of the thrombus.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 59-year-old Japanese male. He has a heart condition where the heart muscle is too thick and blocks blood flow in the middle of the heart. He came in because a blood clot was found in a bulging area at the bottom of his heart. The blood clot was not causing any symptoms. The patient did not want to stay in the hospital. He chose to get treatment at an outpatient clinic instead. The patient was started on a blood-thinning medicine called dabigatran. Blood tests showed the medicine was working to slow down blood clotting. Other blood tests that check for clot activity stayed about the same. Heart ultrasound tests were done every week to check the blood clot. The blood clot got smaller over time. It went from 15.0mm by 17.0mm to 6.8mm by 6.0mm after two weeks. After three and four weeks of treatment, no blood clot could be seen. The patient did not have any problems with blood clots traveling to other parts of the body. The patient kept taking dabigatran even after the blood clot went away.

Diagnosis:

The patient was diagnosed with a blood clot in a bulging area at the bottom of his heart. The blood clot was not causing any symptoms.

Treatment:

The patient chose to get treatment at an outpatient clinic. He was started on a blood-thinning medicine called dabigatran. He takes 150mg two times a day. Heart ultrasound tests were done every week. These tests showed the blood clot getting smaller over time. After four weeks of treatment, the blood clot appeared to be gone. The patient did not have any problems with blood clots traveling to other parts of the body during treatment.

Follow-up:

The patient was told to keep taking dabigatran. Follow-up visits were recommended to check on the patient's health.

Discharge Medications:

Dabigatran 150mg two times a day. The patient will keep taking this medicine even though the blood clot is gone.

input:
Note: To maintain patient privacy, some details have been fictionalized.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with presumptive diagnosis of LVAD pump thrombosis. During his hospital stay, he was managed with argatroban infusion and other medications including bumetanide, carvedilol, digoxin, spironolactone, and milrinone infusion. However, despite being on argatroban therapy, there was a significant increase in LVAD flows and power as well as a steadily increasing LDH. All options were discussed with the patient including LVAD exchange that he declined at that time.

After considering the patient's medical history and current condition, the clinical team decided to start tissue plasminogen activator (tPa) therapy, in which initial dosing included a 10 mg IV bolus followed by 1 mg/min for 20 min and finally 1 mg/h for 24 h. The patient showed an immediate improvement in the device parameters after administration of the initial tPa bolus. Over the following days, the patient's condition continued to improve as evidenced by falling LDH levels.

After 13 days of stabilization and various medical interventions, the patient was discharged from the hospital in stable condition. Subcutaneous enoxaparin and warfarin were instituted to manage the patient's condition while awaiting a suitable transplant donor.

Discharge Diagnosis:
LVAD pump thrombosis

Discharge Instructions:
- Continue taking prescribed medications as directed by your healthcare provider
- Follow a low-sodium diet, and manage fluid intake as recommended
- Continue all outpatient therapies as directed
- Monitor and report any unusual or significant symptoms such as chest pain, shortness of breath, dizziness, or swelling.

Follow-Up Appointments:
Please follow up with your cardiologist and other healthcare providers as recommended.

Signed,
[REDACTED]
[Date]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital because doctors thought there was a blood clot in his heart pump. During his stay, he received a blood thinner called argatroban through an IV. He also took other medicines to help his heart work better. These included bumetanide, carvedilol, digoxin, spironolactone, and milrinone. Even with the blood thinner, the heart pump was working harder than it should. A blood test called LDH kept going up, which showed the clot was still a problem. The care team talked with the patient about all choices, including replacing the heart pump. The patient said no to pump replacement at that time.

The care team looked at the patient's health history and current condition. They decided to try a strong clot-busting medicine called tPa. First, the patient got 10 mg through the IV all at once. Then he got 1 mg per minute for 20 minutes. After that, he got 1 mg per hour for 24 hours. The heart pump started working better right after the first dose of tPa. Over the next few days, the patient kept getting better. The LDH blood test levels went down, which meant the clot was going away.

After 13 days of treatment and getting stable, the patient left the hospital in good condition. The patient now takes two blood thinners called enoxaparin and warfarin. These are given as shots under the skin and pills. They will help prevent new clots while the patient waits for a heart transplant.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Blood clot in heart pump

Discharge Instructions:
Keep taking all medicines the way the doctor told the patient to take them. Eat foods low in salt and drink only as much fluid as the doctor recommended. Keep going to all outside treatments as directed. Watch for and report any unusual signs like chest pain, trouble breathing, feeling dizzy, or swelling.

Follow-Up Appointments:
Please see the heart doctor and other care providers as they recommended.

Signed,
[REDACTED]
[Date]

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 44-year-old Hispanic male who was admitted for the evaluation of bilateral ocular inflammation in the setting of treatment with ipilimumab for metastatic cutaneous melanoma. He had received his third infusion two months prior to presentation, which led to systemic inflammatory side effects from therapy including rash, diarrhea, fever, and persistent rectal abscesses.

Physical Exam:
Upon presentation, the patient had central scotoma in the left eye, and both eyes showed pigment on the endothelium without keratic precipitates. Bilateral optic nerve edema with Paton’s lines and macular edema were noted on fundus examination. Cystic intraretinal and subfoveal fluid was also identified during Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT).

Treatment:
The patient was initially treated with topical therapy with prednisolone acetate and was referred to the uveitis service for further evaluation. Bilateral prednisolone acetate 1% was started four times a day with 0.2% brimonidine tartrate-0.5% timolol maleate ophthalmic solution twice a day and increased to 80 mg of oral prednisone daily. The patient was continually monitored and had his medication doses adjusted to promote healing.

Improvement and Discharge:
Over the course of following three weeks, his vision steadily improved with resolution of the optic nerve and retinal edema and subsequent development of a macular star. At the 2-month follow-up, his vision in both eyes improved to 20/25 and his anterior chamber reaction stabilized at 1+ flare and rare cell while taking prednisolone acetate four times a day. The patient was then lost to follow-up.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 44-year-old Hispanic male who was admitted for eye swelling in both eyes. This happened while he was being treated with a cancer drug called ipilimumab for skin cancer that had spread to other parts of his body. He had received his third treatment two months before coming to the hospital. The cancer treatment caused side effects throughout his body including a skin rash, loose stools, fever, and ongoing sores near his bottom.

Physical Exam:
When the patient arrived, he had a blind spot in the center of his left eye. Both eyes showed color deposits on the inner surface of the clear front layer of the eye. There were no clumps of cells stuck to this layer. The doctor looked at the back of both eyes and found swelling of the nerve that connects the eye to the brain. There were also curved lines around this nerve caused by the swelling. The central part of the back of the eye was also swollen. A special scan of the eye found small pockets of fluid in and under the center of the back of the eye.

Treatment:
The patient was first treated with steroid eye drops called prednisolone acetate. He was then sent to doctors who specialize in eye swelling for more care. He was given prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops for both eyes four times a day. He also received eye drops twice a day to lower eye pressure. These drops contained brimonidine tartrate 0.2% and timolol maleate 0.5%. His treatment was increased to include 80 mg of prednisone pills taken by mouth each day. The doctors kept checking on the patient and changed his medicine amounts to help him heal.

Improvement and Discharge:
Over the next three weeks, the patient's vision got steadily better. The swelling in the eye nerve and back of the eye went away. A star-shaped pattern then appeared in the central part of the back of the eye. At a check-up two months later, his vision in both eyes improved to nearly normal at 20/25. The swelling inside the front of his eyes became stable with only mild signs of irritation while using prednisolone acetate eye drops four times a day. The patient then stopped coming to his appointments.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [XXXXXX]
Age: [27]
Gender: [Male]
Date of Admission: [DD/MM/YYYY]
Date of Discharge: [DD/MM/YYYY]

Medical History:
The patient presented with symptoms of poor educational performance at the age of 8 years, followed by gait abnormalities and facial abnormal movements at the age of 12 years. Subsequent symptoms included dysarthria, frequent falls, ataxia and dystonia of right limbs, supranuclear vertical gaze palsy, and swallowing difficulties. Brain MRI at 24 years of age showed a generalized cortical atrophy. A muscle biopsy analyzed by light microscopy showed a nonspecific chronic inflammatory process. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated splenomegaly. The suspicion index for Niemann-Pick disease type C (NPC) scored 227 points. Nucleotide sequencing found two heterozygous changes in NPC1, which confirmed the diagnosis of NPC.

Hospital Course:
The patient was initiated on miglustat late in the progression of NPC disease with a modified functional disability scale score of 17 points for a short period (less than 6 months). He had complicated aspiration pneumonia, which required admission to ICU, pulmonary artificial ventilation, and tracheostomy. He is now relatively stable with tracheostomy and GI tube for feeding and restarted very recently treatment with miglustat 200 mg every 8 hours. However, he shows a higher disability score of 21.

Medical Condition at Discharge:
The patient's medical condition at the time of discharge was stable.

Follow-up Care:
The patient will be followed up by the neurology department for treatment and management of his condition.

Recommendations:
The patient was recommended to have regular follow-up with the neurology department. Any significant changes in his condition should be reported immediately.

Signed by:
[Doctor's Name]
[Designation]
[Date]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [XXXXXX]
Age: [27]
Gender: [Male]
Date of Admission: [DD/MM/YYYY]
Date of Discharge: [DD/MM/YYYY]

Medical History:
The patient had trouble learning in school starting at age 8. At age 12, the patient began having problems walking and unusual movements in the face. Later, the patient developed slurred speech, frequent falls, poor balance, and stiff movements in the right arm and leg. The patient also had trouble moving the eyes up and down and difficulty swallowing. A brain scan at age 24 showed the brain had shrunk. A small piece of muscle was removed and tested. It showed ongoing irritation in the tissue. A scan of the belly area showed an enlarged spleen. Doctors suspected a rare disease called Niemann-Pick disease type C. A gene test found two changes in a gene called NPC1. This confirmed the patient has Niemann-Pick disease type C.

Hospital Course:
The patient started taking a medicine called miglustat late in the disease. At that time, the disability score was 17 points. The patient took this medicine for less than 6 months. The patient then got a serious lung infection from food or liquid going into the lungs. This required a stay in the intensive care unit. The patient needed a breathing machine. Doctors also made an opening in the throat to help with breathing. The patient is now fairly stable with this breathing tube in place. The patient now gets food through a tube in the stomach. The patient recently started taking miglustat again at 200 mg three times a day. However, the disability score is now higher at 21 points.

Medical Condition at Discharge:
The patient was in stable condition when leaving the hospital.

Follow-up Care:
The patient will see the brain and nerve specialists for ongoing care and treatment.

Recommendations:
The patient should keep regular appointments with the brain and nerve specialists. Any big changes in how the patient feels should be reported right away.

Signed by:
[Doctor's Name]
[Designation]
[Date]

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 55-year-old woman, was admitted to the hospital due to the sudden onset of severe headache, syncope, left hemiparesis, and aphasia. A computed tomography (CT) scan revealed a right frontal brain hematoma and vasogenic brain edema, while cerebral angiography showed evidence of cerebral vasculitis in branches of the anterior, middle, and posterior right carotid artery system, with a small aneurysm and infundibular dilation of the left posterior communicating artery. A brain biopsy indicated perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate in meninges.

Treatment:

The patient was admitted to the ICU for monitoring and medical management. She was treated with a bolus of methylprednisolone (1 gr each day per three days) and cyclophosphamide (1 gr IV monthly per six doses). She was discharged on a regimen of prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day and phenytoin 300 mg/day.

Follow-up:

The patient is currently under monthly monitoring, receiving cyclophosphamide and prednisolone orally with dose tapering.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 55-year-old woman, was admitted to the hospital after she suddenly had a severe headache, fainted, felt weakness on the left side of her body, and had trouble speaking. A brain scan showed bleeding in the right front part of her brain and swelling around it. A test that looks at blood vessels in the brain showed inflamed blood vessels on the right side of the brain. The test also showed a small bulge in a blood vessel and a widened area in another blood vessel on the left side of the brain. A small sample of brain tissue was taken and tested. It showed signs of inflammation with certain white blood cells gathering around blood vessels in the brain covering.

Treatment:

The patient was admitted to the intensive care unit for close watching and treatment. She was given high doses of a strong anti-inflammatory medicine called methylprednisolone through her vein for three days. She was also given a medicine called cyclophosphamide through her vein once a month for six doses. This medicine helps calm down the immune system. She left the hospital with a prescription for prednisolone, a steroid medicine, at a dose based on her weight. She was also given phenytoin, a medicine to prevent seizures, at 300 mg per day.

Follow-up:

The patient is now being checked every month. She is taking cyclophosphamide and prednisolone by mouth. Her doses are being slowly lowered over time.

input:
Patient Information:
Name: [Patient's name]
Gender: Female
Age: 38 years

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient presented with painful, red raised lesions on the right forearm with fever, which gradually coalesced to form larger lesions. The patient had a prior history of upper respiratory tract infection and similar episodes three years back. On examination, a large erythematous plaque was present on the right upper extremity along with small erythematous plaques on both upper limbs, right side of the neck, and chest. Few lesions showed typical “relief of mountain range appearance”. Local temperature was raised in all the lesions. Superficial nerves were not thickened and sensations on the plaques were normal. Histopathology showed a diffuse infiltrate consisting predominantly of mature neutrophils located in the upper dermis without evidence of primary leukocytoclastic vasculitis.

Treatment:
The patient was initially treated with tablet dapsone 100 mg HS with dramatic early improvement, and relapse on discontinuation. The second episode was treated with a combination of dapsone and oral prednisolone started at 30 mg/day and gradually tapered over 4 weeks. The patient is now in remission and discharged. 

Follow-Up Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow-up with their primary care provider for further evaluation and management. 

Summary:
The patient presented with red raised, painful lesions and fever with a prior history of upper respiratory tract infection. Histopathology showed a diffuse infiltrate consisting predominantly of mature neutrophils located in the upper dermis without evidence of primary leukocytoclastic vasculitis. The patient was initially treated with tablet dapsone 100 mg HS with dramatic early improvement and relapse on discontinuation. The second episode was treated with a combination of dapsone and oral prednisolone started at 30 mg/day and gradually tapered over 4 weeks. The patient is now in remission and discharged with follow-up instructions to their primary care provider.
clean_ref:
Patient Information:
Name: [Patient's name]
Gender: Female
Age: 38 years

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came in with painful, red, raised skin bumps on the right forearm along with fever. Over time, these bumps joined together to form larger patches. The patient had a cold before this happened and had similar skin problems three years ago. During the exam, doctors found a large red patch on the right upper arm. There were also smaller red patches on both arms, the right side of the neck, and the chest. Some patches had a bumpy surface that looked like a mountain range. All the patches felt warm to the touch. The nerves near the skin were normal and the patient could feel touch on the patches normally. A skin sample test showed a buildup of white blood cells called neutrophils in the upper layer of skin. There were no signs of blood vessel damage.

Treatment:
The patient first took a pill called dapsone 100 mg at bedtime. The skin got much better quickly. But the problem came back when the patient stopped taking the medicine. For the second round, the patient took dapsone along with a steroid pill called prednisolone. The prednisolone started at 30 mg per day. The dose was slowly lowered over 4 weeks. The patient's skin has now cleared up and the patient is going home.

Follow-Up Instructions:
The patient should see their regular doctor for more checkups and care.

Summary:
The patient came in with red, raised, painful skin bumps and fever after having a cold. A skin sample test showed a buildup of white blood cells in the upper layer of skin with no blood vessel damage. The patient first took dapsone 100 mg at bedtime and got better quickly. The problem came back when the medicine was stopped. For the second round, the patient took dapsone with prednisolone starting at 30 mg per day. The steroid dose was slowly lowered over 4 weeks. The patient's skin has now cleared up and the patient is going home. The patient should follow up with their regular doctor.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 46
Sex: Male
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]

Admitting Diagnosis: Peptic perforation

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the emergency department with symptoms of pain in the epigastrium, constipation, nausea, vomiting, fever, and abdominal distension. Physical examination revealed distension, board-like rigidity, marked rebound tenderness, and absent liver dullness. A provisional diagnosis of peptic perforation was made.
Exploratory laparotomy was planned, which revealed a submucosal nodular tumor in the anti-mesenteric border of the jejunum, fifty cm from the duodeno-jejunal flexure. The growth had a central perforation with intestinal contents effusing through the rent. The growth was resected, and an end-to-end, single layer, interrupted anastomosis was performed using 2′0′ polyglycolic suture. The specimen was sent for histopathological examination, which showed a jejunal GIST of intermediate risk. Surgical lines of resection were free.

Post-Operative Course:

The postoperative period was uneventful, and the patient was discharged on the tenth post-op day after stitch removal. The patient is on a six-month follow-up receiving oral imatinib 300 mg twice a day.

Diagnosis at Discharge: None

Instructions on Discharge:

The patient was discharged in a stable condition with instructions to continue taking oral imatinib 300 mg twice a day. No further specific instructions were given.

Follow-up with:

The patient is advised to follow up with the physician for further monitoring and assessment of the condition.

Signed by: [Redacted]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 46
Sex: Male
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]

Admitting Diagnosis: Hole in the stomach lining

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the emergency room with stomach pain, constipation, nausea, vomiting, fever, and a swollen belly. The physical exam showed a swollen belly, very stiff stomach muscles, strong pain when touched, and signs of air in the belly. Doctors believed there was a hole in the stomach lining.

The patient had surgery to look inside the belly. Doctors found a small growth in the wall of the small intestine, about twenty inches from where the stomach connects to it. The growth had a hole in the center, and intestinal contents were leaking out. Doctors removed the growth and sewed the healthy ends of the intestine back together using dissolvable stitches. The growth was sent to a lab for testing. Test results showed it was a GIST, which is a type of tumor in the digestive tract with medium risk. The edges of the removed tissue were clear of any tumor.

Post-Operative Course:

The patient recovered well after surgery. The patient went home on the tenth day after surgery, after the stitches were taken out. The patient is being checked every six months and is taking imatinib 300 mg by mouth twice a day.

Diagnosis at Discharge: None

Instructions on Discharge:

The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient was told to keep taking imatinib 300 mg by mouth twice a day. No other special instructions were given.

perturbed:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 46
Sex: Male
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]

Admitting Diagnosis: Hole in the stomach lining

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the emergency room with stomach pain, constipation, nausea, vomiting, fever, and a swollen belly. The physical exam showed a swollen belly, very stiff stomach muscles, strong pain when touched, and signs of air in the belly. Doctors believed there was a hole in the stomach lining.

The patient had surgery to look inside the belly. Doctors found a small growth in the wall of the small intestine, about twenty inches from where the stomach connects to it. The growth had a hole in the center, and intestinal contents were leaking out. Doctors removed the growth and sewed the healthy ends of the intestine back together using dissolvable stitches. The growth was sent to a lab for testing. Test results showed it was a GIST, which is a type of tumor in the digestive tract with medium risk. The edges of the removed tissue were clear of any tumor.

Post-Operative Course:

The patient recovered well after surgery. The patient went home on the tenth day after surgery, after the stitches were taken out. The patient is being checked every six months and is taking imatinib 3000 mg by mouth twice a day.

Diagnosis at Discharge: None

Instructions on Discharge:

The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient was told to keep taking imatinib 300 mg by mouth twice a day. No other special instructions were given.

Follow-up with:

The patient should see the doctor for regular check-ups to monitor the condition.

Signed by: [Redacted]

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 23-year-old male with chronic HCV, presented to our care for combination therapy based on the National Committee for Control and Prevention of viral Hepatitis "C" guidelines in Egypt. The therapy regimen included pegylated interferon α 2a 180 ug/week and ribavirin 1000 mg/day. However, at week 12 of therapy, the patient was discontinued due to a lack of early virologic response.

Discharge Summary:
The patient was diagnosed with HCV in a pre-employment checkup 2 years ago. The patient's BMI was 27.5 and had a liver biopsy score of A2 F2 without steatosis. During therapy, the patient's laboratory parameters recorded OBI with HBV DNA values greater than 2000 IU. To treat this activation, lamivudine was administered and continued for 6 months. During this time, the patient continued to be exposed to both HBV and HCV viral load assessment and HBV was undetected, and the HCV RNA level decreased. The patient discontinued lamivudine due to financial constraints and was exposed to HBV and HCV viral load determination 6 months later, where HBV was undetected, and HCV RNA level decreased to 7062 IU/ml.

Overall, the patient's medical history, including HCV and the occurrence of OBI with HBV DNA values over 2000 IU during therapy, was managed effectively with the use of lamivudine. The patient discontinued lamivudine due to financial constraints and was not followed up for HCV RNA levels. No further course of action was taken at the time of the patient's discharge.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 23-year-old male with long-term hepatitis C virus infection. He came for treatment following Egypt's national guidelines for hepatitis C. The treatment plan included two medicines: a weekly injection called pegylated interferon and a daily pill called ribavirin. However, after 12 weeks of treatment, the doctors stopped the therapy. This happened because the virus levels did not drop enough.

Discharge Summary:
The patient found out he had hepatitis C during a job-related health check 2 years ago. The patient's body mass index was 27.5, which is slightly overweight. A liver tissue sample showed moderate liver damage and scarring but no fat buildup. During treatment, blood tests showed a hidden hepatitis B infection became active. The hepatitis B virus levels rose above 2000 units. To treat this, doctors gave the patient a medicine called lamivudine for 6 months. During this time, doctors kept checking both hepatitis B and hepatitis C virus levels. The hepatitis B virus became undetectable and the hepatitis C virus levels went down. The patient stopped taking lamivudine because he could not afford it. Six months later, tests showed hepatitis B was still undetectable. The hepatitis C virus level had dropped to 7062 units per milliliter.

Overall, the patient had hepatitis C and developed an active hepatitis B infection during treatment. The lamivudine medicine worked well to control the hepatitis B. The patient stopped lamivudine because of money problems. After stopping, no one checked the patient's hepatitis C virus levels. No additional treatment steps were planned when the patient left the hospital.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with hair loss, skin lesions, and itching on their face, trunk, and limbs. Previously diagnosed with folliculitis and eczema, the patient did not respond to treatment with cetirizine, hydrocortisone, and mupirocin. The patient experienced symmetrical pain in their wrist and knee joints after exposure to sunlight. Upon examination, the patient had large alopecia patches on the scalp, erythematous plaques on the scalp and face with slight scales, violaceous lichenoid papules, and blisters on the back of the hands and feet, and papules on their trunk and arms. Laboratory tests revealed WBC count of 3.31 × 109, RBC count of 3.7 × 1012, HGB level of 110 g/L, 24-h urine protein at 248 mg, and ANA at 1:160 (homogeneous spot), 1:80 (cytoplasm), dsDNA (-). The patient's ESR was 58 mm/h, and BP180 was 8 RU/ml. Pathological examination of the scalp indicated hair follicle angle plug and epidermal atrophy. The scalp pathology and laboratory tests were consistent with SLE. Pathological examination of the upper arm, hand, and back supported the diagnosis of bullous lichen planus-systemic lupus erythematosus (LP/LE) overlap syndrome.

Treatment:
The patient was given hydroxychloroquine sulfate, prednisone acetate tablets, topical halometasone cream, and tacrolimus cream. Two weeks later, the patient's symptoms showed significant improvement. The prednisone acetate dosage was decreased to 20 mg daily, acitretin 30 mg daily, and oral methotrexate 10 mg weekly. Three weeks later, the hair loss stopped, joint pain disappeared, and partial lesions regressed. Upon recent follow-up, the patient's symptoms remained stable, skin lesions disappeared, and there was some hair regrowth. The patient was prescribed hydroxychloroquine sulfate 200 mg daily, acitretin 10 mg daily, and oral methotrexate 2.5 mg weekly for continued treatment.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition with improved symptoms and prescribed medication for continued treatment. The patient should continue to follow up with their healthcare provider regularly to monitor their condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with hair loss, skin sores, and itching on their face, body, and arms and legs. The patient was told before that they had infected hair roots and a skin rash. Treatment with an allergy pill, a steroid cream, and an antibiotic cream did not help. The patient had matching pain in both wrists and both knees after being in the sun. During the exam, the doctor found large bald patches on the scalp. The patient also had red patches with light flaking on the scalp and face. There were purple-red raised bumps and blisters on the backs of the hands and feet. The patient also had bumps on their body and arms. Blood tests showed low white blood cell count, low red blood cell count, and low blood protein levels. The 24-hour urine test showed some protein in the urine. A test for self-attacking proteins was positive. The speed at which red blood cells settle was high. A skin protein test was slightly above normal. A tissue sample from the scalp showed blocked hair roots and thinning of the top skin layer. The scalp tissue sample and lab tests matched a disease called lupus where the body attacks itself. Tissue samples from the upper arm, hand, and back showed the patient has a mix of two conditions: a skin disease with bumps and lupus.

Treatment:
The patient was given a lupus medicine, a steroid pill, and two skin creams to reduce swelling. Two weeks later, the patient got much better. The steroid pill was lowered to 20 mg once a day. The patient also took a skin medicine at 30 mg once a day. The patient took a medicine to calm the immune system at 10 mg once a week. Three weeks later, the hair stopped falling out. The joint pain went away. Some skin sores started to heal. At a recent check-up, the patient stayed stable. The skin sores were gone and some hair grew back. The patient was told to take the lupus medicine at 200 mg once a day. The patient was also told to take the skin medicine at 10 mg once a day. The immune system medicine was lowered to 2.5 mg once a week.

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition with better symptoms. The patient was given medicine to keep taking at home. The patient should keep seeing their doctor regularly to check on their condition.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: XX/XX/XXXX
Gender: Female
Admission Date: XX/XX/XXXX
Discharge Date: XX/XX/XXXX

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the emergency department of our hospital due to iatrogenic acute ascending aortic dissection during coronary angioplasty. Upon arrival, the patient was sedated, afebrile, and diagnosed with atrial fibrillation. An emergent trans-thoracic echography revealed an enlarged ascending aorta. The patient underwent emergency CABG surgery and aortic dissection repair. During the procedure, Stanford type A DeBakey type II ascending aortic dissection with intramural hematoma behind brachiocephalic trunk was found. The aortic valve cusp was decalcified, and a supracoronary aortic tube graft and saphenous vein grafts were successfully placed. The patient was weaned from cardiopulmonary bypass and recovered over the course of 11 days with no reported postoperative complications.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Iatrogenic acute ascending aortic dissection during coronary angioplasty, Stanford type A DeBakey type II ascending aortic dissection with intramural hematoma behind brachiocephalic trunk, atrial fibrillation

Hospital Course:
Upon discharge, the patient was stable and fully recovered. Follow-up appointments with the patient's healthcare provider to monitor cardiac health were recommended. Continued use of prescribed medications was advised, unless otherwise directed.

Discharge Medications:
Aspirin 100 mg (Bayer Bitterfeld GmbH, Germany), Lovenox (Sanofi Winthrop Industrie, France), Plavix 75 mg (Sanofi Pharma Bristol Myers Squibb SNC, France), Concor 5 mg (Merck KGaA, Germany), Glucophage 850 mg (Merck Santé SAS, France)

Follow-up Care:
Follow-up appointments with the patient's healthcare provider to monitor cardiac health were recommended. Continued use of prescribed medications was advised, unless otherwise directed. Any significant changes in condition or concerns should be reported immediately.

clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: XX/XX/XXXX
Gender: Female
Admission Date: XX/XX/XXXX
Discharge Date: XX/XX/XXXX

Hospital Course:
The patient was brought to the emergency room because of a tear in the main blood vessel leaving the heart. This happened by accident during a procedure to open blocked heart arteries. When the patient arrived, she was given medicine to sleep and had no fever. She had an irregular heartbeat. A heart ultrasound showed that the main blood vessel leaving the heart was larger than normal. The patient needed emergency heart bypass surgery and repair of the torn blood vessel. During surgery, doctors found a serious tear in the upper part of the main blood vessel. There was also bleeding inside the blood vessel wall near where it branches to the arm and head. Doctors removed calcium buildup from the heart valve. They replaced part of the blood vessel with a tube graft. They also used a vein from the leg to bypass blocked heart arteries. The patient was taken off the heart-lung machine after surgery. She recovered over 11 days with no problems after surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Accidental tear in the main blood vessel leaving the heart during a procedure to open blocked heart arteries, serious tear in the upper part of the main blood vessel with bleeding inside the vessel wall near the branch to the arm and head, irregular heartbeat

Hospital Course:
The patient was stable and fully recovered when leaving the hospital. Follow-up visits with the patient's doctor to check heart health were recommended. The patient was told to keep taking prescribed medicines unless the doctor says otherwise.

Discharge Medications:
Aspirin 100 mg (Bayer Bitterfeld GmbH, Germany), Lovenox (Sanofi Winthrop Industrie, France), Plavix 75 mg (Sanofi Pharma Bristol Myers Squibb SNC, France), Concor 5 mg (Merck KGaA, Germany), Glucophage 850 mg (Merck Santé SAS, France)

Follow-up Care:
Follow-up visits with the patient's doctor to check heart health were recommended. The patient was told to keep taking prescribed medicines unless the doctor says otherwise. Any big changes in how the patient feels or any concerns should be reported right away.

input:
Patient: 61-year-old male with a history of recurrent chest discomfort and palpitations.

Hospital Course:
The patient was referred to our hospital due to frequent ventricular premature contractions, with no positive results found in examinations. RFCA was performed, and the patient experienced dizziness, tightness in the chest, cold sweat, bradycardia, and hypotension afterward. Further examination of the patient showed hydropericardium, and the patient underwent pericardiocentesis drainage to drain off 800 mL blood. The patient's tight chest improved, and their blood pressure increased. However, the patient experienced chest tightness again two hours later, and an additional 750 mL of blood was drained from the indwelling catheter in the pericardium. This time, the patient was given volume expansion and dopamine to sustain normal blood pressure. During the next hour, the patient experienced further circulatory unstability, and a transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular wall rupture and a hematoma in the left ventricular wall. The patient was then transferred to the operating room for surgical exploration.

Surgical intervention:
The patient underwent median sternotomy, and CPB was established with cannulation of the ascending aorta and right atrium quickly after the pericardium cavity was evacuated. Cardioplegic arrest was used, and further exploration of the heart showed a 6 × 8 cm2 area of contusion in the posterolateral wall with a 5-cm epicardial tear at the center. Teflon-buttressed sutures were used to close the tear, and a pericardium patch with continuous sutures around and bioglue inside was applied to the contusion region. The patient was discontinued from CPB uneventfully with excellent hemodynamic status.

Discharge Summary:
The patient has recovered well from their surgical intervention and was discharged from the hospital on the 15th postoperative day. Final examination showed that the injury region of the left ventricular wall had healed. The patient is advised to maintain a healthy diet and exercise regimen and to follow up with their primary care physician as needed.
clean_ref:
Patient: 61-year-old male with a history of repeated chest discomfort and feelings of a racing or pounding heart.

Hospital Course:
The patient was sent to our hospital because of frequent extra heartbeats coming from the lower heart chambers. Tests did not find any problems. A procedure was done to fix the irregular heartbeat using heat energy. After the procedure, the patient felt dizzy and had chest tightness, cold sweats, a slow heartbeat, and low blood pressure. More tests showed fluid had built up around the heart. A needle was used to drain 800 mL of blood from around the heart. The patient's chest tightness got better and blood pressure went up. But two hours later, the chest tightness came back. Another 750 mL of blood was drained through a tube left in place around the heart. The patient was given extra fluids and a medicine called dopamine to keep blood pressure normal. Over the next hour, the patient's blood flow became more unstable. An ultrasound of the heart showed a tear in the wall of the lower left heart chamber and a blood clot in that wall. The patient was then moved to the operating room for surgery.

Surgical intervention:
The patient had surgery where the chest was opened through the breastbone. A heart-lung machine was set up by placing tubes in the main blood vessel leaving the heart and the upper right heart chamber. This was done quickly after blood was removed from around the heart. The heart was stopped using a special solution. Looking at the heart showed a bruised area about 6 by 8 centimeters on the back and side wall. There was a 5-centimeter tear in the outer heart layer in the middle of this area. The tear was closed using stitches with supportive pads. A patch made from heart lining tissue was sewn around the bruised area with medical glue inside. The patient was taken off the heart-lung machine without problems. Blood was flowing well through the heart.

Discharge Summary:
The patient has healed well from the surgery and left the hospital 15 days after the operation. The final tests showed that the damaged area of the lower left heart chamber wall had healed. The patient should eat healthy foods and exercise regularly. The patient should see their regular doctor for follow-up visits as needed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 35
Gender: Female
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital with a 2-month history of fever, night sweats and fatigue. She had undergone a kidney transplant 5 years ago and was taking tacrolimus and prednisone (2.5 mg daily). A chest magnetic resonance imaging revealed multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, up to 2.5 cm in diameter. The patient was initially treated empirically for tuberculosis. The serum cryptococcal antigen and Histoplasma immune diffusion tests were negative, and microscopic examination of bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) fluid was negative for fungi. The BAL galactomannan index was 0.3. An open lung biopsy revealed a granulocytic infiltrate, with spherical budding structures suggestive of Histoplasma capsulatum.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with disseminated histoplasmosis.

Treatment:
The patient received antifungal therapy for the disseminated histoplasmosis. The medical team carefully monitored the patient's response to treatment and the patient was eventually discharged from the hospital.

Follow-Up:
The patient will follow up with her transplant clinic to ensure proper management of her antifungal treatment and overall health. It is important that the patient stays vigilant for any signs of recurrence or other complications following treatment.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 35
Gender: Female
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital after having fever, night sweats, and tiredness for 2 months. She had a kidney transplant 5 years ago. She was taking two medicines to prevent her body from rejecting the new kidney: tacrolimus and prednisone (2.5 mg daily). A special scan of her chest showed several round spots in both lungs. The largest spot was about 1 inch wide. At first, doctors treated her for a lung infection called tuberculosis. Blood tests for two types of fungus came back negative. Doctors also tested fluid from her lungs and found no fungus. A sample of lung tissue was taken during surgery. This sample showed signs of infection and small round shapes. These shapes pointed to a fungus called Histoplasma capsulatum.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a fungal infection called histoplasmosis that had spread through her body.

Treatment:
The patient received medicine to fight the fungal infection. The care team watched closely to see how well the treatment worked. The patient got better and was sent home from the hospital.

Follow-Up:
The patient will see her transplant doctors for a check-up. They will make sure her fungal infection medicine is working well. They will also check her overall health. The patient should watch for any signs that the infection has come back. She should also look out for any other health problems after treatment.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Age: 33

Sex: Male

Hospital Admission Date: [Redacted]

Hospital Discharge Date: [Redacted]

Diagnosis: CP CML

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to [Redacted] Hospital on [Redacted] with a diagnosis of CP CML. The white blood cell (WBC) count at the time of admission was 25.5×109/l and the platelet count was 432×109/l. The patient presented with splenomegaly, weight loss, and fever. The patient was treated with IM at a dose of 400 mg/day for a total of 54 months, after which the relevant symptoms appeared to have improved.

On [Redacted], the patient presented again with a WBC count of 14.6×109/l and a platelet count of 117×109/l. The patient was treated with IM at a dose of 800 mg/day for a total of 6 months. The patient had a history of a brother diagnosed with CML in 1994 who succumbed following 6 months of chemotherapy.

On [Redacted], the patient was discharged in stable condition with follow-up appointments advised.

Discharge Medications:

The patient was discharged with medications which are not mentioned in the case report.

Follow-Up:

The patient was advised to follow up with his treating physician and undergo a complete physical examination, laboratory tests, and other necessary evaluation tests for CML.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Age: 33

Sex: Male

Hospital Admission Date: [Redacted]

Hospital Discharge Date: [Redacted]

Diagnosis: Chronic phase chronic myeloid leukemia (a type of blood cancer)

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to [Redacted] Hospital on [Redacted] with a type of blood cancer called chronic myeloid leukemia in its early stage. At admission, the white blood cell count was high at 25.5 billion cells per liter. The platelet count was also high at 432 billion cells per liter. The patient had an enlarged spleen, weight loss, and fever. The patient was treated with a cancer medicine called imatinib at 400 mg per day for 54 months. After this treatment, the symptoms got better.

On [Redacted], the patient came back with a white blood cell count of 14.6 billion cells per liter. The platelet count was 117 billion cells per liter. The patient was then treated with imatinib at a higher dose of 800 mg per day for 6 months. The patient had a brother who was diagnosed with the same type of blood cancer in 1994. The brother died after 6 months of cancer treatment.

On [Redacted], the patient left the hospital in stable condition. Follow-up visits were recommended.

Discharge Medications:

The patient was sent home with medications that are not listed in this report.

Follow-Up:

The patient was told to schedule a visit with the doctor providing treatment. The patient should have a full physical exam, blood tests, and other tests needed to check on the blood cancer.

On [Redacted], the patient came back with a white blood cell count of 14.6 billion cells per liter. The platelet count was 117 billion cells per liter. The patient was then treated with imatinib at a higher dose of 800 mg per day for 6 months. The patient had a brother who was diagnosed with the same type of blood cancer in 1994. The brother died after 6 months of cancer treatment.

On [Redacted], the patient left the hospital in stable condition. Follow-up visits were recommended.

Discharge Medications:

The patient was sent home with medications that are not listed in this report.

Follow-Up:

The patient was told to schedule a visit with the doctor providing treatment. The patient should have a full physical exam, blood tests, and other tests needed to check on the blood cancer.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with c-kit-positive indolent systemic mastocytosis (ISM) based on her clinical presentation and laboratory tests.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with a history of anaphylaxis due to a Hymenoptera sting. She had no visible skin manifestations of mastocytosis. The patient was promptly treated with cetirizine and ranitidine, which resulted in some improvement but with occasional symptoms such as flushing, lightheadedness, vomiting, drenching night sweats, and bone pain.

The patient was started on omalizumab at a dose of 300 mg every 4 weeks which was continued for 14 months. During this period, she also underwent rush venom desensitization. The treatment led to complete resolution of the underlying symptoms of ISM. The patient's tryptase levels also showed a decrease from 40.5 ng/ml to 36.8 ng/ml.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's symptoms have resolved completely on ongoing omalizumab therapy. The patient has been discharged in stable condition with advice to continue the treatment for mastocytosis.

Follow-Up:
The patient has to follow-up regularly with her treating physician for monitoring of the disease and symptom control.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a rare condition called systemic mastocytosis. This means the body makes too many mast cells, which are a type of immune cell. Tests showed a specific marker called c-kit was positive. The patient has a mild form of this condition.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital after having a severe allergic reaction to a bee or wasp sting. The patient did not have any rashes or skin changes linked to this condition. The patient was given two medicines, cetirizine and ranitidine, to help with symptoms. These medicines helped some, but the patient still had problems at times. These problems included skin flushing, feeling dizzy, throwing up, heavy sweating at night, and bone pain.

The patient was started on a medicine called omalizumab. The patient received a shot of 300 mg every 4 weeks for 14 months. During this time, the patient also received treatment to reduce allergic reactions to insect stings. This treatment slowly got the body used to the venom. The treatment worked well and all symptoms went away. A blood test that measures mast cell activity dropped from 40.5 to 36.8, which is a good sign.

Discharge Condition:
The patient no longer has any symptoms while taking omalizumab. The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient should keep taking the medicine to control the mast cell condition.

Follow-Up:
The patient needs to see the doctor regularly. The doctor will check on the condition and make sure symptoms stay under control.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Unknown

Age: 51-year-old

Gender: Female

Medical Record Number: Unknown

Admission Date: Unknown

Discharge Date: Unknown

Reason for Admission: Fever and Profound Neutropenia

Diagnosis: Metastatic Squamous Cell Carcinoma with Vitamin B6 Deficiency

Hospital Course:

The patient was a 51-year-old woman with relapsed non-Hodgkin lymphoma treated four years earlier with an allogeneic stem cell transplant. She presented with fever and profound neutropenia. A complete blood count showed a normal total leukocyte count with 2% granulocytes together with 84% lymphocytes and 14% monocytes. Red blood cells were normal. The patient had a history of chronic extensive graft-versus-host disease (GvHD) involving the skin and dermal squamous cell carcinoma. Immunosuppression had been discontinued two months earlier due to the development of squamous cell carcinoma. The patient's blood cultures showed a micrococcus species, and her fever resolved with a course of vancomycin.

Histological examination of the patient's marrow demonstrated marked hypocellularity, and biopsy of a cervical lymph node showed poorly differentiated carcinoma consistent with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma. The patient was treated with granulocyte colony stimulating factor (G-CSF) without any improvement in her neutropenia. Following daily supplementation with 150 mg Vitamin B6, blood B6 levels normalized and the neutropenia resolved. The cervical adenopathy completely regressed by clinical examination and became scintographically negative by PET/CT.

The patient's medications were adjusted throughout her hospital course. Valganciclovir, voriconazole, and Soriatane (acitretin) were discontinued, and prophylactic fluconazole and acyclovir were started. The patient was discharged in stable condition.

Instructions at Discharge:

The patient was advised to follow up with her oncologist for continued management of her metastatic squamous cell carcinoma. She was instructed to continue daily Vitamin B6 supplementation, and to avoid exposure to infection. The patient was given a follow-up appointment with her primary care physician in three to four weeks.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Unknown

Age: 51-year-old

Gender: Female

Medical Record Number: Unknown

Admission Date: Unknown

Discharge Date: Unknown

Reason for Admission: Fever and very low white blood cell count

Diagnosis: Skin cancer that has spread to other parts of the body with low Vitamin B6 levels

Hospital Course:

The patient was a 51-year-old woman with blood cancer that came back after treatment. She had received donor stem cells four years earlier. She came to the hospital with fever and very low white blood cell count. Blood tests showed a normal total white blood cell count. However, only 2% were infection-fighting cells. The rest were 84% immune cells and 14% other white blood cells. Red blood cells were normal. The patient had a history of her body attacking itself after the transplant. This affected her skin and caused skin cancer. Her medicines to lower her immune system were stopped two months earlier because of the skin cancer. Blood tests found bacteria called micrococcus. Her fever went away after taking an antibiotic called vancomycin.

A test of the patient's bone marrow showed very few cells. A sample taken from a neck lymph node showed cancer cells. This meant the skin cancer had spread. The patient was given medicine to boost white blood cell growth. This did not help raise her white blood cell count. After taking 150 mg of Vitamin B6 each day, her B6 levels became normal. Her white blood cell count also returned to normal. The swollen lymph nodes in her neck went away completely. Imaging scans showed no signs of cancer in that area.

The patient's medicines were changed during her hospital stay. Three medicines were stopped: valganciclovir, voriconazole, and Soriatane (acitretin). Two new medicines were started to prevent infections: fluconazole and acyclovir. The patient left the hospital in stable condition.

Instructions at Discharge:

The patient was told to see her cancer doctor for ongoing care of her skin cancer that has spread. She was told to keep taking Vitamin B6 every day. She was also told to stay away from people who are sick. The patient was given an appointment to see her regular doctor in three to four weeks.
