Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 56-year-old male with a history of dilated cardiomyopathy and atrial fibrillation, presented with epigastralgia and nonsustained VT. The patient was treated with amiodarone, nifekalant hydrochloride, and lidocaine, but the ventricular arrhythmias persisted which led to a decrease in atrial flutter. Severe anemia and blastoid cells were observed in the peripheral blood sample, and the patient was diagnosed with Ph+ ALL. Dasatinib 50 mg twice daily and prednisolone 100 mg daily were initiated to treat the leukemia. The dasatinib was discontinued after five days due to an increase in nonsustained VT although the QTc interval was not significantly prolonged. Bisoprolol was gradually increased to 3.75 mg daily, and dasatinib was rechallenged. The patient showed a complete hematological response and a 2-log reduction in minor bcr-abl mRNA transcripts in the bone marrow. Three weeks after the retreatment with dasatinib, blastoid cells reemerged in the peripheral blood, and the dasatinib was discontinued due to refractoriness of leukemia. The patient received steroid pulse therapy and furosemide, and VT did not recur after. Although, dexamethasone and the short-term use of mercaptopurine were used, the patient expired from leukemia on the 183rd day after admission.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was admitted with epigastralgia and nonsustained VT which was treated with amiodarone, nifekalant hydrochloride, and lidocaine. After a diagnosis of Ph+ ALL, the patient was initiated with dasatinib 50 mg twice daily and prednisolone 100 mg daily to manage leukemia. Due to complications, dasatinib and bisoprolol doses were adjusted. After the first retreatment with dasatinib, a complete hematological response was observed and minor bcr-abl mRNA transcripts reduced. The patient expired from leukemia on the 183rd day after admission.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 56-year-old male with a history of a weakened, enlarged heart and irregular heartbeat. He came to the hospital with stomach pain and short bursts of fast heartbeats from the lower heart chambers. The patient was treated with three heart rhythm medicines called amiodarone, nifekalant hydrochloride, and lidocaine. The abnormal heart rhythms did not stop, which led to a slower type of irregular heartbeat. Blood tests showed very low red blood cell counts and abnormal young blood cells. The patient was found to have a type of blood cancer called Ph-positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. The patient started taking dasatinib 50 mg twice a day and prednisolone 100 mg once a day to treat the cancer. Dasatinib was stopped after five days because the short bursts of fast heartbeats got worse. The heart rhythm measurement stayed close to normal during this time. A heart medicine called bisoprolol was slowly raised to 3.75 mg daily. Then dasatinib was started again. The blood cell counts returned to normal. Cancer markers in the bone marrow dropped by a large amount. Three weeks after restarting dasatinib, abnormal cancer cells came back in the blood. Dasatinib was stopped because the cancer was no longer responding to it. The patient received high-dose steroids and a water pill called furosemide. The fast heartbeat problem did not come back after this treatment. The patient also received dexamethasone and a short course of mercaptopurine. The patient died from the blood cancer on day 183 after being admitted to the hospital.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient came to the hospital with stomach pain and short bursts of fast heartbeats. These were treated with amiodarone, nifekalant hydrochloride, and lidocaine. After being diagnosed with Ph-positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia, the patient started dasatinib 50 mg twice daily and prednisolone 100 mg daily. The doses of dasatinib and bisoprolol were changed due to problems that came up. After restarting dasatinib, blood cell counts returned to normal and cancer markers went down. The patient died from the blood cancer on day 183 after being admitted to the hospital.

input:
Hospital Course:

An 8-year-old male child was admitted to our hospital with cardiac failure. The patient had a history of TOF repair and left pulmonary arterioplasty two years ago at the referring hospital. Echocardiogram conducted during admission showed the presence of a large residual muscular outlet ventricular septal defect (VSD) likely following VSD patch dehiscence. Cardiac catheterization was performed for further evaluation which confirmed the presence of VSD via angiogram. The right ventricular pressure was high at systole, and angiogram in the main pulmonary artery showed dilated right pulmonary artery while the left pulmonary artery (LPA) was completely occluded.

Intervention:

Radio-frequency (RF) energy was applied to the blind-ending channel of the proximal LPA to recanalize the occluded artery. A 2mm × 10mm coronary balloon was inflated at the atretic segment of LPA and a 6mm × 20mm cutting balloon was used to perform balloon dilatation. A 10mm × 17mm stent was used to address the atretic segment, which was further dilated using an 8mm×40mm Powerflex PTA balloon. The patient was discharged 3 days after the procedure with aspirin at 5mg/kg.

Follow-up:

The patient was readmitted 4 weeks after the procedure for the revision of the VSD patch. Repeat cardiac catheterization conducted three months later shows that the LPA stent is patent and the LPA had increased in size with improved left lung arborization. A localized segment of the left lower lobe artery was stenosed (2.4mm), which was subsequently redilated using an 8mm × 20mm peripheral Flextome cutting balloon.

Outcome:

A Formula 418 Expandable stent was implanted to the stenotic segment distal to the first stent with satisfactory results. The LPA and distal pulmonary arterial tree appear healthy and are of satisfactory size for future growth. Repeat cardiac catheterization conducted four months after the initial intervention showed a reduction of the right ventricular pressure of 41 end-diastolic pressure 8 mmHg (simultaneous aortic pressure: 91/50 mmHg, mean: 66 mmHg).

Conclusion:

The patient was discharged in stable condition with aspirin therapy. The guardian of the patient was instructed to follow-up with their local pediatric cardiologist for routine monitoring and care.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

An 8-year-old boy was admitted to our hospital because his heart was not pumping well. Two years ago, the patient had surgery at another hospital to fix a heart defect called Tetralogy of Fallot. That surgery also included repair of the left lung artery. A heart ultrasound done when he arrived showed a large hole between the lower chambers of his heart. This hole was likely caused by a surgical patch that had come loose. A procedure using a thin tube inserted into the heart was done to learn more. This test confirmed the hole was there. The pressure in the right side of the heart was too high. The test also showed the right lung artery was enlarged. The left lung artery was completely blocked.

Intervention:

Doctors used a special energy tool to open the blocked left lung artery. A small balloon was placed in the blocked area and inflated to widen it. A larger balloon with tiny blades was then used to open it more. A small metal tube called a stent was placed to keep the artery open. Another balloon was used to widen the stent further. The patient went home 3 days after the procedure. The patient was given aspirin at a dose of 5mg for every kilogram of body weight.

Follow-up:

The patient came back to the hospital 4 weeks later to fix the loose heart patch. Three months after the first procedure, another heart tube test was done. This test showed the metal tube in the left lung artery was open and working. The left lung artery had grown larger. Blood flow to the left lung had improved. However, a small section of an artery in the lower left lung was too narrow. Doctors widened this narrow spot using another balloon with tiny blades.

Outcome:

A second metal tube that can expand was placed in the narrow section beyond the first tube. The results were good. The left lung artery and the smaller arteries in the lung look healthy. They are a good size to allow for future growth. A heart tube test done four months after the first procedure showed the pressure in the right side of the heart had gone down. The pressure was now 41 when the heart squeezes and 8 when it relaxes. At the same time, the pressure in the main body artery was 91 over 50 with an average of 66.

Conclusion:

The patient went home in stable condition and will continue taking aspirin. The guardian of the patient was told to schedule regular visits with their local childrens heart doctor for ongoing check-ups and care.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Patient underwent a right parietal craniotomy for evacuation of a subdural hematoma, with good post-operative progress. The patient was then started on unfractionated heparin infusion, given an intravenous bolus of 5000 U and infusion rate of 1000 U/hr with an aPTT goal of 50 to 70 s for 3 days. Daily echocardiographic evaluation did not reveal any thrombosis in the prosthetic cardiac valves. On the seventh day after surgery, subcutaneous enoxaparin (2 × 0.6 cc) was started, and anti-factor Xa level was monitored until discharge, with a therapeutic range of 0.8 to 1.2 IU/mL. The patient was discharged after her anti-factor Xa level reached 1.1 IU/mL with the recommendation of twice daily 0.6 cc subcutaneous enoxaparin, and the goal INR of 3 to 3.5. Patient delivered a healthy baby via Cesarean delivery under unfractionated heparin treatment, with no complications.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

The patient had surgery on the right side of the head to remove a blood clot on the brain. The patient recovered well after surgery. The patient was then given a blood thinner medicine through a vein. This started with a first dose of 5000 units, then 1000 units per hour. The goal was to keep blood clotting time between 50 and 70 seconds for 3 days. Heart scans were done every day to check for blood clots on the artificial heart valves. No clots were found. On the seventh day after surgery, the patient started getting a different blood thinner shot under the skin twice a day at 0.6 cc each time. Blood tests checked how well this medicine was working until discharge. The target range was 0.8 to 1.2 units per milliliter. The patient was sent home when the blood test result reached 1.1 units per milliliter. The patient should continue the blood thinner shots of 0.6 cc under the skin twice a day. The goal is to keep the blood clotting measure between 3 and 3.5. The patient gave birth to a healthy baby by surgery while on the blood thinner given through a vein. There were no problems.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]
Admission Diagnosis: Anthrax lesion on the left upper eyelid 
Discharge Diagnosis: Healing of the anthrax lesion on the left upper eyelid 

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the clinic with a 1 cm round, blackish, ulcerated, and crusted surface lesion on the left upper eyelid that started 5 days ago. Gram(+) bacilli compatible with B. anthracis were observed in Gram staining performed with the wound culture taken from the lesion. The patient had contact with meat bought from a butcher during the regional anthrax endemic period in 2018 and had no direct contact with an infected animal. The patient was started on ciprofloxacin 500 mg every 8 hours and amoxicillin 875 mg + clavulanic acid 125 mg every 12 hours for oral treatment. For topical treatment, chloramphenicol ointment was initiated twice daily. Eyelid edema slightly increased 1 day after the treatment was initiated. A sample was taken from the wound on the second day, and no growth was observed due to antibiotic use. 

On the seventh day of treatment, significant improvement was observed in the lesion, with healing zones seen in the middle of the crust and on the intact skin border. Eyelid edema and redness regressed. The antibiotic treatment was stopped on the 16th day of treatment based on signs of significant improvement in the lesion. All vital signs remained within normal limits throughout the patient's hospital course. 

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was discharged with instructions to follow up with their primary care physician and to continue applying chloramphenicol ointment twice daily on the healed area of the lesion, as directed. The patient was also instructed to practice good hand hygiene and to avoid contact with animals or contaminated products. In case of any additional symptoms or concerns, the patient was advised to contact their healthcare provider.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]
Admission Diagnosis: Anthrax sore on the left upper eyelid
Discharge Diagnosis: The anthrax sore on the left upper eyelid is healing

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the clinic with a sore on the left upper eyelid. The sore was round, about half an inch wide, dark in color, and had a scab. It started 5 days before the visit. A sample was taken from the sore and tested. The test showed bacteria that matched anthrax germs. The patient had touched meat from a butcher shop in 2018 when anthrax was spreading in the area. The patient did not touch any sick animals directly. The patient was given two medicines to take by mouth. The first was ciprofloxacin 500 mg taken every 8 hours. The second was amoxicillin 875 mg plus clavulanic acid 125 mg taken every 12 hours. The patient also used chloramphenicol ointment on the sore twice a day. One day after starting treatment, the eyelid swelling got a little worse. A second sample was taken from the sore on day two. No bacteria grew because the medicines were already working.

By day seven, the sore was much better. The middle of the scab and the skin around it showed signs of healing. The eyelid swelling and redness went down. The patient stopped taking the medicines on day 16 because the sore had improved a lot. All body signs like heart rate and temperature stayed normal during the hospital stay.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was sent home with the following instructions. The patient should see their regular doctor for a follow-up visit. The patient should keep putting chloramphenicol ointment on the healing sore twice a day as told. The patient should wash hands well and often. The patient should stay away from animals and products that might carry germs. If the patient has new symptoms or worries, the patient should call their doctor.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Undisclosed

Date of Admission: Undisclosed

Date of Discharge: Undisclosed

Admission Diagnosis: Discharging sinuses over the lower anterior abdominal wall

Discharge Diagnosis: Dual infection caused by Mycobacterium szulgai and M. intermedium

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 46-year-old female with a history of laparoscopic appendectomy in 1998, presented with discharging sinuses over the lower anterior abdominal wall for the past two years. The initial incision wound did not heal properly and was later reopened after one month. Despite receiving treatment off and on with multiple injectable and oral antibiotics, there was no definitive microbiological diagnosis prior to admission. The patient did not have any history of local trauma/injury, immunosuppressive drugs/corticosteroids, or tuberculosis.

On admission, a previous surgical scar was noticed on the lower anterior abdominal wall in the midline with a large keloid formed over it. Six sinus openings were noted over the lower anterior abdominal wall with a single sinus discharging pus at the time of presentation. Tenderness was noted over and around the sinuses. Gram variable bacilli and numerous acid-fast bacilli were observed in the discharge. Following culture and antibiotic susceptibility testing, the patient was started on a combination of rifampicin, ethambutol, and clarithromycin.

After one month of therapy, there was no pus discharge from the sinus. The patient was advised to continue the therapy and report back to the hospital after another month. After two months of therapy, the patient was found to be completely all right with no such discharge.

Discharge Medications:

The patient was advised to continue taking rifampicin 600 mg once daily, ethambutol 600 mg once daily, and clarithromycin 500 mg twice daily.

Follow-Up:

The patient was advised to continue with periodic follow-up based upon clinical response and microbiological response.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Undisclosed

Date of Admission: Undisclosed

Date of Discharge: Undisclosed

Admission Diagnosis: Draining wounds on the lower front part of the belly

Discharge Diagnosis: Infection caused by two types of bacteria called Mycobacterium szulgai and M. intermedium

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 46-year-old woman. She had surgery to remove her appendix using small cuts in 1998. For the past two years, she had draining wounds on the lower front part of her belly. The cut from her surgery did not heal the right way. It opened up again after one month. She took many antibiotic medicines by mouth and by injection on and off. But doctors did not find the exact cause of the infection before this hospital stay. The patient had no history of injury to the area. She did not take medicines that weaken the body's defense system. She did not have tuberculosis.

When the patient came to the hospital, doctors saw an old surgery scar on the lower front part of her belly. A large raised scar had formed over it. There were six small tunnel openings on the lower front part of her belly. One of these tunnels was releasing thick fluid at the time. The area over and around these tunnels was sore to touch. Tests of the fluid showed bacteria. After testing which medicines would work best, doctors started the patient on three medicines called rifampicin, ethambutol, and clarithromycin.

After one month of treatment, the tunnel stopped releasing fluid. The patient was told to keep taking the medicines and come back in another month. After two months of treatment, the patient was doing well with no more fluid coming out.

Discharge Medications:

The patient was told to keep taking rifampicin 600 mg once a day, ethambutol 600 mg once a day, and clarithromycin 500 mg two times a day.

Follow-Up:

The patient was told to keep coming back for checkups. How often depends on how well she is healing and what tests show.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 48-year-old woman, presented with a history of low mood, anhedonia, easy fatigability, decreased concentration, hopelessness, and decreased sleep and appetite. The patient mentioned suicidal ideations and had a past incident of attempting suicide by setting herself on fire. These symptoms developed abruptly following a minor stressor at work. The patient had no previous history of psychiatric illness. She had tried to receive treatment from private psychiatrists, however, she discontinued medication due to the lack of improvement. The patient was diagnosed with Recurrent Brief Depressive Disorder (RBDD) as per ICD 10. She was started on paroxetine at 10 mg which was eventually increased to 50 mg.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted to the hospital presenting with symptoms of RBDD. After a thorough evaluation, the patient received an accurate diagnosis and was started on medication. During the course of the hospital stay, the patient showed symptomatic improvement. She was eventually discharged from the hospital and continued on regular follow-up.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 48-year-old woman. She came to the hospital feeling sad and had lost interest in things she used to enjoy. She felt very tired and had trouble focusing. She felt hopeless and had problems sleeping and eating. The patient had thoughts of ending her life. In the past, she tried to hurt herself by setting herself on fire. These feelings started suddenly after a small problem at work. The patient had never had mental health problems before. She had seen private mental health doctors before but stopped taking her medicine because it was not helping. The doctors found that the patient has a condition called Recurrent Brief Depressive Disorder. This means she has short periods of feeling very sad that keep coming back. She was given a medicine called paroxetine. She started with a low dose of 10 mg. Over time, the dose was raised to 50 mg.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came to the hospital with symptoms of a condition that causes short periods of deep sadness that keep coming back. The doctors did a full check and found what was wrong. They started her on medicine. While in the hospital, the patient started to feel better. She was sent home and will keep seeing her doctors for regular check-ups.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Age: 87
Gender: Female

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the accident and emergency department with complaints of pain and reduced range of movement in her left shoulder caused by a minimally displaced and impacted fracture of her proximal humerus. She was examined and sent home to attend the next available fracture clinic with a collar and cuff. Upon review in the following fracture clinic, she was diagnosed with thrombophlebitis of her external jugular vein and was prescribed an antibiotic (flucloxacillin 500mg) for five days and placed in a better fitting sling. As the patient was only on holiday in the region, further follow-up was arranged for two weeks in her local hospital. Two days later, the patient returned to the accident and emergency department with pain and gross pitting oedema in her left arm. A Doppler scan demonstrated near complete occlusion of the left subclavian and axillary veins, consistent with a deep vein thrombosis. The patient was started on warfarin and treatment dose enoxaparin (1mg/kg) to be taken once a day until her INR was therapeutic. She was then anticoagulated for 3 months.

Diagnosis:
Left shoulder minimally displaced and impacted fracture, thrombophlebitis of external jugular vein, deep vein thrombosis

Medical History:
The patient had a history of hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and underwent thyroidectomy in 2000 for hyperthyroidism. There was no known history of drug allergies.

Treatment:
The patient was placed in a collar and cuff and later a better fitting sling for nonsurgical management of her left shoulder fracture. She was prescribed antibiotics for five days and anticoagulants for 3 months to manage her thrombophlebitis of external jugular vein and deep vein thrombosis.

Follow-up Care:
The patient made a full recovery and has had no further episodes of thrombosis so far.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Age: 87
Gender: Female

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room with pain and trouble moving her left shoulder. She had a small break in the upper arm bone near the shoulder. The bone pieces had not moved much out of place. The patient was checked and sent home with an arm support called a collar and cuff. She was told to come back to the bone injury clinic. At that next visit, doctors found a vein infection in her neck. The patient was given an antibiotic called flucloxacillin 500mg for five days. She also got a better fitting arm sling. Since the patient was only visiting the area, a follow-up was set up at her local hospital in two weeks. Two days later, the patient came back to the emergency room. Her left arm was painful and very swollen with fluid. A special scan showed that the veins in her upper arm and under her collarbone were almost fully blocked. This was a blood clot in a deep vein. The patient was started on two blood thinning medicines called warfarin and enoxaparin at 1mg per kg of body weight. She took these once a day until her blood tests showed the medicine was working. She then took blood thinners for 3 months total.

Diagnosis:
Small break in the left upper arm bone near the shoulder, vein infection in the neck, blood clot in a deep vein

Medical History:
The patient had a history of high blood pressure and an irregular heartbeat. She had surgery to remove her thyroid gland in 2000 because it was overactive. There were no known drug allergies.

Treatment:
The patient wore an arm support and later a better fitting sling to let her shoulder bone heal without surgery. She took antibiotics for five days for the vein infection. She took blood thinning medicine for 3 months for the blood clot.

Follow-up Care:
The patient got fully better and has not had any more blood clots so far.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 44
Gender: Female
Chief Complaint: Painful oral ulcer

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with a painful oral ulcer, which had developed 6 weeks prior. She had a history of aphthous stomatitis and received systemic steroid treatment. However, her condition worsened with an erythematous maculopapular rash on her body, conjunctival injection, fever, diarrhea, and weight loss. Leukocytosis and eosinophilia were noted. Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) was suspected, and she was treated with systemic steroids, which showed slight improvement in skin condition but unresponsiveness in stomatitis.
A skin biopsy showed interface dermatitis and subepidermal blister formation with basal vacuolization consistent with PNP. Additional tests showed IgG deposition on normal human skin, mouse bladder epithelium, and mouse lung epithelium. The patient was ultimately diagnosed with stage IV follicular lymphoma with BO and invasive pulmonary aspergillosis. 
Chemotherapy resulted in a complete remission of lymphoma, which was confirmed through positron emission tomography. The patient's stomatitis and skin rash were well-maintained with a low-dose steroid therapy of 5mg/day for 8 months. However, she developed BO and invasive pulmonary aspergillosis, which led to respiratory failure. 

Course of Treatment:
Treatment included chemotherapy with rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisolone, which resulted in complete remission of the patient's lymphoma. The patient was also treated with a low-dose steroid therapy of 5mg/day, which maintained the improvement of stomatitis and skin rash. 

Follow-Up Care:
No specific follow-up care is necessary since the patient passed away from respiratory failure. 

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with PNP, which was later associated with stage IV follicular lymphoma with BO and invasive pulmonary aspergillosis. 

[REDACTED] Hospital
[REDACTED] Medical Center
[REDACTED]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 44
Gender: Female
Main Problem: Painful mouth sore

Hospital Stay:
The patient came to the hospital with a painful mouth sore that started 6 weeks before. She had a history of recurring mouth sores and was given steroid medicine that works throughout the body. Her condition got worse and she developed a red, bumpy rash on her body. She also had red eyes, fever, loose stools, and lost weight. Blood tests showed high levels of white blood cells and a type of white blood cell called eosinophils. Doctors thought she might have Stevens-Johnson Syndrome, a serious skin reaction. She was treated with steroids that work throughout the body. Her skin got a little better but her mouth sores did not improve.
A small sample of skin was tested and showed damage where the skin layers meet. It also showed blisters forming under the top skin layer. These findings matched a condition called paraneoplastic pemphigus. More tests showed that her immune system was attacking normal skin and organ tissue. The patient was finally found to have stage IV follicular lymphoma, a type of blood cancer that had spread. She also had a lung condition called bronchiolitis obliterans and a serious fungal lung infection called invasive pulmonary aspergillosis.
Cancer treatment led to complete disappearance of the lymphoma. This was confirmed through a special imaging scan that detects cancer activity. The patient's mouth sores and skin rash stayed under control with a low dose of steroid medicine for 8 months. However, she developed the lung condition and serious fungal lung infection. These caused her lungs to stop working.

Treatment Given:
Treatment included cancer-fighting drugs called rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisolone. These medicines led to complete disappearance of the patient's lymphoma. The patient also took a low dose of steroid medicine at 5mg per day. This kept her mouth sores and skin rash under control.

Follow-Up Care:
No follow-up care is needed because the patient died from lung failure.

Diagnosis:
The patient was found to have paraneoplastic pemphigus, a rare skin blistering disease. This was linked to stage IV follicular lymphoma, a blood cancer that had spread. She also had bronchiolitis obliterans, a lung condition, and invasive pulmonary aspergillosis, a serious fungal lung infection.

[REDACTED] Hospital
[REDACTED] Medical Center
[REDACTED]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 53-year-old male, professional chef

Admission Date: January 2017

Discharge Date: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 53-year-old male professional chef, was admitted due to a severe exacerbation of atopic dermatitis (AD) with involvement of the face, neckline, trunk, and forearms. The patient's medical history was positive for allergic rhino-conjunctivitis and the allergological work-up found the patient to be positive for pollens, foods, and latex. The patient was prescribed oral steroids and anti-histamines, but these did not result in any improvement. Additionally, the patient experienced episodes of diarrhea and frequent severe exacerbations due to exposure to cooking vapors.

The patient followed a pseudo-allergen-free diet and exclusion of grains and tomatoes for six months. A three-day treatment with 6-methyl prednisolone i.v. was administered, which was progressively tapered over the course of three weeks. However, a rapid relapse of the skin legions and severe pruritus was observed.

Treatment with Cyclosporine A (CoA) was started but after three months, the patient saw no significant improvement. An off-label treatment with Omalizumab was proposed and initiated, resulting in clinical improvement after just about two months of treatment as confirmed by a SCORAD of 1 and a clear improvement of quality of life. After one year of Omalizumab treatment, the patient maintains good control for both skin and gastrointestinal symptoms.

Discharge Recommendations:

The patient is advised to implement strict environmental, hygiene, and diet restrictions based on the test results. The patient is discharged from the hospital and may return to work as a chef without complications and eat grains again with no gastrointestinal problems or relapse of dermatitis. The patient should continue reducing steroid doses in anticipation of complete suspension and continue taking CoA at a maintenance dose of 1 mg/kg/day. Follow-up appointments should be scheduled as needed to monitor the patient's condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 53-year-old male, professional chef

Admission Date: January 2017

Discharge Date: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 53-year-old male professional chef, was admitted for a severe flare-up of eczema. The rash affected the face, neck area, chest, back, and forearms. The patient had a history of hay fever and itchy, watery eyes from allergies. Allergy testing showed reactions to pollens, foods, and latex. The patient was given steroid pills and allergy medicine by mouth, but these did not help. The patient also had loose stools and frequent severe flare-ups from being around cooking fumes.

The patient followed a special diet that avoided common allergy triggers. The patient also stopped eating grains and tomatoes for six months. The patient received a steroid medicine through a vein for three days. The dose was slowly lowered over three weeks. However, the skin rash came back quickly along with severe itching.

Treatment with a medicine called Cyclosporine A was started. After three months, the patient did not get much better. A different medicine called Omalizumab was offered and started. This medicine is not normally used for this condition. After about two months, the patient got better. A skin scoring test showed the rash was almost gone. The patient also felt much better in daily life. After one year on Omalizumab, the patient has good control of both skin and stomach symptoms.

Discharge Recommendations:

The patient should follow strict rules about surroundings, cleanliness, and diet based on test results. The patient is leaving the hospital and may go back to work as a chef without problems. The patient can eat grains again without stomach issues or skin flare-ups. The patient should keep lowering steroid doses until they can stop completely. The patient should keep taking Cyclosporine A at a low ongoing dose of 1 mg per kg of body weight per day. The patient should schedule follow-up visits as needed to check on the condition.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 36-year-old prisoner

Diagnosis: Candida lung abscess

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital due to fever, asthenia, cough, and hemoptysis, which did not respond to antibiotic therapy. It was revealed that the patient had a history of smoking crack cocaine, injecting heroin, and untreated liver disease related to HCV. Laboratory tests indicated an increase in white blood cell count and inflammation. Further, a 7 cm round and excavated lesion was also detected in the right lower lobe of the lungs. The patient was diagnosed with a Candida lung abscess and started on intravenous fluconazole 400 mg daily followed by its oral formulation, leading to clinical recovery. In addition to the treatment, the patient was monitored under our hepatological unit, and DAAs were prescribed.

Course of Treatment:
The patient was treated with intravenous fluconazole 400 mg followed by an oral formulation until complete clinical recovery and was later discharged. The patient was given an appointment to be followed up with our hepatological unit and to perform the treatment with DAAs. After three months of therapy with sofosbuvir 400 mg/velpatasvir 100 mg once daily, the sustained virological response (SVR) was reached and it’s still active.

Follow-up Care:
The patient was given an appointment to be followed up with our hepatological unit to ensure the continued management of the HCV-related liver disease. The patient was also advised to maintain healthy habits and refrain from drug consumption.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 36-year-old prisoner

Diagnosis: Fungal infection causing a pocket of pus in the lung

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital with fever, weakness, cough, and coughing up blood. These symptoms did not get better with antibiotics. The patient had a history of smoking crack cocaine, injecting heroin, and untreated liver disease caused by the hepatitis C virus. Blood tests showed a high number of white blood cells and signs of infection in the body. Doctors also found a 7 cm round cavity in the lower part of the right lung. The patient was diagnosed with a fungal lung infection caused by Candida. Treatment began with fluconazole 400 mg given through a vein, then switched to pills. This led to recovery. The patient was also watched by our liver care team. Medicines to treat hepatitis C were prescribed.

Course of Treatment:
The patient received fluconazole 400 mg through a vein first, then as pills until fully recovered. The patient was then sent home. The patient was given an appointment to follow up with our liver care team and start hepatitis C treatment. After three months of taking sofosbuvir 400 mg and velpatasvir 100 mg once a day, the hepatitis C virus was no longer found in the blood. This result is still holding.

Follow-up Care:
The patient was given an appointment to follow up with our liver care team. This is to keep managing the liver disease caused by hepatitis C. The patient was also told to keep healthy habits and to stop using drugs.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 45 years
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: 7 days

Admitting Diagnosis:
Undifferentiated Spindle Cell Sarcoma Involving the Pancreas

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital urgently due to the acute onset of severe abdominal pain and collapse. Upon examination, she was found to be in class III haemorrhagic shock with distended and diffusely tender abdomen. She was immediately resuscitated with blood transfusions and intravenous crystalloid boluses. Hematological and basic investigations revealed haemoglobin level of 5g/dl and moderate amount of free fluid with mass around the proximal part of the pancreas, respectively. Following which, an emergency exploratory laparotomy was performed, and the source of bleeding was identified as the nidus, which was situated posterior to the body of the pancreas and had ruptured through the inferior border of the pancreas. The histopathological analysis of the biopsy revealed an infiltrating tumour composed of fascicles of spindle cells with oval, vesicular nuclei with scanty eosinophilic cytoplasm that was diagnosed as undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma, and the tumour was surgically excised.

Treatment:
The patient was managed in the intensive care unit for 4 days and received a course of Ifosfamide and Doxorubicin for 6 months as adjuvant chemotherapy. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography was performed before the commencement of chemotherapy, which showed a 7 × 4.8 × 4.9 cm mass lesion with a thin wall containing hypodense material, attached to the inferior border of the pancreas. There were no other metastatic lesions identified.

Outcome:
The patient recovered following surgery and was discharged 7 days after admission. She was referred to the oncologist and offered a pancreatectomy with excision of the tumour, which was refused. The patient received six months of chemotherapy and finally succumbed to the disease 18 months after the presentation.

Summary:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma involving the pancreas that was treated with surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy. The patient was discharged following a 7-day hospitalization but eventually succumbed to the disease after 18 months.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 45 years
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: 7 days

Admitting Diagnosis:
A rare type of cancer made of long, thin cells affecting the pancreas

Hospital Course:
The patient was rushed to the hospital because of sudden, severe belly pain and collapse. Doctors found the patient had lost a large amount of blood and was in shock. The belly was swollen and painful to touch. The patient was given blood transfusions and fluids through a vein right away. Blood tests showed very low blood levels at 5g/dl. Imaging tests showed fluid in the belly and a growth near the upper part of the pancreas. Doctors then did emergency surgery to open the belly and find the bleeding. The bleeding came from a growth behind the pancreas that had burst through the lower edge of the pancreas. A tissue sample was taken and tested. The test showed a cancer made of bundles of long, thin cells. This cancer was diagnosed as a rare type called spindle cell sarcoma. The growth was removed during surgery.

Treatment:
The patient stayed in the intensive care unit for 4 days. The patient then received two cancer-fighting drugs called Ifosfamide and Doxorubicin for 6 months after surgery. Before starting these drugs, a special scan with dye was done. The scan showed a growth measuring about 7 by 4.8 by 4.9 centimeters with thin walls attached to the lower edge of the pancreas. The cancer had not spread to other parts of the body.

Outcome:
The patient got better after surgery and left the hospital 7 days after being admitted. The patient was sent to a cancer specialist. Doctors offered surgery to remove the pancreas and the growth. The patient said no to this surgery. The patient received six months of cancer-fighting drugs. The patient died from the disease 18 months after first coming to the hospital.

Summary:
The patient came to the hospital with a rare cancer affecting the pancreas. The cancer was treated with surgery and cancer-fighting drugs given after surgery. The patient left the hospital after staying for 7 days. The patient died from the disease 18 months later.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: March 31, 2020
Date of Discharge: N/A (Patient under observation)

History:
The patient, a 46-year-old male, with a history of mild depressive episode treated with medication, was admitted to the isolated COVID-19 ward with symptoms of COVID-19 infection, including cough, fever, sore throat, and fatigue. The patient tested positive for COVID-19. The patient was treated with chloroquine-azithromycin association. The patient's Electrocardiogram (ECG) was monitored regularly, and the QT interval was normal to prolonged (corrected QT max: 490ms). On the ninth day of treatment, the patient developed symptoms of distress and insomnia. 

Hospital Course:
On the evening of the same day, the patient showed abrupt onset of psychotic symptoms such as visual hallucinations and incoherent speech, with the outburst of odd behavior and repeated attempts to run away from hospital. The patient's insight was preserved. The cerebral CT scan was normal. Standard laboratory tests were performed and revealed normal results.

Interventions:
COVID-19 medication protocol along with the pre-existing medication vortioxetine was discontinued. Amilsulpride was initiated at a dose of 100 mg per day. Psychotic symptoms disappeared totally after 48 hours. Amilsulpride was tapered off and then stopped within a week.

Follow-up:
The patient remained asymptomatic, and the case was monitored once every two weeks. On the latest psychiatric assessment (May 20), the patient showed no psychotic symptoms.

Impression:
The patient developed psychotic symptoms after nine days of COVID-19 treatment. Clinical diagnosis suggests drug-induced psychosis. The patient responded well to the discontinuation of medication and treatment with amilsulpride. There were no further symptoms observed, and on the latest psychiatric assessment (May 20), the patient showed no psychotic symptoms.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: March 31, 2020
Date of Discharge: N/A (Patient under observation)

History:
The patient is a 46-year-old male. He has a history of mild depression treated with medication. The patient was admitted to a special ward for COVID-19 patients. He had symptoms of COVID-19, including cough, fever, sore throat, and feeling very tired. The patient tested positive for COVID-19. The patient was treated with chloroquine and azithromycin together. The patient's heart rhythm was checked regularly using a heart monitor test. The heart rhythm was normal to slightly slow at times. On day nine of treatment, the patient began feeling upset and had trouble sleeping.

Hospital Course:
That same evening, the patient suddenly developed mental health symptoms. He saw things that were not there and spoke in ways that did not make sense. He acted strangely and tried to leave the hospital several times. The patient knew something was wrong with him. A brain scan came back normal. Blood tests and other standard tests also came back normal.

Interventions:
The doctors stopped the COVID-19 medicines. They also stopped vortioxetine, a medicine the patient was already taking for depression. The doctors started a new medicine called amilsulpride at 100 mg per day. The mental health symptoms went away completely after 48 hours. The doctors slowly reduced and then stopped amilsulpride within a week.

Follow-up:
The patient had no more symptoms. The care team checked on the patient every two weeks. At the most recent mental health check on May 20, the patient had no mental health symptoms.

Impression:
The patient developed mental health symptoms after nine days of COVID-19 treatment. Doctors believe the medicines caused these symptoms. The patient got better after stopping the medicines and taking amilsulpride. No more symptoms were seen. At the most recent mental health check on May 20, the patient had no mental health symptoms.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: 22 July 2013
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 14-year-old female, was admitted to our center due to recurrent palpitation and tremor. She was diagnosed with hyperthyroidism in another hospital, for which she had been prescribed methimazole primarily followed by propylthiouracil. Her thyroid function remained abnormal over the subsequent three years. All antithyroid drugs were discontinued for five months prior to her admission to our center.

Physical examination on admission revealed an increased heart rate, accentuation of the first heart sound, and tremor. Thyroid enlargement without any discernible nodule was also observed. Diagnostic workup included thyroid function testing, measurements of TSH receptor antibody (TRAb), a small dose of 131iodine and a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan that revealed a slightly enlarged pituitary gland not occupied by a lesion. A FT3 concentration exceeding the normal range, along with an elevated FT4 concentration, and suppressed TSH concentration were noted during both thyroid function tests. Elevated TRAb levels were also detected. A point mutation in the TR β gene was identified in the patient and her father, indicating THR syndrome.

The patient's case was complicated by Graves' disease, and propranolol and methimazole were prescribed for treatment. The patient was discharged in stable condition and has since been followed up regularly. 

Discharge Diagnoses: 
- Toxic multinodular goiter complicated by Graves' disease

Discharge Medications: 
- Methimazole 30 mg/day
- Propranolol 60 mg/day

Follow-Up Plans:
The patient is advised to continue taking prescribed medications and attend regular follow-up appointments. 

Primary Care Provider/Referring Provider: [Redacted]
Consultants: [Redacted]
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: 22 July 2013
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 14-year-old female, was admitted to our center for a fast heartbeat and shaking that kept coming back. She was told she had an overactive thyroid at another hospital. She was given medicine called methimazole at first, then another medicine called propylthiouracil. Her thyroid hormone levels stayed abnormal for the next three years. She stopped taking all thyroid medicines five months before coming to our center.

During the physical exam, the patient had a fast heart rate, a louder than normal first heart sound, and shaking. The thyroid gland in her neck was also larger than normal but had no lumps. Tests included thyroid hormone levels, a test for thyroid antibodies, a small dose of radioactive iodine, and a brain scan. The brain scan showed her pituitary gland was slightly larger than normal but had no growths. Two thyroid tests showed high levels of FT3 and FT4 hormones and very low TSH hormone levels. Antibody levels were also high. A gene change was found in the patient and her father. This showed they have a rare inherited thyroid condition.

The patient also had an immune system problem affecting the thyroid called Graves' disease. She was given propranolol to slow her heart rate and methimazole to lower her thyroid hormone levels. The patient left the hospital in stable condition and has been seen for regular checkups since then.

Discharge Diagnoses:
Overactive thyroid with multiple enlarged areas, along with Graves' disease

Discharge Medications:
Methimazole 30 mg per day
Propranolol 60 mg per day

Follow-Up Plans:
The patient should keep taking her medicines as directed and go to all scheduled checkup visits.

Primary Care Provider/Referring Provider: [Redacted]
Consultants: [Redacted]

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient presented with sudden onset and severe occipital headache accompanied by bending his neck downwards. He was given loxoprofen sodium hydrate which momentarily relieved the headache but it persisted. Neurological examination shows no abnormalities. Brain CT and MRI were normal, but MRA showed multisegmental narrowing of the basilar artery, identified via BPAS-MRI agreement. After ruling out inflammation or infection by CSF investigation, a diagnosis of RCVS was presumed, and the patient was put on lomerizine hydrochloride (10 mg/day). Repeat MRA and BPAS-MRI were done, which showed a mild resolution of vasoconstriction. The symptoms resolved by post-admission day 8, and the patient was discharged uneventfully.

Hospitalization Summary:

A 41-year-old man presented with sudden onset and severe occipital headache, accompanied by bending his neck downwards. Nothing seen in the neurological examination, brain CT and MRI were normal, but MRA showed multisegmental narrowing of the basilar artery, in agreement with BPAS-MRI. CSF investigation was conducted, which ruled out inflammation or infection. RCVS was presumed, and the patient was treated with lomerizine hydrochloride (10 mg/day). Repeat MRA and BPAS-MRI showed a mild resolution of vasoconstriction, with symptoms resolving by post-admission day 8. The patient was then discharged.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with a sudden and severe headache at the back of the head. This happened while bending his neck down. He was given a pain reliever called loxoprofen sodium hydrate. This helped the headache for a short time, but the pain came back. The exam of the brain and nerves showed nothing wrong. Brain scans using CT and MRI were normal. However, another type of scan called MRA showed narrowing in several parts of a major blood vessel at the back of the brain. This was confirmed by a special imaging test. A spinal fluid test was done to check for swelling or infection. The test ruled out both of these problems. The doctors believed the patient had a condition where blood vessels in the brain tighten temporarily. The patient was given a medicine called lomerizine hydrochloride at 10 mg per day. Follow-up scans showed the blood vessels were starting to return to normal. The symptoms went away by day 8 after being admitted. The patient left the hospital without any problems.

input:
Discharge Summary:

This is a summary of admission details for a 20-year-old female with a medical history of post-traumatic stress syndrome and facial acne. The patient was admitted to the emergency department after ingesting seven pills of paroxetine.

Course of Hospitalization:

Initial evaluation in the emergency department yielded minimal symptoms, stable vital signs, and no arrhythmia on ECG. The patient was observed for six hours and complained of flushing and became agitated. The patient sustained witnessed spontaneous aborted tonic clinic seizure activity of 90 seconds. She exhibited tachycardia, tachypnea, hypertension, and hyperreflexia. Blood workup, CT of the head without contrast, urine drug screen, and ethanol level were normal. ECG showed a prolonged corrected QT interval of >540. SS was suspected, and the patient was given IV fluids and lorazepam. Cyproheptadine was given 12 mg orally as a single dose and 4 mg every eight hours as needed. The patient was admitted to the intensive care unit for close monitoring with serial ECG. QT interval normalization was observed 16 hours later, and no visual seizures were noted during hospitalization.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Suspected serotonin syndrome 

Treatment:

The patient was given IV fluids and lorazepam. Cyproheptadine was given 12 mg orally as a single dose and 4 mg every eight hours as needed.

Follow-up:

The patient was discharged in stable condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

This is a summary of admission details for a 20-year-old female with a medical history of post-traumatic stress syndrome and facial acne. The patient was admitted to the emergency department after swallowing seven pills of paroxetine.

Course of Hospitalization:

The first check in the emergency department showed very few symptoms, steady body function measures, and a normal heart rhythm test. The patient was watched for six hours and said she felt warm and flushed and became restless. The patient had a seizure that staff saw happen, which lasted 90 seconds and stopped on its own. She had a fast heart rate, fast breathing, high blood pressure, and overactive reflexes. Blood tests, a brain scan, a drug test, and an alcohol test were all normal. The heart rhythm test showed the heart was taking too long to reset between beats, more than 540 units. A condition called serotonin syndrome was suspected, and the patient was given fluids through a vein and lorazepam. Cyproheptadine was given as a 12 mg pill one time, then 4 mg every eight hours if needed. The patient was moved to the intensive care unit for close watching with regular heart rhythm tests. The heart rhythm went back to normal 16 hours later, and no seizures were seen during the hospital stay.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Suspected serotonin syndrome

Treatment:

The patient was given fluids through a vein and lorazepam. Cyproheptadine was given as a 12 mg pill one time, then 4 mg every eight hours if needed.

Follow-up:

The patient was sent home in stable condition.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Purpuric rash, fever, shortness of breath

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 10-year-old Caucasian girl, was admitted with complaints of progressively worsening rash, fever, and shortness of breath. Prior to admission, she had a normal complete blood count, electrolyte panel, liver function tests, urinalysis, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). C-reactive protein (CRP) was 1.2 mg/dL, serum IgA 310 mg/dL, and anti-streptolysin O (ASO) titer was elevated. The patient was suspected to have HSP. On admission, she had a purpuric rash on her upper and lower extremities, buttocks, and back. She also presented with oxygen desaturation, shortness of breath, and a dry cough. Her hemoglobin had decreased, and a chest radiograph showed patchy bibasilar opacities. The patient was started on intravenous ampicillin and azithromycin. However, shortly after admission, the patient developed worsening respiratory distress, grunting, and worsening chest imaging. The patient was found to have severe SVV and vasculitis and was started on intravenous methylprednisolone and gammaglobulin. Echocardiogram showed mitral regurgitation, and the patient was started on intravenous furosemide, milrinone, and intravenous epinephrine. After five days of mechanical ventilation, the patient steadily improved and was extubated. The patient continued treatment with oral prednisone taper and enalapril and was discharged after completing 10 days of intravenous ceftriaxone and 7 days of intravenous azithromycin.

Summary of Events:
The patient was admitted with symptoms of rash, fever, and shortness of breath. The medical team suspected HSP, but further analysis revealed severe SVV and vasculitis. The patient was started on intravenous methylprednisolone and gammaglobulin. Treatment with intravenous furosemide, milrinone, and intravenous epinephrine was initiated to treat the patient's worsening respiratory distress, while oral prednisone taper and enalapril were used for afterload reduction. After mechanical ventilation, the patient steadily improved and was discharged with a moderately dilated left atrium, moderate mitral regurgitation, and mild mitral valve prolapse. Follow-up echocardiogram indicated mild to moderate mitral regurgitation.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Purple skin rash, fever, trouble breathing

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 10-year-old Caucasian girl, was admitted with a worsening rash, fever, and trouble breathing. Before admission, blood tests for cell counts, body salts, liver function, and urine were normal. A test for body swelling was also normal. A test for general swelling showed a slightly high result at 1.2 mg/dL. A protein linked to the immune system was high at 310 mg/dL. A test showing recent strep infection was also high. The medical team thought the patient might have a condition called HSP that affects blood vessels. When admitted, the patient had a purple rash on her arms, legs, bottom, and back. She also had low oxygen levels, trouble breathing, and a dry cough. Her red blood cell levels had dropped. A chest x-ray showed cloudy patches in both lower lungs. The patient was started on two antibiotics given through an IV. Shortly after, her breathing got much worse with grunting sounds. New chest images also looked worse. The team found she had serious blood vessel swelling throughout her body. She was given a strong steroid medicine and immune-boosting medicine through an IV. A heart ultrasound showed a leaky heart valve. The patient was given a water pill, a heart-strengthening medicine, and a medicine to support blood pressure through an IV. After five days on a breathing machine, the patient slowly got better and was taken off the machine. The patient continued taking a steroid pill in slowly smaller doses and a blood pressure medicine. She was sent home after finishing 10 days of one antibiotic and 7 days of another antibiotic through an IV.

Summary of Events:
The patient was admitted with a rash, fever, and trouble breathing. The medical team first thought it was HSP. More tests showed serious blood vessel swelling throughout her body. The patient was given a strong steroid and immune-boosting medicine through an IV. A water pill, heart-strengthening medicine, and blood pressure support medicine were given for her worsening breathing problems. A steroid pill in slowly smaller doses and a blood pressure medicine were used to reduce strain on the heart. After being on a breathing machine, the patient slowly got better. She was sent home with a slightly enlarged upper left heart chamber, a moderately leaky heart valve, and a slightly bulging heart valve. A follow-up heart ultrasound showed the valve was still mildly to moderately leaky.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, an 80-year-old non-smoker woman with a history of hypertension and diabetes mellitus was admitted to the Emergency Department with a one-week history of dry cough and high fever. The chest computed tomography showed bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities, and the real-time reverse transcriptase-polymerase chain reaction test for severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) returned positive.

Treatment and Response:
The patient was started on supplemental oxygen, favipiravir, and dexamethasone. Three days later, she was admitted to the ICU due to respiratory failure and was put on mechanical ventilation. She continued to receive dexamethasone and was also given antibiotics and remdesivir. The respiratory condition and chest infiltration improved, and she was extubated after seven days of mechanical ventilation. Dexamethasone was later discontinued after 10 days.

Relapse and Co-infection:
The patient developed a fever and quickly progressive hypoxaemia two days after the extubation and was reintubated. Cultures and laboratory tests were performed with no evidence of co-infection. She was then given dexamethasone again with a subsequent improvement in the chest shadows and was tapered weekly. However, her chest shadows worsened again, and her serum β-d-glucan was found to be elevated. She was given antifungal treatment, and the CMV antigenemia assay was elevated. Ganciclovir was immediately initiated, but the patient died four days after the positive CMV antigenemia test result.

Autopsy:
A needle autopsy was performed approximately 3 h after her death, and CMV-infected cells were found. This confirmed the diagnosis of CMV pneumonia as the cause of her respiratory failure and bilateral chest infiltrations.

Follow-up:
The patient's family was informed about her condition and treatment. The discharge summary along with instructions for follow-up care, including any medication changes, and any scheduled appointments were explained to the family.

Condition:
The patient's condition had initially improved but worsened due to CMV pneumonia, leading to her death.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is an 80-year-old woman who does not smoke. She has a history of high blood pressure and diabetes. She came to the Emergency Department with a dry cough and high fever that lasted one week. A special scan of her chest showed cloudy patches in both lungs. A test confirmed she had COVID-19.

Treatment and Response:
The patient was given extra oxygen, favipiravir, and dexamethasone to help fight the virus. Three days later, her lungs stopped working well enough on their own. She was moved to the Intensive Care Unit and put on a breathing machine. She kept taking dexamethasone and also received antibiotics and remdesivir. Her breathing and lungs got better after seven days. The breathing tube was removed. Dexamethasone was stopped after 10 days.

Relapse and Co-infection:
Two days after the breathing tube was removed, the patient got a fever again. Her oxygen levels dropped quickly. The breathing tube had to be put back in. Tests were done but showed no signs of a new infection at first. She was given dexamethasone again, and her lungs looked better for a while. The medicine was slowly reduced each week. But her lungs got worse again. A blood test showed signs of a fungal infection. She was given medicine to treat the fungus. Another test showed she had a virus called CMV. Medicine for CMV was started right away. Sadly, the patient died four days after the CMV test came back positive.

Autopsy:
A small tissue sample was taken from the patient about 3 hours after she died. Doctors found cells infected with CMV. This confirmed that CMV lung infection caused her breathing failure and the cloudy patches in both lungs.

Follow-up:
The patient's family was told about her condition and treatment. The discharge papers and care instructions were explained to them. This included any changes to medicines and any planned appointments.

Condition:
The patient got better at first but then got worse because of the CMV lung infection. This led to her death.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Hospital Admission: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Chief Complaint:
The patient presented to the department of Oral Medicine and Oral Surgery for the removal of fibrous hyperplasia in the mandibular vestibule caused by ill-fitting prostheses resulting in epulis fissuratum and vestibular deepening.

Hospital Course Summary:
During the extra-oral examination, a decrease in the vertical dimension of occlusion was noted. Intra-oral examination revealed hyperplastic mandibular lesion of the anterior alveolar mucosa, extending to the vestibular sulcus. Topical mouthwashes with Fungizone and chlorhexidine were prescribed to treat denture-induced hyperplasia and prosthetic candidiasis. After 3 weeks, no symptomatology was noted. Surgical resection combined with gingival graft was carried out to excise fibrous hyperplasia. The resultant lower boundary of the incision was sutured in position. Free gingival graft was carried out from the palate. The final total prosthesis was delivered to the patient 2 months later resulting in perfect integration of the prosthetic volume.

Findings:
The anatomopathological examination confirmed the clinical diagnosis of epulis fissuratum. Favorable postoperative healing was observed 2 weeks later during the removal of sutures.

Treatment:
Topical mouthwashes with Fungizone and chlorhexidine were prescribed to treat denture-induced hyperplasia and prosthetic candidiasis. Antibiotics and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs were administered for 5 days. Chlorhexidine mouth rinse was prescribed twice a day for 2 weeks. The patient was asked to have a soft diet, limited facial movement for 14 days, no brushing around the surgical site for 3 days after the procedure, and to place an ice pack on the lower lip.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should continue to follow the prescribed soft diet, use chlorhexidine mouth rinse twice a day, and avoid brushing around the surgical site for 3 days after the procedure. The patient should also place an ice pack on the lower lip as needed. In case of discomfort, the patient is advised to contact the hospital. 

Follow-Up:
The patient should have regular follow-up sessions with the oral medicine and oral surgery department to ensure there is no recurrence of the fibrous hyperplasia and that the total prosthesis remains stable and well-extended. 

Signed, 
[REDACTED]
Oral Surgeon
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Hospital Admission: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Main Problem:
The patient came to the Mouth Medicine and Mouth Surgery department. The patient needed to have extra gum tissue removed from the lower jaw. This tissue grew because of poorly fitting dentures. The patient also needed surgery to make the area deeper so new dentures would fit better.

Hospital Stay Summary:
During the outside exam of the face, the care team noticed the upper and lower teeth did not meet properly. The inside exam of the mouth showed extra tissue growth on the lower front gum area. This growth spread to the space between the gum and lip. The care team gave the patient two mouth rinses called Fungizone and chlorhexidine. These treated the tissue growth and a yeast infection caused by the dentures. After 3 weeks, the patient had no symptoms. The care team then did surgery to remove the extra tissue and add a gum tissue patch. The lower edge of the cut was stitched closed. The tissue patch came from the roof of the mouth. The patient got new complete dentures 2 months later. The dentures fit well.

Findings:
Lab tests on the removed tissue confirmed the diagnosis of extra gum tissue growth from denture irritation. Two weeks later, the stitches were removed. The surgical area was healing well.

Treatment:
The care team prescribed Fungizone and chlorhexidine mouth rinses. These treated the tissue growth and yeast infection caused by the dentures. The patient took germ-fighting medicine and pain medicine for 5 days. The patient used chlorhexidine mouth rinse twice a day for 2 weeks. The patient was told to eat soft foods and limit jaw movement for 14 days. The patient should not brush near the surgery area for 3 days after the procedure. The patient should put an ice pack on the lower lip.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should keep eating soft foods. The patient should use chlorhexidine mouth rinse twice a day. The patient should not brush near the surgery area for 3 days after the procedure. The patient should put an ice pack on the lower lip as needed. If there is pain or problems, the patient should contact the hospital.

Follow-Up:
The patient should come back for regular visits with the Mouth Medicine and Mouth Surgery department. These visits make sure the extra tissue does not grow back. These visits also check that the new dentures still fit well and stay in place.

Signed,
[REDACTED]
Mouth Surgeon

input:
Hospital Course:
This 7-month-old boy was admitted to our hospital for treatment of respiratory syncytial viral pneumonia, with a history of tracheoesophageal fistula and esophageal atresia surgery performed at 1 day of age. The patient showed recurrent cough and sputum, and was afebrile. The chest radiograph showed increased peribronchial infiltration and air bronchogram. Due to the risk of aspiration pneumonia, oral feeding was discontinued and the patient was given parenteral nutrition via the peripheral venous route.

Extravasation Event:
On day 6 of admission, while the patient was receiving total parenteral nutrition, a 24-gauge intravenous catheter was newly inserted into the dorsal cephalic vein at the dorsum of the patient's right hand. The patient showed signs of irritability, and the obstruction alarm of the infusion pump was triggered. Without any alarm sign, approximately 9 hours after the repositioning of the intravenous catheter, the patient's right hand and arm appeared severely swollen, and capillary refill was absent. There were several blisters observed under the catheter, and an emergency fasciotomy was performed under the suspicion of compartment syndrome.

Treatment and Care:
The patient received orthopedic surgery and fascia suturing, followed by irrigation of the area several times with saline solution. Delayed primary skin closure was performed 8 days after the fasciotomy. A skin graft was scheduled for treatment of the skin necrosis after debriding the necrotic tissue with a moist dressing. Rehabilitation treatment consisting of wrist articulation exercises was started due to the high chance of wrist joint contracture. Skin graft surgery was delayed due to pneumonia, which was diagnosed during the patient's regular wound dressing and rehabilitation treatment. It was performed 4 months after the extravasation event. At the time of this report, skin engraftment had been successfully completed, and the patient was receiving continued rehabilitation treatment.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
This 7-month-old boy came to our hospital because he had a lung infection caused by a common virus called RSV. When he was 1 day old, he had surgery to fix a birth defect that connected his windpipe and food pipe in a way it should not be. The patient kept coughing and had mucus, but did not have a fever. A chest x-ray showed signs of infection in his lungs. Because food might go into his lungs by mistake, the patient stopped eating by mouth. Instead, he received nutrition through a small tube in his vein.

Extravasation Event:
On day 6 in the hospital, the patient was getting nutrition through his vein. A new small tube was placed into a vein on the back of his right hand. The patient became fussy, and the feeding pump showed a warning that something was blocking the flow. About 9 hours after the tube was moved, the patient's right hand and arm became very swollen. There was no warning sign before this happened. The skin did not turn pink when pressed, which meant blood was not flowing well. Blisters appeared under the tube. Doctors did emergency surgery to cut open the tissue covering the muscles because pressure was building up inside the arm.

Treatment and Care:
The patient had bone and muscle surgery, and doctors sewed the tissue covering back together. The area was washed out several times with salt water. The skin was closed 8 days after the first surgery. Doctors planned to move healthy skin to cover the dead skin area after cleaning it with wet bandages. The patient started exercises to move his wrist because the joint might become stiff. The skin surgery was delayed because the patient got a lung infection during regular bandage changes and exercises. The skin surgery happened 4 months after the fluid leaked out of the vein. At the time of this report, the new skin had attached well. The patient was still doing exercises to help his wrist move better.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 74
Sex: Male

Diagnosis:
- HIV infection with very high viral load
- Pneumocystis pneumonia
- Congestive heart failure

Hospital Course:
- The patient presented to the ER with shortness of breath, coughing, anorexia, and weight loss
- He was fully alert and oriented with an elevated jugular venous pulse pressure
- Bilateral fine basal crackles were audible on pulmonary auscultation
- Chest radiography revealed cephalization and alveolar and reticular opacities
- An examination revealed that the patient had previously been diagnosed with congestive heart failure, atrial fibrillation, left ventricular aneurysm with left ventricular thrombus, and stroke
- He was started on 2 L of intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride
- His blood pressure improved and his creatinine levels improved as well
- He was diagnosed with PCP after a fourth-generation HIV test was positive and the CD4 count was found to be low
- Treatment was initiated with intravenous trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, which the patient tolerated well
- The patient's chest radiography revealed bilateral alveolar opacities, pneumatoceles, and small bilateral pleural effusion
- The patient was discharged after a few days in the hospital

Follow-up:
- The patient started antiretroviral therapy and was attending an outpatient rehabilitation center once a week
- He was planning to visit his home country in Africa in several weeks
- His family was updated with his progress five months later.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 74
Sex: Male

Diagnosis:
HIV infection with very high levels of the virus in the blood
Pneumocystis pneumonia, a lung infection common in people with weakened immune systems
Heart failure, when the heart does not pump blood as well as it should

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room with trouble breathing, coughing, loss of appetite, and weight loss.
He was fully awake and aware of his surroundings. The veins in his neck showed higher than normal pressure.
Crackling sounds were heard in both lower parts of his lungs when listening with a stethoscope.
A chest X-ray showed fluid buildup and cloudy patches in the lungs.
Medical records showed the patient had been diagnosed before with heart failure, an irregular heartbeat, a bulge in the heart wall with a blood clot, and a stroke.
He was given fluids through a vein using a salt water solution.
His blood pressure got better and his kidney function improved as well.
He was diagnosed with a lung infection after an HIV test came back positive and his immune cell count was found to be low.
Treatment was started with an antibiotic given through a vein, which the patient handled well.
A chest X-ray showed cloudy patches in both lungs, air-filled sacs, and small amounts of fluid around both lungs.
The patient left the hospital after a few days.

Follow-up:
The patient started HIV medication and was going to a recovery center once a week.
He was planning to visit his home country in Africa in several weeks.
His family was given updates on how he was doing five months later.
