input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age/Sex: 29/Female

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]

Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Achalasia

Hospital Course:

The patient was a 29-year-old female with a history of mild progressive dysphagia who presented with severe dysphagia. The patient had been diagnosed with achalasia three years ago but refused any treatment options. She was given ferrous sulfate capsules for iron therapy by her general practitioner about a month earlier, which led to the development of severe dysphagia after three weeks. She was referred to a gastroenterologist for further evaluation.

On admission, the patient had no odynophagia, nausea, or vomiting. Physical examination was unremarkable.

A barium swallow esophagography showed a dilated esophagus with a bird's beak appearance of the lower esophageal sphincter and a filling defect in the distal part of the esophagus. An EGD revealed a dilated esophagus and an elliptical-shaped, black-colored bezoar in the distal part of the esophagus. There were no ulcers or erosions in the esophagus. The lower esophageal sphincter was severely spastic.

Attempts were made to fragment and push the bezoar into the stomach, but it was discontinued due to high LES pressure and resistance and the risk of aspiration. Finally, the bezoar was retrieved with a basket.

An esophageal manometer was carried out and the results were compatible with the diagnosis of achalasia. In the next endoscopic session, achalasia balloon dilation (size 30 mm) was successfully applied.

The patient was asymptomatic during the 3-year follow-up.

Discharge Condition:

The patient's condition improved after treatment with achalasia balloon dilation. The patient was discharged in good condition and was advised to follow up with her gastroenterologist as scheduled.

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to follow up with her gastroenterologist as scheduled.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age/Sex: 29/Female

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]

Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Achalasia (a condition where the tube that carries food to the stomach does not work properly)

Hospital Course:

The patient was a 29-year-old female who had trouble swallowing that got worse over time. She came to the hospital because swallowing became very difficult. The patient was told she had achalasia three years ago but chose not to get treatment. About a month before coming to the hospital, her regular doctor gave her iron pills. After taking the pills for three weeks, her swallowing problems became much worse. She was sent to a stomach and digestive system doctor for more tests.

When the patient arrived, she had no pain when swallowing, no upset stomach, and no throwing up. The physical exam showed nothing unusual.

The patient had an X-ray test where she swallowed a special liquid. This showed a widened food tube with a narrowed bottom that looked like a bird's beak. It also showed something stuck in the lower part of the food tube. A camera test was done to look inside the food tube. This showed the food tube was widened. There was an oval-shaped, black mass stuck in the lower part of the food tube. The mass was made of the iron pills that had clumped together. There were no sores inside the food tube. The muscle at the bottom of the food tube was very tight.

The doctors tried to break up the mass and push it into the stomach. They had to stop because the tight muscle made it too risky. There was also a chance food could go into the lungs. The doctors used a small basket tool to pull the mass out instead.

A test was done to measure how well the food tube muscles work. The results matched the diagnosis of achalasia. In a later procedure, the doctors stretched the tight muscle using a balloon. The balloon was about 1.2 inches wide. The procedure worked well.

The patient had no symptoms during the 3 years of check-ups after treatment.

Discharge Condition:

The patient got better after the balloon stretching treatment. The patient left the hospital in good condition. The patient was told to see her stomach and digestive system doctor at her scheduled visits.

Discharge Medications:

None

Follow-up:

The patient was told to see her stomach and digestive system doctor at her scheduled visits.

input:
Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 17 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: [REDACTED] days

Hospital Course:

The patient presented with necrosis of the right first and second toes and a soft tissue defect on the plantar forefoot after suffering a crushing injury in a traffic accident.

Medical Management:

The necrotized toes were amputated from the metatarsophalangeal joint and the recipient sites were prepared by debridement. To reconstruct the affected area, a medial plantar sensory free flap was elevated on the contralateral side of the plantar region, and the medial plantar artery was anastomosed to the dorsal pedis artery of the recipient site. Furthermore, two venae comitantes were anastomosed end-to-end to the great saphenous vein and the vena comitante of the dorsalis pedis artery. The medial plantar nerve innervating the flap was anastomosed to the deep peroneal nerve of the recipient site. A full-thickness skin graft was performed at the donor sites.

Recovery:

The patient recovered without any complications and was discharged home in stable condition. It is recommended that he receives follow-up care to ensure proper wound healing and mobility rehabilitation.

Follow-up Care:

It is recommended that the patient schedule a follow-up appointment with his healthcare provider to monitor his progress and to address any concerns, if any. The patient should continue to take any prescribed medication as directed and avoid placing excessive pressure on the affected foot. It is also advised that the patient follow up with a physical therapist to improve his gait and regain mobility.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Necrosis of the right first and second toes after a crushing injury and a plantar forefoot soft tissue defect.

Disposition:

Discharged home in stable condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 17 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: [REDACTED] days

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with dead tissue on the right big toe and second toe. The patient also had damaged skin on the bottom of the front part of the foot. These injuries happened after a car accident where the foot was crushed.

Medical Management:
The dead toes were removed at the joint where they connect to the foot. The area was cleaned to prepare for surgery. To repair the damaged area, doctors took a piece of skin with nerves from the bottom of the other foot. The blood vessels in this skin flap were connected to blood vessels in the injured foot. Two small veins were connected to larger veins in the foot and leg. The nerve in the skin flap was connected to a nerve in the injured foot. This helps the patient feel sensation in the new skin. The area where the skin was taken from was covered with a full layer of skin from another part of the body.

Recovery:
The patient healed well with no problems. The patient was sent home in stable condition. The patient should get follow-up care to make sure the wounds heal and to work on walking again.

Follow-up Care:
The patient should make an appointment with the doctor to check on healing. The patient should also talk to the doctor about any concerns. The patient should keep taking all medicines as directed. The patient should not put too much weight or pressure on the injured foot. The patient should also see a physical therapist to help with walking and movement.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Dead tissue on the right big toe and second toe from a crushing injury. Damaged skin on the bottom of the front part of the foot.

Disposition:
Discharged home in stable condition.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
Patient Name: Not Provided
Gender: Female
Age: 7 years
Medical Record Number: Not Provided

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with various clinical symptoms including lethargy, muscle weakness, skin issues, abdominal distension, polyuria, and polydipsia. Blood work and urinalysis showed increased blood glucose levels, hypercholesterolemia, and glycosuria. After the diagnosis of diabetes mellitus was confirmed, the patient was treated with insulin glargine and a commercial diet for diabetic cats. The insulin dosage was adjusted, and the patient showed considerable improvement in clinical symptoms. An abdominal ultrasound revealed bilateral adrenal hyperplasia and hepatic steatosis. Further tests ruled out spontaneous hypersomatotropism, hyperthyroidism, and feline immunodeficiency virus and feline leukemia virus. However, spontaneous hyperadrenocorticism (HAC) was suspected and diagnosed based on clinical signs, increased urinary cortisol:creatinine ratio, and lack of suppression on the low-dose dexamethasone suppression test. A dosage of cabergoline was prescribed to address the HAC. After the second dose, the patient experienced clinical hypoglycemia that required emergency veterinary assistance. The symptoms of hypoglycemia were managed via appropriate treatment, and the patient was kept under medical observation for several hours. Following successful treatment and management of clinical symptoms, the patient was discharged.

Diagnosis:
1. Diabetes Mellitus
2. Spontaneous Hyperadrenocorticism (HAC)

Treatment:
1. Insulin glargine
2. Commercial diet for diabetic cats
3. Cabergoline

Condition at Discharge:
The patient had stable blood glucose levels and no clinical signs of diabetes mellitus or hypoglycemia. The HAC was treated with cabergoline and patient symptoms showed marked improvement. However, the patient experienced acute decompensation after seven months, and euthanasia was carried out upon the owner's request. 

Recommendations:
None provided due to the patient's clinical outcome.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
Patient Name: Not Provided
Gender: Female
Age: 7 years
Medical Record Number: Not Provided

Hospital Course:
The patient was brought to the hospital with several health problems. These included tiredness, weak muscles, skin problems, a swollen belly, peeing a lot, and drinking a lot of water. Blood tests and urine tests showed high blood sugar, high cholesterol, and sugar in the urine. After doctors confirmed the patient had diabetes, treatment began with a long-acting insulin called insulin glargine. The patient was also put on a special diet made for diabetic cats. The insulin dose was changed over time, and the patient got much better. A belly scan using sound waves showed that both adrenal glands were enlarged. The scan also showed fatty liver disease. More tests ruled out other conditions including growth hormone problems, overactive thyroid, and two cat viruses. However, doctors thought the patient might have a condition where the body makes too much of a stress hormone called cortisol. This condition is called Cushing's disease. It was confirmed using signs the patient showed, high cortisol levels in urine tests, and a special hormone test. A medicine called cabergoline was given to treat the Cushing's disease. After the second dose, the patient's blood sugar dropped too low. This needed emergency care from the vet. The low blood sugar was treated properly, and the patient was watched closely for several hours. After successful treatment and the health problems were under control, the patient went home.

Diagnosis:
1. Diabetes, a condition where the body cannot control blood sugar properly
2. Cushing's disease, a condition where the body makes too much stress hormone

Treatment:
1. Insulin glargine, a long-acting insulin
2. Special diet made for diabetic cats
3. Cabergoline, a medicine for Cushing's disease

Condition at Discharge:
The patient had steady blood sugar levels. There were no signs of diabetes or low blood sugar problems. The Cushing's disease was being treated with cabergoline, and the patient showed good improvement. However, after seven months the patient became very sick very quickly. The owner asked for the patient to be put to sleep, and this was done.

Recommendations:
None provided due to the patient's clinical outcome.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 22 years old

Sex: Female

Chief Complaint: Lower back pain

Medical History: None

Hospital Course:

Admission: The patient presented to our center with continuous lower back pain for the past 6 months. She had no history of other symptoms or medical conditions.

Examination: On examination, the patient was afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and fully conscious. Her physical exam was unremarkable except for her back pain. Blood counts were within normal ranges, and liver and renal function tests were unremarkable.

Imaging: Transabdominal ultrasonography of the pelvis showed a complex cystic lesion with dimensions of 10.2 cm × 6.4 cm. A subsequent magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan revealed a 10.6 cm × 10 cm × 9.4 cm sized multiloculated cystic lesion in the presacral space with variable signal intensities, giving an impression of a type IV sacrococcygeal teratoma.

Treatment: The patient underwent a laparoscopic excision of the cyst

Age: 22 years old

Sex: Female

Chief Complaint: Lower back pain

Medical History: None

Hospital Course:

Admission: The patient came to our center with ongoing lower back pain for the past 6 months. She had no history of other symptoms or health problems.

Examination: During the exam, the patient had no fever, had stable blood pressure and heart rate, and was fully awake and alert. Her physical exam was normal except for her back pain. Blood tests showed normal results. Liver and kidney function tests were also normal.

Imaging: An ultrasound of the pelvic area showed a fluid-filled growth with solid parts measuring 10.2 cm by 6.4 cm. A follow-up MRI scan showed a larger growth with multiple fluid-filled pockets in the area in front of the tailbone. It measured 10.6 cm by 10 cm by 9.4 cm. The doctors believed this was a type of rare tumor called a sacrococcygeal teratoma, which is a growth present from birth that forms near the tailbone.

Treatment: The patient had surgery using small cuts and a camera to remove the growth.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: II-13

Date of Admission: November 2018

Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital Course: 

This discharge summary outlines the case of a 47-year-old Vietnamese woman, who was diagnosed with left breast cancer in November 2018. The patient's diagnostic process involved an x-ray analysis, pathology interpretation, and breast ultrasound. The patient detected a lump in her left breast by herself and underwent a lumpectomy in a private clinic. Post-surgery, the patient had a total removal of the breast with revision PATEY protocol and was treated with adjuvant chemotherapy, including paclitaxel and trastuzumab. Hereditary cancer-predisposing syndrome was suspected in the patient's family after it was found that cancer was present in several family members across different generations.

Findings: 

The patient's pathological features were consistent with invasive carcinoma with the molecular subtype HER2. Postoperative stage was pT1cN0Mx, and no malignancy was found in the 20 lymph nodes removed during surgery. Immunohistochemical stains revealed estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR) negative, Her2/neu positive 3 plus, and Ki67 index 75%.

Family History:

The patient's mother died of an unknown cause while her father remained healthy. Among nine siblings, two older brothers died of unknown cancers. Her younger sister was diagnosed with left breast cancer in March 2015, and her daughter had left breast cancer at the age of 22 years. Moreover, a genetic factor related to breast cancer was hypothesized, leading to a screening mutation test with her blood by applying targeted next-generation sequencing. Her blood showed a novel mutation of BRCA1 gene while the BRCA2 gene was not mutated. 

Conclusion:

The screening test detected that the patient inherited a mutant allele from her father. Both the patient's daughter and several siblings had the same mutation of BRCA1 gene, including one of the children of the deceased brothers. The BRCA1 gene mutation was identified as a novel heterogenous genetic mutation, named NM_007294.3 (BRCA1): c.4998insA (p. Tyr1666Terfs), according to ClinVar Database of the US National Institutes of Health. The sequencing output was verified via Sanger sequencing and gave the same result.

Recommendations:

The patient's clinical features and family medical history indicate that there may be a hereditary risk for cancer within her family. The discharge summary recommendation is to follow up with the patient and her family members to discuss genetic counseling and determine the best plan of action for future screenings or other medical interventions.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: II-13

Date of Admission: November 2018

Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital Course:

This discharge summary describes the case of a 47-year-old Vietnamese woman. She was told she had cancer in her left breast in November 2018. The patient found a lump in her left breast on her own. Doctors used chest pictures, tissue samples, and sound wave imaging to confirm the diagnosis. The patient had surgery to remove the lump at a private clinic. After that surgery, the patient had her whole breast removed using a specific surgical method. The patient then received cancer-fighting drugs called paclitaxel and trastuzumab. Doctors thought cancer might run in the patient's family. This was because several family members in different generations had cancer.

Findings:

The tissue sample showed the patient had a type of breast cancer that spreads. This cancer type is called HER2. After surgery, the cancer was at an early stage called pT1cN0Mx. Doctors removed 20 small glands called lymph nodes during surgery. None of these glands showed any cancer. Lab tests showed the cancer does not respond to the hormones estrogen or progesterone. The cancer tested strongly positive for a protein called HER2. The cancer cells were growing quickly, shown by a Ki67 score of 75 percent.

Family History:

The patient's mother died from an unknown cause. Her father was still healthy. The patient has nine brothers and sisters. Two older brothers died from cancers that were not identified. Her younger sister was told she had left breast cancer in March 2015. Her daughter was told she had left breast cancer at age 22. Because of this family history, doctors thought a gene problem might cause breast cancer in this family. They tested the patient's blood using a special method to look for gene changes. The test found a new change in the BRCA1 gene. The BRCA2 gene was normal.

Conclusion:

The test showed the patient got the changed gene from her father. The patient's daughter and some brothers and sisters have the same BRCA1 gene change. One child of the deceased brothers also has this gene change. This gene change is a new finding. It is named NM_007294.3 (BRCA1): c.4998insA (p. Tyr1666Terfs) in a US health database. Doctors confirmed the test results using a second testing method. Both tests showed the same results.

Recommendations:

The patient's health history and family medical history suggest cancer risk may run in her family. The recommendation is to schedule follow-up visits with the patient and her family members. These visits should include genetic counseling to talk about their options. The family should discuss a plan for future cancer screening tests or other medical care.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 5-year-old Chinese woman, presented to the emergency department with complaints of cough, fever, headache, vomiting, and altered interactions with her parents. She was admitted to the pediatric intensive care unit and diagnosed with sepsis and pneumonia. Empiric antibiotic therapy, immunoglobulin supply, and restricted fluid administration were given. The patient's condition deteriorated, and she became comatose, hypotensive, and suffered from arrhythmia and hypoxia. Electrocardiogram confirmed prolonged Q–T interval while the thyroid test results were received, indicating untreated hypothyroidism secondary to autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's thyroiditis). Hormone replacement was immediately started, and the patient was administered levothyroxine via nasogastric tube. She also received vasopressor, mechanical ventilation, and continuous renal replacement treatment. Two days after starting levothyroxine, her consciousness was restored, and temperature returned to normal. She was extubated on the fifth day after the initial hormone administration. The dosage of oral levothyroxine was reduced, and she was discharged on a regimen of 3 μg/kg/d once daily.

Discharge Summary:
The patient, a 5-year-old Chinese woman, was admitted to the pediatric intensive care unit with symptoms of cough, fever, headache, vomiting, and altered interactions with her parents. She developed septicemia and pneumonia and was treated with empiric antibiotic therapy, immunoglobulin supply, and restricted fluid administration. She later deteriorated and became comatose, hypotensive, and suffered from arrhythmia and hypoxia. Her condition was diagnosed with untreated hypothyroidism secondary to autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's thyroiditis), and hormone replacement was immediately initiated. The patient received levothyroxine, vasopressor, mechanical ventilation, and continuous renal replacement treatment. After two days of hormone therapy, her consciousness was restored, and temperature returned to normal. She was extubated on the fifth day after the initial hormone administration and discharged on a regimen of 3 μg/kg/d once daily. The patient's family was recommended to undergo thyroid tests, and her father and grandmother were confirmed with Hashimoto's thyroiditis as well.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 5-year-old Chinese girl, came to the emergency room with cough, fever, headache, vomiting, and unusual behavior with her parents. She was placed in the children's intensive care unit. Doctors found she had a serious blood infection and a lung infection. She received antibiotics to fight infection, medicine to boost her immune system, and limited fluids. The patient got worse and fell into a deep sleep state where she could not wake up. Her blood pressure dropped too low. Her heart beat in an uneven pattern and her oxygen levels fell too low. A heart test showed her heart's electrical signals were too slow. Blood tests showed she had an underactive thyroid gland that was never treated. This was caused by her own immune system attacking her thyroid gland. Doctors quickly started giving her thyroid medicine called levothyroxine through a tube in her nose to her stomach. She also received medicine to raise her blood pressure, a machine to help her breathe, and a machine to help clean her blood like her kidneys would. Two days after starting the thyroid medicine, she woke up and her body temperature went back to normal. The breathing tube was removed on the fifth day after starting the thyroid medicine. Her thyroid medicine dose was lowered before she went home. She left the hospital taking thyroid medicine once a day at a dose of 3 micrograms per kilogram per day.

Discharge Summary:

She developed a serious blood infection and a lung infection. She was treated with antibiotics to fight infection, medicine to boost her immune system, and limited fluids. She got worse and fell into a deep sleep state where she could not wake up. Her blood pressure dropped too low. Her heart beat in an uneven pattern and her oxygen levels fell too low. Doctors found she had an underactive thyroid gland that was never treated. This was caused by her own immune system attacking her thyroid gland. Doctors quickly started giving her thyroid medicine. The patient received thyroid medicine called levothyroxine, medicine to raise her blood pressure, a machine to help her breathe, and a machine to help clean her blood. Two days after starting the thyroid medicine, she woke up and her body temperature went back to normal. The breathing tube was removed on the fifth day after starting the thyroid medicine. She left the hospital taking thyroid medicine once a day at a dose of 3 micrograms per kilogram per day. Doctors told the family to get their thyroid glands tested. Her father and grandmother were found to have the same thyroid condition.

input:
Discharge Summary: 

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Hospital Course: 

The patient, a 45-year-old male, was admitted to the hospital with symptoms of left flank pain, hypoesthesia, and weakness at both lower extremities. Upon examination, the patient had motor weakness in both lower extremities and elevated deep tendon reflex of the right knee. Diagnostic tests indicated fracture of T8, T9 vertebral body by bone tumor, pathologic fracture of T9 vertebral body by bone tumor, T8–10 myelopathy, and cord compression.

The patient underwent T9 corpectomy and mesh cage insertion with T8–10 posterior screw fixation. Pathological examination confirmed a diagnosis of Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH). The patient underwent radiotherapy at T8–10 spine and showed remarkable improvement in motor weaknesses and hypoesthesia.

The patient has been followed for almost 6 years without any recurrence. Discharge instructions included follow-up care with the primary care physician and regular imaging surveillance to ensure no recurrence of the bone tumor. 

Overall, the patient received excellent medical care from the healthcare team and responded well to treatment.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 45-year-old man. He came to the hospital with pain in his left side, numbness, and weakness in both legs. During the exam, the patient had weak muscles in both legs. The patient also had stronger than normal reflexes in the right knee. Tests showed that a bone tumor had caused breaks in the spine bones called T8 and T9. The tumor was also pressing on the spinal cord and causing nerve problems.

The patient had surgery to remove the damaged spine bone at T9. Doctors put in a metal cage to support the spine. They also placed screws at T8 through T10 to hold the spine steady. Lab tests of the removed tissue showed a condition called Langerhans cell histiocytosis. This is a rare disease where abnormal cells build up and can damage bones. The patient then had radiation treatment on the T8 through T10 area of the spine. After treatment, the leg weakness and numbness got much better.

The patient has been checked for almost 6 years with no sign of the tumor coming back. Discharge instructions included follow-up visits with the regular doctor. The patient should also get regular scans to make sure the bone tumor does not return.

Overall, the patient received excellent medical care from the healthcare team and responded well to treatment.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

PATIENT NAME: N/A
MRN: N/A
DATE OF ADMISSION: N/A
DATE OF DISCHARGE: N/A
ADMITTING DIAGNOSIS: N/A
DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS: Giant cell variant of GBM

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient, a 51-year-old woman, was admitted with complaints of headache, vomiting, and gait ataxia of 1 month duration. She also noted diminution of her vision during the course of illness. The patient was evaluated and found to have 6/36 visual acuity and mild papilledema bilaterally. Her gait was ataxic in nature and had positive cerebellar signs. Magnetic resonance imaging scan showed a left cerebellar intra-axial mass lesion near cerebellopontine angle with T1-weighted hypointensity and T2-weighted hyperintensity with mild perifocal edema and mass effect. On contrast study, the lesion was enhancing homogeneously with area of unenhanced cystic component in it. Magnetic resonance (MR) spectroscopy showed choline peak with reduced N-acetyl aspartate (NAA) and choline: NAA ratio of 5.74. Lactate peak was also observed at some places. Metastatic tumor, malignant lymphoma, and GBM were considered for radiological differential diagnosis. Workups for common metastatic lesions from breast, lung, colon, and secondary central nervous system lymphoma were found to be negative.

Through a left retromastoid suboccipital craniectomy, a greyish-pink, soft, non-capsulated, vascular tumor in the left cerebellar hemisphere was excised in piecemeal near totally. The diagnosis of giant cell variant of GBM was made on the histopathological section. The patient was referred for radiotherapy. At follow up after 2 months, the patient has been doing well without any cranial nerve deficit.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Follow-up with your primary physician for any further evaluation.
2. Continue to take medications as prescribed.
3. Do not hesitate to contact your physician if any new symptoms appear.
4. Take care of your health.
5. Follow a healthy lifestyle and diet.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS: N/A

DISCHARGE CONDITION: N/A

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: N/A
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

PATIENT NAME: N/A
MRN: N/A
DATE OF ADMISSION: N/A
DATE OF DISCHARGE: N/A
ADMITTING DIAGNOSIS: N/A
DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS: Giant cell type of glioblastoma (a type of brain tumor)

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient, a 51-year-old woman, came to the hospital with headaches, vomiting, and trouble walking for 1 month. She also noticed her vision getting worse during this time. Doctors checked her and found she had reduced vision and mild swelling of the nerve at the back of both eyes. She had trouble walking steadily and showed signs of problems with the brain area that controls balance. A special brain scan called an MRI showed a growth in the left side of the lower back part of her brain. The growth was near an area where the brain connects to nerves for hearing and balance. The scan showed some swelling around the growth and pressure on nearby brain tissue. When dye was used during the scan, the growth lit up evenly and had a fluid-filled pocket inside it. Another special test looked at the chemicals in the growth. This test showed patterns that suggested the growth might be harmful. Doctors thought the growth could be cancer that spread from another body part, a type of blood cancer, or a brain tumor. Tests ruled out cancer spreading from the breast, lung, colon, or blood cancer in the brain.

The patient had surgery through an opening made in the skull behind the left ear. Doctors found a grayish-pink, soft tumor with many blood vessels in the left lower back part of the brain. The tumor did not have a clear outer covering. Doctors removed almost all of the tumor in small pieces. Lab tests on the tumor tissue showed it was a giant cell type of glioblastoma. The patient was sent for radiation treatment. At a checkup 2 months later, the patient was doing well with no problems with the nerves in her head.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Follow up with the primary doctor for any further tests or care.
2. Continue to take medicines as told.
3. Do not wait to call the doctor if any new symptoms appear.
4. Take care of overall health.
5. Follow a healthy lifestyle and diet.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS: N/A

DISCHARGE CONDITION: N/A

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: N/A

[Header]
Discharge Summary

[Patient Information]
Patient Name: [insert patient name]
Medical Record Number: [insert assigned number]
Sex: Female 
Age: 66 

[Hospital Course and Reason for Admission]
The patient was admitted to our medical institution due to intermittent pin-prick pain occurring in the right chest. During a 4-month period, the patient underwent intermittent nerve blocks at a local clinic. The symptoms were not improved. Therefore, the patient was admitted to our medical institution.

[History and Physical Examination]
On physical examination, the patient complained of allodynia and hyperesthesia. At the time of admission, VAS score was 7-8/10 points. The patient had a concurrent presence of the scar tissue.

[Diagnostic Tests]
No diagnostic tests were performed.

[Treatment and Condition at Discharge]
For the treatment of the patient, a needle was placed in the multifidus muscle at the level of T5 and the tissue around the facet at the level of T5-6. Four weeks after the treatment, VAS score decreased from 7-8 points at the time of admission to 3 points. Two weeks later, VAS score further improved to 2 points. The patient's pain was managed with analgesics and anti-depressants.

[Discharge Medication and Instructions]
The patient was discharged with instructions to continue taking her analgesics and anti-depressants.

[Follow-up]
No follow-up was scheduled at discharge.

[Diagnoses]
The patient was diagnosed with neuralgia. 

[Prognosis]
The prognosis for the patient is good, but symptoms can reoccur. 

[Summary]
The patient was admitted for the management of neuralgia occurring in the right chest. Pain was managed with a multifidus muscle needle placement at T5 and tissue around the facet at T5-6. The patient made a good recovery and was discharged with instructions to continue taking pain medications.

Hospital Course and Reason for Admission
The patient came to our hospital because of sharp, stabbing pain in the right side of the chest. The pain came and went. Over 4 months, the patient had nerve-numbing shots at another clinic. These shots did not help. So the patient came to our hospital for more care.

History and Physical Examination
During the exam, the patient had pain from light touch that should not hurt. The patient also felt more pain than normal from things that usually cause only mild pain. At the time of coming to the hospital, the pain level was 7-8 out of 10. The patient also had scar tissue in the area.

Diagnostic Tests
No tests were done.

Treatment and Condition at Discharge
To treat the patient, a needle was placed in a deep back muscle near the spine at the upper back level. The needle was also placed in tissue near the spine joints in the same area. Four weeks after treatment, the pain level dropped from 7-8 out of 10 to 3 out of 10. Two weeks later, the pain level got even better at 2 out of 10. The patient took pain medicine and mood medicine to help with the pain.

Discharge Medication and Instructions
The patient was told to keep taking the pain medicine and mood medicine at home.

Follow-up
No follow-up visit was set up when the patient left the hospital.

Diagnoses
The patient was found to have nerve pain.

Prognosis
The patient should do well, but the pain may come back.

Summary
The patient came to the hospital for nerve pain in the right side of the chest. The pain was treated with needle placement in a deep back muscle and near spine joints in the upper back. The patient got much better and went home with instructions to keep taking pain medicine.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: OR, 43-year-old male

Admission Date: [DATE]

Discharge Date: [DATE]

Hospital: Private Hospital

Reason for Admission:

OR presented with painful micturition and poor urinary stream for a duration of 4 years, along with severe low back pain and right lower limb weakness for 2 years. The patient also experienced occasional abdominal swelling with hiccups that could last for 2-3 days before resolving spontaneously.

Hospital Course:

OR underwent retrograde urethrocystogram (RUCG) and prostate biopsy, which were followed by urinary retention and subsequent catheterisation. Removing the catheter was associated with persistent mucoid and blood stained discharge. Cranial computed tomography (CT) scan and lumbo-sacral magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) were performed.

Physical Exam:

OR is a well-built 43-year-old male (body mass index of 28.5), not in obvious distress, afebrile and anicteric. Chest, abdomen, and rectal examinations were essentially normal. There was mild loss of sensation to fine touch, with reduced power movement on both lower limbs, with more power loss on the right (3/5) than on the left (4/5). There was no muscular atrophy or obvious physical deformity in either the erect or supine position.

Medical History:

OR had previously been treated for sexually transmitted diseases (STD) by a chemist/pharmacist about 5 years before presentation.

Assessment and Plan:

OR will be discharged with appropriate care instructions, including follow-up appointments with the treating healthcare team as necessary. Detailed test results and imaging reports are available for the outpatient clinic evaluation. 

Abbreviations:

• RUCG: Retrograde Urethrocystogram
• MRI: Magnetic Resonance Imaging
• CT: Computed Tomography
• STD: Sexually Transmitted Disease

Discharge Summary:

Patient: OR, 43-year-old male

Admission Date: [DATE]

Discharge Date: [DATE]

Hospital: Private Hospital

Reason for Admission:

OR came to the hospital with painful urination and a weak urine stream for 4 years. The patient also had severe lower back pain and weakness in the right leg for 2 years. Sometimes the patient had belly swelling with hiccups that lasted 2-3 days before going away on their own.

Hospital Course:

OR had a special X-ray test to look at the bladder and urine tube. The patient also had a small tissue sample taken from the prostate gland. After these tests, the patient could not urinate and needed a tube placed to drain urine. When the tube was removed, the patient kept having mucus and bloody discharge.

The patient also had a scan of the head and a detailed scan of the lower back.

Physical Exam:

OR is a well-built 43-year-old male with a body weight score of 28.5, which is slightly above normal. The patient was comfortable, had no fever, and had no yellowing of the skin. The chest, belly, and rectal exams were normal. The patient had some loss of feeling to light touch in both legs. The right leg was weaker than the left leg. There was no muscle wasting or visible body changes when standing or lying down.

Medical History:

OR was treated for a sexually passed infection by a pharmacist about 5 years before this hospital visit.

Assessment and Plan:

OR will go home with care instructions. The patient should attend follow-up visits with the care team as needed. Test results and scan reports will be ready for review at the outpatient clinic visit.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Confidential
Medical Record Number: Confidential
Date of Admission: Confidential
Date of Discharge: Confidential

Hospital Course:
The patient is an 82-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital as an emergency with gross abdominal distension and lower abdominal pain. She had a six-week history of nonspecific malaise, loss of appetite, and dizziness, along with a longstanding history of constipation. Her past medical history included hypertension and impaired glucose tolerance, while her past surgical history included a previous caesarean section.

On examination, the patient had a nontender, grossly distended abdomen with an indentable mass in the lower left quadrant. Blood tests revealed acute renal failure and microcytic anaemia. A CT scan revealed massive dilatation of the sigmoid colon with faecal material within, resulting in bilateral renal tract obstruction. Following a clinical decision, she underwent a laparotomy, during which she was found to have massive colonic dilatation of the sigmoid and descending colon, with dense perirectal fibrosis throughout the length of the rectum. The rectum was resected at the mid rectum, and a Hartman's procedure was performed. Her renal function normalised, and she made an uneventful recovery. 

On histology from the resected specimen, it was observed that the longitudinal muscle layer was degenerate and absent in many areas of the specimen. The myenteric and submucosal plexuses were degenerate where longitudinal muscle was absent, while polyglucosan inclusion bodies were present throughout the longitudinal and circular layers of the muscularis propria, with almost total degeneration of the longitudinal layer, suggesting a myopathic process. The occurrence of longitudinal loss was in areas where the overlying muscle and mucosa were intact indicating that this was likely to be a primary myopathy. There was also a replacement of myocytes in both muscularis layers with elastosis and fibrosis, along with additional areas of elastosis in the submucosa and widespread smooth muscle metaplasia.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with acute renal failure and massive colonic dilatation of the sigmoid and descending colon, with dense perirectal fibrosis throughout the length of the rectum. Histology from the resected specimen revealed a primary myopathy with elastosis and fibrosis.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient is advised to follow a healthy diet and exercise regimen and to attend regular follow-up appointments with the healthcare provider. She is also advised to take appropriate measures to prevent constipation, such as increasing fibre intake and staying hydrated.

Discharge Medications:
None.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to avoid strenuous physical activity and to take adequate rest for at least two weeks following the surgery. She is also advised to attend regular follow-up appointments with the healthcare provider to monitor her renal and colonic function and to ensure that her postoperative recovery is proceeding smoothly. In case of any unwanted side effects or complications, the patient is advised to immediately contact the healthcare provider.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Confidential
Medical Record Number: Confidential
Date of Admission: Confidential
Date of Discharge: Confidential

Hospital Course:
The patient is an 82-year-old female. She came to the hospital as an emergency with a very swollen belly and pain in the lower belly. For six weeks before this, she had been feeling unwell, not wanting to eat, and feeling dizzy. She also had a long history of trouble passing stool. Her past health problems included high blood pressure and blood sugar levels that were higher than normal. She had a past surgery to deliver a baby.

During the exam, the patient had a very swollen belly that did not hurt when touched. There was a soft mass in the lower left side of her belly. Blood tests showed her kidneys had suddenly stopped working well. The tests also showed she had low iron in her blood. A scan showed her large bowel was very stretched and full of stool. This was blocking both tubes that carry urine from the kidneys to the bladder. The doctors decided to do surgery. During surgery, they found the lower part of her large bowel was very stretched. They also found thick scar tissue around the end of the bowel. The doctors removed part of the bowel and created an opening in the belly for waste to leave the body. After surgery, her kidneys started working normally again. She recovered well without any problems.

The doctors looked at the removed bowel tissue under a microscope. They found that the muscle layer running along the length of the bowel was damaged and missing in many areas. The nerve networks in the bowel wall were damaged where the muscle was missing. There were unusual clumps of a starchy substance in the muscle layers. The muscle layer running along the bowel was almost completely damaged. This pointed to a disease of the muscle itself. The muscle loss happened in areas where the outer lining and inner lining were still healthy. This meant the muscle disease was the main problem and not caused by something else. Muscle cells in both muscle layers were replaced by stretchy tissue and scar tissue. There was also extra stretchy tissue in other layers. Normal tissue had changed into smooth muscle tissue in many areas.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient was found to have sudden kidney failure and a very stretched lower large bowel with thick scar tissue around the end of the bowel. Looking at the removed tissue showed a muscle disease with stretchy tissue and scar tissue.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient should eat healthy foods and exercise regularly. She should go to all follow-up visits with her healthcare provider. She should also take steps to prevent trouble passing stool. This includes eating more fiber and drinking plenty of water.

Discharge Medications:
None.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should not do hard physical work. She should rest well for at least two weeks after the surgery. She should go to all follow-up visits with her healthcare provider. These visits will check how her kidneys and bowel are working. They will also make sure she is healing well after surgery. If there are any bad effects or problems, the patient should contact her healthcare provider right away.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]

Age: 38

Sex: Female

Medical Record Number: [Number]

Date of Admission: [Date]

Date of Discharge: [Date]

Admission Diagnosis: Multiple left axillary nodes

Discharge Diagnosis: Multiple left axillary nodes

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 38-year-old female, was admitted to the hospital for Fine Needle Aspiration Cytology (FNAC) of multiple left axillary nodes, one of which was located in the axillary tail of the left breast and appeared as a breast lump in the upper outer quadrant. The nodes ranged from 1 to 3 cm in size, were well-defined, mobile, firm, and non-tender. No systemic symptoms or organomegaly were noted during admission. Peripheral blood counts and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) were within normal limits.

During hospitalization, the patient was closely monitored, and vital signs were recorded at regular intervals. Appropriate measures were taken to manage pain and ensure patient comfort. The patient was also provided with adequate nutrition and hydration throughout their stay.

The patient was discharged in stable condition, with follow-up appointments scheduled to monitor their condition.

Recommendations:
- Follow-up appointments with a healthcare provider to monitor the condition of the axillary nodes and breast lump.
- Avoid activities that may aggravate the axillary nodes and breast lump.
- Follow a balanced and healthy diet to promote overall wellbeing.

Allergies:
No known drug allergies were identified during the hospitalization.

Briefings:
The patient is advised to contact their healthcare provider immediately if they experience any unusual symptoms, such as fever or persistent pain, or if there are any changes in the axillary nodes or breast lump.

Primary Care Follow-up:
Follow-up with primary care physician for further evaluation and management of axillary nodes and breast lump.

Final Diagnosis:
Multiple left axillary nodes.

Chief Complaint:
Axillary nodes.

Patient Name: [Name]

Age: 38

Sex: Female

Medical Record Number: [Number]

Date of Admission: [Date]

Date of Discharge: [Date]

Admission Diagnosis: Multiple lumps in the left armpit area

Discharge Diagnosis: Multiple lumps in the left armpit area

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 38-year-old female, was admitted to the hospital for a needle test to collect cells from multiple lumps in the left armpit area. One of the lumps was found near the outer part of the left breast and felt like a breast lump. The lumps were between 1 and 3 centimeters in size. They had clear edges, could be moved, felt firm, and did not cause pain. The patient had no body-wide symptoms and no swelling of organs during the stay. Blood tests and tests for swelling in the body came back normal.

During the hospital stay, the patient was watched closely. Body temperature, heart rate, and blood pressure were checked regularly. Steps were taken to manage pain and keep the patient comfortable. The patient received proper food and fluids throughout the stay.

The patient was sent home in stable condition. Follow-up visits were scheduled to check on the lumps.

Recommendations:
Follow-up visits with a healthcare provider to check on the armpit lumps and breast lump.
Avoid activities that may make the armpit lumps or breast lump worse.
Eat a balanced and healthy diet to support overall health.

Allergies:
No drug allergies were found during the hospital stay.

Briefings:
The patient should call their healthcare provider right away if they notice any unusual symptoms. These include fever or pain that does not go away. The patient should also call if there are any changes in the armpit lumps or breast lump.

Primary Care Follow-up:
Follow up with the primary care doctor for more tests and care of the armpit lumps and breast lump.

Final Diagnosis:
Multiple lumps in the left armpit area.

Chief Complaint:
Lumps in the armpit area.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 87 years
Date of admission: [Redacted]
Date of discharge: [Redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: Poor oral intake, pneumonia, and hypokalemia
Discharge Diagnosis: Hypokalemia and possible medication-induced hypokalemia

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital for investigation of poor oral intake, hypokalemia, and pneumonia. The patient had discontinued medication for benign prostatic hyperplasia. The patient had been recently diagnosed with cognitive dysfunction and was started on choline alfoscerate and donepezil. Investigations showed hypokalemia and the presence of subtle pneumonia. Further investigations were done to determine the cause of hypokalemia which revealed signs of renal potassium wasting. CT scan showed no abnormalities. A thorough review of the therapeutic agents prescribed for cognitive dysfunction was conducted, which identified donepezil as a possible cause of hypokalemia. Donepezil was immediately discontinued. Potassium supplementation using intravenous and oral formulations was initiated. The patient showed a gradual increase in serum potassium level and on the third day following donepezil cessation, the patient's serum potassium level was 3.5 mmol/L. During this time, proper management of pneumonia was also done. BP was noted to be high for 24 hours after hospitalization and amlodipine and olmesartan were initiated.

Discharge Medications:
Amlodipine and olmesartan were prescribed for BP management.

Follow-up:
The patient was discharged on the ninth day of hospitalization. The patient was advised to continue potassium supplementation for a total of 3 weeks. Olmesartan and amlodipine were prescribed to be continued. The patient was advised to have regular BP monitoring and to follow-up with a primary care doctor.

Instructions to Patient:
The patient was advised to follow a low sodium and low fat diet as well as avoid food high in potassium. The patient was advised to adhere to prescribed medications and avoid over-the-counter medications without consulting a physician. The patient was advised to return to the hospital if any concerning symptoms are noted.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 87 years
Date of admission: [Redacted]
Date of discharge: [Redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: Not eating well, lung infection, and low potassium levels
Discharge Diagnosis: Low potassium levels, possibly caused by a medication

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital because of not eating well, low potassium levels, and a lung infection. The patient had stopped taking medication for an enlarged prostate. The patient was recently told about memory problems and started taking choline alfoscerate and donepezil. Tests showed low potassium levels and a mild lung infection. More tests were done to find out why potassium was low. The tests showed the kidneys were losing too much potassium. A CT scan showed nothing unusual. The care team looked at the memory medications and found donepezil might be causing the low potassium. Donepezil was stopped right away. The patient was given potassium through an IV and by mouth. The patient's potassium levels slowly went up. Three days after stopping donepezil, potassium reached a normal level of 3.5 mmol/L. The lung infection was also treated during this time. Blood pressure was high for 24 hours after coming to the hospital. The patient was started on amlodipine and olmesartan for this.

Discharge Medications:
Amlodipine and olmesartan were prescribed to control blood pressure.

Follow-up:
The patient left the hospital on the ninth day. The patient was told to keep taking potassium for 3 weeks total. The patient should keep taking olmesartan and amlodipine. The patient was told to check blood pressure regularly and see a primary care doctor.

Instructions to Patient:
The patient was told to eat less salt and fat and avoid foods high in potassium. The patient was told to take all prescribed medications. The patient should not take any store-bought medications without asking a doctor first. The patient was told to come back to the hospital if any worrying symptoms appear.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The 31-year-old male patient was admitted for progressive blurred vision for 3 weeks. Brain MRI revealed multiple cystic lesions in the suprasellar cistern. Lumbar puncture was performed, which showed an increased opening pressure, elevated white blood cell count, elevated protein level, and decreased glucose level. Cerebrospinal fluid cytology revealed increased eosinophils. Cryptococcus antigen tests and an Xpert-MTB assay of the cerebrospinal fluid were negative. Next-generation sequencing of the cerebrospinal fluid identified T. solium DNA sequences. Serum and CSF were positive for C. cellulosae IgG antibodies. The patient was diagnosed with basal subarachnoid NCC and was treated with albendazole, dexamethasone, and ETV. Significant improvement was observed in the patient's symptoms and CSF findings after treatment.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Basal subarachnoid NCC.

Discharge Medications:
Albendazole, Dexamethasone, and ETV were administered.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to carefully follow up with the treatment for the condition as prescribed. It is recommended to avoid exposure to any uncooked pork or contaminated water. The patient is advised to seek medical attention immediately if there are any new or worsening symptoms.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The 31-year-old male patient was admitted because his vision had been getting more blurry for 3 weeks. A brain scan showed several fluid-filled sacs in an area at the base of the brain. A spinal tap was done to collect fluid from around the spine. The test showed higher than normal pressure, more white blood cells than normal, higher protein, and lower sugar. The spinal fluid also showed more of a type of white blood cell linked to parasites. Tests for fungal infection and tuberculosis in the spinal fluid came back negative. Special DNA testing of the spinal fluid found signs of a pork tapeworm. Blood and spinal fluid tests also showed signs that the body was fighting this tapeworm infection. The patient was told he had a tapeworm infection at the base of the brain. He was treated with a medicine to kill parasites, a medicine to reduce swelling, and a procedure to drain fluid from the brain. The patient's symptoms and spinal fluid test results got much better after treatment.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Tapeworm infection at the base of the brain.

Discharge Medications:
A medicine to kill parasites, a medicine to reduce swelling, and a procedure to drain fluid from the brain were given.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should keep taking all medicines and follow all care steps as told by the doctor. The patient should not eat raw or undercooked pork or drink water that may be dirty. The patient should get medical help right away if any new symptoms appear or if current symptoms get worse.

Tapeworm infection at the base of the brain.

Discharge Medications:
A medicine to kill parasites, a medicine to reduce swelling, and a procedure to drain fluid from the brain were given.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should keep taking all medicines and follow all care steps as told by the doctor. The patient should eat raw or undercooked pork or drink water that may be dirty. The patient should get medical help right away if any new symptoms appear or if current symptoms get worse.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Patient Information:
34-year-old male with an injury to the right hand sustained while grinding sugarcane pulp. Presented within 3 hours of injury.

Clinical Findings:
Injury involved four fingers of the non-dominant hand, with macerations along the margins of the skin. There were longitudinal lacerations over the fingers, both on the dorsal and volar sides, with the extensor expansion having vertical slits running along its length till the metacarpophalangeal joints. The proximal interphalangeal joints were exposed with the avulsion of the central slips and loss of continuity of the covering capsules, whereas, the flexor tendons were in continuity with loss of the flexor tendon sheath in the region of the volar skin laceration. Two of the three patients suffered from bone continuity loss.

Treatment:
Wounds were washed and covered with sterile dressings, with broad-spectrum antibiotics being initiated. Within the operation room, wound debridement and lavage with 6 liters of normal saline were carried out under regional block. The vertical midline slits in the extensor expansion were stitched using interrupted 4-0 nylon sutures. The skin was approximated over tendons to provide them with a biological coverage using sutures i.e. nylon 3-0.

Two of these patients required split thickness skin grafting over the dorsum of hand, which was undertaken after three days of sustaining the injury. The hand was immobilized using a dorsal plaster slab for pain relief, and the patients were allowed to flex their fingers within the slab. The slab was removed in the second week and the K-wires in case 2 were removed after confirming union at three weeks. Patients underwent rehabilitation under the supervision of a physiotherapist, where they practiced passive, active-assisted, and active stretching of the involved joint.

Outcomes:
The patient was assessed for post-traumatic stiffness, which is clinically apparent in inter-phalyngeal, meta-phalyngeal, and wrist joints. The aim is to restore the function to normal or near-normal dexterity. The patients were assessed using mini-dash score at the end of 6 months. The score was 0 in two patients, and one patient (case 3) was lost to follow-up. The hand function was restored to normal in the two patients who were evaluated.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Patient Information:
34-year-old male with an injury to the right hand that happened while grinding sugarcane pulp. The patient came to the hospital within 3 hours of the injury.

Clinical Findings:
The injury affected four fingers on the hand the patient does not mainly use. The skin edges were torn and crushed. There were lengthwise cuts on both the top and palm sides of the fingers. The tendons on the back of the hand had lengthwise tears running down to the knuckle joints. The middle finger joints were open to the air. The tendons that straighten the fingers were partly torn away. The covering around these joints was also torn. The tendons that bend the fingers were still connected but their protective covering was lost where the palm-side skin was cut. Two of the three patients had broken bones.

Treatment:
The wounds were cleaned and covered with germ-free bandages. The patient was given medicines to fight infection. In the operating room, the doctors removed damaged tissue and washed the wounds with 6 liters of salt water. The patient was given medicine to numb the arm during the procedure. The lengthwise tears in the tendons on the back of the hand were stitched closed with small stitches. The skin was stitched over the tendons to protect them.

Two of these patients needed skin taken from another body area and placed on the back of the hand. This was done three days after the injury. A hard bandage was placed on the back of the hand to reduce pain. The patients could still bend their fingers while wearing the bandage. The bandage was taken off during the second week. The metal pins in case 2 were removed after three weeks once the bones had healed. Patients worked with a physical therapist to do stretching exercises for the injured joints. They did exercises where the therapist moved their fingers and exercises where they moved their own fingers.

Outcomes:
The patient was checked for joint stiffness caused by the injury. This stiffness can happen in the finger joints and wrist. The goal was to bring hand movement back to normal or close to normal. The patients were tested using a hand function score at 6 months. Two patients scored 0, which means normal function. One patient (case 3) did not return for follow-up. Hand function returned to normal in the two patients who completed testing.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 50 years
Sex: Female
Race: South Indian

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the outpatient department with a productive cough with hemoptysis for the past 2 months. She had a history of pulmonary tuberculosis, which was discontinued within 3 weeks of starting the treatment. She also suffered from diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy for the past 8 years and was taking oral hypoglycemic agents for the same. The patient was diagnosed with an indwelling aspergilloma and was treated with an oral loading dose of Voriconazole followed by an oral maintenance dose. The patient responded well to the treatment, and the hemoptysis resolved after a lobectomy. After 3 weeks, the sputum became negative for acid-fast bacilli. The patient is currently in the 4th month of treatment and is doing well.

Laboratory Results:
Admission baseline investigations showed anemia, and the renal and liver function tests were within normal limits.

Diagnostic Study:
Computed tomogram of the thorax revealed a thick-walled cavity in the right lung upper lobe with an indwelling aspergilloma. 3 sputum samples were tested positive for acid-fast bacilli by Ziehl Neelsen's staining technique. Conventional method of culture on Lowenstein Jensen's medium yielded growth of M. tuberculosis. The anti-tuberculosis drug susceptibility test showed resistance to Isoniazid and Rifampicin but sensitive to Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin in their critical concentrations.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with pulmonary tuberculosis and an indwelling aspergilloma.

Treatment:
The patient was initially started on a daily dose of Isoniazid, Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin. However, after culture demonstrated MDR-TB, Isoniazid was stopped, and oral Levofloxacin and Ethionamide were included in the treatment regimen. Rifampicin was not included due to its interactions with Voriconazole. The patient was given an oral loading dose of Voriconazole 400 mg 12th hourly for 2 doses, which was followed by an oral maintenance dose of 200 mg 12th hourly for 6 weeks.

Follow-up:
After the treatment, the patient was counseled on the importance of adhering to medication and follow-up visits. She was advised to return to the hospital for follow-up visits and monitoring of symptoms.

Summary:
The 50-year-old South Indian woman presented with complaints of productive cough with hemoptysis for the past 2 months. She suffered from diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy and had a history of pulmonary tuberculosis. The patient was diagnosed with an indwelling aspergilloma and treated with an oral loading dose of Voriconazole followed by an oral maintenance dose. Hemoptysis resolved after lobectomy, and the sputum became negative for acid-fast bacilli after 3 weeks. The patient responded well to the treatment and is currently in the 4th month of treatment. The patient was advised to adhere to medication and follow-up visits.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 50 years
Sex: Female
Race: South Indian

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the clinic with a cough that brought up mucus and blood for the past 2 months. She had lung tuberculosis before but stopped taking her medicine within 3 weeks of starting it. She also has diabetes with nerve damage in her hands and feet for the past 8 years. She takes pills to control her blood sugar. The patient was found to have a fungus ball growing in her lung. She was treated with an antifungal medicine called Voriconazole, first at a higher dose, then at a lower dose. The patient got better with treatment. The coughing up of blood stopped after surgery to remove part of her lung. After 3 weeks, tests showed no more tuberculosis germs in her mucus. The patient is now in month 4 of treatment and is doing well.

Laboratory Results:
Tests done when the patient arrived showed low red blood cells. Her kidney and liver were working normally.

Diagnostic Study:
A CT scan of the chest showed a hollow space with thick walls in the upper part of the right lung. A fungus ball was growing inside this space. Three mucus samples tested positive for tuberculosis germs using a special staining test. Lab growth tests confirmed the germ was tuberculosis. Drug testing showed the tuberculosis was resistant to two main medicines, Isoniazid and Rifampicin. The tuberculosis could still be treated with Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with lung tuberculosis and a fungus ball in the lung.

Treatment:
The patient first started taking daily doses of Isoniazid, Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin. After tests showed drug-resistant tuberculosis, Isoniazid was stopped. Two new medicines, Levofloxacin and Ethionamide, were added. Rifampicin was not used because it does not work well with the antifungal medicine. For the fungus ball, the patient took Voriconazole 400 mg twice for the first day. Then she took 200 mg twice a day for 6 weeks.

Follow-up:
After treatment, the patient was told how important it is to take all medicines as directed. She was also told to come back to the hospital for check-up visits. The doctors will watch for any returning symptoms.

Summary:
The 50-year-old South Indian woman came in with a cough that brought up mucus and blood for 2 months. She has diabetes with nerve damage and had lung tuberculosis before. The patient was found to have a fungus ball in her lung. She was treated with the antifungal medicine Voriconazole, first at a higher dose, then at a lower dose. The coughing up of blood stopped after surgery to remove part of her lung. After 3 weeks, tests showed no more tuberculosis germs. The patient responded well to treatment and is now in month 4. She was told to keep taking her medicines and come back for follow-up visits.

Medical history:
The patient is a 24-year-old female with mild asthma and a history of atrial septal defect repair in 2007. She has never been hospitalized for asthma and does not require regular treatment for it.

Hospital admission:
The patient presented with vomiting, diarrhoea, malaise, fever, headache, and dyspnoea. On admission (Day 3), a CXR showed extensive right lower lobe and patchy left basal consolidation, and she had a productive cough. Broad-spectrum antibiotics were started on Day 3, and the patient was transferred to the ICU on Day 6. 

ICU stay:
The patient was intubated on Day 6 and remained in the ICU for 11 days, with only one organ failure (lungs).

Final blood sample:
A peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) sample was obtained on Day 34.

Diagnosis:
No explicit diagnosis was mentioned in the case report.

Conclusion:
The patient was discharged after an extended stay in the ICU and a PBMC sample was taken on Day 34. Further information about the patient's condition and treatment is not available in the case report.
clean_ref:
Medical history:
The patient is a 24-year-old female with mild asthma and a heart defect that was fixed with surgery in 2007. She has never been hospitalized for asthma and does not need regular treatment for it.

Hospital admission:
The patient came to the hospital with vomiting, diarrhea, weakness, fever, headache, and trouble breathing. When she arrived on Day 3, a chest X-ray showed infection in parts of both lungs. She also had a cough with mucus. Strong antibiotics were started on Day 3. The patient was moved to the intensive care unit on Day 6.

ICU stay:
The patient was put on a breathing machine on Day 6. She stayed in the intensive care unit for 11 days. Only her lungs were not working properly during this time.

Final blood sample:
A blood sample was taken on Day 34 to study her immune cells.

Diagnosis:
No specific diagnosis was listed in the medical report.

Conclusion:
The patient was sent home after a long stay in the intensive care unit. A blood sample was taken on Day 34. More details about the patient's condition and treatment are not available in the medical report.

Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

Brief Hospitalization Summary:
The patient, a 32-year-old lady presented with a pathological fracture of the right humerus, previously treated with interlocked, intramedullary nail at an outside facility, with subsequent development of significant pain and nonunion. Biopsy at our institution revealed diagnosis of high-grade fibroblastic osteosarcoma, leading to resection of the entire right humerus with extra-articular glenohumeral excision (modified Tikhoff-Linberg).

Pertinent findings in the admission note:
The patient presented with significant shoulder pain that worsened with motion, with disuse atrophy of the deltoid muscle and an unsightly prominence of the proximal end cap of the prosthesis.

Management Course:
The patient was managed with revision surgery in the form of a reduction in length of the prosthesis by removal of the end cap and reattachment through drill holes to the scapula with mersilene tape. The patient remained relatively pain-free until her 12th annual follow-up, during which identical symptoms recurred. Revision surgery was performed again, involving a reduction in prosthetic length by an additional two centimeters, which resulted in satisfactory pain control that was maintained till her latest follow-up.

Summary:
The patient presented with a pathological fracture of the right humerus, which was diagnosed as high-grade fibroblastic osteosarcoma and treated with the resection of the entire right humerus with extra-articular glenohumeral excision (modified Tikhoff-Linberg). The patient experienced significant complaints of pain and nonunion after the initial treatment. Revision surgeries were performed, including a reduction in length of the prosthesis by removal of the end cap and reattachment through drill holes to the scapula with mersilene tape, and ultimately, a reduction in prosthetic length by an additional two centimeters. The patient is currently maintaining satisfactory pain control.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

Brief Hospitalization Summary:
The patient, a 32-year-old woman came in with a broken right upper arm bone. The break was caused by disease in the bone. Another hospital had placed a metal rod inside the bone to fix it. After that surgery, the patient had bad pain and the bone did not heal. A tissue test at our hospital showed the patient had a serious type of bone cancer. The medical team removed the entire upper arm bone and part of the shoulder joint using a special surgery method.

Pertinent findings in the admission note:
The patient had bad shoulder pain that got worse with movement. The shoulder muscle had gotten smaller from not being used. Part of the artificial arm piece was sticking out in a visible way.

Management Course:
The medical team did a follow-up surgery to fix the problem. They made the artificial arm piece shorter by taking off the end cap. They attached it to the shoulder blade using strong medical tape threaded through small holes drilled in the bone. The patient had little to no pain until the 12-year checkup. At that time, the same problems came back. The medical team did another surgery to make the artificial arm piece two centimeters shorter. This gave good pain control that lasted until the most recent checkup.

Summary:
The patient came in with a broken right upper arm bone caused by disease. Tests showed it was a serious type of bone cancer. The medical team removed the entire upper arm bone and part of the shoulder joint using a special surgery method. After the first treatment, the patient had bad pain and the bone did not heal. The medical team did follow-up surgeries. They made the artificial arm piece shorter and attached it to the shoulder blade with strong medical tape. Later, they made it two centimeters shorter. The patient now has good pain control.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Male
Age: 6 years old

Chief Complaint: Painful intumescence of the left inguinal area

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 6-year-old Greek boy, was admitted to our hospital with a painful intumescence of the left inguinal area. Prior to admission, the patient was referred to a general hospital where he was diagnosed with an incarcerated left inguinal hernia. The hernia was subsequently reduced by a local surgeon, but the patient continued to experience discomfort and pain. Upon physical examination, the left inguinal area appeared normal, with only subcutaneous edema and a hematoma due to the reduction manipulations observed. Mild discomfort and pain was present in the left testicle without clinical evidence of testicular torsion. These symptoms persisted for three days before the decision was made to operate on the patient.

During the operation, a lower Spigelian hernia was identified. The hernia sac penetrated from a small defect in the Spigelian fascia within Hesselbach's triangle. The orifice diameter was about 1.5 cm, and the sac extended up to 7 cm and contained a small part of the large omentum which was successfully reduced. Reconstruction of the hernia was performed with non-absorbable sutures.

The patient remained in stable condition throughout his hospital stay and was discharged without any complications. The patient was instructed to rest and was prescribed appropriate follow-up care.

Follow-Up Care:

The patient remains free of symptoms and recurrence for eight years after the operation.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Male
Age: 6 years old

Chief Complaint: Painful swelling in the left groin area

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 6-year-old Greek boy, came to our hospital with a painful swelling in the left groin area. Before coming here, the patient went to another hospital. Doctors there found that part of the intestine had pushed through a weak spot in the groin and got stuck. A surgeon at that hospital pushed the bulge back into place. However, the patient still had discomfort and pain. When doctors examined the patient, the left groin area looked normal. There was only some swelling under the skin and bruising from the earlier treatment. The patient had mild discomfort and pain in the left testicle. There were no signs that the testicle had twisted. These symptoms lasted for three days before doctors decided to do surgery.

During surgery, doctors found a different type of hernia in the lower belly wall. The hernia sac pushed through a small hole in the side belly muscle wall. The opening was about 1.5 cm wide. The sac stretched about 7 cm long. It contained a small piece of fatty tissue from inside the belly. Doctors pushed this tissue back into place. They closed the weak spot using stitches that do not dissolve over time.

The patient stayed in good condition during the hospital stay. The patient went home without any problems. The patient was told to rest. The patient was given instructions for follow-up care.

Follow-Up Care:

The patient has had no symptoms and the hernia has not come back for eight years after the surgery.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: N/A
Primary Care Physician: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 12-year-old female with a history of multiple medical comorbidities including Chiari II malformation, hydrocephalus, agenesis of the corpus callosum, microcephaly, bilateral renal duplicated collecting system, scoliosis, and myelomeningocele associated with neurogenic bladder and bladder reflux. She was admitted to the neonatal intensive care unit soon after birth and has undergone multiple surgeries and evaluations. She was recently evaluated by us at 11 years of age, and we found her to have recurrent gastrointestinal illnesses of unknown etiology and hyperopia, striae over the breasts, pubic hair, and scoliosis. Levetiracetam and levocarnitine control her seizures, and she has been seizure free for over three years. Echocardiogram and electrocardiogram have been normal.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient has multiple medical comorbidities including Chiari II malformation, hydrocephalus, agenesis of the corpus callosum, microcephaly, bilateral renal duplicated collecting system, scoliosis, and myelomeningocele associated with neurogenic bladder and bladder reflux. She has a chromosomal abnormality with a duplication of 6p25.3p24.3 (7.585 Mb) and a deletion of 6q27q27 (6.244 Mb). The patient also has hyperopia, striae over the breasts, pubic hair, and scoliosis.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient is a 12-year-old female with multiple medical comorbidities and a chromosomal abnormality. She has undergone multiple surgeries and evaluations and was recently evaluated by us at 11 years of age. She has recurrent gastrointestinal illnesses of unknown etiology and has hyperopia, striae over the breasts, pubic hair, and scoliosis. Levetiracetam and levocarnitine control her seizures, and she has been seizure free for over three years. Echocardiogram and electrocardiogram have been normal.
