Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: N/A
Primary Care Physician: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 12-year-old girl with many health conditions. These include a brain condition called Chiari II where part of the brain sits too low. She also has too much fluid in the brain and is missing a part that connects the two sides of the brain. She has a smaller than normal head size. She has an extra set of tubes in both kidneys that carry urine. She has a curved spine. She was born with an opening in her spine that affects her bladder. Her bladder does not work normally and urine flows backward from the bladder toward the kidneys. She was in the newborn intensive care unit soon after birth. She has had many surgeries and check-ups since then. She was seen by us at 11 years old. At that time, we found she had repeated stomach and gut problems with no known cause. She also had trouble seeing things up close clearly. She had stretch marks on her breasts, hair in the private area, and a curved spine. Her seizures are controlled by two medicines called levetiracetam and levocarnitine. She has not had a seizure in over three years. Heart tests including an ultrasound and electrical recording of the heart have been normal.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient has many health conditions. These include a brain condition called Chiari II where part of the brain sits too low. She has too much fluid in the brain. She is missing the part that connects the two sides of the brain. She has a smaller than normal head size. She has an extra set of tubes in both kidneys that carry urine. She has a curved spine. She was born with an opening in her spine that affects her bladder. Her bladder does not work normally and urine flows backward toward the kidneys. She has a chromosome problem. Part of chromosome 6 has extra copies and another part is missing. The patient also has trouble seeing things up close clearly. She has stretch marks on her breasts, hair in the private area, and a curved spine.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient is a 12-year-old girl with many health conditions and a chromosome problem. She has had many surgeries and check-ups. She was seen by us at 11 years old. She has repeated stomach and gut problems with no known cause. She has trouble seeing things up close clearly. She has stretch marks on her breasts, hair in the private area, and a curved spine. Her seizures are controlled by two medicines called levetiracetam and levocarnitine. She has not had a seizure in over three years. Admitting Diagnosis: N/A
Primary Care Physician: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 12-year-old girl with many health conditions. These include a brain condition called Chiari II where part of the brain sits too low. She also has too much fluid in the brain and is missing a part that connects the two sides of the brain. She has a smaller than normal head size. She has an extra set of tubes in both kidneys that carry urine. She has a curved spine. She was born with an opening in her spine that affects her bladder. Her bladder does work normally and urine flows backward from the bladder toward the kidneys. She was in the newborn intensive care unit soon after birth. She has had many surgeries and check-ups since then. She was seen by us at 11 years old. At that time, we found she had repeated stomach and gut problems with no known cause. She also had trouble seeing things up close clearly. She had stretch marks on her breasts, hair in the private area, and a curved spine. Her seizures are controlled by two medicines called levetiracetam and levocarnitine. She has not had a seizure in over three years. Heart tests including an ultrasound and electrical recording of the heart have been normal.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient has many health conditions. These include a brain condition called Chiari II where part of the brain sits too low. She has too much fluid in the brain. She is missing the part that connects the two sides of the brain. She has a smaller than normal head size. She has an extra set of tubes in both kidneys that carry urine. She has a curved spine. She was born with an opening in her spine that affects her bladder. Her bladder does not work normally and urine flows backward toward the kidneys. She has a chromosome problem. Part of chromosome 6 has extra copies and another part is missing. The patient also has trouble seeing things up close clearly. She has stretch marks on her breasts, hair in the private area, and a curved spine.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient is a 12-year-old girl with many health conditions and a chromosome problem. She has had many surgeries and check-ups. She was seen by us at 11 years old. She has repeated stomach and gut problems with no known cause. She has trouble seeing things up close clearly. She has stretch marks on her breasts, hair in the private area, and a curved spine. Her seizures are controlled by two medicines called levetiracetam and levocarnitine. She has not had a seizure in over three years. Heart tests including an ultrasound and electrical recording of the heart have been normal.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 8 years old
Gender: Male
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Poor feeding, repeated upper respiratory infections, and inability to verbalize due to a wide cleft palate.

HOSPITAL COURSE:
An 8-year-old male child with Seckel syndrome and intrauterine growth retardation was admitted to our outpatient department with complaints of a wide cleft palate. After careful examination and investigation, it was decided to perform pushback palatoplasty under general anesthesia.

GA was administered using inhalational induction with sevoflurane in titrated doses. Anesthesia was maintained with oxygen and air along with titrated doses of sevoflurane and intermittent doses of atracurium. Pushback palatoplasty was done for incomplete cleft palate. The intraoperative period was uneventful, with no complications. The operation took approximately 75 minutes.

Postoperatively, the patient was monitored in the intensive care unit for any apneic spells, respiratory failure, or oral bleed. The postoperative period was uneventful, and the patient's vital signs remained stable. 

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Wide cleft palate in a patient with Seckel syndrome and intrauterine growth retardation.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient was discharged home in stable condition. It is recommended to continue feeding the patient with soft and pureed foods that are easy to swallow. The patient should avoid foods that are hard, crunchy, or require significant chewing. Additionally, patients with Seckel syndrome should be monitored for potential developmental delays and co-morbidities. Follow-up with a pediatrician and a specialist for further evaluation and management is recommended.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient should schedule follow-up appointments with their pediatrician and specialists for further evaluation and management.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
None.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES AND PROCEDURES:
Wide cleft palate and pushback palatoplasty.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 8 years old
Gender: Male
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

MAIN PROBLEM:
Poor feeding, repeated colds and lung infections, and trouble speaking due to a large opening in the roof of the mouth.

WHAT HAPPENED DURING THE HOSPITAL STAY:
An 8-year-old boy with Seckel syndrome and slow growth before birth was seen at our clinic. He had a large opening in the roof of his mouth. After a full checkup and tests, doctors decided to do surgery to close the opening. The patient was put to sleep using a breathing gas called sevoflurane. The patient stayed asleep with oxygen, air, sevoflurane, and a muscle relaxer called atracurium. The surgery closed the opening in the roof of the mouth. The surgery went well with no problems. It took about 75 minutes. After surgery, the patient was watched closely in the intensive care unit. Doctors checked for breathing pauses, breathing problems, or bleeding from the mouth. Recovery went well with no problems. The patient's heart rate, breathing, and other body signs stayed normal.

DIAGNOSIS AT DISCHARGE:
Large opening in the roof of the mouth in a patient with Seckel syndrome and slow growth before birth.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient went home in good condition. The patient should eat soft foods and foods mashed into a smooth paste. These foods are easy to swallow. The patient should not eat hard or crunchy foods. The patient should not eat foods that need a lot of chewing. Children with Seckel syndrome should be watched for slow development and other health problems. The patient should see a children's doctor and a specialist for more checkups and care.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient should make appointments with the children's doctor and specialists for more checkups and care.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
None.

DIAGNOSES AND PROCEDURES AT DISCHARGE:
Large opening in the roof of the mouth and surgery to close it.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 17-year-old male, was admitted to our hospital complaining of left thumb pain following a rugby-tackling accident. On physical examination, diffuse swelling and tenderness over his left thumb CMC joint were noted. Radiographs revealed that the patient had sustained a trapezium body fracture and dorsal dislocation of the CMC joint. Open reduction and internal fixation surgery was performed 5 days after the initial injury. Postoperative immobilization of the thumb spica was maintained for 4 weeks, after which ROM exercises were initiated. The K-wire was removed, and the patient had returned to playing rugby within 3 months of the initial injury. 

Discharge Summary:

The patient, a 17-year-old male, was admitted to our hospital complaining of left thumb pain following a rugby-tackling injury. The patient was diagnosed with a trapezium body fracture and dorsal dislocation of the CMC joint. He underwent open reduction and internal fixation surgery using double-thread headless screws. Postoperative immobilization was maintained for four weeks. ROM exercises were initiated after the removal of the K-wire, and the patient had returned to playing rugby within three months of the initial injury. The patient's final follow-up (one year post-injury) showed full ROM in the CMC and dorsal joints, no pain, or instability, and radiographs showed a congruent CMC joint and trapezium fracture union.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 17-year-old male. He came to our hospital with left thumb pain after a rugby tackling accident. During the physical exam, the doctor found swelling and tenderness around the base of his left thumb. X-rays showed that the patient had a broken bone at the base of his thumb. The X-rays also showed that the thumb joint was pushed out of place. The patient had surgery 5 days after the injury to put the bones back in place and hold them together. After surgery, the thumb was kept still in a cast for 4 weeks. Then the patient started exercises to help move the thumb again. The metal pin was removed, and the patient was able to play rugby again within 3 months of the injury.

Discharge Summary:

The patient is a 17-year-old male. He came to our hospital with left thumb pain after a rugby tackling injury. The patient was diagnosed with a broken bone at the base of his thumb. The thumb joint was also pushed out of place. He had surgery to put the bones back in place. The doctor used special screws to hold the bones together. After surgery, the thumb was kept still in a cast for four weeks. The metal pin was removed, and then the patient started exercises to move his thumb. The patient was able to play rugby again within three months of the injury. At the final checkup one year after the injury, the patient had full movement in his thumb. He had no pain and the thumb felt stable. X-rays showed the joint and broken bone had healed properly.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Patient was a 71-year-old female who presented to our clinic with an abnormal ECG finding during a scheduled appointment. She had a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, essential hypertension, and dyslipidemia. During the consultation, the ECG reading showed premature ventricular contraction at a 1:1 ratio (ventricular bigeminy) with a heart rate of 46/min. Therefore, various blood investigations were ordered to rule out secondary causes that could have led to the changes in the ECG. An urgent appointment was scheduled a week later to review these results. The patient was also advised to seek urgent medical attention if she felt palpitations or giddiness.

During the second consultation, a week later, the repeat ECG showed atrial flutter with a 3:1 atrioventricular block. All the results of the blood investigations were normal, except for an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Urgent transthoracic echocardiography revealed a dilated left atrium and ventricle without any myocardial hypokinetic changes. A large echogenic mass measuring 3.1×4.1 cm was identified at the left atrium. The mass was solid and appeared pedunculated, arising from a stalk at the endocardium. The patient had a left ventricular ejection fraction of 54%.

The patient was immediately referred to a tertiary care cardiology clinic where a second transthoracic echocardiography was performed. An imaging diagnosis of left atrial myxoma was confirmed. The tumor measured approximately 2.9×3.1 cm. A paroxysmal atrial fibrillation pattern was observed on the ECG. Warfarin treatment was initiated while aiming for a target international normalized ratio between 2 and 3. Currently, the patient is jointly cared for by the primary care physician and a multidisciplinary team that is helping to manage the anticoagulation therapy. The patient is unwilling to undergo surgery to remove the tumor.

The patient has been advised to continue with the recommended medical management and to seek medical attention immediately in case of any symptoms suggestive of cardiac compromise. The patient has been provided with educational resources to manage their condition and follow up with the healthcare team.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was a 71-year-old woman who came to our clinic after a heart test showed unusual results during a regular visit. She had a history of type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, and high cholesterol. During the visit, the heart test showed extra heartbeats happening in an unusual pattern with a slow heart rate of 46 beats per minute. Because of this, several blood tests were ordered to find out what might be causing these heart changes. An urgent follow-up visit was set for one week later to go over these results. The patient was also told to get medical help right away if she felt her heart racing or felt dizzy.

During the second visit one week later, a new heart test showed a different irregular heart rhythm with a problem in how signals moved through the heart. Most blood test results were normal, except two tests that showed signs of swelling in the body were higher than normal. An urgent heart ultrasound showed that the left upper and lower chambers of the heart were larger than normal but were still squeezing well. A large solid growth measuring about 3.1 by 4.1 centimeters was found in the left upper chamber of the heart. The growth was attached to the heart wall by a stalk. The heart was pumping out 54 percent of its blood with each beat.

The patient was sent right away to a specialized heart clinic where another heart ultrasound was done. The doctors confirmed the growth was a type of non-cancerous heart tumor called a left atrial myxoma. The tumor measured about 2.9 by 3.1 centimeters. The heart test also showed episodes of an irregular heartbeat. Blood-thinning medicine called Warfarin was started to prevent blood clots with a goal to keep the blood at a certain thinness level between 2 and 3. Right now, the patient is being cared for by both her regular doctor and a team of specialists who are helping manage her blood-thinning treatment. The patient does not want to have surgery to remove the tumor.

The patient has been told to keep following the recommended treatment plan and to get medical help right away if she has any signs that her heart is not working well. The patient has been given learning materials to help manage her condition and to stay in touch with her healthcare team.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name], [Age], [Gender]

Admission date: [Date]
Discharge date: [Date]

Admission diagnosis: Abdominal pain 
Discharge diagnosis: Abscess of urachal remnants

Hospital course: 

The patient presented to the emergency department with severe abdominal pain associated with fever and nausea but without lower urinary tract symptoms. Physical examination revealed tenderness and focal rigidity over the umbilical area. Laboratory data showed leucocytosis with neutrophil predominance, and abdominal imaging confirmed the diagnosis of abscess of urachal remnants. 

The patient underwent medical-surgical treatment, which resulted in no postoperative complications. Pathological examination revealed non-malignant tissue, while a pus culture showed an Escherichia coli infection that was treated with ciprofloxacin. 

The patient remained symptom-free during the hospital course, and on follow-up examination two years later, was asymptomatic with no abnormalities of the abdominal wall.

Instructions at discharge: The patient was instructed on the administration of medications and wound care, as well as when to return for follow-up appointments. The patient was advised to contact the healthcare provider if there were any concerning symptoms or complications. 

Follow-up plan: 
-The patient was advised to return for follow-up appointments at regular intervals to monitor healing and ensure there were no recurrent abscesses.

Signed, [Your name], MD
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name], [Age], [Gender]

Admission date: [Date]
Discharge date: [Date]

Admission diagnosis: Belly pain
Discharge diagnosis: Infected pocket of pus in tissue left over from before birth near the belly button

Hospital course:

The patient came to the emergency room with bad belly pain along with fever and feeling sick to the stomach. The patient did not have problems with peeing. The physical exam showed pain and tightness when touching the belly button area. Blood tests showed a high number of white blood cells, which means the body was fighting an infection. Pictures of the belly confirmed an infected pocket of pus in tissue left over from before birth near the belly button.

The patient had treatment with medicine and surgery. There were no problems after the surgery. Tests on the removed tissue showed it was not cancer. The pus contained a common germ called E. coli. This infection was treated with an antibiotic called ciprofloxacin.

The patient had no symptoms during the rest of the hospital stay. At a check-up two years later, the patient felt fine and the belly area looked normal.

Instructions at discharge: The patient was told how to take medicines and care for the wound. The patient was also told when to come back for check-up visits. The patient was told to call the doctor if there were any worrying symptoms or problems.

Follow-up plan: The patient was told to come back for regular check-up visits. These visits are to make sure healing is going well and the infection does not come back.

Signed, [Your name], MD

input:
Admission Diagnosis:  Stage IV poorly differentiated ovarian carcinoma on palliative carboplatin-based chemotherapy.

Hospital Course Summary:

Admission:
The patient was admitted due to a brief syncopal episode with associated lethargy, nausea, light-headedness and increased watery ileostomal output following chemotherapy one week earlier. Clinical examination including central nervous system was unremarkable, and the patient was haemodynamically stable and afebrile.

Medical History:
The patient has a history of hypertension, multiple pulmonary emboli on enoxaparin and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. In the past, the patient had undergone bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and hysterectomy, and had an ileostomy due to small bowel obstruction with subsequent fistulae formation and bilateral nephrostomy drainage after radiation-induced ureteric obstruction. She had previously experienced recurrent urinary tract infections, which led to colonisation of her nephrostomies with methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), Staphylococcus epidermidis and Candida albicans. The patient had never received anti-fungal prophylaxis.

Initial Assessment:
Laboratory investigations revealed abnormal levels of sodium, creatinine, haemoglobin, white blood cells and lymphocytes. On day 2 of admission, the patient developed fevers of 38.5°C and was empirically commenced on piperacillin-tazobactam and vancomycin. The isolation of Staphylococcus epidermidis from the nephrostomy fluid was considered colonisation and the nephrostomies were changed. Initial septic screen was non-contributory, including negative faecal microscopy.

Diagnostic Assessment:
Persisting fever during her third week of admission prompted assessment for metastatic disease progression with FDG-PET scan. There was no evidence of metastatic malignant disease detected, but an area of abnormal avidity was identified on the aortic valve. Subsequent transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) revealed a small mobile echo-density superior to the aortic valve. Transoesophageal echocardiogram (TOE) revealed a pedunculated mass within the ascending aorta arising from the commissure of the right & non-coronary cusp of the aortic valve with a calcified base. Brain MRI identified a contrast-enhancing right parasagittal frontal lobe lesion consistent with an embolic lesion.

Treatment:
Fungi were isolated from repeat blood cultures on day 22 of admission and subsequently identified as Lomentospora prolificans with high MIC to amphotericin (>32 mg/l) and itraconazole (>32 mg/L), and in-vitro sensitivity to voriconazole (0.5 mg/L). The patient was commenced on therapy with voriconazole (loading dose 600 mg IV 12 hourly). Unfortunately, due to her underlying advanced malignancy, disseminated fungal infection and poor clinical status, the patient was considered unsuitable for surgical management. She was managed with palliative intent.

Outcome:
The patient passed away five days after the fungus was isolated on day 27 of her admission.
clean_ref:
Admission Diagnosis: The patient had advanced ovarian cancer (stage IV) that had spread and was receiving chemotherapy to help with symptoms.

Hospital Course Summary:

Admission:
The patient came to the hospital after fainting briefly. She also felt tired, sick to her stomach, dizzy, and had more watery output from her stoma bag. This happened one week after her last chemotherapy treatment. The physical exam showed nothing unusual. Her blood pressure and heart rate were normal. She had no fever.

Medical History:
The patient has high blood pressure and has had blood clots in her lungs. She takes a blood thinner called enoxaparin. She also has a heart rhythm problem that comes and goes. In the past, the patient had surgery to remove her ovaries, fallopian tubes, and uterus. She has a stoma bag because her small intestine was blocked. This later caused abnormal connections between organs. She also has tubes in both kidneys to drain urine because radiation treatment caused blockages. She has had many bladder infections. These infections left germs in her kidney tubes, including a drug-resistant bacteria called MRSA and a yeast called Candida. She had never received medicine to prevent fungal infections.

Initial Assessment:
Blood tests showed problems with salt levels, kidney function, red blood cells, white blood cells, and infection-fighting cells. On day 2, the patient developed a fever of 38.5 degrees Celsius. Doctors started her on two strong antibiotics called piperacillin-tazobactam and vancomycin. Doctors found bacteria in the kidney tube fluid but thought it was not causing infection. The kidney tubes were replaced. Other tests to find the infection source showed nothing. Stool tests were also negative.

Diagnostic Assessment:
The patient still had fevers during her third week in the hospital. Doctors did a full body scan to check if her cancer had spread. The scan showed no new cancer spread. However, it showed something unusual on her heart valve. A heart ultrasound through the chest showed a small moving object above the heart valve. A more detailed heart ultrasound through the throat showed a growth hanging inside the main blood vessel. It was attached to the heart valve and had a hardened base. A brain scan showed a spot in the right front part of the brain. This spot was likely caused by something traveling through the blood.

Treatment:
On day 22, blood tests grew a fungus. It was identified as Lomentospora prolificans. This fungus did not respond to two common antifungal medicines. It did respond to a medicine called voriconazole. The patient started treatment with voriconazole through an IV. Sadly, the patient was too sick for surgery. She had advanced cancer, a fungal infection throughout her body, and was very weak. The care team focused on keeping her comfortable.

Outcome:
The patient died five days after the fungus was found. This was on day 27 of her hospital stay.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [patient name]
Medical Record Number: [medical record number]

Admission Date: [admission date]
Discharge Date: [discharge date]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 43-year-old female admitted with acute hypoxic respiratory failure, requiring 15 litres per minute of oxygen. Physical exam was significant for wheezing in lungs bilaterally. Electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm. Troponin and electrolytes were in normal range. She had normocytic anemia with hemoglobin 10.8 g/dl, leukocytes 7.8 X 109/L, serum procalcitonin at 0.10 ng/mL. Arterial blood gases showed respiratory acidosis. Chest X-ray showed extensive bilateral lung infiltrates.

The patient was managed empirically for community acquired pneumonia with broad-spectrum antibiotics. On day 3 of hospital stay, her symptoms and oxygen requirement improved. However, she was noted to have hyperkalemia (potassium: 5.8 mmol/L) and elevated CK at 10,000 U/L. She denied any history of myalgias, muscle weakness, trauma, recent exercise, illicit drug use, and statins. An impression of rhabdomyolysis was made and aggressively hydrated. Surprisingly, the CK level increased despite resuscitation, raising diagnostic curiosity.

Detailed inflammatory workup was negative. Her course of hospitalization was complicated by volume overload requiring aggressive diuresis. A detailed discussion with the patient was held explaining enzyme abnormality and risk versus benefits of invasive workup. She refused further investigations including biopsy and imaging. A CK electrophoresis was ordered and the patient was discharged to follow up as an outpatient. Surprisingly, her isoenzyme panel showed CK MM with type 1 macroCK-macroCKemia.

On post-hospital follow up, subsequent normalization of enzyme levels were seen.

Discharge Diagnosis:

1. Acute hypoxic respiratory failure.
2. Community-acquired pneumonia.
3. Rhabdomyolysis.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient should follow up with her primary care physician. She should monitor for any recurrence of symptoms related to acute hypoxic respiratory failure or pneumonia and report them immediately if they do occur.

The patient should maintain her hydration level, follow a healthy diet and a normal lifestyle.

She should avoid strenuous physical activity until medical clearance is obtained. Emergency medical services should be contacted in case of any emergency issues.

The patient should be advised to go through regular lab tests to address any abnormality issues. 

Follow-up plan:

The patient should be followed up in the outpatient clinic within 2 weeks.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [patient name]
Medical Record Number: [medical record number]

Admission Date: [admission date]
Discharge Date: [discharge date]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 43-year-old female. She was admitted because her body was not getting enough oxygen. She needed 15 litres of oxygen per minute. Her lungs had wheezing sounds on both sides. Her heart test showed a normal heartbeat. Her heart enzyme and body salts were normal. Her blood test showed mild low red blood cells at 10.8 g/dl. Her white blood cells were normal at 7.8 X 109/L. Her infection marker was low at 0.10 ng/mL. Her blood gas test showed too much carbon dioxide in her blood. Her chest X-ray showed fluid or infection spread across both lungs.

The patient was treated for a lung infection caught outside the hospital. She was given strong antibiotics that work on many types of germs. On day 3, her breathing got better and she needed less oxygen. However, her potassium level was too high at 5.8 mmol/L. Her muscle enzyme was very high at 10,000 U/L. She said she had no muscle pain, weakness, injury, recent hard exercise, drug use, or cholesterol medicines. The doctors thought her muscles were breaking down. She was given lots of fluids through an IV. The muscle enzyme kept rising even with treatment. This was unexpected and needed more looking into.

Tests for body-wide swelling and immune problems were normal. Her hospital stay became harder because she had too much fluid in her body. She needed strong medicines to remove the extra fluid. The doctors talked with the patient about her unusual test results. They explained the good and bad sides of more testing. She did not want more tests like tissue samples or scans. A special test was ordered to look at the type of muscle enzyme. The patient went home and would get results later. The test showed a rare harmless condition called macroCK-macroCKemia. This means the enzyme clumps together and looks high but is not dangerous.

At her visit after leaving the hospital, her enzyme levels went back to normal.

Discharge Diagnosis:

1. Sudden breathing failure from low oxygen.
2. Lung infection caught outside the hospital.
3. Muscle breakdown.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient should see her regular doctor. She should watch for any return of breathing problems or lung infection signs. She should tell her doctor right away if these happen.

The patient should drink plenty of fluids. She should eat healthy foods and live a normal lifestyle.

She should not do hard exercise until her doctor says it is safe. She should call emergency services if any urgent problems happen.

The patient should get regular blood tests to check for any problems.

Follow-up plan:

The patient should come back to the clinic within 2 weeks.

input:
Discharge Summary:
Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]
Patient Name: [patient name]
Medical Record Number: [medical record number]

Hospital Course:
The patient, an 89-year-old female with a past medical history of vascular dementia, hypertension, and B12 deficiency secondary to pernicious anemia, was admitted with a 3-month history of a left-sided neck lump that had been constantly increasing in size. On physical examination, a large firm, irregular mass was noted in the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe with a background of multinodular goiter. There was no evidence of lymphadenopathy and the trachea was central with no signs of stridor.

The patient underwent a needle core biopsy to increase diagnostic accuracy. TFTs results showed hypothyroidism due to Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Two core biopsies were obtained, which showed a heavy plasma cell infiltrate and admixed B- and T-lymphocytes. The histological findings were consistent with a plasma cell granuloma of the thyroid with underlying Hashimoto's thyroiditis.

Operative intervention was deemed inappropriate due to the patient's frailty and comorbidities. The patient was started on Thyroxine and regularly reviewed in the clinic. TSH levels improved with modification of T4 dosage. The neck lump remained stable for several months until it showed signs of regression. The patient remained asymptomatic with respect to breathing and swallowing.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Plasma cell granuloma of the thyroid with underlying Hashimoto's thyroiditis.

Follow-up Plans:
The patient will continue to be followed-up in the clinic and thyroid function tests will be performed periodically to ensure appropriate TSH levels.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:
Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]
Patient Name: [patient name]
Medical Record Number: [medical record number]

Hospital Course:
The patient is an 89-year-old woman. She has a history of memory problems from poor blood flow to the brain, high blood pressure, and low vitamin B12 levels. The low B12 is caused by a condition where the body cannot absorb this vitamin properly. She came to the hospital because of a lump on the left side of her neck. The lump had been growing bigger over the past 3 months. During the exam, doctors found a large, hard lump with uneven edges in the upper part of her thyroid gland on the left side. The thyroid also had several other smaller lumps. There were no swollen lymph nodes. The windpipe was in the normal position and the patient had no trouble breathing.

The patient had a needle test to take small tissue samples from the lump. Blood tests showed her thyroid was underactive because of a condition where the body attacks its own thyroid gland. Two tissue samples were taken. They showed a buildup of certain white blood cells. The results showed a non-cancerous growth in the thyroid along with the thyroid condition mentioned above.

Surgery was not a safe choice because of the patient's age, weakness, and other health problems. The patient was started on thyroid medicine. She came back to the clinic for regular checkups. Her thyroid hormone levels got better after the doctors changed her medicine dose. The neck lump stayed the same size for several months and then started to shrink. The patient had no problems with breathing or swallowing.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Non-cancerous growth in the thyroid with an underlying condition where the body attacks its own thyroid gland.

Follow-up Plans:
The patient will keep coming back to the clinic for checkups. Blood tests will be done from time to time to make sure her thyroid hormone levels stay normal.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]                                                   

MR#: [REDACTED]                                                           

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]                                     

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]                                       

Admitting Diagnosis: Extensive stage small cell lung cancer

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 43-year-old non-smoking woman, was admitted with a six-month history of deteriorating dyspnea on exertion. The patient underwent various diagnostic tests, including a chest X-ray, chest computed tomography, bronchoscopic washing, biopsies, magnetic resonance imaging, and positron emission tomography-computed tomography, which led to the diagnosis of extensive stage small cell lung cancer. The patient was started on first-line chemotherapy with cisplatin and etoposide, which resulted in a significant improvement of the tumor. However, after six cycles, the patient developed progressive disease, and second-line chemotherapy with carboplatin and irinotecan was initiated. Unfortunately, the patient developed obstructive pneumonia after two cycles, and the regimen was changed. The patient was placed in the refractory relapse group and started on third-line chemotherapy using CAV (Adriamycin, vincristine, and cyclophosphamide), which was maintained for 11 cycles. The patient developed progressive disease after the 11th cycle and the regimen was unable to be continued. The patient was then started on fourth-line therapy with ICE (ifosfamide, carboplatin, and etoposide), but had to discontinue due to intolerable toxicity. Later, an ICE regimen without etoposide was commenced as a fourth-line therapy, resulting in a partial response to therapy. The patient’s pain gradually disappeared during this regimen, and she refused further treatment. Eight months after completing the fifth cycle of carboplatin and ifosfamide, the patient presented to the emergency department for dyspnea and rib pain, and a chest CT showed an increase in the size of her mass. Palliative treatment with a sixth cycle of carboplatin and ifosfamide was initiated, and her symptoms improved. However, the patient expired due to aspiration pneumonia 11 days later. The patient survived for approximately 27 months after beginning chemotherapy.

Discharge Diagnosis: Extensive stage small cell lung cancer

Discharge Medications: [REDACTED]

Summary of Hospitalization:

The patient was admitted with extensive stage small cell lung cancer and treated with various chemotherapy regimens, including cisplatin and etoposide, carboplatin and irinotecan, CAV, and ICE. A partial response to therapy was achieved with ICE without etoposide, which resulted in the reduction of the size and uptake of the tumor. The patient was later admitted to the hospital for palliation of symptoms, and dyspnea and chest pain improved after treatment with a sixth cycle of carboplatin and ifosfamide. However, the patient later died from aspiration pneumonia.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

MR#: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Advanced small cell lung cancer that has spread

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient is a 43-year-old woman who never smoked. The patient was admitted after having trouble breathing during activity for six months. The patient had several tests done to find the cause. These tests included a chest X-ray and CT scan of the chest. The patient also had a scope passed into the lungs to collect samples. Brain scans and full body scans were also done. These tests showed advanced small cell lung cancer that had spread. The patient started the first round of cancer-fighting drugs called cisplatin and etoposide. The tumor got much smaller with this treatment. After six rounds, the cancer started growing again. The patient then started a second set of drugs called carboplatin and irinotecan. After two rounds, the patient got a lung infection caused by a blockage. The treatment plan had to change. The cancer was no longer responding to standard treatments. The patient started a third set of drugs called CAV. This included three medicines: Adriamycin, vincristine, and cyclophosphamide. The patient received 11 rounds of this treatment. After the 11th round, the cancer started growing again. This treatment could not continue. The patient then started a fourth set of drugs called ICE. This included ifosfamide, carboplatin, and etoposide. The patient had to stop because of bad side effects. A new plan using ICE without etoposide was started as the fourth treatment. The cancer partly shrank with this treatment. The patient's pain slowly went away during this time. The patient chose to stop further treatment. Eight months after finishing five rounds of carboplatin and ifosfamide, the patient came to the emergency room. The patient had trouble breathing and rib pain. A chest CT scan showed the tumor had grown larger. Comfort care was started with a sixth round of carboplatin and ifosfamide. The patient's symptoms got better. Sadly, the patient died from a lung infection caused by food or liquid going into the lungs. This happened 11 days later. The patient lived for about 27 months after starting cancer treatment.

Discharge Diagnosis: Advanced small cell lung cancer that has spread

Discharge Medications: [REDACTED]

Summary of Hospitalization:

The patient was admitted with advanced small cell lung cancer that had spread. The patient received several different cancer-fighting drug treatments. These included cisplatin and etoposide, carboplatin and irinotecan, CAV, and ICE. The ICE treatment without etoposide worked partly. The tumor got smaller and was less active. The patient later came back to the hospital for comfort care. Trouble breathing and chest pain got better after a sixth round of carboplatin and ifosfamide. Sadly, the patient later died from a lung infection caused by food or liquid going into the lungs.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Name: [REDACTED]
- Age: 15 years old
- Gender: Female
- Medical History: No previous surgeries

Hospital Course:
- The patient presented with a first episode of medial patellar dislocation of the left knee due to a skiing accident.
- Over a period of 9 months, the patient experienced 8 more spontaneous luxation episodes, which resolved after auto repositioning.
- The patient underwent pre-operative imaging assessment, which included weight-bearing anteroposterior and lateral views of both knees, as well as a magnetic resonance imaging and computed tomography-scan of the left knee.
- Pathological MRI findings revealed substantial hypertrophy of both the medial patellar retinaculum and the tendon of the oblique medial vastus muscle.
- Following the initial conservative treatment with the use of a brace during sports activities and muscular imbalance correction, the patient underwent an open medial retinacular release with complete dissection of the hypertrophic MPFL and a transfer of the vastus medialis oblique (VMO) to the superior border of the patella.
- The post-operative protocol included partial weight bearing for the first 4 weeks, with flexion limited at 45° within the first 2 weeks and at 90° within the first 4 weeks. Recovery to a full weight bearing was permitted 4 weeks after surgery, and range of motion was gradually increased.
- At the 6 and 12-week post-operative follow-up, the patient showed no pain or sensation of instability.
- Eight months after surgery on the left knee, the patient experienced a medial patellar dislocation in the right knee, which was surgically addressed with the same procedure performed on the left knee.
- The patient was reassessed 7 years after the surgical operation on the left knee and 6 years after surgical correction of the right one, and has had no further episodes of dislocation.
- The patient reported being highly satisfied with the procedure, with the only complaint being slight difficulty in squatting, jumping, and running.

Assessment and Plan:
- The patient's left and right knees were treated with an open medial retinacular release with complete dissection of the hypertrophic MPFL and a transfer of the vastus medialis oblique (VMO) to the superior border of the patella.
- The post-operative protocol included partial weight bearing for the first 4 weeks, with flexion limited at 45° within the first 2 weeks and at 90° within the first 4 weeks. Recovery to a full weight bearing was permitted 4 weeks after surgery, and range of motion was gradually increased.
- The patient reported no further episodes of dislocation and was highly satisfied with the procedure. The only complaint was slight difficulty in squatting, jumping, and running.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 15 years old
Gender: Female
Medical History: No previous surgeries

Hospital Course:
The patient had her kneecap slip out of place on the inner side of her left knee for the first time during a skiing accident. Over the next 9 months, the kneecap slipped out 8 more times on its own and went back into place by itself each time. The patient had imaging tests done before surgery. These tests included X-rays of both knees taken while standing, an MRI scan, and a CT scan of the left knee. The MRI showed that the tissue on the inner side of the kneecap and a nearby thigh muscle tendon had grown much thicker than normal. The patient first tried wearing a knee brace during sports and doing exercises to strengthen weak muscles. When this did not work, the patient had surgery. The surgery cut and removed the thickened tissue on the inner side of the kneecap. It also moved a thigh muscle to attach to the top edge of the kneecap. After surgery, the patient could only put part of her weight on the leg for the first 4 weeks. Knee bending was limited to 45 degrees for the first 2 weeks and 90 degrees for the first 4 weeks. The patient could put full weight on the leg after 4 weeks. Knee movement was slowly increased over time. At the 6-week and 12-week checkups after surgery, the patient had no pain and the knee felt stable. Eight months after the left knee surgery, the kneecap in the right knee slipped out of place. The right knee was treated with the same surgery as the left knee. The patient was checked 7 years after the left knee surgery and 6 years after the right knee surgery. There have been no more episodes of the kneecap slipping out of place. The patient was very happy with the surgery. The only issue was some trouble squatting, jumping, and running.

Assessment and Plan:
The left and right knees were both treated with surgery. The surgery cut and removed the thickened tissue on the inner side of the kneecap. It also moved a thigh muscle to attach to the top edge of the kneecap. After surgery, the patient could only put part of her weight on the leg for the first 4 weeks. Knee bending was limited to 45 degrees for the first 2 weeks and 90 degrees for the first 4 weeks. The patient could put full weight on the leg after 4 weeks. Knee movement was slowly increased over time. The patient has had no more episodes of the kneecap slipping out of place. The patient was very happy with the surgery. The only issue was some trouble squatting, jumping, and running.

input:
Hospital Course:
A 41-year-old nonsmoking Caucasian male was admitted to our hospital in August 2011 for the evaluation and management of a locally recurring adulthood sc-rms on the floor of his mouth. The patient had previously undergone surgical resection, multiagent chemotherapy, and radiation therapy, but these treatments were unsuccessful. A total glossectomy with modified radical neck dissection, as well as reconstruction with a latissimus dorsi free flap, were performed, followed by a thorough histological examination. The patient continued to receive close follow-up care, including speech therapy. In September 2012 and May 2013, the patient presented with two more local recurrences, which were resected and histologically analyzed.      
      
Medical History:
The patient had no significant medical history prior to his initial presentation in June 2007 with a painless swelling under his tongue, which was later diagnosed as a myofibrosarcoma via biopsy. Subsequently, he was treated with chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and surgical resection.      
      
Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital in August 2011 for the evaluation and management of a locally recurring adulthood sc-rms on the floor of his mouth. A total glossectomy with modified radical neck dissection, as well as reconstruction with a latissimus dorsi free flap, were performed, followed by a thorough histological examination. The patient continued to receive close follow-up care, including speech therapy. In September 2012 and May 2013, the patient presented with two more local recurrences, which were resected and histologically analyzed.      
      
Disposition:
The patient was discharged from our hospital in May 2013 in stable condition with instructions to continue speech therapy and to undergo close follow-up care. The patient continues to have disease and is being nourished via a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) feeding tube.      
      
Diagnosis:
Adult rhabdomyosarcoma.      
      
Procedures:
Total glossectomy with modified radical neck dissection, and reconstruction with a latissimus dorsi free flap.      
      
Condition/Treatment:
The patient was treated with aggressive multimodal chemotherapy, followed by surgical resection and radiation therapy.      
      
Medications on Admission:
The patient was not taking any medications on admission.      
      
Allergies:
No drug allergies were reported.      
      
Code Status:
Full code.      
      
Hospital Course:
The patient presented with a locally recurring adulthood sc-rms on the floor of his mouth. The tumor had previously been treated with two separate multiagent chemotherapies, but the disease persisted and ultimately spread. Therefore, a total glossectomy with modified radical neck dissection, as well as reconstruction with a latissimus dorsi free flap, were performed to control the disease.      
      
Hospital Course:
The patient underwent surgery and has had several follow-up visits to assess his progress. He remains alive with disease and is being nourished via a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) feeding tube.      
      
Hospital Course:
The patient was discharged with instructions to continue speech therapy and to undergo close follow-up care. The patient continues to have disease and will require further monitoring.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
A 41-year-old white male who does not smoke was admitted to our hospital in August 2011. The patient came in for care of a rare muscle cancer that had come back in the floor of his mouth. This type of cancer is called rhabdomyosarcoma. The patient had already had surgery, several cancer-fighting drugs, and radiation treatment. These treatments did not get rid of the cancer. Doctors removed the entire tongue and some lymph nodes in the neck. They rebuilt the area using muscle tissue taken from the back. The removed tissue was studied carefully under a microscope. The patient kept getting regular checkups and worked with a speech therapist. In September 2012 and May 2013, the cancer came back again in the same area. Each time, doctors removed the growths and studied the tissue.

Medical History:
The patient had no major health problems before June 2007. At that time, the patient noticed painless swelling under the tongue. A small tissue sample showed a type of muscle cancer called myofibrosarcoma. After this, the patient was treated with cancer-fighting drugs, radiation, and surgery.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital in August 2011 for care of a returning muscle cancer in the floor of his mouth. Doctors removed the entire tongue and some lymph nodes in the neck. They rebuilt the area using muscle tissue taken from the back. The removed tissue was studied carefully under a microscope. The patient kept getting regular checkups and worked with a speech therapist. In September 2012 and May 2013, the cancer came back two more times. Each time, doctors removed the growths and studied the tissue.

Disposition:
The patient left our hospital in May 2013 in stable condition. The patient was told to keep working with a speech therapist and to come back for regular checkups. The patient still has cancer. The patient gets nutrition through a feeding tube placed through the belly into the stomach.

Diagnosis:
Muscle cancer in an adult, called rhabdomyosarcoma.

Procedures:
Removal of the entire tongue and some lymph nodes in the neck. Rebuilding of the area using muscle tissue taken from the back.

Condition and Treatment:
The patient was treated with strong cancer-fighting drugs given in different ways. This was followed by surgery to remove the cancer and radiation treatment.

Medications on Admission:
The patient was not taking any medicines when admitted.

Allergies:
The patient has no known drug allergies.

Code Status:
Full code. This means all lifesaving treatments will be used if needed.

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with a returning muscle cancer in the floor of his mouth. The cancer had been treated before with two rounds of cancer-fighting drugs. The disease did not go away and eventually spread. Because of this, doctors removed the entire tongue and some lymph nodes in the neck. They rebuilt the area using muscle tissue from the back. This was done to control the cancer.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery and has come back for several visits to check on healing. The patient is alive but still has cancer. The patient gets nutrition through a feeding tube placed through the belly into the stomach.

Hospital Course:
The patient left the hospital with instructions to keep working with a speech therapist. The patient was also told to come back for regular checkups. The patient still has cancer and will need ongoing monitoring.

The patient kept getting regular checkups and worked with a speech therapist. In September 2012 and May 2013, the cancer came back again in the same area. Each time, doctors removed the growths and studied the tissue.

Medical History:
The patient had no major health problems before June 2007. At that time, the patient noticed painless swelling under the tongue. A small tissue sample showed a type of muscle cancer called myofibrosarcoma. After this, the patient was treated with cancer-fighting drugs, radiation, and surgery.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital in August 2011 for care of a returning muscle cancer in the floor of his mouth. Doctors removed the entire tongue and some lymph nodes in the neck. They rebuilt the area using muscle tissue taken from the back. The removed tissue was studied carefully under a microscope. The patient kept getting regular checkups and worked with a speech therapist. In September 2012 and May 2013, the cancer came back two more times. Each time, doctors removed the growths and studied the tissue.

Disposition:
The patient left our hospital in May 2013 in stable condition. The patient was told to keep working with a speech therapist and to come back for regular checkups. The patient still has cancer. The patient gets nutrition through a feeding tube placed through the belly into the stomach.

Diagnosis:
Muscle cancer in an adult, called rhabdomyosarcoma.

Procedures:
Removal of the entire tongue and some lymph nodes in the neck. Rebuilding of the area using muscle tissue taken from the back.

Condition and Treatment:
The patient was treated with strong cancer-fighting drugs given in different ways. This was followed by surgery to remove the cancer and radiation treatment.

Medications on Admission:
The patient was not taking any medicines when admitted.

Allergies:
The patient has no known drug allergies.

Code Status:
Full code. This means all lifesaving treatments will be used if needed.

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with a returning muscle cancer in the floor of his mouth. The cancer had been treated before with two rounds of cancer-fighting drugs. The disease did not go away and eventually spread. Because of this, doctors removed the entire tongue and some lymph nodes in the neck. They rebuilt the area using muscle tissue from the back. This was done to control the cancer.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery and has come back for several visits to check on healing. The patient is alive but still has cancer. The patient gets nutrition through a feeding tube placed through the belly into the stomach.

Hospital Course:
The patient left the hospital with instructions to keep working with a speech therapist. The patient was also told to come back for regular checkups. The patient still has cancer and will need ongoing monitoring.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name], [Age]-year-old male

Discharge Date: [Date]

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our pediatric neurology clinic for hyperactivity, speech delay, and behavioral problems. A detailed evaluation by the child psychiatrist revealed that the patient was suffering from attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. The patient was initiated on treatment with antipsychotic drugs (risperidone); however, no significant improvement was noted after 6 months. The patient also had a history of recurrent upper respiratory tract infections.

On physical examination, the patient had mildly coarse facial features, slightly depressed nasal bridge with frontal bossing, and stocky hands with short fingers. Neurological examination showed normal tone, power, and deep tendon reflexes with bilateral flexor plantar response. Laboratory investigations showed normal blood counts, chemistry, electrolytes, urine organic acids, tandem mass, plasma lactate, pyruvate, thyroid function tests, and karyotype, and fragile X tests. In light of these findings, the diagnostic possibility of MPS was suspected, but subsequent laboratory examination revealed normal urine glycosaminoglycans (GAGs). Skeletal survey was not suggestive of the characteristic dysostosis complex. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain showed J-shaped sella turcica, mild widening of the lateral ventricles with thinning of the corpus callosum.

Further enzyme analysis for MPS IIIA showed null activity of the enzyme sulfamidase activity in leukocytes, confirming the diagnosis. The patient was initiated on rehabilitation and behavior therapy for hyperactivity.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Mucopolysaccharidosis type IIIA

Discharge Condition:

Stable

Discharge Instructions:

The patient's parents were advised to follow up on the rehabilitation and behavior therapy. They were advised to monitor the patient's language development, behavior, and overall growth and development. The patient's parents were also advised to ensure that the patient received appropriate medical care for recurrent upper respiratory tract infections. Finally, the patient's parents were advised to follow up with the pediatric neurology clinic and seek help if any new symptoms or problems appear.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name], [Age]-year-old male

Discharge Date: [Date]

Hospital Course:

The patient came to our children's brain and nerve clinic for being overly active, slow speech, and behavior problems. A children's mental health doctor did a full check-up. The doctor found that the patient has trouble paying attention and staying still. The patient started taking a medicine called risperidone to help with behavior. After 6 months, the medicine did not help much. The patient also had a history of getting many colds and throat infections.

During the physical exam, the doctor noticed the patient had slightly rough facial features. The patient had a slightly flat nose bridge and a bulging forehead. The patient also had thick hands with short fingers. The brain and nerve exam showed normal muscle strength and reflexes. Many lab tests were done on blood, urine, and other body fluids. These tests came back normal. The doctors thought the patient might have a rare storage disease. But a urine test for this disease came back normal. X-rays of the bones did not show the usual signs of this disease. A scan of the brain showed some differences. These included a curved bone structure near the brain and slightly larger fluid spaces. The tissue connecting the two sides of the brain was thinner than normal.

More testing was done on a specific body chemical. This test showed that an important chemical was not working in the blood cells. This confirmed the diagnosis. The patient started physical therapy and behavior therapy to help with being overly active.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Mucopolysaccharidosis type IIIA, which is a rare disease where the body cannot break down certain sugars properly

Discharge Condition:

Stable

Discharge Instructions:

The patient's parents were told to keep up with physical therapy and behavior therapy. They were told to watch the patient's speech, behavior, and overall growth. The patient's parents were also told to make sure the patient gets proper care for frequent colds and throat infections. Finally, the patient's parents were told to come back to the children's brain and nerve clinic. They should get help right away if any new symptoms or problems come up.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [patient name]
Age: 2 years and 10 months
Gender: Female
Medical Record Number: [patient's medical record number]
Length of Stay: [length of stay]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with complaints of enlarged abdominal volume. Physical examination revealed hepatomegaly and liver steatosis, and laboratory tests showed elevated levels of ALT, AST, cholesterol, HDL, and triglycerides. The patient was diagnosed with a possible glycogen storage disease and underwent a liver biopsy, which showed diffuse enlargement of hepatocyte volume with intense microvesicular steatosis and ballooning, as well as deposits of triglycerides in Kupffer cells and portal macrophages, consistent with Wolman Disease/CESD. Acid lipase activity in leukocytes tested low, consistent with a biochemical diagnosis of LAL deficiency. Electron microscopy revealed massive distension of hepatocytes due to a large amount of round triglyceride deposits, consistent with the previous morphological diagnosis. 

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient is diagnosed with Wolman Disease/CESD.

Consults:
The patient was seen by a hepatologist and a medical geneticist.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient's general health was good, and liver function was preserved at the time of discharge.

Discharge Medications:
None specified in case report.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient's parents were informed of the diagnosis and provided with appropriate genetic counseling. Follow-up care with a hepatologist and medical geneticist was arranged.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [patient name]
Age: 2 years and 10 months
Gender: Female
Medical Record Number: [patient's medical record number]
Length of Stay: [length of stay]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital because her belly was bigger than normal. The doctor's exam showed her liver was larger than it should be. Her liver also had too much fat in it. Blood tests showed high levels of liver enzymes and fats in her blood. The doctors thought she might have a disease that affects how the body stores sugar. The patient had a small piece of liver removed to test it. The test showed her liver cells were very large and full of fat droplets. Fat was also found in special cleaning cells in the liver. These findings matched a condition called Wolman Disease or CESD. Another test checked a certain enzyme in her white blood cells. The enzyme level was low. This confirmed that the patient does not have enough of an enzyme called acid lipase. A special test using a powerful microscope showed her liver cells were stretched out. They were filled with round fat droplets. This matched what the other tests showed.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient has Wolman Disease or CESD. This is a rare condition where the body cannot break down certain fats properly.

Consults:
The patient was seen by a liver specialist and a doctor who studies inherited conditions.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient was in good overall health when she left the hospital. Her liver was still working well.

Discharge Medications:
None listed in the report.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient's parents were told about the diagnosis. They received information about how this condition runs in families. The patient will need to see a liver specialist and a genetics doctor for ongoing care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Sex: Male
Age: 71 years
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:
- Cardiomegaly

Hospital Course:
- The patient was admitted due to cardiomegaly found on chest radiograph and was treated for pneumonia.
- He reported reduced effort tolerance for 2 weeks without chest pain or angina symptoms.
- Clinical examination was unremarkable with non-displaced cardiac apex.
- ECG showed no significant abnormality.
- DSE revealed normal myocardial wall motion, consistent with absence of regional or global myocardial ischaemia.
- Incidental cystic masses were seen adjacent to the right and left atrial free walls on DSE.
- CMR imaging and CTCA demonstrated giant aneurysms with circumferential intramural thrombus of the RCA and LAD arteries, with saccular aneurysms of the proximal LCx artery.
- Multiple dilated venous structures were also noted on CTA.
- The patient was treated medically with an antiplatelet drug and no active management was offered due to the large size of all three CAAs.
- At 1 year follow-up, the patient remained stable, symptom-free, and classified as class I NYHA.

Discharge Diagnosis:
- Coronary artery aneurysms

Hospital Course Summary:
- The patient was admitted for management of cardiomegaly and was treated for pneumonia.
- Imaging tests performed revealed giant coronary artery aneurysms with intramural thrombus, likely related to undiagnosed childhood Kawasaki disease or congenital in origin.
- No active management was offered due to the large size of all three CAAs and the patient was treated with an antiplatelet drug.
- At 1 year follow-up, the patient remained stable and symptom-free.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Sex: Male
Age: 71 years
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:
Enlarged heart

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted because a chest X-ray showed an enlarged heart. He was also treated for a lung infection. He said he had been getting tired more easily for 2 weeks. He did not have chest pain. The physical exam did not show any problems. The heartbeat was in the normal position. A heart tracing test showed no major problems. A stress test with heart pictures showed the heart muscle was moving normally. This meant there was no sign of poor blood flow to the heart. During the stress test, fluid-filled sacs were seen near the upper right and left heart chambers. More detailed heart scans showed very large bulges in the walls of the heart arteries. These bulges had blood clots lining the inside walls. The bulges were found in the right heart artery and the front left heart artery. Smaller bulges were also found in part of another heart artery. The scans also showed some enlarged blood vessels. The patient was given medicine to prevent blood clots from forming. No surgery or procedure was done because the bulges in all three heart arteries were too large. At a check-up one year later, the patient was doing well with no symptoms. His heart function was rated as normal for daily activities.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Bulges in the heart arteries

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted for an enlarged heart and was treated for a lung infection. Heart scans showed very large bulges in the heart arteries with blood clots lining the walls. These bulges may have been caused by an illness in childhood that was never found, or the patient may have been born with them. No surgery or procedure was done because the bulges were too large. The patient was given medicine to prevent blood clots. At a check-up one year later, the patient was doing well with no symptoms.

input:
Patient: 32-year-old Caucasian Scottish female

Admission Date: [Date]

Discharge Date: [Date]

Admission Diagnosis: Dermographism

Summary of Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted for rest and expedited investigations due to reported symptoms of generalized itch and elevated muscle enzymes. Dermatomyositis was suspected after the patient exhibited associated muscle symptoms, and a muscle biopsy and electromyography confirmed inflammatory myositis consistent with dermatomyositis. Treatment with systemic corticosteroids and high-dose human intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy was initiated. 

The patient responded well to treatment, and symptoms and biochemical features started to improve quickly after the first IVIG infusion. After a few months of regular IVIG infusions, the patient's skin and muscle symptoms and signs resolved, and her CK levels reached near normal population values. The IVIG infusion frequency was gradually reduced and then stopped, and the patient was maintained on a low dose of prednisolone during a subsequent pregnancy. 

At discharge, the patient was stable, and her dermatomyositis had resolved. The patient is to follow up with her primary care physician for ongoing management. 

Discharge Diagnosis: Dermatomyositis

Discharge Instructions:

1. Follow up with your primary care physician for ongoing management of your condition.
2. Continue taking any prescribed medications as directed.
3. Avoid excessive exercise and other strenuous activities until cleared by your doctor.
4. Report any new or worsening symptoms to your doctor immediately.
5. Adhere to a healthy diet and lifestyle to help manage your condition.
clean_ref:
Patient: 32-year-old white Scottish female

Admission Date: [Date]

Discharge Date: [Date]

Admission Diagnosis: Dermographism (a skin condition where light scratching causes raised, red lines)

Summary of Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted for rest and faster testing because of itching all over the body and high levels of muscle enzymes in the blood. Doctors thought the patient might have dermatomyositis, a disease that causes skin rashes and weak muscles. The patient had muscle symptoms that supported this idea. A small piece of muscle was removed for testing, and a test to check how the muscles and nerves work was done. Both tests showed the muscles were inflamed, which confirmed dermatomyositis. Treatment was started with steroid pills and high doses of medicine given through a vein to help the immune system.

The patient got better quickly after the first treatment through the vein. Symptoms and blood test results improved soon after. After a few months of regular treatments through the vein, the skin and muscle problems went away. The muscle enzyme levels in the blood dropped to nearly normal. The treatments through the vein were slowly reduced and then stopped. The patient took a low dose of a steroid medicine called prednisolone during a pregnancy that followed.

At discharge, the patient was doing well, and the dermatomyositis had gone away. The patient should see her regular doctor for continued care.

Discharge Diagnosis: Dermatomyositis (a disease causing skin rashes and muscle weakness)

Discharge Instructions:

1. See your regular doctor for continued care of your condition.
2. Keep taking any medicines your doctor prescribed, following the directions given.
3. Do not do hard exercise or tiring activities until your doctor says it is okay.
4. Tell your doctor right away if you have any new symptoms or if symptoms get worse.
5. Eat healthy foods and live a healthy lifestyle to help manage your condition.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE

The patient presented with postauricular mass which was asymptomatic and had been present for 10 years. Preoperative ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic mass suspected to be an epidermal inclusion cyst. However, after surgical removal, the mass was confirmed through permanent section biopsy to be a well-encapsulated schwannoma of the posterior branch of the GAN.

The tumor was completely removed after meticulous dissection, and the nerve was found to be intact. There were no post-operative complications and the patient was discharged after five days.

FOLLOW-UP

The patient showed no evidence of recurrence or neurological deficits during a three year follow-up after the surgery.

DISCHARGE CONDITION

The patient was discharged in stable condition.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS

The patient is advised to follow up regularly with their primary care physician for monitoring and routine check-ups. If there are any signs and symptoms of recurrence or new symptoms, the patient should seek medical attention immediately.

FINAL DIAGNOSIS

Well-encapsulated schwannoma of the posterior branch of the GAN.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE

The patient came in with a lump behind the ear. The lump caused no pain or problems and had been there for 10 years. Before surgery, an ultrasound showed a dark mass that doctors thought was a fluid-filled sac under the skin. After the lump was removed, lab tests showed it was actually a slow-growing tumor on a nerve behind the ear. This type of tumor grows from the covering of the nerve.

The tumor was carefully removed in full. The nerve was not damaged during surgery. The patient had no problems after surgery and went home after five days.

FOLLOW-UP

The patient was checked for three years after surgery. There were no signs the tumor came back. The patient had no nerve problems during this time.

DISCHARGE CONDITION

The patient left the hospital in stable condition.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS

The patient should see their regular doctor for check-ups on a regular basis. If the patient notices any signs the lump may be coming back or has new symptoms, the patient should see a doctor right away.

FINAL DIAGNOSIS

A slow-growing tumor wrapped in a thin cover on a nerve behind the ear.

input:
Hospital Course:

The 68-year-old female patient presented with sudden onset of skin rash, redness, and itching all over her body, along with shortness of breath and a generalized uncomfortable feeling. The symptoms occurred after administering her daily subcutaneous humulin 70/30 insulin injection. The patient was found to have an erythematous, pruritic urticarial rash spread over the body and mild dyspnea, consistent with a type-1 hypersensitivity reaction. The patient was treated with antihistamines and the symptoms were resolved without complications.

Past Medical History:

The patient had a past medical history of T2DM, hypertension, and grade-2 diastolic dysfunction. She was compliant with her prescribed medications until she started developing severe allergic reactions to insulin a week after acquiring the COVID-19 infection.

Course of Treatment:

The patient was diagnosed with COVID-19 infection eight days ago, after which she was self-quarantined in her home. To avoid further allergic reactions, switching to a different insulin formulation was recommended. The patient refused treatment with a different insulin regimen despite extensive counseling. She was prescribed oral antihistamines to be taken before her regular insulin injections, and also an EpiPen (epinephrine injection, USP) 0.3 mg, with the instructions to visit the ER if she develops symptoms again.

Outcome:

The patient completely recovered from the COVID-19 infection, but continued having insulin allergies with her humulin 70/30 insulin injections. Eventually, the patient agreed to switch to different insulin formulations after about two weeks of her initial ER presentation. The new insulin formulations brought the glucose levels down to her usual glycemic range without any further allergic reactions.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The 68-year-old female patient came in with a sudden skin rash, redness, and itching all over her body. She also had trouble breathing and felt unwell overall. These symptoms started after she gave herself her daily insulin shot under the skin. The patient had a red, itchy, hive-like rash all over her body and mild trouble breathing. This was caused by a severe allergic reaction. The patient was given allergy medicine and her symptoms went away without any problems.

Past Medical History:

The patient had type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, and a heart problem where the heart does not relax well between beats. She took all her medicines as directed until she started having bad allergic reactions to insulin. These reactions began about a week after she got COVID-19.

Course of Treatment:

The patient was found to have COVID-19 eight days before this visit. After that, she stayed home to avoid spreading the illness. To prevent more allergic reactions, doctors suggested she try a different type of insulin. The patient did not want to change her insulin even after doctors talked with her about it many times. She was given allergy pills to take before her regular insulin shots. She was also given an EpiPen, which is an emergency shot with 0.3 mg of medicine to stop severe allergic reactions. She was told to go to the emergency room if her symptoms come back.

Outcome:

The patient fully recovered from COVID-19. However, she kept having allergic reactions to her insulin shots. About two weeks after her first emergency room visit, the patient agreed to try different types of insulin. The new insulin brought her blood sugar levels back to her normal range. She did not have any more allergic reactions.
