input:
Hospital Course:

Patient is a 77-year-old female who presented with right L2-3 radiculopathy that was refractory to maximum medical management. This was secondary to foraminal compression at L2-3 and L3-4 due to degenerative disc disease, levoscoliosis, as well as grade 1 spondylolisthesis at both levels.

Treatment:

Given the patient’s prior history of posterior decompressive laminectomy, it was felt that the best approach would be a minimally invasive lateral approach to obtain indirect decompression of the foramina as well as correct her spinal deformity. This will achieve the goals of decompression and fusion as well as deformity correction while avoiding the risk of operating through scar tissue. A retroperitoneal transpsoas approach was used to expose the disc spaces from the patient’s left side.

Complete discectomy was performed with the release of the contralateral annulus fibrosis followed by the interbody fusion. A lordotic PEEK graft was used at each level, 50 mm length x 18 mm width x 9 mm height, packed with demineralized bone matrix (DBM).

Outcome:

Postoperative anteroposterior and lateral x-ray imaging confirmed good position of interbody grafts, correction of scoliosis as well as spondylolisthesis, and restoration of disc height achieving satisfactory foraminal indirect decompression. A routine postoperative x-ray at three months demonstrated lateral cage migration on the left side at the L2-3 level with evidence of arthrodesis in the disc space.

The patient remained asymptomatic and follow-up imaging at one year demonstrated completed fusion without further migration of the interbody graft at L2-3.

Conclusion:

Informed patient consent was obtained prior to treatment and with the patient’s permission, we report on this case of successful minimally invasive lateral approach to obtain indirect decompression of the foramina as well as correct spinal deformity. The patient had significant improvement and remained asymptomatic.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 77-year-old female who came in with nerve pain in her lower back and right leg. The pain did not get better with medicine and other treatments. This was caused by narrowing of the openings where nerves exit the spine at two levels in the lower back. The narrowing happened because of worn-down discs between the bones, a curve in the spine, and bones that had slipped slightly out of place at both levels.

Treatment:

The patient had back surgery before to remove part of the bone to relieve pressure. Because of this, the doctors decided to do surgery through the side of the body instead. This approach would open up space for the nerves and straighten the spine. It would also join the bones together while avoiding cutting through scar tissue from the old surgery. The doctors went in through the left side of the belly area and through a muscle to reach the disc spaces.

The doctors removed the full disc and loosened the tissue on the opposite side. Then they placed a spacer between the bones to hold them together. A plastic-like spacer was used at each level. Each spacer was about 2 inches long, less than 1 inch wide, and about one-third inch tall. The spacers were filled with a bone-growing material.

Outcome:

X-rays taken after surgery from the front and side showed the spacers were in good position. The images also showed the spine curve and slipped bones were fixed. The space between the bones was restored, which gave the nerves more room. An x-ray taken three months later showed one spacer had shifted slightly to the left side. However, there were signs that the bones were starting to grow together.

The patient had no pain or symptoms from this shift. An x-ray taken one year later showed the bones had fully joined together. The spacer did not move any further.

Conclusion:

The patient agreed to the treatment after being told about the risks and benefits. With the patient's permission, we share this case of successful surgery through the side of the body. This approach opened up space for the nerves and straightened the spine. The patient got much better and had no symptoms.

Hospital Course:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]

Chief Complaint: Infertility

History of Present Illness:
The patient was a 37-year-old Korean woman with a history of oligomenorrhea and infertility. She was referred to the Reproductive Medicine and Infertility Clinic at our institution for treatment. The patient had a history of delivering a baby with androgen insensitivity syndrome, who had undergone herniorrhaphy due to bilateral inguinal hernias. She had no growth or developmental abnormalities or any family history of diabetes or cardiovascular disease.

Past Medical History:
The patient had a history of oligomenorrhea and infertility. She had a body mass index of 22.1 and hirsutism with a Ferriman-Gallwey index score of 5.

Physical Examination:
On physical examination, the patient had no growth or developmental abnormalities and no abnormal gynecological symptoms were noted.

Diagnostic Assessment:
The patient underwent a scrotal ultrasonography and an abdomino-pelvic computed tomography scan, which revealed the presence of both testes in both inguinal canals. The uterus, vagina, and ovaries were not identified in the scan. The patient was diagnosed with complete androgen insensitivity syndrome. Genotyping for the AR gene polymorphism was performed, and it was consistent with carrier of AIS. Routine hormone assay revealed elevated antimullerian hormone and increased testosterone and free testosterone concentrations. Transvaginal USG showed polycystic ovary morphology, and the patient was diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome.

Hospital Course:
The patient received in vitro fertilization treatment for preimplantation genetic diagnosis.

Discharge medications:
N/A

Follow-up instructions:
The patient was advised to follow up with her provider for further management and regular check-ups.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]

Chief Complaint: Unable to get pregnant

History of Present Illness:
The patient was a 37-year-old Korean woman who had irregular periods and trouble getting pregnant. She was sent to the fertility clinic at this hospital for treatment. The patient had previously given birth to a baby with a rare condition where the body does not respond normally to male hormones. That child had surgery to repair bulges in the groin area on both sides. The patient had no problems with growth or development. She had no family history of diabetes or heart disease.

Past Medical History:
The patient had a history of irregular periods and trouble getting pregnant. She had a body mass index of 22.1, which is in the normal weight range. She also had extra body hair growth, rated as mild on a standard scale.

Physical Examination:
The physical exam showed no problems with growth or development. No problems with the female reproductive organs were found.

Diagnostic Assessment:
The patient had an ultrasound and a CT scan of the belly and pelvis area. The scans showed that both male sex glands were present in the groin areas on both sides. The womb, birth canal, and egg-producing organs were not seen on the scan. The patient was diagnosed with complete androgen insensitivity syndrome. This is a condition where the body does not respond to male hormones at all. Genetic testing of the hormone receptor gene was done. The results showed the patient carries the gene for this condition. Blood tests showed high levels of a hormone that measures egg supply. Male hormone levels were also higher than normal. An internal ultrasound showed the egg-producing organs had many small fluid-filled sacs. The patient was diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome.

Hospital Course:
The patient received fertility treatment. This included testing embryos for genetic conditions before placing them in the womb.

Discharge medications:
None

Follow-up instructions:
The patient was told to see her doctor for ongoing care and regular check-ups.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 57-year-old woman with a history of high myopia, retinal tear in the left eye treated with focal laser photocoagulation and pneumatic retinopexy and cryotherapy for a retinal tear in the right eye. Past medical history includes osteoarthritis and breast cancer treated with bilateral mastectomy 6 years prior to presentation.

Hospital Course:

The patient was referred to our facility for sudden onset of decreased vision in the right eye, 32 days after a recent procedure. On examination, 2+ anterior chamber and vitreous cells were noted without hypopyon. The patient was started on topical steroid therapy with prednisolone acetate 1 %, which resulted in improvement of inflammation and vision.

However, after 2 weeks, the inflammation worsened, and her vision declined to 2/200. A systemic workup was initiated which was negative for infectious, inflammatory, and vasculitic processes known to cause intermediate and pan-uveitis. Topical steroid therapy was increased, and the patient returned 4 weeks later with no inflammation and 20/30 vision.

Two months later, the patient returned with 2+ cell and flare in the anterior chamber and vitreous cell, again without hypopyon, and a vitreous biopsy was performed, yielding results consistent with intraocular inflammation without evidence of malignancy. Over the next 3 months, despite treatment, there was no resolution of a persistent anterior and intermediate uveitis.

The patient eventually underwent retinal detachment repair with pars plana vitrectomy, air–fluid exchange, 360° of endolaser, and a 12 % C3F8 gas injection, with vitreous biopsy results similar to prior studies. The patient's clinical course progressed to recurrent retinal detachment, requiring repair with scleral buckling and silicone oil tamponade.

Six months after this procedure, the patient was no longer able to perceive light, and given her chronic discomfort, she opted to undergo enucleation of the right eye. Histology of the enucleated globe showed a granulomatous inflammatory membrane, with giant cells, blanketing the angle structures and iris, and forming a pupillary/cyclitic membrane, which was fused to a totally detached, gliotic retina.

Diagnosis:

Granulomatous anterior and intermediate uveitis.

Follow-Up:

The patient's case is an example of the rare and complex nature of this subtype of uveitis, which is often refractory to treatment and can result in severe visual loss. The patient's overall prognosis was poor, and despite multiple interventions, there was no successful resolution of the uveitis. Close monitoring and vigilant follow-up is recommended for patients presenting with similar symptoms.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: The patient is a 57-year-old woman. She has severe nearsightedness. She had a torn retina in her left eye that was fixed with laser treatment and a gas bubble procedure. She also had freezing treatment for a torn retina in her right eye. She has joint pain from wear and tear. She had breast cancer and had both breasts removed 6 years before this visit.

Hospital Course:

The patient was sent to our facility because she suddenly lost vision in her right eye. This happened 32 days after her recent eye procedure. The eye exam showed swelling inside the eye but no pus buildup. The patient started using steroid eye drops called prednisolone acetate 1 percent. This helped reduce the swelling and improved her vision.

After 2 weeks, the swelling got worse. Her vision dropped so she could only see very little. Doctors ran tests to check for infections and other diseases that cause eye swelling. All tests came back normal. The patient used more steroid eye drops. She came back 4 weeks later with no swelling and near normal vision.

Two months later, the patient came back with swelling inside the eye again. There was no pus buildup. Doctors took a fluid sample from inside the eye. The results showed swelling but no cancer. Over the next 3 months, treatment did not stop the ongoing swelling in the front and middle parts of the eye.

The patient then had surgery to fix a detached retina. Doctors removed the gel inside the eye, replaced fluid with air, used laser around the whole eye, and put in a gas bubble. The fluid sample results were the same as before. The retina detached again. The patient needed another surgery with a band around the eye and oil inside the eye to hold the retina in place.

Six months after this surgery, the patient could no longer see any light. She was in constant pain. She chose to have her right eye removed. When doctors looked at the removed eye, they found a sheet of swelling with large cells. This covered the drain area and colored part of the eye. It formed a membrane across the pupil. This was attached to a fully detached and scarred retina.

Diagnosis:

Swelling in the front and middle parts of the eye caused by clumps of immune cells.

Follow-Up:

The patient's case shows how rare and difficult this type of eye swelling can be. It often does not respond to treatment. It can cause severe vision loss. The patient's outlook was not good. Many treatments did not cure the swelling. Patients with similar symptoms need close watching and regular checkups.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 42 years 
Hospital Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Diagnosis: Ischemic Stroke

Hospital Course:

The patient presented to the emergency room with slurred speech, dizziness, and left-sided weakness which started about two hours before presentation. The patient did not report any cough, fever, shortness of breath, loss of smell, or body aches. On examination, she was hemodynamically stable. In neurological examination, she had slurred speech and weakness in the left upper and lower extremity but normal sensations. The rest of the neurological examination as well as chest, cardiovascular, abdominal, and musculoskeletal examination was normal. 

The emergency CT scan did not show any evidence of major territorial infarction, intracranial hemorrhage, or focal mass lesions. The patient was within the window of tissue plasminogen activator (TPA) for acute stroke and was treated with it. The patient was also screened for COVID-19 on admission as per hospital protocol and the result came back later as positive.

The patient was admitted to the ICU for monitoring and did not have any abnormal electrocardiogram (EKG) rhythm while in ICU. She had full recovery of her neurological symptoms within few hours without any bleeding complications. The patient was subsequently discharged in good health and advised home isolation.

Discharge Diagnosis: Ischemic Stroke with COVID-19

Discharge Instructions: 

The patient is advised to follow safe sleep and nutrition practices and to avoid stress. The patient should be advised not to engage in driving or other actions that require quick reflex. The patient should be advised to follow home isolation and avoid close contact with family members or friends. The patient should also be advised to monitor for any symptoms of COVID-19 and seek medical attention if needed.

Follow-up: The patient should be advised to follow up with the primary care provider or neurologist within one week of discharge.


Dictated and signed by: N/A

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 42 years
Hospital Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Diagnosis: Stroke caused by a blocked blood vessel in the brain

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the emergency room with slurred speech, dizziness, and weakness on the left side of her body. These symptoms started about two hours before she arrived. The patient did not have a cough, fever, trouble breathing, loss of smell, or body aches. When checked, her heart rate and blood pressure were stable. During the brain and nerve exam, she had slurred speech and weakness in her left arm and leg. Her sense of touch was normal. All other exams of her chest, heart, belly, and muscles were normal.

A brain scan done right away did not show any major damage, bleeding, or growths in the brain. The patient arrived early enough to receive a clot-busting medicine for her stroke. The patient was also tested for COVID-19 when she arrived, following hospital rules. The test result came back later and showed she had COVID-19.

The patient was moved to the intensive care unit so doctors could watch her closely. Her heart rhythm stayed normal while she was there. Her brain and nerve symptoms went away completely within a few hours. She did not have any bleeding problems. The patient was sent home in good health and told to stay home away from others.

Discharge Diagnosis: Stroke caused by a blocked blood vessel in the brain with COVID-19

Discharge Instructions:

The patient is advised to get good sleep, eat healthy foods, and avoid stress. The patient should not drive or do other things that need quick reactions. The patient should stay home and avoid close contact with family or friends. The patient should watch for any signs of COVID-19 and get medical help if needed.

Follow-up: The patient should see her regular doctor or brain specialist within one week after leaving the hospital.

Hospital Course:
Josh, an 18 year-old male with a history of alcohol and substance abuse, identity problems, and parent-child relational problems, was admitted for 9 weeks to a mental health care institution in 2013 following a suicide attempt. He was diagnosed with dysthymic disorder, personality disorder not otherwise specified, and histrionic, borderline, and narcissistic features. His global assessment of functioning score was 41–50. 

Discharge Summary:
Josh was discharged from the mental health care institution with ongoing support. He continues to experience mood changes and has had limited peer contact but has had some short relationships with other young men. Primary support group problems, social environment problems, and educational difficulties were identified. His parents, who are musicians, have their own psychiatric problems which have affected their involvement in the treatment. 

Follow-up: 
Josh will continue to receive ongoing support and counseling to address his mental health and substance abuse issues. Close monitoring of mood changes will be necessary. Referrals to educational resources and social support groups will be made.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
Josh is an 18 year old male. He has a history of alcohol and drug use problems. He also has struggled with understanding who he is. He has had problems in his relationship with his parents. In 2013, Josh tried to end his own life. After this, he stayed at a mental health care facility for 9 weeks. Doctors found that Josh has long-lasting low mood. He also has a personality disorder that does not fit one specific type. He shows some traits like needing a lot of attention, having unstable emotions, and thinking highly of himself. His ability to handle daily life was rated as serious to moderate difficulty.

Discharge Summary:
Josh left the mental health care facility with continued help in place. He still has mood changes from time to time. He has not spent much time with people his own age. He has had a few short friendships with other young men. Doctors noted problems with his main support system at home. They also noted problems with his social surroundings and school. His parents are musicians. They have their own mental health problems. This has made it hard for them to be part of his treatment.

Follow-up:
Josh will keep getting support and counseling. This will help with his mental health and drug use problems. His mood changes will need to be watched closely. He will be connected to school help and support groups.

Hospital Course:

The 27-year-old Japanese woman was admitted to our institution with a fever, polyarthralgia affecting multiple joints, edema of the lower extremities, disturbance of consciousness, and renal dysfunction. Further evaluation revealed elevated levels of serum creatinine, urea nitrogen, and lactate dehydrogenase, as well as low levels of white blood cell counts, hemoglobin, and platelet count. The sediment analysis of the urine revealed the presence of erythrocytes. Immunological tests confirmed the presence of anti-double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) antibody and antinuclear antibody with low serum C3, C4 and CH50 levels but no antiphospholipid Syndrome. The patient was diagnosed with lupus nephritis (LN) with thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) based on these findings, and treatment was initiated with corticosteroids, plasma exchange using fresh frozen plasma (FFP), and hemodialysis.

Discharge Summary:

The 27-year-old female with a history of LN with TTP was admitted to
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The 27-year-old Japanese woman came to our hospital with a fever, pain in many joints, swelling in the lower legs and feet, confusion, and kidneys that were not working well. Tests showed high levels of creatinine, urea nitrogen, and lactate dehydrogenase in the blood. These are waste products that build up when the kidneys do not work properly. The patient also had low levels of white blood cells, red blood cells, and platelets. A urine test showed red blood cells in the urine. Tests of the immune system found two types of harmful proteins that attack the body. The tests also showed low levels of proteins that help fight infection. The patient did not have a blood clotting disorder called antiphospholipid syndrome. Based on these results, the patient was diagnosed with lupus nephritis and thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura. Lupus nephritis is a disease where the immune system attacks the kidneys. Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura is a blood disorder that causes small clots to form throughout the body. Treatment began with steroid medicines to calm the immune system. The patient also received plasma exchange, which replaces the liquid part of blood with healthy donor plasma. The patient also received dialysis to clean the blood because the kidneys were not working.

Discharge Summary:

The 27-year-old woman with a history of lupus nephritis and thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura was admitted to

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [patient name]

Gender: [patient gender]

Date of Admission: [date of admission]

Reason for Hospitalization:

The patient was admitted at 22 weeks’ gestation to the Emergency Department due to miscarriage. Upon admission, the patient presented shaking chills, fever, stomatitis, sore throat, abdominal pain, and minimal vaginal bleeding for the previous three days.

Hospital Course:

The laboratory findings upon admission showed a low white blood cell count and high C-reactive protein levels. After induction of labour, the patient delivered a stillborn female fetus. The patient was treated with meropenem, followed by piperacillin/tazobactam for 6 days.

Medical History:

The patient had a complex medical history of monoarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis completely resolved at 15 years of age. The patient reported bilateral gonalgia six years before the current hospitalization. Laboratory results showed severe neutropenia, but no ANCA titers. The patient had undergone a bone marrow biopsy, which was reported within normal limits. The patient presented with low neutrophil counts in the past six years but had never contracted opportunistic infections. One year before admission, the patient had delivered a male infant with severe mastitis, followed by two episodes of bilateral knee arthritis.

Postmortem Examination:

The postmortem examination on the nonmacerated female fetus revealed intra-alveolar, gastric, and intestinal neutrophils with oedema, microhemorrhages, and acute tubular necrosis (NTA) of the renal parenchyma. The placenta showed necrotizing AC corresponding to a maternal inflammatory response stage 3/3 and grade 2/2. Funisitis was noted with neutrophilic infiltrate of the umbilical vein and the two arteries with extension to Wharton’s jelly consistent with fetal inflammatory response stage 2/3 and grade 2/2. Initial thrombosis was detected in few spiral arteries of the decidua.

Microbiological Analysis:

Autoptical samples were cultured on different media and identified as K. kingae using MALDI-ToF technology. The microorganism grew in pure culture in all the samples.

Medical Treatment:

The patient was treated with Granulocyte-Colony Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) and cyclosporine (200 mg a day) in addition to hydroxychloroquine due to low neutrophil count and recent intrauterine infection.

Discharge Condition:

The patient fully recovered and was discharged after 10 days.

Follow-Up Plan:

The patient will have regular follow-up appointments with her primary care physician to monitor her medical condition.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [patient name]

Gender: [patient gender]

Date of Admission: [date of admission]

Reason for Hospitalization:

The patient came to the Emergency Room at 22 weeks of pregnancy because of a miscarriage. When the patient arrived, she had shaking chills, fever, mouth sores, sore throat, belly pain, and light vaginal bleeding for the past three days.

Hospital Course:

Blood tests at arrival showed a low count of white blood cells that fight infection. The tests also showed high levels of a protein that signals swelling in the body. After medicine was given to start labor, the patient gave birth to a baby girl who was not alive. The patient was given strong antibiotics called meropenem, then piperacillin/tazobactam for 6 days.

Medical History:

The patient had a history of joint swelling in one joint as a child. This cleared up completely by age 15. Six years before this hospital stay, the patient reported pain in both knees. Blood tests showed a very low count of infection-fighting white blood cells. Tests for certain immune system problems were negative. The patient had a bone marrow test, which came back normal. The patient has had low counts of these white blood cells for six years. However, the patient never got infections that people with weak immune systems often get. One year before this hospital stay, the patient gave birth to a baby boy. After that birth, the patient had a serious breast infection. This was followed by two episodes of swelling in both knees.

Postmortem Examination:

The exam of the baby girl showed infection-fighting cells in the lungs, stomach, and intestines. There was also fluid buildup, small areas of bleeding, and kidney damage. The placenta showed severe infection and swelling from the mother at the highest levels. The umbilical cord also showed signs of infection. Infection-fighting cells were found in the cord vein and two arteries. The infection spread to the jelly-like tissue in the cord. This showed a strong response from the baby at high levels. A few blood vessels in the uterus lining showed early signs of blood clots.

Microbiological Analysis:

Samples taken during the exam were tested and grown in the lab. A germ called K. kingae was found using special testing technology. This same germ grew in all the samples tested.

Medical Treatment:

The patient was given medicine called G-CSF to help make more infection-fighting white blood cells. The patient also took cyclosporine (200 mg a day) and hydroxychloroquine. These medicines were given because of the low white blood cell count and the recent infection during pregnancy.

Discharge Condition:

The patient got better and left the hospital after 10 days.

Follow-Up Plan:

The patient will see her regular doctor for check-ups to watch her health condition.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 75-year-old female with hypertension, presented to the emergency department complaining of odynophagia and neck pain following consumption of chicken porridge. No fever or dyspnea was reported upon admission. Simple radiography suspected esophageal foreign body (FB), which was later confirmed by neck CT. POCUS was utilized to detect the esophageal FB and reveal dilatation with pooling of secretions. The patient underwent urgent esophagogastroduodenoscopy during which the chicken bone was removed after self-vomiting during forceps retrieval. The patient was discharged from the ED two hours post-procedure without complications. 

Impressions:
- Esophageal FB, suspected foreign body ingestion 
- Odynophagia 
- Neck pain 

Procedures:
- Neck CT 
- POCUS 
- Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) 
- Foreign body retrieval 

Follow-Up:
The patient was discharged from the emergency department with no further follow-up required.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 75-year-old woman with high blood pressure. She came to the emergency room with painful swallowing and neck pain after eating chicken porridge. She did not have a fever or trouble breathing when she arrived. A basic X-ray showed there might be an object stuck in her food pipe. A scan of her neck confirmed this. Doctors also used an ultrasound to find the stuck object. The ultrasound showed the food pipe was wider than normal with fluid buildup. The patient had an urgent procedure to look inside her food pipe and stomach using a thin flexible tube with a camera. During the procedure, the patient vomited on her own while doctors were using a tool to grab the chicken bone. This helped remove the bone. The patient left the emergency room two hours after the procedure with no problems.

Impressions:
Object stuck in the food pipe, believed to be swallowed food
Painful swallowing
Neck pain

Procedures:
Neck scan
Ultrasound
Procedure to look inside the food pipe and stomach using a camera
Removal of the stuck object

Follow-Up:
The patient left the emergency room and does not need any follow-up visits.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]

Discharge Date: [Date]

Patient Name: [Name]

Medical Record Number: [Number]

Age: 32

Sex: Female

Date of Birth: [Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Second-stage uterine prolapse

Chief Complaint: Intermittent vaginal blood spotting

Hospital Course: 

The patient was admitted at 28+0 weeks of gestation with complaints of intermittent vaginal blood spotting and diagnosed with second-stage uterine prolapse. Pelvic examination revealed point C as the leading edge according to the pelvic organ prolapse quantification staging system. Sonographic examination revealed a short cervix with 17 mm length and a T-shape. A progesterone capsule was administered vaginally daily.

Two weeks later, at 30+0 weeks of gestation, the patient was admitted again because of regular uterine contractions. She was diagnosed with second-degree uterine prolapse. A more shortened cervix with 10-mm length and a T-shape was noted on sonographic examination. To control preterm uterine contractions, tocolytic therapy was initiated with a favorable outcome. Corticosteroids were also administered. The patient was advised to rest in bed with a suggestion of a slight Trendelenburg position. Vaginal culture revealed mycoplasma infection, which was treated with a single dose of azithromycin 1,000 mg.

At 34+0 weeks of gestation, uterine contractions disappeared without tocolytics, and the patient was discharged. However, she was readmitted at 37+0 weeks of gestation due to regular uterine contractions. The cervix was still prolapsed at the same level and the cervical orifice was opened to about 3 cm width. After a latency period, a healthy male neonate was delivered with an estimated blood loss of about 400 mL, which was not different from that of a normal pregnancy. Apgar scores were 10 at 1 minute and 10 at 5 minutes. 

The prolapsed uterus was manually reduced, and the postnatal period was uneventful. Two days after delivery, the patient was discharged in good health with complete resolution of the cervical prolapse. Follow-up examination 1 and 6 months postpartum revealed no evidence of uterine prolapse.

Discharge Diagnosis: Second-stage uterine prolapse

Condition at Discharge: Good

Discharge Instructions:

- Follow up with obstetrician as recommended for postpartum care.
- Avoid heavy lifting for at least 6 weeks.
- Resume normal activities gradually as tolerated.
- Review vaginal hygiene practices with a healthcare provider.
- Seek medical attention immediately if vaginal bleeding or abnormal discharge occurs.
- Take prescribed medications as directed.
- Follow a healthy diet and maintain a regular exercise routine.

Primary Care Provider: [Name]

Follow-Up Appointment: [Date and Time]

Follow-Up Phone Number: [Number]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]

Discharge Date: [Date]

Patient Name: [Name]

Medical Record Number: [Number]

Age: 32

Sex: Female

Date of Birth: [Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Uterus slipping down into the vagina (second stage)

Chief Complaint: Occasional vaginal bleeding

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital at 28 weeks of pregnancy. She had occasional vaginal bleeding. Doctors found that her uterus had slipped down into the vagina (second stage). An exam showed the lowest part of the uterus had dropped down. An ultrasound showed her cervix was short at 17 mm and shaped like a T. She was given a hormone capsule to place in the vagina each day.

Two weeks later, at 30 weeks of pregnancy, the patient came back. She was having regular tightening of the uterus. Her cervix was now even shorter at 10 mm. Doctors gave her medicine to stop early labor, and it worked well. She also received medicine to help the baby's lungs develop faster. The patient was told to stay in bed with her feet slightly higher than her head. A test found a vaginal infection. It was treated with a single dose of azithromycin 1,000 mg.

At 34 weeks of pregnancy, the tightening stopped without medicine. The patient went home. She came back at 37 weeks because the tightening started again. Her cervix was still slipping down and had opened to about 3 cm. After a waiting period, she gave birth to a healthy baby boy. She lost about 400 mL of blood, which is normal for childbirth. The baby scored 10 out of 10 on health tests at 1 minute and 5 minutes after birth.

The doctor gently pushed the uterus back into place by hand. Recovery after birth went smoothly. Two days after giving birth, the patient went home in good health. Her cervix was no longer slipping down. Checkups at 1 month and 6 months after birth showed no signs of the uterus slipping down.

Discharge Diagnosis: Uterus slipping down into the vagina (second stage)

Condition at Discharge: Good

Discharge Instructions:

Follow up with the pregnancy doctor as recommended for care after birth.
Do not lift heavy things for at least 6 weeks.
Slowly return to normal activities as the patient feels able.
Talk to a healthcare provider about keeping the vaginal area clean.
Get medical help right away if vaginal bleeding or unusual discharge happens.
Take all prescribed medicines as directed.
Eat healthy foods and exercise regularly.

Primary Care Provider: [Name]

Follow-Up Appointment: [Date and Time]

Follow-Up Phone Number: [Number]

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY 

Patient: 44-year-old woman 

Admission Date: [Date] Discharge Date: [Date] 

Diagnosis: Dilated common bile duct, cholecystectomy history, and splenic heterogeneous subcapsular hematoma 

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to the hospital with intractable nausea and vomiting associated with severe epigastric pain. Physical examination was only notable for mild epigastric tenderness. Laboratory tests showed mildly elevated liver enzymes, and a right upper quadrant ultrasound showed dilation of the common bile duct, which was later confirmed on magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography as well as a 7-mm common bile duct stone. The patient underwent ERCP with sphincterotomy, which demonstrated a dilated common bile duct with no obvious cause. She subsequently experienced severe epigastric and left upper quadrant abdominal pain with hypotension after the procedure. Repeat laboratory testing at the time showed elevated lipase as well as a decline in hemoglobin. A CT scan of the abdomen illustrated a large splenic heterogeneous subcapsular hematoma and peripancreatic stranding. The patient was transferred to the intensive care unit and managed conservatively with fluids and blood transfusions. The hematoma regressed, and her hemoglobin remained stable. With clinical improvement, she was discharged home. 

Follow-up Care: The patient was advised to follow up with her primary care provider for further management and was instructed to return to the emergency department if she experiences any further symptoms. 

Discharge Medications: N/A 

Discharge Instructions: 

1. Rest and avoid strenuous activities 
2. Keep the incision areas clean and dry 
3. Take prescribed medications as directed by your healthcare provider
4. Follow up with your primary care provider for further management of your condition 
5. Return to the emergency department if you experience any further symptoms. 

Discharge Disposition: Home 

Attending Physician: [Physician's Name] 

Dictated By: [Physician's Name] 

Keywords: Dilated common bile duct, cholecystectomy history, splenic heterogeneous subcapsular hematoma.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: 44-year-old woman

Admission Date: [Date] Discharge Date: [Date]

Diagnosis: Widened bile duct, past gallbladder removal surgery, and blood clot under the spleen surface

Hospital Course: The patient came to the hospital with severe nausea and vomiting that would not stop. She also had bad pain in the upper belly area. The physical exam showed only mild tenderness in the upper belly. Blood tests showed slightly high liver levels. An ultrasound of the upper right belly showed the bile duct was wider than normal. A special scan confirmed this and also found a small stone in the bile duct. The patient had a procedure using a thin tube through the mouth to look at the bile duct. During this, a small cut was made to help the stone pass. The test showed a wide bile duct but no clear reason why. After the procedure, the patient had severe pain in the upper belly and left side. Her blood pressure also dropped. New blood tests showed high levels of a pancreas enzyme and lower red blood cell counts. A scan of the belly showed a large blood clot forming under the spleen surface. It also showed swelling around the pancreas. The patient was moved to the intensive care unit for close watching. She was treated with fluids given through a vein and blood transfusions. The blood clot got smaller and her blood counts stayed stable. As she got better, she was sent home.

Follow-up Care: The patient was told to see her regular doctor for ongoing care. She was also told to go to the emergency room if she has any new symptoms.

Discharge Medications: None

Discharge Instructions:

1. Rest and do not do hard physical activities
2. Keep the procedure areas clean and dry
3. Take any medicines as told by your doctor
4. See your regular doctor for ongoing care of your condition
5. Go to the emergency room if you have any new symptoms.

Discharge Disposition: Home

Attending Physician: [Physician's Name]

Dictated By: [Physician's Name]

Keywords: Widened bile duct, past gallbladder removal surgery, blood clot under the spleen surface.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name]
Date of Admission: [Admission Date]
Date of Discharge: [Discharge Date]
Admitting Diagnosis: AKI, Lower Extremity Edema, Resting Dyspnea, Afebrile, Jaundice, Ulcero-Necrotic Lesions

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 58-year-old male, was admitted with complaints of oliguria, lower extremity edema, and resting dyspnea. The physical examination showed an afebrile, jaundiced patient with ulcero-necrotic lesions on the inner aspect of the thighs. Elevated creatinine and urea levels confirmed AKI, and transaminases with conjugated bilirubin were moderately elevated. The patient's lab results showed elevated IgM and decreased complement (C3/C4). Rheumatoid factor, cryoglobulins, and serum viscosity were increased, while antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies and antinuclear antibodies were negative.

Abdominal ultrasound revealed no calcifications in the kidneys, which were of normal size. Hepatic ultrasound revealed two hypoechogenic masses with absent Doppler signs, located in the right and left hepatic lobes.

A suspected hematologic neoplasia with monoclonal gammopathy was suspected as the cause of the patient's neuropathy and cutaneous vasculitis. The patient's renal function and cutaneous lesions improved under therapy with loop diuretics and high-dose glucocorticoids.

Histopathologic and immunohistochemical findings from the hepatic biopsy done on day three indicated a primary hepatic MALT lymphoma in the absence of lymphoproliferative disease outside the liver.

The patient was referred to oncology and began the R-CHOP regimen for treatment. Three months after discharge, the patient was re-admitted with fulminant hepatitis that was ultimately fatal. The viral load of HBV and hepatitis D virus was high despite negative serology for the surface antigen.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient is advised to seek medical attention in case of appearance of new or worsening jaundice, fever, abdominal pain, or other concerning symptoms. The patient should avoid consuming alcohol, taking acetaminophen-containing products, or taking any medications without consulting their physician. The patient's medical records and biopsy results should be shared with any future healthcare providers.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name]
Date of Admission: [Admission Date]
Date of Discharge: [Discharge Date]
Admitting Diagnosis: Sudden kidney problems, leg swelling, trouble breathing at rest, no fever, yellow skin, and open sores with dead tissue

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 58-year-old male, came to the hospital because he was not making much urine, had swollen legs, and had trouble breathing while resting. The exam showed the patient had no fever but had yellow skin and open sores with dead tissue on the inner thighs. Blood tests showed the kidneys were not working well. Liver tests were also higher than normal. The patient's blood showed signs that the immune system was not working right. Some antibody tests came back high, while others were normal.

An ultrasound of the belly showed the kidneys looked normal in size with no calcium deposits. The liver ultrasound showed two dark spots in both sides of the liver with no blood flow.

Doctors thought a blood cancer with abnormal proteins might be causing the nerve problems and blood vessel damage in the skin. The patient's kidney function and skin sores got better with water pills and high-dose steroids.

A sample of liver tissue taken on day three showed a type of cancer called MALT lymphoma that started in the liver. There were no signs of this cancer in other parts of the body.

The patient was sent to a cancer doctor and started a treatment plan called R-CHOP. Three months after leaving the hospital, the patient came back with severe liver failure that led to death. Tests showed high levels of hepatitis B and hepatitis D viruses even though earlier blood tests did not show signs of infection.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient should get medical help right away if there is new or worse yellow skin, fever, belly pain, or other worrying signs. The patient should not drink alcohol, take products with acetaminophen, or take any medicines without asking a doctor first. The patient's medical records and tissue test results should be shared with any future doctors or nurses.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: Anonymous

Medical Record Number: XXXXXX

Date of Admission: XXXXXXX           
Date of Discharge: XXXXXXX

Admitting Diagnosis: Incomplete spinal cord injury 

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the Spinal Cord Injury Center at Balgrist University Hospital, Switzerland, with an incomplete spinal cord injury. Clinical examination revealed the patient was suffering from central cord syndrome, at-level neuropathic pain, and mechanical allodynia (brush allodynia) on both forearms. Light touch was normal within the cervical segments, and sharp dull discrimination was preserved while the intensity of the pinprick stimulus was only mildly attenuated. The upper extremity motor score (left: 11/25, right: 11/25) showed a profound weakness of elbow extensors, and no voluntary muscle activity in the finger flexors and abductors bilaterally. The lower extremity motor score was normal (left: 25/25, right: 25/25). Biceps and brachioradialis reflexes, as well as knee-jerk and ankle-jerk reflexes were bilaterally exaggerated. The Babinski sign was negative. Muscle tone was normal without signs of spasticity. Coordination and gait showed discrete signs of ataxia.

Diagnostic testing revealed a multi-segmental (C5-C7), longitudinal “snake eye” appearance myelopathy. Ulnar somatosensory evoked potentials (SEPs) and sensory nerve conduction studies were normal. Noxious mechanical stimuli were applied with a 256 mN pinprick stimulator, and CHEPs were recorded, which appeared to be normal at C4 and T4 with high pain ratings to the heat stimulation, but almost complete loss of painful heat sensation and absent CHEPs at the level of C6.

The patient was treated for the duration of their stay in the hospital. Written informed consent was obtained from the patient before conducting any study, and the study was approved by the “Kantonale Ethikkommission Zürich”.

Discharge Status:

The patient was discharged from the hospital in stable condition. The patient’s condition and prognosis were discussed with them and their families, and the necessary education and instructions regarding self-care, follow-up care, and rehabilitation were provided. The patient and their families were advised to seek immediate medical attention if there were any signs of deterioration in their condition.

Follow-Up:

The patient was instructed to follow up with their primary care physician or specialist for ongoing management of their condition. Any changes in the patient's condition should be reported immediately to their healthcare provider.

Signed,

Dr. Anonymous
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: Anonymous

Medical Record Number: XXXXXX

Date of Admission: XXXXXXX
Date of Discharge: XXXXXXX

Admitting Diagnosis: Partial spinal cord injury

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the Spinal Cord Injury Center at Balgrist University Hospital, Switzerland, with a partial spinal cord injury. The medical exam showed the patient had central cord syndrome. This means the middle part of the spinal cord was damaged. The patient also had nerve pain at the injury site. Both forearms felt pain from light touch that should not hurt. Light touch feeling was normal in the neck area. The patient could tell the difference between sharp and dull touch. However, sharp pokes felt slightly less intense than normal. Arm strength tests showed major weakness in muscles that straighten the elbows. The patient could not move the finger muscles on either hand. Leg strength was normal on both sides. Reflexes in the arms and legs were stronger than normal on both sides. The toe reflex test was normal. Muscle stiffness was normal with no signs of tight muscles. Balance and walking showed mild problems with coordination.

Tests showed damage to multiple levels of the spinal cord from C5 to C7. The damage had a pattern doctors call snake eye appearance. Nerve tests on the arm and hand were normal. Doctors applied painful pressure using a special tool. They also tested responses to heat pain. These tests were normal at the C4 and T4 levels. The patient felt strong pain from the heat at those levels. However, at the C6 level, the patient could barely feel painful heat. The brain wave response to heat was also missing at C6.

The patient received treatment during their hospital stay. The patient gave written permission before any study was done. The local ethics board in Zurich approved the study.

Discharge Status:

The patient left the hospital in stable condition. Doctors talked with the patient and their families about the condition and what to expect. The patient and their families received teaching about self-care, follow-up care, and rehabilitation. They were told to get medical help right away if the condition gets worse.

Follow-Up:

The patient was told to schedule a visit with their regular doctor or specialist. This is needed for ongoing care of their condition. Any changes in the patient's condition should be reported right away to their healthcare provider.

Signed,

Dr. Anonymous

The toe reflex test was normal. Muscle stiffness was normal with no signs of tight muscles. Balance and walking showed mild problems with coordination.

Tests showed damage to multiple levels of the spinal cord from C5 to C7. The damage had a pattern doctors call snake eye appearance. Nerve tests on the arm and hand were normal. Doctors applied painful pressure using a special tool. They also tested responses to heat pain. These tests were normal at the C4 and T4 levels. The patient felt strong pain from the heat at those levels. However, at the C6 level, the patient could barely feel painful heat. The brain wave response to heat was also missing at C6.

The patient received treatment during their hospital stay. The patient gave written permission before any study was done. The local ethics board in Zurich approved the study.

Discharge Status:

The patient left the hospital in stable condition. Doctors talked with the patient and their families about the condition and what to expect. The patient and their families received teaching about self-care, follow-up care, and rehabilitation. They were told to get medical help right away if the condition gets worse.

Follow-Up:

The patient was told to schedule a visit with their regular doctor or specialist. This is needed for ongoing care of their condition. Any changes in the patient's condition should be reported right away to their healthcare provider.

Signed,

Dr. Anonymous

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 64-year-old male
Medical History: Postthoracotomy pain syndrome

Presenting Symptoms:
- Severe pain (9/10 on NRS) over the anterior and posterior right chest area
- Burning, tingling, and current-like sensation around the thoracotomy scar at the T8-T9 level
- Despite high dosage of oral analgesics, significant pain persisted

Treatment:
- Right ESP block performed with 20 mL of 0.375% ropivacaine and 40 mg of triamcinolone at the T8 level
- Pain immediately relieved (0 on 10 on NRS) with sensory blockade from T5 to T12 on the right side
- NRS score monitored for 24 hours
- Discharged with tapered oral medications

Outcomes:
- Patient remained pain-free for 1.5 months without consumption of any other oral analgesic
- A second ESP block performed with 20 mL of 0.25% ropivacaine and 40 mg triamcinolone, which produced significant analgesia lasting 2 weeks
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 64-year-old male
Medical History: Long-term pain after chest surgery

Presenting Symptoms:
The patient had very bad pain, rated 9 out of 10, in the front and back of the right chest area. The patient felt burning, tingling, and electric-like feelings around the surgery scar between the 8th and 9th ribs. The pain stayed bad even with high doses of pain pills taken by mouth.

Treatment:
The patient received a nerve block injection on the right side of the back. The doctor injected 20 mL of numbing medicine called ropivacaine and 40 mg of a steroid called triamcinolone near the 8th rib. The pain went away right away, dropping to 0 out of 10. The numbing spread from the 5th to 12th rib area on the right side. The care team checked the pain level for 24 hours. The patient went home with a slow reduction in pain pills.

Outcomes:
The patient had no pain for 1.5 months and did not need any pain pills by mouth. The patient received a second nerve block injection with 20 mL of a weaker numbing medicine and 40 mg of steroid. This second injection gave good pain relief that lasted 2 weeks.

input:
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Patient: 47-year-old female

Admission Date: May 2018
Discharge Date: N/A

TREATMENT:
- Crizotinib therapy initiated on June 1st, 2018.
- Alectinib therapy initiated on August 10, 2018.
- Ceritinib therapy initiated for targeted therapy on January 10th, 2020.
- Anlotinib therapy initiated on June 8th, 2020.
- Abusartan combined with carboplatin chemotherapy received five cycles from September 9th, 2020, up to December 31, 2020.

CURRENT CONDITION:
- Stable disease (assessment as per CT results) as of December 31st, 2020.
- Patient continued taking alectinib for maintenance treatment.

MEDICAL HISTORY:
- Intermittent dry cough with back pain without obvious inducement since May 2018.
- Never-smoker.
- Chest CT showed a mass (about 42 × 55 mm) in the patient's left lower lobe, accompanied by liver, scapula, and right frontal lobe metastases.
- Pathological examination suggested that the patient's liver metastasis was a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma.
- EML4-ALK fusion detected by mutational analysis using an amplification refractory mutation system-PCR.
- ALT was 1079 U/L (reference range 5–40 U/L), and CK-MB was 250 U/L (reference range <24 U/L) on July 26th, 2018.
- Tumor recurrence in the left hilum and lower left lung was found in the chest CT scan on May 19th, 2020.
- Poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma discovered through biopsy pathology.
- Fusion of EML4 Exon 13 and ALK Exon 20 found in both squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma samples through Nanodigmbio(Nanjing) Biotechnology Co., Ltd's NGS-based assay for detecting cancer-relevant genes.
- PD-L1 (Ventana Medical Systems, Inc.) TPS was 80% and 85% in both specimens' tumors as determined with the SP263 assay.

FOLLOW-UP RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Patient has received comprehensive hospital treatment, and follow-up recommendations will be discussed in further consultations.
clean_ref:
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Patient: 47-year-old female

Admission Date: May 2018
Discharge Date: N/A

TREATMENT:
Crizotinib treatment started on June 1st, 2018.
Alectinib treatment started on August 10, 2018.
Ceritinib treatment started as a targeted cancer therapy on January 10th, 2020.
Anlotinib treatment started on June 8th, 2020.
The patient received five rounds of Abusartan combined with carboplatin cancer-killing drug treatment from September 9th, 2020, up to December 31, 2020.

CURRENT CONDITION:
The cancer was stable based on CT scan results as of December 31st, 2020.
The patient continued taking alectinib to help keep the cancer under control.

MEDICAL HISTORY:
The patient had an on-and-off dry cough with back pain for no clear reason since May 2018.
The patient never smoked.
A chest CT scan showed a growth about 42 by 55 mm in the lower part of the left lung. The cancer had also spread to the liver, shoulder blade, and right front part of the brain.
Lab tests on tissue from the liver showed a fast-growing cancer that started in gland cells.
Gene testing found an abnormal joining of two genes called EML4 and ALK.
On July 26th, 2018, the liver enzyme ALT level was 1079 U/L, which is much higher than the normal range of 5 to 40 U/L. The heart enzyme CK-MB level was 250 U/L, which is much higher than the normal level of less than 24 U/L.
A chest CT scan on May 19th, 2020, found the cancer had come back near the center of the left lung and in the lower left lung.
A tissue sample test found a fast-growing cancer that started in flat cells lining the lung.
Advanced gene testing found the same abnormal joining of EML4 and ALK genes in both cancer types.
A test showed that 80 percent and 85 percent of the cancer cells in both samples had a protein called PD-L1, which can affect how the body fights cancer.

FOLLOW-UP RECOMMENDATIONS:
The patient has received full hospital treatment. Next steps for care will be talked about at future doctor visits.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient: 63-year-old male with a history of hepatitis B-associated liver cirrhosis

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the emergency room due to upper gastrointestinal bleeding caused by a cardiac varix (gastro-esophageal varices-2). Hemostasis was achieved by the injection of N-butyl-2-cyanoacrylate, and the patient was discharged without any complications.

The patient re-admitted a month later because of fever and myalgia, and Viridans streptococcus was found in the blood cultures. It was suspected that the infection was caused by the N-butyl-2-cyanoacrylate plug used for bleeding control during the previous admission. Antibiotics were given for four weeks, and there were no signs of relapse. Follow-up imaging showed a marked decrease in FDG uptake around the N-butyl-2-cyanoacrylate plug.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in a stable condition with no fever or sign of infection.

Recommendations/Follow-up Care:
The patient is advised to continue taking medications as prescribed and to maintain regular follow-up appointments with his primary care physician to monitor his medical history and current health condition. 

Signed: [Physician Name]
Date: [DATE]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient: 63-year-old male with a history of liver scarring caused by hepatitis B infection

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room because of bleeding in the upper part of the digestive system. The bleeding came from swollen blood vessels near the stomach and food pipe. Doctors stopped the bleeding by injecting a special medical glue. The patient went home without any problems.

The patient came back to the hospital a month later with fever and muscle pain. Blood tests showed a type of bacteria called Viridans streptococcus. Doctors believed the infection came from the medical glue used to stop the bleeding during the first visit. The patient received antibiotics for four weeks. The infection did not come back. Follow-up scans showed much less swelling and activity around the medical glue.

Discharge Condition:
The patient went home in stable condition with no fever or signs of infection.

Recommendations and Follow-up Care:
The patient should keep taking all medicines as directed. The patient should keep all scheduled visits with his regular doctor. These visits will help check on his health history and current condition.

Signed: [Physician Name]
Date: [DATE]

Patient Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Not disclosed
Date of admission: Not disclosed
Date of discharge: Not disclosed

Admission Diagnoses: Hypertension, High cholesterol, Diabetes mellitus, ACS (non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction)

Medical History: The patient had a medical history of hypertension, high cholesterol, and diabetes mellitus.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted to the Coronary Care Unit because of sudden onset of chest pain at rest. ECG abnormalities associated with an increase of cardiac enzymes (hs troponin 70 pg/ml and CK-MB 8 ng/ml) were suggestive of an ACS (non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction). Echocardiogram showed left ventricular hypertrophy, end-diastolic diameter, and mild hypokinesia of the basal segment of the inferior wall with normal systolic function. In the parasternal short axis view, there was an abnormal diastolic flow inside the ventricular inferior wall. This flow was directed from the basal segment of the inferior wall into the left ventricular cavity. In the apical two-chamber view, there was the entire intramural course under the mitral valve posterior leaflet, where it was thrown into the ventricular cavity during diastole.

Treatment: According to ESC guidelines, the GRACE (Global Registry of Acute Coronary Events) risk score of the patient was 146 and early invasive strategy of myocardial revascularization (within the first 24 hours from admission) was indicated. The patient underwent coronary angiography, which revealed 90% stenosis in the middle portion and 70% stenosis in the distal portion of the left anterior descending artery, a double 70% stenosis in the proximal and the middle portion of the circumflex artery and the right coronary artery, dominant, with stenosis of 50% at the end of the proximal portion. Angiography confirmed the presence of a tortuous voluminous coronary fistula: it originated from the interventricular posterior artery and coursed along the posterior interventricular sulcus from the apex to the base of the left ventricle. The patient underwent percutaneous coronary angioplasty with the placement of two drug-eluting stents in the left anterior descending artery and a single drug-eluting stent in the circumflex artery.

Follow up: To complete evaluation, cardiac CT was performed to detect coronary fistula anatomy, its relationships with cardiac structures and its course. CT images documented that coronary fistula originated from the distal portion of the right coronary artery, ran into the left ventricular inferior wall, and drained into the left ventricular chamber under the mitral valve, with a final tract with an intramyocardial course. The patient was asymptomatic at 24-month follow-up.

Disposition: The patient was discharged in good condition.
clean_ref:
Patient Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Not disclosed
Date of admission: Not disclosed
Date of discharge: Not disclosed

Admission Diagnoses: High blood pressure, High cholesterol, Diabetes, Heart attack (a type that does not show major changes on heart rhythm tests)

Medical History: The patient had a history of high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and diabetes.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted to the heart care unit because of sudden chest pain while resting. Heart rhythm tests and blood tests showed signs of a heart attack. An ultrasound of the heart showed the heart wall was thicker than normal. It also showed part of the lower heart wall was not moving as well as it should. The heart was still pumping normally overall. The ultrasound found an unusual blood flow pattern inside the lower heart wall. This blood flow was moving from the heart wall into the main pumping chamber. The flow path ran under one of the heart valves.

Treatment: Based on medical guidelines, the patient had a high risk score for heart problems. Doctors recommended a procedure to open blocked heart arteries within 24 hours. The patient had a test where dye was put into the heart arteries to look for blockages. This test showed several arteries were narrowed or blocked. The main artery on the front of the heart was 90 percent blocked in one spot and 70 percent blocked in another. Other arteries had 50 to 70 percent blockages. The test also showed an unusual extra blood vessel. This vessel connected a heart artery to the inside of the heart pumping chamber. The patient had a procedure to open the blocked arteries. Doctors placed two small mesh tubes in the front artery and one mesh tube in another artery. These tubes are coated with medicine to keep the arteries open.

Follow up: The patient had a heart scan to get a better picture of the unusual extra blood vessel. The scan showed where this vessel started, where it traveled, and where it drained into the heart chamber. At a checkup 24 months later, the patient had no symptoms.

Disposition: The patient was sent home in good condition.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The 16-year-old Bangladeshi girl presented to our hospital with hoarseness of voice, which had been gradually worsening over the last 4 months. She was treated with antibiotics by her local physician. Evaluation with a fiberoptic endoscopy revealed a polyp in the anterior 2/3 of the right vocal cord with impaired right vocal cord mobility. The patient underwent excision of the polyp, and subsequent histopathology showed well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. She was referred to the oncology clinic for further management.

The patient had no history of using tobacco or consuming alcohol, and no exposure to radiation/carcinogenic agents. There was no family history of malignancy. Computed tomography scan of the neck showed an irregularity of the right vocal cord, and no neck nodes were observed. Her biopsy slides confirmed the diagnosis of carcinoma, which was staged as T2N0M0 well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the larynx.

The patient was planned for radical radiotherapy. The treatment fields were as per the standard guidelines, as described previously. Neck nodes were not prophylactically treated, as the incidence of nodal positivity is <2% in T2 lesions. The patient was treated with radical radiotherapy using 6 MV photons in a linear accelerator. The patient tolerated the radiotherapy well and is on regular follow-up.

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Follow-Up Plans:

The patient is advised to continue 6-month follow-up visits for appropriate monitoring.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The 16-year-old Bangladeshi girl came to our hospital because her voice had become hoarse. This problem had been getting worse over the past 4 months. Her local doctor had treated her with antibiotics. Doctors used a thin, flexible camera to look at her throat. They found a growth on the front part of her right voice box. Her right voice box was also not moving properly. The patient had surgery to remove the growth. Lab tests showed the growth was a type of cancer called squamous cell carcinoma. It was a slow-growing type. She was sent to the cancer clinic for more care.

The patient had never used tobacco or alcohol. She had not been around radiation or cancer-causing substances. No one in her family had cancer. A special scan of her neck showed a problem with her right voice box. No spread to the neck glands was found. The lab tests confirmed cancer. The cancer was at an early stage and had not spread to other parts of the body.

The patient was scheduled for radiation therapy to destroy the cancer. The treatment followed standard guidelines used before. The neck glands were not treated because this type of early cancer rarely spreads there. The patient received radiation therapy using a special machine. The patient handled the radiation treatment well. She is now coming back for regular check-ups.

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Follow-Up Plans:

The patient is advised to come back for check-up visits every 6 months so doctors can watch her progress.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
- The patient, a 39-year-old female, was admitted to the outpatient clinic with the complaints of recurrent painless swelling in the left thigh and had a history of surgery twice for its removal.
- She underwent surgical excision in 2005 and 2009.
- She had a recurrent swelling over the left distal thigh at the time of presentation to us, which was progressively increasing in size and was associated with some discomfort.
- The swelling was intramuscular on clinical examination.
- Radiographs of the thigh were suggestive of fibrous dysplasia.
- A thorough skeletal survey was done, which showed the involvement of the left femur and tibia.
- Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the whole of the thigh was done, which showed two additional soft tissue masses; one in left mid-thigh and the other in the left inguinal region.
- The clinical diagnosis of Mazabraud’s syndrome was made and the patient decided for operative management of the large distal thigh symptomatic swelling and conservative management for the remaining two small, asymptomatic swelling.
- The patient underwent wide excision of the distal thigh swelling through the previous skin incision. The mass was intramuscular, located within vastus medialis.
- Histopathological examination was suggestive of intramuscular myxoma.
- The patient was managed with weekly oral bisphosphonates for fibrous dysplasia, which was stopped after 1.5 years.
- A regular MRI every 6 months was advised to assess the recurrence and interval enlargement of the remaining two lesions.
- The patient was pain-free with no recurrence at the latest follow-up after 2 years.

Diagnosis at discharge:
- Mazabraud’s syndrome
- Fibrous dysplasia

Follow-up instructions:
- Regular MRI every 6 months to assess the recurrence and interval enlargement of the remaining two lesions.

Discharge medications:
- N/A

Discharge status:
- The patient was discharged in good condition.

Instructions provided:
- The patient was advised to follow-up regularly with her physician and to visit the clinic if there is any recurrence or new symptoms.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 39-year-old woman. She came to the clinic because of a painless lump in her left thigh that kept coming back. She had surgery twice before to remove it. The first surgery was in 2005 and the second was in 2009. When she came to see us, the lump had returned in the lower part of her left thigh. It was getting bigger over time and causing some discomfort. The doctor found that the lump was inside the muscle. X-rays of the thigh showed a bone condition where normal bone is replaced by softer tissue. A full check of all the bones was done. This showed the same bone problem in the left thigh bone and shin bone. A special scan called an MRI was done on the whole thigh. It showed two more soft tissue lumps. One was in the middle of the left thigh and one was in the left groin area. The doctors determined the patient had Mazabraud's syndrome. This is a rare condition that causes bone problems and soft tissue lumps together. The patient chose to have surgery to remove the large, bothersome lump in the lower thigh. She chose to watch and wait for the two smaller lumps that were not causing problems. The patient had surgery to remove the lower thigh lump through the same cut from her past surgery. The lump was inside a thigh muscle called the vastus medialis. Lab tests of the removed lump showed it was a type of harmless soft tissue growth called an intramuscular myxoma. The patient took a bone-strengthening pill once a week to treat the bone condition. She stopped taking this medicine after one and a half years. The patient was told to get an MRI scan every 6 months. This would check if the lumps came back or if the two remaining lumps grew bigger. At her last check-up two years later, the patient had no pain and the lump had not come back.

Diagnosis at discharge:
Mazabraud's syndrome, which is a rare condition that causes bone problems and harmless soft tissue lumps.
Fibrous dysplasia, which is a bone condition where normal bone is replaced by softer tissue.

Follow-up instructions:
The patient should get an MRI scan every 6 months. This will check if the lumps come back or if the two remaining lumps grow bigger.

Discharge medications:
None.

Discharge status:
The patient was sent home in good health.

Instructions provided:
The patient was told to see her doctor regularly. She should come back to the clinic if the lump returns or if she notices any new problems.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with complaints of a white mass on the lower conjunctiva, associated with pain and redness in the right eye, and a right eye vision of 20/100. The patient was diagnosed with a circumscribed area of scleral abscess with engorged episcleral and conjunctival vessel around it caused by Nocardia species.

Treatment:
The patient was treated with topical amikacin 2.5% every half hour, oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole twice daily, and topical gatifloxacin every hour was added to the previous treatment postoperatively. Systemic antibiotics were continued for 2 months.

Progress:
After four months of treatment, the patient reported improved vision of 20/60. A slit lamp examination revealed that he had nuclear sclerosis, and there was granulation tissue at the lesion site.

Follow-up Care:
The patient was discharged with instructions for continued follow-up care with his ophthalmologist. No further hospitalization was necessary.

Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital because of a white lump on the lower part of the clear skin covering the white of the right eye. The patient also had pain and redness in the right eye. The right eye vision was 20/100, which means the patient could not see well. Doctors found that the patient had a pocket of infection on the white part of the eye. The blood vessels around the infection were swollen and enlarged. The infection was caused by a germ called Nocardia.

Treatment:
The patient received antibiotic eye drops called amikacin 2.5% every half hour. The patient also took antibiotic pills called trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole twice a day. After surgery, the patient also received another antibiotic eye drop called gatifloxacin every hour. The patient continued taking antibiotic pills for 2 months.

Progress:
After four months of treatment, the patient could see better. The vision improved to 20/60. The eye doctor looked at the eye with a special microscope. The doctor found that the lens inside the eye was starting to get cloudy. There was also healing tissue where the infection used to be.

Follow-up Care:
The patient left the hospital with instructions to keep seeing the eye doctor for checkups. The patient does not need to stay in the hospital again.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: XXX, Gender: Male, DOB: XX/XX/XXXX
Medical Record Number: XXXXX

Admission date: XX/XX/XXXX, Discharge date: XX/XX/XXXX

Hospital course:
The patient presented to the ED with a four-day history of high-grade fever, vomiting and loose stools, and a sudden headache. He was diagnosed with meningitis based on an elevated total leukocyte count with 84% lymphocytes found in the cerebrospinal fluid. An EV PCR was also detected in the cerebrospinal fluid using a biofire filmarray meningitis/encephalitis panel. The patient was started on empirical IV antibiotics, which were later discontinued after a more thorough evaluation. Symptomatic treatment was given along with IV hydration, and the patient remained afebrile and stable throughout hospitalization. The headache improved with oral analgesics and the patient was discharged home on the third day. 

Discharge diagnosis: 
Meningitis 

Discharge condition: 
The patient's condition improved during the hospital stay and he is now clinically stable with complete resolution of symptoms. 

Treatment plan: 
The patient was discharged with instruction to continue the oral analgesics for headache relief. No further treatment is required at this time. 

Follow-up plan:
The patient was advised to follow-up with his primary care physician if symptoms reoccur or new symptoms develop. 

Summary of results: 
Laboratory investigations revealed no significant abnormalities other than an elevated total leukocyte count with 84% lymphocytes, which is consistent with viral meningitis. MRI brain with contrast was unremarkable. 

Summary of procedures:
No procedures were performed during this hospitalization.

Medications on Discharge: 
None prescribed 

Allergies:
None mentioned on admission. 

Instructions: 
The patient was advised to get plenty of rest and adequate hydration. He was instructed to follow-up with his primary care physician if symptoms return or new symptoms develop.

Discharge condition: 
The patient was discharged in good condition with complete resolution of symptoms and no future follow-up is required.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: XXX, Gender: Male, DOB: XX/XX/XXXX
Medical Record Number: XXXXX

Admission date: XX/XX/XXXX, Discharge date: XX/XX/XXXX

Hospital course:
The patient came to the emergency room with four days of high fever, vomiting, loose stools, and sudden headache. The patient was found to have meningitis, which is swelling of the protective layers around the brain and spinal cord. A spinal fluid test showed a high number of white blood cells, with 84% being a type called lymphocytes. A special test also found a virus in the spinal fluid. The patient was first given antibiotics through an IV, but these were stopped after more tests were done. The patient received treatment for symptoms and fluids through an IV. The patient had no fever and stayed stable during the hospital stay. The headache got better with pain medicine taken by mouth. The patient went home on the third day.

Discharge diagnosis:
Meningitis, which is swelling of the protective layers around the brain and spinal cord.

Discharge condition:
The patient got better during the hospital stay. The patient is now stable and all symptoms have gone away.

Treatment plan:
The patient was sent home with instructions to keep taking pain medicine by mouth for headache relief. No other treatment is needed at this time.

Follow-up plan:
The patient was told to see his regular doctor if symptoms come back or new symptoms appear.

Summary of results:
Lab tests showed no major problems except for a high white blood cell count with 84% lymphocytes. This matches what is seen with viral meningitis. A brain scan with dye showed no problems.

Summary of procedures:
No procedures were done during this hospital stay.

Medications on Discharge:
None prescribed.

Allergies:
None listed when the patient arrived.

Instructions:
The patient was told to get plenty of rest and drink enough fluids. The patient was told to see his regular doctor if symptoms come back or new symptoms appear.

Discharge condition:
The patient left the hospital in good condition. All symptoms have gone away. No future follow-up visits are needed.

Medical History: Myasthenia gravis with thymoma, hypertension, diabetes mellitus 2, diverticulosis, fecal incontinence

Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted for VATS thymoma resection. Throughout her hospital stay, she remained pain-free, with the exception of scaling 1 on the first postoperative day. The patient did not require any pain medication and her pain scale was 0 at rest. The catheter was removed on the second postoperative day.

Functional Status:
The patient was able to perform her activities of daily living without assistance. She is able to walk short distances and perform self-care.

Follow-up Care:
The patient was advised to have a follow-up appointment with her primary care doctor to discuss her current medical status and to monitor her condition.

Summary:
The patient was admitted for VATS thymoma resection. She did not require any piritramide therapy and was pain-free throughout her hospital stay, with the exception of scaling 1 on the first postoperative day. The catheter was removed on the second postoperative day. The patient is able to perform her activities of daily living without assistance. Follow-up care was advised.
