input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient, a 10 year old female, was admitted with spontaneous pain in the mandibular right second premolar. On clinical examination, the tooth showed no response to electric pulp testing. Radiographic examination revealed a large periapical radiolucency. The tooth was diagnosed with pulp necrosis and periapical abscess, and revascularization using an amoxicillin-containing Triple Antibiotic Paste (TAP) was planned.

After receiving local anesthesia, the access cavity was prepared and TAP was delivered to the root canal. The access cavity was temporarily sealed and the patient was discharged. After three weeks, the patient's symptoms were relieved. The root canal was gently irrigated and bleeding was induced at the apical end. An absorbable atelocollagen sponge was delivered to the blood filled root end space to create an apical matrix, and ProRoot MTA was applied to the root canal. The tooth was permanently restored with a dual-cure composite resin at a later date.

At the 1 year follow up, the mandibular right second premolar was asymptomatic. Radiographic examination revealed complete resolution of the periapical radiolucency and a closed apex with obvious periodontal ligament space. However, the root growth was limited, and the treatment outcome was apexification rather than pulp revascularization.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
- Pulp necrosis
- Periapical abscess

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
- Continue with good oral hygiene practices
- Follow up with dentist for routine dental check ups and cleanings 
- Schedule follow up appointments as directed by dentist

FOLLOW UP APPOINTMENT:
- Follow up appointment with dentist recommended for routine dental check up and cleaning

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
- None prescribed

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
- Improved

DISCHARGE DISPOSITION:
- Discharged home 

SIGNATURE:
- [Redacted] MD
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient, a 10 year old female, was admitted with pain in the lower right tooth (second small back tooth). The pain started on its own. During the exam, the tooth did not respond to an electric test that checks if the tooth nerve is alive. An x-ray showed a large dark area around the root tip. This means there was an infection. The tooth nerve had died and there was a pocket of infection at the root tip. The care team planned a treatment to try to bring the tooth back to life using a paste with three germ-fighting medicines.

The patient received medicine to numb the area. The dentist made an opening in the tooth and placed the paste with three germ-fighting medicines inside. The opening was sealed with a temporary filling and the patient went home. After three weeks, the patient felt better. The dentist gently rinsed out the inside of the tooth and caused some bleeding near the root tip. A small sponge was placed in the bleeding area to help form a base. Then a special cement was placed in the tooth. Later, the tooth was filled with a long-lasting tooth-colored filling.

At the 1 year check up, the lower right tooth had no pain or problems. The x-ray showed the infection had fully healed. The root tip had closed. The tissue around the root looked normal. However, the root did not grow much longer. The treatment helped close the root tip but did not bring the tooth fully back to life.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Dead tooth nerve
Infection pocket at the root tip

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
Continue brushing and flossing teeth well
See a dentist for regular check ups and cleanings
Make follow up visits as the dentist tells the patient

FOLLOW UP APPOINTMENT:
A follow up visit with the dentist is recommended for a regular check up and cleaning

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
None prescribed

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
Improved

DISCHARGE DISPOSITION:
Discharged home

SIGNATURE:
[Redacted] MD

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 25-year-old male from Iraq, presented with painful cutaneous swellings, abnormal gait, and recurrent high-grade fever. He had a history of chronic glomerulonephritis and was on hemodialysis twice a week. His renal allograft had failed due to acute rejection, leading to graft nephrectomy. He had previously been treated for pulmonary tuberculosis and chronic active hepatitis C with interferon, which was discontinued due to pancytopenia.

Diagnostic Assessment:
Physical examination revealed subcutaneous masses with reduced joint movements and painful indurated ulcers. Tests showed anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, elevated BUN and creatinine, and altered calcium and phosphorus levels. Intact PTH was significantly elevated, while 25-hydroxy-vitamin D3 was within normal limits. Alkaline phosphatase was elevated, and urine aluminum levels were elevated. A radioisotope bone-scan revealed ectopic calcification, and a CT scan showed heterotopic soft tissue calcification.

Diagnosis and Management:
The patient was diagnosed with tumoral calcinosis and treated with low-calcium dialysate, Sevelemer phosphate binder, and desferal chelation for aluminum toxicity. His fever and painful joints improved after eight sessions of dialysis, after which he was discharged to continue further treatment in his hometown.

Follow-Up and Recommendations:
The patient was advised to continue low-calcium dialysate and phosphate-binder treatment and to seek regular follow-up with his nephrologist. He was advised to maintain adequate hydration, adhere to a low-calcium and low-phosphorus diet, and avoid aluminum-containing antacids and medications. He was also advised to avoid activities that could lead to trauma or pressure on the affected areas and to seek medical attention promptly if he developed any new symptoms.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 25-year-old man from Iraq. He came to the hospital with painful lumps under his skin, trouble walking, and repeated high fevers. He has long-term kidney disease and needs dialysis two times a week to clean his blood. He had a kidney transplant in the past, but his body rejected it. Doctors had to remove the transplanted kidney. He was treated before for a lung infection called tuberculosis. He also had a liver infection called hepatitis C. He took medicine called interferon for this, but it was stopped because his blood cell counts dropped too low.

Diagnostic Assessment:
The physical exam showed lumps under his skin. His joints were stiff and hard to move. He had painful sores that were hard and swollen. Blood tests showed low red blood cells, low white blood cells, and low platelets. His kidney waste levels were high. His calcium and phosphorus levels were not normal. A hormone that controls calcium was very high. His vitamin D level was normal. A liver enzyme was high. Aluminum levels in his urine were high. A bone scan showed calcium buildup in places it should not be. A CT scan also showed calcium buildup in soft body tissues.

Diagnosis and Management:
The patient was diagnosed with tumoral calcinosis. This means calcium lumps formed in his body tissues. He was treated with dialysis fluid that has less calcium. He took a medicine called Sevelemer to lower phosphorus. He also got treatment to remove extra aluminum from his body. After eight dialysis sessions, his fever went down and his joint pain got better. He was sent home to continue treatment in his hometown.

Follow-Up and Recommendations:
The patient should keep using low-calcium dialysis fluid and phosphorus-lowering medicine. He should see his kidney doctor for regular checkups. He should drink enough fluids. He should eat foods low in calcium and phosphorus. He should not take antacids or medicines that contain aluminum. He should avoid activities that could hurt or put pressure on the affected areas. He should see a doctor right away if he notices any new symptoms.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: XX/XX/XXXX
Gender: Female
Admission Date: XX/XX/XXXX
Discharge Date: XX/XX/XXXX

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the emergency department of our hospital due to iatrogenic acute ascending aortic dissection during coronary angioplasty. Upon arrival, the patient was sedated, afebrile, and diagnosed with atrial fibrillation. An emergent trans-thoracic echography revealed an enlarged ascending aorta. The patient underwent emergency CABG surgery and aortic dissection repair. During the procedure, Stanford type A DeBakey type II ascending aortic dissection with intramural hematoma behind brachiocephalic trunk was found. The aortic valve cusp was decalcified, and a supracoronary aortic tube graft and saphenous vein grafts were successfully placed. The patient was weaned from cardiopulmonary bypass and recovered over the course of 11 days with no reported postoperative complications.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Iatrogenic acute ascending aortic dissection during coronary angioplasty, Stanford type A DeBakey type II ascending aortic dissection with intramural hematoma behind brachiocephalic trunk, atrial fibrillation

Hospital Course:
Upon discharge, the patient was stable and fully recovered. Follow-up appointments with the patient's healthcare provider to monitor cardiac health were recommended. Continued use of prescribed medications was advised, unless otherwise directed.

Discharge Medications:
Aspirin 100 mg (Bayer Bitterfeld GmbH, Germany), Lovenox (Sanofi Winthrop Industrie, France), Plavix 75 mg (Sanofi Pharma Bristol Myers Squibb SNC, France), Concor 5 mg (Merck KGaA, Germany), Glucophage 850 mg (Merck Santé SAS, France)

Follow-up Care:
Follow-up appointments with the patient's healthcare provider to monitor cardiac health were recommended. Continued use of prescribed medications was advised, unless otherwise directed. Any significant changes in condition or concerns should be reported immediately.

clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: XX/XX/XXXX
Gender: Female
Admission Date: XX/XX/XXXX
Discharge Date: XX/XX/XXXX

Hospital Course:
The patient was brought to the emergency room because of a tear in the main blood vessel leaving the heart. This happened by accident during a procedure to open blocked heart arteries. When the patient arrived, she was given medicine to sleep and had no fever. She had an irregular heartbeat. A heart ultrasound showed that the main blood vessel leaving the heart was larger than normal. The patient needed emergency heart bypass surgery and repair of the torn blood vessel. During surgery, doctors found a serious tear in the upper part of the main blood vessel. There was also bleeding inside the blood vessel wall near where it branches to the arm and head. Doctors removed calcium buildup from the heart valve. They replaced part of the blood vessel with a tube graft. They also used a vein from the leg to bypass blocked heart arteries. The patient was taken off the heart-lung machine after surgery. She recovered over 11 days with no problems after surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Accidental tear in the main blood vessel leaving the heart during a procedure to open blocked heart arteries, serious tear in the upper part of the main blood vessel with bleeding inside the vessel wall near the branch to the arm and head, irregular heartbeat

Hospital Course:
The patient was stable and fully recovered when leaving the hospital. Follow-up visits with the patient's doctor to check heart health were recommended. The patient was told to keep taking prescribed medicines unless the doctor says otherwise.

Discharge Medications:
Aspirin 100 mg (Bayer Bitterfeld GmbH, Germany), Lovenox (Sanofi Winthrop Industrie, France), Plavix 75 mg (Sanofi Pharma Bristol Myers Squibb SNC, France), Concor 5 mg (Merck KGaA, Germany), Glucophage 850 mg (Merck Santé SAS, France)

Follow-up Care:
Follow-up visits with the patient's doctor to check heart health were recommended. The patient was told to keep taking prescribed medicines unless the doctor says otherwise. Any big changes in how the patient feels or any concerns should be reported right away.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admitted to the emergency department (ED) with altered mental status, a 66-year-old man with hypertension and high cholesterol was found to have nystagmus, dysarthria, discoordination, and ataxia. While in the ED, the patient's condition worsened and concerned about a posterior circulation stroke, a head computed tomography angiogram was obtained, which was negative for any abnormality. An electrocardiogram showed a normal sinus rhythm. The patient was evaluated by the neurology consultant team, who determined the patient had a National Institute of Health Stroke Scale of 11, and tissue plasminogen activator was administered for thrombolysis. He was subsequently intubated for airway protection and admitted to the ICU.

While intubated in the ICU, the patient developed status epilepticus and was treated with multiple doses of benzodiazepine and phenytoin, resulting in cessation of seizure activity. A comprehensive urine drug screen by gas chromatography and mass spectrometry was positive for clozapine and clozapine metabolites, in addition to caffeine and bupropion, a home medication. It was concluded that the patient must have accidently ingested the family member's clozapine, as the patient's blood work showed no evidence of abuse or other medications. The patient had never been prescribed clozapine but managed the family member's medication and often took their respective medications at the same time. The patient was extubated on hospital day three and discharged with a normal neurologic exam and no further seizure activity on hospital day five.

In summary, the patient presented with altered mental status and exhibited symptoms of nystagmus, dysarthria, discoordination, and ataxia. Concerned about a potential stroke, a head CT angiogram was performed, and the patient was evaluated by the neurology team. After admission to the ICU, the patient developed status epilepticus, and a comprehensive urine drug screen was performed, which identified the presence of clozapine that the patient had entered. Ultimately, the patient was extubated and, after determining there was no further seizure activity, discharged with normal neurologic exam results.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

A 66-year-old man came to the emergency room because he was confused and not thinking clearly. He has high blood pressure and high cholesterol. Doctors found that his eyes were moving in an unusual way. He also had trouble speaking clearly and controlling his body movements. While in the emergency room, the patient got worse. Doctors worried he might be having a stroke in the back of his brain. They did a scan of his head and blood vessels, which showed nothing wrong. A heart test showed his heart rhythm was normal. A brain specialist team checked the patient. They measured how bad his stroke symptoms were using a standard test. He scored 11 out of a possible 42 points. Doctors gave him a clot-busting medicine through his vein. After that, the patient needed a breathing tube to protect his airway. He was moved to the intensive care unit.

While on the breathing machine in the intensive care unit, the patient had seizures that would not stop. Doctors treated him with anti-seizure medicines, and the seizures stopped. A detailed urine test was done to check for drugs. The test found a medicine called clozapine and its breakdown products. It also found caffeine and bupropion, which is a medicine the patient takes at home. Doctors concluded that the patient must have taken a family member's clozapine by accident. Blood tests showed no signs of drug misuse or other medicines. The patient had never been prescribed clozapine. However, he helps manage a family member's medicines. He often takes his own medicines at the same time as the family member. The patient had his breathing tube removed on day three in the hospital. He left the hospital on day five with a normal brain and nerve exam and no more seizures.

In summary, the patient came in confused and had unusual eye movements, trouble speaking, and trouble controlling his movements. Doctors worried about a possible stroke. They did a head and blood vessel scan and had brain specialists check him. After going to the intensive care unit, the patient had seizures that would not stop. A detailed urine drug test found clozapine that the patient had taken by accident. In the end, the patient had his breathing tube removed. After doctors confirmed he had no more seizures, he left the hospital with normal brain and nerve exam results.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with lower extremity edema and proteinuria on urinalysis. A renal biopsy established the diagnosis of lambda light chain amyloidosis. Treatment included melphalan- and dexamethasone-based chemotherapy followed by autologous stem cell transplantation. Unfortunately, despite therapy, total serum-free light chain levels and massive nephrotic syndrome persisted. Nine months later, the patient underwent hand-assisted laparoscopic ligation of both native ureters to decrease daily urinary protein loss and restore plasma oncotic pressure. Post-operative renal ultrasound confirmed mild hydronephrosis. During the 12-day hospitalization, nutritional and functional status improved, and he was dismissed home on long-term hemodialysis requiring minimal assistance for ambulation.

Final Diagnoses:
Lambda light chain amyloidosis with massive nephrotic syndrome and autonomic dysfunction with disabling orthostatic hypotension.

Procedures:
Hand-assisted laparoscopic ligation of both native ureters.

Follow-Up Plans:
The patient will follow up with the nephrologist for long-term management of his renal disease. Hemodialysis will be continued as needed. Further imaging studies may be considered to evaluate the patient's renal function.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with swelling in the legs and protein in the urine. A kidney tissue sample showed a disease called amyloidosis. This disease happens when an abnormal protein builds up in the body. Treatment included chemotherapy drugs called melphalan and dexamethasone. The patient also had a transplant using their own stem cells. Sadly, the treatment did not work well enough. The protein levels in the blood stayed high. The patient also kept losing large amounts of protein through urine. Nine months later, the patient had surgery to tie off both tubes that carry urine from the kidneys. This was done to stop protein loss and help the body hold onto important fluids. After surgery, an imaging test showed mild fluid backup in the kidneys. During the 12-day hospital stay, the patient ate better and got stronger. The patient went home on long-term dialysis, which is a treatment that cleans the blood when kidneys cannot. The patient needed only a little help with walking.

Final Diagnoses:
Amyloidosis caused by an abnormal protein, with large protein loss in urine. The patient also has nerve damage that causes a big drop in blood pressure when standing up.

Procedures:
Surgery to tie off both tubes that carry urine from the kidneys. This was done through small cuts with a camera and hand assistance.

Follow-Up Plans:
The patient will see the kidney doctor for ongoing care of the kidney disease. Dialysis will continue as needed. More imaging tests may be done to check how the kidneys are working.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Name]
Age: 37
Sex: Female
Date of Admission: [Admission Date]
Date of Discharge: [Discharge Date]
Admitting Physician: [Physician Name]
Primary Diagnosis: Urticaria

Hospital Course:

The patient presented to the outpatient allergy clinic with a complaint of recurrent generalized urticaria, rash, and facial angioedema, worsened by high-seasoned, spicy or Chinese foods. Previously, she had been treated for allergic rhinitis and experienced severe urticaria with a generalized rash after taking non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). Peripheral eosinophil counts and serum total IgE in her blood were elevated, and a skin prick test showed weakly positive reactions to silk worm pupa, herring, cabbage, celery, and walnut. However, none of them induced urticaria in her history.

The patient underwent basophil activation test (BAT), in which a total of 18.7% of basophils were activated after stimulation with monosodium glutamate (MSG). No other additives induced significant basophil activation compared to the negative control. T4 and thyroid stimulating hormone levels were within normal range.

The patient was diagnosed with urticaria and was advised to avoid foods that tend to trigger her symptoms. No medications were prescribed.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient was provided with instructions on how to manage her symptoms at home and advised to avoid the foods that tend to trigger urticaria. She was also advised to return to the clinic if her symptoms persist or worsen.

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to follow-up with the clinic for further evaluation and management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Name]
Age: 37
Sex: Female
Date of Admission: [Admission Date]
Date of Discharge: [Discharge Date]
Admitting Physician: [Physician Name]
Primary Diagnosis: Hives

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the allergy clinic with a problem of hives that kept coming back all over her body. She also had a rash and swelling of her face. These problems got worse when she ate very spicy foods, heavily seasoned foods, or Chinese foods. In the past, she was treated for allergies that caused a stuffy or runny nose. She also had bad hives and a rash all over her body after taking pain medicines like ibuprofen. Blood tests showed she had higher than normal levels of certain allergy-related cells and proteins. A skin test showed small reactions to silkworm pupa, herring, cabbage, celery, and walnut. However, the patient had never gotten hives from eating any of these foods before.

The patient had a special blood test to check how her allergy cells react to different things. The test showed that her allergy cells reacted to MSG, a flavor enhancer often used in food. Her allergy cells did not react to any other food additives that were tested. Tests for thyroid hormone levels came back normal.

The patient was told she has hives. She was told to stay away from foods that seem to cause her symptoms. No medicines were given.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient was given information on how to take care of her symptoms at home. She was told to avoid foods that seem to cause her hives. She was also told to come back to the clinic if her symptoms do not go away or get worse.

Follow-up:

The patient was told to come back to the clinic for more tests and care.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital following a stab wound to the left chest. He was transferred to the emergency department with hypotension, tachycardia, tachypnea, and Glasgow Coma Scale score of 14. The initial FAST revealed a pleural effusion in the left thorax. A chest drain was inserted, and more than 700 mL of blood was drained. Subsequent FAST had revealed a pericardial effusion.

A Clamshell thoracotomy was started due to the patient's unstable hemodynamic status, and surgical exploration showed a massive hemothorax in the left thorax, left lung posterior lobe injury, pericardial injury, and hemopericardium with blood clots. A 10-mm defect was found in the right ventricle near the left anterior descending coronary artery. Chemical cardiac arrest was induced using ATP, and the cardiac wall was repaired surgically using a horizontal mattress suture. The patient recovered after the surgery and received intensive care in the hospital for 13 days.

Discharge Summary:

The patient was discharged after a 13-day hospital stay without complications. The postoperative echocardiographic check-ups showed a septal injury, but the patient did not exhibit any symptoms of heart failure.

The patient should avoid strenuous activities and get plenty of rest for at least two weeks. It is essential to monitor for signs of infection, such as fever, inflammation, and excessive wound discharge. The patient should report chest pain, shortness of breath, or lightheadedness immediately.

Follow-up care should include a visit with a primary care provider or specialist, depending on the patient's condition, within one week. They should return to the hospital immediately if new symptoms appear or existing symptoms worsen.

The patient's condition is stable currently, and they have been discharged home with follow-up treatment as suggested above.
clean_ref:
Hospital Stay:

The patient came to the hospital after being stabbed in the left side of the chest. When he arrived at the emergency room, his blood pressure was very low. His heart was beating too fast, and he was breathing too fast. He was also slightly less alert than normal. A quick scan showed fluid had collected around his left lung. Doctors put in a tube to drain the fluid, and more than 700 mL of blood came out. Another scan showed fluid had also collected around his heart.

Doctors had to open the patient's chest because his condition was not stable. During surgery, they found a large amount of blood in the left chest area. They also found damage to the back part of the left lung and the sac around the heart. Blood and clots had built up around the heart. There was a small hole in the right side of the heart near a major blood vessel. Doctors used medicine to temporarily stop the heart so they could fix the hole with stitches. The patient recovered after surgery and stayed in the intensive care unit for 13 days.

Discharge Summary:

The patient left the hospital after 13 days with no problems. Heart scans after surgery showed some damage to the wall between the heart chambers. However, the patient did not show any signs that the heart was not pumping well.

The patient should not do hard physical activities and should rest for at least two weeks. It is important to watch for signs of infection, such as fever, redness or swelling, and fluid leaking from the wound. The patient should tell a doctor right away about any chest pain, trouble breathing, or feeling dizzy.

The patient should see a regular doctor or heart doctor within one week, depending on how he is doing. He should go back to the hospital right away if new problems start or current problems get worse.

The patient is doing well right now and has gone home with the care plan described above.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED] 
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED] 
Date of Admission: [REDACTED] 
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED] 

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 27-year-old female with a history significant for CREST syndrome, limited scleroderma, interstitial lung disease, mild pulmonary hypertension, and chronic kidney disease (CKD) from scleroderma renal crises. She presented with intermittent atypical chest pain that remained consistent despite physical activity. Her chest pain was relieved with nitroglycerin. During one episode of chest pain, the patient showed elevated troponin levels. Further testing was done which included a nuclear stress test. Her condition showed improvement upon use of nifedipine.

The patient underwent a coronary angiography which revealed severe stenosis of the proximal left anterior descending (LAD) artery, as well as collaterals from the right coronary artery and slow flow. The right coronary artery and left circumflex were normal. She was initially managed conservatively due to her CKD and equivocal nuclear stress test findings.

Using an XB 3.5 guide catheter, a pressure wire was advanced across the LAD lesion and the resting Pd/Pa ratio was found to be ischemic. The lesion was pre-dilated and then a coronary stent was placed, followed by post-dilatation. Intravascular imaging with OCT was performed, and showed stent malapposition and insufficient expansion of the distal portion of the stent. The stent was post-dilated with a noncompliant balloon with excellent results.

The patient's condition showed marked improvement after stenting. The patient's pain went away and she was discharged in stable condition.

Discharge Diagnoses:

1. CREST syndrome 
2. Limited scleroderma 
3. Interstitial lung disease 
4. Mild pulmonary hypertension 
5. Chronic kidney disease 

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Discharge Instructions:

1. Follow-up with cardiology in 2 weeks.
2. Continue taking all current prescribed medications.
3. Return to the emergency department immediately if chest pain or any other concerning symptoms occur.
4. Avoid any strenuous physical activity for the next week.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 27-year-old female with several health conditions. These include CREST syndrome, a type of autoimmune disease that affects the skin and other organs. She also has limited scleroderma, which causes hardening of the skin. She has scarring in the lungs, mild high blood pressure in the lungs, and kidney damage from her autoimmune disease. She came to the hospital with chest pain that came and went. The pain did not change with movement or exercise. A medication called nitroglycerin helped relieve her chest pain. During one episode of chest pain, blood tests showed signs of possible heart muscle damage. More tests were done, including a heart stress test with imaging. A blood pressure medication called nifedipine helped her feel better.

The patient had a procedure to look at the blood vessels in her heart. This showed a major blockage in one of the main heart arteries called the LAD. There were also extra small blood vessels growing from another artery and slow blood flow. Two other heart arteries looked normal. At first, doctors chose to watch her closely because of her kidney problems and unclear stress test results.

Doctors used a special tube to guide a thin wire to the blocked area. Measurements showed the blockage was causing poor blood flow to the heart. The blocked area was opened up with a small balloon. Then a small mesh tube called a stent was placed to keep the artery open. The artery was stretched again after the stent was placed. A special camera inside the artery showed the stent was not fitting well against the artery wall. The stent was stretched again with a firm balloon. This gave excellent results.

The patient got much better after the stent was placed. Her pain went away and she left the hospital in stable condition.

Discharge Diagnoses:

1. CREST syndrome
2. Limited scleroderma
3. Scarring in the lungs
4. Mild high blood pressure in the lungs
5. Long-term kidney disease

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Discharge Instructions:

1. See the heart doctor in 2 weeks.
2. Keep taking all current prescribed medications.
3. Go to the emergency room right away if chest pain or any other worrying symptoms happen.
4. Do not do hard exercise or heavy lifting for the next week.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 55 years
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital with pleuritic right chest pain, high fever (40ºC), and severe dyspnea. The laboratory results showed an elevated CRP, hypoxemia, and hyperlactatemia. The chest x-ray showed the presence of a right lower lung field infiltrate. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed hepatic steatosis. The patient was diagnosed with sepsis caused by right lower lung pneumonia, and started on piperacillin/tazobactam. On the third day of antibiotic treatment, the patient developed right upper quadrant pain and was diagnosed with subhepatic appendicitis with perforation and local abscess.

Treatment:

Exploratory laparoscopic surgery was performed, which revealed subhepatic appendicitis with perforation and local abscess. Infection source control was implemented, and the patient was treated with multidrug-resistant Escherichia coli, which was isolated from the drainage fluid. The antibiotic therapy was switched to meropenem, and the patient's condition rapidly improved. The patient was discharged after 6 days of targeted antibiotic therapy.

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to follow-up with the Internal Medicine Department and continue taking the prescribed antibiotics for the recommended duration. The patient was also advised to take necessary precautions to avoid any future infections.

Diagnosis:

The patient was diagnosed with sepsis caused by right lower lung pneumonia which was later complicated by subhepatic appendicitis with perforation and local abscess.

Summary:

The patient, a 55-year-old male with hypertension and dyslipidemia, presented to the Emergency Department with pleuritic right chest pain and was eventually diagnosed with sepsis caused by right lower lung pneumonia. The patient later developed subhepatic appendicitis with perforation and local abscess and underwent exploratory laparoscopic surgery. The patient's condition improved rapidly with targeted antibiotic therapy and he was discharged after 6 days of treatment. The patient was advised to follow-up with the Internal Medicine Department and continue taking prescribed antibiotics.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 55 years
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with sharp chest pain on the right side that got worse with breathing. The patient also had a high fever (40ºC) and serious trouble breathing. Blood tests showed signs of infection, low oxygen levels, and high lactic acid in the blood. A chest x-ray showed an infection in the lower part of the right lung. An ultrasound of the belly showed fatty liver. The patient was diagnosed with a serious body-wide infection caused by pneumonia in the right lower lung. The patient was started on an antibiotic called piperacillin/tazobactam. On the third day of antibiotics, the patient felt pain in the upper right belly area. Doctors found that the appendix had moved under the liver, burst open, and formed a pocket of infection.

Treatment:

Doctors performed surgery using a small camera through tiny cuts in the belly. The surgery confirmed the burst appendix under the liver with a pocket of infection. The source of infection was cleaned out and controlled. A germ called Escherichia coli that does not respond to many antibiotics was found in the fluid drained during surgery. The antibiotic was changed to meropenem. The patient got better quickly after this change. The patient went home after 6 days of this targeted antibiotic treatment.

Follow-up:

The patient was told to schedule a visit with the Internal Medicine Department. The patient should keep taking the prescribed antibiotics for the full time the doctor ordered. The patient was also told to take steps to prevent future infections.

Diagnosis:

The patient had a serious body-wide infection caused by pneumonia in the right lower lung. This was followed by a burst appendix located under the liver with a pocket of infection.

Summary:

The patient is a 55-year-old male with high blood pressure and high cholesterol. The patient came to the Emergency Department with sharp chest pain on the right side. The patient was diagnosed with a serious body-wide infection caused by pneumonia in the right lower lung. Later, the patient developed a burst appendix under the liver with a pocket of infection. The patient had surgery using a small camera through tiny cuts. The patient got better quickly with targeted antibiotic treatment and went home after 6 days. The patient was told to follow up with the Internal Medicine Department and keep taking the prescribed antibiotics.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Name: [Name]
- Age: 61
- Gender: Female
- Admitted: [Admission Date]
- Discharged: [Discharge Date]
- Diagnosis: Popliteal artery pseudoaneurysm with common peroneal nerve palsy

Hospital Course:
- The patient was admitted to our emergency department with right knee and calf swelling, intolerable pain in the right lower limb, and weakness of right ankle dorsiflexors.
- A provisional diagnosis of common peroneal nerve palsy was made based on examination findings, and a foot drop splint was immediately applied.
- Further examination revealed a large popliteal artery pseudoaneurysm, which was treated with placement of a covered endovascular stent graft.
- The patient was discharged after 2 weeks of hospitalization.
- At a 6-month follow-up examination, the patient remained asymptomatic without pain or swelling, but still exhibited a right foot drop with normal distal pulsation.

Follow-Up Instructions:
- Continue to follow up with vascular surgeon for any residual symptoms and recommend regular vascular surveillance.
- Refer the patient to a physiotherapist for further management of her right foot drop and to receive home exercises.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Name]
Age: 61
Gender: Female
Admitted: [Admission Date]
Discharged: [Discharge Date]
Diagnosis: A bulge in the artery behind the knee that caused damage to a nerve controlling the foot

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room with swelling in the right knee and calf. The patient had severe pain in the right leg and weakness when trying to lift the right foot upward. Doctors found that a nerve near the knee was damaged, causing the foot to drop down. A special brace was put on the foot right away. More tests showed a bulge in the blood vessel behind the knee. This was fixed by placing a small tube inside the blood vessel to keep it open. The patient stayed in the hospital for 2 weeks before going home. At a checkup 6 months later, the patient had no pain or swelling. However, the patient still could not lift the right foot normally. Blood flow to the foot was normal.

Follow-Up Instructions:
The patient should keep seeing the blood vessel surgeon for any remaining problems. The patient should have regular tests to check the blood vessels. The patient should see a physical therapist for help with the foot drop. The physical therapist will give exercises to do at home.

input:
Discharge summary:

Patient Information:

Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 12

Sex: Male

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Reason for Admission:

The patient presented with complaints of constipation, intermittent episodes of pain, and distension of the abdomen for a year associated with poor weight gain.

Hospital Course:

The patient was evaluated with X-rays and CT scans of the abdomen that showed multiple air fluid levels, dilation of small bowel loops, and was managed as subacute intestinal obstruction. The hemogram, liver, kidney, and thyroid function tests were normal. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy revealed a dilated body of stomach with bile reflux, dilated duodenum, and ileocolonoscopy was normal. The biopsy samples from the esophagus, stomach, ileum, and colon were normal on histopathology. High-resolution esophageal manometry showed weak peristalsis with small peristaltic breaks; anorectal manometry was normal. A whole bowel transit study revealed normal gastric emptying with delayed colonic transit time. 

Treatment:

The patient received medical therapy (polyethylene glycol and itopride) with only a partial response.

Diagnosis:

Due to familial occurrence, a possibility of primary CIPO was suspected and next-generation sequencing performed in the index patient revealed a homozygous missense variation in exon 2 of SGOL1 gene (chr3; c67A>G; p.Lys23Glu; ).

Condition at Discharge:

The patient was discharged in stable condition with a recommendation for an age-appropriate protein-calorie diet with supplemented vitamins and micronutrients.

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to follow up with their primary care physician for further management.
clean_ref:
Discharge summary:

Patient Information:

Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 12

Sex: Male

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Reason for Admission:

The patient came to the hospital because of constipation, belly pain that came and went, and a swollen belly for one year. The patient also was not gaining weight well.

Hospital Course:

The patient had X-rays and CT scans of the belly. These showed signs of a partial blockage in the intestines with air and fluid buildup and swollen small intestines. Blood tests for the liver, kidneys, and thyroid were all normal. A camera test looking at the stomach showed the stomach and first part of the small intestine were enlarged with bile backing up. A camera test looking at the large intestine was normal. Small tissue samples taken from the food pipe, stomach, and intestines looked normal under a microscope. A test measuring how the food pipe squeezes food showed weak movement. A test measuring how the bottom of the intestine works was normal. A test tracking how food moves through the gut showed normal stomach emptying but slow movement through the large intestine.

Treatment:

The patient was given medicines (polyethylene glycol and itopride) to help with bowel movements. These medicines only helped a little.

Diagnosis:

Because other family members have similar problems, doctors thought the patient might have a condition where the intestines do not move food properly from birth. Genetic testing found a change in a gene called SGOL1 that causes this condition.

Condition at Discharge:

The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient should eat foods with enough protein and calories for their age along with added vitamins and minerals.

Follow-up:

The patient should see their regular doctor for ongoing care.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: [Patient's Name]

Gender: Male

Age: 76 years

Medical Record Number: [Medical Record Number]

Admission Date: [Admission Date]

Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Elevated creatinine levels, elevated IgE level, proteinuria, and glucosuria

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the rheumatology clinic with severe fatigue, unintentional weight loss, memory loss, and ambulatory dysfunction. The patient had a 120 pack-year smoking history and also smoked marijuana. The patient had a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, COPD, and diabetes mellitus.

The labs showed an elevated creatinine level, elevated IgE level, proteinuria, and glucosuria. An ultrasound image of the Kidneys showed echogenic foci in the right kidney. The CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast showed a scar in the interpolar region of the right kidney. CT scan of the chest with contrast showed a 10 mm low-density area in the right thyroid lobe. Thyroid ultrasound showed bilateral thyroid nodules, including a 3.5 cm hypoechoic nodule in the lower pole. Fine-needle aspiration cytology of the nodule showed benign follicular nodule.

The patient had a repeat CT chest with contrast, which showed a 1.4 cm right lower lobe lung nodule, bilateral interlobular septal thickening with lower lobe predominance, multiple bilateral ground-glass opacities, cystic changes, and multiple enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes. PET scanning was done, which showed right lower lobe hypermetabolic nodule, highly suspicious for malignancy with extensive smoking history and significant weight loss.

The patient underwent right lower lobe basilar segmentectomy and mediastinal lymph node dissection. Histology was negative for malignancy and showed numerous positive IgG4 plasma cells, along with obliterative phlebitis, significant fibrosis, and arteritis as lymphoplasmacytic infiltration. IgG4:IgG ratio on the lung biopsy was greater than 40%. The lung biopsy was highly suggestive of IgG4-related disease. The lymph node also showed lymphoplasmacytic infiltration, IgG4-positive cells, irregular fibrous bands, but no obliterative phlebitis or arteritis.

Treatment and Procedures:

The patient was started on 40 mg of prednisone, which resolved his fatigue and had significant weight gain, and his prednisone was tapered down and stopped in 4 months. He was not placed on any immunosuppression and was monitored for any further relapse. He is in remission for the last one year off any medications without any relapses.

Diagnosis on Discharge:

IgG4-related disease.

Condition at Discharge:

The patient is asymptomatic with no need for further follow-up.

Follow-up Recommendations:

The patient is advised follow-up with the rheumatology clinic.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: [Patient's Name]

Gender: Male

Age: 76 years

Medical Record Number: [Medical Record Number]

Admission Date: [Admission Date]

Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: High kidney waste levels in blood, high allergy-related protein level, protein in urine, and sugar in urine

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the joint and immune disease clinic with severe tiredness, weight loss without trying, memory problems, and trouble walking. The patient had smoked heavily for many years (equal to 6 packs a day for 20 years) and also smoked marijuana. The patient had a history of high blood pressure, high cholesterol, lung disease from smoking, and diabetes.

Blood tests showed high kidney waste levels, high allergy-related protein levels, protein in urine, and sugar in urine. An ultrasound of the kidneys showed bright spots in the right kidney. A detailed scan of the belly area showed scar tissue in the right kidney. A chest scan showed a small area of concern in the right side of the thyroid gland in the neck. A thyroid ultrasound showed lumps on both sides, including a 3.5 cm lump in the lower part. A needle test of the lump showed it was not cancer.

The patient had another chest scan. It showed a 1.4 cm lump in the right lower lung, thickening between lung sections mostly in the lower lungs, hazy patches in both lungs, small air pockets, and several swollen lymph nodes in the chest. A special scan to look for cancer was done. It showed the right lower lung lump was very active and likely cancer given the heavy smoking history and weight loss.

The patient had surgery to remove part of the right lower lung and lymph nodes in the chest. Lab testing of the tissue showed no cancer. Instead, it showed many immune cells called IgG4, blocked veins, heavy scarring, and swollen blood vessels. The ratio of IgG4 cells was very high at over 40 percent. The lung tissue strongly pointed to a rare immune disease called IgG4-related disease. The lymph node also showed similar immune cells and scarring but no blocked veins or swollen blood vessels.

Treatment and Procedures:

The patient started taking 40 mg of prednisone, a steroid medicine. This helped the tiredness go away and the patient gained back weight. The steroid dose was slowly lowered and stopped after 4 months. The patient did not need other immune-lowering medicines and was watched for signs of the disease coming back. The patient has been free of disease for the last one year without taking any medicines and without the disease returning.

Diagnosis on Discharge:

IgG4-related disease (a rare condition where the immune system causes swelling and scarring in different body parts).

Condition at Discharge:

The patient has no symptoms and does not need further follow-up at this time.

Follow-up Recommendations:

The patient is advised to follow up with the joint and immune disease clinic.

input:
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:

Name: [redacted]
Age: 28
Gender: Female
Medical Record Number: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]

Clinical Course:

The patient was admitted presenting with seizures and severe hypoglycemia, specifically due to glycogen storage disease type Ia. She was started on parenteral nutrition, then continuous enteric nutrition, and later maltodextrin and uncooked cornstarch were added to her diet. Enzymatic studies were suggestive of the disease, and genetic testing confirmed she has a homozygous R83C mutation in exon 2 of G6PC. 

During childhood, the patient was frequently admitted due to seizures and gastrointestinal and upper airway infections. She was diagnosed with hepatomegaly and multiple hepatocellular adenomas at the age of 16. She also has low bone mineral density, sideropenic anemia, and polycystic ovaries. The patient has hypouricemia and a normal lipid profile. 

The patient also has an elevated Apolipoprotein A1, with normal Apolipoprotein B100 and Apolipoprotein B100/Apolipoprotein A1 ratio. Short stature and cognitive dysfunction were present from a young age. The patient has an increased body mass index and lipomatosis due to an enriched carbohydrate diet and frequent administrations of UCCS to prevent hypoglycemia. 

The patient suffers from a refractory form of epilepsy, with daily crisis. The MRI showed subcortical gliosis, right parieto-occipital atrophy, with enhanced sulcus and smaller right hemisphere. The electroencephalogram revealed interictal epileptic discharges in the right frontal region. 

The patient is currently under therapy with levetiracetam, oxcarbamazepine, clobazam, sertraline, folic acid, ferrous sulfate, and omeprazole.

Discharge Conditions:

The patient is being discharged with ongoing therapy and follow-up appointments arranged. They are to follow their prescribed regiment and update their medical care team with any concerns. 

Discharge Diagnoses: 

- Glycogen storage disease type Ia
- Refractory epilepsy
- Hepatocellular adenoma
- Sideropenic anemia
- Polycystic ovaries
- Short stature

Discharge Medications:

N/A.
clean_ref:
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:

Name: [redacted]
Age: 28
Gender: Female
Medical Record Number: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]

Clinical Course:

The patient was admitted for seizures and very low blood sugar. This was caused by a rare condition where the body cannot release stored sugar properly. She was first given nutrition through an IV, then through a feeding tube. Later, special starches were added to her diet to help keep her blood sugar stable. Lab tests suggested she had this condition. A gene test confirmed she has a specific change in a gene that causes this disease.

As a child, the patient was often in the hospital for seizures and infections in the stomach, gut, and throat. At age 16, doctors found her liver was enlarged and had several noncancerous growths. She also has weak bones, low iron in her blood, and cysts on her ovaries. The patient has low levels of uric acid and normal fat levels in her blood.

The patient has high levels of a protein that helps move fat in the blood. Other related proteins are at normal levels. She has been short and had learning difficulties since childhood. The patient is overweight and has fatty lumps under her skin. This is due to a high-starch diet and frequent doses of raw cornstarch to prevent low blood sugar.

The patient has a hard-to-treat form of seizures that happen every day. A brain scan showed scarring, shrinking of part of the right side of the brain, and deeper grooves in the brain. A test of brain waves showed abnormal electrical activity in the right front part of the brain.

The patient is currently taking medicine for seizures, depression, low iron, folic acid, and stomach acid.

Discharge Conditions:

The patient is being discharged with ongoing therapy and follow-up appointments arranged. They are to follow their prescribed treatment plan and update their medical care team with any concerns.

Discharge Diagnoses:

A rare disease that prevents the body from releasing stored sugar properly.
Hard-to-treat seizures.
Noncancerous liver growths.
Low iron in the blood.
Cysts on the ovaries.
Short height.

Discharge Medications:

N/A.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

Patient Name: [insert name]               Medical Record Number: [insert number]

Reason for Admission:
The patient was admitted due to suspicion of a mycotic aortic aneurysm and severe infection.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent intravenous antibiotic therapy for four weeks and was started on tazobactam/piperacillin hydrate. Computed tomography angiography showed a descending aortic aneurysm measuring 70 × 70 mm, which extended 20 mm in only 2 weeks. Transcatheter endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) was performed under local anesthesia using two 37 × 200 mm Conformable stent grafts (W. L. Gore & Associates, Inc., Newark, DE, USA) to prevent aortic rupture.

Upon discharge, the patient was afebrile with a normal white blood cell count and C-reactive protein level. Oral antibiotics were continued for 18 months. The patient underwent monitoring by computed tomography 15 months after the TEVAR, and the aneurysm had regressed significantly, measuring 53 × 53 mm. The patient had no further signs of infection at the time of writing.

Diagnosis:
Descending aortic aneurysm

Procedures:
Transcatheter endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR)

Condition at Discharge:
The patient was discharged without serious complications.

Follow-Up:
The patient was monitored via computed tomography 15 months after TEVAR repair, at which point there was significant regression of the aneurysm. The patient was free from antibiotics at the time of writing.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

Patient Name: [insert name]               Medical Record Number: [insert number]

Reason for Admission:
The patient was admitted because doctors thought there was a bulge in the main blood vessel caused by an infection. The patient also had a serious infection.

Hospital Course:
The patient received antibiotics through a vein for four weeks. The medicine used was tazobactam/piperacillin hydrate. A special scan of the blood vessels showed a bulge in the lower part of the main artery. The bulge measured 70 by 70 mm. It had grown 20 mm in just 2 weeks. Doctors did a procedure to fix the bulge using a tube inserted through a blood vessel. The patient was awake but the area was numbed. Doctors placed two mesh tubes inside the artery to stop it from bursting. The mesh tubes measured 37 by 200 mm and were made by W. L. Gore and Associates, Inc., Newark, DE, USA.

When the patient left the hospital, there was no fever. Blood tests for infection were normal. The patient took antibiotic pills for 18 months. A scan was done 15 months after the procedure. The bulge had shrunk a lot and now measured 53 by 53 mm. The patient showed no signs of infection when this report was written.

Diagnosis:
Bulge in the lower part of the main artery

Procedures:
Repair of the artery bulge using a tube inserted through a blood vessel

Condition at Discharge:
The patient left the hospital without serious problems.

Follow-Up:
A scan was done 15 months after the repair. The bulge had shrunk a lot. The patient had stopped taking antibiotics when this report was written.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 26 years

Gender: Male

Presentation and Admission: The patient presented with chronic diarrhea, occasional rectal bleeding, and intermittent diffuse abdominal pain for the last nine months. He also complained of anasarca and dystrophic nail changes for the last three months. The patient denied any vomiting and had no significant change in appetite. There was no fever, cough, abdominal lump, or extraintestinal manifestations. On examination, he had mild anemia, dystrophic nail changes of both upper and lower limbs, and diffuse hyperpigmentation of the skin. His BMI was 18 kg/m2.

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to the hospital for further evaluation, and a comprehensive workup was conducted to determine the underlying cause of his symptoms. The initial tests showed mild anemia and dystrophic nail changes. A skin biopsy was performed, and the results indicated benign melanonychia. The patient was given dietary advice, including increasing his caloric intake and maintaining a healthy weight.

Discharge Diagnosis: The patient was diagnosed with chronic diarrhea, rectal bleeding, and dystrophic nail changes.

Discharge Medications: None prescribed.

Follow-up Recommendations: The patient was advised to follow-up with his primary care physician for continued management of his symptoms, including anemia, dystrophic nail changes, and chronic diarrhea.

Discharge Instructions: The patient was discharged in stable condition. The patient was advised to continue with a healthy and well-balanced diet. The patient was advised to avoid smoking and alcohol consumption. It is recommended to follow-up with his primary care physician for further evaluation and treatment.

Disposition: Discharged

Principal Diagnosis: Chronic diarrhea, rectal bleeding, dystrophic nail changes

Complications: None


Attending Physician: [REDACTED]

Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Medical History: A 12-year-old female patient was admitted with a history of polyuria, polydipsia, and secondary amenorrhea.
Clinical Findings: A complete preoperative endocrinological assessment revealed mild prolactin hormone elevation, hypothyroidism, and hypocortisolism. Neuroradiological investigation showed a large mass in the sella with compression of the optic chiasm and extending into the third ventricle.
Diagnosis: A craniopharyngioma or a GCT were the suspected diagnoses. However, the symptoms presented by the patient suggested a germinoma.
Procedure: The patient underwent an extended endoscopic endonasal transtuberculum/transplanum approach for tumor removal.
Postoperative Course: The patient demonstrated a significant improvement in her condition with light perception reported in the left eye and improvement in right eye temporal hemianopsia. There were no postoperative complications.
Postoperative Follow-up: Serum levels of AFP, and beta-HCG were checked, suggesting confirmation of germinoma malignancy. The patient was referred to a pediatric oncologist for further treatment, which includes adjunctive chemotherapy and radiation therapy.
Condition at Discharge: The patient was discharged in a normal condition but still receiving desmopressin acetate for diabetes insipidus treatment.
Prognosis: The patient's condition remains optimistic, and a follow-up appointment is necessary.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Medical History: A 12-year-old female patient was admitted. She had been urinating a lot, feeling very thirsty, and her periods had stopped.

Clinical Findings: Doctors did hormone tests before surgery. The tests showed slightly high levels of a hormone called prolactin. The tests also showed her thyroid gland was underactive. Her body was not making enough stress hormones. Brain scans showed a large growth in the lower part of the brain. This growth was pressing on the nerves that control vision. It was also reaching into a fluid-filled space in the brain.

Diagnosis: Doctors first thought it might be one of two types of brain tumors. But based on the patient's symptoms, they believed it was a type called germinoma.

Procedure: The patient had surgery to remove the tumor. Doctors went through the nose using a small camera and tools. This allowed them to reach the tumor without opening the skull.

Postoperative Course: The patient got much better after surgery. She could see light with her left eye. Her side vision in her right eye also improved. There were no problems after surgery.

Postoperative Follow-up: Blood tests checked for certain markers. These results confirmed the tumor was a type of cancer called germinoma. The patient was sent to a children's cancer doctor for more treatment. She will need chemotherapy and radiation therapy.

Condition at Discharge: The patient left the hospital in normal condition. She is still taking a medicine called desmopressin acetate. This treats a condition where her body cannot control water balance.

Prognosis: The outlook for the patient is hopeful. She will need to come back for a follow-up visit.

Discharge Summary:
Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Patient Name: [PATIENT NAME]
Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Hospital Course:
The patient, [PATIENT NAME], was admitted to the hospital for elective intracardiac repair due to incomplete shone syndrome. She presented with Grade III DOE and was diagnosed with severe MS due to supramitral ring, moderate MR, severe periductal CoA, large PDA, severe pulmonary arterial hypertension, moderate TR, and severe LV dysfunction. On [DATE], the patient underwent ligation of PDA and resection of coarctation with end-to-end anastomosis of the descending thoracic aorta through lateral thoracotomy approach. This was followed by resection of the supramitral ring under CPB that was carried out through sternotomy in a single setting. The postoperative patient had mild MR and mild TR with LVEF of 30%. The patient had an uneventful course postoperatively and was discharged on [DATE].

Discharge Diagnosis: Incomplete Shone Syndrome, Severe Mitral Stenosis, Moderate Mitral Regurgitation, Severe Periductal Coarctation, Large Patent Ductus Arteriosus, Severe Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension, Moderate Tricuspid Regurgitation, and Severe Left Ventricular Dysfunction.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow up with her cardiologist for regular monitoring and management of her cardiac condition. The patient should also continue taking her prescribed medications and follow a healthy lifestyle with a balanced diet and regular exercise. In case of any symptoms such as chest pain, shortness of breath, or palpitations, the patient should immediately seek medical attention.

Follow-Up:
Cardiology follow-up appointment on [DATE].

Provider Signature: [PROVIDER SIGNATURE]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:
Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Patient Name: [PATIENT NAME]
Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Hospital Course:
The patient, [PATIENT NAME], was admitted to the hospital for a planned heart repair surgery. The patient has a rare heart condition present from birth called incomplete Shone syndrome. The patient came in with severe trouble breathing during normal activities. The patient was found to have a severely narrowed heart valve between the upper and lower left heart chambers. This was caused by a ring of tissue that should not be there. The patient also had a moderately leaky valve in the same area. The patient had a severe narrowing of the main blood vessel leaving the heart. There was also a large opening between two blood vessels that should have closed after birth. The patient had severely high blood pressure in the lungs. The valve on the right side of the heart was moderately leaky. The left lower heart chamber was pumping very poorly. On [DATE], the patient had surgery through the side of the chest. The doctors closed the extra opening between the blood vessels. They also removed the narrowed part of the main blood vessel and reconnected the healthy ends. Next, through a cut in the chest bone, the doctors removed the extra ring of tissue near the heart valve. The heart was connected to a machine that pumped blood during this part of the surgery. After surgery, the heart valves had only mild leaking. The left lower heart chamber was pumping at 30 percent strength. The patient recovered well after surgery with no problems. The patient was sent home on [DATE].

Discharge Diagnosis: The patient has a rare birth defect affecting the left side of the heart. The patient has a severely narrowed valve between the upper and lower left heart chambers. The patient has a moderately leaky valve in the same area. The patient has a severe narrowing of the main blood vessel leaving the heart. The patient has a large opening between two blood vessels that should have closed after birth. The patient has severely high blood pressure in the lungs. The patient has a moderately leaky valve on the right side of the heart. The left lower heart chamber is pumping very poorly.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should see the heart doctor regularly to check on the heart condition. The patient should keep taking all medicines as directed. The patient should eat healthy foods and exercise regularly. If the patient has chest pain, trouble breathing, or feels the heart racing, the patient should get medical help right away.

Follow-Up:
Heart doctor appointment on [DATE].

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient name: [REDACTED]
Medical record number: [REDACTED]

Admission date: [REDACTED] | Discharge date: [REDACTED]

Admitting diagnosis: Subcutaneous emphysema

Hospital course:

The patient presented to the Accident and Emergency Department with a history of fall and sustaining a trivial laceration to the right olecranon region. The wound was cleaned and closed with adhesive skin strips and the patient was discharged with analgesia without any radiological evaluation.

Five to six hours later, the patient returned with extensive swelling of the right forearm. Radiograph of the right forearm revealed subcutaneous emphysema from distal arm to the dorsum of wrist. The patient was then referred to the tertiary hospital for further evaluation and management.

On examination at the tertiary hospital, the patient had localised redness at the wound site over the olecranon region. Crepitus was palpable throughout the forearm on both flexor and extensor compartments extending proximally into the distal arm and distally onto the dorsal aspect of wrist. Radiographs of the forearm and arm revealed air in the subcutaneous tissues of the right lower arm, whole forearm and dorsal aspect of the wrist. The patient was afebrile with stable vital signs. There was no evidence of infection on the blood tests with normal white cell count and C-reactive protein.

A dressing was applied over the laceration and the arm rested in a sling. The patient was admitted for observation and prophylactic antibiotics. Swab taken from the wound was negative for any microorganisms and the patient was discharged after 48 hours.

At the last review, three weeks after discharge, there was complete healing of the laceration with resolution of the subcutaneous emphysema.

Discharge medications: None

Follow-up instructions: The patient is advised to make an appointment with the primary care provider for further monitoring.

Signed by: [REDACTED]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient name: [REDACTED]
Medical record number: [REDACTED]

Admission date: [REDACTED] | Discharge date: [REDACTED]

Admitting diagnosis: Air trapped under the skin

Hospital course:

The patient came to the Emergency Department after a fall. The patient had a small cut on the back of the right elbow. The wound was cleaned and closed with sticky strips. The patient was sent home with pain medicine. No x-rays were taken at that time.

Five to six hours later, the patient came back with major swelling in the right forearm. An x-ray of the right forearm showed air trapped under the skin. The air went from the lower arm to the back of the wrist. The patient was then sent to a larger hospital for more care.

At the larger hospital, doctors found redness at the wound site on the back of the elbow. When pressed, the skin made a crackling feeling throughout the forearm. This was on both the front and back of the forearm. It spread up into the lower arm and down to the back of the wrist. X-rays showed air under the skin in the right lower arm, whole forearm, and back of the wrist. The patient had no fever and normal vital signs. Blood tests showed no signs of infection. White blood cell count and inflammation markers were normal.

A bandage was placed over the cut. The arm was rested in a sling. The patient was kept in the hospital to watch for problems. The patient was given medicine to prevent infection. A sample taken from the wound showed no germs. The patient went home after 48 hours.

At the last check-up, three weeks after leaving the hospital, the cut had fully healed. The air under the skin was completely gone.

Discharge medications: None

Follow-up instructions: The patient is advised to make an appointment with the regular doctor for further monitoring.

Signed by: [REDACTED]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 32-year-old female with chief complaint of pain in upper anterior tooth.

Hospital Course:
The patient reported to the Department of Conservative Dentistry and Endodontics and was diagnosed with an impacted permanent canine and a palatally placed upper permanent right lateral incisor. A radiograph showed loss of lamina dura and slight radiolucency in relation to the periapical area of the upper right central incisor. A CBCT revealed a periapical lesion related to both roots of the central incisor and an additional root positioned more facially than expected. A nonsurgical endodontic therapy was decided after a discussion with the patient. The root canals were cleaned and shaped, copiously irrigated, and obturated with corresponding Protaper gutta-percha and AH Plus sealer. The patient refused restoration of the facial groove with composite resin.

Outcome:
The patient was asymptomatic after 2 weeks and at a one-year follow-up. The patient was satisfied with the treatment outcome and would continue to be monitored by her dentist. 

Impression:
The patient presented with a rare case of an additional root present with the central incisor, which was successfully treated with nonsurgical endodontic therapy.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 32-year-old female who came in because of pain in an upper front tooth.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the dental department and was found to have a stuck adult canine tooth and an upper right side tooth that was positioned toward the roof of the mouth. An x-ray showed bone loss and a small dark area near the root tip of the upper right front tooth. A 3D scan showed an infection at the root tips of the front tooth and an extra root that was more toward the front of the mouth than normal. After talking with the patient, the team chose to do a root canal treatment without surgery. The root canals were cleaned, shaped, rinsed well, and filled with rubber-like filling material and a sealing paste. The patient chose not to have a groove on the front of the tooth filled with tooth-colored filling material.

Outcome:
The patient had no pain or problems after 2 weeks and at a one-year checkup. The patient was happy with how the treatment turned out and would keep seeing her dentist for regular checkups.

Impression:
The patient had a rare case of an extra root on her front tooth, which was treated successfully with root canal treatment without surgery.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Age: 23 years
Gender: Female
Date of Admission: [redacted]
Date of Discharge: [redacted]

Reason for Admission:
The patient presented with a history of scalp scaling and itching accompanied by hair loss for 2 years, and scaly plaques on both feet associated with fissuring for 8 years.

Hospital Course:
Physical examination of the scalp revealed diffuse white scales, while the soles showed thick and diffuse scaling. Oral cavity and nails were clinically normal.

Dermatoscopic examination of the scalp revealed peripilar tubular casts with miniaturized vellus hairs, granular grey dots, white dots, and crystalline structures predominantly around hair follicles. Furthermore, histopathological examination of the scalp exhibited basket weave hyperkeratosis, follicular plugging, basal cell vacuolization in the epidermis, and band-like lymphohistiocytic infiltrate in the superficial dermis extending around the infundibulum till the proximal part of the isthmus of the hair follicle with pigment incontinence. Biopsy features were consistent with LPP, and direct immunofluorescence was negative. Histopathology of the sole showed compact hyperkeratosis, acanthosis, and vertical streaking of collagen in the papillary dermis without interface changes.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with Lichen Planopilaris (LPP), which is indicated by basket weave hyperkeratosis, follicular plugging, and band-like lymphohistiocytic infiltrate observed in the histopathological examination.

Treatment:
The discharge summary does not contain any information about the patient's treatment plan.

Follow-up:
The patient will follow up with her healthcare provider for proper management of her LPP. 

Disposition:
The patient was discharged and was advised to continue her follow-up with her healthcare provider.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Age: 23 years
Gender: Female
Date of Admission: [redacted]
Date of Discharge: [redacted]

Reason for Admission:
The patient came in with a flaky, itchy scalp and hair loss for 2 years. The patient also had thick, scaly skin on both feet with cracks for 8 years.

Hospital Course:
A physical exam showed white flaky skin spread across the scalp. The soles of the feet had thick, widespread flaky skin. The mouth and nails looked normal.

A close-up exam of the scalp showed tube-shaped buildup around hairs, thin baby-fine hairs, and grey and white dots. There were also crystal-like shapes mainly around hair roots. A skin sample from the scalp was studied under a microscope. It showed a thick outer skin layer and blocked hair openings. There was damage to the bottom layer of skin cells. White blood cells had gathered in the upper skin layer around the hair roots. There was also color leaking into the skin. These findings matched a condition called LPP. A special dye test came back normal. A skin sample from the sole showed thick outer skin, thickened middle skin, and stretched tissue fibers in the upper skin layer. There were no signs of skin cell damage at the base.

Diagnosis:
The patient was found to have Lichen Planopilaris, also called LPP. This is a condition that causes swelling around hair roots and can lead to hair loss. The diagnosis was based on the skin sample findings.

Treatment:
The discharge summary does not contain any information about the patient's treatment plan.

Follow-up:
The patient will see her healthcare provider for care of her LPP.

Disposition:
The patient was sent home. The patient was told to keep seeing her healthcare provider.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient 4, an 8-month-old female, was admitted due to episodic binocular upward gaze for 7 months. She also had delayed growth development and was unable to sit alone steadily until the present. The patient's physical examination showed paroxysmal slanting neck, and the Children's Developmental Center of China scores were 78/81, indicating a deviation from the normal reference. 

No specific perinatal and family history was reported. Episodic upward gaze started when the patient was 1 month old, occurring daily after waking up and resolved in about 10 seconds without physical movements. The recurrent episodes lasted up to several hours with no apparent relationship to fatigue or infection. 

The patient underwent several examinations, including a brain MRI and an EEG. Both tests showed no abnormalities during the episodes, but slow waves in the posterior brain were observed during the interictal period. Topiramate 2.5 mg was found to be ineffective in treating the episodes.

After careful evaluation and consideration, the patient was discharged and advised to follow up with a pediatric neurologist for further evaluation and management.

Hospital Course Summary

Patient 4, an 8-month-old female, was admitted due to episodic binocular upward gaze for 7 months. The patient underwent several examinations, including a brain MRI and an EEG. Both tests showed no abnormalities during the episodes, but slow waves in the posterior brain were observed during the interictal period.

The patient received several medications, including valproic acid, methocarbamol, and topiramate. However, the patient discontinued these medications due to ineffectiveness or exacerbation of episodes.

The patient's general state was observed to be good during the interictal period, and no evidence showed the episodes were related to fatigue or infection. Upon careful evaluation and consideration, the patient was advised to follow up with a pediatric neurologist for further evaluation and management.

Discharge Summary

Patient 4 is an 8-month-old baby girl. She was admitted to the hospital because her eyes kept rolling upward. This has been happening for 7 months. She also has slower growth than expected. She cannot sit up on her own yet. During the physical exam, doctors noticed her neck sometimes tilts to one side. Her development test scores were 78 out of 81. This is lower than what is normal for her age.

There were no problems during pregnancy or birth. There is no family history of this condition. The eye-rolling episodes started when she was 1 month old. They happen every day after she wakes up. Each episode lasts about 10 seconds. Her body does not move during these episodes. The episodes can repeat for several hours. Being tired or sick does not seem to cause these episodes.

The patient had several tests done. These included a brain scan and a test to measure brain waves. Both tests looked normal during the episodes. Between episodes, the brain wave test showed slower activity in the back of the brain. A medicine called Topiramate at 2.5 mg did not help stop the episodes.

After careful review, the patient was sent home. She should see a children's brain and nerve doctor for more tests and care.

Hospital Course Summary

Patient 4 is an 8-month-old baby girl. She was admitted because her eyes kept rolling upward for 7 months. She had a brain scan and a brain wave test. Both tests looked normal during the episodes. Between episodes, the brain wave test showed slower activity in the back of the brain.

The patient was given several medicines. These included valproic acid, methocarbamol, and topiramate. She stopped taking all of these medicines. They either did not work or made the episodes worse.

Between episodes, the patient seemed healthy overall. Being tired or sick did not seem to cause the episodes. After careful review, she should see a children's brain and nerve doctor for more tests and care.
