input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 12-year-old Japanese girl (III-1 in Fig. b)

Medical History: Patient presented with recurrent fevers, abdominal pain, diarrhea, ankle arthralgia, oral ulcers, pharyngalgia, and enlarged tonsils from 2 months of age. She also had perianal ulcers, bloody stool, and weight loss at 4 and 5 years old, respectively. At 9 years old, she was diagnosed with left ankle arthritis and prescribed naproxen and methotrexate, which were discontinued due to abdominal pain. She was treated with colchicine and mesalazine for abdominal pain, but was later found to have multiple ulcers throughout her intestinal tract. At 11 years old, she was diagnosed with bilateral non-granulomatous acute anterior uveitis and Behcet's disease.

Hospital Course: Patient was treated with colchicine, cimetidine, and corticosteroid eye drops. Her abdominal pain has improved and fevers are less frequent.

Assessment and Plan: Patient is to continue treatment with colchicine, cimetidine, and corticosteroid eye drops. Follow-up appointments will be scheduled as needed. Further monitoring and evaluation will be discussed as necessary.

Discharge Diagnosis: Behcet's disease with gastrointestinal involvement and bilateral non-granulomatous acute anterior uveitis.

Discharge Summary:

Patient: 12-year-old Japanese girl (III-1 in Fig. b)

Medical History: The patient has had fevers that keep coming back, belly pain, loose stools, ankle joint pain, mouth sores, sore throat, and swollen tonsils since she was 2 months old. She also had sores around her bottom, blood in her stool, and weight loss at ages 4 and 5. At 9 years old, doctors found swelling in her left ankle joint. She was given naproxen and methotrexate, but these were stopped because of belly pain. She was then treated with colchicine and mesalazine for belly pain. Later, doctors found many sores throughout her digestive tract. At 11 years old, she was found to have eye swelling in both eyes without lumpy tissue buildup. She was also diagnosed with Behcet's disease, a condition that causes ongoing inflammation in different parts of the body.

Hospital Course: The patient was treated with colchicine, cimetidine, and steroid eye drops. Her belly pain has gotten better. Her fevers do not happen as often.

Assessment and Plan: The patient should keep taking colchicine, cimetidine, and steroid eye drops. Follow-up visits will be set up as needed. More check-ups and tests will be talked about when necessary.

Discharge Diagnosis: Behcet's disease affecting the digestive system and eye swelling in the front part of both eyes without lumpy tissue buildup.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: _____________
60-year-old Female

Admission Date: 
Discharge Date: 

Admission Diagnosis: A painless, progressive swelling in the pre-sternal region

Hospital Course:
During the hospitalization, ultrasonography, fine needle aspiration cytology, computed tomography, and Magnetic resonance imaging were performed. The findings showed a 15 cm × 8 cm vertically aligned, non-tender, well defined swelling on the pre-sternal region. The patient had undergone a total thyroidectomy for papillary carcinoma 10 years back. It was suspected that the previous cancer had recurred. PET scan was performed which confirmed the increased FDG uptake by the lesion and the level IV lymph node. USG-guided fine needle aspiration cytology was done again and confirmed a Bethesda IV category. The patient underwent wide excision of the mass with right functional neck dissection. Post-operative radioactive iodine ablation 70 mCi was given approximately 8 weeks after the surgery. The patient was monitored for a total of 30 months and no recurrence was reported.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Well differentiated papillary thyroid carcinoma

Discharge Medications:
None prescribed.

Discharge Instructions:
Weight Limitation:
Avoid lifting any weight that is more than 10 pounds or 4.53 kgs for the next two weeks.

Exercise:
Avoid any strenuous exercise for the next two weeks.

Driving:
The patient is advised not to drive for at least 1 week to allow for proper recovery.

Follow-up:
The patient is advised to follow-up with their physician in three months, and if there is no recurrence, then in six months. 

Dietary Instructions:
The patient is advised to continue with their normal diet without any restriction. 

Laboratory Results:
Thyroglobulin levels have been less than 1 microgram/L. 

Summary:
The patient presented a painless, progressive swelling in the pre-sternal region that was diagnosed as well differentiated papillary thyroid carcinoma. Wide excision of the mass with right functional neck dissection was done, and post-operative radioactive iodine ablation 70 mCi was given. The patient is advised to follow-up with their physician regularly.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: _____________
60-year-old Female

Admission Date:
Discharge Date:

Admission Diagnosis: A painless lump in front of the chest that kept growing

Hospital Course:
During the hospital stay, several tests were done. These included an ultrasound, a needle test to check cells, a CT scan, and an MRI scan. The tests showed a large lump in front of the chest. The lump measured 15 cm by 8 cm, stood upright, was not painful, and had clear edges. The patient had surgery to remove the entire thyroid gland 10 years ago. That surgery was done because of thyroid cancer. Doctors thought the old cancer might have come back. A PET scan was done to check for cancer activity. The scan showed the lump and a lymph node in the neck were active. Another needle test was done using ultrasound to guide it. The results suggested the lump might be cancer. The patient had surgery to remove the lump completely. Lymph nodes on the right side of the neck were also removed. About 8 weeks after surgery, the patient received a radiation treatment using radioactive iodine. The dose was 70 mCi. Doctors watched the patient for 30 months total. The cancer did not come back during that time.

Discharge Diagnosis:
A slow-growing type of thyroid cancer called papillary thyroid cancer

Discharge Medications:
None prescribed.

Discharge Instructions:
Weight Limitation:
Do not lift anything heavier than 10 pounds or 4.53 kilograms for the next two weeks.

Exercise:
Do not do hard exercise for the next two weeks.

Driving:
The patient should not drive for at least 1 week. This allows time to heal properly.

Follow-up:
The patient should see their doctor in three months. If the cancer has not come back, the next visit will be in six months.

Dietary Instructions:
The patient can eat their normal foods. There are no food limits.

Laboratory Results:
A blood test called thyroglobulin has stayed below 1 microgram per liter. This is a good sign.

Summary:
The patient had a painless lump in front of the chest that kept growing. Tests showed it was a slow-growing type of thyroid cancer. Surgery removed the lump and lymph nodes on the right side of the neck. The patient received radiation treatment about 8 weeks after surgery. The patient should see their doctor regularly for checkups.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 47-year-old edentulous female with maxillary complete denture

Chief Complaint: Dysfunctional maxillary complete denture

History of Present Illness: The patient presented with an unuseful maxillary complete denture, resulting from cervical radiotherapy. She had a limited oral opening and insufficient saliva quantity and flowability.

Hospital Course: The patient's treatment plan consisted of fabricating complete dentures with proper extension of the flanges. Flexible impression trays were used to obtain preliminary impressions for both arches. Custom trays for the final impressions were fabricated with autopolimerized acrylic resin. Final maxillary impressions were made by using polysulfide material alternately for the first and second halves of the sectional trays. Record bases were fabricated and occlusion rims were prepared. A centric relation record was made, and the record was transferred from the patient to a semiadjustable articulator. The try-in was evaluated to verify jaw relations and teeth arrangement. After patient acceptance, final festooning and flasking of the dentures were completed. The final dentures were manufactured using similar designs to that of the record bases. The prostheses were deflasked, finished, and polished. The patient was provided with instructions for cleaning, inserting, and removing the prostheses. She was recalled for follow-up visits to check for the maintenance and her adaptability with the new dentures.

Outcome: The patient reported difficulty in the insertion and removal of denture initially, but progressively, she became used to it.

Follow-up: The patient will continue to be monitored during follow-up visits to check the maintenance and adaptability of her new dentures.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 47-year-old female who has no natural teeth and wears a full upper denture

Chief Complaint: Upper denture that does not work properly

History of Present Illness: The patient came in because her upper denture was not working well. This problem happened because of radiation treatment she had on her neck area. She could not open her mouth very wide. She also did not have enough saliva, and her saliva did not flow well.

Hospital Course: The care team planned to make new full dentures with edges that fit better. They used bendable trays to take first molds of both the upper and lower mouth. They made custom trays for the final molds using a self-hardening plastic material. They took final upper molds using a rubber-like material on one half of the tray at a time. They made base plates and wax rims to help set the bite. They recorded how the jaws line up when closed and moved this information to a device that copies jaw movement. They checked the test dentures to make sure the jaw position and tooth placement were correct. After the patient agreed the fit was good, the team shaped and prepared the dentures in molds. They made the final dentures using the same design as the base plates. They removed the dentures from the molds, smoothed them, and shined them. The patient received instructions on how to clean, put in, and take out her dentures. She was asked to come back for checkup visits to make sure the dentures were in good shape and that she was adjusting to them.

Outcome: The patient had trouble putting in and taking out her denture at first. Over time, she got used to doing this.

Follow-up: The patient will keep coming to checkup visits. The care team will make sure her dentures are in good condition and that she is adjusting well to them.

Hospital Course Summary:

Admission:
A 43-year-old male with primary hypothyroidism was admitted for evaluation of progressive fatigue, weakness, muscle and joint pain in the setting of recurrent fractures.

History of Present Illness:
The patient had been experiencing musculoskeletal pain in several locations including bilateral knees, ankles, left shoulder, left arm, and lower back which affected his overall functional status. He struggled to climb a flight of stairs, often grabbing the railing to pull himself up. A nuclear medicine bone scan showed abnormal uptake in bilateral ribs, vertebrae, right knee, hips, ankles, and feet. A lower extremity MRI showed stress fractures in left fibula, fifth metatarsal, and right tibia.

Course in Hospital:
The patient was started on calcitriol and phosphate supplements which significantly improved muscle weakness. Due to concern for TIO, a positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) scan with 68Gallium (68Ga)-DOTATATE was performed which showed multiple lytic osseous lesions with increased somatostatin positive uptake seen throughout pelvis, ribs, ankles, and feet. However, after re-review with radiology, multiple areas of increased uptake were considered to be secondary to fractures, and left posterior acetabular lesion was presumed to be the tumor of interest given a remarkably higher standard uptake values (SUVmax) in this lesion compared to other lesions (SUVmax 20 vs. 4–5).

Procedure:
The left acetabular lesion was biopsied and pathology demonstrated a 1.4 cm phosphaturic mesenchymal tumor of the left acetabulum with uninvolved margins. The patient underwent radical resection of the tumor of the posterior left acetabulum with reconstruction of the posterior wall and posterior column of the acetabulum through a Kocher-Langenbach approach. His hip joint was able to be spared utilizing a 3D-printed custom bone cutting guide (CG) and intra-operative O-arm guided surgical navigation (Medtronic© Stealth) utilizing a navigated Sonopet Ultrasonic Bone Cutting Bovie (Stryker). The posterior wall and column of his acetabulum were constructed with a tantalum augment (Zimmer) and a pelvic reconstruction plate and screw construct (Synthes) after tumor removal.

Discharge Summary:
The patient was discharged with normal phosphorus and FGF23 levels, pain-free left hip, no evidence of hardware complication, back to hiking, and walking on the treadmill. Clinical and radiographic follow-up at 6 months postoperatively found him to be pain-free in his left hip, with almost complete resolution of bone pain, and no recurrence of fractures. Phosphorus level also remained normal at 3.4 mg/dL 6 months after surgery.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission:
A 43-year-old male with an underactive thyroid was admitted. Doctors needed to find out why he had ongoing tiredness, weakness, muscle and joint pain, and broken bones that kept happening.

History of Present Illness:
The patient had muscle and bone pain in many areas. These included both knees, both ankles, left shoulder, left arm, and lower back. This pain made it hard for him to do daily activities. He had trouble walking up stairs and had to pull himself up using the railing. A bone scan showed problem areas in both sides of his ribs, spine, right knee, hips, ankles, and feet. An imaging test of his lower legs showed small cracks in the left calf bone, a foot bone, and right shin bone.

Course in Hospital:
The patient was given vitamin D and phosphorus supplements. These greatly improved his muscle weakness. Doctors thought a rare tumor might be causing his problems. A special full-body scan was done to look for it. The scan showed many bone spots with holes that were picking up the scan marker. These spots were in the pelvis, ribs, ankles, and feet. After doctors looked at the scan again, most of these spots seemed to be from broken bones. One spot on the left hip socket stood out. It picked up much more of the scan marker than the other spots. This spot was most likely the tumor causing his problems.

Procedure:
Doctors took a small sample from the left hip socket spot. Testing showed a small tumor measuring 1.4 cm. This type of tumor causes the body to lose phosphorus. The edges of the sample were free of tumor cells. The patient had surgery to fully remove the tumor from the back of his left hip socket. Surgeons rebuilt the back wall and back support of the hip socket. They used a special approach from behind the hip. The hip joint itself was saved. Surgeons used a custom three-dimensional printed guide to cut the bone precisely. They also used a computer system to guide them during surgery. A special ultrasonic bone-cutting tool was used. After removing the tumor, the back wall and support were rebuilt. Surgeons used a metal support piece and a plate with screws made for pelvis repair.

Discharge Summary:
The patient went home with normal phosphorus levels. His hormone levels that control phosphorus were also normal. His left hip had no pain. The metal parts in his hip showed no problems. He was back to hiking and walking on a treadmill. At a checkup six months after surgery, he had no left hip pain. Almost all of his bone pain was gone. He had no new broken bones. His phosphorus level stayed normal at 3.4 mg per deciliter six months after surgery.

Electronic Health Record Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 50

Gender: Female

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Reason for Admission: Right Pancoast tumor

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our institution with a presentation of right arm pain due to a right Pancoast tumor diagnosed as adenocarcinoma by fine needle aspiration cytology. A thorough evaluation using chest computed tomography (CT), brain CT, positron emission tomography (PET), and subsequent thoracoscopy showed N0 status diagnosed by CT and PET; the involvement of the right brachiocephalic and subclavian veins was also highly suspected at the pleural inspection (stage cT4N0M0). The patient underwent four cycles of cisplatin and Pemetrexed plus 60 Gy irradiation with partial tumor regression seen on CT while PET scan became negative.

Surgical Intervention:

The patient underwent successful Video-Assisted Thoracoscopic Surgery (VATS) lobectomy for the right upper lobe of the lung. The surgery was preceded by thoracoscopy to evaluate the resectability of the tumor. The procedure was judged feasible and a V-shaped contra-incision was made along the anterior border of the right sternocleidomastoid muscle, extended parallel to the second intercostal space. Through VATS accesses, sections of the right superior bronchus and the remaining fissure were completed, and the “en bloc” specimen was extracted through the apical incision. After lymphadenectomy, the sternum was sutured with metallic stitches and the anterior defect of the thoracic wall was repaired with a synthetic patch.

Outcome:

Postoperative pain remained under control and never exceeded four on the visual analog scale. Neither intraoperative nor postoperative complications were observed and the patient was discharged after nine days. The patient's pathological result was ypT0N0M0. At the twelve-month follow-up, the patient was free from disease and post-thoracotomy syndrome.
clean_ref:
Electronic Health Record Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 50

Gender: Female

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Reason for Admission: A tumor at the top of the right lung called a Pancoast tumor

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with right arm pain caused by a tumor at the top of the right lung. A needle test showed the tumor was a type of lung cancer called adenocarcinoma. Doctors used several scans to learn more about the tumor. These included chest scans, brain scans, and a full body scan to check for cancer spread. The cancer had not spread to the lymph nodes. However, doctors believed the tumor was affecting two major blood vessels near the collarbone. The patient received four rounds of chemotherapy using two drugs called cisplatin and Pemetrexed. The patient also received radiation treatment totaling 60 doses. After treatment, scans showed the tumor had gotten smaller. The full body scan showed no remaining signs of active cancer.

Surgical Intervention:

The patient had successful surgery to remove the upper part of the right lung. Doctors used a camera and small cuts to do this surgery. Before the main surgery, doctors looked inside the chest to make sure the tumor could be removed safely. The surgery was possible, so doctors made a V-shaped cut along the neck muscle. This cut continued along the space between the second and third ribs. Using the camera tools, doctors cut the airway tube to the upper lung and separated the lung section. The tumor and lung section were removed together through the top cut. Doctors then removed nearby lymph nodes to check for cancer. They closed the chest bone with metal stitches. They used a man-made patch to repair the opening in the chest wall.

Outcome:

Pain after surgery stayed under control and never went above four out of ten on the pain scale. There were no problems during or after the surgery. The patient went home after nine days in the hospital. Lab tests on the removed tissue showed no remaining cancer cells. At the twelve-month checkup, the patient had no signs of cancer. The patient also had no long-term pain problems from the surgery.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Sex: Male
Age: 39
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Diagnosis: Allergy to ketamine and morphine

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our hospital with a diagnosis of chronic rhinosinusitis. He had a history of multiple anaphylactic reactions during surgical procedures and eye examinations performed under general anesthesia at various hospitals, since he was 11 years old. The patient and his relatives had been reluctant to come to the hospital due to extreme fear and discouragement for 10 years after his last episode of anaphylaxis during eye enucleation at the age of 29.

Following his evaluation by the allergist, skin testing was performed for morphine and ketamine, using standard concentrations according to the EAACI recommendation. The first test results were negative but due to his history of severe reactions, further testing was recommended 6 weeks later. The second round of skin tests was positive for ketamine and morphine. Hence, we provided a recommendation for the patient to avoid ketamine and morphine for future anesthetic procedures. The ADR card was updated accordingly, and alternative drugs were suggested, tested negative and were used for the patient's sinus surgery, which was performed without any adverse reactions.

The patient was advised to maintain close observation for allergic symptoms in the case of future anesthetic procedures with alternative drugs. The patient expressed full understanding of the recommendations and was discharged home in good condition. A comprehensive discharge report was provided to the patient and his carers.

Discharge Medications:

None recommended.

Follow-up:

The patient is advised to follow-up with the allergist as needed.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Sex: Male
Age: 39
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Diagnosis: Allergic to ketamine and morphine (two drugs used to make patients sleep during surgery)

Hospital Course:

The patient came to our hospital because of long-term sinus problems. Since the age of 11, the patient had several serious allergic reactions during surgeries and eye exams. These reactions happened when doctors gave medicine to make him sleep. The patient and his family were very scared to go to hospitals for 10 years. This fear started after his last serious allergic reaction at age 29 during eye removal surgery.

An allergy doctor checked the patient. The doctor did skin tests for morphine and ketamine using standard amounts. The first tests showed no allergy. But because the patient had such bad reactions before, doctors did more tests 6 weeks later. The second round of skin tests showed the patient is allergic to ketamine and morphine. We told the patient to never use ketamine or morphine for future surgeries. We updated the patient's drug allergy card. We tested other drugs that could be used instead. Those drugs did not cause a reaction. Doctors used these safe drugs for the patient's sinus surgery. The surgery went well with no allergic reactions.

The patient was told to watch carefully for allergy signs if given other sleep medicines in the future. The patient understood all the instructions. The patient went home feeling well. We gave the patient and his caregivers a full report about this hospital stay.

Discharge Medications:

None recommended.

Follow-up:

The patient should see the allergy doctor if needed.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: N/A (protected health information removed)
Gender: Male
Age: 33 years old
Admission Date: N/A (not specified)
Discharge Date: N/A (not specified)
Admitting Diagnosis: Papillary oral mass (right ventral surface of the tongue)
Final Diagnosis: Oral VX
Treatment Provided: Excisional biopsy

Hospital Course: 
A 33-year-old male patient was admitted to the oral pathology center with a non-tender, pink to yellowish mass with a papillary surface on the right ventral surface of the tongue. The lesion consistency was soft on palpation, and laboratory results were normal. An excisional biopsy was performed, and microscopic sections showed papillary hyperparakeratotic acanthotic epithelium with macrophages with foamy cytoplasm (xanthoma cells). The diagnosis of oral VX was confirmed based on clinical and histopathologic features. The patient was free of disease within 15 months following the operation.

Discharge Diagnosis: Oral VX
Follow-up: The patient is advised to follow up regularly with his primary care provider.

Discharge Instructions:
1. Protect the surgical area from trauma and infection.
2. Keep the surgical area clean and dry.
3. Do not consume hot or spicy foods.
4. If any complications arise, seek medical attention immediately.
5. Follow up regularly with your primary care provider to monitor any changes in your health.

Discharge Medications: None.

Name of Attending Physician: N/A (not specified)

Date and Time of Discharge: N/A (not specified)

Signature of Discharge Physician: N/A (not specified)

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: N/A (protected health information removed)
Gender: Male
Age: 33 years old
Admission Date: N/A (not specified)
Discharge Date: N/A (not specified)
Admitting Diagnosis: A bumpy growth on the right underside of the tongue
Final Diagnosis: Oral verruciform xanthoma (a rare, harmless mouth growth)
Treatment Provided: Surgery to remove the growth for testing

Hospital Course:
A 33-year-old male patient came to the mouth disease center with a painless, pink to yellowish bumpy growth on the right underside of the tongue. The growth was soft to the touch. All lab tests came back normal. The doctors did surgery to remove the growth and look at it under a microscope. The tissue showed a bumpy outer layer with special fat-filled cells inside. Based on how the growth looked and what the tissue showed, the doctors confirmed it was a harmless mouth growth called oral verruciform xanthoma. The patient showed no signs of the growth coming back within 15 months after surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis: Oral verruciform xanthoma (a rare, harmless mouth growth)
Follow-up: The patient should see his regular doctor for checkups.

Discharge Instructions:
1. Protect the surgery site from injury and germs.
2. Keep the surgery site clean and dry.
3. Do not eat hot or spicy foods.
4. If any problems happen, get medical help right away.
5. See your regular doctor for checkups to watch for any changes in your health.

Discharge Medications: None.

Name of Attending Physician: N/A (not specified)

Date and Time of Discharge: N/A (not specified)

Signature of Discharge Physician: N/A (not specified)

Based on how the growth looked and what the tissue showed, the doctors confirmed it was a harmless mouth growth called oral verruciform xanthoma. The patient showed no signs of the growth coming back within 15 months after surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis: Oral verruciform xanthoma (a rare, harmless mouth growth)
Follow-up: The patient should see his regular doctor for checkups.

Discharge Instructions:
1. Protect the surgery site from injury and germs.
2. Keep the surgery site clean and dry.
3. do eat hot or spicy foods.
4. If any problems happen, get medical help right away.
5. See your regular doctor for checkups to watch for any changes in your health.

Discharge Medications: None.

Name of Attending Physician: N/A (not specified)

Date and Time of Discharge: N/A (not specified)

Signature of Discharge Physician: N/A (not specified)

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: N/A (anonymous)
Gender: Male
Age: 23
Medical Record Number: N/A

Hospital Course:
The patient was referred to the Endocrinology Department for follow-up of an abdominal PGL that had been operated during childhood and subsequently relapsed. He had undergone two surgical interventions that were unsuccessful in completely removing the tumor mass. The patient had a 123I-MIBG scintigraphy which was negative and a 68GaDOTATATE-PET that revealed the tumor to express somatostatin receptors. Due to this, he underwent therapy with somatostatin analogs and a radiometabolic treatment with 90Y-DOTATATE. At the time of referral to our Endocrinology Department, the patient had local relapse of the disease, associated with multiple bone metastases. The biochemical evaluation showed high chromogranin-A levels, urinary catecholamines, and urinary vanilmandelic acid. Radiological assessment revealed evidence of progressive abdominal disease. Therapy with somatostatin analogs was continued and therapy with sunitinib was started.

Current Condition:
Patient's condition has been stable since initiation of therapy with somatostatin analogs, which has been continued along with sunitinib.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has not been discharged and will continue as an outpatient in the Endocrinology Department.

Follow-up:
The patient will continue therapy with somatostatin analogs and sunitinib as an outpatient. Due to the presence of a mutation in the SDHB gene, the patient's family members have undergone genetic testing. The patient's mother and sister resulted negative, and further genetic workup of the father will be pursued. The patient will continue to be closely monitored by the Endocrinology Department.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: N/A (anonymous)
Gender: Male
Age: 23
Medical Record Number: N/A

Hospital Course:
The patient was sent to the Hormone Department for care of a rare tumor in the belly area. This tumor was removed during childhood but came back. The patient had two surgeries, but doctors could not remove all of the tumor. The patient had a special scan called 123I-MIBG that did not show the tumor. Another scan called 68GaDOTATATE-PET showed the tumor had certain proteins on its surface. Because of this, the patient received hormone-blocking drugs and a radiation treatment called 90Y-DOTATATE. When the patient came to our Hormone Department, the tumor had come back in the same spot. The tumor had also spread to the bones in several places. Blood and urine tests showed high levels of certain chemicals that the tumor makes. These chemicals were chromogranin-A, catecholamines, and vanilmandelic acid. Imaging tests showed the belly tumor was getting worse. The patient kept taking hormone-blocking drugs and started a new cancer drug called sunitinib.

Current Condition:
The patient has been stable since starting the hormone-blocking drugs. The patient continues to take these drugs along with sunitinib.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has not been sent home. The patient will keep getting care at the Hormone Department as an outpatient.

Follow-up:
The patient will keep taking hormone-blocking drugs and sunitinib while visiting the clinic. The patient has a change in a gene called SDHB that can be passed down in families. Because of this, family members had genetic testing. The patient's mother and sister do not have this gene change. The father will have more genetic testing. The Hormone Department will keep watching the patient closely.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The 62-year-old male was admitted to the hospital with a complaint of abdominal pain, vomiting, and ambulatory dysfunction. The initial examination showed no abnormalities. The patient was suspected to have viral gastroenteritis, and hence he was hospitalized for dehydration management. The patient's nausea and vomiting improved after admission, but it was noted that he was leaning backwards and to the right when standing or attempting to ambulate on the first day of hospitalization.

Diagnostic Evaluations:
The neurology service was consulted after assessing the patient's condition on his second day of admission. A CT angiogram of the head and neck showed a congenitally hypoplastic left vertebral artery and no other abnormality was observed in the vertebrobasilar system. MRI of the brain showed acute/subacute infarct in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The echocardiogram showed moderate diastolic dysfunction with trace aortic regurgitation.

Clinical Impression:
The patient was diagnosed with acute/subacute infarct in the right cerebellar hemisphere secondary to chronic atherosclerotic disease that had extended and caused APS. The left-sided dysmetria was observed, which further indicated his neurological impairment.

Hospital Treatment:
The patient was monitored on telemetry for several days without any events or abnormalities noted. He was started on a scheduled antiemetic regimen of metoclopramide 10 mg four times daily and ondansetron 8 mg every eight hours that yielded positive results. Within one day, the patient's nausea and vomiting were controlled, and he was able to eat without difficulty. The patient was successfully discharged from the hospital to rehabilitation.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient is recommended to follow up with the neurology service as an outpatient and undergo regular checkups to monitor his neurological function and the progression of his cerebrovascular disease. He is also advised to maintain strict blood glucose control and hypertension management. The family members are recommended to observe the patient's activities, as he is more vulnerable to falls.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The 62-year-old male came to the hospital with belly pain, vomiting, and trouble walking. The first exam showed nothing wrong. Doctors thought he had a stomach bug and kept him in the hospital to treat fluid loss. His upset stomach and vomiting got better after he was admitted. On the first day, staff noticed he was leaning backward and to the right when standing or trying to walk.

Diagnostic Evaluations:
The brain and nerve doctors were called in on the second day. A scan of the blood vessels in his head and neck showed he was born with a smaller than normal artery on the left side of his neck. No other problems were found in those blood vessels. A brain scan showed he had a recent stroke in the right side of the back part of his brain. A heart test showed the heart does not relax well between beats. It also showed a small amount of blood leaking back through one heart valve.

Clinical Impression:
The patient was found to have a recent stroke in the right side of the back part of his brain. This was caused by long-term hardening of the arteries that had spread and blocked blood flow. The left side of his body showed trouble with coordination. This pointed to damage in his brain function.

Hospital Treatment:
The patient's heart rhythm was watched for several days with no problems found. He was given medicine to stop nausea. He took metoclopramide 10 mg four times a day and ondansetron 8 mg every eight hours. This helped him feel better. Within one day, his upset stomach and vomiting stopped. He was able to eat without problems. The patient left the hospital and went to a rehab center.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient should see the brain and nerve doctors for regular visits. These visits will check how his brain is working and watch for changes in his blood vessel disease. He should also keep his blood sugar and high blood pressure under control. Family members should watch him closely during activities because he has a higher chance of falling.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 20-year-old female diagnosed with pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and von Willebrand Disease (vWD) who presented to the clinic at 13 weeks of gestation. The patient underwent a caesarian section at 26 weeks of gestation due to decompensated PAH and fetal bradycardia. Post-procedure, the patient went into cardiac arrest and was placed on femoral-femoral venoarterial (VA) ECMO for life support. She remained in the ICU for an extended period of time and required continuous inotropes and vasopressors support. After a prolonged stay in the hospital, she was discharged on postoperative day 101.

Discharge Diagnosis:

1. Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH)
2. Von Willebrand Disease (vWD)

Procedures:

1. emergent caesarian section
2. femoral-femoral venoarterial (VA) ECMO
3. pericardial window

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was diagnosed with PAH and vWD, and presented at 13 weeks of gestation. Despite counseling sessions, the patient decided to continue her pregnancy, and all teratogenic medications were stopped while tadalafil and treprostinil were continued. The patient was admitted and transitioned from SC treprostinil to intravenous epoprostenol. The patient did well on this regimen until 25 weeks of gestation when she presented to the emergency department with complaints of recurrent hemoptysis and acute respiratory distress. Due to concerns of further decompensation, an emergent caesarian section was performed, and a healthy infant was delivered. However, during the closure of the hysterotomy, the patient went into cardiac arrest and was placed on VA ECMO for life support. The patient remained in the ICU for a prolonged period and required continuous inotropes and vasopressors support. After successful management, the patient was weaned off ECMO support and had a prolonged stay in the hospital. The patient was subsequently discharged on postoperative day 101.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 20-year-old female with high blood pressure in the lungs and a bleeding disorder. She came to the clinic when she was 13 weeks pregnant. The patient had an emergency surgery to deliver the baby at 26 weeks of pregnancy. This was done because her lung condition got worse and the baby's heart rate dropped too low. After the surgery, the patient's heart stopped beating. She was placed on a machine that pumped blood for her heart and added oxygen to her blood. She stayed in the intensive care unit for a long time. She needed medicines to help her heart pump and keep her blood pressure stable. After a long hospital stay, she went home 101 days after her surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis:

1. High blood pressure in the lungs
2. A bleeding disorder that prevents blood from clotting normally

Procedures:

1. Emergency surgery to deliver the baby
2. A machine to pump blood and add oxygen when the heart cannot work on its own
3. A small surgery to drain fluid from around the heart

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was diagnosed with high blood pressure in the lungs and a bleeding disorder. She came to the clinic at 13 weeks of pregnancy. Doctors advised her about the risks, but she chose to continue her pregnancy. Doctors stopped medicines that could harm the baby. They kept her on two safe medicines for her lung condition. The patient was admitted to the hospital. Doctors changed one medicine given under her skin to a different medicine given through a vein. The patient did well on these medicines until 25 weeks of pregnancy. Then she came to the emergency room. She was coughing up blood and having trouble breathing. Doctors were worried her condition would get worse. They did emergency surgery and delivered a healthy baby. But during the surgery to close her womb, the patient's heart stopped beating. She was placed on a machine to pump her blood and add oxygen. The patient stayed in the intensive care unit for a long time. She needed medicines to help her heart pump and keep her blood pressure stable. After successful treatment, doctors slowly took her off the blood pumping machine. She stayed in the hospital for a long time to recover. The patient went home 101 days after her surgery.

input:
Hospital Course:
A 66-year-old female patient was admitted to the Department of Periodontics, M. S. Ramaiah Dental College and Hospital, Bangalore, with the complaint of burning sensation in the gums and bleeding gums for the past one month.

Medical History:
The patient had no relevant drug history or significant medical history. She had not received any dental treatment before.

Examination Findings:
On extra oral examination, the patient had some obvious skin lesions on the scalp and a hyperkeratotic plaque behind her left ear. Intra oral examination of gingiva revealed reddish marginal, attached gingiva and interdental papilla in both maxillary and mandibular anterior region with desquamation of epithelium present in certain regions.

Diagnosis:
Based on the clinical and histopathologic features, the lesion was suggestive of discoid lupus erythematosus.

Treatment:
Hand scaling was performed as gently as possible in order to minimize tissue disruption. Soft tooth brush and mouth rise (chlorhexidine 0.12%) was recommended. Triamcinolone acteonide oral paste was advised to apply two to three times per day.

Follow-up:
The patient was recalled for follow-up and maintenance.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
A 66-year-old female patient came to the gum care department at M. S. Ramaiah Dental College and Hospital in Bangalore. She had a burning feeling in her gums and bleeding gums for the past month.

Medical History:
The patient was not taking any medicines and had no major health problems. She had never had dental treatment before.

Examination Findings:
The dentist looked at the outside of the patient's face and body. The patient had some skin patches on her scalp. She also had a thick, rough patch of skin behind her left ear. The dentist then looked inside the patient's mouth. The gums around the upper and lower front teeth were red. This included the gums at the edges, the gums attached to the teeth, and the gums between the teeth. In some areas, the top layer of gum tissue was peeling off.

Diagnosis:
Based on what the dentist saw and lab test results, the patient likely has discoid lupus erythematosus. This is a condition where the body's defense system attacks its own skin and gum tissue.

Treatment:
The dentist gently cleaned the teeth to remove buildup. This was done carefully to avoid hurting the gums. The patient was told to use a soft toothbrush and rinse with chlorhexidine 0.12 percent mouthwash. The patient was also told to apply triamcinolone acetonide paste inside the mouth two to three times per day. This paste helps reduce soreness and swelling.

Follow-up:
The patient was asked to come back for check-up visits and ongoing care.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]          Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Pain at left temporomandibular joint (TMJ) and numerous radiopaque masses around left TMJ

Discharge Diagnosis: Synovial chondromatosis

Medical History: None provided

Hospital Course: The patient, a 50-year-old male, presented at Dankook University Dental Hospital with complaints of pain and swelling at his left TMJ and numerous radiopaque masses around the joint on panoramic radiography. The patient showed spontaneous and opening pain and swelling at the left TMJ, but showed no tenderness or limitation of mouth opening. Through imaging tests, including panoramic imaging, CBCT, and MR imaging, numerous radiopaque loose bodies of various shapes and sizes surrounding the left condyle, and hypertrophy with sclerotic changes on the anterior and lateral sides of the temporal bone, the articular eminence, and the articular fossa were observed. A surgical excision under general anesthesia was performed to remove approximately 120 pieces of the loose bodies. Histopathologic examination confirmed the diagnosis of synovial chondromatosis. On a postoperative panoramic image, remaining flat hypertrophy of the articular eminence was observed, without any loose bodies. The patient's symptoms were relieved, and no signs of recurrence were observed at 1-month and 3-month follow-up visits.

Discharge Condition: The patient's condition has improved significantly, and he is discharged from the hospital in good condition.

Current Medications Upon Discharge: None provided

Follow-Up Care: The patient is advised to continue to attend follow-up visits to monitor his condition.

Instructions at Discharge: The patient is instructed to avoid any strenuous activities for a few weeks, avoid opening his mouth too wide, and not to eat hard or chewy foods for at least a week after the surgery. The patient is advised to continue oral hygiene practices, including brushing and flossing regularly.

Primary Care Physician: None provided

Attending Physician: [REDACTED]

Other contributors to care: None provided
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]          Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Pain at the left jaw joint and many small solid masses seen on X-ray around the left jaw joint

Discharge Diagnosis: Synovial chondromatosis (a condition where small, hard pieces of cartilage grow inside a joint)

Medical History: None provided

Hospital Course: The patient, a 50-year-old male, came to Dankook University Dental Hospital. He had pain and swelling at his left jaw joint. X-rays showed many small solid masses around the joint. The patient had pain on his own and when opening his mouth. He also had swelling at the left jaw joint. He did not have soreness when touched or trouble opening his mouth. Doctors used different imaging tests to look at the joint. These included regular X-rays, 3D X-rays, and MRI scans. The tests showed many loose pieces of different shapes and sizes around the lower jaw bone. The tests also showed thickened and hardened bone on the front and side of the skull bone near the joint. Doctors did surgery while the patient was fully asleep. They removed about 120 loose pieces from the joint. Lab tests on the removed pieces confirmed the diagnosis of synovial chondromatosis. An X-ray taken after surgery showed some flat, thickened bone still in the joint area. No loose pieces remained. The patient felt better after surgery. At check-up visits one month and three months later, there were no signs the condition had come back.

Discharge Condition: The patient has improved a lot. He is leaving the hospital in good condition.

Current Medications Upon Discharge: None provided

Follow-Up Care: The patient should keep going to check-up visits so doctors can watch his condition.

Instructions at Discharge: The patient should not do hard physical activities for a few weeks. The patient should not open his mouth too wide. The patient should not eat hard or chewy foods for at least one week after surgery. The patient should keep taking care of his teeth and gums by brushing and flossing every day.

Primary Care Physician: None provided

Attending Physician: [REDACTED]

Other contributors to care: None provided

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Medical Record Number: [redacted]
Sex: Male
Age: 44

Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]

Diagnosis: Severe maxillary atrophy and previous mandibular all-on-6 rehabilitation with current peri-implantitis

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our department due to complaints of mobility on the inferior implant-supported prosthesis and the superior prosthetic bridge, causing difficulty in chewing and speech. The patient reported a history of congenital dental agenesis, with complete mandibular rehabilitation with six implants and a superior bilateral canine to central incisor prosthetic bridge.
Clinical examination and orthopantomography indicated a combined horizontal and vertical osseous deficiency and active peri-implantitis in all six implants previously placed in the inferior arch, confirmed through cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT). Maxillary vertical and alveolar ridge deficiency was also noted. Bimaxillary custom-made implants were proposed, using an innovative design in the mandible including areas of endosseous support for adequate osseointegration.

The patient underwent surgery under general anesthesia. On the maxilla, a crestal incision was performed, exposing the anterior nasal spine, the pyriform apertures, the canine fossae, the zygomatic buttresses, and the posterolateral maxillae. The implant was tested and fixed with osteosynthesis screws. On the mandible, a crestal incision was performed around the arch to the contralateral side. The implant was tested, fixed with osteosynthesis screws, and bone grafting was carried out in the endosseous zone.

Abutments were placed, and flaps were closed. Prosthetic impressions were taken immediately after closure, and a provisional prosthesis was successfully adapted 12 h later, before patient discharge.

Follow-Up:

Over 1 year and 3 months of follow-up time, there were no complications. The patient reported excellent implant stability, adequate masticatory function, no implant exposure, and absence of pain.

Summary:

The patient with severe maxillary atrophy and previous mandibular all-on-6 rehabilitation with current peri-implantitis underwent bimaxillary custom-made implants. The surgery was successful with no complications, and the patient reported significant improvement in implant stability, masticatory function, and absence of pain after 1 year and 3 months of follow-up time.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Medical Record Number: [redacted]
Sex: Male
Age: 44

Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]

Diagnosis: Severe bone loss in the upper jaw and infection around the six dental implants in the lower jaw from a previous procedure

Hospital Course:

The patient came to our department because the lower implant-supported teeth and upper dental bridge were loose. This made it hard to chew and speak. The patient was born missing many teeth. The patient had six implants placed in the lower jaw and a bridge on the upper teeth in the past.

A physical exam and jaw x-rays showed bone loss in both height and width. All six implants in the lower jaw had infection around them. A detailed 3D scan confirmed this. The upper jaw also had bone loss. The care team suggested placing custom-made implants in both jaws. The lower jaw implants used a new design with areas that go into the bone for better attachment.

The patient had surgery while fully asleep. For the upper jaw, the surgeon made a cut along the gum line. This opened up the front and side areas of the upper jaw bone. The implant was tested and secured with small metal screws. For the lower jaw, the surgeon made a cut along the gum from one side to the other. The implant was tested, secured with small metal screws, and bone graft material was added to help healing.

Posts were attached to the implants, and the gums were stitched closed. Molds for the new teeth were taken right after surgery. Temporary teeth were fitted 12 hours later, before the patient left the hospital.

Follow-Up:

Over 1 year and 3 months of check-ups, there were no problems. The patient said the implants felt very stable. Chewing worked well. The implants stayed covered by gum tissue. The patient had no pain.

Summary:

The patient with severe upper jaw bone loss and infected lower jaw implants received custom-made implants in both jaws. The surgery went well with no problems. After 1 year and 3 months, the patient reported much better implant stability, good chewing ability, and no pain.

The upper jaw also had bone loss. The care team suggested placing custom-made implants in both jaws. The lower jaw implants used a new design with areas that go into the bone for better attachment.

The patient had surgery while fully asleep. For the upper jaw, the surgeon made a cut along the gum line. This opened up the front and side areas of the upper jaw bone. The implant was tested and secured with small metal screws. For the lower jaw, the surgeon made a cut along the gum from one side to the other. The implant was tested, secured with small metal screws, and bone graft material was added to help healing.

Posts were attached to the implants, and the gums were stitched closed. Molds for the new teeth were taken right after surgery. Temporary teeth were fitted 12 hours later, before the patient left the hospital.

Follow-Up:

Over 1 year and 3 months of check-ups, there were no problems. The patient said the implants felt very stable. Chewing worked well. The implants stayed covered by gum tissue. The patient had pain.

Summary:

The patient with severe upper jaw bone loss and infected lower jaw implants received custom-made implants in both jaws. The surgery went well with no problems. After 1 year and 3 months, the patient reported much better implant stability, good chewing ability, and no pain.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 65 years
Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: 5th postoperative day

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with left-sided neck pain and swelling with a gradual onset of dysphagia. A linear foreign body with horizontal orientation was seen in the superior part of the pharynx along with a 40 × 20 mm collection around it on a CT scan. There was also thrombosis with an air bubble in the left internal jugular vein and the distal end of the fishbone was adjacent to the left internal jugular vein. Surgery was performed to remove the impacted fishbone, repair the pharyngeal perforation, ligate the injured internal jugular vein, and drain the abscess cavities. The patient made an uneventful recovery and was started on a liquid diet on the 4th postoperative day.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Left-sided neck abscess with hypopharyngeal fishbone impaction and associated thrombosis of the left internal jugular vein.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was discharged on the 5th postoperative day and was advised to follow up with her primary care physician for further evaluation and care. She was instructed to avoid solid foods and to continue on a liquid diet for the first week after discharge. Pain management should continue as needed, and the Penrose drain should remain in place until it is removed by her primary care physician. The patient was advised to avoid smoking, alcohol, and any activities that could lead to excessive stress on the neck and to maintain proper oral hygiene. She was advised to watch for any signs of infection, such as fever, chills, and redness or swelling around the incision site. If any of these symptoms occur, she should seek medical attention immediately.

Follow-Up:
The patient was advised to follow up with her primary care physician within the next seven days for a review of her medical condition and to manage her medications. The patient should continue to be monitored closely for any potential complications relating to the abscess and internal jugular vein ligation.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 65 years
Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: 5th day after surgery

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with pain and swelling on the left side of her neck. She also had trouble swallowing that got worse over time. A scan showed a fishbone stuck sideways in the upper part of her throat. There was also a pocket of infected fluid about 40 by 20 millimeters around the fishbone. A blood clot with an air bubble had formed in a large vein on the left side of her neck. The end of the fishbone was touching this vein. The patient had surgery to remove the stuck fishbone. The doctors also fixed the hole in her throat and tied off the damaged vein. They drained the pockets of infected fluid. The patient healed well without problems. She started drinking liquids on the 4th day after surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient had a pocket of infection on the left side of her neck. This was caused by a fishbone stuck in her lower throat. She also had a blood clot in the large vein on the left side of her neck.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient went home on the 5th day after surgery. She should see her regular doctor for more care and checkups. She should not eat solid foods for the first week after leaving the hospital. She should only drink liquids during this time. She should keep taking pain medicine as needed. The drain tube in her neck should stay in place. Her regular doctor will take it out later. The patient should not smoke or drink alcohol. She should not do anything that puts too much strain on her neck. She should keep her mouth clean. She should watch for signs of infection. These include fever, chills, and redness or swelling near the cut. If she notices any of these signs, she should get medical help right away.

Follow-Up:
The patient should see her regular doctor within the next seven days. The doctor will check how she is healing and manage her medicines. The patient needs to be watched closely for any problems. These could be related to the infection or the vein that was tied off.

Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course: The patient underwent a simple orthodontic preprosthetic treatment to address a scissor-bite between the upper and lower second molars on the right side. A buccal orthodontic button was placed on the crown of the upper second molar and another button on the lingual surface of the lower second molar. An orthodontic elastic was worn all day, except during meals and oral hygiene. The patient was recalled monthly to monitor tooth movements. After a few months, the patient experienced discomfort in the gum at the level of the second lower molar, where the rubber band was applied. A clinical examination showed slight mobility of the lower molar and a periapical radiograph revealed a small periodontal angular defect mesial to the molar. Elastic use was suspended, and the lingual button removed. Further treatment included subgingival root planning under anesthesia, chlorhexidine gel insertion, and a systemic antibiotic therapy. A week later, rather than an improvement, significant mobility of the lower molar was observed and the worsening of the mesial angular defect affecting the root length was noted. Intraoral elastic around the root was discovered during the extraction procedure, which was not visible in the radiological examination.

Condition at Discharge: The patient was discharged after extraction of the second lower molar.

Discharge Medications: None prescribed.

Discharge Instructions: The patient was advised to maintain a good oral hygiene routine, including regular brushing and flossing. The patient was instructed to avoid hard and sticky foods for a few days after the extraction and to avoid using a straw for about a week. The patient was asked to contact the dentist if any signs of infection, such as swelling, fever, or pain, were observed.

Follow-Up Care: The patient is advised to schedule regular dental check-ups to ensure the good health of the remaining teeth and implant success.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course: The patient had braces treatment to fix a bite problem. The upper and lower back teeth on the right side were not lining up correctly. Small buttons were placed on the upper and lower back teeth. A small rubber band was worn all day except when eating or brushing teeth. The patient came back each month so the dentist could check how the teeth were moving. After a few months, the patient felt pain in the gum near the lower back tooth where the rubber band was placed. The dentist found that the tooth was slightly loose. An x-ray showed some bone loss next to the tooth. The rubber band was removed and the button was taken off. The dentist did a deep cleaning of the tooth root while the area was numbed. A germ-killing gel was placed in the area and the patient took antibiotics by mouth. One week later, the tooth became even more loose. The bone loss had gotten worse. When the dentist removed the tooth, a rubber band was found wrapped around the root. This rubber band did not show up on the x-ray.

Condition at Discharge: The patient left after having the lower back tooth removed.

Discharge Medications: No medicines were prescribed.

Discharge Instructions: The patient was told to keep brushing and flossing the teeth well. The patient should not eat hard or sticky foods for a few days after the tooth was removed. The patient should not drink through a straw for about one week. The patient should call the dentist if there are signs of infection such as swelling, fever, or pain.

Follow-Up Care: The patient should make regular dental appointments to keep the remaining teeth healthy and to check on any future tooth replacement.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Patient Name: [NAME]
Gender: Female
Age: 31 years
Medical Record Number: [NUMBER]

Hospital Course Summary:

[DATE]: The patient presented with dysmenorrhea and a cystic lesion in the vesico-uterine pouch was detected. The diagnosis of a cystic endometriotic lesion was established, and the plan for laparoscopic surgery was made.

[DATE]: The patient underwent oocyte retrieval and complained of severe abdominal pain six days after the procedure. She was diagnosed with peritonitis with an abscessed cystic endometriotic lesion in the vesico-uterine pouch. Conservative treatment with sulbactam/ampicillin was attempted but was judged to be ineffective, and laparoscopic surgery was performed. Drainage of the abscess and ablation of the cystic lesion were performed.

[DATE]: The patient's condition improved, and she was discharged home. Two months after surgery, CA125 was normalized to 36.9 U/mL. The first embryo transfer was performed 3 months after surgery at another hospital, resulting in pregnancy.

Diagnosis:
- Cystic Endometriotic Lesion in the Vesico-Uterine Pouch
- Peritonitis with an Abscessed Cystic Endometriotic Lesion in the Vesico-Uterine Pouch

Summary of Hospital Course:
The patient, a 31-year-old nulliparous Japanese woman, was admitted due to dysmenorrhea and a cystic lesion detected in the vesico-uterine pouch. She underwent laparoscopic surgery and was diagnosed with a cystic endometriotic lesion. Following oocyte retrieval, the patient developed peritonitis with an abscessed cystic endometriotic lesion. Conservative treatment was attempted but proved ineffective, and laparoscopic surgery was performed. Biopsy results confirmed the diagnosis of cystic endometriotic lesion. The patient's condition improved, and she was discharged home. Two months after surgery, CA125 was normalized to 36.9 U/mL. The first embryo transfer was successful resulting in pregnancy. 

Follow-up Plan:
- Continue infertility treatment with regular monitoring
- Follow-up with obstetrician-gynecologist as scheduled 
- Current medications include sulbactam/ampicillin doses which are not defined here
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Patient Name: [NAME]
Gender: Female
Age: 31 years
Medical Record Number: [NUMBER]

Hospital Course Summary:

[DATE]: The patient came in with painful periods. A fluid-filled sac was found in the space between the bladder and uterus. This sac was caused by tissue similar to the uterus lining growing where it should not. A plan was made to do surgery using a small camera and tools through tiny cuts.

[DATE]: The patient had eggs collected from her ovaries. Six days later, she had very bad belly pain. The fluid-filled sac had become infected and formed a pocket of pus. The infection also spread to the lining of the belly. The patient was given antibiotics through a vein, but they did not work well enough. Surgery using a small camera was done. The doctors drained the pus and removed the damaged tissue.

[DATE]: The patient got better and went home. Two months after surgery, a blood test that checks for this condition came back normal at 36.9 U/mL. Three months after surgery, the patient had a fertilized egg placed in her uterus at another hospital. This led to pregnancy.

Diagnosis:
Fluid-filled sac caused by uterus-like tissue growing in the space between the bladder and uterus
Infected fluid-filled sac with pus and belly lining infection in the space between the bladder and uterus

Summary of Hospital Course:
The patient, a 31-year-old Japanese woman who has never given birth, came in with painful periods. A fluid-filled sac was found in the space between her bladder and uterus. She had surgery using a small camera. Doctors found the sac was caused by tissue like the uterus lining growing in the wrong place. After having eggs collected from her ovaries, the sac became infected and filled with pus. The infection spread to her belly lining. Antibiotics did not work well enough, so surgery was done again. Tissue samples confirmed what the problem was. The patient got better and went home. Two months after surgery, her blood test results were normal at 36.9 U/mL. The first fertilized egg placement worked and led to pregnancy.

Follow-up Plan:
Continue fertility treatment with regular check-ups
See the women's health doctor as scheduled
Current medicines include antibiotics, but the exact amounts are not listed here

Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [Date of admission]
Discharge Date: [Date of discharge]

Chief Complaint: Fracture of jaw and missing central incisors

History of Present Illness: A 12-year-old female with a history of road side accident was brought to the Department of Oral Surgery with the chief complaint of fracture of lower jaw in parasymphysis region and missing both central incisors. She had no history of orthodontic treatment. On examination, the patient was found to have impacted both lower canine and crowding in both jaws.

Hospital Course: Nonextraction treatment was planned, and the treatment objective was to repair the jaw fracture, regain space for prosthetic replacement of central incisors, relieve crowding, and maintain the existing class I molar relationship. After wiring of the arch to hold the fracture segment on the same day, bone plating was done when the patient was fit for surgery. Once the fracture segment was healed, fixed mechanotherapy was started with Roth, 0.022'' appliances in both maxillary and mandibular arches to align, level and create space for the central incisors. The mandibular canines were kept in place as they were close to the screw and disimpaction was challenging due to their location on the fracture line. Riding pontics were prepared, assessed, and adjusted to the rectangular archwire by stainless steel ligature. The esthetic and smile of the patient was improved after ligating riding pontics.

Summary: The 12-year-old female patient with a history of fracture of lower jaw in parasymphysis region and missing both central incisors due to a road side accident successfully underwent the nonextraction treatment plan. After fixing the fracture segment, the patient's malocclusion was treated with fixed mechanotherapy which aligned and leveled both maxillary and mandibular arches and created space for the central incisors, while keeping the mandibular canines in place due to their location on the fracture line. The final step of the treatment was the preparation and ligating of riding pontics that significantly improved the patient's esthetic and smile.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [Date of admission]
Discharge Date: [Date of discharge]

Chief Complaint: Broken lower jaw and two missing front teeth

History of Present Illness: A 12-year-old girl was brought to the Oral Surgery Department after a roadside accident. She had a broken lower jaw near the chin area and was missing both lower front teeth. She had never had braces before. The doctors found that both lower eye teeth were stuck under the gums. Both her upper and lower teeth were also crowded.

Hospital Course: The doctors planned a treatment that would not require pulling any teeth. The goals were to fix the broken jaw, make room for replacement front teeth, fix the crowding, and keep the back teeth lined up correctly. On the same day, wires were placed to hold the broken jaw pieces together. When the patient was ready for surgery, a metal plate was attached to the bone. After the jaw healed, braces were placed on both the upper and lower teeth. The braces helped straighten the teeth and make space for the missing front teeth. The lower eye teeth were left in place because they were too close to the screw and hard to move due to where the break was. Temporary false teeth were made and attached to the braces with metal ties. The patient's appearance and smile looked much better after the false teeth were attached.

Summary: The 12-year-old girl had a broken lower jaw near the chin and two missing front teeth from a roadside accident. She completed a treatment plan that did not require pulling any teeth. After the broken jaw was fixed, braces were used to straighten the upper and lower teeth and make space for the front teeth. The lower eye teeth were left in place because of where the break was. The last step was attaching temporary false teeth to the braces. This greatly improved how the patient's smile looked.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 41
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Admission Diagnosis: Suspected COVID-19 infection

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with symptoms of a dry cough, shortness of breath, chest tightness, fever, chills, myalgias, mild sore throat, and watery diarrhea. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) tests ruled out several respiratory infections, but a COVID-19 test came back positive after intubation. A computed tomography scan of the chest showed bilateral infiltrates. The patient was then started on empiric treatment with IV vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam, azithromycin, and hydroxychloroquine.

Over time, her condition worsened, and she developed severe acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), requiring higher mechanical ventilation settings and continuous prone positioning. Liver enzymes continually increased, and acute kidney injury occurred due to decreased organ perfusion. 

The patient was given high-dose vitamin C, zinc sulfate, and tocilizumab to control her cytokine storm. Despite initial management, her condition still declined, and continuous venovenous hemodialysis (CVVHD) was started for renal failure, along with venovenous extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (VV-ECMO).

During the VV-ECMO treatment, the patient developed HIT and ischemia in her fingers and toes bilaterally. Although the HIT panel returned negative, Argatroban was administered as anticoagulation due to the significant drop in her platelet count on heparin products. Despite ongoing hemodynamic stability, the patient ultimately passed away four days after the initiation of VV-ECMO due to an asystole rhythm.

Discharge Plan:
The patient passed away during her VV-ECMO treatment. Hospice and supportive care were provided to the family. The next of kin was advised to follow up with her primary care provider for any further concerns.  No further treatment or diagnostic evaluations were scheduled.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 41
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Admission Diagnosis: Suspected COVID-19 infection

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with a dry cough, trouble breathing, chest tightness, fever, chills, body aches, mild sore throat, and watery diarrhea. Tests ruled out several lung infections, but a COVID-19 test came back positive after a breathing tube was placed. A special scan of the chest showed infection in both lungs. The patient was then started on medicines through an IV to fight infection, including vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam, azithromycin, and hydroxychloroquine.

Over time, her condition got worse. She developed a serious lung problem where the lungs cannot get enough oxygen into the blood. She needed stronger breathing machine settings and had to lie face down to help her breathe. Her liver showed signs of damage, and her kidneys started to fail because not enough blood was reaching her organs.

The patient was given high doses of vitamin C, zinc sulfate, and tocilizumab to control a dangerous overreaction of her immune system. Even with treatment, her condition kept getting worse. A machine was started to filter her blood because her kidneys failed. Another machine was added to put oxygen directly into her blood.

During treatment with the blood oxygen machine, the patient developed a blood clotting problem and loss of blood flow to her fingers and toes on both sides. Even though a blood test for this problem came back negative, a blood thinner called Argatroban was given because her blood cell count dropped while on another blood thinner. Even though her heart and blood pressure stayed stable, the patient died four days after starting the blood oxygen machine because her heart stopped beating.

Discharge Plan:
The patient passed away during her treatment with the blood oxygen machine. Comfort care and support were given to the family. The next of kin was told to follow up with her regular doctor for any further concerns. No further treatment or tests were scheduled.

input:
Discharge Summary

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 6-year-old female with mosaic Turner syndrome who was referred for cardiac evaluation secondary to her genetic condition. Physical exam revealed short stature, nevi on skin, and a broad-based gait. An echocardiogram was remarkable for severe apical and posterior wall left ventricular hypertrophy with a spongy appearance. The echocardiogram pictures raised concern for left ventricular noncompaction (LVNC), so a cardiac MRI was obtained. Cardiac MRI revealed increased trabeculations along the posterior apical aspect of left ventricle with noncompacted to compacted myocardial ratio >2.3 consistent with LVNC.

Medical Management:

The patient is being managed conservatively since she is clinically asymptomatic. Her follow-up visits with cardiology show preserved cardiac function (ejection fraction of 54% on the right side and 53% on the left side). She is receiving multidisciplinary care as an outpatient.

Plan:

The patient is recommended to receive continuous follow-up visits with cardiology to monitor her cardiac function. She is advised to continue with multidisciplinary care as an outpatient. 

Recommended Follow-up:

The patient is recommended to receive continuous follow-up visits with cardiology to monitor her cardiac function.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 6-year-old girl with a partial form of Turner syndrome. Turner syndrome is a genetic condition that affects girls. She was sent to a heart doctor because of her genetic condition. During the physical exam, doctors noticed she was shorter than average. She also had moles on her skin and walked with her feet wider apart than usual. A heart ultrasound showed that parts of her heart muscle were too thick. The lower left part of her heart had a spongy look. The heart ultrasound raised concern for a condition where the heart muscle does not form properly. Doctors ordered a special heart scan using magnets to get a better look. This scan showed extra finger-like tissue in parts of the lower left heart chamber. The results confirmed that the patient has a heart condition where the muscle did not fully develop before birth.

Medical Management:

The patient is being watched carefully without treatment because she has no symptoms. Her follow-up visits with the heart doctor show her heart is pumping well. The right side of her heart pumps out 54 percent of its blood with each beat. The left side pumps out 53 percent. She is seeing several different specialists as an outpatient.

Plan:

The patient should keep seeing her heart doctor regularly to check how well her heart is working. She should continue seeing her team of different specialists as an outpatient.

Recommended Follow-up:

The patient should keep seeing her heart doctor regularly to check how well her heart is working.

The patient is being watched carefully without treatment because she has no symptoms. Her follow-up visits with the heart doctor show her heart is pumping well. The right side of her heart pumps out 54 percent of its blood with each beat. The left side pumps out 53 percent. She is seeing several different specialists as an outpatient.

Plan:

The patient should keep seeing her heart doctor regularly to check how well her heart is working. She should continue seeing her team of different specialists as an outpatient.

Recommended Follow-up:

The patient should keep seeing her heart doctor regularly to check how well her heart is working.
