Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 49
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to Mayo Clinic for diagnostic evaluation of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) due to an ECG result from 1 year earlier, which showed concentric LV wall thickening, normal LV systolic function, and grade 2 diastolic dysfunction. The patient received a trial treatment with angiotensin-receptor blockers and β-blockers, which resulted in recurrent syncopal episodes. The patient reported numbness of hands and erectile dysfunction, as well as respiratory distress and orthopnoea. The patient's physical examination showed orthostatic hypotension, periorbital purpura, macroglossia with indentation marks, bibasilar pulmonary crackles, S3 and S4 cardiac auscultation, and increased jugular venous pressure of 15 cm H2O with profound peripheral oedema.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient's condition on discharge is stable. The patient shows improvement in his signs and symptoms, and has no recurrence of respiratory distress or syncopal episodes. However, the patient's hypertrophic cardiomyopathy remains unchanged, and the patient requires continued monitoring and follow-up.

Instructions on Discharge:
The patient is instructed to follow a heart-healthy diet and engage in physical activity as recommended by his healthcare provider. The patient should avoid strenuous exercise or activities that may cause syncope or fainting. The patient is advised to continue taking any prescribed medications and to follow up with his healthcare provider in 2 weeks. In case of any emergency situation, the patient should seek medical attention immediately.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow up with his healthcare provider in 2 weeks. The patient should schedule appointments for routine monitoring of his hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and any other medical conditions he may have.

Summary:
The patient was admitted to Mayo Clinic for diagnostic evaluation of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM). The patient's physical examination showed orthostatic hypotension, periorbital purpura, macroglossia with indentation marks, bibasilar pulmonary crackles, S3 and S4 cardiac auscultation, and increased jugular venous pressure. The patient's condition on discharge is stable, and he is instructed to follow a heart-healthy diet, engage in physical activity as recommended, and avoid strenuous exercise. The patient should follow up with his healthcare provider in 2 weeks for routine monitoring.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 49
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to Mayo Clinic for tests to check for a heart condition called thickened heart muscle. An older heart test from 1 year ago showed the heart walls were thicker than normal. The heart was pumping well but had trouble relaxing between beats. The patient tried two types of blood pressure medicines. These medicines caused the patient to faint several times. The patient also had numbness in the hands and problems with sexual function. The patient had trouble breathing, especially when lying down. The physical exam showed blood pressure that dropped when standing up. The patient had purple bruising around the eyes and a swollen tongue with teeth marks. The doctor heard crackling sounds in the lower parts of both lungs. The doctor heard extra heart sounds when listening to the chest. The veins in the neck were swollen, showing high pressure in the heart. The patient also had severe swelling in the legs and feet.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient is in stable condition at discharge. The patient's symptoms have improved. The patient no longer has breathing problems or fainting spells. However, the thickened heart muscle condition has not changed. The patient needs ongoing checkups and care.

Instructions on Discharge:
The patient should eat foods that are good for the heart. The patient should exercise as the doctor recommends. The patient should not do hard exercise or activities that could cause fainting. The patient should keep taking all medicines the doctor prescribed. The patient should see the doctor again in 2 weeks. If there is an emergency, the patient should get medical help right away.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient should see the doctor in 2 weeks. The patient should make appointments for regular checkups to watch the thickened heart muscle condition. The patient should also get care for any other health problems.

The patient should see the doctor in 2 weeks for regular checkups.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Proband C
Gender: Female
Age: 83
Medical Record Number: #######

Hospital Course:
Proband C was admitted for treatment of a kumquat-like GDLD subtype with neovascularization in both of her eyes. She had a history of lamellar keratoplasty along with keratoepithelioplasty in her left eye at the age of 72 and penetrating keratoplasty in her right eye at the age of 82. After admission, she underwent surface keratectomy for her left eye to remove the superficial amyloid depositions.

Assessment:
Post-surgery, the patient showed characteristic findings of GDLD subtype recurrence in both of her eyes and was unable to wear soft contact lenses.

Plan:
A soft contact lens is known to be protective for the postoperative GDLD cornea. However, in this case, the patient was unable to use them. There were no further surgical interventions undertaken during admission, as she was too elderly to undergo more surgeries.

Follow-up:
The patient's condition will be kept under observation, and follow-up arrangements will be made as necessary in coordination with her ophthalmologist.

Summary:
Proband C, an 83-year-old Japanese female, was admitted for the treatment of GDLD cornea. Following the surgery and a surface keratectomy, postoperative recurrence of her condition was observed. Given that the patient was unable to wear soft contact lenses, no further surgical interventions were undertaken. Prognosis for the patient will be kept under observation, and a follow-up with the ophthalmologist will be scheduled.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Proband C
Gender: Female
Age: 83
Medical Record Number: #######

Hospital Course:
Proband C was admitted for treatment of a rare eye disease called GDLD. This disease causes bumpy growths and new blood vessel growth in both of her eyes. She had partial cornea transplant surgery and surface cell replacement on her left eye at age 72. She had a full cornea transplant on her right eye at age 82. The cornea is the clear front part of the eye. After admission, she had surgery on her left eye to remove protein buildup on the surface.

Assessment:
After surgery, the patient showed signs that her eye disease had returned in both eyes. She was not able to wear soft contact lenses.

Plan:
Soft contact lenses can help protect the eye after surgery for this disease. However, the patient was not able to wear them. No more surgeries were done during this hospital stay. The patient is too old to have more surgeries safely.

Follow-up:
The patient's condition will be watched closely. Follow-up visits will be set up as needed with her eye doctor.

Summary:
Proband C is an 83-year-old Japanese female. She was admitted for treatment of her GDLD eye disease. After her surgery to remove surface protein buildup, her eye disease came back. The patient cannot wear soft contact lenses. No more surgeries were done. Her condition will be watched closely. A follow-up visit with her eye doctor will be scheduled.

input:
Medical History:
The patient is a 24-year-old male with severe Hemophilia A. He has been receiving Factor VIII replacement therapy three times a week but reported missing the last three doses.

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with left medial elbow pain and swelling, along with pain and paresthesias in the ring and small fingers. On examination, there was moderate swelling and tenderness to palpation over the medial epicondyle. Sensation was decreased over the small and ring fingers in the ulnar nerve distribution with intrinsic strength decreased to 4/5 on the MRC grading scale. There was a positive Tinel’s sign at the elbow. The patient was administered Factor VIII and admitted for observation. Following day, the symptoms worsened, and he was taken to the operating room for surgical decompression. The procedure involved decompression of the ulnar nerve at several sites around the elbow. There were no neuroma noted or obvious constriction deformity as seen with chronic compression. 

Discharge Condition: 
The patient was discharged from the hospital and followed up by hematology for maintenance of his Factor VIII levels.

Follow-up Plan: 
At the 6-week follow-up, the patient subjectively reported doing well without activity restrictions, with his paresthesias having completely resolved, and grip strength returned to full 5/5. 

Additional Information:
Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for which identifying information is included in this article.
clean_ref:
Medical History:
The patient is a 24-year-old male with a serious bleeding disorder called Hemophilia A. This means his blood does not clot normally. He has been getting medicine called Factor VIII three times a week to help his blood clot. He reported missing his last three doses.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with pain and swelling on the inner side of his left elbow. He also had pain and tingling in his ring finger and pinky finger. During the exam, the doctor found moderate swelling and tenderness on the inner elbow. The patient had less feeling in his pinky and ring fingers. These fingers are controlled by a nerve called the ulnar nerve. His hand strength was slightly weaker than normal. Tapping on the nerve at the elbow caused tingling, which is a sign of nerve irritation. The patient was given Factor VIII medicine and stayed in the hospital for monitoring. The next day, his symptoms got worse. He was taken to surgery to relieve pressure on the nerve. The surgeon released the trapped nerve at several spots around the elbow. There were no nerve growths or signs of long-term nerve damage.

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital. The blood disorder team followed up with him to keep his Factor VIII levels in a healthy range.

Follow-up Plan:
At the 6-week checkup, the patient said he was doing well. He had no limits on his activities. His tingling had gone away completely. His grip strength was back to full normal.

Additional Information:
The patient gave written permission to share his information in this article.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of admission: [REDACTED]
Date of discharge: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Basilar Artery Aneurysm
Discharge Diagnosis: Basilar Artery Aneurysm treated with flow diverting Pipeline stent

Hospital Course:
A 60-year-old male was admitted for investigation of headaches, and a contrast enhanced computed tomography scan of his brain revealed a 20 mm × 10 mm basilar artery aneurysm that was not surgically operable. His past medical history included coronary artery disease, atrial fibrillation, a metallic mitral valve, a prior ischaemic stroke, hypertension and a previous provoked deep vein thrombosis.

The patient underwent a right CCVAB to allow endovascular access to the aneurysm. The mid portion of the right common carotid artery was exposed in the neck above the omohyoid muscle via a longitudinal incision anterior to the medial border of the sternocleidomastoid. The long saphenous vein was harvested from the left thigh, anastomosed in an end to side fashion to the CCA and to the vertebral artery. A contrast enhanced computed tomography scan confirmed patency of the graft on the Day 1 post procedure.

In the early post-operative period, the patient had ongoing hypotension that was likely secondary to cerebral autoregulation of blood pressure post CCVAB. The patient also suffered from a line sepsis, which delayed the second stage of the operation. Six weeks after the bypass procedure, the patient underwent an angiogram from the right common femoral artery. Access to the basilar artery aneurysm was achieved via a coaxial system consisting of a 6F long sheath to the origin of the anastomosis, a 5F intermediate guide within the graft and a 025 microcatheter to deliver a 3 × 35 mm flow diverting Pipeline stent to exclude the basilar artery aneurysm. There were no significant complications, and the patient recovered well.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow-up with their primary care physician and to continue taking any prescribed medications. They are also advised to monitor for any signs of fever, swelling, or redness at the site of the surgery. Additionally, they are advised to avoid any strenuous activities or heavy lifting for at least 4-6 weeks.

Follow-up:
The patient is advised to follow-up with their primary care physician within 2-4 weeks for a routine check-up. The patient should also schedule a follow-up appointment with their surgeon to monitor their condition and to ensure appropriate care.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of admission: [REDACTED]
Date of discharge: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Bulge in a major blood vessel at the base of the brain
Discharge Diagnosis: Bulge in a major blood vessel at the base of the brain treated with a small tube to redirect blood flow

Hospital Course:
A 60-year-old male came to the hospital because of headaches. A special scan of his brain showed a bulge in a major blood vessel at the base of the brain. The bulge measured about 20 mm by 10 mm. Regular surgery could not fix this problem. The patient had other health issues including heart disease, an irregular heartbeat, a metal heart valve, a past stroke caused by blocked blood flow, high blood pressure, and a past blood clot in a leg vein.

The patient had a bypass surgery to create a new path to reach the bulge. The doctor made a cut in the right side of the neck to reach a main neck blood vessel. A vein was taken from the left thigh. This vein was connected to the neck blood vessel and to a blood vessel near the spine. A scan the next day showed blood was flowing well through the new path.

After surgery, the patient had low blood pressure. This was likely because the brain was adjusting blood flow after the bypass. The patient also got an infection from a medical tube. This delayed the second part of the treatment. Six weeks after the bypass, the doctor did another procedure. A thin tube was put into a blood vessel in the groin. The doctor guided the tube through the blood vessels to reach the bulge in the brain. A small mesh tube was placed to redirect blood flow away from the bulge. There were no major problems. The patient recovered well.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow up with their primary care physician. The patient should keep taking any prescribed medications. The patient should watch for signs of fever, swelling, or redness where the surgery was done. The patient should not do hard exercise or lift heavy things for at least 4 to 6 weeks.

Follow-up:
The patient is advised to see their primary care physician within 2 to 4 weeks for a regular check-up. The patient should also make an appointment with their surgeon. This visit will check how the patient is healing and make sure they get the right care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
The patient, a 43-year-old man with gluten intolerance and bullous dermatosis, presented with progressive vision loss, photosensitivity, foreign body sensation, and burning sensation in both eyes. He had a previous diagnosis of dermatitis herpetiformis and was undergoing treatment with dapsone and prednisolone.

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient underwent a keratolimbal allograft transplantation and cryopreserved amniotic membrane implant in his right eye due to the presence of corneal melt. Unfortunately, he persisted with severe ocular surface disease, leading to retinal and choroidal detachments in both eyes. Despite undergoing IVIG therapy, the patient's dermatologic lesions were the only ones that improved significantly. The left eye slightly improved to hand motion but the right eye displayed LP without color discrimination.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Severe ocular surface disease with retinal and choroidal detachments in both eyes.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient has been advised to follow up regularly with the ophthalmologist for monitoring and management of his ocular condition. Additionally, he has been advised to avoid any activities that may cause further damage to his eyes, including prolonged computer and smartphone use, UV exposure, and dusty environments. The patient has been informed about artificial tears and other lubricants that can provide relief for his eye symptoms.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient will follow up with the ophthalmologist per the provider's recommendations.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
The patient is a 43-year-old man who cannot eat gluten and has a skin condition that causes blisters. He came in because his vision was getting worse in both eyes over time. He was also sensitive to light and felt like something was in his eyes. His eyes had a burning feeling too. He was already being treated for a blistering skin rash with two medicines called dapsone and prednisolone.

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient had surgery on his right eye. Doctors moved healthy tissue from a donor to help repair the clear front part of his eye. They also placed a thin protective covering from donated tissue. This was done because the clear front part of his eye was breaking down. Sadly, the surface of both eyes stayed very damaged. The back layers inside both eyes also pulled away from where they should be. The patient received a special treatment through an IV to help his immune system. This treatment only helped his skin problems get much better. His left eye got a little better and can now see hand movement. His right eye can only see light and cannot tell colors apart.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Severe damage to the surface of both eyes with the back layers inside both eyes pulled away from where they should be.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should see his eye doctor regularly. The eye doctor will check his eyes and manage his eye problems. The patient should not do things that could hurt his eyes more. This includes using computers and phones for long periods of time. He should also stay away from sunlight and dusty places. The patient has been told about eye drops and other wetting products that can help his eye symptoms feel better.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient will see his eye doctor as the doctor recommends.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 48-year-old woman, presented with a history of low mood, anhedonia, easy fatigability, decreased concentration, hopelessness, and decreased sleep and appetite. The patient mentioned suicidal ideations and had a past incident of attempting suicide by setting herself on fire. These symptoms developed abruptly following a minor stressor at work. The patient had no previous history of psychiatric illness. She had tried to receive treatment from private psychiatrists, however, she discontinued medication due to the lack of improvement. The patient was diagnosed with Recurrent Brief Depressive Disorder (RBDD) as per ICD 10. She was started on paroxetine at 10 mg which was eventually increased to 50 mg.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted to the hospital presenting with symptoms of RBDD. After a thorough evaluation, the patient received an accurate diagnosis and was started on medication. During the course of the hospital stay, the patient showed symptomatic improvement. She was eventually discharged from the hospital and continued on regular follow-up.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 48-year-old woman. She came to the hospital feeling sad and had lost interest in things she used to enjoy. She felt very tired and had trouble focusing. She felt hopeless and had problems sleeping and eating. The patient had thoughts of ending her life. In the past, she tried to hurt herself by setting herself on fire. These feelings started suddenly after a small problem at work. The patient had never had mental health problems before. She had seen private mental health doctors before but stopped taking her medicine because it was not helping. The doctors found that the patient has a condition called Recurrent Brief Depressive Disorder. This means she has short periods of feeling very sad that keep coming back. She was given a medicine called paroxetine. She started with a low dose of 10 mg. Over time, the dose was raised to 50 mg.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came to the hospital with symptoms of a condition that causes short periods of deep sadness that keep coming back. The doctors did a full check and found what was wrong. They started her on medicine. While in the hospital, the patient started to feel better. She was sent home and will keep seeing her doctors for regular check-ups.

input:
Discharge Summary for Patient with Achalasia Cardia

Demographics:
- Patient is a 43-year-old Indian man

Reason for Admission:
- Patient was referred to the gastroenterology department because of dysphagia for both solids and liquids, recurrent vomiting, regurgitating undigested food, and considerable weight loss.

Medical History:
- No significant medical history was reported.

Hospital Course:
- Ultrasound scan of the abdomen showed normal findings.
- Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy showed a dilated esophageal body and no peristalsis was seen, while the lower esophageal sphincter was tightly closed.
- Retroflexion of the endoscope revealed no mass lesion in the esophagogastric junction or in the cardia.
- Chest X-ray showed an air bubble below the left hemi-diaphragm in the gastric region.
- Barium swallow showed dilated esophagus, "bird beak" appearance of the distal esophagus, and an esophageal air fluid level.
- Diagnosis of primary achalasia cardia was made based on clinical history, examination, upper gastrointestinal endoscopy, and barium swallow X-ray.
- Pneumatic balloon dilatation of the lower esophageal sphincter was performed successfully under sedation with no complications.
- Chest X-ray after the procedure showed two air bubbles under the left hemi-diaphragm in the gastric region, the first of which was confirmed as a colonic air bubble and the second as a gastric air bubble.

Discharge Status:
- Patient was discharged after a successful pneumatic balloon dilatation of the lower esophageal sphincter with no complications.

Instructions:
- Follow up with the primary care physician for further management of achalasia cardia.
- Provide a detailed report of this hospital course to the primary care physician.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary for Patient with Achalasia Cardia (a swallowing disorder where the food tube cannot move food into the stomach properly)

Demographics:
The patient is a 43-year-old Indian man.

Reason for Admission:
The patient was sent to the stomach and digestive system department. The patient had trouble swallowing both solid foods and liquids. The patient also had repeated vomiting, bringing up undigested food, and major weight loss.

Medical History:
The patient had no important past health problems.

Hospital Course:
A sound wave scan of the belly showed normal results. A camera test to look inside the upper digestive system showed the food tube was wider than normal. The food tube was not squeezing to push food down. The valve at the bottom of the food tube was closed too tightly. The doctor turned the camera around to check for any growths where the food tube meets the stomach. No growths were found. A chest X-ray showed an air bubble below the left side of the muscle that helps with breathing, near the stomach area. A special X-ray test using a chalky drink showed the food tube was wider than normal. The bottom of the food tube had a narrow pointed shape. There was also air and fluid sitting in the food tube. Based on the patient's symptoms, exam, camera test, and X-ray tests, the doctors found the patient has a swallowing disorder called primary achalasia cardia. The doctors used a balloon to stretch open the tight valve at the bottom of the food tube. The patient was given medicine to sleep during this procedure. There were no problems during the procedure. A chest X-ray after the procedure showed two air bubbles below the left side of the breathing muscle near the stomach area. Doctors confirmed one bubble was from the large intestine and one was from the stomach.

Discharge Status:
The patient went home after a successful balloon stretching of the tight valve at the bottom of the food tube. There were no problems.

Instructions:
The patient should see the regular doctor for ongoing care of this swallowing disorder. The patient should give a full report of this hospital stay to the regular doctor.

input:
Hospital Course:
The 45-year-old female with a history of asthma and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) presented to our clinic with a chronic productive cough. She had previously been treated with levofloxacin, which improved her symptoms but they recurred after discontinuing the antibiotic. Chest X-ray and CT scan revealed infiltrates in both lungs, along with bronchiectatic changes in the lower lobes. Her autoimmune screen came back negative for rheumatoid arthritis and Sjogren's syndrome. Immunoglobulins were elevated; however, alpha one antitrypsin and immunoglobulin E (IgE) were normal. Chronic recurrent aspiration was suspected, and esophageal pH monitoring with manometry confirmed the suspicion of chronic aspiration from severe acid reflux with an elevated DeMeester score. Bronchoscopy was performed to rule out obstructive disease and obtain samples to rule out mycobacterial disease, which were positive for heavy growth of Achromobacter xylosoxidans, subspecies denitrificans. The bacteria was responsive to levofloxacin and other antibiotics such as amikacin, cefepime, ceftazidime, gentamicin, meropenem, piperacillin/tazobactam, tobramycin, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, but resistant to ciprofloxacin, cefotaxime, and aztreonam.

Management:
Airway clearance techniques with percussion and flutter valve and bronchodilator therapy with hypertonic saline nebulizers were initiated, and the patient was started on a 3-week course of levofloxacin. One month after stopping antibiotics, her symptoms recurred, and again, levofloxacin was initiated for two more weeks with good clinical response. She was referred for surgical intervention to address ongoing injury to the airways and prevent aspiration insult.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition.

Follow-Up:
The patient was advised to continue airway clearance techniques with percussion, flutter valve, and bronchodilator therapy with hypertonic saline nebulizers.

Other Notes:
This discharge summary does not contain any clinical information or details such as medication names, dosages, treatment plans, diagnoses, procedures, or test results that are not explicitly mentioned within the given case report.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The 45-year-old female has a history of asthma and acid reflux. She came to the clinic with a long-lasting cough that brought up mucus. She was given an antibiotic called levofloxacin before. It helped, but her symptoms came back when she stopped taking it. A chest X-ray and CT scan showed signs of infection in both lungs. The scans also showed widened airways in the lower parts of her lungs. Blood tests ruled out certain conditions where the body attacks itself. These included rheumatoid arthritis and Sjogren's syndrome. Some proteins in her blood were high, but others were normal. Doctors thought stomach acid was going into her lungs over time. A test measured acid levels and muscle movement in her food pipe. This test confirmed that severe acid reflux was causing liquid to enter her airways. The acid damage score was higher than normal. A camera was used to look inside her airways. This ruled out any blockages. Samples were also taken to check for certain infections. The samples showed heavy growth of a bacteria called Achromobacter xylosoxidans, subspecies denitrificans. This bacteria could be killed by levofloxacin and other antibiotics. These include amikacin, cefepime, ceftazidime, gentamicin, meropenem, piperacillin/tazobactam, tobramycin, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. The bacteria could not be killed by ciprofloxacin, cefotaxime, and aztreonam.

Management:
The patient started treatments to help clear mucus from her airways. These included chest tapping and a flutter valve device. She also used a saltwater mist through a breathing machine to open her airways. She took levofloxacin for 3 weeks. One month after stopping the antibiotic, her symptoms came back. She took levofloxacin again for 2 more weeks and felt better. She was sent to a surgeon to help stop the ongoing damage to her airways from acid reflux.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was sent home in stable condition.

Follow-Up:
The patient was told to keep doing treatments to clear mucus from her airways. These include chest tapping, the flutter valve device, and saltwater mist through a breathing machine.

Other Notes:
This discharge summary does not contain any health information that is not written in the case report. This includes medicine names, doses, treatment plans, diagnoses, procedures, or test results.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 32
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Hospital: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to our facility with swelling in his right cheek, which had been present for two weeks. A malignant lesion of the oral cavity was suspected. However, a thorough examination revealed an oval, firm swelling measuring 2.5 × 2 cm in size, located 2 cm above and medial to the right angle of the mandible. An ultrasound of the cheek revealed that the swelling was a cystic lesion in the bucco-masseteric region with perilesional edema.

Further testing, including an MRI of the face and neck, was performed, and a hyperechoic intralesional structure consistent with the scolex was identified. Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) was performed but showed no evidence of malignancy. Radiographs of the chest, bilateral arms and thighs were taken, and an MRI of the head and orbit was performed, revealing no additional lesions.

The patient was advised to take analgesics and steroids for seven days, and the swelling gradually reduced in size. Deworming therapy with albendazole was also prescribed.

Discharge Medications:
Albendazole

Discharge Diagnosis:
Involvement of the facial musculature manifesting as a cystic swelling in the bucco-masseteric region consistent with cysticercosis.

Follow-Up:
The patient should follow up with his primary healthcare provider for continued monitoring.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's condition has improved since admission. He is stable and ready for discharge.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to continue taking his albendazole medication as prescribed. He should avoid tobacco use as it has been associated with cysticercosis. He is encouraged to follow up with his primary healthcare provider for continued monitoring and to report any new or concerning symptoms.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 32
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Hospital: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital with swelling in his right cheek. The swelling had been there for two weeks. Doctors first thought it might be cancer in the mouth area. After a careful exam, doctors found an oval, firm lump about 1 inch by 0.8 inches in size. The lump was located near the right side of the jawbone. An ultrasound of the cheek showed the lump was a fluid-filled sac in the cheek muscle area. There was also some swelling in the tissue around it.

More tests were done, including an MRI scan of the face and neck. The MRI found a small structure inside the sac that looked like a tapeworm head. A small sample was taken from the lump using a thin needle. The sample showed no signs of cancer. X-rays of the chest, both arms, and both thighs were taken. An MRI of the head and eye area was also done. These tests showed no other problem areas in the body.

The patient was told to take pain medicine and medicine to reduce swelling for seven days. The lump slowly got smaller. Medicine to kill the parasite was also given.

Discharge Medications:
Albendazole

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient had a parasitic infection called cysticercosis. This caused a fluid-filled sac to form in the cheek muscle area.

Follow-Up:
The patient should see his regular doctor for continued check-ups.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has gotten better since coming to the hospital. He is doing well and is ready to go home.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should keep taking his albendazole medicine as directed. He should not use tobacco because it has been linked to this type of infection. He should see his regular doctor for continued check-ups. He should tell his doctor about any new or worrying symptoms.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 9-year-old male

Date of Admission: [Date]

Date of Discharge: [Date]

Admission Diagnosis: Continuous pain in the lower left back tooth region for 1 week

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient presented with a chief complaint of continuous pain in the lower left back tooth region for 1 week. Clinical examination revealed large disto-occlusal caries on tooth 36, with exaggerated response to vitality tests (sensibility) with lingering pain. Intraoral periapical (IOPA) radiograph revealed blunderbuss canals for both mesial and distal roots. A diagnosis of irreversible pulpitis was formulated.

Following administration of local anesthesia (2% Lignox with adrenaline, 2% Lignox with adrenaline, Indoco Remedies Ltd, Mumbai, India) and rubber dam application, access cavity was made, and three canals were located. Mesial canal orifices were slit-shape and the distal orifice was large oval-shaped. In the mesial canals, #30 k-file (Mani Inc., Tochigi, Japan) was snugly fit in the middle third. Working length for the distal canal was determined using the bent file technique, and confirmed radiographically. In the distal canal, #80 k-file was loose and going beyond the apex without any resistance. Circumferential filing was done in the distal root, and the mesial root was minimally prepared till #30 k-file. 5 mL of 3% sodium hypochlorite solution (Parcan, Septodont Healthcare India Pvt. Ltd, Maharashtra, India) was used as an irrigant for each canal along with normal saline. Triple antibiotic paste (TAP) (metronidazole, ciprofloxacin, and minocycline) was made as paste slurry and placed inside the canal using lentulospiral (Mani Inc., Tochigi, Japan) and k files.

After 3 weeks, TAP was removed, and irrigation was performed using 10 mL of 3% sodium hypochlorite (Dentwash, Prime Dental Products Pvt Ltd; Thane, Mumbai, India), followed by 10 mL of saline and a final flush of 10 mL of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) (Dentwash, Prime, Bhiwandi, Maharashtra, India). Bleeding was induced in the mesial root canals using a #15 k file by taking it beyond the apex. Platelet-rich fibrin (PRF) was separated from the blood corpuscles and squeezed between sterile gauze pieces to remove the trapped fluids. Following which, the PRF membrane was separated into small fragments using sterile scissors. A metal plugger (Manipal instruments, Parkala, India) with a rubber stopper set at 1 mm short of working length was used to pack the first increment inside the distal root. On reaching the desired length, a 0.5 mm diameter metal plugger was used in circumferential tapping manner to pack the PRF, and a flat surface was achieved. Following which, the butt end of sterile No. 80 paper point (DiaDent, Chungcheongbuk-do, Korea) was used to compact the PRF further and to remove any remaining entrapped fluid. Next increment was placed in a similar way after shortening the length. Once the entire canal was filled till the orifice with the PRF, MTA was packed inside the pulp chamber to a thickness of 3 mm. Before temporizing, a moist cotton pellet was placed, and the patient was recalled the next day for the placement of Glass Ionomer Cement (GC type 2, GC Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) seal and Composite restoration (3M Z350 XT, 3M ESPE Dental Products, MN, USA).

The patient received follow-up visits at 6 months, 1 year, 2 years, 3 years and 5 years, during which pulp sensibility tests done using Endofrost (Coltène/Whaledent Private Ltd, Raigad, Maharashtra) were negative for the tooth at all recall visits. Follow-up radiographs at the 2-year recall showed complete closure of the root end along with increased root length. The 5-year recall radiograph showed no signs of pathology, and canals were patent and not obliterated. The patient was completely asymptomatic during all the follow-up visits.

Discharge Diagnosis: Irreversible pulpitis, treated with maturogenesis technique using triple antibiotic paste and platelet-rich fibrin.

Management Plan: None. The patient was discharged in good condition and advised to continue oral hygiene practices.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 9-year-old male

Date of Admission: [Date]

Date of Discharge: [Date]

Admission Diagnosis: Ongoing pain in the lower left back tooth area for 1 week

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient came in with ongoing pain in the lower left back tooth area for 1 week. The dentist found a large cavity on tooth 36, which is a lower left molar. The tooth was very sensitive to tests and the pain lasted a long time after. An x-ray showed that the tooth roots were not fully formed yet. The dentist determined the tooth pulp, which is the soft tissue inside the tooth, was badly damaged and could not heal on its own.

The dentist numbed the area and placed a rubber sheet to keep the tooth dry. The dentist made an opening in the tooth and found three root canals. The canals in the front roots were narrow and slit-shaped. The canal in the back root was large and oval-shaped. The dentist measured how deep each canal was. The back root canal was very wide and open at the tip. The dentist cleaned and shaped all the canals. A germ-killing rinse and salt water were used to clean each canal. A paste made of three antibiotics was placed inside the canals to kill germs.

After 3 weeks, the antibiotic paste was removed. The canals were cleaned again with germ-killing rinse, salt water, and a solution to prepare the canal walls. The dentist poked past the root tip in the front canals to cause bleeding on purpose. A healing material called platelet-rich fibrin was made from blood. This material was squeezed to remove extra fluid and cut into small pieces. The dentist packed this healing material into the back root first using small tools. The material was pressed down firmly to make a flat surface. More material was added layer by layer until the canal was filled. The same was done for the other canals. A special cement was placed on top to seal everything. A wet cotton ball was put in, and the patient came back the next day. A glass filling material and a tooth-colored filling were placed to close the tooth.

The patient came back for check-ups at 6 months, 1 year, 2 years, 3 years, and 5 years. At each visit, the tooth did not respond to cold tests, which was expected. X-rays at 2 years showed the root tips had closed and the roots had grown longer. The x-ray at 5 years showed no problems. The root canals stayed open and healthy. The patient had no pain or problems at any of the follow-up visits.

Discharge Diagnosis: Badly damaged tooth pulp, treated with a technique to help the tooth roots finish growing using antibiotic paste and a healing material made from blood.

Management Plan: None. The patient was sent home in good health. The patient was told to keep brushing and flossing as usual.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 68-year-old Japanese man, underwent resection surgery for a leiomyosarcoma at the mediastinum. Due to dissemination in the pleura, the patient received radiation therapy and a second operation. Ten months post-surgery, the patient developed pain and numbness in the left upper limb, and an enhanced solid lesion and a non-enhanced cystic lesion were observed in the apex of the left lung using contrast enhanced CT scan. Ablation using radiofrequency ablation (RFA) was performed after radiation and chemotherapy were deemed ineffective, and surgery was thought to carry a high risk of bleeding. The patient underwent RFA with a LeVeen electrode, with retractable hooks opened for ablation. A total of three ablations were performed, with the output started at 20 W and increased by 10 W every 2 minutes in each session. The procedure was performed under local anesthesia to monitor the appearance of sharp pain of the left upper limb. A maximum output of 80 W and total ablation time of 57 minutes and 14 seconds were achieved. Contrast enhanced CT performed two days after RFA showed that the enhanced solid lesion in the tumor was smaller in size compared to pre-RFA CT. The patient's pain disappeared for approximately 3 months, accompanied by an improvement in motor function disorder. However, the pain of the left upper limb reappeared due to growth of the tumor, three months after RFA.

Condition at Discharge:

The patient's pain had reappeared, and thus, he was discharged from the hospital with instructions to follow up with his healthcare provider.

Instructions:

The patient is advised to follow up with his healthcare provider regarding his condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 68-year-old Japanese man. He had surgery to remove a rare muscle cancer found in the chest area between the lungs. The cancer had spread to the lining of the lungs. Because of this, the patient had radiation treatment and a second surgery. Ten months after surgery, the patient started having pain and numbness in his left arm. A special scan using dye showed two growths at the top of the left lung. One growth was solid and the other was filled with fluid. Radiation and chemotherapy did not work well. Surgery was too risky because it could cause heavy bleeding. So doctors used a heat treatment called RFA to destroy the growths. The doctor inserted a special needle with small hooks into the tumor. The needle sent heat into the tumor to kill the cancer cells. The doctor did this treatment three times total. The heat was started low and slowly increased every 2 minutes each time. The patient was kept awake with numbing medicine only. This let doctors know right away if the patient felt sharp pain in his left arm. The highest heat used was 80 W. The total treatment time was 57 minutes and 14 seconds. A scan done two days later showed the solid growth had gotten smaller. The patient had no pain for about 3 months. His arm also moved better during this time. But the pain in his left arm came back after 3 months. This happened because the tumor started growing again.

Condition at Discharge:

The patient's pain had come back. He was sent home from the hospital. He was told to see his doctor for follow-up care.

Instructions:

The patient should see his doctor to check on his condition.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Age: 30 years
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A

Reason for Admission:
The patient was admitted with a complaint of painless hematuria with clots of 2 month's duration.

History:
At 3 years of age, the patient was diagnosed with retinoblastoma in both eyes and was treated with 18 cycles of radiation to both the orbits.

Hospital Course:
On examination, the patient had enlarged inguinal lymph nodes that were found to be inflammatory on biopsy. Ultrasonography and computed tomographic urography showed a large (6 cm × 6 cm) mass arising from the right anterolateral wall of the urinary bladder. The upper tract was normal. Cystoscopy revealed a large nodular lesion arising from the right anterolateral wall and hence only transurethral biopsy was performed. On histopathology, a high-grade sarcoma with only spindle cells and no epithelial cells was noted with possible differential diagnosis of high-grade sarcoma (leiomyosarcoma/malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor) and sarcomatoid variant of urothelial carcinoma (carcinosarcoma).

Diagnosis:
On immunohistochemistry (IHC), the tumor was strongly positive for cytoplasmic smooth muscle actin and cytoplasmic vimentin, and focally positive for cytoplasmic desmin and cytokeratin, suggesting leiomyosarcoma. Further, negative IHC for epithelial membrane antigen and S100 ruled out carcinosarcoma and malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor, respectively, thus confirming urinary bladder leiomyosarcoma.

Treatment and Follow-up:
The patient refused further genetic analysis and was not willing for radical surgery. He was referred to the oncologist for further management and subsequently lost to follow-up.

Final Diagnosis:
Urinary bladder leiomyosarcoma with enlarged inguinal lymph nodes.

Discharge Condition:
The patient is lost to follow-up. 

Signed,
[Physician Name]
[Medical Institution Name]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Age: 30 years
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A

Reason for Admission:
The patient was admitted because of blood in the urine with clots for 2 months. There was no pain.

History:
At 3 years old, the patient had eye cancer in both eyes. The patient was treated with 18 rounds of radiation to both eye areas.

Hospital Course:
During the exam, the patient had swollen lymph nodes in the groin area. A tissue sample showed these nodes had swelling from irritation, not cancer. Imaging tests showed a large growth about 2.4 inches by 2.4 inches on the right side of the bladder wall. The kidneys and tubes leading to the bladder were normal. A camera exam of the bladder showed a large bumpy growth on the right bladder wall. Doctors took a small tissue sample through a tube. Lab tests showed a fast-growing cancer made of muscle-like cells. Doctors thought it could be one of a few types of cancer.

Diagnosis:
Special lab tests were done on the tissue sample. The cancer cells tested positive for certain muscle cell markers. The cells tested negative for markers of other cancer types. These results confirmed the cancer was a type called bladder muscle cancer.

Treatment and Follow-up:
The patient did not want genetic testing. The patient also did not want major surgery. The patient was sent to a cancer specialist for more care. After that, the patient stopped coming to appointments.

Final Diagnosis:
Bladder muscle cancer with swollen lymph nodes in the groin area.

Discharge Condition:
The patient stopped coming to appointments.

[Medical Institution Name]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted due to a rapidly enlarging mass in the right submandibular gland. Computed tomography scan revealed a 5-cm-sized movable mass with sialolithiasis in the right submandibular area. Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) showed single malignant epithelial cells and atypical mucin-containing cells with necrotic debris and inflammatory cells in the background. A few slide fragments showed squamous differentiation. The patient underwent surgery to remove the mass and stones.

Hospital Stay:
The patient's hospital stay was uneventful.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient was discharged in a stable condition.

Final Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a submandibular mass that was primarily composed of undifferentiated carcinoma (UC) and undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS) with a central necrosis. 

Treatment:
The patient underwent surgery to remove the mass and stones, and was subsequently managed by radiation therapy.

Follow-Up:
Follow-up appointments were scheduled for the patient to monitor their recovery.

Treatment:
The patient had surgery to remove the lump and stones. After surgery, the patient received radiation treatment.

Follow-Up:
Follow-up visits were set up for the patient to check on their recovery.

input:
HOSPITAL COURSE:
A 62-year-old male, a smoker, with a history of non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), was admitted to the hospital. A loading dose of ticagrelor was administered, and the patient was transferred to the Cath lab. Coronary angioplasty with DES implantation was performed, and the patient showed no adverse reaction initially.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Acute Bronchial Obstruction due to Ticagrelor

HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:
Two hours after administration of ticagrelor, the patient developed strong anxiety followed by heavy dyspnea and generalised sweating. On auscultation, heavy bronchial obstruction was observed, and there was a significant drop in blood oxygen saturation. The patient was administered inhalation of beta-mimetics, cholinolytics, and corticosteroids, followed by corticosteroids intravenously. This treatment provided only partial relief for a short time and the respiratory attacks were recurrent and numerous, each time starting with a panic and anxiety. Finally, intravenous infusion of theophylline 200mg provided relief, and dyspnea episodes ceased to occur.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient has been advised to stop the use of ticagrelor and switch to clopidogrel upon discharge from the hospital. The patient is requested to remain vigilant and inform the medical professional immediately if any adverse respiratory reaction recurs. Further pulmonary therapy is not required.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
The patient has been discharged in a stable condition.
clean_ref:
HOSPITAL COURSE:
A 62-year-old man who smokes was admitted to the hospital. He had a history of a type of heart attack called NSTEMI. This happens when blood flow to the heart is partly blocked. He was given a starting dose of a blood thinner called ticagrelor. Then he was moved to a special room for heart procedures. Doctors placed a small mesh tube coated with medicine in his heart artery to keep it open. At first, the patient had no bad reactions.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Sudden airway blockage caused by ticagrelor

HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:
Two hours after getting ticagrelor, the patient became very anxious. Then he had severe trouble breathing and sweating all over his body. When doctors listened to his lungs, they heard that his airways were very blocked. His blood oxygen level dropped a lot. The patient was given breathing treatments to open his airways. He also received medicines to reduce swelling, both through a mask and through a vein. These treatments only helped a little and not for long. The breathing problems kept coming back many times. Each episode started with panic and anxiety. Finally, a medicine called theophylline was given through a vein. This worked and the breathing problems stopped.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient must stop taking ticagrelor. He should start taking a different blood thinner called clopidogrel when he leaves the hospital. The patient should watch for any breathing problems. He must tell a doctor right away if breathing problems come back. No other lung treatment is needed.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
The patient has left the hospital in stable condition.

input:
Medical History:
The patient is a 39-year-old Vietnamese male with no significant past medical history.

Chief Complaint:
Sharp 5/10 right upper quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and chills

Admission:
The patient was admitted with an increased anion gap, an acute kidney injury, alkaline phosphatase, AST, ALT, ferritin, and a white blood cell count.

Diagnostic Results:
CT scan with contrast of the chest and abdomen showed a large, complex mass in the right lobe of the liver with multiple septations. Ultrasound of the abdomen showed a heterogeneous, hypoechoic, multiseptated hepatic mass with internal vascularity.

Treatment:
The patient was started on vancomycin and piperacillin/tazobactam with fluid resuscitation consisting of crystalloids.

Medical Intensive Care Unit:
The patient was transferred to the medical intensive care unit (MICU) due to tachypnea, tachycardia, and hypoxia.

Arterial Blood Gas:
ABG showed a pH of 7.34 and a bicarbonate of 14. The patient was placed on bilevel positive airway pressure ventilation and continued on antibiotics.

Repeat CT:
The repeat CT of the thorax and abdomen showed areas of consolidated infiltrate with some associated atelectatic change in the posterior right lower lobe.

Magnetic Resonance Imaging:
MRI showed a peripherally enhancing mass in the anterior segment of the right hepatic lobe that measured 3.5 cm x 3.3 cm with areas of central septations.

Blood Cultures:
Blood cultures grew Klebsiella pneumonia.

Discharge:
The patient was discharged after 11 days. A repeat CT of the abdomen showed interval improvement of the abscess. The patient was referred to an infectious disease specialist for follow-up.
clean_ref:
Medical History:
The patient is a 39-year-old Vietnamese male with no major health problems in the past.

Chief Complaint:
Sharp pain in the upper right belly rated 5 out of 10, feeling sick to the stomach, throwing up, fever, and chills.

Admission:
The patient was admitted with high acid levels in the blood, sudden kidney problems, high liver enzyme levels, high iron storage levels, and a high white blood cell count.

Diagnostic Results:
A CT scan of the chest and belly showed a large, complicated growth in the right side of the liver with many divided sections. An ultrasound of the belly showed an uneven liver growth with many sections and blood flow inside it.

Treatment:
The patient was given two strong antibiotics called vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam. The patient also received fluids through a vein to restore body fluids.

Medical Intensive Care Unit:
The patient was moved to the intensive care unit because of fast breathing, fast heart rate, and low oxygen levels.

Arterial Blood Gas:
A blood test showed the blood was slightly acidic with low bicarbonate levels. The patient was placed on a breathing machine that helps push air into the lungs. The patient kept receiving antibiotics.

Repeat CT:
A second CT scan of the chest and belly showed infected lung tissue with some collapsed areas in the lower right lung.

Magnetic Resonance Imaging:
An MRI showed a growth in the right side of the liver measuring about 3.5 cm by 3.3 cm with divided sections in the middle.

Blood Cultures:
Blood tests found a type of bacteria called Klebsiella pneumonia causing the infection.

Discharge:
The patient left the hospital after 11 days. A follow-up CT scan of the belly showed the infected pocket was getting better. The patient was sent to an infection specialist for follow-up care.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [omitted]
Age: 60
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: Brachial plexopathy
Length of Stay: [omitted]

HOSPITAL COURSE

This patient was admitted to our facility for an intense pruritus on her right arm, which had been ongoing for six months. She also complained of right brachial paresis.

COMORBIDITIES

The patient has a known history of multiple myeloma.

DIAGNOSTIC TESTS AND PROCEDURES

Computed tomography of the cervical spine, magnetic resonance imaging, and electromyography of the brachial plexus were performed, which showed right cervical disc herniation with nerve root compression of C6-C7 and right brachial plexus inflammation (Parsonage-Turner syndrome).

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS

The patient was treated with methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and oral hydroxyzine 25mg every 8 hours, which resulted in little improvement of the pruritus. Gabapentin was introduced at 900mg/day, which achieved optimal control after three months of follow-up.

FOLLOW-UP INSTRUCTIONS

The patient was referred to neurosurgery and oncology consultation for additional therapeutic orientation.

SUMMARY

The patient presented with brachial plexopathy, which is attributed to neoplastic infiltration of multiple myeloma. The treatment plan included the application of methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and oral hydroxyzine 25mg every 8 hours, along with the introduction of gabapentin at 900mg/day. The patient was referred to neurosurgery and oncology for further management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [omitted]
Age: 60
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: Nerve damage in the shoulder and arm area
Length of Stay: [omitted]

HOSPITAL COURSE

This patient was admitted to our facility for severe itching on her right arm. This problem had been going on for six months. She also had weakness in her right arm.

COMORBIDITIES

The patient has a known history of multiple myeloma. This is a type of blood cancer.

DIAGNOSTIC TESTS AND PROCEDURES

A CT scan of the neck, an MRI, and a nerve test of the shoulder and arm area were performed. These tests showed a bulging disc in the right side of the neck. This disc was pressing on nerves at the C6-C7 level. The tests also showed swelling of the nerves in the right shoulder and arm area. This condition is called Parsonage-Turner syndrome.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS

The patient was treated with methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1%. This is a steroid cream. She also took hydroxyzine 25mg by mouth every 8 hours. This helped only a little with the itching. Gabapentin was added at 900mg per day. After three months, this medicine controlled the itching well.

FOLLOW-UP INSTRUCTIONS

The patient was sent to see a brain and spine surgeon. She was also sent to see a cancer doctor for more treatment advice.

SUMMARY

The patient had nerve damage in the shoulder and arm area. This was caused by the spread of her blood cancer. The treatment plan included methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and hydroxyzine 25mg by mouth every 8 hours. Gabapentin at 900mg per day was also added. The patient was sent to a brain and spine surgeon and a cancer doctor for more care.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 23-year-old female, presented with paroxysmal hypertension, palpitations, headache, diaphoresis and vomiting for 11 months. After extensive workup, the patient was diagnosed with retroperitoneal PGL and underwent surgical excision. Immunohistochemical staining revealed positive Syn and CgA, negative Melan A, HMB45 and α-inhibin, positive S-100 cells and positive CD31 vascular endothelium marker. Genetic testing showed that the patient carried a likely pathogenic missense mutation in exon 6 of the SDHB gene [c.563 T > C]. The patient was medically managed with doxazosin and metoprolol for 2 weeks as preoperative preparation. Clinical follow-up examinations were performed three times after surgery and showed no recurrence or symptoms of paroxysmal hypertension. The patient is recommended to have long-term follow-up and screening over her lifetime.

Hospital Course Summary:
23-year-old female presented with paroxysmal hypertension, palpitations, headache, diaphoresis and vomiting for 11 months. Retroperitoneal PGL was diagnosed, and the patient underwent surgical excision. Immunohistochemical staining and genetic testing were performed, which revealed positive Syn and CgA, negative Melan A, HMB45, α-inhibin, and a likely pathogenic missense mutation in exon 6 of the SDHB gene [c.563 T > C]. Preoperative preparation was managed with doxazosin and metoprolol. Clinical follow-up examinations showed no recurrence or symptoms of paroxysmal hypertension, and long-term follow-up and screening are recommended.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 23-year-old female. She came to the hospital with symptoms that lasted for 11 months. These symptoms included sudden spikes in blood pressure, a racing heart, headache, heavy sweating, and vomiting. After many tests, doctors found a rare tumor behind her belly area. The tumor was removed with surgery. Lab tests on the tumor tissue showed certain markers that helped confirm the type of tumor. Genetic testing found a change in a gene called SDHB that likely caused the tumor. Before surgery, the patient took two medicines called doxazosin and metoprolol for 2 weeks. These medicines helped control her blood pressure and heart rate. The patient had three check-up visits after surgery. These visits showed no signs the tumor came back. The patient also had no more sudden spikes in blood pressure. Doctors recommend the patient have regular check-ups and screening tests for the rest of her life.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient is a 23-year-old female. She had sudden spikes in blood pressure, a racing heart, headache, heavy sweating, and vomiting for 11 months. Doctors found a rare tumor behind her belly area and removed it with surgery. Lab tests and genetic testing were done on the tumor tissue. The tests showed certain markers and found a gene change in the SDHB gene that likely caused the tumor. Before surgery, the patient took doxazosin and metoprolol to prepare her body. Check-up visits after surgery showed no signs the tumor came back. The patient had no more sudden spikes in blood pressure. Doctors recommend regular check-ups and screening tests for life.

input:
Hospital Course:

The 75-year-old male patient was admitted to our tertiary care center due to prolonged aPTT discovered during preoperative laboratory tests. Prior to admission, the patient reported bilateral parotid enlargement and cervical lymphadenopathies.

Medical History:

The patient had no significant medical history.

Diagnostic Findings:

Biological work-up showed normal kidney, liver, pancreas, and thyroid functions. Acute phase reactants and immunological tests were within normal range. The prolonged aPTT was confirmed with normal PT and found decreased FVIII plasma levels associated with positive anti-FVIII IgG4 autoantibodies. An axillary lymph node biopsy was performed, revealing benign follicular hyperplasia with elevated IgG4/IgG ratio and no evidence of lymphoma. Further laboratory tests showed elevated serum IgG4 levels with other IgG subclasses within normal range.

Treatment:

The patient was diagnosed with IgG4-RD-associated AHA and treated with oral prednisone 1mg/kg/day for both diseases. The treatment led to a regression of parotid and lymph node enlargement, a decrease in serum IgG4 levels, a progressive correction of FVIII and aPTT values, and a suppression of anti-FVIII plasma activity. Prednisone was then slowly tapered and discontinued after 9 months.

Follow-up:

Nine months after treatment withdrawal, the patient relapsed with a new increase in IgG4 serum levels without organ involvement and a decrease in FVIII plasma levels with elevated anti-FVIII autoantibodies but without bleeding. The patient was started on a new course of oral corticosteroids associated with 2 infusions of rituximab 1g, which led to a sustained remission. The patient had a total follow-up of 3 years after diagnosis.

Summary:

The patient was admitted to our tertiary care center due to prolonged aPTT. Diagnostic findings revealed IgG4-RD-associated AHA. The patient was treated with oral prednisone and achieved sustained remission with the addition of rituximab. The patient had a total follow-up of 3 years after diagnosis.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The 75-year-old male patient was admitted to our specialized hospital because a blood test showed his blood was taking too long to clot. This was found during tests before a planned surgery. Before coming to the hospital, the patient noticed swelling in the glands near the jaw on both sides and swollen glands in the neck.

Medical History:

The patient had no major past health problems.

Diagnostic Findings:

Blood tests showed the kidneys, liver, pancreas, and thyroid were all working normally. Tests for inflammation and immune system problems came back normal. Doctors confirmed the blood was clotting too slowly. They found low levels of a clotting protein called Factor VIII. The patient's immune system was making antibodies that attacked this clotting protein. Doctors removed a small piece of a gland from under the arm to study it. The tissue showed the gland was overactive but not cancerous. There was no sign of cancer in the lymph system. More blood tests showed high levels of a specific antibody type called IgG4. Other antibody types were normal.

Treatment:

The patient was diagnosed with a rare immune disorder that caused both the gland swelling and the clotting problem. The patient was treated with a steroid medicine called prednisone taken by mouth once a day. The dose was based on body weight. The treatment worked well. The swelling in the jaw glands and neck glands went down. The IgG4 antibody levels dropped. The clotting protein levels slowly returned to normal. The blood started clotting at a normal speed again. The harmful antibodies went away. The prednisone dose was slowly reduced over time. The patient stopped taking it after 9 months.

Follow-up:

Nine months after stopping treatment, the condition came back. The IgG4 antibody levels rose again. No glands or organs were swollen this time. The clotting protein levels dropped again. The harmful antibodies came back. The patient did not have any bleeding problems. The patient started taking steroid medicine again. The patient also received two doses of a different medicine called rituximab through an IV. This treatment worked well and kept the disease under control. The patient was followed by doctors for 3 years after the first diagnosis.

Summary:

The patient was admitted to our specialized hospital because blood tests showed clotting was taking too long. Tests showed a rare immune disorder was causing the problem. The patient was treated with steroid medicine and got better. When the disease came back, adding rituximab helped keep it under control for good. The patient was followed by doctors for 3 years after the first diagnosis.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [...]
Medical Record Number: [...]
Discharge Date: [...]

Hospital Course: 
The patient was admitted to our hospital due to suddenly developing aconuresis and paraplegia. The patient had a two-week history of abdominal pain and weakness in the lower extremities for one day. Past medical history was unremarkable, and no family history of malignant tumors was noted. Neurological examination revealed bladder-urinary dysfunction and hypoesthesia beneath the T8 sensory dermatome. A CT scan on admission showed acute pancreatitis and acute cholecystitis. The patient underwent MRI of the abdomen and lumbar spine, which showed a swollen pancreas and gallbladder with less peripancreatic exudation and liquid collection. The MRI also showed multiple enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes and a well-demarcated, posterolateral extradural mass between T9 and T12, with definite bony destruction. PET-CT scan revealed multifocal malignant lesions, indicating multiple metastases of malignant tumors. Gastroduodenal endoscopy showed massive involvement of the gastric body and duodenum with tumors. Histology and immunohistochemistry of gastric biopsies were suggestive of BL.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was referred to our hospital due to a sudden onset of aconuresis and paraplegia. The patient had a two-week history of abdominal pain and weakness in the lower extremities. The patient was diagnosed with acute pancreatitis and acute cholecystitis with peripancreatic exudation and liquid collection. MRI showed multiple enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes and a well-demarcated, posterolateral extradural mass between T9 and T12, with definite bony destruction. PET-CT scan revealed multifocal malignant lesions, indicating multiple metastases of malignant tumors. Gastric biopsies were suggestive of BL. Subsequent course was palliative care and the patient was discharged for hospice care.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [...]
Medical Record Number: [...]
Discharge Date: [...]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital because of sudden loss of bladder control and loss of movement in both legs. The patient had belly pain for two weeks and leg weakness for one day before coming in. The patient had no past health problems and no family history of cancer. A nerve and muscle exam showed bladder problems and reduced feeling below the mid-chest area. A CT scan when the patient arrived showed sudden swelling of the pancreas and gallbladder. The patient had an MRI of the belly and lower spine. The MRI showed a swollen pancreas and gallbladder with some fluid buildup around them. The MRI also showed many swollen lymph nodes deep in the belly and a growth pressing on the spinal cord between the middle and lower back. This growth was damaging the bones. A full body PET-CT scan showed cancer that had spread to many parts of the body. A camera test of the stomach and upper gut showed tumors covering large areas of the stomach and first part of the small intestine. Tissue samples from the stomach suggested a type of cancer called BL.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was sent to our hospital because of sudden loss of bladder control and loss of movement in both legs. The patient had belly pain for two weeks and leg weakness before coming in. The patient was found to have sudden swelling of the pancreas and gallbladder with fluid buildup around them. The MRI showed many swollen lymph nodes deep in the belly and a growth pressing on the spinal cord in the back. This growth was damaging the bones. A full body scan showed cancer that had spread to many parts of the body. Tissue samples from the stomach suggested a type of cancer called BL. The care plan focused on comfort, and the patient was sent home for end of life care.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our hospital with complaints of asymptomatic dark-colored lesions on his hands and feet and light-colored skin lesions involving the trunk for the past three years. Over time, these dark-colored lesions increased in number and involved other parts of his body. Dermatological examination revealed mildly atrophic hyperpigmented macules in a reticulate pattern. In addition, there were multiple hypopigmented macules widely distributed over the trunk and anterior aspect of both legs. The patient also had multiple pits on both palms, with breaks in dermatoglyphics, and multiple nonacne facial scars predominantly on the nose and malar areas. 

Diagnostic Assessment:

Skin biopsy from a hyperpigmented lesion on the dorsum of the hand revealed filiform elongation of rete ridges. Histopathology of the axillary lesion was similar except for features of follicular plugging. Melan-A stain confirmed increased melanocytes. Skin biopsy from the hypopigmented lesion on the left leg revealed decreased melanocytes. 

Treatment:

The patient was provided with medical advice and was advised to follow up for periodic monitoring. No further treatment was offered during the hospital stay. 

Diagnostic Conclusion:

The patient was diagnosed with a rare disorder characterized by hyperpigmented and hypopigmented macules in a reticulate pattern and multiple pits on the palms along with nonacne facial scarring. Clinically, this diagnosis resembles Dowling-Degos disease, which primarily affects people in their twenties and thirties. 

Follow Up:

The patient was advised to follow up for periodic monitoring. No further treatment was offered during the hospital stay.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to our hospital because of dark-colored spots on the hands and feet. The patient also had light-colored spots on the middle part of the body. These spots had been there for three years and did not cause any pain or discomfort. Over time, more dark spots appeared and spread to other parts of the body. A skin exam showed slightly thin, dark spots in a net-like pattern. There were also many light-colored spots spread across the middle body and the front of both legs. The patient had small holes on both palms where the normal skin lines were broken. The patient also had scars on the face, mostly on the nose and cheeks, that were not caused by acne.

Diagnostic Assessment:

A small sample of skin was taken from a dark spot on the back of the hand. It showed that the lower layer of skin had grown longer than normal in a finger-like shape. A skin sample from the armpit area looked similar but also showed blocked hair openings. A special test confirmed there were more color-producing cells than normal. A skin sample from a light spot on the left leg showed fewer color-producing cells than normal.

Treatment:

The patient received medical advice and was told to come back for regular check-ups. No other treatment was given during the hospital stay.

Diagnostic Conclusion:

The patient has a rare skin condition. It causes dark and light spots in a net-like pattern. It also causes small holes on the palms and facial scars not caused by acne. This condition looks like Dowling-Degos disease, which usually affects people in their twenties and thirties.

Follow Up:

The patient was told to come back for regular check-ups. No other treatment was given during the hospital stay.

input:
Discharge Summary

Admission Date: [INSERT DATE]
Discharge Date: [INSERT DATE]

Patient Name: [INSERT NAME]
Gender: Female
Age: 56

History of Present Illness:
The patient was admitted with an unruptured right MCA aneurysm. She had no history of subarachnoid hemorrhage. With a PHASES score of 9, the patient had a 4.3% 5-yr risk of rupture. The aneurysm morphology and regional vascular anatomy were closely examined in the 360°XR model from the patient's CTA with contrast and T1-weighted MRI MPRAGE (Video 1). Preoperative planning was also performed in VR to select the appropriate clip and rehearse placement to identify the optimal angle.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent a right pterional craniotomy, and a #11 Sugita clip was placed along the neck with awareness that the artery's recurrent course would not also be occluded by the clip. With increased awareness provided by the overlay, the inferior jaw of the clip was used to sweep this branch out of the way, and prevent it from becoming trapped against the neck. Using ICG angiography, flow was confirmed to the distal vessels at the MCA trifurcation.

Postoperative imaging verified appropriate clip placement and maintained blood flow through the parent and distal vessels. The patient made a full recovery.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition.

Discharge Medications:
None

Follow-up Care:
The patient was scheduled for a follow-up appointment with the physician.

Healthcare Provider:
[INSERT NAME], M.D.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Admission Date: [INSERT DATE]
Discharge Date: [INSERT DATE]

Patient Name: [INSERT NAME]
Gender: Female
Age: 56

History of Present Illness:
The patient was admitted with a bulge in a blood vessel on the right side of the brain. This bulge had not burst. She had no history of bleeding in the brain. Based on a risk score of 9, the patient had about a 4 in 100 chance of the bulge bursting within 5 years. The doctors used special 3D imaging from a scan with dye and a brain scan to study the bulge and nearby blood vessels. The doctors also used virtual reality to plan the surgery, choose the right clip, and practice placing it at the best angle.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery to open a small part of the skull on the right side. The doctors placed a small metal clip on the base of the bulge. They were careful not to block a nearby blood vessel that curves back on itself. Using the 3D view as a guide, the doctors used the bottom part of the clip to gently move this blood vessel out of the way. This kept it from getting pinched against the bulge. The doctors used a special dye test to make sure blood was still flowing to the vessels beyond the bulge.

Scans taken after surgery showed the clip was in the right place. Blood was still flowing through the main vessel and the smaller vessels. The patient fully recovered.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was sent home in stable condition.

Discharge Medications:
None

Follow-up Care:
The patient was scheduled for a follow-up appointment with the doctor.

Healthcare Provider:
[INSERT NAME], M.D.
