input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient name: not identified
Gender: Male
Age: 60 years

Medical History:
-15-year history of diabetes, medically controlled

Hospital Course:

The patient was referred to us with severe pain and decreased vision in both eyes two days after undergoing cataract surgery on the left eye and one month ago on the right eye. The patient had undergone phacoemulsification under topical anesthesia in both eyes one month apart with uneventful postoperative period after the first surgery. The patient presented with bilateral flat anterior chambers and high intraocular pressure (IOP) four hours after his cataract surgery. The patient was diagnosed and treated as malignant glaucoma and was given intravenous (IV) mannitol 20% (1-2 g/kg body weight), tab acetazolamide 250 mg thrice daily and timolol maleate 0.5% eye drops twice daily in both eyes (B/E), peripheral iridotomy in the right eye and YAG hyloidotomy in the right eye, and posterior capsulotomy in the left eye.

On further examination, visual acuity was finger counting at half meter in the right eye and one meter in the left eye. There was bilateral lid edema, conjunctival chemosis, corneal edema, shallow ACs, mid-dilated pupils, patent peripheral iridotomy in the right eye, in-the-bag posterior chamber pseudophakos with opening in the posterior capsule. IOP was 72 mmHg in the right eye and 68 mmHg in the left eye. Fundus was unclear. B-scan Ultrasonography revealed annular cilio-choroidal effusion in both eyes.

A possible drug reaction was suspected and the systemic drug list was reviewed. Acetazolamide, a sulphonamide derivative was suspected as the possible cause and was discontinued. Topical antiglaucoma medications along with atropine, topical steroids, and antibiotics were continued. Over the next two weeks, the patient's condition improved. Five weeks following the initial surgery, his vision was 20/20, N6 with correction and IOP 21 mm Hg in the right eye and 20 mm Hg in the left eye without medications. ACs were quiet with clear corneas in both eyes and gonioscopy showed open angles in both eyes. Fundus examination revealed healthy optic discs with 0.5:1 cup and resolved choroidals in both eyes.

Disposition: The patient's vision was stable and his condition had improved over the two weeks. The patient was discharged in good condition with instructions to follow up as an outpatient.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient name: not identified
Gender: Male
Age: 60 years

Medical History:
The patient has had diabetes for 15 years. It is controlled with medicine.

Hospital Course:

The patient came to us with strong pain and loss of vision in both eyes. This happened two days after cataract surgery on the left eye. The right eye had cataract surgery one month before. Both surgeries went well at first. The surgeon removed the cloudy lens using sound waves and numbing eye drops. Four hours after the second surgery, both eyes had problems. The front parts of the eyes were too flat. The pressure inside the eyes was too high. Doctors thought this was a serious type of high eye pressure called malignant glaucoma. The patient received medicine through a vein to lower eye pressure. The patient also took pills three times a day to reduce eye fluid. Eye drops were given twice a day in both eyes to lower pressure. Doctors made small holes in parts of both eyes to help fluid drain.

Doctors checked the eyes more closely. The patient could only see fingers held very close to the face. Both eyelids were swollen. The clear front part of the eyes was swollen. The colored part of the eyes was puffy. The space at the front of the eyes was too shallow. The pupils were partly open. The new lenses from surgery were in the right place. The pressure was 72 in the right eye and 68 in the left eye. Normal is much lower. Doctors could not see the back of the eyes clearly. A sound wave test showed fluid buildup around the inside of both eyes.

Doctors thought a medicine might be causing the problem. They looked at all the medicines the patient was taking. One pill used to lower eye pressure seemed to be the cause. Doctors stopped that pill right away. The patient kept using eye drops for pressure, swelling, and infection. Over the next two weeks, the patient got much better. Five weeks after the first surgery, vision was very good in both eyes with glasses. Eye pressure was back to normal in both eyes without any medicine. The front of the eyes looked clear and calm. The drainage areas in both eyes were open. The back of the eyes looked healthy. The fluid buildup was gone.

Disposition: The patient's vision was steady and the condition got better over two weeks. The patient left the hospital in good health. The patient should come back for check-up visits.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 53-year-old male, presented with a swelling and pain in his left shoulder that had developed over 5 months. Radiological examination revealed an irregular bone lesion in the S1 region of the scapula. A CT-guided needle biopsy indicated grade I chondrosarcoma. The diagnosis of left scapular chondrosarcoma, Malawer S1 region, Enneking IA stage, grade I, was confirmed. The patient underwent a partial scapulectomy and a titanium alloy prosthesis was implanted.

Procedures:
- Partial scapulectomy
- Implantation of titanium alloy prosthesis

Diagnosis:
- Left scapular chondrosarcoma, Malawer S1 region, Enneking IA stage, grade I

Follow-up Plan:
- Patient was instructed to move his hand and elbow joint immediately after surgery
- Upper extremity was placed in a functional position of external support for 4 weeks post-surgery
- The stitches were removed 2 weeks after surgery
- Patient showed good recovery of shoulder joint function

Discharge Medications:
- None

Discharge Instructions:
- None

Condition at Discharge:
- Patient's condition was stable at the time of discharge
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 53-year-old man. He came to the hospital with swelling and pain in his left shoulder. These symptoms had been going on for 5 months. Imaging tests showed an abnormal growth in part of the shoulder blade. A doctor used a needle to take a small sample of the growth for testing. The tests showed a type of bone cancer called chondrosarcoma. This cancer was low-grade and had not spread. The cancer was located in the left shoulder blade. The patient had surgery to remove part of the shoulder blade. Doctors put in a metal replacement piece made of titanium.

Procedures:
Removal of part of the shoulder blade.
Placement of a titanium metal replacement piece.

Diagnosis:
Low-grade bone cancer in the left shoulder blade that has not spread.

Follow-up Plan:
The patient was told to move his hand and elbow right after surgery. The upper arm was held in a supportive position for 4 weeks after surgery. The stitches were taken out 2 weeks after surgery. The patient's shoulder movement recovered well.

Discharge Medications:
None.

Discharge Instructions:
None.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient was in stable condition when leaving the hospital.

input:
Note: To maintain patient privacy, some details have been fictionalized.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with presumptive diagnosis of LVAD pump thrombosis. During his hospital stay, he was managed with argatroban infusion and other medications including bumetanide, carvedilol, digoxin, spironolactone, and milrinone infusion. However, despite being on argatroban therapy, there was a significant increase in LVAD flows and power as well as a steadily increasing LDH. All options were discussed with the patient including LVAD exchange that he declined at that time.

After considering the patient's medical history and current condition, the clinical team decided to start tissue plasminogen activator (tPa) therapy, in which initial dosing included a 10 mg IV bolus followed by 1 mg/min for 20 min and finally 1 mg/h for 24 h. The patient showed an immediate improvement in the device parameters after administration of the initial tPa bolus. Over the following days, the patient's condition continued to improve as evidenced by falling LDH levels.

After 13 days of stabilization and various medical interventions, the patient was discharged from the hospital in stable condition. Subcutaneous enoxaparin and warfarin were instituted to manage the patient's condition while awaiting a suitable transplant donor.

Discharge Diagnosis:
LVAD pump thrombosis

Discharge Instructions:
- Continue taking prescribed medications as directed by your healthcare provider
- Follow a low-sodium diet, and manage fluid intake as recommended
- Continue all outpatient therapies as directed
- Monitor and report any unusual or significant symptoms such as chest pain, shortness of breath, dizziness, or swelling.

Follow-Up Appointments:
Please follow up with your cardiologist and other healthcare providers as recommended.

Signed,
[REDACTED]
[Date]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital because doctors thought there was a blood clot in his heart pump. During his stay, he received a blood thinner called argatroban through an IV. He also took other medicines to help his heart work better. These included bumetanide, carvedilol, digoxin, spironolactone, and milrinone. Even with the blood thinner, the heart pump was working harder than it should. A blood test called LDH kept going up, which showed the clot was still a problem. The care team talked with the patient about all choices, including replacing the heart pump. The patient said no to pump replacement at that time.

The care team looked at the patient's health history and current condition. They decided to try a strong clot-busting medicine called tPa. First, the patient got 10 mg through the IV all at once. Then he got 1 mg per minute for 20 minutes. After that, he got 1 mg per hour for 24 hours. The heart pump started working better right after the first dose of tPa. Over the next few days, the patient kept getting better. The LDH blood test levels went down, which meant the clot was going away.

After 13 days of treatment and getting stable, the patient left the hospital in good condition. The patient now takes two blood thinners called enoxaparin and warfarin. These are given as shots under the skin and pills. They will help prevent new clots while the patient waits for a heart transplant.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Blood clot in heart pump

Discharge Instructions:
Keep taking all medicines the way the doctor told the patient to take them. Eat foods low in salt and drink only as much fluid as the doctor recommended. Keep going to all outside treatments as directed. Watch for and report any unusual signs like chest pain, trouble breathing, feeling dizzy, or swelling.

Follow-Up Appointments:
Please see the heart doctor and other care providers as they recommended.

Signed,
[REDACTED]
[Date]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
DOB: [Date of Birth]
Admission Date: [Admission Date]
Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Medical History:
The patient had a history of obesity and underwent anterior cervical discectomy and fusion five years prior.

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the emergency room with a persistent headache and was admitted to the hospital. A CT scan and MRI of the brain and spine showed extensive pneumocephalus and potential meningitis. After imaging and consultation with the neurosurgery team, the patient was found to have a CSF leak, and surgery was recommended.

The otolaryngology/rhinology team was consulted for further evaluation of the CSF leak. The patient was taken to the operating room for identification and repair of the CSF leak. The location of the potential defect was confirmed with intraoperative surgical navigation, and an area of mucosal thickening was noted. A small, pinpoint fistula with actively leaking CSF was also found in the posterior oropharynx.

The head and neck surgery team was asked to assist with repair of the fistula. An anterior neck approach to the cervical hardware was performed, and the anterior cervical hardware was removed, revealing a profuse CSF leak. The defect was repaired with DuraSeal followed by rotation of the right omohyoid muscle into the site of the defect between the prevertebral fascia and the oropharyngeal mucosa. The patient was then monitored in the neurosurgery intensive care unit.

Outcome:
The patient's headaches resolved, and repeat imaging showed resolution of the pneumocephalus. He remained in the ICU on bed rest with the lumbar drain open at 10 ml/hr for 5 days. The patient was then allowed to mobilize, and the lumbar drain was removed. He was subsequently discharged and continued to do well with no evidence of CSF leak on follow-up imaging.

Follow-up:
A repeat CT myelogram of the spine at 8 months showed no signs of persistent leak. The patient was advised to follow-up with his primary care provider for routine care.

Diagnosis:
CSF Leak

Procedure:
Repair of CSF Leak with anterior neck approach to cervical hardware and DuraSeal repair, followed by the rotation of right omohyoid muscle into the site of the defect.

Disclaimer:
The information contained in this document is confidential and intended for the use of the patient only. If you are not the intended recipient, please notify the sender and delete this message immediately.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
DOB: [Date of Birth]
Admission Date: [Admission Date]
Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Medical History:
The patient had a history of obesity. Five years ago, the patient had neck surgery to remove a damaged disc and join the bones together.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room with a headache that would not go away. The patient was admitted to the hospital. Brain and spine scans showed air inside the skull and a possible brain infection. After reviewing the scans, the brain surgery team found a spinal fluid leak. They recommended surgery to fix it.

The ear, nose, and throat team was asked to help find the spinal fluid leak. The patient had surgery to find and fix the leak. Doctors used a computer guide during surgery to find the problem area. They saw thickened tissue and a tiny hole in the back of the throat that was leaking spinal fluid.

The head and neck surgery team helped fix the hole. They went in through the front of the neck to reach the hardware from the old surgery. They removed the metal hardware and found a large spinal fluid leak. They sealed the hole with a special medical glue. Then they moved a small neck muscle over the hole to protect it. The patient was watched closely in the brain surgery intensive care unit.

Outcome:
The patient's headaches went away. New scans showed the air inside the skull was gone. The patient stayed in the intensive care unit on bed rest for five days. A tube in the lower back drained spinal fluid at a slow, steady rate during this time. After five days, the patient was allowed to move around. The drain tube was removed. The patient went home and continued to do well. Follow-up scans showed no signs of spinal fluid leak.

Follow-up:
A spine scan with dye eight months later showed no signs of ongoing leak. The patient was told to see the regular doctor for routine care.

Diagnosis:
Spinal Fluid Leak

Procedure:
Repair of spinal fluid leak through the front of the neck. Removal of old neck hardware. Sealing of the hole with medical glue. Moving of a small neck muscle over the hole to protect the repair.

Disclaimer:
The information contained in this document is confidential and intended for the use of the patient only. If you are not the intended recipient, please notify the sender and delete this message immediately.

They saw thickened tissue and a tiny hole in the back of the throat that was leaking spinal fluid. The head and neck surgery team helped fix the hole. They went in through the front of the neck to reach the hardware from the old surgery. They removed the metal hardware and found a large spinal fluid leak. They sealed the hole with a special medical glue. Then they moved a small neck muscle over the hole to protect it. The patient was watched closely in the brain surgery intensive care unit. Outcome:
The patient's headaches went away. New scans showed the air inside the skull was gone. The patient stayed in the intensive care unit on bed rest for five days. A tube in the lower back drained spinal fluid at a slow, steady rate during this time. After five days, the patient was allowed to move around. The drain tube was removed. The patient went home and continued to do well. Follow-up scans showed no signs of spinal fluid leak. Follow-up:
A spine scan with dye eight months later showed no signs of ongoing leak. The patient was told to see the regular doctor for routine care. Removal of old neck hardware. Sealing of the hole with medical glue. Moving of a small neck muscle over the hole to protect the repair. Disclaimer:
The information contained in this document is confidential and intended for the use of the patient only. If you are not the intended recipient, please notify the sender and delete this message immediately.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was a 43-year-old male with a 17-year history of paraplegia who presented with dark purple discoloration of the left second and third toes. He had been experiencing a cold sensation in both feet for the past year. The patient had a history of hypertension and was taking hydrochlorothiazide-valsartan, kallidinogenase, ranitidine, levosulpiride, phloroglucinol, and loxoprofen. Physical examination showed both feet were cold, although the pulse was palpable on ankles and heels bilaterally. A portable Doppler device showed a decrease in pulse signal intensity on the left side and prolonged capillary refilling time on both toes. Despite a vascular function test showing normal leg circulation levels, the skin over the left toes showed dark discoloration with skin necrosis. The patient's antihypertensive medication was switched to a calcium channel blocker, amlodipine besylate, to address suspicion of Raynaud's phenomenon. Within 3 days, the patient saw a decrease in cold sensation and normalization of capillary refilling time. A medium split-thickness skin graft was performed on the wound, which healed without complications.

Consultations:

A consultation was requested with vascular and plastic surgery, who recommended wound monitoring and a skin graft to address the skin necrosis.

Discharge Condition:

The patient was discharged improved after treatment of his toe discoloration and skin necrosis.

Discharge Medications:

The patient was prescribed amlodipine besylate.

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to follow-up with his primary care physician for further management of his hypertension and any changes to his medication.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient was a 43-year-old man who had been unable to move his legs for 17 years. He came to the hospital because his left second and third toes had turned dark purple. His feet had felt cold for about a year. The patient had high blood pressure. He was taking several medicines including a water pill, a blood pressure pill, a stomach medicine, and pain medicine. When doctors checked his feet, both feet felt cold to the touch. However, they could still feel a pulse at his ankles and heels on both sides. A device that checks blood flow showed weaker blood flow signals on the left side. Blood took longer than normal to return to the skin when pressed on both feet. A test of blood vessel function showed normal blood flow in the legs. But the skin on the left toes had turned dark and the tissue was dying. Doctors thought the patient might have a condition where blood vessels tighten too much in the cold. They changed his blood pressure medicine to a different type called amlodipine besylate. Within 3 days, his feet felt less cold. Blood flow to the skin returned to normal. Doctors did surgery to place healthy skin over the damaged area. The wound healed well without any problems.

Consultations:

Doctors who specialize in blood vessels and skin repair were asked to help. They suggested watching the wound closely and doing surgery to cover the damaged skin.

Discharge Condition:

The patient left the hospital in better health after treatment for his dark toe color and dying skin tissue.

Discharge Medications:

The patient was given amlodipine besylate to take at home.

Follow-up:

The patient was told to see his regular doctor. This visit is to check on his high blood pressure and any changes to his medicines.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with complaints of nausea, vomiting, and epigastric pain. On examination, mild epigastric tenderness was noted. The patient had been experiencing intermittent epigastric discomfort for more than 12 months but had not sought medical attention until now. 

The differential diagnosis included peptic ulcer disease, malignancy, and gastritis. Tests revealed the presence of H. pylori infection and the patient underwent endoscopy. The endoscopic findings were consistent with moderate nodular mucosal changes throughout the entire gastric region, with the most prominent changes being in the gastric body and angle. Biopsies revealed increased intraepithelial lymphocytes without gland destruction. Immunohistochemistry showed these cells to be mostly positive for CD3 and CD8 but negative for CD4 or CD20.

Eradication therapy was started, but follow-up tests showed persistence of the H. pylori infection. The patient underwent a second eradication therapy, which was successful in resolving the infection. Clinical symptoms also resolved, and follow-up tests showed no evidence of recurrent infection or mucosal changes.

Summary:

The patient was admitted with complaints of nausea, vomiting, and epigastric pain. Endoscopy and biopsies revealed moderate nodular mucosal changes throughout the entire gastric region, with increased intraepithelial lymphocytes without gland destruction. Immunohistochemistry showed these cells to be mostly positive for CD3 and CD8 but negative for CD4 or CD20, consistent with gastritis. H. pylori infection was present and eradicated with a second course of treatment. The patient's symptoms resolved, and follow-up tests showed no evidence of recurrence or mucosal changes.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with an upset stomach, throwing up, and pain in the upper belly area. During the exam, the doctor found mild tenderness in the upper belly. The patient had been having on-and-off upper belly discomfort for more than 12 months but had not seen a doctor until now.

The doctors thought the problem could be a stomach ulcer, cancer, or stomach lining irritation. Tests found a stomach infection caused by a germ called H. pylori. The patient had a procedure where a camera looked inside the stomach. The camera showed moderate bumpy changes in the lining throughout the whole stomach area. The most noticeable changes were in the main part and curve of the stomach. Small tissue samples showed too many immune cells in the stomach lining, but no damage to the glands. Special lab tests showed these cells were mostly one type of immune cell, not others.

Treatment to kill the germ was started, but follow-up tests showed the infection was still there. The patient received a second round of treatment, which worked and cleared the infection. The patient's symptoms went away. Follow-up tests showed no sign of the infection coming back or changes in the stomach lining.

Summary:

The patient came to the hospital with an upset stomach, throwing up, and upper belly pain. A camera procedure and tissue samples showed moderate bumpy changes in the stomach lining with too many immune cells but no gland damage. Special lab tests showed these were a certain type of immune cell, which matched stomach lining irritation. An H. pylori germ infection was found and cleared with a second round of treatment. The patient's symptoms went away. Follow-up tests showed no sign of the infection or stomach changes coming back.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient: 11-year-old female child of South-Asian Indian origin

Date of Discharge: [Date]

Admission Date: [Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Septic leg infection

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the orthopaedics department with complaints of fever, non-traumatic pain and swelling in the right leg for about 3 days. The patient was diagnosed with a septic leg infection. Blood investigations showed a raised white cell count and marginally increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Blood sample was sent for culture and sensitivity which later showed gram negative bacilli.

Incision and drainage was performed the following day. Thick purulent discharge was drained and about 5 ml of the discharge was sent for culture and sensitivity. The wound was left open and dressed appropriately. The organism was identified as C. indologenes.

The patient was empirically started on parenteral cefotaxime, metronidazole, and gentamicin, was changed over to intravenous ceftazidime and metronidazole infusion based on the sensitivity report following which significant clinical improvement was noted. Subsequently secondary closure was done 10 days later. The wound healed well and the patient was discharged on oral antibiotics with follow-up.

Discharge Diagnosis: Septic leg infection

Course of Hospitalization:

The patient presented with a septic leg infection. Blood investigations showed a raised white cell count and marginally increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Blood sample was sent for culture and sensitivity which showed gram negative bacilli.

The patient underwent incision and drainage the following day. The organism was identified as C. indologenes. The patient was empirically started on parenteral cefotaxime, metronidazole, and gentamicin, was changed over to intravenous ceftazidime and metronidazole infusion based on the sensitivity report. Subsequently secondary closure was done 10 days later. The wound healed well and the patient was discharged on oral antibiotics with follow-up.

Summary:

The patient was diagnosed with a septic leg infection and underwent incision and drainage. The organism was identified as C. indologenes. The patient received appropriate treatment and showed significant clinical improvement. The secondary closure was done 10 days later, and the wound healed well. The patient was discharged on oral antibiotics with follow-up.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient: 11-year-old female child of South-Asian Indian origin

Date of Discharge: [Date]

Admission Date: [Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Serious leg infection caused by germs

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the bone and joint department with fever, pain, and swelling in the right leg for about 3 days. The leg pain was not caused by an injury. The patient was found to have a serious leg infection caused by germs. Blood tests showed a high number of white blood cells. The blood tests also showed the body was fighting an infection. A blood sample was sent to the lab to find out what germ was causing the infection. The lab found a type of bacteria in the blood.

The next day, doctors made a cut to let the infected fluid drain out. Thick yellow-green fluid came out of the leg. About 5 ml of this fluid was sent to the lab for testing. The wound was left open and covered with clean bandages. The lab found the germ was a type of bacteria called C. indologenes.

The patient was first given three germ-fighting medicines through a tube in the vein. These medicines were cefotaxime, metronidazole, and gentamicin. After the lab showed which medicines would work best, the doctors changed to ceftazidime and metronidazole given through the vein. The patient got much better after this change. Ten days later, doctors closed the wound with stitches. The wound healed well. The patient went home with germ-fighting pills to take by mouth and a follow-up visit planned.

Discharge Diagnosis: Serious leg infection caused by germs

Course of Hospitalization:

The patient had a procedure the next day where doctors made a cut to let infected fluid drain out. The lab found the germ was a type of bacteria called C. indologenes. The patient was first given three germ-fighting medicines through a tube in the vein. These were cefotaxime, metronidazole, and gentamicin. After the lab showed which medicines would work best, doctors changed to ceftazidime and metronidazole given through the vein. Ten days later, doctors closed the wound with stitches. The wound healed well. The patient went home with germ-fighting pills to take by mouth and a follow-up visit planned.

Summary:

The patient was found to have a serious leg infection caused by germs. Doctors made a cut to let the infected fluid drain out. The lab found the germ was a type of bacteria called C. indologenes. The patient received the right treatment and got much better. Doctors closed the wound with stitches ten days later. The wound healed well. The patient went home with germ-fighting pills to take by mouth and a follow-up visit planned.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Chief Complaint:
The patient presented with sudden onset breathlessness and right sided pleuritic chest pain.

History of Present Illness:
The patient had a painful swelling of his right leg while he was being treated in the hospital for recurrent episodes of ENL. On examination, the patient was dyspneic at rest without central cyanosis. He had leonine facies and generalized erythematous skin with multiple nodules. His right leg was swollen with pitting ankle edema while having normal arterial pulses. His respiratory system examination revealed a respiratory rate of 30 per minute and signs of right lower zone pleural effusion.

Past Medical History:
The patient had lepromatous leprosy following a positive slit skin smear for mycobacterium leprae. He was commenced on World Health Organization (WHO) multi-drug therapy (MDT) regimen for multi-bacillary (MB) leprosy, which was complicated by type II lepra reaction (ENL) with right sided panuveitis and treated with oral prednisolone. 

Review of Systems:
There was no history suggestive of past venous or arterial thrombosis and tendency to develop thrombosis in his family. Neurology revealed a glove and stocking sensory peripheral neuropathy. There was no organomegaly noted on abdominal examination.

Diagnostic Testing:
A venous doppler ultrasound of the lower limb confirmed a deep vein thrombosis in the right femoral vein. Subsequently, a computed tomography pulmonary angiography (CTPA) confirmed pulmonary emboli in the right and left main pulmonary arteries with right lower lobe lung collapse and a mild pleural effusion.

Treatment:
The patient was started on enoxaparin and warfarin concomitantly. Thalidomide and anti-leprosy therapy were stopped temporarily while low dose corticosteroid was continued. After one week, the patient's dyspnea and general condition improved.

Follow-up:
The patient was discharged and provided with instructions to follow up with his healthcare provider for ongoing treatment and monitoring.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Chief Complaint:
The patient came to the hospital because of sudden trouble breathing and sharp pain on the right side of the chest.

History of Present Illness:
The patient had a painful swollen right leg while being treated in the hospital for repeated skin reactions from leprosy. When checked, the patient had trouble breathing while resting but did not have blue skin. The patient had a lion-like face and red skin all over with many small bumps. The right leg was swollen with ankle puffiness that left dents when pressed. The blood flow to the foot was normal. The patient was breathing 30 times per minute. There were signs of fluid around the right lower lung.

Past Medical History:
The patient had a serious form of leprosy found through a skin test. The patient started taking a mix of medicines from the World Health Organization to treat leprosy. This treatment caused a skin reaction with eye swelling on the right side. The patient took a steroid medicine called prednisolone by mouth to treat this.

Review of Systems:
The patient had no past history of blood clots. No family members had problems with blood clots. The patient had nerve damage causing numbness in the hands and feet. The belly organs were normal size.

Diagnostic Testing:
An ultrasound of the leg veins showed a blood clot in the right thigh vein. A special scan of the lungs showed blood clots in the main lung blood vessels on both sides. The scan also showed the right lower lung had collapsed and had some fluid around it.

Treatment:
The patient started taking two blood thinner medicines called enoxaparin and warfarin at the same time. The leprosy medicines and thalidomide were stopped for now. A low dose steroid was continued. After one week, the patient was breathing better and feeling better overall.

Follow-up:
The patient left the hospital with instructions to see a doctor for continued care and check-ups.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Insert Name]

Medical Record Number: [Insert Number]

Admission Date: [Insert Date]

Discharge Date: [Insert Date]

Diagnosis:

- Congenital Left Diaphragmatic Hernia with Volvulus and Obstruction of Herniated Part of Stomach
- Acutely Torted Intrathoracic Spleen and Stomach
- Left Part of Transverse Colon, and Small Bowel Loops

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with complaints of decreased appetite, dysphagia, epigastric pain, and shortness of breath. The chest X-ray and CT scan revealed a distended stomach and enlarged spleen with shift of mediastinal structures to the right side suggesting Bochdalek hernia. 

The patient underwent laparoscopic repair that was converted to laparotomy. During surgery, a large congenital left diaphragmatic hernia was observed with acutely torted intrathoracic spleen and stomach. The hernia was reduced successfully and a Gore-Tex mesh was applied to the diaphragmatic defect. 

The patient had an uneventful recovery and was discharged 10 days after the procedure. The patient was advised to follow-up with their primary care physician and continue treatment as prescribed. 

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient presented with typical symptoms of Bochdalek hernia and was diagnosed with the condition. A laparoscopic repair was initially attempted but was converted to laparotomy. The hernia was reduced successfully and a Gore-Tex mesh was applied. The patient recovered well and was discharged. The patient was advised to continue treatment as prescribed and follow-up with their primary care physician.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Insert Name]

Medical Record Number: [Insert Number]

Admission Date: [Insert Date]

Discharge Date: [Insert Date]

Diagnosis:

Birth defect causing a hole in the left side of the muscle that separates the chest from the belly. This hole allowed the stomach, spleen, part of the large intestine, and part of the small intestine to move up into the chest. The stomach and spleen became twisted and blocked.

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with less hunger than usual, trouble swallowing, pain in the upper belly, and trouble breathing. A chest X-ray and CT scan showed a stretched stomach and enlarged spleen. These organs had pushed into the chest and shifted the heart and nearby parts to the right side. This pointed to a type of birth defect called a hole in the muscle below the lungs.

The patient had surgery. Doctors first tried to operate using small cuts and a camera. They had to switch to a larger cut to complete the repair. During surgery, doctors found a large hole in the muscle below the lungs on the left side. The spleen and stomach had moved into the chest and become twisted. Doctors moved these organs back to where they belong. They used a special patch to close the hole in the muscle.

The patient healed well with no problems. The patient left the hospital 10 days after surgery. The patient was told to see their regular doctor for follow-up care. The patient was told to keep taking medicine as directed.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient had signs of a birth defect causing a hole in the muscle below the lungs. Tests confirmed this problem. Doctors first tried surgery with small cuts and a camera. They had to switch to a larger cut. Doctors moved the organs back into place and used a special patch to close the hole. The patient healed well and left the hospital. The patient was told to keep taking medicine as directed and see their regular doctor.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: ________________

Date of Admission: ________________          Date of Discharge: ________________

Hospital: ________________                Hospitalist: Dr. ________________

Clinical History:
The 30-year-old female patient presented with radicular pain in both lower limbs which had been ongoing for the past two years, with increased symptoms for the past two months associated with urinary incontinence. The patient underwent clinical examination, which revealed motor weakness of 4/5 in L5 and S1 myotomes in bilateral lower limbs with perianal hypoesthesia. MRI tests showed a diffuse annular bulge with a large posterocentral extrusion at L4-L5. The patient was planned for L4-L5 discectomy. Intraoperative adherent intradural disc material was carefully dissected from adjacent rootlets and dura.

Hospital Course:
The patient received the L4-L5 discectomy procedure as planned. The postoperative period saw some improvement in the patient's symptoms, though the motor strength and urinary incontinence did not improve. On retrospective reanalysis of MRI, an abnormal hypointense intradural structure was noted at the level of L5.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with diffuse annular bulge with a large posterocentral extrusion at L4-L5 with an abnormal hypointense intradural structure at L5.

Treatment:
The patient underwent L4-L5 discectomy and had adherent intradural disc material carefully dissected from adjacent rootlets and dura.

Prognosis: 
The patient showed improvement in symptoms but motor strength and urinary incontinence did not improve.

Follow-up:
The patient is advised to undergo a regular follow-up with the healthcare provider for further medical consultation and diagnostic tests.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: ________________

Date of Admission: ________________          Date of Discharge: ________________

Hospital: ________________                Hospitalist: Dr. ________________

Clinical History:
The 30-year-old female patient came in with shooting pain down both legs. This pain had lasted for two years. The pain got worse over the past two months. The patient also had trouble controlling the bladder. A physical exam showed mild muscle weakness in both legs. The patient also had reduced feeling in the area around the bottom. An MRI scan showed a disc problem in the lower back at the L4-L5 level. Part of the disc was bulging and pushing out of place. The doctors planned surgery to remove the damaged disc material. During surgery, disc material that had moved into the spinal fluid space was carefully removed from nearby nerves.

Hospital Course:
The patient had the planned surgery to remove the disc material at L4-L5. After surgery, some symptoms got better. However, the muscle weakness and bladder control problems did not improve. When doctors looked at the MRI again, they found an unusual dark structure inside the spinal fluid space at the L5 level.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a bulging disc at L4-L5. Part of the disc was pushing out in the center toward the back. There was also an unusual structure found inside the spinal fluid space at L5.

Treatment:
The patient had surgery to remove the damaged disc at L4-L5. Disc material that had moved into the spinal fluid space was carefully removed from nearby nerves.

Prognosis:
The patient showed some improvement in symptoms. However, muscle strength and bladder control did not get better.

Follow-up:
The patient should see the doctor regularly for more check-ups and tests.

The patient had the planned surgery to remove the disc material at L4-L5. After surgery, some symptoms got better. However, the muscle weakness and bladder control problems did not improve. When doctors looked at the MRI again, they found an unusual dark structure inside the spinal fluid space at the L5 level. Part of the disc was pushing out in the center toward the back. There was also an unusual structure found inside the spinal fluid space at L5. Treatment:
The patient had surgery to remove the damaged disc at L4-L5. Disc material that had moved into the spinal fluid space was carefully removed from nearby nerves. Prognosis:
The patient showed some improvement in symptoms. However, muscle strength and bladder control did not get better. Follow-up:
The patient should see the doctor regularly for more check-ups and tests.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 68
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Diagnosis: 
- Aphakic bullous keratopathy 
- Corneal scarring

Hospital Course: 
The patient underwent penetrating keratoplasty for aphakic bullous keratopathy and corneal scarring. The donor cornea was replaced due to a large dendritic-geographic epithelial ulcer noted immediately after placement. Perioperative administration of intravenous aciclovir was given. Postoperatively, the patient was started on systemic and local antiviral therapy, and local antibiotic treatment. The graft was clear and re-epithelialized at 1 week. Long term prophylactic acyclovir 400 mg was started. However, six months later the patient developed a geographic corneal ulcer involving the graft-host interface with keratic precipitates and corneal edema. The patient was treated with acyclovir 400 mg and topical aciclovir both five times a day for 2 weeks with resolution of the ulcer and residual scarring.

Summary: 
The patient who had aphakic bullous keratopathy and corneal scarring underwent a successful penetrating keratoplasty with the use of a donor corneoscleral disc. The initial donor corneoscleral disc was removed and replaced with a second donor corneoscleral disc which was available. The patient was treated with antiviral and antibiotic medications following the surgery. The patient developed a geographic corneal ulcer, which was treated successfully with antiviral medications. The patient was discharged with instructions for a follow-up within a week.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 68
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Diagnosis:
The patient has swelling and blisters on the clear front part of the eye after lens removal. The patient also has scarring on the clear front part of the eye.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery to replace the damaged clear front part of the eye with tissue from a donor. The first donor tissue showed a large sore right after it was placed. It was removed and replaced with new donor tissue. The patient received antiviral medicine through a vein during surgery. After surgery, the patient was given antiviral pills and eye drops. The patient also received antibiotic eye drops to prevent infection. After one week, the new tissue was clear and the surface had healed. The patient started taking acyclovir 400 mg pills for long term protection. Six months later, a sore appeared on the new tissue where it meets the original eye tissue. There was also clouding and small spots on the clear front part of the eye. The patient took acyclovir 400 mg pills and used antiviral eye drops five times a day for two weeks. The sore healed but left some scarring.

Summary:
The patient had swelling and scarring on the clear front part of the eye. The patient had surgery to replace this damaged tissue with donor tissue. The first donor tissue had to be replaced with a second one. After surgery, the patient took antiviral and antibiotic medicines. Later, a sore developed on the clear front part of the eye. This was treated successfully with antiviral medicines. The patient was sent home with instructions to return for a check-up within one week.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: XXX, Gender: Male, DOB: XX/XX/XXXX
Medical Record Number: XXXXX

Admission date: XX/XX/XXXX, Discharge date: XX/XX/XXXX

Hospital course:
The patient presented to the ED with a four-day history of high-grade fever, vomiting and loose stools, and a sudden headache. He was diagnosed with meningitis based on an elevated total leukocyte count with 84% lymphocytes found in the cerebrospinal fluid. An EV PCR was also detected in the cerebrospinal fluid using a biofire filmarray meningitis/encephalitis panel. The patient was started on empirical IV antibiotics, which were later discontinued after a more thorough evaluation. Symptomatic treatment was given along with IV hydration, and the patient remained afebrile and stable throughout hospitalization. The headache improved with oral analgesics and the patient was discharged home on the third day. 

Discharge diagnosis: 
Meningitis 

Discharge condition: 
The patient's condition improved during the hospital stay and he is now clinically stable with complete resolution of symptoms. 

Treatment plan: 
The patient was discharged with instruction to continue the oral analgesics for headache relief. No further treatment is required at this time. 

Follow-up plan:
The patient was advised to follow-up with his primary care physician if symptoms reoccur or new symptoms develop. 

Summary of results: 
Laboratory investigations revealed no significant abnormalities other than an elevated total leukocyte count with 84% lymphocytes, which is consistent with viral meningitis. MRI brain with contrast was unremarkable. 

Summary of procedures:
No procedures were performed during this hospitalization.

Medications on Discharge: 
None prescribed 

Allergies:
None mentioned on admission. 

Instructions: 
The patient was advised to get plenty of rest and adequate hydration. He was instructed to follow-up with his primary care physician if symptoms return or new symptoms develop.

Discharge condition: 
The patient was discharged in good condition with complete resolution of symptoms and no future follow-up is required.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: XXX, Gender: Male, DOB: XX/XX/XXXX
Medical Record Number: XXXXX

Admission date: XX/XX/XXXX, Discharge date: XX/XX/XXXX

Hospital course:
The patient came to the emergency room with four days of high fever, vomiting, loose stools, and sudden headache. The patient was found to have meningitis, which is swelling of the protective layers around the brain and spinal cord. A spinal fluid test showed a high number of white blood cells, with 84% being a type called lymphocytes. A special test also found a virus in the spinal fluid. The patient was first given antibiotics through an IV, but these were stopped after more tests were done. The patient received treatment for symptoms and fluids through an IV. The patient had no fever and stayed stable during the hospital stay. The headache got better with pain medicine taken by mouth. The patient went home on the third day.

Discharge diagnosis:
Meningitis, which is swelling of the protective layers around the brain and spinal cord.

Discharge condition:
The patient got better during the hospital stay. The patient is now stable and all symptoms have gone away.

Treatment plan:
The patient was sent home with instructions to keep taking pain medicine by mouth for headache relief. No other treatment is needed at this time.

Follow-up plan:
The patient was told to see his regular doctor if symptoms come back or new symptoms appear.

Summary of results:
Lab tests showed no major problems except for a high white blood cell count with 84% lymphocytes. This matches what is seen with viral meningitis. A brain scan with dye showed no problems.

Summary of procedures:
No procedures were done during this hospital stay.

Medications on Discharge:
None prescribed.

Allergies:
None listed when the patient arrived.

Instructions:
The patient was told to get plenty of rest and drink enough fluids. The patient was told to see his regular doctor if symptoms come back or new symptoms appear.

Discharge condition:
The patient left the hospital in good condition. All symptoms have gone away. No future follow-up visits are needed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
Patient Name: Not Provided
Gender: Female
Age: 7 years
Medical Record Number: Not Provided

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with various clinical symptoms including lethargy, muscle weakness, skin issues, abdominal distension, polyuria, and polydipsia. Blood work and urinalysis showed increased blood glucose levels, hypercholesterolemia, and glycosuria. After the diagnosis of diabetes mellitus was confirmed, the patient was treated with insulin glargine and a commercial diet for diabetic cats. The insulin dosage was adjusted, and the patient showed considerable improvement in clinical symptoms. An abdominal ultrasound revealed bilateral adrenal hyperplasia and hepatic steatosis. Further tests ruled out spontaneous hypersomatotropism, hyperthyroidism, and feline immunodeficiency virus and feline leukemia virus. However, spontaneous hyperadrenocorticism (HAC) was suspected and diagnosed based on clinical signs, increased urinary cortisol:creatinine ratio, and lack of suppression on the low-dose dexamethasone suppression test. A dosage of cabergoline was prescribed to address the HAC. After the second dose, the patient experienced clinical hypoglycemia that required emergency veterinary assistance. The symptoms of hypoglycemia were managed via appropriate treatment, and the patient was kept under medical observation for several hours. Following successful treatment and management of clinical symptoms, the patient was discharged.

Diagnosis:
1. Diabetes Mellitus
2. Spontaneous Hyperadrenocorticism (HAC)

Treatment:
1. Insulin glargine
2. Commercial diet for diabetic cats
3. Cabergoline

Condition at Discharge:
The patient had stable blood glucose levels and no clinical signs of diabetes mellitus or hypoglycemia. The HAC was treated with cabergoline and patient symptoms showed marked improvement. However, the patient experienced acute decompensation after seven months, and euthanasia was carried out upon the owner's request. 

Recommendations:
None provided due to the patient's clinical outcome.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
Patient Name: Not Provided
Gender: Female
Age: 7 years
Medical Record Number: Not Provided

Hospital Course:
The patient was brought to the hospital with several health problems. These included tiredness, weak muscles, skin problems, a swollen belly, peeing a lot, and drinking a lot of water. Blood tests and urine tests showed high blood sugar, high cholesterol, and sugar in the urine. After doctors confirmed the patient had diabetes, treatment began with a long-acting insulin called insulin glargine. The patient was also put on a special diet made for diabetic cats. The insulin dose was changed over time, and the patient got much better. A belly scan using sound waves showed that both adrenal glands were enlarged. The scan also showed fatty liver disease. More tests ruled out other conditions including growth hormone problems, overactive thyroid, and two cat viruses. However, doctors thought the patient might have a condition where the body makes too much of a stress hormone called cortisol. This condition is called Cushing's disease. It was confirmed using signs the patient showed, high cortisol levels in urine tests, and a special hormone test. A medicine called cabergoline was given to treat the Cushing's disease. After the second dose, the patient's blood sugar dropped too low. This needed emergency care from the vet. The low blood sugar was treated properly, and the patient was watched closely for several hours. After successful treatment and the health problems were under control, the patient went home.

Diagnosis:
1. Diabetes, a condition where the body cannot control blood sugar properly
2. Cushing's disease, a condition where the body makes too much stress hormone

Treatment:
1. Insulin glargine, a long-acting insulin
2. Special diet made for diabetic cats
3. Cabergoline, a medicine for Cushing's disease

Condition at Discharge:
The patient had steady blood sugar levels. There were no signs of diabetes or low blood sugar problems. The Cushing's disease was being treated with cabergoline, and the patient showed good improvement. However, after seven months the patient became very sick very quickly. The owner asked for the patient to be put to sleep, and this was done.

Recommendations:
None provided due to the patient's clinical outcome.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name:
Age:
Sex:
Date of admission:
Date of discharge:

Reason for admission:
The patient presented to the emergency department with a 2-day history of headache, photophobia, chills, night sweats, decreased appetite, weight loss, and fever.

Hospital Course:
The patient was started on empiric meningitis treatment with vancomycin and ceftriaxone. The initial laboratory results showed leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, hemoglobin, transaminitis, elevated C-reactive protein, and hypoalbuminemia. Lumbar puncture revealed elevated protein in the cerebrospinal fluid and vancomycin was discontinued. The patient was treated for presumed neurosyphilis with decreasing doses of ceftriaxone, and ART was initiated for acute retroviral syndrome. Computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis showed mesenteric adenitis. Noninvasive tests confirmed the suspicion of HLH secondary to acute HIV.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient presented with a headache and fever and was diagnosed with acute retroviral syndrome and HLH secondary to acute HIV. The patient was treated with ART, decreasing doses of ceftriaxone, and noninvasive tests to confirm the diagnosis.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient was discharged in stable condition with complete resolution of symptoms.

Follow-up:
The patient followed up outpatient and reported complete resolution of symptoms.

Age:
Sex:
Date of admission:
Date of discharge:

Reason for admission:
The patient came to the emergency room with symptoms that started 2 days before. These included headache, sensitivity to light, chills, night sweats, poor appetite, weight loss, and fever.

Hospital Course:
The patient was started on medicines to treat a possible brain infection. These medicines were vancomycin and ceftriaxone. Early blood tests showed low white blood cell count, low platelet count, low red blood cell levels, liver problems, high inflammation markers, and low protein levels. A spinal tap showed high protein in the spinal fluid. Vancomycin was then stopped. The patient was treated for a possible syphilis infection in the brain with lower doses of ceftriaxone over time. Medicine to treat HIV was also started because the patient had a new HIV infection. A scan of the belly area showed swollen lymph nodes in the gut. Tests confirmed that the patient had a condition called HLH caused by the new HIV infection. HLH is when the immune system becomes overactive and harms the body.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came in with headache and fever. The patient was found to have a new HIV infection and HLH caused by the HIV. The patient received HIV medicine and lower doses of ceftriaxone over time. Tests confirmed the diagnosis.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition. All symptoms had gone away.

Follow-up:
The patient had a follow-up visit after leaving the hospital. The patient reported that all symptoms had gone away.

input:
Discharge Summary: 

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Hospital Course: 

The patient, a 45-year-old male, was admitted to the hospital with symptoms of left flank pain, hypoesthesia, and weakness at both lower extremities. Upon examination, the patient had motor weakness in both lower extremities and elevated deep tendon reflex of the right knee. Diagnostic tests indicated fracture of T8, T9 vertebral body by bone tumor, pathologic fracture of T9 vertebral body by bone tumor, T8–10 myelopathy, and cord compression.

The patient underwent T9 corpectomy and mesh cage insertion with T8–10 posterior screw fixation. Pathological examination confirmed a diagnosis of Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH). The patient underwent radiotherapy at T8–10 spine and showed remarkable improvement in motor weaknesses and hypoesthesia.

The patient has been followed for almost 6 years without any recurrence. Discharge instructions included follow-up care with the primary care physician and regular imaging surveillance to ensure no recurrence of the bone tumor. 

Overall, the patient received excellent medical care from the healthcare team and responded well to treatment.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 45-year-old man. He came to the hospital with pain in his left side, numbness, and weakness in both legs. During the exam, the patient had weak muscles in both legs. The patient also had stronger than normal reflexes in the right knee. Tests showed that a bone tumor had caused breaks in the spine bones called T8 and T9. The tumor was also pressing on the spinal cord and causing nerve problems.

The patient had surgery to remove the damaged spine bone at T9. Doctors put in a metal cage to support the spine. They also placed screws at T8 through T10 to hold the spine steady. Lab tests of the removed tissue showed a condition called Langerhans cell histiocytosis. This is a rare disease where abnormal cells build up and can damage bones. The patient then had radiation treatment on the T8 through T10 area of the spine. After treatment, the leg weakness and numbness got much better.

The patient has been checked for almost 6 years with no sign of the tumor coming back. Discharge instructions included follow-up visits with the regular doctor. The patient should also get regular scans to make sure the bone tumor does not return.

Overall, the patient received excellent medical care from the healthcare team and responded well to treatment.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
A 75-year-old male patient was admitted to the hospital due to the development of acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) following surgery to drain a peritonsillar abscess that had extended down to the hypopharynx. Upon admission, the patient exhibited decreased peripheral oxygen saturation and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates, which were treated with noninvasive ventilation with a positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP). However, the patient's respiratory insufficiency continued to deteriorate, and after three days, the patient was intubated and treated with invasive ventilation. Two days later, the patient was tracheostomized, and CT scans of the lungs showed bilateral patchy infiltrates and dorsal consolidation extending from the cranial to caudal lung regions.

After six days of ARDS and three days of invasive continuous positive pressure ventilation (CPPV), a decision was made to apply continuous end-expiratory negative pressure ventilation (CENPV). During the first application, the patient's PaO2/FiO2 value increased significantly, and alveolar infiltrates showed impressive reduction. During the second application, the patient's PaO2/FiO2 value increased to more than 500 mmHg, indicating significant recruitment of lung volume without the use of any recruitment maneuvers. After five days of CENPV, the patient was weaned from the ventilator and discharged to the normal ward.

Summary of Treatment:
Upon admission, the patient received noninvasive ventilation with a positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) to improve their respiratory insufficiency. However, after the condition continued to deteriorate, the patient was treated with invasive continuous positive pressure ventilation (CPPV) followed by continuous end-expiratory negative pressure ventilation (CENPV), which improved the patient's respiratory status significantly. Following five days of CENPV, the patient was weaned from the ventilator and discharged to the normal ward.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's respiratory condition has significantly improved, and they have been discharged in stable condition. It is recommended that the patient continue to follow up with their primary care provider for further evaluation and monitoring. 

Follow-up Care:
The patient is recommended to maintain close follow-up care with their primary care provider. No further follow-up care is required from this hospital.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
A 75-year-old male patient was admitted to the hospital. The patient had serious breathing problems after surgery to drain a pocket of infection near the tonsils. This infection had spread down into the lower throat. When the patient arrived, oxygen levels in the blood were low. Both lungs showed signs of fluid buildup. The patient was first treated with a breathing mask that pushed air into the lungs. However, the breathing problems got worse. After three days, a breathing tube was placed into the windpipe. The patient was then put on a machine to help with breathing. Two days later, doctors made a small opening in the neck to help with breathing. Scans of the lungs showed patchy areas of fluid and collapsed tissue in both lungs from top to bottom.

After six days of serious breathing problems and three days on the breathing machine, doctors tried a different type of breathing support. This new method uses gentle suction instead of pushing air. During the first treatment, oxygen levels in the blood improved a lot. The fluid in the lungs also went down greatly. During the second treatment, oxygen levels got even better. This showed that more of the lungs were working again. After five days on this new breathing support, the patient was slowly taken off the breathing machine. The patient was then moved to a regular hospital room.

Summary of Treatment:
When the patient arrived, a breathing mask was used to push air into the lungs. This was meant to help with the breathing problems. However, the condition got worse. The patient was then put on a breathing machine that pushed air into the lungs. Later, doctors switched to a different method that uses gentle suction. This helped the patient breathe much better. After five days on this treatment, the patient was slowly taken off the breathing machine. The patient was then moved to a regular hospital room.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's breathing has improved a lot. The patient has been sent home in stable condition. The patient should see their regular doctor for checkups and monitoring.

Follow-up Care:
The patient should keep seeing their regular doctor. No other follow-up visits are needed at this hospital.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

PATIENT INFORMATION:
Name: [insert name]
Gender: Male
Age: 58 years old
Race: Korean
Medical record number: [insert number]
Admitting Diagnosis: Progressive Neuropathy

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient was admitted with complaints of involuntary facial twitching and weakness, loose skin, and a deep furrowed tongue which had been progressively worsening for four years. The symptoms resulted in paresthesia of the right hand, intermittent cramps on the right calf, and decreased sweating on both hands. Neurological exam revealed mild weakness in the orbicularis oculi and orbicularis oris muscles without any motor weakness or sensory changes observed in the four extremities. A family history of the same symptoms was found in his mother, maternal uncle, and two sisters but no non-Korean relatives. Electrophysiological studies, including nerve conduction study (NCS), electromyography (EMG), and autonomic function tests (AFTs), were performed which demonstrated signs of bilateral facial nerve dysfunction, chronic denervation of the cervical and lumbar regions, and sudomotor dysfunction. To identify the etiology of the undiagnosed neuropathy, whole-exome sequencing (WES) was performed following informed consent and it revealed the presence of a p.D214Y mutation in GSN.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Progressive neuropathy with associated mutation in GSN

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
[No medication information was provided in the case report]

clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

PATIENT INFORMATION:
Name: [insert name]
Gender: Male
Age: 58 years old
Race: Korean
Medical record number: [insert number]
Admitting Diagnosis: Nerve damage that is getting worse over time

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came to the hospital with face twitching and weakness that he could not control. He also had loose skin and deep grooves on his tongue. These problems had been getting worse for four years. The symptoms caused tingling in his right hand and muscle cramps in his right calf. He also noticed less sweating on both hands. The doctor checked his nerves and found mild weakness in the muscles around his eyes and mouth. There was no weakness or feeling changes in his arms or legs. His mother, uncle on his mother's side, and two sisters have the same symptoms. No family members who are not Korean have these symptoms. The doctors did tests to check how his nerves and muscles work. These tests showed problems with the nerves on both sides of his face. The tests also showed long-term nerve damage in his neck and lower back areas. There were also problems with his sweat glands. To find the cause of his nerve damage, the doctors did a special blood test to look at his genes. The patient agreed to this test. The test found a change in a gene called GSN.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Nerve damage that is getting worse over time, caused by a change in the GSN gene

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
[No medication information was provided in the case report]

FOLLOW-UP RECOMMENDATIONS:
Follow-up with primary care physician recommended.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name]
Sex: Female
Age: 61 years old
Date of admission: [Date]
Date of discharge: [Date]
Diagnosis: Papillary Fibroelastoma (PF)

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with recurrent paroxysmal pleuritic chest pain and severe dyspnea. Pulmonary CT angiography did not reveal any emboli, and cardiac tests showed a mobile mass attached to the septal tricuspid valve (TV) leaflet, measuring 1.3 × 0.9 × 1.1 cm with tricuspid regurgitation. A differential diagnosis of papillary fibroelastoma (PF) versus cardiac myxoma was entertained. Cardiac surgery was advised due to the established recurrent embolic potential of the tumor and size (>1 cm).

Surgery:

Shave excision of the sessile mass and TV repair using a 30-mm Medtronic Contour ring was performed in this patient.

Postoperative Procedures:

The patient's condition was monitored postoperatively with adequate treatment. Postoperatively, the patient continued anticoagulation for 8 weeks. To date, the patient remains asymptomatic at rest and exercise.

Follow-Up:

The patient is advised to follow a healthy lifestyle and to ensure adequate follow-up with the treating physician.

Addendum:

The patient did not experience any complications and remains stable during the hospital course and at the time of discharge.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name]
Sex: Female
Age: 61 years old
Date of admission: [Date]
Date of discharge: [Date]
Diagnosis: Papillary Fibroelastoma, a rare non-cancerous heart tumor

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with repeated sharp chest pain that got worse with breathing and serious trouble breathing. A CT scan of the lungs did not show any blood clots. Heart tests found a small growth attached to a flap in the right side of the heart. The growth measured about half an inch in size. The heart valve was also leaking slightly. Doctors thought the growth was most likely a papillary fibroelastoma or a myxoma, which are two types of non-cancerous heart tumors. Surgery was recommended because the tumor could break off and block blood flow. The size of the tumor, larger than one centimeter, also made surgery necessary.

Surgery:

Doctors removed the growth by carefully shaving it off the heart valve. The heart valve was also repaired using a special ring to help it close properly.

Postoperative Procedures:

The patient was watched closely after surgery and received proper care. The patient took blood-thinning medicine for 8 weeks after surgery. As of now, the patient has no symptoms during rest or physical activity.

Follow-Up:

The patient should live a healthy lifestyle. The patient should also keep all future appointments with the doctor.

Addendum:

The patient had no problems during the hospital stay. The patient was in stable condition at the time of discharge.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 13 years old
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Diagnosis: Incomplete pancreatic duct (PD) with pancreatic pseudocyst, acute recurrent pancreatitis

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the Department of Hepatopancreatobiliary Surgery with complaints of abdominal pain, abdominal distension, fatigue, and anorexia lasting for 8 days. The patient had a significant medical history of recurrent pancreatitis with one episode of acute pancreatitis. Diagnostic tests revealed pseudocyst lesions of the pancreas, indicating incomplete PD and pancreatic pseudocyst. Laboratory test results showed increased levels of amylase, lipase, and cholestatic indexes, leading to a diagnosis of acute recurrent pancreatitis.

Further investigation showed pancreatic duct stenosis, which led to endoscopic pancreatic duct drainage, papillotomy, and stent insertion to successfully excise the pancreatic pseudocyst. The patient was observed to be stable, and the stent was replaced after four months without any complications.

After a few days of observation, the patient was discharged in stable condition. The patient recovered uneventfully without any evidence of pseudocyst recurrence. During the follow-up period of eight months, follow-up CT and ERCP revealed no signs of pseudocyst recurrence, and the stent was removed.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in a stable condition without any complications.

Discharge Medications:
Not applicable as no medications were prescribed at discharge.

Discharge Diagnoses:
- Incomplete pancreatic duct (PD)
- Pancreatic pseudocyst
- Acute recurrent pancreatitis

Discharge Instructions:
- Eat a healthy and balanced diet.
- Avoid foods that are high in fat and cholesterol.
- Take prescribed medications according to the instructions.
- Follow up with the treating physician as recommended.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 13 years old
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Diagnosis: Incomplete tube in the pancreas with a fluid-filled sac on the pancreas, repeated swelling of the pancreas

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the Liver, Pancreas, and Bile Duct Surgery Department. The patient had belly pain, a swollen belly, tiredness, and loss of appetite for 8 days. The patient had a history of repeated pancreas swelling, including one sudden episode before. Tests showed a fluid-filled sac on the pancreas. This pointed to an incomplete tube in the pancreas. Blood tests showed high levels of digestive enzymes and signs of bile backup. Doctors found the patient had repeated swelling of the pancreas.

More tests showed a narrowed tube in the pancreas. Doctors used a thin, flexible tube to drain the pancreas tube. They made a small cut to widen the opening and placed a small tube called a stent. This helped remove the fluid-filled sac. The patient stayed stable. The stent was replaced after four months with no problems.

After a few days of watching, the patient went home in stable condition. The patient healed well with no signs of the fluid-filled sac coming back. Over eight months of check-ups, scans and special tube tests showed no return of the fluid-filled sac. The stent was then removed.

Discharge Condition:
The patient went home in stable condition with no problems.

Discharge Medications:
None needed. No medicines were given at discharge.

Discharge Diagnoses:
Incomplete tube in the pancreas
Fluid-filled sac on the pancreas
Repeated swelling of the pancreas

Discharge Instructions:
Eat healthy, well-balanced meals.
Stay away from foods high in fat and cholesterol.
Take any prescribed medicines as directed.
See the treating doctor for follow-up visits as told.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Age: 67
Sex: Male
Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: Day 11

Summary:

A 67-year-old male with severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and ischaemic heart disease presented with myalgia and weakness in his shoulders and lower limbs that led to reduced mobility. The patient was diagnosed with rhabdomyolysis secondary to simvastatin and clarithromycin, which were both ceased. Intravenous fluid therapy was administered and the patient developed AKI. The patient's AKI improved with intravenous fluid therapy and was ultimately discharged home after improving his functionality by physiotherapy. Long-term prophylactic doxycycline and ezetimibe were started by his respiratory physician. 

Diagnosis:

Rhabdomyolysis secondary to simvastatin and clarithromycin 

Treatment:

Intravenous fluid therapy, insulin/dextrose, calcium resonium, and calcium gluconate 

Concomitant Conditions:

Severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and ischaemic heart disease 

Follow-Up:

Follow-up with his respiratory physician 

Documentation Source:

Clinical notes, laboratory results, and imaging reports

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Age: 67
Sex: Male
Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: Day 11

Summary:

A 67-year-old male with serious lung disease and heart disease came to the hospital with muscle pain and weakness in his shoulders and legs. This made it hard for him to move around. The patient was found to have muscle breakdown caused by two medicines called simvastatin and clarithromycin. Both medicines were stopped. The patient was given fluids through a vein. The patient then developed kidney problems. The kidney problems got better with fluids given through a vein. The patient worked with a physical therapist to get stronger. The patient was sent home after 11 days. His lung doctor started two new medicines for long-term use called doxycycline and ezetimibe.

Diagnosis:

Muscle breakdown caused by two medicines called simvastatin and clarithromycin

Treatment:

Fluids given through a vein, insulin with sugar water, a medicine to lower potassium levels called calcium resonium, and calcium gluconate

Concomitant Conditions:

Serious lung disease and heart disease

Follow-Up:

Follow up with his lung doctor

Documentation Source:

Clinical notes, laboratory results, and imaging reports

input:
Hospital Course:
The 13-year-old Asian girl weighing 24 kg was admitted with a progressive thoracolumbar kyphoscoliosis deformity since 2 years. She underwent tracheostomy 6 years before, related to severe bronchopneumonia management. Since then, she continued to require ventilatory support at night. Genetic study confirmed PD and she was started on ERT.

Physical Exam:
At admission, the patient had thoracolumbar kyphoscoliosis extending from D1 to L4 level, varus deformity of the left foot, bilateral fixed flexor deformity of lower limbs, and wasting of the lower limb and paraspinal muscles with 3/5 motor power. Oxygen saturation was 95% on room air, and chest X-ray showed normal lung fields. Echocardiography showed normal ventricles with ejection fraction of 54% with no evidence of hypertrophy or obstructive cardiomyopathy. Serum creatinine kinase was raised.

Procedure:
General anaesthesia was planned for motor evoked potential (MEP) monitoring during the correction of kyphoscoliosis. The child was induced with dexmedetomidine infusion, ketamine, and morphine, and anaesthesia was maintained with dexmedetomidine infusion, oxygen, and sevoflurane. A total of 450 ml of blood was lost, and 2.3 L of Ringers lactate were infused. The surgery was successful.

Follow-up:
On the 2nd post-operative day, she developed bilateral mild pleural effusion which was treated with diuretics and antibiotics. On the 5th post-operative day, patient was weaned off from ventilator. However, she continued to require nocturnal ventilatory support. Rest of the hospital stay was uneventful, and she was discharged on the 10th day.

Plan:
The patient will continue with ERT and nocturnal ventilatory support. Follow-up appointments will be made with the appropriate specialists.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The 13-year-old Asian girl weighing 24 kg was admitted with a curved and twisted spine that had been getting worse for 2 years. She had a breathing tube placed in her neck 6 years before because of a serious lung infection. Since then, she needed a breathing machine at night. A gene test confirmed Pompe disease and she was started on enzyme replacement therapy.

Physical Exam:
At admission, the patient had a curved and twisted spine from the upper back to the lower back. She had an inward turning of the left foot. Both legs were bent and could not fully straighten. The muscles in her legs and along her spine were weak and thin with reduced strength. Her blood oxygen level was 95 percent on room air. A chest X-ray showed her lungs looked normal. A heart ultrasound showed her heart was working normally with 54 percent pumping strength. There were no signs of thickened or blocked heart muscle. A blood test showed high levels of a muscle enzyme.

Procedure:
The child was put to sleep with general anesthesia. Doctors watched her nerve signals during surgery to fix her curved spine. She was given a mix of medicines to keep her asleep and comfortable. She lost about 450 ml of blood during surgery. Doctors gave her 2.3 liters of fluid through an IV. The surgery went well.

Follow-up:
On day 2 after surgery, fluid built up around both lungs. This was treated with water pills and antibiotics. On day 5 after surgery, the patient was taken off the breathing machine. She still needed the breathing machine at night as before. The rest of her hospital stay went smoothly. She went home on day 10.

Plan:
The patient will keep getting enzyme replacement therapy and using the breathing machine at night. Follow-up visits will be scheduled with the right doctors.
