Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Not provided
Date of Admission: Not provided
Date of Discharge: Not provided

History of Present Illness:
The patient was admitted with complaints of flank pain which had been developing over the previous two months. Ultrasound and CT of the abdomen showed nodular lesions in both kidneys. Further investigation and immunohistochemistry confirmed a diagnosis of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL). The patient underwent R-CHOP regimen treatment and salvage therapy R-DHAOx regimen.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted in December 2020 with complaints of flank pain. Exam and tests revealed nodular lesions in both kidneys. CT and PET/CT scans revealed a pathology consistent with a diagnosis of DLBCL, NOS. The patient was treated with R-CHOP regimen with good tolerance, resulting in the reduction of nodular lesions. However, after assessment through PET/CT and multidisciplinary consultation, the patient was given salvage therapy, R-DHAOx regimen, and consolidation therapy with ASCT. The patient tolerated this treatment with no significant complications.

Past Medical History:
The patient was diagnosed with Turner Syndrome and was being treated for hypertension, hypothyroidism post-thyroiditis and obesity (BMI, 30.3). The patient also had a known hypertransaminasemia, which was being monitored.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, not otherwise specified (DLBCL, NOS).

Condition at Discharge:
The patient was discharged in stable condition with instructions for follow-up care.

Instructions at Discharge:
The patient was advised to continue taking their routine medications and report any side effects immediately. The patient was instructed to schedule regular follow-up visits as advised by their healthcare provider.

Primary Care Provider:
The patient was referred to their primary care provider for post-treatment follow-up and care.

Diet:
No specific dietary restrictions were recommended upon discharge.

Activity:
The patient was advised to take it easy and avoid strenuous activities for a few days after the procedure.

Prognosis:
It is too early to make a precise prognosis for the patient's health. The effectiveness of treatment will be determined over time through regular post-treatment follow-up appointments.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Not provided
Date of Admission: Not provided
Date of Discharge: Not provided

History of Present Illness:
The patient came to the hospital with pain in the side of the body. This pain had been getting worse over the past two months. Imaging tests of the belly showed abnormal growths in both kidneys. More tests confirmed the patient had a type of blood cancer called diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. The patient received two rounds of cancer-fighting drug treatments.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital in December 2020 with side pain. Tests and exams found abnormal growths in both kidneys. Scans showed the patient had diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, a type of blood cancer. The patient received the first round of cancer-fighting drugs and handled it well. The growths got smaller after this treatment. However, after more scans and talks with different doctors, the patient needed a second round of treatment. The patient also had a stem cell transplant using their own cells to help the body recover. The patient handled all treatments well with no major problems.

Past Medical History:
The patient has Turner Syndrome, a genetic condition. The patient was also being treated for high blood pressure, low thyroid function after thyroid swelling, and obesity. The patient also had high liver enzymes, which doctors were watching.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, a type of blood cancer.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition with instructions for follow-up care.

Instructions at Discharge:
The patient was told to keep taking their regular medicines. The patient should report any side effects right away. The patient should set up regular follow-up visits as told by their doctor.

Primary Care Provider:
The patient was sent to their regular doctor for care and check-ups after treatment.

Diet:
No special food limits were given when the patient left the hospital.

Activity:
The patient was told to rest and avoid hard physical activity for a few days.

Prognosis:
It is too soon to know how well the treatment worked. Doctors will check on the patient's health over time through regular follow-up visits.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: Not Provided
Gender: Female
Age: 31 years
Admission Date: Not Provided
Discharge Date: Not Provided
Admitting Diagnosis: Not Provided
Discharge Diagnosis: Hydatid cysts of the bone

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient presented with a history of increasing pain in the right hip and pelvis for the last 3 years, radiating to the right lower limb and back, progressively increasing in intensity, and associated with tingling sensation since last 3 months. She had become quite incapacitated due to painful restricted movements. Her past history showed that at 22 years of age, she had presented with a large abdominal mass. Her CT at that time showed a multicystic lesion measuring 18.0×9.8×7.6 cm in the right abdomen, which appeared to be arising from the right ovary. She underwent surgery which showed a hydatid cyst which was confirmed on histopathology. After remaining asymptomatic for 3 years, in 2007, she again gradually developed pain in the right pelvic region. This time MRI showed a large solid cystic mass measuring 10×6.3 cm in the right iliac fossa and right gluteal region. She underwent a diagnostic laparatomy where multiple cysts in the iliac bone with osteolysis were seen. Two cystic pieces were removed which were diagnosed as hydatid cyst. The patient continued with the pelvic pain although postoperative MRI showed reduction in size of the lesion along the right iliac bone and sacroiliac joint. The x-ray of the pelvis and hips revealed cortical irregularity with ill defined lytic defects and linear areas of patchy sclerosis along the right iliac blade. She continued with pain and follow-up CT revealed extension of the disease. Resection of the hydatid cyst of sacroiliac region with allograft and autograft with lumbosacroiliac fixation was done. Histopathology results revealed hydatid cysts of the bone. The patient was also put on a medical treatment with albendazole for 1 month. The postoperative period was uneventful and patient was discharged on the 10th day of surgery. The follow-up of the patient at 6 months showed no detectable abnormality on radiology, and she was doing well.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient is advised to follow up with the treating physician for regular check-ups and medication management. The patient should continue taking the prescribed medication as per the treatment plan. She should follow a healthy diet and lifestyle to maintain optimal health. The patient is also advised to rest and avoid physical activity until further notice from the treating physician. Any new or worsening symptoms should be reported promptly to the physician.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: Not Provided
Gender: Female
Age: 31 years
Admission Date: Not Provided
Discharge Date: Not Provided
Admitting Diagnosis: Not Provided
Discharge Diagnosis: Parasitic cysts in the bone

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came in with pain in the right hip and pelvis that had been getting worse for 3 years. The pain spread to the right leg and back and kept getting stronger over time. She also had a tingling feeling for the last 3 months. The pain made it very hard for her to move around. When she was 22 years old, she had a large growth in her belly. A scan showed a growth with many fluid-filled sacs measuring about 18 by 10 by 8 centimeters on the right side of her belly. Doctors thought it came from the right ovary. She had surgery that showed it was a parasitic cyst, which lab tests confirmed. She felt fine for 3 years after that surgery. In 2007, she slowly started having pain in the right pelvic area again. This time, a scan showed a large growth measuring about 10 by 6 centimeters in the right lower belly and right buttock area. Doctors did surgery to look inside and found many cysts in the hip bone with bone damage. They removed two pieces of cyst tissue, which were confirmed to be parasitic cysts. The patient still had pelvic pain after surgery. A follow-up scan showed the growth along the right hip bone and the joint between the hip and spine had gotten smaller. An x-ray of the pelvis and hips showed uneven bone surface with unclear areas of bone loss and patches of hardened bone along the right hip blade. The pain continued and a later scan showed the disease had spread. Doctors did surgery to remove the parasitic cyst near the hip and spine joint. They used bone from a donor and bone from the patient's own body to rebuild the area. They also placed metal hardware to hold the lower spine, hip, and tailbone area together. Lab results confirmed parasitic cysts in the bone. The patient was given albendazole medicine to take for 1 month. Recovery after surgery went smoothly with no problems. The patient left the hospital 10 days after surgery. At a check-up 6 months later, scans showed nothing wrong and she was doing well.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should see the treating doctor for regular check-ups and to manage her medicine. The patient should keep taking the medicine as the doctor ordered. She should eat healthy foods and live a healthy lifestyle to stay well. The patient should rest and not do physical activities until the doctor says it is okay. If any new symptoms appear or current symptoms get worse, the patient should tell the doctor right away.

The patient left the hospital 10 days after surgery. At a check-up 6 months later, scans showed nothing wrong and she was doing well. DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should see the treating doctor for regular check-ups and to manage her medicine. The patient should keep taking the medicine as the doctor ordered. She should eat healthy foods and live a healthy lifestyle to stay well. The patient should rest and not do physical activities until the doctor says it is okay. If any new symptoms appear or current symptoms get worse, the patient should tell the doctor right away.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]

Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Date of Admission: [Date of admission]

Date of Discharge: [Date of discharge]

Hospital: [Hospital name]

Clinical Summary
The patient, a 32-year-old male, presented with complaints of breathlessness, low-grade fever, and weight loss. He had a history of treated tuberculous lymphadenitis and was diagnosed with disseminated tuberculosis (probably relapse) on examination.

Hospital Course
During the hospital stay, the patient was subjected to further investigations, which included blood tests, liver function tests, radiography, as well as ultrasonography of the abdomen. FNAC of the axillary and cervical lymph nodes were also performed to make a definitive diagnosis.

Diagnosis
The possibility of cryptococcal infection was considered based on the patient's blood reports, radiography, and FNAC results. The definitive diagnosis of C. neoformans was confirmed after isolating the fungus from the lymph node aspirate culture.

Treatment
The patient underwent treatment for cryptococcal infection as per hospital protocol. There were no reported complications during the course of treatment.

Follow-Up Plan
The patient was advised to take his medications regularly and was scheduled for a follow-up after one month.

Prognosis
The patient's prognosis was good, and he was advised to continue his medication as directed without fail.

Discharge Instructions
The patient was discharged with instructions to take his prescribed medications regularly and report back to the hospital if he experienced any adverse effects. Additionally, he was advised to adopt a healthy lifestyle and increase his water intake. The patient was also advised to continue his routine check-ups with a physician.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]

Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Date of Admission: [Date of admission]

Date of Discharge: [Date of discharge]

Hospital: [Hospital name]

Clinical Summary
The patient is a 32-year-old man. He came to the hospital with trouble breathing, a mild fever, and weight loss. In the past, he had treatment for a type of infection that causes swollen glands. Doctors found that this infection had spread through his body and likely came back.

Hospital Course
While in the hospital, the patient had several tests done. These included blood tests, tests to check how well the liver works, and X-rays. The patient also had an ultrasound of the belly area. Doctors used a needle to take small samples from swollen glands in the neck and armpit areas. These tests helped doctors find out what was causing the illness.

Diagnosis
Test results from blood work, X-rays, and gland samples pointed to a fungal infection. Doctors grew the fungus from the gland sample in the lab. This confirmed the patient had a fungal infection called Cryptococcus.

Treatment
The patient received treatment for the fungal infection following the hospital's standard care plan. The patient did not have any problems during treatment.

Follow-Up Plan
The patient was told to take all medicines as directed. The patient has an appointment to come back in one month.

Prognosis
The patient is expected to do well. The patient must keep taking all medicines exactly as directed.

Discharge Instructions
The patient was sent home with orders to take all prescribed medicines on schedule. The patient should return to the hospital right away if any bad reactions happen. The patient should also eat well, stay active, and drink plenty of water. The patient should keep seeing a doctor for regular check-ups.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: 

Discharge Date: 

Patient Name: 

Medical Record Number: 

Principal Diagnosis: Prostatic TB with Intracranial TB 

Hospital Course:

The patient was a 54-year old man who was admitted to the hospital due to persistent fever and weight loss. Upon admission, various tests were conducted, including blood counts, liver and renal function tests, and blood culture for common pathogens. However, no significant abnormality was discovered. 

As a result, the patient underwent a whole body F-18 FDG PET-CECT, which revealed intense FDG uptake in the right lobe of the prostate gland and asymmetric heterogeneous FDG uptake in the left frontal lobe peripherally. Following this finding, an MRI scan of the pelvic region was advised. 

Subsequently, a TRUS-guided biopsy was carried out, which ultimately revealed multiple caseous epithelioid granulomas containing giant cells. This validated the diagnosis of prostatic TB, and the patient was prescribed anti-tubercular therapy (ATT) immediately. 

However, before the patient was scheduled to undergo an MRI brain, the patient developed sudden altered mental state, which lasted for a brief period. The provisional diagnosis for this episode was Ethambutol and Isoniazid induced psychosis. Later, an MRI brain with gadolinium contrast demonstrated asymmetrical exaggerated irregular leptomeningeal enhancement in the left frontal region with enhancing granulomas in the supra and infratentorial brain parenchyma, which represented intracranial TB. 

Overall, the patient responded favorably to the ATT treatment, with a subsided fever and normalized ESR within 2 months. The patient did not experience any other neurological episodes. Serum PSA was observed to be normal after the first three months. 

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Follow-up:

The patient is advised to remain aware of the symptoms of TB and seek help if such symptoms reoccur in the future.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date:

Discharge Date:

Patient Name:

Medical Record Number:

Main Diagnosis: Tuberculosis (TB) in the prostate gland and brain

Hospital Stay:

The patient was a 54-year-old man who came to the hospital with ongoing fever and weight loss. Doctors ran many tests when the patient arrived. These included blood tests and tests to check how the liver and kidneys were working. Doctors also tested the blood for germs. All these tests came back normal.

Next, the patient had a special full-body scan called a PET-CECT. This scan showed unusual activity in the right side of the prostate gland. The prostate is a small gland below the bladder in men. The scan also showed unusual activity on the left front part of the brain. Doctors then ordered an MRI scan of the pelvic area to learn more.

After that, doctors took a small tissue sample from the prostate using ultrasound to guide them. The sample showed signs of TB infection. This confirmed that the patient had TB in the prostate. Doctors started TB medicine right away.

Before the patient could get a brain MRI, the patient suddenly became confused. This lasted only a short time. Doctors believed two TB medicines caused this mental change. Later, the patient had a brain MRI with a special dye. The scan showed signs of TB infection in the brain, including the covering of the brain and brain tissue.

The patient got better with TB treatment. The fever went away within 2 months. Blood tests for swelling in the body also returned to normal. The patient had no more episodes of confusion. A blood test for prostate health was normal after three months.

Discharge Medications:

None listed

Follow-up:

The patient should watch for signs of TB coming back. The patient should get medical help if any TB symptoms return.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:
Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 67 years
Sex: Female

Chief Complaint:
Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon. Right hemicolectomy was performed, and the pathology confirmed stage III (pT3N1) adenocarcinoma. Adjuvant chemotherapy was initiated but was discontinued after four of eight cycles due to severe neuropathy. After completion of adjuvant treatment, the patient underwent active surveillance. In November 2012, a recurrence near the right psoas muscle was identified and excised in December. Immunohistochemical workup showed an abnormal mismatch repair profile with loss of MLH1 protein. The patient underwent radiotherapy concurrent with capecitabine, but again, significant neutropenia necessitated a capecitabine dose reduction. In October 2013, the patient relapsed with disease in the L3 spinous process and received 42 Gy of stereotactic radiotherapy in 10 fractions. In June 2014, the tumor recurred at the same site, and the patient underwent palliative resection of the mass.

Enter Personalized OncoGenomics (POG) initiative:
The patient consented to genomic analysis of the tumor resected from the L3 spinous process by the POG initiative at the BC Cancer Agency. The genomic analysis revealed overexpression of FOS and JUN genes that encode the AP-1 transcriptional complex. Immunohistochemical workup confirmed robust expression of c-JUN protein. It was suggested that irbesartan could be a potential option due to the blockade of the renin–angiotensin pathway.

Treatment:
The patient had a pretreatment baseline PET/CT scan and started irbesartan at a dose of 150 mg daily in December 2014. After 5 weeks of therapy, a follow-up PET/CT was performed, and there was virtually a complete functional radiological resolution of her disease. CEA levels decreased from 18 to 3.1.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has been discharged, and her condition is stable. Follow-up visits have been scheduled, and medication and dietary instructions have been provided.

Medications:
Irbesartan 150 mg daily.

Follow-up:
Follow-up appointments have been scheduled. The patient has been advised to adhere to a healthy diet and lifestyle and continue medication as prescribed.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:
Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 67 years
Sex: Female

Chief Complaint:
Medium-grade colon cancer in the right side of the colon.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with medium-grade cancer in the right side of the colon. Surgery was done to remove the right part of the colon. Lab tests confirmed stage III cancer that had spread to nearby lymph nodes. The patient started cancer-killing drug treatment but had to stop after four of eight rounds. This was because of serious nerve damage in the hands and feet. After finishing treatment, the patient had regular check-ups to watch for cancer return. In November 2012, the cancer came back near a muscle in the lower back area. It was removed by surgery in December. Special lab tests showed a problem with how the body fixes damaged genes. The patient received radiation treatment along with a cancer pill called capecitabine. But the patient's white blood cell count dropped too low. The dose of capecitabine had to be lowered. In October 2013, the cancer came back in a bone in the lower spine. The patient received focused radiation treatment in 10 sessions. In June 2014, the cancer returned in the same spot. The patient had surgery to remove the growth to help with symptoms.

Enter Personalized OncoGenomics (POG) initiative:
The patient agreed to have the tumor from the spine tested for gene changes. This was done by the POG program at the BC Cancer Agency. The gene tests showed that certain genes called FOS and JUN were too active. These genes control cell growth. Lab tests confirmed high levels of a protein called c-JUN. Doctors suggested trying a blood pressure drug called irbesartan. This drug might help by blocking a body system that could be helping the cancer grow.

Treatment:
The patient had a full body scan before starting treatment. The patient began taking irbesartan at 150 mg once a day in December 2014. After 5 weeks of treatment, another full body scan was done. The scan showed the cancer was almost completely gone. A blood test that measures cancer activity dropped from 18 to 3.1.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Medium-grade colon cancer in the right side of the colon.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has been discharged, and her condition is stable. Follow-up visits have been scheduled. Instructions for medicine and diet have been given.

Medications:
Irbesartan 150 mg once a day.

Follow-up:
Follow-up appointments have been scheduled. The patient has been told to eat healthy foods and live a healthy lifestyle. The patient should keep taking medicine as directed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
MRN: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 78-year-old woman with a history of Parkinson's disease, cerebral lacunar infarction, and high cholesterol, was admitted to the hospital with a complaint of fever and general weakness. Upon examination, the patient was found to have acute gastric dilatation in the presence of pneumonia. The initial treatment included intravenous fluid replacement, antibiotics, and vasopressor administration. A nasogastric tube was inserted, which drained 4 L of liquid. An esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) was performed 24 hours after admission, which revealed acute gastric mucosal erosion and multiple gastric superficial ulcers. The patient's vital signs stabilized, and her mental status improved after initial treatment.

However, approximately 36 hours after admission, the patient suddenly complained of severe dyspnea, chest pain, and aggravated abdominal pain. Follow-up imaging revealed free air in both subphrenic spaces and pulmonary edema in both lung fields. Despite aggressive intervention, the patient's condition worsened rapidly with a dramatic decrease in mental condition and blood pressure, resulting in her demise.

Diagnostic Summary:

The patient was admitted with acute gastric dilatation in the presence of pneumonia and was found to have acute gastric mucosal erosion and multiple gastric superficial ulcers on EGD. Follow-up imaging revealed free air in both subphrenic spaces and pulmonary edema in both lung fields.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was admitted to the hospital with a complaint of fever and general weakness and was found to have acute gastric dilatation in the presence of pneumonia. Upon admission, she was treated with intravenous fluid replacement, antibiotics, and vasopressor administration, with a nasogastric tube inserted to drain liquid from her

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
MRN: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 78-year-old woman. She has a history of Parkinson's disease, small strokes in the brain, and high cholesterol. She came to the hospital with a fever and feeling weak all over. Doctors found that her stomach had swelled up very large. She also had a lung infection. Treatment started with fluids given through a vein, medicines to fight infection, and medicines to raise her blood pressure. A tube was placed through her nose into her stomach. This tube drained 4 liters of fluid. About 24 hours after arriving, doctors used a camera on a thin tube to look inside her stomach. They found damage to the stomach lining and several shallow sores in the stomach. After this early treatment, the patient's heart rate and blood pressure became steady. Her thinking also got better.

About 36 hours after arriving, the patient suddenly had serious trouble breathing. She also had chest pain and worse belly pain. New scans showed air had leaked under her diaphragm on both sides. There was also fluid in both lungs. Doctors worked hard to help her. But the patient got much worse very quickly. Her thinking became very poor and her blood pressure dropped sharply. The patient passed away.

Diagnostic Summary:

The patient came in with a very swollen stomach and a lung infection. The camera test showed damage to the stomach lining and several shallow stomach sores. Later scans showed leaked air under the diaphragm on both sides and fluid in both lungs.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient came to the hospital with fever and feeling weak all over. Doctors found her stomach was very swollen and she had a lung infection. She was treated with fluids through a vein, medicines to fight infection, and medicines to raise blood pressure. A tube was placed through her nose to drain fluid from her stomach.

input:
Hospital Course Summary

Patient: Female, 83 years old

Admission date: TBD        Discharge date: Day 25

Admission diagnosis: Left pelvic hematoma

Past medical history: Osteoporosis, chronic atrial fibrillation

Hospital course: Upon admission to the ED, patient presented with acute abdominal pain in the left lower quadrant. A CT scan was performed, revealing a left pelvic hematoma neighboring the fracture site. Warfarin was withheld and anticoagulation was reversed using 2 units of fresh frozen plasma (FFP) and 5mg vitamin K. The patient was monitored closely and received 4 units of packed red blood cells and 2 units of FFP after a drop in hemoglobin levels. Throughout the hospital course, the patient remained stable with no evidence of recurrent pelvic hemorrhage. Abdominal ultrasonography performed 5 and 10 days after admission showed a progressive resorption of the hematoma. The patient was transferred to the cardiologic unit for a cardiac failure and discharged uneventfully for a cardiac rehabilitation program on day 25. Four months later, the patient returned to her pre-injury level of activity. 

Discharge instructions: The patient should continue her cardiac rehabilitation program. Follow up appointments with primary care doctor and cardiologist are recommended.

Signed,
Attending physician.

Hospital Course Summary

Patient: Female, 83 years old

Admission date: TBD        Discharge date: Day 25

Admission diagnosis: Blood clot in the left side of the pelvis

Past medical history: Weak bones, long-term irregular heartbeat

Hospital course: When the patient arrived at the emergency room, she had sudden belly pain on the left lower side. A scan of the body was done. It showed a blood clot in the left pelvis near the broken bone. The patient stopped taking her blood thinner medicine called Warfarin. The care team gave her 2 bags of plasma and a vitamin K shot to help her blood clot normally again. The care team watched the patient closely. Her blood count dropped, so she received 4 bags of red blood cells and 2 more bags of plasma. During the hospital stay, the patient stayed stable. There were no signs of new bleeding in the pelvis. Ultrasound pictures taken 5 and 10 days after arrival showed the blood clot was slowly going away. The patient was moved to the heart care unit because of heart failure. She left the hospital on day 25 to start a heart recovery program. Four months later, the patient was back to doing the same activities she did before her injury.

Discharge instructions: The patient should keep going to her heart recovery program. The patient should make follow up visits with her regular doctor and heart doctor.

Signed,
Attending physician.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
This is the discharge summary for a 69-year-old woman with a history of coronary artery disease, hypertension, and dyslipidemia who was admitted for rapidly progressive muscle weakness.

Hospital Course:
Upon admission, the patient presented with predominantly proximal muscle weakness, mild back pain, poor appetite, and dysgeusia. Physical examination showed marked hepatosplenomegaly, and laboratory studies revealed severely elevated serum total calcium and creatinine levels indicative of acute renal injury. Subsequent testing revealed suppressed parathyroid hormone levels, negative PTH-related peptide, mildly elevated lactate dehydrogenase, and almost 5 times the upper range of normal for serum angiotensin-converting enzyme activity along with elevated levels of vitamin D metabolites.

Diagnostic Assessment:
Given strong suspicion for multiple myeloma, a skeletal survey was conducted, which revealed a 1.7-cm punched-out lytic lesion of the vertex of the skull. Serum and urine protein electrophoresis with immunofluorescence failed to demonstrate a monoclonal spike or clonal light chains. Serum free light chains demonstrated an elevated serum Kappa free light chain concentration and a normal Lambda free light chain concentration along with an imbalanced Kappa/Lambda free light chain ratio. A bone marrow biopsy with flow cytometry studies were performed and showed a low-grade B-cell lymphoma with a prominent histiocytic reaction. Subsequently, she underwent a lymph node biopsy, which revealed grade 3B follicular lymphoma. Further staging was performed with positron emission tomography, which showed diffuse patchy uptake within the proximal appendicular and axial skeleton in addition to increased uptake of the large lytic lesion of the skull.

Treatment:
The patient was treated with aggressive hydration and Zoledronate, which resulted in resolution of her hypercalcemia and acute renal injury. R-CHOP chemotherapy was later initiated with significant improvement of her other presenting symptoms.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow-up with her oncologist for further treatment and management of her lymphoma. She should continue to manage her other comorbid conditions including coronary artery disease, hypertension, and dyslipidemia. She should be mindful of her diet and stay active to promote optimal health. She should report any new symptoms or concerns to her healthcare provider.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
This is the discharge summary for a 69-year-old woman with a history of heart disease, high blood pressure, and high cholesterol who was admitted for quickly worsening muscle weakness.

Hospital Course:
When the patient arrived, she had muscle weakness mainly in her upper arms and thighs, mild back pain, poor appetite, and a changed sense of taste. A physical exam showed her liver and spleen were much larger than normal. Blood tests showed very high calcium levels and signs that her kidneys were not working well. More tests showed low levels of a hormone that controls calcium. Other test results pointed toward a problem with how her body handles calcium and vitamin D.

Diagnostic Assessment:
Doctors thought the patient might have a blood cancer called multiple myeloma. An x-ray scan of her bones found a small hole in the top of her skull. Blood and urine tests did not show the usual signs of myeloma. However, other blood tests showed an imbalance in certain proteins made by immune cells. A sample of bone marrow was taken and tested. It showed a slow-growing type of blood cell cancer with many immune cells around it. A sample from a lymph node was also taken. It showed a faster-growing type of lymph node cancer called follicular lymphoma. A full body scan showed the cancer had spread to bones throughout her body, including the hole in her skull.

Treatment:
The patient was given lots of fluids through an IV and a bone-strengthening medicine. This brought her calcium levels back to normal and helped her kidneys work again. She was then started on a cancer treatment plan called R-CHOP. This helped improve her other symptoms.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow-up with her cancer doctor for more treatment and care of her lymphoma. She should continue to manage her other health conditions including heart disease, high blood pressure, and high cholesterol. She should be mindful of her diet and stay active to promote optimal health. She should report any new symptoms or concerns to her healthcare provider.

input:
Hospital Course:

47-year-old male was admitted following a car accident. The patient presented with forehead abrasion and confusion. A brain CT scan and cervical X-rays showed no hematoma or cerebral edema, but cervical instability at C6-7. The patient was immobilized with a neck collar and closely monitored. Two weeks after admission, the patient complained of severe posterior neck pain (VAS > 9) and bilateral upper extremity numbness. Subsequent surgical stabilization was performed. An anterior cervical plate was positioned, and pilot holes were tapped. The upper left pilot hole was stripped due to repeated drilling, and PMMA augmentation was performed, which resulted in a mass lesion at the spinal canal. C6 corpectomy was promptly performed to remove the mass. Anterior reconstruction with an anterior cervical plate and mesh cage from C5 through C7 was done. Postoperatively, the patient had left side weakness and impairment of the left 4th and 5th digit extension, but he improved without intervention.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Cervical injury with spinal cord compression leading to cervical corpectomy.

Hospital course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital following a car accident. During his hospital stay, he underwent surgical stabilization for cervical instability at C6-7. There were some complications during the procedure, which led to a mass lesion at the spinal canal and cervical corpectomy. The patient had some postoperative weakness and impairment of the left hand digits, but he improved without intervention. The patient was discharged in good condition with a follow-up appointment with a spinal specialist.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

A 47-year-old male was admitted after a car accident. The patient had a cut on the forehead and was confused. A brain scan and neck X-rays showed no bleeding or swelling in the brain. However, the images showed the neck bones were not stable at the lower neck area. The patient wore a neck brace and was watched closely. Two weeks after being admitted, the patient reported very bad pain in the back of the neck. The patient also had numbness in both arms and hands. Surgery was done to fix the unstable neck bones. During surgery, a metal plate was placed on the front of the neck. Holes were drilled to hold the plate in place. One hole in the upper left area was damaged from drilling too many times. Bone cement was used to fix this problem. This caused a lump to form in the space around the spinal cord. The surgical team quickly removed part of the damaged neck bone to take out the lump. The neck was rebuilt using a metal plate and a support cage from the fifth to seventh neck bones. After surgery, the patient had weakness on the left side. The patient also had trouble straightening the ring and pinky fingers on the left hand. These problems got better on their own without extra treatment.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Neck injury with pressure on the spinal cord. This led to surgery to remove part of a neck bone.

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital after a car accident. During the hospital stay, the patient had surgery to fix unstable bones in the lower neck. There were some problems during the surgery. These problems caused a lump near the spinal cord. Part of a neck bone had to be removed. After surgery, the patient had some weakness and trouble moving fingers on the left hand. These problems got better without extra treatment. The patient left the hospital in good condition. The patient has a follow-up visit scheduled with a spine doctor.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Admitting Diagnosis: Relapsing episodes of progressive swelling in the patellar region in both knees.


Hospital Course:

The patient, a 12-year-old boy diagnosed with Globoid cell leukodystrophy, was admitted to the Department of Pediatric Rheumatology with progressive swelling in the patellar region of both knees. Diagnostic arthroscopy revealed severe synovitis in the joint space of the right knee. Synovial tissue and fluid analysis revealed foamy macrophages consistent with the previous diagnosis of Globoid cell leukodystrophy.

Treatment consisted of aspiration of excessive joint fluid and subsequent intra-articular injection of triamcinolonhexacetonide, which was highly effective in all episodes. The duration of clinical remission after injection of corticosteroids was approximately 1 year.

He was discharged in a stable condition.


Patient Instructions:

The patient was advised to follow up with the treating physician.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Admitting Diagnosis: Repeated episodes of growing swelling in the kneecap area in both knees.

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 12-year-old boy with a rare brain disease called Globoid cell leukodystrophy, was admitted to the Children's Joint and Bone Disease Department with growing swelling in the kneecap area of both knees. A small camera was used to look inside the right knee joint. This showed serious swelling of the joint lining. Samples of joint tissue and fluid showed certain cells that matched the patient's known brain disease.

Treatment included draining extra fluid from the joint. Then a steroid medicine called triamcinolone hexacetonide was injected into the joint. This treatment worked very well each time it was given. After each steroid injection, the symptoms stayed away for about 1 year.

The patient was sent home in stable condition.

Patient Instructions:

The patient was advised to schedule a follow-up visit with the treating doctor.

input:
Hospital Course:
A 41-year-old nonsmoking Caucasian male was admitted to our hospital in August 2011 for the evaluation and management of a locally recurring adulthood sc-rms on the floor of his mouth. The patient had previously undergone surgical resection, multiagent chemotherapy, and radiation therapy, but these treatments were unsuccessful. A total glossectomy with modified radical neck dissection, as well as reconstruction with a latissimus dorsi free flap, were performed, followed by a thorough histological examination. The patient continued to receive close follow-up care, including speech therapy. In September 2012 and May 2013, the patient presented with two more local recurrences, which were resected and histologically analyzed.      
      
Medical History:
The patient had no significant medical history prior to his initial presentation in June 2007 with a painless swelling under his tongue, which was later diagnosed as a myofibrosarcoma via biopsy. Subsequently, he was treated with chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and surgical resection.      
      
Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital in August 2011 for the evaluation and management of a locally recurring adulthood sc-rms on the floor of his mouth. A total glossectomy with modified radical neck dissection, as well as reconstruction with a latissimus dorsi free flap, were performed, followed by a thorough histological examination. The patient continued to receive close follow-up care, including speech therapy. In September 2012 and May 2013, the patient presented with two more local recurrences, which were resected and histologically analyzed.      
      
Disposition:
The patient was discharged from our hospital in May 2013 in stable condition with instructions to continue speech therapy and to undergo close follow-up care. The patient continues to have disease and is being nourished via a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) feeding tube.      
      
Diagnosis:
Adult rhabdomyosarcoma.      
      
Procedures:
Total glossectomy with modified radical neck dissection, and reconstruction with a latissimus dorsi free flap.      
      
Condition/Treatment:
The patient was treated with aggressive multimodal chemotherapy, followed by surgical resection and radiation therapy.      
      
Medications on Admission:
The patient was not taking any medications on admission.      
      
Allergies:
No drug allergies were reported.      
      
Code Status:
Full code.      
      
Hospital Course:
The patient presented with a locally recurring adulthood sc-rms on the floor of his mouth. The tumor had previously been treated with two separate multiagent chemotherapies, but the disease persisted and ultimately spread. Therefore, a total glossectomy with modified radical neck dissection, as well as reconstruction with a latissimus dorsi free flap, were performed to control the disease.      
      
Hospital Course:
The patient underwent surgery and has had several follow-up visits to assess his progress. He remains alive with disease and is being nourished via a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) feeding tube.      
      
Hospital Course:
The patient was discharged with instructions to continue speech therapy and to undergo close follow-up care. The patient continues to have disease and will require further monitoring.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
A 41-year-old white male who does not smoke was admitted to our hospital in August 2011. The patient came in for care of a rare muscle cancer that had come back in the floor of his mouth. This type of cancer is called rhabdomyosarcoma. The patient had already had surgery, several cancer-fighting drugs, and radiation treatment. These treatments did not get rid of the cancer. Doctors removed the entire tongue and some lymph nodes in the neck. They rebuilt the area using muscle tissue taken from the back. The removed tissue was studied carefully under a microscope. The patient kept getting regular checkups and worked with a speech therapist. In September 2012 and May 2013, the cancer came back again in the same area. Each time, doctors removed the growths and studied the tissue.

Medical History:
The patient had no major health problems before June 2007. At that time, the patient noticed painless swelling under the tongue. A small tissue sample showed a type of muscle cancer called myofibrosarcoma. After this, the patient was treated with cancer-fighting drugs, radiation, and surgery.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital in August 2011 for care of a returning muscle cancer in the floor of his mouth. Doctors removed the entire tongue and some lymph nodes in the neck. They rebuilt the area using muscle tissue taken from the back. The removed tissue was studied carefully under a microscope. The patient kept getting regular checkups and worked with a speech therapist. In September 2012 and May 2013, the cancer came back two more times. Each time, doctors removed the growths and studied the tissue.

Disposition:
The patient left our hospital in May 2013 in stable condition. The patient was told to keep working with a speech therapist and to come back for regular checkups. The patient still has cancer. The patient gets nutrition through a feeding tube placed through the belly into the stomach.

Diagnosis:
Muscle cancer in an adult, called rhabdomyosarcoma.

Procedures:
Removal of the entire tongue and some lymph nodes in the neck. Rebuilding of the area using muscle tissue taken from the back.

Condition and Treatment:
The patient was treated with strong cancer-fighting drugs given in different ways. This was followed by surgery to remove the cancer and radiation treatment.

Medications on Admission:
The patient was not taking any medicines when admitted.

Allergies:
The patient has no known drug allergies.

Code Status:
Full code. This means all lifesaving treatments will be used if needed.

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with a returning muscle cancer in the floor of his mouth. The cancer had been treated before with two rounds of cancer-fighting drugs. The disease did not go away and eventually spread. Because of this, doctors removed the entire tongue and some lymph nodes in the neck. They rebuilt the area using muscle tissue from the back. This was done to control the cancer.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery and has come back for several visits to check on healing. The patient is alive but still has cancer. The patient gets nutrition through a feeding tube placed through the belly into the stomach.

Hospital Course:
The patient left the hospital with instructions to keep working with a speech therapist. The patient was also told to come back for regular checkups. The patient still has cancer and will need ongoing monitoring.

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 75-year-old male, was admitted to the hospital with upper abdominal pain. The blood tests showed elevated levels of total and direct bilirubin, γ-GTP, ALP, AST, and ALT, along with radiological evidence of dilation of hepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts and wall thickening of the common bile duct. Subsequent ERCP revealed defects in intrahepatic and common bile ducts, leading to a diagnosis of perihilar cholangiocarcinoma. The patient underwent left hepatectomy, caudate lobectomy, extrahepatic bile duct resection, and right hepaticojejunostomy. The patient was discharged 23 days after surgery in stable condition.

Follow-up:
Two years and 9 months after the first operation, follow-up CT showed a liver metastasis. The patient underwent a limited liver resection of segment 8. During surgery, another liver metastasis was detected intraoperatively. Two limited liver resections were performed for these two lesions, which were later diagnosed as NET G2. Five years and 4 months after the first operation, four liver metastases were detected, for which the patient received monthly octreotide LAR followed by lanreotide depot/autogel 120 mg. Six years and 3 months after the first operation, the patient underwent a third hepatectomy that revealed seven liver metastases with NET G2 recurrence. In the fourth surgery, the patient had no newly detected tumor and was discharged in stable condition.

Medical Management:
Seven months after the initiation of the third hepatectomy, the patient's follow-up showed four liver metastases. After 6 months of everolimus (10 mg/day) administration, all tumors shrank, but the chemotherapy was discontinued due to its side effects.

Clinical Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with perihilar cholangiocarcinoma based on clinical, radiological, and pathological findings. Later, the patient developed liver metastases that were diagnosed as NET G2, leading to multiple surgical resections followed by chemotherapy with everolimus. The patient was discharged in stable condition after each surgery with no recurrence on follow-up. 

Impressions:
This is a case of perihilar cholangiocarcinoma with NET G2 liver metastases. The patient has undergone multiple surgical resections and chemotherapy with everolimus with no recurrence on follow-up.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 75-year-old man who came to the hospital with pain in the upper belly area. Blood tests showed problems with how the liver was working. Scans showed that the tubes that carry bile from the liver were swollen and thickened. A special test using a small camera found blockages in the bile tubes. Doctors found that the patient had bile duct cancer near the liver. The patient had surgery to remove the left side of the liver, a small part called the caudate lobe, the bile tube outside the liver, and to reconnect the bile system. The patient left the hospital 23 days after surgery and was doing well.

Follow-up:
Two years and 9 months after the first surgery, a scan showed the cancer had spread to another part of the liver. The patient had surgery to remove a small part of the liver called segment 8. During that surgery, doctors found another tumor in the liver. Doctors removed both tumors, which turned out to be a different type of slow-growing cancer called NET G2. Five years and 4 months after the first surgery, four more liver tumors were found. The patient started getting monthly shots of medicine called octreotide LAR, then switched to another shot called lanreotide. Six years and 3 months after the first surgery, the patient had a third liver surgery. Doctors found seven tumors that were the same NET G2 cancer type. In the fourth surgery, no new tumors were found. The patient left the hospital in stable condition.

Medical Management:
Seven months after the third liver surgery, a check-up showed four liver tumors. The patient took a pill called everolimus for 6 months. All the tumors got smaller. However, the patient had to stop taking this medicine because of side effects.

Clinical Diagnosis:
The patient was found to have bile duct cancer near the liver based on test results, scans, and tissue samples. Later, the cancer spread to other parts of the liver. These new tumors were a different type called NET G2. The patient had several surgeries to remove the tumors and took everolimus medicine. The patient left the hospital in stable condition after each surgery. Follow-up visits showed no new cancer growth.

Impressions:
This patient had bile duct cancer near the liver and later developed a different type of slow-growing cancer called NET G2 that spread within the liver. The patient has had several surgeries and took everolimus medicine. Follow-up visits show no new cancer growth.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [INSERT NAME]
Sex: Female
Age: 65 years
Medical record number: [INSERT NUMBER]
Discharge Date: [INSERT DATE]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with complaints of pain and swelling of the left hand along the thenar eminence and the proximal aspect of the thumb. She also had cervical lymphadenopathy. A plain radiograph showed pathological fracture of the base of 1st metacarpal along with lytic lesion at the base of the 1st metacarpal with haziness of the surrounding soft tissue. An ultrasound revealed presence of an abscess. The MRI scan suggested an abscess like picture most likely due to infective etiology and less likely to be neoplastic lesion. 

The patient underwent drainage of abscess and debridement with stabilization of the fracture fragment. Intra-operatively we found thick caseous material within the soft tissue and the fragment at base of the 1st metacarpal was freely mobile similar to a loose body. The fragment was removed and sent for biopsy and culture sensitivity along with the surrounding soft-tissue. A kirschner wire was passed through the 1st carpo-metacarpal joint to stabilize it. 

The biopsy report came back positive for tuberculous osteomyelitis which suggested fibro-collagenous tissue and few bony fragments showing large caseous necrosis with few epitheloidid cell granuloma. The patient was then started on anti-tuberculous treatment emprerically while awaiting the culture report. A Combination of Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide and ethambutol was started. 

At the end of 5 weeks the kirschner wire backed out partially which was then removed and range of movement exercises were started. The cervical lymphadenopathy regressed by the end of 3 months. The patient had 1 episode of recurrence of cervical lymphandenopathy at the end of 7 months of treatment which eventually regressed on the continuous phase of AKT. 

At the end of one year, the patient had almost complete range of movement with no sequel due to tuberculosis as compared to the opposite hand. Plain radiographs showed some degree of joint remodelling at one year. 

Discharge Medications:
The patient is dispensed her usual home medications.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow up with the orthopedic surgeon regularly and continue taking the prescribed medication. The patient is counselled to adopt a healthy lifestyle and a balanced diet. Any symptoms of recurrence or deterioration should be reported to the physician immediately. Additionally, the patient is advised regarding the importance and significance of regular follow-up, which is critical to the management of her condition.

Follow-up:
The patient is referred to the orthopedic surgeon for follow-up to assess progress and discuss the patient's ongoing management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [INSERT NAME]
Sex: Female
Age: 65 years
Medical record number: [INSERT NUMBER]
Discharge Date: [INSERT DATE]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with pain and swelling in her left hand. The swelling was near the base of her thumb and the fleshy part of her palm. She also had swollen glands in her neck. An X-ray showed a broken bone at the base of her thumb. The break happened because the bone was weak from disease. There was also a dark spot on the bone and cloudy tissue around it. An ultrasound showed a pocket of infection. An MRI scan suggested the pocket was most likely caused by infection and not cancer.

The patient had surgery to drain the infection and clean out the damaged tissue. The doctors also fixed the broken bone to keep it stable. During surgery, the doctors found thick cheese-like material in the soft tissue. The bone piece at the base of the thumb was loose and moving freely. The loose piece was taken out and sent to the lab for testing. The tissue around it was also sent for testing. A thin metal pin was placed through the thumb joint to hold it steady.

The lab results showed the patient had a bone infection caused by tuberculosis. The tissue samples showed dead tissue and signs of this specific infection. The patient was started on tuberculosis medicine right away while waiting for more test results. She was given four medicines called Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol.

After 5 weeks, the metal pin started to come loose on its own. It was then taken out. The patient started doing exercises to move her thumb. The swollen neck glands went down after 3 months. At 7 months, the neck glands swelled up again one time. They went back down while the patient kept taking her tuberculosis medicine.

After one year, the patient could move her thumb almost as well as her other hand. There were no lasting problems from the tuberculosis. X-rays showed the joint had healed and reshaped itself after one year.

Discharge Medications:
The patient is given her regular home medicines.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is told to see the bone doctor regularly and keep taking the medicines as directed. The patient is told to eat healthy foods and live a healthy lifestyle. Any signs that the infection is coming back or getting worse should be reported to the doctor right away. The patient is also told how important it is to keep all follow-up visits. These visits are very important for treating her condition.

Follow-up:
The patient is scheduled to see the bone doctor for follow-up visits. These visits are to check her healing and plan her ongoing care.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 23
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient presented to the outpatient department with pain and swelling in the left side of the face following a road traffic accident. The patient was subjected to an ophthalmological examination, and the results revealed no abnormality. Computerized tomography suggested a fracture to the inferior wall of the left orbit which allowed the herniation/protrusion of infraorbital content into the left maxillary sinus. Accordingly, the patient underwent left orbital floor reconstruction using the right conchal cartilage. The patient's post-operative period was uneventful, and she was discharged after recovery.

DIAGNOSIS:

Fracture of the inferior wall of the left orbit with herniation of infraorbital content into the left maxillary sinus.

PROCEDURE:

1. Left orbital floor reconstruction using the right conchal cartilage.

CONDITION ON DISCHARGE:

The patient's condition improved significantly, and she was discharged from the hospital.

FOLLOW-UP:

Follow-up was done both clinically and radiographically, and the patient showed no signs of infection in the operated site. Computed tomography (CT) scan was done after 22 months to confirm the proper positioning of the soft tissues within the orbit.

RECOMMENDATIONS:

The patient is advised to follow-up with the surgical department if any concerns or complications arise following the discharge.

Age: 23
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient came to the clinic with pain and swelling on the left side of her face after a car accident. The patient had an eye exam, and the results showed no problems. A special scan showed a break in the bone under the left eye. This break caused tissue to push down into the air space in the cheekbone. The patient had surgery to rebuild the bone under the left eye using cartilage from the right ear. The patient healed well after surgery and was sent home after recovery.

DIAGNOSIS:

Break in the bone under the left eye with tissue pushing into the air space in the cheekbone.

PROCEDURE:

1. Surgery to rebuild the bone under the left eye using cartilage from the right ear.

CONDITION ON DISCHARGE:

The patient got much better and was sent home from the hospital.

FOLLOW-UP:

The patient was checked in person and with scans. There were no signs of infection where the surgery was done. A scan was done 22 months later to make sure the tissue under the eye was in the right place.

RECOMMENDATIONS:

The patient should contact the surgery department if she has any concerns or problems after leaving the hospital.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was a 68-year-old male who was admitted with weakness and aggravation. He had a past history of gastric stromal tumors and type-B hepatitis. On admission, he had mild yellowing of the skin and sclera. Laboratory examination showed liver dysfunction. Abdominal CT showed pelvic and peritoneal cavity fluids, occupation of the left lateral lobe of the liver, and an unclear margin of the pancreas. PET-CT suggested swelling of the pancreas and metastases in the liver. The patient received comprehensive internal treatment combined with artificial liver treatment, but his condition deteriorated, and he became unconscious and developed hepatic encephalopathy. He was put on the transplant waiting list and received a liver transplant from a deceased donor on day 4 due to his critical condition. After the transplantation, the patient was sent to the ICU for further treatment.

Postoperative Course:

The patient's condition improved after the transplant, and he was started on immunosuppressive agents. Abdominal CT showed pancreatic swelling with massive exudation and effusion, indicating acute pancreatitis on postoperative day 8. His pathogen culture of various body fluids suggested multiple bacterial infections. An arrow tube was placed around the liver and tail of the pancreas under B-mode ultrasound guidance instead of operative treatment on postoperative day 11, and coffee-like turbid liquids flowed from the arrow tube. 

On postoperative day 20, the patient experienced a sudden increase in heart rate, decrease in blood pressure, increase in blood gas lactate, blurred consciousness, and poor light reflex. He received emergency trachea cannula, continuous renal replacement therapy for anuria, and norepinephrine to maintain blood pressure. On postoperative day 21, he was unconscious and died of septic shock.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient was a 68-year-old male who came to the hospital because he felt weak and uncomfortable. He had a history of stomach tumors and a liver infection called hepatitis B. When he arrived, his skin and the white parts of his eyes looked slightly yellow. Blood tests showed his liver was not working well. A belly scan showed fluid in his lower belly area, a growth on the left side of his liver, and unclear edges around his pancreas. A special body scan showed his pancreas was swollen and cancer had spread to his liver. The patient received medicine and special liver cleaning treatments, but he got worse. He became confused and his brain stopped working well because of liver failure. He was put on a waiting list for a new liver. On day 4, he received a liver from a donor who had died because his condition was very serious. After the surgery, the patient was moved to the intensive care unit for more treatment.

Postoperative Course:

The patient got better after receiving the new liver. He was given medicines to stop his body from fighting the new liver. A belly scan on day 8 after surgery showed his pancreas was swollen with a lot of fluid leaking around it. This meant he had sudden pancreas swelling. Tests of fluids from his body showed he had several different infections. On day 11 after surgery, doctors used an ultrasound to guide a drainage tube near his liver and pancreas instead of doing another surgery. Dark brown cloudy fluid came out of the tube.

On day 20 after surgery, the patient suddenly had a fast heartbeat and low blood pressure. His blood showed high acid levels. He became confused and his eyes did not react to light normally. Doctors put a breathing tube in his throat. He could not make urine so he was put on a machine to clean his blood. He was given medicine to raise his blood pressure. On day 21 after surgery, he was not awake and died from a serious blood infection that spread through his whole body.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: N/A
Patient ID: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Discharge Diagnosis: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML)

Hospital Course Summary:

A 56-year-old male patient presented to the hospital with a history of progressive shortness of breath. After initial blood work revealed abnormalities, further investigations identified AML. The patient’s bone marrow was infiltrated with blasts, confirming the diagnosis.

Treatment was initiated promptly, and the patient responded well to therapy. There were no significant complications during the patient’s admission, and they were closely monitored by a multidisciplinary team of healthcare professionals.

Throughout their stay, the patient was provided with appropriate supportive care and advice, including guidance regarding healthy lifestyle choices. The healthcare team worked collaboratively to develop a comprehensive and personalized treatment plan for the patient.

The patient also received counseling regarding their history of alcohol-dependence syndrome, as well as advice on how to manage their comorbid condition of chronic obstructive airways disease.

Upon discharge, the patient was provided with clear instructions regarding medication regimens and follow-up appointments. They were also advised to seek medical help immediately if they experienced any concerning symptoms.

In summary, the patient’s admission was characterized by a prompt diagnosis of AML and successful intervention. The healthcare team provided the patient with high-quality care, and the patient was able to safely return home with a comprehensive treatment plan and ongoing support.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: N/A
Patient ID: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Discharge Diagnosis: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), a type of blood cancer

Hospital Course Summary:

A 56-year-old male patient came to the hospital because he was having more and more trouble breathing. Blood tests showed something was wrong. More tests found that the patient has AML, a cancer of the blood. The patient's bone marrow had cancer cells in it, which proved the diagnosis.

Treatment started right away, and the patient did well with the treatment. The patient had no serious problems during the hospital stay. A team of doctors and nurses watched the patient closely.

During the stay, the patient received good care and helpful advice. This included tips about making healthy choices in daily life. The care team worked together to create a full treatment plan made just for the patient.

The patient also got help for a past problem with alcohol dependence. The care team gave advice on how to handle the patient's lung disease, called COPD, which makes breathing harder.

When leaving the hospital, the patient got clear directions about what medicines to take and when. The patient also learned when to come back for follow-up visits. The patient was told to get medical help right away if any worrying signs appeared.

In summary, the patient was quickly found to have AML and treatment worked well. The care team gave the patient very good care. The patient was able to go home safely with a full treatment plan and continued support.

input:
Admission Diagnosis:  Stage IV poorly differentiated ovarian carcinoma on palliative carboplatin-based chemotherapy.

Hospital Course Summary:

Admission:
The patient was admitted due to a brief syncopal episode with associated lethargy, nausea, light-headedness and increased watery ileostomal output following chemotherapy one week earlier. Clinical examination including central nervous system was unremarkable, and the patient was haemodynamically stable and afebrile.

Medical History:
The patient has a history of hypertension, multiple pulmonary emboli on enoxaparin and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. In the past, the patient had undergone bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and hysterectomy, and had an ileostomy due to small bowel obstruction with subsequent fistulae formation and bilateral nephrostomy drainage after radiation-induced ureteric obstruction. She had previously experienced recurrent urinary tract infections, which led to colonisation of her nephrostomies with methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), Staphylococcus epidermidis and Candida albicans. The patient had never received anti-fungal prophylaxis.

Initial Assessment:
Laboratory investigations revealed abnormal levels of sodium, creatinine, haemoglobin, white blood cells and lymphocytes. On day 2 of admission, the patient developed fevers of 38.5°C and was empirically commenced on piperacillin-tazobactam and vancomycin. The isolation of Staphylococcus epidermidis from the nephrostomy fluid was considered colonisation and the nephrostomies were changed. Initial septic screen was non-contributory, including negative faecal microscopy.

Diagnostic Assessment:
Persisting fever during her third week of admission prompted assessment for metastatic disease progression with FDG-PET scan. There was no evidence of metastatic malignant disease detected, but an area of abnormal avidity was identified on the aortic valve. Subsequent transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) revealed a small mobile echo-density superior to the aortic valve. Transoesophageal echocardiogram (TOE) revealed a pedunculated mass within the ascending aorta arising from the commissure of the right & non-coronary cusp of the aortic valve with a calcified base. Brain MRI identified a contrast-enhancing right parasagittal frontal lobe lesion consistent with an embolic lesion.

Treatment:
Fungi were isolated from repeat blood cultures on day 22 of admission and subsequently identified as Lomentospora prolificans with high MIC to amphotericin (>32 mg/l) and itraconazole (>32 mg/L), and in-vitro sensitivity to voriconazole (0.5 mg/L). The patient was commenced on therapy with voriconazole (loading dose 600 mg IV 12 hourly). Unfortunately, due to her underlying advanced malignancy, disseminated fungal infection and poor clinical status, the patient was considered unsuitable for surgical management. She was managed with palliative intent.

Outcome:
The patient passed away five days after the fungus was isolated on day 27 of her admission.
clean_ref:
Admission Diagnosis: The patient had advanced ovarian cancer (stage IV) that had spread and was receiving chemotherapy to help with symptoms.

Hospital Course Summary:

Admission:
The patient came to the hospital after fainting briefly. She also felt tired, sick to her stomach, dizzy, and had more watery output from her stoma bag. This happened one week after her last chemotherapy treatment. The physical exam showed nothing unusual. Her blood pressure and heart rate were normal. She had no fever.

Medical History:
The patient has high blood pressure and has had blood clots in her lungs. She takes a blood thinner called enoxaparin. She also has a heart rhythm problem that comes and goes. In the past, the patient had surgery to remove her ovaries, fallopian tubes, and uterus. She has a stoma bag because her small intestine was blocked. This later caused abnormal connections between organs. She also has tubes in both kidneys to drain urine because radiation treatment caused blockages. She has had many bladder infections. These infections left germs in her kidney tubes, including a drug-resistant bacteria called MRSA and a yeast called Candida. She had never received medicine to prevent fungal infections.

Initial Assessment:
Blood tests showed problems with salt levels, kidney function, red blood cells, white blood cells, and infection-fighting cells. On day 2, the patient developed a fever of 38.5 degrees Celsius. Doctors started her on two strong antibiotics called piperacillin-tazobactam and vancomycin. Doctors found bacteria in the kidney tube fluid but thought it was not causing infection. The kidney tubes were replaced. Other tests to find the infection source showed nothing. Stool tests were also negative.

Diagnostic Assessment:
The patient still had fevers during her third week in the hospital. Doctors did a full body scan to check if her cancer had spread. The scan showed no new cancer spread. However, it showed something unusual on her heart valve. A heart ultrasound through the chest showed a small moving object above the heart valve. A more detailed heart ultrasound through the throat showed a growth hanging inside the main blood vessel. It was attached to the heart valve and had a hardened base. A brain scan showed a spot in the right front part of the brain. This spot was likely caused by something traveling through the blood.

Treatment:
On day 22, blood tests grew a fungus. It was identified as Lomentospora prolificans. This fungus did not respond to two common antifungal medicines. It did respond to a medicine called voriconazole. The patient started treatment with voriconazole through an IV. Sadly, the patient was too sick for surgery. She had advanced cancer, a fungal infection throughout her body, and was very weak. The care team focused on keeping her comfortable.

Outcome:
The patient died five days after the fungus was found. This was on day 27 of her hospital stay.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: 
Age: 52
Gender: Female

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the cardiology department ICU with generalized edema and orthopnea in July 2008. An electrocardiogram revealed atrial fibrillation with a heart rate of 178 beats per minute. A chest X-ray detected pulmonary edema and cardiomegaly. A two-dimensional echocardiogram showed an ejection fraction of 57%. The patient was prescribed diltiazem for heart rate control. A physical examination revealed a large asymmetrical goiter, which was easily visible and palpable. The patient was diagnosed with NTI accompanying heart failure and discharged.

Follow-up:
The patient came to the endocrinology department for follow-up in February 2012. A follow-up thyroid ultrasonogram revealed an increase in the size of the previously observed nodule in the right lobe and contralateral lobe atrophy. A 99m-Technetium-pertechnetate thyroid scan demonstrated a functioning toxic nodule. The patient refused surgery or radioactive iodine therapy and was prescribed methimazole.

Outstanding Clinical Issues:
The patient was diagnosed with thyroid disease and had an increase in the size of the previously observed nodule in the right lobe and contralateral lobe atrophy at the time of the follow-up. The patient was prescribed methimazole and TSH levels normalized two weeks later. 

Discharge Medications:
Calcium channel blocker, angiotensin receptor antagonist, diuretic and aspirin.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient should continue follow-up for thyroid disease and any heart failure symptoms.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name:
Age: 52
Gender: Female

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the heart care intensive care unit with swelling all over the body and trouble breathing while lying down in July 2008. A heart rhythm test showed an irregular heartbeat with a heart rate of 178 beats per minute. A chest X-ray showed fluid in the lungs and an enlarged heart. A heart ultrasound showed the heart was pumping out 57% of its blood with each beat. The patient was given diltiazem to help control the heart rate. A physical exam showed a large uneven thyroid gland in the neck that could be seen and felt easily. The patient was found to have thyroid problems along with heart failure and was sent home.

Follow-up:
The patient came to the hormone specialist department for a check-up in February 2012. A follow-up thyroid ultrasound showed that a growth on the right side of the thyroid had gotten bigger and the left side had shrunk. A thyroid scan using a special dye showed an overactive thyroid growth. The patient did not want surgery or treatment with radioactive iodine and was given methimazole instead.

Outstanding Clinical Issues:
The patient was found to have thyroid disease with a growth on the right side of the thyroid that had gotten bigger and shrinking on the left side at the time of the check-up. The patient was given methimazole and thyroid hormone levels returned to normal two weeks later.

Discharge Medications:
Heart rate and blood pressure medicine, water pill, and aspirin.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient should keep going to check-ups for thyroid disease and any signs of heart failure.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Not Provided
Medical Record Number: Not Provided
Gender: Female
Age: 100 years

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]

Admission Diagnosis: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
Final Diagnosis: Successfully treated infrarenal AAA by EVAR

Hospital Course:

The 100-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department with central abdominal pain which emerged over four days. On examination, a pulsatile expansile aneurysm was observed. The computed tomography (CT) aortogram showed an 8 cm infrarenal AAA. Under informed consent, she underwent EVAR due to the high likelihood of mortality without surgery and favorable factors such as good preoperative functional status, limited comorbidities, anatomical suitability for EVAR under local anaesthetic and good recovery following a recent hemiarthroplasty. The patient went through the procedure successfully with an uneventful postoperative recovery. She had no signs of endoleak or complications following the graft during follow-up. The patient survived for two years following the intervention.

Discharge Condition: Stable

Discharge Medications: None provided in report

Follow-up Instructions: None provided in report

Discharge Instructions: None provided in report

Condition on Discharge:
The patient was discharged in a stable condition with no complications, successfully treated for the infrarenal AAA.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Not Provided
Medical Record Number: Not Provided
Gender: Female
Age: 100 years

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]

Admission Diagnosis: Bulge in the large blood vessel in the belly
Final Diagnosis: Successfully treated bulge in the belly blood vessel using a small tube placed through a blood vessel

Hospital Course:

The 100-year-old female patient came to the emergency room with pain in the middle of her belly. The pain had been going on for four days. During the exam, doctors found a bulging blood vessel that was getting bigger. A special scan of the blood vessel showed a 3-inch bulge in the large belly blood vessel below the kidneys. The doctors explained the treatment options and got permission to do the procedure. They chose to place a small tube through a blood vessel to fix the bulge. This was done because the bulge could be deadly without treatment. The patient was also a good fit for this procedure. She was healthy before this problem and had few other health issues. Her blood vessel shape worked well for this type of fix. The procedure could be done with numbing medicine instead of full sleep. She had also healed well from a recent partial hip replacement. The procedure went well with no problems during recovery. Follow-up visits showed no leaking around the tube and no other problems. The patient lived for two years after the procedure.

Discharge Condition: Stable

Discharge Medications: None provided in report

Follow-up Instructions: None provided in report

Discharge Instructions: None provided in report

Condition on Discharge:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition with no problems. The bulge in the belly blood vessel was successfully treated.
