input:
Hospital Course:

The patient is an 8-year-old female with tufting enteropathy who presented on three separate occasions with central venous catheter infection due to Bacillus species. The patient had initially presented in the first few months of life with chronic watery diarrhoea and impaired growth, and was found to have tufting enteropathy - a rare congenital enteropathy that requires indefinite dependence on parenteral nutrition from early infancy. 

On each occasion the patient presented with fever after flushing of the central venous catheter. The first episode of infection was treated with 4 weeks of intravenous flucloxacillin because bacteraemia had persisted despite 14 days of treatment.

The patient was transferred to our hospital following the recurrence of fever and rigors, 10 days after stopping the antibiotics. Bacillus species was isolated from central venous catheter cultures both before and whilst on cefotaxime and flucloxacillin, later identified as Bacillus pumilus at the National Reference Laboratory. There were concerns that the patient had previously reacted to systemic vancomycin, so antibiotics were changed to intravenous clindamycin with vancomycin line locks given for 2 weeks. Blood cultures taken both during and after this treatment were negative. Echocardiography showed no evidence of vegetations at the tip of the catheter or in the heart.

Ten days after stopping the intravenous antibiotics the child presented for the third time with fever and rigors. A Bacillus species was again grown from blood taken from the central venous catheter. The central venous catheter was removed after 5 days treatment with intravenous vancomycin and a new central venous catheter was inserted. Subsequent blood cultures were negative and there has been no recurrence of further fever or infections over a 9-month period, suggesting the infection has been eradicated.

Discharge Summary:

The patient is an 8-year-old female with tufting enteropathy on long-term parenteral nutrition, presenting on three separate occasions with central venous catheter infection due to Bacillus species. The patient was treated with multiple courses of antibiotics and required catheter replacement once due to recurrence of fever and rigors.

The patient is being discharged without any active infections or clinical concerns at this time. The patient's care plan will continue to include regular follow-up with the primary care physician and periodic bloodwork to monitor for any potential infections.

The patient and family were counseled on signs and symptoms of catheter-related infections and the importance of prompt reporting to medical professionals if fever or other concerning symptoms develop. The family was also provided with written and verbal information regarding infection prevention measures, such as strict hand hygiene and catheter care, to minimize the risk of future infections.

The patient was discharged home in good condition and was advised to follow up with the primary care physician for routine care and blood work. The patient's parents were instructed to monitor the patient for any signs of infection and to seek prompt medical attention if any concerning symptoms develop.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is an 8-year-old girl with a rare gut condition called tufting enteropathy. She came to the hospital three times with an infection in her feeding tube line caused by a germ called Bacillus. The patient first showed signs of this gut condition in the first few months of life. She had long-lasting watery diarrhea and was not growing well. This rare condition means the patient needs to get nutrition through a tube into her bloodstream for life, starting from early childhood.

Each time, the patient got a fever after her feeding tube line was flushed. The first infection was treated with 4 weeks of an antibiotic given through the tube. The infection stayed in her blood even after 14 days of treatment.

The patient was moved to our hospital after her fever and chills came back. This happened 10 days after she stopped taking antibiotics. The Bacillus germ was found in samples taken from her tube line. It was found both before and while she was on two different antibiotics. A special lab later found the exact type of germ was called Bacillus pumilus. There were worries that the patient had a bad reaction to one antibiotic called vancomycin in the past. So the doctors changed her medicine to a different antibiotic through the tube. They also used vancomycin flushes in her line for 2 weeks. Blood tests taken during and after this treatment showed no signs of the germ. A heart scan showed no signs of infection on the tip of the tube or in the heart.

Ten days after stopping the antibiotics given through the tube, the child came back a third time with fever and chills. The Bacillus germ was again found in blood taken from her tube line. The old tube line was taken out after 5 days of treatment with vancomycin given through the tube. A new tube line was put in. Blood tests after this showed no germs. There has been no return of fever or infections for 9 months. This suggests the infection is gone.

Discharge Summary:

The patient is an 8-year-old girl with tufting enteropathy who gets long-term nutrition through a tube into her bloodstream. She came to the hospital three times with infections in her tube line caused by a Bacillus germ. The patient was treated with several rounds of antibiotics. Her tube line had to be replaced once because her fever and chills came back.

The patient is going home without any active infections or health concerns at this time. The patient will keep seeing her regular doctor for checkups. She will also have regular blood tests to watch for any possible infections.

The patient and family were taught about the signs of tube line infections. They learned how important it is to tell medical staff right away if fever or other worrying signs happen. The family was also given written and spoken information about how to prevent infections. This includes careful handwashing and proper care of the tube line to lower the chance of future infections.

The patient went home in good health. She was told to see her regular doctor for routine care and blood tests. The patient's parents were told to watch for any signs of infection. They should get medical help right away if any worrying signs happen.

The patient and family were taught about the signs of tube line infections. They learned how important it is to tell medical staff right away if fever or other worrying signs happen. The family was also given written and spoken information about how to prevent infections. This includes careful handwashing and proper care of the tube line to lower the chance of future infections. The patient went home in good health. She was told to see her regular doctor for routine care and blood tests. They should get medical help right away if any worrying signs happen.

input:
Hospital Course:
A 27-year-old male with a history of recent COVID-19 infection presented with fever, sore throat, multiple joint pain, and body rash mainly on the forearms. Physical examination revealed tracheal cartilage tenderness and macular rashes on both arms. Laboratory investigations revealed leukocytosis with neutrophilia, elevated serum C-reactive protein, elevated serum ferritin, and abnormal liver function. Nasopharyngeal SARS CoV-2 PCR and serum SARS CoV-2 immunoglobulin G were positive. The patient was admitted under the care of internal medicine as a case of possible cytokines storm syndrome related to COVID-19 versus post viral bacterial infection. Despite treatment with 5 days of antibiotic therapy, the patient showed no improvement. The patient’s clinical presentation and labs met the Yamaguchi criteria for Adult Onset Still’s disease. The patient was cleared by the infectious disease team with an impression of the positive COVID-19 result being most likely due to viral shedding. The patient was started on IV methylprednisolone, resulting in clinical improvement with resolving joint pain and disappearance of the rash. The patient’s fever resolved and he was discharged on oral Prednisolone with follow-up appointments in the rheumatology clinic. 

Hospital Course Summary:
27-year-old male presented with fever, sore throat, multiple joint pain, and body rash mainly on the forearms, eight weeks after recovery from symptomatic COVID-19 infection. Physical examination showed tracheal cartilage tenderness and macular rashes on both arms. Lab investigations revealed leukocytosis with neutrophilia, elevated serum C-reactive protein, elevated serum ferritin, and abnormal liver function. Patient’s clinical presentation and labs met the Yamaguchi criteria for Adult Onset Still’s disease. The patient was started on IV methylprednisolone, resulting in clinical improvement, and was discharged on oral Prednisolone with follow-up appointments in the rheumatology clinic, following which he was cleared by the infectious disease team with an impression of the positive COVID-19 result being most likely due to viral shedding.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
A 27-year-old man who recently had COVID-19 came to the hospital with fever, sore throat, pain in many joints, and a rash mostly on his forearms. The doctor's exam found tenderness in the windpipe area and flat red spots on both arms. Blood tests showed high white blood cell counts, high levels of inflammation markers, high iron storage levels, and liver problems. Tests for COVID-19 were positive. The patient was admitted to the internal medicine team. Doctors thought he might have a severe immune reaction from COVID-19 or a bacterial infection after his virus. The patient took antibiotics for 5 days but did not get better. The patient's symptoms and test results matched the signs of Adult Onset Still's disease, a rare condition that causes inflammation throughout the body. The infection specialists said the positive COVID-19 test was likely from leftover virus particles, not a new infection. The patient started getting a strong anti-inflammation medicine called methylprednisolone through an IV. The patient got better, with less joint pain and the rash going away. The fever went away and the patient went home with prednisolone pills to take by mouth. The patient has follow-up visits scheduled at the joint and immune system clinic.

The infection specialists said the positive COVID-19 test was likely from leftover virus particles.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 57-year-old male who presented to the emergency department with confusional state, headaches, and worsening memory. Imaging revealed acute hydrocephalus and trans-ependymal CSF seepage, consistent with cryptococcal ventriculitis. CSF diversion was performed via an external ventricular drain and antifungal therapy was initiated. Repeat CSF studies showed resolution of infection and a lack of ventricular compliance was confirmed via CT scan. A ventriculoperitoneal shunt was placed successfully and the patient was discharged on long-term antifungal therapy.

Discharge Diagnosis:

1. Acute hydrocephalus
2. Trans-ependymal CSF seepage
3. Cryptococcal ventriculitis
4. Intracranial hypertension

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was admitted with worsening mental status, headaches, and memory loss. Imaging revealed acute hydrocephalus and trans-ependymal CSF seepage, consistent with cryptococcal ventriculitis. CSF diversion was performed via an external ventricular drain and the patient was started on antifungal therapy. Repeat CSF studies showed resolution of infection. During an EVD clamp trial, the patient showed clinical and radiological evidence of a lack of ventricular compliance, leading to the placement of a ventriculoperitoneal shunt. The patient recovered neurologically and was discharged on long-term antifungal therapy. At a three-month follow-up, the patient remains neurologically intact with no evidence of intracranial hypertension.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 57-year-old male who came to the emergency room feeling confused, having headaches, and having trouble with memory. Scans showed a buildup of fluid in the brain and fluid leaking into brain tissue. This was caused by a fungal infection in the brain. Doctors placed a tube to drain the extra fluid and started medicine to fight the fungal infection. Follow-up tests showed the infection was gone. A scan showed the brain could not handle fluid on its own. Doctors placed a permanent tube to move fluid from the brain to the belly. The patient went home with long-term medicine to fight fungal infection.

Discharge Diagnosis:

1. Sudden fluid buildup in the brain
2. Fluid leaking into brain tissue
3. Fungal infection in the brain fluid spaces
4. High pressure inside the skull

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was admitted with worsening confusion, headaches, and memory loss. Scans showed fluid buildup in the brain and fluid leaking into brain tissue. This was caused by a fungal infection. Doctors placed a tube to drain the extra fluid and started medicine to fight the fungal infection. Follow-up tests showed the infection was gone. When doctors tested if the brain could handle fluid on its own, scans and symptoms showed it could not. Doctors then placed a permanent tube to move fluid from the brain to the belly. The patient's brain function improved and the patient went home with long-term medicine to fight fungal infection. At a three-month checkup, the patient's brain function remains normal with no signs of high pressure inside the skull.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Name: [REDACTED]
- Age: 30 years
- Gender: Female
- Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
- Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
- Diagnosis: Acute T cell lymphoblastic leukemia

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the Department of Radiology at Ondokuz Mayis University Hospital due to palpable masses in both breasts. The mammograms revealed ill-defined, denser mass-like lesions in upper outer quadrants of both breasts and an ultrasonographic evaluation showed lobular-shaped and partly ill-defined hypoechoic masses. Both axilla indicated pathologically enlarged lymph nodes with skin thickening, increased echo texture in the breast parenchyma, and fine fluid between the fat lobules, which were consistent with edema of the breasts. A bone marrow biopsy confirmed a diagnosis of acute T cell lymphoblastic leukemia. The patient received chemotherapy which resulted in the disappearance of the breast lesions and decreased number and size of axillary lymph nodes.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient's condition significantly improved after receiving chemotherapy. The patient was deemed ready for discharge.

Follow-up:
The patient is advised to follow-up as scheduled with their oncologist for further management and evaluation. Appropriate imaging studies and laboratory tests may be repeated if clinically indicated. The patient is advised to maintain a healthy lifestyle, avoid exposure to sick individuals and engage in regular exercise. 

Disposition:
The patient was discharged to their residence with instructions on medication regimen and follow-up care.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 30 years
Gender: Female
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Diagnosis: A fast-growing blood cancer that starts in certain white blood cells called T cells

Hospital Course:
The patient came to Ondokuz Mayis University Hospital because of lumps that could be felt in both breasts. Breast x-rays showed areas that looked denser than normal in the upper outer parts of both breasts. An ultrasound test showed lumps with uneven edges. Both armpits had swollen lymph nodes, thicker skin, and signs of fluid buildup in the breast tissue. This pointed to swelling in the breasts. A test of the bone marrow showed the patient had a fast-growing blood cancer in certain white blood cells. The patient received cancer-fighting drugs. After treatment, the breast lumps went away. The swollen lymph nodes in the armpits also got smaller and fewer in number.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient got much better after receiving the cancer-fighting drugs. The patient was ready to go home.

Follow-up:
The patient should see their cancer doctor at the scheduled times for more care and checkups. The patient may need more imaging tests and blood tests if the doctor thinks they are needed. The patient should stay healthy, stay away from people who are sick, and exercise regularly.

Disposition:
The patient went home with instructions about how to take medicines and when to come back for checkups.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]      
Date of Admission: [redacted]       
Date of Discharge: [redacted]        

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 47-year-old woman, was admitted to the hospital with bilateral groin pain, inability to urinate, and bloody vaginal discharge. Clinical vaginal examination revealed the presence of a large necrotic mass filling the whole vaginal tract. Transabdominal ultrasound examination showed a cervico-vaginal mass measuring 112 × 114 mm that protruded from the uterine cavity with a distorted view of the uterus. After consultation and an MRI examination, a large heterogeneous signal intensity of a well-defined submucosal fundal fibroid mass was confirmed. Uterine anatomy was distorted as seen as “V shape” fundus in sagittal plane due to traction of the mass. 

Laboratory tests showed a decreased hemoglobin level of 10.1 g/dL, hematocrit of 30.9%, and elevated CRP level of 37.5 mg/L. The patient's fasting glucose level, AST and ALT liver enzymes were within normal range, but the CA125 level was 152 U/mL. 

At laparotomy, a large uterine mass with complete uterine inversion was confirmed. The mass was taken out via transverse uterine incision after depicting the myoma’s pedicle. Total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy was performed due to the patient's request before surgery. The excised specimen revealed a giant mass with extensive necrosis and multiple hemorrhagic foci. Pathology report confirmed 12 × 12 × 10 cm sized leiomyoma including large ischemic necrotic areas. There was no evidence of malignancy. 

The patient was discharged without any complications five days after the surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Uterine inversion with uterine fibroid.

Discharge Condition:
Stable. 

Discharge Medication:
N/A. 

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was instructed to follow up with her gynecologist and primary care physician. She was advised to avoid heavy lifting and intercourse for six weeks to allow for full recovery. She was also instructed to monitor for any signs of vaginal bleeding, pain or fever. If any of these symptoms appeared, she was advised to contact her physician immediately. 

Follow-Up:
The patient was advised to schedule a follow-up appointment with her gynecologist and primary care physician for recovery monitoring.

clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Date of Admission: [redacted]
Date of Discharge: [redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 47-year-old woman, came to the hospital with pain in both sides of her groin, not being able to urinate, and bloody discharge from her vagina. A physical exam showed a large dying tissue mass filling the entire vaginal canal. An ultrasound of the belly showed a mass in the cervix and vagina area measuring about 4.4 by 4.5 inches. The mass was coming out from inside the uterus, and the uterus looked twisted out of shape. After more tests and an MRI scan, doctors confirmed a large non-cancerous growth called a fibroid attached to the inside top of the uterus. The uterus was pulled into a V-shape because the mass was dragging on it.

Blood tests showed the patient had low red blood cell levels at 10.1 g/dL and 30.9 percent, which means mild blood loss. The patient also had high inflammation markers at 37.5 mg/L. Blood sugar and liver tests were normal. A test called CA125, which checks for certain problems, was high at 152 U/mL.

During surgery through the belly, doctors found a large growth in the uterus that had caused the uterus to turn inside out. The growth was removed by cutting into the uterus after finding where it was attached. The uterus, both fallopian tubes, and both ovaries were then removed because the patient asked for this before surgery. The removed tissue showed a very large mass with dead tissue and areas of bleeding. Lab tests on the tissue confirmed a fibroid measuring about 4.7 by 4.7 by 4 inches with large areas of dead tissue. There were no signs of cancer.

The patient left the hospital five days after surgery with no problems.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Uterus turned inside out with a non-cancerous uterine growth called a fibroid.

Discharge Condition:
Stable.

Discharge Medication:
None.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was told to follow up with her women's health doctor and regular doctor. She was told not to lift heavy things or have sex for six weeks so her body can fully heal. She was also told to watch for any signs of vaginal bleeding, pain, or fever. If any of these happen, she should call her doctor right away.

Follow-Up:
The patient was told to make an appointment with her women's health doctor and regular doctor to check on her healing.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient 1, a 47-year-old man with a history of spinal injury resulting in complete paraplegia, was admitted to the department of physical medicine and rehabilitation at our university hospital due to spasticity. Despite taking baclofen and diazepam, the patient reported experiencing 3-4 beats of knee extension clonus and spasm of adductor thigh muscles. The spasticity was not controlled and was interfering with the patient's sleep.

The patient underwent pulsed radiofrequency (PRF) procedure on both L2 and L3 DRGs with 5 Hz and 5 ms pulsed width for 360 seconds at 45 V under the C-arm fluoroscopy guide. There were no complications after the PRF. Following the procedure, the clonus of both knee extensors disappeared completely, and the spasticity on both hip adductors was reduced from a score of 3 to 1+. The patient reported significant improvement in quality of sleep and a 50% reduction in spasticity on both hip adductors.

The spasticity returned at nearly the same degree six months after the procedure, and a repeat PRF was performed with similar results, which were also sustained for six months.

Overall, the PRF procedure was effective in reducing the spasticity of the patient's adductor thigh muscles and improving his quality of sleep. The patient was discharged in stable condition and advised to follow up with the clinic as needed.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient 1 is a 47-year-old man. He has a spinal cord injury that left him unable to move his legs. He came to the hospital's physical therapy department because of muscle stiffness and tightness.

The patient was already taking two medicines called baclofen and diazepam. Even with these medicines, his leg muscles were still having problems. His knees would jerk and bounce 3-4 times on their own. The muscles on the inner part of his thighs would also tighten up by themselves. This muscle stiffness was not under control and was making it hard for him to sleep.

The patient had a treatment called pulsed radiofrequency. This treatment uses mild electrical pulses on nerves in the lower back. The doctor used an X-ray machine to guide the treatment. The treatment was done on nerves on both sides of the spine. There were no problems during or after the treatment. After the treatment, the knee jerking stopped completely. The thigh muscle tightness got much better, going from severe to mild. The patient said he was sleeping much better. He also said the thigh muscle tightness was half as bad as before.

About six months later, the muscle stiffness came back to almost the same level. The patient had the same treatment again. The results were the same as the first time. These results also lasted for six months.

Overall, this treatment worked well to reduce the tightness in the patient's inner thigh muscles. It also helped him sleep better. The patient left the hospital in stable condition. He was told to come back to the clinic if needed.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Discharge Diagnosis: Thrombocytopenia and Pulmonary Emboli

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 38-year-old man with cerebral palsy, was admitted on D+16 after experiencing bruising and petechiae post Vaxzevria vaccination. Upon admission, thrombocytopenia was noted with elevated D-dimers. A haematology assessment was requested on D+17 after a family member noticed a subtle tachypnoea. A CT scan revealed two proximal pulmonary emboli. Anti-PF4 IgG ELISA test was positive, and platelet activation assays on D+17 showed negative HIPA and positive PIPA assays. The patient was treated with IVIg (1 g/kg for 2 days) and therapeutic anticoagulation with argatroban. This was transitioned to apixaban on D+19, and platelet count gradually increased to 84 x 109/l. 

On discharge, the patient was in stable condition and was provided with appropriate follow-up instructions. His recovery was unremarkable. 

Discharge Medications:

None.

Follow-up Instructions:

The patient was advised to report any adverse symptoms, including headaches, fever, shortness of breath, chest pain, or unusual sweating. Additionally, he was advised to stay hydrated, keep up with his prescribed medications, and avoid any strenuous activities. 

Signed,

[REDACTED]

MD, Attending Physician
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Discharge Diagnosis: Low platelet count and blood clots in the lungs

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 38-year-old man with cerebral palsy, was admitted on Day 16 after getting bruises and small red spots on the skin after receiving the Vaxzevria vaccine. When the patient arrived, doctors found a low platelet count and high levels of a substance that shows blood clotting. A blood specialist was asked to check the patient on Day 17 after a family member noticed slightly fast breathing. A scan showed two blood clots in the main blood vessels of the lungs. A blood test showed signs that the vaccine caused the immune system to react against platelets. Other lab tests on Day 17 helped confirm this diagnosis. The patient was treated with a medicine that helps the immune system given through a vein for 2 days and a blood thinner called argatroban. The blood thinner was changed to apixaban on Day 19, and the platelet count slowly went up to a safer level.

On discharge, the patient was in stable condition and was given instructions for follow-up care. The patient recovered without any problems.

Discharge Medications:

None.

Follow-up Instructions:

The patient was told to report any bad symptoms, including headaches, fever, trouble breathing, chest pain, or unusual sweating. The patient was also told to drink plenty of fluids, keep taking any prescribed medicines, and avoid hard physical activity.

Signed,

[REDACTED]

MD, Attending Physician

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 27-year-old pregnant female with a history of congenital heart disease, was admitted to the hospital at 34 weeks of gestation due to pain abdomen for 2 days. On examination, the patient was found to be cyanotic with clubbed fingers and raised Jugular venous pressure, and there was a pansystolic murmur and bilateral basal crepitations present. The patient was diagnosed to have a large perimembranous ventricular septal defect (VSD) with bidirectional shunt and severe pulmonary hypertension (PAH), tricuspid regurgitation, and a dilated right atrium seen on echocardiography.

Treatment:

The patient had been on irregular treatment with sildenafil 25 mg twice a day since 4 months, and given her previous obstetric history, lower segment cesarean section (LSCS) was planned. All risks were explained, and written informed consent was obtained. Prophylaxis against bacterial endocarditis was provided, and an epidural catheter was inserted for pain management during the surgery. Ampicillin and gentamicin were administered as per standard prophylaxis guidelines.

Procedure:

Over a period of 30 min, a total of 7cc of lignocaine with adrenaline solution and 8 cc of bupivacaine with 25 μg of fentanyl produced a block height of T8 tested to touch with blunt forceps. CVP was maintained between10-12 mm throughout surgery. The procedure was uneventful, and after delivery of the baby, furosemide 10 mg was given intravenously. A total of 450 cc of colloid was given.

Postoperative:

After surgery, the patient was shifted to the intensive care unit (ICU) for monitoring and was discharged on the 10th day. Epidural analgesia was maintained with 0.125% bupivacaine and 20 μg of fentanyl every 8 hourly for 72 h.

Summary:

The patient was diagnosed to have a large perimembranous ventricular septal defect (VSD) with bidirectional shunt and severe pulmonary hypertension (PAH), tricuspid regurgitation, and a dilated right atrium seen on echocardiography. She had a history of a stillborn delivery in her previous pregnancy and was advised against conceiving again, but she refused. She had been on irregular treatment with sildenafil, and given her previous obstetric history, LSCS was planned. The procedure was uneventful, and the patient was discharged on the 10th day. Epidural analgesia was maintained for pain management after surgery.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 27-year-old pregnant woman with a heart problem she was born with. She came to the hospital at 34 weeks of pregnancy because of stomach pain for 2 days. During the exam, the patient had bluish skin, curved fingernails, and swollen neck veins. The doctor heard a heart murmur and crackling sounds in both lungs. A heart ultrasound showed the patient has a large hole between the lower chambers of the heart. Blood was flowing in both directions through this hole. The patient also has very high blood pressure in the lungs, a leaky heart valve, and an enlarged upper right heart chamber.

Treatment:

The patient had been taking sildenafil 25 mg twice a day for 4 months, but not regularly. Because of her past pregnancy history, doctors planned a cesarean section to deliver the baby. The medical team explained all the risks to the patient. The patient signed a consent form agreeing to the surgery. The patient received medicine to prevent heart infection. A thin tube was placed in the lower back to control pain during surgery. The patient was given two antibiotics called ampicillin and gentamicin to prevent infection.

Procedure:

Over 30 minutes, the doctors gave numbing medicine through the tube in the lower back. This blocked pain from the chest down to the belly button area. The pressure in the large vein near the heart was kept at a safe level during surgery. The surgery went well with no problems. After the baby was born, the patient received furosemide 10 mg through a vein to remove extra fluid. The patient also received fluids through a vein during surgery.

Postoperative:

After surgery, the patient was moved to the intensive care unit for close watching. Pain medicine was given through the tube in the lower back every 8 hours for 3 days. The patient left the hospital on the 10th day after surgery.

Summary:

The patient has a large hole between the lower chambers of the heart she was born with. Blood flows in both directions through this hole. She also has very high blood pressure in the lungs, a leaky heart valve, and an enlarged upper right heart chamber. In her last pregnancy, the baby did not survive. Doctors told her not to get pregnant again, but she chose to anyway. She had been taking sildenafil, but not regularly. Doctors planned a cesarean section because of her past pregnancy. The surgery went well with no problems. Pain medicine was given through a tube in the lower back after surgery. The patient left the hospital on the 10th day.

input:
Discharge Summary:

This is a summary of admission details for a 20-year-old female with a medical history of post-traumatic stress syndrome and facial acne. The patient was admitted to the emergency department after ingesting seven pills of paroxetine.

Course of Hospitalization:

Initial evaluation in the emergency department yielded minimal symptoms, stable vital signs, and no arrhythmia on ECG. The patient was observed for six hours and complained of flushing and became agitated. The patient sustained witnessed spontaneous aborted tonic clinic seizure activity of 90 seconds. She exhibited tachycardia, tachypnea, hypertension, and hyperreflexia. Blood workup, CT of the head without contrast, urine drug screen, and ethanol level were normal. ECG showed a prolonged corrected QT interval of >540. SS was suspected, and the patient was given IV fluids and lorazepam. Cyproheptadine was given 12 mg orally as a single dose and 4 mg every eight hours as needed. The patient was admitted to the intensive care unit for close monitoring with serial ECG. QT interval normalization was observed 16 hours later, and no visual seizures were noted during hospitalization.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Suspected serotonin syndrome 

Treatment:

The patient was given IV fluids and lorazepam. Cyproheptadine was given 12 mg orally as a single dose and 4 mg every eight hours as needed.

Follow-up:

The patient was discharged in stable condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

This is a summary of admission details for a 20-year-old female with a medical history of post-traumatic stress syndrome and facial acne. The patient was admitted to the emergency department after swallowing seven pills of paroxetine.

Course of Hospitalization:

The first check in the emergency department showed very few symptoms, steady body function measures, and a normal heart rhythm test. The patient was watched for six hours and said she felt warm and flushed and became restless. The patient had a seizure that staff saw happen, which lasted 90 seconds and stopped on its own. She had a fast heart rate, fast breathing, high blood pressure, and overactive reflexes. Blood tests, a brain scan, a drug test, and an alcohol test were all normal. The heart rhythm test showed the heart was taking too long to reset between beats, more than 540 units. A condition called serotonin syndrome was suspected, and the patient was given fluids through a vein and lorazepam. Cyproheptadine was given as a 12 mg pill one time, then 4 mg every eight hours if needed. The patient was moved to the intensive care unit for close watching with regular heart rhythm tests. The heart rhythm went back to normal 16 hours later, and no seizures were seen during the hospital stay.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Suspected serotonin syndrome

Treatment:

The patient was given fluids through a vein and lorazepam. Cyproheptadine was given as a 12 mg pill one time, then 4 mg every eight hours if needed.

Follow-up:

The patient was sent home in stable condition.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was a 50-year-old Chinese male who presented with cough and expectoration of black phlegm for about 2 weeks, sometimes accompanied by bloody sputum and breathlessness. A computed tomography (CT) scan of his chest revealed a rough-edged shadow measuring nearly 32×22×10 mm in the right upper lobe of his lung. The patient was diagnosed with a giant cell carcinoma-like tumor (GCCL) of the lung after undergoing a right-sided upper lobectomy and systematic mediastinal lymphadenectomy. Histopathologic examination of the tumor cells revealed a positive reaction for cytokeratin (CK) and vimentin, while immunostaining for chromogranin A (CgA) and synaptophysin (Syn) was negative.

The patient had several chronic disorders, including a 10-year history of bronchitis and emphysema, and was a smoker with a 30-pack-year history, but had no family history of lung cancer. He underwent combined chemotherapy using gemcitabine and cisplatin for four cycles and was followed up with whole-body radiography examinations every year in a local hospital. As of 7 years after surgery, he remained alive and free from disease progression.

The patient's hospital course was notable for his successful treatment of GCCL, good response to treatment, and his long-term follow-up. The patient was able to communicate clearly and effectively with the healthcare team during his stay. He was compliant with his treatment regimen and was actively involved in discharge planning. The healthcare team discussed the importance of smoking cessation and provided resources for smoking cessation for the patient.

Diagnoses:

1. Giant cell carcinoma-like tumor (GCCL) of the lung.

Discharge Medications:

None.

Follow-Up Care:

The patient is advised to continue yearly follow-up visits with a local hospital to monitor his condition. He is also encouraged to continue smoking cessation efforts.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was a 50-year-old Chinese male who came in with a cough and black mucus for about 2 weeks. Sometimes the patient also coughed up blood and had trouble breathing. A special X-ray scan of the chest showed a rough-edged mass about 32×22×10 mm in the upper part of the right lung. The patient had surgery to remove the upper part of the right lung and nearby lymph nodes. After surgery, doctors found a rare type of lung cancer called giant cell carcinoma-like tumor. Lab tests on the tumor cells showed positive results for certain markers called cytokeratin and vimentin. Tests for other markers called chromogranin A and synaptophysin were negative.

The patient had long-term health problems, including 10 years of swollen airways and damaged air sacs in the lungs. The patient smoked for many years, equal to one pack a day for 30 years. No one in the patient's family had lung cancer before. The patient received cancer-fighting drugs called gemcitabine and cisplatin for four rounds of treatment. The patient had full body scans every year at a local hospital. Seven years after surgery, the patient was still alive with no signs of the cancer coming back.

The patient did well during the hospital stay. The treatment worked well, and the patient was watched closely over many years. The patient talked clearly with the care team during the stay. The patient followed the treatment plan and helped plan for going home. The care team talked about the need to stop smoking and gave the patient information on how to quit.

Diagnoses:

1. Giant cell carcinoma-like tumor of the lung, a rare type of lung cancer.

Discharge Medications:

None.

Follow-Up Care:

The patient should keep going to yearly check-up visits at a local hospital to watch for any changes. The patient should also keep trying to stop smoking.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The 62-year-old male was admitted to the hospital with a complaint of abdominal pain, vomiting, and ambulatory dysfunction. The initial examination showed no abnormalities. The patient was suspected to have viral gastroenteritis, and hence he was hospitalized for dehydration management. The patient's nausea and vomiting improved after admission, but it was noted that he was leaning backwards and to the right when standing or attempting to ambulate on the first day of hospitalization.

Diagnostic Evaluations:
The neurology service was consulted after assessing the patient's condition on his second day of admission. A CT angiogram of the head and neck showed a congenitally hypoplastic left vertebral artery and no other abnormality was observed in the vertebrobasilar system. MRI of the brain showed acute/subacute infarct in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The echocardiogram showed moderate diastolic dysfunction with trace aortic regurgitation.

Clinical Impression:
The patient was diagnosed with acute/subacute infarct in the right cerebellar hemisphere secondary to chronic atherosclerotic disease that had extended and caused APS. The left-sided dysmetria was observed, which further indicated his neurological impairment.

Hospital Treatment:
The patient was monitored on telemetry for several days without any events or abnormalities noted. He was started on a scheduled antiemetic regimen of metoclopramide 10 mg four times daily and ondansetron 8 mg every eight hours that yielded positive results. Within one day, the patient's nausea and vomiting were controlled, and he was able to eat without difficulty. The patient was successfully discharged from the hospital to rehabilitation.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient is recommended to follow up with the neurology service as an outpatient and undergo regular checkups to monitor his neurological function and the progression of his cerebrovascular disease. He is also advised to maintain strict blood glucose control and hypertension management. The family members are recommended to observe the patient's activities, as he is more vulnerable to falls.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The 62-year-old male came to the hospital with belly pain, vomiting, and trouble walking. The first exam showed nothing wrong. Doctors thought he had a stomach bug and kept him in the hospital to treat fluid loss. His upset stomach and vomiting got better after he was admitted. On the first day, staff noticed he was leaning backward and to the right when standing or trying to walk.

Diagnostic Evaluations:
The brain and nerve doctors were called in on the second day. A scan of the blood vessels in his head and neck showed he was born with a smaller than normal artery on the left side of his neck. No other problems were found in those blood vessels. A brain scan showed he had a recent stroke in the right side of the back part of his brain. A heart test showed the heart does not relax well between beats. It also showed a small amount of blood leaking back through one heart valve.

Clinical Impression:
The patient was found to have a recent stroke in the right side of the back part of his brain. This was caused by long-term hardening of the arteries that had spread and blocked blood flow. The left side of his body showed trouble with coordination. This pointed to damage in his brain function.

Hospital Treatment:
The patient's heart rhythm was watched for several days with no problems found. He was given medicine to stop nausea. He took metoclopramide 10 mg four times a day and ondansetron 8 mg every eight hours. This helped him feel better. Within one day, his upset stomach and vomiting stopped. He was able to eat without problems. The patient left the hospital and went to a rehab center.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient should see the brain and nerve doctors for regular visits. These visits will check how his brain is working and watch for changes in his blood vessel disease. He should also keep his blood sugar and high blood pressure under control. Family members should watch him closely during activities because he has a higher chance of falling.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Not specified
Hospital Name: Limi Children's Hospital
Admission Date: Not specified
Discharge Date: Not applicable (deceased)

Hospital Course:
The patient was an 8-week-old female infant who was referred to the Emergency Paediatric Unit from a peripheral hospital due to recurrent fast breathing, darkened mucous membrane of the oral cavity and lips, and digits noticed since the 1st week of life. She also had feeding difficulties. The patient had previously received oral medications including amoxicillin and Vitamin C. Chest radiograph showed enlarged cardiac shadow with increased pulmonary vascular markings, and a previous echo report from a peripheral hospital reported Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF). Pertinent examination findings at presentation include marked respiratory distress with mild cyanosis. The patient was diagnosed with Pulmonary Trunk Atresia (PTA), malaligned ventricular septal defect, Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA), and pulmonary hypertension.

Treatment:
The patient was managed on diuretics and vasopressors, and maintained on intranasal oxygen at a low flow rate of 1 L/min.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient's condition deteriorated and she died within the first 2 days of hospitalization.

Follow-Up Care:
Not applicable (deceased)

Summary:
The 8-week-old female infant presented to the Emergency Paediatric Unit with respiratory complications, darkened mucous membrane of the oral cavity and lips, and digits noticed since the 1st week of life. She was diagnosed with multiple congenital heart defects including Pulmonary Trunk Atresia (PTA), malaligned ventricular septal defect, Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA), and pulmonary hypertension. Despite receiving diuretics and vasopressors, and maintaining on intranasal oxygen at a low flow rate of 1 L/min, the patient's condition deteriorated and she passed away.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Not specified
Hospital Name: Limi Children's Hospital
Admission Date: Not specified
Discharge Date: Not applicable (deceased)

Hospital Course:
The patient was an 8-week-old baby girl. She was sent to the Emergency Children's Unit from another hospital. Since her first week of life, she had repeated episodes of fast breathing. The skin inside her mouth, her lips, and her fingers and toes had turned a darker color. She also had trouble feeding. The patient had been given medicines by mouth before, including an antibiotic and Vitamin C. A chest X-ray showed that her heart looked bigger than normal. It also showed more blood vessel markings in her lungs. An earlier heart ultrasound from another hospital suggested a heart problem called Tetralogy of Fallot. When doctors examined her, she was having a lot of trouble breathing. Her skin had a slight bluish color from low oxygen. The patient was found to have several heart problems she was born with. These included a blocked main lung artery, a hole between the lower heart chambers that was not lined up correctly, an open blood vessel that should have closed after birth, and high blood pressure in the lungs.

Treatment:
The patient was given medicines to remove extra fluid from her body and medicines to support her blood pressure. She was also given oxygen through her nose at a low level of 1 liter per minute.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient's health got worse. She passed away within the first 2 days of her hospital stay.

Follow-Up Care:
Not applicable (deceased)

Summary:
The 8-week-old baby girl came to the Emergency Children's Unit with breathing problems. The skin inside her mouth, her lips, and her fingers and toes had been darker since her first week of life. She was given medicines to remove extra fluid and support her blood pressure. She was also given oxygen through her nose at a low level. Her health got worse, and she passed away.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the emergency department with complaints of fatigue and fever. After conducting various tests, the patient was diagnosed with Legionella pneumonia. Treatment with antibiotics was started immediately. During the course of the hospital stay, the patient showed significant improvement, and the symptoms gradually subsided. The patient was monitored closely, and regular laboratory tests were performed to evaluate the treatment status.

Clinical Findings:
The patient, a 54-year-old male, had a medical history of hypertension and was on medication. Physical examination showed an elevated blood pressure, high pulse rate, and fever. Additionally, crackles were detected in the lung on examination. The CT scan revealed ground-glass opacities in the right lung. The laboratory data showed mild renal dysfunction, hypophosphatemia, and respiratory alkalosis. Urinary analysis revealed some abnormalities, including proteinuria, glycosuria, panaminoaciduria, and phosphaturia.

Course of Treatment:
The patient was treated with antibiotics for Legionella pneumonia. The response to treatment was positive, and the patient showed significant improvement in the symptoms. Additional laboratory tests showed improvement in renal function and electrolyte balance. The patient was closely monitored throughout the stay and provided with appropriate care as required.

Condition on Discharge:
On discharge, the patient's condition showed significant improvement, and the symptoms had subsided. The laboratory tests were within the normal range except for mild renal dysfunction, which showed some improvement. The patient was discharged with adequate instructions on follow-up care and further treatment plan, if required.

Summary:
The patient was admitted with symptoms of fatigue and fever, and after various tests, he was diagnosed with Legionella pneumonia. The patient was treated with antibiotics, and the response to treatment was positive. The laboratory results showed improvement during the course of the hospital stay. The patient was discharged with instructions for follow-up care and further evaluation if required.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room feeling very tired and had a fever. After running several tests, doctors found the patient had a lung infection caused by Legionella bacteria. The patient started taking antibiotics right away. During the hospital stay, the patient got much better and the symptoms slowly went away. Doctors watched the patient closely and did regular blood tests to check how well the treatment was working.

Clinical Findings:
The patient is a 54-year-old man with a history of high blood pressure who takes medicine for it. The physical exam showed high blood pressure, fast heart rate, and fever. Doctors also heard crackling sounds in the lungs during the exam. A CT scan showed hazy patches in the right lung. Blood tests showed the kidneys were not working quite right, low phosphorus levels, and the blood was less acidic than normal. Urine tests showed some problems, including protein in the urine, sugar in the urine, too many amino acids in the urine, and phosphorus in the urine.

Course of Treatment:
The patient received antibiotics for the lung infection caused by Legionella bacteria. The treatment worked well, and the patient got much better. More blood tests showed the kidneys were working better and body minerals were more balanced. Doctors watched the patient closely during the whole stay and gave proper care when needed.

Condition on Discharge:
When leaving the hospital, the patient was much better and the symptoms had gone away. Most blood test results were normal, but the kidneys still showed mild problems that had improved. The patient left the hospital with clear instructions about follow-up care and what to do if more treatment is needed.

Summary:
The patient came to the hospital feeling very tired and had a fever. After several tests, doctors found a lung infection caused by Legionella bacteria. The patient took antibiotics and responded well to treatment. Blood test results improved during the hospital stay. The patient went home with instructions for follow-up care and what to do if more check-ups are needed.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

Patient Name: [insert name]               Medical Record Number: [insert number]

Reason for Admission:
The patient was admitted due to suspicion of a mycotic aortic aneurysm and severe infection.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent intravenous antibiotic therapy for four weeks and was started on tazobactam/piperacillin hydrate. Computed tomography angiography showed a descending aortic aneurysm measuring 70 × 70 mm, which extended 20 mm in only 2 weeks. Transcatheter endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) was performed under local anesthesia using two 37 × 200 mm Conformable stent grafts (W. L. Gore & Associates, Inc., Newark, DE, USA) to prevent aortic rupture.

Upon discharge, the patient was afebrile with a normal white blood cell count and C-reactive protein level. Oral antibiotics were continued for 18 months. The patient underwent monitoring by computed tomography 15 months after the TEVAR, and the aneurysm had regressed significantly, measuring 53 × 53 mm. The patient had no further signs of infection at the time of writing.

Diagnosis:
Descending aortic aneurysm

Procedures:
Transcatheter endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR)

Condition at Discharge:
The patient was discharged without serious complications.

Follow-Up:
The patient was monitored via computed tomography 15 months after TEVAR repair, at which point there was significant regression of the aneurysm. The patient was free from antibiotics at the time of writing.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

Patient Name: [insert name]               Medical Record Number: [insert number]

Reason for Admission:
The patient was admitted because doctors thought there was a bulge in the main blood vessel caused by an infection. The patient also had a serious infection.

Hospital Course:
The patient received antibiotics through a vein for four weeks. The medicine used was tazobactam/piperacillin hydrate. A special scan of the blood vessels showed a bulge in the lower part of the main artery. The bulge measured 70 by 70 mm. It had grown 20 mm in just 2 weeks. Doctors did a procedure to fix the bulge using a tube inserted through a blood vessel. The patient was awake but the area was numbed. Doctors placed two mesh tubes inside the artery to stop it from bursting. The mesh tubes measured 37 by 200 mm and were made by W. L. Gore and Associates, Inc., Newark, DE, USA.

When the patient left the hospital, there was no fever. Blood tests for infection were normal. The patient took antibiotic pills for 18 months. A scan was done 15 months after the procedure. The bulge had shrunk a lot and now measured 53 by 53 mm. The patient showed no signs of infection when this report was written.

Diagnosis:
Bulge in the lower part of the main artery

Procedures:
Repair of the artery bulge using a tube inserted through a blood vessel

Condition at Discharge:
The patient left the hospital without serious problems.

Follow-Up:
A scan was done 15 months after the repair. The bulge had shrunk a lot. The patient had stopped taking antibiotics when this report was written.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification: Baby S P, 6-year-old girl

Hospital Course:
Baby S P was admitted with a complaint of progressive bilateral breast enlargement, cyclical vaginal bleeding, and pubic hair development. She had a recent increase in height, but no neck swelling, pigmented skin marks, bone pain/fractures, or clitoral enlargement. Her birth and neonatal period were normal. 

On examination, her vitals were normal, and she had no thyroid swelling, café au lait spots, or any bony abnormality. Tanners staging revealed B3P3, with no axillary hair or genital ambiguity. There were no abnormalities noted in her cardiac, thoracic, or neurological examinations. Abdominal examination was normal. 

Investigations showed normal hemogram, liver and renal functions, and thyroid functions. However, her hormonal profile revealed low levels of LH and FSH, and high levels of testosterone and DHEAS. CT pelvis showed a bulky left ovary of size 5.4 cm predominantly solid with follicles seen at periphery and pubertal uterus. 

She underwent laparoscopic resection of right tuboovarian mass, which on histopathological examination was found to be mixed germ cell sex cord stromal tumor. The post-operative period was marked by withdrawal vaginal bleeding lasting for 72 hours. A repeat hormonal profile done on the 6th post-operative day showed reduced estradiol levels.

At the 6-month follow-up, there was no vaginal bleeding reported.

Diagnosis: Mixed germ cell sex cord stromal tumor

Treatment: Laparoscopic resection of right tuboovarian mass

Follow-up: 6-months follow-up showed no vaginal bleeding.

Course and Outcome: Baby S P was admitted for evaluation of progressive bilateral breast enlargement, cyclical vaginal bleeding, and pubic hair development. Following laparoscopic resection of right tuboovarian mass, post-operative bleeding was noted, and a repeat hormonal profile showed reduced estradiol levels. She was discharged with follow-up appointments to monitor her condition.

Patient Education: The patient's caregivers were advised to contact the hospital in case of any complications or recurrence of symptoms.

Disposition: Discharged with follow-up appointments.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification: Baby S P, 6-year-old girl

Hospital Course:
Baby S P came to the hospital because her breasts were growing on both sides, she was having monthly bleeding from her private area, and hair was growing in her private area. She had also grown taller recently. She did not have neck swelling, dark skin patches, bone pain or breaks, or growth of her private parts. Her birth and early baby period were normal.

During the physical exam, her heart rate, blood pressure, and temperature were normal. She did not have thyroid swelling, light brown skin spots, or bone problems. Her breast and pubic hair development were at stage 3. She had no underarm hair or unclear private parts. Her heart, chest, brain, and belly exams were all normal.

Blood tests showed normal blood cell counts, liver function, kidney function, and thyroid function. However, her hormone tests showed low levels of LH and FSH, which are hormones that control growth and development. Her testosterone and DHEAS levels were high. A scan of her lower belly showed her left ovary was large, measuring 5.4 cm. It was mostly solid with small fluid sacs around the edges. Her uterus looked like it was developing.

The patient had surgery using small cuts and a camera to remove a growth from her right ovary and tube. Lab tests on the growth showed it was a type of tumor made of mixed cell types. After surgery, she had bleeding from her private area for 72 hours. A hormone test done 6 days after surgery showed her estradiol levels had gone down.

At her 6-month checkup, she had no bleeding from her private area.

Diagnosis: A tumor made of mixed cell types found in the ovary area

Treatment: Surgery using small cuts and a camera to remove the growth from the right ovary and tube

Follow-up: At the 6-month checkup, there was no bleeding from the private area.

Course and Outcome: Baby S P came to the hospital because her breasts were growing on both sides, she was having monthly bleeding from her private area, and hair was growing in her private area. After surgery to remove the growth from her right ovary and tube, she had some bleeding. A hormone test showed her estradiol levels had gone down. She left the hospital with future appointments to check on her health.

Patient Education: The patient's caregivers were told to call the hospital if there were any problems or if symptoms came back.

Disposition: The patient left the hospital with future appointments scheduled.

Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [Insert Date]
Discharge Date: [Insert Date]

Patient Name: [Insert Name]
Age: 34
Gender: Male
Medical Record Number: [Insert Number]

Admission Diagnosis: 8% total body surface area, third-to-fourth degree electric flame burns.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with extensive burns covering 8% of his body after an electrical fire. He was immediately given standard treatment for this kind of injury, which included fluid resuscitation, early enteral nutrition, and regular dressing changes. On the fourth day of his admission, he underwent an eschar excision procedure in his right upper limb. However, on the seventh day, the patient experienced a burst of heavy bleeding. He immediately notified the nursing staff and medical team. The volume of blood loss was estimated to be more than 2 L, and the patient was extremely pale with cold skin. The nurse and doctors responded quickly, and fluid resuscitation was done immediately. Additionally, a tourniquet cuff was used to prevent further bleeding, and the NPWT material was removed. After the bleeding vessels were blocked, the patient was given a balanced solution, manufactured colloid, plasma, and red cell suspension to replace the volume of blood lost. The patient gradually recovered, and his hemoglobin levels and blood pressure returned to normal.

Course of Treatment:
The patient was given a thorough examination and was given the appropriate treatment for his burns. His wounds were regularly dressed, and he was given enteral nutrition. Due to the sudden burst of heavy bleeding, he required emergency fluid resuscitation and blood transfusion. Upon successful completion of the treatment, the patient's condition improved, and he was ready for discharge.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient was discharged from the hospital in a stable condition. Follow-up appointments with a burn specialist were scheduled.

Discharge Medications:
[None mentioned in the case report]

Discharge Instructions:
[None mentioned in the case report]

Follow-up Orders:
The patient was instructed to follow up with a burn specialist after discharge.

Hospital Stay Summary:

Admission Date: [Insert Date]
Discharge Date: [Insert Date]

Patient Name: [Insert Name]
Age: 34
Gender: Male
Medical Record Number: [Insert Number]

Reason for Hospital Stay: Burns from an electrical fire covering 8% of the body. The burns were very deep, damaging all layers of skin and some tissue underneath.

Hospital Stay:
The patient came to the hospital with serious burns covering 8% of his body after an electrical fire. The patient was given standard care for this type of injury right away. This included fluids through a vein, feeding through a tube, and regular bandage changes. On day four, doctors did surgery to remove dead skin from the right arm. On day seven, the patient suddenly started bleeding heavily. The patient told the nurses and doctors right away. The patient lost more than 2 liters of blood. The patient looked very pale and had cold skin. The nurses and doctors acted quickly and gave fluids through a vein right away. They also used a tight band on the arm to stop more bleeding. They took off the wound dressing. After stopping the bleeding, the patient was given fluids, blood products, and red blood cells to replace what was lost. The patient slowly got better. Blood levels and blood pressure went back to normal.

Treatment Given:
The patient was fully checked and given the right care for the burns. The wounds were cleaned and bandaged regularly. The patient was fed through a tube. Because of the sudden heavy bleeding, the patient needed emergency fluids and blood. After treatment was finished, the patient got better and was ready to go home.

Condition When Leaving:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition. Visits with a burn doctor were set up.

Medications When Leaving:
None listed in the report.

Instructions When Leaving:
None listed in the report.

Follow-up Visits:
The patient was told to see a burn doctor after leaving the hospital.

Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
A 22-year-old female presented to the emergency department (ED) with left-sided jaw pain, left cheek swelling, dysphagia, and odynophagia 48 hours after a dental extraction. The patient was observed for 3 days and discharged after complete resolution of symptoms and follow-up phone call one week later. 

Reason for Hospitalization:
Left-sided jaw pain, left cheek swelling, dysphagia, and odynophagia 48 hours after a dental extraction

History of Presenting Illness:
The patient reported pain in the left jaw, cheek swelling, dysphagia, and odynophagia, which developed 48 hours after a tooth extraction, which she received bupivacaine with epinephrine injection for local anesthesia. Later review of dental records indicated that a pressurized air turbine dental drill had then been used for resection of the tooth prior to extraction. 

Diagnostic Assessment:
Laboratory findings showed that the white blood cell count was 6.8 thousand per microliter (K/uL), hemoglobin was 15.2 grams per deciliter (g/dL), platelet count was 300 (K/uL), and C-reactive protein 8 milligrams per liter (mg/L). CT soft tissue neck with intravenous (IV) contrast demonstrated extensive air accumulation involving the oral, retropharyngeal space, and pneumomediastinum.

Therapeutic Assessment:
The patient was started on IV maintenance fluids and ampicillin-sulbactam for broad spectrum coverage including oral flora.

Summary:
The 22-year-old female presented to the ED with left-sided jaw pain, left cheek swelling, dysphagia, and odynophagia 48 hours after a dental extraction. Laboratory findings showed abnormalities in the white blood cell count, hemoglobin, platelet count, and C-reactive protein. CT soft tissue neck with intravenous (IV) contrast demonstrated extensive air accumulation involving the oral, retropharyngeal space, and pneumomediastinum. The patient was treated with IV maintenance fluids and ampicillin-sulbactam for broad spectrum coverage including oral flora. After 3 days, the patient was discharged with complete resolution of symptoms and reported no further complications one week later.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
A 22-year-old female came to the emergency room with left jaw pain, left cheek swelling, trouble swallowing, and pain when swallowing. These problems started 48 hours after having a tooth pulled. The patient stayed in the hospital for 3 days. The patient went home after all symptoms went away. A follow-up phone call one week later showed the patient was doing well.

Reason for Hospitalization:
Left jaw pain, left cheek swelling, trouble swallowing, and pain when swallowing 48 hours after having a tooth pulled

History of Presenting Illness:
The patient reported left jaw pain, cheek swelling, trouble swallowing, and pain when swallowing. These problems started 48 hours after having a tooth pulled. The patient received a numbing shot with medicine to reduce bleeding before the procedure. Dental records later showed that a high-powered air drill was used to break up the tooth before removing it.

Diagnostic Assessment:
Blood tests showed the white blood cell count was 6.8 thousand per microliter. The hemoglobin level was 15.2 grams per deciliter. The platelet count was 300 thousand per microliter. The C-reactive protein level was 8 milligrams per liter. A CT scan of the neck with contrast dye injected into a vein showed air had spread into the mouth area, the space behind the throat, and the chest area around the heart.

Therapeutic Assessment:
The patient received fluids through a vein. The patient also received an antibiotic called ampicillin-sulbactam through a vein. This medicine fights many types of germs found in the mouth.

Summary:
The 22-year-old female came to the emergency room with left jaw pain, left cheek swelling, trouble swallowing, and pain when swallowing 48 hours after having a tooth pulled. Blood tests showed some values outside the normal range for white blood cells, hemoglobin, platelets, and inflammation markers. A CT scan of the neck with contrast dye showed air had spread into the mouth area, behind the throat, and into the chest. The patient received fluids and antibiotics through a vein to fight mouth germs. After 3 days, the patient went home with all symptoms gone. One week later, the patient reported no further problems.

input:
[Patient Name] was discharged on [Date] following admission to the hospital with abdominal pain and vomiting. Upon admission, the patient was found to have elevated white cell counts, impaired renal profile, and elevated serum lactate levels. An abdominal X-ray revealed multiple dilated loops of small bowel, which was confirmed by a CT scan. Multiple jejunal diverticula with two pinhole perforations were identified during a laparotomy and closed primarily. Extensive abdominal washout was performed.

The patient's post-operative course was complicated by an episode of aspiration pneumonia, but she subsequently made a full recovery and was discharged in stable condition. The patient was prescribed a follow-up appointment with her primary care physician within one week and instructed to follow a low-fiber diet for two weeks and maintain bed rest for seven days. The patient was also instructed to avoid heavy lifting or strenuous activity for four to six weeks. 

Follow-up instructions, including taking oral medication, visiting specialists, and scheduling diagnostic tests, were provided to the patient upon discharge to ensure continued care and recovery. The patient was advised to follow up with her primary care physician promptly if any new symptoms or concerns arise.

[Patient Name] was sent home on [Date] after being in the hospital for belly pain and throwing up. When the patient arrived at the hospital, tests showed high levels of white blood cells, kidneys that were not working well, and high levels of a substance called lactate in the blood. An X-ray of the belly showed swollen sections of the small intestine. A CT scan confirmed this finding. During surgery, doctors found several small pouches in the upper part of the small intestine with two tiny holes. The holes were stitched closed. The doctors also cleaned out the belly area thoroughly.
After surgery, the patient developed a lung infection caused by food or liquid getting into the lungs. The patient fully recovered from this problem and went home in stable condition. The patient was given an appointment to see her regular doctor within one week. The patient was told to eat foods low in fiber for two weeks and to stay in bed for seven days. The patient was also told not to lift heavy things or do hard physical activity for four to six weeks.
The patient received instructions at discharge about taking pills by mouth, seeing specialist doctors, and scheduling future tests. These steps will help the patient continue to heal. The patient was told to see her regular doctor right away if any new symptoms or problems come up.

input:
Patient Information:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 53 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted for evaluation of an ulcerative lesion on the left side of the lower lip. Biopsies confirmed the diagnosis of well-differentiated infiltrating squamous cell carcinoma, which was staged at T2N0M0 according to TNM guidelines. The patient opted for HDRIB treatment and received five equidistant metallic needles to insert plastic catheters through the tissue. CT image was taken to plan HDRIB treatment. The patient received one fraction of 5.00 Gy on the first day of treatment and two daily fractions of 5.00 Gy for the next 4 days. 

One month post-treatment, the patient had localised acute radiation toxicity with bleeding ulcerative mucositis at the treatment site. The patient was given analgesics, anti-inflammatory drugs, and a mouthwash to treat his symptoms. At the second follow-up, a month later, the ulcer measured 1.5 × 1.5 cm with an indurated area. The LL was epithelialised and tumour-free at his third follow-up, a month later. Finally, a two-year-long evaluation was conducted, which revealed that the patient was asymptomatic and disease-free, albeit mild atrophy and skin hypopigmentation were observed as long-term side effects of the treatment. 

The patient stated that he was pleased with the results and aesthetic outcome of his procedure.
clean_ref:
Patient Information:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 53 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came to the hospital for a sore on the left side of the lower lip. Tests showed the patient had a type of skin cancer that was growing into nearby tissue. The cancer was at an early stage and had not spread to other parts of the body. The patient chose to have a type of radiation treatment. Doctors placed five metal needles with thin tubes through the tissue to deliver the treatment. A scan was taken to plan the radiation treatment. The patient received one dose of radiation on the first day. Then the patient received two doses per day for the next four days.

One month after treatment, the patient had some side effects at the treatment area. These included bleeding and a painful open sore in the mouth. The patient was given pain medicine, medicine to reduce swelling, and a mouth rinse to help with symptoms. At the second check-up one month later, the sore measured 1.5 by 1.5 cm with a firm area around it. At the third check-up one month after that, the lower lip had healed and showed no signs of cancer. After two years of check-ups, the patient had no symptoms and no cancer. However, there was some mild thinning of the skin and lighter skin color as lasting side effects from treatment.

The patient said he was happy with the results and how his lip looked after the procedure.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Age: 15
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [redacted]
Date of Discharge: [redacted]
Admitting Diagnosis: Transverse fracture of the left little finger proximal phalanx with ulnar displacement of the phalangeal head and an undisplaced fracture of the basal metaphysis of the middle phalanx

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the emergency department with a history of hyperextension injury to the left little finger while playing football. Radiographs of the hand confirmed a transverse fracture through the neck of the little finger proximal phalanx with ulnar displacement of the proximal phalangeal head and also an undisplaced fracture of the basal metaphysis of the middle phalanx. The fracture was manipulated to a satisfactory alignment under adequate analgesia and digital anaesthetic block. The injury was stabilized with neighbour strapping and a dorsal extension block splinting at 30 degrees flexion.

Post reduction radiographs revealed an improvement in the position of the head of the proximal phalanx with unchanged appearance of the middle phalangeal fracture. The PIPJ alignment was satisfactory. Clinically the injury was stable and there was no change in the neurovascular status. The patient was further followed up in the fracture clinic and reduction was assessed both clinically and radiologically with serial radiographs for 2 weeks since the injury.

At 4 weeks, dorsal extension block splinting was discontinued as clinical healing was confirmed. The patient was discharged to physiotherapy and further assessed at 8 and 12 weeks. By 12 weeks, the injury had fully healed with mild ulnar deviation at the proximal phalanx but with complete restoration of function.

Impression:

The patient presented to the emergency department with a transverse fracture of left little finger proximal phalanx with ulnar displacement of the phalangeal head and an undisplaced fracture of basal metaphysis of middle phalanx. The fracture was manipulated and stabilized with neighbour strapping and a dorsal extension block splinting at 30 degrees flexion. The patient was further followed up in the fracture clinic and reduction was assessed both clinically and radiologically. By 12 weeks, the injury had fully healed with complete restoration of function despite mild ulnar deviation at the proximal phalanx. The patient was discharged to physiotherapy.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Age: 15
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [redacted]
Date of Discharge: [redacted]
Admitting Diagnosis: A broken bone going straight across the first bone of the left pinky finger, with the bone end pushed toward the outer side of the hand, and a small crack at the base of the second bone of the same finger

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the emergency room after bending the left pinky finger too far back while playing football. Hand x-rays showed a break going straight across the neck of the first pinky bone. The bone end was pushed toward the outer side of the hand. There was also a small crack at the base of the second pinky bone that had not moved out of place. The doctor put the bone back in place after giving pain medicine and numbing the finger. The finger was taped to the next finger and held in a splint that kept it slightly bent at 30 degrees.

X-rays taken after the treatment showed the first bone was in a better position. The second bone looked the same. The middle joint of the finger lined up well. The finger was stable and blood flow and feeling were normal. The patient came back to the bone clinic for checkups. Doctors checked the finger and took x-rays over the next 2 weeks.

At 4 weeks, the splint was removed because the bone was healing well. The patient started physical therapy. Doctors checked the finger again at 8 and 12 weeks. By 12 weeks, the injury had fully healed. The first bone was slightly tilted toward the outer side of the hand, but the finger worked normally again.

Impression:

The patient came to the emergency room with a broken first bone in the left pinky finger. The bone end was pushed toward the outer side of the hand. There was also a small crack at the base of the second pinky bone. The doctor put the bone back in place, taped the finger to the next finger, and used a splint to keep it slightly bent. The patient came back to the bone clinic for checkups with exams and x-rays. By 12 weeks, the injury had fully healed. The finger worked normally again, even though the first bone was slightly tilted. The patient was sent to physical therapy.

The first bone was slightly tilted toward the outer side of the hand, but the finger worked normally again. Impression:

The patient came to the emergency room with a broken first bone in the left pinky finger. The bone end was pushed toward the outer side of the hand. There was also a small crack at the base of the second pinky bone. The doctor put the bone back in place, taped the finger to the next finger, and used a splint to keep it slightly bent. The patient came back to the bone clinic for checkups with exams and x-rays. By 12 weeks, the injury had fully healed. The finger worked normally again, even though the first bone was slightly tilted. The patient was sent to physical therapy.
