DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:
Name: Not provided
Age: 57
Sex: Male

Admission Date: Not provided
Discharge Date: Not provided

Reason for Admission:
Decreased vision in the left eye of 1-month duration

Hospital Course:
The patient was diagnosed with primary intraocular lymphoma (PIOL) based on the results of his diagnostic vitrectomy. As a PCNSL was not detected by magnetic resonance imaging, the patient was treated with weekly intravitreal injections of methotrexate (MTX) for 1 month, followed by monthly maintenance injections for seven more months.

Assessment:
The patient showed significant improvement in his best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA), from 20/25 to 20/16 after 9 months. Fundus examination showed regression of the yellowish lesions in the posterior fundus, changing gradually to atrophy of the RPE. Fundus autofluorescence (FAF) and spectral domain optical coherence tomography (SD-OCT) showed significant improvement in the degree of hyperreflective infiltration in the inner retina, restoration of the RPE and IS/OS junction, and recovery of foveal thickness. Enhanced depth imaging OCT (EDI-OCT) showed a gradual decrease in the subfoveal choroidal thickness during the treatment. The total macular volume reduced significantly after the treatment.

The mean deviation (MD) on standard automated perimetry increased from -13.95 dB before treatment to -7.23 dB after treatment. The mean retinal sensitivity within the central 10° measured with a fundus-related microperimeter increased from 0.3 dB before treatment to 4.1 dB after treatment. Full-field electroretinograms (ERGs) and multifocal ERGs (mfERGs) showed decreased amplitudes.

Final Diagnosis:
Primary intraocular lymphoma (PIOL)

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow up with his ophthalmologist regularly for monitoring of his visual acuity and ocular health. Any new or worsening symptoms should be reported to his healthcare provider immediately.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:
Name: Not provided
Age: 57
Sex: Male

Admission Date: Not provided
Discharge Date: Not provided

Reason for Admission:
The patient had decreased vision in the left eye for 1 month.

Hospital Course:
The patient was diagnosed with a type of eye cancer called primary intraocular lymphoma. Doctors found this after doing surgery to remove and test fluid from inside the eye. A brain scan showed the cancer had not spread to the brain. The patient received weekly injections of a cancer-fighting medicine called methotrexate into the eye for 1 month. After that, the patient received monthly injections for seven more months.

Assessment:
The patient's vision improved a lot over 9 months. Vision went from 20/25 to 20/16, which is better than normal. Eye exams showed the yellowish spots in the back of the eye went away. The tissue layer at the back of the eye showed some thinning where the spots used to be. Special eye imaging tests showed the cancer was going away. The inner layers of the eye healed and returned to normal thickness. The tissue layer behind the light-sensing cells also healed. The thickness of the blood vessel layer under the back of the eye got thinner during treatment. The overall size of the central seeing area got smaller after treatment. Tests that check side vision showed improvement. The patient could see more of the area around the center of vision after treatment. Tests that measure how well the back of the eye responds to light showed some reduced activity.

Final Diagnosis:
Primary intraocular lymphoma, which is a type of eye cancer

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should see his eye doctor regularly. The eye doctor will check his vision and overall eye health. The patient should tell his healthcare provider right away about any new symptoms. The patient should also report any symptoms that get worse.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [redacted] 

Medical Record Number: [redacted]

Admission Date: [redacted] 

Discharge Date: [redacted]

Diagnosis and Treatment

The patient presented to our hospital with an induration felt on the inner upper quadrant of the left breast. Further examination revealed an irregular shaped tumor in the left A region. Subsequent histopathologic examination confirmed the presence of a granular cell tumor.

The patient received prompt and appropriate treatment, and following an uneventful course of hospitalization, has been discharged with instructions to follow up with their referring physician.

Course of Hospitalization

The patient's hospitalization was uneventful with no significant complications or intercurrent illnesses. The patient remained stable throughout the hospital stay and responded well to the treatment plan. Medications were administered as prescribed, and the patient experienced no adverse reactions.

During the course of hospitalization, the patient received appropriate medical care, including necessary laboratory and diagnostic testing, and prompt specialist consultation.

Disposition

The patient is being discharged with instructions to follow up with their referring physician for further management of their condition and any necessary ongoing treatment.

The patient has been advised to continue monitoring their breast health through regular self-examination and follow-up appointments with their physician as needed.

Follow-Up and Outpatient Care

The patient has been advised to follow up with their referring physician for further management of their condition. They have been instructed to continue monitoring their breast health through regular self-examination and follow-up appointments with their physician as needed.

It is recommended that the patient be included in a breast cancer screening program and receive regular mammograms to ensure early detection of any recurrence or other breast health concerns.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Sex: Male
Age: 39
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Diagnosis: Allergy to ketamine and morphine

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our hospital with a diagnosis of chronic rhinosinusitis. He had a history of multiple anaphylactic reactions during surgical procedures and eye examinations performed under general anesthesia at various hospitals, since he was 11 years old. The patient and his relatives had been reluctant to come to the hospital due to extreme fear and discouragement for 10 years after his last episode of anaphylaxis during eye enucleation at the age of 29.

Following his evaluation by the allergist, skin testing was performed for morphine and ketamine, using standard concentrations according to the EAACI recommendation. The first test results were negative but due to his history of severe reactions, further testing was recommended 6 weeks later. The second round of skin tests was positive for ketamine and morphine. Hence, we provided a recommendation for the patient to avoid ketamine and morphine for future anesthetic procedures. The ADR card was updated accordingly, and alternative drugs were suggested, tested negative and were used for the patient's sinus surgery, which was performed without any adverse reactions.

The patient was advised to maintain close observation for allergic symptoms in the case of future anesthetic procedures with alternative drugs. The patient expressed full understanding of the recommendations and was discharged home in good condition. A comprehensive discharge report was provided to the patient and his carers.

Discharge Medications:

None recommended.

Follow-up:

The patient is advised to follow-up with the allergist as needed.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Sex: Male
Age: 39
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Diagnosis: Allergic to ketamine and morphine (two drugs used to make patients sleep during surgery)

Hospital Course:

The patient came to our hospital because of long-term sinus problems. Since the age of 11, the patient had several serious allergic reactions during surgeries and eye exams. These reactions happened when doctors gave medicine to make him sleep. The patient and his family were very scared to go to hospitals for 10 years. This fear started after his last serious allergic reaction at age 29 during eye removal surgery.

An allergy doctor checked the patient. The doctor did skin tests for morphine and ketamine using standard amounts. The first tests showed no allergy. But because the patient had such bad reactions before, doctors did more tests 6 weeks later. The second round of skin tests showed the patient is allergic to ketamine and morphine. We told the patient to never use ketamine or morphine for future surgeries. We updated the patient's drug allergy card. We tested other drugs that could be used instead. Those drugs did not cause a reaction. Doctors used these safe drugs for the patient's sinus surgery. The surgery went well with no allergic reactions.

The patient was told to watch carefully for allergy signs if given other sleep medicines in the future. The patient understood all the instructions. The patient went home feeling well. We gave the patient and his caregivers a full report about this hospital stay.

Discharge Medications:

None recommended.

Follow-up:

The patient should see the allergy doctor if needed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Patient ID: [ID]
Date of Admission: [Date of admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of discharge]

Admitting Diagnosis: HTLV-I associated myelopathy (HAM) following renal transplantation

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 65-year-old man with a history of diabetic nephropathy, underwent renal transplantation using his HTLV-I seropositive wife as the donor. Eight months after the transplant, he presented with weakness of his legs, which progressed rapidly, leaving him wheelchair-bound 12 days later. Despite receiving pulsed steroid therapy, he did not regain his leg muscle strength, and was transferred to our hospital.

At our facility, the patient underwent neurological examinations which showed flaccid paraparesis of his legs with diminished deep tendon reflexes. Nerve conduction studies did not reveal any evidence of peripheral neuropathy. His serum anti-HTLV-I antibody titer was elevated, and the CSF protein content was elevated with an increased cell count. Western blotting identified HTLV-I antibodies in his spinal cord fluid.

MRI scans of the thoracic spinal cord displayed T2 high signal intensities with slight swelling. A diagnosis of HAM was made based on these findings. The patient was started on interferon-α treatment, but showed little improvement. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) therapy was added along with interferon-α, following which the patient showed improvement and was able to move his legs more easily. MRI examinations showed spinal cord atrophy. His OMSD improved during his inpatient stay.

Discharge Condition:

The patient was discharged in stable condition with recommendation for continued outpatient follow-up by his primary care physician and neurologist. Instructions were given to continue with interferon-α and IVIg therapy at his follow-up appointment. It was recommended that the patient continue to participate in physical therapy and rehabilitation programs to improve his mobility.

Summary:

A 65-year-old man with a history of diabetic nephropathy and renal transplantation, presented with flaccid paraparesis of the legs. A diagnosis of HTLV-I associated myelopathy (HAM) was made. The patient was treated with interferon-α and IVIg therapy, and showed improvement. The patient was discharged in stable condition with recommendation for continued outpatient follow-up.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Patient ID: [ID]
Date of Admission: [Date of admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of discharge]

Admitting Diagnosis: A spinal cord disease called HAM caused by a virus (HTLV-I) that developed after a kidney transplant

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 65-year-old man. He had kidney disease caused by diabetes. He received a kidney transplant from his wife. His wife carried a virus called HTLV-I in her blood. Eight months after the transplant, the patient's legs became weak. The weakness got worse very quickly. Within 12 days, he needed a wheelchair to move around. He was given high doses of steroids, but his leg strength did not come back. He was then moved to our hospital.

At our hospital, doctors checked his nervous system. His legs were weak and floppy. His reflex responses were lower than normal. Tests on his nerves showed they were working fine. Blood tests showed he had the HTLV-I virus. Fluid from his spine had too much protein and too many cells. Tests confirmed the virus was in his spinal fluid.

Scans of his middle back showed signs of swelling and damage in his spinal cord. Based on all these findings, doctors diagnosed him with HAM. This is a spinal cord disease caused by the HTLV-I virus. The patient started treatment with a medicine called interferon-alpha. This did not help much. Doctors then added another treatment that uses antibodies from donated blood. This is called IVIg. After getting both treatments together, the patient got better. He was able to move his legs more easily. Later scans showed his spinal cord had gotten smaller. His ability to move improved during his hospital stay.

Discharge Condition:

The patient left the hospital in stable condition. He should keep seeing his regular doctor and brain and nerve specialist. He should keep taking interferon-alpha and IVIg at his follow-up visits. He should also keep going to physical therapy to help him move better.

Summary:

A 65-year-old man had kidney disease from diabetes and received a kidney transplant. He developed weak and floppy legs. Doctors found he had a spinal cord disease called HAM caused by the HTLV-I virus. He was treated with interferon-alpha and IVIg. He got better and left the hospital in stable condition. He will continue to see his doctors for follow-up care.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient presented with sudden onset abdominal pain accompanied by nausea, and tenderness in the right iliac fossa. Blood tests revealed raised inflammatory markers. An emergency diagnostic laparoscopy was performed which was converted to open surgery due to the identification of a caecal mass causing ileocoecal intussusception. A radical right hemicolectomy was performed, and the patient made a good postoperative recovery.

Diagnosis:

Histopathology revealed an intussusception of caecal pole and ileum into caecum/colon with a head containing a well-differentiated grade 1 endometrioid adenocarcinoma arising in a focus of endometriosis centered in the muscularis propria of the intussuscepted caecum.

Treatment:

A radical right hemicolectomy was performed, and no residual disease or any evidence of metastasis was identified in the staging CT scan performed postoperatively.

Follow-up:

The patient is advised to follow-up with the oncology department for further surveillance and management.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with sudden belly pain, an upset stomach, and soreness on the lower right side. Blood tests showed signs of infection or swelling in the body. Doctors first used a small camera through a tiny cut to look inside the belly. They had to switch to a larger cut when they found a growth in the large intestine. This growth was causing part of the intestine to fold into itself. Doctors removed the right side of the large intestine. The patient healed well after surgery.

Diagnosis:

Lab tests on the removed tissue showed that part of the intestine had folded into itself. The cause was a slow-growing cancer in the intestine wall. This cancer started from tissue that normally lines the womb but was growing in the wrong place.

Treatment:

Doctors removed the right side of the large intestine. A full body scan done after surgery showed no signs that the cancer had spread. There was no disease left behind.

Follow-up:

The patient should see the cancer care team for regular check-ups and any future care that may be needed.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia

Discharge Diagnosis: Aggressive diffuse PTCL

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with symptoms of fever, nonproductive cough, shortness of breath, and audible wheezing. The initial diagnosis was community-acquired pneumonia, which was treated with IV antibiotics, oral steroids, and nebulized bronchodilators. Despite treatment, the patient's shortness of breath progressed and she was readmitted to the hospital. Workup revealed eosinophilia, and a biopsy confirmed a diagnosis of eosinophilic pneumonitis. The patient was discharged home with oral steroids and was counseled on the possibility of the black mold in her home causing her symptoms. However, she was readmitted to the hospital 2 weeks later with profound shortness of breath and hypoxia. Imaging studies showed worsening lung infiltrates and liver lesions. Despite continued treatment with antibiotics, steroids, and ventilator support, the patient eventually died before a liver biopsy could be performed. The autopsy revealed diffuse eosinophils in all organs, and immunohistochemistry staining showed positive staining for T-cell markers, which confirmed the diagnosis of aggressive diffuse PTCL.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted with symptoms of pneumonia and was treated with IV antibiotics, oral steroids, and nebulized bronchodilators. Following discharge, she was readmitted with worsening symptoms of eosinophilic pneumonitis. Despite treatment, the patient's condition deteriorated and she eventually developed aggressive diffuse PTCL. Unfortunately, the patient passed away before further testing could be performed.

Discharge Disposition:
Deceased. The patient passed away during hospitalization.

Follow-up Care:
N/A

Condition at Discharge:
Deceased.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Lung infection caught outside the hospital

Discharge Diagnosis: A fast-spreading type of blood cancer called peripheral T-cell lymphoma

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with fever, dry cough, trouble breathing, and wheezing sounds. Doctors first thought the patient had a lung infection caught outside the hospital. The patient received antibiotics through a vein, steroid pills, and breathing treatments through a mist machine. The treatment did not work well, and the patient's breathing got worse. The patient came back to the hospital. Tests showed too many white blood cells called eosinophils in the blood. A tissue sample showed the lungs were swollen due to these cells. The patient went home with steroid pills. Doctors told the patient that black mold at home might be causing the symptoms. Two weeks later, the patient came back to the hospital with very bad breathing problems and low oxygen levels. Scans showed the lungs looked worse and there were spots on the liver. Doctors kept giving antibiotics, steroids, and breathing machine support. Sadly, the patient died before doctors could take a liver tissue sample. After death, an exam of the body found too many eosinophil cells in all organs. Special lab tests showed signs of T-cell cancer. This confirmed the patient had a fast-spreading type of blood cancer.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came in with signs of a lung infection and received antibiotics through a vein, steroid pills, and breathing treatments. After going home, the patient came back with worse symptoms from lung swelling caused by too many white blood cells. The patient's health kept getting worse. Doctors later found a fast-spreading blood cancer. Sadly, the patient died before more tests could be done.

Discharge Disposition:
Deceased. The patient died while in the hospital.

Follow-up Care:
Not applicable.

Condition at Discharge:
Deceased.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]

Gender: Male

Age: 40 years

Date of Admission: [redacted]

Date of Discharge: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: Painless swelling over the left side of the face

Discharge Diagnosis: Schwannoma, most likely arising from the terminal branch of the infra-orbital nerve

Hospital Course Summary: 

The patient was admitted with the chief complaint of painless swelling over the left side of the face. On examination, a localized, nontender, firm, round swelling of around 4 cm was evident over the left side of the mid-face, extending from below the infra-orbital margin to the upper lip. The overlying skin appeared stretched and tense. The skin was pinch-able over the lesion, except at the center of the lesion. The naso-labial fold was obliterated. Intra-oral examination showed no swelling over the anterior maxilla. The maxillary anterior teeth were firm and nontender on percussion. There was no proptosis, and vision was normal. The cervical lymph nodes were not palpable. 

Ultrasonography was advised to study the size and extent of the lesion. The ultrasonogram showed a well-defined, heterogeneous oval mass of 45mm x 30mm x 23mm within the buccal sub-cutaneous tissue with no orbital, maxillary sinus, or underlying bone involvement. 

Aspiration biopsy of the lesion showed spindle-shaped cells predominantly arranged in Antoni A pattern around verocay bodies, with less organized Antoni B tissue in a few places. A diagnosis of schwannoma, probably arising from the terminal branch of the infra-orbital nerve, was established.

Surgical excision of the lesion was planned and executed under local anesthesia. After attaining adequate anesthesia, a linear incision of about 3 cm was placed over the skin along the natural skin crease of naso-labial fold. The lesion was well-encapsulated and freed from surrounding tissue by blunt dissection. The portion of skin overlying the center of the lesion appeared thinned and was included in the excised nodule. At the time of exploration, the lesion was found to emanate from the nerve trunk of the peripheral branch of the infra-orbital nerve, which was dissected and preserved. Histopathological examination of the excised lesion confirmed diagnosis of schwannoma.

The patient has had an uneventful recovery post-surgery and is advised to follow up with their primary care physician for routine check-ups.

Condition at Discharge: Stable

Discharge Medications: Please refer to the medication summary provided to the patient

Discharge Instructions: The patient is advised to rest and avoid any strenuous activities. They are encouraged to follow up with their primary care physician for routine check-ups as advised. 

Follow-up / Appointments: Follow-up appointments are to be scheduled with the primary care physician.

Primary Care Physician: [redacted]

Patient Name: [redacted]

Gender: Male

Age: 40 years

Date of Admission: [redacted]

Date of Discharge: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: Painless swelling on the left side of the face

Discharge Diagnosis: Schwannoma, a non-cancerous tumor growing from a nerve below the eye

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient came to the hospital with a painless swelling on the left side of the face. During the exam, doctors found a firm, round lump about 4 cm wide. It was on the left side of the mid-face, from below the eye down to the upper lip. The lump was not painful to touch. The skin over it looked stretched and tight. The skin could be pinched over most of the lump, but not at the center. The normal fold between the nose and lip was flattened. A look inside the mouth showed no swelling in the upper jaw area. The upper front teeth were firm and not painful when tapped. The eye was not bulging, and vision was normal. No swollen glands were felt in the neck.

An ultrasound test was done to see the size and shape of the lump. The ultrasound showed an oval mass measuring 45mm by 30mm by 23mm in the cheek tissue. The lump had not spread to the eye socket, sinus, or bone.

A tissue sample was taken from the lump using a needle. The cells showed a pattern typical of a schwannoma. This is a non-cancerous tumor that grows from the covering of a nerve. It was likely growing from a small nerve branch below the eye.

Surgery to remove the lump was planned and done using numbing medicine. After the area was numb, a cut about 3 cm long was made along the natural skin fold between the nose and lip. The lump had a clear outer covering and was gently separated from the tissue around it. The thin skin over the center of the lump was removed along with it. During surgery, doctors found the tumor was growing from a small nerve branch below the eye. This nerve was carefully saved. Lab tests on the removed lump confirmed it was a schwannoma.

The patient healed well after surgery with no problems. The patient should see their regular doctor for check-ups.

Condition at Discharge: Stable

Discharge Medications: Please refer to the medication list given to the patient

Discharge Instructions: The patient should rest and avoid hard physical activity. The patient should see their regular doctor for check-ups as advised.

Follow-up and Appointments: The patient should schedule follow-up visits with their regular doctor.

Primary Care Physician: [redacted]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Age: 24 years
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: [Date of Admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

Diagnosis:
Transverse fracture of the patella with a lytic lesion, suggestive of chondroblastoma.

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 24-year-old male, was admitted with complaints of inability to walk after a trivial fall. He had pain and noticed mild swelling in the right knee for the past one year. On clinical examination, the patient had tense swelling and tenderness over the right patella, with painful movements and straight leg raising test not possible. X-ray showed a transverse fracture of the patella with a lytic lesion occupying most of the two halves of the patella. 

After considering clinicoradiological differential diagnoses including solitary bone cysts, aneurysmal bone cyst, giant cell tumor, chondroblastoma, a fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) was performed, which revealed a few osteoclastic giant cells and occasional osteoblasts against a hemorrhagic background. Subsequently, patellectomy was performed, and histopathology was suggestive of chondroblastoma. 

The patient had an uneventful recovery and was discharged with a full range of movements after two years.

Follow-up:
The patient will be advised to follow up with regular orthopedic consultations, with specific emphasis on keeping the surgical site clean and free of infection. 

Keywords:
Transverse fracture, patella, lytic lesion, chondroblastoma, patellectomy.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Age: 24 years
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: [Date of Admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

Diagnosis:
A break across the kneecap with a bone growth that appears to be a rare, non-cancerous tumor called chondroblastoma.

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 24-year-old male, came to the hospital because he could not walk after a minor fall. He had pain and some swelling in his right knee for the past year. When the doctor checked his knee, there was tight swelling and tenderness over the right kneecap. Moving the knee was painful, and the patient could not lift his leg while lying down. An X-ray showed a break across the kneecap with an area of bone damage taking up most of both pieces of the kneecap.

The doctors thought of several possible causes, including different types of bone cysts and tumors. They did a test using a thin needle to take a small sample of cells from the area. The sample showed some large cells that break down bone and some cells that build bone, with blood in the background. After this, the doctors did surgery to remove the entire kneecap. The tissue was studied and showed a rare, non-cancerous bone tumor called chondroblastoma.

The patient healed well without any problems. He was sent home after two years with full ability to move his knee.

Follow-up:
The patient should see a bone and joint doctor for regular check-ups. It is important to keep the surgery area clean to prevent infection.

Keywords:
Break across kneecap, kneecap, bone damage area, chondroblastoma, kneecap removal surgery.

Discharge Summary: 

Patient Information: 
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 73
Gender: Female
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]

Diagnosis: 
The patient was admitted to the hospital to undergo coronary angiography for her deteriorating angina symptoms.

Hospital Course: 
The patient, who has a history of coronary artery disease with previous PCI of LAD and recanalization of LCx chronic total occlusion with proximal stent implantation, underwent coronary angiography at the hospital. During the procedure, a subtotal occlusion of the LCx distally to the previously implanted stent was discovered, and a decision was made to revascularize the LCx with a drug-eluting stent implantation using a radial approach and a guiding catheter. The procedure encountered difficulties, and a buddy balloon catheter technique was eventually employed to deliver the stent successfully. After the procedure, the patient was monitored and received further treatment.

Discharge Condition: 
The patient's condition improved following the procedure, and she was discharged in a stable condition with instructions to continue taking her regular medication and to follow up with her cardiologist regularly.

Recommendation:
The patient is advised to regularly follow up with her cardiologist to monitor her cardiac health and manage her angina symptoms to avoid future complications.

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 73
Gender: Female
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]

Diagnosis:
The patient came to the hospital for a heart test. The patient was having worsening chest pain caused by reduced blood flow to the heart.

Hospital Course:
The patient has a history of blocked heart arteries. The patient had two procedures in the past to open blocked arteries using small tubes and metal mesh supports called stents. During this hospital visit, doctors performed a test to look at the heart arteries using a special dye and X-rays. The test showed that one artery was almost completely blocked again near the old stent. Doctors decided to open the blocked artery and place a new medicated stent. They went in through the wrist using a thin tube. The procedure was difficult. Doctors used a special technique with a small balloon to help place the stent. The stent was placed successfully. After the procedure, the patient was watched closely and given more treatment.

Discharge Condition:
The patient got better after the procedure. The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient was told to keep taking regular medicines. The patient was told to see the heart doctor for regular checkups.

Recommendation:
The patient should see the heart doctor regularly. This will help check on heart health and control chest pain. Regular visits will help prevent future problems.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

Patient Information:
The patient is a 48-year-old male who was admitted for treatment of renal cell carcinoma (RCC) with synchronous metastases to the liver. The patient was initially started on combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab and nivolumab, with Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT) to the primary tumor.

Medical History:
The patient's medical history was unremarkable except for a diagnosis of RCC.

Hospital Course:
During hospitalization, the patient experienced an elevation in creatinine (Cr) levels and decreased estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), which were managed with prednisone and mycophenolate. The patient had gradual improvement in kidney function over a period of six weeks. Imaging at that time showed a remarkable response, with shrinkage of all lesions. The patient ultimately elected to undergo consolidative nephrectomy.

Discharge Plan:
Discharge instructions were given to the patient, and he was advised to follow up with his urologist regularly. The patient is doing well without any evidence of disease. No further therapy is planned at this time.

Summary:
The patient was admitted for the treatment of RCC with synchronous liver metastases. The patient was treated with a combination of ipilimumab and nivolumab, with SBRT to the primary tumor. The patient developed a kidney dysfunction, which was managed with prednisone and mycophenolate. Imaging showed a remarkable response, and the patient underwent consolidative nephrectomy. The patient is doing well without any evidence of disease, and no further therapy is planned at this time.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

Patient Information:
The patient is a 48-year-old male. He was admitted for treatment of kidney cancer that had spread to the liver. The patient was started on two medicines that help the immune system fight cancer, called ipilimumab and nivolumab. The patient also received targeted radiation treatment to the main tumor.

Medical History:
The patient had no other health problems before being diagnosed with kidney cancer.

Hospital Course:
During the hospital stay, the patient had signs that the kidneys were not working as well as they should. Blood tests showed higher waste levels and lower filtering ability. This was treated with two medicines called prednisone and mycophenolate. The patient's kidney function slowly got better over six weeks. Scans at that time showed very good results. All the tumors had gotten smaller. The patient chose to have surgery to remove the affected kidney.

Discharge Plan:
The patient received instructions before leaving the hospital. He was told to schedule regular visits with his kidney and bladder doctor. The patient is doing well with no signs of cancer. No more treatment is needed at this time.

Summary:
The patient was admitted for treatment of kidney cancer that had spread to the liver. The patient received two immune system medicines, ipilimumab and nivolumab, along with targeted radiation to the main tumor. The patient developed kidney problems, which were treated with prednisone and mycophenolate. Scans showed the tumors responded very well to treatment. The patient had surgery to remove the affected kidney. The patient is doing well with no signs of cancer. No more treatment is needed at this time.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]

Patient Name: [name]
Age: 68 years
Sex: Male

Medical History:
- Prostate cancer treated with radiation therapy, 3 years ago
- Endoscopic polypectomy for colonic polyps, 1 year ago
- Chronic constipation

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with sudden-onset upper abdominal pain and was found to have a fist-sized mass lesion located between the stomach and pancreas on computed tomography (CT) without contrast. He was initially diagnosed with a malignant tumor with peritonitis carcinomatosa and hospitalized for further examinations and observation. However, the next day, he was found to have intra-abdominal hemorrhage and was transferred to our hospital, a tertiary emergency center, for further diagnosis and treatments.

On arrival at our hospital, the patient was alert and well oriented. A physical examination revealed moderate tenderness in his left upper abdomen with a peritoneal sign. His laboratory data showed mild leukocytosis, moderate anemia, elevated urea nitrogen level, and high serum amylase. The results of a CT with contrast demonstrated an unenhanced, high-density, homogeneous, mass-like lesion located to the ventral side of the pancreatic tail, and a large amount of ascites, which suggested a hematoma and massive intraabdominal hemorrhage. 

An emergency exploratory laparotomy was performed and a 5 cm laceration was found in the mesentery of transverse colon, which arose from the left margin of Treitz's ligament extending in the left direction. Hemostasis was achieved by suture ligations and absorbable hemostat materials were applied on the oozing points. Intraoperative blood transfusion was performed, and the operation was uncomplicated except for temporary paralytic ileus. The patient was discharged in a good postoperative condition on the postoperative day 20.

During the patient's hospital stay, we discovered that he had a history of intractable constipation and had a habit of massaging his abdomen to facilitate defecation, which likely caused the laceration. We advised the patient to avoid massaging his abdomen in the future and to seek medical attention if he experiences any abdominal symptoms.

In summary, the patient with a medical history of prostate cancer, endoscopic polypectomy, and chronic constipation presented with sudden-onset upper abdominal pain and was found to have intra-abdominal hemorrhage due to a mesenteric laceration caused by self-massage for defecation. An emergency exploratory laparotomy was performed to achieve hemostasis, and the operation was uncomplicated except for temporary paralytic ileus. The patient was discharged on the postoperative day 20 with instructions to avoid abdominal self-massage and seek medical attention for abdominal symptoms in the future.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]

Patient Name: [name]
Age: 68 years
Sex: Male

Medical History:
Prostate cancer treated with radiation therapy, 3 years ago
Removal of growths from the large intestine using a small camera tube, 1 year ago
Long-term difficulty having bowel movements

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with sudden pain in the upper belly. A scan showed a large lump between the stomach and pancreas. Doctors first thought it might be cancer that had spread, causing swelling in the belly lining. The patient was admitted for more tests and watching. The next day, doctors found bleeding inside the belly. The patient was moved to our hospital for emergency care.

When the patient arrived at our hospital, he was awake and thinking clearly. A physical exam showed pain in the left upper belly when pressed. The belly lining also showed signs of irritation. Blood tests showed a slightly high white blood cell count and low red blood cell count. Tests also showed high levels of a waste product in the blood and a digestive enzyme. A scan with dye showed a solid lump near the tail of the pancreas. There was also a large amount of fluid in the belly. This pointed to a blood clot and heavy bleeding inside the belly.

The patient needed emergency surgery to open and check the belly. Doctors found a 2-inch tear in the tissue that holds the large intestine in place. This tear started near where the small intestine meets the large intestine and went to the left. Doctors stopped the bleeding with stitches and special materials that help blood clot. The patient received blood during surgery. The surgery went well, but the bowels stopped moving for a short time afterward. The patient left the hospital in good health 20 days after surgery.

While in the hospital, doctors learned that the patient had ongoing trouble having bowel movements. The patient had been pressing on his belly to help pass stool. This likely caused the tear. Doctors told the patient to stop pressing on his belly. They also said to get medical help right away if he has any belly problems.

In summary, the patient has a history of prostate cancer, removal of growths from the large intestine, and long-term trouble having bowel movements. He came in with sudden upper belly pain. Doctors found bleeding inside the belly from a tear caused by pressing on his belly to help pass stool. The patient had emergency surgery to stop the bleeding. The surgery went well, but the bowels stopped moving for a short time afterward. The patient left the hospital 20 days after surgery. He was told not to press on his belly and to get medical help for any belly problems in the future.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of admission: [Redacted]
Date of discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the fast-track ENT clinic with persistent left-sided throat discomfort and pain. The patient reported positive reflux symptoms with heartburn. Medical management was implemented, but it yielded no symptomatic relief. The patient underwent flexible nasendoscopy and a contrast-enhanced MRI scan, which revealed post-cricoid oedema consistent with laryngopharyngeal reflux. As the symptoms continued to persist, a transnasal oesophagoscopy was performed, and a proximal oesophageal lesion was identified. Multiple punch biopsies were taken.

The histological examination of the specimen revealed squamous cell papilloma, which tested positive for human papilloma virus (HPV). Oesophageal manometry and 24-hour oesophageal pH monitoring indicated borderline pathological reflux with some proximal penetration. The patient then underwent complete endoscopic excision of the lesion. Histology confirmed HPV-positive squamous papilloma.

The patient's symptoms resolved after the excision, and the patient received continued anti-reflux treatment, including lifestyle changes. A yearly follow-up confirmed complete remission of symptoms.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with squamous cell papilloma of the oesophagus, which tested positive for human papilloma virus (HPV).

Procedures:
Transnasal oesophagoscopy was performed, and a proximal oesophageal lesion was identified. Multiple punch biopsies were taken. Complete endoscopic excision of the lesion was performed.

Follow-Up:
A yearly follow-up confirmed complete remission of symptoms. The patient received continued anti-reflux treatment, including lifestyle changes.

Summary:
The patient presented with persistent left-sided throat discomfort with positive reflux symptoms and was diagnosed with squamous cell papilloma of the oesophagus, which was HPV-positive. The patient underwent transnasal oesophagoscopy and biopsy, followed by complete endoscopic excision of the lesion. Anti-reflux treatment was continued with lifestyle changes, leading to complete remission of symptoms. A yearly follow-up confirmed the remission of symptoms.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of admission: [Redacted]
Date of discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the ear, nose, and throat clinic with ongoing pain and discomfort on the left side of the throat. The patient had acid reflux symptoms, including heartburn. Medicine was given, but it did not help the symptoms. The patient had a thin camera placed through the nose to look at the throat. The patient also had a special scan with dye to see inside the body. These tests showed swelling below the voice box caused by stomach acid coming up. Because the symptoms did not go away, a thin camera was passed through the nose into the food pipe. A growth was found in the upper part of the food pipe. Small tissue samples were taken for testing.

The lab tests showed the growth was a non-cancerous wart-like bump. It tested positive for human papilloma virus, also called HPV. More tests checked how well the food pipe muscles worked and measured acid levels over 24 hours. These tests showed mild acid reflux that sometimes reached the upper food pipe. The patient then had a procedure to fully remove the growth using a camera and tools. Lab tests confirmed it was an HPV-positive non-cancerous wart-like bump.

The patient's symptoms went away after the growth was removed. The patient continued treatment for acid reflux, including changes to daily habits. A check-up one year later showed the symptoms were completely gone.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a non-cancerous wart-like growth in the food pipe. It tested positive for human papilloma virus, also called HPV.

Procedures:
A thin camera was passed through the nose into the food pipe, and a growth was found in the upper part. Small tissue samples were taken for testing. The growth was fully removed using a camera and tools.

Follow-Up:
A check-up one year later showed the symptoms were completely gone. The patient continued treatment for acid reflux, including changes to daily habits.

Summary:
The patient came in with ongoing pain on the left side of the throat and acid reflux symptoms. The patient was diagnosed with a non-cancerous wart-like growth in the food pipe that tested positive for HPV. A thin camera was used to find the growth and take tissue samples. The growth was then fully removed. Treatment for acid reflux continued along with changes to daily habits. This led to the symptoms going away completely. A check-up one year later showed the symptoms were still gone.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Medical Record Number: [redacted]

Admission Date: [redacted] Discharge Date: [redacted]

Diagnoses:
1. Sarcoidosis
2. Pseudopapillary tumor (Frantz tumor) of the pancreas

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with complaints of right flank pain, low-grade fever, nausea, anorexia, and unintentional weight loss. Upon physical examination, she was found to have tenderness to palpation in the right upper quadrant and epigastric regions. Ultrasound of the abdomen showed a well-defined complex solid-cystic mass in the neck of the pancreas. Additionally, a routine chest X-ray revealed suspicious mediastinal lymphadenopathy. CT scans of the chest and abdomen were subsequently sought that revealed mediastinal lymphadenopathy, bilateral pleural effusions, and a 4.5 x 5.5 cm mass in the neck of the pancreas with mixed solid and cystic features.

Further investigations revealed multiple non-caseating granulomas, negative flow cytometry and special stains for microorganisms, and elevated angiotensin-converting enzyme level. These findings eventually led to the diagnosis of sarcoidosis in this patient. A CT-guided biopsy of the pancreatic mass revealed polygonal epithelial cells with characteristic pseudopapillary changes, leading to the diagnosis of pseudopapillary tumor (Frantz tumor) of the pancreas. Special stains including vimentin and beta-catenin were positive, thus confirming our diagnosis.

The patient underwent a Whipple pancreaticoduodenectomy for her pancreatic mass and she recovered well post-operatively. On discharge, she was in a stable hemodynamic condition.

Instructions upon Discharge:
1. Continue taking prescribed medications as directed by your healthcare provider.
2. Follow-up with your healthcare provider for routine monitoring and management of your medical condition.
3. If you experience any symptoms or concerns, contact your healthcare provider immediately.

Discharge Medications:
[No medications listed in the case report.]

Follow-up:
[No follow-up information provided in the case report.]

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Medical Record Number: [redacted]

Admission Date: [redacted] Discharge Date: [redacted]

Diagnoses:
1. Sarcoidosis (a disease that causes small clumps of swelling in the body's organs)
2. A rare tumor in the pancreas called a Frantz tumor

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came to the hospital with pain in the right side of the back, mild fever, upset stomach, loss of appetite, and weight loss without trying. During the exam, the patient had pain when the doctor pressed on the upper right belly area. An ultrasound of the belly showed a growth in the pancreas that had both solid and fluid-filled parts. A chest X-ray also showed swollen lymph nodes in the chest area. CT scans of the chest and belly were then done. These scans showed swollen lymph nodes in the chest, fluid around both lungs, and a growth in the pancreas about 2 inches in size.

More tests were done. These tests found small clusters of swelling in the tissue. Tests for cancer cells and germs came back negative. A blood test showed high levels of a certain enzyme. All of these findings led to a diagnosis of sarcoidosis. A needle was used to take a small sample from the pancreas growth. The sample showed this was a rare type of tumor called a Frantz tumor. More lab tests confirmed this diagnosis.

The patient had surgery to remove the tumor and parts of the pancreas and small intestine. The patient healed well after surgery. When leaving the hospital, the patient was in stable condition.

Instructions upon Discharge:
1. Keep taking all medicines as told by the doctor.
2. See the doctor for regular checkups to watch and manage the medical conditions.
3. Call the doctor right away if there are any new symptoms or concerns.

Discharge Medications:
[No medications listed in the case report.]

Follow-up:
[No follow-up information provided in the case report.]

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age/Sex: 23-year-old male
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with a 2-year history of progressive left greater than the right-hand weakness/myelopathy. On physical examination, left greater than the right-sided atrophy of the thenar/hypothenar eminences, and interosseous muscles were observed. Motor nerve conduction/electromyographic studies showed left greater than the right-sided C7-T1 denervation, while sensory conduction studies were normal.

The patient underwent both neutral and flexion MRI scans, revealing diffuse lower cervical spinal cord swelling most severely at C5-C6. A cervical laminectomy with durotomy was performed to obtain an intramedullary cord biopsy to exclude an intrinsic cord tumor. The biopsy showed focal cord ischemia consistent with intrinsic HD.

A C5-C6 ACDF was performed for anterior decompression. Postoperative cervical MRI showed a reduction of the ischemic C5-C6 cord focus, and the patient exhibited no clinical changes. Follow-up MRI scans over the next two years showed a mild progressive focal atrophy of the cord at the C5-C6 level with selective involvement of the anterior horns.

Discharge Diagnosis:
1. Intrinsic HD with cord ischemia.

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Follow-up:
The patient is advised to follow up with the treating neurologist for regular check-ups and further management of his medical condition. Further scans assessing the disease progression may also be ordered. 

Discharge Condition:
The patient's condition has not improved, but the hospitalization resulted in the successful exclusion of an intrinsic cord tumor and confirmation of the diagnosis of Intrinsic HD. The patient is now able to continue his treatment under the guidance of his neurologist as an outpatient.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age/Sex: 23-year-old male
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with weakness in both hands that had been getting worse for 2 years. The left hand was weaker than the right hand. The doctor found that muscles in the palms and between the fingers had shrunk, more on the left side. Tests of the nerves and muscles showed nerve damage in the lower neck area on both sides, worse on the left. Tests that check feeling in the hands were normal.

The patient had special scans of the neck in both a straight position and a bent position. These scans showed swelling of the spinal cord in the lower neck, worst at the C5-C6 level. The patient had surgery to open the back of the neck and take a small tissue sample from inside the spinal cord. This was done to make sure there was no tumor. The tissue sample showed reduced blood flow to part of the spinal cord. This matched a condition called Hirayama Disease.

The patient then had surgery from the front of the neck to take pressure off the spinal cord at the C5-C6 level. A scan after surgery showed the damaged area in the spinal cord had gotten smaller. The patient did not have any new problems after surgery. Scans done over the next two years showed the spinal cord slowly getting thinner at C5-C6. The front part of the spinal cord was most affected.

Discharge Diagnosis:
1. Hirayama Disease with reduced blood flow to the spinal cord.

Discharge Medications:
None

Follow-up:
The patient should see his nerve specialist for regular check-ups and ongoing care of his condition. More scans may be ordered to check if the disease is getting worse.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's condition has not improved. However, the hospital stay helped rule out a spinal cord tumor. It also confirmed that the patient has Hirayama Disease. The patient can now continue treatment with his nerve specialist without needing to stay in the hospital.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Admitting Diagnosis: Hypocalcemia and generalized tonic-clonic seizure

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the hospital with a new-onset generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Physical examination revealed short stature, hypertelorism, hooded upper eyelids, prominent forehead, hypernasal speech, and fullness of the nose over the bridge. Laboratory tests showed marked hypocalcemia, with further work-up revealing hypoparathyroidism. Genetic testing was performed due to the presence of subtle dysmorphic facial features, revealing a deletion of the DiGeorge/velocardiofacial syndrome critical region (TUPLE1) at 22q11.2 locus. The patient received treatment with calcium citrate and calcitriol and remained asymptomatic with normalization of serum calcium levels. Echocardiogram, abdominal ultrasound, and electroencephalogram were normal, and a computer tomography of the brain showed basal ganglia calcification.

Diagnoses:
1. Hypocalcemia
2. Generalized tonic-clonic seizure
3. Hypoparathyroidism
4. Chromosome 22q11.2 deletion syndrome

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with a new-onset generalized tonic-clonic seizure. The etiology of the seizure was attributed to marked hypocalcemia, with further work-up revealing hypoparathyroidism and a deletion of the DiGeorge/velocardiofacial syndrome critical region (TUPLE1) at 22q11.2 locus. Treatment was administered with calcium citrate and calcitriol, resulting in normalization of serum calcium levels. Echocardiogram, abdominal ultrasound, and electroencephalogram were normal, and a computer tomography of the brain showed basal ganglia calcification. The patient is being discharged in stable condition with close follow-up recommended.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Admitting Diagnosis: Low calcium levels in the blood and a full-body seizure with shaking and stiffness

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with a new full-body seizure with shaking and stiffness. A physical exam showed the patient had short height, wide-set eyes, droopy upper eyelids, a large forehead, a nasal-sounding voice, and a wide nose bridge. Blood tests showed very low calcium levels. More tests found that the parathyroid glands were not working well. The patient also had some unusual facial features. Because of this, doctors did genetic testing. The tests found a missing piece of genetic material linked to DiGeorge syndrome. The patient was given calcium citrate and calcitriol to raise calcium levels. After treatment, the patient had no more symptoms and calcium levels returned to normal. A heart ultrasound, belly ultrasound, and brain wave test were all normal. A brain scan showed calcium buildup in a deep part of the brain called the basal ganglia.

Diagnoses:
1. Low calcium levels in the blood
2. Full-body seizure with shaking and stiffness
3. Parathyroid glands not working properly
4. DiGeorge syndrome caused by a missing piece of chromosome 22

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came to the hospital with a new full-body seizure with shaking and stiffness. The seizure happened because of very low calcium levels in the blood. A brain scan showed calcium buildup in a deep part of the brain. The patient is leaving the hospital in stable condition. Close follow-up visits with doctors are recommended.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: N/A (anonymous)
Gender: Male
Age: 23
Medical Record Number: N/A

Hospital Course:
The patient was referred to the Endocrinology Department for follow-up of an abdominal PGL that had been operated during childhood and subsequently relapsed. He had undergone two surgical interventions that were unsuccessful in completely removing the tumor mass. The patient had a 123I-MIBG scintigraphy which was negative and a 68GaDOTATATE-PET that revealed the tumor to express somatostatin receptors. Due to this, he underwent therapy with somatostatin analogs and a radiometabolic treatment with 90Y-DOTATATE. At the time of referral to our Endocrinology Department, the patient had local relapse of the disease, associated with multiple bone metastases. The biochemical evaluation showed high chromogranin-A levels, urinary catecholamines, and urinary vanilmandelic acid. Radiological assessment revealed evidence of progressive abdominal disease. Therapy with somatostatin analogs was continued and therapy with sunitinib was started.

Current Condition:
Patient's condition has been stable since initiation of therapy with somatostatin analogs, which has been continued along with sunitinib.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has not been discharged and will continue as an outpatient in the Endocrinology Department.

Follow-up:
The patient will continue therapy with somatostatin analogs and sunitinib as an outpatient. Due to the presence of a mutation in the SDHB gene, the patient's family members have undergone genetic testing. The patient's mother and sister resulted negative, and further genetic workup of the father will be pursued. The patient will continue to be closely monitored by the Endocrinology Department.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: N/A (anonymous)
Gender: Male
Age: 23
Medical Record Number: N/A

Hospital Course:
The patient was sent to the Hormone Department for care of a rare tumor in the belly area. This tumor was removed during childhood but came back. The patient had two surgeries, but doctors could not remove all of the tumor. The patient had a special scan called 123I-MIBG that did not show the tumor. Another scan called 68GaDOTATATE-PET showed the tumor had certain proteins on its surface. Because of this, the patient received hormone-blocking drugs and a radiation treatment called 90Y-DOTATATE. When the patient came to our Hormone Department, the tumor had come back in the same spot. The tumor had also spread to the bones in several places. Blood and urine tests showed high levels of certain chemicals that the tumor makes. These chemicals were chromogranin-A, catecholamines, and vanilmandelic acid. Imaging tests showed the belly tumor was getting worse. The patient kept taking hormone-blocking drugs and started a new cancer drug called sunitinib.

Current Condition:
The patient has been stable since starting the hormone-blocking drugs. The patient continues to take these drugs along with sunitinib.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has not been sent home. The patient will keep getting care at the Hormone Department as an outpatient.

Follow-up:
The patient will keep taking hormone-blocking drugs and sunitinib while visiting the clinic. The patient has a change in a gene called SDHB that can be passed down in families. Because of this, family members had genetic testing. The patient's mother and sister do not have this gene change. The father will have more genetic testing. The Hormone Department will keep watching the patient closely.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
Name: Not provided
Sex: Female
Age: 3 years old
Medical Record Number: Not provided

Admission Date: Not provided
Discharge Date: Not provided

Diagnoses: 
- Progressive neurologic regression
- Focal seizures
- Aspiration pneumonia
- Diffuse encephalopathic process
- Underlying neurodegenerative disease (suspected)

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 3-year-old female who presented with progressive neurologic regression that was first noticed at the age of 18 months. Basic metabolic testing indicated a high plasma lactate level of 39.7 mmol/L while other metabolic screening tests were normal. Imaging showed abnormal deep white matter signal changes especially in the posterior periventricular area and thinning of the corpus callosum. Whole-exome sequencing (WES) was considered to diagnose a possible underlying neurodegenerative disease. 

During the patient’s second hospitalization for aspiration pneumonia, the spasms and dystonia were aggravated, leading to severe respiratory distress that required admission to the pediatric intensive care unit (PICU) and tracheal intubation. Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) was placed for the patient to facilitate her difficulty feeding.

At the age of 36 months, the patient’s problems with limb spasticity and dystonia were still present, but her feeding problem had been ameliorated. Unfortunately, she died 1 month later due to cardiorespiratory arrest at home.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient was discharged home with instructions for the family to return if symptoms should worsen. The family was advised to continue with the prescribed medication regimen, and to maintain the PEG site as ordered. The patient’s parents were counselled about the nature of the underlying neurodegenerative disease. 

Disposition:
Discharged home 

Signed, 
Not provided 

Date/Time:
Not provided

Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
Name: Not provided
Sex: Female
Age: 3 years old
Medical Record Number: Not provided

Admission Date: Not provided
Discharge Date: Not provided

Diagnoses:
The patient had brain function that was getting worse over time. She had seizures affecting one part of the body. She had a lung infection caused by food or liquid going into the lungs. She had a brain disease affecting the whole brain. Doctors believe she had a disease that slowly damages the brain and nerves.

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 3-year-old female who came to the hospital because her brain function was getting worse. This was first noticed when she was 18 months old. Blood tests showed a very high level of lactic acid at 39.7 mmol/L. Other blood tests were normal. Brain scans showed problems with the white part of the brain. These problems were mostly in the back part of the brain. The scans also showed that the tissue connecting the two sides of the brain was thinner than normal. Doctors wanted to do a special gene test to find out what disease was causing these problems.

During the second hospital stay for a lung infection, the muscle spasms and uncontrolled movements got worse. This caused serious breathing problems. The patient needed to go to the children's intensive care unit. She needed a breathing tube placed in her throat. Doctors put a feeding tube directly into her stomach through the skin. This helped because she had trouble eating.

At 36 months old, the patient still had stiff muscles and uncontrolled movements in her arms and legs. Her eating problems had gotten better. Sadly, she died 1 month later at home. Her heart and breathing stopped.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient was sent home. The family was told to come back if problems got worse. The family was told to keep giving the medicines as ordered. They were told to take care of the stomach feeding tube as directed. The doctors talked to the parents about the brain disease causing these problems.

Disposition:
Sent home

Signed,
Not provided

Date/Time:
Not provided

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient presented to the Emergency Department with pain in their right hand after punching a locker. The patient had significant soft tissue swelling over the dorsum of the right hand with tenderness over the third through fifth metacarpals. The patient had significantly limited range of motion at the metacarpophalangeal joints and interphalangeal joints and was diagnosed with comminuted intra-articular fractures through the radial aspect of the base of the third metacarpal with dorsal carpometacarpal dislocations of the third through fifth metacarpals.

The patient received a hematoma block and CMC dislocation was reduced by applying axial traction at the metacarpal phalangeal joints with pressure at the dorsum of the proximal CMC joints. The reduction was successful and the patient's hand was immobilized. The patient was advised to follow up with Orthopedic Surgery and ultimately underwent closed reduction with percutaneous placement of Kirschner wires and was immobilized for six weeks.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:

Comminuted intra-articular fractures through the radial aspect of the base of the third metacarpal, with dorsal carpometacarpal dislocations of the third through fifth metacarpals.

TREATMENT:

Hematoma block, closed reduction, percutaneous placement of Kirschner wires, and immobilization for six weeks.

FOLLOW-UP:

The patient was advised to follow up with Orthopedic Surgery.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient came to the Emergency Department with pain in the right hand after punching a locker. The patient had a lot of swelling on the back of the right hand. There was tenderness over the third through fifth hand bones. The patient could not move the finger joints well. The patient was found to have broken bones in the hand that were shattered into pieces. The breaks went into the joint area at the base of the third hand bone. The third through fifth hand bones were also pushed out of place at the wrist.

The doctors numbed the area by injecting medicine into the blood collection around the injury. They put the bones back in place by pulling on the fingers and pushing on the back of the hand near the wrist. This worked well and the hand was kept still with a splint. The patient was told to see a bone doctor. Later the patient had a procedure to put the bones back in place. Small metal pins were placed through the skin to hold the bones together. The hand was kept still for six weeks.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:

Broken bones in the hand that were shattered into pieces at the base of the third hand bone. The third through fifth hand bones were also pushed out of place at the wrist.

TREATMENT:

Numbing medicine injected into the injured area. Bones put back in place. Small metal pins placed through the skin to hold bones together. Hand kept still for six weeks.

FOLLOW-UP:

The patient was advised to follow up with a bone doctor.

Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 15
Dates of Admission: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with fever and headache for 6 days following a cold. Nervous system examination, electroencephalogram, and head MRI were completed and showed no abnormalities. No obvious blood, urine, or stool abnormalities were detected by routine observations and blood biochemical tests. Autoimmune encephalitis antibody was negative, and the patient's CSF results showed the presence of HSV-1, confirmed by PCR. The patient was diagnosed with HSV encephalitis and was treated with ganciclovir for 14 days. During the treatment, the patient's condition improved. 

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was discharged with no symptoms of neurological impairment during the 3 months after discharge. No further neurological follow-up was necessary. 

Follow-Up Instructions:
None 

Plan:
Complete antibiotic treatment as prescribed. 

Procedures:
None 

Medications:
No new medication was prescribed during the hospital stay.

Dates of Admission: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with a fever and headache that lasted 6 days after having a cold. Doctors did tests on the brain and nervous system. These included a brain wave test and a brain scan. All results were normal. Blood, urine, and stool tests also showed no problems. A test for a type of brain swelling caused by the body attacking itself was negative. A test of the spinal fluid found a virus called herpes simplex virus type 1. The patient was diagnosed with brain swelling caused by this virus. The patient received a medicine called ganciclovir for 14 days to fight the virus. The patient got better during treatment.

The patient left the hospital and had no brain or nerve problems for 3 months after going home. No more brain or nerve check-ups are needed.

None

Finish taking the germ-fighting medicine as the doctor ordered.

Discharge Summary for Patient 2:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Diagnosis: Esophageal fistula with massive hematemesis; 6 cm saccular thoracic aortic aneurysm distal to the left subclavian artery

Hospital Course:

The patient was a 48-year-old male with a history of hypertension and a percutaneous coronary intervention with drug-eluting stents three years ago. He presented to the emergency with one episode of massive hematemesis. An urgent UGI endoscopy revealed a large depressed ulcer with a red spot located in the middle third of the esophagus along with a diverticulum located in the lower end of the ulcer. Imaging studies revealed a 6 cm saccular thoracic aortic aneurysm eroding into the esophagus.

Surgery was performed through a posterolateral thoracotomy. The aneurysmal segment of the aorta was replaced with a Dacron graft while taking care to preserve as many intercostal arteries as possible while suture ligating the others. The esophageal opening of the fistula was debrided and repaired with interrupted polypropylene sutures, superimposed with a vascularized intercostal muscle pedicle. The patient was placed on broad-spectrum antibiotics postoperatively.

Postoperatively, the patient recovered well and had an uneventful course. He was successfully separated from the partial bypass, and a gastrostomy and jejunostomy were created. He was discharged and advised to follow up with his primary care physician and the treating surgeon as part of a long-term management plan. 

Discharge Medications: None

Instructions: No heavy lifting, straining or strenuous activity for the next 6-8 weeks. Follow-up appointments were scheduled with the primary care physician and surgeon.

Condition at Discharge: Stable
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary for Patient 2:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Diagnosis: An abnormal opening between the food pipe and a blood vessel, causing the patient to vomit large amounts of blood; a 6 cm balloon-like bulge in the main blood vessel in the chest, located just past the artery that supplies the left arm

Hospital Course:

The patient was a 48-year-old male with a history of high blood pressure. Three years ago, he had a procedure where small mesh tubes were placed in his heart arteries to keep them open. He came to the emergency room after vomiting a large amount of blood. Doctors used a camera to look inside his food pipe. They found a large sore with a red spot in the middle part of the food pipe. They also found a small pouch at the lower end of the sore. Scans showed a 6 cm balloon-like bulge in the main chest blood vessel. This bulge was wearing through into the food pipe.

The patient had surgery through a cut in the back and side of the chest. Doctors replaced the bulging part of the blood vessel with a fabric tube. They saved as many small blood vessels between the ribs as they could. They tied off the others with stitches. Doctors cleaned the opening between the food pipe and blood vessel. They closed it with stitches. They covered the repair with a piece of muscle from between the ribs that still had its blood supply. After surgery, the patient received strong medicines to fight infection.

After surgery, the patient recovered well without any problems. Doctors safely took him off the machine that was helping his blood flow. They made two new openings, one into his stomach and one into his small intestine, for feeding. The patient was sent home. He was told to see his regular doctor and his surgeon for long-term care.

Discharge Medications: None

Instructions: No heavy lifting, straining, or hard physical activity for the next 6 to 8 weeks. Follow-up visits were set up with the regular doctor and surgeon.

Condition at Discharge: Stable

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Reason for Admission:
The patient was admitted due to ongoing pain in his right Achilles tendon, painful recurrent swelling in both ankles for the last two years, and one episode of joint pain at the MTP-I several months earlier.

Hospital Course:
During the hospital course, the patient underwent a thorough clinical examination. No signs of local inflammation or tophus formation were observed. Blood uric acid levels were found to be high. Native CT images showed calcaneal enthesophytes and high-density zones in the right Achilles tendon at the insertion with the absorption spectrum of MSU crystals. The patient had no joint degeneration or erosions in neither of the feet. After a thorough evaluation, the patient was diagnosed with gout and MSU crystal deposition in the Achilles tendon.

Treatment Provided:
The patient was treated using standard medical procedures for managing gout and MSU crystal deposition in Achilles tendon, as per the hospital protocol. The treatment included medications, rest, and regular follow-up appointments.

Condition at Discharge:
At the time of discharge, the patient’s condition had significantly improved. The patient was advised to continue with the prescribed medication and follow-up appointment.

Instructions to Follow at Home:
The patient was advised to follow certain instructions at home to manage his condition. These instructions include a proper diet, exercise regime, and other lifestyle changes to manage the symptoms of gout and MSU crystal deposition in Achilles tendon.

Follow-Up:
The patient was advised to schedule a follow-up appointment with the hospital physician to monitor his health and ensure that the treatment is working accurately.

Summary:
The patient was diagnosed with gout and MSU crystal deposition in the Achilles tendon. During the hospital course, the patient underwent a thorough clinical examination, and standard medical procedures were implemented for managing the patient’s condition. At the time of discharge, the patient’s condition had improved, and the patient was given instructions to follow at home. A follow-up appointment was scheduled to ensure that the treatment was working effectively.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Reason for Admission:
The patient came to the hospital because of ongoing pain in the back of his right heel. The patient also had painful swelling in both ankles that kept coming back over the past two years. A few months earlier, the patient had one episode of pain in the big toe joint.

Hospital Course:
During the hospital stay, the doctors did a full physical exam. There were no signs of redness, warmth, or lumps in the joints. Blood tests showed high levels of uric acid. A scan showed small bone spurs on the heel bone. The scan also showed crystal buildup in the right heel tendon where it connects to the bone. The patient did not have any joint damage or bone loss in either foot. After reviewing all the results, the doctors found that the patient has gout. Gout is a condition where uric acid crystals build up in the body. These crystals had formed in the heel tendon.

Treatment Provided:
The patient received standard treatment for gout and crystal buildup in the heel tendon. The treatment followed the hospital's guidelines. This included medicines, rest, and plans for regular check-up visits.

Condition at Discharge:
When the patient left the hospital, the patient's condition had gotten much better. The patient was told to keep taking the prescribed medicine and to come back for a follow-up visit.

Instructions to Follow at Home:
The patient was given directions to follow at home to help manage the condition. These directions include eating a healthy diet, exercising regularly, and making other changes to daily habits. These steps help control gout symptoms and crystal buildup in the heel tendon.

Follow-Up:
The patient was told to make a follow-up appointment with the hospital doctor. This visit will help check on the patient's health and make sure the treatment is working well.

Summary:
The patient was found to have gout and crystal buildup in the heel tendon. During the hospital stay, the doctors did a full exam and gave standard treatment for this condition. When the patient left the hospital, the patient's condition had improved. The patient was given directions to follow at home. A follow-up visit was planned to make sure the treatment is working well.
