input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 32-year-old female with chief complaint of pain in upper anterior tooth.

Hospital Course:
The patient reported to the Department of Conservative Dentistry and Endodontics and was diagnosed with an impacted permanent canine and a palatally placed upper permanent right lateral incisor. A radiograph showed loss of lamina dura and slight radiolucency in relation to the periapical area of the upper right central incisor. A CBCT revealed a periapical lesion related to both roots of the central incisor and an additional root positioned more facially than expected. A nonsurgical endodontic therapy was decided after a discussion with the patient. The root canals were cleaned and shaped, copiously irrigated, and obturated with corresponding Protaper gutta-percha and AH Plus sealer. The patient refused restoration of the facial groove with composite resin.

Outcome:
The patient was asymptomatic after 2 weeks and at a one-year follow-up. The patient was satisfied with the treatment outcome and would continue to be monitored by her dentist. 

Impression:
The patient presented with a rare case of an additional root present with the central incisor, which was successfully treated with nonsurgical endodontic therapy.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 32-year-old female who came in because of pain in an upper front tooth.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the dental department and was found to have a stuck adult canine tooth and an upper right side tooth that was positioned toward the roof of the mouth. An x-ray showed bone loss and a small dark area near the root tip of the upper right front tooth. A 3D scan showed an infection at the root tips of the front tooth and an extra root that was more toward the front of the mouth than normal. After talking with the patient, the team chose to do a root canal treatment without surgery. The root canals were cleaned, shaped, rinsed well, and filled with rubber-like filling material and a sealing paste. The patient chose not to have a groove on the front of the tooth filled with tooth-colored filling material.

Outcome:
The patient had no pain or problems after 2 weeks and at a one-year checkup. The patient was happy with how the treatment turned out and would keep seeing her dentist for regular checkups.

Impression:
The patient had a rare case of an extra root on her front tooth, which was treated successfully with root canal treatment without surgery.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]   Date of Admission: [REDACTED]    Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Poorly controlled type 1 diabetes (T1D) and marked hepatomegaly.

Hospital Course:
The patient, an 11-year old boy with a history of T1D, was hospitalized in the Emergency Department due to symptoms of polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and weakness. He presented with diabetic ketoacidosis, for which he received insulin therapy according to the International Society of Pediatric and Adolescent Diabetes guidelines. Following this treatment, he was prescribed multiple daily insulin injections and discharged after a week. However, the patient did not attend the follow-up visit at the Diabetes Centre, resulting in poor glycometabolic control and recurrent episodes of diabetic ketoacidosis.

At the age of 10, the patient moved to Southern Italy with his family and was eventually admitted to our Paediatric Diabetes Clinic after an episode of severe diabetic ketoacidosis, accompanied by marked hepatomegaly and short stature. Secondary sexual characteristics were absent, and the patient's hormonal levels were evaluated.

Laboratory tests were performed to determine the differential diagnosis of his hepatomegaly, but autoimmune hepatitis and infectious causes were excluded and no inflammation or fibrosis was noted in the liver biopsy. A diagnosis of hepatic glycogenosis was confirmed.

While in the hospital, the patient received education on diabetes management, and his daily insulin dosage was titrated to achieve good metabolic control. A follow-up examination three months later, however, showed poor metabolic control, severe hepatomegaly, and abnormal lipid levels, leading to another hospitalization.

During this second hospitalization, the patient received intravenous continuous insulin treatment for normalization of aminotransferases and to achieve good glycaemic control, which was reached after eight days. The patient maintained good glycemic control at the last follow-up visit, with complete remission of biochemical, clinical signs, and hepatomegaly resolution.

Diagnostic Impression:
The patient is diagnosed with poorly controlled type 1 diabetes, marked hepatomegaly, and hepatic glycogenosis.

Follow-Up Recommendations:
The patient is advised to continue managing his diabetes as planned and maintain regular follow-ups with his healthcare provider. The patient should also continue monitoring his lipid levels periodically.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]   Date of Admission: [REDACTED]    Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Type 1 diabetes that was not well controlled and a very enlarged liver.

Hospital Course:
The patient, an 11-year old boy with type 1 diabetes, was brought to the Emergency Room. He was urinating a lot, very thirsty, losing weight, and feeling weak. He had a serious problem called diabetic ketoacidosis, where the body has too much acid in the blood. He was given insulin through an IV following standard guidelines for children. After a week, he was sent home with a plan to take insulin shots every day. The patient did not come back for his scheduled visit at the Diabetes Centre. Because of this, his blood sugar stayed poorly controlled and he had more episodes of diabetic ketoacidosis.

At age 10, the patient moved to Southern Italy with his family. He was then brought to our Children's Diabetes Clinic after another serious episode of diabetic ketoacidosis. At that time, he also had a very enlarged liver and was shorter than expected for his age. He had not started puberty yet, and his hormone levels were checked.

Blood tests were done to find the cause of his enlarged liver. Tests ruled out liver disease caused by the immune system and infections. A small sample of liver tissue showed no swelling or scarring. The doctors confirmed that his liver was storing too much sugar, a condition called hepatic glycogenosis.

While in the hospital, the patient learned how to manage his diabetes. His daily insulin dose was adjusted to help control his blood sugar. A check-up three months later showed his blood sugar was still poorly controlled. He also had a very enlarged liver and unhealthy fat levels in his blood. He was admitted to the hospital again.

During this second hospital stay, the patient received insulin through an IV to bring his liver enzyme levels back to normal. The goal was also to get his blood sugar under control. This was reached after eight days. At his last check-up, the patient's blood sugar was well controlled. His lab results and symptoms had improved, and his liver was back to normal size.

Diagnostic Impression:
The patient has type 1 diabetes that was not well controlled, a very enlarged liver, and a condition where the liver stores too much sugar.

Follow-Up Recommendations:
The patient should keep following his diabetes care plan and see his doctor regularly. The patient should also have his blood fat levels checked from time to time.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, an 89-year-old male, was admitted with complaints of cough and haemoptysis. The patient had a history of bronchiectasis in the right middle lobe bronchus for 30 years. One month before admission, the patient suffered a massive haemoptysis, which was controlled through bronchial artery embolization. However, haemoptysis recurred, and the patient was admitted to our hospital. Bronchoscopy performed on the patient showed active bleeding from the right middle lobe bronchus, which was temporarily controlled through haemostatic medications and cryotherapy.

Management:
On the next day, an 8 mm x 3 cm silicon stent was deployed using biopsy forceps after multiple attempts. Another customized stent with a diameter of about 6 mm was placed from the right intermediate bronchus to the right lower lobe bronchus, to avoid stent migration. Bleeding stopped promptly after the placement of stents. The patient received successive antibiotic therapy, and no obvious complication of obstructive pneumonia developed.

Outcome:
One month after the operation of stents implantation, the patient experienced complete atelectasis of the right middle and lower lobe with the complication of right pleural effusion. The patient received further surgery to remove the silicon stent inside the right intermediate bronchus. Thoracic puncture and drainage were done after stent extraction for relieving pleural effusion. Post-operation CT scan reviewed the patient's chest and revealed the right lower lobe recruitment. The patient did not experience any more haemoptysis during the three-month follow-up time and left the hospital ten days after stent extraction.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is an 89-year-old man. He came to the hospital because he was coughing and coughing up blood. The patient had a lung condition called widened airways in the right middle part of his lung for 30 years. One month before coming to the hospital, the patient coughed up a large amount of blood. Doctors blocked the bleeding blood vessel to stop it. But the patient started coughing up blood again and came to our hospital. Doctors used a small camera to look inside the patient's airways. They saw active bleeding from the right middle part of the lung. They used cold treatment and medicine to stop the bleeding for a short time.

Management:
The next day, doctors placed a small tube called a stent to keep the airway open. The stent was about 8 mm wide and 3 cm long. They used a gripping tool and tried several times to put it in place. They also placed another custom-made tube in a lower part of the right lung. This second tube helped keep the first one from moving out of place. The bleeding stopped right away after the tubes were placed. The patient took medicine to fight infection. The patient did not develop lung blockage or infection.

Outcome:
One month after the tubes were placed, the right middle and lower parts of the lung collapsed. Fluid also built up around the right lung. The patient had another procedure to remove the tube from the lower right airway. Doctors also drained the fluid from around the lung using a needle. A scan of the chest showed the right lower part of the lung opened back up. The patient did not cough up any more blood during the next three months. The patient left the hospital ten days after the tube was removed.

The patient left the hospital ten days after the tube was removed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Facility: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis:
Fetal perianal mass detected at 23 weeks' gestation

Hospital Course:
The patient was evaluated for a fetal perianal mass that was detected during routine antenatal care. At the initial visit, an echogenic perivaginal mass was visible on 2D transabdominal ultrasound. Further evaluation with 3D ultrasound revealed that the mass was originated from the skin apart from anus or vagina. Amniocentesis was performed which revealed normal fetal karyotype and no acetylcholinesterase level was detected. As the gestational age progressed, the size of the mass increased. A female neonate was delivered at GA 41 weeks and a pedunculated soft mass was visible on the right posterior labium major of the neonate. A complete physical and laboratory evaluation was performed preoperatively, including routine postnatal cardiac echo which showed a patent foramen ovale with a flow from left to right. There was no significant congenital anomaly identified in vertebral body, both kidney, and anus on MRI. Simple surgical excision was planned for the lesion but during the operation, the right anal external muscle complex was displaced due to the original mass and perineal groove type anorectal malformation was found. Posterior sagittal anorectoplasty (PSARP) was performed with complete mass excision. The histopathological result was lipoblastoma with free resection margin. The neonate was discharged from the hospital without any complication on the postoperative seventh day.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Fetal perianal mass and lipoblastoma

Discharge Condition:
Stable

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Discharge Instructions:
The care team discussed the details of the operation and postoperative care with the patient and their family. The patient is advised to return to the follow-up clinic as scheduled for further evaluation. They were also advised to avoid any strenuous physical activity or heavy lifting for several weeks after surgery. If any signs of infection, such as fever or drainage at the surgical site, develop, the patient should contact their health care provider immediately. It is also recommended to maintain routine postnatal care and follow-up evaluations to monitor neonate's health.

Follow-up:
The patient is scheduled for regular follow-up appointments to monitor their recovery and evaluate any potential complications.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Facility: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis:
A growth near the bottom area was found in the baby at 23 weeks of pregnancy

Hospital Course:
The patient was checked for a growth near the baby's bottom area that was found during a routine pregnancy visit. At the first visit, a bright-looking growth near the private area was seen on a standard ultrasound. A more detailed 3D ultrasound showed that the growth came from the skin and was not connected to the anus or vagina. A test was done to check the fluid around the baby. The test showed the baby had normal genes and no signs of a spine problem. As the pregnancy continued, the growth got bigger. A baby girl was born at 41 weeks of pregnancy. A soft growth on a stalk was seen on the right side of the baby's outer private area. Before surgery, the doctors did a full body check and lab tests. A heart ultrasound showed a small opening between the two upper heart chambers with blood flowing from left to right. This is common in newborns. An MRI scan showed no major birth defects in the spine, both kidneys, or the anus. The doctors planned a simple surgery to remove the growth. During surgery, they found that the muscles around the anus had been pushed out of place by the growth. They also found a problem with how the bottom area had formed. The surgeons did a special repair surgery called PSARP to fix the bottom area and remove the growth completely. Lab tests on the removed growth showed it was a lipoblastoma, which is a rare harmless fatty tumor. The growth was fully removed with clear edges. The baby left the hospital seven days after surgery with no problems.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Growth near the bottom area before birth and lipoblastoma, a harmless fatty tumor

Discharge Condition:
Stable

Discharge Medications:
None

Discharge Instructions:
The care team talked with the patient and their family about the surgery and care after surgery. The patient should come back to the follow-up clinic as planned for more checks. They should avoid hard physical activity or lifting heavy things for several weeks after surgery. If there are any signs of infection, such as fever or fluid coming from the surgery site, the patient should call their health care provider right away. The patient should also keep up with regular newborn care visits to watch the baby's health.

Follow-up:
The patient has regular follow-up visits set up to check healing and watch for any problems.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 30-year-old male, was admitted to the orthopedic outpatient department (OPD) with complaints of lower back pain radiating to the left lower limb and difficulty in walking associated with low-grade fever. The physical examination revealed tenderness in the lumbosacral area of the spine, reduced power, and sensory changes in the left lower limb. Laboratory investigations showed decreased hemoglobin levels, total leucocyte count of 14800/cumm, and platelet count of 688000/cumm. Magnetic resonance imaging of the lumbar spine revealed T2 hyperintense and T1 iso to hyperintense moderately enhancing soft tissue in the spinal cord, extending within neural foramina with obliteration of neural foraminal fat. The impression of diffuse Kochs involvement of the spine was given along with suspicion of neoplastic etiology like lymphoma. Vertebral bone biopsy from left L3 and L4 region was performed and sent for histopathological examination which revealed actinomycosis of the lumbosacral spine. The patient was started on penicillin group antibiotics and tolerated the therapy well.

The patient was started on penicillin group antibiotics and tolerated the therapy well.

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 30-year-old male. He came to the bone and joint clinic with lower back pain spreading down to his left leg. He also had trouble walking and a mild fever. During the exam, the doctor found pain when pressing on the lower back area. The patient had weakness and numbness in his left leg. Blood tests showed low red blood cell levels. His white blood cell count was high at 14800, which can mean infection. His platelet count was also high at 688000. A special scan called an MRI was done on the lower back. The scan showed abnormal tissue in the spinal area that was pressing on nearby nerves. Doctors first thought it might be a type of tuberculosis in the spine or possibly a cancer called lymphoma. A small sample of bone was taken from the lower back area for testing. The test showed the patient had a bacterial infection called actinomycosis in the lower spine. The patient was given penicillin antibiotics to treat the infection. The patient handled the treatment well.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the community hospital with a complaint of syncope. The patient reported experiencing numbness in her arms while kayaking, followed by a loss of consciousness. The patient had a history of dizziness two weeks prior to the current episode. Initial laboratory tests showed low serum potassium and elevated troponin levels. An electrocardiogram revealed sinus tachycardia with monomorphic premature ventricular complexes (PVCs), and a corrected QT (QTc) interval of 520 ms. The QTc interval was normalized with potassium repletion. Echocardiography and cardiac catheterization findings were normal. A treadmill test showed multifocal PVCs and the QT interval shortened with exertion. The patient was discharged on nadolol and a LifeVest® wearable cardioverter-defibrillator.

Later, the patient presented with tachycardia for which the WCD alarmed. The patient pressed the response button to suppress a shock for more than nine minutes but eventually received two shocks. An MRI scan with gadolinium enhancement showed delayed myocardial enhancement in the midmyocardium and epicardium, indicating a nonischemic origin such as myocarditis, amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, or some other form of nonischemic cardiomyopathy. The patient was diagnosed with a phenomenon of residual fibrosis from a past viral myocarditis.

Treatment:

A dual-chamber implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) was placed, and sotalol was initiated at 80 mg twice daily and later increased to 120 mg twice daily. The patient was found to be noninducible via noninvasive programmed stimulation. The patient has been arrhythmia- and symptom-free and is scheduled for further follow-up.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the community hospital after fainting. The patient reported feeling numbness in her arms while kayaking, then passed out. The patient had felt dizzy two weeks before this happened. First blood tests showed low potassium and high levels of a heart protein called troponin. A heart tracing test showed the heart was beating fast with extra beats from the lower heart chambers. The test also showed the heart took too long to reset between beats. This timing problem went back to normal after the patient received potassium. An ultrasound of the heart and a test to look at the heart blood vessels were normal. A walking test on a treadmill showed extra heartbeats coming from different spots. The heart reset time got better during exercise. The patient went home taking nadolol and wearing a special vest that can shock the heart if needed.

Later, the patient came back because the heart was beating too fast and the vest gave a warning. The patient pressed a button to stop the vest from giving a shock for more than nine minutes. The patient then received two shocks from the vest. A special scan of the heart showed scarring in the heart muscle walls. This scarring was not caused by blocked blood vessels. It could be from heart swelling, protein buildup, or other heart muscle diseases. The doctors found that the patient had scarring left over from a past infection that caused heart swelling.

Treatment:

Doctors placed a small device inside the chest that can shock the heart back to a normal rhythm if needed. The patient started taking sotalol at 80 mg two times a day. This was later raised to 120 mg two times a day. Doctors tested the heart and could not make it go into a dangerous rhythm. The patient has had no irregular heartbeats or symptoms since then. The patient has a follow-up visit scheduled.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Medical Record Number: [redacted]
Gender: Female
Age: 67 years

Hospital Course:
The 67-year-old female patient with a stage 3 macular hole was admitted on [redacted] with a complaint of decreased vision in the left eye for the past one month. The patient underwent pars plana vitrectomy, triamcinolone acetonide-assisted internal limiting membrane peeling, and fluid-gas exchange. The postoperative OCT scans obtained at four weeks demonstrated the photoreceptor layer with 1820 µm wide disorganization. The patient had a visual acuity of 20/100. At six weeks, a macula spared retinal detachment with multiple breaks was found. She underwent pars plana vitrectomy, laser photocoagulation, and fluid-gas exchange. Her visual acuity was 20/40 at six months, and the OCT studies disclosed reorganization of the photoreceptor layer at multiple locations along the macula. Slight disorganization of the photoreceptor layer was noted under the center of the fovea. By 1 year, the patient's visual acuity had improved further to 20/25, and the photoreceptor layer of the fovea had been reorganized on the OCT images.

Diagnosis:
Stage 3 macular hole, macula-spared retinal detachment with multiple breaks.

Treatment:
Pars plana vitrectomy, triamcinolone acetonide-assisted internal limiting membrane peeling, fluid-gas exchange, laser photocoagulation.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient's condition has improved significantly, with visual acuity improving to 20/25, and there being a complete reorganization of the photoreceptor layer of the fovea.

Follow-up Care:
The patient will require regular follow-up to monitor her vision and retina health.

Summary:
The 67-year-old female patient with a stage 3 macular hole who presented with decreased vision in the left eye underwent successful pars plana vitrectomy, triamcinolone acetonide-assisted internal limiting membrane peeling, fluid-gas exchange, and laser photocoagulation. The postoperative follow-up scans showed a reorganization of the photoreceptor layer at multiple locations along the macula, and a slight disorganization was noted under the center of the fovea. The patient's condition steadily improved over the course of one year, with visual acuity improving to 20/25, and the photoreceptor layer of the fovea being reorganized on the OCT images. Regular follow-up care will be required to monitor vision and retina health.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Medical Record Number: [redacted]
Gender: Female
Age: 67 years

Hospital Course:
The 67-year-old female patient had a stage 3 hole in the macula, the central part of the eye. She came to the hospital on [redacted] because her left eye vision had gotten worse over the past month. The patient had eye surgery to remove the gel inside the eye and peel a thin layer from the retina. A special dye was used to help the surgeon see better. Gas was put in the eye to help it heal. Eye scans taken four weeks after surgery showed the light-sensing layer was still damaged over a wide area. The patient could see 20/100, which means her vision was still limited. At six weeks, the retina had pulled away from the back of the eye in some areas, but not the central vision area. There were several tears in the retina. She had another eye surgery to fix the detached retina. Laser treatment was used to seal the tears. Gas was again put in the eye. At six months, the patient could see 20/40. Eye scans showed the light-sensing layer was healing in many areas of the macula. There was still some damage under the very center of the macula. By one year, the patient could see 20/25, which is close to normal. Eye scans showed the light-sensing layer in the center of the macula had fully healed.

Diagnosis:
Stage 3 hole in the macula. Detached retina with several tears, but the central vision area was not affected.

Treatment:
Eye surgery to remove the gel inside the eye. Peeling of a thin layer from the retina using a special dye. Gas placed in the eye to help healing. Laser treatment to seal retina tears.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient has gotten much better. Her vision improved to 20/25, which is close to normal. The light-sensing layer in the center of the macula has fully healed.

Follow-up Care:
The patient will need regular check-ups to watch her vision and the health of her retina.

Summary:
The 67-year-old female patient had a stage 3 hole in the macula and came in with worse vision in her left eye. She had successful eye surgeries to fix the hole and later to repair a detached retina. Laser treatment sealed the retina tears. Eye scans after surgery showed the light-sensing layer was healing in many areas. There was some remaining damage under the very center of the macula at first. Over one year, the patient kept getting better. Her vision improved to 20/25, which is close to normal. The light-sensing layer in the center of the macula fully healed. The patient will need regular check-ups to watch her vision and retina health.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

PATIENT INFORMATION:
- Name: [Name]
- Age: 35 years
- Gender: Female

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient was admitted for refractory chyluria and underwent Gd-RGP-MRI using 1.5 T MR Scanner (Signa; GE Healthcare, Milwaukee, USA). Coronal fat suppressed T2 and T1 sequences were taken before instillation of gadolinium MR contrast (MultiHance; Bracco, Milan, Italy) mixed in normal saline through the ureteric catheter. Post-gadolinium injection abdominal angiography sequences were acquired and maximum intensity projection (MIP) images were reconstructed.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
The patient's refractory chyluria was investigated using Gd-RGP-MRI.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
The patient's discharge condition is stable.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
No medications were prescribed at the time of discharge.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient should follow up with the referring physician for further evaluation.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
- The patient should follow up with the referring physician.
- If the patient experiences any new symptoms or changes in symptoms, she should contact her physician.

Age: 35 years
Gender: Female

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient was admitted for a condition where fat leaks into the urine that has not responded to treatment. The patient had a special scan called an MRI to look at the urinary system. These pictures helped doctors see the urinary system more clearly.

The patient's condition where fat leaks into the urine was studied using the special MRI scan.

The patient is in stable condition at discharge.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should follow up with the doctor who sent her to the hospital.
If the patient has any new symptoms or if symptoms change, she should contact her doctor.

HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Patient name: M.K
Age: 9 months
Date of admission: [insert date]
Date of discharge: [insert date]

Admission diagnosis: Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) suspicion

Hospital course:
M.K is a 9-month-old boy who was admitted to our hospital with poor weight gain, recurrent diarrhea, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, pancytopenia, increased serum ferritin, and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) level. He was born from a consanguineous marriage and had been breastfed for seven months with normal growth. Upon food diversification, he started exhibiting the aforementioned symptoms.

During the hospital stay, the patient received appropriate supportive care and was closely monitored for the progression of the symptoms. The patient's condition improved significantly and all symptoms were resolved satisfactorily. Therefore, the patient was deemed medically stable and was discharged in good condition. 

Discharge medications: None

Follow-up instructions:
The patient's caregivers were given detailed instructions regarding the appropriate measures to be taken with regard to home care, nutrition, and follow-up appointments. They were advised to contact the hospital immediately if any symptoms resurface or worsen.

Discharge diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) suspicion during the hospital stay. 

Disposition:
The patient was safely discharged in good condition to be followed up in the pediatric outpatient clinic.

HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Patient name: M.K
Age: 9 months
Date of admission: [insert date]
Date of discharge: [insert date]

Admission diagnosis: Suspected HLH (a rare condition where the body's immune system attacks its own cells)

Hospital course:
M.K is a 9-month-old boy who came to our hospital. He had trouble gaining weight and repeated loose stools. His liver and spleen were larger than normal. His blood cell counts were low. He also had high levels of ferritin and LDH in his blood (these show inflammation in the body). His parents are related by blood. He was breastfed for seven months and grew normally during that time. After he started eating solid foods, he began showing these health problems.

During his hospital stay, the patient received proper care and support. The medical team watched him closely to see if his symptoms got better or worse. The patient got much better and all symptoms went away. The patient was healthy enough to go home and left the hospital in good condition.

Discharge medications: None

Follow-up instructions:
The patient's caregivers received clear instructions about how to care for him at home. They were told what foods to give him and when to come back for check-up visits. They were told to call the hospital right away if any symptoms come back or get worse.

Discharge diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with suspected HLH (a rare condition where the body's immune system attacks its own cells) during the hospital stay.

Disposition:
The patient went home safely in good condition. He will have follow-up visits at the children's outpatient clinic.

input:
Discharge Summary:

This is a summary of the hospital course of a 62-year-old male with Kearns-Sayre syndrome (KSS) who presented to the emergency department (ED) after two syncopal episodes. He has a past medical history of multi-system involvement including skeletal muscle myopathy, pigmentary retinopathy, heart failure, non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, pulmonary hypertension, chronic heart block, chronic kidney disease, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and mitral valve prolapse.

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted for syncope secondary to VT, and 150 mg of IV amiodarone bolus was administered. He was started on amiodarone drip at 0.5 mg/min. Follow-up AICD interrogation yielded sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia to the 140s during both syncopal episodes. Chest X-ray revealed right pleural effusion. On day two, the patient was noted to have non-sustained VT with increasing oxygen requirements. A plan was in place to transition the patient from IV to PO amiodarone, but instead, he developed VT storm and was thus continued on amiodarone drip. He was transferred to the critical care unit (CCU) for further monitoring and started on lidocaine drip (1 mg/min) with continued amiodarone and milrinone drips. In the CCU, he continued to have non-sustained VT with symptoms of palpitations despite the AICD reprogramming and medical management. He also continued to have shortness of breath with minimal improvement on bilevel positive airway pressure (BIPAP). Unfortunately, recurrent VT progressed to a VT storm, requiring multiple boluses of IV amiodarone, IV lidocaine, and multiple shocks from his AICD which were futile. The VT storm was refractory to his current medical management. The patient eventually passed away after the withdrawal of care.

Final Diagnosis:

The final diagnosis of the patient was VT storm in the setting of KSS with multi-system involvement including heart failure.

Follow-Up:

The patient was not discharged due to his unfortunate clinical outcome.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

This is a summary of the hospital stay of a 62-year-old male with Kearns-Sayre syndrome (KSS). KSS is a rare disease that affects many parts of the body. The patient came to the emergency room after fainting two times. He had many health problems including weak muscles, an eye disease, heart failure, fast heartbeats from the lower heart chambers, high blood pressure in the lungs, problems with the heart's electrical signals, kidney disease, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and a leaky heart valve.

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted because he fainted due to a fast heart rhythm called VT. He was given a heart medicine called amiodarone through an IV. He was then given this same medicine slowly through the IV over time. A check of his heart device showed that during both fainting spells, his heart was beating too fast from the lower chambers. A chest X-ray showed fluid around his right lung. On day two, the patient had more fast heartbeats and needed more oxygen. The plan was to switch his medicine from IV to pills. Instead, he had many episodes of dangerous fast heartbeats called VT storm. He stayed on the IV medicine. He was moved to the intensive care unit for closer watching. He was given another heart medicine called lidocaine through an IV. He also continued getting amiodarone and a heart pump medicine through the IV. In the intensive care unit, he kept having fast heartbeats and felt his heart racing. This happened even after his heart device was adjusted and he was given medicines. He also kept having trouble breathing. A breathing machine that helps push air into the lungs did not help much. Sadly, the dangerous fast heartbeats kept coming back. The doctors gave him many doses of amiodarone and lidocaine through the IV. His heart device shocked his heart many times. These treatments did not work. The fast heartbeats did not respond to the medicines. The patient passed away after the decision was made to stop treatment.

Final Diagnosis:

The final diagnosis was VT storm, which means many episodes of dangerous fast heartbeats. This happened because of his KSS disease that affected many parts of his body including his heart.

Follow-Up:

The patient was not sent home because he passed away during his hospital stay.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 56-year-old male with a history of dilated cardiomyopathy and atrial fibrillation, presented with epigastralgia and nonsustained VT. The patient was treated with amiodarone, nifekalant hydrochloride, and lidocaine, but the ventricular arrhythmias persisted which led to a decrease in atrial flutter. Severe anemia and blastoid cells were observed in the peripheral blood sample, and the patient was diagnosed with Ph+ ALL. Dasatinib 50 mg twice daily and prednisolone 100 mg daily were initiated to treat the leukemia. The dasatinib was discontinued after five days due to an increase in nonsustained VT although the QTc interval was not significantly prolonged. Bisoprolol was gradually increased to 3.75 mg daily, and dasatinib was rechallenged. The patient showed a complete hematological response and a 2-log reduction in minor bcr-abl mRNA transcripts in the bone marrow. Three weeks after the retreatment with dasatinib, blastoid cells reemerged in the peripheral blood, and the dasatinib was discontinued due to refractoriness of leukemia. The patient received steroid pulse therapy and furosemide, and VT did not recur after. Although, dexamethasone and the short-term use of mercaptopurine were used, the patient expired from leukemia on the 183rd day after admission.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was admitted with epigastralgia and nonsustained VT which was treated with amiodarone, nifekalant hydrochloride, and lidocaine. After a diagnosis of Ph+ ALL, the patient was initiated with dasatinib 50 mg twice daily and prednisolone 100 mg daily to manage leukemia. Due to complications, dasatinib and bisoprolol doses were adjusted. After the first retreatment with dasatinib, a complete hematological response was observed and minor bcr-abl mRNA transcripts reduced. The patient expired from leukemia on the 183rd day after admission.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 56-year-old male with a history of a weakened, enlarged heart and irregular heartbeat. He came to the hospital with stomach pain and short bursts of fast heartbeats from the lower heart chambers. The patient was treated with three heart rhythm medicines called amiodarone, nifekalant hydrochloride, and lidocaine. The abnormal heart rhythms did not stop, which led to a slower type of irregular heartbeat. Blood tests showed very low red blood cell counts and abnormal young blood cells. The patient was found to have a type of blood cancer called Ph-positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. The patient started taking dasatinib 50 mg twice a day and prednisolone 100 mg once a day to treat the cancer. Dasatinib was stopped after five days because the short bursts of fast heartbeats got worse. The heart rhythm measurement stayed close to normal during this time. A heart medicine called bisoprolol was slowly raised to 3.75 mg daily. Then dasatinib was started again. The blood cell counts returned to normal. Cancer markers in the bone marrow dropped by a large amount. Three weeks after restarting dasatinib, abnormal cancer cells came back in the blood. Dasatinib was stopped because the cancer was no longer responding to it. The patient received high-dose steroids and a water pill called furosemide. The fast heartbeat problem did not come back after this treatment. The patient also received dexamethasone and a short course of mercaptopurine. The patient died from the blood cancer on day 183 after being admitted to the hospital.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient came to the hospital with stomach pain and short bursts of fast heartbeats. These were treated with amiodarone, nifekalant hydrochloride, and lidocaine. After being diagnosed with Ph-positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia, the patient started dasatinib 50 mg twice daily and prednisolone 100 mg daily. The doses of dasatinib and bisoprolol were changed due to problems that came up. After restarting dasatinib, blood cell counts returned to normal and cancer markers went down. The patient died from the blood cancer on day 183 after being admitted to the hospital.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 8
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient was admitted to our hospital due to abdominal pain, which was relieved by conservative management. He had been diagnosed with congenital IPSVS six years prior and was under medication for attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder.

Laboratory tests revealed elevated ammonia levels and a prolonged coagulation profile. Imaging studies confirmed the presence of IPSVS and the hepatic mass.

The patient's parents opted for close follow-up rather than invasive treatment for the IPSVS. However, at the six-month follow-up, the patient's blood ammonia level had risen significantly, and the shunt ratio indicated the potential progression of hepatic encephalopathy. Hence, endovascular treatment was performed to close the shunt.

During the procedure, the patient experienced bleeding from the sheath's tract, which was managed promptly using US-guided compression. Within six days of the procedure, the patient's serum ammonia levels had decreased to within normal range, and he was discharged after nine days.

FOLLOW-UP:

Two weeks post-embolization, an abdominal US showed occluded shunts with a decrease in the size of the hepatic mass. The right portal vein's blood flow had increased markedly, and the spleen size had minimally increased. A follow-up CT scan a month later showed complete embolization of the IPSVS, improved portal flow to the right lobe, and nearly complete disappearance of the hepatic mass.

On discharge, the patient was stable, alert and oriented. His physical exam and vital signs were normal. His medications were reviewed, and he was instructed to follow up with his referring physician.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:

1. Congenital IPSVS
2. Hepatic mass.

DISCHARGE PLAN:

1. Follow up with the referring physician for routine care and monitoring.
2. Medications to be continued as before.
3. No dietary restrictions.
4. No strenuous physical activity for two weeks.
5. Report back to the hospital if there are any concerns or adverse events.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 8
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient came to the hospital because of belly pain. The pain got better with basic care. Six years ago, he was found to have a blood vessel problem he was born with. This problem causes blood to flow around the liver instead of through it. He also takes medicine for trouble focusing and staying still.

Blood tests showed high ammonia levels in his blood. His blood was also not clotting the way it should. Scans showed the blood vessel problem and a growth in his liver.

The patient's parents chose to watch the condition closely instead of doing a procedure right away. But six months later, his ammonia levels went up a lot. The amount of blood going around his liver had also increased. This meant he could have brain problems from the high ammonia. So, doctors did a procedure through a blood vessel to close off the problem area.

During the procedure, the patient had some bleeding where the tube went in. Doctors stopped it quickly by pressing on the area while watching with an ultrasound. Within six days, his ammonia levels dropped back to normal. He went home after nine days.

FOLLOW-UP:

Two weeks after the procedure, a belly ultrasound showed the problem areas were closed. The liver growth had also gotten smaller. Blood flow to the right side of the liver had increased a lot. The spleen had grown just a little. A scan one month later showed the blood vessel problem was fully fixed. Blood was flowing better to the right side of the liver. The liver growth was almost completely gone.

When the patient left the hospital, he was doing well and thinking clearly. His body check and heart rate, breathing, and temperature were all normal. His medicines were reviewed. He was told to see his regular doctor for follow-up.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:

1. A blood vessel problem present from birth that makes blood flow around the liver.
2. A growth in the liver.

DISCHARGE PLAN:

1. See the regular doctor for checkups and ongoing care.
2. Keep taking all medicines as before.
3. The patient can eat all foods with no limits.
4. No hard exercise or rough play for two weeks.
5. Come back to the hospital if there are any worries or problems.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
DOB: [Redacted]
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]

Admitting Diagnosis:
1. COVID-19 infection
2. Acute onset respiratory distress
3. HIV

Hospital Course:
A 31-year-old female presented with flu-like symptoms and a positive COVID-19 PCR test result. She was transferred to ICU with acute onset respiratory distress and maintained satisfactory oxygenation on nasal prongs. The patient was found to be positive for HIV with a CD4+ count of 3 cells/uL and a viral load of 53 781 copies/mL. Treatment with 1 g meropenem 8-hourly, 60 mg subcutaneous enoxaparin twice daily, 50 mg hydrocortisone 6-hourly, as well as zinc supplementation was given. ART was commenced as well as Pneumocystis prophylaxis. The patient was transferred to the general ward after 3 days and ultimately discharged 4 days later after a negative COVID-19 PCR test result.

Discharge Medications:
1. ART medications as prescribed
2. Pneumocystis prophylaxis

Follow-up:
1. The patient was instructed to continue taking medications as prescribed and to schedule follow-up appointments with her primary care physician.
2. The patient was also counseled on the importance of regular HIV viral load testing and maintaining an appropriate medication regimen for her HIV diagnosis.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition.

Discharge Destination:
The patient was discharged to home.

Signed:
[Redacted], MD

Date:
[Redacted]

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
DOB: [Redacted]
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]

Reasons for Hospital Stay:
1. COVID-19 infection
2. Sudden breathing problems
3. HIV

What Happened During the Hospital Stay:
A 31-year-old female came to the hospital with flu-like symptoms and a positive COVID-19 test. She was moved to the intensive care unit because of sudden breathing problems. She was able to get enough oxygen through small tubes in her nose. The patient also tested positive for HIV. Her immune system was very weak, with very few infection-fighting cells and high levels of the virus in her blood. She received an antibiotic called meropenem through an IV three times a day. She also received blood thinner shots called enoxaparin twice a day. She was given a steroid called hydrocortisone four times a day to reduce swelling. She also took zinc supplements. The patient started HIV medicines and medicine to prevent a type of lung infection. After 3 days, the patient was moved to a regular hospital room. She went home 4 days later after testing negative for COVID-19.

Medicines to Take at Home:
1. HIV medicines as prescribed
2. Medicine to prevent lung infection

Next Steps:
1. The patient was told to keep taking all medicines as prescribed and to make follow-up appointments with her regular doctor.
2. The patient was told how important it is to get regular blood tests to check her HIV levels and to keep taking her HIV medicines.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient was in stable condition when she left the hospital.

Where the Patient Went After Leaving:
The patient went home.

[Redacted], MD

Date:
[Redacted]

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient presented with sudden onset and severe occipital headache accompanied by bending his neck downwards. He was given loxoprofen sodium hydrate which momentarily relieved the headache but it persisted. Neurological examination shows no abnormalities. Brain CT and MRI were normal, but MRA showed multisegmental narrowing of the basilar artery, identified via BPAS-MRI agreement. After ruling out inflammation or infection by CSF investigation, a diagnosis of RCVS was presumed, and the patient was put on lomerizine hydrochloride (10 mg/day). Repeat MRA and BPAS-MRI were done, which showed a mild resolution of vasoconstriction. The symptoms resolved by post-admission day 8, and the patient was discharged uneventfully.

Hospitalization Summary:

A 41-year-old man presented with sudden onset and severe occipital headache, accompanied by bending his neck downwards. Nothing seen in the neurological examination, brain CT and MRI were normal, but MRA showed multisegmental narrowing of the basilar artery, in agreement with BPAS-MRI. CSF investigation was conducted, which ruled out inflammation or infection. RCVS was presumed, and the patient was treated with lomerizine hydrochloride (10 mg/day). Repeat MRA and BPAS-MRI showed a mild resolution of vasoconstriction, with symptoms resolving by post-admission day 8. The patient was then discharged.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with a sudden and severe headache at the back of the head. This happened while bending his neck down. He was given a pain reliever called loxoprofen sodium hydrate. This helped the headache for a short time, but the pain came back. The exam of the brain and nerves showed nothing wrong. Brain scans using CT and MRI were normal. However, another type of scan called MRA showed narrowing in several parts of a major blood vessel at the back of the brain. This was confirmed by a special imaging test. A spinal fluid test was done to check for swelling or infection. The test ruled out both of these problems. The doctors believed the patient had a condition where blood vessels in the brain tighten temporarily. The patient was given a medicine called lomerizine hydrochloride at 10 mg per day. Follow-up scans showed the blood vessels were starting to return to normal. The symptoms went away by day 8 after being admitted. The patient left the hospital without any problems.

input:
Hospital Course:

This 60-year-old male patient presented to our outpatient clinic with a 1-year history of generalized yellowish ovoid skin-colored papular eruption located on the trunk, groin, and extremities. The patient reported that he first noticed pruritic papules, and 2–3 months later many yellow and ovoid pruritic papules emerged. On skin physical examination, firm, well-defined ovoid skin-colored to yellow papules were seen on the distal and proximal of upper and lower extremities, trunk, groins, and abdomen.

Diagnostic laboratory tests such as full blood cell count with differential, peripheral blood smear, renal, and liver function tests all were normal. Histopathological examination of the skin biopsy from one of the lesion on the trunk revealed proliferation of mast cells with ovoid and spindle nuclei with distinct cytoplasm borders around capillaries, which was compatible with mastocytosis. Based on concurrent clinical and pathological features, we diagnosed our case as xanthelasmoid mastocytosis.

Treatment with H1 antihistamine, cetirizine 10 mg orally nightly and fexofenadine 120 mg for every morning was prescribed for pruritus control which was successful, but eruptions were persistent without any changes. Treatment with phototherapy also recommended to the patient, but despite a 1-year course, no significant improvement was seen in the lesions.

The patient refused further investigations such as bone marrow biopsy or alternative treatments and decided to end the hospital course. The patient was advised to monitor the lesions and seek medical attention if he noticed any changes or deterioration. The patient was discharged with no complications.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

This 60-year-old male patient came to our outpatient clinic with skin bumps that had lasted for 1 year. The bumps were yellow, oval-shaped, and skin-colored. They appeared on the chest, back, groin area, arms, and legs. The patient said he first noticed itchy bumps. Then 2 to 3 months later, many more yellow and oval itchy bumps appeared. During the skin exam, doctors found firm, clear-edged, oval bumps. These bumps were skin-colored to yellow. They were located on the upper and lower arms and legs, chest, back, groin area, and belly.

Lab tests checked the blood cells, kidney function, and liver function. All test results came back normal. Doctors took a small skin sample from one bump on the trunk. The sample showed too many mast cells, which are a type of immune cell. These cells had oval and long-shaped centers with clear edges. They were found around tiny blood vessels. This finding matched a condition called mastocytosis. Based on the skin exam and lab results together, doctors determined the patient had a rare type called xanthelasmoid mastocytosis.

The patient received two allergy medicines to control the itching. Cetirizine 10 mg was taken by mouth each night. Fexofenadine 120 mg was taken each morning. These medicines helped stop the itching. However, the skin bumps stayed the same without any changes. Doctors also suggested light therapy treatment. The patient tried this for 1 year, but the bumps did not get much better.

The patient chose not to have more tests like a bone marrow sample. The patient also chose not to try other treatments. The patient decided to end care at this time. The patient was told to watch the bumps and see a doctor if they change or get worse. The patient left the hospital with no problems.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Name]
Age: 37
Sex: Female
Date of Admission: [Admission Date]
Date of Discharge: [Discharge Date]
Admitting Physician: [Physician Name]
Primary Diagnosis: Urticaria

Hospital Course:

The patient presented to the outpatient allergy clinic with a complaint of recurrent generalized urticaria, rash, and facial angioedema, worsened by high-seasoned, spicy or Chinese foods. Previously, she had been treated for allergic rhinitis and experienced severe urticaria with a generalized rash after taking non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). Peripheral eosinophil counts and serum total IgE in her blood were elevated, and a skin prick test showed weakly positive reactions to silk worm pupa, herring, cabbage, celery, and walnut. However, none of them induced urticaria in her history.

The patient underwent basophil activation test (BAT), in which a total of 18.7% of basophils were activated after stimulation with monosodium glutamate (MSG). No other additives induced significant basophil activation compared to the negative control. T4 and thyroid stimulating hormone levels were within normal range.

The patient was diagnosed with urticaria and was advised to avoid foods that tend to trigger her symptoms. No medications were prescribed.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient was provided with instructions on how to manage her symptoms at home and advised to avoid the foods that tend to trigger urticaria. She was also advised to return to the clinic if her symptoms persist or worsen.

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to follow-up with the clinic for further evaluation and management.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Name]
Age: 37
Sex: Female
Date of Admission: [Admission Date]
Date of Discharge: [Discharge Date]
Admitting Physician: [Physician Name]
Primary Diagnosis: Hives

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the allergy clinic with a problem of hives that kept coming back all over her body. She also had a rash and swelling of her face. These problems got worse when she ate very spicy foods, heavily seasoned foods, or Chinese foods. In the past, she was treated for allergies that caused a stuffy or runny nose. She also had bad hives and a rash all over her body after taking pain medicines like ibuprofen. Blood tests showed she had higher than normal levels of certain allergy-related cells and proteins. A skin test showed small reactions to silkworm pupa, herring, cabbage, celery, and walnut. However, the patient had never gotten hives from eating any of these foods before.

The patient had a special blood test to check how her allergy cells react to different things. The test showed that her allergy cells reacted to MSG, a flavor enhancer often used in food. Her allergy cells did not react to any other food additives that were tested. Tests for thyroid hormone levels came back normal.

The patient was told she has hives. She was told to stay away from foods that seem to cause her symptoms. No medicines were given.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient was given information on how to take care of her symptoms at home. She was told to avoid foods that seem to cause her hives. She was also told to come back to the clinic if her symptoms do not go away or get worse.

Follow-up:

The patient was told to come back to the clinic for more tests and care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 29-year-old male of Syrian origin, was admitted for evaluation of a painless right-sided testicular swelling that had been present for 12 months. Upon clinical examination, he had a nontender enlarged right testis. Further evaluation with alpha-fetoprotein (α-FP) and beta human chorionic gonadotropin (β-HCG) tumor markers revealed negative results. An ultrasound scan of the scrotum showed a 7-cm hypoechoic, hypervascular, and cystic mass replacing the right testis. A right radical inguinal orchidectomy was performed, and a staging computed tomography scan was negative for lymphatic or metastatic disease.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was diagnosed with Sertoli cell sarcoma (SCS) with undifferentiated sarcoma confined to the testis. The tumor was composed of small, intermediate, and large epithelioid cells arranged in sheets with broad areas of necrosis. Microscopic features of these cells were consistent with SCS. Additionally, there were ill-defined areas of undifferentiated sarcoma composed of highly pleomorphic, epithelioid, and spindle-shaped cells with high mitotic activity invested in fibrous and fibromyxoid stroma. The tumor did not extend beyond the tunica albuginea. The spermatic cord and epididymis were uninvolved, and no lymphovascular invasion was identified.

Treatment:
The histological diagnosis was SCS with undifferentiated sarcoma confined to the testis. Further oncological treatment with adjuvant chemotherapy was strongly advised, but the patient declined and moved to a different locality.

Follow-up:
The patient should be advised to receive regular follow-up consultations for potential recurrence of the tumor, and to notify his healthcare provider if he develops any symptoms concerning for the same.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 29-year-old man from Syria. He came to the hospital because of painless swelling in his right testicle. The swelling had been there for 12 months. During the exam, the right testicle was larger than normal but did not hurt when touched. Blood tests were done to check for cancer markers. These tests came back normal. An ultrasound of the scrotum showed a 7-centimeter mass in the right testicle. The mass had low echoes, many blood vessels, and fluid-filled areas. Surgery was done to remove the right testicle through a cut in the groin area. A full body scan showed no cancer spread to the lymph nodes or other parts of the body.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was found to have a rare type of testicular cancer called Sertoli cell sarcoma. There was also a fast-growing cancer that had no specific features. Both cancers were only in the testicle. The tumor had small, medium, and large cells grouped together in sheets. Large areas of dead tissue were also found. Some areas had oddly shaped cells that were dividing quickly. These cells were surrounded by tough, fiber-like tissue. The cancer did not grow through the outer covering of the testicle. The tube that carries sperm and the tissue next to the testicle were not affected. The cancer had not spread into blood vessels or lymph channels.

Treatment:
Tests confirmed the patient had Sertoli cell sarcoma with fast-growing cancer in the testicle only. Doctors strongly recommended additional cancer-killing drug treatment after surgery. However, the patient said no to this treatment and moved to a different area.

Follow-up:
The patient should see a doctor regularly to check if the cancer comes back. The patient should tell a doctor right away about any new symptoms that might be related to the cancer.

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 29-year-old man from Syria. He came to the hospital because of painless swelling in his right testicle. The swelling had been there for 12 months. During the exam, the right testicle was larger than normal but did not hurt when touched. Blood tests were done to check for cancer markers. These tests came back normal. An ultrasound of the scrotum showed a 7-centimeter mass in the right testicle. The mass had low echoes, many blood vessels, and fluid-filled areas. Surgery was done to remove the right testicle through a cut in the groin area. A full body scan showed no cancer spread to the lymph nodes or other parts of the body. Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was found to have a rare type of testicular cancer called Sertoli cell sarcoma. Both cancers were only in the testicle. The tumor had small, medium, and large cells grouped together in sheets. Large areas of dead tissue were also found. Some areas had oddly shaped cells that were dividing quickly. These cells were surrounded by tough, fiber-like tissue. The cancer did not grow through the outer covering of the testicle. The tube that carries sperm and the tissue next to the testicle were not affected. The cancer had not spread into blood vessels or lymph channels. Treatment:
Tests confirmed the patient had Sertoli cell sarcoma with fast-growing cancer in the testicle only. Doctors strongly recommended additional cancer-killing drug treatment after surgery. However, the patient said no to this treatment and moved to a different area. Follow-up:
The patient should see a doctor regularly to check if the cancer comes back. The patient should tell a doctor right away about any new symptoms that might be related to the cancer.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [redacted]
Age: 35
Gender: Male
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Admitting Diagnosis: Acute transverse myelitis
Discharge Diagnosis: HIV infection

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the Emergency Room with urinary retention and diminished strength in the lower limbs. He had recently returned from Angola where he had been working. He had been vaccinated for yellow fever, poliomyelitis, typhoid fever, and meningococcal disease in advance. He was initially treated for a possible spider bite and later presented with fever, cough, dyspnea, myalgia, and thoracic pain. After undergoing several tests, he was diagnosed with acute transverse myelitis and started on treatment with ceftriaxone, ganciclovir, and methylprednisolone.

However, there were some deteriorations in his condition, including intensifying upper thoracic pain, progressing hypoaesthesia, dysaesthesia, numbness, and ice cold sensation. A second lumbar puncture was performed which was negative. Doxycycline and human immunoglobulin were added to his medication. The treatment resulted in significant improvement, and he was able to walk unassisted and all symptoms fully resolved within three weeks. His HIV testing was positive and repeated before discharge.

Outcome:
The patient was discharged after completing 21 days of ceftriaxone, 7 days of methylprednisolone, 7 days of doxycycline, and 5 days of human immunoglobulin. He was asymptomatic at his first follow-up appointment and his CD4 count was above 400/mm3. But after a year of follow-up, the count went down to below 400/mm3, and his viral load was stabilized around 130,000 cp/mL. He started his HAART with abacavir/lamivudine and efavirenz six months later, which led to an increase in the CD4+ cell count and undetectable viral load.

Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Reason for Hospital Stay: Sudden swelling of the spinal cord
Final Diagnosis: HIV infection

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the Emergency Room because he could not urinate and had weak legs. He had recently come back from working in Angola. Before his trip, he got shots to protect against yellow fever, polio, typhoid fever, and a type of meningitis. At first, doctors thought he had a spider bite and treated him for that. Later, he came back with fever, cough, trouble breathing, muscle pain, and chest pain. After many tests, doctors found he had sudden swelling of the spinal cord. He started taking three medicines called ceftriaxone, ganciclovir, and methylprednisolone.

His condition got worse for a time. He had more upper back pain, less feeling in parts of his body, strange skin sensations, numbness, and felt very cold. Doctors did a second spinal tap to check the fluid around his spine. The test showed nothing unusual. They added two more treatments called doxycycline and human immunoglobulin. The treatment worked well. Within three weeks, he could walk on his own and all his symptoms went away. His HIV test came back positive. Doctors repeated the test before he left the hospital.

Outcome:
The patient left the hospital after finishing 21 days of ceftriaxone, 7 days of methylprednisolone, 7 days of doxycycline, and 5 days of human immunoglobulin. At his first check-up visit, he had no symptoms. His CD4 count, which measures immune system health, was above 400. After one year, this count dropped below 400. His viral load, which measures the amount of virus in the blood, stayed around 130,000. Six months later, he started HIV treatment with three medicines called abacavir, lamivudine, and efavirenz. This treatment raised his CD4 count and lowered the virus to levels too small to measure.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Overview:
The patient is a 58-year-old male who presented with a painless, asymptomatic lesion on the right upper lid that he has had for 15 years. He had also been diagnosed with right lower lid senile entropion for which an entropion repair was scheduled. 

History and Physical:
On examination, the eyelid nodule was found to be firm, and not adherent to the overlying skin. The size of the nodule was 5mm x 5mm and was mobile. The patient did not show any evidence of skin ulceration, but the eyelid skin had an extra fold due to dermatochalasis. Apart from these findings, the rest of the ocular examination was unremarkable.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent an entropion repair procedure as well as an excision of the eyelid nodule which the patient had requested. The postoperative period was uneventful. 

Outpatient Follow-up:
At the last follow-up appointment, 3 months later, the patient did not show any evidence of recurrence.

Diagnosis:
As per the case report, there is no formal diagnosis mentioned. 

Treatment:
The patient underwent an entropion repair along with an excision of the eyelid nodule. 

Prognosis:
The patient has a good prognosis with no evidence of recurrence at his last follow-up.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Overview:
The patient is a 58-year-old man who came in with a painless bump on his right upper eyelid. He had this bump for 15 years and it did not bother him. He was also found to have a problem with his right lower eyelid turning inward. This is common in older adults. Surgery was planned to fix the eyelid turning inward.

History and Physical:
During the exam, the bump on the eyelid felt firm. It was not attached to the skin above it. The bump was about the size of a small pea and could be moved around. The skin over the bump was not broken or damaged. The patient had extra loose skin on his eyelid, which is normal with aging. Everything else about his eyes looked normal.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery to fix the eyelid that was turning inward. The doctor also removed the bump because the patient asked for it to be taken out. The patient healed well after surgery with no problems.

Outpatient Follow-up:
The patient came back for a checkup 3 months later. The bump had not come back.

Diagnosis:
Based on the medical report, no specific diagnosis was given for the bump.

Treatment:
The patient had surgery to fix the eyelid turning inward. The bump on the eyelid was also removed during the same surgery.

Prognosis:
The patient is expected to do well. At his last visit, there were no signs that the bump had come back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Admission Date: [Admission Date]
Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Diagnosis: Follicular Lymphoma

Hospital Course:

A 72-year-old man presented with an asymptomatic nontender abdominal mass and was found to have a bulky mesenteric mass with a maximum standardized uptake value on PET/CT. Excisional biopsy confirmed the diagnosis of Follicular Lymphoma, which was treated with bendamustine plus rituximab. The patient was followed up on an outpatient basis and has had no recurrent disease to date.

Medical History:

The patient did not have any significant past medical history.

Discussion:

Primary mesenteric tumors are rare, and Follicular Lymphoma is the most common type. The imaging patterns of mesenteric lymphoma at CT can vary, and while the sandwich sign may suggest malignant tumors, its diagnostic performance is unknown. Although the disease may be indolent, vigilance is necessary to ensure early diagnosis. In case of a solid mesenteric tumor, malignant lymphoma should be considered as a priority.

Recommendations:

The patient is advised to continue follow-up with the oncologist and promptly report any symptoms or complications. 

Primary Care Physician:
[Name]
[Contact Information]

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Admission Date: [Admission Date]
Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Diagnosis: Follicular Lymphoma (a slow-growing cancer of the white blood cells)

Hospital Course:

A 72-year-old man came in with a painless lump in his belly that did not bother him. Doctors found a large mass in the middle part of his belly using special scans. A small piece of the mass was removed and tested. The tests showed the patient had Follicular Lymphoma. The patient was treated with two cancer medicines called bendamustine and rituximab. The patient came back for check-ups after leaving the hospital. So far, the cancer has not come back.

Medical History:

The patient did not have any major health problems before this.

Discussion:

Tumors that start in the middle part of the belly are not common. Follicular Lymphoma is the most common type of these tumors. Scans can show different patterns for this kind of cancer. Some signs on scans may point to cancer, but they are not always reliable. This type of cancer often grows slowly, but finding it early is still very important. When doctors find a solid lump in the middle of the belly, they should first think about this type of cancer.

Recommendations:

The patient should keep seeing the cancer doctor for regular check-ups. The patient should tell the doctor right away about any new symptoms or problems.

Primary Care Physician:
[Name]
[Contact Information]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient was diagnosed with annular alopecia areata (AA) based on clinical examination and histopathological findings. The patient presented with asymptomatic loss of hair in an annular pattern at multiple sites over the scalp for 2 months. Hair loss was more pronounced at the periphery compared to the center of the patch. The patient had no history of similar illness in family members and no history of drug intake or trauma.

On examination, there were multiple well-defined annular patches of non-scarring alopecia ranging in size from 2 cm x 3 cm to 4 cm x 4 cm present at the parietal and occipital region of the scalp. The area was smooth with no associated skin changes. Systemic examination was within normal limits. Potassium hydroxide (KOH) examination of hair showed no fungal elements, and fungal culture with Sabouraud's media at 4 weeks revealed no growth.

Histopathological examination from the hairless region showed a few vellus follicles in mid dermis with a sparse perifollicular lymphocytic infiltrate. The dermis and subcutis also showed several follicular stellae representing former follicular papillae. The total number of follicles was substantially reduced, with no terminal follicles.

The patient was treated with intralesional injections of trimacinolone acetonide (5mg/ml) every 4 weeks, with remarkable improvement in the lesions after 3 months (3 intralesional injections) of follow-up.

The patient was discharged with recommendations for follow-up care and to continue treatment with intralesional injections of trimacinolone acetonide. The patient was advised to schedule appointments with their healthcare provider every 4 weeks for follow-up care and to monitor for any signs of recurrent alopecia.

Discharge Summary:

The patient was diagnosed with a type of hair loss called alopecia areata. This was confirmed by a physical exam and by looking at skin samples under a microscope. The patient came in because hair had been falling out in ring-shaped patches on the scalp for 2 months. The hair loss was worse around the edges of the patches than in the middle. No one else in the family had this problem. The patient had not taken any medicines or had any injury that could cause this.

During the exam, the doctor found several ring-shaped bald patches on the back and sides of the scalp. The patches ranged from about 1 inch by 1.5 inches to about 1.5 inches by 1.5 inches. The skin in these areas was smooth with no other changes. The rest of the body exam was normal. Tests for fungus on the hair came back negative. A fungus growth test done over 4 weeks also showed no fungus.

A small piece of skin was removed and studied under a microscope. It showed only a few tiny hair follicles in the middle layer of skin. There were some immune cells around the hair follicles. The deeper skin layers showed signs of where hair follicles used to be. The total number of hair follicles was much lower than normal. There were no full-sized hair follicles left.

The patient was treated with steroid injections into the bald patches every 4 weeks. The steroid used was triamcinolone acetonide at a strength of 5 milligrams per milliliter. After 3 months and 3 injections, the hair loss improved a lot.

The patient was sent home with instructions to continue getting steroid injections into the affected areas. The patient should schedule appointments with their healthcare provider every 4 weeks. These visits are to continue treatment and to watch for any new hair loss.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Medical History: A 12-year-old female patient was admitted with a history of polyuria, polydipsia, and secondary amenorrhea.
Clinical Findings: A complete preoperative endocrinological assessment revealed mild prolactin hormone elevation, hypothyroidism, and hypocortisolism. Neuroradiological investigation showed a large mass in the sella with compression of the optic chiasm and extending into the third ventricle.
Diagnosis: A craniopharyngioma or a GCT were the suspected diagnoses. However, the symptoms presented by the patient suggested a germinoma.
Procedure: The patient underwent an extended endoscopic endonasal transtuberculum/transplanum approach for tumor removal.
Postoperative Course: The patient demonstrated a significant improvement in her condition with light perception reported in the left eye and improvement in right eye temporal hemianopsia. There were no postoperative complications.
Postoperative Follow-up: Serum levels of AFP, and beta-HCG were checked, suggesting confirmation of germinoma malignancy. The patient was referred to a pediatric oncologist for further treatment, which includes adjunctive chemotherapy and radiation therapy.
Condition at Discharge: The patient was discharged in a normal condition but still receiving desmopressin acetate for diabetes insipidus treatment.
Prognosis: The patient's condition remains optimistic, and a follow-up appointment is necessary.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Medical History: A 12-year-old female patient was admitted. She had been urinating a lot, feeling very thirsty, and her periods had stopped.

Clinical Findings: Doctors did hormone tests before surgery. The tests showed slightly high levels of a hormone called prolactin. The tests also showed her thyroid gland was underactive. Her body was not making enough stress hormones. Brain scans showed a large growth in the lower part of the brain. This growth was pressing on the nerves that control vision. It was also reaching into a fluid-filled space in the brain.

Diagnosis: Doctors first thought it might be one of two types of brain tumors. But based on the patient's symptoms, they believed it was a type called germinoma.

Procedure: The patient had surgery to remove the tumor. Doctors went through the nose using a small camera and tools. This allowed them to reach the tumor without opening the skull.

Postoperative Course: The patient got much better after surgery. She could see light with her left eye. Her side vision in her right eye also improved. There were no problems after surgery.

Postoperative Follow-up: Blood tests checked for certain markers. These results confirmed the tumor was a type of cancer called germinoma. The patient was sent to a children's cancer doctor for more treatment. She will need chemotherapy and radiation therapy.

Condition at Discharge: The patient left the hospital in normal condition. She is still taking a medicine called desmopressin acetate. This treats a condition where her body cannot control water balance.

Prognosis: The outlook for the patient is hopeful. She will need to come back for a follow-up visit.

The patient was admitted with left chest discomfort, which was found to be due to a left lung mass invading the heart and causing pleural effusion. The patient had a medical history of encephalitis in childhood and a smoking history of 20 cigarettes per day for 40 years. On physical examination, the patient had an anemic and edematous face with weak respiratory sounds in the left lung. The patient had elevated levels of C-reactive protein, leukocytes, and calcium, and decreased levels of hemoglobin and potassium. The cancer markers such as carcinogenic antigen, neuron-specific enolase, squamous cell carcinoma, and cytokeratin-19 fragment were all elevated. 

Diagnostic Tests:
The thoracic radiography showed cardiomegaly, the computed tomography showed a left lung mass with invasion of the heart and pleural effusion, and the MRI revealed a large mass surrounding the heart. T
