DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Age: 47 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient was admitted to our clinic due to severe menorrhagia and referred for MRI screening to evaluate her suitability for MRgFUS treatment. MRI showed a single intramural fibroid of 560 cc, and she underwent MRgFUS treatment using the ExAblate 2000 system. After the procedure, she reported no complications and was discharged.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient had a significant improvement in her symptoms at the 3-month follow-up visit. Her Symptom Severity Score reduced from 56 points to 28 points, and the fibroid showed 38% shrinkage. At 1-year follow-up, the patient reported no symptoms, and her Symptom Severity Score was reduced to 13 points, which is considered as nonsymptomatic.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Severe menorrhagia due to a single intramural fibroid

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient is advised to monitor her menstrual cycle and report if symptoms reoccur. She is also instructed to follow-up with her gynecologist for further evaluation.

DISCHARGE DISPOSITION:
The patient was discharged from our clinic in stable condition with no complications.

FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
Severe menorrhagia due to a single intramural fibroid.

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN:
[REDACTED]

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Age: 47 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came to our clinic because of very heavy menstrual bleeding. She had an MRI scan to see if a special treatment would work for her. The MRI found one noncancerous growth in the wall of her uterus. The growth was about the size of a large grapefruit. She received treatment using focused ultrasound waves guided by MRI. This treatment uses heat to shrink the growth without surgery. After the treatment, she had no problems and went home.

FOLLOW-UP:
At her 3-month checkup, the patient felt much better. Her symptom score dropped from 56 points to 28 points. The growth shrank by 38 percent. At her 1-year checkup, the patient had no symptoms at all. Her symptom score dropped to 13 points. This score means she no longer has bothersome symptoms.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Very heavy menstrual bleeding caused by one noncancerous growth in the uterus wall.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should keep track of her monthly periods. She should tell her doctor if her symptoms come back. She should also schedule a visit with her women's health doctor for more checkups.

DISCHARGE DISPOSITION:
The patient left our clinic in good condition with no problems.

FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
Very heavy menstrual bleeding caused by one noncancerous growth in the uterus wall.

[REDACTED]

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 44
Gender: Female
Chief Complaint: Painful oral ulcer

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with a painful oral ulcer, which had developed 6 weeks prior. She had a history of aphthous stomatitis and received systemic steroid treatment. However, her condition worsened with an erythematous maculopapular rash on her body, conjunctival injection, fever, diarrhea, and weight loss. Leukocytosis and eosinophilia were noted. Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) was suspected, and she was treated with systemic steroids, which showed slight improvement in skin condition but unresponsiveness in stomatitis.
A skin biopsy showed interface dermatitis and subepidermal blister formation with basal vacuolization consistent with PNP. Additional tests showed IgG deposition on normal human skin, mouse bladder epithelium, and mouse lung epithelium. The patient was ultimately diagnosed with stage IV follicular lymphoma with BO and invasive pulmonary aspergillosis. 
Chemotherapy resulted in a complete remission of lymphoma, which was confirmed through positron emission tomography. The patient's stomatitis and skin rash were well-maintained with a low-dose steroid therapy of 5mg/day for 8 months. However, she developed BO and invasive pulmonary aspergillosis, which led to respiratory failure. 

Course of Treatment:
Treatment included chemotherapy with rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisolone, which resulted in complete remission of the patient's lymphoma. The patient was also treated with a low-dose steroid therapy of 5mg/day, which maintained the improvement of stomatitis and skin rash. 

Follow-Up Care:
No specific follow-up care is necessary since the patient passed away from respiratory failure. 

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with PNP, which was later associated with stage IV follicular lymphoma with BO and invasive pulmonary aspergillosis. 

[REDACTED] Hospital
[REDACTED] Medical Center

clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 44
Gender: Female
Main Problem: Painful mouth sore

Hospital Stay:
The patient came to the hospital with a painful mouth sore that started 6 weeks before. She had a history of recurring mouth sores and was given steroid medicine that works throughout the body. Her condition got worse and she developed a red, bumpy rash on her body. She also had red eyes, fever, loose stools, and lost weight. Blood tests showed high levels of white blood cells and a type of white blood cell called eosinophils. Doctors thought she might have Stevens-Johnson Syndrome, a serious skin reaction. She was treated with steroids that work throughout the body. Her skin got a little better but her mouth sores did not improve.
A small sample of skin was tested and showed damage where the skin layers meet. It also showed blisters forming under the top skin layer. These findings matched a condition called paraneoplastic pemphigus. More tests showed that her immune system was attacking normal skin and organ tissue. The patient was finally found to have stage IV follicular lymphoma, a type of blood cancer that had spread. She also had a lung condition called bronchiolitis obliterans and a serious fungal lung infection called invasive pulmonary aspergillosis.
Cancer treatment led to complete disappearance of the lymphoma. This was confirmed through a special imaging scan that detects cancer activity. The patient's mouth sores and skin rash stayed under control with a low dose of steroid medicine for 8 months. However, she developed the lung condition and serious fungal lung infection. These caused her lungs to stop working.

Treatment Given:
Treatment included cancer-fighting drugs called rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisolone. These medicines led to complete disappearance of the patient's lymphoma. The patient also took a low dose of steroid medicine at 5mg per day. This kept her mouth sores and skin rash under control.

Follow-Up Care:
No follow-up care is needed because the patient died from lung failure.

Diagnosis:
The patient was found to have paraneoplastic pemphigus, a rare skin blistering disease. This was linked to stage IV follicular lymphoma, a blood cancer that had spread. She also had bronchiolitis obliterans, a lung condition, and invasive pulmonary aspergillosis, a serious fungal lung infection.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 22-year-old female with no significant past medical history who presented with altered mental status, acute psychosis with suicidal ideation, decreased oral intake, and reduced urine output. On admission, she was placed on an involuntary hold by a psychiatrist and was noted to be delirious, uncooperative, agitated, demanding, and impulsive. Initial imaging did not show acute intracranial abnormalities, and she was started on sodium valproate for a presumed bipolar disorder with psychotic features in addition to olanzapine and clonazepam.

Diagnosis and Treatment:
The working diagnosis at this time was considered to be encephalopathy, sepsis vs systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) from aspiration and acute hypoxic respiratory failure. She had an extensive infectious, metabolic, autoimmune, and neurologic workup. The initial panels have returned negative. However, her CSF differential showed a high WBC count with lymphocytic pleocytosis. Further investigation showed elevated titers of anti-NMDA antibodies evaluated by Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay. Anti-NMDA antibodies were detected in both serum and CSF. She was started on IV steroids, IV immunoglobulin (IVIG), and plasma exchange.

Hospital Course Post-Surgery:
Following surgical intervention, the patient was started on high dose steroids, IVIG 2 rounds (with one month interval) of 10% IVIG 17.5 g, and repeated cycles of plasma exchange with gradual improvement in symptoms three weeks post-surgery as evidenced by restoration of sleep-wake cycles, increased frequency of alertness, ability to more consistently follow commands, decreased frequency of fevers, improvement of blood pressure, and tachycardia.

Discharge:
After two and a half months post-surgery, the patient was discharged with full return of function. She returned for follow-up six months after surgery, before returning to college, this time she reported feeling that her memory and movement had improved, and she felt more like her normal self. Her neurological and psychiatric workup was completely within normal. It was considered that she did not need future treatment or follow-up as she was completely recovered.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 22-year-old female with no major health problems in the past. She came to the hospital because she was confused, seeing or hearing things that were not real, thinking about hurting herself, not eating well, and not urinating much. When she arrived, a mental health doctor required her to stay in the hospital against her will. She was confused, would not cooperate, restless, demanding, and acting without thinking. Brain scans did not show any sudden problems. Doctors started her on a mood-stabilizing medicine called sodium valproate because they thought she had a mood disorder with symptoms of seeing or hearing things that are not real. She also received olanzapine and clonazepam.

Diagnosis and Treatment:
At this time, doctors thought she had brain swelling, an infection spreading through her body or her body fighting an infection, choking on food or liquid that went into her lungs, and sudden breathing problems from low oxygen. Doctors ran many tests to check for infection, body chemistry problems, immune system problems, and brain problems. The first tests came back normal. However, the fluid around her brain and spine showed a high number of white blood cells, mostly a type that fights infection. More testing found high levels of harmful proteins that attack a part of the brain called NMDA receptors. These harmful proteins were found in both her blood and the fluid around her brain and spine. Doctors gave her steroids through a vein, medicine to boost her immune system through a vein, and a treatment that cleans harmful proteins from her blood.

Hospital Course Post-Surgery:
After surgery, the patient received high doses of steroids. She also received two rounds of immune-boosting medicine through a vein, with one month between each round, at a dose of 17.5 grams. She had repeated blood-cleaning treatments. Her symptoms slowly got better three weeks after surgery. She started sleeping and waking at normal times. She was awake more often. She could follow directions more often. She had fewer fevers. Her blood pressure got better. Her fast heartbeat got better.

Discharge:
Two and a half months after surgery, the patient left the hospital and was back to normal. She came back for a checkup six months after surgery, before going back to college. She said her memory and movement had gotten better. She felt more like her normal self. All brain and mental health tests were normal. Doctors decided she did not need any more treatment or checkups because she had fully recovered.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [patient name]
Age: 2 years and 10 months
Gender: Female
Medical Record Number: [patient's medical record number]
Length of Stay: [length of stay]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with complaints of enlarged abdominal volume. Physical examination revealed hepatomegaly and liver steatosis, and laboratory tests showed elevated levels of ALT, AST, cholesterol, HDL, and triglycerides. The patient was diagnosed with a possible glycogen storage disease and underwent a liver biopsy, which showed diffuse enlargement of hepatocyte volume with intense microvesicular steatosis and ballooning, as well as deposits of triglycerides in Kupffer cells and portal macrophages, consistent with Wolman Disease/CESD. Acid lipase activity in leukocytes tested low, consistent with a biochemical diagnosis of LAL deficiency. Electron microscopy revealed massive distension of hepatocytes due to a large amount of round triglyceride deposits, consistent with the previous morphological diagnosis. 

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient is diagnosed with Wolman Disease/CESD.

Consults:
The patient was seen by a hepatologist and a medical geneticist.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient's general health was good, and liver function was preserved at the time of discharge.

Discharge Medications:
None specified in case report.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient's parents were informed of the diagnosis and provided with appropriate genetic counseling. Follow-up care with a hepatologist and medical geneticist was arranged.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [patient name]
Age: 2 years and 10 months
Gender: Female
Medical Record Number: [patient's medical record number]
Length of Stay: [length of stay]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital because her belly was bigger than normal. The doctor's exam showed her liver was larger than it should be. Her liver also had too much fat in it. Blood tests showed high levels of liver enzymes and fats in her blood. The doctors thought she might have a disease that affects how the body stores sugar. The patient had a small piece of liver removed to test it. The test showed her liver cells were very large and full of fat droplets. Fat was also found in special cleaning cells in the liver. These findings matched a condition called Wolman Disease or CESD. Another test checked a certain enzyme in her white blood cells. The enzyme level was low. This confirmed that the patient does not have enough of an enzyme called acid lipase. A special test using a powerful microscope showed her liver cells were stretched out. They were filled with round fat droplets. This matched what the other tests showed.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient has Wolman Disease or CESD. This is a rare condition where the body cannot break down certain fats properly.

Consults:
The patient was seen by a liver specialist and a doctor who studies inherited conditions.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient was in good overall health when she left the hospital. Her liver was still working well.

Discharge Medications:
None listed in the report.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient's parents were told about the diagnosis. They received information about how this condition runs in families. The patient will need to see a liver specialist and a genetics doctor for ongoing care.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Record Number: [Redacted]
Sex: Male   Age: 47
Date of Admission: [Redacted]   Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Length of Stay: [Redacted]

Admission Diagnosis:
The patient was admitted for malaise, fever, nausea, vomiting and left chest pain with oppressive radiation toward the neck.

Hospital Course:
Upon admission, the patient was previously treated with antibiotics and underwent a left thoracostomy due to pleural effusion. The chest radiograph depicted a pleural effusion in the lung base. On chest auscultation and percussion, air entry was reduced and dullness was perceived in the left lung base. Laboratory tests revealed an elevated lactate dehydrogenase level and the pleural fluid analysis indicated the presence of Klebsiella pneumoniae, chylous effusion and helminth larvae. A chest CT scan showed mediastinal adenomegaly, loculated pleural effusion in the left side, slight pericardial effusion and cardiomegaly. The pleural effusion was classified as exudate based on the laboratory findings.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with pleural effusion, chylous effusion and helminth larvae infection.

Discharge Medications:
Not applicable

Follow-up Appointments:
The patient is advised to follow up with the physician recommended by the hospital.

Instructions to Patient:
The patient is advised to continue monitoring his symptoms and follow-up with proper medical care. 

Summary:
The patient was admitted to our hospital due to malaise, fever, nausea, vomiting, and left chest pain that was oppressive and radiated to his neck. The patient underwent a left thoracostomy after a chest radiograph showed pleural effusion in the lung base. Laboratory tests revealed that the pleural effusion was classified as exudate and the pleural fluid analysis was positive for chylous effusion and helminth larvae. A chest CT scan confirmed mediastinal adenomegaly and loculated pleural effusion on the left, cardiomegaly, and slight pericardial effusion. The patient was treated surgically and medically and is advised to follow-up with a physician.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Record Number: [Redacted]
Sex: Male   Age: 47
Date of Admission: [Redacted]   Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Length of Stay: [Redacted]

Admission Diagnosis:
The patient was admitted for feeling unwell, fever, nausea, vomiting and left chest pain. The pain felt like pressure and spread toward the neck.

Hospital Course:
Upon admission, the patient had already been treated with antibiotics. The patient also had a tube placed in the left side of the chest to drain fluid buildup around the lung. A chest x-ray showed fluid around the base of the left lung. When the doctor listened to and tapped on the chest, less air was moving and there was a dull sound at the left lung base. Blood tests showed high levels of an enzyme that can signal tissue damage. Testing of the fluid found a type of bacteria called Klebsiella pneumoniae, a milky fluid from the lymph system, and parasitic worm young. A detailed scan of the chest showed swollen lymph nodes in the chest, pockets of fluid around the left lung, a small amount of fluid around the heart, and an enlarged heart. Lab results showed the fluid was caused by infection or disease rather than pressure buildup.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with fluid around the lung, milky fluid from the lymph system, and parasitic worm infection.

Discharge Medications:
Not applicable

Follow-up Appointments:
The patient is advised to follow up with the doctor recommended by the hospital.

Instructions to Patient:
The patient is advised to keep watching his symptoms and follow up with proper medical care.

Summary:
The patient was admitted to our hospital for feeling unwell, fever, nausea, vomiting, and left chest pain that felt like pressure and spread to his neck. The patient had a tube placed in the left side of the chest after a chest x-ray showed fluid around the base of the lung. Lab tests showed the fluid was caused by infection or disease. Testing of the fluid found milky fluid from the lymph system and parasitic worm young. A detailed chest scan confirmed swollen lymph nodes in the chest, pockets of fluid around the left lung, an enlarged heart, and a small amount of fluid around the heart. The patient was treated with surgery and medicine and is advised to follow up with a doctor.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient ID: [insert patient ID]
Name: [insert patient name]
Age: 47
Gender: Male
Admission Date: [insert admission date]
Discharge Date: [insert discharge date]

Diagnosis:
- Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM) in left frontal lobe with recurrence

Hospital Course:
- Patient presented with Broca's aphasia and memory disturbance
- MRI revealed a 4-cm-diameter ring-enhanced cystic mass lesion with perifocal edema in the left frontal lobe
- Partial resection was performed to remove the tumor; a diagnosis of GBM was confirmed histopathologically
- Postoperatively, the patient underwent radiotherapy and concomitant chemotherapy with TMZ
- Six months after the initial operation, the patient experienced worsening symptoms and a follow-up MRI revealed tumor recurrence
- Gamma knife stereotactic radiosurgery was performed for the recurrent lesion
- BEV was intravenously administered on an every-2-week schedule, with adjuvant TMZ continued
- Two months of chemotherapy with BEV plus TMZ led to complete remission
- The patient remained in a medically stable state, with no evidence of progression on MRI
- Recurrence was detected 17 months after initiating BEV, and the patient was still followed up at our outpatient clinic
- Twenty-two months after completing administration of BEV, MRI revealed rapid progression of the tumor, with multiple enhanced lesions detected in both hemispheres

Condition on Discharge:
- Patient's condition is extremely serious, and additional therapies are necessary to manage the patient's symptoms

Recommendations:
- The patient should continue to receive close medical follow-up and treatment to manage symptoms
- Additional therapeutic options should be considered to address the patient's GBM recurrence and progression
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient ID: [insert patient ID]
Name: [insert patient name]
Age: 47
Gender: Male
Admission Date: [insert admission date]
Discharge Date: [insert discharge date]

Diagnosis:
The patient has an aggressive brain cancer called glioblastoma in the left front part of the brain. The cancer has come back.

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with trouble speaking and memory problems. A brain scan showed a 4-centimeter fluid-filled tumor with swelling in the left front part of the brain. Doctors removed part of the tumor through surgery. Lab tests confirmed it was an aggressive brain cancer. After surgery, the patient received radiation and a cancer drug called TMZ at the same time. Six months after the first surgery, the patient's symptoms got worse. A new brain scan showed the cancer had come back. Doctors used a focused radiation treatment called Gamma Knife to treat the returned cancer. The patient also received a drug called BEV through an IV every two weeks. The patient kept taking TMZ as well. After two months of treatment with both BEV and TMZ, the cancer was no longer visible. The patient stayed in stable health, and brain scans showed no cancer growth. Seventeen months after starting BEV, the cancer came back again. The patient continued visits at our clinic. Twenty-two months after finishing BEV treatment, a brain scan showed the cancer had spread quickly. Multiple tumors were now found on both sides of the brain.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient's condition is very serious. More treatments are needed to help manage the patient's symptoms.

Recommendations:
The patient should continue regular doctor visits and treatment to manage symptoms. Doctors should look at other treatment options to address the cancer that has returned and spread.

input:
Discharge Summary

Admit Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Patient Name: [NAME]
Sex: Female
Age: 55

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital due to acute onset of hoarseness, voice fatigue, and effort dyspnea. The patient was diagnosed with HHT and presented with characteristic vascular lesions in the oral cavity. A fiberolaryngoscopy and a phoniatric examination were performed, which revealed unchanged mobility and morphology of the right vocal fold and paresis of the left vocal fold. CT and MRI of head, neck, and chest showed no pathologic findings.

The patient was treated for 12 months for unilateral paresis of the vocal fold as idiopathic by means of extensive rehabilitation, but no improvement was observed. Due to persistent symptoms, the patient underwent type I thyroplasty (medialization) under local anesthesia. The surgical procedure was successful, and the patient was advised to avoid vocal effort or extensive physical activity for about 2 weeks post-surgery.

After surgery, the patient's voice quality improved significantly, with most parameters defining amplitude and frequency normalizing. The Voice Handicap Index (VHI) also improved significantly.

The patient is on regular follow-up in the outpatient setting, and during 1-year follow-up, there was no recurrence of symptoms.

Summary:
The patient presented with hoarseness, voice fatigue, and effort dyspnea. Due to persistent symptoms, the patient underwent type I thyroplasty (medialization) after extensive rehabilitation failed to improve the vocal fold paresis. The surgery was successful, and after two weeks of recovery, the patient was discharged with significant improvement in voice quality and the VHI.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Admit Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Patient Name: [NAME]
Sex: Female
Age: 55

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital because of sudden hoarseness, voice tiredness, and trouble breathing during activity. The patient was found to have HHT, a condition that causes abnormal blood vessels. The patient had visible blood vessel spots in the mouth, which is common with this condition. Doctors used a small camera to look at the throat and tested the voice. They found that the right vocal cord moved normally, but the left vocal cord was weak and not moving well. Scans of the head, neck, and chest showed no other problems.

The patient received treatment for the weak vocal cord for 12 months. The cause was unknown, so doctors tried voice therapy and exercises. This treatment did not help. Because the problems continued, the patient had surgery called medialization. This surgery moves the weak vocal cord closer to the middle so it works better. The patient was awake during the surgery with numbing medicine. The surgery went well. The patient was told not to strain the voice or do hard exercise for about 2 weeks after surgery.

After surgery, the patient's voice got much better. Tests showed that voice strength and pitch returned to normal levels. The patient also reported that voice problems affected daily life much less than before.

The patient has been coming back for regular check-ups. After one year, the voice problems have not come back.

Summary:
The patient came in with hoarseness, voice tiredness, and trouble breathing during activity. Voice therapy did not help the weak vocal cord. The patient then had surgery to move the vocal cord to a better position. The surgery worked well. After two weeks of rest, the patient went home with a much better voice and fewer problems in daily life.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Admitting Diagnosis: Hepatitis B virus-related liver cirrhosis with portal vein thrombosis and portal systemic encephalopathy.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent donor liver transplantation and was admitted to the surgical intensive care unit for postoperative care. Medications included aspirin and nafamostat mesilate to prevent thrombosis, prostaglandin E1 to vasodilate the anastomosed vessels, tacrolimus and prednisolone for immunosuppression, and ampicillin/sulbactam, fluconazole, and nystatin for prophylaxis against infection.

On postoperative day 6, the patient showed deteriorated mental status and decreased motor power on both upper extremities (grade III) and lower extremities (grade I). A neurologic examination was performed by a neurologist. Over the next 6 days, the patient experienced seizures with abnormal mouth chewing movements, which were controlled by the administration of antiepileptic drugs (AED). A brain computed tomography (CT) scan on pod 15 showed that the existing intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) had decreased slightly.

Disposition: The patient was discharged two weeks later and demonstrated movement in blinking his eyes and shaking his head. Follow-up brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed a 50% decrease in the size of the ICH three months after liver transplantation. 

Diagnosis at discharge: Hepatitis B virus-related liver cirrhosis with portal vein thrombosis, portal systemic encephalopathy, and intracerebral hemorrhage.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admitting Diagnosis: Liver scarring caused by Hepatitis B virus with a blood clot in a major liver vein and confusion caused by liver problems.

Hospital Course:
The patient received a liver transplant from a donor. After surgery, the patient stayed in the intensive care unit for close monitoring. The patient took aspirin and nafamostat mesilate to prevent blood clots. The patient also took prostaglandin E1 to widen the connected blood vessels. Tacrolimus and prednisolone helped prevent the body from rejecting the new liver. Ampicillin/sulbactam, fluconazole, and nystatin helped prevent infections.

On day 6 after surgery, the patient became confused and had trouble moving. The arms had weak movement and the legs had very weak movement. A brain doctor checked the patient. Over the next 6 days, the patient had seizures with unusual chewing movements. Seizure medicine helped control these episodes. A brain scan on day 15 after surgery showed that bleeding in the brain had gotten slightly smaller.

Disposition: The patient left the hospital two weeks later. At that time, the patient could blink and shake the head. A follow-up brain scan three months after the liver transplant showed the brain bleeding had shrunk by half.

Diagnosis at discharge: Liver scarring caused by Hepatitis B virus with a blood clot in a major liver vein, confusion caused by liver problems, and bleeding in the brain.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 46-year-old woman with a history of appendectomy and mass lesion excision from her right breast, was admitted to our hospital due to pain in the neck region and numbness in her right arm. Physical examination revealed pain at the cervical region, trigger points bilaterally on upper parts of the trapezius muscle on palpation, and restricted movements in all directions. The Spurling test on the right side was considered positive, while upper extremity muscle strength was complete, and no pathology was detected during sensory examination. Deep tendon reflexes were bilaterally normoactive, and no pathological reflex was found.

Diagnostic Assessment:

The patient was referred to our clinic for cervical magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) based on the findings of the present complaints and physical examination. Cervical MRI showed vascular structures with signal void areas that obliterated the right neural foramen at the level of C2-C3 articular disc, compressed the right C3 nerve root, and attracted our attention with curves appearance. Although signal loss consistent with degenerative changes in cervical intervertebral articular discs was observed, no pathological findings were found on both sides occupying other neural foraminal areas. Contrast-enhanced lesion was not observed after contrast injection. Contrast-enhanced MR angiography of the cervical region revealed a significant loop formation in the distal extracranial segment of the right VA. No pathological findings were found in the radius health strikes (RDUS) examination performed following MRI.

Discharge:

The patient was discharged after completion of diagnostic evaluation. The treatment plan was discussed with the patient for pain management and physical therapy. Close follow-up and monitoring of symptoms were recommended. Further evaluation was advised for any recurrence or progression of symptoms. The patient was advised to maintain good posture and avoid repetitive stress in the neck region.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 46-year-old woman. She previously had her appendix removed and a lump taken out of her right breast. She came to the hospital because of neck pain and numbness in her right arm. During the exam, the doctor found pain in her neck area. She had sore spots on both sides of her upper shoulder muscles when pressed. She could not move her neck fully in any direction. A test for nerve problems on the right side of her neck showed a problem. Her arm strength was normal on both sides. Her sense of touch was normal. Her reflexes were normal on both sides. No other nerve problems were found.

Diagnostic Assessment:

The patient was sent for a special scan of her neck called an MRI. The MRI showed blood vessels in an unusual spot on the right side of her neck between the second and third neck bones. These blood vessels were pressing on a nerve on the right side. The blood vessels had a curved shape. The cushions between her neck bones showed normal wear and tear for her age. No other problem areas were found pressing on nerves. No unusual growths were seen after dye was given. A second scan looking at blood vessels showed an unusual loop in an artery on the right side of her neck. An ultrasound test of her blood vessels showed no problems.

Discharge:

The patient went home after all tests were done. The care team talked with her about ways to manage her pain and exercises to help her neck. She should come back for regular checkups and watch for any changes in her symptoms. She should see a doctor again if her symptoms come back or get worse. She was told to keep good posture and avoid repeated strain on her neck.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Diagnosis: Central giant cell tumor (CGCT)

Hospital Course: 
The patient was admitted to our department's consultation for specialized care due to painless slow-growing swelling of the jaw that had persisted for 3 years with dental loss. Clinical examination revealed large tender swelling with a protruding lower lip. Intra-oral examination showed hard swelling of about 50 mm with displacement of teeth leading to malocclusion. Further imaging revealed an iso-dense large destructive cystic mass that was confirmed as a central giant cell tumor (CGCT) through a biopsy. 

The patient underwent a surgical intervention by the chief professor of our department. A unilocular cystic mass without liquid content was excised, and there was a rupture of the external wall of the mandible cortical without iatrogenic fracture. The patient received antibiotics and antalgics daily for 8 days and recovered well postoperatively. 

Recommendations:
The patient was advised for oral rehabilitation and entered into routine follow-up 3, 6, and 12 months later with no signs of recurrence. 

Summary: 
The 8-year-old girl presenting with central giant cell tumor (CGCT) underwent surgical intervention and exhibited no signs of recurrence during routine follow-up 3, 6, and 12 months later. She was advised for oral rehabilitation following the surgery.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Diagnosis: Central giant cell tumor, a non-cancerous growth in the jaw bone

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our department for special care. The patient had a painless lump on the jaw that grew slowly over 3 years. The patient also lost some teeth during this time. The doctor found a large, sore lump that made the lower lip stick out. Inside the mouth, there was a hard lump about 2 inches wide. This lump pushed the teeth out of place so they did not line up right. Pictures of the jaw showed a large growth that was destroying bone and had a hollow space inside. A small tissue sample proved it was a central giant cell tumor, a non-cancerous growth.

The head doctor of our department did the surgery. The doctor removed a hollow growth that had no fluid inside. The outer wall of the jawbone had broken through, but the bone did not break during surgery. The patient took medicine to fight infection and control pain every day for 8 days. The patient healed well after surgery.

Recommendations:
The patient was told to get dental work to fix the teeth and mouth. The patient came back for check-up visits at 3, 6, and 12 months. There were no signs the growth came back.

Summary:
The 8-year-old girl had a central giant cell tumor, a non-cancerous growth in the jaw bone. She had surgery to remove it. At her check-up visits 3, 6, and 12 months later, there were no signs the growth came back. She was told to get dental work to fix her teeth and mouth after surgery.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 32
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Hospital: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to our facility with swelling in his right cheek, which had been present for two weeks. A malignant lesion of the oral cavity was suspected. However, a thorough examination revealed an oval, firm swelling measuring 2.5 × 2 cm in size, located 2 cm above and medial to the right angle of the mandible. An ultrasound of the cheek revealed that the swelling was a cystic lesion in the bucco-masseteric region with perilesional edema.

Further testing, including an MRI of the face and neck, was performed, and a hyperechoic intralesional structure consistent with the scolex was identified. Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) was performed but showed no evidence of malignancy. Radiographs of the chest, bilateral arms and thighs were taken, and an MRI of the head and orbit was performed, revealing no additional lesions.

The patient was advised to take analgesics and steroids for seven days, and the swelling gradually reduced in size. Deworming therapy with albendazole was also prescribed.

Discharge Medications:
Albendazole

Discharge Diagnosis:
Involvement of the facial musculature manifesting as a cystic swelling in the bucco-masseteric region consistent with cysticercosis.

Follow-Up:
The patient should follow up with his primary healthcare provider for continued monitoring.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's condition has improved since admission. He is stable and ready for discharge.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to continue taking his albendazole medication as prescribed. He should avoid tobacco use as it has been associated with cysticercosis. He is encouraged to follow up with his primary healthcare provider for continued monitoring and to report any new or concerning symptoms.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 32
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Hospital: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital with swelling in his right cheek. The swelling had been there for two weeks. Doctors first thought it might be cancer in the mouth area. After a careful exam, doctors found an oval, firm lump about 1 inch by 0.8 inches in size. The lump was located near the right side of the jawbone. An ultrasound of the cheek showed the lump was a fluid-filled sac in the cheek muscle area. There was also some swelling in the tissue around it.

More tests were done, including an MRI scan of the face and neck. The MRI found a small structure inside the sac that looked like a tapeworm head. A small sample was taken from the lump using a thin needle. The sample showed no signs of cancer. X-rays of the chest, both arms, and both thighs were taken. An MRI of the head and eye area was also done. These tests showed no other problem areas in the body.

The patient was told to take pain medicine and medicine to reduce swelling for seven days. The lump slowly got smaller. Medicine to kill the parasite was also given.

Discharge Medications:
Albendazole

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient had a parasitic infection called cysticercosis. This caused a fluid-filled sac to form in the cheek muscle area.

Follow-Up:
The patient should see his regular doctor for continued check-ups.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has gotten better since coming to the hospital. He is doing well and is ready to go home.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should keep taking his albendazole medicine as directed. He should not use tobacco because it has been linked to this type of infection. He should see his regular doctor for continued check-ups. He should tell his doctor about any new or worrying symptoms.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient presented with abdominal pain and distention, elevated white blood cell count and eosinophilia, elevated serum IgE levels, and showed moderate ascites on imaging. Biopsies revealed eosinophilic infiltration of the gastrointestinal tract, suggestive of eosinophilic gastroenteritis. The patient was treated with oral prednisone, which resulted in symptomatic improvement and a complete resolution of ascites and gastrointestinal thickening. Upon completion of the steroids, the patient remained asymptomatic and has not required any further treatment. 

Hospital Course Summary:
A 28-year-old Saudi female with no significant past medical history presented with a two-week history of abdominal pain and distention. Upon physical examination, there was diffuse tenderness and moderate ascites. Blood work showed an elevated white blood count with eosinophilia and elevated serum IgE levels. Imaging studies revealed moderate ascites with gastrointestinal thickening and mesenteric lymphadenopathy. Biopsies from an esophagogastroduodenoscopy and colonoscopy confirmed eosinophilic infiltration of the gastrointestinal tract, consistent with eosinophilic gastroenteritis. 

Treatment was initiated with oral prednisone, resulting in symptomatic improvement and complete resolution of the ascites and gastrointestinal thickening. Upon completion of the steroid regimen, the patient remained asymptomatic without further treatment required. Follow-up peripheral blood count and imaging studies confirmed sustained improvement. The patient was discharged with instructions to follow up with their primary care physician as needed.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient came in with belly pain and swelling, a high number of white blood cells, and too many allergy-related cells in the blood. Blood tests also showed high levels of a protein linked to allergies. Scans showed a moderate amount of fluid buildup in the belly. Tissue samples showed too many allergy-related cells in the stomach and intestines. This pointed to a condition called eosinophilic gastroenteritis, where allergy cells cause gut inflammation. The patient was given a steroid pill called prednisone by mouth. This helped the symptoms go away. The fluid in the belly and the thickening of the gut walls also went away completely. After finishing the steroid treatment, the patient had no more symptoms. No other treatment was needed.

Hospital Course Summary:
A 28-year-old Saudi female with no major past health problems came in with two weeks of belly pain and swelling. During the physical exam, the doctor found tenderness all over the belly and a moderate amount of fluid buildup. Blood tests showed a high white blood cell count with too many allergy-related cells. A protein linked to allergies was also high. Scans showed moderate fluid in the belly, thickened gut walls, and swollen lymph nodes near the intestines. The patient had a scope test to look at the upper gut and another to look at the lower gut. Tissue samples from these tests confirmed too many allergy-related cells in the gut. This matched a diagnosis of eosinophilic gastroenteritis.

Treatment started with a steroid pill called prednisone by mouth. The patient's symptoms got better. The fluid in the belly and the thickened gut walls went away completely. After finishing the steroid treatment, the patient had no symptoms and did not need more treatment. Follow-up blood tests and scans showed the improvement lasted. The patient was sent home with instructions to see their regular doctor if needed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 55 years
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital with pleuritic right chest pain, high fever (40ºC), and severe dyspnea. The laboratory results showed an elevated CRP, hypoxemia, and hyperlactatemia. The chest x-ray showed the presence of a right lower lung field infiltrate. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed hepatic steatosis. The patient was diagnosed with sepsis caused by right lower lung pneumonia, and started on piperacillin/tazobactam. On the third day of antibiotic treatment, the patient developed right upper quadrant pain and was diagnosed with subhepatic appendicitis with perforation and local abscess.

Treatment:

Exploratory laparoscopic surgery was performed, which revealed subhepatic appendicitis with perforation and local abscess. Infection source control was implemented, and the patient was treated with multidrug-resistant Escherichia coli, which was isolated from the drainage fluid. The antibiotic therapy was switched to meropenem, and the patient's condition rapidly improved. The patient was discharged after 6 days of targeted antibiotic therapy.

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to follow-up with the Internal Medicine Department and continue taking the prescribed antibiotics for the recommended duration. The patient was also advised to take necessary precautions to avoid any future infections.

Diagnosis:

The patient was diagnosed with sepsis caused by right lower lung pneumonia which was later complicated by subhepatic appendicitis with perforation and local abscess.

Summary:

The patient, a 55-year-old male with hypertension and dyslipidemia, presented to the Emergency Department with pleuritic right chest pain and was eventually diagnosed with sepsis caused by right lower lung pneumonia. The patient later developed subhepatic appendicitis with perforation and local abscess and underwent exploratory laparoscopic surgery. The patient's condition improved rapidly with targeted antibiotic therapy and he was discharged after 6 days of treatment. The patient was advised to follow-up with the Internal Medicine Department and continue taking prescribed antibiotics.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 55 years
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with sharp chest pain on the right side that got worse with breathing. The patient also had a high fever (40ºC) and serious trouble breathing. Blood tests showed signs of infection, low oxygen levels, and high lactic acid in the blood. A chest x-ray showed an infection in the lower part of the right lung. An ultrasound of the belly showed fatty liver. The patient was diagnosed with a serious body-wide infection caused by pneumonia in the right lower lung. The patient was started on an antibiotic called piperacillin/tazobactam. On the third day of antibiotics, the patient felt pain in the upper right belly area. Doctors found that the appendix had moved under the liver, burst open, and formed a pocket of infection.

Treatment:

Doctors performed surgery using a small camera through tiny cuts in the belly. The surgery confirmed the burst appendix under the liver with a pocket of infection. The source of infection was cleaned out and controlled. A germ called Escherichia coli that does not respond to many antibiotics was found in the fluid drained during surgery. The antibiotic was changed to meropenem. The patient got better quickly after this change. The patient went home after 6 days of this targeted antibiotic treatment.

Follow-up:

The patient was told to schedule a visit with the Internal Medicine Department. The patient should keep taking the prescribed antibiotics for the full time the doctor ordered. The patient was also told to take steps to prevent future infections.

Diagnosis:

The patient had a serious body-wide infection caused by pneumonia in the right lower lung. This was followed by a burst appendix located under the liver with a pocket of infection.

Summary:

The patient is a 55-year-old male with high blood pressure and high cholesterol. The patient came to the Emergency Department with sharp chest pain on the right side. The patient was diagnosed with a serious body-wide infection caused by pneumonia in the right lower lung. Later, the patient developed a burst appendix under the liver with a pocket of infection. The patient had surgery using a small camera through tiny cuts. The patient got better quickly with targeted antibiotic treatment and went home after 6 days. The patient was told to follow up with the Internal Medicine Department and keep taking the prescribed antibiotics.

input:
Discharge Summary
Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Male
Age: 32 years old
Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A

History of Present Illness:
The patient was admitted with symptoms of headaches, nausea, and poor balance for 3 months. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed a pineal region lesion. This was diagnosed as a pineal germ cell tumor, and the patient received 50 Gy/28 fractions of whole brain radiation therapy for treatment. The mass decreased in size and the patient made a full recovery.

Medical History:
The patient has a medical history of a pineal germ cell tumor and underwent radiation therapy ten years ago.

Hospital Course:
Nine years after the initial radiation therapy, the patient presented again with worsening headaches, staggering gait, and mild memory impairment. MRI showed an increase in size of the pineal mass, extending into the third ventricle and causing obstructive hydrocephalus. A ventriculostomy and biopsy of the lesion were performed, followed by a ventriculoperitoneal shunt. The patient underwent a stereotactic guided transcallosal resection of the lesion, revealing mature monodermal teratomatous tissue. The patient made a full recovery and continues to be monitored by serial imaging.

Diagnosis:
Pineal germ cell tumor

Treatment:
Whole brain radiation therapy, ventriculostomy, ventriculoperitoneal shunt, stereotactic guided transcallosal resection

Follow-Up:
The patient will continue to be monitored by serial imaging.

Diet:
N/A

Activity:
N/A

Condition on Discharge:
Stable

Signed,
[N/A]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary
Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Male
Age: 32 years old
Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A

History of Present Illness:
The patient came to the hospital with headaches, nausea, and trouble with balance for 3 months. A special brain scan showed a growth near the center of the brain. Doctors found it was a type of tumor called a germ cell tumor. The patient received radiation treatment to the whole brain over 28 sessions. The growth got smaller and the patient fully recovered.

Medical History:
The patient had this same type of brain tumor ten years ago. The patient had radiation treatment at that time.

Hospital Course:
Nine years after the first radiation treatment, the patient came back with worse headaches. The patient also had trouble walking straight and mild memory problems. A brain scan showed the growth had gotten bigger. It was pressing into a fluid-filled space in the brain and blocking fluid flow. Doctors first did a procedure to drain the fluid and took a small sample of the growth. Then they placed a tube to move extra fluid from the brain to the belly. Finally, doctors did surgery to remove the growth using special guidance tools. They found the growth was made of a type of slow-growing tissue. The patient fully recovered and will keep getting regular brain scans.

Diagnosis:
Brain tumor near the center of the brain called a germ cell tumor

Treatment:
Radiation to the whole brain, procedure to drain brain fluid, tube placed to move fluid from brain to belly, surgery to remove the growth

Follow-Up:
The patient will continue to get regular brain scans to check for changes.

Diet:
N/A

Activity:
N/A

Condition on Discharge:
Stable

Signed,
N/A

Electronic Health Record - Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Attending Physician: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 4-month-old female, was admitted to the hospital due to poor eye contact. She was delivered at 35 weeks of gestation and had an unremarkable family history. The patient was diagnosed with Leber congenital amaurosis (LCA) due to the presence of esotropia and nystagmus, and the extinguished electroretinogram (ERG) in both eyes. Cycloplegic refractive errors were also noted, which were subsequently addressed. A slit lamp examination, fundus examination, and systemic evaluation for any metabolic and organ abnormalities were conducted, but no significant findings were observed except for the aforementioned ocular symptoms.

Diagnostic evaluations were performed, which included genetic testing for CRX, CRB1, GUCY2D, AIPL1, RDH12, RPE65, LRAT, RPGRIP1, and TULP1. However, the results were unremarkable. A brain MRI revealed the presence of a prominent enlargement in the subarachnoid space of both frontal convexities, and absence of the septum pellucidum. However, no focal lesions were found, and the size of the corpus callosum and white matter myelination were normal. Follow-up MRI scans showed the absence of the septum pellucidum, but with stable external hydrocephalus. A diagnosis of septo-optic dysplasia was considered, however, the normal appearance of the optic nerve and normal pituitary function left the diagnosis doubtful.

Medical History:
The patient did not have any notable medical history aside from the ocular and neuroimaging findings with LCA and the subarachnoid space abnormalities observed in the brain MRI scans.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient, a 4-month-old female, was admitted to the hospital for poor eye contact. She was diagnosed with LCA, and further diagnostic evaluations including genetic testing and a brain MRI were conducted. Follow-up MRI scans showed the absence of the septum pellucidum, but with stable external hydrocephalus. At the age of 3 years, the patient showed moderate developmental delay. No noteworthy medical history was obtained during her hospital course.
clean_ref:
Electronic Health Record - Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Attending Physician: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 4-month-old baby girl. She was brought to the hospital because she was not making eye contact. She was born 5 weeks early at 35 weeks of pregnancy. There were no known health problems in the family. The patient was found to have Leber congenital amaurosis, also called LCA. This is a rare eye condition that causes severe vision loss. The doctors found this because her eyes turned inward and moved back and forth on their own. A test that checks how the back of the eye responds to light showed no response in both eyes. The patient also had trouble focusing her eyes, which was treated. The doctors did several exams. They looked at the front and back of her eyes. They also checked her whole body for problems with her organs or body chemistry. These tests were normal except for the eye problems already mentioned.

The doctors did many tests to find the cause. They did genetic testing to look for changes in certain genes. These tests did not find any problems. A brain scan called an MRI was done. It showed extra fluid in the spaces around the front parts of the brain. It also showed that a thin wall in the middle of the brain was missing. This wall is called the septum pellucidum. No other brain problems were found. The main connection between the two sides of the brain looked normal. The protective covering around nerves in the brain was developing normally. Later brain scans still showed the missing wall in the middle of the brain. The extra fluid around the brain stayed the same and did not get worse. The doctors thought about a condition called septo-optic dysplasia. However, the eye nerves looked normal and the hormone gland at the base of the brain worked normally. This made the doctors unsure about that diagnosis.

Medical History:
The patient had no other health problems. Her only issues were the eye condition called LCA and the brain scan findings with extra fluid around the brain.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient is a 4-month-old baby girl who came to the hospital for poor eye contact. She was found to have LCA, a condition that causes severe vision loss. The doctors did genetic testing and brain scans. Later brain scans still showed the missing wall in the middle of the brain. The extra fluid around the brain stayed the same. By age 3, the patient showed some delays in learning and development. No other health problems were found during her time in the hospital.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, an 8-year-old female child, was admitted to our department due to diminution of vision in the right eye since 5 years, with headache and vomiting since 6 months. On examination, no perception of light was observed in the right eye with normal vision in the left eye. An MRI revealed a cystic lesion at the right temporo-frontal-parietal region measuring 8 × 9 × 6.5 cm. A small rounded nodule was seen on the medial aspect of the cystic lesion at the paraventricular location. The lesion was reported as a cystic astrocytoma/ganglioglioma. Endocrine workup subsequently revealed secondary hypothyroidism.  

Treatment:
The patient underwent right fronto-temporo-parietal craniotomy for drainage and marsupialisation of the cystic lesion. The cystic fluid was yellowish and machine oil-like, containing cholesterol crystals and flecks mostly over the medial side. Histological examination revealed inflamed Rathke's cleft cyst with a cystic wall lined by a flattened to pseudostratified squamous epithelium with congested blood vessels, chronic inflammatory infiltrate in the form of lymphocyte, plasma cells, and lymphoid aggregates.

Outcomes:
Postoperatively, the patient's recovery was uneventful, and the headache and vomiting subsided completely. The patient is in regular follow-up with postoperative MRI at 6 months without any recurrence.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient, an 8-year-old girl, was admitted because of vision loss in the right eye for 5 years. She also had headache and vomiting for 6 months. During the exam, the right eye could not see any light at all. The left eye had normal vision. A brain scan showed a fluid-filled sac on the right side of the brain. It measured about 8 by 9 by 6.5 centimeters. A small round lump was seen on the inner side of this sac, near the brain's fluid spaces. Doctors first thought it might be a type of brain tumor. Blood tests later showed that the thyroid gland was not working well due to a problem with hormone signals from the brain.

Treatment:
The patient had surgery to open the skull on the right side of the head. The surgeon drained the fluid-filled sac and created an opening so fluid would not build up again. The fluid inside was yellow and looked like thick oil. It contained fatty crystals and small bits, mostly on the inner side. Lab tests of the tissue showed the sac was a harmless cyst that had become swollen and irritated. The cyst wall was lined with flat to layered skin-like cells. It also had swollen blood vessels and signs of long-term irritation, including certain white blood cells grouped together.

Outcomes:
After surgery, the patient recovered without any problems. The headache and vomiting went away completely. The patient continues to see the doctor regularly. A brain scan done 6 months after surgery showed the cyst has not come back.

input:
Hospital Course:
Case 4: A 10-year-old boy was admitted and diagnosed with progressive weakness of the lower limbs since age 1.3 years. Elevated creatine kinase levels were observed through laboratory testing, and muscle biopsy was performed, revealing myodystrophy changes. The child did not receive any medication.
Cases 5 and 6: Two siblings in the family were also identified. Case 5 was a 5-year-old girl who showed slight weakness when climbing and running and had elevated CK levels. Case 6 was her 3-year-old sister who suffered from retinoblastoma and had the same motor development features. Their mother had intolerance of movement and elevated CK levels. Muscle biopsy of the proband revealed atrophy, mild hyperplasia of connective tissue, negative dystrophin-N staining in most muscle fibers, and decreased expression of dystrophin-C and dystrophin-R. Except for the younger sister of the proband, who underwent eye surgery, the proband did not receive any medication for the condition.
 
Hospital Outcome:
Case 4: Discharge
Cases 5 and 6: Ongoing treatment and management required
 

Discharge Diagnosis:
Progressive muscular myodystrophy
 
Discharge Medication:
None
 
Discharge Follow-up:
Case 4: Recommend follow-up with primary care provider for further monitoring
Cases 5 and 6: Refer to a specialist for the management of their condition.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
Case 4: A 10-year-old boy was admitted to the hospital. He was diagnosed with slowly worsening weakness in his legs that started when he was about 1 year old. Blood tests showed high levels of a muscle enzyme called creatine kinase. A small sample of muscle tissue was taken and tested. The results showed signs of muscle disease. The child did not receive any medicine.
Cases 5 and 6: Two sisters in the same family were also found to have similar problems. Case 5 was a 5-year-old girl who had mild weakness when climbing or running. Her blood tests also showed high muscle enzyme levels. Case 6 was her 3-year-old sister who had eye cancer and the same movement problems. Their mother had trouble with physical activity and also had high muscle enzyme levels. The muscle sample from the main patient showed muscle shrinking and mild growth of tissue between muscle fibers. Testing also showed that an important muscle protein called dystrophin was mostly missing or reduced. The younger sister had eye surgery. The main patient did not receive any medicine for the muscle condition.
Hospital Outcome:
Case 4: Discharge
Cases 5 and 6: Ongoing treatment and care are needed
Discharge Diagnosis:
A muscle disease that slowly gets worse over time
Discharge Medication:
None
Discharge Follow-up:
Case 4: The patient should see a regular doctor for continued check-ups.
Cases 5 and 6: The patients should see a specialist doctor to help manage their condition.

input:
Hospital Admission: Gastrointestinal complaints

History: A 70-year-old female with a history of asthma, eosinophilic esophagitis, and eosinophilic sinusitis presented with postprandial abdominal pain, bloating, and dyspepsia, which had been ongoing intermittently for 2 years.

Significant Findings: Laboratory results revealed elevated eosinophils, lipase, ALT, AST, and alkaline phosphatase. Initial EGD showed a hypertrophic major papilla, antral gastritis, and superficial gastric ulcers. MRI/MRCP revealed CBD dilatation without choledocholithiasis but with tapering of the distal CBD. ERCP revealed an inflamed major ampulla with benign papillary stenosis.

Treatment: The patient underwent sphincterotomy and insertion of a plastic stent into the bile duct, which was followed by sphincteroplasty and dilation of the papilla and distal CBD. Repeat ERCP was performed with stent removal, and biopsies of the stomach and the ampulla showed benign glandular tissue with no eosinophils. Additionally, the patient was started on prednisone and demonstrated significant improvement with systemic steroid therapy.

Follow-Up: Over six months, the patient was monitored and demonstrated controlled symptoms with normal LFTs. The patient was discharged on 40 mg of pantoprazole twice a day and 2 mg of budesonide daily. 

Prognosis: The patient's prognosis is good at this time, with continued surveillance and management of her gastrointestinal symptoms recommended.
clean_ref:
Hospital Admission: Stomach and digestive problems

History: A 70-year-old woman has asthma and a condition where too many white blood cells cause swelling in her food pipe and sinuses. She came to the hospital with stomach pain after eating, a bloated feeling, and upset stomach. These problems had been coming and going for 2 years.

Significant Findings: Blood tests showed high levels of certain white blood cells and liver enzymes. A camera test looking into the stomach showed swelling at the opening where bile enters, irritation in the lower stomach, and shallow stomach sores. An imaging scan showed the main bile tube was wider than normal but had no stones. The end of the bile tube was narrower than it should be. Another test with a camera showed the bile duct opening was swollen and partly blocked but not cancerous.

Treatment: The patient had a procedure to cut open the tight muscle at the bile duct opening. A small plastic tube was placed in the bile duct to keep it open. Later, the opening was stretched wider to improve flow. A follow-up procedure removed the plastic tube. Tissue samples from the stomach and bile duct opening showed normal tissue with no extra white blood cells. The patient also started taking a steroid medicine called prednisone. The patient got much better with this steroid treatment.

Follow-Up: Over six months, the patient was checked regularly. Her symptoms were under control and her liver test results were normal. The patient went home with instructions to take 40 mg of pantoprazole twice a day for stomach acid and 2 mg of budesonide once a day for swelling.

Prognosis: The patient is expected to do well. She should continue regular check-ups and treatment for her stomach and digestive problems.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 35
Gender: Female
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital with a 2-month history of fever, night sweats and fatigue. She had undergone a kidney transplant 5 years ago and was taking tacrolimus and prednisone (2.5 mg daily). A chest magnetic resonance imaging revealed multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, up to 2.5 cm in diameter. The patient was initially treated empirically for tuberculosis. The serum cryptococcal antigen and Histoplasma immune diffusion tests were negative, and microscopic examination of bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) fluid was negative for fungi. The BAL galactomannan index was 0.3. An open lung biopsy revealed a granulocytic infiltrate, with spherical budding structures suggestive of Histoplasma capsulatum.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with disseminated histoplasmosis.

Treatment:
The patient received antifungal therapy for the disseminated histoplasmosis. The medical team carefully monitored the patient's response to treatment and the patient was eventually discharged from the hospital.

Follow-Up:
The patient will follow up with her transplant clinic to ensure proper management of her antifungal treatment and overall health. It is important that the patient stays vigilant for any signs of recurrence or other complications following treatment.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 35
Gender: Female
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital after having fever, night sweats, and tiredness for 2 months. She had a kidney transplant 5 years ago. She was taking two medicines to prevent her body from rejecting the new kidney: tacrolimus and prednisone (2.5 mg daily). A special scan of her chest showed several round spots in both lungs. The largest spot was about 1 inch wide. At first, doctors treated her for a lung infection called tuberculosis. Blood tests for two types of fungus came back negative. Doctors also tested fluid from her lungs and found no fungus. A sample of lung tissue was taken during surgery. This sample showed signs of infection and small round shapes. These shapes pointed to a fungus called Histoplasma capsulatum.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a fungal infection called histoplasmosis that had spread through her body.

Treatment:
The patient received medicine to fight the fungal infection. The care team watched closely to see how well the treatment worked. The patient got better and was sent home from the hospital.

Follow-Up:
The patient will see her transplant doctors for a check-up. They will make sure her fungal infection medicine is working well. They will also check her overall health. The patient should watch for any signs that the infection has come back. She should also look out for any other health problems after treatment.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Medical Record Number: [redacted]
Date of Admission: [redacted]
Date of Discharge: [redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to MEE due to several days of blurry vision and metamorphopsia of the left eye. After a thorough ophthalmological examination, the patient was diagnosed with idiopathic macular choroiditis with choroidal neovascularization (CNV). The patient was started on oral prednisone and received an intravitreal injection of bevacizumab.  On follow-up examination one month later, the patient demonstrated significant improvement, with resolution of subretinal fluid and a smaller, less dense CNV network. A plan for ongoing monitoring and treatment was established.

Diagnosis:
- Idiopathic macular choroiditis with choroidal neovascularization (CNV)

Hospital Course:
- Ophthalmological examination revealed blurry vision and metamorphopsia of the left eye
- Visual acuity demonstrated 20/20 in the right eye and 20/40 in the left eye with normal IOPs
- FA revealed window defects corresponding to the chorioretinal scars and early hyperfluorescence with intense late leakage at the fovea consistent with CNV
- Structural OCT demonstrated subretinal hyperreflective material with disruption of the outer retinal layers and adjacent subretinal fluid
- OCT-A showed a well-circumscribed looped CNV network in the choriocapillaris slab
- Initiating treatment with oral prednisone and intravitreal injection of bevacizumab resulted in significant improvement, with resolution of subretinal fluid and a smaller, less dense CNV network on follow-up examination

Patient Follow-Up:
- A plan for ongoing monitoring and treatment was established to ensure continued improvement and prevent complications. 

Discharge Medications:
- N/A

Condition at Discharge:
- The patient was discharged in stable condition with ongoing treatment for idiopathic macular choroiditis with choroidal neovascularization (CNV).

Follow-up Appointments:
- Follow-up appointments were scheduled to monitor the patient's condition and further evaluate the effectiveness of ongoing treatment.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Medical Record Number: [redacted]
Date of Admission: [redacted]
Date of Discharge: [redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the eye hospital because of several days of blurry vision and wavy or distorted vision in the left eye. After a complete eye exam, the patient was found to have swelling in the back of the eye with abnormal blood vessel growth. The cause of this condition is unknown. The patient was given a steroid pill called prednisone and received a shot of medicine called bevacizumab directly into the eye. At a checkup one month later, the patient showed much better results. The fluid under the back of the eye had gone away. The abnormal blood vessels were smaller and less active. A plan was made to keep watching and treating the eye.

Diagnosis:
Swelling in the back of the eye with abnormal blood vessel growth, cause unknown

Hospital Course:
The eye exam showed blurry vision and wavy or distorted vision in the left eye. The patient could see 20/20 in the right eye and 20/40 in the left eye. Eye pressure was normal in both eyes. Special dye tests showed scars in the back of the eye and leaking abnormal blood vessels in the center of vision. Scans of the eye showed abnormal material and fluid under the back of the eye. The outer layers of the eye were damaged in that area. Another type of scan showed a loop of abnormal blood vessels in the deepest layer of the eye. Treatment with steroid pills and a shot of medicine into the eye helped a lot. The fluid went away and the abnormal blood vessels became smaller and less active.

Patient Follow-Up:
A plan was made to keep watching and treating the eye to help it keep getting better and prevent problems.

Discharge Medications:
None

Condition at Discharge:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition with ongoing treatment for the swelling and abnormal blood vessel growth in the back of the eye.

Follow-up Appointments:
Follow-up visits were scheduled to check on the patient and see how well the treatment is working.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Encounter Date: N/A

Admission Diagnosis:
Invasive lobular carcinoma with metastasis to the colon and axillary lymph nodes.

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with a history of breast benign featuring mass and significant weight loss over a 4-month period. A breast examination revealed two masses, with one being suspicious. Biopsies confirmed the presence of invasive lobular carcinoma with metastasis to a left axillary lymph node and the colon. The patient underwent six cycles of palliative chemotherapy with overall regressive disease and improvement in symptoms. She was placed on hormonal therapy and denosumab for maintenance, with stationary disease noted on regular follow-up.

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Follow-Up:
The patient will continue to be followed up on a regular basis for disease monitoring.

Condition at Discharge:
Stable with stationary disease.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Invasive lobular carcinoma with metastasis to the colon and axillary lymph nodes.

Discharge Instructions:
N/A

Dictated By: N/A
Signed Electronic Signature: N/A
Date: N/A

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Encounter Date: N/A

Admission Diagnosis:
A type of breast cancer called invasive lobular carcinoma that has spread to the colon and lymph nodes under the arm.

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with a history of a non-cancerous breast lump and major weight loss over 4 months. A breast exam found two lumps, and one looked concerning. Tissue samples showed a type of breast cancer that had spread to a lymph node under the left arm and to the colon. The patient received six rounds of chemotherapy to help control the cancer and ease symptoms. The cancer shrank and the patient felt better. The patient was then started on hormone therapy and a bone-protecting medicine called denosumab to keep the cancer under control. Follow-up visits showed the cancer has stayed the same size and has not grown.

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Follow-Up:
The patient will keep having regular check-ups to watch the cancer.

Condition at Discharge:
Stable, with the cancer staying the same size.

Discharge Diagnosis:
A type of breast cancer called invasive lobular carcinoma that has spread to the colon and lymph nodes under the arm.

Discharge Instructions:
N/A

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]

Date of Admission: [redacted]

Date of Discharge: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: Chronic skin ulcer with extensive soft tissue loss and tibial exposure in the left leg.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 30-year-old female, was admitted for nonhealing chronic skin ulcer with extensive soft tissue loss and tibial exposure in the left leg. The patient had a history of complete degloving of the leg with multiple bone fractures in a car accident 5 years prior. She had undergone various treatments, including adipose tissue grafts and negative pressure therapy, but without any improvement.

During the hospital stay, a surgical debridement was performed, but the patient refused further surgery and was directed to outpatient clinic follow-up. Dressing changes were performed with standard saline solution alone for 5 months, with no substantial improvement.

During the sixth month of pregnancy, granulation tissue completely covered the bone and filled the ulcer, which displayed significantly reduced diameters. At this point, the patient moved back to her home country for delivery and was lost to follow-up.

Disposition: Discharged to outpatient clinic follow-up.

Final Diagnosis: Nonhealing chronic skin ulcer with extensive soft tissue loss and tibial exposure in the left leg, healed during pregnancy.

Treatments and Procedures: Surgical debridement, dressing changes with standard saline solution.

Condition at Discharge: The patient's condition had significantly improved at the time of discharge.

Follow-up: Outpatient clinic follow-up is recommended.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]

Date of Admission: [redacted]

Date of Discharge: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: Long-term open wound on the left leg with major skin and tissue loss and exposed shin bone.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient is a 30-year-old woman. She was admitted for a long-term open wound on her left leg that would not heal. The wound had major skin and tissue loss with exposed shin bone. Five years ago, the patient was in a car accident. The skin was completely torn off her leg and she had multiple broken bones. She had tried many treatments before, including fat tissue transfers and vacuum bandage therapy. None of these treatments helped.

During her hospital stay, doctors cleaned out the wound with surgery. The patient did not want more surgery after that. She was told to continue care at an outpatient clinic. For 5 months, her wound was cleaned and bandaged using only salt water. The wound did not get much better during this time.

When the patient was 6 months pregnant, new healing tissue completely covered the bone. The wound filled in and became much smaller. The patient then moved back to her home country to have her baby. The medical team lost contact with her after that.

Disposition: Sent home with outpatient clinic follow-up.

Final Diagnosis: Long-term open wound on the left leg with major skin and tissue loss and exposed shin bone. The wound healed during pregnancy.

Treatments and Procedures: Surgical wound cleaning, bandage changes with salt water.

Condition at Discharge: The patient's condition was much better when she left the hospital.

Follow-up: The patient should continue visits at an outpatient clinic.
