input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Date of Birth: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Reason for Admission: Shortness of breath

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the emergency department with shortness of breath and increasing dyspnea. She was found to have a large goiter that was compressing the trachea and causing respiratory distress. The patient was quickly transferred to the intensive care unit (ICU) where she was intubated using rapid sequence induction to relieve respiratory distress. Subsequent cerebral and thoracic computed tomography (CT) scans revealed a large goiter plunging into the upper mediastinal orifice, which was removed successfully by thyroidectomy 48 hours after admission. Prior to reversal of the neuromuscular blockage, the tube balloon was deflated, and the diagnosis of tracheomalacia was almost confirmed. The endotracheal tube was left in place for 24 hours, and then removed the next day without incident.

Course of Treatment:
The patient received corticotherapy (dexamethasone at 12 mg per day for two days) prior to thyroidectomy to accelerate fetal lung maturation. Surgery was completed without incident, as no intraoperative tracheostomy was necessary. Patient was treated with levothyroxin sodium tablets 75mcg/day post-surgery.

Follow-Up Plan:
Patient was discharged from the hospital on the 5th postoperative day without any complications. Pathology showed multinodular goiter with areas of atypical hyperplasia, but no sign of malignancy. Patient gave birth to a healthy newborn four weeks later.

Recommendations:
Patient should continue taking levothyroxin sodium tablets 75mcg/day as prescribed. She should follow up with her primary care physician for monitoring of her thyroid function and any potential complications related to the goiter removal.

clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Date of Birth: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Reason for Admission: Shortness of breath

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room with shortness of breath that was getting worse. Doctors found a large growth on her thyroid gland. This growth was pressing on her windpipe and making it hard to breathe. The patient was moved quickly to the intensive care unit. There, doctors placed a breathing tube to help her breathe. Brain and chest scans showed the thyroid growth was very large and reaching into the upper chest area. Doctors removed the thyroid gland in surgery 48 hours after the patient arrived. Before waking the patient fully, doctors tested the windpipe. They found the windpipe walls were weak and floppy. The breathing tube stayed in place for 24 hours. It was removed the next day without any problems.

Course of Treatment:
The patient received a steroid medicine called dexamethasone before surgery. She took 12 mg per day for two days. This helped her baby's lungs develop faster. The surgery went well. Doctors did not need to make an opening in her windpipe. After surgery, the patient started taking thyroid hormone pills. The medicine is levothyroxin sodium tablets at 75 mcg per day.

Follow-Up Plan:
The patient left the hospital five days after surgery with no problems. Lab tests on the removed tissue showed a thyroid growth with many lumps. Some cells were growing in an unusual way. However, there was no cancer. The patient gave birth to a healthy baby four weeks later.

Recommendations:
The patient should keep taking levothyroxin sodium tablets at 75 mcg per day as directed. She should see her regular doctor for checkups. The doctor will monitor how well her thyroid medicine is working. The doctor will also watch for any problems from the thyroid removal surgery.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 42 years 
Hospital Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Diagnosis: Ischemic Stroke

Hospital Course:

The patient presented to the emergency room with slurred speech, dizziness, and left-sided weakness which started about two hours before presentation. The patient did not report any cough, fever, shortness of breath, loss of smell, or body aches. On examination, she was hemodynamically stable. In neurological examination, she had slurred speech and weakness in the left upper and lower extremity but normal sensations. The rest of the neurological examination as well as chest, cardiovascular, abdominal, and musculoskeletal examination was normal. 

The emergency CT scan did not show any evidence of major territorial infarction, intracranial hemorrhage, or focal mass lesions. The patient was within the window of tissue plasminogen activator (TPA) for acute stroke and was treated with it. The patient was also screened for COVID-19 on admission as per hospital protocol and the result came back later as positive.

The patient was admitted to the ICU for monitoring and did not have any abnormal electrocardiogram (EKG) rhythm while in ICU. She had full recovery of her neurological symptoms within few hours without any bleeding complications. The patient was subsequently discharged in good health and advised home isolation.

Discharge Diagnosis: Ischemic Stroke with COVID-19

Discharge Instructions: 

The patient is advised to follow safe sleep and nutrition practices and to avoid stress. The patient should be advised not to engage in driving or other actions that require quick reflex. The patient should be advised to follow home isolation and avoid close contact with family members or friends. The patient should also be advised to monitor for any symptoms of COVID-19 and seek medical attention if needed.

Follow-up: The patient should be advised to follow up with the primary care provider or neurologist within one week of discharge.


Dictated and signed by: N/A

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 42 years
Hospital Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Diagnosis: Stroke caused by a blocked blood vessel in the brain

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the emergency room with slurred speech, dizziness, and weakness on the left side of her body. These symptoms started about two hours before she arrived. The patient did not have a cough, fever, trouble breathing, loss of smell, or body aches. When checked, her heart rate and blood pressure were stable. During the brain and nerve exam, she had slurred speech and weakness in her left arm and leg. Her sense of touch was normal. All other exams of her chest, heart, belly, and muscles were normal.

A brain scan done right away did not show any major damage, bleeding, or growths in the brain. The patient arrived early enough to receive a clot-busting medicine for her stroke. The patient was also tested for COVID-19 when she arrived, following hospital rules. The test result came back later and showed she had COVID-19.

The patient was moved to the intensive care unit so doctors could watch her closely. Her heart rhythm stayed normal while she was there. Her brain and nerve symptoms went away completely within a few hours. She did not have any bleeding problems. The patient was sent home in good health and told to stay home away from others.

Discharge Diagnosis: Stroke caused by a blocked blood vessel in the brain with COVID-19

Discharge Instructions:

The patient is advised to get good sleep, eat healthy foods, and avoid stress. The patient should not drive or do other things that need quick reactions. The patient should stay home and avoid close contact with family or friends. The patient should watch for any signs of COVID-19 and get medical help if needed.

Follow-up: The patient should see her regular doctor or brain specialist within one week after leaving the hospital.

Dictated and signed by: N/A

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: ______________

Admission Date: ____________

Discharge Date: ____________

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our institution with progressive neurological symptoms. He had a history of seropositive Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) that was managed with maintenance methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisone. The patient developed fluctuating confusion, word-finding difficulty, right-sided weakness and gait imbalance. An evaluation was done by the neurologist which confirmed the presence of neurological deficits. An MRI scan showed areas of scattered restricted diffusion and enhancement within the cortex and leptomeninges of the left hemisphere near the vertex, suspicious for meningoencephalitis. CSF analysis also demonstrated lymphocytic meningitis with negative, bacterial and fungal cultures. 

The patient was started on antiepileptic medication and a whole-body PET scan was done to rule out underlying malignancy. A bone marrow biopsy was also done to rule out lymphoid neoplasm. An open meningeal biopsy was performed and gross examination revealed thickening and opacification of the meninges. The histopathological findings were consistent with leptomeningeal involvement by nodular rheumatoid meningitis.

Immunosuppressive therapy with monthly cyclophosphamide and high-dose corticosteroids was initiated for 6 months and then the corticosteroids were tapered. The patient's neurological examination improved one month post treatment initiation, although confusion and bilateral postural tremor persisted. Most parkinsonian features also largely resolved following immunosuppressive therapy. 

Repeat MRI 3 months after treatment initiation showed complete resolution of previous findings, and CRP levels also normalized. The patient was eventually transitioned to a long-term care facility due to the persistent behavioral and cognitive deficits with intermittent periods of agitation.

Diagnosis:
Leptomeningeal involvement by nodular rheumatoid meningitis

Course:
The patient was admitted for investigation of progressive neurological symptoms and was treated with immunosuppressive therapy. The patient's neurological examination improved one month post treatment initiation, although confusion and bilateral postural tremor persisted, and most parkinsonian features largely resolved following immunosuppressive therapy. Repeat MRI 3 months after treatment initiation showed complete resolution of previous findings.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has been transitioned to a long-term care facility due to persistent behavioral and cognitive deficits with intermittent periods of agitation.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: ______________

Admission Date: ____________

Discharge Date: ____________

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital with worsening brain and nerve problems. He had a history of rheumatoid arthritis, a condition where the body attacks its own joints. This was being treated with methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, and low-dose prednisone. The patient developed confusion that came and went, trouble finding words, weakness on the right side of the body, and trouble walking. A brain and nerve doctor did an exam and confirmed these problems. A brain scan called an MRI showed areas of swelling in the covering of the brain on the left side near the top. This looked like an infection or swelling of the brain lining. A test of the fluid around the brain and spine showed swelling with certain white blood cells. Tests for bacteria and fungus came back negative.

The patient was started on medicine to prevent seizures. A full body scan was done to check for cancer. A bone marrow test was also done to check for blood cancer. Doctors took a small piece of the brain covering to look at under a microscope. This showed the brain covering was thicker and cloudier than normal. The test results showed that rheumatoid arthritis was affecting the brain covering.

Treatment was started with cyclophosphamide given once a month and high doses of steroids for 6 months. Then the steroids were slowly reduced. The patient's brain and nerve exam got better one month after starting treatment. However, confusion and shaking in both hands when held up continued. Stiffness and slow movement also mostly went away after treatment.

A follow-up brain scan 3 months after starting treatment showed all the earlier problems had gone away. A blood test for swelling in the body also returned to normal. The patient was moved to a care home because of ongoing problems with thinking, behavior, and times of feeling upset or restless.

Diagnosis:
Rheumatoid arthritis affecting the covering of the brain

Course:
The patient was admitted to find the cause of worsening brain and nerve problems. The patient was treated with medicines that calm down the immune system. The patient's brain and nerve exam got better one month after starting treatment. However, confusion and shaking in both hands when held up continued. Stiffness and slow movement also mostly went away after treatment. A follow-up brain scan 3 months after starting treatment showed all the earlier problems had gone away.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has been moved to a care home because of ongoing problems with thinking, behavior, and times of feeling upset or restless.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 91-year-old African-American woman who was referred to the gastroenterology clinic with complaints of mild epigastric pain and intermittent melena. Upon examination, the patient had a soft and non-tender abdomen. Laboratory test results showed anemia and elevated liver function tests. Abdominal CT scans revealed multiple hepatic cysts, but malignancy could not be confirmed. The patient underwent an EGD, which showed multiple blood clots at the ampulla of Vater, indicating hemobilia as the cause of melena and occult biliary obstruction. The patient underwent an ERCP procedure, during which blood clots obstructing the common hepatic duct were successfully removed, and a stent was placed. Cytology brushing results were negative for malignancy. The patient recovered well without complications and was discharged in stable condition. 

Diagnosis and Treatment:
The suspected diagnosis was hemobilia caused by a hepatic cyst. The patient underwent an ERCP procedure to remove the obstructing blood clots from the common hepatic duct and place a stent. Cytology brushing results were negative for malignancy. 

Follow-up Plan:
The patient is advised to follow up with her primary care provider and the gastroenterology clinic for regular check-ups.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 91-year-old African-American woman who was sent to a stomach and digestive system clinic. She had mild pain in her upper belly and was sometimes passing dark, bloody stools. During the exam, her belly was soft and did not hurt when pressed. Blood tests showed low red blood cell counts and signs that her liver was not working normally. A scan of her belly showed several fluid-filled sacs on her liver, but doctors could not tell if cancer was present. The patient had a procedure where a small camera was put down her throat to look at her stomach. This showed blood clots at an opening where bile enters the small intestine. This meant that bleeding from her bile system was causing the dark stools and a blockage in her bile tubes. The patient had another procedure to look at her bile tubes. During this, doctors removed blood clots that were blocking the main bile tube from her liver. They also placed a small tube to keep the path open. Cells collected during the procedure showed no signs of cancer. The patient healed well with no problems and left the hospital in stable condition.

Diagnosis and Treatment:
Doctors believe the patient had bleeding in her bile system caused by a fluid-filled sac on her liver. The patient had a procedure to remove blood clots blocking her main bile tube from the liver. A small tube was placed to keep the path open. Cells collected during the procedure showed no signs of cancer.

Follow-up Plan:
The patient should see her regular doctor and the stomach and digestive system clinic for regular check-ups.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]          Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Pain at left temporomandibular joint (TMJ) and numerous radiopaque masses around left TMJ

Discharge Diagnosis: Synovial chondromatosis

Medical History: None provided

Hospital Course: The patient, a 50-year-old male, presented at Dankook University Dental Hospital with complaints of pain and swelling at his left TMJ and numerous radiopaque masses around the joint on panoramic radiography. The patient showed spontaneous and opening pain and swelling at the left TMJ, but showed no tenderness or limitation of mouth opening. Through imaging tests, including panoramic imaging, CBCT, and MR imaging, numerous radiopaque loose bodies of various shapes and sizes surrounding the left condyle, and hypertrophy with sclerotic changes on the anterior and lateral sides of the temporal bone, the articular eminence, and the articular fossa were observed. A surgical excision under general anesthesia was performed to remove approximately 120 pieces of the loose bodies. Histopathologic examination confirmed the diagnosis of synovial chondromatosis. On a postoperative panoramic image, remaining flat hypertrophy of the articular eminence was observed, without any loose bodies. The patient's symptoms were relieved, and no signs of recurrence were observed at 1-month and 3-month follow-up visits.

Discharge Condition: The patient's condition has improved significantly, and he is discharged from the hospital in good condition.

Current Medications Upon Discharge: None provided

Follow-Up Care: The patient is advised to continue to attend follow-up visits to monitor his condition.

Instructions at Discharge: The patient is instructed to avoid any strenuous activities for a few weeks, avoid opening his mouth too wide, and not to eat hard or chewy foods for at least a week after the surgery. The patient is advised to continue oral hygiene practices, including brushing and flossing regularly.

Primary Care Physician: None provided

Attending Physician: [REDACTED]

Other contributors to care: None provided

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]          Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Pain at the left jaw joint and many small solid masses seen on X-ray around the left jaw joint

Discharge Diagnosis: Synovial chondromatosis (a condition where small, hard pieces of cartilage grow inside a joint)

Medical History: None provided

Hospital Course: The patient, a 50-year-old male, came to Dankook University Dental Hospital. He had pain and swelling at his left jaw joint. X-rays showed many small solid masses around the joint. The patient had pain on his own and when opening his mouth. He also had swelling at the left jaw joint. He did not have soreness when touched or trouble opening his mouth. Doctors used different imaging tests to look at the joint. These included regular X-rays, 3D X-rays, and MRI scans. The tests showed many loose pieces of different shapes and sizes around the lower jaw bone. The tests also showed thickened and hardened bone on the front and side of the skull bone near the joint. Doctors did surgery while the patient was fully asleep. They removed about 120 loose pieces from the joint. Lab tests on the removed pieces confirmed the diagnosis of synovial chondromatosis. An X-ray taken after surgery showed some flat, thickened bone still in the joint area. No loose pieces remained. The patient felt better after surgery. At check-up visits one month and three months later, there were no signs the condition had come back.

Discharge Condition: The patient has improved a lot. He is leaving the hospital in good condition.

Current Medications Upon Discharge: None provided

Follow-Up Care: The patient should keep going to check-up visits so doctors can watch his condition.

Instructions at Discharge: The patient should not do hard physical activities for a few weeks. The patient should not open his mouth too wide. The patient should not eat hard or chewy foods for at least one week after surgery. The patient should keep taking care of his teeth and gums by brushing and flossing every day.

Primary Care Physician: None provided

Attending Physician: [REDACTED]

Other contributors to care: None provided

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 61-year-old male with oral cavity SCC and previous surgical treatment of stage I disease in 2011, presented with cervical and left axillary lymphadenopathy. The patient had been lost to follow-up for over 4 years prior to this presentation. Biopsy of the lymph nodes revealed metastatic SCC. The patient was enrolled in a trial and treated with carboplatin, 5-FU, and pembrolizumab at a dose of 200 mg. After 4 cycles, the patient showed signs of partial response, but presented with fatigue and hypotension to systolic blood pressure of 70 seconds. The patient was diagnosed with immune-related central adrenal insufficiency and was started on high-dose dexamethasone and later transitioned to hydrocortisone. Brain MRI did not show any inflammation of the pituitary stalk and was without metastatic lesions. The patient's fatigue was resolved ten days later and was restarted on pembrolizumab. The patient continues to take hydrocortisone.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 61-year-old man with mouth cancer. He had surgery for early-stage cancer in 2011. He came back after not seeing doctors for over 4 years. He now had swollen lymph nodes in his neck and left armpit. A small tissue sample showed the cancer had spread to these lymph nodes. The patient joined a research study. He received three cancer drugs called carboplatin, 5-FU, and pembrolizumab at a dose of 200 mg. After 4 rounds of treatment, the cancer showed some shrinking. However, he came in feeling very tired and had low blood pressure with the top number at 70. Doctors found that his immune treatment caused his adrenal glands to stop working properly. These glands sit above the kidneys and make important hormones. He was given a strong steroid medicine called dexamethasone. Later, he switched to another steroid called hydrocortisone. A scan of his brain showed no swelling near the pituitary gland. The scan also showed no cancer spread to the brain. His tiredness went away after ten days. He then started taking pembrolizumab again. The patient continues to take hydrocortisone.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Name: [Redacted]
- Age: 2 months
- Gender: Male
- Admitting Diagnosis: Sepsis
- Discharge Diagnosis: Malaria

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 2-month-old male infant, was admitted to our pediatric antiretroviral clinic with the complaint of fever, which was occurring every 2 to 3 days. The mother of the patient reported that the fever was associated with nonprojectile vomit containing recently ingested milk formula. Upon examination, the patient was found to have hepatic and splenic enlargement of 4 cm each. A diagnosis of sepsis was made and the patient was treated with intravenous cefuroxime before complete blood count investigation. Fever resolved 2 days after commencing antibiotics and the patient was discharged home on oral cefuroxime.

The patient was re-admitted a week later with the complaint of fever recurring every 2 to 3 days. A diagnosis of malaria was made upon detection of trophozoites of Plasmodium falciparum in the peripheral blood film. The patient was treated with oral quinine and showed a remarkable improvement in his condition, with the intermittent fever resolving by crisis within 24 hours of administration. The packed cell volume of 26% at readmission increased to 32% on the fourth day of quinine therapy. The child remained well and was discharged home two days after.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient is advised to continue co-trimoxazole prophylaxis as previously prescribed and follow up with the pediatric antiretroviral clinic for routine monitoring.

Patient Education:
The mother of the patient is advised to be vigilant in reporting signs and symptoms of fever and to consider malaria as a potential diagnosis in the event of persistent fever. She is also advised to continue the patient's medication regimen as prescribed and follow up with the pediatric antiretroviral clinic for routine monitoring.

Primary care physician:
Dr. [Redacted]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 2 months
Gender: Male
Admitting Diagnosis: Serious infection in the blood
Discharge Diagnosis: Malaria

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 2-month-old baby boy, was brought to our children's HIV clinic because of a fever. The fever came and went every 2 to 3 days. The mother said the baby was also throwing up milk formula gently after feeding. During the exam, the doctor found that the liver and spleen were both enlarged by about 4 cm. The doctors first thought the baby had a serious blood infection. They gave the patient antibiotic medicine through a vein before blood test results came back. The fever went away 2 days after starting the antibiotic. The patient went home with antibiotic medicine to take by mouth.

The patient came back to the hospital a week later with the same fever pattern. This time, doctors found malaria parasites in the blood. The patient was given malaria medicine by mouth. The baby got much better very quickly. The on-and-off fever stopped within 24 hours of taking the medicine. The amount of red blood cells went up from 26 percent to 32 percent after four days of treatment. The baby stayed healthy and went home two days later.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient should keep taking the infection-prevention medicine as directed before. The patient should return to the children's HIV clinic for regular checkups.

Patient Education:
The mother should watch carefully for signs of fever. She should think about malaria if the fever keeps coming back. She should make sure the patient takes all medicines as directed. She should bring the patient back to the children's HIV clinic for regular checkups.

Primary care physician:
Dr. [Redacted]

Discharge Summary:

Patient: 71-year-old male with a history of heart disease and recent heart surgery.

Admission Date: [Date]

Discharge Date: [Date]

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted for progressive weakness, shortness of breath, and a syncopal episode. On examination, he had peripheral cyanosis, pallor, and elevated jugular venous pressure. Echocardiography showed severe left ventricular (LV) systolic dysfunction with a reduced stroke volume. CT angiogram of the head and neck showed extensive stenosis of arteries. Coronary angiography indicated severe stenosis of the proximal left subclavian artery.

Management:

A multidisciplinary team placed a bypass graft from the left common carotid artery to the left subclavian artery distal to the stenosis, reperfusing the LAD territory myocardium. This required intra-aortic balloon pump support and the patient remained on dobutamine post-operatively. The patient showed remarkable improvement in his symptoms. The patient was weaned off dobutamine and discharged to cardiac rehabilitation.

Follow-up:

The patient should follow up with the team of cardiologists, interventional cardiologists, and vascular surgeons for further evaluation of the bypass graft. The patient should also continue with cardiac rehabilitation to optimize his physical function and quality of life.

Summary:

The patient with a history of heart disease and recent heart surgery presented with progressive weakness, shortness of breath, and a syncopal episode. He was diagnosed with extensive arterial stenosis, which was managed by placing a bypass graft. The patient showed remarkable improvement in his symptoms and was discharged to cardiac rehabilitation. The patient should follow up with the team of specialists for further evaluation.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 71-year-old male with a history of heart disease and recent heart surgery.

Admission Date: [Date]

Discharge Date: [Date]

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted for increasing weakness, trouble breathing, and fainting. During the exam, he had bluish skin on his hands and feet, pale skin, and swollen neck veins. A heart ultrasound showed the left side of the heart was pumping very poorly. A scan of the blood vessels in the head and neck showed major blockages in the arteries. A heart artery test showed a severe blockage in an artery near the left shoulder.

Management:

A team of doctors created a new path for blood flow around the blocked artery. This rerouted blood to the heart muscle that was not getting enough. The patient needed a balloon pump to help the heart during surgery. He also needed medicine to help the heart pump stronger after surgery. The patient got much better. The patient was slowly taken off the heart medicine and sent to a heart recovery program.

Follow-up:

The patient should see the team of heart doctors and blood vessel surgeons for checkups on the new blood flow path. The patient should also keep going to the heart recovery program to build strength and feel better.

Summary:

The patient with a history of heart disease and recent heart surgery came in with increasing weakness, trouble breathing, and fainting. He was found to have major blockages in his arteries. Doctors created a new path for blood to flow around the blockage. The patient got much better and was sent to a heart recovery program. The patient should see the team of doctors for follow-up visits.

input:
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Medical History:
The patient is a 32-year-old woman who presented with a mass in the vulva and intermenstrual bleeding. She had a history of unsuccessful sexual intercourse due to fear of pain, and the mass had gradually enlarged over the past 3 years.

Clinical Findings:
A physical examination revealed a nontender tubular neoplasm outside the intact hymen, with an opening at the distal end. A canal approximately 5 cm long was found in the mass, from which white mucus was secreted. Ultrasonographic evaluation of the pelvis showed a 4.5- × 1-cm stalk originating from the cervix and protruding into the vagina. A mini-hysteroscopy was performed to excise the mass.

Diagnostic Testing:
Hemoglobin level was 9.4 g/dL. The levels of estradiol and progesterone in the luteal phase were 138 pg/mL and 13.77 ng/mL, respectively. Liver and renal functional tests and thyroid hormone levels were all normal.

Treatment:
The patient underwent a mini-hysteroscopy to excise the mass while avoiding damage to the hymen. The mass was found to originate from the squamocolumnar junction at the 3 o’clock position of the left lip of the ectocervix, and it had no communication with the endocervix. The cervical mucosal fold of approximately 0.3 to 0.7 cm in thickness was found inside the canal by mini-hysteroscopy and it had a blind end proximally. The mass was totally excised after suturing of the pedicle. Deep endocervical and endometrial regions were found to be normal.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient's postoperative course was uneventful without bleeding or any other complications. Sexual intercourse was successful for this couple.

Follow-up:
Five weeks after surgery, the patient visited again in the outpatient clinic and was doing well. Written informed consent was obtained from the patient and her husband for publication of this case report and accompanying images. Institutional review board approval was not required for publication of this case report.
clean_ref:
Hospital Stay Summary:

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Medical History:
The patient is a 32-year-old woman who came to the hospital with a growth in the outer private area and bleeding between periods. She had not been able to have sex because she was afraid it would hurt. The growth had slowly gotten bigger over the past 3 years.

Clinical Findings:
A physical exam showed a painless tube-shaped growth outside the thin skin that covers the vaginal opening. The growth had an opening at the end. There was a canal about 2 inches long inside the growth that released white fluid. An ultrasound of the lower belly area showed a 1.8-inch by 0.4-inch stem starting from the lower part of the womb and sticking into the birth canal. A small camera procedure was done to remove the growth.

Diagnostic Testing:
The blood count showed low iron levels at 9.4 g/dL. Hormone levels for the monthly cycle were 138 pg/mL and 13.77 ng/mL. Liver tests, kidney tests, and thyroid hormone levels were all normal.

Treatment:
The patient had a small camera procedure to remove the growth without damaging the thin skin covering the vaginal opening. The growth started from the area where two types of skin meet on the left side of the lower womb opening. It did not connect to the inside of the womb opening. A fold of skin about 0.1 to 0.3 inches thick was found inside the canal using the small camera. This fold had a closed end. The growth was completely removed after stitching the base. The deeper parts of the womb opening and womb lining were normal.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient healed well after surgery with no bleeding or other problems. The couple was able to have sex successfully.

Follow-up:
Five weeks after surgery, the patient came back to the clinic and was doing well. The patient and her husband gave written permission to share this medical story and pictures. No special approval was needed to share this medical story.

[Header]
Discharge Summary

[Patient Information]
Patient Name: [insert patient name]
Medical Record Number: [insert assigned number]
Sex: Female 
Age: 66 

[Hospital Course and Reason for Admission]
The patient was admitted to our medical institution due to intermittent pin-prick pain occurring in the right chest. During a 4-month period, the patient underwent intermittent nerve blocks at a local clinic. The symptoms were not improved. Therefore, the patient was admitted to our medical institution.

[History and Physical Examination]
On physical examination, the patient complained of allodynia and hyperesthesia. At the time of admission, VAS score was 7-8/10 points. The patient had a concurrent presence of the scar tissue.

[Diagnostic Tests]
No diagnostic tests were performed.

[Treatment and Condition at Discharge]
For the treatment of the patient, a needle was placed in the multifidus muscle at the level of T5 and the tissue around the facet at the level of T5-6. Four weeks after the treatment, VAS score decreased from 7-8 points at the time of admission to 3 points. Two weeks later, VAS score further improved to 2 points. The patient's pain was managed with analgesics and anti-depressants.

[Discharge Medication and Instructions]
The patient was discharged with instructions to continue taking her analgesics and anti-depressants.

[Follow-up]
No follow-up was scheduled at discharge.

[Diagnoses]
The patient was diagnosed with neuralgia. 

[Prognosis]
The prognosis for the patient is good, but symptoms can reoccur. 

[Summary]
The patient was admitted for the management of neuralgia occurring in the right chest. Pain was managed with a multifidus muscle needle placement at T5 and tissue around the facet at T5-6. The patient made a good recovery and was discharged with instructions to continue taking pain medications.

Discharge Summary

Patient Information
Patient Name: [insert patient name]
Medical Record Number: [insert assigned number]
Sex: Female
Age: 66

Hospital Course and Reason for Admission
The patient came to our hospital because of sharp, stabbing pain in the right side of the chest. The pain came and went. Over 4 months, the patient had nerve-numbing shots at another clinic. These shots did not help. So the patient came to our hospital for more care.

History and Physical Examination
During the exam, the patient had pain from light touch that should not hurt. The patient also felt more pain than normal from things that usually cause only mild pain. At the time of coming to the hospital, the pain level was 7-8 out of 10. The patient also had scar tissue in the area.

Diagnostic Tests
No tests were done.

Treatment and Condition at Discharge
To treat the patient, a needle was placed in a deep back muscle near the spine at the upper back level. The needle was also placed in tissue near the spine joints in the same area. Four weeks after treatment, the pain level dropped from 7-8 out of 10 to 3 out of 10. Two weeks later, the pain level got even better at 2 out of 10. The patient took pain medicine and mood medicine to help with the pain.

Discharge Medication and Instructions
The patient was told to keep taking the pain medicine and mood medicine at home.

Follow-up
No follow-up visit was set up when the patient left the hospital.

Diagnoses
The patient was found to have nerve pain.

Prognosis
The patient should do well, but the pain may come back.

Summary
The patient came to the hospital for nerve pain in the right side of the chest. The pain was treated with needle placement in a deep back muscle and near spine joints in the upper back. The patient got much better and went home with instructions to keep taking pain medicine.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient name: [Name]
Medical record number: [Number]
Date of admission: [Date]
Date of discharge: [Date]

Hospital Course:
[Name], a 48-year-old female with polycystic liver and kidney diseases, was admitted to our hospital for surgical treatment. She had been followed up for 5 years at another institution and complained of progressive distension of the abdomen and abdominal fullness leading to gradual decrease in body weight without shortness of breath.

Upon admission, the patient was observed regularly without considering palliative surgery or liver transplantation. During the outpatient clinic follow-up for 1 year, the patient's condition worsened with increasing fatigue and dyspnea on exertion. Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status worsened from 2 to 3.

Medical Treatment:
Medical treatment with somatostatin analogue was not considered primarily due to the high medical cost as well as the undetermined therapeutic efficacy. After obtaining consent from the patient on the uncertain effect of fenestration treatment as well as high risk of disease recurrence, the patient underwent surgical fenestration treatment. Multiple cysts in the enlarged liver were opened, and the cyst walls were excised with electrocautery.

At the end of the fenestration procedure, the liver appeared to be markedly shrunken, and three Jackson-Pratt type drains were inserted to evacuate the ascitic fluid. After performing follow-up CT at 1 week, the drains were removed. The total liver volume by using CT volumetry was 3,870 ml before surgery and 3,125 ml at 1 week after surgery, showing a volume reduction of 19.3%.

Clinical Outcome:
After the surgery, ECOG performance status improved to 1 at 1 month and to 0 at 1 year. Gastrointestinal Quality of Life Index (GIQLI) score was significantly increased to 122 after 1 month. During regular follow-up at an interval of 6 months, follow-up CT showed no progression in cyst size and flat abdomen was observed. At 18 months after surgery, the patient is doing well and performing her normal activities with no evidence of disease recurrence.

Conclusion:
In summary, the patient underwent surgical fenestration treatment for polycystic liver and kidney diseases, resulting in significant improvement in clinical outcomes with no evidence of disease recurrence.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient name: [Name]
Medical record number: [Number]
Date of admission: [Date]
Date of discharge: [Date]

Hospital Course:
[Name] is a 48-year-old woman with a condition that causes many cysts to grow in the liver and kidneys. The patient was admitted to our hospital for surgery. The patient had been seen at another hospital for 5 years. The patient had a swelling belly that felt full. This caused the patient to slowly lose weight. The patient did not have trouble breathing.

When the patient first came in, the doctors watched the patient closely. They did not plan for surgery to ease symptoms or a liver transplant at that time. Over the next year of clinic visits, the patient got worse. The patient felt more tired and had trouble breathing during activity. The patient's ability to do daily tasks dropped from being able to do some things to being able to do very little.

Medical Treatment:
A medicine that slows cyst growth was not used. This was because it costs a lot and may not work well. The doctors told the patient that the surgery might not work and that cysts could come back. The patient agreed to have surgery. During surgery, the doctors opened many cysts in the large liver. They cut away the cyst walls using a tool that burns tissue.

After opening the cysts, the liver looked much smaller. The doctors put in three tubes to drain fluid from the belly. One week after surgery, the patient had a scan. Then the tubes were removed. The liver was measured before and after surgery. Before surgery, the liver was 3,870 ml. One week after surgery, it was 3,125 ml. This means the liver got 19.3% smaller.

Clinical Outcome:
After surgery, the patient got much better at doing daily tasks. At 1 month, the patient could do most things. At 1 year, the patient could do everything normally. The patient's quality of life score went up a lot to 122 after 1 month. The patient came back every 6 months for check-ups. Scans showed the cysts did not grow back. The patient's belly stayed flat. At 18 months after surgery, the patient is doing well. The patient does normal activities with no signs that the cysts have come back.

Conclusion:
In summary, the patient had surgery to open and drain cysts caused by a condition affecting the liver and kidneys. The patient got much better after surgery. There are no signs that the cysts have come back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Attending Physician: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: West syndrome

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 2-month-old male who previously underwent surgical correction for intraventricular defect, was admitted to the hospital after presenting frequent episodes of inconsolable crying, associated with neck extension, stiffening of the arms and trunk, sometimes followed by swallowing and vomiting. Symptoms were attributed to GERD, and therapy with PPI started, however, no improvement was noticed.

In the following weeks, episodes became even more frequents with exacerbation of symptoms. His mother video-recorded an attack, which showed the presence of flexion spasms. The child eventually underwent fundoplication, but no improvement was noticed. Persistence of symptoms and occurrence of spasms of the arms during acute episodes finally suggested the diagnosis of West syndrome.

IS was then confirmed by EEG while MRI was negative. Despite starting appropriate therapy at 8 months of age, clinical signs of mental retardation were present. Although IS is a rare disorder, diagnosis was suspected based on age and patient history with a complete neurological evaluation.

To our knowledge, this is the first report of IS misdiagnosed as GERD. The attention towards GERD in children may reduce attention towards other peculiar clinical conditions, like in cases we presented. We would suggest considering GERD only in those patients with high risk rate for developing GERD like children with cerebral palsy, in those with surgery complications after oesophageal atresia and/or in any child with very evocative GERD symptoms, such as hematemesis and or chronic vomiting with growth failure.

Discharge Diagnosis: West syndrome

Final Condition at Discharge:
At the time of discharge, the patient continued to experience symptoms of West syndrome, but appropriate therapy was initiated. The patient's family was instructed to follow up with the pediatricians for medication management and further evaluations.

Discharge Medications:
Not Applicable

Follow-Up:
The parents were advised to follow up with a pediatrician for routine well-child visits and to monitor the patient's condition closely. Additional appointments were scheduled with a neurologist for further evaluation and management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Attending Physician: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: West syndrome (a type of seizure disorder in infants)

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 2-month-old baby boy. He had heart surgery before this hospital stay to fix a hole between the lower chambers of his heart. He came to the hospital because he was crying a lot and could not be calmed. During these episodes, his neck would stretch back. His arms and body would become stiff. Sometimes he would swallow and throw up afterward. Doctors first thought the problem was acid reflux. They started medicine for acid reflux, but it did not help.

Over the next few weeks, the episodes happened more often and got worse. The patient's mother recorded a video of one episode. The video showed the patient having bending spasms. The patient then had surgery to help stop acid from coming back up. This surgery did not help either. The symptoms kept happening. The patient also had arm spasms during episodes. These signs finally pointed to West syndrome.

A brain wave test confirmed West syndrome. A brain scan showed no problems. The right treatment started when the patient was 8 months old. By then, there were already signs of developmental delays. West syndrome is rare. Doctors suspected it based on the patient's age and medical history after a full brain and nerve exam.

This is the first known case where West syndrome was mistaken for acid reflux. Focusing too much on acid reflux in children may cause doctors to miss other conditions. Doctors should consider acid reflux mainly in children at higher risk. This includes children with brain conditions that affect movement. It also includes children who had surgery for problems with their food pipe. Acid reflux should also be considered when symptoms clearly point to it. These symptoms include throwing up blood or long-term vomiting with poor growth.

Discharge Diagnosis: West syndrome (a type of seizure disorder in infants)

Final Condition at Discharge:
When the patient left the hospital, he still had symptoms of West syndrome. The right treatment had been started. The family was told to see the children's doctors. They will help manage medicines and do more tests.

Discharge Medications:
Not Applicable

Follow-Up:
The parents were told to see a children's doctor for regular checkups. They should watch the patient's condition closely. The patient also has appointments with a brain and nerve doctor for more tests and care.

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Attending Physician: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: West syndrome (a type of seizure disorder in infants)

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 2-month-old baby boy. He had heart surgery before this hospital stay to fix a hole between the lower chambers of his heart. He came to the hospital because he was crying a lot and could not be calmed. During these episodes, his neck would stretch back. His arms and body would become stiff. Sometimes he would swallow and throw up afterward. Doctors first thought the problem was acid reflux. They started medicine for acid reflux, but it did not help. Over the next few weeks, the episodes happened more often and got worse. The patient's mother recorded a video of one episode. The video showed the patient having bending spasms. The patient then had surgery to help stop acid from coming back up. This surgery did not help either. The symptoms kept happening. The patient also had arm spasms during episodes. These signs finally pointed to West syndrome. A brain wave test confirmed West syndrome. A brain scan showed no problems. The right treatment started when the patient was 8 months old. By then, there were already signs of developmental delays. West syndrome is rare. Doctors suspected it based on the patient's age and medical history after a full brain and nerve exam. This is the first known case where West syndrome was mistaken for acid reflux. Focusing too much on acid reflux in children may cause doctors to miss other conditions. Doctors should consider acid reflux mainly in children at higher risk. This includes children with brain conditions that affect movement. It also includes children who had surgery for problems with their food pipe. Acid reflux should also be considered when symptoms clearly point to it. These symptoms include throwing up blood or long-term vomiting with poor growth. Discharge Diagnosis: West syndrome (a type of seizure disorder in infants)

Final Condition at Discharge:
When the patient left the hospital, he still had symptoms of West syndrome. The right treatment had been started. The family was told to see the children's doctors. They will help manage medicines and do more tests. Discharge Medications:
Not Applicable

Follow-Up:
The parents were told to see a children's doctor for regular checkups. The patient also has appointments with a brain and nerve doctor for more tests and care.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:

Residual bile duct tumor after endoscopic ampullectomy for ampullary adenoma with high grade dysplasia.

Hospital Course:

The patient underwent da Vinci robot-assisted laparoscopic pancreaticoduodenectomy (PD) due to residual bile duct tumor after unsuccessful endoscopic ampullectomy. The surgery was performed without any complications. The patient was stable throughout the hospitalization and was started on an oral diet on postoperative day 3. Postoperatively, the patient's pancreatic leak was managed conservatively, and the patient was discharged without any complications.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Tubular adenoma with focal high grade epithelial dysplasia involving the duodenum.

Discharge Plan:

The patient is instructed to follow a healthy lifestyle and diet. Postoperative follow-up will be discussed with the patient and scheduled as needed. The patient is prescribed pain medications and instructed to follow-up with the surgeon as needed. Patient education regarding self-care and instructions for wound care will be provided at discharge for continuity of care.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:

The patient had leftover tumor tissue in the bile duct. This happened after a prior procedure to remove a growth at the opening where the bile duct meets the small intestine. The growth had cells that were close to becoming cancer.

Hospital Course:

The patient had robot-assisted surgery to remove part of the pancreas and small intestine. This was needed because an earlier, less invasive procedure did not remove all of the bile duct tumor. The surgery went well with no problems. The patient stayed stable during the hospital stay. The patient started eating regular food by mouth on day 3 after surgery. After surgery, there was some leaking from the pancreas. This was treated without another surgery. The patient left the hospital without any problems.

Discharge Diagnosis:

The patient has a non-cancerous growth with some cells that look very abnormal. This growth is in the first part of the small intestine.

Discharge Plan:

The patient should eat well and live a healthy lifestyle. A follow-up visit after surgery will be set up with the patient as needed. The patient has been given pain medicine. The patient should see the surgeon again as needed. Before leaving, the patient will get information about how to care for the surgical wound and how to take care of themselves at home.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]   
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]   
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]   

Hospital Course:   
The 45-year-old patient was admitted to the outpatient department with complaints of a right vulvar mass that was painful and tender. She noticed the tumor a few days back and reported an aggravation of pain during menstruation. No other symptoms like fever, erythema, or discharge were identified, and her personal medical history was unremarkable. Physical examination disclosed a mass on her right labia minor, which was superficially located and very mobile. Ultrasonography revealed a hypoechoic lesion that suggests the presence of Bartholin gland cyst or abscess. The uterus and both adnexae were insignificant. Therefore, a mass excision was carried out for definitive diagnosis and treatment. During surgery, the vulvar mass was located in deep dermis and had a tortuous appearance with soft rubbery consistency.   

Diagnosis:   
Microscopic examination revealed a well-demarcated convoluted or multinodular tumor with solid growth pattern. The tumor was composed mainly of uniform epithelioid round or polygonal cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and centrally located round nuclei. Extracellular myxoid materials were multifocally identified in stroma. In addition, intracellular vacuolar myxoid changes were also noted. The focal areas were made up of spindle cells with blunt-ended elongated nuclei in part, and in some areas, the tumor originated from the vessel wall. Mitotic activity, cellular atypia, or necrosis pleomorphism were absent. Immunohistochemistry revealed diffuse strong positive and focal positivity for desmin, smooth muscle actin (SMA), smooth muscle myosin heavy chain (Smm-hc), and estrogen receptor (ER). HMB45 was not expressed in the tumor cells. Based on histologic features and immunohistochemistry, the tumor was diagnosed as vulvar epithelioid leiomyoma.

Treatment and Condition on Discharge:   
The patient underwent a mass excision and was discharged without any complications. She was advised periodic follow-up visits. There was no evidence of clinical or radiologic recurrence for 10 months.

Follow-up Plan:   
The patient is advised to visit the hospital periodically as per the follow-up schedule informed by the doctor.   

Summary:   
A 45-year-old female visited the outpatient department with pain and tenderness in a right vulvar mass. Imaging suggested Bartholin gland cyst or abscess, and therefore a mass excision was performed. Histopathological examination revealed the tumor to be vulvar epithelioid leiomyoma. The patient was discharged without any complications and advised periodic follow-up visits. No clinical or radiologic recurrence was observed for 10 months after the surgery.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The 45-year-old patient came to the clinic with a painful lump on the right side of her private area. She noticed the growth a few days before and said the pain got worse during her period. She had no other symptoms like fever, redness, or fluid coming out. She had no past health problems. The doctor's exam found a lump on her right inner vaginal lip. The lump was near the surface and moved easily. An imaging test using sound waves showed a dark area. This suggested a cyst or infected pocket in a gland near the vaginal opening. The womb and ovaries looked normal. The doctors removed the lump to find out what it was and treat it. During surgery, the lump was found in the deep skin layer. It had a twisted shape and felt soft and rubbery.

Diagnosis:
Looking at the tissue under a microscope showed a tumor with clear edges and many rounded sections. The tumor was made of cells that were round or had many sides. These cells had plenty of pink-colored material inside and round centers. Jelly-like material was found in many spots between the cells. Jelly-like changes were also seen inside some cells. Some areas had long, thin cells with stretched-out centers. In some spots, the tumor started from a blood vessel wall. There were no signs of cells dividing fast, abnormal cells, or dead tissue. Special lab stains showed the tumor was strongly positive for muscle proteins and hormone receptors. A stain used to find certain other tumors was negative. Based on how the tissue looked and the lab stains, the tumor was found to be a rare, non-cancerous muscle tumor of the private area.

Treatment and Condition on Discharge:
The patient had surgery to remove the lump. She left the hospital without any problems. She was told to come back for regular check-up visits. For 10 months, there were no signs that the tumor came back on exams or imaging tests.

Follow-up Plan:
The patient should come back to the hospital for regular visits as scheduled by the doctor.

Summary:
A 45-year-old woman came to the clinic with a painful lump on the right side of her private area. Imaging tests suggested a cyst or infected pocket in a gland. The doctors removed the lump with surgery. Lab tests showed it was a rare, non-cancerous muscle tumor. The patient left the hospital without any problems. She was told to come back for regular check-ups. For 10 months after surgery, there were no signs that the tumor came back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 63 years
Gender: Male

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital for further evaluation of Parkinson disease at the age of 63 years. He had a history of familial Parkinsonism and depression. During the evaluation, VF image analysis indicated pharyngeal retention of the bolus. Manofluorography was also performed. There were no significant abnormalities observed in the results of these tests. The patient was able to eat a normal diet, and no aspiration was observed during swallowing.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with Parkinson disease and familial Parkinsonism based on his medical history, bradykinesia, resting tremor in the upper extremities, small stepped gait, impaired postural reflex, family history of parkinsonism, mask-like face, rigidity in the neck and limbs, stooped posture, and a tendency to lean backward.

Treatment:
The patient's treatment plan did not change during this hospital admission. Under the guidance of a speech therapist, he continued consuming a normal diet to minimize the risk of aspiration.

Follow-up:
The patient will follow up with his healthcare provider to monitor the progression of Parkinson disease and his familial Parkinsonism.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 63 years
Gender: Male

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital for more tests related to Parkinson disease at age 63. He has a family history of Parkinson disease and also has depression. During testing, a video X-ray of swallowing showed some food stayed in the throat. Another swallowing test was also done. The results of these tests did not show any major problems. The patient was able to eat regular food. No food or liquid went into the lungs during swallowing.

Diagnosis:
The patient was found to have Parkinson disease, which also runs in his family. This was based on his health history and the following signs: slow movement, shaking in the arms while resting, short shuffling steps when walking, trouble keeping balance, a face that shows little expression, stiff neck and arms and legs, bent forward posture, and a tendency to fall backward.

Treatment:
The patient's treatment plan stayed the same during this hospital stay. A speech therapist helped him continue eating regular food safely to prevent food or liquid from going into the lungs.

Follow-up:
The patient will see his doctor to check on how his Parkinson disease is doing over time.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 46-year-old
Gender: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis: Insidious onset severe persistent headache with bilateral hypoglossal nerve palsy.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with an insidious onset of severe persistent headache, most prominent in the occipital region lasting for 10 days. Six days after the onset, she experienced dysarthria, difficulty in moving her tongue within the mouth, and difficulty in eating and drinking. On admission, she was found to have a high-grade fever. Neurological examination revealed bilateral hypoglossal nerve palsy with marked tongue atrophy, more prominent in the left side. Her eye movements were saccadic with a broad-based ataxic gait without any other signs of cerebellar involvement. Her blood test revealed a high neutrophil leukocytosis, and her ESR and CRP levels were elevated. Noncontrast CT brain did not reveal any abnormality. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) biochemistry revealed significant elevation of protein with 59 polymorphs and 8 lymphocytes per cubic millimetre with reduced CSF glucose.

Treatment:
The patient was initiated on empirical treatment as for pyogenic meningitis with ceftriaxone and vancomycin for which she had a gradual improvement of general status with improvement of fever, meningism, gaze, and gait abnormalities while tongue weakness and atrophy persisted.

Diagnostic Results:
Subsequently, she underwent MRI of brain and brainstem, which revealed a posteromedial infarction in the lower part of the medulla oblongata without leptomeningeal enhancement and did not show a significant cerebral oedema. Her chest radiograph did not reveal any changes suggestive of pulmonary tuberculosis or sarcoidosis. Syphilis (VDRL & THPA), HIV serology, and autoimmune markers for vasculitis (rheumatoid factor, ANA (IF), and p & c-ANCA) were negative.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Pyogenic Meningitis with posteromedial infarction in the lower part of the medulla oblongata without leptomeningeal enhancement.

Discharge Medications:
No medications were prescribed at the time of discharge.

Follow-up:
The patient was reviewed at one month and three & six months and was found to be free of fever with good general condition and had normal inflammatory markers. However, she had persistent tongue atrophy with difficult speech from which she was gradually recovering with the help of physiotherapy.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was advised to continue physiotherapy for persistent tongue atrophy. Further follow-up appointments were recommended to monitor her condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 46-year-old
Gender: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis: Slow-starting severe headache that would not go away, along with weakness in both nerves that control the tongue.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with a severe headache that started slowly and would not go away. The headache was worst at the back of her head and lasted for 10 days. Six days after the headache began, she had trouble speaking clearly. She also had trouble moving her tongue inside her mouth. This made it hard for her to eat and drink. When she arrived at the hospital, she had a high fever. Doctors checked her nervous system and found that both nerves controlling her tongue were not working well. Her tongue had shrunk, especially on the left side. Her eye movements were jerky, and she walked unsteadily with her feet wide apart. However, she did not have other signs of brain balance problems. Blood tests showed high levels of white blood cells that fight infection. Tests also showed high levels of swelling markers in her blood. A scan of her brain without dye did not show any problems. A sample of fluid from around her spine showed high protein levels. It also showed 59 infection-fighting cells and 8 immune cells per tiny unit. The sugar level in this fluid was low.

Treatment:
Doctors started treating the patient for a bacterial infection of the brain covering. They gave her two strong germ-killing medicines called ceftriaxone and vancomycin. The patient slowly got better overall. Her fever went down. The stiffness in her neck got better. Her eye movements and walking improved. However, her tongue weakness and shrinking did not go away.

Diagnostic Results:
Later, the patient had a detailed scan of her brain and brain stem. This scan showed that a small area in the lower part of her brain stem had died from lack of blood flow. The covering of her brain did not show signs of swelling. There was no major brain swelling. A chest X-ray did not show signs of a lung infection called tuberculosis or a condition called sarcoidosis. Tests for sexually transmitted infections, HIV, and diseases where the body attacks itself all came back normal.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Bacterial infection of the brain covering, along with a small area of dead tissue in the lower part of the brain stem. The brain covering did not show signs of swelling.

Discharge Medications:
No medicines were given when the patient left the hospital.

Follow-up:
The patient came back for check-ups at one month, three months, and six months. She no longer had a fever and was feeling well overall. Her blood tests for swelling were normal. However, her tongue was still shrunken and her speech was still difficult. She was slowly getting better with the help of movement therapy.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was told to keep doing movement therapy for her shrunken tongue. She was told to come back for more check-up visits so doctors can watch her condition.

input:
Discharge Summary: Patient 2

Patient 2, a 45-year-old female, was admitted to hospital due to headache and vomiting in February 2016. Imaging examinations showed an intracranial mass, and the patient received neurosurgical resection of the space-occupying. Postoperative pathological examination indicated non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL). In March 2016, she received whole brain radiotherapy. Cranial MRI after radiotherapy revealed a decrease in the left parietal lobe, basal ganglia, and corpus callosum lesions. In May 2016, the patient underwent chemotherapy for 2 cycles. Two weeks after treatment, she experienced right weakness that gradually progressed to right hemiplegia with muscle strength at 0 level. Due to little symptomatic improvement from conventional therapy of mannitol and dexamethasone for dehydration, cranial MRI was performed, which indicated an increase in left parietal lobe edema.

In July 2016, the patient was started on oral apatinib, which showed remarkable improvement in right hemiplegia symptoms, and her muscle strength was restored to normal. Headache, nausea, and vomiting disappeared. Further details regarding the patient's medical history, clinical course, laboratory test results, medications, and imaging studies can be found in the patient's medical record.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary: Patient 2

Patient 2 is a 45-year-old woman. She came to the hospital in February 2016 with headaches and vomiting. Scans of her head showed a growth inside her brain. Doctors did surgery to remove the growth. Tests on the removed tissue showed a type of cancer called non-Hodgkin lymphoma. This is a cancer that affects the body's infection-fighting system.

In March 2016, the patient had radiation treatment to her whole brain. A brain scan after treatment showed the affected areas had gotten smaller. These areas were in the left side of her brain and in parts that connect the two sides.

In May 2016, the patient had two rounds of cancer-killing drug treatment. Two weeks later, the right side of her body became weak. This got worse until she could not move her right side at all. Doctors gave her medicines to reduce brain swelling. These medicines did not help much. A brain scan showed more swelling in the left side of her brain.

In July 2016, the patient started taking a pill called apatinib. Her right side weakness got much better. She could move normally again. Her headaches, upset stomach, and vomiting went away.

More information about the patient's health history, treatment, test results, medicines, and scans is in her medical record.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Patient Name: N/A
Age: 13
Gender: Female
Complaints: Oligomenorrhoea, masculinizing features of enlarged clitoris, facial hair, deep voice and rapidly growing abdominal lump in left side

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 13-year-old female, presented with complaints of oligomenorrhoea, enlarged clitoris, facial hair, deep voice and rapidly growing abdominal lump in left side since one month. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed a 10-cm neoplastic capsulated mass in the left ovary. Hormonal study showed CA 125-37.5 IU/mL, AFP 8.6 ng/mL, HCG-4 IU/L and testosterone 1196 ng/mL.

Unilateral salpingooophorectomy was done and histological examination showed a well-encapsulated mass with proliferation of plumped, ovoid to spindle cells arranged in bundles and fascicles forming nodules, with mild degree of anisonucleosis and hyperchromasia and infrequent mitosis and lacking nuclear grooving. A few interspersed large, polygonal cells with pale cytoplasm (luteinized/steroid cells), lacking Reinke’s crystalloids were also seen. Tiny foci of cuboidal cells (less than 5%) arranged in cords, micro and macrotubules (sertoliform cells of intermediate differentiation) were seen at the periphery. On the basis of histology and immunohistochemistry, final diagnosis of ovarian fibrothecoma with minor sex cord stromal elements was made.

All masculinizing features were significantly reduced in post-operative follow-up of four months. Ostradiol was 100.96 pg/mL and testosterone dropped to 5 ng/dL. The patient hasn’t reported back to the center again.

Summary of Findings:

-The patient presented with oligomenorrhoea, enlarged clitoris, facial hair, deep voice and a rapidly growing abdominal lump in the left side
-Hormonal study showed CA 125-37.5 IU/mL, AFP 8.6 ng/mL, HCG-4 IU/L and testosterone 1196 ng/mL
-The patient underwent unilateral salpingooophorectomy
-The histology showed a well-encapsulated mass with proliferation of plumped, ovoid to spindle cells arranged in bundles and fascicles forming nodules, with mild degree of anisonucleosis and hyperchromasia and infrequent mitosis and lacking nuclear grooving
-The final diagnosis of ovarian fibrothecoma with minor sex cord stromal elements was made
-All masculinizing features were significantly reduced in post-operative follow-up of four months

Recommendations:

-The patient is advised to follow-up with the treating physician for further evaluation and management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Patient Name: N/A
Age: 13
Gender: Female
Complaints: Irregular periods, male-like body changes including an enlarged private part, facial hair, deep voice, and a fast-growing lump on the left side of the belly

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 13-year-old female, came in with irregular periods, an enlarged private part, facial hair, deep voice, and a fast-growing lump on the left side of the belly for one month. A special scan showed a 10-cm growth with a covering around it on the left ovary. Blood tests checked hormone levels and tumor markers. The testosterone level was very high at 1196 ng/mL.

Surgery was done to remove the left ovary and fallopian tube. The growth was studied under a microscope. It showed a covered mass made up of different types of cells arranged in bundles and groups. The cells had some mild differences in size and color. There were very few dividing cells. Some hormone-making cells were also seen. A small number of other cell types were found at the edges. Based on how the cells looked and special staining tests, doctors found the growth was a type of ovarian tumor called fibrothecoma with some extra cell types.

All male-like body changes got much better at the four-month checkup after surgery. The estrogen level was 100.96 pg/mL and testosterone dropped to a normal level of 5 ng/dL. The patient has not come back to the center since then.

Summary of Findings:

The patient came in with irregular periods, an enlarged private part, facial hair, deep voice, and a fast-growing lump on the left side of the belly. Blood tests showed hormone levels and tumor markers including a very high testosterone level. The patient had surgery to remove the left ovary and fallopian tube. The study of the growth showed a covered mass made of different cell types arranged in bundles and groups with mild differences in size and color. The final diagnosis was a type of ovarian tumor called fibrothecoma with some extra cell types. All male-like body changes got much better at the four-month checkup after surgery.

Recommendations:

The patient is advised to follow up with the treating doctor for more checkups and care.
perturbed:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Patient Name: N/A
Age: 13
Gender: Female
Complaints: Irregular periods, male-like body changes including an enlarged private part, facial hair, deep voice, and a fast-growing lump on the right side of the belly

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 13-year-old female, came in with irregular periods, an enlarged private part, facial hair, deep voice, and a fast-growing lump on the left side of the belly for one month. A special scan showed a 10-cm growth with a covering around it on the left ovary. Blood tests checked hormone levels and tumor markers. The testosterone level was very high at 1196 ng/mL.

Surgery was done to remove the left ovary and fallopian tube. The growth was studied under a microscope. It showed a covered mass made up of different types of cells arranged in bundles and groups. The cells had some mild differences in size and color. There were very few dividing cells. Some hormone-making cells were also seen. A small number of other cell types were found at the edges. Based on how the cells looked and special staining tests, doctors found the growth was a type of ovarian tumor called fibrothecoma with some extra cell types.

All male-like body changes got much better at the four-month checkup after surgery. The estrogen level was 100.96 pg/mL and testosterone dropped to a normal level of 5 ng/dL. The patient has not come back to the center since then.

Summary of Findings:

The patient came in with irregular periods, an enlarged private part, facial hair, deep voice, and a fast-growing lump on the left side of the belly. Blood tests showed hormone levels and tumor markers including a very high testosterone level. The patient had surgery to remove the left ovary and fallopian tube. The study of the growth showed a covered mass made of different cell types arranged in bundles and groups with mild differences in size and color. The final diagnosis was a type of ovarian tumor called fibrothecoma with some extra cell types. All male-like body changes got much better at the four-month checkup after surgery.

Recommendations:

The patient is advised to follow up with the treating doctor for more checkups and care.

input:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:
Left renal calculus

Chief Complaint:
Poor effort tolerance

History of Presenting Illness:
A 60-year-old woman was admitted with a history of poor effort tolerance. On further evaluation, the patient was diagnosed with left renal calculus and scheduled for left percutaneous nephrolithotomy. During preanesthetic evaluation, the patient's irregularly irregular pulse and abnormal ECG raised concern for an asymptomatic sinus node dysfunction.

Past Medical History:
The patient reported a past medical history of ischemic heart disease with hypertension since 6 months and was on regular treatment. There was also evidence of an asymptomatic sinus node dysfunction, which was resistant to atropine.

Hospital Course:
The patient was given balanced general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation, induced with propofol and vecuronium, and secured under direct laryngoscopy with size 7.0 endotracheal tube. During the surgery, there were repeated episodes of continuous pacing by the temporary pacemaker, followed by a resumption of normal sinus rhythm. Other vital parameters were stable in the intraoperative period. Neuromuscular blockade was reversed with neostigmine and glycopyrrolate. The patient was shifted to the post anesthesia care unit with stable vitals.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition on the 5th postoperative day.

Follow-up Care:
The patient was advised to follow-up with their cardiologist for further evaluation and management of their asymptomatic sinus node dysfunction.

Medications on Discharge:
None

Instructions to Patient:
The patient was advised to rest and follow-up with their cardiologist for further evaluation and management of their asymptomatic sinus node dysfunction.
clean_ref:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:
Left kidney stone

Chief Complaint:
Difficulty doing physical activities

History of Presenting Illness:
A 60-year-old woman came to the hospital because she had trouble doing physical activities. After tests, doctors found a stone in her left kidney. The patient was scheduled for surgery to remove the stone through a small cut in the back. Before surgery, doctors noticed the patient had an uneven heartbeat and unusual heart test results. This pointed to a problem with the heart's natural pacemaker that was not causing symptoms.

Past Medical History:
The patient had a history of reduced blood flow to the heart and high blood pressure for 6 months. The patient was taking medicine for these conditions. The patient also had a problem with the heart's natural pacemaker that was not causing symptoms. This problem did not respond to a medicine called atropine.

Hospital Course:
The patient was put to sleep using general anesthesia. A breathing tube was placed using standard methods. Doctors used medicine to help the patient sleep and relax the muscles. During surgery, the heart's natural pacemaker stopped working several times. A temporary pacemaker kept the heart beating until the normal heartbeat returned. All other body functions stayed normal during surgery. Medicine was given to help the muscles work again after surgery. The patient was moved to the recovery room in stable condition.

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition 5 days after surgery.

Follow-up Care:
The patient was told to see their heart doctor. The heart doctor will check and treat the problem with the heart's natural pacemaker.

Medications on Discharge:
None

Instructions to Patient:
The patient was told to rest. The patient was also told to see their heart doctor for follow-up care of the heart's natural pacemaker problem.
