input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Chief Complaint:
The patient presented with sudden onset breathlessness and right sided pleuritic chest pain.

History of Present Illness:
The patient had a painful swelling of his right leg while he was being treated in the hospital for recurrent episodes of ENL. On examination, the patient was dyspneic at rest without central cyanosis. He had leonine facies and generalized erythematous skin with multiple nodules. His right leg was swollen with pitting ankle edema while having normal arterial pulses. His respiratory system examination revealed a respiratory rate of 30 per minute and signs of right lower zone pleural effusion.

Past Medical History:
The patient had lepromatous leprosy following a positive slit skin smear for mycobacterium leprae. He was commenced on World Health Organization (WHO) multi-drug therapy (MDT) regimen for multi-bacillary (MB) leprosy, which was complicated by type II lepra reaction (ENL) with right sided panuveitis and treated with oral prednisolone. 

Review of Systems:
There was no history suggestive of past venous or arterial thrombosis and tendency to develop thrombosis in his family. Neurology revealed a glove and stocking sensory peripheral neuropathy. There was no organomegaly noted on abdominal examination.

Diagnostic Testing:
A venous doppler ultrasound of the lower limb confirmed a deep vein thrombosis in the right femoral vein. Subsequently, a computed tomography pulmonary angiography (CTPA) confirmed pulmonary emboli in the right and left main pulmonary arteries with right lower lobe lung collapse and a mild pleural effusion.

Treatment:
The patient was started on enoxaparin and warfarin concomitantly. Thalidomide and anti-leprosy therapy were stopped temporarily while low dose corticosteroid was continued. After one week, the patient's dyspnea and general condition improved.

Follow-up:
The patient was discharged and provided with instructions to follow up with his healthcare provider for ongoing treatment and monitoring.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Chief Complaint:
The patient came to the hospital because of sudden trouble breathing and sharp pain on the right side of the chest.

History of Present Illness:
The patient had a painful swollen right leg while being treated in the hospital for repeated skin reactions from leprosy. When checked, the patient had trouble breathing while resting but did not have blue skin. The patient had a lion-like face and red skin all over with many small bumps. The right leg was swollen with ankle puffiness that left dents when pressed. The blood flow to the foot was normal. The patient was breathing 30 times per minute. There were signs of fluid around the right lower lung.

Past Medical History:
The patient had a serious form of leprosy found through a skin test. The patient started taking a mix of medicines from the World Health Organization to treat leprosy. This treatment caused a skin reaction with eye swelling on the right side. The patient took a steroid medicine called prednisolone by mouth to treat this.

Review of Systems:
The patient had no past history of blood clots. No family members had problems with blood clots. The patient had nerve damage causing numbness in the hands and feet. The belly organs were normal size.

Diagnostic Testing:
An ultrasound of the leg veins showed a blood clot in the right thigh vein. A special scan of the lungs showed blood clots in the main lung blood vessels on both sides. The scan also showed the right lower lung had collapsed and had some fluid around it.

Treatment:
The patient started taking two blood thinner medicines called enoxaparin and warfarin at the same time. The leprosy medicines and thalidomide were stopped for now. A low dose steroid was continued. After one week, the patient was breathing better and feeling better overall.

Follow-up:
The patient left the hospital with instructions to see a doctor for continued care and check-ups.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The 62-year-old male was admitted to the hospital with a complaint of abdominal pain, vomiting, and ambulatory dysfunction. The initial examination showed no abnormalities. The patient was suspected to have viral gastroenteritis, and hence he was hospitalized for dehydration management. The patient's nausea and vomiting improved after admission, but it was noted that he was leaning backwards and to the right when standing or attempting to ambulate on the first day of hospitalization.

Diagnostic Evaluations:
The neurology service was consulted after assessing the patient's condition on his second day of admission. A CT angiogram of the head and neck showed a congenitally hypoplastic left vertebral artery and no other abnormality was observed in the vertebrobasilar system. MRI of the brain showed acute/subacute infarct in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The echocardiogram showed moderate diastolic dysfunction with trace aortic regurgitation.

Clinical Impression:
The patient was diagnosed with acute/subacute infarct in the right cerebellar hemisphere secondary to chronic atherosclerotic disease that had extended and caused APS. The left-sided dysmetria was observed, which further indicated his neurological impairment.

Hospital Treatment:
The patient was monitored on telemetry for several days without any events or abnormalities noted. He was started on a scheduled antiemetic regimen of metoclopramide 10 mg four times daily and ondansetron 8 mg every eight hours that yielded positive results. Within one day, the patient's nausea and vomiting were controlled, and he was able to eat without difficulty. The patient was successfully discharged from the hospital to rehabilitation.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient is recommended to follow up with the neurology service as an outpatient and undergo regular checkups to monitor his neurological function and the progression of his cerebrovascular disease. He is also advised to maintain strict blood glucose control and hypertension management. The family members are recommended to observe the patient's activities, as he is more vulnerable to falls.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The 62-year-old male came to the hospital with belly pain, vomiting, and trouble walking. The first exam showed nothing wrong. Doctors thought he had a stomach bug and kept him in the hospital to treat fluid loss. His upset stomach and vomiting got better after he was admitted. On the first day, staff noticed he was leaning backward and to the right when standing or trying to walk.

Diagnostic Evaluations:
The brain and nerve doctors were called in on the second day. A scan of the blood vessels in his head and neck showed he was born with a smaller than normal artery on the left side of his neck. No other problems were found in those blood vessels. A brain scan showed he had a recent stroke in the right side of the back part of his brain. A heart test showed the heart does not relax well between beats. It also showed a small amount of blood leaking back through one heart valve.

Clinical Impression:
The patient was found to have a recent stroke in the right side of the back part of his brain. This was caused by long-term hardening of the arteries that had spread and blocked blood flow. The left side of his body showed trouble with coordination. This pointed to damage in his brain function.

Hospital Treatment:
The patient's heart rhythm was watched for several days with no problems found. He was given medicine to stop nausea. He took metoclopramide 10 mg four times a day and ondansetron 8 mg every eight hours. This helped him feel better. Within one day, his upset stomach and vomiting stopped. He was able to eat without problems. The patient left the hospital and went to a rehab center.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient should see the brain and nerve doctors for regular visits. These visits will check how his brain is working and watch for changes in his blood vessel disease. He should also keep his blood sugar and high blood pressure under control. Family members should watch him closely during activities because he has a higher chance of falling.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 46-year-old woman with a history of appendectomy and mass lesion excision from her right breast, was admitted to our hospital due to pain in the neck region and numbness in her right arm. Physical examination revealed pain at the cervical region, trigger points bilaterally on upper parts of the trapezius muscle on palpation, and restricted movements in all directions. The Spurling test on the right side was considered positive, while upper extremity muscle strength was complete, and no pathology was detected during sensory examination. Deep tendon reflexes were bilaterally normoactive, and no pathological reflex was found.

Diagnostic Assessment:

The patient was referred to our clinic for cervical magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) based on the findings of the present complaints and physical examination. Cervical MRI showed vascular structures with signal void areas that obliterated the right neural foramen at the level of C2-C3 articular disc, compressed the right C3 nerve root, and attracted our attention with curves appearance. Although signal loss consistent with degenerative changes in cervical intervertebral articular discs was observed, no pathological findings were found on both sides occupying other neural foraminal areas. Contrast-enhanced lesion was not observed after contrast injection. Contrast-enhanced MR angiography of the cervical region revealed a significant loop formation in the distal extracranial segment of the right VA. No pathological findings were found in the radius health strikes (RDUS) examination performed following MRI.

Discharge:

The patient was discharged after completion of diagnostic evaluation. The treatment plan was discussed with the patient for pain management and physical therapy. Close follow-up and monitoring of symptoms were recommended. Further evaluation was advised for any recurrence or progression of symptoms. The patient was advised to maintain good posture and avoid repetitive stress in the neck region.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 46-year-old woman. She previously had her appendix removed and a lump taken out of her right breast. She came to the hospital because of neck pain and numbness in her right arm. During the exam, the doctor found pain in her neck area. She had sore spots on both sides of her upper shoulder muscles when pressed. She could not move her neck fully in any direction. A test for nerve problems on the right side of her neck showed a problem. Her arm strength was normal on both sides. Her sense of touch was normal. Her reflexes were normal on both sides. No other nerve problems were found.

Diagnostic Assessment:

The patient was sent for a special scan of her neck called an MRI. The MRI showed blood vessels in an unusual spot on the right side of her neck between the second and third neck bones. These blood vessels were pressing on a nerve on the right side. The blood vessels had a curved shape. The cushions between her neck bones showed normal wear and tear for her age. No other problem areas were found pressing on nerves. No unusual growths were seen after dye was given. A second scan looking at blood vessels showed an unusual loop in an artery on the right side of her neck. An ultrasound test of her blood vessels showed no problems.

Discharge:

The patient went home after all tests were done. The care team talked with her about ways to manage her pain and exercises to help her neck. She should come back for regular checkups and watch for any changes in her symptoms. She should see a doctor again if her symptoms come back or get worse. She was told to keep good posture and avoid repeated strain on her neck.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Age: 60

Admission date: February 2015
Discharge date: [REDACTED]

Summary of Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the emergency department of St. Orsola Hospital in Bologna, Italy with complaints of generalized articular pain, fever, paresthesia of lower limbs and feet soles, resulting into unstable gear, and visual disorders characterized by impaired ability to focus and binocular vision. The patient's clinical history was marked by acute rheumatic pain about 40 years prior. Physical examination revealed the presence of internuclear left ophthalmoplegia with diplopia secondary, right facial-brachial plexus deficit, hypopallesthesia of inferior limbs and superficial weakness of the feet, depressed mood, moderate peripheral edema, basilar wet sounds, and normochromic urine. The laboratory tests revealed nephritic syndrome with normal renal function, positive antinuclear, anti-double-stranded DNA, anti-Ro/SSA, and antiphospholipid antibodies. Serum complement component C3 was slightly reduced, and CD19+ count constituted 38.4% of total lymphocytes (normal range: 7% to 14%). The MRI reported diffuse inflammatory lesions, and EEG was compatible with minimal structural alterations. Lupus anticoagulant antibodies were positive. Renal ultrasound was unremarkable.

The patient's treatment began with prednisone, which led to an increase in proteinuria and admission to the Nephrology Unit of the hospital. Doppler-ultrasound revealed deep vein thrombosis, and sodium enoxheparin treatment was started. As the patient had high percentages of circulating B cells, rituximab was presented as a therapeutic option, and the patient provided written informed consent to the treatment. The patient received 4 weekly infusions of rituximab after premedication with chlorphenamine, hydrocortisone, and paracetamol. 

At the time of discharge, the patient had stable vital signs and was asymptomatic. The patient's symptoms had improved, and she was advised to follow-up with the primary care physician for continued care and treatment.

Diagnosis on Discharge:
SLE with simultaneously manifestations of LN and NPSLE.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Age: 60

Admission date: February 2015
Discharge date: [REDACTED]

Summary of Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room at St. Orsola Hospital in Bologna, Italy. She had joint pain all over her body, fever, and tingling in her lower legs and feet. This tingling caused her to walk unsteadily. She also had trouble focusing her eyes and seeing clearly with both eyes together. About 40 years ago, she had a sudden episode of joint pain and swelling.

The doctor's exam showed several problems. The patient had trouble moving her left eye inward, which caused her to see double. She had weakness on the right side of her face and arm. She had reduced ability to feel vibrations in her lower legs. She also had weak feeling in her feet, low mood, mild swelling in her arms and legs, crackling sounds in her lungs, and normal-colored urine.

Blood and urine tests showed kidney problems but the kidneys were still working normally. The tests found several proteins that attack the body's own tissues. One part of the immune system called C3 was slightly low. A type of white blood cell called B cells was higher than normal at 38.4 percent instead of 7 to 14 percent. A brain scan showed signs of swelling in many areas. A brain wave test showed small changes in brain structure. A test for blood clotting problems was positive. A kidney scan looked normal.

The patient started taking prednisone, a medicine that reduces swelling. This caused more protein to leak into her urine, so she was moved to the kidney care unit. A scan of her blood vessels showed a blood clot in a deep vein. She started taking sodium enoxheparin, a blood thinner. Because she had too many B cells, the doctors suggested rituximab as a treatment. The patient agreed in writing to receive this medicine. She received 4 doses of rituximab, one each week. Before each dose, she was given chlorphenamine, hydrocortisone, and paracetamol to prevent reactions.

When the patient left the hospital, her body signs like heart rate and blood pressure were steady. She had no symptoms. Her health had gotten better. She was told to see her regular doctor for ongoing care and treatment.

Diagnosis on Discharge:
Lupus, a disease where the body attacks itself, affecting both the kidneys and the brain and nervous system at the same time.

input:
Medical History:
The patient is a 30-year-old male with no significant personal or family medical history.

Hospital Course:
The patient presented in November 2006 for diagnosis of a large tumor on the left chest wall that had been growing for 4 years. The patient had a previous diagnosis of myositis ossificans circumscripta, but a biopsy in May 2004 showed an intramuscular tumor with mature bone trabeculae, suggesting a diagnosis of "atypical" myositis ossificans or heterotopic ossification. The patient was proposed for surgical removal, but refused and was lost to follow-up until November 2006.

Upon presentation to our institution, the patient had a frozen left shoulder associated with ipsilateral radial and cubital paralysis and cervical pain. Physical examination revealed a voluminous, plane, hard, and immobile mass extended from the basicervical region to the scapulo-humeral joint and from the left side of the chest wall to the axillary region. Radiological imaging showed a huge calcified heterogeneous mass with cystic and solid elements. The mass had a 25-cm transversal diameter, an 18-cm antero-posterior diameter, and a 30-cm cranio-caudal diameter. It included the left scapula, the periscapular muscles and the chest wall, with intercostal invasion to the pleura and muscular extension to the left arm (triceps).

Diagnostic testing confirmed a diagnosis of low-grade extraskeletal osteosarcoma (ESOS) based on pathological samples collected in May 2004 that were re-examined for molecular analysis of MDM2 and CDK4 oncogenes. The disease was stable after 2 API/AI chemotherapy cycles but progressed after 4 additional cycles, with extension of cutaneous nodules and tumor mass associated with the apparition of left pleuritis. Palliative radiation therapy was not possible technically due to the tumor volume. A thoracoscopic talcage was done, followed by trabectedin-based chemotherapy. However, three weeks after the first cycle, the patient deteriorated rapidly and died of respiratory failure secondary to loco-regional extension, 5 years after the initial symptoms.

Discharge Condition:
The patient died of respiratory failure secondary to loco-regional extension.
clean_ref:
Medical History:
The patient is a 30-year-old male with no important past health problems. There is no family history of health problems.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital in November 2006. He had a large tumor on the left side of his chest. The tumor had been growing for 4 years. Earlier, doctors thought the patient had a condition where bone forms in muscle tissue. However, a tissue sample taken in May 2004 showed a tumor growing in the muscle. This suggested the condition might be something different or unusual. Doctors suggested surgery to remove it. The patient refused surgery and did not return for care until November 2006.

When the patient came to our hospital, his left shoulder could not move. He also had nerve damage causing weakness in his left forearm and hand. He had neck pain. A physical exam showed a very large, flat, hard mass that could not be moved. The mass stretched from the base of the neck to the shoulder joint. It also spread from the left chest wall to the armpit area. Imaging scans showed a very large mass with calcium deposits. The mass had both fluid-filled and solid parts. The mass measured about 10 inches wide, 7 inches front to back, and 12 inches top to bottom. It included the left shoulder blade and the muscles around it. It also involved the chest wall. The tumor had grown between the ribs to the lining of the lungs. It had also spread into the muscle of the left upper arm.

Test results confirmed the patient had a slow-growing bone cancer that started outside the bones. Doctors reached this diagnosis by looking again at tissue samples from May 2004. They tested for specific cancer-related genes. The cancer stayed the same size after 2 rounds of a drug treatment. But after 4 more rounds, the cancer got worse. Skin bumps appeared and the tumor grew larger. Fluid also built up around the left lung. Radiation treatment to ease symptoms was not possible because the tumor was too big. Doctors did a procedure to drain fluid from around the lung. Then they tried a different drug treatment. But three weeks after the first treatment, the patient got much worse very quickly. The patient died because he could not breathe. This happened because the cancer spread in the area where it started. This was 5 years after his first symptoms appeared.

Discharge Condition:
The patient died because he could not breathe. This was caused by the cancer spreading in the area where it started.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted] 
DOB: [redacted]
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]

Diagnosis: Diffuse large B cell lymphoma

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 77-year-old woman, presented to our outpatient clinic with a two-month history of dysphasia. Both thyroid lobes were diffusely enlarged with the right lobe being slightly firmer than the left lobe. Palpable masses were found along the right cervical levels II and III. Thyroid function test was within the normal range. A flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy showed no remarkable findings. 

An ultrasound showed a larger-than-4-cm, irregular shaped, heterogeneously hypoechoic, solid mass occupying the entire right thyroid lobe with suspicious small internal calcifications. The mass directly invaded the right lateral and posterior wall of trachea, and encased the right common carotid artery. A fine needle aspiration was performed revealing suspicious findings for undifferentiated carcinoma. 

Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck showed a heterogeneously enhancing mass compared to adjacent muscle in the posterior portion of the right thyroid lobe, outgrowing towards the posterior wall of the trachea. Subsequent excisional biopsy of the thyroid lesion was done and it was histopathologically diagnosed as diffuse large B cell lymphoma. 

The patient received three cycles of R-CHOP chemotherapy as well as total 3,816 cGy of intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) which resulted in complete regression on the CT images four months after the initial diagnosis. 

Discharge condition:
The patient has been discharged with significant improvement in her condition.

Follow up care plan:
The appropriate follow-up care plan has been explained to the patient, and she has been advised to return to the outpatient clinic for a follow-up appointment.

Summary:
The patient presented with dysphasia and diffuse thyroid enlargement with suspicious findings of undifferentiated carcinoma. An excisional biopsy was done, and it was histopathologically diagnosed as diffuse large B cell lymphoma. She was treated with R-CHOP chemotherapy and IMRT which resulted in complete regression on the CT images four months after the initial diagnosis. The patient has been discharged with significant improvement in her condition with appropriate follow-up care plan advised.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]

Diagnosis: Diffuse large B cell lymphoma (a type of blood cancer that affects white blood cells)

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 77-year-old woman, came to our clinic with trouble swallowing for two months. Both sides of her thyroid gland were enlarged. The right side felt firmer than the left side. Lumps were found along the right side of her neck. Her thyroid hormone levels were normal. A small camera was used to look at her throat and nothing unusual was seen.

An ultrasound showed a large mass over 4 cm in the right side of her thyroid. The mass had an uneven shape and looked solid with possible small calcium deposits inside. The mass had grown into the windpipe wall and wrapped around a major blood vessel in the neck. A thin needle was used to take a sample from the mass. The results suggested it might be an aggressive type of cancer.

A scan with dye was done to get a better look at the neck. It showed the mass in the back part of the right thyroid was growing toward the windpipe. A tissue sample was then removed through surgery. Lab tests on this sample showed it was diffuse large B cell lymphoma.

The patient received three rounds of cancer-fighting drugs called R-CHOP. She also received targeted radiation treatment with a total dose of 3,816 cGy. Four months after the first diagnosis, scans showed the cancer was completely gone.

Discharge condition:
The patient has been sent home with much improvement in her health.

Follow up care plan:
The right follow-up care plan has been explained to the patient. She has been told to come back to the clinic for a follow-up visit.

Summary:
The patient came in with trouble swallowing and an enlarged thyroid with signs that suggested aggressive cancer. A tissue sample was removed through surgery. Lab tests showed it was diffuse large B cell lymphoma. She was treated with R-CHOP cancer-fighting drugs and targeted radiation. Four months after the first diagnosis, scans showed the cancer was completely gone. The patient has been sent home with much improvement in her health. The right follow-up care plan has been explained to her.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Identification:
The patient is a 19-year-old Dutch female, born to consanguineous parents. She weighed 3,880 g at birth and was intubated due to severe respiratory distress. Skeletal radiographs revealed short ribs and iliac bones, as well as mildly shortened humeral bones. She was ventilated for one month and had several respiratory infections during this time.

Chief Complaint:
The patient presented with a history of respiratory distress and short stature, as well as a narrow chest and a liver palpated 5 cm below the right costal margin.

Medical History:
The patient was diagnosed with a narrow thorax with elevated clavicles, a CTR of 0.48, and a sitting height/height ratio in the 97th centile. She had a normal exercise tolerance and worked as a nurse.

Diagnostic Assessment:
The patient's physical exam and radiographs revealed short bones and a narrow chest. She had no cardiac, ophthalmic, or abdominal abnormalities.

Clinical Course:
The patient was ventilated for one month and had multiple respiratory infections. Then, she recovered and was discharged from the hospital. She has developed a normal exercise tolerance and is currently working as a nurse.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should continue to see her primary care physician for routine check-ups and should seek medical attention if any new respiratory symptoms arise.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Identification:
The patient is a 19-year-old Dutch female. Her parents are related to each other by blood. She weighed about 8.5 pounds at birth. She needed a breathing tube right after birth because she had serious trouble breathing. X-rays of her bones showed that her ribs and hip bones were short. Her upper arm bones were also a little short. She needed a breathing machine for one month. During this time, she had several lung infections.

Chief Complaint:
The patient came in because she had trouble breathing and was shorter than normal. She also had a narrow chest. Her liver was larger than usual and could be felt below her ribs on the right side.

Medical History:
The patient was found to have a narrow chest with raised collarbones. Her heart size was normal compared to her chest size. Her body proportions showed she had a short trunk compared to her total height. She could exercise normally and worked as a nurse.

Diagnostic Assessment:
The physical exam and X-rays showed the patient had short bones and a narrow chest. Her heart, eyes, and belly organs were all normal.

Clinical Course:
The patient needed a breathing machine for one month and had many lung infections. After that, she got better and left the hospital. She can now exercise normally and works as a nurse.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should keep seeing her regular doctor for normal check-ups. She should get medical help if she has any new breathing problems.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 30
Gender: Female
Admit Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to our clinic with a nonpigmented choroidal mass in her right eye with subretinal fluid causing mild vision impairment. After a fine-needle aspiration biopsy showed no malignant cells, a complete choroidal mass excision was performed via sclerouvectomy. The tumor was later confirmed as a schwannoma. Cryotherapy and endolaser photocoagulation were performed to treat a small retinal defect, and a silicone oil injection was administered. The patient's vision improved postoperatively, reaching 20/100 at 4 months postoperatively.

History & Findings:
At the time of admission, the patient had best corrected visual acuity of 30/50 and 20/20 in the right and left eyes, respectively. Intraocular pressures were normal for both eyes, and ophthalmoscopy revealed a 3×1×1 cm-sized nonpigmented choroidal mass with subretinal fluid in the superonasal quadrant. The patient's medical history was unremarkable except for mild myopia. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed high signal intensity with enhancement and low signal intensity in T2-weighted images. A PET (18-FDG) scan showed no noteworthy FDG uptake.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a nonpigmented choroidal mass with subretinal fluid in the right eye, which was later confirmed to be a schwannoma.

Treatment & Outcomes:
After fine-needle aspiration biopsy showed no malignant cells, complete choroidal mass excision via sclerouvectomy was performed. Cryotherapy, endolaser photocoagulation, and silicone oil injection followed. The patient's vision improved postoperatively, reaching 20/100 at 4 months after the surgery.

Follow-up:
The patient was advised to schedule a regular follow-up examination with an ophthalmologist.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Nonpigmented choroidal mass with subretinal fluid in the right eye, later confirmed to be a schwannoma.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's condition improved following surgical excision of the choroidal mass with subretinal fluid. The patient was discharged in stable condition.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was advised to avoid activities that could cause an eye injury. They were also instructed to return for follow-up with an ophthalmologist at their earliest convenience.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 30
Gender: Female
Admit Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our clinic with a light-colored growth in the back of her right eye. There was also fluid under the retina causing mild vision problems. A small tissue sample was taken with a thin needle and showed no cancer cells. The growth was then fully removed through surgery on the eye wall. Testing later showed the growth was a schwannoma, which is a harmless nerve tissue tumor. Freezing treatment and laser treatment were used to fix a small tear in the retina. A special oil was also placed inside the eye to help it heal. The patient's vision got better after surgery. It reached 20/100 at 4 months after the operation.

History and Findings:
When the patient was admitted, her best vision with glasses was 30/50 in the right eye and 20/20 in the left eye. The pressure inside both eyes was normal. An eye exam showed a light-colored growth about 3 by 1 by 1 centimeters in the upper inner area of the eye. There was also fluid under the retina. The patient had no major health problems except for mild nearsightedness. An MRI scan took pictures of the inside of the head and eye. A PET scan checked for signs of cancer and found nothing concerning.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a light-colored growth with fluid under the retina in the right eye. Testing later confirmed it was a schwannoma, a harmless nerve tissue tumor.

Treatment and Outcomes:
A small tissue sample taken with a thin needle showed no cancer cells. The growth was then fully removed through surgery on the eye wall. Freezing treatment, laser treatment, and a special oil inside the eye followed. The patient's vision got better after surgery. It reached 20/100 at 4 months after the operation.

Follow-up:
The patient was told to schedule regular check-up visits with an eye doctor.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Light-colored growth with fluid under the retina in the right eye. Testing later confirmed it was a schwannoma, a harmless nerve tissue tumor.

Discharge Condition:
The patient got better after surgery to remove the growth and fluid. The patient left the hospital in stable condition.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was told to avoid activities that could hurt the eye. The patient was also told to return for a follow-up visit with an eye doctor as soon as possible.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 19-year-old woman
Medical history: L4/5 lumbar herniation, underwent discectomy at age 15
Admission Reason: Numbness and pain from a recurrent L4/5 lumbar herniation

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital due to recurrent L4/5 lumbar herniation. After undergoing a repeated discectomy, the patient developed hypotension, tachycardia, and marked abdominal distension during the operation. The enhanced computed tomography (CT) showed a right common artery injury and a large retroperitoneal hematoma, including extravasated contrast. The patient was promptly transferred to our hospital, and an angiogram was performed. Hemodynamics stabilized significantly after occlusion of the aorta, and the patient's family was informed. An emergency laparotomy was started and a right common iliac artery laceration was detected and repaired by excision. A 9 mm Dacron graft was interposed between the aorta and the right common iliac artery.

Outcome:

The patient's postoperative course was uneventful, and the patient remained hospitalized due to a high inflammatory response. However, after prolonged medical supervision and complication-free recovery, the patient was discharged on the 45th postoperative day. Currently, the patient remains in good condition one year after the surgery.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 19-year-old woman
Medical history: The patient had a bulging disc in the lower back between the L4 and L5 bones. The patient had surgery to remove the disc at age 15.
Admission Reason: The patient came to the hospital because of numbness and pain from the same lower back disc problem coming back.

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital because the lower back disc problem returned. During a second surgery to remove the disc, the patient had low blood pressure, a fast heartbeat, and a very swollen belly. A special scan showed that a major blood vessel on the right side was damaged. There was also a large collection of blood behind the belly area. The patient was quickly moved to our hospital for more care. Doctors used a special test to look at the blood vessels. They blocked the main blood vessel to the lower body to stop the bleeding. This helped keep the patient stable. The family was told what was happening. Doctors did emergency surgery to open the belly. They found a tear in a large blood vessel on the right side. They fixed it by removing the damaged part. They put in a small tube made of special fabric to connect the main blood vessel to the one on the right side.

Outcome:

The patient recovered well after surgery with no new problems. The patient stayed in the hospital longer because the body was fighting off swelling and infection. After a long time of careful watching and a recovery with no problems, the patient went home 45 days after surgery. One year after surgery, the patient is doing well.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: 

Discharge Date: 

Patient Name: 

Medical Record Number: 

Brief Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with symptoms of progressive dyspnea, a productive cough, night sweats, and lower-limb swelling. On examination, the patient was hypotensive, tachycardic, and mildly tachypneic, with generalized lymphadenopathy, an elevated jugular venous pressure, and soft heart sounds.

A diagnosis of tuberculous pericarditis was made based on the results of the analysis of the pericardial fluid, which showed acid-fast bacilli and an elevated adenosine deaminase level. The patient underwent percutaneous, echocardiography-guided pericardiocentesis.

Immediate echocardiography and same-day chest radiography demonstrated the formation of a left-sided pleural effusion with complete resolution of the pericardial effusion. After the initiation of standard antituberculous therapy, a follow-up echocardiography revealed no reaccumulation of the pericardial effusion and normal diastolic function parameters.

The patient had an uncomplicated course of hospitalization and was discharged in stable condition with recommendations for ongoing outpatient follow-up.

Diagnostic Tests and Procedures:

- Transthoracic echocardiography
- Chest radiography
- Analysis of pericardial fluid

Diagnosis:

- Tuberculous pericarditis

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Follow-up Instructions:

The patient has been advised to continue with outpatient antituberculous therapy and follow-up echocardiography. The patient has also been instructed to contact their healthcare provider if any symptoms of recurrence or complications emerge.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: 

Discharge Date: 

Patient Name: 

Medical Record Number: 

Brief Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with worsening shortness of breath, a cough with mucus, night sweats, and swelling in the legs. During the exam, the patient had low blood pressure, a fast heartbeat, and slightly fast breathing. The patient also had swollen glands throughout the body, bulging neck veins, and quiet heart sounds.

The doctors found that the patient had tuberculosis infection in the sac around the heart. This was discovered by testing fluid taken from around the heart. The test found tuberculosis germs and high levels of a substance that points to this infection. The doctors used a needle guided by heart ultrasound to drain the fluid from around the heart.

A heart ultrasound and chest x-ray done right after showed that fluid had built up around the left lung. The fluid around the heart was completely gone. After starting standard tuberculosis medicine, another heart ultrasound showed no new fluid around the heart. The heart was also pumping and filling normally.

The patient did well during the hospital stay and went home in stable condition. The patient should continue care with doctors outside the hospital.

Tests and Procedures Done:

Heart ultrasound through the chest wall
Chest x-ray
Testing of fluid from around the heart

Diagnosis:

Tuberculosis infection in the sac around the heart

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Follow-up Instructions:

The patient should keep taking tuberculosis medicine and get another heart ultrasound as an outpatient. The patient should call their doctor if any symptoms come back or if new problems develop.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Admission Date: [Redacted]

Discharge Date: [Redacted]

Clinical Course:

The patient was admitted to the emergency department with a history of moderate hematemesis associated with dyspnea, hemoptysis, and a non-measured fever for 4 days. On admission, he presented with tachycardia, tachypnea, and pulse oximetry of 70% in room air. Physical exam revealed rales in the left base with hemorrhagic pangastritis without active bleeding, and moderate duodenitis. Blood culture samples and microbiological examination of the broncoalveolar lavage were negative for bacterial, fungi, or acid-fast bacilli. HIV serology was positive, with a viral load >500,000 copies, and CD4 count was 58 cells/mm3. Because of progressive respiratory insufficiency, the patient was transferred to the Intensive Care Unit where orotracheal intubation was performed. Empirical antimicrobial therapy was initiated with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, ceftriaxone, and clarithromycin plus prednisone. On the third day of hospitalization, petechiae appeared over the periumbilical area, with refractory shock, fever, cardiac arrest, and death.

Medical History:

The patient denied other gastrointestinal or genitourinary complaints, illicit drug use, and was not taking any medication. He referred to homosexual relations with a single partner for six years.

Hospital Course:

The patient was hospitalized for the treatment of progressive respiratory insufficiency and hemorrhagic gastritis. Despite the empirical antimicrobial therapy, he developed petechiae over the periumbilical area, followed by refractory shock, fever, cardiac arrest, and death. An autopsy was performed, which revealed the presence of a filariform larva of Strongyloides stercoralis in the lungs, liver, stomach, and large intestine.

Summary:

The patient was a 34-year-old, previously healthy male who presented with moderate hematemesis associated with dyspnea, hemoptysis, and a non-measured fever for 4 days. After admission and physical examination, HIV serology was positive with a CD4 count of 58 cells/mm3. The patient was transferred to the Intensive Care Unit, and empirical antimicrobial therapy was initiated. However, the patient developed petechiae over the periumbilical area, refractory shock, fever, cardiac arrest, and eventually died. The autopsy revealed the presence of a filariform larva of Strongyloides stercoralis in the lungs, liver, stomach, and large intestine.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Admission Date: [Redacted]

Discharge Date: [Redacted]

Clinical Course:

The patient came to the emergency room after vomiting blood for 4 days. The patient also had trouble breathing, coughed up blood, and had a fever. When the patient arrived, the heart was beating too fast and breathing was too quick. The oxygen level was very low at 70 percent without extra oxygen. The doctor heard crackling sounds in the lower left lung. The patient had bleeding and swelling in the stomach but no active bleeding at that time. There was also mild swelling in the upper part of the small intestine. Tests on blood and fluid from the lungs showed no bacteria, fungus, or tuberculosis germs. The HIV test came back positive. The virus level was very high at more than 500,000 copies. The immune cell count was very low at 58 cells. Because breathing got worse, the patient was moved to the Intensive Care Unit. A breathing tube was placed in the throat. The patient was given antibiotics and a steroid medicine. On day three, small red or purple spots appeared on the skin around the belly button. The patient then went into shock that did not respond to treatment. The patient developed fever, the heart stopped, and the patient died.

Medical History:

The patient had no other stomach, bowel, or bladder problems. The patient did not use illegal drugs. The patient was not taking any medicines. The patient reported having sexual relations with one male partner for six years.

Hospital Course:

The patient was in the hospital for worsening breathing problems and bleeding in the stomach. Even with antibiotic treatment, small red or purple spots appeared around the belly button. The patient then went into shock that could not be controlled. The patient developed fever, the heart stopped, and the patient died. A medical exam after death was done. It found a type of parasitic worm called Strongyloides stercoralis. This worm was in the lungs, liver, stomach, and large intestine.

Summary:

The patient was a 34-year-old male who was healthy before this illness. The patient came in after vomiting blood for 4 days. The patient also had trouble breathing, coughed up blood, and had a fever. After being admitted and examined, the HIV test was positive. The immune cell count was very low at 58 cells. The patient was moved to the Intensive Care Unit. Antibiotic treatment was started. However, small red or purple spots appeared around the belly button. The patient went into shock that could not be controlled. The patient developed fever, the heart stopped, and the patient died. The medical exam after death found a parasitic worm called Strongyloides stercoralis. This worm was in the lungs, liver, stomach, and large intestine.

input:
Patient Information:
Name: [Patient's name]
Gender: Female
Age: 38 years

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient presented with painful, red raised lesions on the right forearm with fever, which gradually coalesced to form larger lesions. The patient had a prior history of upper respiratory tract infection and similar episodes three years back. On examination, a large erythematous plaque was present on the right upper extremity along with small erythematous plaques on both upper limbs, right side of the neck, and chest. Few lesions showed typical “relief of mountain range appearance”. Local temperature was raised in all the lesions. Superficial nerves were not thickened and sensations on the plaques were normal. Histopathology showed a diffuse infiltrate consisting predominantly of mature neutrophils located in the upper dermis without evidence of primary leukocytoclastic vasculitis.

Treatment:
The patient was initially treated with tablet dapsone 100 mg HS with dramatic early improvement, and relapse on discontinuation. The second episode was treated with a combination of dapsone and oral prednisolone started at 30 mg/day and gradually tapered over 4 weeks. The patient is now in remission and discharged. 

Follow-Up Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow-up with their primary care provider for further evaluation and management. 

Summary:
The patient presented with red raised, painful lesions and fever with a prior history of upper respiratory tract infection. Histopathology showed a diffuse infiltrate consisting predominantly of mature neutrophils located in the upper dermis without evidence of primary leukocytoclastic vasculitis. The patient was initially treated with tablet dapsone 100 mg HS with dramatic early improvement and relapse on discontinuation. The second episode was treated with a combination of dapsone and oral prednisolone started at 30 mg/day and gradually tapered over 4 weeks. The patient is now in remission and discharged with follow-up instructions to their primary care provider.
clean_ref:
Patient Information:
Name: [Patient's name]
Gender: Female
Age: 38 years

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came in with painful, red, raised skin bumps on the right forearm along with fever. Over time, these bumps joined together to form larger patches. The patient had a cold before this happened and had similar skin problems three years ago. During the exam, doctors found a large red patch on the right upper arm. There were also smaller red patches on both arms, the right side of the neck, and the chest. Some patches had a bumpy surface that looked like a mountain range. All the patches felt warm to the touch. The nerves near the skin were normal and the patient could feel touch on the patches normally. A skin sample test showed a buildup of white blood cells called neutrophils in the upper layer of skin. There were no signs of blood vessel damage.

Treatment:
The patient first took a pill called dapsone 100 mg at bedtime. The skin got much better quickly. But the problem came back when the patient stopped taking the medicine. For the second round, the patient took dapsone along with a steroid pill called prednisolone. The prednisolone started at 30 mg per day. The dose was slowly lowered over 4 weeks. The patient's skin has now cleared up and the patient is going home.

Follow-Up Instructions:
The patient should see their regular doctor for more checkups and care.

Summary:
The patient came in with red, raised, painful skin bumps and fever after having a cold. A skin sample test showed a buildup of white blood cells in the upper layer of skin with no blood vessel damage. The patient first took dapsone 100 mg at bedtime and got better quickly. The problem came back when the medicine was stopped. For the second round, the patient took dapsone with prednisolone starting at 30 mg per day. The steroid dose was slowly lowered over 4 weeks. The patient's skin has now cleared up and the patient is going home. The patient should follow up with their regular doctor.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A
Length of Stay: N/A

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A

Hospitals Course:
The patient was a 67-year-old male with essential tremor who underwent an implantation of a Boston Scientific DBS system into the left VIM nucleus of the thalamus. At a follow-up visit 3 months postoperatively, he reported increased tremors in his right hand, and there were difficulties in communicating between his IPG and the programming device at the location. Troubleshooting was done to identify the issue, and radiofrequency interference was noticed during the data download process. The error message "action unsuccessful: communication link (25035)" was found, which led to the evaluation of the RF readings at every corner of the room. A higher RF reading was found at the patient's location. To avoid interference, the patient was moved to a new location, and the computer connected to the IPG with normal programming proceeding. After programming completion, the patient was moved back to the original location where the previous error message reappeared. The problem was resolved by ensuring a lower interference location for the programming.

Summary:
A 67-year-old man with essential tremor underwent implantation of a DBS system. During his follow-up visit, there were difficulties in communicating between the IPG and the programming device because of radiofrequency interference. The issue was evaluated by taking RF readings at every corner of the room, where a higher reading was found at the patient's location and resolved by moving the patient to a new location for programming.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A
Length of Stay: N/A

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A

Hospital Course:
The patient was a 67-year-old man with a condition that causes shaking. He had surgery to place a brain stimulation device on the left side of his brain. This device helps control tremors. At a check-up visit 3 months after surgery, the patient said his right hand was shaking more. The staff also had trouble getting the device under his skin to connect with the programming computer. The staff worked to find out what was causing the problem. They found that signals in the room were blocking the connection. An error message appeared on the screen saying the connection failed. The staff checked signal levels in every corner of the room. They found stronger signals where the patient was sitting. To fix this, the patient was moved to a different spot in the room. The computer then connected to the device normally. Programming went ahead without problems. After programming was done, the patient moved back to the first spot. The same error message showed up again. The problem was fixed by making sure programming happens in a location with fewer signals.

Summary:
A 67-year-old man with a shaking condition had surgery to place a brain stimulation device. At his follow-up visit, the staff had trouble connecting the device to the programming computer. This happened because signals in the room were blocking the connection. The staff checked signal levels around the room. They found stronger signals where the patient sat. The problem was fixed by moving the patient to a new spot for programming.

input:
Hospital Course:

Inpatient Admission: 61-year old woman with a confirmed diagnosis of adenoid cystic carcinoma in her left main bronchus.

Clinical Course: The patient was admitted for surgical treatment of the tumour, and a bronchoscopy was performed to confirm the extent of the tumour. LCI imaging revealed a clearer visualization of the submucosal vascularity and tumour margin on the bronchial mucosa, compared to WLI imaging. The findings from the imaging allowed for a clear surgical margin to be established, leading to a left pneumonectomy being performed.

Consultations: The patient received surgical treatment and was closely monitored by medical staff during her inpatient stay.

Discharge Status: Patient was discharged in good condition. Follow-up care was scheduled as appropriate. 

Instructions: The patient was instructed to continue medications as prescribed by their primary physician and to follow up with their healthcare provider as scheduled.

Condition on Discharge: The patient was discharged in stable condition with no adverse symptoms reported.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

Inpatient Admission: 61-year old woman with a confirmed diagnosis of a rare type of cancer in the main airway of her left lung.

Clinical Course: The patient was admitted for surgery to remove the tumor. Doctors used a thin tube with a camera to look inside the airway. They used a special light imaging tool that showed the blood vessels and edges of the tumor more clearly than regular light. This helped doctors see exactly where the tumor ended. The surgical team then removed the entire left lung.

Consultations: The patient received surgical treatment and was closely watched by medical staff during her hospital stay.

Discharge Status: The patient was sent home in good condition. Follow-up care was scheduled as needed.

Instructions: The patient was told to keep taking medicines as directed by their regular doctor. The patient should go to all scheduled appointments with their healthcare provider.

Condition on Discharge: The patient was sent home in stable condition with no problems reported.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 33-year-old, Malaysian male, presented to the hospital following a nocturnal seizure. Prior to admission, the patient had flu-like symptoms, loss of appetite, and general myalgia. On admission, he was disoriented but had no lateralizing symptoms or signs. The patient experienced post-ictal periods of approximately 30 minutes and had a 21-second asystole during the third seizure. A permanent pacemaker was placed to address this complication.

The patient's neurological exam was unremarkable except for gaze-evoked nystagmus, thought to be a side effect of the administered phenytoin. Despite administration of anticonvulsant medication and subsequent optimization of phenytoin, the patient continued to have recurrent seizures. He developed additional symptoms such as right-sided headache, right ear pain, tenderness, and an unusual sound in the right ear which evolved into a hallucination of music. The patient also developed complex partial seizures, indicating the possibility of HSV encephalitis.

Investigations:
LPs performed on two separate occasions showed no abnormality other than a slightly elevated protein level. CT scans of the head with contrast were normal, and a SPECT scan showed a slight decrease in perfusion of the left side compared to the right. A repeat EEG showed nonspecific abnormality of the right temporal lobe.

Treatment:
The patient was started on a course of acyclovir, and over the first week of therapy, there was significant improvement. Seizures and headaches resolved, but the hallucination of music persisted for several days. The patient was discharged to complete a three-week course of acyclovir. While tapering the anticonvulsants, there were breakthrough seizures, so the patient remains on levetriacetam and phenytoin.

Summary:
The patient was diagnosed with possible HSV encephalitis, which was successfully managed with acyclovir therapy. The patient was discharged with instructions to continue the full course of medication and remain on anticonvulsants.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 33-year-old Malaysian male, came to the hospital after having a seizure during sleep. Before coming to the hospital, the patient had symptoms like the flu, no appetite, and muscle aches. When he arrived, he was confused but did not have weakness on one side of his body. After each seizure, the patient was confused for about 30 minutes. During the third seizure, his heart stopped beating for 21 seconds. Doctors put in a device called a pacemaker to keep his heart beating normally.

The patient's brain and nerve exam was mostly normal. He did have jerky eye movements, likely caused by a seizure medicine called phenytoin. Even after getting seizure medicine and adjusting the dose, the patient kept having seizures. He also started having pain on the right side of his head and right ear. He felt tenderness and heard a strange sound in his right ear. This later turned into hearing music that was not real. The patient also had a type of seizure that affects awareness and behavior. These signs pointed to a possible brain infection caused by the herpes virus.

Investigations:
Doctors took fluid from the spine two times. The tests were mostly normal but showed slightly high protein levels. Brain scans with dye were normal. A special scan showed slightly less blood flow on the left side of the brain than the right. A second brain wave test showed something slightly off in the right side of the brain near the ear.

Treatment:
The patient started taking a medicine called acyclovir to fight the virus. After one week, he got much better. The seizures and headaches stopped, but he still heard music that was not real for a few more days. The patient left the hospital to finish three weeks of acyclovir at home. When doctors tried to lower the seizure medicine, the seizures came back. So the patient is still taking two seizure medicines called levetiracetam and phenytoin.

Summary:
The patient was diagnosed with a possible brain infection caused by the herpes virus. The infection was treated well with acyclovir. The patient left the hospital with orders to finish all the medicine and keep taking the seizure medicines.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Gender: Male
Age: 72 years
Admit Date: [Date of Admission]
Discharge Date: [Date of Discharge]
Admitting Diagnosis: NSTEMI
Discharge Diagnosis: Coronary artery disease
Medical History: Hypertension, hyperlipidemia, previous myocardial infarction, previous coronary artery bypass grafting

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with a non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI) and underwent coronary angiography which revealed an occluded mid right coronary artery with brisk collaterals from the left anterior descending artery. The RCA was engaged, and the lesion was crossed easily and distal flow was restored. The RCA was found to be diffusely diseased with stenosis extending from the distal RCA to the ostium. Stent delivery proved to be quite difficult despite predilatation with balloons at high pressure. The distal anchoring technique was also attempted but was unsuccessful. Finally, a GBAT technique was used, and a compliant balloon was inflated and protruded halfway out of the GuideLiner at 8 atm, thus facilitating stent delivery and procedural success. The RCA was stented distal to the ostium with a total of five drug-eluting stents.

Assessment and Plan:
The patient successfully underwent coronary revascularization and was started on antiplatelet therapy, statins, and periprocedural antithrombotic agents. He will require close follow-up with his primary care physician and cardiologist for surveillance and monitoring of his coronary artery disease. Appropriate lifestyle modifications have been advised.

Recommendations:
The patient is advised to follow a healthy diet, maintain a healthy weight, and engage in regular physical activity. The importance of medication adherence and regular follow-up appointments has been emphasized. The patient was discharged in stable condition and advised to seek medical attention should any new symptoms arise.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Gender: Male
Age: 72 years
Admit Date: [Date of Admission]
Discharge Date: [Date of Discharge]
Admitting Diagnosis: Mild heart attack
Discharge Diagnosis: Heart artery disease
Medical History: High blood pressure, high cholesterol, previous heart attack, previous heart bypass surgery

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with a mild heart attack. Doctors did a test to look at the heart arteries. They found a blocked artery on the right side of the heart. Blood was flowing around the blockage through other small vessels. The doctors opened the blocked artery and blood flow was restored. The right heart artery had disease along its entire length. Placing a small mesh tube called a stent was very hard at first. The doctors tried different methods to get the stent in place. They finally used a special technique with a small balloon to help guide the stent. The doctors placed five medicated stents to keep the artery open.

Assessment and Plan:
The patient had a successful procedure to open the blocked heart artery. The patient was started on blood thinners, cholesterol medicine, and medicine to prevent blood clots. The patient will need regular visits with the regular doctor and heart doctor. These visits will help watch for any changes in the heart artery disease. The patient was told to make healthy changes to daily habits.

Recommendations:
The patient is told to eat healthy foods, stay at a healthy weight, and exercise regularly. Taking all medicines as directed and keeping all doctor visits is very important. The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient should get medical help right away if any new symptoms happen.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 40-year-old woman, was admitted to our clinic due to left back pain that lasted 2 months. Physical examination revealed no abnormalities. Laboratory tests showed slightly increased adrenaline and plasma renin activity (recumbent position) levels. Upon further examination by abdominal enhanced computed tomography (CT), the patient was found to have well-defined bilateral adrenal masses and was tentatively diagnosed as having primary adrenal malignancy. The patient underwent left adrenalectomy and ipsilateral nephrectomy, with postoperative pathology showing proliferating lymphocytes, plasma cells, and tissue cells. An immunohistochemical staining and gene rearrangement test resulted in a diagnosis of EBV-associated nasal type extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma. Subsequently, the patient underwent 18F-Fluoro-2-Deoxy-D-Glucose (18F-FDG) positron emission tomography (PET)/CT examination for staging, which showed heterogeneous FDG uptake in the left adrenal gland region, homogeneously increased FDG uptake in the right adrenal gland, and left thigh subcutaneous lesion. 

Discharge Summary:
The patient was diagnosed with EBV-associated nasal type extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma, resulting in left adrenalectomy and ipsilateral nephrectomy. The patient underwent 7 cycles of chemotherapy (GLIDE regimen) and autologous stem cell transplantation, and two subsequent follow-up FDG PET/CT scans within 1 year showed complete resolution with no abnormal FDG uptake in the initially involved sites after treatment. The patient was discharged with instructions for follow-up appointments and maintenance. The discharge summary serves as a comprehensive document outlining the patient's medical history, treatment, and prognosis.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 40-year-old woman who came to our clinic for left back pain that lasted 2 months. The physical exam showed nothing unusual. Blood tests showed slightly high levels of adrenaline and another hormone called renin. A detailed scan of the belly area showed growths on both adrenal glands, which are small organs above the kidneys. Doctors first thought these growths might be adrenal gland cancer. The patient had surgery to remove the left adrenal gland and the left kidney. Tests on the removed tissue showed unusual immune cells. More lab tests found that the patient had a rare type of cancer called NK/T-cell lymphoma, which is linked to a virus called EBV. The patient then had a full body scan to see where the cancer had spread. The scan showed the cancer was in the left adrenal gland area, the right adrenal gland, and under the skin of the left thigh.

Discharge Summary:
The patient was found to have NK/T-cell lymphoma linked to the EBV virus. The patient had surgery to remove the left adrenal gland and left kidney. After surgery, the patient received 7 rounds of cancer-fighting drugs using a treatment plan called GLIDE. The patient also had a transplant using their own stem cells. Two follow-up body scans done within one year showed the cancer was gone. There were no signs of cancer in any of the areas where it had been found before. The patient left the hospital with instructions to come back for check-up visits and ongoing care. This summary gives a full record of the patient's health history, treatment, and expected outcome.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name]
DOB: [Date of Birth]
MRN: [Medical Record Number]
Admission Date: [Date of admission]
Discharge Date: [Date of discharge]

Diagnosis:
- Retinitis pigmentosa
- Intermittent diplopia

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with complaints of intermittent diplopia that had been present for the past few years, with recent progression. The patient was diagnosed with retinitis pigmentosa after a fundus examination revealed retinal pigmentary changes, narrowing vessels, and relatively pale discs bilaterally. VF examination showed that only the central island remained bilaterally. Prism-cover testing revealed 30 PD left hypertropia and 4 PD XT. Ocular-motility testing demonstrated slight overaction of the inferior oblique muscle and slight underaction of the superior oblique muscle in the left eye. On prism-adaptation testing, the patient reported no diplopia with a 16 PD prism. However, the patient did not prefer prism glasses and requested surgical correction.

The patient underwent an inferior oblique myectomy in the left eye, which resulted in improvement in the hypertropia. One month after surgery, cover testing revealed an 18 PD left hypertropia and 4 PD XT at the primary position. The patient experienced occasional diplopia only with right-up gazing but no diplopia at the primary position or with near work. At the last follow-up visit 3 months later, the prism-cover testing measurements were unchanged, and the patient reported satisfaction with the resolution of diplopia in daily life.

Follow-Up Plan:
The patient will undergo routine follow-up examinations with an ophthalmologist to monitor the progression of retinitis pigmentosa and to evaluate diplopia if it recurs in the future.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name]
DOB: [Date of Birth]
MRN: [Medical Record Number]
Admission Date: [Date of admission]
Discharge Date: [Date of discharge]

Diagnosis:
Retinitis pigmentosa (a condition that damages the back of the eye and affects vision)
Intermittent diplopia (double vision that comes and goes)

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with double vision that had been happening on and off for a few years. The double vision had recently gotten worse. An eye doctor looked at the back of the patient's eyes. The exam showed damage to the light-sensing tissue, narrowed blood vessels, and pale nerve areas in both eyes. This led to a diagnosis of retinitis pigmentosa. A vision field test showed the patient could only see in a small central area in both eyes. Eye alignment tests showed the left eye was higher than it should be and turned slightly outward. The muscles that move the left eye were not working properly. The patient tried special glasses with prisms that stopped the double vision. However, the patient did not want to wear these glasses and asked for surgery instead.

The patient had surgery to fix a muscle in the left eye. This helped improve the eye alignment. One month after surgery, the left eye was still slightly higher and turned outward. The patient only had double vision when looking up and to the right. There was no double vision when looking straight ahead or at close objects. At a check-up three months later, the eye measurements were the same. The patient was happy because the double vision no longer bothered daily activities.

Follow-Up Plan:
The patient will have regular check-ups with an eye doctor. These visits will track any changes in the eye condition. The doctor will also check if double vision comes back in the future.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 7 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis: Partial complex seizures

Hospital Course:

The patient presented with a 9-month history of partial complex seizures, which were confirmed by EEG testing. An MRI showed the presence of a 4 × 5 × 4-cm mass in the left sylvian fissure region, which was surgically removed through left frontotemporal craniotomy.

During surgery, it was discovered that the tumor was without any dural attachment and primarily intraparenchymal in location. After complete removal of the tumor from the temporal lobe, a portion of it was left attached to the MCA branches to avoid injury to these vessels.

Further evaluation showed that there was a microscopic invasion of the pial surface, which was consistent with the transformation to a WHO grade II meningioma. Fractionated external beam radiation therapy was done instead of further surgery, as it was considered high risk for permanent speech and language deficits.

A repeat MRI showed that the tumor has remained stable in size two years later, but the patient continues to experience occasional seizures despite medication.

Discharge Diagnosis: Partial complex seizures

Discharge Condition: Stable

Discharge Instructions: The patient's family was advised to follow up with their primary care physician for further management of the patient's partial complex seizures.

Discharging Provider: [REDACTED]
Signature: [REDACTED]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 7 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis: Seizures that affect part of the brain and cause confusion

Hospital Course:

The patient came in with seizures that had been happening for 9 months. These seizures affect part of the brain and cause confusion. A brain wave test confirmed the seizures. A brain scan found a growth about the size of a small egg in the left side of the brain. Doctors removed the growth through surgery on the left side of the head.

During surgery, doctors found that the growth was inside the brain tissue. It was not attached to the outer brain covering. Doctors removed most of the growth from the brain. They left a small part attached to major blood vessels to avoid damaging them.

Further testing showed the growth had spread slightly into the brain's surface layer. This meant the growth had changed into a slow-growing type of brain tumor. Doctors used radiation treatment instead of more surgery. More surgery could have caused lasting problems with speaking and understanding words.

A brain scan two years later showed the growth has stayed the same size. However, the patient still has seizures sometimes even with medicine.

Discharge Diagnosis: Seizures that affect part of the brain and cause confusion

Discharge Condition: Stable

Discharge Instructions: The patient's family was told to see their regular doctor. The doctor will help manage the patient's seizures.

Discharging Provider: [REDACTED]
Signature: [REDACTED]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our department with sudden-onset stuporous consciousness. A brain CT scan revealed a subarachnoid hemorrhage with a right temporal lobe intracranial hemorrhage and a right subdural hematoma. Following the CT, angiography demonstrated an aneurysmal sac at the right posterior communicating artery. Emergency decompressive craniectomy (DC) and aneurysmal neck clipping were performed. Following recovery there was some sinking of the scalp flap, and the decision was made to proceed with an autologous cranioplasty 61 days after the initial hemorrhage. There were no complications during the cranioplasty procedure, and there was no evidence of cardiovascular instability or cerebral swelling. Epidural and subdural drains were placed and connected to a suction system during skin closure. Following skin closure the drains were connected to a drainage bag to avoid subgaleal and epidural blood accumulation.

However, the patient had a seizure in the recovery room. Emergency brain CT revealed widespread cerebral edema. Increased intracranial pressure (ICP) and cerebral edema were managed with osmotic diuretics, corticosteroids, and antiepileptic drugs. The edema slowly subsided, but new low-density areas were noted on follow-up brain CT 1 week after the cranioplasty. The decision was made to clamp the drain catheters to control the intracranial pressure.

The patient was monitored closely for any signs of complications, and no further episodes of seizure or cerebral edema were observed. The patient's overall condition improved, and she was discharged in stable condition with instructions for continued follow-up care.

Final Diagnosis:

- Subarachnoid hemorrhage
- Right temporal lobe intracranial hemorrhage
- Right subdural hematoma
- Aneurysmal sac at the right posterior communicating artery
- Widespread cerebral edema

Discharge Instructions:

1. Follow-up appointments with your primary care physician and neurologist are recommended to monitor your recovery and manage any ongoing symptoms.

2. Please continue to take all prescribed medications and follow any instructions provided by your healthcare providers.

3. Adequate rest and nutrition are essential for your continued recovery.

4. Avoid activities that may increase your risk of falls or head injury.

5. In case of any new or worsening symptoms, seek immediate medical attention.

[DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS END]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient came to our department because she suddenly became very drowsy and hard to wake up. A brain scan showed bleeding around the brain, bleeding in the right side of the brain, and a blood clot under the skull. After the scan, a test of the blood vessels showed a weak, bulging spot on a blood vessel in the brain. The doctors did emergency surgery to remove part of the skull to relieve pressure and to put a clip on the weak blood vessel. During recovery, the skin where the skull piece was removed started to sink in. The doctors decided to do surgery to put the skull piece back 61 days after the first bleeding. This surgery went well with no problems. The heart stayed stable and there was no brain swelling. Small tubes were placed to drain fluid from around the brain during the surgery. After closing the skin, the tubes were connected to a bag to stop blood from building up under the skin and around the brain.

However, the patient had a seizure in the recovery room. An emergency brain scan showed swelling throughout the brain. The doctors treated the high pressure in the brain and swelling with water pills, steroid medicines, and seizure medicines. The swelling slowly got better. But a brain scan one week after the skull surgery showed new areas of damaged brain tissue. The doctors decided to close off the drain tubes to help control the pressure in the brain.

The care team watched the patient closely for any problems. The patient did not have any more seizures or brain swelling. The patient got better overall and was sent home in stable condition with plans for follow-up care.

Final Diagnosis:

Bleeding around the brain
Bleeding in the right side of the brain
Blood clot under the skull
Weak, bulging spot on a blood vessel in the brain
Swelling throughout the brain

Discharge Instructions:

1. The patient should schedule follow-up visits with her regular doctor and brain specialist. They will check on her recovery and help with any ongoing health issues.

2. The patient should keep taking all prescribed medicines and follow all instructions from her doctors and nurses.

3. Getting enough rest and eating well are very important for continued healing.

4. The patient should stay away from activities that could cause falls or head injuries.

5. If any new symptoms appear or current symptoms get worse, the patient should get medical help right away.

[DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS END]

input:
Discharge Summary:

This is a discharge summary for a 74-year-old woman with metastatic lung adenocarcinoma. The patient was admitted to the hospital due to pain in the right lower chest wall, which was found to be caused by cancer that had spread to multiple areas of the body, including the right lung, mediastinal lymph nodes, liver, and pleural nodules.

The patient was prescribed various medications to manage her cancer, including gefitinib and osimertinib, but after several months of treatment, disease progression was observed.

In September 2017, the patient began receiving a combination of low-dose decitabine and camrelizumab, which resulted in significant improvement in the patient's mediastinal lymph nodes, liver, and pleural nodule metastases. However, after 7 cycles of treatment, the patient's disease progressed again, and she stopped receiving this treatment altogether. 

Throughout the patient's treatment, various markers were measured and showed fluctuations, including an increase in TMB and a decrease in TNB during combined therapy; however, the MSI status and HLA LOH events remained unchanged. 

In May 2019, the patient ultimately passed away from multiple organ failure.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

This is a discharge summary for a 74-year-old woman with lung cancer that had spread to other parts of her body. The patient came to the hospital because of pain in the right lower chest wall. Tests showed the cancer had spread to the right lung, lymph nodes in the chest, liver, and the lining around the lungs.

The patient was given different medicines to treat her cancer, including gefitinib and osimertinib. After several months, these medicines stopped working and the cancer got worse.

In September 2017, the patient started getting two different medicines together called decitabine and camrelizumab. These medicines helped shrink the cancer in the chest lymph nodes, liver, and lung lining. But after 7 rounds of treatment, the cancer started growing again. The patient stopped taking these medicines.

During treatment, doctors checked different markers in the blood and body. Some of these markers went up and some went down while the patient was getting the two medicines together. Other markers stayed the same throughout treatment.

In May 2019, the patient died because several of her organs stopped working.
