input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [omitted]
Age: 60
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: Brachial plexopathy
Length of Stay: [omitted]

HOSPITAL COURSE

This patient was admitted to our facility for an intense pruritus on her right arm, which had been ongoing for six months. She also complained of right brachial paresis.

COMORBIDITIES

The patient has a known history of multiple myeloma.

DIAGNOSTIC TESTS AND PROCEDURES

Computed tomography of the cervical spine, magnetic resonance imaging, and electromyography of the brachial plexus were performed, which showed right cervical disc herniation with nerve root compression of C6-C7 and right brachial plexus inflammation (Parsonage-Turner syndrome).

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS

The patient was treated with methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and oral hydroxyzine 25mg every 8 hours, which resulted in little improvement of the pruritus. Gabapentin was introduced at 900mg/day, which achieved optimal control after three months of follow-up.

FOLLOW-UP INSTRUCTIONS

The patient was referred to neurosurgery and oncology consultation for additional therapeutic orientation.

SUMMARY

The patient presented with brachial plexopathy, which is attributed to neoplastic infiltration of multiple myeloma. The treatment plan included the application of methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and oral hydroxyzine 25mg every 8 hours, along with the introduction of gabapentin at 900mg/day. The patient was referred to neurosurgery and oncology for further management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [omitted]
Age: 60
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: Nerve damage in the shoulder and arm area
Length of Stay: [omitted]

HOSPITAL COURSE

This patient was admitted to our facility for severe itching on her right arm. This problem had been going on for six months. She also had weakness in her right arm.

COMORBIDITIES

The patient has a known history of multiple myeloma. This is a type of blood cancer.

DIAGNOSTIC TESTS AND PROCEDURES

A CT scan of the neck, an MRI, and a nerve test of the shoulder and arm area were performed. These tests showed a bulging disc in the right side of the neck. This disc was pressing on nerves at the C6-C7 level. The tests also showed swelling of the nerves in the right shoulder and arm area. This condition is called Parsonage-Turner syndrome.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS

The patient was treated with methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1%. This is a steroid cream. She also took hydroxyzine 25mg by mouth every 8 hours. This helped only a little with the itching. Gabapentin was added at 900mg per day. After three months, this medicine controlled the itching well.

FOLLOW-UP INSTRUCTIONS

The patient was sent to see a brain and spine surgeon. She was also sent to see a cancer doctor for more treatment advice.

SUMMARY

The patient had nerve damage in the shoulder and arm area. This was caused by the spread of her blood cancer. The treatment plan included methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and hydroxyzine 25mg by mouth every 8 hours. Gabapentin at 900mg per day was also added. The patient was sent to a brain and spine surgeon and a cancer doctor for more care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Facility: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis:
Fetal perianal mass detected at 23 weeks' gestation

Hospital Course:
The patient was evaluated for a fetal perianal mass that was detected during routine antenatal care. At the initial visit, an echogenic perivaginal mass was visible on 2D transabdominal ultrasound. Further evaluation with 3D ultrasound revealed that the mass was originated from the skin apart from anus or vagina. Amniocentesis was performed which revealed normal fetal karyotype and no acetylcholinesterase level was detected. As the gestational age progressed, the size of the mass increased. A female neonate was delivered at GA 41 weeks and a pedunculated soft mass was visible on the right posterior labium major of the neonate. A complete physical and laboratory evaluation was performed preoperatively, including routine postnatal cardiac echo which showed a patent foramen ovale with a flow from left to right. There was no significant congenital anomaly identified in vertebral body, both kidney, and anus on MRI. Simple surgical excision was planned for the lesion but during the operation, the right anal external muscle complex was displaced due to the original mass and perineal groove type anorectal malformation was found. Posterior sagittal anorectoplasty (PSARP) was performed with complete mass excision. The histopathological result was lipoblastoma with free resection margin. The neonate was discharged from the hospital without any complication on the postoperative seventh day.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Fetal perianal mass and lipoblastoma

Discharge Condition:
Stable

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Discharge Instructions:
The care team discussed the details of the operation and postoperative care with the patient and their family. The patient is advised to return to the follow-up clinic as scheduled for further evaluation. They were also advised to avoid any strenuous physical activity or heavy lifting for several weeks after surgery. If any signs of infection, such as fever or drainage at the surgical site, develop, the patient should contact their health care provider immediately. It is also recommended to maintain routine postnatal care and follow-up evaluations to monitor neonate's health.

Follow-up:
The patient is scheduled for regular follow-up appointments to monitor their recovery and evaluate any potential complications.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Facility: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis:
A growth near the bottom area was found in the baby at 23 weeks of pregnancy

Hospital Course:
The patient was checked for a growth near the baby's bottom area that was found during a routine pregnancy visit. At the first visit, a bright-looking growth near the private area was seen on a standard ultrasound. A more detailed 3D ultrasound showed that the growth came from the skin and was not connected to the anus or vagina. A test was done to check the fluid around the baby. The test showed the baby had normal genes and no signs of a spine problem. As the pregnancy continued, the growth got bigger. A baby girl was born at 41 weeks of pregnancy. A soft growth on a stalk was seen on the right side of the baby's outer private area. Before surgery, the doctors did a full body check and lab tests. A heart ultrasound showed a small opening between the two upper heart chambers with blood flowing from left to right. This is common in newborns. An MRI scan showed no major birth defects in the spine, both kidneys, or the anus. The doctors planned a simple surgery to remove the growth. During surgery, they found that the muscles around the anus had been pushed out of place by the growth. They also found a problem with how the bottom area had formed. The surgeons did a special repair surgery called PSARP to fix the bottom area and remove the growth completely. Lab tests on the removed growth showed it was a lipoblastoma, which is a rare harmless fatty tumor. The growth was fully removed with clear edges. The baby left the hospital seven days after surgery with no problems.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Growth near the bottom area before birth and lipoblastoma, a harmless fatty tumor

Discharge Condition:
Stable

Discharge Medications:
None

Discharge Instructions:
The care team talked with the patient and their family about the surgery and care after surgery. The patient should come back to the follow-up clinic as planned for more checks. They should avoid hard physical activity or lifting heavy things for several weeks after surgery. If there are any signs of infection, such as fever or fluid coming from the surgery site, the patient should call their health care provider right away. The patient should also keep up with regular newborn care visits to watch the baby's health.

Follow-up:
The patient has regular follow-up visits set up to check healing and watch for any problems.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 61-year-old male with history of controlled type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia and past smoking presented with lumbar pain and weight loss.

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to Internal Medicine due to lumbar pain and weight loss. A CT scan revealed fractures and a mass in the left paravertebral region that was suspected to be tumoral in nature. Further imaging studies revealed a mass in the right hilum and another one in the left upper lobe, as well as inflammatory/infectious micronodules. A mass biopsy was performed and aspirated a purulent liquid that was positive for MT complex. The patient was started on quaternary antibacillary therapy with HRZE. The patient also received physiatry and orthopedic consultations, as well as rehabilitation physiotherapy. He was discharged after 25 days and forwarded to rehabilitation physiotherapy and Tuberculosis Differentiated Ambulatory Unit.

Follow-up: The patient has completed a two-month course of quaternary therapy and is currently under two-drug antibacillary therapy with HR. There was a complete disappearance of peripheric masses and a reduction in mass size was observed in the lumbar CT reevaluation.

Diagnosis: Chronic granulomatous inflammatory process consistent with tuberculosis.

Condition at discharge: Stable.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 61-year-old male with a history of controlled type 2 diabetes, high cholesterol, and past smoking. He came to the hospital with lower back pain and weight loss.

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to Internal Medicine because of lower back pain and weight loss. A CT scan showed broken bones and a growth near the left side of the spine. Doctors thought it might be cancer. More imaging tests showed a growth near the right lung and another in the upper left lung. There were also small spots that could be from infection or swelling. Doctors took a sample from the growth and found pus. Tests showed it contained tuberculosis bacteria. The patient started treatment with four medicines to fight tuberculosis, called HRZE. The patient also saw specialists for physical medicine and bone care. He received physical therapy to help him recover. After 25 days, the patient left the hospital. He was sent to continue physical therapy and to visit a special clinic for tuberculosis care.

Follow-up: The patient finished two months of treatment with four medicines. He is now taking two medicines to fight tuberculosis, called HR. The growths outside the lungs have gone away completely. A new CT scan of the lower back showed the growth there has gotten smaller.

Diagnosis: Long-term infection with swelling caused by tuberculosis.

Condition at discharge: Stable.

Doctors thought it might be cancer. More imaging tests showed a growth near the right lung and another in the upper left lung. There were also small spots that could be from infection or swelling. Doctors took a sample from the growth and found pus. Tests showed it contained tuberculosis bacteria. The patient started treatment with four medicines to fight tuberculosis, called HRZE. Condition at discharge: Stable.

input:
Discharge Summary:

The 23-year-old female patient was admitted to the hospital for cardiac workup. The patient presented with negative T waves and cardiac enlargement detected on electrocardiogram and thoracic radiography. She was asymptomatic at presentation and had a history of chronic toxoplasmosis. The patient presented with pallor of skin and mucous membrane, maculo-papular non-itching rash on the forearms and thighs, axillary enlarged lymph nodes, hepatosplenomegaly, blood pressure of 110/60 mmHg, heart rate of 70 b/min, regularly and apexian systolic murmur grade III/VI radiating to the axilla.

Initial laboratory tests showed a discrete inflammatory syndrome and IgG antibodies to Toxoplasma in serum. The echocardiogram confirmed left ventricular enlargement with severe systolic and diastolic dysfunction. A cardiac Magnetic Resonance Imaging was performed and it confirmed dilated cardiomyopathy with excessively prominent trabeculations in left lateral wall and ventricular apex, severe alteration of left ventricular function with diffuse hypokinesia.

The patient was discharged and will be advised to continue antibiotic treatment for the chronic toxoplasmosis. Further follow-up and appropriate care for the dilated cardiomyopathy will be pursued.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

The 23-year-old female patient was admitted to the hospital for heart tests. The patient had abnormal heart wave patterns and an enlarged heart found on a heart tracing test and chest X-ray. She had no symptoms when she came in and had a long-term infection called toxoplasmosis. The patient had pale skin and pale inner mouth lining, a spotty non-itchy rash on the forearms and thighs, swollen lymph nodes under the arms, an enlarged liver and spleen, blood pressure of 110/60 mmHg, heart rate of 70 beats per minute that was regular, and a heart murmur heard at the lower left chest that spread to the armpit area.

First blood tests showed mild signs of inflammation and signs of past toxoplasmosis infection in the blood. The heart ultrasound confirmed the left side of the heart was enlarged with serious problems pumping and filling with blood. A detailed heart scan using magnets was done and it confirmed a weakened and enlarged heart muscle with too much spongy tissue in the left side wall and bottom tip of the heart, and serious weakness of the left heart pumping with poor movement throughout.

The patient was discharged and will be told to keep taking antibiotics for the long-term toxoplasmosis infection. More follow-up visits and proper care for the weakened and enlarged heart muscle will be planned.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:
Patient was a 65-year-old male with a history of alcohol and tobacco abuse who was admitted for evaluation of neuropsychiatric symptoms. He experienced visual hallucinations and partial motor seizures, which were preceded by screams. He also had paranoid delusions, depressed mood, and suicidal ideation.

Initial neurological examination revealed dysarthria, simultanagnosia, prosopagnosia, and bilateral and asymmetric parkinsonism, worse on the left hemibody. The patient also exhibited myoclonus on the left side. The Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score was 13/30, and he demonstrated loss of complex activities of daily living and cognitive impairment related to spatial orientation and episodic memory.

Further laboratory screening for dementia, rheumatologic tests, and serology for HIV, syphilis, and hepatitis were negative. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was unremarkable, and EEG showed slow waves in the right frontotemporal region without epileptiform activity. MRI and 18F-FDG PET-CT were performed, revealing no significant abnormalities. MRI angiography was also normal.

Treatment consisted of adjusting antiepileptic drug doses and corticosteroid pulse therapy. The patient's symptoms improved, and his condition stabilized during hospitalization. Discharge planning and follow-up were arranged upon discharge.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was a 65-year-old man with a history of heavy drinking and smoking. He was admitted to check on problems with his brain and behavior. He saw things that were not there and had seizures with body jerking. These seizures started with screaming. He also had false beliefs that people wanted to harm him. He felt very sad and had thoughts of ending his life.

The first brain and nerve exam showed slurred speech. He had trouble seeing more than one object at a time. He could not recognize faces. He had stiffness and slow movement on both sides of his body, but worse on the left side. He also had muscle twitching on the left side. A test of his thinking skills scored 13 out of 30. He lost the ability to do complex daily tasks. He had trouble knowing where he was and remembering recent events.

Blood tests for causes of memory loss came back normal. Tests for immune system problems were normal. Tests for HIV, syphilis, and liver infections were all negative. A test of his spinal fluid showed nothing unusual. A brain wave test showed slow activity in the right front and side of the brain. There were no signs of seizure patterns. Brain scans and a scan showing brain activity were done. Both showed no major problems. A scan of the blood vessels in the brain was also normal.

Treatment included changing the doses of seizure medicines. The patient also received strong anti-swelling medicine through an IV. His symptoms got better and his condition became stable during his hospital stay. Plans for leaving the hospital and future doctor visits were set up when he left.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]

Medical Diagnosis:
The patient was admitted for acute limb ischemia and septic shock secondary to Streptococcus dysgalactiae bacteremia. A workup for infectious endocarditis was initiated and confirmed by transthoracic and transesophageal echocardiogram demonstrating aortic valve vegetations and severe aortic regurgitation.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent emergent revascularization with left iliac thrombectomy and intravenous antibiotics with gentamicin for 10 days and ceftriaxone for six weeks. Cardiothoracic surgery was consulted, and the patient underwent aortic valve replacement surgery. Upon hemodynamic stabilization, he was discharged to the rehabilitation center one month after the initial hospitalization.

Summary of Admission:
A 56-year-old gentleman with a past medical history of untreated hepatitis C infection, osteomyelitis with right fourth toe amputation, and alcohol abuse presented with paresthesias and a pulseless left leg. Lab work demonstrated normocytic anemia, leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia, and creatinine elevation. Blood cultures were positive for Streptococcus dysgalactiae, and CT angiogram of the left leg demonstrated significant occlusions. The patient was treated for acute limb ischemia and septic shock and underwent an IE workup, which confirmed aortic valve vegetations.

Discharge Medications:
The patient's discharge medications were prescribed by the attending physician and are available in the patient's outpatient medical record.

Follow-up care:
The patient was advised to continue regular follow-up with his primary care physician and cardiothoracic surgeon. Further cardiac rehabilitation is recommended in coordination with the patient's medical team.

Discharge condition:
The patient was discharged with stable vital signs and improved clinical condition. The patient and family were advised to return to the hospital if any new or worsening symptoms persist.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]

Medical Diagnosis:
The patient was admitted because blood flow to the leg was suddenly blocked. The patient also had a serious body-wide infection caused by bacteria called Streptococcus dysgalactiae in the blood. Doctors checked for infection in the heart. Heart scans showed growths on the aortic valve and the valve was leaking badly.

Hospital Course:
The patient had emergency surgery to restore blood flow by removing a clot from the left hip area artery. The patient received antibiotics through a vein. Gentamicin was given for 10 days and ceftriaxone for six weeks. Heart surgeons were asked to help. The patient had surgery to replace the aortic valve. Once blood pressure and heart function became steady, the patient was sent to a rehab center. This was one month after first being admitted to the hospital.

Summary of Admission:
The patient is a 56-year-old man. His health history includes untreated hepatitis C liver infection and a bone infection that led to removal of his right fourth toe. He also has a history of alcohol abuse. He came to the hospital with tingling feelings and no pulse in his left leg. Blood tests showed low red blood cells, high white blood cells, low blood clotting cells, and poor kidney function. Blood tests found bacteria called Streptococcus dysgalactiae. A scan of the leg arteries showed major blockages. The patient was treated for blocked blood flow to the leg and serious body-wide infection. Testing confirmed growths on the heart valve.

Discharge Medications:
The patient's medications were ordered by the main doctor. They can be found in the patient's outpatient medical record.

Follow-up care:
The patient should keep seeing his regular doctor and heart surgeon. The patient should also do heart rehab exercises with help from his medical team.

Discharge condition:
The patient left the hospital with steady vital signs and better health. The patient and family were told to come back to the hospital if any new or worse symptoms continue.

input:
Discharge Summary

Admission Date: [INSERT DATE]
Discharge Date: [INSERT DATE]

Patient Name: [INSERT NAME]
Gender: Female
Age: 56

History of Present Illness:
The patient was admitted with an unruptured right MCA aneurysm. She had no history of subarachnoid hemorrhage. With a PHASES score of 9, the patient had a 4.3% 5-yr risk of rupture. The aneurysm morphology and regional vascular anatomy were closely examined in the 360°XR model from the patient's CTA with contrast and T1-weighted MRI MPRAGE (Video 1). Preoperative planning was also performed in VR to select the appropriate clip and rehearse placement to identify the optimal angle.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent a right pterional craniotomy, and a #11 Sugita clip was placed along the neck with awareness that the artery's recurrent course would not also be occluded by the clip. With increased awareness provided by the overlay, the inferior jaw of the clip was used to sweep this branch out of the way, and prevent it from becoming trapped against the neck. Using ICG angiography, flow was confirmed to the distal vessels at the MCA trifurcation.

Postoperative imaging verified appropriate clip placement and maintained blood flow through the parent and distal vessels. The patient made a full recovery.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition.

Discharge Medications:
None

Follow-up Care:
The patient was scheduled for a follow-up appointment with the physician.

Healthcare Provider:
[INSERT NAME], M.D.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Admission Date: [INSERT DATE]
Discharge Date: [INSERT DATE]

Patient Name: [INSERT NAME]
Gender: Female
Age: 56

History of Present Illness:
The patient was admitted with a bulge in a blood vessel on the right side of the brain. This bulge had not burst. She had no history of bleeding in the brain. Based on a risk score of 9, the patient had about a 4 in 100 chance of the bulge bursting within 5 years. The doctors used special 3D imaging from a scan with dye and a brain scan to study the bulge and nearby blood vessels. The doctors also used virtual reality to plan the surgery, choose the right clip, and practice placing it at the best angle.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery to open a small part of the skull on the right side. The doctors placed a small metal clip on the base of the bulge. They were careful not to block a nearby blood vessel that curves back on itself. Using the 3D view as a guide, the doctors used the bottom part of the clip to gently move this blood vessel out of the way. This kept it from getting pinched against the bulge. The doctors used a special dye test to make sure blood was still flowing to the vessels beyond the bulge.

Scans taken after surgery showed the clip was in the right place. Blood was still flowing through the main vessel and the smaller vessels. The patient fully recovered.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was sent home in stable condition.

Discharge Medications:
None

Follow-up Care:
The patient was scheduled for a follow-up appointment with the doctor.

Healthcare Provider:
[INSERT NAME], M.D.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name]
Sex: Female
Age: 61 years old
Date of admission: [Date]
Date of discharge: [Date]
Diagnosis: Papillary Fibroelastoma (PF)

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with recurrent paroxysmal pleuritic chest pain and severe dyspnea. Pulmonary CT angiography did not reveal any emboli, and cardiac tests showed a mobile mass attached to the septal tricuspid valve (TV) leaflet, measuring 1.3 × 0.9 × 1.1 cm with tricuspid regurgitation. A differential diagnosis of papillary fibroelastoma (PF) versus cardiac myxoma was entertained. Cardiac surgery was advised due to the established recurrent embolic potential of the tumor and size (>1 cm).

Surgery:

Shave excision of the sessile mass and TV repair using a 30-mm Medtronic Contour ring was performed in this patient.

Postoperative Procedures:

The patient's condition was monitored postoperatively with adequate treatment. Postoperatively, the patient continued anticoagulation for 8 weeks. To date, the patient remains asymptomatic at rest and exercise.

Follow-Up:

The patient is advised to follow a healthy lifestyle and to ensure adequate follow-up with the treating physician.

Addendum:

The patient did not experience any complications and remains stable during the hospital course and at the time of discharge.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name]
Sex: Female
Age: 61 years old
Date of admission: [Date]
Date of discharge: [Date]
Diagnosis: Papillary Fibroelastoma, a rare non-cancerous heart tumor

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with repeated sharp chest pain that got worse with breathing and serious trouble breathing. A CT scan of the lungs did not show any blood clots. Heart tests found a small growth attached to a flap in the right side of the heart. The growth measured about half an inch in size. The heart valve was also leaking slightly. Doctors thought the growth was most likely a papillary fibroelastoma or a myxoma, which are two types of non-cancerous heart tumors. Surgery was recommended because the tumor could break off and block blood flow. The size of the tumor, larger than one centimeter, also made surgery necessary.

Surgery:

Doctors removed the growth by carefully shaving it off the heart valve. The heart valve was also repaired using a special ring to help it close properly.

Postoperative Procedures:

The patient was watched closely after surgery and received proper care. The patient took blood-thinning medicine for 8 weeks after surgery. As of now, the patient has no symptoms during rest or physical activity.

Follow-Up:

The patient should live a healthy lifestyle. The patient should also keep all future appointments with the doctor.

Addendum:

The patient had no problems during the hospital stay. The patient was in stable condition at the time of discharge.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
DOB: [Redacted]
Age: 2 months
Gender: Male

Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Length of Stay: 13 days

Diagnosis: Left ventricle tumour, paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our hospital on [Redacted] with complaints of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia. Further examination revealed a left ventricle tumour, which was confirmed by colour Doppler echocardiography and computed tomographic angiography. The tumour was found to be large relative to the left ventricle, occupying 80% of cavity volume of the left ventricular obstructing mitral valve inflow.

Due to the extensive adhesions of the base of the tumour to the anterior wall and side wall of the left ventricle and its proximity to the mitral valve, chordae tendineae, and other important anatomical structures, the orthotopic cardiac tumour resection was deemed difficult and risky. Therefore, the patient underwent a heart autotransplantation procedure, which involved a median sternotomy incision, instituting cardiopulmonary bypass, isolating the heart, removing the tumour, and returning the heart to its original position.

Histology revealed that the tumour was a rhabdomyoma. The patient was discharged on [Redacted] after 13 days of hospitalization. In 5 months of follow-up, there was no tumour recurrence or arrhythmia observed.

Instructions at Discharge:

The patient was instructed to follow up with his primary care physician for routine assessments and to notify our hospital if any symptoms recurred. A referral to a cardiac rehabilitation program was also made to support the patient's continued recovery. The parents were provided with instructions for symptom management and was advised to seek care immediately if there were any changes in the patient's condition.

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Follow-up Appointments:

The patient was scheduled for follow-up appointments with his primary care physician and the cardiac rehabilitation program.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
DOB: [Redacted]
Age: 2 months
Gender: Male

Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Length of Stay: 13 days

Diagnosis: Growth in the left side of the heart, episodes of very fast heartbeat

Hospital Course:

The patient came to our hospital on [Redacted] because of episodes of very fast heartbeat. Doctors found a growth in the left side of the heart. This was confirmed using heart ultrasound and special X-ray scans of blood vessels. The growth was large and took up 80% of the space in the lower left heart chamber. It was blocking blood flow through a heart valve.

The growth was attached to the heart wall in many places. It was also very close to important parts of the heart. This made removing it the usual way too difficult and dangerous. Instead, doctors did a special surgery. They opened the chest, put the patient on a heart-lung machine, and took the heart out of the body. They removed the growth and then put the heart back in place.

Tests showed the growth was a type called rhabdomyoma. The patient left the hospital on [Redacted] after 13 days. During 5 months of checkups, the growth did not come back and the heartbeat stayed normal.

Instructions at Discharge:

The patient should see his regular doctor for routine checkups. The parents should contact our hospital if any symptoms come back. The patient was also referred to a heart recovery program to help with healing. The parents were given instructions on how to watch for problems. They were told to get medical care right away if the patient's condition changes.

Discharge Medications:

None

Follow-up Appointments:

The patient has scheduled visits with his regular doctor and the heart recovery program.

input:
Hospital Course:
This 7-month-old boy was admitted to our hospital for treatment of respiratory syncytial viral pneumonia, with a history of tracheoesophageal fistula and esophageal atresia surgery performed at 1 day of age. The patient showed recurrent cough and sputum, and was afebrile. The chest radiograph showed increased peribronchial infiltration and air bronchogram. Due to the risk of aspiration pneumonia, oral feeding was discontinued and the patient was given parenteral nutrition via the peripheral venous route.

Extravasation Event:
On day 6 of admission, while the patient was receiving total parenteral nutrition, a 24-gauge intravenous catheter was newly inserted into the dorsal cephalic vein at the dorsum of the patient's right hand. The patient showed signs of irritability, and the obstruction alarm of the infusion pump was triggered. Without any alarm sign, approximately 9 hours after the repositioning of the intravenous catheter, the patient's right hand and arm appeared severely swollen, and capillary refill was absent. There were several blisters observed under the catheter, and an emergency fasciotomy was performed under the suspicion of compartment syndrome.

Treatment and Care:
The patient received orthopedic surgery and fascia suturing, followed by irrigation of the area several times with saline solution. Delayed primary skin closure was performed 8 days after the fasciotomy. A skin graft was scheduled for treatment of the skin necrosis after debriding the necrotic tissue with a moist dressing. Rehabilitation treatment consisting of wrist articulation exercises was started due to the high chance of wrist joint contracture. Skin graft surgery was delayed due to pneumonia, which was diagnosed during the patient's regular wound dressing and rehabilitation treatment. It was performed 4 months after the extravasation event. At the time of this report, skin engraftment had been successfully completed, and the patient was receiving continued rehabilitation treatment.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
This 7-month-old boy came to our hospital because he had a lung infection caused by a common virus called RSV. When he was 1 day old, he had surgery to fix a birth defect that connected his windpipe and food pipe in a way it should not be. The patient kept coughing and had mucus, but did not have a fever. A chest x-ray showed signs of infection in his lungs. Because food might go into his lungs by mistake, the patient stopped eating by mouth. Instead, he received nutrition through a small tube in his vein.

Extravasation Event:
On day 6 in the hospital, the patient was getting nutrition through his vein. A new small tube was placed into a vein on the back of his right hand. The patient became fussy, and the feeding pump showed a warning that something was blocking the flow. About 9 hours after the tube was moved, the patient's right hand and arm became very swollen. There was no warning sign before this happened. The skin did not turn pink when pressed, which meant blood was not flowing well. Blisters appeared under the tube. Doctors did emergency surgery to cut open the tissue covering the muscles because pressure was building up inside the arm.

Treatment and Care:
The patient had bone and muscle surgery, and doctors sewed the tissue covering back together. The area was washed out several times with salt water. The skin was closed 8 days after the first surgery. Doctors planned to move healthy skin to cover the dead skin area after cleaning it with wet bandages. The patient started exercises to move his wrist because the joint might become stiff. The skin surgery was delayed because the patient got a lung infection during regular bandage changes and exercises. The skin surgery happened 4 months after the fluid leaked out of the vein. At the time of this report, the new skin had attached well. The patient was still doing exercises to help his wrist move better.

This 7-month-old boy came to our hospital because he had a lung infection caused by a common virus called RSV. When he was 1 day old, he had surgery to fix a birth defect that connected his windpipe and food pipe in a way it should not be. The patient kept coughing and had mucus, but did not have a fever. A chest x-ray showed signs of infection in his lungs. Because food might go into his lungs by mistake, the patient stopped eating by mouth. Instead, he received nutrition through a small tube in his vein.

Extravasation Event:
On day 6 in the hospital, the patient was getting nutrition through his vein. A new small tube was placed into a vein on the back of his left hand. The patient became fussy, and the feeding pump showed a warning that something was blocking the flow. About 9 hours after the tube was moved, the patient's right hand and arm became very swollen. There was no warning sign before this happened. The skin did not turn pink when pressed, which meant blood was not flowing well. Blisters appeared under the tube. Doctors did emergency surgery to cut open the tissue covering the muscles because pressure was building up inside the arm.

Treatment and Care:
The patient had bone and muscle surgery, and doctors sewed the tissue covering back together. The area was washed out several times with salt water. The skin was closed 8 days after the first surgery. Doctors planned to move healthy skin to cover the dead skin area after cleaning it with wet bandages. The patient started exercises to move his wrist because the joint might become stiff. The skin surgery was delayed because the patient got a lung infection during regular bandage changes and exercises. The skin surgery happened 4 months after the fluid leaked out of the vein. At the time of this report, the new skin had attached well. The patient was still doing exercises to help his wrist move better.

input:
FINAL DIAGNOSIS: 

Right hepatic artery pseudoaneurysm

HOSPITAL COURSE: 

A 58-year-old male was admitted with hilar bile ductal carcinoma and underwent preoperative percutaneous transhepatic portal embolization (PTPE) of the anterior and posterior branches of his right portal vein. Two days later, the patient complained of an acute abdominal pain, and a contrast-enhanced computed tomography scan confirmed the presence of a pseudoaneurysm. Percutaneous transarterial embolization (TAE) of the pseudoaneurysm was deemed inappropriate for fear of hepatic infarction complicating an already occluded portal vein.

TREATMENT: 

The patient underwent percutaneous thrombin injection into the pseudoaneurysm under ultrasound and digital subtraction angiography guidance. Follow-up CT revealed complete pseudoaneurysm occlusion, and liver hypertrophy was observed. The patient subsequently underwent right hepatectomy, which was uneventful.

DISPOSITION: 

The patient was discharged in stable condition 30 days post-procedure. The patient was observed for four weeks post-procedure and showed no evidence of pseudoaneurysm recurrence or hepatic infarction.
clean_ref:
FINAL DIAGNOSIS:

A false bulge in the artery that supplies blood to the right side of the liver

HOSPITAL COURSE:

A 58-year-old male was admitted with cancer in the bile ducts near the liver. He had a procedure to block blood flow in part of his right liver vein. This was done through the skin to prepare for surgery. Two days later, the patient had sudden belly pain. A special scan using dye showed a false bulge had formed in a blood vessel. Doctors decided not to block the bulge through the artery. They were worried this could cut off blood flow to the liver. The liver already had one blocked vein.

TREATMENT:

The patient had a medicine called thrombin injected into the false bulge. This was done through the skin using imaging tools to guide the needle. A follow-up scan showed the false bulge was completely closed. The healthy part of the liver had grown larger. The patient then had surgery to remove the right side of his liver. The surgery went well with no problems.

DISPOSITION:

The patient was sent home in stable condition 30 days after the procedure. The patient was watched for four weeks after the procedure. There were no signs that the false bulge came back. There were also no signs of damage to the liver from blocked blood flow.

input:
Discharge Summary:

This is a 48-year-old female patient, who was admitted to our hospital for surgical treatment of a recurrence of squamous cell carcinoma on the left mandible body. She underwent a wide excision and left mandibulectomy followed by a mandible, mouth floor, and tongue reconstruction with a titanium implant and an anterolateral thigh (ALT) free flap. The ALT flap was harvested from the ipsilateral thigh and then anastomosed to the lingual artery and the internal jugular vein. After surgery, the patient was observed to have an acceptable overall facial shape and was capable of mastication.

The patient received adjuvant radiation and chemotherapy due to the recurrence of the disease after previous surgery. The patient did not have any suspicious tumor recurrence on the follow-up CT.

The designed implant was manufactured using an A1 metal 3D printer with a biocompatibility material that was composed of titanium, aluminum, and vanadium. The prosthesis appeared well positioned on the radiograph taken after surgery.

The patient was discharged from the hospital and advised to continue follow-up care with the multidisciplinary team. Further information and recommendations for follow-up care were discussed with the patient and their family.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

This is a 48-year-old female patient. She came to the hospital for surgery because her skin cancer on the left side of her lower jawbone had come back. The cancer type is called squamous cell carcinoma. The doctors removed the cancer and part of her jawbone. Then they rebuilt her jawbone, mouth floor, and tongue. They used a metal implant made of titanium and tissue taken from her thigh. The tissue from her thigh was connected to a blood vessel near the tongue and a large vein in the neck. After surgery, her face looked good and she could chew food.

The patient had radiation treatment and cancer-fighting drugs because the cancer came back after her first surgery. A follow-up scan showed no signs that the cancer had returned.

The metal implant was made using a 3D printer. It was made from a safe material containing titanium, aluminum, and vanadium. An x-ray taken after surgery showed the implant was in the right place.

The patient left the hospital. She was told to keep seeing her team of doctors for follow-up care. The doctors talked with the patient and her family about what to do next and when to come back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]
Patient Name: [Name]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 36-year-old female, was admitted to the hospital after jumping from a four-foot high deck and experiencing extreme pain and inability to bear weight on the right limb. The patient had a positive Lachman test indicating a tear in the anterior cruciate ligament (ACL). Radiographs revealed a joint effusion and abnormalities in the patellar tendon morphology.

Diagnostic arthrogram confirmed a complete midsubstance ACL tear. An ipsilateral hamstring autograft was performed during ACL reconstruction. The patient's post-operative course was complicated by complex regional pain syndrome.

The patient had a delayed recovery and experienced stiffness, difficulty with walking, and had developed quadriceps muscle atrophy at a one-year follow-up. Physical therapy was instituted to help prevent postoperative fibrosis.

Given the patient's continued symptoms, close follow-up with an orthopedic surgeon is recommended.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]
Patient Name: [Name]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 36-year-old female. She was admitted to the hospital after jumping from a four-foot high deck. She had severe pain and could not put weight on her right leg. A physical exam showed that a ligament in her knee was torn. This ligament is called the ACL. It helps keep the knee stable. X-rays showed fluid in the knee joint. They also showed changes in the tendon that connects to the kneecap.

A special imaging test confirmed the ACL was completely torn in the middle. The patient had surgery to rebuild the ACL. The surgeon used tissue from the back of the patient's thigh to replace the torn ligament. After surgery, the patient developed a pain condition that affects the nerves. This made recovery harder.

The patient had a slow recovery over one year. She had stiffness and trouble walking. The muscles in the front of her thigh got weaker and smaller. She started physical therapy to prevent scar tissue from forming inside the knee.

Because the patient still has symptoms, she should continue to see a bone and joint surgeon regularly.

Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Admitting Diagnosis: Hypertension

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with a diagnosis of hypertension. The subsequent sonography revealed a right suprarenal mass and the echotexture of both the kidneys was normal. The urinary vanilyl mandelic acid was elevated. Iodine 131-metaiodobenzyl guanidine (MIBG) scan was normal. The Fluorine 18-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography-computed tomography (F18-FDG PET-CT) scan revealed hypermetabolic right adrenal mass suggestive of pheochromocytoma. There were no hypermetabolic renal cortical or pancreatic lesions. The subsequent high resolution computer tomography of the kidneys revealed multiple hypervascular cortical based lesions in the right kidney. The pancreas was replaced by multiple cysts. Per operative needle aspiration from pancreas did not show malignant cells.

Treatment Provided:

Partial nephrectomy was performed. Histology of the renal lesion showed clear cell carcinoma.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Clear cell carcinoma

Condition at Discharge:

Stable

Follow-up Plan:

The patient is advised to follow up with the physician at regular intervals.

Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Admitting Diagnosis: High blood pressure

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with high blood pressure. An ultrasound showed a mass above the right kidney. Both kidneys looked normal on the ultrasound. A urine test showed high levels of a substance called VMA. This substance can mean there is a tumor making too much hormone. A scan using a special dye was normal. Another scan using a different method showed the mass above the right kidney was very active. This suggested a tumor called pheochromocytoma. This tumor makes too much of certain hormones. There were no active problem areas in the kidneys or the organ behind the stomach. A detailed scan of the kidneys showed several small growths on the outer part of the right kidney. These growths had many blood vessels. The organ behind the stomach had many fluid-filled sacs instead of normal tissue. During surgery, doctors took a sample from this organ with a needle. The sample did not show any cancer cells.

Treatment Provided:

Part of the kidney was removed through surgery. Tests on the kidney growth showed it was a type of kidney cancer called clear cell carcinoma.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Kidney cancer (clear cell carcinoma)

Condition at Discharge:

Stable

Follow-up Plan:

The patient is advised to see the doctor for checkups on a regular basis.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 26 years
Gender: Female

Medical History:
The patient had no comorbid conditions before admission.

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with a 2-month history of cough and intractable hemoptysis. A bronchoscopic examination performed in another hospital revealed a vascular fleshy growth protruding onto the left main bronchus, and an attempted biopsy resulted in profuse bleeding. The patient was then referred to our center for further management. A repeat bronchoscopy performed at our center confirmed the previous bronchoscopy findings, and a biopsy from the lesion was managed conservatively. Histology with immunohistochemistry (IHC) correlation suggested a diagnosis of a primary pulmonary angiosarcoma, intermediate grade. The tumor was found to completely occlude the left main bronchus. A fluorine-18 fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography/computed tomography (18F-FDG PET/CT) done as a part of metastatic workup showed a solitary metabolically active endobronchial lesion in the left main bronchus extending into the left lower bronchus causing complete obstruction and collapse of the entire left lung; further corroborating the diagnosis of a primary pulmonary angiosarcoma.

Treatment:
A formal cardiopulmonary evaluation was done, and the patient was taken up for a definitive surgery. Intraoperatively the tumor was found to originate at the bifurcation of the left main bronchus and a sleeve resection was deemed not possible, the patient, therefore, underwent a left pneumonectomy. Her postoperative recovery was uneventful. The patient received 50 gray of adjuvant radiotherapy.

Follow-up Plan:
The patient is on regular follow-up for the past 7 months.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary pulmonary angiosarcoma.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 26 years
Gender: Female

Medical History:
The patient had no other health problems before this hospital stay.

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with a cough that lasted 2 months and coughing up blood that would not stop. At another hospital, doctors used a thin tube with a camera to look inside the airways. They found a fleshy growth with many blood vessels blocking the left main airway. When doctors tried to take a small tissue sample, it caused heavy bleeding. The patient was then sent to our hospital for more care. Our doctors looked inside the airways again with a camera tube and confirmed what the other hospital found. A tissue sample was taken and the bleeding was controlled without surgery. Lab tests on the tissue showed a type of rare cancer called angiosarcoma. This cancer started in the lung and was medium grade. The tumor completely blocked the left main airway. A special full body scan was done to check if the cancer had spread. The scan showed the cancer was only in the left main airway. It reached into the lower left airway and caused the entire left lung to collapse. This confirmed the cancer started in the lung.

Treatment:
The patient had tests to check heart and lung function before surgery. During surgery, doctors found the tumor started where the left main airway splits into two branches. It was not possible to remove just the tumor and reconnect the airway. The patient had surgery to remove the entire left lung. Recovery after surgery went well with no problems. The patient also received radiation treatment at a dose of 50 gray to kill any remaining cancer cells.

Follow-up Plan:
The patient has been coming back for regular checkups for the past 7 months.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Angiosarcoma, a rare cancer that started in the lung.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: Not Provided
Gender: Female
Age: 31 years
Admission Date: Not Provided
Discharge Date: Not Provided
Admitting Diagnosis: Not Provided
Discharge Diagnosis: Hydatid cysts of the bone

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient presented with a history of increasing pain in the right hip and pelvis for the last 3 years, radiating to the right lower limb and back, progressively increasing in intensity, and associated with tingling sensation since last 3 months. She had become quite incapacitated due to painful restricted movements. Her past history showed that at 22 years of age, she had presented with a large abdominal mass. Her CT at that time showed a multicystic lesion measuring 18.0×9.8×7.6 cm in the right abdomen, which appeared to be arising from the right ovary. She underwent surgery which showed a hydatid cyst which was confirmed on histopathology. After remaining asymptomatic for 3 years, in 2007, she again gradually developed pain in the right pelvic region. This time MRI showed a large solid cystic mass measuring 10×6.3 cm in the right iliac fossa and right gluteal region. She underwent a diagnostic laparatomy where multiple cysts in the iliac bone with osteolysis were seen. Two cystic pieces were removed which were diagnosed as hydatid cyst. The patient continued with the pelvic pain although postoperative MRI showed reduction in size of the lesion along the right iliac bone and sacroiliac joint. The x-ray of the pelvis and hips revealed cortical irregularity with ill defined lytic defects and linear areas of patchy sclerosis along the right iliac blade. She continued with pain and follow-up CT revealed extension of the disease. Resection of the hydatid cyst of sacroiliac region with allograft and autograft with lumbosacroiliac fixation was done. Histopathology results revealed hydatid cysts of the bone. The patient was also put on a medical treatment with albendazole for 1 month. The postoperative period was uneventful and patient was discharged on the 10th day of surgery. The follow-up of the patient at 6 months showed no detectable abnormality on radiology, and she was doing well.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient is advised to follow up with the treating physician for regular check-ups and medication management. The patient should continue taking the prescribed medication as per the treatment plan. She should follow a healthy diet and lifestyle to maintain optimal health. The patient is also advised to rest and avoid physical activity until further notice from the treating physician. Any new or worsening symptoms should be reported promptly to the physician.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: Not Provided
Gender: Female
Age: 31 years
Admission Date: Not Provided
Discharge Date: Not Provided
Admitting Diagnosis: Not Provided
Discharge Diagnosis: Parasitic cysts in the bone

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came in with pain in the right hip and pelvis that had been getting worse for 3 years. The pain spread to the right leg and back and kept getting stronger over time. She also had a tingling feeling for the last 3 months. The pain made it very hard for her to move around. When she was 22 years old, she had a large growth in her belly. A scan showed a growth with many fluid-filled sacs measuring about 18 by 10 by 8 centimeters on the right side of her belly. Doctors thought it came from the right ovary. She had surgery that showed it was a parasitic cyst, which lab tests confirmed. She felt fine for 3 years after that surgery. In 2007, she slowly started having pain in the right pelvic area again. This time, a scan showed a large growth measuring about 10 by 6 centimeters in the right lower belly and right buttock area. Doctors did surgery to look inside and found many cysts in the hip bone with bone damage. They removed two pieces of cyst tissue, which were confirmed to be parasitic cysts. The patient still had pelvic pain after surgery. A follow-up scan showed the growth along the right hip bone and the joint between the hip and spine had gotten smaller. An x-ray of the pelvis and hips showed uneven bone surface with unclear areas of bone loss and patches of hardened bone along the right hip blade. The pain continued and a later scan showed the disease had spread. Doctors did surgery to remove the parasitic cyst near the hip and spine joint. They used bone from a donor and bone from the patient's own body to rebuild the area. They also placed metal hardware to hold the lower spine, hip, and tailbone area together. Lab results confirmed parasitic cysts in the bone. The patient was given albendazole medicine to take for 1 month. Recovery after surgery went smoothly with no problems. The patient left the hospital 10 days after surgery. At a check-up 6 months later, scans showed nothing wrong and she was doing well.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should see the treating doctor for regular check-ups and to manage her medicine. The patient should keep taking the medicine as the doctor ordered. She should eat healthy foods and live a healthy lifestyle to stay well. The patient should rest and not do physical activities until the doctor says it is okay. If any new symptoms appear or current symptoms get worse, the patient should tell the doctor right away.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: _____
Medical Record Number: _____

Hospital Course:
The neonate was admitted to NICU for ear malformation evaluation at one-day-old. His mother was a 24-year-old nulliparous woman who had taken medication for severe acne during pregnancy. The baby's birth was uneventful, and the baby was conscious and lively. He had a right ear deformity and microtia, but there was only a skin tag-like remnant of the left ear's lower part with no opening. Other physical examination findings were within the normal limits. Tympanic bone computer tomography and brain MRI were performed, which showed no significant abnormality.

Hospital Diagnosis:
Ear malformation.

Hospital Course:
The infant was discharged after receiving appropriate care for his malformation. He was recommended to follow-up with a local pediatric specialist for further discussion and management. The mother was advised to continue regular antenatal care to protect the fetal growth and malformation.

Hospital Disposition:
The infant was discharged in good condition to follow-up with local pediatric specialist. The patient was instructed to follow a regular appointment schedule for further management. 

Signed,

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: Confidential 
Age: 60 
Gender: Male 
Medical Record Number: Confidential 

Admission Date: Confidential 
Discharge Date: Confidential 
Duration of Stay: Confidential 

Admitting Diagnosis: Hypercalcaemic hyperparathyroid crisis 

Hospital Course: 

The patient was admitted to our tertiary referral center from a regional hospital with hypercalcaemic hyperparathyroid crisis. He presented with a two-week history of weakness and lethargy and a one-week history of vomiting, polyuria and polydipsia. He became confused 24 hours prior to his admission and registered 12 on the Glasgow Coma Scale. On examination, he had a left-sided mass in the anterior triangle of his neck measuring 3 × 3 cm. An ultrasonography of his neck showed a 4 × 3 cm large cyst in the left lobe of his thyroid gland. He was normotensive, with a regular pulse of 70 beats/min. 

Initial management included aggressive fluid resuscitation, cardiac monitoring and the administration of intravenous bisphosphonates. A left thyroid lobectomy and left lower parathyroidectomy were performed. Biopsy revealed a 4 cm parathyroid carcinoma within the cyst, which was fully excised. The patient remained intubated and ventilated overnight in the intensive care unit. His metabolic laboratory profile resolved quickly following the surgery. A lower respiratory tract infection delayed his initial recovery. 

The patient was discharged home 24 days after surgery on oral calcium supplementation. He has been followed up for 6 months so far without any complications or disease recurrence. 

Summary of Care: 

The patient was admitted with hypercalcaemic hyperparathyroid crisis and managed with aggressive fluid resuscitation, cardiac monitoring and the administration of intravenous bisphosphonates. Following a left thyroid lobectomy and left lower parathyroidectomy, he was discharged home on oral calcium supplementation and has been followed up for 6 months without any complications or disease recurrence. 

Disposition: Discharged. 

Instructions: 
- Continue taking oral calcium supplementation as directed 
- Follow up with your primary care physician in 2 weeks 
- In case of any emergency, call 911 or go to the nearest emergency department. 

Signed, 
Confidential 
Attending Physician.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: Confidential
Age: 60
Gender: Male
Medical Record Number: Confidential

Admission Date: Confidential
Discharge Date: Confidential
Duration of Stay: Confidential

Admitting Diagnosis: A serious condition caused by too much calcium in the blood due to an overactive parathyroid gland

Hospital Course:

The patient was transferred to our main hospital from a smaller regional hospital. He had a serious condition with too much calcium in the blood caused by an overactive parathyroid gland. For two weeks, he felt weak and very tired. For one week, he had been throwing up, urinating a lot, and feeling very thirsty. He became confused 24 hours before coming to the hospital. He was not fully alert and scored 12 on a test that measures awareness. During the exam, doctors found a lump on the left side of his neck. The lump was about 3 by 3 centimeters and located in the front triangle area. An ultrasound of his neck showed a large fluid-filled sac in the left side of his thyroid gland. The sac measured 4 by 3 centimeters. His blood pressure was normal. His heartbeat was steady at 70 beats per minute.

First, the medical team gave him lots of fluids through a vein. They watched his heart closely. They also gave him medicine through a vein to lower his calcium levels. Then, surgeons removed the left side of his thyroid gland. They also removed the lower left parathyroid gland. Tests on the removed tissue showed a 4 centimeter parathyroid cancer inside the fluid-filled sac. The cancer was completely removed. After surgery, the patient stayed in the intensive care unit overnight. He had a breathing tube and was on a breathing machine. His blood test results returned to normal quickly after surgery. A lung infection slowed down his early recovery.

The patient went home 24 days after surgery. He was given calcium pills to take by mouth. He has been checked by doctors for 6 months since then. He has had no problems and the disease has not come back.

Summary of Care:

The patient came to the hospital with a serious condition from too much calcium in the blood. This was caused by an overactive parathyroid gland. The medical team gave him lots of fluids, watched his heart, and gave him medicine to lower calcium. Surgeons removed the left side of his thyroid gland and the lower left parathyroid gland. The patient went home taking calcium pills by mouth. He has been checked for 6 months with no problems and no return of the disease.

Disposition: Discharged.

Instructions:
Keep taking calcium pills by mouth as told.
See the regular doctor in 2 weeks.
In case of any emergency, call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room.

Signed,
Confidential
Attending Physician.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Medical History: A 12-year-old female patient was admitted with a history of polyuria, polydipsia, and secondary amenorrhea.
Clinical Findings: A complete preoperative endocrinological assessment revealed mild prolactin hormone elevation, hypothyroidism, and hypocortisolism. Neuroradiological investigation showed a large mass in the sella with compression of the optic chiasm and extending into the third ventricle.
Diagnosis: A craniopharyngioma or a GCT were the suspected diagnoses. However, the symptoms presented by the patient suggested a germinoma.
Procedure: The patient underwent an extended endoscopic endonasal transtuberculum/transplanum approach for tumor removal.
Postoperative Course: The patient demonstrated a significant improvement in her condition with light perception reported in the left eye and improvement in right eye temporal hemianopsia. There were no postoperative complications.
Postoperative Follow-up: Serum levels of AFP, and beta-HCG were checked, suggesting confirmation of germinoma malignancy. The patient was referred to a pediatric oncologist for further treatment, which includes adjunctive chemotherapy and radiation therapy.
Condition at Discharge: The patient was discharged in a normal condition but still receiving desmopressin acetate for diabetes insipidus treatment.
Prognosis: The patient's condition remains optimistic, and a follow-up appointment is necessary.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Medical History: A 12-year-old female patient was admitted. She had been urinating a lot, feeling very thirsty, and her periods had stopped.

Clinical Findings: Doctors did hormone tests before surgery. The tests showed slightly high levels of a hormone called prolactin. The tests also showed her thyroid gland was underactive. Her body was not making enough stress hormones. Brain scans showed a large growth in the lower part of the brain. This growth was pressing on the nerves that control vision. It was also reaching into a fluid-filled space in the brain.

Diagnosis: Doctors first thought it might be one of two types of brain tumors. But based on the patient's symptoms, they believed it was a type called germinoma.

Procedure: The patient had surgery to remove the tumor. Doctors went through the nose using a small camera and tools. This allowed them to reach the tumor without opening the skull.

Postoperative Course: The patient got much better after surgery. She could see light with her left eye. Her side vision in her right eye also improved. There were no problems after surgery.

Postoperative Follow-up: Blood tests checked for certain markers. These results confirmed the tumor was a type of cancer called germinoma. The patient was sent to a children's cancer doctor for more treatment. She will need chemotherapy and radiation therapy.

Condition at Discharge: The patient left the hospital in normal condition. She is still taking a medicine called desmopressin acetate. This treats a condition where her body cannot control water balance.

Prognosis: The outlook for the patient is hopeful. She will need to come back for a follow-up visit.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Diagnosis: Carbon particle injury to the left eye

Hospital Course:
An 8-year-old boy with no significant medical history was admitted to our urgent ophthalmology clinic with a pencil injury to his left eye. The patient presented with intact corneal epithelium and intrastromal silver-gray carbon particles in the inferonasal quadrant of the cornea. Mild hyperemia was noted in the conjunctiva. However, no sign of anterior chamber reaction was observed. The patient was prescribed Vigamox ophthalmic drops 4 times a day for 3 weeks and preservative-free artificial tears 4 times a day for 4 weeks.

The patient tolerated the treatment well and was discharged with no complications. Follow-up examinations at 1 week, 1, 4, and 6 months showed no signs of inflammatory reaction or injection.

Recommendations:
1. Vision screening should be conducted regularly to monitor for any changes.
2. Regular eye exams are advisable, especially in the presence of eye injuries.
3. Patients should avoid penetrating injuries to the eye.
4. Patients and caregivers should provide immediate medical attention in the event of an eye injury.

Primary Care Follow-up:
The patient is advised to follow up with a pediatric ophthalmologist for regular vision screenings and eye exams. Any changes in vision, pain, or swelling should be reported immediately to their primary care physician.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Diagnosis: Carbon (pencil lead) injury to the left eye

Hospital Course:
An 8-year-old boy with no past health problems came to our eye emergency clinic after a pencil hurt his left eye. The clear front layer of the eye was not broken. Small silver-gray pencil lead bits were found in the lower inner part of the clear front of the eye. The white part of the eye was slightly red. There were no signs of swelling or irritation inside the eye. The patient was given Vigamox eye drops to use 4 times a day for 3 weeks. The patient was also given artificial tears without added chemicals to use 4 times a day for 4 weeks.

The patient handled the treatment well and went home with no problems. Check-up visits at 1 week, 1 month, 4 months, and 6 months showed no signs of redness, swelling, or irritation.

Recommendations:
1. The patient should have regular vision checks to watch for any changes.
2. Regular eye exams are a good idea, especially after eye injuries.
3. The patient should avoid injuries that break through the eye surface.
4. The patient and caregivers should get medical help right away if an eye injury happens.

Primary Care Follow-up:
The patient should see a children's eye doctor for regular vision checks and eye exams. Any changes in vision, pain, or swelling should be reported right away to the regular doctor.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 21-year-old male
Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]
Duration of Stay: [number] days

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the emergency department after losing consciousness. Initial assessment found no abnormalities, and the patient was admitted to the department of internal medicine for further evaluation. Twelve hours later, the patient developed diffuse abdominal pain and demonstrated a drop in hemoglobin levels. Rebound tenderness and decreased abdominal sounds were noted upon examination. CT scan revealed the presence of a ruptured proximal splenic artery aneurysm, then angiography with coil embolization was performed. The patient's condition was stabilized, and he was transferred to the surgical department for further monitoring and treatment with intravenous fluids and blood transfusions. He remained stable and was discharged on the 12th day after embolization. An 8th-month follow-up examination showed preservation of the spleen and complete elimination of the aneurysm.

Diagnoses:
Ruptured proximal splenic artery aneurysm

Procedures:
Angiography with coil embolization of the aneurysm and proximal splenic artery using 2D and 3D mechanically detachable fibered micro coils, and N-butyl-cyanoacrylate (NBCA) glue.

Summary:
The patient was admitted to the hospital after experiencing a loss of consciousness. A ruptured proximal splenic artery aneurysm was identified, and angiography with coil embolization was performed with the aim of preserving all collateral vessels for the spleen. The patient was referred to the surgical department for further treatment and monitoring. The patient's condition remained stable, and he was discharged on the 12th day after embolization. The follow-up examination showed preservation of the spleen and complete elimination of the aneurysm.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 21-year-old male
Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]
Duration of Stay: [number] days

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room after passing out. The first tests showed nothing wrong. The patient was moved to the general medicine unit for more tests. Twelve hours later, the patient had pain all over the belly area. Blood tests showed the patient was losing blood inside the body. The doctor pressed on the belly and found it was very tender. Gut sounds were also quieter than normal. A CT scan showed a bulge in a blood vessel near the spleen had burst open. The spleen is an organ on the left side of the belly. Doctors did a procedure to block the bleeding vessel using tiny coils. The patient became stable and was moved to the surgery unit. There, the patient received fluids and blood through an IV. The patient stayed stable and went home 12 days after the procedure. A checkup 8 months later showed the spleen was saved. The bulge in the blood vessel was completely fixed.

Diagnoses:
Burst bulge in a blood vessel near the spleen

Procedures:
Doctors used a thin tube to reach the blood vessel. They blocked the bleeding with tiny coils and a special medical glue. This stopped the bleeding while keeping blood flow to the spleen.

Summary:
The patient came to the hospital after passing out. Doctors found a burst bulge in a blood vessel near the spleen. They did a procedure to block the bleeding while saving blood flow to the spleen. The patient was moved to the surgery unit for more care and watching. The patient stayed stable and went home 12 days after the procedure. The follow-up visit showed the spleen was saved. The bulge in the blood vessel was completely fixed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 36-year-old prisoner

Diagnosis: Candida lung abscess

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital due to fever, asthenia, cough, and hemoptysis, which did not respond to antibiotic therapy. It was revealed that the patient had a history of smoking crack cocaine, injecting heroin, and untreated liver disease related to HCV. Laboratory tests indicated an increase in white blood cell count and inflammation. Further, a 7 cm round and excavated lesion was also detected in the right lower lobe of the lungs. The patient was diagnosed with a Candida lung abscess and started on intravenous fluconazole 400 mg daily followed by its oral formulation, leading to clinical recovery. In addition to the treatment, the patient was monitored under our hepatological unit, and DAAs were prescribed.

Course of Treatment:
The patient was treated with intravenous fluconazole 400 mg followed by an oral formulation until complete clinical recovery and was later discharged. The patient was given an appointment to be followed up with our hepatological unit and to perform the treatment with DAAs. After three months of therapy with sofosbuvir 400 mg/velpatasvir 100 mg once daily, the sustained virological response (SVR) was reached and it’s still active.

Follow-up Care:
The patient was given an appointment to be followed up with our hepatological unit to ensure the continued management of the HCV-related liver disease. The patient was also advised to maintain healthy habits and refrain from drug consumption.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 36-year-old prisoner

Diagnosis: Fungal infection causing a pocket of pus in the lung

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital with fever, weakness, cough, and coughing up blood. These symptoms did not get better with antibiotics. The patient had a history of smoking crack cocaine, injecting heroin, and untreated liver disease caused by the hepatitis C virus. Blood tests showed a high number of white blood cells and signs of infection in the body. Doctors also found a 7 cm round cavity in the lower part of the right lung. The patient was diagnosed with a fungal lung infection caused by Candida. Treatment began with fluconazole 400 mg given through a vein, then switched to pills. This led to recovery. The patient was also watched by our liver care team. Medicines to treat hepatitis C were prescribed.

Course of Treatment:
The patient received fluconazole 400 mg through a vein first, then as pills until fully recovered. The patient was then sent home. The patient was given an appointment to follow up with our liver care team and start hepatitis C treatment. After three months of taking sofosbuvir 400 mg and velpatasvir 100 mg once a day, the hepatitis C virus was no longer found in the blood. This result is still holding.

Follow-up Care:
The patient was given an appointment to follow up with our liver care team. This is to keep managing the liver disease caused by hepatitis C. The patient was also told to keep healthy habits and to stop using drugs.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
The patient is a 57-year-old man with a history of hypertension and generalized anxiety disorder who presented with dizziness, palpitation, chest tightness, and diaphoresis.

Admission:
The patient was admitted to the hospital due to concerns of a sudden onset of symptoms, which he stated were different from his usual anxiety attack symptoms.

History:
The patient revealed having recurrent pressure-like sensations in his chest, which was accompanied by tightness, palpitation, and diaphoresis. An examination of his vital signs and a physical examination was unremarkable except for the presence of bilateral  DELCs.

Diagnostic Assessment:
An electrocardiogram (EKG) indicated normal sinus rhythm with ST depression. Serial cardiac enzyme levels were normal. A subsequent echocardiogram revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 60% with no wall motion abnormalities. Coronary angiogram revealed an 80% occlusion in the right coronary artery.

Treatment:
The patient underwent angioplasty and insertion of drug-eluting stents.

Discharge:
The patient's symptoms were resolved, and he was discharged in stable condition. 

Instructions:
The patient was advised to continue his medications, follow up with his primary care physician, and make lifestyle changes to reduce the risk of another cardiovascular event.

Follow-Up:
The patient's follow-up and future treatment were discussed with him.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
The patient is a 57-year-old man with a history of high blood pressure and anxiety disorder. He came to the hospital with dizziness, fast or pounding heartbeat, chest tightness, and heavy sweating.

Admission:
The patient was admitted to the hospital because his symptoms came on suddenly. He said these symptoms felt different from his usual anxiety attacks.

History:
The patient reported feeling pressure in his chest that kept coming back. This came with tightness, fast heartbeat, and heavy sweating. His vital signs and physical exam were normal. The doctor did notice creases on both earlobes.

Diagnostic Assessment:
A heart rhythm test showed a normal heartbeat but with some changes that can signal heart problems. Blood tests to check for heart damage came back normal. A heart ultrasound showed the heart was pumping well at 60 percent with no problems in how the heart walls move. A test using dye to look at the heart arteries showed one artery on the right side was 80 percent blocked.

Treatment:
The patient had a procedure to open the blocked artery. A small mesh tube coated with medicine was placed to keep the artery open.

Discharge:
The patient's symptoms went away. He left the hospital in stable condition.

Instructions:
The patient was told to keep taking his medications. He should see his regular doctor for a follow-up visit. He should also make lifestyle changes to lower the chance of another heart problem.

Follow-Up:
The care team talked with the patient about his follow-up visits and future treatment plan.

Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A

Chief Complaint: Increasing eyelid closure, retrocollis, and difficulty in ambulation due to right foot inversion

History of Present Illness: The patient, a 54-year-old male diagnosed with X-linked dystonia-parkinsonism (XDP), was asymptomatic and receiving BoNT-A treatments every 3-4 months. However, he missed some of his treatments resulting in worsening symptoms. On January 23, 2020, the patient received BoNT-A treatment but subsequently reported increasing eyelid closure, retrocollis, and difficulty in ambulation due to right foot inversion.

Hospital Course: The patient received telehealth neurology consultations due to the COVID-19 pandemic. The patients' concerns were addressed, and they were informed of the current situation. Short-term plans were established to address their concerns, facilitate efficient teleconsultations, and schedule a BoNT-A injection in the specialist's clinic with strict implementation of infection control and decontamination measures.

Hospital Course Summary:
 The patient, a 54-year-old male diagnosed with X-linked dystonia-parkinsonism (XDP) was asymptomatic and receiving BoNT-A treatments every 3-4 months. However, due to missed treatments, the patient reported increasing eyelid closure, retrocollis, and difficulty in ambulation due to right foot inversion. Telehealth neurology consultations were conducted due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Short-term plans were established to address the patient's concerns, facilitate efficient teleconsultations, and schedule BoNT-A injection in the specialist's clinic with strict implementation of infection control and decontamination measures.
