Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A

Main Problem: The patient had increasing trouble opening the eyes, the neck tilting backward, and trouble walking because the right foot was turning inward.

Current Illness: The patient is a 54-year-old man with a rare movement disorder that runs in families. This condition causes muscles to move on their own and makes movement difficult. The patient was doing well and getting injections every 3 to 4 months to relax the muscles. However, the patient missed some treatments. The symptoms got worse. On January 23, 2020, the patient got an injection treatment. After that, the patient reported more trouble opening the eyes, the neck tilting backward, and trouble walking because the right foot was turning inward.

Hospital Stay: The patient had video or phone visits with a brain and nerve doctor because of the COVID-19 outbreak. The doctors listened to the patient's concerns. They explained what was happening. They made short-term plans to help the patient. These plans included setting up easy video or phone visits. They also scheduled an injection treatment at the specialist's office. The office would follow strict cleaning and safety steps to prevent infection.

Hospital Stay Summary: The patient is a 54-year-old man with a rare movement disorder that runs in families. The patient was doing well and getting injection treatments every 3 to 4 months. Because of missed treatments, the patient had more trouble opening the eyes, the neck tilting backward, and trouble walking because the right foot was turning inward. Video or phone visits with a brain and nerve doctor were set up because of the COVID-19 outbreak. Short-term plans were made to address the patient's concerns. These plans included setting up easy video or phone visits and scheduling an injection at the specialist's office with strict cleaning and safety steps.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission:
A 43-year-old male with primary hypothyroidism was admitted for evaluation of progressive fatigue, weakness, muscle and joint pain in the setting of recurrent fractures.

History of Present Illness:
The patient had been experiencing musculoskeletal pain in several locations including bilateral knees, ankles, left shoulder, left arm, and lower back which affected his overall functional status. He struggled to climb a flight of stairs, often grabbing the railing to pull himself up. A nuclear medicine bone scan showed abnormal uptake in bilateral ribs, vertebrae, right knee, hips, ankles, and feet. A lower extremity MRI showed stress fractures in left fibula, fifth metatarsal, and right tibia.

Course in Hospital:
The patient was started on calcitriol and phosphate supplements which significantly improved muscle weakness. Due to concern for TIO, a positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) scan with 68Gallium (68Ga)-DOTATATE was performed which showed multiple lytic osseous lesions with increased somatostatin positive uptake seen throughout pelvis, ribs, ankles, and feet. However, after re-review with radiology, multiple areas of increased uptake were considered to be secondary to fractures, and left posterior acetabular lesion was presumed to be the tumor of interest given a remarkably higher standard uptake values (SUVmax) in this lesion compared to other lesions (SUVmax 20 vs. 4–5).

Procedure:
The left acetabular lesion was biopsied and pathology demonstrated a 1.4 cm phosphaturic mesenchymal tumor of the left acetabulum with uninvolved margins. The patient underwent radical resection of the tumor of the posterior left acetabulum with reconstruction of the posterior wall and posterior column of the acetabulum through a Kocher-Langenbach approach. His hip joint was able to be spared utilizing a 3D-printed custom bone cutting guide (CG) and intra-operative O-arm guided surgical navigation (Medtronic© Stealth) utilizing a navigated Sonopet Ultrasonic Bone Cutting Bovie (Stryker). The posterior wall and column of his acetabulum were constructed with a tantalum augment (Zimmer) and a pelvic reconstruction plate and screw construct (Synthes) after tumor removal.

Discharge Summary:
The patient was discharged with normal phosphorus and FGF23 levels, pain-free left hip, no evidence of hardware complication, back to hiking, and walking on the treadmill. Clinical and radiographic follow-up at 6 months postoperatively found him to be pain-free in his left hip, with almost complete resolution of bone pain, and no recurrence of fractures. Phosphorus level also remained normal at 3.4 mg/dL 6 months after surgery.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission:
A 43-year-old male with an underactive thyroid was admitted. Doctors needed to find out why he had ongoing tiredness, weakness, muscle and joint pain, and broken bones that kept happening.

History of Present Illness:
The patient had muscle and bone pain in many areas. These included both knees, both ankles, left shoulder, left arm, and lower back. This pain made it hard for him to do daily activities. He had trouble walking up stairs and had to pull himself up using the railing. A bone scan showed problem areas in both sides of his ribs, spine, right knee, hips, ankles, and feet. An imaging test of his lower legs showed small cracks in the left calf bone, a foot bone, and right shin bone.

Course in Hospital:
The patient was given vitamin D and phosphorus supplements. These greatly improved his muscle weakness. Doctors thought a rare tumor might be causing his problems. A special full-body scan was done to look for it. The scan showed many bone spots with holes that were picking up the scan marker. These spots were in the pelvis, ribs, ankles, and feet. After doctors looked at the scan again, most of these spots seemed to be from broken bones. One spot on the left hip socket stood out. It picked up much more of the scan marker than the other spots. This spot was most likely the tumor causing his problems.

Procedure:
Doctors took a small sample from the left hip socket spot. Testing showed a small tumor measuring 1.4 cm. This type of tumor causes the body to lose phosphorus. The edges of the sample were free of tumor cells. The patient had surgery to fully remove the tumor from the back of his left hip socket. Surgeons rebuilt the back wall and back support of the hip socket. They used a special approach from behind the hip. The hip joint itself was saved. Surgeons used a custom three-dimensional printed guide to cut the bone precisely. They also used a computer system to guide them during surgery. A special ultrasonic bone-cutting tool was used. After removing the tumor, the back wall and support were rebuilt. Surgeons used a metal support piece and a plate with screws made for pelvis repair.

Discharge Summary:
The patient went home with normal phosphorus levels. His hormone levels that control phosphorus were also normal. His left hip had no pain. The metal parts in his hip showed no problems. He was back to hiking and walking on a treadmill. At a checkup six months after surgery, he had no left hip pain. Almost all of his bone pain was gone. He had no new broken bones. His phosphorus level stayed normal at 3.4 mg per deciliter six months after surgery.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
The patient is a 16-year-old boy who presented to the eye casualty with acute inferior hemifield visual loss in the left eye.

Hospital Course:
Upon examination, a clear inferior altitudinal defect was evident with associated arterial attenuation in the left eye. The patient was diagnosed with superior branch retinal artery occlusion (BRAO) and was started on Aspirin. The patient was urgently referred to the local stroke team for further evaluation.

The patient underwent trans-thoracic echocardiography, MRI and MR-angiogram of brain and neck, which were all unremarkable. Haematological investigations revealed a raised homocysteine level, vitamin B12 deficiency, and borderline folate deficiency. Subsequent genetic testing revealed a C677T subtype homozygous mutation for the gene encoding methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR).

Treatment:
The patient was started on vitamin B12 and folate replacement, which led to normalization of homocysteine levels. Aspirin therapy was also continued.

Outcome:
Follow-up visual field testing at two months revealed slight improvement in the visual field defect, and no further vaso-occlusive events were noted.

Summary:
The patient presented with acute inferior hemi-field visual loss in the left eye with a diagnosis of superior branch retinal artery occlusion (BRAO). Through various diagnostic tests, a C677T subtype homozygous mutation for the gene encoding methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR) was identified. The patient was successfully treated with vitamin B12 and folate replacement, and aspirin therapy was continued. Follow-up testing at two months revealed improvement in the visual field defect.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
The patient is a 16-year-old boy who came to the eye emergency room. He had sudden vision loss in the lower half of his left eye.

Hospital Course:
During the eye exam, doctors found the patient could not see in the lower part of his vision. The blood vessels in the left eye also looked narrower than normal. The patient was told he had a blocked blood vessel in the upper part of his left eye. He was started on Aspirin. The patient was quickly sent to the stroke team for more tests.

The patient had an ultrasound of his heart, brain scans, and scans of blood vessels in the brain and neck. All of these tests came back normal. Blood tests showed high levels of a substance called homocysteine. The patient also had low vitamin B12 and slightly low folate levels. Later, a gene test found a change in a gene called MTHFR. This gene change can affect how the body uses certain vitamins.

Treatment:
The patient was given vitamin B12 and folate supplements. This brought the homocysteine levels back to normal. The patient also kept taking Aspirin.

Outcome:
A vision test two months later showed the patient's eyesight had gotten a little better. No new blood vessel blockages happened.

Summary:
The patient came in with sudden vision loss in the lower half of his left eye. This was caused by a blocked blood vessel in the upper part of the eye. Tests found a gene change called MTHFR that affects how the body processes vitamins. The patient was treated with vitamin B12 and folate supplements. The patient also continued taking Aspirin. A follow-up test at two months showed some improvement in vision.

input:
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]
Duration of Hospitalization: [NUMBER] days

FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
Acute myocardial infarction with tubular and diffuse 95% stenosis in proximal and mid LAD respectively resulting in stenting with paclitaxel-eluting coronary stent system. Definite fracture of stent detected in mid part of LAD.

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient, a 63-year-old woman with typical chest pain, was admitted to the hospital and underwent several tests including a transthoracic echocardiography which showed a 40% ejection fraction, left ventricular dilatation with hypokinesia in the lateral walls and dyskinesia of septal and apical walls. Her Coronary angiography revealed a tubular lesion (95%) in the proximal left anterior descending artery (LAD), and a diffuse lesion (95%) in mid part of LAD with a good distal flow. Stenting of proximal lesion with a 3 mm x 19 mm, paclitaxel-eluting coronary stent system (EURoCOR), and mid part of lesion with a 2.5 mm x 32 mm paclitaxel-eluting coronary stent system (EURoCOR) was carried out with success.

After 6 days, the patient referred with a typical chest pain without any response to medication. Coronary angiography was done and showed an optimal extent expansion without any significant residual stenosis. However, the definite fracture of stent that was implanted in the mid part of LAD 6 days before her admission was detected which required further intervention.

During her hospital course, the patient was monitored closely and treated with standard care and preventive measures, including medication administration, diet and exercise counseling and vital signs monitoring.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
The patient's condition is improved and stable upon discharge. Home care instructions were provided along with prescribed medications and follow up visits with her Primary Care Physician and Cardiologist were scheduled.
clean_ref:
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]
Duration of Hospitalization: [NUMBER] days

FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
The patient had a heart attack. A heart artery called the LAD was almost completely blocked in two spots. Doctors placed two small mesh tubes called stents to open the artery. These stents release medicine to help keep the artery open. Later, doctors found that one of the stents had broken.

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient is a 63-year-old woman who came to the hospital with chest pain. Doctors did several tests. A heart ultrasound showed that her heart was pumping at only 40 percent of normal strength. The left side of her heart was enlarged. Some parts of her heart wall were not moving well. Other parts were moving in the wrong direction. A test using dye showed her LAD artery was 95 percent blocked in two places. Blood flow below the blockages was still good. Doctors placed two stents to open the blocked areas. The first stent was 3 mm wide and 19 mm long. The second stent was 2.5 mm wide and 32 mm long. Both procedures went well.

Six days later, the patient came back with chest pain. Medicine did not help the pain. Doctors did another dye test. The stents were open and working well. But the second stent placed in the middle part of the artery had a break in it. This break needed more treatment.

During her hospital stay, the care team watched the patient closely. She received medicine and standard care. The team gave her advice about healthy eating and exercise. Staff checked her blood pressure, heart rate, and other vital signs often.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
The patient is doing better and her condition is stable. She received instructions for care at home. She was given prescriptions for her medicines. She has follow-up visits scheduled with her regular doctor and heart doctor.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient: [name, age, date of admission and discharge]

Medical History:
- Alcohol Abuse with Chronic Hepatopathy, Portal Hypertension, and Esophageal Varices
- Hypothyroidism
- Glandular and Pulmonary Sarcoidosis treated with long-term steroids
- Traumatic Brain Injury

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted to the hospital after suffering a Traumatic Brain Injury following a casual fall which resulted in agitation. A CT scan revealed a right intra-axial parietal lesion, and MRI studies suggested an Intracranial Abscess in the form of a heterogeneous lesion with peripheral contrast enhancement and diffusion restriction. Accordingly, the patient underwent craniotomy and evacuation of the lesion, followed by the initiation of treatment with trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole and meropenem after the cultures tested positive for Nocardia cerradoensis. 

After the initial improvement, the patient's condition started to deteriorate progressively, and he experienced respiratory insufficiency leading to prolonged assisted ventilation. A pulmonary biopsy was obtained, which revealed extensive pulmonary fibrosis. Despite the ongoing medical support, the patient showed no signs of improvement, leading to a mutual decision to withdraw from ventilation. The patient passed away eventually.

Final Diagnosis:
- Traumatic Brain Injury
- Intracranial Abscess secondary to Nocardia Cerradoensis
- Chronic Hepatopathy
- Portal Hypertension
- Esophageal Varices
- Hypothyroidism 
- Glandular and Pulmonary Sarcoidosis
- Progressive Respiratory Insufficiency 
- Extensive Pulmonary Fibrosis 

Disposition:
The patient passed away after being withdrawn from ventilation.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient: [name, age, date of admission and discharge]

Medical History:
Long-term alcohol use causing liver damage, high blood pressure in the liver veins, and swollen veins in the food pipe
Underactive thyroid gland
A condition causing lumps in the glands and lungs, treated with steroids for a long time
Past head injury

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came to the hospital after a head injury from an accidental fall. This caused the patient to become very restless. A CT scan showed a problem area on the right side of the brain. An MRI scan suggested there was a pocket of infection inside the brain. The doctors did surgery to open the skull and remove the infected area. Lab tests found a type of germ called Nocardia cerradoensis. The patient started taking two antibiotics to fight this infection.

At first, the patient got better. Then the patient slowly got worse. The patient had trouble breathing and needed a machine to help with breathing for a long time. Doctors took a small sample of lung tissue to test. The test showed serious scarring in the lungs. Even with continued medical care, the patient did not get better. The family and medical team agreed together to stop using the breathing machine. The patient died.

Final Diagnosis:
Head injury
Brain infection caused by a germ called Nocardia cerradoensis
Long-term liver damage
High blood pressure in the liver veins
Swollen veins in the food pipe
Underactive thyroid gland
A condition causing lumps in the glands and lungs
Worsening breathing problems
Serious lung scarring

Disposition:
The patient died after the breathing machine was stopped.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: November 2018
Discharge Date: [Date not specified]

Hospital Course:
A 53-year-old female patient, with a history of NHL (diffuse large B-cell lymphoma [DLBCL]), presented with a short history of progressively deteriorating pain and weakness in the right lower limb. The patient had completed chemotherapy (8 × R-CHOPP) with a complete metabolic response in January 2018. Further examination revealed mild swelling over the left maxillary region with few palpable nodes in bilateral upper cervical regions. Neurological examination revealed a significant lower motor weakness in the right lower limb (2/5), and sensory examination revealed mild appendicular acral numbness to light touch. There was also evidence of mild motor weakness in both the upper limbs (4/5) without significant abnormality on sensory examination. Biopsy of the left facial swelling revealed DLBCL (CD20 positive with Ki-67 index >90%). Her cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and bone trephine did not reveal lymphomatous infiltration. A low-dose noncontrast 18FDG PET/CT examination was performed, which revealed hypermetabolic soft-tissue deposits and hypermetabolic lymph nodes in various regions, including left Level II, V, and celiac regions. There was also evidence of hypermetabolic uptake (predominantly linear pattern) over the bilateral brachial and right sacral plexuses and bilateral sciatic, right gluteal, and both femoral nerves. Based on the clinical, facial biopsy and 18FDG PET/CT findings, a diagnosis of recurrent lymphoma with NLS was made. The second-line chemotherapy was started, and after four cycles, she became asymptomatic with a resolution of left facial swelling and normalization of sensory and motor deficits too. An interim 18FDG PET/CT revealed a complete metabolic response too.

Hospital Diagnosis:
Recurrent lymphoma with NLS

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with the diagnosis of recurrent lymphoma with NLS. Second-line chemotherapy was administered, resulting in the resolution of left facial swelling and normalization of sensory and motor deficits. An interim 18FDG PET/CT revealed a complete metabolic response. The patient was discharged in stable condition.

Medications on Discharge:
[Not specified]

Follow-Up Instructions:
[Not specified]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: November 2018
Discharge Date: Date not specified

Hospital Course:
A 53-year-old female patient has a history of a type of blood cancer called diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. The patient came to the hospital with worsening pain and weakness in the right leg. The patient had finished cancer treatment with 8 rounds of chemotherapy in January 2018. At that time, the cancer showed no signs of activity. Doctors found mild swelling on the left side of the face. They also felt small lumps on both sides of the upper neck. Tests showed major muscle weakness in the right leg, rated 2 out of 5. The patient had mild numbness in the hands and feet when touched lightly. Both arms also had mild weakness, rated 4 out of 5. Feeling in the arms was normal. A tissue sample from the left facial swelling showed the cancer had returned. Testing of the spinal fluid and bone marrow showed no cancer cells. A full body scan was done to look for cancer activity. The scan showed active cancer deposits in soft tissue. It also showed active cancer in lymph nodes on the left side of the neck and belly area. The scan showed the cancer had spread to nerves in both arms, the lower back, hips, and both legs. Based on all test results, doctors found the cancer had come back and spread to the nerves. The patient started a second round of stronger chemotherapy. After four treatment cycles, the patient had no more symptoms. The facial swelling went away. Muscle strength and feeling returned to normal. A follow-up body scan showed the cancer was no longer active.

Hospital Diagnosis:
Cancer that came back and spread to the nerves

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted because the cancer came back and spread to the nerves. A second round of chemotherapy was given. The left facial swelling went away. Muscle strength and feeling returned to normal. A follow-up body scan showed the cancer was no longer active. The patient left the hospital in stable condition.

Medications on Discharge:
Not specified

Follow-Up Instructions:
Not specified

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
A 9-year-old male patient was admitted to our hospital with sudden loss of vision in his left eye for the past 10 days, following an episode of vigorous eye rubbing. On examination, corneal ectasia with Vogt's striae was observed in the right eye along with a superficial corneal scar. The left eye exhibited dense stromal edema along with a central corneal ectasia, indicating acute hydrops. A diagnosis of bilateral keratoconus with left eye acute hydrops was made based on the clinical findings.

Treatment and Procedures:
The patient underwent pneumodesmetopexy with C3F8 in the left eye, with AC paracentesis using a 26-gauge needle under aseptic conditions. Air was first added to completely fill the anterior chamber (AC) and then replaced with 14% of C3F8 to fill two-thirds of the AC. Topical antibiotics and steroids were prescribed, and the patient was advised to remain in a supine position postoperatively.

Outcome:
The patient has been followed up for 12 months, and the corneal edema has completely resolved. The patient currently has a nebular grade corneal scar and the best-corrected visual acuity is 6/9 (with contact lens). Intraocular pressure (IOP) has been normal postoperatively.

Impressions and Plan:
The patient was treated for bilateral keratoconus with left eye acute hydrops through pneumodesmetopexy with C3F8. The patient’s corneal edema completely resolved, and the best-corrected visual acuity has improved with contact lens. The patient has been discharged with long term follow-up recommended.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
A 9-year-old boy came to our hospital because he suddenly lost vision in his left eye 10 days earlier. This happened after he rubbed his eye very hard. When doctors checked his eyes, they found problems with both. The right eye had a bulging cornea (the clear front part of the eye) with stretch marks and a small surface scar. The left eye had severe swelling and a bulging cornea in the center. This showed a sudden fluid buildup. The doctors found that both eyes had a condition called keratoconus, where the cornea bulges outward. The left eye also had sudden swelling from fluid leaking into the cornea.

Treatment and Procedures:
The patient had a procedure on his left eye to fix the swelling. The doctor used a small needle to drain fluid from the front part of the eye in a clean, germ-free setting. Then the doctor filled this space with air. After that, the air was replaced with a special gas that filled two-thirds of the space. This gas helps push the cornea back into shape. The patient was given eye drops to prevent infection and reduce swelling. The patient was told to lie flat on his back after the procedure.

Outcome:
The patient has been checked by doctors for 12 months. The corneal swelling has completely gone away. The patient now has a light, cloudy scar on the cornea. With contact lenses, the patient can see 6/9, which is close to normal vision. The pressure inside the eye has stayed normal since the procedure.

Impressions and Plan:
The patient was treated for bulging corneas in both eyes and sudden swelling in the left eye. The special gas procedure was used to fix this. The corneal swelling has completely cleared up. The patient's vision has gotten better with contact lenses. The patient has been sent home and should keep seeing eye doctors for a long time.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 26 years
Gender: Female

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with a ten-year history of vomiting that was not relieved by standard antiemetic and behavioral treatments for cyclic vomiting syndrome. After thorough medical examination, a possible diagnosis of epilepsy was considered as the patient experienced cramping of the tongue, clenched her teeth against her will, and had an abnormal EEG showing spike and wave complexes in the left temporal lobe. Clonazepam was administered, which substantially improved background wave activity and decreased vomiting episodes. Psychological treatment, including relaxation techniques, breathing exercises, drawing pictures, and counseling, was also provided as the patient was suspected of having a mental trigger for her epilepsy.

Patient's Social and Family History:
The patient had a noncontributory past and family medical history. Family dynamics were identified as a possible psychological trigger. In her childhood, the patient was well-behaved, but her mother paid little attention to her as she was busy caring for her sick elder sister. When her sister entered college and was seldom at home, the mother began to nag the patient to do things and was overprotective of her. Through therapeutic sessions, the patient discussed her family's history, and psychological interventions were implemented to encourage independence and support her in moving away from her family.

Follow-Up:
After leaving the hospital, the patient experienced some ictal vomiting during periods of high stress. Still, with empathic support and therapy, her condition improved drastically when she obtained job training and subsequently found employment. Her symptoms disappeared entirely within several months, and she has been in good health without medication for over seven years.

Recommendations:
The patient is advised to continue to avoid triggers that may cause stress and has been encouraged to maintain independence and lead a healthy lifestyle. Follow-up clinical and psychological assessments are recommended to monitor her condition and prevent any relapses or complications.

Recommendations:
The patient should keep avoiding things that cause stress. She has been encouraged to stay independent and live a healthy life. Regular checkups with doctors and therapists are suggested. These visits will help watch her condition and prevent her symptoms from coming back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 47-year-old male with known history of high blood pressure and migraine.

Medical History:
The patient presented with sudden right facio-brachial paresis and dysarthria with an NIHSS score of 6. The initial Emergency CT showed a recent hemorrhage in the left basal ganglia with some subarachnoid hemorrhage in the left insula and the cistern of the middle cerebral artery. CTA revealed a small distal lenticulostriate aneurysm which was confirmed by MRA and selective left carotid angiography. The patient was observed conservatively due to the distal location of the aneurysm.

Hospitalization:
During the patient's hospitalization, there was gradual improvement in clinical symptoms, with a persistent slight facial asymmetry. The team followed up the patient with yearly MRA.

Discharge:
The patient was observed and monitored for a sufficient period and they were determined to be stable. With consideration of the patient's condition and improvement, they were discharged and recommended to follow up with their physician regularly.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 47-year-old male with a history of high blood pressure and migraine headaches.

Medical History:
The patient came in with sudden weakness on the right side of the face and arm. The patient also had trouble speaking clearly. A brain scan in the emergency room showed bleeding in the left side of the brain. There was also some bleeding around the brain surface near the left side. Another scan found a small bulge in a blood vessel deep in the brain. More imaging tests confirmed this finding. The doctors decided not to do surgery because the bulge was in a hard to reach spot. Instead, they watched the patient closely.

Hospitalization:
While in the hospital, the patient slowly got better. A small amount of uneven movement on one side of the face remained. The care team planned to check the patient every year with brain scans.

Discharge:
The patient was watched and checked for enough time and was found to be in stable condition. Based on how well the patient was doing, the patient was sent home. The patient should see their doctor on a regular basis for checkups.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 64-year-old male
Medical History: Postthoracotomy pain syndrome

Presenting Symptoms:
- Severe pain (9/10 on NRS) over the anterior and posterior right chest area
- Burning, tingling, and current-like sensation around the thoracotomy scar at the T8-T9 level
- Despite high dosage of oral analgesics, significant pain persisted

Treatment:
- Right ESP block performed with 20 mL of 0.375% ropivacaine and 40 mg of triamcinolone at the T8 level
- Pain immediately relieved (0 on 10 on NRS) with sensory blockade from T5 to T12 on the right side
- NRS score monitored for 24 hours
- Discharged with tapered oral medications

Outcomes:
- Patient remained pain-free for 1.5 months without consumption of any other oral analgesic
- A second ESP block performed with 20 mL of 0.25% ropivacaine and 40 mg triamcinolone, which produced significant analgesia lasting 2 weeks
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 64-year-old male
Medical History: Long-term pain after chest surgery

Presenting Symptoms:
The patient had very bad pain, rated 9 out of 10, in the front and back of the right chest area. The patient felt burning, tingling, and electric-like feelings around the surgery scar between the 8th and 9th ribs. The pain stayed bad even with high doses of pain pills taken by mouth.

Treatment:
The patient received a nerve block injection on the right side of the back. The doctor injected 20 mL of numbing medicine called ropivacaine and 40 mg of a steroid called triamcinolone near the 8th rib. The pain went away right away, dropping to 0 out of 10. The numbing spread from the 5th to 12th rib area on the right side. The care team checked the pain level for 24 hours. The patient went home with a slow reduction in pain pills.

Outcomes:
The patient had no pain for 1.5 months and did not need any pain pills by mouth. The patient received a second nerve block injection with 20 mL of a weaker numbing medicine and 40 mg of steroid. This second injection gave good pain relief that lasted 2 weeks.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 71-year-old Japanese woman, presented with a 10-year history of left hip pain and 1-year history of left leg edema. Physical examination revealed limitation of the range of motion (ROM) of her left hip, with 80° flexion, 20° abduction, 15° adduction, 45° external rotation, and 5° internal rotation. Plain radiographs revealed advanced osteoarthritis (OA) secondary to developmental dysplasia of the left hip. Computed tomography (CT) images and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed compression of the femoral vessels by a mass that was absent 8 years ago on a previous radiograph. The mass was diagnosed as a capital drop secondary to OA and total hip arthroplasty (THA) with resection of the mass was performed. Following surgery, the left leg edema immediately diminished and the postoperative course was uneventful. At the latest follow-up, that is, 3 years after the surgery, the patient was able to walk without an aid and was independent in all activities of daily living, with no evidence of recurrence. 

Diagnoses:
Advanced osteoarthritis secondary to developmental dysplasia of the left hip

Procedures performed:
Total hip arthroplasty (THA) with resection of the mass, using a short tapered wedge stem with a 32mm ceramic head and a 48mm cementless cup with a polyethylene liner

Histopathological findings:
Peripheral cartilage layer, with mature trabecular bone present in the central zone. No malignant cells were observed

Discharge condition:
Stable

Functional status at discharge:
The patient was able to walk independently and had no evidence of recurrence.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 71-year-old Japanese woman. She had left hip pain for 10 years and swelling in her left leg for 1 year. A physical exam showed she could not move her left hip normally. Her hip could only bend to 80 degrees and had limited movement in other directions. X-rays showed severe joint damage in her left hip. This was caused by a hip problem she was born with. CT scans and MRI scans showed that blood vessels in her leg were being pressed by a growth. This growth was not there 8 years ago on an earlier X-ray. Doctors found that the growth was a piece of bone that had broken off due to the joint damage. The patient had surgery to replace her hip and remove the growth. Right after surgery, the swelling in her left leg went down. Her recovery went smoothly with no problems. At her last check-up, 3 years after surgery, the patient could walk without help. She could do all daily activities on her own. The growth had not come back.

Diagnoses:
Severe joint damage in the left hip caused by a hip problem present since birth.

Procedures performed:
Full hip replacement surgery with removal of the growth. Doctors used a short stem that fits into the thigh bone, a ceramic ball measuring 32mm, and a 48mm socket that attaches without cement. The socket has a plastic lining.

Tissue study findings:
The outer layer was made of cartilage. The center had normal bone tissue. No cancer cells were found.

Discharge condition:
Stable.

Functional status at discharge:
The patient was able to walk on her own and the growth had not come back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 27 years old
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Diagnosis:
Hemoptysis and shortness of breath for the past 3 days.

Hospital Course:
The patient was diagnosed with pulmonary embolism along with right lower-limb deep-venous thrombosis, which was confirmed by computed tomography scans of the chest. Warfarin was initiated, but the patient had been taking the drug erratically without proper monitoring. On physical examination, the patient had asymmetry in the girth of the lower limbs with the right limb girth being larger. The chest auscultation revealed bibasilar fine-end inspiratory crepitation with vesicular breath sounds of equal intensity. The initial hemoglobin level was 14.2 gm%, which decreased to 10.6 gm% over the course of 3 days. Moreover, the international normalized ratio (INR) was 7.2, and the patient had bilateral upper-zone infiltrates (right>left) and ground-glass opacities in the upper lobes of the lungs.

Treatment and Procedures:
The patient was immediately taken off Warfarin, and high-flow oxygen was administered. The patient was also given 10-mg intravenous Vitamin K injection followed by transfusion of four units of fresh frozen plasma. In a couple of days, the patient showed improvement and was weaned off the supplemental oxygen.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Anticoagulant (Warfarin)-induced Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (DAH).

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition with an advice of strict INR monitoring in further follow-ups.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was instructed to continue taking the prescribed medications as directed by their primary care physician. The patient was advised to follow-up with their primary care physician for close monitoring of their condition. Moreover, the patient was advised to follow a healthy diet and lifestyle to prevent the recurrence of any possible complications.

Follow-up:
The patient is recommended to follow up with their primary care physician for further treatment and monitoring of their condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 27 years old
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Diagnosis:
Coughing up blood and trouble breathing for the past 3 days.

Hospital Course:
The patient was found to have a blood clot in the lungs along with a blood clot in the deep veins of the right leg. This was confirmed by CT scans of the chest. The patient had been taking a blood thinner called Warfarin but was not taking it regularly or getting blood tests to check if it was working safely. During the exam, the right leg was larger than the left leg. The doctor heard crackling sounds in both lower parts of the lungs when listening with a stethoscope. The patient's blood count dropped from 14.2 to 10.6 over 3 days. Also, a blood test showed the blood was too thin at 7.2, which is much higher than normal. Scans showed cloudy patches in the upper parts of both lungs, worse on the right side.

Treatment and Procedures:
The patient was taken off Warfarin right away. The patient was given high amounts of oxygen through a mask. The patient received a Vitamin K shot through the vein to help the blood clot normally again. The patient also received four bags of plasma to replace clotting factors in the blood. After a couple of days, the patient got better and no longer needed extra oxygen.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Bleeding in the lungs caused by too much blood thinner medicine (Warfarin).

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient was told to get regular blood tests to check if blood thinner levels are safe.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was told to keep taking medicines as directed by their regular doctor. The patient was told to see their regular doctor for close watching of their condition. The patient was also told to eat healthy foods and live a healthy lifestyle to help prevent future problems.

Follow-up:
The patient should see their regular doctor for more treatment and watching of their condition.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 38-year-old male, was admitted to our hospital with complaints of fever and painful swelling in the right proximal leg. He had a history of travel to Malaysia and was suspected to have tuberculosis of the abdomen in a previous hospital, where he was started on antitubercular drugs. He also had an ongoing anorexia with weight loss. On presentation to our hospital, he was in septic shock and was diagnosed with acute osteomyelitis of the right proximal tibia with septic arthritis of the knee joint. He underwent arthrotomy and was found to have a Gram-negative, safety pin-shaped bacteria infection suggestive of B. pseudomallei. He was initiated on six weeks of ceftazidime and levofloxacin, followed by oral eradication therapy with cotrimoxazole and doxycycline for six months. He completed the treatment course without any adverse drug reactions.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted with fever and painful swelling in the right proximal leg. He was suspected to have tuberculosis of the abdomen in a previous hospital where he was started on antitubercular drugs. On presentation to our hospital, he was diagnosed with acute osteomyelitis of the right proximal tibia with septic arthritis of the knee joint, caused by B. pseudomallei infection. He underwent treatment with ceftazidime and levofloxacin for six weeks followed by oral eradication therapy with cotrimoxazole and doxycycline for six months. He completed the treatment course without any adverse drug reactions.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 38-year-old man. He came to our hospital with a fever and painful swelling in his upper right leg. He had recently traveled to Malaysia. At another hospital, doctors thought he might have tuberculosis in his belly. He was given medicine to treat tuberculosis at that time. He also had a poor appetite and was losing weight. When he arrived at our hospital, he had a serious infection that spread to his blood. Doctors found a bone infection in his upper right shinbone. He also had an infection in his knee joint. He had surgery to drain the infected knee joint. Doctors found a type of bacteria called B. pseudomallei causing the infection. He was given two strong antibiotics through an IV for six weeks. These were ceftazidime and levofloxacin. After that, he took two antibiotic pills for six months to clear the infection. These were cotrimoxazole and doxycycline. He finished all his medicine with no bad side effects.

Hospital Course Summary:

He was given two antibiotics through an IV for six weeks. These were ceftazidime and levofloxacin. Then he took two antibiotic pills for six months. These were cotrimoxazole and doxycycline. He finished all his medicine with no bad side effects.

input:
Discharge Summary

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 6-year-old female with mosaic Turner syndrome who was referred for cardiac evaluation secondary to her genetic condition. Physical exam revealed short stature, nevi on skin, and a broad-based gait. An echocardiogram was remarkable for severe apical and posterior wall left ventricular hypertrophy with a spongy appearance. The echocardiogram pictures raised concern for left ventricular noncompaction (LVNC), so a cardiac MRI was obtained. Cardiac MRI revealed increased trabeculations along the posterior apical aspect of left ventricle with noncompacted to compacted myocardial ratio >2.3 consistent with LVNC.

Medical Management:

The patient is being managed conservatively since she is clinically asymptomatic. Her follow-up visits with cardiology show preserved cardiac function (ejection fraction of 54% on the right side and 53% on the left side). She is receiving multidisciplinary care as an outpatient.

Plan:

The patient is recommended to receive continuous follow-up visits with cardiology to monitor her cardiac function. She is advised to continue with multidisciplinary care as an outpatient. 

Recommended Follow-up:

The patient is recommended to receive continuous follow-up visits with cardiology to monitor her cardiac function.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 6-year-old girl with a partial form of Turner syndrome. Turner syndrome is a genetic condition that affects girls. She was sent to a heart doctor because of her genetic condition. During the physical exam, doctors noticed she was shorter than average. She also had moles on her skin and walked with her feet wider apart than usual. A heart ultrasound showed that parts of her heart muscle were too thick. The lower left part of her heart had a spongy look. The heart ultrasound raised concern for a condition where the heart muscle does not form properly. Doctors ordered a special heart scan using magnets to get a better look. This scan showed extra finger-like tissue in parts of the lower left heart chamber. The results confirmed that the patient has a heart condition where the muscle did not fully develop before birth.

Medical Management:

The patient is being watched carefully without treatment because she has no symptoms. Her follow-up visits with the heart doctor show her heart is pumping well. The right side of her heart pumps out 54 percent of its blood with each beat. The left side pumps out 53 percent. She is seeing several different specialists as an outpatient.

Plan:

The patient should keep seeing her heart doctor regularly to check how well her heart is working. She should continue seeing her team of different specialists as an outpatient.

Recommended Follow-up:

The patient should keep seeing her heart doctor regularly to check how well her heart is working.

input:
[Header]
Discharge Summary

[Patient Information]
Patient Name: [insert patient name]
Medical Record Number: [insert assigned number]
Sex: Female 
Age: 66 

[Hospital Course and Reason for Admission]
The patient was admitted to our medical institution due to intermittent pin-prick pain occurring in the right chest. During a 4-month period, the patient underwent intermittent nerve blocks at a local clinic. The symptoms were not improved. Therefore, the patient was admitted to our medical institution.

[History and Physical Examination]
On physical examination, the patient complained of allodynia and hyperesthesia. At the time of admission, VAS score was 7-8/10 points. The patient had a concurrent presence of the scar tissue.

[Diagnostic Tests]
No diagnostic tests were performed.

[Treatment and Condition at Discharge]
For the treatment of the patient, a needle was placed in the multifidus muscle at the level of T5 and the tissue around the facet at the level of T5-6. Four weeks after the treatment, VAS score decreased from 7-8 points at the time of admission to 3 points. Two weeks later, VAS score further improved to 2 points. The patient's pain was managed with analgesics and anti-depressants.

[Discharge Medication and Instructions]
The patient was discharged with instructions to continue taking her analgesics and anti-depressants.

[Follow-up]
No follow-up was scheduled at discharge.

[Diagnoses]
The patient was diagnosed with neuralgia. 

[Prognosis]
The prognosis for the patient is good, but symptoms can reoccur. 

[Summary]
The patient was admitted for the management of neuralgia occurring in the right chest. Pain was managed with a multifidus muscle needle placement at T5 and tissue around the facet at T5-6. The patient made a good recovery and was discharged with instructions to continue taking pain medications.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Information
Patient Name: [insert patient name]
Medical Record Number: [insert assigned number]
Sex: Female
Age: 66

Hospital Course and Reason for Admission
The patient came to our hospital because of sharp, stabbing pain in the right side of the chest. The pain came and went. Over 4 months, the patient had nerve-numbing shots at another clinic. These shots did not help. So the patient came to our hospital for more care.

History and Physical Examination
During the exam, the patient had pain from light touch that should not hurt. The patient also felt more pain than normal from things that usually cause only mild pain. At the time of coming to the hospital, the pain level was 7-8 out of 10. The patient also had scar tissue in the area.

Diagnostic Tests
No tests were done.

Treatment and Condition at Discharge
To treat the patient, a needle was placed in a deep back muscle near the spine at the upper back level. The needle was also placed in tissue near the spine joints in the same area. Four weeks after treatment, the pain level dropped from 7-8 out of 10 to 3 out of 10. Two weeks later, the pain level got even better at 2 out of 10. The patient took pain medicine and mood medicine to help with the pain.

Discharge Medication and Instructions
The patient was told to keep taking the pain medicine and mood medicine at home.

Follow-up
No follow-up visit was set up when the patient left the hospital.

Diagnoses
The patient was found to have nerve pain.

Prognosis
The patient should do well, but the pain may come back.

Summary
The patient came to the hospital for nerve pain in the right side of the chest. The pain was treated with needle placement in a deep back muscle and near spine joints in the upper back. The patient got much better and went home with instructions to keep taking pain medicine.

Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: Not disclosed
DOB: Not disclosed
Sex: Female
Admission Date: Not disclosed
Discharge Date: Not disclosed
Admitting Diagnosis: Acute stroke resulting in dysarthria and left-sided hemiparesis.

Hospital Course:
A 78-year-old female with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and obesity was admitted to our tertiary care hospital following an acute stroke, which resulted in dysarthria and left-sided hemiparesis. Multiple complications were encountered during the hospital course including a code blue due to aspiration pneumonia and prolonged hospitalization. After failing multiple swallow studies, a decision was made to insert a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube for long-term enteral nutrition. A fluoroscopic evaluation identified an air-distended colonic loop, which caused the procedure to be aborted. The gastrointestinal team recommended holding clopidogrel for 5 days before proceeding with endoscopic PEG placement. The procedure was successful, and the patient tolerated tube feeds without any complications. Post-procedure imaging showed that the gastrostomy tube had traversed the lateral margin of hepatic segment 3 with an adjacent hematoma measuring 2 × 2 × 4 cm. The patient tolerated the PEG tube well and was safely discharged to a nursing home for further care.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Acute stroke resulting in dysarthria and left-sided hemiparesis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity, aspiration pneumonia, and hematoma in hepatic segment 3.

Discharge Medications:
None specified.

Follow-up:
The patient expired on day 3 in the nursing home, and the cause of death remains un-established as the patient's family denied completion of an autopsy.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: Not disclosed
DOB: Not disclosed
Sex: Female
Admission Date: Not disclosed
Discharge Date: Not disclosed
Admitting Diagnosis: Sudden stroke causing slurred speech and weakness on the left side of the body.

Hospital Course:
A 78-year-old female with diabetes, high blood pressure, and obesity was admitted to our hospital after a sudden stroke. The stroke caused slurred speech and weakness on the left side of her body. Several problems came up during her hospital stay. She had a medical emergency when food or liquid entered her lungs and caused a serious lung infection. This led to a long hospital stay. The patient failed several swallowing tests. The care team decided to place a feeding tube through the skin into her stomach for long-term nutrition. An X-ray exam found a swollen loop of the large intestine filled with air. This caused the team to stop the procedure. The stomach and intestine team said to stop the blood-thinning medicine for 5 days before trying the feeding tube again. The feeding tube placement worked well. The patient handled tube feedings without any problems. Images taken after the procedure showed the feeding tube had passed through part of the liver. There was also a small collection of blood nearby measuring about 2 by 2 by 4 centimeters. The patient did well with the feeding tube. She was safely sent to a nursing home for more care.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Sudden stroke causing slurred speech and weakness on the left side of the body, diabetes, high blood pressure, obesity, lung infection from food or liquid entering the lungs, and a blood collection in part of the liver.

Discharge Medications:
None specified.

Follow-up:
The patient died on day 3 in the nursing home. The cause of death is not known. The family chose not to have an exam of the body after death.

input:
Hospital Course:
A 66-year-old female patient was admitted to the Department of Periodontics, M. S. Ramaiah Dental College and Hospital, Bangalore, with the complaint of burning sensation in the gums and bleeding gums for the past one month.

Medical History:
The patient had no relevant drug history or significant medical history. She had not received any dental treatment before.

Examination Findings:
On extra oral examination, the patient had some obvious skin lesions on the scalp and a hyperkeratotic plaque behind her left ear. Intra oral examination of gingiva revealed reddish marginal, attached gingiva and interdental papilla in both maxillary and mandibular anterior region with desquamation of epithelium present in certain regions.

Diagnosis:
Based on the clinical and histopathologic features, the lesion was suggestive of discoid lupus erythematosus.

Treatment:
Hand scaling was performed as gently as possible in order to minimize tissue disruption. Soft tooth brush and mouth rise (chlorhexidine 0.12%) was recommended. Triamcinolone acteonide oral paste was advised to apply two to three times per day.

Follow-up:
The patient was recalled for follow-up and maintenance.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
A 66-year-old female patient came to the gum care department at M. S. Ramaiah Dental College and Hospital in Bangalore. She had a burning feeling in her gums and bleeding gums for the past month.

Medical History:
The patient was not taking any medicines and had no major health problems. She had never had dental treatment before.

Examination Findings:
The dentist looked at the outside of the patient's face and body. The patient had some skin patches on her scalp. She also had a thick, rough patch of skin behind her left ear. The dentist then looked inside the patient's mouth. The gums around the upper and lower front teeth were red. This included the gums at the edges, the gums attached to the teeth, and the gums between the teeth. In some areas, the top layer of gum tissue was peeling off.

Diagnosis:
Based on what the dentist saw and lab test results, the patient likely has discoid lupus erythematosus. This is a condition where the body's defense system attacks its own skin and gum tissue.

Treatment:
The dentist gently cleaned the teeth to remove buildup. This was done carefully to avoid hurting the gums. The patient was told to use a soft toothbrush and rinse with chlorhexidine 0.12 percent mouthwash. The patient was also told to apply triamcinolone acetonide paste inside the mouth two to three times per day. This paste helps reduce soreness and swelling.

Follow-up:
The patient was asked to come back for check-up visits and ongoing care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 39-year-old woman diagnosed with plasmablastic IgA lambda multiple myeloma

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our facility in January 2011 with a diagnosis of plasmablastic IgA lambda multiple myeloma, stage III (International Staging System). She underwent four cycles of PAD chemotherapy (bortezomib, adriamycin, and dexamethasone) and zoledronic acid before receiving an autologous peripheral blood stem cell transplant, conditioned with melphalan (200 mg/m2), in August 2011. The patient experienced a symptomatic relapse with a T7 vertebral collapse in March 2013, after which treatment with lenalidomide and dexamethasone was initiated. Due to a loss of response after four cycles of treatment, the patient received VCD chemotherapy (bortezomib, cyclophosphamide, and dexamethasone), achieving a second complete remission. In December 2013, a fully matched sibling hematopoietic stem cell transplantation, after reduced-intensity conditioning regimen, was performed to consolidate the excellent response. The patient experienced another bone marrow relapse, along with a plasmacytoma of tibia in April 2014, following which she was retreated with VCD. This regimen was stopped after two cycles of treatment due to bortezomib-related peripheral neuropathy. The patient was then placed on VBCMP/VBAD chemotherapy (vincristine, carmustine, cyclophosphamide, melphalan, prednisone/vincristine, carmustine, doxorubicin, and high-dose dexamethasone), achieving a third complete remission.

In April 2015, the patient presented with pericardial effusion and echocardiographic signs of cardiac tamponade, which were resolved through pericardiocentesis. In May 2015, the patient presented with massive left-side pleural effusion. Two thoracocentesis revealed plasmablastic cells. Despite this EMM relapse, multiple myeloma was not detected in blood, urine, or bone marrow. The patient was then started on carfilzomib plus dexamethasone, which resulted in a sudden improvement in her general condition and was discharged without pleural effusion.

Follow-Up Recommendations:

The patient is currently receiving the 18th cycle of treatment and remains in complete remission 19 months after the EMM was detected. Further follow-up appointments will be scheduled to monitor the patient's progress.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 39-year-old woman with a blood cancer called multiple myeloma, which affects certain cells in the bone marrow

Hospital Course:

The patient came to our hospital in January 2011 with multiple myeloma at an advanced stage. She received four rounds of cancer treatment with three drugs called bortezomib, adriamycin, and dexamethasone. She also got a bone-strengthening medicine called zoledronic acid. In August 2011, the patient had a transplant using her own stem cells collected from her blood. Before this transplant, she received a strong chemotherapy drug called melphalan to prepare her body. In March 2013, the cancer came back and caused one of her spine bones to collapse. The patient then started treatment with two different drugs called lenalidomide and dexamethasone. After four rounds, this treatment stopped working well. The patient then received a different set of three drugs called bortezomib, cyclophosphamide, and dexamethasone. This treatment worked and the cancer went away completely for the second time. In December 2013, the patient received stem cells from a sibling whose cells were a perfect match. This transplant used a gentler preparation treatment to support her good response. In April 2014, the cancer returned in the bone marrow and also formed a tumor in the shin bone. The patient again received bortezomib, cyclophosphamide, and dexamethasone. This treatment was stopped after two rounds because bortezomib caused nerve damage in the hands and feet. The patient then started a different combination of cancer drugs including vincristine, carmustine, cyclophosphamide, melphalan, prednisone, and doxorubicin with high-dose dexamethasone. This treatment worked and the cancer went away completely for the third time.

In April 2015, the patient had fluid buildup around the heart that was pressing on it dangerously. Doctors drained the fluid with a needle and this problem went away. In May 2015, the patient had a large amount of fluid buildup around the left lung. Doctors drained this fluid twice and found cancer cells in it. Even though the cancer had spread to this new area, tests of the blood, urine, and bone marrow showed no signs of cancer. The patient then started treatment with two drugs called carfilzomib and dexamethasone. The patient quickly felt much better and left the hospital with no more fluid around the lung.

Follow-Up Recommendations:

The patient is now on the 18th round of treatment and the cancer remains gone 19 months after it spread outside the bone marrow. The patient will have more appointments scheduled to check on her health.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: XYZ
Admission Date: MM/DD/YYYY
Discharge Date: MM/DD/YYYY
Admitting Physician: Dr. ABC
Attending Physician: Dr. DEF

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient was admitted to our hospital with the suspicion of MIS-C and myocardial injury. The patient presented with fever, headache, malaise, diarrhea, and dyspnea. The patient had no previous signs of acute infection and had contact with SARS-CoV-2 positive persons in the past. On admission, the patient was febrile, hypotensive, and tachycardic, with a polymorphic rash, palmar erythema, and bilateral bulbar non-exudative conjunctivitis and suffusions. Laboratory analysis showed elevated cTnI value. ECG was normal, but prolonged QTc was observed during the hospital stay. X-ray showed sporadic bilateral pulmonary fibrosis. A chest CT revealed mild hypokinesis of LV and dilated coronary arteries. SARS-CoV-2 was not detected in the nasopharyngeal swab. However, SARS-CoV-2-specific neutralizing IgG antibody was detected in the blood sample using the enzyme-immuno-essay technique (ELISA). The patient was treated with antibiotics, corticosteroids, and IVIG. Dopamine treatment was weaned off after 24 hours. The patient became afebrile on day 2 after the treatment. TTE after IVIG treatment showed normalization of systolic function and diameter of coronary arteries.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
The patient was diagnosed with suspected MIS-C with myocardial injury.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient is advised to follow up with the primary care physician and continue any prescribed medication. The patient is advised to maintain social distancing and practice good hand hygiene to prevent the spread of SARS-CoV-2.

FOLLOW-UP CARE:
The patient is recommended to have a follow-up TTE to monitor any changes in systolic function and diameter of coronary arteries. The patient can return to normal physical activity after clearance from the primary care physician.

SIGNED:
Dr. DEF
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: XYZ
Admission Date: MM/DD/YYYY
Discharge Date: MM/DD/YYYY
Admitting Physician: Dr. ABC
Attending Physician: Dr. DEF

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came to the hospital with a suspected illness called MIS-C and heart muscle damage. MIS-C is a serious condition where different body parts become swollen after a COVID-19 infection. The patient had fever, headache, tiredness, loose stools, and trouble breathing. The patient had no recent signs of infection but had been near people with COVID-19. When the patient arrived, the patient had a fever, low blood pressure, and a fast heartbeat. The patient also had a changing skin rash, red palms, and red eyes without discharge. Blood tests showed high levels of a protein that means the heart was stressed. The heart rhythm test was normal, but later showed a slightly slow electrical signal. A chest X-ray showed some scarring in both lungs. A detailed scan of the chest showed the left side of the heart was pumping weakly and the heart blood vessels were wider than normal. A nose swab test did not find the COVID-19 virus. But a blood test found that the body had made protective proteins against COVID-19. The patient was treated with medicines to fight infection, reduce swelling, and help the immune system. A medicine to help blood pressure was stopped after 24 hours. The patient's fever went away on day 2 of treatment. A heart ultrasound after treatment showed the heart was pumping normally and the heart blood vessels were back to normal size.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
The patient was diagnosed with suspected MIS-C with heart muscle damage.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should see the regular doctor and keep taking any medicines that were prescribed. The patient should stay away from others who are sick and wash hands often to avoid spreading COVID-19.

FOLLOW-UP CARE:
The patient should have another heart ultrasound to check on the heart's pumping and the size of the heart blood vessels. The patient can go back to normal exercise after the regular doctor says it is safe.

SIGNED:
Dr. DEF

input:
Discharge Summary:

Admitting Diagnosis: Suspected Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 36-year-old man with a history of bronchial asthma and smoking, presented with sudden onset right upper limb weakness which spread to his lower limb along with slurring of speech and altered consciousness. He was initially admitted to an outside facility where CT scan and carotid ultrasound came out to be normal. His CBC with differential showed low hemoglobin and platelets with elevated bilirubin, and peripheral blood smear (PBS) indicated fragmented red blood cells. TTP was suspected, and fresh frozen plasma (FFP) infusion started. On transfer to our facility, he received PLEX and pulse steroids for 18 sessions which resulted in the stabilization of the patient's LDH and platelet count. During his hospital stay, he was also diagnosed with eosinophilia. A bone marrow aspirate and core biopsy showed trilineage hematopoiesis with 2% blasts and 30% eosinophils. FISH for FIP1L1-PDGFRA (4q12) rearrangement was positive.
After 18 sessions of PLEX, the patient's LDH and platelet counts normalized. However, as weaning off PLEX was attempted, schistocytes re-emerged with a subsequent drop in platelet count. Consequently, a four-dose course of rituximab and Imatinib 100 mg daily were started. Thirty sessions of PLEX were needed to treat the TTP, and the last session was 4 days after the initiation of Imatinib. After more than 28 months of follow-up, the patient's eosinophil count has normalized, and there has been no recurrence of TTP.

Discharge Diagnosis: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura and Eosinophilia

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted with suspected TTP, and after receiving 18 sessions of PLEX and methylprednisolone, his platelet count and LDH stabilized. The patient was also found to have eosinophilia with trilineage hematopoiesis, which was treated with a four-dose course of rituximab and Imatinib 100 mg daily. After 30 sessions of PLEX, the TTP was resolved, and the patient's eosinophil count had normalized. After more than 28 months of follow-up, there has been no recurrence of TTP.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admitting Diagnosis: Suspected TTP, a rare blood disorder that causes small blood clots to form throughout the body

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 36-year-old man with a history of asthma and smoking. He came to the hospital with sudden weakness in his right arm. The weakness spread to his leg. He also had slurred speech and confusion. He was first seen at another hospital. A brain scan and neck blood vessel test were normal. Blood tests showed low red blood cells and low platelets. A liver waste product was high. A blood sample showed broken red blood cells. Doctors thought he might have TTP. He was given a blood product called frozen plasma. He was then moved to our hospital. He received 18 treatments to filter and clean his blood. He also got high-dose steroids through his vein. These treatments helped his platelet count and a blood waste product return to normal levels. During his stay, doctors found he had too many of a type of white blood cell. A sample of bone marrow was taken and tested. The test showed his bone marrow was making blood cells. It also showed high levels of one type of white blood cell. A gene test came back positive for a certain gene change. After 18 blood filtering treatments, his blood levels became normal. But when doctors tried to stop the treatments, broken red blood cells came back. His platelet count dropped again. So doctors started two new medicines. One was given in four doses. The other was Imatinib, 100 mg taken daily by mouth. He needed 30 blood filtering treatments in total to treat the TTP. The last treatment was 4 days after starting Imatinib. After more than 28 months of checkups, his white blood cell count is normal. The TTP has not come back.

Discharge Diagnosis: TTP, a rare blood clotting disorder, and too many of one type of white blood cell

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came in with suspected TTP. After 18 blood filtering treatments and high-dose steroids, his platelet count and blood levels got better. Doctors also found he had too many white blood cells of one type. This was treated with two medicines. One was given in four doses. The other was Imatinib, 100 mg daily. After 30 blood filtering treatments, the TTP was gone. His white blood cell count became normal. After more than 28 months of checkups, the TTP has not come back.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Chief Complaint: Dyspnea, palpitation, and shortness of breath for two years

Medical History: Pulmonary artery hypertension, received anticoagulation therapy and sildenafil

Assessment and Plan: Upon admission, a physical examination was conducted, revealing grade III systolic murmurs over the left lower sternal border and a wide fixed splitting S2. A thoracic computed tomography scan showed a huge thrombus in the aneurysmal dilatation of the proximal pulmonary arteries. An electrocardiogram revealed a right axis deviation and bi-ventricular hypertrophy with a strain pattern. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed profound pulmonary hypertension with right ventricular hypertrophy and dysfunction. A right heart catheter was also done. Pulmonary thrombosis with an ASD and Eisenmenger syndrome was diagnosed. A heart-lung transplant was arranged for the patient. 

Laboratory Examinations: Normal blood cell counts, with a mildly elevated D-dimer level

Imaging Studies: Chest radiography revealed marked enlargement and a lobulated contour to the hilar shadows of bilateral lungs

Other Risk Assessment Studies: Surveyed possible causes of her pro-coagulant status, including hepatitis serum marker, anti-human immunodeficiency virus antibody, the Venereal Disease Research Laboratory test, anti-double stranded deoxyribonucleic acid antibodies, anti-phospholipid IgG/IgM, anti-beta 2 glycoprotein IgG/IgM, anti-nuclear antibody, lupus anticoagulant, protein C, protein S, anti-thrombin III, and homocysteine level without positive findings. 

Diagnosis: Pulmonary thrombosis with an ASD and Eisenmenger syndrome

Discussion and Follow-Up: Due to the high-risk nature of the recommended surgery (thrombectomy with aneurysm repair), a heart-lung transplant was determined to be the most beneficial course of action. The patient was referred for the transplant.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Chief Complaint: Trouble breathing, feeling the heart beat fast, and being short of breath for two years

Medical History: High blood pressure in the lungs, treated with blood thinners and sildenafil

Assessment and Plan: When the patient arrived, doctors did a physical exam. They heard abnormal heart sounds on the left side of the chest. The second heart sound was also not normal. A scan of the chest showed a large blood clot in the widened blood vessels of the lungs. A heart tracing test showed the heart was working harder than normal on both sides. An ultrasound of the heart showed very high blood pressure in the lungs. The right side of the heart was thickened and not working well. A tube was also placed into the heart to take measurements. Doctors found blood clots in the lungs, a hole in the heart wall, and a serious condition where blood flows the wrong way through the heart. A heart and lung transplant was planned for the patient.

Laboratory Examinations: Blood cell counts were normal. A test for blood clots was slightly higher than normal.

Imaging Studies: A chest x-ray showed the blood vessels near the center of both lungs were much larger than normal and had an uneven shape.

Other Risk Assessment Studies: Doctors tested for many possible causes of blood clots. These tests included checks for liver disease, HIV, sexually transmitted infections, and problems with the immune system. They also tested for conditions that make the blood clot too easily. All of these tests came back normal.

Diagnosis: Blood clots in the lungs with a hole in the heart wall and a serious condition where blood flows the wrong way through the heart

Discussion and Follow-Up: Surgery to remove the blood clots and fix the widened blood vessels would be very risky. Doctors decided a heart and lung transplant would be the best treatment. The patient was sent to another team for the transplant.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 36-year-old male with situs viscerum inversus and dextrocardia, presented to the hospital with endocarditis in May 2016. He had previously undergone permanent VVI pacemaker implantation in 1998 due to complete AV block, with battery replacement in 2006 and dual chamber pacemaker implantation in 2007. The patient had three surgical pocket revisions due to pocket decubitus. In May 2016, he underwent transvenous lead extraction (TLE) of the atrial lead and extraction of the can due to endocarditis. As the patient was pacemaker-dependent, a temporary pacemaker was placed via the left femoral vein before the Micra implantation. Electrical measurements were tested multiple times in different positions before the successful placement of the Micra via the right femoral vein. However, the Micra delivery system was almost completely obstructed by clots after seven attempts, requiring a replacement with a new system. 

Discharge Summary:
The patient with situs viscerum inversus and dextrocardia was admitted to the hospital for transvenous lead extraction and Micra implantation on May 2016. The procedure lasted 119 minutes with a fluoroscopic duration of 35 minutes. After the successful placement of the Micra in the anteroseptal region, the patient experienced no adverse events during a 12-month follow-up period. The electrical measurements were stable, confirmed by chest X-ray, and were within normal range.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 36-year-old male. He was born with his organs on the opposite side of his body, including his heart on the right side instead of the left. He came to the hospital in May 2016 with an infection in his heart. In 1998, he had a pacemaker put in because his heart could not send electrical signals properly. The pacemaker battery was replaced in 2006. A new pacemaker with two parts was put in during 2007. The patient had three surgeries to fix problems with the pacemaker pocket under his skin. In May 2016, doctors removed one of the pacemaker wires and the pacemaker device because of the heart infection. The patient needs a pacemaker to keep his heart beating. So doctors placed a temporary pacemaker through a vein in his left leg before putting in the new tiny pacemaker called Micra. Doctors tested the electrical signals many times in different spots before placing the Micra through a vein in his right leg. Blood clots almost completely blocked the Micra delivery tool after seven tries. Doctors had to use a new delivery tool to finish the procedure.

Discharge Summary:
The patient with reversed organs and a heart on the right side came to the hospital in May 2016. He had the old pacemaker parts removed and a new tiny pacemaker called Micra put in. The procedure took 119 minutes total. Doctors used X-ray imaging for 35 minutes during the procedure. The Micra was placed successfully in the front wall of the heart. The patient had no problems during the 12 months after the procedure. The electrical readings stayed steady and normal. A chest X-ray confirmed everything looked good.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient: [Name], [Age]-year-old female

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the emergency department for uremic symptoms of nausea and general weakness. A work-up revealed increased blood urea nitrogen and serum creatinine levels, as well as a heterogeneously enhancing mass in the upper pole of the right kidney. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging confirmed the diagnosis of renal cell carcinoma, stage T1aN0. Open radical nephrectomy was performed, and a diagnosis of anastomosing hemangioma was made based on the histological report. The patient underwent a living-donor kidney transplantation and achieved good renal function post-transplantation.

Diagnosis:
• Hypertension
• Severe osteoporosis
• Chronic kidney disease due to lupus nephritis
• Renal cell carcinoma, T1aN0
• Anastomosing hemangioma

Treatment:
• Emergent hemodialysis
• Open radical nephrectomy
• Living-donor kidney transplantation

Follow-Up:
The patient's renal function improved post-transplantation, with no signs of tumor recurrence.

Recommendations:
• Follow-up with transplant surgeon and urologist for regular monitoring
• Continue management of hypertension and severe osteoporosis
• Regular monitoring of chronic kidney disease

Signed,
[Attending Physician's Name], MD
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient: [Name], [Age]-year-old female

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room with nausea and general weakness caused by waste buildup in the blood. Tests showed high levels of waste products in the blood that the kidneys normally remove. Scans also found a mass in the upper part of the right kidney. Special imaging scans confirmed the mass was an early-stage kidney cancer that had not spread. Doctors removed the entire right kidney through surgery. Lab tests of the removed tissue showed a rare, non-cancerous blood vessel tumor. The patient then received a kidney donated by a living person. After the transplant, the new kidney worked well.

Diagnosis:
High blood pressure
Severe bone thinning and weakening
Long-term kidney disease caused by an immune system attack on the kidneys
Early-stage kidney cancer that had not spread
A rare, non-cancerous blood vessel tumor in the kidney

Treatment:
Emergency blood filtering treatment to remove waste from the blood
Surgery to remove the entire right kidney
Kidney transplant from a living donor

Follow-Up:
The patient's kidney function got better after the transplant. There are no signs that the cancer has come back.

Recommendations:
See the transplant surgeon and kidney cancer doctor regularly for checkups
Keep treating high blood pressure and severe bone thinning
Get regular tests to check how well the new kidney is working

Signed,
[Attending Physician's Name], MD
