input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Record Number: [Redacted]
Sex: Male   Age: 47
Date of Admission: [Redacted]   Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Length of Stay: [Redacted]

Admission Diagnosis:
The patient was admitted for malaise, fever, nausea, vomiting and left chest pain with oppressive radiation toward the neck.

Hospital Course:
Upon admission, the patient was previously treated with antibiotics and underwent a left thoracostomy due to pleural effusion. The chest radiograph depicted a pleural effusion in the lung base. On chest auscultation and percussion, air entry was reduced and dullness was perceived in the left lung base. Laboratory tests revealed an elevated lactate dehydrogenase level and the pleural fluid analysis indicated the presence of Klebsiella pneumoniae, chylous effusion and helminth larvae. A chest CT scan showed mediastinal adenomegaly, loculated pleural effusion in the left side, slight pericardial effusion and cardiomegaly. The pleural effusion was classified as exudate based on the laboratory findings.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with pleural effusion, chylous effusion and helminth larvae infection.

Discharge Medications:
Not applicable

Follow-up Appointments:
The patient is advised to follow up with the physician recommended by the hospital.

Instructions to Patient:
The patient is advised to continue monitoring his symptoms and follow-up with proper medical care. 

Summary:
The patient was admitted to our hospital due to malaise, fever, nausea, vomiting, and left chest pain that was oppressive and radiated to his neck. The patient underwent a left thoracostomy after a chest radiograph showed pleural effusion in the lung base. Laboratory tests revealed that the pleural effusion was classified as exudate and the pleural fluid analysis was positive for chylous effusion and helminth larvae. A chest CT scan confirmed mediastinal adenomegaly and loculated pleural effusion on the left, cardiomegaly, and slight pericardial effusion. The patient was treated surgically and medically and is advised to follow-up with a physician.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Record Number: [Redacted]
Sex: Male   Age: 47
Date of Admission: [Redacted]   Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Length of Stay: [Redacted]

Admission Diagnosis:
The patient was admitted for feeling unwell, fever, nausea, vomiting and left chest pain. The pain felt like pressure and spread toward the neck.

Hospital Course:
Upon admission, the patient had already been treated with antibiotics. The patient also had a tube placed in the left side of the chest to drain fluid buildup around the lung. A chest x-ray showed fluid around the base of the left lung. When the doctor listened to and tapped on the chest, less air was moving and there was a dull sound at the left lung base. Blood tests showed high levels of an enzyme that can signal tissue damage. Testing of the fluid found a type of bacteria called Klebsiella pneumoniae, a milky fluid from the lymph system, and parasitic worm young. A detailed scan of the chest showed swollen lymph nodes in the chest, pockets of fluid around the left lung, a small amount of fluid around the heart, and an enlarged heart. Lab results showed the fluid was caused by infection or disease rather than pressure buildup.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with fluid around the lung, milky fluid from the lymph system, and parasitic worm infection.

Discharge Medications:
Not applicable

Follow-up Appointments:
The patient is advised to follow up with the doctor recommended by the hospital.

Instructions to Patient:
The patient is advised to keep watching his symptoms and follow up with proper medical care.

Summary:

A detailed chest scan confirmed swollen lymph nodes in the chest, pockets of fluid around the left lung, an enlarged heart, and a small amount of fluid around the heart. The patient was treated with surgery and medicine and is advised to follow up with a doctor.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Not provided
Date of Admission: Not provided
Date of Discharge: Not provided

Hospital Course Summary:

Admission: The patient presented with two weeks of flank pain and four days of cola-colored urine. On physical exam, he had a regular rate and rhythm with a crescendo-decrescendo murmur heard best over the left sternal border. He had severe, left CVA tenderness and bilateral, basilar crackles on auscultation. Initial laboratory data showed low white blood cell count, low hemoglobin, low platelet count, high creatinine levels, and abnormal urinalysis.

Diagnosis: The patient was diagnosed with splenic infarct, bicuspid aortic valve with vegetation, and negative culture endocarditis. A renal biopsy revealed immune complex GN.

Treatment: The patient received 6 weeks of antibiotic therapy with doxycycline and rifampin, and underwent a valve replacement surgery. During the course of treatment, the patient showed improvement in flank pain, decreased RBC count in urine, and a decrease in creatinine levels.

Prognosis: The patient's condition improved during the course of treatment. Repeat proteinase-3 antibodies remained elevated despite antibiotic therapy.

Follow-up: Follow-up with a primary care physician and additional testing as needed is recommended.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Not provided
Date of Admission: Not provided
Date of Discharge: Not provided

Hospital Course Summary:

Admission: The patient came to the hospital with two weeks of pain in the side of the back and four days of dark brown urine. During the physical exam, the heart had a normal beat with an extra heart sound heard best on the left side of the chest. The patient had strong pain when the doctor pressed on the left side of the back near the kidney. The doctor also heard crackling sounds in the lower parts of both lungs. First blood tests showed low white blood cell count, low red blood cell count, and low platelet count. The tests also showed high creatinine levels, which means the kidneys were not working well. The urine test was not normal.

Diagnosis: The patient was found to have a blocked blood vessel in the spleen, which damaged part of the spleen. The patient also had a heart valve that formed with two flaps instead of three. This valve had a growth on it caused by an infection. The infection did not show up in blood tests that look for germs. A small tissue sample was taken from the kidney. It showed the immune system was causing swelling and damage in the kidney.

Treatment: The patient took two antibiotics called doxycycline and rifampin for 6 weeks. The patient also had surgery to replace the damaged heart valve. During treatment, the side pain got better. There were fewer red blood cells in the urine. The creatinine levels went down, which means the kidneys were working better.

Prognosis: The patient got better during treatment. However, a blood test for a certain protein linked to the immune system stayed high even after taking antibiotics.

Follow-up: The patient should see a regular doctor for checkups. More tests may be needed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

This is a discharge summary for a 50-year-old female patient who presented to our department with an intraoral swelling at the left mandible for six months. The patient underwent surgical excision and reconstruction using a submental island flap, under general anesthesia.

History of Present Illness: The patient reported to our department with swelling and pain in the left mandible region for six months, which grew rapidly after the extraction of lower left back teeth. The patient had no history of fever or difficulty swallowing.

Physical Examination: On extraoral examination, facial asymmetry was noted in the lower left facial region, but there was no evidence of any regional lymphadenopathy. Intraoral clinical evaluation revealed the presence of an expansile mass with anteroposterior as well as buccolingual expansion, extending from the region of the first premolar up to retromolar area, measuring approximately 6x4 cm. The overlying mucosal colour and texture appeared normal.

Diagnostic Tests: A radiographic orthopantomogram revealed a well-defined radiolucent lesion extending from the lower left first premolar to retromolar area. Computed tomography (CT) scans confirmed the presence of aggressive bony resorption of the lower left jaw starting from the premolar region and extending well into the mandibular ramus. Histopathological analysis of a biopsy specimen confirmed an aggressive CGCG.

Hospital Course: The patient underwent surgical excision and reconstruction using a submental island flap, under general anesthesia. The surgical procedure involved extraoral and intraoral approach to excise the lesion completely and a submental island flap for reconstruction. The patient was discharged without any complications.

Follow-up: In subsequent follow-ups, until one year, no evidence of recurrence was observed clinically as well as radiographically. The donor site healed uneventfully with minimal scars, which was present away from aesthetically visible regions of the face.

Summary: A 50-year-old female patient presented with an intraoral swelling at the left mandible for six months and was diagnosed with an aggressive CGCG. The patient underwent surgical excision and reconstruction using a submental island flap, under general anesthesia. The patient was discharged without any complications, and subsequent follow-ups showed no evidence of recurrence.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

This is a discharge summary for a 50-year-old female patient who came to our department with swelling inside the mouth on the left side of the lower jaw for six months. The patient had surgery to remove the growth and rebuild the area using skin and tissue from under the chin. The patient was asleep under general anesthesia during the procedure.

History of Present Illness: The patient came to our department with swelling and pain in the left lower jaw area for six months. The swelling grew quickly after the patient had back teeth removed on the lower left side. The patient did not have a fever or trouble swallowing.

Physical Examination: When looking at the outside of the face, the lower left side looked uneven compared to the right side. There were no swollen lymph nodes in the area. When looking inside the mouth, there was a growing mass that spread from front to back and side to side. It went from the area of the first small back tooth to the area behind the last molar. The mass measured about 6 by 4 centimeters. The skin covering the mass looked normal in color and feel.

Diagnostic Tests: A panoramic jaw x-ray showed a clear dark area going from the lower left first small back tooth to the area behind the last molar. CT scans, which are detailed pictures of the inside of the body, showed the growth was breaking down bone in the lower left jaw. This bone loss went from the small back teeth area into the back part of the jawbone. A small tissue sample was taken and looked at under a microscope. This test confirmed the growth was an aggressive central giant cell granuloma, which is a type of noncancerous but fast-growing jaw tumor.

Hospital Course: The patient had surgery to remove the growth and rebuild the area using skin and tissue from under the chin. The surgery was done from both outside and inside the mouth to remove all of the growth. The patient went home without any problems.

Follow-up: At checkup visits over the next year, there were no signs that the growth came back. This was checked by looking in the mouth and taking x-rays. The area under the chin where tissue was taken healed well with very little scarring. The small scar is not in a part of the face that people easily see.

Summary: A 50-year-old female patient came in with swelling inside the mouth on the left side of the lower jaw for six months. The patient was found to have an aggressive central giant cell granuloma, which is a type of noncancerous but fast-growing jaw tumor. The patient had surgery to remove the growth and rebuild the area using skin and tissue from under the chin. The patient was asleep under general anesthesia during the procedure. The patient went home without any problems. Checkup visits over the next year showed no signs that the growth came back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient was admitted with complaints of high-grade fever and left-sided pleuritic chest pain of 3-week duration associated with anorexia and significant weight loss of 6 kg in the last 1 month. The patient's hemogram showed polymorphonuclear leukocytosis and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 70 mm/h. He had a fasting blood sugar value of 236 mg/dL and a postprandial value of 300 mg/dL with a urine sugar of 2%. A chest X-ray showed right upper zone fibrosis, and an ultrasound of the abdomen revealed a collection of heterogeneous echogenicity near the inferior pole of the spleen suggestive of a perisplenic abscess. 

After several diagnostic tests, the patient was diagnosed with melioidosis, a bacterial infection caused by Burkholderia pseudomallei. He was treated with 2 weeks of parenteral ceftazidime (2 g t.i.d.) followed by oral co-trimoxazole (160/800 mg t.i.d.) for 12 weeks. The patient's fever and arthritis subsided, and follow-up ultrasound showed resolution of the abscess. Chest X-ray showed improvement in the right upper lobe lesion. 

The patient's vital signs were stable upon discharge, and there were no further complaints at the time of discharge. His condition has significantly improved, and he was asked to attend regular follow-up appointments with his primary care physician. His medication list was reviewed, and the patient was advised to continue medication for his pre-existing conditions. The patient and family were instructed to monitor the patient's blood sugar level as appropriate diet and exercise guidelines were given. 

The patient was informed of the importance of following medication and lifestyle guidelines for successful management of the disease and advised to seek medical attention if the symptoms worsen. An additional patient education pamphlet was given upon discharge that outlines important lifestyle modifications and risk management practices for the disease.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

The patient came to the hospital with a high fever and chest pain on the left side that hurt when breathing. These problems lasted for 3 weeks. The patient also had no appetite and lost 6 kg (about 13 pounds) in one month. Blood tests showed signs of infection and high inflammation. Blood sugar levels were very high, showing uncontrolled diabetes. A chest X-ray showed scarring in the upper right lung. An ultrasound of the belly showed a pocket of infected fluid near the spleen.

After several tests, doctors found that the patient had melioidosis. This is an infection caused by a type of bacteria called Burkholderia pseudomallei. The patient received a strong antibiotic called ceftazidime through an IV for 2 weeks, given three times a day. After that, the patient took antibiotic pills called co-trimoxazole three times a day for 12 weeks. The fever and joint pain went away. A follow-up ultrasound showed the infected fluid pocket had healed. The chest X-ray showed the lung problem was getting better.

When the patient left the hospital, vital signs like heart rate and blood pressure were normal. The patient had no other health complaints at that time. The patient's health has gotten much better. The patient was told to schedule regular check-ups with the regular doctor. The care team reviewed all medicines. The patient was told to keep taking medicines for health problems that existed before this illness. The patient and family were taught how to check blood sugar levels. They also received advice about healthy eating and exercise.

The patient learned why it is important to take all medicines and follow healthy habits to control this disease. The patient was told to get medical help right away if symptoms get worse. The patient received a handout with tips about lifestyle changes and ways to manage health risks for this disease.

Hospital Course Summary:

Subjective:

The patient is a 65-year-old female with relapsing oral cavity squamous cell carcinoma who presented to our facility after experiencing a left parapharyngeal relapse. The patient underwent cervical radiotherapy and chemotherapy and was subsequently referred to our Phase I unit for enrollment in an early-phase trial.

Objective:

During the screening procedure, chest CT scans showed GGOs, suggestive of COVID-19. IgG antibodies against SARS-CoV-2 were positive, indicating a prior exposure to the virus. The patient was asymptomatic, and two consecutive nasopharyngeal swabs resulted negative. The patient underwent experimental treatment with an anti-PD-L1 mAb administered intravenously every three weeks and a daily oral TKI. The initial tumor assessment showed overall stable disease with an enlargement of GGOs affecting bilateral lower lobes.

Assessment and Plan:

Further investigation revealed no evidence of malignancy, and the interstitial proliferation, CD8+ lymphocytic infiltration, and alveolar damage were consistent with immune-mediated pneumonitis. The immunotherapy with PD-L1 blockade likely led to worsening GGOs initially detected during the screening procedure, with the COVID-19 lung injury acting as an inflammatory primer for subsequent ICI-related pneumonia development. The TKI assumption was unlikely to have played a role in the development of lung damage as it was deemed safe and did not suggest any involvement in its mechanism.

The patient underwent a CT-guided lung biopsy, and the pathological examination supported the diagnosis of immune-mediated pneumonitis. The lung toxicity was classified as grade 1, but the patient experienced no symptoms. Since there was clinical and radiological benefit on the underlying tumor, the patient restarted experimental immunotherapy and was on-treatment at the time of discharge.

Plan:

The patient's discharge instructions include strict adherence to follow-up visits with the primary care physician and the oncologist regularly, especially as part of on-treatment follow-up. Further screening to monitor any progress of immune-mediated pneumonitis and continued radiological monitoring of lung changes. Measurements to monitor respiratory function and continued reporting of any respiratory symptoms are also critical.

Prognosis:

The patient's prognosis is good, and she is expected to continue receiving experimental immunotherapy for the relapsing oral cavity squamous cell carcinoma.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Subjective:

The patient is a 65-year-old female with mouth cancer that came back. She came to our facility after the cancer returned in the area near her throat on the left side. The patient had radiation and chemotherapy to her neck area. She was then sent to our unit to join a new treatment study.

Objective:

During the initial tests, chest scans showed hazy spots in the lungs. These spots suggested a possible COVID-19 infection. Blood tests showed the patient had been exposed to the COVID-19 virus before. The patient had no symptoms. Two nose swab tests came back negative for active infection. The patient started a new treatment plan. She received an immune therapy drug through an IV every three weeks. She also took a cancer-blocking pill every day. The first scans showed the cancer stayed about the same size. The hazy spots in both lower parts of her lungs got bigger.

Assessment and Plan:

More tests showed no signs of cancer in the lungs. The lung tissue showed swelling, immune cell buildup, and damage to air sacs. These findings matched lung irritation caused by the immune therapy. The immune therapy drug likely made the hazy lung spots worse. The earlier COVID-19 infection may have made the lungs more likely to react to the immune therapy. The daily cancer pill probably did not cause the lung problem. The patient had a needle test of her lung tissue guided by a scanner. The test results confirmed lung irritation from the immune therapy. The lung problem was mild and the patient had no symptoms. The cancer was responding well to treatment. The patient started the immune therapy again. She was still receiving treatment when she left the hospital.

Plan:

The patient should follow these instructions after leaving the hospital. She must keep all appointments with her regular doctor and cancer doctor. These visits are important while she is on treatment. She will need regular scans to watch for any changes in the lung irritation. She will need continued scans to check her lungs. Tests to check how well her lungs work are important. She must tell her doctors right away if she has any breathing problems.

Prognosis:

The patient is expected to do well. She will keep receiving the study treatment for her returning mouth cancer.

Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Asymptomatic right renal mass
Discharge Diagnosis: Chromophobe RCC

History of Present Illness:
A 37-year-old male presented with an asymptomatic right renal mass detected by ultrasonography and CT. No apparent cutaneous lesions were found on the skin by careful inspection and palpation. The patient had a history of spontaneous pneumothorax on both sides and underwent pulmonary bullectomy on the right side at the age of 30.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent open partial nephrectomy. Histopathologic examination of the tumor revealed that it was chromophobe RCC, nuclear grade G2, and 2.2 cm × 2.0 cm × 2.0 cm in size. Genetic testing of the patient and his family revealed a mutation that resulted in a deletion of three bases (CCT) at nucleotides c.770-772 in exon 7 (c.770-772delCCT), resulting in deletion of the 257th serine residue (p.257delS). No mutations were found in any other members of the patient’s family.

Past Medical History:
The patient had a history of spontaneous pneumothorax on both sides and underwent pulmonary bullectomy on the right side at the age of 30. There was no history of any other significant medical conditions.

Social History:
The patient lived an active lifestyle with no history of smoking or substance abuse.

Physical Examination:
No apparent cutaneous lesions were found on the skin by careful inspection and palpation.

Laboratory Data:
Laboratory data was not mentioned in the case report.

Imaging:
CT scans revealed an asymptomatic right renal mass.

Consultations:
No consultations were mentioned in the case report.

Medications:
Medications were not mentioned in the case report.

Follow-up:
Follow-up was not mentioned in the case report.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was advised to follow up with his healthcare provider for further evaluation and management of his chromophobe RCC.

Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Right kidney growth with no symptoms
Discharge Diagnosis: A type of kidney cancer called chromophobe RCC

History of Present Illness:
A 37-year-old male came in with a growth on his right kidney that was not causing any symptoms. The growth was found using sound wave imaging and a CT scan. Doctors checked the skin carefully but found no unusual spots or bumps. The patient had a history of collapsed lungs on both sides. At age 30, he had surgery to remove air pockets in his right lung.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery to remove part of his right kidney. Doctors looked at the growth under a microscope. They found it was a type of kidney cancer called chromophobe RCC. The cancer cells were low to medium grade. The growth measured 2.2 cm by 2.0 cm by 2.0 cm in size. Doctors did a gene test on the patient and his family. They found a change in one of the patient's genes. This change removed a small piece of genetic code in a specific location. No family members had this gene change.

Past Medical History:
The patient had a history of collapsed lungs on both sides. At age 30, he had surgery to remove air pockets in his right lung. The patient had no other major health problems.

Social History:
The patient lived an active lifestyle. He did not smoke or use drugs.

Physical Examination:
Doctors checked the skin carefully but found no unusual spots or bumps.

Laboratory Data:
Lab test results were not included in this report.

Imaging:
CT scans showed a growth on the right kidney that was not causing symptoms.

Consultations:
No specialist visits were mentioned in this report.

Medications:
Medications were not mentioned in this report.

Follow-up:
Follow-up plans were not mentioned in this report.

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was told to see his doctor for more check-ups and care for his kidney cancer.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name:
Gender: Male
Age: 38
Date of Admission: [Date of admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of discharge]
Admitting Diagnosis: Painless scrotal mass

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to [Hospital Name] with a chief complaint of a painless left scrotal mass. The patient had no significant past medical history and reported discovering the mass one month prior to admission. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm well-defined mass in the upper pole of the left testis with a firm consistency. Palpation of the right testis and lower pole of the left testis were normal. Routine blood tests were unremarkable. A bioassay of testicular tumor markers was ordered which revealed normal levels of Alpha-fetoprotein, human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). An extratesticular mass was observed on scrotal ultrasound, attached to the upper pole of the patient's testis. The mass was isoechoic to the testis parenchyma and poorly vascularized on Doppler. The patient underwent a radical inguinal orchiectomy. Histological examination of the operative specimen confirmed the presence of regular splenic tissue in the suspect mass without any signs of malignancy. The splenic proliferation had a preserved capsule, demarcating from the testis. Testicular pulp, the albuginea, and the tunica vaginalis had a preserved microscopic architecture. The patient was discharged on the second postoperative day.

Discharge Diagnosis: Painless scrotal mass

Summary:
A 38-year-old North African man presented with a painless left scrotal mass. A scrotal ultrasound showed an extratesticular mass, attached to the upper pole of his testis. The patient underwent a radical inguinal orchiectomy which confirmed the presence of splenic tissue in the suspect mass without any signs of malignancy. The patient was discharged on the second postoperative day and was examined again after 3 weeks and 2 months in our out-patient unit, where his spleen was found to be of habitual location with standard measurements and a regular aspect.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name:
Gender: Male
Age: 38
Date of Admission: [Date of admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of discharge]
Admitting Diagnosis: Painless lump in the scrotum

Hospital Course:
The patient came to [Hospital Name] because of a painless lump on the left side of the scrotum. The patient had no major health problems in the past. The patient found the lump one month before coming to the hospital. A physical exam showed a 2 cm firm lump at the top of the left testicle. The lump had clear edges. The right testicle and the bottom of the left testicle felt normal. Blood tests came back normal. Tests for cancer markers in the blood were also normal. These included Alpha-fetoprotein, pregnancy hormone (hCG), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). An ultrasound of the scrotum showed a mass outside the testicle. The mass was attached to the top of the testicle. The mass looked similar to testicle tissue and had poor blood flow. The patient had surgery to remove the left testicle through a cut in the groin. Lab tests on the removed tissue showed the lump was made of normal spleen tissue. There were no signs of cancer. The spleen tissue had its own covering and was separate from the testicle. The testicle tissue and its outer layers looked normal under the microscope. The patient left the hospital two days after surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis: Painless lump in the scrotum

Summary:
A 38-year-old North African man came in with a painless lump on the left side of his scrotum. An ultrasound showed a mass outside the testicle, attached to the top of the testicle. The patient had surgery to remove the testicle through the groin. Lab tests showed the lump was made of spleen tissue with no signs of cancer. The patient left the hospital two days after surgery. The patient came back for checkups at 3 weeks and 2 months. At those visits, the spleen was in its normal place with normal size and appearance.

input:
Hospital Course:

The 70-year-old female patient was incidentally found to have left maxillary sinus mass on a computed tomography (CT) scan of the facial bones performed for trauma. The patient was referred to Otolaryngology for further workup. On physical examination, a palpable mass was noted below the left zygoma, and on endoscopy, the left ostiomeatal complex was compromised secondary to adjacent soft tissue fullness. Contrast enhanced CT of the paranasal sinuses demonstrated a large, soft tissue density mass without significant enhancement in the left maxillary sinus. Bony destruction was noted along the anterior, posterior and lateral walls of the left maxillary sinus, as well as the left orbital floor. A left maxillectomy was performed under general anesthesia. Repeat CT 1 month later showed no evidence of recurrence. 

Medical History:

The patient had a medical history notable for Human Immunodeficiency Virus, Diabetes Mellitus, Hypertension, and chronic sinusitis. There was no history of tobacco or alcohol use.

Presenting Symptoms:

The patient reported having intermittent blood-tinged mucus for over 2 years with episodes of epistaxis over the last month. They also reported 1 week of left facial numbness, and one day of drooling from their left oral commissure.

Diagnostic Findings:

Non-contrast magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the paranasal sinuses showed the soft tissue mass was isointense to muscle on T1 weighted images, extending anteriorly into the pre-maxillary soft tissue.

Pathology Results:

Fine needle aspiration of the mass revealed scant groups of cohesive cells with nuclear enlargement, hyperchromasia, and mild cellular crowding, without high-grade features. Special staining further revealed positivity for cytokeratin AE1/AE3, p63, and p40, but was negative for S100, diagnostic for ameloblastoma. 

Plan:

Resection of the lateral and most inferior aspects of the mass was performed with a Caldwell-Luc procedure, achieving near total resection.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The 70-year-old female patient was found to have a growth in the left cheek sinus by chance. This was seen on a scan of the face bones done after an injury. The patient was sent to an ear, nose, and throat doctor for more tests. During the exam, a lump could be felt below the left cheekbone. A small camera test showed the sinus drainage area was blocked by nearby tissue swelling. A scan with dye showed a large growth in the left cheek sinus. The growth did not absorb much of the dye. Bone damage was seen in the front, back, and side walls of the left cheek sinus. There was also damage to the bone under the left eye. Surgery was done to remove part of the left upper jaw while the patient was fully asleep. A scan one month later showed the growth had not come back.

Medical History:

The patient had a history of HIV, a disease that weakens the body's defense system. The patient also had diabetes, which causes high blood sugar. The patient had high blood pressure and long-term sinus swelling. There was no history of smoking or drinking alcohol.

Presenting Symptoms:

The patient said they had mucus with streaks of blood on and off for over 2 years. They also had nosebleeds over the last month. They reported numbness on the left side of the face for 1 week. They had drooling from the left corner of the mouth for one day.

Diagnostic Findings:

A scan using magnets without dye showed the growth in the sinus area. The growth looked similar to muscle on certain images. It spread forward into the soft tissue in front of the upper jaw.

Pathology Results:

A test using a thin needle to take cells from the growth was done. It showed small groups of cells stuck together with larger than normal centers. The cells were darker than normal and slightly crowded together. There were no signs of fast-growing cancer. Special dye tests showed the cells were positive for certain markers called cytokeratin AE1/AE3, p63, and p40. The cells were negative for a marker called S100. These results meant the growth was an ameloblastoma, a type of jaw tumor.

Plan:

Surgery was done to remove the side and bottom parts of the growth. This was done using a procedure that goes through the upper jaw area. Almost all of the growth was removed.

input:
Hospital Course:

47-year-old male was admitted following a car accident. The patient presented with forehead abrasion and confusion. A brain CT scan and cervical X-rays showed no hematoma or cerebral edema, but cervical instability at C6-7. The patient was immobilized with a neck collar and closely monitored. Two weeks after admission, the patient complained of severe posterior neck pain (VAS > 9) and bilateral upper extremity numbness. Subsequent surgical stabilization was performed. An anterior cervical plate was positioned, and pilot holes were tapped. The upper left pilot hole was stripped due to repeated drilling, and PMMA augmentation was performed, which resulted in a mass lesion at the spinal canal. C6 corpectomy was promptly performed to remove the mass. Anterior reconstruction with an anterior cervical plate and mesh cage from C5 through C7 was done. Postoperatively, the patient had left side weakness and impairment of the left 4th and 5th digit extension, but he improved without intervention.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Cervical injury with spinal cord compression leading to cervical corpectomy.

Hospital course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital following a car accident. During his hospital stay, he underwent surgical stabilization for cervical instability at C6-7. There were some complications during the procedure, which led to a mass lesion at the spinal canal and cervical corpectomy. The patient had some postoperative weakness and impairment of the left hand digits, but he improved without intervention. The patient was discharged in good condition with a follow-up appointment with a spinal specialist.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

A 47-year-old male was admitted after a car accident. The patient had a cut on the forehead and was confused. A brain scan and neck X-rays showed no bleeding or swelling in the brain. However, the images showed the neck bones were not stable at the lower neck area. The patient wore a neck brace and was watched closely. Two weeks after being admitted, the patient reported very bad pain in the back of the neck. The patient also had numbness in both arms and hands. Surgery was done to fix the unstable neck bones. During surgery, a metal plate was placed on the front of the neck. Holes were drilled to hold the plate in place. One hole in the upper left area was damaged from drilling too many times. Bone cement was used to fix this problem. This caused a lump to form in the space around the spinal cord. The surgical team quickly removed part of the damaged neck bone to take out the lump. The neck was rebuilt using a metal plate and a support cage from the fifth to seventh neck bones. After surgery, the patient had weakness on the left side. The patient also had trouble straightening the ring and pinky fingers on the left hand. These problems got better on their own without extra treatment.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Neck injury with pressure on the spinal cord. This led to surgery to remove part of a neck bone.

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital after a car accident. During the hospital stay, the patient had surgery to fix unstable bones in the lower neck. There were some problems during the surgery. These problems caused a lump near the spinal cord. Part of a neck bone had to be removed. After surgery, the patient had some weakness and trouble moving fingers on the left hand. These problems got better without extra treatment. The patient left the hospital in good condition. The patient has a follow-up visit scheduled with a spine doctor.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Name]

Date of Admission: [Date of Admission]

Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

Hospitalized for: Stenosis of Left Subclavian Artery

Brief Hospital Course: 

The patient, a 4-year-old boy, was admitted with an incidental detection of murmur. Clinical examination revealed low volume radial pulse in the left upper limb and lower BP in the same limb. The right radial pulse was normal. Anthropometrically, his height was 100 cm, and the left upper limb was 10 mm shorter than the right upper limb.  

Echocardiography showed structurally normal heart with RAA and aberrant LSA with flow turbulence. CT angiogram was done, which confirmed the diagnosis. A stenosis of 70-80% just beyond the origin of LSA was detected. The presence of limb length discrepancy justified treatment, and the patient was scheduled for transcatheter placement of stent. 

The procedure was performed successfully, and the patient tolerated it very well. Racer 5 mm × 18 mm stent was positioned across the stenosed segment. Post-procedure angiogram confirmed the stent's correct placement, and good flow was observed in LSA and left axillary artery. The patient was kept on overnight heparin infusion and discharged with oral aspirin.

Follow-up visits on 3 and 6 months showed near-normalization of radial pulse and blood pressure in the left upper limb, and significant improvement of limb length. USG of the left upper limb confirmed a laminar flow pattern in the LSA. The boy has been followed up for the last 4 years, with no remaining limb length discrepancy detected.

Discharge Diagnosis: Stenosis of Left Subclavian Artery.

Discharge Instructions: 

The patient will continue aspirin therapy (as directed by the physician). Follow-up visits should be scheduled as per the physician's instructions. Parents are advised to keep the patient's medical records updated and bring them to each visit. 

Signed, 

[Physician Name]
[Medical Institution]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Name]

Date of Admission: [Date of Admission]

Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

Hospitalized for: Narrowing of the Left Arm Artery

Brief Hospital Course:

The patient, a 4-year-old boy, was admitted after doctors heard an unusual heart sound during a checkup. The doctor found that the pulse in the left arm was weak and blood pressure was low in that arm. The pulse in the right arm was normal. The patient was 100 cm tall. The left arm was 10 mm shorter than the right arm.

A heart ultrasound showed the heart structure was normal. However, it showed the main artery to the left arm was in an unusual position with uneven blood flow. A special scan of the blood vessels was done to confirm what was wrong. The scan showed the artery to the left arm was 70-80% blocked near where it starts. Because one arm was shorter than the other, treatment was needed. The patient was scheduled to have a small tube placed inside the artery to open it up.

The procedure went well, and the patient handled it without problems. A small metal tube was placed inside the narrowed part of the artery. Pictures taken after the procedure showed the tube was in the right place. Blood was flowing well through the left arm artery. The patient received blood-thinning medicine through an IV overnight. The patient went home with aspirin to take by mouth.

At check-up visits 3 and 6 months later, the pulse and blood pressure in the left arm were almost normal. The arm length had improved a lot. An ultrasound of the left arm showed smooth blood flow in the artery. The boy has been checked for 4 years now. There is no longer any difference in arm length.

Discharge Diagnosis: Narrowing of the Left Arm Artery.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient will keep taking aspirin as the doctor ordered. Follow-up visits should be scheduled as the doctor instructs. Parents should keep the patient's medical records up to date. Parents should bring these records to each visit.

Signed,

[Physician Name]
[Medical Institution]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 75-year-old male with hypertension and diabetes mellitus and a history of alcohol addiction. He presented to the hospital with a hyperdense linear foreign body extending from the upper part of the right internal jugular vein and exiting the skull through the right parietal bone. The CT scan revealed the presence of smaller metallic structures in the posterior fossa and right occipital region. A small abscess was observed along the catheter in the right posterior parieto-occipital region. The patient underwent emergency surgery to remove the foreign body, during which a 0.2 × 0.1 mm-sized opening was found in the right posterior parietal bone. The wire was gently pulled out, and hemostasis was achieved. The postoperative CT head revealed a few fragments of retained catheter in the right cerebellar hemisphere, beneath the right tentorial leaflet extra-axially, along the right transverse sinus, and the right occipital extra-axial region, with mild surrounding vasogenic edema, likely postoperative.

Course of Treatment:
The patient underwent emergency surgery to remove the foreign body. The fragments of retained catheter were observed in the postoperative CT scan, which were likely the result of the surgery. The patient was observed for any complications post-surgery, and none were reported.

Summary:
The patient presented with a foreign body extending from the internal jugular vein and exiting the skull through the right parietal bone. The patient underwent emergency surgery, and the foreign body was removed. The fragments of retained catheter were observed in the postoperative CT scan. The patient was discharged in stable condition without any reported complications. Follow-up appointments were scheduled with the patient's primary care physician and the surgical team to monitor for any post-surgical complications.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 75-year-old male with high blood pressure, diabetes, and a history of alcohol addiction. He came to the hospital because a wire-like object was found inside his body. This object went from a large vein in the neck up through the skull, coming out through a bone on the right side of the head. A scan showed smaller metal pieces in the lower back part of the brain and the right side of the head. A small pocket of infection was seen along the tube near the back right part of the head. The patient had emergency surgery to remove the object. During surgery, doctors found a tiny hole in the skull bone on the right side. The wire was carefully pulled out, and the bleeding was stopped. A scan after surgery showed a few small pieces of the tube were still inside. These pieces were found in several areas on the right side of the brain and head. There was mild swelling around these areas, which is normal after surgery.

Course of Treatment:
The patient had emergency surgery to remove the object. The scan after surgery showed some small pieces of the tube were left behind. This was likely caused by the surgery itself. The care team watched the patient closely for any problems after surgery. No problems were reported.

Summary:
The patient came in with a foreign object that went from a vein in the neck through the skull bone. The patient had emergency surgery, and the object was removed. A scan after surgery showed some small pieces of the tube were still inside. The patient left the hospital in stable condition with no problems reported. Follow-up visits were set up with the primary care doctor and the surgery team. These visits will help watch for any problems after surgery.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital Course:

This is a summary of the hospital course of a 22-year-old female patient who presented with one month's history of headache and left hemiparesis. Imaging revealed a hyperdense solitary right parietal parasagittal tumor. Following surgery, histopathological analysis confirmed the tumor to be a pPNET/Ewing's sarcoma (ES). The patient's postoperative treatment included radiotherapy (40Gy/250cgy/16 Frs) and five cycles of chemotherapy with ifosfamide and etoposide. The patient underwent CECT nine months after surgery, which showed no residual or recurrent disease. A follow-up visit was conducted 20 months after the surgery, during which the patient was found to be normal with no evidence of disease recurrence.

Hospital Course Details:

Chief Complaint: Headache and left hemiparesis
Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A

Medical History:
The patient was diagnosed with primary spinal PNET in 2003 and received treatment that included surgery, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy with ifosfamide, adriamycin, and etoposide.

Hospital Course:
A right frontoparietal parasagittal craniotomy was performed, and a highly vascular tumor was found adjacent to the motor cortex, infiltrating into the dura. Near-total excision of the tumor was achieved with a thin rim of tumor left along the superior sagittal sinus. The infiltrated dura was removed, and duraplasty was performed. The tumor was found to be consistent with pPNET/Ewing's sarcoma (ES). The patient experienced mild worsening of motor power on the left side to 3/5 after surgery, which gradually improved over two months. Following surgery, the patient received postoperative radiotherapy (40Gy/250cgy/16 Frs) and five cycles of chemotherapy with ifosfamide and etoposide. Follow-up imaging nine months after surgery showed no recurrence of disease. At a follow-up visit 20 months after surgery, the patient was found to be normal with no evidence of disease recurrence.

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient is to follow up with her healthcare provider as needed.

Final Diagnosis: Parietal parasagittal tumor consistent with pPNET/Ewing's sarcoma (ES).

Procedures:
- Right frontoparietal parasagittal craniotomy
- Excision of a highly vascular tumor with infiltrated dura and duraplasty

Condition at Discharge: Normal with no evidence of disease recurrence.

Attending Physician: N/A

Discharge Date: N/A
Discharge Time: N/A

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital Course:

This is a summary of the hospital stay of a 22-year-old female patient. The patient came in with one month of headaches and weakness on the left side of the body. Scans showed a single tumor on the right side of the brain, near the top of the head. After surgery, lab tests confirmed the tumor was a type of cancer called PNET or Ewing's sarcoma. After surgery, the patient received radiation treatment (40Gy/250cgy/16 Frs) and five rounds of cancer-fighting drugs (ifosfamide and etoposide). A scan done nine months after surgery showed no remaining or returning cancer. At a check-up 20 months after surgery, the patient was doing well with no signs of cancer coming back.

Hospital Course Details:

Chief Complaint: Headache and weakness on the left side of the body
Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: N/A

Medical History:
The patient was found to have a cancer called PNET in the spine in 2003. Treatment at that time included surgery, radiation, and cancer-fighting drugs (ifosfamide, adriamycin, and etoposide).

Hospital Course:
Doctors performed brain surgery on the right side of the head to remove the tumor. The tumor had many blood vessels and was located near the part of the brain that controls movement. It had also grown into the brain's protective covering. Doctors removed almost all of the tumor. A thin edge of tumor along a major blood vessel in the brain was left behind. The affected brain covering was removed and repaired. The tumor was confirmed to be a cancer called PNET or Ewing's sarcoma. After surgery, the patient had more weakness on the left side, with muscle strength dropping to 3 out of 5. This slowly got better over two months. The patient then received radiation treatment (40Gy/250cgy/16 Frs) and five rounds of cancer-fighting drugs (ifosfamide and etoposide). A scan nine months after surgery showed no cancer had returned. At a check-up 20 months after surgery, the patient was doing well with no signs of cancer coming back.

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient should see her healthcare provider as needed.

Final Diagnosis: Tumor near the top right side of the brain, confirmed to be a cancer called PNET or Ewing's sarcoma.

Procedures:
Brain surgery on the right side of the head to remove the tumor
Removal and repair of the brain's protective covering that the tumor had grown into

Condition at Discharge: Normal with no signs of cancer coming back.

Attending Physician: N/A

Discharge Date: N/A
Discharge Time: N/A

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:
Name: Not provided
Age: 57
Sex: Male

Admission Date: Not provided
Discharge Date: Not provided

Reason for Admission:
Decreased vision in the left eye of 1-month duration

Hospital Course:
The patient was diagnosed with primary intraocular lymphoma (PIOL) based on the results of his diagnostic vitrectomy. As a PCNSL was not detected by magnetic resonance imaging, the patient was treated with weekly intravitreal injections of methotrexate (MTX) for 1 month, followed by monthly maintenance injections for seven more months.

Assessment:
The patient showed significant improvement in his best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA), from 20/25 to 20/16 after 9 months. Fundus examination showed regression of the yellowish lesions in the posterior fundus, changing gradually to atrophy of the RPE. Fundus autofluorescence (FAF) and spectral domain optical coherence tomography (SD-OCT) showed significant improvement in the degree of hyperreflective infiltration in the inner retina, restoration of the RPE and IS/OS junction, and recovery of foveal thickness. Enhanced depth imaging OCT (EDI-OCT) showed a gradual decrease in the subfoveal choroidal thickness during the treatment. The total macular volume reduced significantly after the treatment.

The mean deviation (MD) on standard automated perimetry increased from -13.95 dB before treatment to -7.23 dB after treatment. The mean retinal sensitivity within the central 10° measured with a fundus-related microperimeter increased from 0.3 dB before treatment to 4.1 dB after treatment. Full-field electroretinograms (ERGs) and multifocal ERGs (mfERGs) showed decreased amplitudes.

Final Diagnosis:
Primary intraocular lymphoma (PIOL)

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow up with his ophthalmologist regularly for monitoring of his visual acuity and ocular health. Any new or worsening symptoms should be reported to his healthcare provider immediately.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:
Name: Not provided
Age: 57
Sex: Male

Admission Date: Not provided
Discharge Date: Not provided

Reason for Admission:
The patient had decreased vision in the left eye for 1 month.

Hospital Course:
The patient was diagnosed with a type of eye cancer called primary intraocular lymphoma. Doctors found this after doing surgery to remove and test fluid from inside the eye. A brain scan showed the cancer had not spread to the brain. The patient received weekly injections of a cancer-fighting medicine called methotrexate into the eye for 1 month. After that, the patient received monthly injections for seven more months.

Assessment:
The patient's vision improved a lot over 9 months. Vision went from 20/25 to 20/16, which is better than normal. Eye exams showed the yellowish spots in the back of the eye went away. The tissue layer at the back of the eye showed some thinning where the spots used to be. Special eye imaging tests showed the cancer was going away. The inner layers of the eye healed and returned to normal thickness. The tissue layer behind the light-sensing cells also healed. The thickness of the blood vessel layer under the back of the eye got thinner during treatment. The overall size of the central seeing area got smaller after treatment. Tests that check side vision showed improvement. The patient could see more of the area around the center of vision after treatment. Tests that measure how well the back of the eye responds to light showed some reduced activity.

Final Diagnosis:
Primary intraocular lymphoma, which is a type of eye cancer

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should see his eye doctor regularly. The eye doctor will check his vision and overall eye health. The patient should tell his healthcare provider right away about any new symptoms. The patient should also report any symptoms that get worse.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Proband C
Gender: Female
Age: 83
Medical Record Number: #######

Hospital Course:
Proband C was admitted for treatment of a kumquat-like GDLD subtype with neovascularization in both of her eyes. She had a history of lamellar keratoplasty along with keratoepithelioplasty in her left eye at the age of 72 and penetrating keratoplasty in her right eye at the age of 82. After admission, she underwent surface keratectomy for her left eye to remove the superficial amyloid depositions.

Assessment:
Post-surgery, the patient showed characteristic findings of GDLD subtype recurrence in both of her eyes and was unable to wear soft contact lenses.

Plan:
A soft contact lens is known to be protective for the postoperative GDLD cornea. However, in this case, the patient was unable to use them. There were no further surgical interventions undertaken during admission, as she was too elderly to undergo more surgeries.

Follow-up:
The patient's condition will be kept under observation, and follow-up arrangements will be made as necessary in coordination with her ophthalmologist.

Summary:
Proband C, an 83-year-old Japanese female, was admitted for the treatment of GDLD cornea. Following the surgery and a surface keratectomy, postoperative recurrence of her condition was observed. Given that the patient was unable to wear soft contact lenses, no further surgical interventions were undertaken. Prognosis for the patient will be kept under observation, and a follow-up with the ophthalmologist will be scheduled.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Proband C
Gender: Female
Age: 83
Medical Record Number: #######

Hospital Course:
Proband C was admitted for treatment of a rare eye disease called GDLD. This disease causes bumpy growths and new blood vessel growth in both of her eyes. She had partial cornea transplant surgery and surface cell replacement on her left eye at age 72. She had a full cornea transplant on her right eye at age 82. The cornea is the clear front part of the eye. After admission, she had surgery on her left eye to remove protein buildup on the surface.

Assessment:
After surgery, the patient showed signs that her eye disease had returned in both eyes. She was not able to wear soft contact lenses.

Plan:
Soft contact lenses can help protect the eye after surgery for this disease. However, the patient was not able to wear them. No more surgeries were done during this hospital stay. The patient is too old to have more surgeries safely.

Follow-up:
The patient's condition will be watched closely. Follow-up visits will be set up as needed with her eye doctor.

Summary:
Proband C is an 83-year-old Japanese female. She was admitted for treatment of her GDLD eye disease. After her surgery to remove surface protein buildup, her eye disease came back. The patient cannot wear soft contact lenses. No more surgeries were done. Her condition will be watched closely. A follow-up visit with her eye doctor will be scheduled.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Patient Name: [Confidential]

Admission Date: [Confidential]

Discharge Date: [Confidential]

Admission Diagnosis: Stage I moderately differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast (ER-/PR+, Her2+), BRCA2 mutation

Hospital Course: 

The patient was diagnosed with stage I moderately differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast, which was ER-/PR+, Her2+, and also had a BRCA2 mutation. She received neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by a bilateral mastectomy and underwent adjuvant chemotherapy with trastuzumab and pertuzumab. The patient was then started on adjuvant endocrine therapy with toremifene.

Two years after her last treatment, the patient presented with an enlarged left axillary lymph node. Biopsy results showed a recurrence of her breast cancer. Three weeks later, the patient presented to the emergency department with diplopia, weight loss, and frequent urination. Imaging revealed multiple metastases to the brain, liver, and cervical spine.

The patient was started on radiation therapy to the orbits with possible radiation therapy to the pituitary. She was also restarted on trastuzumab and pertuzumab, and began treatment with fulvestrant, goserelin, and zolendronic acid. The patient showed good treatment response with significant improvement in her diplopia.

Discharge Diagnosis: Recurrent metastatic carcinoma of ER/PR-positive breast cancer

Discharge Plan:

The patient remains under close observation and ongoing care for her recurrent metastatic breast cancer. Further diagnostic tests and imaging will be conducted as needed, and additional treatment will be administered as recommended by her oncologist. The patient will be scheduled for regular follow-up appointments to monitor her condition and adjust her treatment plan as necessary.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Patient Name: [Confidential]

Admission Date: [Confidential]

Discharge Date: [Confidential]

Admission Diagnosis: Early stage breast cancer that had spread into nearby tissue. The cancer cells responded to some hormones but not others. The patient also had a gene change that raises breast cancer risk.

Hospital Course:

The patient was found to have early stage breast cancer. The cancer had started to spread into nearby breast tissue. Testing showed the cancer cells respond to some hormones. The patient also had a gene change called BRCA2 that raises cancer risk. She received drug treatment to shrink the cancer before surgery. Then she had surgery to remove both breasts. After surgery, she received more cancer drugs called trastuzumab and pertuzumab. The patient also started taking a hormone-blocking pill called toremifene.

Two years after finishing treatment, the patient found a swollen lymph node under her left arm. A tissue sample showed her breast cancer had come back. Three weeks later, the patient came to the emergency room. She had double vision, weight loss, and was urinating often. Scans showed the cancer had spread to her brain, liver, and neck bones.

The patient started radiation treatment aimed at the area around her eyes. She may also get radiation to a gland at the base of her brain. She restarted the cancer drugs trastuzumab and pertuzumab. She also began taking three new medicines called fulvestrant, goserelin, and zolendronic acid. The patient responded well to treatment. Her double vision got much better.

Discharge Diagnosis: Breast cancer that came back and spread to other parts of the body. The cancer cells respond to hormones.

Discharge Plan:

The patient will stay under close watch for her returning breast cancer that has spread. She will have more tests and scans as needed. Her cancer doctor will guide any new treatments. The patient will have regular check-up visits. Her care team will watch her health and change her treatment plan if needed.

The patient will stay under close watch for her returning breast cancer that has spread. She will have more tests and scans as needed. Her cancer doctor will guide any new treatments. The patient will have regular check-up visits. Her care team will watch her health and change her treatment plan if needed.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 74-year-old
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Chief complaint:
The patient complained of exacerbation of mild dysphagia and an awareness of a tumor in the left side of her neck.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital on [REDACTED] with a complaint of mild dysphagia for the past two months, which had recently worsened. Laboratory tests showed normal thyroid function levels. Pharyngoesophagography showed a large diverticulum at the pharyngoesophageal junction. Ultrasonography revealed a hypoechoic lesion present in the posterior bilateral lobe of the thyroid and an isoechoic mass on the left lobe of the thyroid. Cervical CT scan showed a thyroid nodule, bilateral hypopharyngeal diverticula, and an isodensity area with a hyperdensity area, suggesting calcium deposition. A barium swallow pharyngoesophagogram showed a large left-sided diverticulum and a small right-sided diverticulum.

The patient underwent resection of the diverticula and thyroid tumor surgery for improvement of dysphagia. The surgical procedure was approached through a collar incision in the left anterior neck, and the diverticula were visualized under the cricopharyngeus muscle. The left-sided diverticulum was mobilized from the left lobe of the thyroid and the thyroid tumor. The diverticulotomy was performed. The wound was closed in layers with placement of a closed suction drain.

Histopathological examination showed that the diverticula were false diverticula without a muscular layer, and the thyroid tumor was diagnosed as a thyroid adenoma. The patient was started on a clear liquid diet on postoperative day 4. Two years after surgery, the patient remains free of dysphagia.

Diagnosis:
- Mild dysphagia
- Hypopharyngeal diverticula
- Thyroid adenoma

Treatment:
- Surgery for resection of hypopharyngeal diverticula and thyroid tumor

Follow-up:
The patient is advised to continue follow-up care with her primary care physician. No further appointments with our hospital are required.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 74-year-old
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Main problem:
The patient had trouble swallowing that had gotten worse. She also noticed a growth on the left side of her neck.

Hospital stay:
The patient came to the hospital on [REDACTED]. She had mild trouble swallowing for two months that recently got worse. Blood tests showed her thyroid gland was working normally. An X-ray of her throat and food pipe showed a large pouch where the throat meets the food pipe. An ultrasound found an abnormal area behind both sides of the thyroid and a mass on the left side of the thyroid. A CT scan of the neck showed a thyroid lump, pouches on both sides of the lower throat, and an area that may have calcium buildup. Another swallowing test showed a large pouch on the left side and a small pouch on the right side.

The patient had surgery to remove the pouches and thyroid growth to help with swallowing. The surgeon made a curved cut on the left front of the neck. The pouches were found under a throat muscle. The left pouch was separated from the thyroid and the thyroid growth. The pouches were removed. The wound was closed in layers and a drain tube was placed to remove fluid.

Lab tests on the removed tissue showed the pouches were weak spots in the wall without muscle. The thyroid growth was found to be not cancer. The patient started drinking clear liquids on day 4 after surgery. Two years after surgery, the patient has no trouble swallowing.

Diagnosis:
Mild trouble swallowing
Pouches in the lower throat
Non-cancerous thyroid growth

Treatment:
Surgery to remove the throat pouches and thyroid growth

Follow-up:
The patient should keep seeing her regular doctor. No more visits to this hospital are needed.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: 64-year-old male
Reason for Admission: Implantation of a transcatheter leadless pacemaker
Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]

History of Present Illness:
The patient was admitted to Monaldi Hospital for implantation of a transcatheter leadless pacemaker. During the device implantation procedure, unacceptable electrical measurements and fluoroscopic findings indicated possible TPS dislodgement. An emergency computed tomography (CT) scan was requested and revealed that the TPS had migrated and was embolized in the pulmonary middle lobe artery.

Hospital Course:
Multi-detector CT (MDCT) angiography was performed and confirmed the dislodged device’s presence in the pulmonary middle lobe artery. Successful percutaneous retrieval of the dislodged device was readily performed through the proximal retrieval feature of the device.

Physical Examination:
The patient’s lungs were examined, and there were reduced blood flow and hyperlucent of the tributary lung territory due to the dislodged pacemaker.

Diagnostic Testing:
Computed tomography (CT) scan and Multi-detector CT (MDCT) angiography confirmed the presence of the dislodged pacemaker in the pulmonary middle lobe artery and pulmonary blood flow restrictions.

Course of Treatment:
Successful retrieval of the dislodged pacemaker was made through the proximal retrieval feature of the device. The pacemaker was then correctly reimplanted in the next hours.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient presented to Monaldi Hospital for implantation of a transcatheter leadless pacemaker. The procedure was terminated due to unacceptable electrical measurements and fluoroscopic findings that indicated the possible dislodgement of the device. A CT scan and MDCT angiography were performed, revealing that the TPS had migrated and was embolized in the pulmonary middle lobe artery. Successful percutaneous retrieval of the dislodged device was performed and correctly reimplanted.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged from the hospital in good condition and was advised to follow-up with his physician for further monitoring of the implanted pacemaker.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient was advised to follow up with his physician for further monitoring of the implanted pacemaker. No other follow-up recommendations were provided.

Discharge Medications:
No discharge medications were prescribed.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was advised to follow up with his physician for further monitoring of the implanted pacemaker. No other follow-up recommendations were provided.

Attending Physician:
[Physician Name and Signature]

Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: 64-year-old male
Reason for Admission: Placement of a small wireless pacemaker (a device that helps the heart beat regularly)
Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]

History of Present Illness:
The patient came to Monaldi Hospital to have a small wireless pacemaker put in. During the procedure, the readings and images showed the device may have moved out of place. Doctors ordered an emergency scan right away. The scan showed the pacemaker had moved and traveled into a blood vessel in the right lung.

Hospital Course:
A detailed scan of the blood vessels was done. It confirmed the pacemaker was stuck in a blood vessel in the middle part of the right lung. Doctors were able to remove the loose device using a built-in feature that allows it to be pulled back out.

Physical Examination:
The patient's lungs were checked. The area of the lung near the loose pacemaker had less blood flow. That part of the lung also looked lighter on images than normal.

Diagnostic Testing:
Scans of the chest and blood vessels confirmed the pacemaker was in a lung blood vessel. The scans also showed reduced blood flow in that area.

Course of Treatment:
Doctors successfully removed the loose pacemaker using its built-in removal feature. They then placed the pacemaker correctly in the following hours.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient came to Monaldi Hospital to have a small wireless pacemaker put in. The procedure was stopped because readings and images showed the device may have moved. Scans showed the pacemaker had traveled into a blood vessel in the right lung. Doctors removed the device successfully and placed it correctly afterward.

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital in good health. The patient was told to see a doctor for regular checks on the pacemaker.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient should see a doctor for regular checks on the pacemaker. No other follow-up steps were given.

Discharge Medications:
No medications were prescribed at discharge.

Discharge Condition:
The patient should see a doctor for regular checks on the pacemaker. No other follow-up steps were given.

Attending Physician:
[Physician Name and Signature]

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient was referred to our pain clinic due to an 8-day history of postural headache, which occurred following acupuncture treatment to manage chronic lower back pain caused by postlaminectomy syndrome. The patient had undergone L4-5 laminectomy 7 years prior. The physical and neurological examinations were normal. The patient was diagnosed with a postdural puncture headache (PDPH) and was placed on bed rest. An epidural blood patch (EBP) was performed, which failed to relieve the symptoms. A brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was unremarkable, while an MRI myelogram showed an abnormal fluid signal intensity in the left lumbar area, along with the left paraspinal muscle, and soft tissues at the L3-4-5 level, probably due to CSF leakage.

After conservative care, which included bed rest, hydration, and taking acetaminophen, the patient's postural headaches persisted. A repeat fluoroscopically guided interlaminar EBP was performed with minor relief. A transforaminal EBP at the L3-4 and L4-5 levels on the left side was performed. After the transforaminal EBP, the patient's headache and other symptoms subsided without complications.

At the 2-week follow-up, the patient reported being headache-free, and the activities had returned to normal.

Diagnosis:

Postdural puncture headache (PDPH)

Treatment:

Conservative care, including bed rest, hydration, and acetaminophen, was recommended. Epidural blood patches were performed, including an interlaminar approach using autologous blood at the L2-3 level with 15ml, and a repeat of this procedure using the same volume of blood at the L4-5 level. Finally, a transforaminal EBP with 3ml autologous blood was given at each level at L3-4 and L4-5 on the left side.

Follow-up Care:

The patient was discharged after 4 days. At the 2-week follow-up, the patient reported being headache-free, and the activities had returned to normal. The patient was advised to follow-up with their primary care physician if there were any changes in the symptoms.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient came to our pain clinic with a headache that started 8 days earlier. The headache got worse when sitting or standing up. It happened after acupuncture treatment for long-term lower back pain. The back pain was caused by problems after a spine surgery done 7 years ago. That surgery removed part of the bone in the lower spine at the L4-5 level. The physical exam and brain and nerve tests were normal. The doctors found that the headache was caused by spinal fluid leaking. The patient was told to stay in bed. A treatment was done where the patient's own blood was put into the spine to seal the leak. This did not help. A brain scan was normal. Another scan showed fluid leaking in the lower left back area at the L3-4-5 level of the spine.

The patient rested, drank plenty of fluids, and took acetaminophen for pain. The headache did not go away. The blood patch treatment was done again with some relief. Then a different type of blood patch was done on the left side at the L3-4 and L4-5 levels. After this treatment, the headache and other symptoms went away without any problems.

At the 2-week checkup, the patient had no headache. The patient was back to normal activities.

Diagnosis:

Headache caused by spinal fluid leak

Treatment:

The patient was told to rest in bed, drink plenty of fluids, and take acetaminophen for pain. Blood patch treatments were done to seal the leak. The first was done at the L2-3 level using 15ml of the patient's own blood. This was repeated at the L4-5 level with the same amount of blood. Finally, a different type of blood patch was done at L3-4 and L4-5 on the left side using 3ml of blood at each level.

Follow-up Care:

The patient left the hospital after 4 days. At the 2-week checkup, the patient had no headache and was back to normal activities. The patient was told to see their regular doctor if any symptoms came back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Age: 74
- Gender: Male
- Medical History: Hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease, and end-stage renal disease

Hospital Course:
- The patient was transferred to our hospital due to bilateral intermittent claudication that had been ongoing for 6 months before admission.
- The patient had received clopidogrel and cilostazol as medication but refused surgical revascularization.
- Endovascular therapy (EVT) was chosen as the treatment option, and the patient gave written consent.
- EVT was initiated in the right superficial femoral artery chronic total occlusion (SFA-CTO) using the contralateral approach.
- The antegrade approach with the stiff CTO wire resulted in subintimal wiring. Therefore, bidirectional approach with direct distal-SFA puncture was used.
- Retrograde-antegrade rendezvous technique led to wire externalization, but no devices were able to pass the lesion by the antegrade and retrograde approaches.
- Minimal balloon could not pass the lesion, and the needle cracking technique from outside and inside of the vessel could not modify the lesion fully.
- ELA using the Turbo Elite 0.9 mm (Spectranetics, Co., USA) was applied, and it modified the CTO entry-morphology.
- After ELA ablation, the Caravel microcatheter (ASAHI Intecc) finally crossed the lesion using an antegrade approach with the BAlloon Deployment using FORcible Manner (BADFORM) technique, which enabled change of the wire from conventional the 0.014-inch wire to 0.009-inch RotaWire floppy (Boston Scientific) and RotaLink Plus 1.5mm burr ablation.
- Thereafter, we dilated the entire SFA lesion with conventional balloons and simultaneously achieved hemostasis of the distal-SFA puncture point.
- A bare nitinol stent was deployed due to severe dissection of the partial SFA.
- Treating the bilateral iliac arteries with bare nitinol stents followed, resulting in improvement of the right ABI of the patient from being unmeasurable to 0.79, and his intermittent claudication disappeared.
- The ABI and symptoms have remained satisfactory eight months after the current EVT.

Impression and Plan:
- This patient presented with bilateral intermittent claudication that improved after EVT treatment, including ELA and stent deployment.
- The patient's symptoms and ABI remained satisfactory at the eight-month follow-up.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Age: 74
Gender: Male
Medical History: High blood pressure, high cholesterol, diabetes, heart disease, and kidney failure

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital because of leg pain when walking. This problem affected both legs and had lasted for 6 months before coming to the hospital.
The patient had been taking blood thinner medicine and medicine to help blood flow in the legs. The patient did not want open surgery to fix the blood flow.
A less invasive procedure to open blocked blood vessels was chosen instead. The patient agreed to this treatment in writing.
The doctors started working on a fully blocked artery in the right thigh. They went in through a blood vessel on the opposite side of the body.
The first attempt to pass a wire through the blockage went outside the artery wall. So the doctors tried going in from both directions using a needle in the lower thigh.
The wires from both directions met in the middle and were connected. But no tools could get through the blockage from either direction.
Even the smallest balloon could not get through. Poking the blockage from outside and inside the blood vessel did not fully open it.
A special laser tool was used to break up the blockage. This changed the shape of the blocked area so tools could get through.
After using the laser, a tiny tube finally got through the blockage. Then the doctors used a spinning drill tool to open the artery more.
Next, the doctors used balloons to widen the entire blocked area in the thigh artery. They also stopped any bleeding from the needle site in the lower thigh.
A metal mesh tube was placed because part of the artery wall was badly torn during the procedure.
Metal mesh tubes were also placed in the arteries near the hip on both sides. The blood flow to the right leg improved greatly. The leg pain when walking went away completely.
Blood flow and symptoms have stayed good for eight months after the procedure.

Impression and Plan:
This patient had leg pain when walking in both legs. The pain got better after the procedure to open blocked blood vessels using a laser and metal mesh tubes.
The patient's symptoms and blood flow have stayed good at the eight-month checkup.
