input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 42 years 
Hospital Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Diagnosis: Ischemic Stroke

Hospital Course:

The patient presented to the emergency room with slurred speech, dizziness, and left-sided weakness which started about two hours before presentation. The patient did not report any cough, fever, shortness of breath, loss of smell, or body aches. On examination, she was hemodynamically stable. In neurological examination, she had slurred speech and weakness in the left upper and lower extremity but normal sensations. The rest of the neurological examination as well as chest, cardiovascular, abdominal, and musculoskeletal examination was normal. 

The emergency CT scan did not show any evidence of major territorial infarction, intracranial hemorrhage, or focal mass lesions. The patient was within the window of tissue plasminogen activator (TPA) for acute stroke and was treated with it. The patient was also screened for COVID-19 on admission as per hospital protocol and the result came back later as positive.

The patient was admitted to the ICU for monitoring and did not have any abnormal electrocardiogram (EKG) rhythm while in ICU. She had full recovery of her neurological symptoms within few hours without any bleeding complications. The patient was subsequently discharged in good health and advised home isolation.

Discharge Diagnosis: Ischemic Stroke with COVID-19

Discharge Instructions: 

The patient is advised to follow safe sleep and nutrition practices and to avoid stress. The patient should be advised not to engage in driving or other actions that require quick reflex. The patient should be advised to follow home isolation and avoid close contact with family members or friends. The patient should also be advised to monitor for any symptoms of COVID-19 and seek medical attention if needed.

Follow-up: The patient should be advised to follow up with the primary care provider or neurologist within one week of discharge.


Dictated and signed by: N/A

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 42 years
Hospital Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Diagnosis: Stroke caused by a blocked blood vessel in the brain

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the emergency room with slurred speech, dizziness, and weakness on the left side of her body. These symptoms started about two hours before she arrived. The patient did not have a cough, fever, trouble breathing, loss of smell, or body aches. When checked, her heart rate and blood pressure were stable. During the brain and nerve exam, she had slurred speech and weakness in her left arm and leg. Her sense of touch was normal. All other exams of her chest, heart, belly, and muscles were normal.

A brain scan done right away did not show any major damage, bleeding, or growths in the brain. The patient arrived early enough to receive a clot-busting medicine for her stroke. The patient was also tested for COVID-19 when she arrived, following hospital rules. The test result came back later and showed she had COVID-19.

The patient was moved to the intensive care unit so doctors could watch her closely. Her heart rhythm stayed normal while she was there. Her brain and nerve symptoms went away completely within a few hours. She did not have any bleeding problems. The patient was sent home in good health and told to stay home away from others.

Discharge Diagnosis: Stroke caused by a blocked blood vessel in the brain with COVID-19

Discharge Instructions:

The patient is advised to get good sleep, eat healthy foods, and avoid stress. The patient should not drive or do other things that need quick reactions. The patient should stay home and avoid close contact with family or friends. The patient should watch for any signs of COVID-19 and get medical help if needed.

Follow-up: The patient should see her regular doctor or brain specialist within one week after leaving the hospital.

input:
Discharge Summary

Admitted for: Humeral shaft fracture

Hospital course:

The patient was referred to our institution after falling on his right arm resulting in a transverse-oblique humeral shaft fracture. Upon admission, the arm was deformed but there was no neurovascular deficit. Closed reduction was performed and checked by imaging, followed by surgical treatment via anterolateral approach with an antegrade locked IMN using a reamed, unlocked technique. The patient was discharged with a Velpeau bandage after being in beach-chair position on the table.

Rehabilitation and Follow-up:

A rehabilitation program with Want exercises, isometric exercises, and strength training was initiated at 3 weeks after the surgery. The patient reported mild pain at the entry point of the nail during the whole 22-month follow-up period, which remained stable in severity. At the end of the 22 months post-injury, the patient performed daily living activities and demonstrated minimal impairment. The patient was evaluated using the disability of the arm, shoulder, and hand turkey (DASH-T), upper extremity functional index (UEFI), and short-form 36-questionnaire (SF-36) at the 2, 4, 6, 9, 16, and 22 month marks with an overall DASH-T score of 5.83%, UEFI score of 96%, and SF-36 score of 87.7%.

Overall, the patient was discharged with minimal impairment and would benefit from continued rehabilitation.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Admitted for: Broken upper arm bone

Hospital course:

The patient was sent to our hospital after falling on his right arm. This caused a break in the upper arm bone. When the patient arrived, the arm was out of shape but blood flow and nerve function were normal. The doctors moved the bone back into place and checked it with pictures. Then the patient had surgery through a cut on the front-outer part of the arm. A metal rod was placed inside the bone to hold it together. The patient was in a tilted sitting position during surgery. The patient left the hospital wearing a special arm sling.

Rehabilitation and Follow-up:

A recovery program started 3 weeks after surgery. This included gentle arm movements, muscle-tightening exercises, and strength training. The patient reported mild pain where the metal rod entered the bone. This pain lasted through the entire 22-month follow-up period but did not get worse. At the end of 22 months after the injury, the patient could do everyday tasks with very little trouble. The patient was tested using three different scoring tools at 2, 4, 6, 9, 16, and 22 months. These tools measure arm function and overall health. The final scores showed good arm function and quality of life. The overall arm disability score was 5.83 percent. The arm function score was 96 percent. The general health score was 87.7 percent.

Overall, the patient left the hospital with very little trouble using the arm. The patient would benefit from continued recovery exercises.

Admitted for: Broken upper arm bone

The patient was sent to our hospital after falling on his left arm. This caused a break in the upper arm bone. input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: ______________

Admission Date: ____________

Discharge Date: ____________

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our institution with progressive neurological symptoms. He had a history of seropositive Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) that was managed with maintenance methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisone. The patient developed fluctuating confusion, word-finding difficulty, right-sided weakness and gait imbalance. An evaluation was done by the neurologist which confirmed the presence of neurological deficits. An MRI scan showed areas of scattered restricted diffusion and enhancement within the cortex and leptomeninges of the left hemisphere near the vertex, suspicious for meningoencephalitis. CSF analysis also demonstrated lymphocytic meningitis with negative, bacterial and fungal cultures. 

The patient was started on antiepileptic medication and a whole-body PET scan was done to rule out underlying malignancy. A bone marrow biopsy was also done to rule out lymphoid neoplasm. An open meningeal biopsy was performed and gross examination revealed thickening and opacification of the meninges. The histopathological findings were consistent with leptomeningeal involvement by nodular rheumatoid meningitis.

Immunosuppressive therapy with monthly cyclophosphamide and high-dose corticosteroids was initiated for 6 months and then the corticosteroids were tapered. The patient's neurological examination improved one month post treatment initiation, although confusion and bilateral postural tremor persisted. Most parkinsonian features also largely resolved following immunosuppressive therapy. 

Repeat MRI 3 months after treatment initiation showed complete resolution of previous findings, and CRP levels also normalized. The patient was eventually transitioned to a long-term care facility due to the persistent behavioral and cognitive deficits with intermittent periods of agitation.

Diagnosis:
Leptomeningeal involvement by nodular rheumatoid meningitis

Course:
The patient was admitted for investigation of progressive neurological symptoms and was treated with immunosuppressive therapy. The patient's neurological examination improved one month post treatment initiation, although confusion and bilateral postural tremor persisted, and most parkinsonian features largely resolved following immunosuppressive therapy. Repeat MRI 3 months after treatment initiation showed complete resolution of previous findings.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has been transitioned to a long-term care facility due to persistent behavioral and cognitive deficits with intermittent periods of agitation.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: ______________

Admission Date: ____________

Discharge Date: ____________

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital with worsening brain and nerve problems. He had a history of rheumatoid arthritis, a condition where the body attacks its own joints. This was being treated with methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, and low-dose prednisone. The patient developed confusion that came and went, trouble finding words, weakness on the right side of the body, and trouble walking. A brain and nerve doctor did an exam and confirmed these problems. A brain scan called an MRI showed areas of swelling in the covering of the brain on the left side near the top. This looked like an infection or swelling of the brain lining. A test of the fluid around the brain and spine showed swelling with certain white blood cells. Tests for bacteria and fungus came back negative.

The patient was started on medicine to prevent seizures. A full body scan was done to check for cancer. A bone marrow test was also done to check for blood cancer. Doctors took a small piece of the brain covering to look at under a microscope. This showed the brain covering was thicker and cloudier than normal. The test results showed that rheumatoid arthritis was affecting the brain covering.

Treatment was started with cyclophosphamide given once a month and high doses of steroids for 6 months. Then the steroids were slowly reduced. The patient's brain and nerve exam got better one month after starting treatment. However, confusion and shaking in both hands when held up continued. Stiffness and slow movement also mostly went away after treatment.

A follow-up brain scan 3 months after starting treatment showed all the earlier problems had gone away. A blood test for swelling in the body also returned to normal. The patient was moved to a care home because of ongoing problems with thinking, behavior, and times of feeling upset or restless.

Diagnosis:
Rheumatoid arthritis affecting the covering of the brain

Course:
The patient was admitted to find the cause of worsening brain and nerve problems. The patient was treated with medicines that calm down the immune system. The patient's brain and nerve exam got better one month after starting treatment. However, confusion and shaking in both hands when held up continued. Stiffness and slow movement also mostly went away after treatment. A follow-up brain scan 3 months after starting treatment showed all the earlier problems had gone away.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has been moved to a care home because of ongoing problems with thinking, behavior, and times of feeling upset or restless.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital for the management of a giant hepatic tumor. She underwent a right hepatectomy during which two well-differentiated hepatocellular carcinomas (HCCs) were removed. The patient recovered well after the surgery and was discharged on postoperative day 17.

Past Medical History:
The patient had no significant past medical history.

Hospital Course:
The patient's hospital course was significant for a sensation of abdominal distension and loss of appetite, at which point she underwent abdominal ultrasonography that discovered a giant hepatic mass in the right lobe. Physical examination was significant for hepatomegaly. Liver function tests showed evidence of nonicteric cholestasis with mild transaminase enzyme level elevation. Screening for viral hepatitis yielded negative results. Levels of the liver-specific tumor marker "protein induced by vitamin K absence-II" were highly elevated. Abdominal ultrasonography showed a giant hypoechoic mass measuring 139 × 120 mm in the right lobe of the liver. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) demonstrated a huge mass that occupied the right lobe of the liver and showed inhomogeneous hyperenhancement in the arterial phase, as well as uniform isoenhancement in the portal venous phase. A low-concentration area indicated the presence of necrosis in the tumor center, and a radiological diagnosis of HCC was therefore posited. The surgical team also discovered a congenital absence of the portal vein that indicated the presence of congenital absence of the portal vein type II. 

Disposition and Follow-up:
The patient was discharged on postoperative day 17 and successfully treated for a small recurrent HCC nodule detected 7 months after the operation with radiofrequency ablation. The patient has been on close follow-up for 6 months without any evidence of further recurrences.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital to treat a very large liver tumor. She had surgery to remove the right side of her liver. During surgery, doctors removed two slow-growing liver cancers. The patient healed well after surgery and went home 17 days later.

Past Medical History:
The patient had no major health problems before this.

Hospital Course:
The patient felt bloating in her belly and did not want to eat. Doctors did an ultrasound of her belly and found a very large mass in the right side of her liver. A physical exam showed her liver was larger than normal. Blood tests showed bile was backing up in her liver but she was not yellow. Her liver enzyme levels were slightly high. Tests for liver infections came back negative. A blood test for a liver cancer marker showed very high levels. An ultrasound showed a very large dark mass in the right side of the liver measuring about 139 by 120 millimeters. A special scan with dye showed a huge mass taking up the right side of the liver. The mass lit up unevenly at first and then evenly as blood flowed through. A dark area in the center showed dead tissue inside the tumor. Based on these images, doctors believed it was liver cancer. The surgery team also found that the patient was born without a certain blood vessel in her liver called the portal vein type two.

Disposition and Follow-up:
The patient went home 17 days after surgery. Seven months later, doctors found a small new liver cancer spot. They treated it using heat therapy to destroy the cancer cells. The patient has been checked regularly for 6 months and shows no signs of more cancer coming back.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was a 43-year-old male with a 17-year history of paraplegia who presented with dark purple discoloration of the left second and third toes. He had been experiencing a cold sensation in both feet for the past year. The patient had a history of hypertension and was taking hydrochlorothiazide-valsartan, kallidinogenase, ranitidine, levosulpiride, phloroglucinol, and loxoprofen. Physical examination showed both feet were cold, although the pulse was palpable on ankles and heels bilaterally. A portable Doppler device showed a decrease in pulse signal intensity on the left side and prolonged capillary refilling time on both toes. Despite a vascular function test showing normal leg circulation levels, the skin over the left toes showed dark discoloration with skin necrosis. The patient's antihypertensive medication was switched to a calcium channel blocker, amlodipine besylate, to address suspicion of Raynaud's phenomenon. Within 3 days, the patient saw a decrease in cold sensation and normalization of capillary refilling time. A medium split-thickness skin graft was performed on the wound, which healed without complications.

Consultations:

A consultation was requested with vascular and plastic surgery, who recommended wound monitoring and a skin graft to address the skin necrosis.

Discharge Condition:

The patient was discharged improved after treatment of his toe discoloration and skin necrosis.

Discharge Medications:

The patient was prescribed amlodipine besylate.

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to follow-up with his primary care physician for further management of his hypertension and any changes to his medication.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient was a 43-year-old man who had been unable to move his legs for 17 years. He came to the hospital because his left second and third toes had turned dark purple. His feet had felt cold for about a year. The patient had high blood pressure. He was taking several medicines including a water pill, a blood pressure pill, a stomach medicine, and pain medicine. When doctors checked his feet, both feet felt cold to the touch. However, they could still feel a pulse at his ankles and heels on both sides. A device that checks blood flow showed weaker blood flow signals on the left side. Blood took longer than normal to return to the skin when pressed on both feet. A test of blood vessel function showed normal blood flow in the legs. But the skin on the left toes had turned dark and the tissue was dying. Doctors thought the patient might have a condition where blood vessels tighten too much in the cold. They changed his blood pressure medicine to a different type called amlodipine besylate. Within 3 days, his feet felt less cold. Blood flow to the skin returned to normal. Doctors did surgery to place healthy skin over the damaged area. The wound healed well without any problems.

Consultations:

Doctors who specialize in blood vessels and skin repair were asked to help. They suggested watching the wound closely and doing surgery to cover the damaged skin.

Discharge Condition:

The patient left the hospital in better health after treatment for his dark toe color and dying skin tissue.

Discharge Medications:

The patient was given amlodipine besylate to take at home.

Follow-up:

The patient was told to see his regular doctor. This visit is to check on his high blood pressure and any changes to his medicines.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient: [Patient Name]
DOB: [Patient Date of Birth]
Admission Date: [Admission Date]
Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to our institution with frequent migraines and nasal congestion. Physical exam revealed a polypoid mass emanating from the left frontal sinus outflow tract, which was diagnosed as a recurrent inverting papilloma of the left frontal and ethmoid sinuses. A resection of the tumor was performed with an endoscopic modified Lothrop approach and anterior ethmoidal artery ligation. The tumor was completely removed with no significant blood loss and no CSF leak.

Postoperatively, the patient experienced left sided eye pain and visual disturbance 12 hours after surgery, which was treated with one nitroglycerin sublingual tablet. Repeat fundoscopic examination after nitroglycerin treatment revealed a normal appearing retina and optic disk. The patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout her postoperative course and was discharged in stable condition.

Diagnosis:
Recurrent inverting papilloma of the left frontal and ethmoid sinuses

Treatment:
Endoscopic modified Lothrop with anterior ethmoidal artery ligation and resection of the tumor

Follow-up:
The patient will be followed up in the clinic for further evaluation and monitoring.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient: [Patient Name]
DOB: [Patient Date of Birth]
Admission Date: [Admission Date]
Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with frequent severe headaches and a stuffy nose. A physical exam showed a growth coming from the left sinus area near the forehead. The growth was found to be a type of non-cancerous tumor that had come back. This tumor was in the sinuses near the forehead and between the eyes on the left side. Doctors removed the tumor through the nose using a small camera and special tools. They also tied off a small blood vessel in the area. The tumor was fully removed with very little bleeding and no spinal fluid leaking.

After surgery, the patient had pain in the left eye and trouble seeing clearly about 12 hours later. This was treated with one pill placed under the tongue to help blood flow. A follow-up eye exam showed the back of the eye looked normal. The patient stayed stable throughout recovery and was sent home in good condition.

Diagnosis:
A returning non-cancerous tumor in the left sinuses near the forehead and between the eyes

Treatment:
Removal of the tumor through the nose using a small camera and tying off a small blood vessel

Follow-up:
The patient will return to the clinic for more check-ups and monitoring.

input:
Hospital Course Summary

Patient: Female, 83 years old

Admission date: TBD        Discharge date: Day 25

Admission diagnosis: Left pelvic hematoma

Past medical history: Osteoporosis, chronic atrial fibrillation

Hospital course: Upon admission to the ED, patient presented with acute abdominal pain in the left lower quadrant. A CT scan was performed, revealing a left pelvic hematoma neighboring the fracture site. Warfarin was withheld and anticoagulation was reversed using 2 units of fresh frozen plasma (FFP) and 5mg vitamin K. The patient was monitored closely and received 4 units of packed red blood cells and 2 units of FFP after a drop in hemoglobin levels. Throughout the hospital course, the patient remained stable with no evidence of recurrent pelvic hemorrhage. Abdominal ultrasonography performed 5 and 10 days after admission showed a progressive resorption of the hematoma. The patient was transferred to the cardiologic unit for a cardiac failure and discharged uneventfully for a cardiac rehabilitation program on day 25. Four months later, the patient returned to her pre-injury level of activity. 

Discharge instructions: The patient should continue her cardiac rehabilitation program. Follow up appointments with primary care doctor and cardiologist are recommended.

Signed,
Attending physician.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary

Patient: Female, 83 years old

Admission date: TBD        Discharge date: Day 25

Admission diagnosis: Blood clot in the left side of the pelvis

Past medical history: Weak bones, long-term irregular heartbeat

Hospital course: When the patient arrived at the emergency room, she had sudden belly pain on the left lower side. A scan of the body was done. It showed a blood clot in the left pelvis near the broken bone. The patient stopped taking her blood thinner medicine called Warfarin. The care team gave her 2 bags of plasma and a vitamin K shot to help her blood clot normally again. The care team watched the patient closely. Her blood count dropped, so she received 4 bags of red blood cells and 2 more bags of plasma. During the hospital stay, the patient stayed stable. There were no signs of new bleeding in the pelvis. Ultrasound pictures taken 5 and 10 days after arrival showed the blood clot was slowly going away. The patient was moved to the heart care unit because of heart failure. She left the hospital on day 25 to start a heart recovery program. Four months later, the patient was back to doing the same activities she did before her injury.

Discharge instructions: The patient should keep going to her heart recovery program. The patient should make follow up visits with her regular doctor and heart doctor.

Signed,
Attending physician.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 71
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: December 2009
Date of Discharge: March 2012
Reason for Admission: Dyspnea, right-sided chest wall and upper quadrant abdominal pain, pulmonary embolus, multiple coalesced masses in the liver, and AFP significantly elevated.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the Emergency Department in December 2009 with dyspnea, right-sided chest wall, and upper quadrant abdominal pain. He was diagnosed with pulmonary embolus and multiple coalesced masses in the liver with the largest measuring 6 cm x 9 cm x 9 cm in the right lobe of the liver. The pathology from the biopsy was diagnostic of primary HCC, and he was diagnosed with Stage IV T3N0M1 disease. He underwent 70 Gy treatment in 15 fractions to the liver, which was completed in April 2010. Subsequent follow-up imaging showed that the patient's largest liver lesion had decreased in size from over 14 cm to 3 cm, and his pulmonary metastases had completely resolved. From August 2010 till March 2012, the man enjoyed stable disease with normal liver laboratory results and no imaging evidence of disease recurrence. In March of 2012, a small recurrent nodule was found in the right liver, and he underwent hepatic artery embolization with doxorubicin and cisplatin in May of 2012, which resulted in a complete response at that location.

Medical Care:
The patient received radiotherapy alone for the liver lesions, and hepatic artery embolization with doxorubicin and cisplatin for the small recurrent nodule on the right liver.

Diagnostic Evaluation:
The patient underwent diagnostic evaluation in the form of imaging, such as computed tomography and biopsy, which aided in the diagnosis and monitoring of his liver lesions and pulmonary metastases.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient was stable, asymptomatic with a decrease in AFP from 11 460 µg/L to 3.1 µg/L, and no imaging evidence of disease recurrence at the time of his discharge in March 2012.

Follow-Up:
The patient was advised to follow-up with his physician regularly for clinical evaluations and imaging studies for monitoring purposes.

Discharge Medications:
Not applicable.

Disposition:
The patient was discharged with improvement in his condition, normal liver laboratory results, and no imaging evidence of disease recurrence.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 71
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: December 2009
Date of Discharge: March 2012
Reason for Admission: Shortness of breath, pain in the right side of the chest and upper belly area, a blood clot in the lungs, several large tumors joined together in the liver, and a liver cancer blood marker that was much higher than normal.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the Emergency Department in December 2009 with shortness of breath and pain in the right side of his chest and upper belly. Doctors found a blood clot in his lungs and several large tumors joined together in his liver. The biggest tumor measured about 6 cm x 9 cm x 9 cm and was in the right side of the liver. A tissue sample test showed the patient had liver cancer that started in the liver. The cancer was at an advanced stage and had spread to other parts of the body. He received radiation treatment to the liver, which ended in April 2010. Later scans showed the largest liver tumor had shrunk from over 14 cm to 3 cm. The cancer that had spread to his lungs was completely gone. From August 2010 to March 2012, the patient stayed healthy with normal liver test results and no signs of cancer coming back. In March 2012, doctors found a small tumor had returned in the right side of the liver. In May 2012, he had a treatment where doctors blocked blood flow to the tumor and gave cancer drugs directly to it. This treatment worked completely at that spot.

Medical Care:
The patient received radiation treatment alone for the liver tumors. For the small tumor that came back in the right liver, he had a treatment that blocked blood flow to the tumor and delivered cancer drugs directly to it.

Diagnostic Evaluation:
The patient had tests including body scans and a tissue sample test. These tests helped doctors find and track the liver tumors and the cancer that had spread to the lungs.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient was stable and had no symptoms when he left the hospital in March 2012. His liver cancer blood marker dropped from 11 460 to 3.1. Scans showed no signs of cancer coming back.

Follow-Up:
The patient was told to see his doctor regularly for checkups and scans to watch for any changes.

Discharge Medications:
Not applicable.

Disposition:
The patient left the hospital in better health. His liver test results were normal. Scans showed no signs of cancer coming back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]                 Hospital Medical Record Number: [redacted]

Hospital Course:

 Admission Date: [redacted]       Discharge Date: [redacted]        Length of Stay: 14 days

Diagnosis on Admission: Giant aneurysm in the cavernous portion of the right Internal Carotid Artery (ICA)

Treatment/Procedure: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA), Balloon Occlusion Test (BOT), External carotid artery-saphenous vein graft-middle cerebral artery (M2) bypass, Intraoperative DSA, Intraoperative Ligations, 250 mL of 20% Mannitol, Hyperventilation, 1 mL of 0.02% Nicardipine hydrochloride and skull flap removed.

Outcome: The patient's condition improved after the treatment. CT angiography revealed disappearance of the aneurysm and good patency of the bypass with the cranial defect.

Discharge Condition: Stable

Discharge Medications:

Aspirin

Instructions: 

The patient is advised to continue taking aspirin for life and to follow up regularly with a neurologist. 

The patient should also avoid activities that can cause increased pressure in the skull such as lifting heavy objects, sneezing, coughing, or straining during bowel movements. 

The patient should watch out for any signs of recurrence or worsening of symptoms, such as headache, double vision, or neck pain, and should seek immediate medical attention if any of these appear. 

The patient and family should understand the importance of adhering to the recommended follow-up schedule and should be aware of the potentially life-threatening nature of the patient's condition.

Follow-up Plan:

The patient will be followed up regularly with a neurologist to monitor for recurrence of the aneurysm or any other related symptoms.

Discharge Disposition: The patient was discharged to home with family support. The patient's condition is considered stable at the time of discharge.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]                 Hospital Medical Record Number: [redacted]

Hospital Course:

Admission Date: [redacted]       Discharge Date: [redacted]        Length of Stay: 14 days

Diagnosis on Admission: A very large bulge in a blood vessel on the right side of the brain

Treatment/Procedure: The patient had special X-rays of the blood vessels. Doctors tested if blocking one blood vessel was safe. A new blood flow path was created using a vein from the leg to go around the problem area. X-rays were taken during surgery. Some blood vessels were tied off. Medicine was given to reduce brain swelling. Breathing was controlled to protect the brain. Medicine was given to relax blood vessels. Part of the skull bone was removed.

Outcome: The patient got better after treatment. A scan showed the bulge was gone. The new blood flow path is working well. There is an opening in the skull where bone was removed.

Discharge Condition: Stable

Discharge Medications:

Aspirin

Instructions:

The patient is advised to take aspirin for the rest of their life. The patient should see a brain doctor regularly.

The patient should avoid things that raise pressure in the head. This includes lifting heavy things, sneezing hard, coughing hard, or pushing hard during bathroom use.

The patient should watch for warning signs that the problem may be coming back or getting worse. These signs include headaches, seeing double, or neck pain. The patient should get medical help right away if any of these happen.

The patient and family should know how important it is to keep all follow-up visits. They should know that the patient's condition could become life-threatening.

Follow-up Plan:

The patient will see a brain doctor regularly. The doctor will check for any return of the blood vessel bulge or other related problems.

Discharge Disposition: The patient went home with family to help with care. The patient's condition is stable at this time.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Medical History: 
The patient is a 45-year-old man who underwent nephrectomy and lymphadenectomy due to clear-cell carcinoma of the right kidney in November 2014. They later developed pulmonary metastases and underwent treatment with sunitinib in July 2015, and radiation therapy in May 2016 for a single pulmonary lesion. In February 2019, the patient switched to cabozantinib due to disease progression, and then to nivolumab. 

Course of Hospitalization: 
The patient was admitted for consideration of experimental treatment with immune checkpoint inhibitors. In November 2019, they underwent a biopsy of a lung nodule to participate in a clinical trial. The biopsy confirmed the diagnosis of clear-cell RCC, and the patient started treatment with anti-CD73 monoclonal antibody. They were discharged home with instructions on monitoring and reporting any adverse reactions.

Hospital Course: 
The patient initially showed stable disease according to RECIST 1.1 in February 2020, but later reported the appearance of subcutaneous nodules in the thoracic wall and an enlarging nodule in the second intercostal space. As a result, the patient discontinued the treatment and was referred to their local hospital.

Condition at Discharge: 
The patient's condition is stable at the time of discharge. They are advised to follow up with their local hospital and adhere to all treatment plans. 

Instructions: 
1. Follow up with the local hospital for further management. 
2. Adhere to all treatment plans provided by the local hospital. 
3. Report any adverse reactions or changes in health status to your healthcare provider immediately.

Discharge Medications: N/A

Follow-up Appointment: N/A

Primary Care Provider: [REDACTED]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Medical History:
The patient is a 45-year-old man. In November 2014, the patient had surgery to remove the right kidney and nearby lymph nodes. This was done because of a type of kidney cancer called clear-cell cancer. Later, the cancer spread to the lungs. In July 2015, the patient started taking a cancer drug called sunitinib. In May 2016, the patient had radiation treatment for one spot in the lung. In February 2019, the cancer got worse. The patient switched to a different cancer drug called cabozantinib. After that, the patient switched to another drug called nivolumab.

Course of Hospitalization:
The patient came to the hospital to see if a new study treatment might help. This treatment works by boosting the body's own defense system to fight cancer. In November 2019, the patient had a small tissue sample taken from a lung spot. This was needed to join a research study. The sample showed the patient still had clear-cell kidney cancer. The patient then started a study drug that helps the immune system attack cancer cells. The patient went home with instructions on what to watch for and when to call the doctor.

Hospital Course:
In February 2020, tests showed the cancer had not grown or shrunk. Later, the patient noticed new lumps under the skin on the chest wall. A lump between the ribs also got bigger. Because of this, the patient stopped the study treatment. The patient was sent to their local hospital for more care.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient is in stable condition at the time of leaving the hospital. The patient should follow up with the local hospital. The patient should follow all treatment plans.

Instructions:
1. Follow up with the local hospital for ongoing care.
2. Follow all treatment plans given by the local hospital.
3. Tell the healthcare provider right away about any bad reactions or changes in health.

Discharge Medications: None

Follow-up Appointment: None

Primary Care Provider: [REDACTED]

input:
Discharge Summary for Patient with Achalasia Cardia

Demographics:
- Patient is a 43-year-old Indian man

Reason for Admission:
- Patient was referred to the gastroenterology department because of dysphagia for both solids and liquids, recurrent vomiting, regurgitating undigested food, and considerable weight loss.

Medical History:
- No significant medical history was reported.

Hospital Course:
- Ultrasound scan of the abdomen showed normal findings.
- Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy showed a dilated esophageal body and no peristalsis was seen, while the lower esophageal sphincter was tightly closed.
- Retroflexion of the endoscope revealed no mass lesion in the esophagogastric junction or in the cardia.
- Chest X-ray showed an air bubble below the left hemi-diaphragm in the gastric region.
- Barium swallow showed dilated esophagus, "bird beak" appearance of the distal esophagus, and an esophageal air fluid level.
- Diagnosis of primary achalasia cardia was made based on clinical history, examination, upper gastrointestinal endoscopy, and barium swallow X-ray.
- Pneumatic balloon dilatation of the lower esophageal sphincter was performed successfully under sedation with no complications.
- Chest X-ray after the procedure showed two air bubbles under the left hemi-diaphragm in the gastric region, the first of which was confirmed as a colonic air bubble and the second as a gastric air bubble.

Discharge Status:
- Patient was discharged after a successful pneumatic balloon dilatation of the lower esophageal sphincter with no complications.

Instructions:
- Follow up with the primary care physician for further management of achalasia cardia.
- Provide a detailed report of this hospital course to the primary care physician.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary for Patient with Achalasia Cardia (a swallowing disorder where the food tube cannot move food into the stomach properly)

Demographics:
The patient is a 43-year-old Indian man.

Reason for Admission:
The patient was sent to the stomach and digestive system department. The patient had trouble swallowing both solid foods and liquids. The patient also had repeated vomiting, bringing up undigested food, and major weight loss.

Medical History:
The patient had no important past health problems.

Hospital Course:
A sound wave scan of the belly showed normal results. A camera test to look inside the upper digestive system showed the food tube was wider than normal. The food tube was not squeezing to push food down. The valve at the bottom of the food tube was closed too tightly. The doctor turned the camera around to check for any growths where the food tube meets the stomach. No growths were found. A chest X-ray showed an air bubble below the left side of the muscle that helps with breathing, near the stomach area. A special X-ray test using a chalky drink showed the food tube was wider than normal. The bottom of the food tube had a narrow pointed shape. There was also air and fluid sitting in the food tube. Based on the patient's symptoms, exam, camera test, and X-ray tests, the doctors found the patient has a swallowing disorder called primary achalasia cardia. The doctors used a balloon to stretch open the tight valve at the bottom of the food tube. The patient was given medicine to sleep during this procedure. There were no problems during the procedure. A chest X-ray after the procedure showed two air bubbles below the left side of the breathing muscle near the stomach area. Doctors confirmed one bubble was from the large intestine and one was from the stomach.

Discharge Status:
The patient went home after a successful balloon stretching of the tight valve at the bottom of the food tube. There were no problems.

Instructions:
The patient should see the regular doctor for ongoing care of this swallowing disorder. The patient should give a full report of this hospital stay to the regular doctor.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 44-year-old Hispanic male who was admitted for the evaluation of bilateral ocular inflammation in the setting of treatment with ipilimumab for metastatic cutaneous melanoma. He had received his third infusion two months prior to presentation, which led to systemic inflammatory side effects from therapy including rash, diarrhea, fever, and persistent rectal abscesses.

Physical Exam:
Upon presentation, the patient had central scotoma in the left eye, and both eyes showed pigment on the endothelium without keratic precipitates. Bilateral optic nerve edema with Paton’s lines and macular edema were noted on fundus examination. Cystic intraretinal and subfoveal fluid was also identified during Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT).

Treatment:
The patient was initially treated with topical therapy with prednisolone acetate and was referred to the uveitis service for further evaluation. Bilateral prednisolone acetate 1% was started four times a day with 0.2% brimonidine tartrate-0.5% timolol maleate ophthalmic solution twice a day and increased to 80 mg of oral prednisone daily. The patient was continually monitored and had his medication doses adjusted to promote healing.

Improvement and Discharge:
Over the course of following three weeks, his vision steadily improved with resolution of the optic nerve and retinal edema and subsequent development of a macular star. At the 2-month follow-up, his vision in both eyes improved to 20/25 and his anterior chamber reaction stabilized at 1+ flare and rare cell while taking prednisolone acetate four times a day. The patient was then lost to follow-up.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 44-year-old Hispanic male who was admitted for eye swelling in both eyes. This happened while he was being treated with a cancer drug called ipilimumab for skin cancer that had spread to other parts of his body. He had received his third treatment two months before coming to the hospital. The cancer treatment caused side effects throughout his body including a skin rash, loose stools, fever, and ongoing sores near his bottom.

Physical Exam:
When the patient arrived, he had a blind spot in the center of his left eye. Both eyes showed color deposits on the inner surface of the clear front layer of the eye. There were no clumps of cells stuck to this layer. The doctor looked at the back of both eyes and found swelling of the nerve that connects the eye to the brain. There were also curved lines around this nerve caused by the swelling. The central part of the back of the eye was also swollen. A special scan of the eye found small pockets of fluid in and under the center of the back of the eye.

Treatment:
The patient was first treated with steroid eye drops called prednisolone acetate. He was then sent to doctors who specialize in eye swelling for more care. He was given prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops for both eyes four times a day. He also received eye drops twice a day to lower eye pressure. These drops contained brimonidine tartrate 0.2% and timolol maleate 0.5%. His treatment was increased to include 80 mg of prednisone pills taken by mouth each day. The doctors kept checking on the patient and changed his medicine amounts to help him heal.

Improvement and Discharge:
Over the next three weeks, the patient's vision got steadily better. The swelling in the eye nerve and back of the eye went away. A star-shaped pattern then appeared in the central part of the back of the eye. At a check-up two months later, his vision in both eyes improved to nearly normal at 20/25. The swelling inside the front of his eyes became stable with only mild signs of irritation while using prednisolone acetate eye drops four times a day. The patient then stopped coming to his appointments.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:
Patient Name: [Patient Name]
Medical Record Number: [Medical Record Number]
Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with increasing lower abdominal pain, flank pain, and dysuria for three months. Upon physical examination, the abdomen was slightly rigid, and tenderness and rebound tenderness were observed in the lower left quadrant of the abdomen. Empirical antibiotics were administered immediately with the clinical impression of an intra-abdominal abscess. The patient underwent an explorative laparotomy to determine the definite diagnosis.

FINDINGS:
An extensive, diffusely infiltrating lesion-like mass involving the small bowel, mesentery, and sigmoid colon was observed during the surgery. A sigmoid colon perforation was also detected. Pathology revealed an intense chronic inflammatory reaction.

TREATMENT:
Lysis of adhesions, pus drainage, and sigmoid colon segmentectomy were performed. After 12 weeks of penicillin use, the patient's clinical manifestations and radiologic findings were improved.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
The patient was diagnosed with a diffuse infiltrative lesion-like mass involving the small bowel, mesentery, and sigmoid colon, and with a sigmoid colon perforation.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
At discharge, the patient's condition was stable. There was no recurrence observed during follow-up visits.

FOLLOW-UP CARE:
A follow-up visit is recommended six months from the discharge date to monitor the patient's condition for any potential recurrence.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:
Patient Name: [Patient Name]
Medical Record Number: [Medical Record Number]
Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came to the hospital with pain in the lower belly and side, along with painful urination for three months. During the exam, the belly was a bit stiff and painful to touch, especially on the lower left side. The patient was given antibiotics right away because doctors thought there might be an infection with pus inside the belly. The patient had surgery to open the belly and find out what was wrong.

FINDINGS:
During surgery, doctors found a large area of damaged tissue spreading through the small intestine, the tissue holding the intestines, and the lower part of the large intestine. There was also a hole in the lower part of the large intestine. Tests on the tissue showed serious long-term swelling and irritation.

TREATMENT:
Doctors cut away scar tissue that had grown together, drained the pus, and removed the damaged part of the lower large intestine. The patient took penicillin for 12 weeks. After this, the patient's symptoms and imaging tests showed improvement.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
The patient was found to have a large area of damaged tissue spreading through the small intestine, the tissue holding the intestines, and the lower part of the large intestine. The patient also had a hole in the lower part of the large intestine.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
The patient was in stable condition when leaving the hospital. No signs of the problem coming back were seen during follow-up visits.

FOLLOW-UP CARE:
The patient should return for a check-up six months after leaving the hospital. This visit is to make sure the problem has not come back.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Identification:
Name: [REDACTED]
Sex: Female
Age: 65 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:
Left middle cerebral artery territory infarction, mobile mass in prosthetic mitral valve leaflet

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with a history of left middle cerebral artery territory infarction and a mobile mass in the prosthetic mitral valve leaflet. She was started on heparin to prevent embolic events and decrease the size of the thrombus, but no change was observed. Laboratory examinations were ordered, and the patient was eventually diagnosed with antiphospholipid syndrome (APS).

MV leaflet mass excision was performed, with the patient receiving routine induction and maintenance of anesthesia with IV anesthetics. CPB was initiated with adequate doses of heparin administered. The patient was transferred to the ICU post-surgery, and her bleeding tendency warranted the administration of protamine.

Postoperative PT/INR was over 2 and aPTT was over 400. The patient's bleeding tendency was managed with protamine administration, and warfarin was used for prophylactic anticoagulation. The patient was discharged after 9 days post-operation.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Left middle cerebral artery territory infarction, mobile mass in prosthetic mitral valve leaflet, antiphospholipid syndrome

Discharge Condition:
Stable

Discharge Instructions:
- The patient is advised to continue taking warfarin for prophylactic anticoagulation
- Any changes in medication or treatment plan should be discussed with her physician
- The patient is advised to follow-up with a specialist for management of antiphospholipid syndrome
- In case of any complications or worsening of symptoms, the patient should immediately seek medical attention

Follow-Up Appointments:
[REDACTED]
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Identification:
Name: [REDACTED]
Sex: Female
Age: 65 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:
Stroke in the left side of the brain, moving blood clot on the replacement heart valve

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital after having a stroke in the left side of her brain. She also had a moving blood clot on her replacement heart valve. Doctors gave her a blood thinner called heparin to stop more clots and shrink the existing one. The blood clot did not get smaller. Blood tests were done. Doctors found out the patient has a condition where the blood clots too easily. This condition is called antiphospholipid syndrome.

Doctors did surgery to remove the blood clot from the heart valve. The patient was given medicine through a vein to make her sleep during surgery. A heart-lung machine was used to pump blood during the operation. The patient received blood thinning medicine during surgery. After surgery, the patient went to the intensive care unit. She was bleeding too much, so doctors gave her medicine called protamine to help stop the bleeding.

After surgery, blood tests showed the blood was too thin. Doctors gave more protamine to control the bleeding. The patient was given a blood thinner pill called warfarin to prevent future clots. The patient left the hospital 9 days after surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Stroke in the left side of the brain, moving blood clot on replacement heart valve, a condition that causes blood to clot too easily called antiphospholipid syndrome

Discharge Condition:
Stable

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should keep taking warfarin to prevent blood clots.
The patient should talk to her doctor before making any changes to medicines or treatment.
The patient should see a specialist to help manage her blood clotting condition.
The patient should get medical help right away if she has any problems or feels worse.

Follow-Up Appointments:
[REDACTED]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]

Age: 51

Sex: Female

Admission date: [redacted]

Discharge date: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: Solitary fibrous tumor of the pleura (SFTP)

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted with biopsy-proven right-sided SFTP and worsening dyspnea on exertion and hypoglycemia. Imaging studies revealed a large heterogeneous mass in the right pleural space, causing collapse of the pulmonary vasculature, cardiac shift and compression of the superior and inferior vena cava. Surgical resection via clamshell sternotomy was planned. A balanced anesthetic was maintained throughout the case and planned exposure of the right femoral artery and vein was performed prior to clamshell incision should cannulation for extracorporeal support be required. Clamshell exposure revealed focal, dense adhesions between the mass and the right upper lobe. Wedge resections of these adhesions resulted in hemorrhage requiring massive transfusion. The following shock states required active management during the case: Obstructive, hypovolemic, and distributive. After control of bleeding and appropriate volume resuscitation, the patient remained hypotensive and required vasoactive infusions. In addition, the patient's intraoperative management was complicated by profound hypoglycemia which required ongoing dextrose therapy until mass resection. The case proceeded with successful surgical resection, and the patient was transported under sedation to the intensive care unit.

Discharge Diagnosis: SFTP

Course in Hospital: The patient's course in the hospital was complicated by massive transfusion requiring management of hypotensive states, distributive shock and hypoglycemia. However, with adequate resuscitation and successful surgical resection, the patient's condition improved. The patient was extubated on day 1 and discharged on day 13 without any complications.

Follow-up: The patient was discharged with follow-up CT imaging scheduled to monitor for residual disease. The patient should also schedule follow-up appointments with her primary care physician and oncologist for further management and surveillance of her condition.

Medications: N/A

Allergies: N/A

Procedures: Clamshell sternotomy with surgical resection of SFTP

Condition at Discharge: Stable

Plan: Follow-up imaging with primary care physician and oncologist.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]

Age: 51

Sex: Female

Admission date: [redacted]

Discharge date: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: A rare tumor growing in the lining of the lung on the right side

Hospital Course: The patient came to the hospital with a confirmed tumor in the right lung lining. The patient had trouble breathing during activity and low blood sugar. Scans showed a large uneven mass in the right chest area. The mass was pressing on blood vessels, shifting the heart, and squeezing major veins. Doctors planned surgery to remove the tumor through a large chest incision. The patient was given anesthesia to stay asleep during surgery. Doctors prepared blood vessels in the upper leg in case a heart-lung bypass machine was needed. When doctors opened the chest, they found the tumor was stuck to parts of the right lung. Cutting away these stuck areas caused heavy bleeding that needed many blood transfusions. During surgery, the medical team had to treat three types of dangerous drops in blood flow. These were caused by blockage, blood loss, and widened blood vessels. After stopping the bleeding and replacing fluids, the patient still had low blood pressure. The patient needed medicines to raise blood pressure. The patient also had very low blood sugar during surgery. This needed sugar given through an IV until the tumor was removed. The surgery was successful, and the patient was moved to intensive care while still sedated.

Discharge Diagnosis: A rare tumor in the lining of the lung

Course in Hospital: The patient needed many blood transfusions during the hospital stay. The medical team treated low blood pressure, poor blood flow, and low blood sugar. After proper treatment and successful tumor removal, the patient got better. The breathing tube was removed on day 1. The patient left the hospital on day 13 without any problems.

Follow-up: The patient will have a CT scan to check if any tumor remains. The patient should also make appointments with the regular doctor and cancer doctor. These doctors will continue to watch over the patient's health.

Medications: None

Allergies: None

Procedures: Large chest incision surgery to remove the rare lung lining tumor

Condition at Discharge: Stable

Plan: Follow-up imaging with regular doctor and cancer doctor.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:
Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 67 years
Sex: Female

Chief Complaint:
Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon. Right hemicolectomy was performed, and the pathology confirmed stage III (pT3N1) adenocarcinoma. Adjuvant chemotherapy was initiated but was discontinued after four of eight cycles due to severe neuropathy. After completion of adjuvant treatment, the patient underwent active surveillance. In November 2012, a recurrence near the right psoas muscle was identified and excised in December. Immunohistochemical workup showed an abnormal mismatch repair profile with loss of MLH1 protein. The patient underwent radiotherapy concurrent with capecitabine, but again, significant neutropenia necessitated a capecitabine dose reduction. In October 2013, the patient relapsed with disease in the L3 spinous process and received 42 Gy of stereotactic radiotherapy in 10 fractions. In June 2014, the tumor recurred at the same site, and the patient underwent palliative resection of the mass.

Enter Personalized OncoGenomics (POG) initiative:
The patient consented to genomic analysis of the tumor resected from the L3 spinous process by the POG initiative at the BC Cancer Agency. The genomic analysis revealed overexpression of FOS and JUN genes that encode the AP-1 transcriptional complex. Immunohistochemical workup confirmed robust expression of c-JUN protein. It was suggested that irbesartan could be a potential option due to the blockade of the renin–angiotensin pathway.

Treatment:
The patient had a pretreatment baseline PET/CT scan and started irbesartan at a dose of 150 mg daily in December 2014. After 5 weeks of therapy, a follow-up PET/CT was performed, and there was virtually a complete functional radiological resolution of her disease. CEA levels decreased from 18 to 3.1.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has been discharged, and her condition is stable. Follow-up visits have been scheduled, and medication and dietary instructions have been provided.

Medications:
Irbesartan 150 mg daily.

Follow-up:
Follow-up appointments have been scheduled. The patient has been advised to adhere to a healthy diet and lifestyle and continue medication as prescribed.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Information:
Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 67 years
Sex: Female

Chief Complaint:
Medium-grade colon cancer in the right side of the colon.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with medium-grade cancer in the right side of the colon. Surgery was done to remove the right part of the colon. Lab tests confirmed stage III cancer that had spread to nearby lymph nodes. The patient started cancer-killing drug treatment but had to stop after four of eight rounds. This was because of serious nerve damage in the hands and feet. After finishing treatment, the patient had regular check-ups to watch for cancer return. In November 2012, the cancer came back near a muscle in the lower back area. It was removed by surgery in December. Special lab tests showed a problem with how the body fixes damaged genes. The patient received radiation treatment along with a cancer pill called capecitabine. But the patient's white blood cell count dropped too low. The dose of capecitabine had to be lowered. In October 2013, the cancer came back in a bone in the lower spine. The patient received focused radiation treatment in 10 sessions. In June 2014, the cancer returned in the same spot. The patient had surgery to remove the growth to help with symptoms.

Enter Personalized OncoGenomics (POG) initiative:
The patient agreed to have the tumor from the spine tested for gene changes. This was done by the POG program at the BC Cancer Agency. The gene tests showed that certain genes called FOS and JUN were too active. These genes control cell growth. Lab tests confirmed high levels of a protein called c-JUN. Doctors suggested trying a blood pressure drug called irbesartan. This drug might help by blocking a body system that could be helping the cancer grow.

Treatment:
The patient had a full body scan before starting treatment. The patient began taking irbesartan at 150 mg once a day in December 2014. After 5 weeks of treatment, another full body scan was done. The scan showed the cancer was almost completely gone. A blood test that measures cancer activity dropped from 18 to 3.1.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Medium-grade colon cancer in the right side of the colon.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has been discharged, and her condition is stable. Follow-up visits have been scheduled. Instructions for medicine and diet have been given.

Medications:
Irbesartan 150 mg once a day.

Follow-up:
Follow-up appointments have been scheduled. The patient has been told to eat healthy foods and live a healthy lifestyle. The patient should keep taking medicine as directed.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

PATIENT INFORMATION:
Name: [insert name]
Gender: Male
Age: 58 years old
Race: Korean
Medical record number: [insert number]
Admitting Diagnosis: Progressive Neuropathy

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient was admitted with complaints of involuntary facial twitching and weakness, loose skin, and a deep furrowed tongue which had been progressively worsening for four years. The symptoms resulted in paresthesia of the right hand, intermittent cramps on the right calf, and decreased sweating on both hands. Neurological exam revealed mild weakness in the orbicularis oculi and orbicularis oris muscles without any motor weakness or sensory changes observed in the four extremities. A family history of the same symptoms was found in his mother, maternal uncle, and two sisters but no non-Korean relatives. Electrophysiological studies, including nerve conduction study (NCS), electromyography (EMG), and autonomic function tests (AFTs), were performed which demonstrated signs of bilateral facial nerve dysfunction, chronic denervation of the cervical and lumbar regions, and sudomotor dysfunction. To identify the etiology of the undiagnosed neuropathy, whole-exome sequencing (WES) was performed following informed consent and it revealed the presence of a p.D214Y mutation in GSN.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Progressive neuropathy with associated mutation in GSN

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
[No medication information was provided in the case report]

FOLLOW-UP RECOMMENDATIONS:
Follow-up with primary care physician recommended.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

PATIENT INFORMATION:
Name: [insert name]
Gender: Male
Age: 58 years old
Race: Korean
Medical record number: [insert number]
Admitting Diagnosis: Nerve damage that is getting worse over time

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came to the hospital with face twitching and weakness that he could not control. He also had loose skin and deep grooves on his tongue. These problems had been getting worse for four years. The symptoms caused tingling in his right hand and muscle cramps in his right calf. He also noticed less sweating on both hands. The doctor checked his nerves and found mild weakness in the muscles around his eyes and mouth. There was no weakness or feeling changes in his arms or legs. His mother, uncle on his mother's side, and two sisters have the same symptoms. No family members who are not Korean have these symptoms. The doctors did tests to check how his nerves and muscles work. These tests showed problems with the nerves on both sides of his face. The tests also showed long-term nerve damage in his neck and lower back areas. There were also problems with his sweat glands. To find the cause of his nerve damage, the doctors did a special blood test to look at his genes. The patient agreed to this test. The test found a change in a gene called GSN.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Nerve damage that is getting worse over time, caused by a change in the GSN gene

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
[No medication information was provided in the case report]

FOLLOW-UP RECOMMENDATIONS:
Follow-up with primary care physician recommended.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: October 2004
Date of Discharge: October 2007
Length of Stay: 3 years

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 67-year-old male, was admitted to our hospital in October 2004 with symptoms and signs of intestinal obstruction, which were later diagnosed as stage III adenocarcinoma of the rectosigmoid colon. He underwent a subtotal colectomy and was given six cycles of adjuvant chemotherapy. The pathology report revealed ten peri-tumoral and mesenteric lymph nodes with extracapsular extension.

In December 2006, a total body CT scan identified an enlarged right paratracheal lymph node. A fluor-18-deoxyglucose-positron emission tomography (18F-FDG PET) scan ruled out tumoral metastasis, and the patient was closely monitored.

In October 2007, a follow-up CT scan revealed that the previously identified lymph node had increased in size, and a mediastinoscopic biopsy confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma of colonic origin. The patient did not experience any gastrointestinal or respiratory symptoms throughout the follow-up period. Ca19-9 and CEA serum levels remained within the normal range.

The patient was discharged in October 2007.

Diagnoses:
- Stage III adenocarcinoma of rectosigmoid colon with lymph node metastasis

Procedures:
- Subtotal colectomy
- Mediastinoscopic biopsy

Follow-up:
- The patient is advised to continue routine follow-up and monitoring of his condition.

Consultations:
- Oncology

Summary:
- The patient was admitted to our hospital with symptoms and signs of intestinal obstruction, which were diagnosed as stage III adenocarcinoma of the rectosigmoid colon. He underwent a subtotal colectomy and was given six cycles of adjuvant chemotherapy. The pathology report revealed ten peri-tumoral and mesenteric lymph nodes with extracapsular extension. In December 2006, an enlarged right paratracheal lymph node was identified, and a fluor-18-deoxyglucose-positron emission tomography (18F-FDG PET) scan ruled out tumoral metastasis. In October 2007, a follow-up CT scan revealed that the previously identified lymph node had increased in size, and a mediastinoscopic biopsy confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma of colonic origin. The patient did not experience any gastrointestinal or respiratory symptoms throughout the follow-up period. Ca19-9 and CEA serum levels remained within the normal range. The patient was discharged in October 2007 and advised to continue routine follow-up and monitoring of his condition.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: October 2004
Date of Discharge: October 2007
Length of Stay: 3 years

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 67-year-old man. He came to the hospital in October 2004 with a blocked intestine. Tests showed he had colon cancer that had spread to nearby areas. This is called stage III cancer. The cancer was in the part of the colon near the rectum. He had surgery to remove most of his colon. After surgery, he received six rounds of cancer-fighting medicine to kill any remaining cancer cells. Lab tests found cancer had spread to ten small glands near the tumor. The cancer had grown beyond the walls of these glands.

In December 2006, a body scan found a swollen gland near the windpipe on the right side. A special scan that detects cancer showed no signs the cancer had spread. The doctors watched the patient closely.

In October 2007, another scan showed the swollen gland had grown larger. Doctors took a small tissue sample from the chest area. Tests confirmed the cancer had spread from the colon to this gland. The patient did not have any stomach, bowel, or breathing problems during this time. Blood tests that can detect cancer stayed normal.

The patient left the hospital in October 2007.

Diagnoses:
Stage III colon cancer near the rectum that spread to the glands

Procedures:
Surgery to remove most of the colon
Tissue sample taken from the chest area

Follow-up:
The patient should keep seeing the doctor for regular checkups.

Consultations:
Cancer specialists

Summary:
The patient came to the hospital with a blocked intestine. Tests showed stage III colon cancer near the rectum. He had surgery to remove most of his colon. He received six rounds of cancer-fighting medicine. Lab tests showed cancer had spread to ten small glands near the tumor. The cancer had grown beyond the walls of these glands. In December 2006, a scan found a swollen gland near the windpipe. A special cancer scan showed no spread at that time. In October 2007, another scan showed the gland had grown. A tissue sample confirmed the cancer had spread from the colon. The patient had no stomach, bowel, or breathing problems during this time. Blood tests that can detect cancer stayed normal. The patient left the hospital in October 2007. He should keep seeing the doctor for regular checkups.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Confidential
Medical Record Number: Confidential
Date of Admission: Confidential
Date of Discharge: Confidential

Hospital Course:
The patient is an 82-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital as an emergency with gross abdominal distension and lower abdominal pain. She had a six-week history of nonspecific malaise, loss of appetite, and dizziness, along with a longstanding history of constipation. Her past medical history included hypertension and impaired glucose tolerance, while her past surgical history included a previous caesarean section.

On examination, the patient had a nontender, grossly distended abdomen with an indentable mass in the lower left quadrant. Blood tests revealed acute renal failure and microcytic anaemia. A CT scan revealed massive dilatation of the sigmoid colon with faecal material within, resulting in bilateral renal tract obstruction. Following a clinical decision, she underwent a laparotomy, during which she was found to have massive colonic dilatation of the sigmoid and descending colon, with dense perirectal fibrosis throughout the length of the rectum. The rectum was resected at the mid rectum, and a Hartman's procedure was performed. Her renal function normalised, and she made an uneventful recovery. 

On histology from the resected specimen, it was observed that the longitudinal muscle layer was degenerate and absent in many areas of the specimen. The myenteric and submucosal plexuses were degenerate where longitudinal muscle was absent, while polyglucosan inclusion bodies were present throughout the longitudinal and circular layers of the muscularis propria, with almost total degeneration of the longitudinal layer, suggesting a myopathic process. The occurrence of longitudinal loss was in areas where the overlying muscle and mucosa were intact indicating that this was likely to be a primary myopathy. There was also a replacement of myocytes in both muscularis layers with elastosis and fibrosis, along with additional areas of elastosis in the submucosa and widespread smooth muscle metaplasia.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with acute renal failure and massive colonic dilatation of the sigmoid and descending colon, with dense perirectal fibrosis throughout the length of the rectum. Histology from the resected specimen revealed a primary myopathy with elastosis and fibrosis.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient is advised to follow a healthy diet and exercise regimen and to attend regular follow-up appointments with the healthcare provider. She is also advised to take appropriate measures to prevent constipation, such as increasing fibre intake and staying hydrated.

Discharge Medications:
None.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to avoid strenuous physical activity and to take adequate rest for at least two weeks following the surgery. She is also advised to attend regular follow-up appointments with the healthcare provider to monitor her renal and colonic function and to ensure that her postoperative recovery is proceeding smoothly. In case of any unwanted side effects or complications, the patient is advised to immediately contact the healthcare provider.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Confidential
Medical Record Number: Confidential
Date of Admission: Confidential
Date of Discharge: Confidential

Hospital Course:
The patient is an 82-year-old female. She came to the hospital as an emergency with a very swollen belly and pain in the lower belly. For six weeks before this, she had been feeling unwell, not wanting to eat, and feeling dizzy. She also had a long history of trouble passing stool. Her past health problems included high blood pressure and blood sugar levels that were higher than normal. She had a past surgery to deliver a baby.

During the exam, the patient had a very swollen belly that did not hurt when touched. There was a soft mass in the lower left side of her belly. Blood tests showed her kidneys had suddenly stopped working well. The tests also showed she had low iron in her blood. A scan showed her large bowel was very stretched and full of stool. This was blocking both tubes that carry urine from the kidneys to the bladder. The doctors decided to do surgery. During surgery, they found the lower part of her large bowel was very stretched. They also found thick scar tissue around the end of the bowel. The doctors removed part of the bowel and created an opening in the belly for waste to leave the body. After surgery, her kidneys started working normally again. She recovered well without any problems.

The doctors looked at the removed bowel tissue under a microscope. They found that the muscle layer running along the length of the bowel was damaged and missing in many areas. The nerve networks in the bowel wall were damaged where the muscle was missing. There were unusual clumps of a starchy substance in the muscle layers. The muscle layer running along the bowel was almost completely damaged. This pointed to a disease of the muscle itself. The muscle loss happened in areas where the outer lining and inner lining were still healthy. This meant the muscle disease was the main problem and not caused by something else. Muscle cells in both muscle layers were replaced by stretchy tissue and scar tissue. There was also extra stretchy tissue in other layers. Normal tissue had changed into smooth muscle tissue in many areas.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient was found to have sudden kidney failure and a very stretched lower large bowel with thick scar tissue around the end of the bowel. Looking at the removed tissue showed a muscle disease with stretchy tissue and scar tissue.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient should eat healthy foods and exercise regularly. She should go to all follow-up visits with her healthcare provider. She should also take steps to prevent trouble passing stool. This includes eating more fiber and drinking plenty of water.

Discharge Medications:
None.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should not do hard physical work. She should rest well for at least two weeks after the surgery. She should go to all follow-up visits with her healthcare provider. These visits will check how her kidneys and bowel are working. They will also make sure she is healing well after surgery. If there are any bad effects or problems, the patient should contact her healthcare provider right away.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient presented with weakness in her right hand that had been gradually increasing over the course of a month. She reported a previous history of a fracture to her right elbow when she was six years old. Physical examination revealed reduced power in the extension of the fingers of her right hand, and an anterolateral bony prominence was palpated on her elbow. Radiographs and ultrasonography revealed an elongated radial neck and compression of the posterior interosseous nerve. The patient underwent an exploratory surgery with resection of the radial head, resulting in gradual recovery of her hand function post-surgery.

Hospital Stay:
The patient was admitted to our hospital for a total of 8 months.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with compression of the posterior interosseous nerve and an elongated radial neck.

Treatment:
The patient underwent exploratory surgery with excision of the radial head to release the compressed nerve. 

Follow-up:
At a follow-up examination 8 months after the surgery, the patient's hand function and electrodiagnostic data were normal.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient came in because her right hand had been getting weaker over the past month. She said she broke her right elbow when she was six years old. During the exam, the doctor found that she had trouble straightening her right fingers. The doctor also felt a bump of bone on the outer side of her elbow. X-rays and ultrasound images showed that a bone in her forearm had grown too long. This was pressing on a nerve that controls hand movement. The patient had surgery to remove part of the bone causing the problem. After surgery, her hand slowly started working better again.

Hospital Stay:
The patient stayed in our hospital for a total of 8 months.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a pressed nerve that controls hand movement and a forearm bone that had grown too long.

Treatment:
The patient had surgery to remove part of the bone and take pressure off the nerve.

Follow-up:
At a check-up 8 months after surgery, the patient's hand was working normally. Nerve tests also showed normal results.
