input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [XXXXXX]
Age: [27]
Gender: [Male]
Date of Admission: [DD/MM/YYYY]
Date of Discharge: [DD/MM/YYYY]

Medical History:
The patient presented with symptoms of poor educational performance at the age of 8 years, followed by gait abnormalities and facial abnormal movements at the age of 12 years. Subsequent symptoms included dysarthria, frequent falls, ataxia and dystonia of right limbs, supranuclear vertical gaze palsy, and swallowing difficulties. Brain MRI at 24 years of age showed a generalized cortical atrophy. A muscle biopsy analyzed by light microscopy showed a nonspecific chronic inflammatory process. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated splenomegaly. The suspicion index for Niemann-Pick disease type C (NPC) scored 227 points. Nucleotide sequencing found two heterozygous changes in NPC1, which confirmed the diagnosis of NPC.

Hospital Course:
The patient was initiated on miglustat late in the progression of NPC disease with a modified functional disability scale score of 17 points for a short period (less than 6 months). He had complicated aspiration pneumonia, which required admission to ICU, pulmonary artificial ventilation, and tracheostomy. He is now relatively stable with tracheostomy and GI tube for feeding and restarted very recently treatment with miglustat 200 mg every 8 hours. However, he shows a higher disability score of 21.

Medical Condition at Discharge:
The patient's medical condition at the time of discharge was stable.

Follow-up Care:
The patient will be followed up by the neurology department for treatment and management of his condition.

Recommendations:
The patient was recommended to have regular follow-up with the neurology department. Any significant changes in his condition should be reported immediately.

Signed by:
[Doctor's Name]
[Designation]
[Date]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [XXXXXX]
Age: [27]
Gender: [Male]
Date of Admission: [DD/MM/YYYY]
Date of Discharge: [DD/MM/YYYY]

Medical History:
The patient had trouble learning in school starting at age 8. At age 12, the patient began having problems walking and unusual movements in the face. Later, the patient developed slurred speech, frequent falls, poor balance, and stiff movements in the right arm and leg. The patient also had trouble moving the eyes up and down and difficulty swallowing. A brain scan at age 24 showed the brain had shrunk. A small piece of muscle was removed and tested. It showed ongoing irritation in the tissue. A scan of the belly area showed an enlarged spleen. Doctors suspected a rare disease called Niemann-Pick disease type C. A gene test found two changes in a gene called NPC1. This confirmed the patient has Niemann-Pick disease type C.

Hospital Course:
The patient started taking a medicine called miglustat late in the disease. At that time, the disability score was 17 points. The patient took this medicine for less than 6 months. The patient then got a serious lung infection from food or liquid going into the lungs. This required a stay in the intensive care unit. The patient needed a breathing machine. Doctors also made an opening in the throat to help with breathing. The patient is now fairly stable with this breathing tube in place. The patient now gets food through a tube in the stomach. The patient recently started taking miglustat again at 200 mg three times a day. However, the disability score is now higher at 21 points.

Medical Condition at Discharge:
The patient was in stable condition when leaving the hospital.

Follow-up Care:
The patient will see the brain and nerve specialists for ongoing care and treatment.

Recommendations:
The patient should keep regular appointments with the brain and nerve specialists. Any big changes in how the patient feels should be reported right away.

Signed by:
[Doctor's Name]
[Designation]
[Date]

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Sex: Female
Age: 7 years
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 7-year-old spayed female Nova Scotia duck tolling retriever who was admitted with a firm mass on the frontal bone that was first noted 14 days prior to imaging. CT scan revealed a large mass arising from the left frontal bone that mildly contrast enhanced and invaded the left frontal bone and extended into the dorsolateral aspect of the left frontal sinus. The mass measured 40mm L × 28mm W × 29mm H and had a focal 2.5 mm defect between the lytic frontal bone and the brain.

The patient underwent surgical treatment and the mass was identified to be a squamous cell carcinoma that invaded the calvarium and surrounding tissues. A virtual defect was created before the plate was designed with the collaboration of a surgical oncologist and engineer.

The margin identification, creation, and plate design were completed within 2 hours, with ADEISS printing and post-processing the cranial plate with a turnaround time of 2 weeks. Planning, printing, post-processing, and shipping were completed within 3 weeks of CT scan image availability. 

Discharge Diagnosis:

The discharge diagnosis for the patient is a squamous cell carcinoma that invaded the calvarium and surrounding tissues.

Discharge Condition:

The patient’s condition is currently stable and the surgical procedure was successful.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient should return to the hospital for follow-up appointments as scheduled by the attending veterinarian. Any changes in appetite, demeanor, or activity level should be reported immediately. A check-up with post-operative imaging should be performed as recommended. 

***End of Discharge Summary***
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Sex: Female
Age: 7 years
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 7-year-old fixed female Nova Scotia duck tolling retriever. She came to the hospital with a hard lump on the front of her skull. The lump was first noticed 14 days before the scan. A CT scan is a special picture that shows the inside of the body. The scan showed a large lump growing from the left side of the front skull bone. The lump picked up some of the dye used during the scan. It had grown into the skull bone and spread into the left front sinus area. The sinus is an air-filled space inside the skull. The lump was 40mm long, 28mm wide, and 29mm tall. There was a small 2.5 mm hole between the damaged skull bone and the brain.

The patient had surgery to remove the lump. Tests showed the lump was a type of skin cancer called squamous cell carcinoma. The cancer had spread into the skull and nearby tissues. The doctors used a computer to plan the surgery. They worked with a cancer doctor and an engineer to design a special plate. This plate would cover the hole left after removing the lump.

The team finished planning and designing the plate in 2 hours. A company called ADEISS made and prepared the skull plate. This took 2 weeks. The whole process from scan to shipping the plate took 3 weeks.

Discharge Diagnosis:

The patient has a type of skin cancer called squamous cell carcinoma. The cancer had spread into the skull and nearby tissues.

Discharge Condition:

The patient is in stable condition. The surgery went well.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient should come back to the hospital for follow-up visits. These visits will be set up by the main veterinarian. Tell the vet right away if there are any changes in eating, behavior, or energy level. The patient should have another scan as the vet recommends to check on healing.

Electronic Health Record - Discharge Summary
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Hospital: [REDACTED]

Reason for Admission:
The patient presented with a 3-month history of submandibular swelling, tongue mobility difficulties, and snoring. A biopsy was conducted and a dermoid or epidermoid tumor of heterogeneous viscosity was diagnosed.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent tumor excision under general anesthesia with right nasotracheal intubation. The tumor was resected completely, including the attached submandibular gland. The surgery was successful, and the patient did not experience any post-operative complications.

Diagnostic Results:
MRI revealed a heterogeneous tumor with a low intensity on T1-weighted image and a high intensity on T2-weighted image. FNAC of the tumor contents revealed enucleated squamous cells and inflammatory cells.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a dermoid or epidermoid tumor of heterogeneous viscosity.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in a stable condition with advice to follow up with the consulting physician.

Instructions for Follow-Up:
The patient was advised to follow-up with the consulting physician for further evaluation.

Signed,
Dr. [REDACTED]
[REDACTED] Hospital

Electronic Health Record - Discharge Summary
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Hospital: [REDACTED]

Reason for Admission:
The patient came in with swelling under the jaw that had lasted 3 months. The patient also had trouble moving the tongue and was snoring. A small tissue sample was taken and tested. It showed a type of slow-growing cyst called a dermoid or epidermoid tumor.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery to remove the tumor. The patient was put to sleep with medicine and a breathing tube was placed through the right side of the nose. The tumor was fully removed along with a saliva gland under the jaw. The surgery went well. The patient had no problems after the surgery.

Diagnostic Results:
A special scan called an MRI showed a mixed tumor in the area. A test using a thin needle to take fluid from the tumor showed skin cells and cells that fight infection.

Diagnosis:
The patient was found to have a dermoid or epidermoid tumor. This is a type of slow-growing cyst with mixed contents.

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital in good and steady health. The patient was told to see the doctor again for a check-up.

Instructions for Follow-Up:
The patient should see the doctor again for more checks and care.

Signed,
Dr. [REDACTED]
[REDACTED] Hospital

input:
Hospital Course: 
The 64-year-old female patient with a history of Parkinson's disease and anxiety presented with erythema and minimal serous drainage from her left scalp staging incision, three months post bilateral subthalamic nucleus (STN) deep brain stimulation (DBS) surgery. The incision was locally cleansed, and she was prescribed a two-week course of Augmentin. However, a month later, she presented with erythema and a fluctuant mass over the DBS generator in the right subclavicular region. The patient admitted to swimming in a “homemade hot tub” in the woods, which she sterilized using bleach. After the surgical site appearance and interval history were reviewed, the patient was taken to the operating room for explantation. The left connecting lead was severed distal to the connector at the staging incision, and the right subclavicular incision was opened and exposed, resulting in the egress of non-purulent serous fluid. The left lead connector was removed, and the wound site and serous fluid were cultured for aerobic and anaerobic organisms. The wound cultures revealed a light-growth polymicrobial infection, including the species methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA), Enterobacter amnigenus, and Cupriavidus pauculus. The patient was treated with a six-week course of intravenous ceftazidime and discharged. She returned two months later with wound dehiscence and purulence over her right-sided generator. The generator and the connecting lead were explanted. The operative wound cultures remained negative. She was placed empirically on cefepime for two weeks and declined the reconnection of the intracranial leads to a generator.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 64-year-old woman with Parkinson's disease and anxiety. She came to the hospital because of redness and some clear fluid leaking from a cut on the left side of her scalp. This cut was made three months earlier during brain surgery. The surgery placed a device deep in her brain to help control her Parkinson's symptoms. The doctor cleaned the area and gave her a two-week supply of antibiotics called Augmentin. One month later, the patient came back with redness and a soft lump. This lump was over the battery pack placed under the skin below her right collarbone. The patient said she had been swimming in a homemade hot tub in the woods. She had tried to clean the water with bleach. After looking at the surgical site and hearing about her activities, the doctors decided to remove the device. During surgery, they cut the wire on the left side of her head near the earlier cut. They also opened the area below her right collarbone. Clear fluid that was not pus came out. The doctors removed the wire connector on the left side. They took samples of the fluid and wound to test for germs. The tests showed a mild infection with several types of germs. These included a common skin germ called Staphylococcus aureus and two other types of bacteria. The patient received antibiotics through a vein for six weeks and then went home. Two months later, she came back because her wound on the right side had opened up and had pus. The doctors removed the battery pack and the connecting wire. This time, the germ tests came back negative. The patient was given a different antibiotic through a vein for two weeks. She chose not to have the brain wires reconnected to a new battery pack.

input:
Patient summary:

Ms. X is a 62-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with episodic stridor, exertional dyspnoea, and dry cough. Clinical examination revealed a hard solitary thyroid nodule with ipsilateral cervical lymphadenopathy. Fine-needle aspiration cytology confirmed papillary thyroid carcinoma. CT imaging showed invasion of the thyroid gland and a large mass in the right anterior jugulo-digastric region. Surgery was performed, and a total thyroidectomy with right neck dissection revealed a locally advanced thyroid neoplasm with tracheal invasion. A tumour thrombus was found during surgery and was easily removed. Histopathology revealed undifferentiated anaplastic carcinoma and papillary thyroid carcinoma. The patient received radioiodine plus external beam radiotherapy to the neck and is alive and functional 16 months later with no signs of local recurrence or distant metastasis.

Hospital course:

Admitted with stridor, exertional dyspnoea, and dry cough, Ms. X underwent clinical examination and was diagnosed with papillary thyroid carcinoma. Surgery was performed, and a total thyroidectomy with right neck dissection revealed a locally advanced thyroid neoplasm with tracheal invasion. Histopathology showed undifferentiated anaplastic carcinoma and papillary thyroid carcinoma. The patient received radioiodine plus external beam radiotherapy to the neck and is alive and functional 16 months later with no signs of local recurrence or distant metastasis.
clean_ref:
Patient summary:

Ms. X is a 62-year-old woman who came to the hospital with noisy breathing, trouble breathing during activity, and a dry cough. A physical exam found a hard lump in her thyroid gland and swollen lymph nodes on the same side of her neck. A test using a small needle to collect cells showed thyroid cancer. A CT scan showed the cancer had spread into the thyroid gland and a large mass on the right side of her neck. The patient had surgery to remove the entire thyroid gland and lymph nodes on the right side of the neck. The surgery showed the cancer had grown into the windpipe. A blood clot made of cancer cells was found during surgery and was easily removed. Lab tests on the tissue showed two types of thyroid cancer, one fast-growing and one slower-growing. The patient then received radiation treatment using a pill and a machine that aimed beams at the neck. Sixteen months later, the patient is alive and doing well with no signs that the cancer has come back or spread to other parts of the body.

Hospital course:

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with a complaint of external genitalia malformation. Physical examination revealed a normal-sized penis with phimosis and an extra half glans, horizontally attached to the right side of the normal glans penis. The patient underwent a foreskin circumcision operation under general anesthesia, and no complications were noted during or after the procedure. The patient was instructed to clean the perineal region repeatedly and remove the smegma, and oral estrogen was prescribed to inhibit erection. The patient's condition was monitored over a period of 6 months, during which time the patient's appearance, urination, and erectile function were all normal.

Consultations:

N/A

Diagnostic Imaging:

The voiding cystourethrogram showed single urethras with a single bladder and no vesicoureteral reflux. The CT urography revealed bilateral extrarenal pelvis, and no abnormalities were found in the kidneys, ureter, bladder, or vertebral bodies.

Laboratory Studies:

No apparent abnormalities were found in biochemical blood tests.

Medications:

The patient was prescribed oral estrogen to inhibit erection.

Discharge Condition:

The patient was content with the external genitalia's appearance, and the urination and erectile function were normal. The patient was discharged in good condition.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital because of a problem with the shape of the private parts. The doctor found a normal-sized penis with tight foreskin that could not pull back. There was also an extra half tip of the penis attached to the right side of the normal tip. The patient had surgery to remove the foreskin while fully asleep under anesthesia. There were no problems during or after the surgery. The patient was told to clean the area between the legs often and remove any buildup under the skin. The doctor gave medicine by mouth to stop erections while healing. The doctors checked on the patient for 6 months. During this time, the appearance, ability to urinate, and ability to have erections were all normal.

Consultations:

None needed.

Diagnostic Imaging:

A special X-ray test showed one normal urine tube and one bladder with no urine backing up. A scan of the body showed that the part of the kidney that collects urine sits outside the kidney on both sides. The kidneys, urine tubes, bladder, and spine bones were all normal.

Laboratory Studies:

Blood tests showed no problems.

Medications:

The patient was given medicine by mouth to stop erections while healing.

Discharge Condition:

The patient was happy with how the private parts looked. Urination and the ability to have erections were both normal. The patient left the hospital in good health.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 72

Sex: Female

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our hospital due to cervical bleeding caused by a giant ulcerous goiter. She had a long-standing history of neglected thyroid carcinoma, which had undergone excisional biopsy 7 years prior, revealing papillary carcinoma (PTC). Despite the diagnosis, the patient refused therapeutic treatment and instead had the growing mass regularly monitored by computed tomography (CT) over the following 7 years.

Physical examination upon admission revealed a cauliflower-like cervical mass with a giant cystic section on the right side, presumably due to the previously diagnosed thyroid carcinoma. Routine laboratory tests, including hematocrit, were within normal limits. The latest CT image showed a large, heterogeneous mass localized to the left thyroid lobe and extended to the anterior neck space, measuring 17 x 12 cm and causing a displacement of the trachea to the right side.

The patient was successfully discharged after managing the surface bleeding with common bedside hemostatic maneuvers and use of local electrocauterization. Despite the success of these treatments, the patient refused any further management, such as surgical debulking and radioiodine therapy.

Follow-up:

The patient is recommended to schedule follow-up appointments with her primary care provider to continue monitoring her condition. If any new developments arise, she is advised to seek medical attention immediately.

Summary:

The 72-year-old female patient with a long-standing history of neglected thyroid carcinoma was admitted to our hospital with cervical bleeding caused by a giant ulcerous goiter. After managing the surface bleeding, the patient was discharged without further treatment due to her refusal to undergo surgical debulking and radioiodine therapy. She is advised to schedule follow-up appointments with her primary care provider to continue monitoring her condition.

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital because of bleeding from a large open sore on a growth in the neck area. She had thyroid cancer that was not treated for many years. Seven years ago, doctors removed a small piece of the growth to test it. The test showed a type of thyroid cancer called papillary carcinoma. After learning this, the patient chose not to have treatment. Instead, she had special scans done regularly over the next 7 years to watch the growth.

When the patient arrived at the hospital, doctors found a large bumpy mass in the neck area. The mass had a big fluid-filled section on the right side. This was likely caused by the thyroid cancer found before. Blood tests, including tests to check blood cell levels, came back normal. The most recent scan showed a large uneven mass in the left side of the thyroid. The mass spread to the front of the neck. It measured 17 by 12 centimeters and pushed the windpipe to the right side.

The patient was able to go home after doctors stopped the bleeding. They used basic methods to stop the bleeding at the bedside. They also used a tool that seals blood vessels with heat. Even though these treatments worked, the patient did not want more treatment. She refused surgery to remove part of the growth. She also refused treatment with a special type of radiation.

Follow-up:

The patient should make appointments with her regular doctor to keep watching her condition. If anything new happens, she should get medical help right away.

Summary:

The 72-year-old female patient had thyroid cancer that was not treated for many years. She came to the hospital with bleeding from a large open sore on a growth in the neck. Doctors stopped the bleeding and the patient went home. She chose not to have surgery or radiation treatment. She should make appointments with her regular doctor to keep watching her condition.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was a 68-year-old male who was admitted with weakness and aggravation. He had a past history of gastric stromal tumors and type-B hepatitis. On admission, he had mild yellowing of the skin and sclera. Laboratory examination showed liver dysfunction. Abdominal CT showed pelvic and peritoneal cavity fluids, occupation of the left lateral lobe of the liver, and an unclear margin of the pancreas. PET-CT suggested swelling of the pancreas and metastases in the liver. The patient received comprehensive internal treatment combined with artificial liver treatment, but his condition deteriorated, and he became unconscious and developed hepatic encephalopathy. He was put on the transplant waiting list and received a liver transplant from a deceased donor on day 4 due to his critical condition. After the transplantation, the patient was sent to the ICU for further treatment.

Postoperative Course:

The patient's condition improved after the transplant, and he was started on immunosuppressive agents. Abdominal CT showed pancreatic swelling with massive exudation and effusion, indicating acute pancreatitis on postoperative day 8. His pathogen culture of various body fluids suggested multiple bacterial infections. An arrow tube was placed around the liver and tail of the pancreas under B-mode ultrasound guidance instead of operative treatment on postoperative day 11, and coffee-like turbid liquids flowed from the arrow tube. 

On postoperative day 20, the patient experienced a sudden increase in heart rate, decrease in blood pressure, increase in blood gas lactate, blurred consciousness, and poor light reflex. He received emergency trachea cannula, continuous renal replacement therapy for anuria, and norepinephrine to maintain blood pressure. On postoperative day 21, he was unconscious and died of septic shock.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient was a 68-year-old male who came to the hospital because he felt weak and uncomfortable. He had a history of stomach tumors and a liver infection called hepatitis B. When he arrived, his skin and the white parts of his eyes looked slightly yellow. Blood tests showed his liver was not working well. A belly scan showed fluid in his lower belly area, a growth on the left side of his liver, and unclear edges around his pancreas. A special body scan showed his pancreas was swollen and cancer had spread to his liver. The patient received medicine and special liver cleaning treatments, but he got worse. He became confused and his brain stopped working well because of liver failure. He was put on a waiting list for a new liver. On day 4, he received a liver from a donor who had died because his condition was very serious. After the surgery, the patient was moved to the intensive care unit for more treatment.

Postoperative Course:

The patient got better after receiving the new liver. He was given medicines to stop his body from fighting the new liver. A belly scan on day 8 after surgery showed his pancreas was swollen with a lot of fluid leaking around it. This meant he had sudden pancreas swelling. Tests of fluids from his body showed he had several different infections. On day 11 after surgery, doctors used an ultrasound to guide a drainage tube near his liver and pancreas instead of doing another surgery. Dark brown cloudy fluid came out of the tube.

On day 20 after surgery, the patient suddenly had a fast heartbeat and low blood pressure. His blood showed high acid levels. He became confused and his eyes did not react to light normally. Doctors put a breathing tube in his throat. He could not make urine so he was put on a machine to clean his blood. He was given medicine to raise his blood pressure. On day 21 after surgery, he was not awake and died from a serious blood infection that spread through his whole body.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: __________     Admission Date: __________     Discharge Date: __________ 

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 34-year-old female at 24 gestational weeks, was admitted to our emergency service with complaints of vomiting, abdominal pain, weakness, and dyspnea. Upon physical examination, the patient showed reduced breath sounds on the left side of the chest and diffuse abdominal tenderness. Laboratory tests revealed several abnormalities including an elevated white cell count, low hemoglobin levels and a low platelet count. A chest X-ray showed free air under the left hemidiaphragm, and a left lateral decubitus abdominal radiograph displayed that the free air moved to the right side. 

During the attempted surgical intervention, an emergency obstetric USG examination was performed which showed the intrauterine fetal demise. The patient was operated on under emergency conditions. A 14 × 8 cm defect was detected in the posterior diaphragm and the spleen was significantly damaged. A large hernia sac was detected in the diaphragmatic cavity. The defect in the posterior diaphragm was closed by primary repair following the insertion of a gastrostomy tube. After the improvement of hemodynamics and oxygen saturation of the patient, a dead fetus was manually delivered via caesarian section and a total gastrectomy followed by Roux-en-Y anastomosis was performed. 

On the 27th day of treatment, the patient died due to brain hypoxia caused by cardiopulmonary arrest. 

Diagnosis: 
The patient's presenting symptoms and pathology were suggestive of an abdominal and cardiothoracic catastrophe, resulting in the intrauterine fetal demise, posterior diaphragm hernia and necrosis of the stomach. 

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient's hospital course was marked by a series of complications from the presenting pathology. Despite surgical intervention the patient developed the intrauterine fetal demise and complications from posterior diaphragm hernia, resulting in the need for a cesarean delivery and complicated surgery to repair multiple organ damage. Ultimately, the patient succumbed to brain hypoxia caused by cardiopulmonary arrest.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: __________     Admission Date: __________     Discharge Date: __________

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 34-year-old woman who was 24 weeks pregnant, came to the emergency room. She had vomiting, belly pain, weakness, and trouble breathing. During the physical exam, doctors found less air movement on the left side of her chest. She also had pain across her whole belly when touched. Blood tests showed several problems. Her white blood cell count was high. Her red blood cell levels were low. Her blood clotting cells were also low. A chest X-ray showed air leaking under the left side of her breathing muscle. Another X-ray showed this air had moved to the right side.

During the attempt to do surgery, an emergency ultrasound was done. It showed the baby had died in the womb. The patient had emergency surgery. Doctors found a large hole in the back of her breathing muscle. The hole was about 14 by 8 centimeters. Her spleen was badly damaged. A large sac of tissue was found bulging through the hole in the breathing muscle. Doctors closed the hole with stitches after placing a feeding tube into her stomach. After her blood flow and oxygen levels got better, doctors delivered the baby who had died. This was done through a surgery to open the belly. Then doctors removed her whole stomach. They reconnected her digestive system using a special method.

On the 27th day of treatment, the patient died. This happened because her brain lost oxygen after her heart and breathing stopped.

Diagnosis:
The patient's symptoms and test results showed a serious emergency in her belly and chest area. This led to her baby dying in the womb. She had a hole in her breathing muscle with organs pushing through. Her stomach tissue had also died.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient had many problems during her hospital stay because of her serious condition. Even after surgery, her baby died in the womb. She also had problems from the hole in her breathing muscle. Doctors had to deliver the baby through surgery. They also did a difficult surgery to fix damage to several organs. In the end, the patient died because her brain lost oxygen after her heart and breathing stopped.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 51-year-old female

Admission Date: February 2013

Discharge Date: October 2015

Diagnosis: Recurrent Ameloblastoma

Procedure: Right mandibular resection and reconstruction with nonvascularized free iliac bone followed by dental implant placement and fixed prosthesis

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the department in February 2013 due to discomfort in the mandible. She had been diagnosed with recurrent ameloblastoma and had undergone multiple fenestration procedures in the past. The patient was dissatisfied with the outcomes of previous hospital's treatment policy and came to our department for the complete resection of the tumor. 
After thorough evaluation and diagnosis, we proposed a treatment protocol of radical resection and immediate bony reconstruction using nonvascularized free iliac bone graft. The patient underwent right mandibular resection (teeth 43 to 46; length 35 mm) in March 2013 and was closely monitored for tumor recurrence. 
Follow-up examinations showed no signs of tumor recurrence, but the patient continued to experience right-sided difficulty in mastication. Therefore, a fixed prosthesis was recommended. In November 2014, the mini-plate was removed from the grafted bone, and four dental implants were placed. Subsequently, the patient underwent vestibuloplasty (vestibular extension with periosteal separation procedures) and confirmed osseointegration in all four implants. The final fixed prosthesis was worn by the patient in October 2015. 
The patient's oral hygiene has been regularly managed, and no signs of tumor recurrence or abnormal bone absorption or disintegration of the dental implants have been observed. The patient was discharged in October 2015.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient is advised to continue maintaining good oral hygiene and to follow the regular maintenance therapy schedule. The patient should keep all appointments with the department for proper monitoring of tumor recurrence and dental implant health. If there are any concerns or questions, the patient should contact the department for further consultations.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 51-year-old female

Admission Date: February 2013

Discharge Date: October 2015

Diagnosis: Returning jaw tumor (ameloblastoma)

Procedure: Surgery to remove part of the right lower jawbone and rebuild it using bone from the hip, followed by placing dental implants and a permanent set of replacement teeth

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the department in February 2013 because of pain in the lower jaw. She had a jaw tumor that kept coming back and had several surgeries before to drain it. The patient was not happy with her care at the other hospital and came to our department to have the tumor fully removed. After a complete checkup, we planned to remove all of the tumor and rebuild the jaw right away using bone from the hip. In March 2013, the patient had surgery to remove part of the right lower jawbone (teeth 43 to 46, about 35 mm long). The care team watched closely to make sure the tumor did not come back. Follow-up visits showed no signs of the tumor returning, but the patient still had trouble chewing on the right side. Because of this, we suggested a permanent set of replacement teeth. In November 2014, the metal plate was taken out of the new bone, and four dental implants were placed. After that, the patient had surgery to create more space in the mouth for the new teeth. All four implants joined well with the bone. The patient received the final set of permanent replacement teeth in October 2015. The patient has kept good mouth care habits. There are no signs of the tumor coming back or problems with the bone or implants. The patient was sent home in October 2015.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient should keep brushing and cleaning the mouth well and follow the regular care schedule. The patient should come to all follow-up visits so the care team can check for any return of the tumor and make sure the implants stay healthy. If the patient has any worries or questions, the patient should call the department for help.

input:
Hospital Course:
-The patient was admitted to the cardiac catheterization laboratory with sudden onset chest pain associated with anterior ST-segment elevation on the electrocardiogram. The chest pain occurred during a bowel movement and he was found to be in cardiogenic shock upon arrival.
-Emergency diagnostic coronary angiography was performed, revealing severe proximal stenosis in the left anterior descending (LAD) artery. An extravasation of contrast media from the LAD artery into the pericardium was noted, with no flow in the distal LAD artery, resulting in a suspected dehiscence of the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) graft.
-Injection of contrast media discovered that the LIMA graft's flow of contrast only reached the mid-segment and stopped, while the saphenous vein grafts (SVGs) in the right coronary artery (RCA) and circumflex (LCx) artery were patent.
-After diagnostic coronary angiography, the patient experienced pulseless electrical activity cardiac arrest, but spontaneous circulation was achieved with CPR, and an intra-aortic balloon pump was inserted.
-Urgent echocardiography revealed a large global pericardial effusion with right atrial and right ventricular collapse. Emergency pericardiocentesis was performed to aspirate 700 mL of haemorrhagic fluid that contained an organized thrombus.
-Due to on-going haemodynamic compromise and non-availability of cardiac surgery on-site, it was decided to perform percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to treat the LAD artery stenosis. Following PCI, thrombolysis in myocardial infarction (TIMI) 3 flow was achieved, and no further extravasation was seen at the dehiscence site.
-Assessment of the LIMA graft showed a sluggish flow with extravasation of contrast media into the pericardium, requiring four coils (Tornado Embolisation Microcoils. Cook Medical, Bloomington, IN, USA) for embolization to halt the flow.
-The patient was then transferred to the local cardiac surgery unit and underwent emergency re-sternotomy and evacuation of the blood clots. His haemoglobin level post CABG (4 weeks prior to this admission) was measured at 100 g/L and dropped to 79 g/L following LIMA avulsion. He received 2 units of blood transfusion.
-The echocardiogram post-PCI revealed residual blood clots in the pericardial space that were difficult to extract percutaneously.
-The patient made a full recovery with no neurological sequelae and was discharged after a few days.
-Outpatient review at 3 months confirmed an excellent recovery without report of cardiovascular symptoms. Control coronary angiography was performed at 5 months, and the patient underwent successful PCI to mid and proximal LAD into the left main (LM) artery with LCx ostium optimization.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to the heart procedure room with sudden chest pain. The heart tracing showed signs of a heart attack affecting the front wall of the heart. The chest pain started while having a bowel movement. When the patient arrived, the heart was not pumping well enough to support the body.

Doctors did an emergency test to look at the heart arteries. They found a major blockage in the main artery on the front of the heart. Dye leaked from this artery into the sac around the heart. Blood was not flowing past the blockage. Doctors suspected that the bypass graft from the chest wall artery had come loose.

Doctors injected dye and found that blood flow in the chest wall artery graft stopped halfway through. The vein grafts to the other two heart arteries were open and working.

After the test, the patient's heart stopped but still showed electrical activity. The medical team did chest compressions and got the heart beating again on its own. They placed a balloon pump to help the heart.

An urgent heart ultrasound showed a large buildup of fluid all around the heart. This fluid was pressing on the right side of the heart. Doctors drained 700 mL of bloody fluid that contained a blood clot using a needle.

The patient was still not stable and heart surgery was not available at this hospital. Doctors decided to open the blocked artery using a tube threaded through the blood vessels. After this procedure, blood flow returned to normal. No more dye leaked at the loose graft site.

Checking the chest wall artery graft showed slow flow with dye still leaking into the sac around the heart. Doctors placed four small metal coils to block this graft and stop the bleeding.

The patient was then moved to a hospital with heart surgery. Doctors opened the chest again in an emergency operation to remove the blood clots. The patient's blood count was 100 four weeks earlier after bypass surgery. It dropped to 79 after the graft came loose. The patient received two bags of donated blood.

A heart ultrasound after the artery-opening procedure showed leftover blood clots around the heart. These clots were hard to remove with a needle.

The patient fully recovered with no brain damage. The patient left the hospital after a few days.

At a checkup three months later, the patient had an excellent recovery with no heart symptoms. Five months later, doctors did another test to look at the heart arteries. The patient had a successful procedure to open blockages in the main heart artery and the branch to the side of the heart.

input:
Hospital Course:
The 76-year-old male patient presented with visual impairment of his right eye 50 years after receiving penetrating keratoplasty (PKP). This followed an explosion injury that resulted in bilateral corneal opacities before the 1960s trauma. The patient reported seeing his ophthalmologist annually for follow-up examinations for 50 years following his first PKP, and control examinations were conducted in recent years due to primary open angle glaucoma. Cataract surgery was performed externally, 42 years after PKP in the right eye, and six years following surgery, he reported a deterioration of his vision. Follow-up examinations were conducted regularly by his ophthalmologist, with depositing in the periphery of the graft being described 48 years after the first PKP. Upon slit-lamp examination, the graft diameters of the former grafts were 6 mm with a central corneal thickness of 584 µm in the right eye and 544 µm in the left eye. The recipient cornea was cloudy, and gray with an endothelial cell count of 1162 c/mm2 in the right eye and 1320 c/mm2 in the left eye. As a result of progressive visual impairment and discomfort caused by the blurred vision, a repeated PKP was offered on the worse right eye. The patient accepted, and surgery was performed without any complications. The excised primary corneal graft with a rim of the recipient cornea was sent for histopathological analysis.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The 76-year-old male patient came in because of vision problems in his right eye. He had a cornea transplant in that eye 50 years ago. Before that surgery in the 1960s, an explosion had damaged both of his corneas and made them cloudy. The patient saw his eye doctor every year for 50 years after his first cornea transplant. In recent years, he also had checkups for a type of eye pressure problem called glaucoma. He had cataract surgery done at another location 42 years after his cornea transplant in the right eye. Six years after the cataract surgery, he noticed his vision getting worse. His eye doctor continued to see him regularly. About 48 years after the first cornea transplant, the doctor noticed buildup forming at the edges of the transplanted tissue. During an eye exam using a special microscope, the doctor measured the transplanted tissue in both eyes. The transplant was 6 mm wide in each eye. The center of the cornea was 584 micrometers thick in the right eye and 544 micrometers thick in the left eye. The original cornea around the transplant looked cloudy and gray. The layer of cells lining the inside of the cornea showed 1162 cells per square millimeter in the right eye and 1320 cells per square millimeter in the left eye. Because the patient's vision kept getting worse and the blurry vision was bothering him, the doctors suggested another cornea transplant on the right eye since it was worse. The patient agreed, and the surgery went well with no problems. The doctors removed the old transplanted cornea along with a small ring of the surrounding tissue. This tissue was sent to a lab to be studied under a microscope.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 96-year-old female with drug-induced agranulocytosis and mild pneumonia

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with severe leucopenia due to drug-induced agranulocytosis, presumably caused by antibiotics. She was also found to have mild pneumonia, which was treated with intravenous meropenem hydrate and oral levofloxacin hydrate for 10 days followed by a short course of sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim for 4 days. She was given subcutaneous injections of filgrastim to treat her neutropenia, which eventually normalized after 6 days and stabilized at around 3000 to 4000/µL after day 13. Her fever receded after day 10, and she was discharged on day 18 without any further complications.

Clinical Findings:
The patient's blood pressure was 136/99 mmHg, pulse rate was 87 beats per minute, and body temperature was 37.4°C during presentation. Her respiratory rate was 20 breaths per minute, and peripheral oxygen saturation was 97%. Chest sounds were normal, and other findings of the physical examination were unremarkable. Full blood count revealed severe leucopenia with a WBC count of 830/µL (neutrophils 7/µL, eosinophils 8/μL, basophils 18/μL, monocytes 378/μL, and lymphocytes 415/μL). A chest radiograph and computed tomography showed mild infiltrative shadow in her left lung.

Medical History:
The patient had a medical history of atrial fibrillation, stroke, hypertension, hypotonic urinary bladder, lower back pain, and gastritis.

Medications:
The patient's daily medications included candesartan, lansoprazole, amlodipine, carbocysteine, zaltoprofen, tranexamic acid, rivaroxaban, bisoprolol, brotizolam, and celecoxib. Candesartan, lansoprazole, amlodipine, and celecoxib were discontinued due to their potential to cause agranulocytosis.

Follow-up:
The patient was discharged from the hospital after the normalization of her neutrophil count and stabilization of her WBC count. She will continue with follow-up appointments with her primary care physician to ensure proper monitoring and further management of her medical conditions.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 96-year-old female with a very low white blood cell count caused by medication and mild lung infection

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted because her white blood cell count dropped very low due to a reaction to antibiotics. She also had a mild lung infection. The lung infection was treated with antibiotics given through an IV and by mouth for 10 days. After that, she took another antibiotic for 4 more days. She received shots under the skin to help her body make more white blood cells. Her white blood cell count returned to normal after 6 days. It stayed steady at a healthy level after day 13. Her fever went away after day 10. She left the hospital on day 18 with no other problems.

Clinical Findings:
When the patient arrived, her blood pressure was 136/99, which is slightly high. Her heart rate was 87 beats per minute. Her temperature was 99.3 degrees Fahrenheit, which is a mild fever. She was breathing 20 times per minute. Her blood oxygen level was 97 percent, which is normal. Her lungs sounded normal, and the rest of her physical exam showed nothing unusual. Blood tests showed her white blood cell count was very low at 830, far below normal. A chest x-ray and CT scan showed a small area of infection in her left lung.

Medical History:
The patient has a history of irregular heartbeat, stroke, high blood pressure, weak bladder, lower back pain, and stomach irritation.

Medications:
The patient was taking several daily medications. These included candesartan, lansoprazole, amlodipine, carbocysteine, zaltoprofen, tranexamic acid, rivaroxaban, bisoprolol, brotizolam, and celecoxib. Four of these medications were stopped because they may have caused her low white blood cell count. These were candesartan, lansoprazole, amlodipine, and celecoxib.

Follow-up:
The patient left the hospital after her white blood cell count returned to normal and stayed steady. She will keep seeing her regular doctor for checkups. Her doctor will watch her health closely and manage her ongoing medical conditions.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was a 33-year-old female who presented to the hospital with a sudden onset of right-sided weakness. CT scan of the brain showed that the patient had an ischemic stroke which affected the left middle cerebral artery territory. An MRI of the brain revealed altered signal intensity in the left parietal, temporal cortices, and left basal ganglia. The rest of the brain appeared structurally normal, and both cerebellar hemispheres showed no abnormality.

Diagnosis:

The patient was diagnosed with an ischemic stroke in the left middle cerebral artery territory. Additionally, a hypo-dense lesion was found in the medial part of the left basifrontal lobe, in the left Putamen extending superiorly to the left para-ventricular white matter, and around the frontal horn of the left lateral ventricle.

Treatment:

The patient underwent FDG PET/CT as part of routine evaluation before being planned for stem cell transplant for stroke. The PET/CT of the brain revealed reduced FDG uptake in the frontal, parietal, and temporal cortices, and basal ganglia on the left side. Correlative CT revealed a hypo-dense lesion as described above. The patient was treated under the care of the hospital's medical team.

Assessment and Plan:

During the hospital stay, the patient received therapy aimed at preventing further ischemic stroke. The medical team monitored the patient's condition closely and ordered various diagnostic tests, which revealed reduced FDG uptake in the frontal, parietal, and temporal cortices, and basal ganglia on the left side.

Summary:

The patient was admitted to the hospital with an acute onset of right-sided weakness due to an ischemic stroke in the left middle cerebral artery territory. Various diagnostic tests were performed which revealed a hypo-dense lesion in the medial part of the left basifrontal lobe, in the left Putamen extending superiorly to the left para-ventricular white matter, and around the frontal horn of the left lateral ventricle. The patient received treatment for ischemic stroke and was monitored closely by the hospital's medical team.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient was a 33-year-old female who came to the hospital with sudden weakness on the right side of her body. A CT scan of the brain showed that the patient had a stroke caused by a blocked blood vessel. This stroke affected the left side of the brain in an area supplied by a major blood vessel called the middle cerebral artery. An MRI of the brain showed damage in the left side of the brain, including areas that control movement and sensation. The rest of the brain looked normal, and both sides of the lower back part of the brain showed no problems.

Diagnosis:

The patient was diagnosed with a stroke caused by a blocked blood vessel on the left side of the brain. The doctors also found an area of damaged tissue in several parts of the left side of the brain. This included the front lower part, a deep brain structure involved in movement, the white matter near the fluid-filled spaces, and the area around one of these fluid-filled spaces.

Treatment:

The patient had a special brain scan that shows how active different parts of the brain are. This scan was done to prepare for a possible stem cell treatment for the stroke. The scan showed that parts of the left side of the brain were less active than normal. These areas included the front, side, and top parts of the brain, as well as deep brain structures. A regular scan also showed the damaged tissue described above. The patient was cared for by the hospital medical team.

Assessment and Plan:

During the hospital stay, the patient received treatment to help prevent another stroke. The medical team watched the patient closely and ordered several tests. These tests showed reduced brain activity in the front, side, and top parts of the left side of the brain, as well as in the deep brain structures.

Summary:

The patient came to the hospital with sudden right-sided weakness caused by a stroke from a blocked blood vessel on the left side of the brain. Several tests were done and showed an area of damaged tissue in multiple parts of the left side of the brain. This included the front lower part, a deep movement control area, the white matter near fluid-filled spaces, and around one of these spaces. The patient received stroke treatment and was watched closely by the hospital medical team.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY 

Patient: 44-year-old woman 

Admission Date: [Date] Discharge Date: [Date] 

Diagnosis: Dilated common bile duct, cholecystectomy history, and splenic heterogeneous subcapsular hematoma 

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to the hospital with intractable nausea and vomiting associated with severe epigastric pain. Physical examination was only notable for mild epigastric tenderness. Laboratory tests showed mildly elevated liver enzymes, and a right upper quadrant ultrasound showed dilation of the common bile duct, which was later confirmed on magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography as well as a 7-mm common bile duct stone. The patient underwent ERCP with sphincterotomy, which demonstrated a dilated common bile duct with no obvious cause. She subsequently experienced severe epigastric and left upper quadrant abdominal pain with hypotension after the procedure. Repeat laboratory testing at the time showed elevated lipase as well as a decline in hemoglobin. A CT scan of the abdomen illustrated a large splenic heterogeneous subcapsular hematoma and peripancreatic stranding. The patient was transferred to the intensive care unit and managed conservatively with fluids and blood transfusions. The hematoma regressed, and her hemoglobin remained stable. With clinical improvement, she was discharged home. 

Follow-up Care: The patient was advised to follow up with her primary care provider for further management and was instructed to return to the emergency department if she experiences any further symptoms. 

Discharge Medications: N/A 

Discharge Instructions: 

1. Rest and avoid strenuous activities 
2. Keep the incision areas clean and dry 
3. Take prescribed medications as directed by your healthcare provider
4. Follow up with your primary care provider for further management of your condition 
5. Return to the emergency department if you experience any further symptoms. 

Discharge Disposition: Home 

Attending Physician: [Physician's Name] 

Dictated By: [Physician's Name] 

Keywords: Dilated common bile duct, cholecystectomy history, splenic heterogeneous subcapsular hematoma.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: 44-year-old woman

Admission Date: [Date] Discharge Date: [Date]

Diagnosis: Widened bile duct, past gallbladder removal surgery, and blood clot under the spleen surface

Hospital Course: The patient came to the hospital with severe nausea and vomiting that would not stop. She also had bad pain in the upper belly area. The physical exam showed only mild tenderness in the upper belly. Blood tests showed slightly high liver levels. An ultrasound of the upper right belly showed the bile duct was wider than normal. A special scan confirmed this and also found a small stone in the bile duct. The patient had a procedure using a thin tube through the mouth to look at the bile duct. During this, a small cut was made to help the stone pass. The test showed a wide bile duct but no clear reason why. After the procedure, the patient had severe pain in the upper belly and left side. Her blood pressure also dropped. New blood tests showed high levels of a pancreas enzyme and lower red blood cell counts. A scan of the belly showed a large blood clot forming under the spleen surface. It also showed swelling around the pancreas. The patient was moved to the intensive care unit for close watching. She was treated with fluids given through a vein and blood transfusions. The blood clot got smaller and her blood counts stayed stable. As she got better, she was sent home.

Follow-up Care: The patient was told to see her regular doctor for ongoing care. She was also told to go to the emergency room if she has any new symptoms.

Discharge Medications: None

Discharge Instructions:

1. Rest and do not do hard physical activities
2. Keep the procedure areas clean and dry
3. Take any medicines as told by your doctor
4. See your regular doctor for ongoing care of your condition
5. Go to the emergency room if you have any new symptoms.

Discharge Disposition: Home

Attending Physician: [Physician's Name]

Dictated By: [Physician's Name]

Keywords: Widened bile duct, past gallbladder removal surgery, blood clot under the spleen surface.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 40
Gender: Female
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
The patient was referred for an evaluation of right periorbital pain, right eye ptosis, and diplopia that had first appeared 20 days earlier. She had restricted adduction of the right eyeball. Initial MRIs showed enlargement of the right medial rectus muscle with mild contrast enhancement, and additional lesions in the right retrobulbar optic nerve sheath and preseptal area.

MEDICAL MANAGEMENT:
The initial diagnosis was orbital IIP, and the patient was started on 60 mg of prednisone daily for 10 days, which greatly improved her symptoms. The dosage was gradually reduced over the next 8 weeks, but her symptoms relapsed when prednisone was reduced to below 20 mg per day. Azathioprine and mycophenolate mofetil also could not be administered because of potential direct hepatotoxicity. The patient was started on 3 mg of tacrolimus while discontinuing prednisolone over 8 weeks. After 6 months of tacrolimus administration, the right periorbital pain had completely disappeared, and the restriction of right eye abduction had resolved. The second follow-up MRIs were obtained at 16 months after the onset, by which time the lesions had nearly resolved. Tacrolimus was gradually reduced over a period of 7 months before being discontinued, and the patient had no further relapse during the 1-year follow-up.

CLINICAL FINDINGS:
The patient had painful tenderness of the right medial orbital region. Routine serological tests, autoimmune screening tests, and CSF studies all produced normal findings. However, her hepatitis C virus RNA level was 1,724,673 IU/mL in the quantification test, indicating active hepatitis C.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient is advised to follow up with their healthcare provider for further management of hepatitis C. If any orbital IIP concerns arise, they are to contact their healthcare provider immediately.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 40
Gender: Female
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
The patient came in because of pain around the right eye, a drooping right eyelid, and double vision. These problems started 20 days before the visit. The patient had trouble moving the right eye toward the nose. Brain scans showed that a muscle controlling the right eye was swollen. The scans also showed other problem areas near the eye and behind it.

MEDICAL MANAGEMENT:
Doctors first thought the patient had swelling in the eye socket area. The patient started taking 60 mg of prednisone, a steroid medicine, every day for 10 days. This helped a lot. Doctors slowly lowered the dose over the next 8 weeks. But when the dose dropped below 20 mg per day, the symptoms came back. Two other medicines could not be used because they might harm the liver. The patient then started taking 3 mg of tacrolimus, a different medicine. The steroid was slowly stopped over 8 weeks. After 6 months on tacrolimus, the eye pain was completely gone. The patient could also move the right eye normally again. New brain scans done 16 months after symptoms first started showed the problem areas were almost gone. Doctors slowly reduced tacrolimus over 7 months and then stopped it. The patient had no more problems during the next year of checkups.

CLINICAL FINDINGS:
The patient had pain when the inner part of the right eye area was touched. Blood tests and tests for immune system problems came back normal. Fluid from around the spine was also tested and was normal. But a blood test showed the patient has an active hepatitis C infection. This is a virus that affects the liver.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should see their doctor to treat the hepatitis C. If any eye swelling problems come back, the patient should contact their doctor right away.

input:
Hospital Course:

A 42-year-old male with a past medical history of diabetes and hypertension presented to our hospital with recurrent episodes of right limb-shaking, right facial muscles involuntary twitching and slurred speech, which were more prominent with exercise. The patient was initially diagnosed with focal motor seizures but carbamazepine failed to prevent his symptoms. Further investigations including laboratory tests, electrocardiogram, electroencephalography, and imaging studies revealed significant stenosis of the left internal carotid artery (ICA) and scans showed a large heterogeneous plaque at the beginning of the left ICA. The diagnosis of limb-shaking TIA caused by left hemisphere perfusion impairment was considered.

Treatment:

The patient was given fluid replacement therapy, antiplatelet therapy, clopidogrel and aspirin, and statins. ICA angioplasty and stent implantation were performed with excellent restoration flow. No neurological dysfunction was observed post-operation, and the procedure was successful in completely resolving his symptoms. The patient was maintained on antiplatelet therapy and statins after stenting, and no recurrences of his limb-shaking TIA and facial muscles involuntary twitch. 

Clinical courses:

The patient had at least 3 months without any symptoms, and no new TIA. He was discharged to the care of his primary physician with recommendations to continue taking anti-platelets and statins. Follow-up visits were suggested to monitor the progress of the stenosis. The patient follows up with his primary care provider, and no other issues were reported. 

Diagnosis:

Limb-shaking TIA caused by severe left internal carotid artery stenosis. 

Benefit and risk:

The benefit of the ICA angioplasty and stent implantation is that it restored blood flow in the left hemisphere and cleared the symptoms. The patient was maintained on antiplatelet therapy and statins after stenting, and no recurrences of his limb-shaking TIA and facial muscles involuntary twitch. The risks of the procedure may include long-term stent failure, stroke, infection, and bleeding, although no complications were encountered in this case. 

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to follow-up regularly with his primary physician for monitoring and management of his diabetes and hypertension. Further follow-up visits were suggested to monitor the progress of the stenosis, and to adjust the treatment plan if needed. 

Prognosis:

The angioplasty and stenting were successful in resolving the patient's symptoms. The patient had at least 3 months without any symptoms, and no new TIA. The prognosis of this patient depends on his compliance with medical management and follow-up, and his ability to manage his underlying comorbidities, diabetes, and hypertension.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

A 42-year-old man with a history of diabetes and high blood pressure came to our hospital. He had repeated episodes of right arm and leg shaking, right face twitching, and trouble speaking clearly. These symptoms got worse with physical activity. At first, doctors thought he had a type of seizure and gave him seizure medicine called carbamazepine. The medicine did not stop his symptoms. More tests were done, including blood tests, heart rhythm tests, brain wave tests, and imaging scans. These tests showed a major blockage in the left neck artery that sends blood to the brain. Scans also showed a large buildup of plaque at the start of this artery. Doctors then believed his symptoms were caused by mini-strokes due to poor blood flow to the left side of his brain.

Treatment:

The patient received fluids through an IV and medicines to prevent blood clots, including clopidogrel and aspirin. He also received medicine to lower cholesterol called statins. Doctors did a procedure to open the blocked neck artery and placed a small tube called a stent to keep it open. Blood flow was restored very well after the procedure. The patient had no brain or nerve problems after the procedure. The procedure worked and completely stopped his symptoms. The patient continued taking blood clot prevention medicines and statins after the procedure. His arm and leg shaking and face twitching did not come back.

Clinical Courses:

The patient had no symptoms for at least 3 months and no new mini-strokes. He was sent home under the care of his regular doctor. He was told to keep taking his blood clot prevention medicines and statins. Doctors suggested follow-up visits to check on the artery blockage. The patient saw his regular doctor for follow-up, and no other problems were reported.

Diagnosis:

Mini-strokes with arm and leg shaking caused by a severe blockage in the left neck artery going to the brain.

Benefit and Risk:

The benefit of the procedure to open the artery and place the stent is that it restored blood flow to the left side of the brain. It also cleared up the symptoms. The patient continued taking blood clot prevention medicines and statins after the procedure. His arm and leg shaking and face twitching did not come back. The risks of this procedure may include the stent not working over time, stroke, infection, and bleeding. No problems happened in this case.

Follow-up:

The patient was told to see his regular doctor on a regular basis. These visits are to watch and manage his diabetes and high blood pressure. More follow-up visits were suggested to check on the artery blockage. The treatment plan may be changed if needed.

Prognosis:

The procedure to open the artery and place the stent worked well and stopped the patient's symptoms. The patient had no symptoms for at least 3 months and no new mini-strokes. How well the patient does in the future depends on taking medicines as directed and going to follow-up visits. It also depends on how well he manages his diabetes and high blood pressure.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Name: [redacted]
- Age: 36 years old
- Gender: Female
- Diagnosis: Mucoepidermoid carcinoma (MEC) in the background of Warthin tumor (WT)

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with a two-year history of a painless mass on the right side of her face and was diagnosed with a cystic lesion on ultrasonography. The patient underwent mass resection surgery with a pre-operative diagnosis of lymphangioma and WT. On histopathological evaluation, the diagnosis of MEC in the background of WT was established. The patient underwent radiation therapy after surgery. In the 5-year follow-up, the patient was in good general condition and reported no problems with the physical examination.

Hospital Admission Details:
- Date of admission: [redacted]
- Date of discharge: [redacted]
- Length of stay: [redacted] days

Clinical Findings:
On examination, there was a soft, round, and mobile mass in the right mandibular angle with erythematous overlying skin adhered to the mass. Facial nerve function was normal, and no lymphadenopathy was found.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with MEC in the background of WT. Dermal and deep margin involvement was observed in some foci, and the lymph nodes showed reactive features.

Treatment:
After surgery, the patient underwent radiation therapy.

Follow-up:
In the 5-year follow-up, the patient was in good general condition and reported no problems with the physical examination. However, the patient refused to take further diagnostic procedures.

Discharge Instructions:
- The patient is advised to follow up with her healthcare provider regularly.
- The patient should report any new symptoms or concerns to her healthcare provider immediately.
- The patient should continue with her recommended treatment plan as advised by her healthcare provider.

Final Diagnosis: Mucoepidermoid carcinoma (MEC) in the background of Warthin tumor (WT)
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [redacted]
Age: 36 years old
Gender: Female
Diagnosis: A type of cancer called mucoepidermoid carcinoma that grew within a usually non-cancerous salivary gland tumor called Warthin tumor

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with a painless lump on the right side of her face that had been there for two years. An ultrasound test showed a fluid-filled sac. Doctors first thought it might be a harmless growth in the lymph vessels or a Warthin tumor. The patient had surgery to remove the lump. After looking at the tissue under a microscope, doctors found it was a type of cancer growing inside a Warthin tumor. The patient received radiation treatment after surgery. At a checkup five years later, the patient was doing well and had no problems found during the exam.

Hospital Admission Details:
Date of admission: [redacted]
Date of discharge: [redacted]
Length of stay: [redacted] days

Clinical Findings:
During the exam, doctors found a soft, round lump near the right jawbone that moved when touched. The skin over the lump was red and stuck to it. The nerves that control face movement were working normally. There were no swollen lymph nodes.

Diagnosis:
The patient was found to have cancer growing inside a Warthin tumor. The cancer had spread to the skin and some deeper tissue edges. The lymph nodes showed normal immune system activity, not cancer.

Treatment:
After surgery, the patient received radiation treatment.

Follow-up:
At a checkup five years later, the patient was doing well with no problems found during the exam. However, the patient chose not to have more tests done.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should see her doctor for regular checkups.
The patient should tell her doctor right away about any new symptoms or concerns.
The patient should keep following the treatment plan her doctor recommended.

Final Diagnosis: A type of cancer called mucoepidermoid carcinoma that grew within a Warthin tumor

The patient came in with a painless lump on the left side of her face that had been there for two years. An ultrasound test showed a fluid-filled sac. At a checkup five years later, the patient was doing well and had no problems found during the exam.

During the exam, doctors found a soft, round lump near the right jawbone that moved when touched. The skin over the lump was red and stuck to it. The nerves that control face movement were working normally. There were no swollen lymph nodes.

Diagnosis:
The patient was found to have cancer growing inside a Warthin tumor. The cancer had spread to the skin and some deeper tissue edges. The lymph nodes showed normal immune system activity, not cancer.

Treatment:
After surgery, the patient received radiation treatment.

Follow-up:
At a checkup five years later, the patient was doing well with no problems found during the exam. However, the patient chose not to have more tests done.

The patient should see her doctor for regular checkups.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient presented to our institution for regular follow-up, 2 years after a right radical nephrectomy for a primary renal cell carcinoma (RCC). The patient was asymptomatic, but an abdominal computed tomography (CT) scan on follow-up revealed a mass in the left adrenal gland. A multidisciplinary team meeting determined that a laparoscopic transperitoneal adrenalectomy was the best course of action.

Procedure:

The patient underwent a laparoscopic transperitoneal adrenalectomy, performed under general anesthesia. The patient was placed in the lateral decubitus position with the left side up. The pneumoperitoneum was established, and a 30° laparoscope and three additional working ports were placed under direct vision. The tumor was exposed and isolated before being excised.

Histological examination:

The histological examination of the specimen demonstrated a metastatic RCC with features similar to the primary renal tumor. All surgical margins were free of tumor.

Outcome:

The postoperative period was uneventful, and the patient was discharged on postoperative day two. The patient is well and has no recurrence or adrenal insufficiency 18 months post-surgery.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to our hospital for a routine checkup. This was 2 years after surgery to remove the right kidney because of kidney cancer. The patient had no symptoms. However, a CT scan of the belly showed a growth on the left adrenal gland. The adrenal gland is a small organ that sits on top of the kidney. A team of doctors met to discuss the best treatment. They decided to remove the adrenal gland using a small camera and tiny cuts.

Procedure:

The patient had surgery to remove the left adrenal gland. The surgery was done while the patient was fully asleep under general anesthesia. The patient was placed on their side with the left side facing up. The doctors filled the belly with gas to create space to work. They used a small camera and three small openings to do the surgery. The doctors found the tumor and carefully separated it from surrounding tissue. Then they removed it.

Histological examination:

Doctors looked at the removed tissue under a microscope. The growth was cancer that had spread from the original kidney cancer. The edges of the removed tissue showed no signs of cancer. This means the doctors removed all of the tumor.

Outcome:

The patient recovered well after surgery with no problems. The patient left the hospital 2 days after the operation. At 18 months after this surgery, the patient is doing well. There are no signs that the cancer has come back. The patient also has no problems with the adrenal gland being gone.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 50 years
Sex: Female
Race: South Indian

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the outpatient department with a productive cough with hemoptysis for the past 2 months. She had a history of pulmonary tuberculosis, which was discontinued within 3 weeks of starting the treatment. She also suffered from diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy for the past 8 years and was taking oral hypoglycemic agents for the same. The patient was diagnosed with an indwelling aspergilloma and was treated with an oral loading dose of Voriconazole followed by an oral maintenance dose. The patient responded well to the treatment, and the hemoptysis resolved after a lobectomy. After 3 weeks, the sputum became negative for acid-fast bacilli. The patient is currently in the 4th month of treatment and is doing well.

Laboratory Results:
Admission baseline investigations showed anemia, and the renal and liver function tests were within normal limits.

Diagnostic Study:
Computed tomogram of the thorax revealed a thick-walled cavity in the right lung upper lobe with an indwelling aspergilloma. 3 sputum samples were tested positive for acid-fast bacilli by Ziehl Neelsen's staining technique. Conventional method of culture on Lowenstein Jensen's medium yielded growth of M. tuberculosis. The anti-tuberculosis drug susceptibility test showed resistance to Isoniazid and Rifampicin but sensitive to Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin in their critical concentrations.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with pulmonary tuberculosis and an indwelling aspergilloma.

Treatment:
The patient was initially started on a daily dose of Isoniazid, Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin. However, after culture demonstrated MDR-TB, Isoniazid was stopped, and oral Levofloxacin and Ethionamide were included in the treatment regimen. Rifampicin was not included due to its interactions with Voriconazole. The patient was given an oral loading dose of Voriconazole 400 mg 12th hourly for 2 doses, which was followed by an oral maintenance dose of 200 mg 12th hourly for 6 weeks.

Follow-up:
After the treatment, the patient was counseled on the importance of adhering to medication and follow-up visits. She was advised to return to the hospital for follow-up visits and monitoring of symptoms.

Summary:
The 50-year-old South Indian woman presented with complaints of productive cough with hemoptysis for the past 2 months. She suffered from diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy and had a history of pulmonary tuberculosis. The patient was diagnosed with an indwelling aspergilloma and treated with an oral loading dose of Voriconazole followed by an oral maintenance dose. Hemoptysis resolved after lobectomy, and the sputum became negative for acid-fast bacilli after 3 weeks. The patient responded well to the treatment and is currently in the 4th month of treatment. The patient was advised to adhere to medication and follow-up visits.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 50 years
Sex: Female
Race: South Indian

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the clinic with a cough that brought up mucus and blood for the past 2 months. She had lung tuberculosis before but stopped taking her medicine within 3 weeks of starting it. She also has diabetes with nerve damage in her hands and feet for the past 8 years. She takes pills to control her blood sugar. The patient was found to have a fungus ball growing in her lung. She was treated with an antifungal medicine called Voriconazole, first at a higher dose, then at a lower dose. The patient got better with treatment. The coughing up of blood stopped after surgery to remove part of her lung. After 3 weeks, tests showed no more tuberculosis germs in her mucus. The patient is now in month 4 of treatment and is doing well.

Laboratory Results:
Tests done when the patient arrived showed low red blood cells. Her kidney and liver were working normally.

Diagnostic Study:
A CT scan of the chest showed a hollow space with thick walls in the upper part of the right lung. A fungus ball was growing inside this space. Three mucus samples tested positive for tuberculosis germs using a special staining test. Lab growth tests confirmed the germ was tuberculosis. Drug testing showed the tuberculosis was resistant to two main medicines, Isoniazid and Rifampicin. The tuberculosis could still be treated with Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with lung tuberculosis and a fungus ball in the lung.

Treatment:
The patient first started taking daily doses of Isoniazid, Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and Streptomycin. After tests showed drug-resistant tuberculosis, Isoniazid was stopped. Two new medicines, Levofloxacin and Ethionamide, were added. Rifampicin was not used because it does not work well with the antifungal medicine. For the fungus ball, the patient took Voriconazole 400 mg twice for the first day. Then she took 200 mg twice a day for 6 weeks.

Follow-up:
After treatment, the patient was told how important it is to take all medicines as directed. She was also told to come back to the hospital for check-up visits. The doctors will watch for any returning symptoms.

Summary:
The 50-year-old South Indian woman came in with a cough that brought up mucus and blood for 2 months. She has diabetes with nerve damage and had lung tuberculosis before. The patient was found to have a fungus ball in her lung. She was treated with the antifungal medicine Voriconazole, first at a higher dose, then at a lower dose. The coughing up of blood stopped after surgery to remove part of her lung. After 3 weeks, tests showed no more tuberculosis germs. The patient responded well to treatment and is now in month 4. She was told to keep taking her medicines and come back for follow-up visits.
