input:
Discharge Summary:

This is a summary of the hospital course of a 62-year-old male with Kearns-Sayre syndrome (KSS) who presented to the emergency department (ED) after two syncopal episodes. He has a past medical history of multi-system involvement including skeletal muscle myopathy, pigmentary retinopathy, heart failure, non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, pulmonary hypertension, chronic heart block, chronic kidney disease, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and mitral valve prolapse.

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted for syncope secondary to VT, and 150 mg of IV amiodarone bolus was administered. He was started on amiodarone drip at 0.5 mg/min. Follow-up AICD interrogation yielded sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia to the 140s during both syncopal episodes. Chest X-ray revealed right pleural effusion. On day two, the patient was noted to have non-sustained VT with increasing oxygen requirements. A plan was in place to transition the patient from IV to PO amiodarone, but instead, he developed VT storm and was thus continued on amiodarone drip. He was transferred to the critical care unit (CCU) for further monitoring and started on lidocaine drip (1 mg/min) with continued amiodarone and milrinone drips. In the CCU, he continued to have non-sustained VT with symptoms of palpitations despite the AICD reprogramming and medical management. He also continued to have shortness of breath with minimal improvement on bilevel positive airway pressure (BIPAP). Unfortunately, recurrent VT progressed to a VT storm, requiring multiple boluses of IV amiodarone, IV lidocaine, and multiple shocks from his AICD which were futile. The VT storm was refractory to his current medical management. The patient eventually passed away after the withdrawal of care.

Final Diagnosis:

The final diagnosis of the patient was VT storm in the setting of KSS with multi-system involvement including heart failure.

Follow-Up:

The patient was not discharged due to his unfortunate clinical outcome.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

This is a summary of the hospital stay of a 62-year-old male with Kearns-Sayre syndrome (KSS). KSS is a rare disease that affects many parts of the body. The patient came to the emergency room after fainting two times. He had many health problems including weak muscles, an eye disease, heart failure, fast heartbeats from the lower heart chambers, high blood pressure in the lungs, problems with the heart's electrical signals, kidney disease, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and a leaky heart valve.

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted because he fainted due to a fast heart rhythm called VT. He was given a heart medicine called amiodarone through an IV. He was then given this same medicine slowly through the IV over time. A check of his heart device showed that during both fainting spells, his heart was beating too fast from the lower chambers. A chest X-ray showed fluid around his right lung. On day two, the patient had more fast heartbeats and needed more oxygen. The plan was to switch his medicine from IV to pills. Instead, he had many episodes of dangerous fast heartbeats called VT storm. He stayed on the IV medicine. He was moved to the intensive care unit for closer watching. He was given another heart medicine called lidocaine through an IV. He also continued getting amiodarone and a heart pump medicine through the IV. In the intensive care unit, he kept having fast heartbeats and felt his heart racing. This happened even after his heart device was adjusted and he was given medicines. He also kept having trouble breathing. A breathing machine that helps push air into the lungs did not help much. Sadly, the dangerous fast heartbeats kept coming back. The doctors gave him many doses of amiodarone and lidocaine through the IV. His heart device shocked his heart many times. These treatments did not work. The fast heartbeats did not respond to the medicines. The patient passed away after the decision was made to stop treatment.

Final Diagnosis:

The final diagnosis was VT storm, which means many episodes of dangerous fast heartbeats. This happened because of his KSS disease that affected many parts of his body including his heart.

Follow-Up:

The patient was not sent home because he passed away during his hospital stay.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Name: [Redacted]
- Age: 65
- Gender: Male
- Admission Date: September 17, 2019
- Date of Discharge: September 30, 2019

Hospital Course Summary:
- The patient presented with nausea, abdominal distension, and weight loss, and was diagnosed with chronic viral hepatitis B.
- He underwent transcatheter hepatic arterial chemoembolization (TACE) to treat his massive left lobe hepatocellular carcinoma with multiple loci.
- After each TACE procedure, the patient developed fever with no other apparent discomfort, and was effectively treated with non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.
- The patient underwent several radiographic imaging tests to evaluate the progression of his cancer, which did not show significant improvement despite treatment.
- The patient died due to complications of advanced malignancy and end-stage hepatic cirrhosis in early February 2020.

Discharge Instructions:
- The patient has been discharged to his home after completing his hospital course.
- Family members should seek medical attention immediately if the patient experiences any signs of fever or discomfort.
- The patient's medical history and relevant health information should be provided to any healthcare providers who may treat him in the future for continuity of care.

Follow-up Recommendations:
- The patient's family members should schedule a follow-up appointment with their primary care physician.
- Additional medical services may be recommended based on the patient's condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Redacted]
Age: 65
Gender: Male
Admission Date: September 17, 2019
Date of Discharge: September 30, 2019

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came to the hospital with an upset stomach, a swollen belly, and weight loss. The patient was found to have a long-term liver infection caused by the hepatitis B virus. The patient also had a large liver cancer on the left side of the liver that had spread to several spots. The patient had a treatment where medicine was sent through a tube in the blood vessels to the liver to fight the cancer. After each treatment, the patient had a fever but felt okay otherwise. The fever was treated with pain and fever medicine, which worked well. The patient had several scans to check if the cancer was getting better. The scans showed the cancer did not improve much even with treatment. The patient passed away in early February 2020. This happened because the cancer had spread too far and the liver had stopped working properly.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient has been sent home after finishing care at the hospital. Family members should get medical help right away if the patient has a fever or feels unwell. Family members should share the patient's health history with any new doctors or nurses. This will help them give the best care possible.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient's family members should make an appointment with the patient's regular doctor. The doctor may suggest other care based on how the patient is doing.

Hospital Course:

The patient, an 8-year-old female, was admitted with complaints of retro-orbital pain and headache. Imaging revealed a mass lesion at the right orbital apex with intracranial extension. Magnetic resonance imaging brain contrast enhancement showed a heterogeneous mass lesion in the sella with suprasellar extension. The patient was diagnosed with RIM based on neuroimaging, and gamma knife radiosurgery was planned as the preferred treatment option.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name:
Date of Admission:
Date of Discharge:

The patient, an 8-year-old female, was admitted with complaints of retro-orbital pain and headache. Imaging revealed a mass lesion at the right orbital apex with intracranial extension and a heterogeneous mass lesion in the sella with suprasellar extension. The patient was diagnosed with RIM based on neuroimaging, and gamma knife radiosurgery was planned as the preferred treatment option.

During the admission, the patient underwent gamma knife radiosurgery for RIM and was clinically stable for six months. Additional follow-up appointments were scheduled with the neurology department. The patient and family were educated on the importance of adhering to recommended radiation safety precautions.

The patient was discharged in a stable condition with a follow-up plan in place. Further treatment and management will be coordinated with the Neurology Department.

Hospital Course:

The patient, an 8-year-old girl, came to the hospital with pain behind her right eye and headaches. Scans showed a growth near the back of her right eye socket. This growth had spread into the brain area. A special brain scan with dye showed a mixed-looking growth in the area at the base of the brain. This growth had also spread upward. Doctors found the patient has a rare condition called RIM based on the scan pictures. The care team planned to treat it with a focused radiation treatment called gamma knife.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name:
Date of Admission:
Date of Discharge:

The care team planned to treat it with a focused radiation treatment called gamma knife.

During the hospital stay, the patient had the gamma knife radiation treatment for RIM. She stayed healthy and stable for six months after the treatment. The patient left the hospital in stable condition with a follow-up plan ready. The brain and nerve doctors will continue to manage her care and any future treatment.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 36-year-old female, presented to the hospital with a 4-year history of dull pain on the left back and a foreign body on the left side of the spine. She denied hematuria, dysuria, or frequency. The patient was admitted, and a computed tomography (CT) scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed moderate hydronephrosis of the left kidney. A surgical procedure was successfully performed to remove a 3-cm sewing needle that was found around the upper part of the left ureter. A double-J ureteral stent was placed to relieve the obstruction. After 6 months of follow-up, the patient's ureteral obstruction and hydronephrosis were significantly reduced, and the double-J ureteral stent was removed.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient presented to the hospital with a 4-year history of dull pain on the left back and a foreign body on the left side of the spine. She denied hematuria, dysuria, or frequency. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed moderate hydronephrosis of the left kidney. A surgical procedure was performed to remove a 3-cm sewing needle that was found around the upper part of the left ureter and a double-J ureteral stent was placed to relieve the obstruction. After 6 months of follow-up, the patient's ureteral obstruction and hydronephrosis were significantly reduced, and the double-J ureteral stent was removed.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 36-year-old woman. She came to the hospital with dull pain in her left back that had lasted 4 years. She also had a foreign object on the left side of her spine. She did not have blood in her urine, pain when urinating, or a need to urinate often. The patient was admitted to the hospital. A CT scan took pictures of her belly and lower body. The scan showed that her left kidney was swollen with fluid buildup. Doctors did surgery to remove a 3-cm sewing needle. The needle was found near the upper part of the tube that carries urine from the kidney to the bladder. A small tube called a stent was placed to help urine flow better. After 6 months of checkups, the blockage and swelling in the kidney were much better. The stent was then removed.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Patient underwent a right parietal craniotomy for evacuation of a subdural hematoma, with good post-operative progress. The patient was then started on unfractionated heparin infusion, given an intravenous bolus of 5000 U and infusion rate of 1000 U/hr with an aPTT goal of 50 to 70 s for 3 days. Daily echocardiographic evaluation did not reveal any thrombosis in the prosthetic cardiac valves. On the seventh day after surgery, subcutaneous enoxaparin (2 × 0.6 cc) was started, and anti-factor Xa level was monitored until discharge, with a therapeutic range of 0.8 to 1.2 IU/mL. The patient was discharged after her anti-factor Xa level reached 1.1 IU/mL with the recommendation of twice daily 0.6 cc subcutaneous enoxaparin, and the goal INR of 3 to 3.5. Patient delivered a healthy baby via Cesarean delivery under unfractionated heparin treatment, with no complications.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

The patient had surgery on the right side of the head to remove a blood clot on the brain. The patient recovered well after surgery. The patient was then given a blood thinner medicine through a vein. This started with a first dose of 5000 units, then 1000 units per hour. The goal was to keep blood clotting time between 50 and 70 seconds for 3 days. Heart scans were done every day to check for blood clots on the artificial heart valves. No clots were found. On the seventh day after surgery, the patient started getting a different blood thinner shot under the skin twice a day at 0.6 cc each time. Blood tests checked how well this medicine was working until discharge. The target range was 0.8 to 1.2 units per milliliter. The patient was sent home when the blood test result reached 1.1 units per milliliter. The patient should continue the blood thinner shots of 0.6 cc under the skin twice a day. The goal is to keep the blood clotting measure between 3 and 3.5. The patient gave birth to a healthy baby by surgery while on the blood thinner given through a vein. There were no problems.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

This hospital course summary is for a 65-year-old male who was admitted with dyspnea and deteriorating consciousness after ingesting Arvalin(R) that contained ZnP. The patient's history included chronic alcoholism and depression. The patient's laboratory tests indicated low calcium and potassium levels and elevated CRP with low WBC count. After admission, the patient's SpO2 was normalized using a reservoir mask, and he was intubated due to impaired consciousness levels. The patient's lung imaging showed extensive alveolar infiltration, and chest X-rays revealed dull percussion notes and muffled breath sounds. The patient received electrolyte replacement using intravenous crystalloid solutions and polystyrene sulfonate. A central venous catheter was inserted into the right jugular vein, and the patient was sedated with midazolam. The patient was given antibiotics and anti-inflammatory drugs, and his kidney function was monitored. Due to tachycardia and respiratory insufficiency, endotracheal intubation was required again. The patient later underwent tracheostomy. During his psychiatric care, the patient had bronchoscopy, and subacute mucosal inflammation was detected. His tracheostoma was removed after 20 days, and he was discharged after 1 month of psychiatric inpatient care.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

This hospital course summary is for a 65-year-old male who was admitted with trouble breathing and decreasing alertness after swallowing Arvalin(R), a product that contained zinc phosphide poison. The patient had a history of long-term alcohol use and depression. The patient's blood tests showed low calcium and potassium levels and signs of infection with a low white blood cell count. After admission, the patient's oxygen levels were brought back to normal using an oxygen mask, and a breathing tube was placed in his airway because he was not fully awake. The patient's lung scans showed fluid filling the air spaces in his lungs, and chest X-rays showed dull sounds when tapping the chest and quiet breathing sounds. The patient received fluids through a vein to replace missing minerals and a medicine to balance potassium levels. A special tube was placed into a large vein in the right side of his neck, and the patient was given medicine to help him sleep. The patient was given medicines to fight infection and reduce swelling, and his kidney function was checked regularly. Because of a fast heart rate and breathing problems, a breathing tube had to be placed again. The patient later had surgery to create an opening in his throat for breathing. During his mental health care, the patient had a procedure to look inside his airways, and mild irritation of the airway lining was found. His throat opening was closed after 20 days, and he was sent home after 1 month of mental health hospital care.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient: [Name]

Age: 21

Sex: Female

Admission Date: [Date]

Discharge Date: [Date]

Medical Record Number: [Number]

Summary:

The patient presented with skin-colored swelling over the right frontotemporal region since birth. The overlying skin was devoid of hair. She also had a nodular lesion in the right upper eyelid and scleral mass in the right ocular globe, which were increasing in size in proportion to the patient's generalized body growth. The patient underwent plain computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) brain. A subcutaneous lipoma in the right frontotemporal scalp and a lipoma in the middle cranial fossa on the right side were detected. The patient's final diagnosis is ectomesodermal dysplasia, ectodermal dysplasia and cysts (ECCL), which is a rare genetic disease. The patient met the criteria for a definite diagnosis with three main systems’ involvement, including eyelid choristoma, intracranial lipoma, and a large temporal calvarial exostosis. The minor criteria were possible NP, patchy non-scarring alopecia, subcutaneous lipoma in the right frontotemporal region, and small nodular skin tags over ipsilateral face. The patient underwent surgery to remove the scleral mass and the scalp swelling for cosmetic reasons.

Course of Hospitalization:

The patient was admitted for evaluation and underwent plain CT and MRI brain. The imaging studies revealed subcutaneous lipoma in the right frontotemporal region and a lipoma in the middle cranial fossa on the right side. The patient underwent surgery to remove the scleral mass and the scalp swelling for cosmetic reasons. The patient was closely monitored throughout the hospitalization and was discharged without any complications.

Follow-up Plan:

The patient will require regular follow-up visits with a geneticist, ophthalmologist, and dermatologist for close monitoring of the disease progression and treatment. A multidisciplinary approach will be taken to manage the patient's symptoms and to improve the patient's quality of life.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Ectomesodermal dysplasia, ectodermal dysplasia and cysts (ECCL)

Discharge Condition:

Stable

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Discharge Instructions:

The patient is advised to follow up with a geneticist, ophthalmologist, and dermatologist for ongoing monitoring and treatment. The patient should continue to follow recommended health practices to maintain overall health and wellness. Any concerning symptoms or changes should be reported to the healthcare provider immediately.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient: [Name]

Age: 21

Sex: Female

Admission Date: [Date]

Discharge Date: [Date]

Medical Record Number: [Number]

Summary:

The patient was born with a skin-colored lump on the right side of the forehead and temple area. The skin over this lump had no hair. The patient also had a small growth on the right upper eyelid and a lump on the white part of the right eye. These growths got bigger as the patient grew. The patient had a CT scan and MRI of the brain. These are special tests that take pictures of the inside of the body. The tests found a fatty lump under the skin on the right forehead and temple area. They also found a fatty lump inside the skull on the right side. The patient was diagnosed with a rare condition passed down through genes called ECCL. This stands for ectomesodermal dysplasia, ectodermal dysplasia and cysts. The diagnosis was confirmed because the patient had problems in three main areas. These included a growth on the eyelid, a fatty lump inside the skull, and a bony bump on the side of the skull. The patient also had other signs of this condition. These included possible brain differences, patches of hair loss without scarring, a fatty lump under the skin, and small skin bumps on the same side of the face. The patient had surgery to remove the lump on the white of the eye and the lump on the scalp. This was done to improve how things looked.

Course of Hospitalization:

The patient came to the hospital for testing. The patient had a CT scan and MRI of the brain. These tests found a fatty lump under the skin on the right forehead and temple area. They also found a fatty lump inside the skull on the right side. The patient had surgery to remove the lump on the white of the eye and the lump on the scalp. This was done to improve how things looked. The care team watched the patient closely during the hospital stay. The patient left the hospital without any problems.

Follow-up Plan:

The patient will need regular visits with several doctors. These include a gene specialist, an eye doctor, and a skin doctor. These doctors will watch for any changes in the condition and provide treatment. The doctors will work together as a team to manage the patient's symptoms. The goal is to help the patient live a better life.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Ectomesodermal dysplasia, ectodermal dysplasia and cysts (ECCL). This is a rare condition passed down through genes.

Discharge Condition:

Stable

Discharge Medications:

None

Discharge Instructions:

The patient should see a gene specialist, an eye doctor, and a skin doctor for ongoing care and treatment. The patient should keep up with healthy habits to stay well. The patient should tell a doctor right away about any new or worrying symptoms or changes.

Hospital Course:
A 77-year-old male patient was admitted to the hospital after undergoing a burr hole irrigation for a left chronic subdural hematoma (CSDH). Despite the hematoma being organized, the patient's neurological symptoms, including motor aphasia and right mild hemiparesis, did not improve. Following a referral to our hospital, a CT scan showed a thick hematoma in the patient's left convexity.

Treatment:
MMA embolization procedure was performed using Embosphere® and three Target Helical Ultra (Stryker), followed by a craniotomy for evacuation of the OSDH under local anesthesia. The patient experienced minimal oozing from the HOM or hematoma itself during the surgery. Following the procedure, the patient's neurological symptoms improved significantly.

Diagnosis:
Left chronic subdural hematoma (CSDH)

Outcome:
The patient showed no evidence of neurological deficits after 1-year follow-up, although a CT scan showed that the hematoma remained.

Recommendations:
Ongoing monitoring of the patient's condition is recommended to ensure the continued absence of neurological deficits.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
A 77-year-old male patient came to the hospital after having a small hole drilled in the skull to drain an old blood clot on the left side of the brain. The blood clot had hardened over time. The patient still had trouble speaking and weakness on the right side of the body. After being sent to our hospital, a brain scan showed a thick blood clot on the left side of the brain.

Treatment:
Doctors blocked the blood vessel feeding the clot using tiny beads and three small coils. Then they opened part of the skull to remove the old blood clot while the patient was awake with numbing medicine. There was very little bleeding from the outer brain covering or the blood clot during surgery. After the procedure, the patient's speaking and movement problems got much better.

Diagnosis:
Old blood clot on the left side of the brain

Outcome:
One year later, the patient had no brain or nerve problems. A brain scan showed some of the blood clot was still there.

Recommendations:
The patient should keep having regular checkups to make sure no brain or nerve problems come back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of admission: [Redacted]
Date of discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the fast-track ENT clinic with persistent left-sided throat discomfort and pain. The patient reported positive reflux symptoms with heartburn. Medical management was implemented, but it yielded no symptomatic relief. The patient underwent flexible nasendoscopy and a contrast-enhanced MRI scan, which revealed post-cricoid oedema consistent with laryngopharyngeal reflux. As the symptoms continued to persist, a transnasal oesophagoscopy was performed, and a proximal oesophageal lesion was identified. Multiple punch biopsies were taken.

The histological examination of the specimen revealed squamous cell papilloma, which tested positive for human papilloma virus (HPV). Oesophageal manometry and 24-hour oesophageal pH monitoring indicated borderline pathological reflux with some proximal penetration. The patient then underwent complete endoscopic excision of the lesion. Histology confirmed HPV-positive squamous papilloma.

The patient's symptoms resolved after the excision, and the patient received continued anti-reflux treatment, including lifestyle changes. A yearly follow-up confirmed complete remission of symptoms.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with squamous cell papilloma of the oesophagus, which tested positive for human papilloma virus (HPV).

Procedures:
Transnasal oesophagoscopy was performed, and a proximal oesophageal lesion was identified. Multiple punch biopsies were taken. Complete endoscopic excision of the lesion was performed.

Follow-Up:
A yearly follow-up confirmed complete remission of symptoms. The patient received continued anti-reflux treatment, including lifestyle changes.

Summary:
The patient presented with persistent left-sided throat discomfort with positive reflux symptoms and was diagnosed with squamous cell papilloma of the oesophagus, which was HPV-positive. The patient underwent transnasal oesophagoscopy and biopsy, followed by complete endoscopic excision of the lesion. Anti-reflux treatment was continued with lifestyle changes, leading to complete remission of symptoms. A yearly follow-up confirmed the remission of symptoms.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of admission: [Redacted]
Date of discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the ear, nose, and throat clinic with ongoing pain and discomfort on the left side of the throat. The patient had acid reflux symptoms, including heartburn. Medicine was given, but it did not help the symptoms. The patient had a thin camera placed through the nose to look at the throat. The patient also had a special scan with dye to see inside the body. These tests showed swelling below the voice box caused by stomach acid coming up. Because the symptoms did not go away, a thin camera was passed through the nose into the food pipe. A growth was found in the upper part of the food pipe. Small tissue samples were taken for testing.

The lab tests showed the growth was a non-cancerous wart-like bump. It tested positive for human papilloma virus, also called HPV. More tests checked how well the food pipe muscles worked and measured acid levels over 24 hours. These tests showed mild acid reflux that sometimes reached the upper food pipe. The patient then had a procedure to fully remove the growth using a camera and tools. Lab tests confirmed it was an HPV-positive non-cancerous wart-like bump.

The patient's symptoms went away after the growth was removed. The patient continued treatment for acid reflux, including changes to daily habits. A check-up one year later showed the symptoms were completely gone.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a non-cancerous wart-like growth in the food pipe. It tested positive for human papilloma virus, also called HPV.

Procedures:
A thin camera was passed through the nose into the food pipe, and a growth was found in the upper part. Small tissue samples were taken for testing. The growth was fully removed using a camera and tools.

Follow-Up:
A check-up one year later showed the symptoms were completely gone. The patient continued treatment for acid reflux, including changes to daily habits.

Summary:
The patient came in with ongoing pain on the left side of the throat and acid reflux symptoms. The patient was diagnosed with a non-cancerous wart-like growth in the food pipe that tested positive for HPV. A thin camera was used to find the growth and take tissue samples. The growth was then fully removed. Treatment for acid reflux continued along with changes to daily habits. This led to the symptoms going away completely. A check-up one year later showed the symptoms were still gone.

input:
Discharge Summary: 

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Hospital Course: 

The patient, a 45-year-old male, was admitted to the hospital with symptoms of left flank pain, hypoesthesia, and weakness at both lower extremities. Upon examination, the patient had motor weakness in both lower extremities and elevated deep tendon reflex of the right knee. Diagnostic tests indicated fracture of T8, T9 vertebral body by bone tumor, pathologic fracture of T9 vertebral body by bone tumor, T8–10 myelopathy, and cord compression.

The patient underwent T9 corpectomy and mesh cage insertion with T8–10 posterior screw fixation. Pathological examination confirmed a diagnosis of Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH). The patient underwent radiotherapy at T8–10 spine and showed remarkable improvement in motor weaknesses and hypoesthesia.

The patient has been followed for almost 6 years without any recurrence. Discharge instructions included follow-up care with the primary care physician and regular imaging surveillance to ensure no recurrence of the bone tumor. 

Overall, the patient received excellent medical care from the healthcare team and responded well to treatment.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 45-year-old man. He came to the hospital with pain in his left side, numbness, and weakness in both legs. During the exam, the patient had weak muscles in both legs. The patient also had stronger than normal reflexes in the right knee. Tests showed that a bone tumor had caused breaks in the spine bones called T8 and T9. The tumor was also pressing on the spinal cord and causing nerve problems.

The patient had surgery to remove the damaged spine bone at T9. Doctors put in a metal cage to support the spine. They also placed screws at T8 through T10 to hold the spine steady. Lab tests of the removed tissue showed a condition called Langerhans cell histiocytosis. This is a rare disease where abnormal cells build up and can damage bones. The patient then had radiation treatment on the T8 through T10 area of the spine. After treatment, the leg weakness and numbness got much better.

The patient has been checked for almost 6 years with no sign of the tumor coming back. Discharge instructions included follow-up visits with the regular doctor. The patient should also get regular scans to make sure the bone tumor does not return.

Overall, the patient received excellent medical care from the healthcare team and responded well to treatment.

input:
Hospital Course:
The 27-year-old African-American woman was admitted to the hospital with chest pain, dyspnea on exertion, and a malar rash. Positive tests for anti-nuclear antibodies and anti-double-stranded DNA confirmed SLE-related pericarditis. Transthoracic echocardiography showed severe PH with an estimated RVSP of 92 mmHg and severe right ventricular dysfunction. After initiation of appropriate treatment, the patient showed improvement of muscle weakness and exercise capacity. Repeat transthoracic echocardiography four months later showed a normal right ventricular size and function with an estimated RVSP of 29 mmHg. Repeat right heart catheterization showed a reduction in the mean pulmonary artery pressure, an increase in cardiac output, and a reduction in pulmonary vascular resistance. The patient was started on oral therapy with sildenafil and treprostinil was tapered until discontinuation.

Diagnosis:
SLE-associated PH and polymyositis.

Treatment:
Pulse dose methylprednisolone, intravenous treprostinil, prednisone, and mycophenolate mofetil were prescribed. The patient was discharged on low-dose treprostinil and oral therapy with sildenafil was initiated. Prednisone was tapered over three months.

Follow-up:
The patient showed improvement in her leg weakness and exercise capacity. Repeat transthoracic echocardiography showed a normal right ventricular size and function. Repeat right heart catheterization showed a reduction in the mean pulmonary artery pressure, an increase in cardiac output, and a reduction in pulmonary vascular resistance. The patient continues to be followed up as an outpatient.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The 27-year-old African-American woman came to the hospital with chest pain, trouble breathing during activity, and a butterfly-shaped rash on her face. Blood tests showed she had lupus, which was causing swelling around her heart. A heart ultrasound showed very high pressure in her lungs and her heart's right side was not working well. The estimated pressure in her right heart was 92, which is very high. After starting the right treatment, the patient got stronger and could exercise better. A follow-up heart ultrasound four months later showed her right heart was now normal in size and working well. The pressure dropped to 29, which is normal. Another test using a thin tube in her heart showed lower pressure in her lung arteries and better blood flow. The patient started taking sildenafil by mouth and slowly stopped taking treprostinil.

Diagnosis:
Lupus-related high lung pressure and muscle swelling disease.

Treatment:
The patient received high doses of a strong anti-swelling medicine called methylprednisolone through an IV. She also got treprostinil through an IV and took prednisone and mycophenolate mofetil by mouth. When she left the hospital, she was on a low dose of treprostinil and started taking sildenafil by mouth. Her prednisone dose was slowly lowered over three months.

Follow-up:
The patient's leg weakness got better and she could exercise more easily. A follow-up heart ultrasound showed her right heart was normal in size and working well. A heart tube test showed lower pressure in her lung arteries and better blood flow. The patient keeps coming back for check-ups at the clinic.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 68 years
Sex: Male
Hospitalization Date Range: Unknown
Discharge Date: Unknown

Comprehensive Summary of Hospitalization:

The patient, a 68-year-old male with a history of malignant mesothelioma, was admitted to our department with gradually worsening bilateral low extremity weakness. The patient had previous occupational exposure to asbestos and was diagnosed with asbestosis in 1989. The neurological examination revealed a paraparesis of lower extremities, decreased sensation on the right side below T1, increased reflexes in the lower extremities, and partial Brown–Sèquard syndrome. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the cervical-thoracic spine revealed a hypointense left intramedullary anterolateral lesion at the level of C6–C7, which was strongly enhancing after gadolinium administration.

The patient underwent surgery through median posterior short incision and C6–C7 laminectomy. The intramedullary grayish lesion was completely removed, even though a clear plane was difficult to identify between the tumor and surrounding spinal cord. Pathological examination of the specimen revealed malignant mesothelioma.

The postoperative course was favorable, and the patient's neurological condition improved. Twenty days after surgery, the patient had completely regained normal lower extremity function. However, eight months after surgery, the patient died due to the progression of the thoracic disease.

Overall, the patient’s hospital course was successful and without complications. The patient was discharged in stable condition with appropriate follow-up arranged. The patient's family was informed about the condition and the plan going forward.

Discharge Diagnosis:
- Malignant mesothelioma

Follow-up Recommendations:
- The patient's family should be informed about the condition and the plan going forward.
- Appropriate follow-up should be arranged.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 38-year-old woman who presented with a six-month history of progressive exertional dyspnea and new onset of syncopal episodes. Auscultation reveals a continuous murmur at the left sternal border. Transthoracic echocardiography reveals an anomalous vascular structure origin from circumflex artery and draining to the main pulmonary artery with significant left to right shunt, mild dilatation of right heart chambers and 60% ejection fraction. Magnetic resonance imaging and coronary angiography confirms the presence of a coronary artery fistula. The pulmonary to systemic flow ratio was calculated as 1.8 to 1. Left anterior mini-thoracotomy was performed, the fistulous tracts were identified, and the fistula on the pulmonary artery side was obliterated with multiple pledgeted 5-0 polypropylene sutures. Transesophageal echocardiography confirmed a successful closure of the fistula, lack of residual shunt and lack of wall motion abnormalities. Electrocardiogram did not show any signs of ischemia. The patient was extubated one hour after completion of the procedure and discharged on the second postoperative day. At 6 and 12 months follow-up, the patient remained free of any cardiovascular symptoms, while echocardiography showed no residual flow. An excellent cosmetic result was achieved.

Hospital Course Summary:
A 38-year-old female presented with dyspnea and syncopal episodes, and murmur on auscultation. The patient's medical examination revealed coronary artery fistula with significant left to right shunt and mild dilatation of the right heart chambers. The patient underwent surgical correction by left anterior mini-thoracotomy, and successful obliteration of the fistulous tracts was noted under transesophageal echocardiography with no residual shunt or wall motion abnormalities seen. The patient remained symptom-free at the six and twelve months follow-up visits, with no residual flow detected on echocardiography.@endsection
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 38-year-old woman who came in with six months of worsening shortness of breath during activity and new fainting spells. Listening to the heart revealed an ongoing heart murmur on the left side of the chest. An ultrasound of the heart showed an abnormal blood vessel coming from a heart artery and connecting to the lung artery. This caused blood to flow the wrong way from the left side of the heart to the right side. There was mild widening of the right heart chambers and the heart was pumping at 60 percent strength. Special imaging and dye tests of the heart arteries confirmed an abnormal connection between a heart artery and another blood vessel. The amount of extra blood flow to the lungs was measured at 1.8 times higher than normal. Surgery was done through a small cut on the left side of the chest. The abnormal connections were found and closed with several stitches backed by small fabric patches. An ultrasound probe placed in the throat confirmed the abnormal connection was fully closed. There was no leftover abnormal blood flow and the heart wall was moving normally. A heart tracing showed no signs of poor blood flow to the heart muscle. The patient had the breathing tube removed one hour after surgery and went home two days after the procedure. At the six month and twelve month checkups, the patient had no heart or blood vessel symptoms. Heart ultrasounds showed no leftover abnormal blood flow. The small scar healed very well.

Hospital Course Summary:
A 38-year-old woman came in with shortness of breath, fainting spells, and a heart murmur heard during the exam. Testing showed an abnormal connection between a heart artery and the lung artery. This caused blood to flow the wrong way and mild widening of the right heart chambers. The patient had surgery through a small cut on the left side of the chest to close the abnormal connections. An ultrasound during surgery confirmed full closure with no leftover abnormal blood flow and normal heart wall movement. The patient had no symptoms at the six month and twelve month follow-up visits. Heart ultrasounds showed no leftover abnormal blood flow.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:
The patient, a 61-year-old woman presented to the department with vague neck pain, rachialgia, and left forearm numbness. She was started on corticosteroids and anti-inflammatory medications while waiting for radiological diagnosis. The cervical MRI revealed a left cervical plexus neurinoma, but as the patient's symptoms worsened, an urgent CT scan with 3D reconstruction was performed and the possibility of a vascular lesion was raised. A contrast-enhanced MRI/MRA scan of the cervical region and brain confirmed the presence of a vertebral artery pseudoaneurysm, with a double lumen right above the lesion that was attributed to a vertebral artery dissection. The patient received warfarin and endovascular proximal occlusion of the left vertebral artery to treat this condition. Her thorough history revealed no history of trauma, but the patient had received chiropractic treatment for a sore neck two weeks prior to the onset of symptoms. The MRA verified the absence of an intracranial aneurysm and confirmed the presence of adequate collateral blood supply by the contralateral vertebral artery in the face of a possible vertebral artery occlusion.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:
The patient, a 61-year-old woman came to the hospital with unclear neck pain, back pain, and numbness in her left forearm. She was given steroid medicines and medicines to reduce swelling while waiting for imaging tests. A neck MRI showed a nerve tumor on the left side of her neck. However, her symptoms got worse, so doctors did an urgent CT scan with detailed 3D images. This scan suggested there might be a blood vessel problem. A special MRI scan of the neck and brain with dye showed a bulge in the wall of an artery in her neck. There was also a tear in the inner wall of this artery, causing blood to flow in two separate channels. The patient was given a blood-thinning medicine called warfarin. Doctors also did a procedure through her blood vessels to block the damaged artery. When asked about her health history, the patient reported no injuries. However, she had received a chiropractic neck treatment two weeks before her symptoms started. The imaging tests confirmed there was no bulge in the brain arteries. The tests also showed that the artery on the other side of her neck could supply enough blood to the brain if the damaged artery was blocked.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 47-year-old right-handed man who presented with progressive headaches and facial numbness. MRI revealed a 7 cm extra-axial, enhancing mass on the left frontal para-sagittal convexity. There was significant mass effect and edema present, along with invasion of the superior sagittal sinus and frontal calvarium. Angiography revealed significant blood supply to the tumor from the left external carotid artery, primarily via the superficial temporal artery and middle meningeal artery. Preoperative embolization with Onyx allowed significant devascularization of the majority of the tumor. The patient underwent total surgical resection of the tumor, and pathology confirmed the diagnosis of WHO grade II hemangiopericytoma. Postoperatively, the patient remained neurologically intact. On the 2-year follow-up MRI, no evidence of tumor recurrence was seen.

Diagnosis and Treatment:

The patient presented with a 7 cm extra-axial, enhancing mass on the left frontal para-sagittal convexity, which was confirmed on MRI. The diagnosis of WHO grade II hemangiopericytoma was made via pathology following surgical resection of the tumor. Treatment involved preoperative embolization with Onyx, followed by total surgical resection of the tumor.

Follow-up and Prognosis:

On the 2-year follow-up MRI, no evidence of tumor recurrence was seen. The patient was discharged in stable condition with no neurological deficits.

Discharge Medications:

None specified.

Discharge Instructions:

None specified.

Follow-up:

The patient is advised to follow up with their primary care provider and neurosurgeon as directed.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 47-year-old right-handed man who came in with worsening headaches and numbness in his face. A brain scan showed a large tumor about 7 centimeters wide on the left front side of his brain. The tumor was pressing on the brain and causing swelling. It had also grown into a major blood vessel and part of the skull bone. A test to look at blood vessels showed the tumor was getting a lot of blood from arteries on the left side of the head. Before surgery, doctors injected a special glue-like material to block blood flow to most of the tumor. The patient then had surgery to remove the entire tumor. Lab tests on the tumor showed it was a slow-growing blood vessel tumor called a grade II hemangiopericytoma. After surgery, the patient's brain function stayed normal. A brain scan done two years later showed no signs that the tumor had come back.

Diagnosis and Treatment:

The patient had a large tumor about 7 centimeters wide on the left front side of his brain. This was seen on a brain scan. Lab tests after surgery confirmed the tumor was a slow-growing blood vessel tumor called a grade II hemangiopericytoma. Treatment included blocking blood flow to the tumor before surgery. Then doctors did surgery to remove the entire tumor.

Follow-up and Prognosis:

A brain scan done two years later showed no signs that the tumor had come back. The patient left the hospital in stable condition with normal brain function.

Discharge Medications:

None specified.

Discharge Instructions:

None specified.

Follow-up:

The patient should see their regular doctor and brain surgeon as directed.

input:
Patient Information:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 53 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted for evaluation of an ulcerative lesion on the left side of the lower lip. Biopsies confirmed the diagnosis of well-differentiated infiltrating squamous cell carcinoma, which was staged at T2N0M0 according to TNM guidelines. The patient opted for HDRIB treatment and received five equidistant metallic needles to insert plastic catheters through the tissue. CT image was taken to plan HDRIB treatment. The patient received one fraction of 5.00 Gy on the first day of treatment and two daily fractions of 5.00 Gy for the next 4 days. 

One month post-treatment, the patient had localised acute radiation toxicity with bleeding ulcerative mucositis at the treatment site. The patient was given analgesics, anti-inflammatory drugs, and a mouthwash to treat his symptoms. At the second follow-up, a month later, the ulcer measured 1.5 × 1.5 cm with an indurated area. The LL was epithelialised and tumour-free at his third follow-up, a month later. Finally, a two-year-long evaluation was conducted, which revealed that the patient was asymptomatic and disease-free, albeit mild atrophy and skin hypopigmentation were observed as long-term side effects of the treatment. 

The patient stated that he was pleased with the results and aesthetic outcome of his procedure.
clean_ref:
Patient Information:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 53 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came to the hospital for a sore on the left side of the lower lip. Tests showed the patient had a type of skin cancer that was growing into nearby tissue. The cancer was at an early stage and had not spread to other parts of the body. The patient chose to have a type of radiation treatment. Doctors placed five metal needles with thin tubes through the tissue to deliver the treatment. A scan was taken to plan the radiation treatment. The patient received one dose of radiation on the first day. Then the patient received two doses per day for the next four days.

One month after treatment, the patient had some side effects at the treatment area. These included bleeding and a painful open sore in the mouth. The patient was given pain medicine, medicine to reduce swelling, and a mouth rinse to help with symptoms. At the second check-up one month later, the sore measured 1.5 by 1.5 cm with a firm area around it. At the third check-up one month after that, the lower lip had healed and showed no signs of cancer. After two years of check-ups, the patient had no symptoms and no cancer. However, there was some mild thinning of the skin and lighter skin color as lasting side effects from treatment.

The patient said he was happy with the results and how his lip looked after the procedure.

HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Patient: 47-year-old female

Admission Date: May 2018
Discharge Date: N/A

TREATMENT:
- Crizotinib therapy initiated on June 1st, 2018.
- Alectinib therapy initiated on August 10, 2018.
- Ceritinib therapy initiated for targeted therapy on January 10th, 2020.
- Anlotinib therapy initiated on June 8th, 2020.
- Abusartan combined with carboplatin chemotherapy received five cycles from September 9th, 2020, up to December 31, 2020.

CURRENT CONDITION:
- Stable disease (assessment as per CT results) as of December 31st, 2020.
- Patient continued taking alectinib for maintenance treatment.

MEDICAL HISTORY:
- Intermittent dry cough with back pain without obvious inducement since May 2018.
- Never-smoker.
- Chest CT showed a mass (about 42 × 55 mm) in the patient's left lower lobe, accompanied by liver, scapula, and right frontal lobe metastases.
- Pathological examination suggested that the patient's liver metastasis was a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma.
- EML4-ALK fusion detected by mutational analysis using an amplification refractory mutation system-PCR.
- ALT was 1079 U/L (reference range 5–40 U/L), and CK-MB was 250 U/L (reference range <24 U/L) on July 26th, 2018.
- Tumor recurrence in the left hilum and lower left lung was found in the chest CT scan on May 19th, 2020.
- Poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma discovered through biopsy pathology.
- Fusion of EML4 Exon 13 and ALK Exon 20 found in both squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma samples through Nanodigmbio(Nanjing) Biotechnology Co., Ltd's NGS-based assay for detecting cancer-relevant genes.
- PD-L1 (Ventana Medical Systems, Inc.) TPS was 80% and 85% in both specimens' tumors as determined with the SP263 assay.

FOLLOW-UP RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Patient has received comprehensive hospital treatment, and follow-up recommendations will be discussed in further consultations.
clean_ref:
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Patient: 47-year-old female

Admission Date: May 2018
Discharge Date: N/A

TREATMENT:
Crizotinib treatment started on June 1st, 2018.
Alectinib treatment started on August 10, 2018.
Ceritinib treatment started as a targeted cancer therapy on January 10th, 2020.
Anlotinib treatment started on June 8th, 2020.
The patient received five rounds of Abusartan combined with carboplatin cancer-killing drug treatment from September 9th, 2020, up to December 31, 2020.

CURRENT CONDITION:
The cancer was stable based on CT scan results as of December 31st, 2020.
The patient continued taking alectinib to help keep the cancer under control.

MEDICAL HISTORY:
The patient had an on-and-off dry cough with back pain for no clear reason since May 2018.
The patient never smoked.
A chest CT scan showed a growth about 42 by 55 mm in the lower part of the left lung. The cancer had also spread to the liver, shoulder blade, and right front part of the brain.
Lab tests on tissue from the liver showed a fast-growing cancer that started in gland cells.
Gene testing found an abnormal joining of two genes called EML4 and ALK.
On July 26th, 2018, the liver enzyme ALT level was 1079 U/L, which is much higher than the normal range of 5 to 40 U/L. The heart enzyme CK-MB level was 250 U/L, which is much higher than the normal level of less than 24 U/L.
A chest CT scan on May 19th, 2020, found the cancer had come back near the center of the left lung and in the lower left lung.
A tissue sample test found a fast-growing cancer that started in flat cells lining the lung.
Advanced gene testing found the same abnormal joining of EML4 and ALK genes in both cancer types.
A test showed that 80 percent and 85 percent of the cancer cells in both samples had a protein called PD-L1, which can affect how the body fights cancer.

FOLLOW-UP RECOMMENDATIONS:
The patient has received full hospital treatment. Next steps for care will be talked about at future doctor visits.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: _______________                Date of Admission: __________
MRN: _____________________                Date of Discharge: __________

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 60-year-old, morbidly obese male with a medical history of uncontrolled hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus with microvascular complications, stroke without residual weakness, and obstructive sleep apnea who was admitted to the hospital with a three-day history of severe headache. An initial CT scan of the brain showed no evidence of subarachnoid hemorrhage. However, an MRI scan of the brain revealed generalized volume loss.

Physical Examination:
On physical examination, the patient had mild bilateral pedal edema, and we were unable to evaluate jugular venous pulsations due to a thick short neck. Neurological examination was unremarkable, and fundoscopic examination did not reveal any papilledema. Electrocardiogram showed low voltage sinus rhythm. Chest x-ray showed mild left pleural effusion and cardiomegaly. An echocardiogram revealed a large pericardial effusion with features of tamponade.

Diagnostic Studies:
The patient underwent emergent pericardial drainage, and the fluid analysis revealed it as a hemorrhagic pericardial effusion. The septic screen was negative, and vasculitis screening with ANA and ANCA was also negative. A PET CT scan did not detect any malignant focus. An MRI scan of the heart showed basal septal mid-wall myocardial enhancement of non-ischemic pattern, likely due to myocarditis or cardiomyopathy.

Diagnosis:
After ruling out secondary causes of hemorrhagic pericardial effusion, such as malignancy, autoimmune diseases, and TB, our patient was labeled as idiopathic myocarditis leading to chronic pericardial effusion based on MRI scan of the heart.

Management:
The patient's headache was relieved after pericardiocentesis, and his blood pressure stabilized after resuming his home antihypertensive medications. The patient was observed in the hospital for ten days with regular echocardiogram studies. He was asymptomatic and discharged with regular follow-up in the cardiology clinic. The cardiologist outpatient follow-ups with repeated echocardiogram studies for a further five months did not show any fluid reaccumulation. The patient was followed for one year after discharge where he remained clinically stable.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Idiopathic myocarditis leading to chronic pericardial effusion.

Follow-up:
The patient will have regular follow-up in the cardiology clinic to monitor the progress of the patient's condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: _______________                Date of Admission: __________
MRN: _____________________                Date of Discharge: __________

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 60-year-old, very overweight male with a health history of uncontrolled high blood pressure, type 2 diabetes with damage to small blood vessels, stroke without lasting weakness, and a sleep condition that blocks breathing. He was admitted to the hospital after having a bad headache for three days. A first brain scan showed no bleeding around the brain. However, another type of brain scan showed some brain shrinkage.

Physical Examination:
During the physical exam, the patient had mild swelling in both feet. The doctors could not check the neck veins because of a thick short neck. The nerve and brain exam was normal. An eye exam did not show any swelling of the nerve in the back of the eye. A heart tracing showed weak electrical signals but normal rhythm. A chest x-ray showed mild fluid buildup around the left lung and an enlarged heart. A heart ultrasound showed a large amount of fluid around the heart that was pressing on it.

Diagnostic Studies:
The patient had an urgent procedure to drain fluid from around the heart. Tests showed the fluid contained blood. Tests for infection came back negative. Tests for diseases where the body attacks itself were also negative. A full body scan did not find any cancer. A detailed heart scan showed unusual tissue in part of the heart wall. This was likely caused by heart muscle swelling or heart muscle disease.

Diagnosis:
The doctors ruled out other causes of bloody fluid around the heart, such as cancer, diseases where the body attacks itself, and tuberculosis. The patient was diagnosed with heart muscle swelling of unknown cause. This led to long-term fluid buildup around the heart, based on the detailed heart scan.

Management:
The patient's headache went away after the fluid was drained from around the heart. His blood pressure became stable after he restarted his home blood pressure medicines. The patient stayed in the hospital for ten days with regular heart ultrasounds. He had no symptoms and was sent home with regular follow-up at the heart clinic. Heart clinic visits with repeat heart ultrasounds over the next five months showed no new fluid buildup. The patient was checked for one year after leaving the hospital and remained in stable health.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Heart muscle swelling of unknown cause leading to long-term fluid buildup around the heart.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: _______________                Date of Admission: __________
MRN: _____________________                Date of Discharge: __________

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 60-year-old, very overweight male with a health history of uncontrolled high blood pressure, type 2 diabetes with damage to small blood vessels, stroke without lasting weakness, and a sleep condition that blocks breathing. He was admitted to the hospital after having a bad headache for three days. A first brain scan showed no bleeding around the brain. However, another type of brain scan showed some brain shrinkage.

Physical Examination:
During the physical exam, the patient had mild swelling in both feet. The doctors could not check the neck veins because of a thick short neck. The nerve and brain exam was normal. An eye exam did not show any swelling of the nerve in the back of the eye. A heart tracing showed weak electrical signals but normal rhythm. A chest x-ray showed mild fluid buildup around the right lung and an enlarged heart. A heart ultrasound showed a large amount of fluid around the heart that was pressing on it.

Diagnostic Studies:
The patient had an urgent procedure to drain fluid from around the heart. Tests showed the fluid contained blood. Tests for infection came back negative. Tests for diseases where the body attacks itself were also negative. A full body scan did not find any cancer. A detailed heart scan showed unusual tissue in part of the heart wall. This was likely caused by heart muscle swelling or heart muscle disease.

Diagnosis:
The doctors ruled out other causes of bloody fluid around the heart, such as cancer, diseases where the body attacks itself, and tuberculosis. The patient was diagnosed with heart muscle swelling of unknown cause. This led to long-term fluid buildup around the heart, based on the detailed heart scan.

Management:
The patient's headache went away after the fluid was drained from around the heart. His blood pressure became stable after he restarted his home blood pressure medicines. The patient stayed in the hospital for ten days with regular heart ultrasounds. He had no symptoms and was sent home with regular follow-up at the heart clinic. Heart clinic visits with repeat heart ultrasounds over the next five months showed no new fluid buildup. The patient was checked for one year after leaving the hospital and remained in stable health.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Heart muscle swelling of unknown cause leading to long-term fluid buildup around the heart.

Follow-up:
The patient will have regular follow-up at the heart clinic to check on the patient's condition.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 65 years
Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: 5th postoperative day

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with left-sided neck pain and swelling with a gradual onset of dysphagia. A linear foreign body with horizontal orientation was seen in the superior part of the pharynx along with a 40 × 20 mm collection around it on a CT scan. There was also thrombosis with an air bubble in the left internal jugular vein and the distal end of the fishbone was adjacent to the left internal jugular vein. Surgery was performed to remove the impacted fishbone, repair the pharyngeal perforation, ligate the injured internal jugular vein, and drain the abscess cavities. The patient made an uneventful recovery and was started on a liquid diet on the 4th postoperative day.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Left-sided neck abscess with hypopharyngeal fishbone impaction and associated thrombosis of the left internal jugular vein.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was discharged on the 5th postoperative day and was advised to follow up with her primary care physician for further evaluation and care. She was instructed to avoid solid foods and to continue on a liquid diet for the first week after discharge. Pain management should continue as needed, and the Penrose drain should remain in place until it is removed by her primary care physician. The patient was advised to avoid smoking, alcohol, and any activities that could lead to excessive stress on the neck and to maintain proper oral hygiene. She was advised to watch for any signs of infection, such as fever, chills, and redness or swelling around the incision site. If any of these symptoms occur, she should seek medical attention immediately.

Follow-Up:
The patient was advised to follow up with her primary care physician within the next seven days for a review of her medical condition and to manage her medications. The patient should continue to be monitored closely for any potential complications relating to the abscess and internal jugular vein ligation.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 65 years
Admission Date: N/A
Discharge Date: 5th day after surgery

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with pain and swelling on the left side of her neck. She also had trouble swallowing that got worse over time. A scan showed a fishbone stuck sideways in the upper part of her throat. There was also a pocket of infected fluid about 40 by 20 millimeters around the fishbone. A blood clot with an air bubble had formed in a large vein on the left side of her neck. The end of the fishbone was touching this vein. The patient had surgery to remove the stuck fishbone. The doctors also fixed the hole in her throat and tied off the damaged vein. They drained the pockets of infected fluid. The patient healed well without problems. She started drinking liquids on the 4th day after surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient had a pocket of infection on the left side of her neck. This was caused by a fishbone stuck in her lower throat. She also had a blood clot in the large vein on the left side of her neck.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient went home on the 5th day after surgery. She should see her regular doctor for more care and checkups. She should not eat solid foods for the first week after leaving the hospital. She should only drink liquids during this time. She should keep taking pain medicine as needed. The drain tube in her neck should stay in place. Her regular doctor will take it out later. The patient should not smoke or drink alcohol. She should not do anything that puts too much strain on her neck. She should keep her mouth clean. She should watch for signs of infection. These include fever, chills, and redness or swelling near the cut. If she notices any of these signs, she should get medical help right away.

Follow-Up:
The patient should see her regular doctor within the next seven days. The doctor will check how she is healing and manage her medicines. The patient needs to be watched closely for any problems. These could be related to the infection or the vein that was tied off.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]
Patient Name: [Name]
Age: 46
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: Schwannoma, Cognard IIa+b Dural Arteriovenous Fistula

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with symptoms of right ear congestion, pulsatile tinnitus, and mild hearing loss. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a lobulated mass at the cerebellopontine angle. An Octreoscan was performed, which showed intense tracer uptake in the mass, thereby supporting the diagnosis of paraganglioma. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) was conducted, which demonstrated a lack of vascularity associated with the tumor. However, it revealed an incidental discovery of a Cognard IIa+b dural arteriovenous fistula in the right posterior fossa. Due to the extensive volume of the tumor, the patient underwent a staged surgical resection.
The first surgery included a modified radical right neck dissection, right superficial parotidectomy with facial nerve dissection, as well as the initial approach to the tumor and partial extradural resection. One week later, the patient underwent the second stage surgery where the tumor was radically resected using a right transcochlear approach. The right sigmoid sinus was ligated. The facial nerve was mobilized and transposed. Postoperative MRI confirmed complete resection of the tumor. Histological analysis of samples from both surgeries confirmed the tumor not to be a paraganglioma, but instead a schwannoma.
The patient had a long recovery from the surgery due to the lower cranial nerve palsies but has shown gradual improvement. Her latest follow-up MRI showed no evidence of recurrent tumor. DSA revealed large feeders from the right middle meningeal artery (MMA) and occipital arteries into the residual dural arteriovenous fistula. The fistula was embolized utilizing Onyx in the right MMA. Final right common carotid artery (CCA) angiogram demonstrated resolution in arteriovenous shunting. Vertebral artery injection after embolization demonstrated minimal residual shunting via the right anterior inferior cerebellar artery (AICA). Further intervention was declined by the patient, and angiographic follow-up in one year has been scheduled.

Discharge Medications:
[Not applicable]

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should follow up with their primary care physician as well as their neurologist. It is essential to report any new or recurrent symptoms immediately. The patient should take good care of their health by maintaining a balanced diet and regular exercise routine.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]
Patient Name: [Name]
Age: 46
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: Schwannoma (a non-cancerous nerve tumor), Cognard IIa+b Dural Arteriovenous Fistula (an abnormal connection between blood vessels in the brain covering)

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with a plugged feeling in the right ear, a whooshing sound matching the heartbeat, and mild hearing loss. A brain scan using magnets and radio waves showed a bumpy mass near the brain stem. A special scan using a tracer was done. It showed the mass absorbed the tracer strongly. This suggested the mass might be a certain type of tumor called a paraganglioma. An imaging test using dye in the blood vessels was performed. It showed the tumor did not have many blood vessels. However, it found an unexpected abnormal connection between blood vessels in the brain covering on the right side. Because the tumor was very large, the patient needed two separate surgeries.
The first surgery removed tissue from the right side of the neck, part of a saliva gland near the ear, and part of the tumor. The facial nerve was carefully worked around. One week later, the patient had the second surgery. The rest of the tumor was removed through an approach near the inner ear on the right side. A large blood vessel on the right side of the brain was tied off. The facial nerve was moved and repositioned. A brain scan after surgery confirmed all of the tumor was removed. Lab tests on tissue from both surgeries showed the tumor was not a paraganglioma. It was a schwannoma, which is a non-cancerous tumor that grows from nerve covering cells.
The patient had a long recovery because some nerves in the lower part of the head were weakened. However, the patient has slowly gotten better. The most recent brain scan showed no sign the tumor has come back. The blood vessel imaging test showed blood was still flowing into the abnormal vessel connection from two main blood vessels. The abnormal vessel connection was treated by injecting a special glue-like material to block it. A final blood vessel image showed the abnormal blood flow was mostly fixed. A small amount of abnormal blood flow remained through a smaller blood vessel. The patient chose not to have more treatment. A follow-up blood vessel imaging test has been set for one year from now.

Discharge Medications:
[Not applicable]

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should schedule visits with their regular doctor and their brain and nerve specialist. It is very important to report any new symptoms or returning symptoms right away. The patient should take care of their health by eating well and exercising regularly.

Hospital Course:
The patient, an 8-year-old female child, was admitted to our department due to diminution of vision in the right eye since 5 years, with headache and vomiting since 6 months. On examination, no perception of light was observed in the right eye with normal vision in the left eye. An MRI revealed a cystic lesion at the right temporo-frontal-parietal region measuring 8 × 9 × 6.5 cm. A small rounded nodule was seen on the medial aspect of the cystic lesion at the paraventricular location. The lesion was reported as a cystic astrocytoma/ganglioglioma. Endocrine workup subsequently revealed secondary hypothyroidism.  

Treatment:
The patient underwent right fronto-temporo-parietal craniotomy for drainage and marsupialisation of the cystic lesion. The cystic fluid was yellowish and machine oil-like, containing cholesterol crystals and flecks mostly over the medial side. Histological examination revealed inflamed Rathke's cleft cyst with a cystic wall lined by a flattened to pseudostratified squamous epithelium with congested blood vessels, chronic inflammatory infiltrate in the form of lymphocyte, plasma cells, and lymphoid aggregates.

Outcomes:
Postoperatively, the patient's recovery was uneventful, and the headache and vomiting subsided completely. The patient is in regular follow-up with postoperative MRI at 6 months without any recurrence.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient, an 8-year-old girl, was admitted because of vision loss in the right eye for 5 years. She also had headache and vomiting for 6 months. During the exam, the right eye could not see any light at all. The left eye had normal vision. A brain scan showed a fluid-filled sac on the right side of the brain. It measured about 8 by 9 by 6.5 centimeters. A small round lump was seen on the inner side of this sac, near the brain's fluid spaces. Doctors first thought it might be a type of brain tumor. Blood tests later showed that the thyroid gland was not working well due to a problem with hormone signals from the brain.

Treatment:
The patient had surgery to open the skull on the right side of the head. The surgeon drained the fluid-filled sac and created an opening so fluid would not build up again. The fluid inside was yellow and looked like thick oil. It contained fatty crystals and small bits, mostly on the inner side. Lab tests of the tissue showed the sac was a harmless cyst that had become swollen and irritated. The cyst wall was lined with flat to layered skin-like cells. It also had swollen blood vessels and signs of long-term irritation, including certain white blood cells grouped together.

Outcomes:
After surgery, the patient recovered without any problems. The headache and vomiting went away completely. The patient continues to see the doctor regularly. A brain scan done 6 months after surgery showed the cyst has not come back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:

Name: [Redacted]
Age: 60 Years
Gender: Male
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Diagnosis: Membranous nephropathy

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 60-year-old male, was admitted to our hospital with a two-month history of fatigue and trace lower extremity edema. The patient was also suffering from arthralgias of both knees and hands. Upon examination, it was found that the patient had been diagnosed with newly proteinuria. The patient's nocturia also stood out, alongside an increased urinary frequency.

Physical examination revealed trace lower extremity edema, however, there were no other abnormalities in cardiac, pulmonary, or abdominal exam. The patient had undergone routine cholecystectomy four months prior to admission. Laboratory examination showed a mild elevation in erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Urinalysis revealed 3+ protein but no nitrites or glucose. Renal function was normal. Urine protein was quantified as 10.68 gr/24h.

Further investigation was carried out to determine the cause of membranous glomerulonephritis. The patient was tested for secondary causes which included anti-double stranded deoxyribonucleic acid (anti-dsDNA) antibodies, antinuclear antibodies (ANA), titers for antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA), complement levels, and tests for cryoglobulins and rheumatoid factor. The patient's results were negative or within normal ranges. Serologies for hepatitis A, B, C, and HIV were also non-reactive.

A thoracoabdominal tomography was performed on the patient, which was normal. The renal biopsy showed that the membranous nephropathy was the cause of nephrotic syndrome. IgG deposition was seen along the capillary wall.

Upon review, there was no evidence of acute or chronic infections, nor any other features of related medical issues. The patient was diagnosed with membranous nephropathy.

The patient was discharged in good condition with a diagnosis of membranous nephropathy. The patient received no medications during the hospitalization of his condition.

Follow-Up Instructions:

The patient should seek medical advice from his physician if trace lower extremity edema or any other symptoms recur.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:

Name: [Redacted]
Age: 60 Years
Gender: Male
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Diagnosis: Membranous nephropathy (a kidney disease that causes protein to leak into the urine)

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 60-year-old male, came to the hospital after feeling tired for two months. The patient also had mild swelling in the legs. The patient was having joint pain in both knees and hands. Tests showed that protein was leaking into the urine, which was a new problem. The patient was also waking up at night to urinate and going to the bathroom more often than usual.

A physical exam showed mild swelling in the legs. The heart, lungs, and belly were all normal. The patient had surgery to remove the gallbladder four months before this hospital stay. Blood tests showed the body had slightly higher than normal signs of swelling inside. A urine test showed high levels of protein but no signs of infection or sugar. The kidneys were working normally. The amount of protein lost in urine over one day was very high.

More tests were done to find out why protein was leaking from the kidneys. The patient was tested for diseases that attack the body's own tissues. These included tests for lupus and other immune system problems. All these test results came back normal or negative. Tests for hepatitis A, B, C, and HIV were also negative.

The patient had a scan of the chest and belly, which was normal. A small piece of kidney tissue was removed and looked at under a microscope. This test showed that the kidney's filters were damaged, causing protein to leak into the urine. A type of protein made by the immune system was found stuck to the kidney's tiny blood vessels.

Doctors found no signs of infection or other related health problems. The patient was diagnosed with membranous nephropathy.

The patient left the hospital in good condition. The diagnosis was membranous nephropathy. The patient did not need any medicines during the hospital stay.

Follow-Up Instructions:

The patient should contact a doctor if mild leg swelling or any other symptoms come back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Diagnosis: Carbon particle injury to the left eye

Hospital Course:
An 8-year-old boy with no significant medical history was admitted to our urgent ophthalmology clinic with a pencil injury to his left eye. The patient presented with intact corneal epithelium and intrastromal silver-gray carbon particles in the inferonasal quadrant of the cornea. Mild hyperemia was noted in the conjunctiva. However, no sign of anterior chamber reaction was observed. The patient was prescribed Vigamox ophthalmic drops 4 times a day for 3 weeks and preservative-free artificial tears 4 times a day for 4 weeks.

The patient tolerated the treatment well and was discharged with no complications. Follow-up examinations at 1 week, 1, 4, and 6 months showed no signs of inflammatory reaction or injection.

Recommendations:
1. Vision screening should be conducted regularly to monitor for any changes.
2. Regular eye exams are advisable, especially in the presence of eye injuries.
3. Patients should avoid penetrating injuries to the eye.
4. Patients and caregivers should provide immediate medical attention in the event of an eye injury.

Primary Care Follow-up:
The patient is advised to follow up with a pediatric ophthalmologist for regular vision screenings and eye exams. Any changes in vision, pain, or swelling should be reported immediately to their primary care physician.
