Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Admission Date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Diagnosis: Carbon (pencil lead) injury to the left eye

Hospital Course:
An 8-year-old boy with no past health problems came to our eye emergency clinic after a pencil hurt his left eye. The clear front layer of the eye was not broken. Small silver-gray pencil lead bits were found in the lower inner part of the clear front of the eye. The white part of the eye was slightly red. There were no signs of swelling or irritation inside the eye. The patient was given Vigamox eye drops to use 4 times a day for 3 weeks. The patient was also given artificial tears without added chemicals to use 4 times a day for 4 weeks.

The patient handled the treatment well and went home with no problems. Check-up visits at 1 week, 1 month, 4 months, and 6 months showed no signs of redness, swelling, or irritation.

Recommendations:
1. The patient should have regular vision checks to watch for any changes.
2. Regular eye exams are a good idea, especially after eye injuries.
3. The patient should avoid injuries that break through the eye surface.
4. The patient and caregivers should get medical help right away if an eye injury happens.

Primary Care Follow-up:
The patient should see a children's eye doctor for regular vision checks and eye exams. Any changes in vision, pain, or swelling should be reported right away to the regular doctor.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, an 8-year-old female, was admitted with complaints of retro-orbital pain and headache. Imaging revealed a mass lesion at the right orbital apex with intracranial extension. Magnetic resonance imaging brain contrast enhancement showed a heterogeneous mass lesion in the sella with suprasellar extension. The patient was diagnosed with RIM based on neuroimaging, and gamma knife radiosurgery was planned as the preferred treatment option.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name:
Date of Admission:
Date of Discharge:

The patient, an 8-year-old female, was admitted with complaints of retro-orbital pain and headache. Imaging revealed a mass lesion at the right orbital apex with intracranial extension and a heterogeneous mass lesion in the sella with suprasellar extension. The patient was diagnosed with RIM based on neuroimaging, and gamma knife radiosurgery was planned as the preferred treatment option.

During the admission, the patient underwent gamma knife radiosurgery for RIM and was clinically stable for six months. Additional follow-up appointments were scheduled with the neurology department. The patient and family were educated on the importance of adhering to recommended radiation safety precautions.

The patient was discharged in a stable condition with a follow-up plan in place. Further treatment and management will be coordinated with the Neurology Department.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient, an 8-year-old girl, came to the hospital with pain behind her right eye and headaches. Scans showed a growth near the back of her right eye socket. This growth had spread into the brain area. A special brain scan with dye showed a mixed-looking growth in the area at the base of the brain. This growth had also spread upward. Doctors found the patient has a rare condition called RIM based on the scan pictures. The care team planned to treat it with a focused radiation treatment called gamma knife.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name:
Date of Admission:
Date of Discharge:

The patient, an 8-year-old girl, came to the hospital with pain behind her right eye and headaches. Scans showed a growth near the back of her right eye socket that had spread into the brain area. There was also a mixed-looking growth at the base of the brain that spread upward. Doctors found the patient has RIM based on the scan pictures. The care team planned to treat it with a focused radiation treatment called gamma knife.

During the hospital stay, the patient had the gamma knife radiation treatment for RIM. She stayed healthy and stable for six months after the treatment. More follow-up visits were set up with the brain and nerve doctors. The care team taught the patient and family about important safety steps to follow after radiation.

The patient left the hospital in stable condition with a follow-up plan ready. The brain and nerve doctors will continue to manage her care and any future treatment.

Doctors found the patient has a rare condition called RIM based on the scan pictures. The care team planned to treat it with a focused radiation treatment called gamma knife.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name:
Date of Admission:
Date of Discharge:

The patient, an 8-year-old girl, came to the hospital with pain behind her right eye and headaches. Scans showed a growth near the back of her right eye socket that had spread into the brain area. There was also a mixed-looking growth at the base of the brain that spread upward. Doctors found the patient has RIM based on the scan pictures. The care team planned to treat it with a focused radiation treatment called gamma knife.

During the hospital stay, the patient had the gamma knife radiation treatment for RIM. She stayed healthy and stable for six months after the treatment. More follow-up visits were set up with the brain and nerve doctors. The care team taught the patient and family about important safety steps to follow after radiation.

The patient left the hospital in stable condition with a follow-up plan ready. The brain and nerve doctors will continue to manage her care and any future treatment.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [Date of admission]
Discharge Date: [Date of discharge]

Chief Complaint: Fracture of jaw and missing central incisors

History of Present Illness: A 12-year-old female with a history of road side accident was brought to the Department of Oral Surgery with the chief complaint of fracture of lower jaw in parasymphysis region and missing both central incisors. She had no history of orthodontic treatment. On examination, the patient was found to have impacted both lower canine and crowding in both jaws.

Hospital Course: Nonextraction treatment was planned, and the treatment objective was to repair the jaw fracture, regain space for prosthetic replacement of central incisors, relieve crowding, and maintain the existing class I molar relationship. After wiring of the arch to hold the fracture segment on the same day, bone plating was done when the patient was fit for surgery. Once the fracture segment was healed, fixed mechanotherapy was started with Roth, 0.022'' appliances in both maxillary and mandibular arches to align, level and create space for the central incisors. The mandibular canines were kept in place as they were close to the screw and disimpaction was challenging due to their location on the fracture line. Riding pontics were prepared, assessed, and adjusted to the rectangular archwire by stainless steel ligature. The esthetic and smile of the patient was improved after ligating riding pontics.

Summary: The 12-year-old female patient with a history of fracture of lower jaw in parasymphysis region and missing both central incisors due to a road side accident successfully underwent the nonextraction treatment plan. After fixing the fracture segment, the patient's malocclusion was treated with fixed mechanotherapy which aligned and leveled both maxillary and mandibular arches and created space for the central incisors, while keeping the mandibular canines in place due to their location on the fracture line. The final step of the treatment was the preparation and ligating of riding pontics that significantly improved the patient's esthetic and smile.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [Date of admission]
Discharge Date: [Date of discharge]

Chief Complaint: Broken lower jaw and two missing front teeth

History of Present Illness: A 12-year-old girl was brought to the Oral Surgery Department after a roadside accident. She had a broken lower jaw near the chin area and was missing both lower front teeth. She had never had braces before. The doctors found that both lower eye teeth were stuck under the gums. Both her upper and lower teeth were also crowded.

Hospital Course: The doctors planned a treatment that would not require pulling any teeth. The goals were to fix the broken jaw, make room for replacement front teeth, fix the crowding, and keep the back teeth lined up correctly. On the same day, wires were placed to hold the broken jaw pieces together. When the patient was ready for surgery, a metal plate was attached to the bone. After the jaw healed, braces were placed on both the upper and lower teeth. The braces helped straighten the teeth and make space for the missing front teeth. The lower eye teeth were left in place because they were too close to the screw and hard to move due to where the break was. Temporary false teeth were made and attached to the braces with metal ties. The patient's appearance and smile looked much better after the false teeth were attached.

Summary: The 12-year-old girl had a broken lower jaw near the chin and two missing front teeth from a roadside accident. She completed a treatment plan that did not require pulling any teeth. After the broken jaw was fixed, braces were used to straighten the upper and lower teeth and make space for the front teeth. The lower eye teeth were left in place because of where the break was. The last step was attaching temporary false teeth to the braces. This greatly improved how the patient's smile looked.

Hospital Course:
The 60-year-old female patient with a palpable mass on the dorsal surface of her right foot underwent a lesion biopsy, which revealed calcifying aponeurotic fibroma. The lesion was totally excised, and the patient had an uneventful postoperative recovery. The patient has been well with no signs of recurrence during the last six months. 

Diagnosis:
Calcifying aponeurotic fibroma 

Inpatient Progress:
The patient was in good general condition without any history of trauma. A physical examination revealed a hard, painless, and immobile mass on the dorsal surface of the right foot. Radiographs revealed calcifying soft tissue mass without bone involvement. The lesion biopsy confirmed the diagnosis of calcifying aponeurotic fibroma. 

Treatment:
Total excision of the lesion 

Outpatient Treatment and Follow-up:
The patient has been well with no signs of recurrence during the last six months. 

Summary:
The 60-year-old female patient presented with a palpable mass on the dorsal surface of her right foot. The lesion was histologically identified as calcifying aponeurotic fibroma, which was totally excised, and the patient had an uneventful postoperative recovery. The patient has been well with no signs of recurrence during the last six months.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The 60-year-old female patient had a lump on the top of her right foot that could be felt by touch. Doctors took a small tissue sample to test it. The test showed a rare, slow-growing, non-cancerous growth called calcifying aponeurotic fibroma. This type of growth contains calcium deposits and forms in the tough tissue that connects muscles. The entire growth was removed by surgery. The patient healed well after surgery with no problems. The patient has been doing well with no return of the growth over the past six months.

Diagnosis:
Calcifying aponeurotic fibroma, which is a rare, slow-growing, non-cancerous growth that contains calcium deposits and forms in the tough tissue that connects muscles.

Inpatient Progress:
The patient was in good overall health and had no past injury to the area. A physical exam showed a hard lump on the top of the right foot. The lump did not cause pain and did not move when touched. X-rays showed a lump in the soft tissue that contained calcium. The x-rays showed no damage to the bones. A tissue sample test confirmed the growth was calcifying aponeurotic fibroma.

Treatment:
The entire growth was removed by surgery.

Outpatient Treatment and Follow-up:
The patient has been doing well with no return of the growth over the past six months.

Summary:
The 60-year-old female patient came in with a lump on the top of her right foot that could be felt by touch. Testing of the tissue showed it was calcifying aponeurotic fibroma. This is a rare, slow-growing, non-cancerous growth. The entire growth was removed by surgery. The patient healed well after surgery with no problems. The patient has been doing well with no return of the growth over the past six months.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 32
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Hospital: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to our facility with swelling in his right cheek, which had been present for two weeks. A malignant lesion of the oral cavity was suspected. However, a thorough examination revealed an oval, firm swelling measuring 2.5 × 2 cm in size, located 2 cm above and medial to the right angle of the mandible. An ultrasound of the cheek revealed that the swelling was a cystic lesion in the bucco-masseteric region with perilesional edema.

Further testing, including an MRI of the face and neck, was performed, and a hyperechoic intralesional structure consistent with the scolex was identified. Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) was performed but showed no evidence of malignancy. Radiographs of the chest, bilateral arms and thighs were taken, and an MRI of the head and orbit was performed, revealing no additional lesions.

The patient was advised to take analgesics and steroids for seven days, and the swelling gradually reduced in size. Deworming therapy with albendazole was also prescribed.

Discharge Medications:
Albendazole

Discharge Diagnosis:
Involvement of the facial musculature manifesting as a cystic swelling in the bucco-masseteric region consistent with cysticercosis.

Follow-Up:
The patient should follow up with his primary healthcare provider for continued monitoring.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's condition has improved since admission. He is stable and ready for discharge.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to continue taking his albendazole medication as prescribed. He should avoid tobacco use as it has been associated with cysticercosis. He is encouraged to follow up with his primary healthcare provider for continued monitoring and to report any new or concerning symptoms.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 32
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Hospital: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital with swelling in his right cheek. The swelling had been there for two weeks. Doctors first thought it might be cancer in the mouth area. After a careful exam, doctors found an oval, firm lump about 1 inch by 0.8 inches in size. The lump was located near the right side of the jawbone. An ultrasound of the cheek showed the lump was a fluid-filled sac in the cheek muscle area. There was also some swelling in the tissue around it.

More tests were done, including an MRI scan of the face and neck. The MRI found a small structure inside the sac that looked like a tapeworm head. A small sample was taken from the lump using a thin needle. The sample showed no signs of cancer. X-rays of the chest, both arms, and both thighs were taken. An MRI of the head and eye area was also done. These tests showed no other problem areas in the body.

The patient was told to take pain medicine and medicine to reduce swelling for seven days. The lump slowly got smaller. Medicine to kill the parasite was also given.

Discharge Medications:
Albendazole

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient had a parasitic infection called cysticercosis. This caused a fluid-filled sac to form in the cheek muscle area.

Follow-Up:
The patient should see his regular doctor for continued check-ups.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has gotten better since coming to the hospital. He is doing well and is ready to go home.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should keep taking his albendazole medicine as directed. He should not use tobacco because it has been linked to this type of infection. He should see his regular doctor for continued check-ups. He should tell his doctor about any new or worrying symptoms.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Overview:
The patient is a 58-year-old male who presented with a painless, asymptomatic lesion on the right upper lid that he has had for 15 years. He had also been diagnosed with right lower lid senile entropion for which an entropion repair was scheduled. 

History and Physical:
On examination, the eyelid nodule was found to be firm, and not adherent to the overlying skin. The size of the nodule was 5mm x 5mm and was mobile. The patient did not show any evidence of skin ulceration, but the eyelid skin had an extra fold due to dermatochalasis. Apart from these findings, the rest of the ocular examination was unremarkable.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent an entropion repair procedure as well as an excision of the eyelid nodule which the patient had requested. The postoperative period was uneventful. 

Outpatient Follow-up:
At the last follow-up appointment, 3 months later, the patient did not show any evidence of recurrence.

Diagnosis:
As per the case report, there is no formal diagnosis mentioned. 

Treatment:
The patient underwent an entropion repair along with an excision of the eyelid nodule. 

Prognosis:
The patient has a good prognosis with no evidence of recurrence at his last follow-up.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Overview:
The patient is a 58-year-old man who came in with a painless bump on his right upper eyelid. He had this bump for 15 years and it did not bother him. He was also found to have a problem with his right lower eyelid turning inward. This is common in older adults. Surgery was planned to fix the eyelid turning inward.

History and Physical:
During the exam, the bump on the eyelid felt firm. It was not attached to the skin above it. The bump was about the size of a small pea and could be moved around. The skin over the bump was not broken or damaged. The patient had extra loose skin on his eyelid, which is normal with aging. Everything else about his eyes looked normal.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery to fix the eyelid that was turning inward. The doctor also removed the bump because the patient asked for it to be taken out. The patient healed well after surgery with no problems.

Outpatient Follow-up:
The patient came back for a checkup 3 months later. The bump had not come back.

Diagnosis:
Based on the medical report, no specific diagnosis was given for the bump.

Treatment:
The patient had surgery to fix the eyelid turning inward. The bump on the eyelid was also removed during the same surgery.

Prognosis:
The patient is expected to do well. At his last visit, there were no signs that the bump had come back.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 37-year-old male with a history of anxiety and migraines who presented to the ED reporting a constant, sharp pain on the right side of his head that had persisted for 12 hours. The patient reported that the pain was typical for his migraine but had not responded to his usual home abortive medication, ibuprofen, or hydrocodone/acetaminophen. 

Physical examination revealed right occipital tenderness upon palpation, but no midline spinal tenderness or cranial nerve deficits. Metoclopramide, normal saline, and diphenhydramine were administered initially, after which the patient reported improvement from a 10/10 to an 8/10 in pain. Subsequently, the patient received an occipital nerve block with 1% lidocaine, and three minutes later, the patient reported further improvement to 2/10 in pain.

Follow-up phone call interview at day 8 revealed complete resolution of the patient's migraine symptoms, and the patient expressed preference for seeking out an occipital nerve block if a migraine were to recur.

Summary:

A 37-year-old male presented with a 12-hour history of severe right-sided head pain consistent with his migraines but unresponsive to his usual home abortive medication. Initial treatment with metoclopramide, normal saline, and diphenhydramine failed to provide adequate relief, but a subsequent occipital nerve block with 1% lidocaine resulted in resolution of the patient's symptoms. Follow-up phone call interview at day 8 revealed continued symptom resolution and expressed patient preference for future use of occipital nerve block.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 37-year-old male with a history of anxiety and migraines who came to the emergency room with a constant, sharp pain on the right side of his head. The pain had lasted for 12 hours. The patient said the pain felt like his usual migraine but his regular migraine medicine, ibuprofen, and pain medicine had not helped.

During the physical exam, the doctor found tenderness at the back right side of the head when pressing on it. There was no tenderness along the spine or problems with the nerves in the head and face. The patient was first given medicine for nausea, fluids through an IV, and medicine for allergies that also helps with pain. After this, the patient said his pain went down from 10 out of 10 to 8 out of 10. Then, the doctor gave a numbing shot to the nerves at the back of the head using a numbing medicine. Three minutes later, the patient said the pain dropped to 2 out of 10.

A follow-up phone call on day 8 showed the patient's migraine symptoms were completely gone. The patient said he would prefer to get the numbing shot at the back of the head if another migraine happens.

Summary:

A 37-year-old male came in with 12 hours of severe pain on the right side of his head that matched his usual migraines. His regular migraine medicine had not worked. The first treatments with nausea medicine, IV fluids, and allergy medicine did not give enough relief. However, a numbing shot to the nerves at the back of the head made the symptoms go away. A follow-up phone call on day 8 showed the symptoms were still gone. The patient said he would prefer to get the numbing shot for future migraines.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [redacted] 
Age: 18 years
Sex: Female 
Medical Record Number: [redacted]

Clinical Course:
The patient was admitted with complaints of scholastic backwardness and was found to have clitoromegaly and hirsutism upon physical examination. Psychological assessment showed that the patient had a mild intellectual disability. Further investigations revealed the presence of a pelvic mass and left ovarian tumor for which the patient underwent an ovariectomy. The histopathology reports did not reveal the presence of any testicular tissue. 

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with female pseudohermaphroditism.

Management:
The patient underwent an ovariectomy as part of her treatment. 

Follow-up:
The patient is advised to undertake regular follow-up visits with her primary care physician to monitor her condition. 

Prognosis:
The prognosis of the patient's condition depends on the extent of virilization and the presence of testicular tissue. Further evaluation may be necessary to determine the patient's long-term prognosis. 

Summary:
An 18-year-old girl with mild intellectual disability was diagnosed with female pseudohermaphroditism, which was confirmed through a pelvic mass and left ovarian tumor. Ovariectomy was performed and histopathological examination showed no presence of testicular tissue. The patient is advised to undertake regular follow-up visits to monitor her condition. The prognosis is uncertain and further evaluation may be required.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [redacted]
Age: 18 years
Sex: Female
Medical Record Number: [redacted]

Clinical Course:
The patient came to the hospital because of problems with learning in school. During the physical exam, doctors found she had an enlarged private area and extra body hair. Mental health testing showed the patient has a mild learning disability. More tests found a growth in her lower belly area and a tumor on her left ovary. The patient had surgery to remove the ovary. Lab tests on the removed tissue did not show any male reproductive tissue.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a condition where a female is born with some male physical features.

Management:
The patient had surgery to remove her ovary as part of her treatment.

Follow-up:
The patient should see her regular doctor for checkups to keep track of her condition.

Prognosis:
How well the patient does over time depends on how much her body developed male features. It also depends on whether any male reproductive tissue is present. More tests may be needed to understand what to expect in the future.

Summary:
An 18-year-old girl with a mild learning disability was found to have a condition causing some male physical features. This was confirmed after finding a growth in her lower belly and a tumor on her left ovary. Surgery was done to remove the ovary. Lab tests showed no male reproductive tissue was present. The patient should see her doctor regularly for checkups. What to expect in the future is not clear, and more tests may be needed.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Age: 45
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Medical History:
The patient had multiple rib fractures with right-sided hemothorax following a road traffic accident and underwent thoracostomy tube insertion at the primary treating hospital. She was referred to our tertiary care center after 1½ months from the antecedent injury and was diagnosed with necrotic sloughed-out the right dome of the diaphragm with collapsed right lung.

Hospital Course:
The patient was intubated and shifted to ICU for ventilatory support. A repeat Chest X-Ray and CECT thorax showed bilateral multiple rib fractures, right-sided hemothorax with air foci in the collection, and an indistinct outline of the diaphragm. In view of a possible diaphragmatic injury, the patient underwent right posterolateral thoracotomy. Intraoperatively, necrotic tissues and organized hematomas were removed, and an intercostal tube was placed between the liver and the collapsed lung. No repair of diaphragm could be done. The patient was stable postoperatively but could not be weaned off mechanical ventilation. A tracheostomy was done in view of long-term intubation. Enteral feeds were started through nasogastric tube. After 3 weeks, the patient was transferred to the cardiothoracic surgery unit for further management but could not undergo any surgical procedures and died after 1 month due to her poor general condition.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient had an unfortunate outcome and could not be discharged.

Plan:
No further plan needed.

Follow-up:
None needed.

Signed,
[REDACTED]
Doctor's Signature
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Age: 45
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Medical History:
The patient had several broken ribs and blood collecting in the right side of the chest after a car accident. A drainage tube was placed in the chest at the first hospital. She came to our hospital about one and a half months after the accident. Doctors found that part of the muscle wall between the chest and belly on the right side had died. The right lung had also collapsed.

Hospital Course:
The patient was put on a breathing machine and moved to the intensive care unit. New chest scans showed several broken ribs on both sides and blood with air pockets on the right side of the chest. The muscle wall between the chest and belly looked damaged. The patient had surgery to open the right side of the chest. During surgery, dead tissue and old blood clots were removed. A drainage tube was placed between the liver and the collapsed lung. The damaged muscle wall could not be fixed. The patient was stable after surgery but could not breathe on her own without the machine. A small opening was made in the throat to help with long-term breathing support. The patient was fed through a tube placed through the nose into the stomach. After three weeks, the patient was moved to the heart and chest surgery unit for more care. However, she was too weak for more surgery. The patient passed away one month later because her body was too weak to recover.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient had a sad outcome and could not leave the hospital.

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 45
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Medical History:
The patient had several broken ribs and blood collecting in the right side of the chest after a car accident. A drainage tube was placed in the chest at the first hospital. She came to our hospital about one and a half months after the accident. Doctors found that part of the muscle wall between the chest and belly on the right side had died. The right lung had also collapsed.

Hospital Course:
The patient was put on a breathing machine and moved to the intensive care unit. New chest scans showed several broken ribs on both sides and blood with air pockets on the right side of the chest. The muscle wall between the chest and belly looked damaged. The patient had surgery to open the right side of the chest. During surgery, dead tissue and old blood clots were removed. A drainage tube was placed between the liver and the collapsed lung. The damaged muscle wall could not be fixed. The patient was stable after surgery but could not breathe on her own without the machine. A small opening was made in the throat to help with long-term breathing support. The patient was fed through a tube placed through the nose into the stomach. After three weeks, the patient was moved to the heart and chest surgery unit for more care. However, she was too weak for more surgery. The patient passed away one month later because her body was too weak to recover.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient had a sad outcome and could not leave the hospital.

Plan:
No further plan needed.

Follow-up:
None needed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 74-year-old
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Chief complaint:
The patient complained of exacerbation of mild dysphagia and an awareness of a tumor in the left side of her neck.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital on [REDACTED] with a complaint of mild dysphagia for the past two months, which had recently worsened. Laboratory tests showed normal thyroid function levels. Pharyngoesophagography showed a large diverticulum at the pharyngoesophageal junction. Ultrasonography revealed a hypoechoic lesion present in the posterior bilateral lobe of the thyroid and an isoechoic mass on the left lobe of the thyroid. Cervical CT scan showed a thyroid nodule, bilateral hypopharyngeal diverticula, and an isodensity area with a hyperdensity area, suggesting calcium deposition. A barium swallow pharyngoesophagogram showed a large left-sided diverticulum and a small right-sided diverticulum.

The patient underwent resection of the diverticula and thyroid tumor surgery for improvement of dysphagia. The surgical procedure was approached through a collar incision in the left anterior neck, and the diverticula were visualized under the cricopharyngeus muscle. The left-sided diverticulum was mobilized from the left lobe of the thyroid and the thyroid tumor. The diverticulotomy was performed. The wound was closed in layers with placement of a closed suction drain.

Histopathological examination showed that the diverticula were false diverticula without a muscular layer, and the thyroid tumor was diagnosed as a thyroid adenoma. The patient was started on a clear liquid diet on postoperative day 4. Two years after surgery, the patient remains free of dysphagia.

Diagnosis:
- Mild dysphagia
- Hypopharyngeal diverticula
- Thyroid adenoma

Treatment:
- Surgery for resection of hypopharyngeal diverticula and thyroid tumor

Follow-up:
The patient is advised to continue follow-up care with her primary care physician. No further appointments with our hospital are required.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 74-year-old
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Main problem:
The patient had trouble swallowing that had gotten worse. She also noticed a growth on the left side of her neck.

Hospital stay:
The patient came to the hospital on [REDACTED]. She had mild trouble swallowing for two months that recently got worse. Blood tests showed her thyroid gland was working normally. An X-ray of her throat and food pipe showed a large pouch where the throat meets the food pipe. An ultrasound found an abnormal area behind both sides of the thyroid and a mass on the left side of the thyroid. A CT scan of the neck showed a thyroid lump, pouches on both sides of the lower throat, and an area that may have calcium buildup. Another swallowing test showed a large pouch on the left side and a small pouch on the right side.

The patient had surgery to remove the pouches and thyroid growth to help with swallowing. The surgeon made a curved cut on the left front of the neck. The pouches were found under a throat muscle. The left pouch was separated from the thyroid and the thyroid growth. The pouches were removed. The wound was closed in layers and a drain tube was placed to remove fluid.

Lab tests on the removed tissue showed the pouches were weak spots in the wall without muscle. The thyroid growth was found to be not cancer. The patient started drinking clear liquids on day 4 after surgery. Two years after surgery, the patient has no trouble swallowing.

Diagnosis:
Mild trouble swallowing
Pouches in the lower throat
Non-cancerous thyroid growth

Treatment:
Surgery to remove the throat pouches and thyroid growth

Follow-up:
The patient should keep seeing her regular doctor. No more visits to this hospital are needed.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 38-year-old woman who presented with a six-month history of progressive exertional dyspnea and new onset of syncopal episodes. Auscultation reveals a continuous murmur at the left sternal border. Transthoracic echocardiography reveals an anomalous vascular structure origin from circumflex artery and draining to the main pulmonary artery with significant left to right shunt, mild dilatation of right heart chambers and 60% ejection fraction. Magnetic resonance imaging and coronary angiography confirms the presence of a coronary artery fistula. The pulmonary to systemic flow ratio was calculated as 1.8 to 1. Left anterior mini-thoracotomy was performed, the fistulous tracts were identified, and the fistula on the pulmonary artery side was obliterated with multiple pledgeted 5-0 polypropylene sutures. Transesophageal echocardiography confirmed a successful closure of the fistula, lack of residual shunt and lack of wall motion abnormalities. Electrocardiogram did not show any signs of ischemia. The patient was extubated one hour after completion of the procedure and discharged on the second postoperative day. At 6 and 12 months follow-up, the patient remained free of any cardiovascular symptoms, while echocardiography showed no residual flow. An excellent cosmetic result was achieved.

Hospital Course Summary:
A 38-year-old female presented with dyspnea and syncopal episodes, and murmur on auscultation. The patient's medical examination revealed coronary artery fistula with significant left to right shunt and mild dilatation of the right heart chambers. The patient underwent surgical correction by left anterior mini-thoracotomy, and successful obliteration of the fistulous tracts was noted under transesophageal echocardiography with no residual shunt or wall motion abnormalities seen. The patient remained symptom-free at the six and twelve months follow-up visits, with no residual flow detected on echocardiography.@endsection
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 38-year-old woman who came in with six months of worsening shortness of breath during activity and new fainting spells. Listening to the heart revealed an ongoing heart murmur on the left side of the chest. An ultrasound of the heart showed an abnormal blood vessel coming from a heart artery and connecting to the lung artery. This caused blood to flow the wrong way from the left side of the heart to the right side. There was mild widening of the right heart chambers and the heart was pumping at 60 percent strength. Special imaging and dye tests of the heart arteries confirmed an abnormal connection between a heart artery and another blood vessel. The amount of extra blood flow to the lungs was measured at 1.8 times higher than normal. Surgery was done through a small cut on the left side of the chest. The abnormal connections were found and closed with several stitches backed by small fabric patches. An ultrasound probe placed in the throat confirmed the abnormal connection was fully closed. There was no leftover abnormal blood flow and the heart wall was moving normally. A heart tracing showed no signs of poor blood flow to the heart muscle. The patient had the breathing tube removed one hour after surgery and went home two days after the procedure. At the six month and twelve month checkups, the patient had no heart or blood vessel symptoms. Heart ultrasounds showed no leftover abnormal blood flow. The small scar healed very well.

Hospital Course Summary:
A 38-year-old woman came in with shortness of breath, fainting spells, and a heart murmur heard during the exam. Testing showed an abnormal connection between a heart artery and the lung artery. This caused blood to flow the wrong way and mild widening of the right heart chambers. The patient had surgery through a small cut on the left side of the chest to close the abnormal connections. An ultrasound during surgery confirmed full closure with no leftover abnormal blood flow and normal heart wall movement. The patient had no symptoms at the six month and twelve month follow-up visits. Heart ultrasounds showed no leftover abnormal blood flow.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Patient was a 71-year-old female who presented to our clinic with an abnormal ECG finding during a scheduled appointment. She had a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, essential hypertension, and dyslipidemia. During the consultation, the ECG reading showed premature ventricular contraction at a 1:1 ratio (ventricular bigeminy) with a heart rate of 46/min. Therefore, various blood investigations were ordered to rule out secondary causes that could have led to the changes in the ECG. An urgent appointment was scheduled a week later to review these results. The patient was also advised to seek urgent medical attention if she felt palpitations or giddiness.

During the second consultation, a week later, the repeat ECG showed atrial flutter with a 3:1 atrioventricular block. All the results of the blood investigations were normal, except for an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Urgent transthoracic echocardiography revealed a dilated left atrium and ventricle without any myocardial hypokinetic changes. A large echogenic mass measuring 3.1×4.1 cm was identified at the left atrium. The mass was solid and appeared pedunculated, arising from a stalk at the endocardium. The patient had a left ventricular ejection fraction of 54%.

The patient was immediately referred to a tertiary care cardiology clinic where a second transthoracic echocardiography was performed. An imaging diagnosis of left atrial myxoma was confirmed. The tumor measured approximately 2.9×3.1 cm. A paroxysmal atrial fibrillation pattern was observed on the ECG. Warfarin treatment was initiated while aiming for a target international normalized ratio between 2 and 3. Currently, the patient is jointly cared for by the primary care physician and a multidisciplinary team that is helping to manage the anticoagulation therapy. The patient is unwilling to undergo surgery to remove the tumor.

The patient has been advised to continue with the recommended medical management and to seek medical attention immediately in case of any symptoms suggestive of cardiac compromise. The patient has been provided with educational resources to manage their condition and follow up with the healthcare team.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was a 71-year-old woman who came to our clinic after a heart test showed unusual results during a regular visit. She had a history of type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, and high cholesterol. During the visit, the heart test showed extra heartbeats happening in an unusual pattern with a slow heart rate of 46 beats per minute. Because of this, several blood tests were ordered to find out what might be causing these heart changes. An urgent follow-up visit was set for one week later to go over these results. The patient was also told to get medical help right away if she felt her heart racing or felt dizzy.

During the second visit one week later, a new heart test showed a different irregular heart rhythm with a problem in how signals moved through the heart. Most blood test results were normal, except two tests that showed signs of swelling in the body were higher than normal. An urgent heart ultrasound showed that the left upper and lower chambers of the heart were larger than normal but were still squeezing well. A large solid growth measuring about 3.1 by 4.1 centimeters was found in the left upper chamber of the heart. The growth was attached to the heart wall by a stalk. The heart was pumping out 54 percent of its blood with each beat.

The patient was sent right away to a specialized heart clinic where another heart ultrasound was done. The doctors confirmed the growth was a type of non-cancerous heart tumor called a left atrial myxoma. The tumor measured about 2.9 by 3.1 centimeters. The heart test also showed episodes of an irregular heartbeat. Blood-thinning medicine called Warfarin was started to prevent blood clots with a goal to keep the blood at a certain thinness level between 2 and 3. Right now, the patient is being cared for by both her regular doctor and a team of specialists who are helping manage her blood-thinning treatment. The patient does not want to have surgery to remove the tumor.

The patient has been told to keep following the recommended treatment plan and to get medical help right away if she has any signs that her heart is not working well. The patient has been given learning materials to help manage her condition and to stay in touch with her healthcare team.

input:
Hospital Course:

An 82-year-old woman was admitted with right upper extremity muscle weakness. Brain MRI showed a mass in the left fronto-parietal lobe which was removed by craniotomy. A TES-MEP and DCS-MEP monitoring was performed intraoperatively. Unexpectedly, there was no significant change in TES-MEP while significant loss in DCS-MEP was observed. Physicians decided to perform repetitive TESMEP, resulting in the same unexpected result. Postoperative neurological examination revealed motor weakness of the right upper and lower extremities. Histology revealed glioblastoma. The patient had a postoperative seizure, and a postoperative transcranial magnetic stimulation MEP was not performed. The patient was discharged on postoperative day 13 with an unchanged neurological status.

Discharge Diagnosis:

- Glioblastoma

Hospital Course Summary:

An 82-year-old woman with right upper extremity muscle weakness was admitted and diagnosed with glioblastoma, a tumor in the left fronto-parietal lobe. A craniotomy was performed for the tumor removal. During the surgery, TES-MEP and DCS-MEP monitoring were utilized, and an unexpected result was observed. Postoperative neurological examination revealed motor weakness of the right upper and lower extremities. Histology confirmed the glioblastoma diagnosis. Due to a postoperative seizure, a postoperative transcranial magnetic stimulation MEP was not performed. The patient was discharged on postoperative day 13 with an unchanged neurological status.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

An 82-year-old woman came to the hospital because of weakness in her right arm muscles. A brain scan showed a growth in the left side of her brain. Doctors removed the growth through surgery that opened part of her skull. During surgery, doctors used two different tests to check how her nerves and muscles were working. The test results were not what doctors expected. One test showed no big changes while the other showed a major loss in nerve signals. Doctors repeated one of the tests several times but got the same unexpected results. After surgery, the patient had weakness in her right arm and right leg. Lab tests showed the growth was a very serious type of brain cancer called glioblastoma. The patient had a seizure after surgery. Because of this, doctors could not do a follow-up nerve test. The patient left the hospital 13 days after surgery. Her nerve and muscle problems were the same as right after surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Glioblastoma, which is a serious type of brain cancer

Hospital Course Summary:

An 82-year-old woman with right arm weakness came to the hospital. Doctors found a tumor in the left side of her brain. The tumor was a serious brain cancer called glioblastoma. Doctors did surgery to open her skull and remove the tumor. During surgery, doctors used special tests to watch her nerve and muscle activity. The test results were not what doctors expected. After surgery, the patient had weakness in both her right arm and right leg. Lab tests confirmed the brain cancer diagnosis. The patient had a seizure after surgery. Because of the seizure, doctors could not do a follow-up nerve test. The patient left the hospital 13 days after surgery. Her weakness had not changed since right after surgery.

input:
Hospital Course: 

A 30-year-old male presented with fever and was transferred from a local hospital in Yunnan Province. He was diagnosed with acute purulent appendicitis and underwent an appendectomy seven days prior to admission. On admission, he had a temperature of 39.5°C and a blood pressure of 108/78 mmHg. The rest of his physical examination was normal. The laboratory investigations revealed an elevated white blood cell count and an elevated procalcitonin level. The contrast-enhanced computerized tomography (CT) scan of his abdomen showed an ileocecal abscess with a size of 2.3 cm x 2.9 cm. He was treated empirically with intravenous moxifloxacin before the results of blood culture became available.

Management and Treatment:

On Day 3, gram-positive bacilli were identified in two sets of anaerobic blood cultures. The isolate was identified as E. Lenta using MALDI-TOF MS. The appropriate antibiotics were started, and intravenous meropenem was administered at a dose of 1g every 8 hours. The patient responded promptly to meropenem therapy, and percutaneous or surgical drainage of the abscess was not performed. His fever, elevated procalcitonin level, and leukocytosis resolved without surgical intervention.

Discharge Plan:

No microorganisms were identified in the follow-up blood cultures from Day 5. On Day 10, abdominal color Doppler ultrasonography was normal. After a further 6-day course of intravenous meropenem, the patient recovered and was discharged from the hospital. Follow-up appointments were scheduled to monitor his progress and ensure complete recovery.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

A 30-year-old man came to the hospital with a fever. He was moved from another hospital in Yunnan Province. Seven days before coming to this hospital, he had surgery to remove his appendix. The doctors found his appendix was badly infected. When he arrived, his body temperature was high at 39.5 degrees Celsius. His blood pressure was 108 over 78, which was normal. The rest of his body check was normal. Blood tests showed his white blood cell count was high. This means his body was fighting an infection. Another blood test called procalcitonin was also high. This test helps find serious infections. A special scan of his belly area was done using dye and x-rays. The scan showed a pocket of infection near where his appendix was removed. The pocket was about 2.3 cm by 2.9 cm in size. The doctors gave him an antibiotic medicine through a vein. The medicine was called moxifloxacin. They started this medicine before they knew exactly what germ was causing the infection.

Management and Treatment:

On Day 3, blood tests found a type of bacteria in his blood. The bacteria was called E. Lenta. The doctors then changed his medicine to one that works better against this germ. The patient received a strong antibiotic called meropenem through a vein. He got 1 gram of this medicine every 8 hours. The patient got better quickly with this treatment. The doctors did not need to drain the infection pocket with a needle or surgery. His fever went away. His blood test numbers went back to normal. He did not need any surgery.

Discharge Plan:

On Day 5, new blood tests showed no germs in his blood. On Day 10, a sound wave test of his belly area was normal. The patient kept getting the meropenem medicine through a vein for 6 more days. The patient got better and left the hospital. The patient has appointments set up to check on his healing. These visits will make sure he fully recovers.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [...]
Medical Record Number: [...]
Discharge Date: [...]

Hospital Course: 
The patient was admitted to our hospital due to suddenly developing aconuresis and paraplegia. The patient had a two-week history of abdominal pain and weakness in the lower extremities for one day. Past medical history was unremarkable, and no family history of malignant tumors was noted. Neurological examination revealed bladder-urinary dysfunction and hypoesthesia beneath the T8 sensory dermatome. A CT scan on admission showed acute pancreatitis and acute cholecystitis. The patient underwent MRI of the abdomen and lumbar spine, which showed a swollen pancreas and gallbladder with less peripancreatic exudation and liquid collection. The MRI also showed multiple enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes and a well-demarcated, posterolateral extradural mass between T9 and T12, with definite bony destruction. PET-CT scan revealed multifocal malignant lesions, indicating multiple metastases of malignant tumors. Gastroduodenal endoscopy showed massive involvement of the gastric body and duodenum with tumors. Histology and immunohistochemistry of gastric biopsies were suggestive of BL.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was referred to our hospital due to a sudden onset of aconuresis and paraplegia. The patient had a two-week history of abdominal pain and weakness in the lower extremities. The patient was diagnosed with acute pancreatitis and acute cholecystitis with peripancreatic exudation and liquid collection. MRI showed multiple enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes and a well-demarcated, posterolateral extradural mass between T9 and T12, with definite bony destruction. PET-CT scan revealed multifocal malignant lesions, indicating multiple metastases of malignant tumors. Gastric biopsies were suggestive of BL. Subsequent course was palliative care and the patient was discharged for hospice care.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [...]
Medical Record Number: [...]
Discharge Date: [...]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital because of sudden loss of bladder control and loss of movement in both legs. The patient had belly pain for two weeks and leg weakness for one day before coming in. The patient had no past health problems and no family history of cancer. A nerve and muscle exam showed bladder problems and reduced feeling below the mid-chest area. A CT scan when the patient arrived showed sudden swelling of the pancreas and gallbladder. The patient had an MRI of the belly and lower spine. The MRI showed a swollen pancreas and gallbladder with some fluid buildup around them. The MRI also showed many swollen lymph nodes deep in the belly and a growth pressing on the spinal cord between the middle and lower back. This growth was damaging the bones. A full body PET-CT scan showed cancer that had spread to many parts of the body. A camera test of the stomach and upper gut showed tumors covering large areas of the stomach and first part of the small intestine. Tissue samples from the stomach suggested a type of cancer called BL.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was sent to our hospital because of sudden loss of bladder control and loss of movement in both legs. The patient had belly pain for two weeks and leg weakness before coming in. The patient was found to have sudden swelling of the pancreas and gallbladder with fluid buildup around them. The MRI showed many swollen lymph nodes deep in the belly and a growth pressing on the spinal cord in the back. This growth was damaging the bones. A full body scan showed cancer that had spread to many parts of the body. Tissue samples from the stomach suggested a type of cancer called BL. The care plan focused on comfort, and the patient was sent home for end of life care.

A full body PET-CT scan showed cancer that had spread to many parts of the body. A camera test of the stomach and upper gut showed tumors covering large areas of the stomach and first part of the small intestine. Tissue samples from the stomach suggested a type of cancer called BL.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was sent to our hospital because of sudden loss of bladder control and loss of movement in both legs. The patient had belly pain for two weeks and leg weakness before coming in. The patient was found to have sudden swelling of the pancreas and gallbladder with fluid buildup around them. The MRI showed many swollen lymph nodes deep in the belly and a growth pressing on the spinal cord in the back. This growth was damaging the bones. A full body scan showed cancer that had spread to many parts of the body. Tissue samples from the stomach suggested a type of cancer called BL. The care plan focused on comfort, and the patient was sent home for end of life care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age/Sex: 23-year-old male
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with a 2-year history of progressive left greater than the right-hand weakness/myelopathy. On physical examination, left greater than the right-sided atrophy of the thenar/hypothenar eminences, and interosseous muscles were observed. Motor nerve conduction/electromyographic studies showed left greater than the right-sided C7-T1 denervation, while sensory conduction studies were normal.

The patient underwent both neutral and flexion MRI scans, revealing diffuse lower cervical spinal cord swelling most severely at C5-C6. A cervical laminectomy with durotomy was performed to obtain an intramedullary cord biopsy to exclude an intrinsic cord tumor. The biopsy showed focal cord ischemia consistent with intrinsic HD.

A C5-C6 ACDF was performed for anterior decompression. Postoperative cervical MRI showed a reduction of the ischemic C5-C6 cord focus, and the patient exhibited no clinical changes. Follow-up MRI scans over the next two years showed a mild progressive focal atrophy of the cord at the C5-C6 level with selective involvement of the anterior horns.

Discharge Diagnosis:
1. Intrinsic HD with cord ischemia.

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Follow-up:
The patient is advised to follow up with the treating neurologist for regular check-ups and further management of his medical condition. Further scans assessing the disease progression may also be ordered. 

Discharge Condition:
The patient's condition has not improved, but the hospitalization resulted in the successful exclusion of an intrinsic cord tumor and confirmation of the diagnosis of Intrinsic HD. The patient is now able to continue his treatment under the guidance of his neurologist as an outpatient.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age/Sex: 23-year-old male
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with weakness in both hands that had been getting worse for 2 years. The left hand was weaker than the right hand. The doctor found that muscles in the palms and between the fingers had shrunk, more on the left side. Tests of the nerves and muscles showed nerve damage in the lower neck area on both sides, worse on the left. Tests that check feeling in the hands were normal.

The patient had special scans of the neck in both a straight position and a bent position. These scans showed swelling of the spinal cord in the lower neck, worst at the C5-C6 level. The patient had surgery to open the back of the neck and take a small tissue sample from inside the spinal cord. This was done to make sure there was no tumor. The tissue sample showed reduced blood flow to part of the spinal cord. This matched a condition called Hirayama Disease.

The patient then had surgery from the front of the neck to take pressure off the spinal cord at the C5-C6 level. A scan after surgery showed the damaged area in the spinal cord had gotten smaller. The patient did not have any new problems after surgery. Scans done over the next two years showed the spinal cord slowly getting thinner at C5-C6. The front part of the spinal cord was most affected.

Discharge Diagnosis:
1. Hirayama Disease with reduced blood flow to the spinal cord.

Discharge Medications:
None

Follow-up:
The patient should see his nerve specialist for regular check-ups and ongoing care of his condition. More scans may be ordered to check if the disease is getting worse.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's condition has not improved. However, the hospital stay helped rule out a spinal cord tumor. It also confirmed that the patient has Hirayama Disease. The patient can now continue treatment with his nerve specialist without needing to stay in the hospital.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]

Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Diagnosis: Scar endometriosis

Hospital Course:

The patient presented with a nodule at the lower abdominal caesarean section scar, which appeared four years after surgery and gradually increased in size. The mass was clinically diagnosed as a tumor on the abdominal wall and was found to have a black appearance. A fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) was performed and the aspirate was collected on glass slides and was wet fixed and then stained by the Papanicolaou method. The diagnosis given on FNAC was suggestive of scar endometriosis. The cell block findings were confirmatory of endometriosis as they revealed endometrial lining columnar epithelium with subepithelial dense and compact endometrial stroma along with extensive areas of hemorrhage. 

Histopathological findings after complete excision of the nodule also confirmed endometriosis. The patient was found physically fit otherwise and was discharged from the hospital in stable condition. 

Follow-Up Recommendations:

The patient should follow up with her primary care provider for further evaluation and management of her scar endometriosis. She may need to undergo further imaging and testing to monitor the condition and to determine the most appropriate treatment option. The patient should also be advised to seek medical attention if she experiences any new symptoms or changes in her condition. 

Signed, 
[REDACTED]
Date: [REDACTED]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]

Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Diagnosis: Scar endometriosis (tissue that normally lines the womb growing in a scar)

Hospital Course:

The patient came in with a lump on her lower belly at the site of her C-section scar. The lump appeared four years after her surgery and slowly got bigger over time. Doctors thought it was a growth on the belly wall and saw that it looked dark in color. A test was done where a thin needle was used to take a small sample from the lump. The sample was placed on glass slides, prepared with liquid, and colored with a special dye. The test results suggested the lump was scar endometriosis. A second test on the sample confirmed this finding. It showed cells from the womb lining along with thick tissue and large areas of bleeding. After the lump was fully removed, lab tests on the tissue also confirmed endometriosis. The patient was healthy otherwise and left the hospital in stable condition.

Follow-Up Recommendations:

The patient should see her regular doctor for more checkups and care for her scar endometriosis. She may need scans and other tests to watch the condition and find the best treatment. The patient should also get medical help if she notices any new symptoms or changes in how she feels.

Signed,
[REDACTED]
Date: [REDACTED]

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient: [INSERT NAME HERE]

Gender: Male

Age: 63

Chief Complaint: Erythematous patches and excoriated crust on the scalp

History of Present Illness:

The patient presented to a local dermatology clinic with a month-long complaint of erythematous patches and excoriated crust in the scalp. The diagnosis was initially chronic ulceration related to focal infection. Despite four weeks of follow-up, the patient's symptoms did not improve and referred to a tertiary referral university hospital for excisional biopsy and further management. On the excisional biopsy, the patient was diagnosed with angiosarcoma with microscopic involvement of the margins.

Hospital Course:

The patient underwent MRI, which showed that the tumor invaded subcutaneous fat with reticular patterns and spread galea aponeurotica and epicranial muscles. PET-CT showed diffuse uptake in the scalp and faint uptake in both level VA lymph nodes. He performed wide excision and treated with free latissimus dorsi flap and split-thickness skin graft. After wide resection and reconstruction surgery, multiple lymph node metastases were detected on PET-CT. After radiotherapy and adjuvant chemotherapy, increased uptake lesions were disappeared.

Summary:

In summary, the patient presented to the hospital with angiosarcoma with microscopic involvement of the margins. The treatment plan included wide excision and reconstruction surgery, followed by radiotherapy and adjuvant chemotherapy, which led to the disappearance of the increased uptake lesions. The patient was discharged in stable condition and instructed to follow up with his primary care physician and oncologist for further evaluation and management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient: [INSERT NAME HERE]

Gender: Male

Age: 63

Main Problem: Red patches and scabbed sores on the scalp

What Happened Before Coming to This Hospital:

The patient went to a skin doctor with red patches and scabbed sores on the scalp that had been there for one month. The first diagnosis was a long-lasting open sore caused by an infection. After four weeks of treatment, the patient did not get better. The patient was then sent to a larger hospital for surgery to remove a tissue sample and for more care. The tissue sample showed a type of blood vessel cancer called angiosarcoma. The cancer cells had spread to the edges of the removed tissue.

What Happened During the Hospital Stay:

The patient had an MRI scan. The scan showed the tumor had grown into the fat layer under the skin. It also spread to the thin tissue covering the skull and the muscles around the skull. A PET-CT scan showed the cancer had spread across the scalp. The scan also showed small signs of cancer in lymph nodes on both sides of the neck. The patient had surgery to remove a large area of tissue. Doctors rebuilt the area using muscle from the back and a thin layer of skin from another part of the body. After surgery, another PET-CT scan found the cancer had spread to several lymph nodes. The patient received radiation treatment and cancer-fighting drugs. After these treatments, the scans showed the cancer signs were gone.

Summary:

In summary, the patient came to the hospital with a type of blood vessel cancer called angiosarcoma. The cancer cells had spread to the edges of the removed tissue. The treatment plan included surgery to remove a large area of tissue and rebuild the scalp. This was followed by radiation treatment and cancer-fighting drugs. After treatment, the cancer signs on the scans were gone. The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient was told to follow up with the regular doctor and cancer doctor for more check-ups and care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

This is a discharge summary for a 74-year-old woman with metastatic lung adenocarcinoma. The patient was admitted to the hospital due to pain in the right lower chest wall, which was found to be caused by cancer that had spread to multiple areas of the body, including the right lung, mediastinal lymph nodes, liver, and pleural nodules.

The patient was prescribed various medications to manage her cancer, including gefitinib and osimertinib, but after several months of treatment, disease progression was observed.

In September 2017, the patient began receiving a combination of low-dose decitabine and camrelizumab, which resulted in significant improvement in the patient's mediastinal lymph nodes, liver, and pleural nodule metastases. However, after 7 cycles of treatment, the patient's disease progressed again, and she stopped receiving this treatment altogether. 

Throughout the patient's treatment, various markers were measured and showed fluctuations, including an increase in TMB and a decrease in TNB during combined therapy; however, the MSI status and HLA LOH events remained unchanged. 

In May 2019, the patient ultimately passed away from multiple organ failure.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

This is a discharge summary for a 74-year-old woman with lung cancer that had spread to other parts of her body. The patient came to the hospital because of pain in the right lower chest wall. Tests showed the cancer had spread to the right lung, lymph nodes in the chest, liver, and the lining around the lungs.

The patient was given different medicines to treat her cancer, including gefitinib and osimertinib. After several months, these medicines stopped working and the cancer got worse.

In September 2017, the patient started getting two different medicines together called decitabine and camrelizumab. These medicines helped shrink the cancer in the chest lymph nodes, liver, and lung lining. But after 7 rounds of treatment, the cancer started growing again. The patient stopped taking these medicines.

During treatment, doctors checked different markers in the blood and body. Some of these markers went up and some went down while the patient was getting the two medicines together. Other markers stayed the same throughout treatment.

In May 2019, the patient died because several of her organs stopped working.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with fever, myalgia, vomiting, nausea, headache, and abdominal pain. Physical examination revealed hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, rash, and conjunctival injection. Laboratory tests showed acute renal failure, albuminuria, hematuria, and thrombocytopenia. The patient had a history of cutting trees in the forest eight days prior but had not traveled to any other HFRS endemic area.

The patient developed seizures on the third day of hospitalization and was transferred to the Intensive Care Unit. CT and MRI scans showed bilateral hypodensities and hyperintensities in frontal, parietal, and occipital regions. No microhemorrhages were observed. The electroencephalogram showed slow wave activity on the left temporal and frontal lobe.

Blood tests revealed hantavirus IgM and IgG antibodies, indicating HFRS. Other serological tests for Leptospira, HBV, and HCV were negative. The patient refused to have a lumbar puncture.

Treatment included supportive therapy with ceftriaxone, corticosteroids, antiepileptics, saline infusions, electrolytes, antipyretics, and oxygen therapy. The patient was discharged after 16 days and underwent another head MRI after 2 months, which resulted normal.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with fever, muscle pain, throwing up, feeling sick to the stomach, headache, and belly pain. The doctor's exam showed an enlarged liver, enlarged spleen, a rash, and red eyes. Blood and urine tests showed the kidneys were not working well, protein in the urine, blood in the urine, and low platelet counts. The patient had been cutting trees in the forest eight days before but had not visited any other areas where this type of infection is common.

The patient had seizures on the third day in the hospital and was moved to the Intensive Care Unit. Brain scans showed unusual areas in the front, top, and back parts of the brain on both sides. There was no small bleeding in the brain. A test that measures brain waves showed slow activity in the left side of the brain near the temple and forehead.

Blood tests found signs of hantavirus infection, which confirmed the illness. Tests for other infections including Leptospira and liver viruses were negative. The patient chose not to have a spinal tap.

Treatment included supportive care with antibiotics, medicines to reduce swelling, medicines to prevent seizures, fluids through an IV, minerals, medicines to lower fever, and extra oxygen. The patient left the hospital after 16 days and had another brain scan two months later, which came back normal.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient: 9-month-old male patient

Date of Discharge: [Insert Date of Discharge]

Chief Complaint: Painless, localized mass over the right shoulder

History of Present Illness:

The patient presented with a painless, localized mass over the right shoulder, which has been present since birth. No further workup was performed at the time of delivery. As the patient aged, the parents grew increasingly concerned about the prominence, as it appeared to them to have grown in size. At that time, X-rays of the affected shoulder were obtained and a right midshaft clavicle fracture nonunion was diagnosed.

History:

The patient was delivered via cesarean section after failure to progress with difficult vaginal delivery. The obstetrician documented the swelling and associated it with lymphadenopathy at the time of delivery. No further relevant medical or surgical history reported.

Physical Examination:

The patient was well developed and well nourished. The patient exhibited an elevated prominence over the area of the right midshaft clavicle. No erythema or warmth was appreciated. During the exam, the patient raised his right arm with no apparent discomfort and he reached out and grasped using the right hand. Sensation was intact to the right hand and grip strength was symmetric.

Diagnostic Studies:

X-rays of the right shoulder demonstrated 2 distinct, separate portions of clavicle: a medial portion with a sternal attachment and a lateral portion with an acromial attachment. There was no evidence of reactive bone formation and both ends were corticated with a smooth appearance.

Hospital Course:

The patient was treated for a right midshaft clavicle fracture nonunion and was evaluated for possible child abuse by CPS. The parents were interviewed in the emergency department and 2 home visits were conducted.

Condition on Discharge:

The patient was discharged in stable condition with no active medical conditions.

Follow-Up:

The patient may require further evaluation with a specialist for fracture nonunion management.

Signed,

[Insert Physician Name]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient: 9-month-old baby boy

Date of Discharge: [Insert Date of Discharge]

Main Problem: A painless lump on the right shoulder

What Happened:

The patient had a painless lump on the right shoulder that has been there since birth. No tests were done at birth. As the baby grew, the parents worried more about the lump because it seemed to get bigger. At that point, doctors took pictures of the shoulder bones using X-rays. The X-rays showed that the collarbone was broken and did not heal back together.

Background:

The patient was born by surgery to remove him from the womb after a difficult birth. The doctor who delivered the baby noticed the swelling. The doctor thought it was swollen glands at the time of birth. There is no other important health history to report.

Body Exam:

The patient was healthy and well fed. There was a raised bump over the middle of the right collarbone. The skin was not red or warm. During the exam, the patient lifted his right arm without any sign of pain. He also reached out and grabbed things with his right hand. He could feel touch in his right hand. His grip strength was the same in both hands.

Test Results:

X-rays of the right shoulder showed the collarbone was in two separate pieces. One piece was attached to the breastbone. The other piece was attached to the shoulder blade. There were no signs of new bone trying to form. Both ends of the bone were smooth and covered with a hard outer layer.

What Was Done in the Hospital:

The patient was treated for a broken collarbone that did not heal. Child Protective Services checked to see if child abuse might have happened. The parents were asked questions in the emergency room. Two visits were made to the family home.

Condition When Leaving the Hospital:

The patient left the hospital in stable condition with no ongoing health problems.

Next Steps:

The patient may need to see a bone doctor to help manage the collarbone that did not heal.

Signed,

[Insert Physician Name]

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Admitting Diagnosis: Relapsing episodes of progressive swelling in the patellar region in both knees.


Hospital Course:

The patient, a 12-year-old boy diagnosed with Globoid cell leukodystrophy, was admitted to the Department of Pediatric Rheumatology with progressive swelling in the patellar region of both knees. Diagnostic arthroscopy revealed severe synovitis in the joint space of the right knee. Synovial tissue and fluid analysis revealed foamy macrophages consistent with the previous diagnosis of Globoid cell leukodystrophy.

Treatment consisted of aspiration of excessive joint fluid and subsequent intra-articular injection of triamcinolonhexacetonide, which was highly effective in all episodes. The duration of clinical remission after injection of corticosteroids was approximately 1 year.

He was discharged in a stable condition.


Patient Instructions:

The patient was advised to follow up with the treating physician.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Admitting Diagnosis: Repeated episodes of growing swelling in the kneecap area in both knees.

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 12-year-old boy with a rare brain disease called Globoid cell leukodystrophy, was admitted to the Children's Joint and Bone Disease Department with growing swelling in the kneecap area of both knees. A small camera was used to look inside the right knee joint. This showed serious swelling of the joint lining. Samples of joint tissue and fluid showed certain cells that matched the patient's known brain disease.

Treatment included draining extra fluid from the joint. Then a steroid medicine called triamcinolone hexacetonide was injected into the joint. This treatment worked very well each time it was given. After each steroid injection, the symptoms stayed away for about 1 year.

The patient was sent home in stable condition.

Patient Instructions:

The patient was advised to schedule a follow-up visit with the treating doctor.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [omitted]
Age: 60
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: Brachial plexopathy
Length of Stay: [omitted]

HOSPITAL COURSE

This patient was admitted to our facility for an intense pruritus on her right arm, which had been ongoing for six months. She also complained of right brachial paresis.

COMORBIDITIES

The patient has a known history of multiple myeloma.

DIAGNOSTIC TESTS AND PROCEDURES

Computed tomography of the cervical spine, magnetic resonance imaging, and electromyography of the brachial plexus were performed, which showed right cervical disc herniation with nerve root compression of C6-C7 and right brachial plexus inflammation (Parsonage-Turner syndrome).

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS

The patient was treated with methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and oral hydroxyzine 25mg every 8 hours, which resulted in little improvement of the pruritus. Gabapentin was introduced at 900mg/day, which achieved optimal control after three months of follow-up.

FOLLOW-UP INSTRUCTIONS

The patient was referred to neurosurgery and oncology consultation for additional therapeutic orientation.

SUMMARY

The patient presented with brachial plexopathy, which is attributed to neoplastic infiltration of multiple myeloma. The treatment plan included the application of methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and oral hydroxyzine 25mg every 8 hours, along with the introduction of gabapentin at 900mg/day. The patient was referred to neurosurgery and oncology for further management.

Patient Name: [omitted]
Age: 60
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: Nerve damage in the shoulder and arm area
Length of Stay: [omitted]

HOSPITAL COURSE

This patient was admitted to our facility for severe itching on her right arm. This problem had been going on for six months. She also had weakness in her right arm.

COMORBIDITIES

The patient has a known history of multiple myeloma. This is a type of blood cancer.

DIAGNOSTIC TESTS AND PROCEDURES

A CT scan of the neck, an MRI, and a nerve test of the shoulder and arm area were performed. These tests showed a bulging disc in the right side of the neck. This disc was pressing on nerves at the C6-C7 level. The tests also showed swelling of the nerves in the right shoulder and arm area. This condition is called Parsonage-Turner syndrome.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS

The patient was treated with methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1%. This is a steroid cream. She also took hydroxyzine 25mg by mouth every 8 hours. This helped only a little with the itching. Gabapentin was added at 900mg per day. After three months, this medicine controlled the itching well.

FOLLOW-UP INSTRUCTIONS

The patient was sent to see a brain and spine surgeon. She was also sent to see a cancer doctor for more treatment advice.

SUMMARY

The patient had nerve damage in the shoulder and arm area. This was caused by the spread of her blood cancer. The treatment plan included methylprednisolone aceponate cream 0.1% and hydroxyzine 25mg by mouth every 8 hours. Gabapentin at 900mg per day was also added. The patient was sent to a brain and spine surgeon and a cancer doctor for more care.
