input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Date of Admission: [Redacted]

Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Reason for Admission: Electrical injury 

Hospital Course: The patient, a 49-year-old male electrician, was brought to the emergency department after an electrical injury from contact with a bare cable while working on a rainy day. He lost consciousness but regained it after hitting his back on a cabinet. On examination, a first-degree burn of about 1 cm diameter was observed on his left hand, which correlated with the injury. Laboratory test results were generally normal except for slightly elevated myoglobin levels. Imaging studies showed no abnormalities. He was discharged on the same day after being diagnosed with an electrical injury.

Reason for Hospital Stay: Mild rhabdomyolysis 

During a subsequent follow-up, the patient developed bilateral lower leg soreness and pain, and mild rhabdomyolysis was suspected due to elevated myoglobin levels. He was advised to rest for 2 months, after which he returned to work. However, during this time, he developed numbness in both hands that gradually worsened, causing him discomfort. 

Diagnosis: Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) and bilateral cubital tunnel syndrome 

The patient presented with numbness in both hands, and physical examination demonstrated the presence of a positive Phalen's test but not Tinel's sign. A nerve conduction velocity (NCV) test revealed bilateral CTS and bilateral cubital tunnel syndrome, and the results were near the lower normal limit.

Treatment: Physical therapy and occupational therapy 

The patient was treated with physical therapy and occupational therapy for about half a year. After a significant improvement in the CTS symptoms, he was able to return to work.

Follow-up: Continued observation 

The patient will continue to be followed up to monitor his progress. No other issues were identified during his hospital stay, and he was discharged with no complications. 

Discharge Medications: None 

Discharge Instructions: The patient was advised to continue to monitor his symptoms and return to the hospital if his condition worsens.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Date of Admission: [Redacted]

Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Reason for Admission: Electrical injury

Hospital Course: The patient is a 49-year-old male electrician. He was brought to the emergency room after touching a bare wire while working in the rain. He passed out but woke up after hitting his back on a cabinet. Doctors found a small burn about 1 cm wide on his left hand from the injury. Blood tests were mostly normal but showed slightly high levels of a muscle protein called myoglobin. Scans of his body showed nothing wrong. He was sent home the same day after being told he had an electrical injury.

Reason for Hospital Stay: Mild muscle damage

At a later visit, the patient had soreness and pain in both lower legs. Doctors thought he might have mild muscle damage because of the high myoglobin levels. He was told to rest for 2 months before going back to work. During this time, he started feeling numbness in both hands that slowly got worse and bothered him.

Diagnosis: Nerve problems in both wrists and both elbows

The patient came in with numbness in both hands. During the exam, one test for nerve problems was positive but another was not. A test that measures how well nerves send signals showed nerve problems in both wrists and both elbows. The results were close to the low end of normal.

Treatment: Physical therapy and occupational therapy

The patient received physical therapy and occupational therapy for about six months. After his wrist and elbow symptoms got much better, he was able to go back to work.

Follow-up: Continued check-ups

The patient will keep coming back for check-ups so doctors can watch his progress. No other problems were found during his hospital stay. He was sent home without any issues.

Discharge Medications: None

Discharge Instructions: The patient was told to keep watching his symptoms. He should come back to the hospital if his condition gets worse.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Case 2
Date of Service: [DATE]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 71-year-old man with metastatic EGFR-mutated adenocarcinoma of the lung. He received first-line afatinib and palliative radiotherapy for a metastatic lesion at T8 of the spine. The primary tumor in his left upper lung initially regressed, but demonstrated apparent enlargement 7 months later. Salvage treatment was given using pemetrexed/cisplatin, and the tumor showed partial regression. The patient underwent maintenance pemetrexed for two years with no major side effects. Chest CT revealed enlargement of the primary tumor, with no evidence of extra-thoracic progression. Exertional dyspnea and chest pain also developed. SABR targeted the tumor in his left upper lung with good results and has been unchanged with postradiation fibrotic changes. Currently, the patient has remained on maintenance pemetrexed for more than 48 months with no evidence of disease recurrence.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Metastatic EGFR-mutated adenocarcinoma of the lung with primary tumor enlargement.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient with metastatic EGFR-mutated adenocarcinoma of the lung received multiple lines of therapy, including afatinib, palliative radiotherapy, pemetrexed/cisplatin, and maintenance pemetrexed. He also underwent SABR for a targeted tumor in his left upper lung. Despite experiencing slight side effects from chemotherapy, the patient tolerated treatment well and continues to be on maintenance pemetrexed with a favorable response.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Case 2
Date of Service: [DATE]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 71-year-old man with lung cancer that has spread to other parts of his body. His cancer has a specific gene change called EGFR. He first received a cancer drug called afatinib. He also had radiation treatment to help with cancer that had spread to his spine at the T8 level. The main tumor in his left upper lung got smaller at first. However, the tumor grew bigger again after 7 months. The patient then received two chemotherapy drugs called pemetrexed and cisplatin. This treatment made the tumor shrink partly. The patient continued taking pemetrexed alone for two years with no major side effects. A scan of his chest showed the main tumor had grown again. The cancer had not spread to other parts of his body outside the chest. The patient started having trouble breathing during activity and chest pain. He received a focused radiation treatment called SABR to the tumor in his left upper lung. This treatment worked well. The area now shows scar tissue from the radiation but has not changed otherwise. The patient has now been on pemetrexed for more than 48 months. There are no signs that the cancer has come back.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Lung cancer with a specific gene change that has spread to other body parts. The main tumor in the lung had grown larger.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient has lung cancer that has spread and has a gene change called EGFR. He received several treatments. These included the drug afatinib, radiation to ease symptoms, chemotherapy with pemetrexed and cisplatin, and ongoing pemetrexed. He also had focused radiation treatment called SABR to the tumor in his left upper lung. The patient had only mild side effects from chemotherapy. He handled the treatments well overall. He continues to take pemetrexed and is responding well to treatment.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [patient name]
Admission Date: [admission date]
Discharge Date: [discharge date]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient, a 65-year-old man with no medical history, was admitted to the hospital after presenting with sudden severe right flank pain and haemodynamic instability. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a right large subcapsular, perirenal, and retroperitoneal haematoma compressing and exerting a mass effect on the kidney.

TREATMENT:
The patient was resuscitated and underwent embolisation to control the bleeding. MRI of the abdomen showed a soft tissue mass confined to the perinephric space. A right open radical nephrectomy was performed, and the nephrectomy specimen revealed an infiltrative spindle cell tumour consistent with synovial sarcoma, monophasic type. The patient was started on chemotherapy with docetaxel and gemcitabine combination for six cycles.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient was evaluated by an oncologist and underwent monitoring for one year after surgery. A CT urogram at nine months showed no signs of recurrence.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
The patient was discharged in a stable condition with instructions to continue with the prescribed chemotherapy. The patient was advised to follow up with the oncologist and to report any symptoms to the healthcare team. 

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
Please refer to the most recent medication list provided to the patient and communicate any changes in medication to the prescribing physician.

FOLLOW-UP INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient is advised to follow up with the oncologist for monitoring and treatment. The patient should report any symptoms to the healthcare team immediately.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [patient name]
Admission Date: [admission date]
Discharge Date: [discharge date]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient is a 65-year-old man with no past health problems. He came to the hospital with sudden, severe pain on the right side of his back. His blood pressure was dangerously low. A CT scan took pictures of his belly and lower body. The scan showed a large collection of blood around and behind his right kidney. This blood buildup was pressing on the kidney.

TREATMENT:
The medical team gave the patient fluids and care to stabilize him. Then doctors did a procedure to block the blood vessels and stop the bleeding. An MRI scan showed a growth in the area around the kidney. Doctors performed surgery to remove the entire right kidney. Tests on the removed kidney found a type of cancer called synovial sarcoma. The patient started cancer treatment with two medicines called docetaxel and gemcitabine. This treatment was given for six rounds.

FOLLOW-UP:
A cancer doctor checked on the patient over the year after surgery. A scan at nine months showed no signs that the cancer had come back.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition. He was told to continue his cancer treatment as directed. He should keep seeing his cancer doctor. He should tell his care team about any new symptoms right away.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
Please look at the most recent list of medicines given to the patient. Tell the doctor who prescribed the medicines about any changes.

FOLLOW-UP INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should keep seeing his cancer doctor for checkups and treatment. The patient should report any symptoms to his care team right away.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]

Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Date of Admission: [Date of admission]

Date of Discharge: [Date of discharge]

Hospital: [Hospital name]

Clinical Summary
The patient, a 32-year-old male, presented with complaints of breathlessness, low-grade fever, and weight loss. He had a history of treated tuberculous lymphadenitis and was diagnosed with disseminated tuberculosis (probably relapse) on examination.

Hospital Course
During the hospital stay, the patient was subjected to further investigations, which included blood tests, liver function tests, radiography, as well as ultrasonography of the abdomen. FNAC of the axillary and cervical lymph nodes were also performed to make a definitive diagnosis.

Diagnosis
The possibility of cryptococcal infection was considered based on the patient's blood reports, radiography, and FNAC results. The definitive diagnosis of C. neoformans was confirmed after isolating the fungus from the lymph node aspirate culture.

Treatment
The patient underwent treatment for cryptococcal infection as per hospital protocol. There were no reported complications during the course of treatment.

Follow-Up Plan
The patient was advised to take his medications regularly and was scheduled for a follow-up after one month.

Prognosis
The patient's prognosis was good, and he was advised to continue his medication as directed without fail.

Discharge Instructions
The patient was discharged with instructions to take his prescribed medications regularly and report back to the hospital if he experienced any adverse effects. Additionally, he was advised to adopt a healthy lifestyle and increase his water intake. The patient was also advised to continue his routine check-ups with a physician.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]

Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Date of Admission: [Date of admission]

Date of Discharge: [Date of discharge]

Hospital: [Hospital name]

Clinical Summary
The patient is a 32-year-old man. He came to the hospital with trouble breathing, a mild fever, and weight loss. In the past, he had treatment for a type of infection that causes swollen glands. Doctors found that this infection had spread through his body and likely came back.

Hospital Course
While in the hospital, the patient had several tests done. These included blood tests, tests to check how well the liver works, and X-rays. The patient also had an ultrasound of the belly area. Doctors used a needle to take small samples from swollen glands in the neck and armpit areas. These tests helped doctors find out what was causing the illness.

Diagnosis
Test results from blood work, X-rays, and gland samples pointed to a fungal infection. Doctors grew the fungus from the gland sample in the lab. This confirmed the patient had a fungal infection called Cryptococcus.

Treatment
The patient received treatment for the fungal infection following the hospital's standard care plan. The patient did not have any problems during treatment.

Follow-Up Plan
The patient was told to take all medicines as directed. The patient has an appointment to come back in one month.

Prognosis
The patient is expected to do well. The patient must keep taking all medicines exactly as directed.

Discharge Instructions
The patient was sent home with orders to take all prescribed medicines on schedule. The patient should return to the hospital right away if any bad reactions happen. The patient should also eat well, stay active, and drink plenty of water. The patient should keep seeing a doctor for regular check-ups.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient presented to our institution for regular follow-up, 2 years after a right radical nephrectomy for a primary renal cell carcinoma (RCC). The patient was asymptomatic, but an abdominal computed tomography (CT) scan on follow-up revealed a mass in the left adrenal gland. A multidisciplinary team meeting determined that a laparoscopic transperitoneal adrenalectomy was the best course of action.

Procedure:

The patient underwent a laparoscopic transperitoneal adrenalectomy, performed under general anesthesia. The patient was placed in the lateral decubitus position with the left side up. The pneumoperitoneum was established, and a 30° laparoscope and three additional working ports were placed under direct vision. The tumor was exposed and isolated before being excised.

Histological examination:

The histological examination of the specimen demonstrated a metastatic RCC with features similar to the primary renal tumor. All surgical margins were free of tumor.

Outcome:

The postoperative period was uneventful, and the patient was discharged on postoperative day two. The patient is well and has no recurrence or adrenal insufficiency 18 months post-surgery.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to our hospital for a routine checkup. This was 2 years after surgery to remove the right kidney because of kidney cancer. The patient had no symptoms. However, a CT scan of the belly showed a growth on the left adrenal gland. The adrenal gland is a small organ that sits on top of the kidney. A team of doctors met to discuss the best treatment. They decided to remove the adrenal gland using a small camera and tiny cuts.

Procedure:

The patient had surgery to remove the left adrenal gland. The surgery was done while the patient was fully asleep under general anesthesia. The patient was placed on their side with the left side facing up. The doctors filled the belly with gas to create space to work. They used a small camera and three small openings to do the surgery. The doctors found the tumor and carefully separated it from surrounding tissue. Then they removed it.

Histological examination:

Doctors looked at the removed tissue under a microscope. The growth was cancer that had spread from the original kidney cancer. The edges of the removed tissue showed no signs of cancer. This means the doctors removed all of the tumor.

Outcome:

The patient recovered well after surgery with no problems. The patient left the hospital 2 days after the operation. At 18 months after this surgery, the patient is doing well. There are no signs that the cancer has come back. The patient also has no problems with the adrenal gland being gone.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY: 

Patient: 51-year-old male 

Admission Date: [DATE] 
Discharge Date: [DATE] 

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted with a 1-month history of a palpable mass on the left nasolabial fold. Physical examination revealed a firm, noninflammatory, and well-demarcated swelling below the left nasal ala. The patient underwent excision of the mass under local anesthesia. Histopathology revealed a thick-walled blood vessel filled with papillary structures lined by flattened endothelial cells and supported by fibrous stroma. Endothelial cells were positive for CD34. 

Condition on Discharge: The patient was discharged with no significant complaints or complications. 

Instructions on Discharge: The patient was advised to follow up with their physician in 1 week for wound check, and thereafter as needed. No further follow-up or interventions were required at this time. 

Medications on Discharge: None were prescribed. 

Follow-Up: The patient was instructed to follow up with their physician for any new or worsening symptoms. The patient was advised to call the hospital immediately if there were any signs of infection or bleeding. 

Diagnosis: Papillary intralymphatic angioendothelioma 

Procedures: Excision of mass 

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted and underwent excision of a palpable mass on the left nasolabial fold. Histopathology revealed papillary intralymphatic angioendothelioma. Following discharge, the patient was advised to follow up with their physician in 1 week and thereafter as needed. No further treatment was required at this time. 

Condition on Discharge: The patient was discharged with no significant complaints or complications. 

Instructions on Discharge: The patient was advised to follow up with their physician in 1 week for wound check, and thereafter as needed. No further follow-up or interventions were required at this time. 

Medications on Discharge: None were prescribed. 

Follow-up: The patient was instructed to follow up with their physician for any new or worsening symptoms. The patient was advised to call the hospital immediately if there were any signs of infection or bleeding.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient: 51-year-old male

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted with a lump on the left side of the face near the nose. The lump had been there for about 1 month. A physical exam showed a firm, round bump below the left side of the nose. The bump was not red or swollen. The patient had surgery to remove the lump while awake with numbing medicine. Lab tests on the lump showed it was a rare, non-cancerous growth made of blood vessel tissue. The growth had small finger-like shapes inside a blood vessel with a thick wall. Special testing confirmed the type of cells in the growth.

Condition on Discharge: The patient left the hospital feeling well with no problems.

Instructions on Discharge: The patient was told to see their doctor in 1 week to check how the wound is healing. After that, the patient should see their doctor as needed. No other visits or treatments were needed at this time.

Medications on Discharge: No medicines were prescribed.

Follow-Up: The patient was told to see their doctor if any new symptoms appear or if symptoms get worse. The patient was told to call the hospital right away if there are signs of infection or bleeding.

Diagnosis: Papillary intralymphatic angioendothelioma, which is a rare, non-cancerous growth of blood vessel tissue.

Procedures: Surgery to remove the lump.

Instructions on Discharge: The patient was told to see their doctor in 1 week to check how the wound is healing. After that, the patient should see their doctor as needed. No other visits or treatments were needed at this time.

Medications on Discharge: No medicines were prescribed.

Follow-up: The patient was told to see their doctor if any new symptoms appear or if symptoms get worse. The patient was told to call the hospital right away if there are signs of infection or bleeding.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our hospital with symptoms of left upper arm weakness and paresthesia. Physical examination revealed left hemiparesis, with diminished left upper and lower strength, and extensor plantar response on the left side. Cranial multimodal computed tomography (CT) imaging showed hypodensity on cortical and subcortical frontoparietal regions, compatible with ischemic stroke, and cranial Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) revealed occlusion of the M2 segment of the middle cerebral artery.

Clinical Findings:

The patient reported a history of substance abuse, with habitual consumption of heroin, methadone, cocaine, and alcohol, as well as smoking crack cocaine the night before the episode. A toxicological screen yielded positive results for cocaine and opioids, and a carotid ultrasound (CU) showed a large soft plaque with an irregular surface and circumferential blood flow at the distal end, inside the internal carotid artery.

Treatment and Management:

The patient was treated with enoxaparin and warfarin therapy, which resulted in gradual dissolution of the thrombus. The patient's symptoms gradually improved during the next few weeks, and she was discharged from the hospital in good general condition.

Follow-Up:

Despite being discharged in good general condition, the patient continued to abuse drugs and experienced another cerebral stroke a year later.

Conclusion:

The patient presented with left hemiparesis, indicating ischemic stroke, and revealed a history of substance abuse. Treatment with enoxaparin and warfarin therapy resulted in the gradual dissolution of the thrombus, and the patient's symptoms improved over time. However, the patient continued to abuse drugs and was readmitted to the hospital a year later after a second cerebral stroke.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to our hospital because of weakness and tingling in the left upper arm. The physical exam showed weakness on the left side of the body. The patient had reduced strength in the left arm and leg. There was also an abnormal toe reflex on the left side. A CT scan of the head showed damaged brain tissue in the front and side areas. This damage was caused by a stroke from blocked blood flow. An MRI scan of the head showed a blockage in a blood vessel in the brain.

Clinical Findings:

The patient reported a history of drug and alcohol use. This included regular use of heroin, methadone, cocaine, and alcohol. The patient also smoked crack cocaine the night before the symptoms started. Drug tests came back positive for cocaine and opioids. An ultrasound of the neck arteries showed a large buildup of soft fatty material. This buildup had an uneven surface and was inside a major neck artery that supplies blood to the brain.

Treatment and Management:

The patient was given two blood-thinning medicines called enoxaparin and warfarin. These medicines helped slowly break up the blood clot. The patient's symptoms slowly got better over the next few weeks. The patient left the hospital in good overall health.

Follow-Up:

The patient left the hospital in good overall health. However, the patient kept using drugs. One year later, the patient had another stroke.

Conclusion:

The patient came in with weakness on the left side of the body caused by a stroke. The patient had a history of drug use. Treatment with blood-thinning medicines helped break up the blood clot. The patient's symptoms got better over time. However, the patient kept using drugs. One year later, the patient came back to the hospital after having a second stroke.

Discharge Summary:
Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Patient Name: [PATIENT NAME]
Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Hospital Course:
The patient, [PATIENT NAME], was admitted to the hospital for elective intracardiac repair due to incomplete shone syndrome. She presented with Grade III DOE and was diagnosed with severe MS due to supramitral ring, moderate MR, severe periductal CoA, large PDA, severe pulmonary arterial hypertension, moderate TR, and severe LV dysfunction. On [DATE], the patient underwent ligation of PDA and resection of coarctation with end-to-end anastomosis of the descending thoracic aorta through lateral thoracotomy approach. This was followed by resection of the supramitral ring under CPB that was carried out through sternotomy in a single setting. The postoperative patient had mild MR and mild TR with LVEF of 30%. The patient had an uneventful course postoperatively and was discharged on [DATE].

Discharge Diagnosis: Incomplete Shone Syndrome, Severe Mitral Stenosis, Moderate Mitral Regurgitation, Severe Periductal Coarctation, Large Patent Ductus Arteriosus, Severe Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension, Moderate Tricuspid Regurgitation, and Severe Left Ventricular Dysfunction.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow up with her cardiologist for regular monitoring and management of her cardiac condition. The patient should also continue taking her prescribed medications and follow a healthy lifestyle with a balanced diet and regular exercise. In case of any symptoms such as chest pain, shortness of breath, or palpitations, the patient should immediately seek medical attention.

Follow-Up:
Cardiology follow-up appointment on [DATE].

Provider Signature: [PROVIDER SIGNATURE]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:
Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Patient Name: [PATIENT NAME]
Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Hospital Course:
The patient, [PATIENT NAME], was admitted to the hospital for a planned heart repair surgery. The patient has a rare heart condition present from birth called incomplete Shone syndrome. The patient came in with severe trouble breathing during normal activities. The patient was found to have a severely narrowed heart valve between the upper and lower left heart chambers. This was caused by a ring of tissue that should not be there. The patient also had a moderately leaky valve in the same area. The patient had a severe narrowing of the main blood vessel leaving the heart. There was also a large opening between two blood vessels that should have closed after birth. The patient had severely high blood pressure in the lungs. The valve on the right side of the heart was moderately leaky. The left lower heart chamber was pumping very poorly. On [DATE], the patient had surgery through the side of the chest. The doctors closed the extra opening between the blood vessels. They also removed the narrowed part of the main blood vessel and reconnected the healthy ends. Next, through a cut in the chest bone, the doctors removed the extra ring of tissue near the heart valve. The heart was connected to a machine that pumped blood during this part of the surgery. After surgery, the heart valves had only mild leaking. The left lower heart chamber was pumping at 30 percent strength. The patient recovered well after surgery with no problems. The patient was sent home on [DATE].

Discharge Diagnosis: The patient has a rare birth defect affecting the left side of the heart. The patient has a severely narrowed valve between the upper and lower left heart chambers. The patient has a moderately leaky valve in the same area. The patient has a severe narrowing of the main blood vessel leaving the heart. The patient has a large opening between two blood vessels that should have closed after birth. The patient has severely high blood pressure in the lungs. The patient has a moderately leaky valve on the right side of the heart. The left lower heart chamber is pumping very poorly.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should see the heart doctor regularly to check on the heart condition. The patient should keep taking all medicines as directed. The patient should eat healthy foods and exercise regularly. If the patient has chest pain, trouble breathing, or feels the heart racing, the patient should get medical help right away.

Follow-Up:
Heart doctor appointment on [DATE].

Provider Signature: [PROVIDER SIGNATURE]

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with a four-month history of a progressively relapsing painless mass in the neck, despite conservative treatments with antimicrobials and anti-inflammatory medications. A clinical examination showed a firm, oval-shaped mass of approximately 7 cm in the long axis at level IV of the left neck. Imaging studies revealed a left lower neck mass with a heterogeneous short T1 signal and heterogeneous post-contrast enhancement at the lateral aspect of the lesion, consistent with a complex tumor with internal hemorrhage or a pseudoaneurysm.

Diagnosis:

The patient's laboratory survey showed a large elevation in the α-fetoprotein level and the β-human chorionic gonadotropin level. Surgical extirpation revealed a large tumor at the left lower neck measuring approximately 10 cm in length. Pathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of a left testicular immature teratoma of high grade with positive left inguinal lymph node metastases.

Treatment:

The patient received left radical inguinal orchiectomy and left inguinal lymph node dissection. Adjuvant chemotherapy with a bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin regimen was administered soon after completion of surgical interventions. Persistent elevated β-human chorionic gonadotropin levels were observed after a full course of four cycles of monthly chemotherapy with an episode of flare-up, which suggested relapse. Salvage second-line chemotherapy was initiated with a paclitaxel, ifosfamide, and cisplatin regimen thereafter.

Follow-up:

The patient tolerated the treatments well without complications and was discharged from the hospital in stable condition. The patient was advised to follow up regularly with his healthcare team for continued monitoring and management of his condition.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with a lump in the neck that had been growing for four months. The lump was not painful but kept coming back. Medicines for infection and swelling did not help. The doctor felt a firm, oval-shaped lump about 7 cm long on the lower left side of the neck. Scans showed a lump with mixed signals that could be a growth with bleeding inside or a blood vessel problem.

Diagnosis:

Blood tests showed very high levels of two markers called alpha-fetoprotein and beta-human chorionic gonadotropin. Surgery removed a large growth from the lower left neck that measured about 10 cm long. Lab tests on the growth confirmed it was a high-grade cancer that started in the left testicle. The cancer had also spread to lymph nodes in the left groin area.

Treatment:

The patient had surgery to remove the left testicle and lymph nodes in the left groin. After surgery, the patient received cancer-killing drugs called bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin. After four monthly rounds of these drugs, one blood marker stayed high and spiked up. This suggested the cancer came back. The patient then started a second set of cancer-killing drugs called paclitaxel, ifosfamide, and cisplatin.

Follow-up:

The patient handled all treatments well with no problems and left the hospital in stable condition. The patient was told to see the healthcare team regularly for ongoing check-ups and care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Date of Birth: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:
Atrial septal defect (ASD), Total anomalous pulmonary venous return (TAPVR), congenital second-degree heart block, 2:1 conduction, mild cyanosis, and pulmonary congestion.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital due to the above-listed conditions. The initial physical examination revealed moderate cyanosis with mildly decreased oxygen saturation values of 85%-94%. A postnatal echocardiogram confirmed the presence of TAPVR and bilateral superior vena cava without bridging vein, interrupted inferior vena cava, and a secundum type ASD. The patient underwent a successful cardiac surgery and permanent pacemaker placement at 9 days of age.

Diagnostic Assessments:
CGH array analysis of a peripheral blood sample revealed an 818 kb duplication of the long arm of chromosome 1, and single-nucleotide polymorphism oligonucleotide microarray analysis indicated a gain of chromosome 1. The duplicated region contained 11 genes, seven of which are OMIM annotated, and four have known disease associations.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient was discharged at 3 weeks of age and was doing well. A 2-month follow-up revealed no complications.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient and her family were educated on the importance of close follow-up with a pediatric cardiologist. They were instructed to monitor the infant's health at home, including daily oxygen saturation levels and feeding patterns. The family was also advised to continue the infant's pacemaker monitoring and receive regular immunizations as recommended by her pediatrician.

Follow-up:
The patient is scheduled for regular follow-up appointments with the pediatric cardiologist and primary care physician to monitor the cardiovascular condition, growth, and development. A clinical genetics evaluation will be recommended to assess the chromosomal abnormality's impact on the patient's long-term health. 

Disposition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition, improved cardiovascular symptoms, and with a positive outlook for long-term outcomes.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Date of Birth: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:
The patient was admitted with a hole in the heart wall, blood vessels connected to the wrong part of the heart, a slow heart rhythm that only beats every other time, slightly blue skin color, and fluid buildup in the lungs.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital for the conditions listed above. The first exam showed the patient had bluish skin and lower than normal oxygen levels between 85% and 94%. A heart ultrasound done after birth confirmed the blood vessels were connected wrong. It also showed two large veins in the upper body instead of one, a missing large vein in the lower body, and a hole in the wall between the upper heart chambers. The patient had successful heart surgery and received a device to help the heart beat regularly at 9 days old.

Diagnostic Assessments:
A blood test that looks at genes found a small extra copy of part of one chromosome. This copied section contains 11 genes. Seven of these genes are listed in a medical database. Four of them are linked to known health conditions.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient went home at 3 weeks old and was doing well. A checkup at 2 months showed no problems.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient's family learned why regular visits with a children's heart doctor are important. They were told to watch the baby's health at home. This includes checking oxygen levels every day and watching how the baby feeds. The family was also told to keep up with pacemaker checkups and get all vaccines the baby's doctor recommends.

Follow-up:
The patient will have regular visits with the children's heart doctor and regular doctor. These visits will check the heart condition, growth, and development. A visit with a genetics doctor will be suggested to understand how the chromosome change might affect the patient's health over time.

Disposition:
The patient went home in stable condition with improved heart symptoms and a good chance for healthy long-term results.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: Female, 78 years old
Chief Complaint: Crusted nail bed lesion and nail plate dystrophy of the right index finger
Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with a crusted nail bed lesion and nail plate dystrophy of the right index finger. She also had a medical history of type 2 diabetes, gout and hypertension.
Histology revealed a moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) arising from bowenoid dysplasia of the right index finger.
The patient underwent right index finger amputation through the distal interphalangeal joint (DIPJ) using a volar skin flap for coverage under local anaesthetic.
The SCC was found to be completely excised; however, Bowen's disease was present at the lateral margin and was therefore treated topically. 
The patient remained without any sign of recurrence or metastasis for 14 months.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Moderately differentiated SCC arising from bowenoid dysplasia

Procedures:
Right index finger amputation through the DIPJ using a volar skin flap for coverage

Medications:
None documented

Follow-Up Instructions:
The patient should have follow-up appointments with her primary care doctor and dermatologist as recommended to monitor for any signs of recurrence or metastasis.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: Female, 78 years old
Chief Complaint: Crusty sore on the nail bed and damaged nail on the right index finger
Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with a crusty sore on the nail bed and a damaged nail on the right index finger. She also has a history of type 2 diabetes, gout and high blood pressure.
Lab tests on the tissue showed a medium-grade skin cancer that started from an early form of skin cancer on the right index finger.
The patient had surgery to remove the tip of the right index finger at the last joint. Skin from the palm side of the finger was used to cover the wound. The patient was awake with numbing medicine during the surgery.
The skin cancer was fully removed. However, early skin cancer cells were found at the edge of the wound. This area was treated with medicine applied to the skin.
The patient showed no signs of the cancer coming back or spreading for 14 months.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Medium-grade skin cancer that started from an early form of skin cancer

Procedures:
Removal of the tip of the right index finger at the last joint with skin from the palm side used to cover the wound

Medications:
None documented

Follow-Up Instructions:
The patient should have follow-up visits with her regular doctor and skin doctor as recommended. These visits will check for any signs of the cancer coming back or spreading.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:
Patient Name: [Patient Name]
Medical Record Number: [Medical Record Number]
Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with increasing lower abdominal pain, flank pain, and dysuria for three months. Upon physical examination, the abdomen was slightly rigid, and tenderness and rebound tenderness were observed in the lower left quadrant of the abdomen. Empirical antibiotics were administered immediately with the clinical impression of an intra-abdominal abscess. The patient underwent an explorative laparotomy to determine the definite diagnosis.

FINDINGS:
An extensive, diffusely infiltrating lesion-like mass involving the small bowel, mesentery, and sigmoid colon was observed during the surgery. A sigmoid colon perforation was also detected. Pathology revealed an intense chronic inflammatory reaction.

TREATMENT:
Lysis of adhesions, pus drainage, and sigmoid colon segmentectomy were performed. After 12 weeks of penicillin use, the patient's clinical manifestations and radiologic findings were improved.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
The patient was diagnosed with a diffuse infiltrative lesion-like mass involving the small bowel, mesentery, and sigmoid colon, and with a sigmoid colon perforation.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
At discharge, the patient's condition was stable. There was no recurrence observed during follow-up visits.

FOLLOW-UP CARE:
A follow-up visit is recommended six months from the discharge date to monitor the patient's condition for any potential recurrence.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:
Patient Name: [Patient Name]
Medical Record Number: [Medical Record Number]
Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came to the hospital with pain in the lower belly and side, along with painful urination for three months. During the exam, the belly was a bit stiff and painful to touch, especially on the lower left side. The patient was given antibiotics right away because doctors thought there might be an infection with pus inside the belly. The patient had surgery to open the belly and find out what was wrong.

FINDINGS:
During surgery, doctors found a large area of damaged tissue spreading through the small intestine, the tissue holding the intestines, and the lower part of the large intestine. There was also a hole in the lower part of the large intestine. Tests on the tissue showed serious long-term swelling and irritation.

TREATMENT:
Doctors cut away scar tissue that had grown together, drained the pus, and removed the damaged part of the lower large intestine. The patient took penicillin for 12 weeks. After this, the patient's symptoms and imaging tests showed improvement.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
The patient was found to have a large area of damaged tissue spreading through the small intestine, the tissue holding the intestines, and the lower part of the large intestine. The patient also had a hole in the lower part of the large intestine.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
The patient was in stable condition when leaving the hospital. No signs of the problem coming back were seen during follow-up visits.

FOLLOW-UP CARE:
The patient should return for a check-up six months after leaving the hospital. This visit is to make sure the problem has not come back.

input:
Hospital Course

This 51-year-old male weighing 131 kg presented to the ED with altered mental status and slurred speech after undergoing cervical epidural injection. He became unresponsive during the injection with subsequent brief convulsive activity. Emergency medical services was called and found him obtunded with shallow breathing and low oxygen saturations requiring ventilation assistance. On arrival to the ED his breathing was erratic with low oxygen saturations. He remained somnolent with slurred speech, unable to answer questions appropriately or follow commands. On cardiopulmonary monitor, he was noted to have an irregularly irregular heart rhythm. Electrocardiogram showed atrial fibrillation with a rate of 82 beats per minute. Hemodynamically he was stable. Within 60 minutes of ED arrival, the patient’s mental status was back to baseline without recollection of the events that had occurred after the start of the procedure. Imaging studies obtained were all negative. 

Hospital Course: Diagnosis and Treatment

The patient was observed overnight in the hospital and remained asymptomatic and without any further dysrhythmia. He only had chest wall pain, possibly from sternal rub or from any bystander chest compressions that may have been performed when he became unresponsive. Serum/plasma levels of lidocaine and its primary active metabolite, monoethylglycinexylidide (MEGX), were obtained and returned undetectable. Aspirin 325 mg daily was prescribed and a referral to outpatient cardiology was made. 

Condition at Discharge

The patient was discharged with no symptoms and normal mental status.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course

This 51-year-old male weighing 131 kg came to the emergency room confused and with slurred speech. This happened after getting a pain injection in his neck. During the injection, he passed out and had a brief seizure. Emergency workers were called and found him barely awake with shallow breathing and low oxygen levels. He needed help breathing. When he arrived at the emergency room, his breathing was uneven and his oxygen levels were low. He stayed very drowsy with slurred speech. He could not answer questions correctly or follow directions. Heart monitors showed his heart was beating in an uneven pattern. A heart test showed he had a condition called atrial fibrillation, which means the heart beats in an irregular way. His heart rate was 82 beats per minute. His blood pressure and blood flow stayed normal. Within 60 minutes of arriving at the emergency room, the patient was thinking clearly again. He did not remember anything that happened after the procedure started. All imaging tests came back normal.

Hospital Course: Diagnosis and Treatment

The patient stayed in the hospital overnight for watching. He had no symptoms and no more heart rhythm problems. He only had chest wall pain. This was possibly from rubbing on his chest bone or from chest pushes that may have been done when he passed out. Blood tests checked for lidocaine and a related substance called MEGX. Lidocaine is a numbing medicine. These tests showed none of these substances in his blood. The patient was given aspirin 325 mg to take once a day. He was also given a referral to see a heart doctor as an outpatient.

Condition at Discharge

The patient was sent home with no symptoms and normal thinking.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient 1, a 21-year-old man with bilateral temporal lobe epilepsy, autism and intellectual disability, was admitted for the management of medically intractable focal onset impaired awareness seizures and focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures. The patient was on lacosamide and levetiracetam, having previously been administered topiramate, carbamazepine and lamotrigine without complete resolution of his seizures. Vagus nerve stimulation therapy was also attempted which failed to prevent the seizures and the patient had suffered frequent shoulder joint dislocations. 

The patient underwent electroencephalography which showed epileptiform discharges over bilateral temporal regions, and brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) which showed no significant abnormalities except for relative atrophy for his age. During the hospitalization the patient was stabilized on his current regimen and monitored for adverse effects of the medications. The patient was discharged and instructed to follow up with his treating physician within a week for further management of his condition. 

At the time of discharge, the patient’s condition was deemed stable, without any notable complications. The patient was advised to avoid activities that could result in dislocation of the shoulder joint and to adhere to his prescribed medications. The patient and the family were educated on the importance of medication compliance, monitoring seizure activity and recognizing possible adverse effects of the antiepileptic medications used. 

The discharge summary serves as a comprehensive report of the clinical findings, management, and instructions for follow-up care, with details of the nature and severity of the patient’s condition, medication regimen, and recommendations regarding activity restrictions. It is expected that the patient will comply with the prescribed medications, follow-up with his physician, and monitor his condition closely.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient 1 is a 21-year-old man with seizures affecting both sides of his brain, autism, and learning difficulties. He was admitted to the hospital because his seizures were not controlled by medicine. He has two types of seizures. One type causes him to lose awareness. The other type causes shaking of his whole body.

The patient takes two seizure medicines called lacosamide and levetiracetam. In the past, he tried three other seizure medicines called topiramate, carbamazepine, and lamotrigine. None of these fully stopped his seizures. He also tried a device that sends small electrical signals to his brain through a nerve in his neck. This device did not stop his seizures either. The patient has also had his shoulder pop out of place many times.

The patient had a test that records brain wave activity. This test showed abnormal electrical activity on both sides of his brain. He also had a brain scan. The scan looked mostly normal but showed his brain was slightly smaller than expected for his age. During his hospital stay, the patient was kept on his current medicines. The care team watched for any bad reactions to the medicines.

The patient was sent home and told to see his regular doctor within one week. His doctor will help manage his condition going forward.

When the patient left the hospital, his condition was stable. There were no new problems. The patient was told to avoid activities that could make his shoulder pop out of place. He was also told to keep taking his medicines as directed. The patient and his family learned why it is important to take medicines on time. They also learned how to watch for seizures and spot possible side effects from the seizure medicines.

This discharge summary gives a full report of what was found, how the patient was treated, and what to do next. It explains the patient's condition, his medicines, and limits on his activities. The patient is expected to take his medicines as directed, see his doctor for follow-up visits, and watch his condition closely.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 22 years old

Sex: Female

Chief Complaint: Lower back pain

Medical History: None

Hospital Course:

Admission: The patient presented to our center with continuous lower back pain for the past 6 months. She had no history of other symptoms or medical conditions.

Examination: On examination, the patient was afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and fully conscious. Her physical exam was unremarkable except for her back pain. Blood counts were within normal ranges, and liver and renal function tests were unremarkable.

Imaging: Transabdominal ultrasonography of the pelvis showed a complex cystic lesion with dimensions of 10.2 cm × 6.4 cm. A subsequent magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan revealed a 10.6 cm × 10 cm × 9.4 cm sized multiloculated cystic lesion in the presacral space with variable signal intensities, giving an impression of a type IV sacrococcygeal teratoma.

Treatment: The patient underwent a laparoscopic excision of the cyst

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 22 years old

Sex: Female

Chief Complaint: Lower back pain

Medical History: None

Hospital Course:

Admission: The patient came to our center with ongoing lower back pain for the past 6 months. She had no history of other symptoms or health problems.

Examination: During the exam, the patient had no fever, had stable blood pressure and heart rate, and was fully awake and alert. Her physical exam was normal except for her back pain. Blood tests showed normal results. Liver and kidney function tests were also normal.

Imaging: An ultrasound of the pelvic area showed a fluid-filled growth with solid parts measuring 10.2 cm by 6.4 cm. A follow-up MRI scan showed a larger growth with multiple fluid-filled pockets in the area in front of the tailbone. It measured 10.6 cm by 10 cm by 9.4 cm. The doctors believed this was a type of rare tumor called a sacrococcygeal teratoma, which is a growth present from birth that forms near the tailbone.

Treatment: The patient had surgery using small cuts and a camera to remove the growth.

input:
Patient Identification:
Patient: 20-year-old Japanese woman with mental retardation and epilepsy

History and Physical Examination:
The patient presented with lower abdominal pain and was diagnosed with a multicystic tumor in her pelvic cavity. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed her bilateral ovaries were swollen. A right oophorectomy and left ovarian cystectomy were performed. Two years and nine months later, a multilocular tumor was detected in her pelvic cavity during follow-up.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent a second surgery and a multicystic tumor was found in her pelvic cavity that consisted of many non-isolated cysts of different sizes. A hysterectomy, left oophorectomy, omentectomy, pelvic lymph node biopsy, appendectomy, and tumor resection of her abdomen were performed. Complete resection of the lesion was achieved. The patient received estrogen replacement therapy.

Diagnosis:
BMPM, a rare and aggressive tumor

Follow-up:
The patient remains free from recurrence nine months after the second operation.
clean_ref:
Patient Identification:
Patient: 20-year-old Japanese woman with intellectual disability and seizure disorder

History and Physical Examination:
The patient came in with pain in her lower belly. Doctors found a growth with many fluid-filled sacs in her pelvic area. A special scan showed that both of her ovaries were swollen. Doctors removed her right ovary and took out a cyst from her left ovary. Two years and nine months later, a check-up found another growth with many sections in her pelvic area.

Hospital Course:
The patient had a second surgery. Doctors found a growth with many fluid-filled sacs of different sizes in her pelvic area. Doctors removed her uterus, left ovary, a fatty tissue layer near her stomach, some lymph nodes from her pelvic area, her appendix, and the growth from her belly. They were able to remove all of the growth. The patient started taking hormone medicine to replace what her ovaries used to make.

Diagnosis:
BMPM, a rare and fast-growing tumor

Follow-up:
Nine months after the second surgery, the tumor has not come back.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: Confidential
Age: 83 years
Gender: Male

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Nasal deformity

Hospital Course:

The patient presented to the otolaryngology clinic with a nasal deformity that had been present for several years, but the patient claimed growth of the mass over the previous year. The patient had no history of alcoholism or dermatological disease. On physical examination, there was a large, pedunculated, swollen, irregular mass measuring 9 cm × 8 cm, occupying the area at the tip and the dorsum of the nose. The remaining head and neck examination was normal.

The lesion was excised completely with sharp scalpel incision and coblation-assisted dissection under general anesthesia. The Artrocare probe of coblation device designed for turbinate surgery was used. Reconstruction of the defect was performed with a full-thickness skin graft obtained from the upper arm. No recurrence was seen during the 1-year follow-up period.

Pathology:

Histopathological examination of the biopsy specimens showed hyperkeratosis of the epidermis, increased sebaceous glands, and cystic expansion of ducts in the dermis. Duct lumen was dilated and filled with keratin plugs. Fibrosis and chronic lymphoplasmocytic cell infiltration were observed around the ducts. Neoplastic cells were not seen.

Summary:

The patient was admitted to the otolaryngology clinic with nasal deformity. The lesion was excised completely with sharp scalpel incision and coblation-assisted dissection under general anesthesia. Reconstruction of the defect was performed with a full-thickness skin graft obtained from the upper arm. The pathology report showed no evidence of neoplastic cells. The patient remained free from recurrence during the 1-year follow-up period.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: Confidential
Age: 83 years
Gender: Male

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]

Admitting Diagnosis: Nose shape problem

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the ear, nose, and throat clinic with a nose shape problem. This problem had been there for several years. The patient said the growth had gotten bigger over the past year. The patient had no history of heavy drinking or skin disease. During the exam, the doctor found a large, hanging, swollen, uneven growth. It measured 9 cm by 8 cm. The growth covered the tip and top of the nose. The rest of the head and neck exam was normal.

The growth was removed completely using a surgical knife and a special heat-based cutting tool. The patient was asleep under general anesthesia during the surgery. The doctor used a special probe made for nose surgery. The area was repaired using a skin graft taken from the upper arm. This graft included all layers of skin. No regrowth was seen during the 1-year follow-up period.

Pathology:

Lab tests on the tissue samples showed thickening of the outer skin layer. There were more oil glands than normal. Skin tubes in the deeper layer were stretched into sac-like shapes. These tubes were widened and filled with hard skin plugs. There was scarring and long-term swelling around the tubes caused by certain white blood cells. No cancer cells were found.

Summary:

The patient came to the ear, nose, and throat clinic with a nose shape problem. The growth was removed completely using a surgical knife and a special heat-based cutting tool. The patient was asleep under general anesthesia during the surgery. The area was repaired using a skin graft taken from the upper arm. This graft included all layers of skin. The lab report showed no cancer cells. The patient had no regrowth during the 1-year follow-up period.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED] | Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitted Diagnosis: Atrial fibrillation with features of LVH and SARS-CoV-2 positive infection

Major Procedures: None

Hospital Course:

The patient presented to the ED with malaise and palpitations, and a confirmed diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2. Upon admission, he was noted to be febrile with left lower lobe infiltrates and patchy perihilar and right middle lobe ground-glass airspace disease on chest x-ray. The patient's vital signs were monitored closely, and he was started on intravenous diltiazem for atrial fibrillation control. The patient showed an improvement in his PR after treatment and was then started on intravenous diltiazem drip at 5mg/hour. The patient received symptomatic treatment for fever given the presence of a fever on admission. The patient was maintained in droplet and contact isolation with eye protection.

Treatment:

The patient was treated intravenous diltiazem (10 mg bolus) and a 500 ml normal saline intravenous fluid bolus, which led to an improvement in his PR to approximately 100 bpm. After this, he was started on intravenous diltiazem drip at 5 mg/hour for atrial fibrillation control and admitted for further monitoring and workup.

Outcome:

The final diagnosis was atrial fibrillation with features of LVH and SARS-CoV-2 positive infection. The patient's tachycardia and fever subsided during his hospitalization course. He was discharged in a stable condition for further follow-up.

Recommendations:

The patient was advised to continue home isolation and complete the 14-day quarantine period. The patient was provided with a list of symptoms that he should report to his primary care physician if they occur. A follow-up appointment to his primary care physician was scheduled for further management after completing the quarantine period.

Overarching Risk Factors:

The patient had a past medical history of untreated essential hypertension.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED] | Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitted Diagnosis: Irregular heartbeat with thickening of the heart muscle and COVID-19 infection

Major Procedures: None

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the emergency room feeling unwell with a racing heart and tested positive for COVID-19. When admitted, the patient had a fever and chest x-rays showed signs of lung infection. The care team watched the patient's vital signs closely. The patient received diltiazem through an IV to help control the irregular heartbeat. The patient's heart rate got better after treatment. The patient then received a slow, steady dose of diltiazem through the IV at 5mg per hour. The patient received medicine to treat the fever. The patient was kept in a special room with safety measures to prevent spreading the virus.

Treatment:

The patient received diltiazem medicine through an IV and fluids through an IV. This helped lower the heart rate to about 100 beats per minute. After this, the patient received a slow, steady dose of diltiazem at 5 mg per hour to control the irregular heartbeat. The patient stayed in the hospital for more monitoring and tests.

Outcome:

The final diagnosis was irregular heartbeat with thickening of the heart muscle and COVID-19 infection. The patient's fast heart rate and fever went away during the hospital stay. The patient left the hospital in stable condition for follow-up care.

Recommendations:

The patient was told to stay home in isolation and complete 14 days of quarantine. The patient received a list of warning signs to report to the regular doctor if they happen. A follow-up visit with the regular doctor was scheduled after the quarantine period ends.

Overarching Risk Factors:

The patient had a history of high blood pressure that was not being treated.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Age: 47 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient was admitted to our clinic due to severe menorrhagia and referred for MRI screening to evaluate her suitability for MRgFUS treatment. MRI showed a single intramural fibroid of 560 cc, and she underwent MRgFUS treatment using the ExAblate 2000 system. After the procedure, she reported no complications and was discharged.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient had a significant improvement in her symptoms at the 3-month follow-up visit. Her Symptom Severity Score reduced from 56 points to 28 points, and the fibroid showed 38% shrinkage. At 1-year follow-up, the patient reported no symptoms, and her Symptom Severity Score was reduced to 13 points, which is considered as nonsymptomatic.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Severe menorrhagia due to a single intramural fibroid

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient is advised to monitor her menstrual cycle and report if symptoms reoccur. She is also instructed to follow-up with her gynecologist for further evaluation.

DISCHARGE DISPOSITION:
The patient was discharged from our clinic in stable condition with no complications.

FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
Severe menorrhagia due to a single intramural fibroid.

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN:
[REDACTED]

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Age: 47 years
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came to our clinic because of very heavy menstrual bleeding. She had an MRI scan to see if a special treatment would work for her. The MRI found one noncancerous growth in the wall of her uterus. The growth was about the size of a large grapefruit. She received treatment using focused ultrasound waves guided by MRI. This treatment uses heat to shrink the growth without surgery. After the treatment, she had no problems and went home.

FOLLOW-UP:
At her 3-month checkup, the patient felt much better. Her symptom score dropped from 56 points to 28 points. The growth shrank by 38 percent. At her 1-year checkup, the patient had no symptoms at all. Her symptom score dropped to 13 points. This score means she no longer has bothersome symptoms.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Very heavy menstrual bleeding caused by one noncancerous growth in the uterus wall.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should keep track of her monthly periods. She should tell her doctor if her symptoms come back. She should also schedule a visit with her women's health doctor for more checkups.

DISCHARGE DISPOSITION:
The patient left our clinic in good condition with no problems.

FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
Very heavy menstrual bleeding caused by one noncancerous growth in the uterus wall.

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN:
[REDACTED]

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY 

Patient: 44-year-old woman 

Admission Date: [Date] Discharge Date: [Date] 

Diagnosis: Dilated common bile duct, cholecystectomy history, and splenic heterogeneous subcapsular hematoma 

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to the hospital with intractable nausea and vomiting associated with severe epigastric pain. Physical examination was only notable for mild epigastric tenderness. Laboratory tests showed mildly elevated liver enzymes, and a right upper quadrant ultrasound showed dilation of the common bile duct, which was later confirmed on magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography as well as a 7-mm common bile duct stone. The patient underwent ERCP with sphincterotomy, which demonstrated a dilated common bile duct with no obvious cause. She subsequently experienced severe epigastric and left upper quadrant abdominal pain with hypotension after the procedure. Repeat laboratory testing at the time showed elevated lipase as well as a decline in hemoglobin. A CT scan of the abdomen illustrated a large splenic heterogeneous subcapsular hematoma and peripancreatic stranding. The patient was transferred to the intensive care unit and managed conservatively with fluids and blood transfusions. The hematoma regressed, and her hemoglobin remained stable. With clinical improvement, she was discharged home. 

Follow-up Care: The patient was advised to follow up with her primary care provider for further management and was instructed to return to the emergency department if she experiences any further symptoms. 

Discharge Medications: N/A 

Discharge Instructions: 

1. Rest and avoid strenuous activities 
2. Keep the incision areas clean and dry 
3. Take prescribed medications as directed by your healthcare provider
4. Follow up with your primary care provider for further management of your condition 
5. Return to the emergency department if you experience any further symptoms. 

Discharge Disposition: Home 

Attending Physician: [Physician's Name] 

Dictated By: [Physician's Name] 

Keywords: Dilated common bile duct, cholecystectomy history, splenic heterogeneous subcapsular hematoma.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: 44-year-old woman

Admission Date: [Date] Discharge Date: [Date]

Diagnosis: Widened bile duct, past gallbladder removal surgery, and blood clot under the spleen surface

Hospital Course: The patient came to the hospital with severe nausea and vomiting that would not stop. She also had bad pain in the upper belly area. The physical exam showed only mild tenderness in the upper belly. Blood tests showed slightly high liver levels. An ultrasound of the upper right belly showed the bile duct was wider than normal. A special scan confirmed this and also found a small stone in the bile duct. The patient had a procedure using a thin tube through the mouth to look at the bile duct. During this, a small cut was made to help the stone pass. The test showed a wide bile duct but no clear reason why. After the procedure, the patient had severe pain in the upper belly and left side. Her blood pressure also dropped. New blood tests showed high levels of a pancreas enzyme and lower red blood cell counts. A scan of the belly showed a large blood clot forming under the spleen surface. It also showed swelling around the pancreas. The patient was moved to the intensive care unit for close watching. She was treated with fluids given through a vein and blood transfusions. The blood clot got smaller and her blood counts stayed stable. As she got better, she was sent home.

Follow-up Care: The patient was told to see her regular doctor for ongoing care. She was also told to go to the emergency room if she has any new symptoms.

Discharge Medications: None

Discharge Instructions:

1. Rest and do not do hard physical activities
2. Keep the procedure areas clean and dry
3. Take any medicines as told by your doctor
4. See your regular doctor for ongoing care of your condition
5. Go to the emergency room if you have any new symptoms.

Discharge Disposition: Home

Attending Physician: [Physician's Name]

Dictated By: [Physician's Name]

Keywords: Widened bile duct, past gallbladder removal surgery, blood clot under the spleen surface.

The physical exam showed only mild tenderness in the upper belly. Blood tests showed slightly high liver levels. An ultrasound of the upper right belly showed the bile duct was wider than normal. A special scan confirmed this and also found a small stone in the bile duct. The patient had a procedure using a thin tube through the mouth to look at the bile duct. During this, a small cut was made to help the stone pass. The test showed a wide bile duct but no clear reason why. After the procedure, the patient had severe pain in the upper belly and left side. Her blood pressure also dropped. New blood tests showed high levels of a pancreas enzyme and lower red blood cell counts. A scan of the belly showed a large blood clot forming under the spleen surface. It also showed swelling around the pancreas. The patient was moved to the intensive care unit for close watching. She was treated with fluids given through a vein and blood transfusions. The blood clot got smaller and her blood counts stayed stable. As she got better, she was sent home.

Follow-up Care: The patient was told to see her regular doctor for ongoing care. She was also told to go to the emergency room if she has any new symptoms.

Discharge Medications: None

Discharge Instructions:

1. Rest and do not do hard physical activities
2. Keep the procedure areas clean and dry
3. Take any medicines as told by your doctor
4. See your regular doctor for ongoing care of your condition
5. Go to the emergency room if you have any new symptoms.

Discharge Disposition: Home

Attending Physician: [Physician's Name]

Dictated By: [Physician's Name]

Keywords: Widened bile duct, past gallbladder removal surgery, blood clot under the spleen surface.

input:
[Discharge Summary]

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 25
Gender: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Adult-onset Still's disease (AOSD) and pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH)
Final Diagnosis: AOSD with MAS and co-existing PAH

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the rheumatology and immunology department with recurrent joint pain, fever, and rash. Laboratory data revealed elevated inflammatory markers, hepatic enzymes, and spleen enlargement that led to a diagnosis of AOSD according to Yamaguchi criteria. Supportive treatments were initiated, but the patient's condition deteriorated, and MAS was diagnosed. The patient received methylprednisolone and showed significant improvement within a week. During the course of the hospitalization, chest CT and echocardiography showed the presence of PAH, a rare complication of AOSD.

Imaging Findings:
CT angiography revealed anomalous drainage of the right superior, middle, and inferior pulmonary veins into the right atrium, a significantly enlarged pulmonary artery, and a small atrial septal defect.

Course of Treatment:
The patient received supportive care and methylprednisolone for MAS. No treatment for PAH was initiated during the hospitalization period.

Clinical Course and Follow-up:
The patient's symptoms improved significantly with methylprednisolone treatment, and she was discharged. Plans for further management of her co-existing PAH were discussed with her upon discharge.

Summary:
This patient was admitted with AOSD and was subsequently diagnosed with MAS. Co-existing PAH was incidentally discovered during evaluation. The patient received appropriate treatment for her diagnoses and improved significantly. Plans for further management of her PAH were discussed with her upon discharge.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 25
Gender: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Adult-onset Still's disease and high blood pressure in the lungs
Final Diagnosis: Adult-onset Still's disease with a serious complication called macrophage activation syndrome and high blood pressure in the lungs

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital for joint pain, fever, and rash that kept coming back. Blood tests showed high levels of inflammation and liver enzymes. The patient also had an enlarged spleen. These findings led doctors to diagnose Adult-onset Still's disease. The care team started treatment, but the patient got worse. Doctors then found a serious complication called macrophage activation syndrome. The patient received a strong steroid medicine called methylprednisolone. The patient got much better within a week. During the hospital stay, a chest scan and heart ultrasound showed high blood pressure in the lungs. This is a rare problem linked to Adult-onset Still's disease.

Imaging Findings:
A special scan of the blood vessels showed some problems with the heart and lungs. Blood vessels from the right lung were draining into the wrong part of the heart. The main blood vessel to the lungs was much larger than normal. There was also a small hole between the upper chambers of the heart.

Course of Treatment:
The patient received supportive care and the steroid medicine methylprednisolone for macrophage activation syndrome. No treatment was started for the high blood pressure in the lungs during this hospital stay.

Clinical Course and Follow-up:
The patient got much better with the steroid treatment and was sent home. The care team talked with the patient about plans to treat the high blood pressure in the lungs.

Summary:
This patient came to the hospital with Adult-onset Still's disease. Doctors also found a serious complication called macrophage activation syndrome. High blood pressure in the lungs was found by chance during testing. The patient received the right treatment and got much better. The care team talked with the patient about plans to treat the high blood pressure in the lungs.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Gender: Female
Age: 57
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

This is a discharge summary for [Redacted], a 57-year-old female with a history of recurrent breast cancer who was admitted to our facility with bilateral thigh pain and weakness. She was previously diagnosed with left breast cancer at age 50 and underwent adjuvant chemotherapy and mastectomy followed by tamoxifen for 5 years. She was admitted to our facility with a diagnosis of rhabdomyolysis after undergoing chemotherapy with docetaxel and cyclophosphamide. She was initially resistant to physical therapy, but after referral to PT, she was found to have generalized weakness in left triceps, wrist and finger extension, finger flexion, and dorsal interossei. Consequently, she was referred to our inpatient rehabilitation unit for further management.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a series of interventions which were divided into four phases. The first phase focused on cognitive behavioral therapy interventions including deep breathing and calming procedures. In the second phase, she progressed to light resistance exercises with elastic exercise bands and ankle weights. The third phase focused on neuromuscular re-education, standing static and dynamic balance activities, and ambulation on a balance beam with upper extremity support. In the fourth and final phase, she underwent dynamic gait activities including turning her head or body, speed variation, and ambulation over or around obstacles. She was discharged home 29 days after her initial presentation to the hospital, with a marked improvement in functional status as well as subjective reports in confidence and pain.

Upon discharge, the patient demonstrated improvement with pain at 0/10. Her quadriceps and hip strength improved bilaterally. Her TUG test improved to 10 seconds and she was able to ambulate community distances with a walker at discharge. Her Berg Balance Scale results improved to indicate a low fall risk at 55/56. Follow-up appointments were scheduled to monitor her progress.

The patient's discharge medications were not available for inclusion in this summary as the patient was not prescribed any medication during her inpatient stay. 

Signed, 
[Redacted]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Gender: Female
Age: 57
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

This is a discharge summary for [Redacted], a 57-year-old female with a history of breast cancer that came back. She was admitted to our facility with pain and weakness in both thighs. She was first told she had breast cancer in her left breast at age 50. At that time, she had cancer drug treatment and surgery to remove her breast. She then took a cancer-blocking medicine called tamoxifen for 5 years. She was admitted to our facility because her muscles broke down after receiving cancer drugs called docetaxel and cyclophosphamide. At first, she did not want to do physical therapy. After meeting with the physical therapy team, they found she had overall weakness. This weakness was in her left upper arm, wrist, fingers, and hand muscles. Because of this, she was sent to our hospital's rehab unit for more care.

During her hospital stay, she went through a treatment plan with four parts. The first part focused on mental health exercises like deep breathing and relaxation methods. In the second part, she moved on to light strength exercises using stretchy bands and ankle weights. The third part focused on retraining her muscles and nerves. She also worked on balance while standing still and moving. She practiced walking on a balance beam while holding on for support. In the fourth and final part, she did walking activities that were more challenging. These included turning her head or body, changing her speed, and walking over or around objects. She went home 29 days after first coming to the hospital. She showed much better ability to do daily activities. She also said she felt more confident and had less pain.

When she left the hospital, the patient showed improvement with pain at 0 out of 10. Her upper leg and hip muscles got stronger on both sides. Her Timed Up and Go test improved to 10 seconds. This test measures how well someone can stand up, walk, and sit back down. She was able to walk long distances in the community using a walker when she left. Her balance test score improved to 55 out of 56. This means she has a low chance of falling. Follow-up appointments were scheduled to check on her progress.

The patient's discharge medicines were not available to include in this summary. This is because she was not given any medicine during her hospital stay.

Signed,
[Redacted]
