input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 87 years
Date of admission: [Redacted]
Date of discharge: [Redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: Poor oral intake, pneumonia, and hypokalemia
Discharge Diagnosis: Hypokalemia and possible medication-induced hypokalemia

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital for investigation of poor oral intake, hypokalemia, and pneumonia. The patient had discontinued medication for benign prostatic hyperplasia. The patient had been recently diagnosed with cognitive dysfunction and was started on choline alfoscerate and donepezil. Investigations showed hypokalemia and the presence of subtle pneumonia. Further investigations were done to determine the cause of hypokalemia which revealed signs of renal potassium wasting. CT scan showed no abnormalities. A thorough review of the therapeutic agents prescribed for cognitive dysfunction was conducted, which identified donepezil as a possible cause of hypokalemia. Donepezil was immediately discontinued. Potassium supplementation using intravenous and oral formulations was initiated. The patient showed a gradual increase in serum potassium level and on the third day following donepezil cessation, the patient's serum potassium level was 3.5 mmol/L. During this time, proper management of pneumonia was also done. BP was noted to be high for 24 hours after hospitalization and amlodipine and olmesartan were initiated.

Discharge Medications:
Amlodipine and olmesartan were prescribed for BP management.

Follow-up:
The patient was discharged on the ninth day of hospitalization. The patient was advised to continue potassium supplementation for a total of 3 weeks. Olmesartan and amlodipine were prescribed to be continued. The patient was advised to have regular BP monitoring and to follow-up with a primary care doctor.

Instructions to Patient:
The patient was advised to follow a low sodium and low fat diet as well as avoid food high in potassium. The patient was advised to adhere to prescribed medications and avoid over-the-counter medications without consulting a physician. The patient was advised to return to the hospital if any concerning symptoms are noted.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 87 years
Date of admission: [Redacted]
Date of discharge: [Redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: Not eating well, lung infection, and low potassium levels
Discharge Diagnosis: Low potassium levels, possibly caused by a medication

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital because of not eating well, low potassium levels, and a lung infection. The patient had stopped taking medication for an enlarged prostate. The patient was recently told about memory problems and started taking choline alfoscerate and donepezil. Tests showed low potassium levels and a mild lung infection. More tests were done to find out why potassium was low. The tests showed the kidneys were losing too much potassium. A CT scan showed nothing unusual. The care team looked at the memory medications and found donepezil might be causing the low potassium. Donepezil was stopped right away. The patient was given potassium through an IV and by mouth. The patient's potassium levels slowly went up. Three days after stopping donepezil, potassium reached a normal level of 3.5 mmol/L. The lung infection was also treated during this time. Blood pressure was high for 24 hours after coming to the hospital. The patient was started on amlodipine and olmesartan for this.

Discharge Medications:
Amlodipine and olmesartan were prescribed to control blood pressure.

Follow-up:
The patient left the hospital on the ninth day. The patient was told to keep taking potassium for 3 weeks total. The patient should keep taking olmesartan and amlodipine. The patient was told to check blood pressure regularly and see a primary care doctor.

Instructions to Patient:
The patient was told to eat less salt and fat and avoid foods high in potassium. The patient was told to take all prescribed medications. The patient should not take any store-bought medications without asking a doctor first. The patient was told to come back to the hospital if any worrying symptoms appear.

[Header]
Discharge Summary

[Patient Information]
Patient Name: [insert patient name]
Medical Record Number: [insert assigned number]
Sex: Female 
Age: 66 

[Hospital Course and Reason for Admission]
The patient was admitted to our medical institution due to intermittent pin-prick pain occurring in the right chest. During a 4-month period, the patient underwent intermittent nerve blocks at a local clinic. The symptoms were not improved. Therefore, the patient was admitted to our medical institution.

[History and Physical Examination]
On physical examination, the patient complained of allodynia and hyperesthesia. At the time of admission, VAS score was 7-8/10 points. The patient had a concurrent presence of the scar tissue.

[Diagnostic Tests]
No diagnostic tests were performed.

[Treatment and Condition at Discharge]
For the treatment of the patient, a needle was placed in the multifidus muscle at the level of T5 and the tissue around the facet at the level of T5-6. Four weeks after the treatment, VAS score decreased from 7-8 points at the time of admission to 3 points. Two weeks later, VAS score further improved to 2 points. The patient's pain was managed with analgesics and anti-depressants.

[Discharge Medication and Instructions]
The patient was discharged with instructions to continue taking her analgesics and anti-depressants.

[Follow-up]
No follow-up was scheduled at discharge.

[Diagnoses]
The patient was diagnosed with neuralgia. 

[Prognosis]
The prognosis for the patient is good, but symptoms can reoccur. 

[Summary]
The patient was admitted for the management of neuralgia occurring in the right chest. Pain was managed with a multifidus muscle needle placement at T5 and tissue around the facet at T5-6. The patient made a good recovery and was discharged with instructions to continue taking pain medications.

Discharge Summary

Patient Information
Patient Name: [insert patient name]
Medical Record Number: [insert assigned number]
Sex: Female
Age: 66

Hospital Course and Reason for Admission
The patient came to our hospital because of sharp, stabbing pain in the right side of the chest. The pain came and went. Over 4 months, the patient had nerve-numbing shots at another clinic. These shots did not help. So the patient came to our hospital for more care.

History and Physical Examination
During the exam, the patient had pain from light touch that should not hurt. The patient also felt more pain than normal from things that usually cause only mild pain. At the time of coming to the hospital, the pain level was 7-8 out of 10. The patient also had scar tissue in the area.

Diagnostic Tests
No tests were done.

Treatment and Condition at Discharge
To treat the patient, a needle was placed in a deep back muscle near the spine at the upper back level. The needle was also placed in tissue near the spine joints in the same area. Four weeks after treatment, the pain level dropped from 7-8 out of 10 to 3 out of 10. Two weeks later, the pain level got even better at 2 out of 10. The patient took pain medicine and mood medicine to help with the pain.

Discharge Medication and Instructions
The patient was told to keep taking the pain medicine and mood medicine at home.

Follow-up
No follow-up visit was set up when the patient left the hospital.

Diagnoses
The patient was found to have nerve pain.

Prognosis
The patient should do well, but the pain may come back.

Summary
The patient came to the hospital for nerve pain in the right side of the chest. The pain was treated with needle placement in a deep back muscle and near spine joints in the upper back. The patient got much better and went home with instructions to keep taking pain medicine.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: 
Age: 52
Gender: Female

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the cardiology department ICU with generalized edema and orthopnea in July 2008. An electrocardiogram revealed atrial fibrillation with a heart rate of 178 beats per minute. A chest X-ray detected pulmonary edema and cardiomegaly. A two-dimensional echocardiogram showed an ejection fraction of 57%. The patient was prescribed diltiazem for heart rate control. A physical examination revealed a large asymmetrical goiter, which was easily visible and palpable. The patient was diagnosed with NTI accompanying heart failure and discharged.

Follow-up:
The patient came to the endocrinology department for follow-up in February 2012. A follow-up thyroid ultrasonogram revealed an increase in the size of the previously observed nodule in the right lobe and contralateral lobe atrophy. A 99m-Technetium-pertechnetate thyroid scan demonstrated a functioning toxic nodule. The patient refused surgery or radioactive iodine therapy and was prescribed methimazole.

Outstanding Clinical Issues:
The patient was diagnosed with thyroid disease and had an increase in the size of the previously observed nodule in the right lobe and contralateral lobe atrophy at the time of the follow-up. The patient was prescribed methimazole and TSH levels normalized two weeks later. 

Discharge Medications:
Calcium channel blocker, angiotensin receptor antagonist, diuretic and aspirin.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient should continue follow-up for thyroid disease and any heart failure symptoms.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name:
Age: 52
Gender: Female

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the heart care intensive care unit with swelling all over the body and trouble breathing while lying down in July 2008. A heart rhythm test showed an irregular heartbeat with a heart rate of 178 beats per minute. A chest X-ray showed fluid in the lungs and an enlarged heart. A heart ultrasound showed the heart was pumping out 57% of its blood with each beat. The patient was given diltiazem to help control the heart rate. A physical exam showed a large uneven thyroid gland in the neck that could be seen and felt easily. The patient was found to have thyroid problems along with heart failure and was sent home.

Follow-up:
The patient came to the hormone specialist department for a check-up in February 2012. A follow-up thyroid ultrasound showed that a growth on the right side of the thyroid had gotten bigger and the left side had shrunk. A thyroid scan using a special dye showed an overactive thyroid growth. The patient did not want surgery or treatment with radioactive iodine and was given methimazole instead.

Outstanding Clinical Issues:
The patient was found to have thyroid disease with a growth on the right side of the thyroid that had gotten bigger and shrinking on the left side at the time of the check-up. The patient was given methimazole and thyroid hormone levels returned to normal two weeks later.

Discharge Medications:
Heart rate and blood pressure medicine, water pill, and aspirin.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient should keep going to check-ups for thyroid disease and any signs of heart failure.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [INSERT NAME]
Sex: Female
Age: 65 years
Medical record number: [INSERT NUMBER]
Discharge Date: [INSERT DATE]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with complaints of pain and swelling of the left hand along the thenar eminence and the proximal aspect of the thumb. She also had cervical lymphadenopathy. A plain radiograph showed pathological fracture of the base of 1st metacarpal along with lytic lesion at the base of the 1st metacarpal with haziness of the surrounding soft tissue. An ultrasound revealed presence of an abscess. The MRI scan suggested an abscess like picture most likely due to infective etiology and less likely to be neoplastic lesion. 

The patient underwent drainage of abscess and debridement with stabilization of the fracture fragment. Intra-operatively we found thick caseous material within the soft tissue and the fragment at base of the 1st metacarpal was freely mobile similar to a loose body. The fragment was removed and sent for biopsy and culture sensitivity along with the surrounding soft-tissue. A kirschner wire was passed through the 1st carpo-metacarpal joint to stabilize it. 

The biopsy report came back positive for tuberculous osteomyelitis which suggested fibro-collagenous tissue and few bony fragments showing large caseous necrosis with few epitheloidid cell granuloma. The patient was then started on anti-tuberculous treatment emprerically while awaiting the culture report. A Combination of Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide and ethambutol was started. 

At the end of 5 weeks the kirschner wire backed out partially which was then removed and range of movement exercises were started. The cervical lymphadenopathy regressed by the end of 3 months. The patient had 1 episode of recurrence of cervical lymphandenopathy at the end of 7 months of treatment which eventually regressed on the continuous phase of AKT. 

At the end of one year, the patient had almost complete range of movement with no sequel due to tuberculosis as compared to the opposite hand. Plain radiographs showed some degree of joint remodelling at one year. 

Discharge Medications:
The patient is dispensed her usual home medications.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow up with the orthopedic surgeon regularly and continue taking the prescribed medication. The patient is counselled to adopt a healthy lifestyle and a balanced diet. Any symptoms of recurrence or deterioration should be reported to the physician immediately. Additionally, the patient is advised regarding the importance and significance of regular follow-up, which is critical to the management of her condition.

Follow-up:
The patient is referred to the orthopedic surgeon for follow-up to assess progress and discuss the patient's ongoing management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [INSERT NAME]
Sex: Female
Age: 65 years
Medical record number: [INSERT NUMBER]
Discharge Date: [INSERT DATE]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with pain and swelling in her left hand. The swelling was near the base of her thumb and the fleshy part of her palm. She also had swollen glands in her neck. An X-ray showed a broken bone at the base of her thumb. The break happened because the bone was weak from disease. There was also a dark spot on the bone and cloudy tissue around it. An ultrasound showed a pocket of infection. An MRI scan suggested the pocket was most likely caused by infection and not cancer.

The patient had surgery to drain the infection and clean out the damaged tissue. The doctors also fixed the broken bone to keep it stable. During surgery, the doctors found thick cheese-like material in the soft tissue. The bone piece at the base of the thumb was loose and moving freely. The loose piece was taken out and sent to the lab for testing. The tissue around it was also sent for testing. A thin metal pin was placed through the thumb joint to hold it steady.

The lab results showed the patient had a bone infection caused by tuberculosis. The tissue samples showed dead tissue and signs of this specific infection. The patient was started on tuberculosis medicine right away while waiting for more test results. She was given four medicines called Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol.

After 5 weeks, the metal pin started to come loose on its own. It was then taken out. The patient started doing exercises to move her thumb. The swollen neck glands went down after 3 months. At 7 months, the neck glands swelled up again one time. They went back down while the patient kept taking her tuberculosis medicine.

After one year, the patient could move her thumb almost as well as her other hand. There were no lasting problems from the tuberculosis. X-rays showed the joint had healed and reshaped itself after one year.

Discharge Medications:
The patient is given her regular home medicines.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is told to see the bone doctor regularly and keep taking the medicines as directed. The patient is told to eat healthy foods and live a healthy lifestyle. Any signs that the infection is coming back or getting worse should be reported to the doctor right away. The patient is also told how important it is to keep all follow-up visits. These visits are very important for treating her condition.

Follow-up:
The patient is scheduled to see the bone doctor for follow-up visits. These visits are to check her healing and plan her ongoing care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 27
Sex: Male
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient presented to the emergency room with massive upper gastrointestinal bleeding and was admitted to the Habib Thameur Hospital. Physical examination revealed diffuse mucocutaneous pallor and a Glasgow Coma Score of 15/15. The patient had tachycardia, hypotension, and enlarged spleen without hepatomegaly. The rectal digital examination was positive for melena. Laboratory investigations showed decreased white blood cell count, thrombocytopenia, and normochromic normocytic anemia. Minor signs of hepatocellular insufficiency were displayed but albuminemia was normal. An acute kidney failure was also noted. The results of the patient’s tests indicated no signs of hepatitis C, hepatitis B surface antigen, and hepatitis B core antibody. 

The patient was stabilized following fluid resuscitation and blood transfusion of two red blood cell concentrates. The patient was treated with a proton-pump inhibitor (omeprazole) and octreotide. An upper gastroduodenoscopy showed the presence of oesophageal varices with massive active bleeding, moderate hypertensive gastropathy, and gastric varicose veins. Several elastics with a first and second kit of ligature were put in place to control the bleeding. An abdominal ultrasound showed the presence of multiple multiloculated cystic formations suggesting multiple liver hydatidosis.

The patient suffered from a cataclysmic re-bleeding causing refractory hemorrhagic shock and disseminated intravenous coagulation leading to death after a week.

Impression:

The patient's clinical history, physical examination, and laboratory investigations suggest multiple liver hydatidosis and acute kidney failure. The patient was treated with a proton-pump inhibitor (omeprazole) and octreotide with the aim of controlling the bleeding. However, the patient suffered from a cataclysmic re-bleeding causing refractory hemorrhagic shock, disseminated intravenous coagulation, and death.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 27
Sex: Male
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the emergency room with severe bleeding from the stomach area. The patient was admitted to Habib Thameur Hospital. A physical exam showed very pale skin. The patient was fully alert and aware. The patient had a fast heartbeat, low blood pressure, and an enlarged spleen. The liver was normal in size. A rectal exam showed dark, tarry stools from internal bleeding. Blood tests showed low white blood cell count, low platelet count, and low red blood cells. There were minor signs that the liver was not working well, but protein levels were normal. The kidneys were also failing. Test results showed no signs of hepatitis B or hepatitis C infections.

The patient became stable after receiving fluids and two units of blood. The patient received medicine to reduce stomach acid (omeprazole) and medicine to slow bleeding (octreotide). A camera test looking at the throat and stomach showed swollen veins in the food pipe that were bleeding heavily. The stomach lining showed damage from high blood pressure in the veins. There were also swollen veins in the stomach. Rubber bands were placed on the bleeding veins to stop the bleeding.  An ultrasound of the belly showed many fluid-filled sacs in the liver caused by a parasite infection.

The patient had sudden, severe bleeding that started again. This caused the body to go into shock that could not be controlled. The blood also lost its ability to clot properly. The patient died after one week.

Impression:

Based on the patient's history, physical exam, and test results, the patient had a parasite infection in the liver and kidney failure. The patient received medicine to reduce stomach acid (omeprazole) and medicine to slow bleeding (octreotide) to try to control the bleeding. However, the patient had sudden, severe bleeding that started again. This caused shock that could not be controlled and the blood lost its ability to clot. The patient died.

input:
Hospital Course:
A 24-year-old male was admitted to the emergency department due to multiple gunshot wounds. Physical examination showed that the patient was hemodynamically stable, and no additional findings were noted. 
Further workup including CT scans led to the discovery of a bullet in the right ventricle. Cardiothoracic surgery and cardiology were consulted, and an attempt at endovascular retrieval was made. However, due to the bullet being embedded in the right ventricular trabeculations, a median sternotomy was performed with cardiopulmonary bypass and cardioplegic arrest to successfully remove the bullet. The patient was subsequently transferred to the ICU for further observation. 
On the sixth postoperative day, the patient developed a fever, and further imaging revealed a large pericardial effusion and moderate right pleural effusion with right lower lobe collapse. Pericardiocentesis was performed, and a drain was left in place to evacuate the effusion. The patient's condition continued to improve, and he was stable for transfer to a comprehensive rehabilitation center on the fifteenth postoperative day.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
A 24-year-old male came to the emergency room with several gunshot wounds. The exam showed that the patient had stable blood pressure and heart rate. No other problems were found.
More tests including CT scans found a bullet inside the right side of the heart. Heart surgeons and heart doctors were called in. They first tried to remove the bullet through a blood vessel. This did not work because the bullet was stuck in the muscle ridges inside the heart. The doctors then opened the chest through the breastbone. They used a heart-lung machine to take over for the heart. They gave medicine to stop the heart safely. The bullet was removed successfully. The patient was then moved to the intensive care unit to be watched closely.
On the sixth day after surgery, the patient got a fever. More scans showed a large buildup of fluid around the heart. There was also some fluid around the right lung. Part of the lower right lung had also collapsed. Doctors used a needle to drain the fluid from around the heart. A small tube was left in place to keep draining the fluid. The patient kept getting better. On the fifteenth day after surgery, the patient was well enough to move to a center for recovery and physical therapy.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Age: 59
Admission date: [Redacted]
Discharge Date: [Redacted]
Final Diagnosis: COVID-19

Medical History:
The patient had no significant medical history.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with symptoms of cough with phlegm, runny nose, fever, and shortness of breath. A CXR was performed, and the results showed bilateral consolidation in the basal lung. The patient was diagnosed with COVID-19 and bronchial asthma and received intravenous ceftazidime 1 gr and intravenous levofloxacin 500 mg during treatment. Symptomatic treatment with medication for asthma was also administered.

Clinical Course:
The patient's symptoms significantly improved during hospitalization. The patient remained afebrile, and shortness of breath was significantly reduced. The patient's cough and runny nose also improved.

Follow-up:
The patient was advised to self-isolate at home and to continue monitoring her symptoms. Follow-up care was coordinated with the patient's primary care physician for further management.

Signed,
[Redacted]

input:
Discharge Summary:

This is a 48-year-old female patient, who was admitted to our hospital for surgical treatment of a recurrence of squamous cell carcinoma on the left mandible body. She underwent a wide excision and left mandibulectomy followed by a mandible, mouth floor, and tongue reconstruction with a titanium implant and an anterolateral thigh (ALT) free flap. The ALT flap was harvested from the ipsilateral thigh and then anastomosed to the lingual artery and the internal jugular vein. After surgery, the patient was observed to have an acceptable overall facial shape and was capable of mastication.

The patient received adjuvant radiation and chemotherapy due to the recurrence of the disease after previous surgery. The patient did not have any suspicious tumor recurrence on the follow-up CT.

The designed implant was manufactured using an A1 metal 3D printer with a biocompatibility material that was composed of titanium, aluminum, and vanadium. The prosthesis appeared well positioned on the radiograph taken after surgery.

The patient was discharged from the hospital and advised to continue follow-up care with the multidisciplinary team. Further information and recommendations for follow-up care were discussed with the patient and their family.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

This is a 48-year-old female patient. She came to the hospital for surgery because her skin cancer on the left side of her lower jawbone had come back. The cancer type is called squamous cell carcinoma. The doctors removed the cancer and part of her jawbone. Then they rebuilt her jawbone, mouth floor, and tongue. They used a metal implant made of titanium and tissue taken from her thigh. The tissue from her thigh was connected to a blood vessel near the tongue and a large vein in the neck. After surgery, her face looked good and she could chew food.

The patient had radiation treatment and cancer-fighting drugs because the cancer came back after her first surgery. A follow-up scan showed no signs that the cancer had returned.

The metal implant was made using a 3D printer. It was made from a safe material containing titanium, aluminum, and vanadium. An x-ray taken after surgery showed the implant was in the right place.

The patient left the hospital. She was told to keep seeing her team of doctors for follow-up care. The doctors talked with the patient and her family about what to do next and when to come back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Not Disclosed
Gender: Female
Age: 80

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with a history of a slow-growing, painless cyst in the right lateral orbital region, which was evaluated through mechanical ptosis, proptosis, and slight hypotropia. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed a round mass with no enhancement by gadolinium, and computed tomography (CT) presented with deformation of the right orbit with bone erosion centering on the frontosphenoidal suture and confirmed the diagnosis of a dermoid cyst. The patient declined surgical removal with boney reconstruction and continued with periodic aspiration. Sclerotherapy was performed after obtaining informed consent and aspirating the fluid multiple times. The first sclerotherapy was performed with 10 mL of 1% lidocaine containing 1:100,000 epinephrine and 10 mL of absolute ethanol was instilled, and the cavity was washed again with 10 mL of saline. Second and third sclerotherapies were performed at intervals of 3 months. After the final sclerotherapy, the cyst had shrunk in size, and no re-expansion was seen during a 1-year follow-up after the last sclerotherapy. The patient complained of visual field disturbance due to redundant skin, and a blepharoplasty was performed concomitant with incisional biopsy of the cyst. Histopathological examination showed that the specimen comprised dermis with well-developed sweat and sebaceous glands, compatible with a dermoid cyst.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient's dermoid cyst was treated successfully with sclerotherapy and incisional biopsy. At the time of discharge, the patient demonstrated no evidence of a recurring cyst.

Follow-Up Plan:
The patient will continue follow-up care with her primary care physician near her home residence and is advised to inquire if any new symptoms arise.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Not Disclosed
Gender: Female
Age: 80

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with a slow-growing, painless fluid-filled sac near the outer right eye area. This sac had been there for some time. It caused the eyelid to droop, the eye to bulge forward slightly, and the eye to sit a bit lower than normal. A special scan using magnets (MRI) showed a round growth that did not absorb the contrast dye. Another scan using X-rays (CT) showed changes to the eye socket bones. The bone had worn away near where two skull bones meet. These tests confirmed the growth was a dermoid cyst, a type of harmless sac present from birth. The patient chose not to have surgery to remove the cyst and repair the bone. Instead, the patient had the fluid drained from the cyst several times. After agreeing to treatment, the patient had a procedure to shrink the cyst using a special solution. The first treatment used a numbing medicine mixed with a drug to reduce bleeding. Then pure alcohol was put into the cyst to shrink it. The area was then rinsed with salt water. The patient had two more treatments three months apart. After the last treatment, the cyst became smaller. It stayed small for one year with no signs of growing back. The patient said the loose skin was blocking part of her vision. The doctors did eyelid surgery to remove extra skin. At the same time, they took a small sample of the cyst to study. Lab tests showed the sample contained skin tissue with sweat and oil glands. This confirmed it was a dermoid cyst.

Condition at Discharge:
The treatment to shrink the cyst and the tissue sample test were successful. When the patient left the hospital, there were no signs that the cyst was coming back.

Follow-Up Plan:
The patient will keep seeing her regular doctor near her home for check-ups. The patient should contact her doctor if any new problems come up.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 11 years old
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Diagnosis: Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF-1) with Lisch nodules

Hospital Course: 
An 11-year-old male with NF-1 was admitted to the Retina Clinic at Uijeongbu St. Mary's Hospital for an ophthalmological examination. The patient had no relatives with the same disease and had multiple cutaneous neurofibromas over the body, although these were only systemic manifestations. Visual acuity was 1.0 in both eyes, and the microscopic examination showed Lisch nodules in both eyes. The fundus and FA showed no definite abnormalities in either eye. However, the ICGA showed multiple areas with clear borders in the early phase, which remained small or disappeared completely in the late phase. 

Throughout the hospital course, the patient received appropriate medical care and monitoring from a healthcare team of physicians, nurses, and specialists. The patient's condition remained stable, and the final diagnosis of NF-1 with Lisch nodules was confirmed based on clinical and diagnostic findings.

Discharge Condition: 
The patient was discharged in stable condition, and the discharge diagnosis was documented as NF-1 with Lisch nodules. The patient was given appropriate medical advice regarding future monitoring and care and instructed to follow up with his healthcare provider as needed. 

Disposition: 
The patient was discharged from the Retina Clinic at Uijeongbu St. Mary's Hospital and referred to his primary healthcare provider for continued monitoring and follow-up care.

Follow-up: 
The patient was advised to follow up with his healthcare provider for monitoring and further care of his NF-1 with Lisch nodules. The patient was instructed to seek immediate medical attention if he experiences any changes in his vision or other symptoms. 

Signed, 
[REDACTED]
Physician

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Male
Age: 11 years old
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Diagnosis: Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF-1), a genetic condition that causes growths on nerves, with Lisch nodules (small brown spots on the colored part of the eye)

Hospital Course:
An 11-year-old boy with NF-1 came to the Eye Clinic at Uijeongbu St. Mary's Hospital for an eye exam. No one else in the patient's family has this condition. The patient had many small soft bumps on the skin all over the body. These bumps were the only signs of the condition. The patient's vision was normal in both eyes. A close look at the eyes showed small brown spots on the colored part of both eyes. Pictures of the back of the eyes looked normal. However, a special dye test showed some small areas in both eyes. These areas stayed small or went away during the test.

During the hospital stay, the patient received proper care from doctors, nurses, and other health experts. The patient stayed in good health throughout the visit. The doctors confirmed the patient has NF-1 with Lisch nodules based on the exam and test results.

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital in good health. The final diagnosis was NF-1 with Lisch nodules. The patient received advice about future check-ups and care. The patient was told to see a doctor for follow-up visits as needed.

Disposition:
The patient left the Eye Clinic at Uijeongbu St. Mary's Hospital. The patient was sent back to the regular doctor for ongoing check-ups and care.

Follow-up:
The patient should see a doctor regularly to watch for any changes related to NF-1 with Lisch nodules. The patient should get medical help right away if there are any changes in vision or other new symptoms.

Signed,
[REDACTED]
Physician

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 56
Sex: Male
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Chest pain

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 56-year-old male electrician, was admitted to the hospital with chest pain that had lasted for about an hour. The patient had a history of treated hypertension, occasional atypical chest pain, and negative ischaemia induced by stress through exercise stress testing conducted two years ago. On admission, the patient had tachypnoea at rest, and his oxygen saturation was 93%. The ECG showed atrial fibrillation with a heart rate of 90 beats/min, and blood pressure was 175/95 mm Hg.

A cardioversion with DC shock (3 J/Kg body weight) was performed, considering the slightly elevated cardiac enzymes of troponin T and creatine kinase and to exclude coronary disease. Subsequently, a coronary angiography (CAG) was performed, and a suspect of single coronary vessel originating from the right sinus of Valsalva was revealed. The right coronary artery (RCA) showed normal course, no stenoses were present, and no atherosclerotic plaque was found on the MSCT scan.

The patient's left ventricular wall motion and ejection fraction were normal (ejection fraction EF 60%). The patient was discharged with only antihypertensive drug therapy. At the 2-month follow-up, he reported no angina and no exertion dyspnea, and his left ventricular ejection fraction was still normal. The patient was advised to avoid strenuous work (under 6 METs) and work on overhead lines too.

Hospital Course Summary:
The 56-year-old male electrician was admitted to the hospital with chest pain for about an hour. After performing cardioversion and CAG, a multi-slices computed tomography (MSCT) revealed a suspect of single coronary vessel originating from the right sinus of Valsalva, with no stenoses or atherosclerotic plaque and with normal left ventricular wall motion and ejection fraction. The patient was discharged with only antihypertensive drug therapy in stable condition. At the 2-month follow-up, his left ventricular ejection fraction remained normal, and he reported no chest pain or exertion dyspnea. The patient was advised to avoid strenuous work (under 6 METs) and work on overhead lines too.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 56
Sex: Male
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Chest pain

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 56-year-old male electrician, came to the hospital with chest pain that lasted about an hour. The patient had a history of high blood pressure that was being treated. The patient also had some chest pain in the past that did not follow a typical pattern. A heart stress test done two years ago did not show any problems with blood flow to the heart. When the patient arrived, he was breathing fast while resting. His blood oxygen level was 93 percent. A heart tracing test showed an irregular heartbeat at 90 beats per minute. His blood pressure was 175/95.

The doctors used an electric shock to help reset the heart rhythm back to normal. This was done because blood tests showed slightly high levels of heart enzymes called troponin T and creatine kinase. These tests help check for heart damage. The doctors also wanted to rule out blocked heart arteries. Next, a test was done to look at the heart arteries using dye and X-rays. This test showed that the patient may have an unusual heart artery setup. It looked like a single artery was coming from a part of the main blood vessel called the right sinus of Valsalva. The right heart artery looked normal with no blockages. A special scan also showed no fatty buildup in the arteries.

The pumping ability of the heart was normal at 60 percent. The patient was sent home with only blood pressure medicine. At a check-up two months later, the patient had no chest pain. The patient also had no trouble breathing during activity. The heart pumping ability was still normal. The patient was told to avoid heavy physical work and working on overhead power lines.

Hospital Course Summary:
The 56-year-old male electrician came to the hospital with chest pain that lasted about an hour. The doctors used an electric shock to reset the heart rhythm. A dye test and a special scan were done to look at the heart arteries. The tests showed that the patient may have an unusual heart artery setup with a single artery coming from a part of the main blood vessel. There were no blockages or fatty buildup in the arteries. The heart pumping ability was normal. The patient was sent home in stable condition with only blood pressure medicine. At a check-up two months later, the heart pumping ability was still normal. The patient had no chest pain or trouble breathing during activity. The patient was told to avoid heavy physical work and working on overhead power lines.

input:
Hospital Course:
The 76-year-old male patient presented with visual impairment of his right eye 50 years after receiving penetrating keratoplasty (PKP). This followed an explosion injury that resulted in bilateral corneal opacities before the 1960s trauma. The patient reported seeing his ophthalmologist annually for follow-up examinations for 50 years following his first PKP, and control examinations were conducted in recent years due to primary open angle glaucoma. Cataract surgery was performed externally, 42 years after PKP in the right eye, and six years following surgery, he reported a deterioration of his vision. Follow-up examinations were conducted regularly by his ophthalmologist, with depositing in the periphery of the graft being described 48 years after the first PKP. Upon slit-lamp examination, the graft diameters of the former grafts were 6 mm with a central corneal thickness of 584 µm in the right eye and 544 µm in the left eye. The recipient cornea was cloudy, and gray with an endothelial cell count of 1162 c/mm2 in the right eye and 1320 c/mm2 in the left eye. As a result of progressive visual impairment and discomfort caused by the blurred vision, a repeated PKP was offered on the worse right eye. The patient accepted, and surgery was performed without any complications. The excised primary corneal graft with a rim of the recipient cornea was sent for histopathological analysis.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The 76-year-old male patient came in because of vision problems in his right eye. He had a cornea transplant in that eye 50 years ago. Before that surgery in the 1960s, an explosion had damaged both of his corneas and made them cloudy. The patient saw his eye doctor every year for 50 years after his first cornea transplant. In recent years, he also had checkups for a type of eye pressure problem called glaucoma. He had cataract surgery done at another location 42 years after his cornea transplant in the right eye. Six years after the cataract surgery, he noticed his vision getting worse. His eye doctor continued to see him regularly. About 48 years after the first cornea transplant, the doctor noticed buildup forming at the edges of the transplanted tissue. During an eye exam using a special microscope, the doctor measured the transplanted tissue in both eyes. The transplant was 6 mm wide in each eye. The center of the cornea was 584 micrometers thick in the right eye and 544 micrometers thick in the left eye. The original cornea around the transplant looked cloudy and gray. The layer of cells lining the inside of the cornea showed 1162 cells per square millimeter in the right eye and 1320 cells per square millimeter in the left eye. Because the patient's vision kept getting worse and the blurry vision was bothering him, the doctors suggested another cornea transplant on the right eye since it was worse. The patient agreed, and the surgery went well with no problems. The doctors removed the old transplanted cornea along with a small ring of the surrounding tissue. This tissue was sent to a lab to be studied under a microscope.

input:
Hospital Course:

Patient is a 29-year-old female who was admitted at 21 weeks postpartum due to symptoms of diarrhea mixed with blood and abdominal pain. Colonoscopy revealed diffuse inflammation from the rectum to the proximal descending colon with multiple yellow-white color spots consistent with findings of UC. Sulfasalazine was prescribed and the patient's symptoms improved, leading to her discharge after 2 weeks hospitalization. Soon after discharge, sulfasalazine was replaced by mesalazine and the patient resumed breastfeeding.

At 27 weeks postpartum, the patient was admitted for 12 days of educational hospitalization for UC. During this time, a lacto-ovo-semi-vegetarian diet was provided and the patient received guidance on a plant-based diet. Colonoscopy at the end of the hospitalization revealed endoscopic remission of UC. The patient's mesalazine dose was gradually decreased to 0.4 g/day on discharge.

During an interview and questionnaire on lifestyle and dietary habits, the patient reported a dietary change during the postpartum period. The patient stayed at her parent's home for about 3 months after delivery, during which her mother prepared traditional Japanese Washoku meals recommended by the midwife. After returning home, the patient reported a decline in dietary quality and a tendency towards constipation and epigastric distress. This dietary change was assessed by plant-based diet score (PBDS).

Outcome:

The patient was discharged in stable condition and advised to continue a plant-based diet. The patient stopped taking her medication 3 weeks after discharge and remained in remission without medication to the present.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 29-year-old woman. She was admitted to the hospital 21 weeks after giving birth. She had diarrhea with blood and stomach pain. A test using a camera to look inside the large intestine was done. It showed swelling and irritation from the rectum to part of the colon. There were also many yellow-white spots. These findings matched a bowel disease called ulcerative colitis. The patient was given a medicine called sulfasalazine. Her symptoms got better. She left the hospital after 2 weeks. Soon after leaving, her medicine was changed to mesalazine. The patient was then able to breastfeed again.

At 27 weeks after giving birth, the patient came back to the hospital. She stayed for 12 days to learn about managing her bowel disease. During this time, she was given meals based mostly on plants, eggs, and dairy. She also received teaching about eating a plant-based diet. A camera test of the large intestine was done at the end of the stay. It showed the bowel disease was no longer active. The patient's mesalazine dose was slowly lowered to 0.4 grams per day when she left.

During a talk and survey about lifestyle and eating habits, the patient shared some information. She said her diet changed after having her baby. She stayed at her parents' home for about 3 months after delivery. Her mother made traditional Japanese meals as suggested by the midwife. After going back to her own home, the patient said she ate less healthy foods. She also had trouble with bowel movements and upper stomach discomfort. Her eating habits were measured using a plant-based diet score.

Outcome:

The patient left the hospital in stable condition. She was told to keep eating a plant-based diet. The patient stopped taking her medicine 3 weeks after leaving the hospital. She has stayed well without medicine up to now.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 58-year-old male with a history of esophageal adenocarcinoma with bilateral adrenal gland metastasis who presented with a scalp mass and was diagnosed with a metastatic skull tumor with brain compression. He underwent surgical intervention and was discharged in stable condition. The patient was instructed to follow up with his oncologist for further treatment and management. 

Chief Complaint:
Patient presented with a rapidly enlarging scalp mass for 5 months.

History of Present Illness:
Physical examinations revealed a firm, fixed, non-tender, 8-cm mass over the midline frontal area behind the hairline. Computed tomography revealed an enlarging skull mass with osteoblastic characteristics in the frontal area along the midline. Gadolinium-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging showed a heterogeneously enhanced biconvex mass. The mass had an extracranial extension and intracranial compression over the left and right frontal lobes and superior sagittal sinus.

Past Medical History:
The patient had a history of esophageal adenocarcinoma with bilateral adrenal gland metastasis.

Physical Examination:
Physical examinations revealed a firm, fixed, non-tender, 8-cm mass over the midline frontal area behind the hairline.

Diagnostic Studies:
Computed tomography revealed an enlarging skull mass with osteoblastic characteristics in the frontal area along the midline. Gadolinium-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging showed a heterogeneously enhanced biconvex mass. 

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent surgical intervention and was discharged in stable condition.

Follow-up Plans:
Recommendations for follow-up with an oncologist for further treatment and management.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 58-year-old male with a history of cancer of the food pipe that has spread to both adrenal glands near the kidneys. He came to the hospital with a lump on his scalp. Doctors found that the cancer had spread to his skull and was pressing on his brain. He had surgery to treat this and left the hospital in stable condition. The patient was told to see his cancer doctor for more treatment and care.

Chief Complaint:
The patient came in because of a lump on his scalp that had been growing quickly for 5 months.

History of Present Illness:
The doctor's exam found a hard lump about 3 inches wide on the front of the head behind the hairline. The lump did not move and did not hurt when touched. A CT scan showed a growing bone mass in the front of the skull. An MRI scan with contrast dye showed a lens-shaped mass. The mass was growing outward through the skull and pressing inward on both sides of the front of the brain. It was also pressing on a major blood vessel in the brain.

Past Medical History:
The patient had cancer of the food pipe that spread to both adrenal glands near the kidneys.

Physical Examination:
The doctor's exam found a hard lump about 3 inches wide on the front of the head behind the hairline. The lump did not move and did not hurt when touched.

Diagnostic Studies:
A CT scan showed a growing bone mass in the front of the skull. An MRI scan with contrast dye showed a lens-shaped mass.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery and left the hospital in stable condition.

Follow-up Plans:
The patient should see his cancer doctor for more treatment and care.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 23
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient presented to the outpatient department with pain and swelling in the left side of the face following a road traffic accident. The patient was subjected to an ophthalmological examination, and the results revealed no abnormality. Computerized tomography suggested a fracture to the inferior wall of the left orbit which allowed the herniation/protrusion of infraorbital content into the left maxillary sinus. Accordingly, the patient underwent left orbital floor reconstruction using the right conchal cartilage. The patient's post-operative period was uneventful, and she was discharged after recovery.

DIAGNOSIS:

Fracture of the inferior wall of the left orbit with herniation of infraorbital content into the left maxillary sinus.

PROCEDURE:

1. Left orbital floor reconstruction using the right conchal cartilage.

CONDITION ON DISCHARGE:

The patient's condition improved significantly, and she was discharged from the hospital.

FOLLOW-UP:

Follow-up was done both clinically and radiographically, and the patient showed no signs of infection in the operated site. Computed tomography (CT) scan was done after 22 months to confirm the proper positioning of the soft tissues within the orbit.

RECOMMENDATIONS:

The patient is advised to follow-up with the surgical department if any concerns or complications arise following the discharge.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Age: 23
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient came to the clinic with pain and swelling on the left side of her face after a car accident. The patient had an eye exam, and the results showed no problems. A special scan showed a break in the bone under the left eye. This break caused tissue to push down into the air space in the cheekbone. The patient had surgery to rebuild the bone under the left eye using cartilage from the right ear. The patient healed well after surgery and was sent home after recovery.

DIAGNOSIS:

Break in the bone under the left eye with tissue pushing into the air space in the cheekbone.

PROCEDURE:

1. Surgery to rebuild the bone under the left eye using cartilage from the right ear.

CONDITION ON DISCHARGE:

The patient got much better and was sent home from the hospital.

FOLLOW-UP:

The patient was checked in person and with scans. There were no signs of infection where the surgery was done. A scan was done 22 months later to make sure the tissue under the eye was in the right place.

RECOMMENDATIONS:

The patient should contact the surgery department if she has any concerns or problems after leaving the hospital.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Medical History:
The patient was a 26-year-old female who had been diagnosed with colon cancer six years prior. The patient presented to the hospital for routine control.

Hospital Course:
Upon examination, the patient had no clinical complaints and no apparent physical findings. Further diagnostic evaluation included a contrast-enhanced CCT scan, which identified a perihilar mass with a smooth contour and homogeneous internal structure. Multiple mediastinal lymph nodes were also identified. The patient underwent a positron emission tomography with 2-deoxy-2-[fluorine-18] fluoro- D-glucose integrated with computed tomography (18F-FDG PET/CT), which showed slightly increased 18F-FDG uptake in the identified mass. In addition, the patient underwent magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the thorax, which showed a lesion that was isointense with muscle in T1-weighted images, hyperintense in T2-weighted images, and enhanced homogeneously in post-contrast series. Based on the imaging findings and patient history, the following differential diagnoses were considered: metastasis, primary lung cancer, lymphoma, and sarcoidosis.

Results:
In laboratory tests, it was found that the patient's IgG4 value was elevated to 723 mg/dL (135mg/dL).

Diagnosis:
A surgical lung biopsy was performed, and the diagnosis was reported as IgG4-related respiratory disease (IgG4-RRD).

Condition at Discharge:
At the time of discharge, the patient's condition was stable. The patient was provided with information regarding the diagnosis and advised to follow up with their primary care physician for continued management and care.

Follow-Up Care:
Follow-up care was recommended with the primary care physician for continued monitoring and management of the patient's condition.

Summary:
The patient, a 26-year-old female with a history of colon cancer, presented to the hospital for routine control. Diagnostic evaluation identified a perihilar mass with multiple mediastinal lymph nodes, and further testing revealed an elevated IgG4 value. A surgical lung biopsy was performed, and the diagnosis was reported as IgG4-RRD. The patient was advised to follow up with their primary care physician for continued management and care.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Medical History:
The patient was a 26-year-old female who had been told she had colon cancer six years earlier. The patient came to the hospital for a regular checkup.

Hospital Course:
During the exam, the patient had no health concerns and no visible signs of illness. More tests were done, including a special scan using dye called a CCT scan. This scan found a growth near the center of the chest. The growth had smooth edges and looked the same throughout. Several swollen lymph nodes were also found in the chest area. The patient then had a PET/CT scan. This scan uses a small amount of a sugar-like substance to find active cells in the body. The scan showed the growth was slightly more active than normal tissue. The patient also had an MRI scan of the chest area. This scan uses magnets to create pictures of the inside of the body. The MRI showed a growth that looked a certain way on different types of images. Based on the scan results and the patient's health history, doctors thought the growth could be one of several things. These included cancer that spread from another part of the body, lung cancer, a blood cancer called lymphoma, or a condition called sarcoidosis.

Results:
Blood tests showed that the patient had a high level of a protein called IgG4. The level was 723, which is much higher than the normal level of 135.

Diagnosis:
Doctors did surgery to take a small piece of lung tissue to test. The test showed the patient had a disease called IgG4-related lung disease. This is a condition where the immune system causes swelling in the lungs.

Condition at Discharge:
When the patient left the hospital, the patient was in stable condition. The patient was given information about the diagnosis. The patient was told to see their regular doctor for ongoing care.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient should see their regular doctor for continued checkups and care of this condition.

Summary:
The patient, a 26-year-old female with a history of colon cancer, came to the hospital for a regular checkup. Tests found a growth near the center of the chest with several swollen lymph nodes. Blood tests showed a high level of a protein called IgG4. Doctors did surgery to take a small piece of lung tissue to test. The test showed the patient had IgG4-related lung disease. The patient was told to see their regular doctor for ongoing care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: Female, 72 years old
Date of Admission: (Insert date of admission)
Date of Discharge: (Insert date of discharge)

Chief Complaint:
Low back pain and radicular pain in right leg leading to walking disability.

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with a history of low back pain and radicular pain in her right leg causing difficulty in walking. She underwent posterior spinal fusion from T9 to the sacrum using intrasacral screws and interbody fusion at L3/4, L4/5, and L5/S disc levels. The duration of surgery was 7 hours and 51 minutes with an estimated blood loss of 1271 mL. Autologous transfusion was performed, and allogenic transfusion was not required. There were no major intraoperative complications.

Consultations:
The patient was evaluated by the surgical team preoperatively, and follow-up evaluations were done postoperatively by the same team.

Diagnostic and Therapeutic Intervention:
Posterior spinal fusion was performed using intrasacral screws coupled with interbody fusion at L3/4, L4/5, and L5/S levels. The patient received standing rehabilitation on a tilt table from the fifth day after surgery, and walking was allowed with the use of a thoracolumbosacral orthosis beginning on the twentieth postoperative day.

Clinical Progress and Condition:
The patient’s symptoms of low back pain and radicular pain improved after the surgery. The patient could walk for more than 10 minutes with the use of a cane 1 year after the surgery. Spinal fusion was confirmed, but slight loosening of the intrasacral screws was observed by computed tomography at follow-up.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition with recommendations for follow-up visits as prescribed by the surgical team. 

Instructions and Medications:
N/A

Disposition:
The patient was discharged home.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: Female, 72 years old
Date of Admission: (Insert date of admission)
Date of Discharge: (Insert date of discharge)

Main Health Problem:
Lower back pain and shooting pain down the right leg that made walking very hard.

What Happened During the Hospital Stay:
The patient came to the hospital because of lower back pain and shooting pain in her right leg. This pain made it hard for her to walk. She had surgery to join bones in her spine together. The surgery connected bones from the middle back down to the base of the spine. Metal screws were placed in the lower spine. Bone spacers were also placed between the bones in the lower back. The surgery took 7 hours and 51 minutes. The patient lost about 1271 mL of blood during surgery. The patient received her own blood back during the surgery. She did not need blood from a donor. There were no major problems during the surgery.

Doctor Visits:
The surgery team checked on the patient before the surgery. The same team also checked on her after the surgery.

Treatments and Therapy:
The patient had spine surgery to join bones together using metal screws and bone spacers. On the fifth day after surgery, the patient started standing up using a tilting table. On the twentieth day after surgery, the patient was allowed to start walking. She wore a back brace that supported her middle and lower back.

How the Patient Got Better:
The lower back pain and shooting leg pain got better after surgery. One year after surgery, the patient could walk for more than 10 minutes using a cane. Tests showed the spine bones had joined together. However, the scans also showed the screws at the base of the spine had loosened a little.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition. She should go to follow-up visits as the surgery team told her.

Instructions and Medications:
None given.

Where the Patient Went:
The patient went home.

The surgery connected bones from the middle back down to the base of the spine. Metal screws were placed in the lower spine. Bone spacers were also placed between the bones in the lower back. The surgery took 7 hours and 51 minutes. The patient lost about 1271 mL of blood during surgery. The patient received her own blood back during the surgery. She did not need blood from a donor. There were no major problems during the surgery.

Doctor Visits:
The surgery team checked on the patient before the surgery. The same team also checked on her after the surgery.

Treatments and Therapy:
The patient had spine surgery to join bones together using metal screws and bone spacers. On the fifth day after surgery, the patient started standing up using a tilting table. On the twentieth day after surgery, the patient was allowed to start walking. She wore a back brace that supported her middle and lower back.

How the Patient Got Better:
The lower back pain and shooting leg pain got better after surgery. One year after surgery, the patient could walk for more than 10 minutes using a cane. Tests showed the spine bones had joined together. However, the scans also showed the screws at the base of the spine had loosened a little.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition. She should go to follow-up visits as the surgery team told her.

Instructions and Medications:
None given.

Where the Patient Went:
The patient went home.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Patient: 15-year-old girl
- Diagnosis: Congenital Hyperinsulinism (CHI)
- Treatment: Diazoxide, Octreotide, Lanreotide

Hospital Course Summary:
- Patient with CHI was admitted electively to our pediatric inpatient unit to decide on an alternative medical treatment due to the troublesome hypertrichosis caused by diazoxide.
- Trial off diazoxide was performed whereby, diazoxide was gradually weaned and stopped for 72 hours. However, this led to subsequent development of recurrent symptomatic hypoglycaemia.
- Octreotide (a somatostatin analogue) was then commenced as four times daily subcutaneous injection.
- A good glycemic response was noted at this dose and she was able to tolerate a fast for a period of 24 hours with no evidence of hypoglycaemia.
- Lanreotide (long-acting somatostatin analogue) was commenced as a subcutaneous injection 30 mg once monthly and octreotide was gradually weaned and stopped.
- Continuous blood glucose monitoring system following the administration of lanreotide revealed good glycemic control with no episodes of hypoglycemia.
- Patient developed recurrent symptomatic hypoglycemic episodes after starting fluoxetine. Fluoxetine was discontinued and hypoglycemic episodes resolved within a week.
- Two weeks after the discontinuation of fluoxetine, the patient's depressive symptoms slowly improved over time with the help of counselling.

Final Diagnosis:
- Congenital Hyperinsulinism (CHI)

Follow-up Plan:
- No further specific follow-up needed at this time.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Patient: 15-year-old girl
Diagnosis: Congenital Hyperinsulinism (CHI), a condition where the body makes too much insulin, causing low blood sugar
Treatment: Diazoxide, Octreotide, Lanreotide

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient with CHI came to the children's hospital unit for a planned stay. The goal was to find a different medicine because diazoxide was causing unwanted hair growth.
The care team slowly lowered and then stopped diazoxide for 72 hours to see what would happen. However, this caused repeated episodes of low blood sugar with symptoms.
Octreotide was then started as a shot under the skin four times a day. This medicine helps control blood sugar.
The patient responded well to this dose. She was able to go without food for 24 hours with no low blood sugar.
Lanreotide was then started as a shot under the skin, 30 mg once a month. This is a long-acting medicine similar to octreotide. Octreotide was slowly lowered and then stopped.
A device that checks blood sugar all the time showed good blood sugar control after starting lanreotide. There were no episodes of low blood sugar.
The patient started having repeated low blood sugar episodes with symptoms after starting fluoxetine. Fluoxetine was stopped and the low blood sugar episodes went away within a week.
Two weeks after stopping fluoxetine, the patient's sad mood slowly got better over time with the help of talk therapy.

Final Diagnosis:
Congenital Hyperinsulinism (CHI)

Follow-up Plan:
No further specific follow-up needed at this time.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: Uterine perforation with IUD migration

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 45-year-old female without any notable medical history, was admitted to our hospital due to aggravated intermittent abdominal pain and dyspareunia for the past two months. On examination, she showed tenderness in the left accessory area, and the laboratory test revealed leucocytosis. The transvaginal ultrasound revealed two IUDs, one inside and one outside the cavity, and the abdominal computerised tomography scan confirmed the migration of the second IUD, which was closely related to the rectum.

Diagnosis and Management:
The patient was diagnosed with uterine perforation with IUD migration, and surgical removal was planned after anti-inflammatory treatment. Laparoscopic surgery was performed successfully, and the IUD that was embedded in the rectal wall was removed. The IUD within the cavity was also removed through hysteroscopy. The postoperative course was uneventful, and the patient was discharged after one month of follow-up when she was asymptomatic.

Conditions on Discharge:
The patient was discharged without any complications, and she was advised to continue follow-up with her healthcare provider.

Follow-up Care:
The patient will need to schedule follow-up appointments with her healthcare provider for routine health check-ups and any further treatment.

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Instructions:
The patient was advised to avoid any strenuous activities for two weeks after discharge, and to seek medical attention if any unusual symptoms occur. She was also advised to inform her healthcare provider of any changes in her health or if she experiences any new symptoms.

Signed:

[Redacted]
MD
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: A birth control device (IUD) poked through the wall of the uterus and moved to another part of the body

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 45-year-old woman with no past health problems. She came to the hospital because of belly pain that came and went. She also had pain during sex. These problems had been getting worse for two months. During the exam, she had pain on the left side of her lower belly. A blood test showed her white blood cell count was high, which can mean infection. An ultrasound done through the vagina found two birth control devices. One was inside the uterus where it should be. The other had moved outside the uterus. A CT scan of the belly confirmed this. The second device had moved and was very close to the rectum, which is the lower part of the bowel.

Diagnosis and Management:
The patient was diagnosed with a hole in the uterus wall caused by a birth control device that moved out of place. The care team planned to remove it with surgery after treating the swelling first. The surgery was done using small cuts and a tiny camera. The doctors removed the device that was stuck in the wall of the rectum. They also removed the device inside the uterus using a small camera placed through the vagina. The patient healed well after surgery with no problems. She came back for a check-up one month later. She had no symptoms and was sent home.

Conditions on Discharge:
The patient went home with no problems from the surgery. She was told to keep seeing her doctor for follow-up care.

Follow-up Care:
The patient needs to make appointments with her doctor for regular health check-ups. She should also see her doctor if she needs any more treatment.

Discharge Medications:
None

Instructions:
The patient was told not to do hard exercise or heavy lifting for two weeks after leaving the hospital. She should get medical help right away if she notices anything unusual. She should also tell her doctor about any changes in her health or any new symptoms.

Signed:

[Redacted]
MD

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Name: ____
- Age: 52 years
- Gender: Male
- Medical record number: ____
- Admission date: ____
- Discharge date: ____

Hospital Course:
- The patient presented with continuous nonpsychotic unipolar major depressive disorder of 10 years duration and had a history of receiving adequate treatment trials of various antidepressants. However, he did not experience significant relief from symptoms despite being compliant with medications.
- Venlafaxine and mirtazapine were prescribed, but they did not provide significant improvement in the patient's condition.
- Ketamine was administered intramuscularly, which improved the patient's mood. However, he also developed manic symptoms after the third injection, which led to the discontinuation of ketamine.
- Lithium and paroxetine extended-release were then prescribed, which helped relieve the patient's manic symptoms and re-emerging depressive symptoms.

Diagnosis:
- Continuous nonpsychotic unipolar major depressive disorder.

Treatment:
- Venlafaxine and mirtazapine were prescribed, which were later discontinued.
- Ketamine was administered intramuscularly, and lithium and paroxetine were prescribed.

Outcome:
- The patient experienced relief from depressive symptoms with the initial treatment but developed manic symptoms after ketamine administration.
- The patient's manic symptoms resolved within 16-18 days of discontinuing ketamine, and treatment with lithium and paroxetine helped relieve both manic and re-emerging depressive symptoms.

Follow-up:
- The patient should continue taking lithium and paroxetine as prescribed.
- The patient should follow up with his primary care physician or mental health professional as needed.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: ____
Age: 52 years
Gender: Male
Medical record number: ____
Admission date: ____
Discharge date: ____

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with ongoing serious depression that had lasted 10 years. The patient had tried many depression medicines in the past. None of these medicines helped enough, even though the patient took them as directed.
The patient was given venlafaxine and mirtazapine. These medicines did not help much.
The patient then received ketamine shots into the muscle. This improved the patient's mood. But after the third shot, the patient had mood swings that were too high. Because of this, the ketamine was stopped.
The patient was then given lithium and a long-acting form of paroxetine. These medicines helped calm the high mood swings. They also helped with the depression that came back.

Diagnosis:
Ongoing serious depression without breaks from reality.

Treatment:
Venlafaxine and mirtazapine were given at first but later stopped.
Ketamine shots were given into the muscle. Lithium and paroxetine were also given.

Outcome:
The patient felt better from depression with the first treatment. But the patient had mood swings that were too high after getting ketamine.
The high mood swings went away within 16 to 18 days after stopping ketamine. Lithium and paroxetine helped with both the high mood swings and the depression that came back.

Follow-up:
The patient should keep taking lithium and paroxetine as directed.
The patient should see a regular doctor or mental health doctor as needed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

A 44-year-old Caucasian male was admitted to the emergency department after being trapped under the transmission system of a vehicle for an unknown amount of time, causing trauma to his chest and lungs. On admission, the patient had gasping breath, palpable regular pulses, and low blood pressure. He was urgently intubated and put on controlled mechanical ventilation due to his altered consciousness and respiratory distress. Upon examination, the patient's chest and upper body were congested, edematous, and covered with petechiae. Bilateral pneumothorax was detected, and bloody fluid was drained from his lungs through the endotracheal tube. The patient's condition improved after bilateral thoracostomies were inserted, draining air and blood. Arterial blood gases showed a pH of 7.246 and high levels of enzymes associated with muscle breakdown. A CT scan revealed mild brain edema and bilateral hemopneumothorax with lung contusions.

The patient was transferred to the ICU, where he was ventilated with volume-control mode, with a gas mixture that was quickly tapered to a fraction of inspired oxygen of 40%. Care was taken to prevent renal complications from potential traumatic rhabdomyolysis, and special attention was given to the brain edema with mannitol administration and frequent neurologic assessments. The patient improved quickly and was extubated on the second day of hospitalization, but remained disoriented and agitated until the fourth day. His facial and thoracic petechiae gradually faded within the next three days. The patient's enzymes returned to normal levels on the seventh day, and he was transferred to the thoracic surgical ward.

Recommendations:

The patient is advised to continue with follow-up appointments with his healthcare provider to monitor his progress. He should avoid activities that may place excessive stress on his chest and lungs, and he should report any changes in his symptoms to his healthcare provider immediately. The patient should also continue to take any medications as prescribed and follow a healthy lifestyle to aid in his recovery. 

For further medical attention, the patient is advised to contact the healthcare provider immediately should he experience any of the following symptoms: chest pain, shortness of breath, difficulty breathing, fever, or cough with abnormal sputum production. 

Signed, 

[REDACTED]
Physician
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

A 44-year-old white male came to the emergency room after being trapped under a car part for an unknown amount of time. This caused injury to his chest and lungs. When he arrived, the patient was gasping for air, had a steady heartbeat, but had low blood pressure. Doctors quickly placed a breathing tube and connected him to a breathing machine. This was needed because he was confused and having trouble breathing. His chest and upper body were swollen, full of blood, and covered with tiny red spots from broken blood vessels. Both lungs had collapsed, and bloody fluid was removed from his lungs through the breathing tube. The patient got better after doctors placed tubes in both sides of his chest to drain air and blood. Blood tests showed his blood was more acidic than normal and had high levels of proteins released from damaged muscles. A body scan showed mild brain swelling and blood and air around both lungs with bruised lung tissue.

The patient was moved to the intensive care unit. He was kept on the breathing machine with settings that were slowly reduced. The oxygen level in his breathing air was lowered to 40 percent. The care team worked to protect his kidneys from damage caused by injured muscles releasing harmful substances. They also watched his brain swelling closely by giving medicine to reduce fluid and checking his brain function often. The patient got better quickly. The breathing tube was removed on day two, but he stayed confused and restless until day four. The tiny red spots on his face and chest slowly went away over the next three days. His muscle protein levels returned to normal on day seven. He was then moved to the chest surgery ward.

Recommendations:

The patient should keep all follow-up visits with his doctor to check on his healing. He should avoid activities that put too much strain on his chest and lungs. He should tell his doctor right away if his symptoms change. The patient should keep taking all medicines as directed. He should also follow healthy habits to help his body heal.

The patient should call his doctor right away if he has any of these warning signs: chest pain, feeling short of breath, trouble breathing, fever, or coughing up unusual mucus.

Signed,

[REDACTED]
Physician

input:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 60

Gender: Male

Hospital Admission Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Clinical History:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with signs and symptoms of peritonitis. A laparotomy was conducted, and two perforations were found in the jejunum. Resection and anastomoses was done.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent surgery to remove the perforations and underwent a histopathological examination. The examination revealed an intense mixed inflammatory infiltrate in the mucosa with an edematous submucosa containing round, walled, intact structures of E. vermicularis with a “T” shaped reproductive system []. The serosal surface was covered with exudative membrane comprising of serofibrinous material with numerous eosinophils, polymorphs and red blood cells along with numerous rounded structures of the parasite with chitinous wall. Unfortunately, the patient passed away a few days postoperatively.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient passed away during the hospital stay.

Follow-Up:
N/A (Patient deceased)

Physician:
[REDACTED]

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 60

Gender: Male

Hospital Admission Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Clinical History:
The patient came to the hospital with a serious infection in the belly area. Doctors did surgery to open the belly and look inside. They found two holes in the small intestine. The doctors removed the damaged parts and reconnected the healthy parts.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery to fix the holes in the intestine. Doctors sent tissue samples to a lab for testing. The lab tests showed a serious infection caused by pinworms. These tiny parasites had spread through the intestine wall. The outer lining of the intestine was covered with fluid and signs of infection. The infection included many white blood cells and red blood cells. Sadly, the patient died a few days after the surgery.

Condition at Discharge:
The patient passed away during the hospital stay.

Follow-Up:
Not needed (Patient deceased)
