input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admitted: A 43-year-old woman with chest pain, night sweats, fevers, and weight loss
Diagnoses: Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (XGP) with coexisting malakoplakia
Treatment: Intravenously administered broad-spectrum antibiotics, laparoscopic total left nephrectomy, oral antibiotics
Outcome: Medically and functionally well, discharged after a 7-day course of oral antibiotics

Hospital course: The patient was admitted with chest pain, fevers, night sweats, and weight loss. After a thorough investigation, the patient was diagnosed with Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (XGP) with coexisting malakoplakia. The patient was administered intravenously administered broad-spectrum antibiotics and underwent a successful laparoscopic total left nephrectomy. Empiric antibiotics were continued for 7 days preoperatively and 7 days postoperatively. The patient was discharged home after completion of a 7-day course of oral antibiotics. The patient has been medically and functionally well for nearly 8 months after hospitalization.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admitted: A 43-year-old woman with chest pain, night sweats, fevers, and weight loss
Diagnoses: A rare kidney infection that causes long-term swelling and damage, along with another condition where unusual cells build up in the kidney
Treatment: Strong infection-fighting medicines given through a vein, surgery to remove the left kidney using small cuts, infection-fighting pills taken by mouth
Outcome: Feeling healthy and doing well, sent home after 7 days of infection-fighting pills taken by mouth

Hospital course: The patient was admitted with chest pain, fevers, night sweats, and weight loss. After careful testing, the patient was found to have a rare kidney infection that causes long-term swelling and damage. The patient also had another condition where unusual cells build up in the kidney. The patient was given strong infection-fighting medicines through a vein. The patient also had surgery to remove the left kidney using small cuts. Infection-fighting medicines were given for 7 days before surgery and 7 days after surgery. The patient was sent home after finishing 7 days of infection-fighting pills taken by mouth. The patient has been feeling healthy and doing well for almost 8 months since leaving the hospital.

input:
Hospital Course:

The 8-year-old female patient presented for evaluation of a slowly growing, plaque-like skin lesion on her left chest wall. The lesion was approximately 0.5 cm in maximum diameter in size and was too small and with a light violet color. Ultrasound examination revealed an ovoid, well-demarcated lesion in the subcutaneous region with dermal involvement and without clear separation from the underlying muscle. The patient underwent surgical excision and biopsy, and the pathology report described the specimen as having a white-gray nodular lesion with spindle cells developed in a storiform to whorled pattern. The differential diagnosis included DFSP and Dermatofibroma. A confident diagnosis of DFSP was made based on immunohistochemical expression of CD34 and negative FXIIIA and S100. 

After the diagnosis, the patient underwent extensive imaging and hematological testing which were without pathological findings. Although the margins of the removed mass were healthy, we decided to perform extended surgical excision to decrease the possibility of recurrence. The child's postoperative course was uneventful, and she will be examined every 6 months for the next 3 years for palpation of her scar tissue and examination of her regional lymph nodes to exclude recurrence or the possibility of metastases.

Impression & Plan:

The patient was diagnosed with DFSP based on biopsy. Extended surgical excision was performed, and the patient will undergo regular follow-up appointments. Further treatment is not required at this time.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The 8-year-old female patient came in for a check on a slow-growing skin bump on her left chest. The bump was about 0.5 cm wide and had a light purple color. A sound wave scan showed a small oval-shaped growth under the skin. The growth reached into the skin layer and was close to the muscle below. The patient had surgery to remove the bump and test it. The test showed the tissue had a gray-white lump with cells arranged in a swirling pattern. Doctors thought it could be one of two types of skin tumors. Tests on the tissue confirmed it was a rare type of skin tumor called DFSP. This tumor grows slowly and rarely spreads to other body parts.

After finding this, the patient had scans and blood tests done. All results came back normal. Even though the edges around the removed bump looked healthy, doctors did a second surgery to remove more tissue. This was done to lower the chance of the tumor coming back. The patient healed well after surgery. She will be checked every 6 months for the next 3 years. Doctors will feel the scar area and check nearby glands to make sure the tumor has not come back or spread.

Impression and Plan:

The patient was found to have a rare slow-growing skin tumor called DFSP after testing the removed tissue. A second surgery was done to remove more tissue around the area. The patient will have regular check-up visits. No other treatment is needed at this time.

Doctors will feel the scar area and check nearby glands to make sure the tumor has not come back or spread.

Impression and Plan:

The patient was found to have a rare slow-growing skin tumor called DFSP after testing the removed tissue. A second surgery was done to remove more tissue around the area. The patient will have regular check-up visits. No other treatment is needed at this time.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]
Date of Admission: [Date]
Date of Discharge: [Date]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient, a 70-year-old male with rheumatoid arthritis, was admitted with complaints of shortness of breath and productive cough. His RA had been controlled well without any symptoms such as arthritis before the presentation, indicating a low activity of the disease. A blood test showed elevated C-reactive protein, Lactate Dehydrogenase, and soluble interleukin-2 receptor levels. EBV antibody tests confirmed prior infection.

Chest computed tomography revealed interstitial pneumonia affecting the bilateral lower lung fields with multiple nodular shadows. Sputum cytology performed showed atypical lymphoid cells with low cell connectivity, a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, foamy cytoplasm, coarsely granular chromatin and distinct nucleoli. Lung biopsy demonstrated atypical mid- to large-sized lymphoid cells with a high N/C ratio, cleaved nuclei and distinct nucleoli. The patient was diagnosed with MTX-LPD (non-germinal center B-cell-like DLBCL histological type).

Bronchoscopy showed protuberating mucosa with vascular proliferation and bleeding tendency. The patient was treated by withdrawing MTX. Most of the nodules disappeared spontaneously about 3 weeks after MTX withdrawal; thereafter, only tacrolimus was used. The patient was observed for a period of 15 months after MTX withdrawal, and there was no progression of the disease.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:

1. MTX-LPD (non-germinal center B-cell-like DLBCL histological type)

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:

1. Continue to take prescribed medications as directed by the primary care physician.
2. Avoid exposure to airborne irritants to prevent respiratory infections.
3. Follow up with regular doctor appointments to monitor health status and medication response.

FOLLOW-UP CARE:

The patient has been referred to a hematologist for ongoing management of lymphoproliferative disorder after MTX withdrawal.

Reviewed and approved by:
[Physician Name], M.D.
[Physician Specialty]
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]
Date of Admission: [Date]
Date of Discharge: [Date]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient is a 70-year-old male with a joint disease called rheumatoid arthritis. He came to the hospital because he had trouble breathing and was coughing up mucus. His joint disease had been well controlled with no joint pain or swelling before this visit. This meant his joint disease was not very active. Blood tests showed high levels of certain markers that point to swelling in the body. Tests also showed the patient had a past infection with a common virus called Epstein-Barr virus.

A scan of the chest showed lung swelling in the lower parts of both lungs with several small round spots. A test of the mucus coughed up from the lungs showed unusual immune cells that looked abnormal. A small piece of lung tissue was removed and tested. It showed unusual medium to large immune cells that looked abnormal. The patient was found to have a condition where the immune cells grow too much because of a medicine called methotrexate. This is a type of blood cancer.

A tube with a camera was used to look inside the airways. It showed swollen tissue with extra blood vessels that bled easily. The patient was treated by stopping the methotrexate medicine. Most of the spots in the lungs went away on their own about 3 weeks after stopping this medicine. After that, only a medicine called tacrolimus was used. The patient was watched for 15 months after stopping methotrexate. The disease did not get worse during this time.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:

1. Immune cell overgrowth caused by methotrexate, a type of blood cancer

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:

1. The patient should keep taking prescribed medicines as told by the main doctor.
2. The patient should stay away from things in the air that can bother the lungs to prevent lung infections.
3. The patient should go to regular doctor visits to check health and how well the medicines are working.

FOLLOW-UP CARE:

The patient has been sent to a blood disease doctor for ongoing care of the immune cell overgrowth after stopping methotrexate.

Reviewed and approved by:
[Physician Name], M.D.
[Physician Specialty]

input:
Patient Information:
The patient is a 28-year-old male who was treated for PEComa in 2006 and 2014. He underwent a lower anterior resection followed by segmental hepatectomy. The patient is currently undergoing regular surveillance with no recurrence of PEComa identified since the primary surgery in 2006.

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the hospital in November 2006 with symptoms of cramping, abdominal pain, and intermittent melena. Physical examination revealed generalized weakness, dizziness, and massive bloody stool passage. Laboratory tests showed an elevated white blood cell count, low hemoglobin and hematocrit levels, and a normal serum carcinoembryonic antigen level. Diagnostic imaging and biopsy identified a transmural tumor in the rectum, which was later diagnosed as metastatic PEComa.

The patient underwent lower anterior resection in 2006, followed by regular surveillance. The distant liver metastasis progressed slowly over the years, which prompted the patient to undergo segmental hepatectomy in 2014. The pathology report confirmed the presence of metastatic PEComa.

Upon the postoperative analysis, the patient remained free of disease even after 28 months of follow-up.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Metastatic PEComa of rectum and liver

Follow-Up:
The patient is currently undergoing regular surveillance without any reported symptoms or signs of PEComa recurrence. The general clinical examination, subsequent colonoscopies, and abdominal CT scans are performed once every 3 months.
clean_ref:
Patient Information:
The patient is a 28-year-old male who was treated for a rare tumor called PEComa in 2006 and 2014. He had surgery to remove part of his rectum and later part of his liver. The patient now gets regular check-ups. No signs of the tumor have come back since the first surgery in 2006.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital in November 2006 with stomach cramps, belly pain, and dark bloody stools that came and went. The exam showed he felt weak and dizzy. He also passed a large amount of bloody stool. Blood tests showed a high number of white blood cells. His red blood cell levels were low. A cancer marker test was normal. Scans and a tissue sample found a tumor that had grown through the wall of the rectum. It was found to be a PEComa tumor that had spread.

The patient had surgery in 2006 to remove part of his rectum. After that, he had regular check-ups. Over the years, the tumor slowly spread to his liver. This led to another surgery in 2014 to remove part of his liver. Tests on the removed tissue confirmed the spreading PEComa tumor.

After the second surgery, the patient showed no signs of disease for 28 months of follow-up visits.

Discharge Diagnosis:
PEComa tumor that spread from the rectum to the liver

Follow-Up:
The patient is getting regular check-ups. He has no symptoms or signs that the tumor has come back. He gets a physical exam, a scope test to look inside the colon, and a belly scan every 3 months.

Medical history:
The patient is a 24-year-old female with mild asthma and a history of atrial septal defect repair in 2007. She has never been hospitalized for asthma and does not require regular treatment for it.

Hospital admission:
The patient presented with vomiting, diarrhoea, malaise, fever, headache, and dyspnoea. On admission (Day 3), a CXR showed extensive right lower lobe and patchy left basal consolidation, and she had a productive cough. Broad-spectrum antibiotics were started on Day 3, and the patient was transferred to the ICU on Day 6. 

ICU stay:
The patient was intubated on Day 6 and remained in the ICU for 11 days, with only one organ failure (lungs).

Final blood sample:
A peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) sample was obtained on Day 34.

Diagnosis:
No explicit diagnosis was mentioned in the case report.

Conclusion:
The patient was discharged after an extended stay in the ICU and a PBMC sample was taken on Day 34. Further information about the patient's condition and treatment is not available in the case report.
clean_ref:
Medical history:
The patient is a 24-year-old female with mild asthma and a heart defect that was fixed with surgery in 2007. She has never been hospitalized for asthma and does not need regular treatment for it.

Hospital admission:
The patient came to the hospital with vomiting, diarrhea, weakness, fever, headache, and trouble breathing. When she arrived on Day 3, a chest X-ray showed infection in parts of both lungs. She also had a cough with mucus. Strong antibiotics were started on Day 3. The patient was moved to the intensive care unit on Day 6.

ICU stay:
The patient was put on a breathing machine on Day 6. She stayed in the intensive care unit for 11 days. Only her lungs were not working properly during this time.

Final blood sample:
A blood sample was taken on Day 34 to study her immune cells.

Diagnosis:
No specific diagnosis was listed in the medical report.

Conclusion:
The patient was sent home after a long stay in the intensive care unit. A blood sample was taken on Day 34. More details about the patient's condition and treatment are not available in the medical report.

Discharge Summary:

Patient: 51-year-old female

Admission Date: February 2013

Discharge Date: October 2015

Diagnosis: Recurrent Ameloblastoma

Procedure: Right mandibular resection and reconstruction with nonvascularized free iliac bone followed by dental implant placement and fixed prosthesis

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the department in February 2013 due to discomfort in the mandible. She had been diagnosed with recurrent ameloblastoma and had undergone multiple fenestration procedures in the past. The patient was dissatisfied with the outcomes of previous hospital's treatment policy and came to our department for the complete resection of the tumor. 
After thorough evaluation and diagnosis, we proposed a treatment protocol of radical resection and immediate bony reconstruction using nonvascularized free iliac bone graft. The patient underwent right mandibular resection (teeth 43 to 46; length 35 mm) in March 2013 and was closely monitored for tumor recurrence. 
Follow-up examinations showed no signs of tumor recurrence, but the patient continued to experience right-sided difficulty in mastication. Therefore, a fixed prosthesis was recommended. In November 2014, the mini-plate was removed from the grafted bone, and four dental implants were placed. Subsequently, the patient underwent vestibuloplasty (vestibular extension with periosteal separation procedures) and confirmed osseointegration in all four implants. The final fixed prosthesis was worn by the patient in October 2015. 
The patient's oral hygiene has been regularly managed, and no signs of tumor recurrence or abnormal bone absorption or disintegration of the dental implants have been observed. The patient was discharged in October 2015.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient is advised to continue maintaining good oral hygiene and to follow the regular maintenance therapy schedule. The patient should keep all appointments with the department for proper monitoring of tumor recurrence and dental implant health. If there are any concerns or questions, the patient should contact the department for further consultations.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 51-year-old female

Admission Date: February 2013

Discharge Date: October 2015

Diagnosis: Returning jaw tumor (ameloblastoma)

Procedure: Surgery to remove part of the right lower jawbone and rebuild it using bone from the hip, followed by placing dental implants and a permanent set of replacement teeth

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the department in February 2013 because of pain in the lower jaw. She had a jaw tumor that kept coming back and had several surgeries before to drain it. The patient was not happy with her care at the other hospital and came to our department to have the tumor fully removed. After a complete checkup, we planned to remove all of the tumor and rebuild the jaw right away using bone from the hip. In March 2013, the patient had surgery to remove part of the right lower jawbone (teeth 43 to 46, about 35 mm long). The care team watched closely to make sure the tumor did not come back. Follow-up visits showed no signs of the tumor returning, but the patient still had trouble chewing on the right side. Because of this, we suggested a permanent set of replacement teeth. In November 2014, the metal plate was taken out of the new bone, and four dental implants were placed. After that, the patient had surgery to create more space in the mouth for the new teeth. All four implants joined well with the bone. The patient received the final set of permanent replacement teeth in October 2015. The patient has kept good mouth care habits. There are no signs of the tumor coming back or problems with the bone or implants. The patient was sent home in October 2015.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient should keep brushing and cleaning the mouth well and follow the regular care schedule. The patient should come to all follow-up visits so the care team can check for any return of the tumor and make sure the implants stay healthy. If the patient has any worries or questions, the patient should call the department for help.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Insert Name]
Age: 29
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [Insert Date]
Date of Discharge: [Insert Date]

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 29-year-old Caucasian male, was admitted to the hospital due to dyspnea, fatigue, edema of the legs, and abdominal pain. The physical examination revealed LV enlargement with congestive hepatomegaly, symmetrical edema of both lower extremities, and orthopnea. The patient also had a grade III/VI systolic murmur at the upper left sternal border, increased jugular venous pressure, and normal heart sounds. The patient's medical imaging revealed cardiomegaly, a dilated and remodeled, spherical LV, with morphological changes, and multiple mobile thrombi located between apical trabeculations. The patient exhibited ventricular tachycardia episodes, with RBBB morphology, frequent PVC, functional mitral regurgitation due to LV remodeling, decreased LV systolic function, and restrictive LV filling pattern. Additional findings included LV diastolic dysfunction, hepatic and renal infarctions, high levels of NT-pBNP, and moderate tricuspid regurgitation. Genetic tests for Fabry disease, as well as for Gaucher and Niemann–Peck diseases, were negative.

During the patient's hospital course, he was treated with anticoagulants, antiarrhythmics, diuretics, beta-blockers, and angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors. After 2 months of anticoagulation and treatment of heart failure, follow-up echocardiographic evaluation detected no thrombi in the apex of the LV between trabeculations, and the patient had no signs of heart failure. A cardioverter defibrillator was implanted for the prevention of sudden cardiac death, and the patient was proposed for cardiac transplantation.

In conclusion, a 29-year-old Caucasian male was admitted to the hospital due to symptoms of LV enlargement with congestive hepatomegaly, symmetrical edema of both lower extremities, and orthopnea. The patient exhibited a restrictive LV filling pattern, hepatic and renal infarctions, and moderate tricuspid regurgitation. Medical management with anticoagulants, antiarrhythmics, diuretics, beta-blockers, and angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors was initiated, and the patient was proposed for cardiac transplantation.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Insert Name]
Age: 29
Gender: Male
Date of Admission: [Insert Date]
Date of Discharge: [Insert Date]

Hospital Stay Summary:

The patient, a 29-year-old white male, was admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath, tiredness, swelling in the legs, and belly pain. The physical exam showed an enlarged left lower heart chamber with liver swelling due to heart backup. The patient had even swelling in both legs and trouble breathing while lying flat. The patient also had a loud heart murmur heard on the upper left chest. The neck veins were swollen, and heart sounds were normal. Heart scans showed an enlarged heart with the left lower chamber stretched and shaped like a ball. There were also several loose blood clots found in the lower part of the heart. The patient had episodes of fast, abnormal heart rhythms. The heart's electrical signals were blocked on the right side. There were frequent extra heartbeats. A heart valve was leaking because the heart chamber had changed shape. The left lower heart chamber was not pumping well and was too stiff when filling with blood. The patient also had damage to the liver and kidneys from blocked blood flow. A blood test showed high levels of a hormone that rises when the heart is stressed. Another heart valve was leaking a medium amount. Genetic tests for Fabry disease, Gaucher disease, and Niemann-Pick disease all came back normal.

During the hospital stay, the patient was treated with blood thinners, heart rhythm medicines, water pills, medicines to slow the heart rate, and medicines to relax blood vessels. After 2 months of blood thinners and heart failure treatment, a follow-up heart ultrasound showed no blood clots in the left lower heart chamber. The patient no longer showed signs of heart failure. A small device was placed in the chest to shock the heart if it stops or beats dangerously fast. The patient was recommended for a heart transplant.

In conclusion, a 29-year-old white male was admitted to the hospital with symptoms of an enlarged left lower heart chamber, liver swelling from heart backup, even swelling in both legs, and trouble breathing while lying flat. The patient had a stiff left lower heart chamber, damage to the liver and kidneys from blocked blood flow, and medium leaking of a heart valve. Treatment was started with blood thinners, heart rhythm medicines, water pills, medicines to slow the heart rate, and medicines to relax blood vessels. The patient was recommended for a heart transplant.

input:
Discharge Summary:
Patient: 39-year-old female

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with complaints of foreign-body sensation and epiphora in her left eye. A slit-lamp examination revealed multiple milky-white soft masses on the corneal surface of her left eye. A slight opacity was suspected in the anterior stroma under the slit-lamp examination. Upon investigation, these masses were classified as having a gelatinous dystrophy-like appearance and were located at the cilia-attached region. An OCT scan revealed a large mass under the thinned epithelial layer with no destruction on Bowman’s layer throughout the region. The fellow cornea exhibited a linear subepithelial opacity that was not stained by fluorescein under a slit-lamp examination, and an OCT scan revealed a high-density spot in Bowman’s layer, which was coincident with the cilia-attached region and linear line observed under slit-lamp examination.

Treatment:
To treat the foreign-body sensation, the patient underwent lamellar keratoplasty. The corneal tissues were excised, and the excised specimens were frozen and cut into sections. These sections were incubated with antilactoferrin antibody and fixed with Congo red. Histological analysis revealed that the eosinophilic material was positively stained with Congo red showing apple-green birefringence under polarized light. The material was also positive when using the antilactoferrin antibody, with this area matching the Congo red-positive region.

Outcome:
Postoperative recovery was smooth, and there was no recurrence of amyloid deposition. Ten months after the operation, the corrected visual acuity of the patient’s left eye was 20/20. Epilation of the cilia is performed regularly.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:
Patient: 39-year-old female

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital because her left eye felt like something was stuck in it. She also had watery eyes. An eye exam using a special microscope showed several soft, milky-white lumps on the clear front part of her left eye. There was also a slight cloudy area just below the surface. Doctors found that these lumps looked like a jelly-like growth. They were located where an eyelash was touching the eye. A special scan of the eye showed a large lump under the thin outer layer. The deeper protective layer of the eye was not damaged. The right eye had a line-shaped cloudy spot under the surface. This spot did not pick up yellow dye during the exam. A scan showed a dense spot in the protective layer. This spot matched where the eyelash touched and where the line appeared during the exam.

Treatment:
To fix the feeling of something stuck in her eye, the patient had surgery to replace part of the clear front layer of her eye. Doctors removed the affected eye tissue. They froze the removed tissue and cut it into thin slices for testing. They used special dyes and markers to study the tissue. The tests showed the material had a specific protein buildup. This buildup glowed apple-green under special light. The tissue also tested positive for a protein called lactoferrin in the same area.

Outcome:
The patient healed well after surgery. The protein buildup did not come back. Ten months after surgery, the patient's left eye vision with glasses was perfect at 20/20. The eyelashes in the problem area are removed regularly.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient presented with a 24-hour history of lower abdominal pain and was diagnosed with a small bowel obstruction in the midabdomen encased by a thickened peritoneum. Conservative treatment was attempted but was unsuccessful, and an exploratory laparotomy was performed. During the procedure, the entire small intestine was found to be encapsulated by a thick membrane forming a cocoon-like lump. The fibrous membrane was excised and the small bowel loops were released. The patient had no postoperative complications and was discharged on postoperative day seven.

Diagnosis:

Small bowel obstruction in the midabdomen encased by a thickened peritoneum due to peritoneal fibrosis.

Follow-Up:

The patient will be monitored for signs of recurrence, but as of the most recent follow-up 28 months after surgery, the patient has shown no signs of recurrence.

Overall Impression:

The patient presented with a rare case of small bowel obstruction due to peritoneal fibrosis which required surgical intervention. The surgery was successful, and the patient had an uneventful postoperative course with no signs of recurrence at the most recent follow-up.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient came in with belly pain in the lower area that had lasted for 24 hours. The patient was found to have a blockage in the small intestine in the middle of the belly. The blockage was wrapped in a thick layer of tissue. The doctors first tried to treat this without surgery, but it did not work. The patient then had surgery to open the belly and look inside. During surgery, the doctors found that the entire small intestine was wrapped in a thick layer of tissue shaped like a cocoon. The doctors cut away this thick tissue and freed the small intestine. The patient healed well after surgery with no problems. The patient left the hospital seven days after the surgery.

Diagnosis:

The patient had a blockage in the small intestine in the middle of the belly. This was caused by thick scar tissue that had formed around the lining of the belly.

Follow-Up:

The doctors will watch the patient for signs that the problem has come back. At the last check-up, 28 months after surgery, the patient showed no signs that the problem had returned.

Overall Impression:

The patient had a rare case of a blocked small intestine caused by thick scar tissue in the belly lining. The patient needed surgery to fix this problem. The surgery went well. The patient healed without any problems. At the most recent check-up, there were no signs that the problem had come back.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

PATIENT INFORMATION:
Name: [insert name]
Gender: Male
Age: 58 years old
Race: Korean
Medical record number: [insert number]
Admitting Diagnosis: Progressive Neuropathy

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient was admitted with complaints of involuntary facial twitching and weakness, loose skin, and a deep furrowed tongue which had been progressively worsening for four years. The symptoms resulted in paresthesia of the right hand, intermittent cramps on the right calf, and decreased sweating on both hands. Neurological exam revealed mild weakness in the orbicularis oculi and orbicularis oris muscles without any motor weakness or sensory changes observed in the four extremities. A family history of the same symptoms was found in his mother, maternal uncle, and two sisters but no non-Korean relatives. Electrophysiological studies, including nerve conduction study (NCS), electromyography (EMG), and autonomic function tests (AFTs), were performed which demonstrated signs of bilateral facial nerve dysfunction, chronic denervation of the cervical and lumbar regions, and sudomotor dysfunction. To identify the etiology of the undiagnosed neuropathy, whole-exome sequencing (WES) was performed following informed consent and it revealed the presence of a p.D214Y mutation in GSN.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Progressive neuropathy with associated mutation in GSN

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
[No medication information was provided in the case report]

FOLLOW-UP RECOMMENDATIONS:
Follow-up with primary care physician recommended.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

PATIENT INFORMATION:
Name: [insert name]
Gender: Male
Age: 58 years old
Race: Korean
Medical record number: [insert number]
Admitting Diagnosis: Nerve damage that is getting worse over time

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came to the hospital with face twitching and weakness that he could not control. He also had loose skin and deep grooves on his tongue. These problems had been getting worse for four years. The symptoms caused tingling in his right hand and muscle cramps in his right calf. He also noticed less sweating on both hands. The doctor checked his nerves and found mild weakness in the muscles around his eyes and mouth. There was no weakness or feeling changes in his arms or legs. His mother, uncle on his mother's side, and two sisters have the same symptoms. No family members who are not Korean have these symptoms. The doctors did tests to check how his nerves and muscles work. These tests showed problems with the nerves on both sides of his face. The tests also showed long-term nerve damage in his neck and lower back areas. There were also problems with his sweat glands. To find the cause of his nerve damage, the doctors did a special blood test to look at his genes. The patient agreed to this test. The test found a change in a gene called GSN.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Nerve damage that is getting worse over time, caused by a change in the GSN gene

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
[No medication information was provided in the case report]

FOLLOW-UP RECOMMENDATIONS:
Follow-up with primary care physician recommended.

input:
Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 70-year-old male with a history of chronic myeloid leukemia, peripheral arterial disease, hypothyroidism, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, high cholesterol, and gout, presented with complaints of altered mental status and abdominal pain. On examination, the patient was found to be hypotensive and tachycardic, and a blocked chronic Foley catheter was noted with purulent drainage. The patient was diagnosed with acute kidney injury, leukocytosis, and lactic acidosis upon initial laboratory testing. CT abdomen pelvis without contrast showed gastric pneumatosis with adjacent left upper quadrant portal venous gas along with branching portal venous gas throughout the liver.

Treatment and Management:
The patient was started on a broad-spectrum antibiotic regimen that included cefepime, vancomycin and meropenem. Blood and urine cultures were sent for analysis. The patient was also administered fluid resuscitation, and vasopressor infusion was initiated when blood pressure didn't improve. The general surgery and gastroenterology teams were consulted; esophagogastroduodenoscopy were performed, which revealed proximal stomach ischemia with some necrosis. Biopsies revealed acute hemorrhagic gastritis secondary to ischemia with a recommendation of surgical resection. Surgical resection was deemed inappropriate as the patient was not an eligible candidate due to multiple comorbidities and was managed conservatively.

Follow Up:
The patient's acute renal failure resolved days later, and repeat CT angiography revealed no apparent issues of concern. Thereafter the patient slowly exhibited clinical improvement and repeat EGD revealed gastritis in the proximal stomach with healthy pink mucosa and no signs of ischemia or necrosis. The patient was gradually resumed on an oral diet and was later transferred to the medical floor upon completion of antibiotic therapy. The patient was then discharged to a long-term acute care facility.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 70-year-old male. He has a history of blood cancer, poor blood flow in the legs, low thyroid, lung disease, high cholesterol, and gout. He came to the hospital because he was confused and had belly pain. His blood pressure was too low and his heart was beating too fast. His bladder tube was blocked and showed signs of infection with pus. Lab tests showed his kidneys were not working well. He also had high white blood cell counts and too much acid in his blood. A scan of his belly showed air in his stomach wall and gas in the blood vessels of his liver.

Treatment and Management:
The patient was given three strong medicines to fight infection. Samples of his blood and urine were sent to check for germs. He was given fluids through a vein. He also needed medicine to raise his blood pressure when fluids did not help enough. Surgery and stomach doctors were asked to help. A camera was used to look inside his stomach. This showed poor blood flow and some dead tissue in the upper stomach. Small tissue samples showed bleeding and damage from poor blood flow. Doctors suggested removing part of the stomach. However, surgery was not safe for this patient because of his many health problems. He was treated without surgery instead.

Follow Up:
The patient's kidney problems got better after a few days. A new scan of his blood vessels showed no new problems. The patient slowly started to get better. Another camera look inside his stomach showed the tissue was now healthy and pink. There were no more signs of poor blood flow or dead tissue. The patient was slowly able to start eating again by mouth. He was moved to a regular hospital room after finishing all his infection medicines. The patient was then sent to a care facility for longer recovery.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A

Chief Complaint:
Accidental injection of EpiPen

Hospital Course Summary:

The 5-year-old male patient presented to the pediatric ED after accidentally injecting himself in the lower shin with a relative's EpiPen. Examination under fluoroscopy revealed that the needle was bent underneath the patient's skin. After administration of 1% lidocaine, attempts to remove the needle were unsuccessful. The proximal end of the needle was cut free from the device and the distal tip was manipulated up, poked through the skin, and removed. Fluoroscopic images of the needle prior to removal, as well as photographs following removal, revealed that the needle was hooked and the tip was split. No further treatment was required, and the patient was discharged.

Diagnostic Findings:
- Fluoroscopic examination revealed a bent needle underneath the patient's skin.

Hospital Course:
- Administration of 1% lidocaine to facilitate removal of the needle.
- The proximal end of the needle was cut free from the device and the distal tip was manipulated up, poked through the skin, and removed.
- No further treatment was required, and the patient was discharged.

Summary:
The patient accidentally injected himself in the lower shin with a relative's EpiPen. The needle was bent underneath the patient's skin and required the administration of lidocaine to facilitate removal. The proximal end of the needle was cut free from the device and the distal tip was manipulated up, poked through the skin, and removed. The needle was hooked and the tip was split. The patient had an uneventful hospital course and was discharged without further treatment.

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A

Chief Complaint:
Accidental injection with an EpiPen

Hospital Course Summary:

The 5-year-old male patient came to the children's emergency room. The patient had accidentally injected himself in the lower leg with a family member's EpiPen. A special X-ray showed the needle was bent under the skin. The doctor gave numbing medicine called lidocaine to reduce pain. The first try to remove the needle did not work. The doctor cut the top part of the needle free from the pen. Then the doctor pushed the bottom tip up through the skin and removed it. The X-ray and photos showed the needle was curved with a split tip. The patient did not need any more treatment. The patient was sent home.

A special X-ray showed a bent needle under the skin.

Hospital Course:
The doctor gave numbing medicine called lidocaine to help remove the needle. The top part of the needle was cut free from the pen. The bottom tip was pushed up through the skin and removed. The patient did not need any more treatment. The patient was sent home.

Summary:

The patient did well during the hospital stay. The patient was sent home without needing more treatment.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]

Patient Name: [name]
Age: 68 years
Sex: Male

Medical History:
- Prostate cancer treated with radiation therapy, 3 years ago
- Endoscopic polypectomy for colonic polyps, 1 year ago
- Chronic constipation

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with sudden-onset upper abdominal pain and was found to have a fist-sized mass lesion located between the stomach and pancreas on computed tomography (CT) without contrast. He was initially diagnosed with a malignant tumor with peritonitis carcinomatosa and hospitalized for further examinations and observation. However, the next day, he was found to have intra-abdominal hemorrhage and was transferred to our hospital, a tertiary emergency center, for further diagnosis and treatments.

On arrival at our hospital, the patient was alert and well oriented. A physical examination revealed moderate tenderness in his left upper abdomen with a peritoneal sign. His laboratory data showed mild leukocytosis, moderate anemia, elevated urea nitrogen level, and high serum amylase. The results of a CT with contrast demonstrated an unenhanced, high-density, homogeneous, mass-like lesion located to the ventral side of the pancreatic tail, and a large amount of ascites, which suggested a hematoma and massive intraabdominal hemorrhage. 

An emergency exploratory laparotomy was performed and a 5 cm laceration was found in the mesentery of transverse colon, which arose from the left margin of Treitz's ligament extending in the left direction. Hemostasis was achieved by suture ligations and absorbable hemostat materials were applied on the oozing points. Intraoperative blood transfusion was performed, and the operation was uncomplicated except for temporary paralytic ileus. The patient was discharged in a good postoperative condition on the postoperative day 20.

During the patient's hospital stay, we discovered that he had a history of intractable constipation and had a habit of massaging his abdomen to facilitate defecation, which likely caused the laceration. We advised the patient to avoid massaging his abdomen in the future and to seek medical attention if he experiences any abdominal symptoms.

In summary, the patient with a medical history of prostate cancer, endoscopic polypectomy, and chronic constipation presented with sudden-onset upper abdominal pain and was found to have intra-abdominal hemorrhage due to a mesenteric laceration caused by self-massage for defecation. An emergency exploratory laparotomy was performed to achieve hemostasis, and the operation was uncomplicated except for temporary paralytic ileus. The patient was discharged on the postoperative day 20 with instructions to avoid abdominal self-massage and seek medical attention for abdominal symptoms in the future.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]

Patient Name: [name]
Age: 68 years
Sex: Male

Medical History:
Prostate cancer treated with radiation therapy, 3 years ago
Removal of growths from the large intestine using a small camera tube, 1 year ago
Long-term difficulty having bowel movements

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with sudden pain in the upper belly. A scan showed a large lump between the stomach and pancreas. Doctors first thought it might be cancer that had spread, causing swelling in the belly lining. The patient was admitted for more tests and watching. The next day, doctors found bleeding inside the belly. The patient was moved to our hospital for emergency care.

When the patient arrived at our hospital, he was awake and thinking clearly. A physical exam showed pain in the left upper belly when pressed. The belly lining also showed signs of irritation. Blood tests showed a slightly high white blood cell count and low red blood cell count. Tests also showed high levels of a waste product in the blood and a digestive enzyme. A scan with dye showed a solid lump near the tail of the pancreas. There was also a large amount of fluid in the belly. This pointed to a blood clot and heavy bleeding inside the belly.

The patient needed emergency surgery to open and check the belly. Doctors found a 2-inch tear in the tissue that holds the large intestine in place. This tear started near where the small intestine meets the large intestine and went to the left. Doctors stopped the bleeding with stitches and special materials that help blood clot. The patient received blood during surgery. The surgery went well, but the bowels stopped moving for a short time afterward. The patient left the hospital in good health 20 days after surgery.

While in the hospital, doctors learned that the patient had ongoing trouble having bowel movements. The patient had been pressing on his belly to help pass stool. This likely caused the tear. Doctors told the patient to stop pressing on his belly. They also said to get medical help right away if he has any belly problems.

In summary, the patient has a history of prostate cancer, removal of growths from the large intestine, and long-term trouble having bowel movements. He came in with sudden upper belly pain. Doctors found bleeding inside the belly from a tear caused by pressing on his belly to help pass stool. The patient had emergency surgery to stop the bleeding. The surgery went well, but the bowels stopped moving for a short time afterward. The patient left the hospital 20 days after surgery. He was told not to press on his belly and to get medical help for any belly problems in the future.

input:
Medical History:
The patient is a 24-year-old male with severe Hemophilia A. He has been receiving Factor VIII replacement therapy three times a week but reported missing the last three doses.

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with left medial elbow pain and swelling, along with pain and paresthesias in the ring and small fingers. On examination, there was moderate swelling and tenderness to palpation over the medial epicondyle. Sensation was decreased over the small and ring fingers in the ulnar nerve distribution with intrinsic strength decreased to 4/5 on the MRC grading scale. There was a positive Tinel’s sign at the elbow. The patient was administered Factor VIII and admitted for observation. Following day, the symptoms worsened, and he was taken to the operating room for surgical decompression. The procedure involved decompression of the ulnar nerve at several sites around the elbow. There were no neuroma noted or obvious constriction deformity as seen with chronic compression. 

Discharge Condition: 
The patient was discharged from the hospital and followed up by hematology for maintenance of his Factor VIII levels.

Follow-up Plan: 
At the 6-week follow-up, the patient subjectively reported doing well without activity restrictions, with his paresthesias having completely resolved, and grip strength returned to full 5/5. 

Additional Information:
Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for which identifying information is included in this article.
clean_ref:
Medical History:
The patient is a 24-year-old male with a serious bleeding disorder called Hemophilia A. This means his blood does not clot normally. He has been getting medicine called Factor VIII three times a week to help his blood clot. He reported missing his last three doses.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with pain and swelling on the inner side of his left elbow. He also had pain and tingling in his ring finger and pinky finger. During the exam, the doctor found moderate swelling and tenderness on the inner elbow. The patient had less feeling in his pinky and ring fingers. These fingers are controlled by a nerve called the ulnar nerve. His hand strength was slightly weaker than normal. Tapping on the nerve at the elbow caused tingling, which is a sign of nerve irritation. The patient was given Factor VIII medicine and stayed in the hospital for monitoring. The next day, his symptoms got worse. He was taken to surgery to relieve pressure on the nerve. The surgeon released the trapped nerve at several spots around the elbow. There were no nerve growths or signs of long-term nerve damage.

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital. The blood disorder team followed up with him to keep his Factor VIII levels in a healthy range.

Follow-up Plan:
At the 6-week checkup, the patient said he was doing well. He had no limits on his activities. His tingling had gone away completely. His grip strength was back to full normal.

Additional Information:
The patient gave written permission to share his information in this article.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Attending Physician: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: West syndrome

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 2-month-old male who previously underwent surgical correction for intraventricular defect, was admitted to the hospital after presenting frequent episodes of inconsolable crying, associated with neck extension, stiffening of the arms and trunk, sometimes followed by swallowing and vomiting. Symptoms were attributed to GERD, and therapy with PPI started, however, no improvement was noticed.

In the following weeks, episodes became even more frequents with exacerbation of symptoms. His mother video-recorded an attack, which showed the presence of flexion spasms. The child eventually underwent fundoplication, but no improvement was noticed. Persistence of symptoms and occurrence of spasms of the arms during acute episodes finally suggested the diagnosis of West syndrome.

IS was then confirmed by EEG while MRI was negative. Despite starting appropriate therapy at 8 months of age, clinical signs of mental retardation were present. Although IS is a rare disorder, diagnosis was suspected based on age and patient history with a complete neurological evaluation.

To our knowledge, this is the first report of IS misdiagnosed as GERD. The attention towards GERD in children may reduce attention towards other peculiar clinical conditions, like in cases we presented. We would suggest considering GERD only in those patients with high risk rate for developing GERD like children with cerebral palsy, in those with surgery complications after oesophageal atresia and/or in any child with very evocative GERD symptoms, such as hematemesis and or chronic vomiting with growth failure.

Discharge Diagnosis: West syndrome

Final Condition at Discharge:
At the time of discharge, the patient continued to experience symptoms of West syndrome, but appropriate therapy was initiated. The patient's family was instructed to follow up with the pediatricians for medication management and further evaluations.

Discharge Medications:
Not Applicable

Follow-Up:
The parents were advised to follow up with a pediatrician for routine well-child visits and to monitor the patient's condition closely. Additional appointments were scheduled with a neurologist for further evaluation and management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Attending Physician: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: West syndrome (a type of seizure disorder in infants)

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 2-month-old baby boy. He had heart surgery before this hospital stay to fix a hole between the lower chambers of his heart. He came to the hospital because he was crying a lot and could not be calmed. During these episodes, his neck would stretch back. His arms and body would become stiff. Sometimes he would swallow and throw up afterward. Doctors first thought the problem was acid reflux. They started medicine for acid reflux, but it did not help.

Over the next few weeks, the episodes happened more often and got worse. The patient's mother recorded a video of one episode. The video showed the patient having bending spasms. The patient then had surgery to help stop acid from coming back up. This surgery did not help either. The symptoms kept happening. The patient also had arm spasms during episodes. These signs finally pointed to West syndrome.

A brain wave test confirmed West syndrome. A brain scan showed no problems. The right treatment started when the patient was 8 months old. By then, there were already signs of developmental delays. West syndrome is rare. Doctors suspected it based on the patient's age and medical history after a full brain and nerve exam.

This is the first known case where West syndrome was mistaken for acid reflux. Focusing too much on acid reflux in children may cause doctors to miss other conditions. Doctors should consider acid reflux mainly in children at higher risk. This includes children with brain conditions that affect movement. It also includes children who had surgery for problems with their food pipe. Acid reflux should also be considered when symptoms clearly point to it. These symptoms include throwing up blood or long-term vomiting with poor growth.

Discharge Diagnosis: West syndrome (a type of seizure disorder in infants)

Final Condition at Discharge:
When the patient left the hospital, he still had symptoms of West syndrome. The right treatment had been started. The family was told to see the children's doctors. They will help manage medicines and do more tests.

Discharge Medications:
Not Applicable

Follow-Up:
The parents were told to see a children's doctor for regular checkups. They should watch the patient's condition closely. The patient also has appointments with a brain and nerve doctor for more tests and care.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Attending Physician: [redacted]

Admission Diagnosis: West syndrome (a type of seizure disorder in infants)

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 2-month-old baby boy. He had heart surgery before this hospital stay to fix a hole between the lower chambers of his heart. He came to the hospital because he was crying a lot and could not be calmed. During these episodes, his neck would stretch back. His arms and body would become stiff. Sometimes he would swallow and throw up afterward. Doctors first thought the problem was acid reflux. They started medicine for acid reflux, but it did not help.

Over the next few weeks, the episodes happened more often and got worse. The patient's mother recorded a video of one episode. The video showed the patient having bending spasms. The patient then had surgery to help stop acid from coming back up. This surgery did not help either. The symptoms kept happening. The patient also had arm spasms during episodes. These signs finally pointed to West syndrome.

A brain wave test confirmed West syndrome. A brain scan showed no problems. The right treatment started when the patient was 8 months old. By then, there were already signs of developmental delays. West syndrome is rare. Doctors suspected it based on the patient's age and medical history after a full brain and nerve exam.

This is the first known case where West syndrome was mistaken for acid reflux. Focusing too much on acid reflux in children may cause doctors to miss other conditions. Doctors should consider acid reflux mainly in children at higher risk. This includes children with brain conditions that affect movement. It also includes children who had surgery for problems with their food pipe. Acid reflux should also be considered when symptoms clearly point to it. These symptoms include throwing up blood or long-term vomiting with poor growth.

Discharge Diagnosis: West syndrome (a type of seizure disorder in infants)

Final Condition at Discharge:
When the patient left the hospital, he still had symptoms of West syndrome. The right treatment had been started. The family was told to see the children's doctors. They will help manage medicines and do more tests.

Discharge Medications:
Not Applicable

Follow-Up:
The parents were told to see a children's doctor for regular checkups. They should watch the patient's condition closely. The patient also has appointments with a brain and nerve doctor for more tests and care.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted after being diagnosed with cerebral autosomal dominant arteriopathy with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy (CADASIL). The patient presented with a history of vertigo, migraine headaches, numbness and tingling in her hands and feet, memory loss, fatigue, hypersomnolence, anxiety, and depression. Upon admission, a brain MRI was performed which showed worsening of the white matter hyperintensity with the involvement of bilateral temporal and external capsules.

Treatment:

The patient was not started on any treatment during her initial presentation and her symptoms resolved after a few months. The patient later developed migraine headaches refractory to typical migraine medications, but no treatment was given at that time. The patient was diagnosed with multiple sclerosis by a general neurologist and was referred to an MS clinic where a repeated MRI showed the involvement of bilateral temporal and external capsules leading to a diagnosis of CADASIL. 

Consultations:

The patient received a consultation with a neuroimmunologist, and eventually, the diagnosis was changed based on the new MRI findings and a positive NOTCH3 genetic test.

Follow Up:

The patient was advised to follow up with a neurologist for further management of their CADASIL. 

Discharge:

The patient was discharged from the hospital with a diagnosis of CADASIL and instructions to follow up with a neurologist. There were no complications during their hospital stay.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted after being diagnosed with a rare genetic brain disease called CADASIL. This disease affects blood vessels in the brain. The patient came in with a history of dizziness, severe headaches, numbness and tingling in the hands and feet, memory loss, tiredness, sleeping too much, anxiety, and depression. After being admitted, a brain scan was done. The scan showed more damage to the white matter in the brain, including areas on both sides of the brain.

Treatment:

The patient did not receive any treatment during the first visit. The symptoms went away after a few months. Later, the patient developed severe headaches that did not get better with usual headache medicines. No treatment was given at that time. A brain doctor first thought the patient had multiple sclerosis. The patient was sent to a special clinic. A new brain scan showed damage in specific areas on both sides of the brain. This led to a diagnosis of CADASIL.

Consultations:

The patient saw a brain specialist who focuses on the immune system. The diagnosis was changed based on the new brain scan results. A genetic test for the NOTCH3 gene came back positive, which confirmed the diagnosis.

Follow Up:

The patient was told to see a brain doctor for ongoing care of their CADASIL.

Discharge:

The patient left the hospital with a diagnosis of CADASIL. The patient was told to follow up with a brain doctor. There were no problems during the hospital stay.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 52-year-old multiparous African American woman

Diagnosis: Urethral diverticulum

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted after presenting with syncope following a 2-day history of heavy vaginal bleeding, passing large clots, and suprapubic pain. Pelvic examination revealed blood clots in the vagina along with a friable, fibrous ulcerated lesion on the anterior suburethral vagina. A computed tomography scan revealed a soft tissue mass within the lumen of the vagina and an enlarged lymph node in the right ovarian chain. On hospital day 2, the patient underwent an examination under anesthesia with repeat biopsies, which revealed non-diagnostic pathology.

During HDs 3 through 5, the patient’s bleeding decreased significantly, and on HD 6, a second examination under anesthesia revealed biopsies negative for malignancy. A Tonsil clamp was placed into the urethra, and the urology service was intraoperatively consulted, confirming the diagnosis of urethral diverticulum. The diverticulum was excised, and a multilayered repair over the Foley catheter was performed.

On postoperative day 9, the patient re-presented with bleeding, and several rejected sutures in the necrotic tissue were removed. Interrupted mattress sutures were placed to reapproximate the tissue. Final pathology revealed benign squamous mucosa.

Treatment:

Excision of urethral diverticulum and multilayered repair over the Foley catheter.

Follow-up:

In the urology clinic 2 weeks later, a voiding cystourethrogram was performed, revealing no extravasation from the urethra or bladder. A physical examination revealed proper vaginal healing and no defects.

Disposition:

Discharged in stable condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 52-year-old African American woman who has had multiple pregnancies

Diagnosis: Urethral diverticulum (a pouch or sac that forms in the tube that carries urine out of the body)

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted after she fainted. She had heavy vaginal bleeding for 2 days before admission. She was passing large blood clots and had pain in her lower belly. A pelvic exam showed blood clots in the vagina. There was also a raw, damaged area on the vaginal wall near the urine tube. A CT scan (a special X-ray) showed a growth inside the vagina. It also showed a swollen lymph node near the right ovary. On day 2 in the hospital, the patient had a procedure while asleep. Doctors took tissue samples, but the results did not show a clear answer.

During days 3 through 5, the patient's bleeding slowed down a lot. On day 6, doctors did another procedure while the patient was asleep. The tissue samples showed no cancer. Doctors used a special clamp in the urine tube. The urology team (doctors who treat bladder and urine problems) was called in during the procedure. They confirmed the patient had a pouch in the urine tube. The pouch was removed. Doctors then repaired the area in layers around a urine drainage tube.

On day 9 after surgery, the patient came back with bleeding. Some stitches had come loose in the damaged tissue. These were removed. New stitches were placed to close the tissue. The final tissue tests showed the tissue was not cancerous.

Treatment:

Removal of the pouch in the urine tube and repair in layers around a urine drainage tube.

Follow-up:

The patient visited the urology clinic 2 weeks later. A special X-ray test was done to check the urine tube and bladder. It showed no leaking. A physical exam showed the vagina was healing well with no problems.

Disposition:

The patient left the hospital in stable condition.

input:
Hospital Course:
A 66-year-old female patient was admitted to the Department of Periodontics, M. S. Ramaiah Dental College and Hospital, Bangalore, with the complaint of burning sensation in the gums and bleeding gums for the past one month.

Medical History:
The patient had no relevant drug history or significant medical history. She had not received any dental treatment before.

Examination Findings:
On extra oral examination, the patient had some obvious skin lesions on the scalp and a hyperkeratotic plaque behind her left ear. Intra oral examination of gingiva revealed reddish marginal, attached gingiva and interdental papilla in both maxillary and mandibular anterior region with desquamation of epithelium present in certain regions.

Diagnosis:
Based on the clinical and histopathologic features, the lesion was suggestive of discoid lupus erythematosus.

Treatment:
Hand scaling was performed as gently as possible in order to minimize tissue disruption. Soft tooth brush and mouth rise (chlorhexidine 0.12%) was recommended. Triamcinolone acteonide oral paste was advised to apply two to three times per day.

Follow-up:
The patient was recalled for follow-up and maintenance.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
A 66-year-old female patient came to the gum care department at M. S. Ramaiah Dental College and Hospital in Bangalore. She had a burning feeling in her gums and bleeding gums for the past month.

Medical History:
The patient was not taking any medicines and had no major health problems. She had never had dental treatment before.

Examination Findings:
The dentist looked at the outside of the patient's face and body. The patient had some skin patches on her scalp. She also had a thick, rough patch of skin behind her left ear. The dentist then looked inside the patient's mouth. The gums around the upper and lower front teeth were red. This included the gums at the edges, the gums attached to the teeth, and the gums between the teeth. In some areas, the top layer of gum tissue was peeling off.

Diagnosis:
Based on what the dentist saw and lab test results, the patient likely has discoid lupus erythematosus. This is a condition where the body's defense system attacks its own skin and gum tissue.

Treatment:
The dentist gently cleaned the teeth to remove buildup. This was done carefully to avoid hurting the gums. The patient was told to use a soft toothbrush and rinse with chlorhexidine 0.12 percent mouthwash. The patient was also told to apply triamcinolone acetonide paste inside the mouth two to three times per day. This paste helps reduce soreness and swelling.

Follow-up:
The patient was asked to come back for check-up visits and ongoing care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 42 years 
Hospital Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Diagnosis: Ischemic Stroke

Hospital Course:

The patient presented to the emergency room with slurred speech, dizziness, and left-sided weakness which started about two hours before presentation. The patient did not report any cough, fever, shortness of breath, loss of smell, or body aches. On examination, she was hemodynamically stable. In neurological examination, she had slurred speech and weakness in the left upper and lower extremity but normal sensations. The rest of the neurological examination as well as chest, cardiovascular, abdominal, and musculoskeletal examination was normal. 

The emergency CT scan did not show any evidence of major territorial infarction, intracranial hemorrhage, or focal mass lesions. The patient was within the window of tissue plasminogen activator (TPA) for acute stroke and was treated with it. The patient was also screened for COVID-19 on admission as per hospital protocol and the result came back later as positive.

The patient was admitted to the ICU for monitoring and did not have any abnormal electrocardiogram (EKG) rhythm while in ICU. She had full recovery of her neurological symptoms within few hours without any bleeding complications. The patient was subsequently discharged in good health and advised home isolation.

Discharge Diagnosis: Ischemic Stroke with COVID-19

Discharge Instructions: 

The patient is advised to follow safe sleep and nutrition practices and to avoid stress. The patient should be advised not to engage in driving or other actions that require quick reflex. The patient should be advised to follow home isolation and avoid close contact with family members or friends. The patient should also be advised to monitor for any symptoms of COVID-19 and seek medical attention if needed.

Follow-up: The patient should be advised to follow up with the primary care provider or neurologist within one week of discharge.


Dictated and signed by: N/A
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Female
Age: 42 years
Hospital Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Diagnosis: Stroke caused by a blocked blood vessel in the brain

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the emergency room with slurred speech, dizziness, and weakness on the left side of her body. These symptoms started about two hours before she arrived. The patient did not have a cough, fever, trouble breathing, loss of smell, or body aches. When checked, her heart rate and blood pressure were stable. During the brain and nerve exam, she had slurred speech and weakness in her left arm and leg. Her sense of touch was normal. All other exams of her chest, heart, belly, and muscles were normal.

A brain scan done right away did not show any major damage, bleeding, or growths in the brain. The patient arrived early enough to receive a clot-busting medicine for her stroke. The patient was also tested for COVID-19 when she arrived, following hospital rules. The test result came back later and showed she had COVID-19.

The patient was moved to the intensive care unit so doctors could watch her closely. Her heart rhythm stayed normal while she was there. Her brain and nerve symptoms went away completely within a few hours. She did not have any bleeding problems. The patient was sent home in good health and told to stay home away from others.

Discharge Diagnosis: Stroke caused by a blocked blood vessel in the brain with COVID-19

Discharge Instructions:

The patient is advised to get good sleep, eat healthy foods, and avoid stress. The patient should not drive or do other things that need quick reactions. The patient should stay home and avoid close contact with family or friends. The patient should watch for any signs of COVID-19 and get medical help if needed.

Follow-up: The patient should see her regular doctor or brain specialist within one week after leaving the hospital.

Dictated and signed by: N/A

input:
Discharge Summary:

This is a discharge summary for a 50-year-old female patient who presented to our department with an intraoral swelling at the left mandible for six months. The patient underwent surgical excision and reconstruction using a submental island flap, under general anesthesia.

History of Present Illness: The patient reported to our department with swelling and pain in the left mandible region for six months, which grew rapidly after the extraction of lower left back teeth. The patient had no history of fever or difficulty swallowing.

Physical Examination: On extraoral examination, facial asymmetry was noted in the lower left facial region, but there was no evidence of any regional lymphadenopathy. Intraoral clinical evaluation revealed the presence of an expansile mass with anteroposterior as well as buccolingual expansion, extending from the region of the first premolar up to retromolar area, measuring approximately 6x4 cm. The overlying mucosal colour and texture appeared normal.

Diagnostic Tests: A radiographic orthopantomogram revealed a well-defined radiolucent lesion extending from the lower left first premolar to retromolar area. Computed tomography (CT) scans confirmed the presence of aggressive bony resorption of the lower left jaw starting from the premolar region and extending well into the mandibular ramus. Histopathological analysis of a biopsy specimen confirmed an aggressive CGCG.

Hospital Course: The patient underwent surgical excision and reconstruction using a submental island flap, under general anesthesia. The surgical procedure involved extraoral and intraoral approach to excise the lesion completely and a submental island flap for reconstruction. The patient was discharged without any complications.

Follow-up: In subsequent follow-ups, until one year, no evidence of recurrence was observed clinically as well as radiographically. The donor site healed uneventfully with minimal scars, which was present away from aesthetically visible regions of the face.

Summary: A 50-year-old female patient presented with an intraoral swelling at the left mandible for six months and was diagnosed with an aggressive CGCG. The patient underwent surgical excision and reconstruction using a submental island flap, under general anesthesia. The patient was discharged without any complications, and subsequent follow-ups showed no evidence of recurrence.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

This is a discharge summary for a 50-year-old female patient who came to our department with swelling inside the mouth on the left side of the lower jaw for six months. The patient had surgery to remove the growth and rebuild the area using skin and tissue from under the chin. The patient was asleep under general anesthesia during the procedure.

History of Present Illness: The patient came to our department with swelling and pain in the left lower jaw area for six months. The swelling grew quickly after the patient had back teeth removed on the lower left side. The patient did not have a fever or trouble swallowing.

Physical Examination: When looking at the outside of the face, the lower left side looked uneven compared to the right side. There were no swollen lymph nodes in the area. When looking inside the mouth, there was a growing mass that spread from front to back and side to side. It went from the area of the first small back tooth to the area behind the last molar. The mass measured about 6 by 4 centimeters. The skin covering the mass looked normal in color and feel.

Diagnostic Tests: A panoramic jaw x-ray showed a clear dark area going from the lower left first small back tooth to the area behind the last molar. CT scans, which are detailed pictures of the inside of the body, showed the growth was breaking down bone in the lower left jaw. This bone loss went from the small back teeth area into the back part of the jawbone. A small tissue sample was taken and looked at under a microscope. This test confirmed the growth was an aggressive central giant cell granuloma, which is a type of noncancerous but fast-growing jaw tumor.

Hospital Course: The patient had surgery to remove the growth and rebuild the area using skin and tissue from under the chin. The surgery was done from both outside and inside the mouth to remove all of the growth. The patient went home without any problems.

Follow-up: At checkup visits over the next year, there were no signs that the growth came back. This was checked by looking in the mouth and taking x-rays. The area under the chin where tissue was taken healed well with very little scarring. The small scar is not in a part of the face that people easily see.

Summary: A 50-year-old female patient came in with swelling inside the mouth on the left side of the lower jaw for six months. The patient was found to have an aggressive central giant cell granuloma, which is a type of noncancerous but fast-growing jaw tumor. The patient had surgery to remove the growth and rebuild the area using skin and tissue from under the chin. The patient was asleep under general anesthesia during the procedure. The patient went home without any problems. Checkup visits over the next year showed no signs that the growth came back.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient was a 33-year-old female who presented with abdominal pain, diarrhea, and fever. She was admitted and subsequently discharged after 2 days of oral ciprofloxacin treatment. However, she was readmitted two days after discharge due to worsening abdominal pain and other symptoms.

Diagnostic Studies:
The patient had a CT scan which revealed a left partially rim enhancing structure 2x2cm, suggestive of a ruptured corpus luteal cyst. There was minimal free fluid in the pelvis and no evidence of other intra-abdominal pathology.

Treatment:
The patient was treated with IV antibiotics and underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy. Generalized profuse seropurulent fluid was taken out, and a normal appendix was identified. The patient was continued on antibiotics and treated for primary streptococcal peritonitis. Her condition improved with conservative symptomatic management, and she was discharged after 10 days of treatment in the hospital. 

Follow-Up:
The patient showed improvement to repeat blood tests which showed the total whites and CRP had normalized. She reported minor lower abdominal pain which resolved spontaneously after conservative symptomatic management. Follow-up was established with the infectious disease team in clinic.  

Plan:
Continued outpatient oral antibiotics with clindamycin per culture sensitivities. Follow-up with the infectious disease team to monitor improvement and continued outpatient care.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient was a 33-year-old woman who came to the hospital with belly pain, loose stools, and fever. She was admitted and sent home after 2 days of taking an antibiotic pill called ciprofloxacin. She came back to the hospital two days later because her belly pain got worse and she had other problems.

Diagnostic Studies:
The patient had a special body scan. The scan showed a small fluid-filled sac on her left ovary that had burst. It was about 2 centimeters wide. There was a small amount of fluid in her lower belly area. The scan did not show any other problems inside her belly.

Treatment:
The patient received antibiotics through a needle in her vein. Doctors also did a surgery using a small camera to look inside her belly. They removed cloudy infected fluid from inside her belly. They saw that her appendix looked normal. The patient kept taking antibiotics for a serious infection in the lining of her belly caused by strep bacteria. She got better with care to help her symptoms. She went home after 10 days in the hospital.

Follow-Up:
The patient got better based on new blood tests. Her white blood cell count and infection levels went back to normal. She had some mild lower belly pain that went away on its own with basic care. She was set up to see the infection specialist team at the clinic.

Plan:
The patient will keep taking an antibiotic pill called clindamycin at home. This antibiotic was chosen based on lab tests of her infection. She will see the infection specialist team to make sure she keeps getting better and to continue her care.
