input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 23-year-old male with chronic HCV, presented to our care for combination therapy based on the National Committee for Control and Prevention of viral Hepatitis "C" guidelines in Egypt. The therapy regimen included pegylated interferon α 2a 180 ug/week and ribavirin 1000 mg/day. However, at week 12 of therapy, the patient was discontinued due to a lack of early virologic response.

Discharge Summary:
The patient was diagnosed with HCV in a pre-employment checkup 2 years ago. The patient's BMI was 27.5 and had a liver biopsy score of A2 F2 without steatosis. During therapy, the patient's laboratory parameters recorded OBI with HBV DNA values greater than 2000 IU. To treat this activation, lamivudine was administered and continued for 6 months. During this time, the patient continued to be exposed to both HBV and HCV viral load assessment and HBV was undetected, and the HCV RNA level decreased. The patient discontinued lamivudine due to financial constraints and was exposed to HBV and HCV viral load determination 6 months later, where HBV was undetected, and HCV RNA level decreased to 7062 IU/ml.

Overall, the patient's medical history, including HCV and the occurrence of OBI with HBV DNA values over 2000 IU during therapy, was managed effectively with the use of lamivudine. The patient discontinued lamivudine due to financial constraints and was not followed up for HCV RNA levels. No further course of action was taken at the time of the patient's discharge.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 23-year-old male with long-term hepatitis C virus infection. He came for treatment following Egypt's national guidelines for hepatitis C. The treatment plan included two medicines: a weekly injection called pegylated interferon and a daily pill called ribavirin. However, after 12 weeks of treatment, the doctors stopped the therapy. This happened because the virus levels did not drop enough.

Discharge Summary:
The patient found out he had hepatitis C during a job-related health check 2 years ago. The patient's body mass index was 27.5, which is slightly overweight. A liver tissue sample showed moderate liver damage and scarring but no fat buildup. During treatment, blood tests showed a hidden hepatitis B infection became active. The hepatitis B virus levels rose above 2000 units. To treat this, doctors gave the patient a medicine called lamivudine for 6 months. During this time, doctors kept checking both hepatitis B and hepatitis C virus levels. The hepatitis B virus became undetectable and the hepatitis C virus levels went down. The patient stopped taking lamivudine because he could not afford it. Six months later, tests showed hepatitis B was still undetectable. The hepatitis C virus level had dropped to 7062 units per milliliter.

Overall, the patient had hepatitis C and developed an active hepatitis B infection during treatment. The lamivudine medicine worked well to control the hepatitis B. The patient stopped lamivudine because of money problems. After stopping, no one checked the patient's hepatitis C virus levels. No additional treatment steps were planned when the patient left the hospital.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Admission Date: [Admission Date]
Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Diagnosis: Follicular Lymphoma

Hospital Course:

A 72-year-old man presented with an asymptomatic nontender abdominal mass and was found to have a bulky mesenteric mass with a maximum standardized uptake value on PET/CT. Excisional biopsy confirmed the diagnosis of Follicular Lymphoma, which was treated with bendamustine plus rituximab. The patient was followed up on an outpatient basis and has had no recurrent disease to date.

Medical History:

The patient did not have any significant past medical history.

Discussion:

Primary mesenteric tumors are rare, and Follicular Lymphoma is the most common type. The imaging patterns of mesenteric lymphoma at CT can vary, and while the sandwich sign may suggest malignant tumors, its diagnostic performance is unknown. Although the disease may be indolent, vigilance is necessary to ensure early diagnosis. In case of a solid mesenteric tumor, malignant lymphoma should be considered as a priority.

Recommendations:

The patient is advised to continue follow-up with the oncologist and promptly report any symptoms or complications. 

Primary Care Physician:
[Name]
[Contact Information]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [MRN]

Admission Date: [Admission Date]
Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Diagnosis: Follicular Lymphoma (a slow-growing cancer of the white blood cells)

Hospital Course:

A 72-year-old man came in with a painless lump in his belly that did not bother him. Doctors found a large mass in the middle part of his belly using special scans. A small piece of the mass was removed and tested. The tests showed the patient had Follicular Lymphoma. The patient was treated with two cancer medicines called bendamustine and rituximab. The patient came back for check-ups after leaving the hospital. So far, the cancer has not come back.

Medical History:

The patient did not have any major health problems before this.

Discussion:

Tumors that start in the middle part of the belly are not common. Follicular Lymphoma is the most common type of these tumors. Scans can show different patterns for this kind of cancer. Some signs on scans may point to cancer, but they are not always reliable. This type of cancer often grows slowly, but finding it early is still very important. When doctors find a solid lump in the middle of the belly, they should first think about this type of cancer.

Recommendations:

The patient should keep seeing the cancer doctor for regular check-ups. The patient should tell the doctor right away about any new symptoms or problems.

Primary Care Physician:
[Name]
[Contact Information]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 47-year-old edentulous female with maxillary complete denture

Chief Complaint: Dysfunctional maxillary complete denture

History of Present Illness: The patient presented with an unuseful maxillary complete denture, resulting from cervical radiotherapy. She had a limited oral opening and insufficient saliva quantity and flowability.

Hospital Course: The patient's treatment plan consisted of fabricating complete dentures with proper extension of the flanges. Flexible impression trays were used to obtain preliminary impressions for both arches. Custom trays for the final impressions were fabricated with autopolimerized acrylic resin. Final maxillary impressions were made by using polysulfide material alternately for the first and second halves of the sectional trays. Record bases were fabricated and occlusion rims were prepared. A centric relation record was made, and the record was transferred from the patient to a semiadjustable articulator. The try-in was evaluated to verify jaw relations and teeth arrangement. After patient acceptance, final festooning and flasking of the dentures were completed. The final dentures were manufactured using similar designs to that of the record bases. The prostheses were deflasked, finished, and polished. The patient was provided with instructions for cleaning, inserting, and removing the prostheses. She was recalled for follow-up visits to check for the maintenance and her adaptability with the new dentures.

Outcome: The patient reported difficulty in the insertion and removal of denture initially, but progressively, she became used to it.

Follow-up: The patient will continue to be monitored during follow-up visits to check the maintenance and adaptability of her new dentures.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 47-year-old female who has no natural teeth and wears a full upper denture

Chief Complaint: Upper denture that does not work properly

History of Present Illness: The patient came in because her upper denture was not working well. This problem happened because of radiation treatment she had on her neck area. She could not open her mouth very wide. She also did not have enough saliva, and her saliva did not flow well.

Hospital Course: The care team planned to make new full dentures with edges that fit better. They used bendable trays to take first molds of both the upper and lower mouth. They made custom trays for the final molds using a self-hardening plastic material. They took final upper molds using a rubber-like material on one half of the tray at a time. They made base plates and wax rims to help set the bite. They recorded how the jaws line up when closed and moved this information to a device that copies jaw movement. They checked the test dentures to make sure the jaw position and tooth placement were correct. After the patient agreed the fit was good, the team shaped and prepared the dentures in molds. They made the final dentures using the same design as the base plates. They removed the dentures from the molds, smoothed them, and shined them. The patient received instructions on how to clean, put in, and take out her dentures. She was asked to come back for checkup visits to make sure the dentures were in good shape and that she was adjusting to them.

Outcome: The patient had trouble putting in and taking out her denture at first. Over time, she got used to doing this.

Follow-up: The patient will keep coming to checkup visits. The care team will make sure her dentures are in good condition and that she is adjusting well to them.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]

Patient:
The patient is a 61-year-old Japanese man who was admitted with coxalgia.

Presenting Issues:
The patient presented with coxalgia, a condition that causes pain in the hip joint. Radiography revealed osseous projections at the knee and shoulder joints. The patient had also developed coxarthrosis and severe pain of the right hip joint. The range of motion of the right hip joint was limited in flexion and abduction.

Treatment:
The patient underwent right total hip arthroplasty using the direct lateral approach. A gigantic femoral head and large diameter of the femoral neck were noted. The soft tissue appeared normal, and an incision was made in the articular capsule. Hip dislocation was difficult because of the gigantic femoral head and caused a trochanteric fracture. The bone substance of the greater trochanter, femoral neck, and femoral trochanter was poor and showed bone fragility. An acetabular cup was implanted by press fit without screws at the site of the original acetabulum. An osteotomy was performed in a more distal than usual part of the femoral neck, and a femoral polished tapered stem was implanted using bone cement. The fractured greater trochanter was fixed with tension band wiring using Kirschner wire and nylon tape. The articular capsule, gluteus minimus muscle, gluteus medius muscle, and tensor fasciae latae muscle were repaired.

Outcomes:
Postoperatively, the implant setting was unproblematic, and the exostosis at the femoral neck had been removed. Walking with full weight-bearing was permitted, and there were no complications such as dislocation or infection. Thirteen months postoperatively, the patient had no coxalgia, but the range of motion of the right hip joint was still limited in flexion and abduction. The patient had no limp and no interference with his activities of daily living. There was an ectopic ossification seen radiographically. The patient had returned to his previous work and had no difficulties with everyday activities.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient was discharged from the hospital with a crutch.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [Date]
Discharge Date: [Date]

Patient:
The patient is a 61-year-old Japanese man who was admitted with hip pain.

Presenting Issues:
The patient came in with hip pain. X-rays showed bony growths near the knee and shoulder joints. The patient had also developed wear and tear of the hip joint and severe pain in the right hip. The patient could not bend or move the right hip outward as much as normal.

Treatment:
The patient had surgery to replace the right hip joint. The doctors went in through the side of the hip. They found that the ball of the hip joint was very large. The soft tissue looked normal. The doctors cut into the tissue covering the joint. It was hard to move the hip out of place because the ball was so big. This caused a break in the bony bump on the side of the thigh bone. The bone in this area was weak and broke easily. A new hip socket was pressed into place without screws. The doctors cut the thigh bone lower than usual. They put in a smooth metal stem using bone cement. The broken bone piece was fixed back in place with wire and tape. The doctors then repaired the joint covering and the muscles around the hip.

Outcomes:
After surgery, the new hip parts were in good position. The bony growth on the thigh bone had been removed. The patient was allowed to walk and put full weight on the leg. There were no problems like the hip slipping out of place or infection. Thirteen months after surgery, the patient had no hip pain. The right hip still could not bend or move outward as much as normal. The patient did not limp and had no trouble with daily tasks. X-rays showed some extra bone had formed near the hip. The patient had gone back to work and had no problems with everyday activities.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient left the hospital using a crutch.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 68
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Diagnosis: 
- Aphakic bullous keratopathy 
- Corneal scarring

Hospital Course: 
The patient underwent penetrating keratoplasty for aphakic bullous keratopathy and corneal scarring. The donor cornea was replaced due to a large dendritic-geographic epithelial ulcer noted immediately after placement. Perioperative administration of intravenous aciclovir was given. Postoperatively, the patient was started on systemic and local antiviral therapy, and local antibiotic treatment. The graft was clear and re-epithelialized at 1 week. Long term prophylactic acyclovir 400 mg was started. However, six months later the patient developed a geographic corneal ulcer involving the graft-host interface with keratic precipitates and corneal edema. The patient was treated with acyclovir 400 mg and topical aciclovir both five times a day for 2 weeks with resolution of the ulcer and residual scarring.

Summary: 
The patient who had aphakic bullous keratopathy and corneal scarring underwent a successful penetrating keratoplasty with the use of a donor corneoscleral disc. The initial donor corneoscleral disc was removed and replaced with a second donor corneoscleral disc which was available. The patient was treated with antiviral and antibiotic medications following the surgery. The patient developed a geographic corneal ulcer, which was treated successfully with antiviral medications. The patient was discharged with instructions for a follow-up within a week.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 68
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Diagnosis:
The patient has swelling and blisters on the clear front part of the eye after lens removal. The patient also has scarring on the clear front part of the eye.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery to replace the damaged clear front part of the eye with tissue from a donor. The first donor tissue showed a large sore right after it was placed. It was removed and replaced with new donor tissue. The patient received antiviral medicine through a vein during surgery. After surgery, the patient was given antiviral pills and eye drops. The patient also received antibiotic eye drops to prevent infection. After one week, the new tissue was clear and the surface had healed. The patient started taking acyclovir 400 mg pills for long term protection. Six months later, a sore appeared on the new tissue where it meets the original eye tissue. There was also clouding and small spots on the clear front part of the eye. The patient took acyclovir 400 mg pills and used antiviral eye drops five times a day for two weeks. The sore healed but left some scarring.

Summary:
The patient had swelling and scarring on the clear front part of the eye. The patient had surgery to replace this damaged tissue with donor tissue. The first donor tissue had to be replaced with a second one. After surgery, the patient took antiviral and antibiotic medicines. Later, a sore developed on the clear front part of the eye. This was treated successfully with antiviral medicines. The patient was sent home with instructions to return for a check-up within one week.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [redacted] 

Medical Record Number: [redacted]

Admission Date: [redacted] 

Discharge Date: [redacted]

Diagnosis and Treatment

The patient presented to our hospital with an induration felt on the inner upper quadrant of the left breast. Further examination revealed an irregular shaped tumor in the left A region. Subsequent histopathologic examination confirmed the presence of a granular cell tumor.

The patient received prompt and appropriate treatment, and following an uneventful course of hospitalization, has been discharged with instructions to follow up with their referring physician.

Course of Hospitalization

The patient's hospitalization was uneventful with no significant complications or intercurrent illnesses. The patient remained stable throughout the hospital stay and responded well to the treatment plan. Medications were administered as prescribed, and the patient experienced no adverse reactions.

During the course of hospitalization, the patient received appropriate medical care, including necessary laboratory and diagnostic testing, and prompt specialist consultation.

Disposition

The patient is being discharged with instructions to follow up with their referring physician for further management of their condition and any necessary ongoing treatment.

The patient has been advised to continue monitoring their breast health through regular self-examination and follow-up appointments with their physician as needed.

Follow-Up and Outpatient Care

The patient has been advised to follow up with their referring physician for further management of their condition. They have been instructed to continue monitoring their breast health through regular self-examination and follow-up appointments with their physician as needed.

It is recommended that the patient be included in a breast cancer screening program and receive regular mammograms to ensure early detection of any recurrence or other breast health concerns.

Diagnosis and Treatment

The patient came to our hospital because of a hard lump felt in the upper inner part of the left breast. A closer look showed a tumor with uneven edges in the left breast area. A tissue sample test showed the tumor was a granular cell tumor. This is a type of growth that is usually not cancer.

The patient received the right treatment quickly. The hospital stay went smoothly with no problems. The patient has been sent home with instructions to see their regular doctor for follow-up care.

Course of Hospitalization

The patient's hospital stay went smoothly with no major problems or other illnesses. The patient stayed stable the whole time and did well with the treatment plan. The patient took all medicines as directed and had no bad reactions.

During the hospital stay, the patient received proper medical care. This included needed lab work, imaging tests, and advice from specialists.

Disposition

The patient is going home with instructions to see their regular doctor. The doctor will help manage the condition and provide any ongoing treatment that may be needed.

The patient has been told to keep checking their breast health. This means doing regular self-exams at home and seeing their doctor for check-ups as needed.

Follow-Up and Outpatient Care

The patient has been told to see their regular doctor for ongoing care of their condition. The patient should keep checking their breast health through regular self-exams and doctor visits as needed.

The patient should join a breast cancer screening program. The patient should also get regular mammograms. These tests help find any new problems or other breast health concerns early.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Medical History: A 12-year-old female patient was admitted with a history of polyuria, polydipsia, and secondary amenorrhea.
Clinical Findings: A complete preoperative endocrinological assessment revealed mild prolactin hormone elevation, hypothyroidism, and hypocortisolism. Neuroradiological investigation showed a large mass in the sella with compression of the optic chiasm and extending into the third ventricle.
Diagnosis: A craniopharyngioma or a GCT were the suspected diagnoses. However, the symptoms presented by the patient suggested a germinoma.
Procedure: The patient underwent an extended endoscopic endonasal transtuberculum/transplanum approach for tumor removal.
Postoperative Course: The patient demonstrated a significant improvement in her condition with light perception reported in the left eye and improvement in right eye temporal hemianopsia. There were no postoperative complications.
Postoperative Follow-up: Serum levels of AFP, and beta-HCG were checked, suggesting confirmation of germinoma malignancy. The patient was referred to a pediatric oncologist for further treatment, which includes adjunctive chemotherapy and radiation therapy.
Condition at Discharge: The patient was discharged in a normal condition but still receiving desmopressin acetate for diabetes insipidus treatment.
Prognosis: The patient's condition remains optimistic, and a follow-up appointment is necessary.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Medical History: A 12-year-old female patient was admitted. She had been urinating a lot, feeling very thirsty, and her periods had stopped.

Clinical Findings: Doctors did hormone tests before surgery. The tests showed slightly high levels of a hormone called prolactin. The tests also showed her thyroid gland was underactive. Her body was not making enough stress hormones. Brain scans showed a large growth in the lower part of the brain. This growth was pressing on the nerves that control vision. It was also reaching into a fluid-filled space in the brain.

Diagnosis: Doctors first thought it might be one of two types of brain tumors. But based on the patient's symptoms, they believed it was a type called germinoma.

Procedure: The patient had surgery to remove the tumor. Doctors went through the nose using a small camera and tools. This allowed them to reach the tumor without opening the skull.

Postoperative Course: The patient got much better after surgery. She could see light with her left eye. Her side vision in her right eye also improved. There were no problems after surgery.

Postoperative Follow-up: Blood tests checked for certain markers. These results confirmed the tumor was a type of cancer called germinoma. The patient was sent to a children's cancer doctor for more treatment. She will need chemotherapy and radiation therapy.

Condition at Discharge: The patient left the hospital in normal condition. She is still taking a medicine called desmopressin acetate. This treats a condition where her body cannot control water balance.

Prognosis: The outlook for the patient is hopeful. She will need to come back for a follow-up visit.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 71-year-old female, was admitted to our hospital due to anorexia, left inguinal lymph node swelling, low platelet count, and elevated liver enzymes following a tick bite while harvesting. The patient was suspected to have a severe fever with thrombocytopenia syndrome (SFTS), and diagnostic tests confirmed SFTS infection based on ELISA and RT-PCR results. 

Clinical Findings:
On admission, the patient presented with mild headache and left inguinal lymph node swelling, with no erythema, rash, or wheal. Laboratory tests revealed low platelet and white blood cell counts, atypical lymphocytes, and elevated liver enzymes.

Treatment:
The patient was treated with adequate fluid replacement therapy due to mild hypovolemia upon admission. Minocycline was administered intravenously, as Rickettsia tsutsugamushi was considered a differential diagnosis. However, treatment with minocycline was discontinued after the definitive diagnosis of SFTS infection.

Outcome:
The platelet count became markedly elevated during hospitalization, and serum transaminases returned to almost normal levels. The patient's anorexia improved, and she was discharged after 13 days without experiencing any fever during her hospitalization. 

Follow-up Plan:
No specific follow-up plan was mentioned in the case report.

Doctors thought she might have a different tick-borne illness. However, doctors stopped giving minocycline after tests confirmed the SFTS infection.

The platelet count went up a lot during the hospital stay. Liver enzyme levels returned to almost normal levels. The patient's appetite improved. The patient was sent home after 13 days. The patient did not have any fever during her hospital stay.

Follow-up Plan:
No specific follow-up plan was mentioned in the case report.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: [REDACTED]

Medical History:
No relevant medical history was reported.

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the emergency clinic with a sudden decrease in vision following a blunt trauma to the right eye with a tennis ball. On presentation, his best corrected visual acuity was hand movement with perception of rays in all quadrants. The patient was managed conservatively with topical steroid eye drops, cycloplegics, and anti-glaucoma medications. The hyphema and vitreous hemorrhage settled down in three weeks, and the patient's visual acuity increased to 20/60. However, the IOP remained high, and prostaglandin analogue bimatoprost 0.01% was added. As the IOP did not show substantial improvement in the following weeks, the patient was advised for filtering surgery under guarded visual prognosis. 
Intraoperatively, there was a vitreous prolapse during deep block excision, which was managed with anterior vitrectomy. On postoperative day one, the visual acuity was 20/125, and the IOP was non-recordable. The patient was treated with topical and oral prednisolone for serous choroidal detachment, and the effusion settled in two weeks. Gradually, the patient's vision and IOP improved, and at the last follow-up visit after three months, the best corrected visual acuity and IOP were 20/25 and 12 mm Hg off any medication, respectively.

Summary:
An 18-year-old male presented with a blunt trauma to the right eye resulting in a decrease in visual acuity and high IOP. The patient was conservatively managed with topical steroid eye drops, cycloplegics, and anti-glaucoma medications, followed by filtering surgery. The intraoperative management included anterior vitrectomy for vitreous prolapse. The patient developed serous choroidal detachment, which resolved with prednisolone treatment. The patient was gradually tapered off prednisolone and showed improvement in visual acuity and IOP at the last follow-up visit after three months.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: [REDACTED]

Medical History:
No relevant medical history was reported.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency clinic with sudden vision loss. This happened after a tennis ball hit the right eye. At first, the patient could only see hand movements and some light in all directions. The patient was treated without surgery at first. Treatment included steroid eye drops to reduce swelling, drops to relax the eye, and medicines to lower eye pressure. There was bleeding in the front and middle parts of the eye. This bleeding cleared up in three weeks. The patient's vision improved to 20/60. However, the pressure inside the eye stayed too high. A medicine called bimatoprost was added to help lower the eye pressure. The eye pressure did not get much better over the following weeks. The patient was told that surgery to drain fluid from the eye was needed. The doctors explained that vision results could not be guaranteed. During surgery, the gel inside the eye pushed forward when tissue was removed. This was fixed by removing some of the gel. One day after surgery, vision was 20/125 and eye pressure was too low to measure. Fluid collected under a layer at the back of the eye. This was treated with steroid medicine as drops and pills. The fluid went away in two weeks. Over time, the patient's vision and eye pressure got better. At the last checkup three months later, vision was 20/25 and eye pressure was 12, which is normal. The patient no longer needed any eye pressure medicine.

Summary:
An 18-year-old male came in after a tennis ball hit his right eye. This caused vision loss and high pressure inside the eye. The patient was first treated with steroid eye drops, drops to relax the eye, and medicines to lower eye pressure. Later, the patient had surgery to drain fluid from the eye. During surgery, the gel inside the eye pushed forward and some had to be removed. After surgery, fluid collected under a layer at the back of the eye. This was treated with steroid medicine and went away. The steroid medicine was slowly reduced over time. At the last checkup three months later, the patient's vision and eye pressure had improved.

input:
Hospital Course:
The 54-year-old male patient, with a history of smoking and multiple gallstones, underwent intraoperative ERCP followed by LC. A successful endoscopic sphincterotomy was performed to access the gallbladder. The patient had high liver function tests, hyperbilirubinemia, and jaundice, but anesthesia and surgical plans were the same as another patient. The surgical and anesthesia times were 92 and 108 minutes, respectively. After the surgery, the patient didn't require any analgesic medication for the first 12 hours, and their NRS rating was <3/10. However, during the 13th hour, the NRS rating went up to 5/10 on coughing and 3/10 at rest, requiring rescue analgesic. The patient was discharged with a prescription for pain medication after 24 hours. 

Hospital course was otherwise unremarkable, and there were no complications with the patient's recovery. The patient was advised to follow up with their primary caregiver upon discharge.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 54-year-old man who has a history of smoking and gallstones. He had two procedures done together. First, doctors used a thin tube with a camera to look at the bile ducts and remove any stones. Then they removed the gallbladder through small cuts. Doctors made a small opening to reach the gallbladder. Before surgery, the patient had yellow skin and eyes, and blood tests showed the liver was not working normally. The surgery took 92 minutes, and the patient was under sleep medicine for 108 minutes. For the first 12 hours after surgery, the patient did not need pain medicine. The patient rated the pain as less than 3 out of 10. At hour 13, the pain went up to 5 out of 10 when coughing and 3 out of 10 when resting. The patient needed extra pain medicine at that time. The patient went home after 24 hours with a prescription for pain medicine.
The rest of the hospital stay went well. There were no problems during recovery. The patient was told to schedule a visit with their regular doctor after leaving the hospital.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name]
Sex: Female
Age: 61 years old
Date of admission: [Date]
Date of discharge: [Date]
Diagnosis: Papillary Fibroelastoma (PF)

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with recurrent paroxysmal pleuritic chest pain and severe dyspnea. Pulmonary CT angiography did not reveal any emboli, and cardiac tests showed a mobile mass attached to the septal tricuspid valve (TV) leaflet, measuring 1.3 × 0.9 × 1.1 cm with tricuspid regurgitation. A differential diagnosis of papillary fibroelastoma (PF) versus cardiac myxoma was entertained. Cardiac surgery was advised due to the established recurrent embolic potential of the tumor and size (>1 cm).

Surgery:

Shave excision of the sessile mass and TV repair using a 30-mm Medtronic Contour ring was performed in this patient.

Postoperative Procedures:

The patient's condition was monitored postoperatively with adequate treatment. Postoperatively, the patient continued anticoagulation for 8 weeks. To date, the patient remains asymptomatic at rest and exercise.

Follow-Up:

The patient is advised to follow a healthy lifestyle and to ensure adequate follow-up with the treating physician.

Addendum:

The patient did not experience any complications and remains stable during the hospital course and at the time of discharge.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name]
Sex: Female
Age: 61 years old
Date of admission: [Date]
Date of discharge: [Date]
Diagnosis: Papillary Fibroelastoma, a rare non-cancerous heart tumor

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with repeated sharp chest pain that got worse with breathing and serious trouble breathing. A CT scan of the lungs did not show any blood clots. Heart tests found a small growth attached to a flap in the right side of the heart. The growth measured about half an inch in size. The heart valve was also leaking slightly. Doctors thought the growth was most likely a papillary fibroelastoma or a myxoma, which are two types of non-cancerous heart tumors. Surgery was recommended because the tumor could break off and block blood flow. The size of the tumor, larger than one centimeter, also made surgery necessary.

Surgery:

Doctors removed the growth by carefully shaving it off the heart valve. The heart valve was also repaired using a special ring to help it close properly.

Postoperative Procedures:

The patient was watched closely after surgery and received proper care. The patient took blood-thinning medicine for 8 weeks after surgery. As of now, the patient has no symptoms during rest or physical activity.

Follow-Up:

The patient should live a healthy lifestyle. The patient should also keep all future appointments with the doctor.

Addendum:

The patient had no problems during the hospital stay. The patient was in stable condition at the time of discharge.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, an 81-year-old female with a significant history of prediabetes and end stage renal disease on hemodialysis, underwent coronary artery bypass graft surgery and replacement of mitral valve with postoperative complications. The patient presented with a dehiscence of the sternal wound, which required wound vacuum assisted closure, as well as a sacral decubitus ulcer, and multiple episodes of sepsis and respiratory failure.

Assessment and Plan:

During the first 45 days of admission, the patient's blood glucose was well-controlled with only 2 units total of sliding scale insulin required. However, soon after the start of intravenous ascorbic acid to aid in sternal wound healing, the patient experienced multiple high FSBG levels over 200 mg/dL, resulting in a total of 9 units of insulin treatment in 24 hours and subsequent FSBG levels remaining high over the next few days. Further investigation revealed a marked discrepancy of greater than 100 mg/dL between simultaneous FSBG and BGMP readings, with BGMP readings ranging from 72 to 146 mg/dL. Given the suspicion for falsely high FSBG readings caused by intravenous ascorbic acid, it was discontinued and FSBG values returned to normal. The patient did not experience hypoglycemia while receiving unnecessary insulin treatment.

Summary:

This discharge summary documents the hospital course of an elderly female with a history of renal disease and prediabetes who underwent coronary artery bypass graft surgery and replacement of mitral valve, complicated by postoperative complications. The patient had high FSBG levels after starting intravenous ascorbic acid, which were believed to be falsely elevated, resulting in unnecessary insulin treatment. The FSBG values returned to normal after discontinuation of intravenous ascorbic acid.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is an 81-year-old woman with a history of prediabetes and kidney failure requiring dialysis. She had heart bypass surgery and a heart valve replacement. After surgery, she had several problems. Her chest wound opened up and needed special vacuum bandages to help it heal. She also had a pressure sore on her lower back. She had several infections that spread to her blood and had trouble breathing on her own.

Assessment and Plan:

For the first 45 days in the hospital, the patient's blood sugar was well controlled. She only needed 2 units of insulin total during that time. However, soon after starting vitamin C through an IV to help heal her chest wound, her blood sugar readings became very high. The readings went above 200. She needed 9 units of insulin in one day, and her blood sugar stayed high for several days. Doctors then found a big difference between two ways of measuring blood sugar. The difference was more than 100 points. One method showed high readings. The other method showed normal readings between 72 and 146. Doctors believed the vitamin C was causing false high readings on one type of test. They stopped the vitamin C, and her blood sugar readings went back to normal. The patient did not have low blood sugar from getting insulin she did not need.

Summary:

This discharge summary describes the hospital stay of an elderly woman with kidney disease and prediabetes. She had heart bypass surgery and a heart valve replacement with problems after surgery. Her blood sugar readings became high after starting vitamin C through an IV. Doctors believe these readings were falsely high. This led to insulin treatment she did not need. Her blood sugar readings returned to normal after stopping the vitamin C.

input:
Discharge Summary:
Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]
Patient Name: [NAME]
DOB: [DOB]
Gender: Male

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with complaints of progressive facial asymmetry, difficulty in speech and mastication secondary to restricted movement of the mandible, and intermittent pain in the right TMJ region. The patient reported progressive reduction in the vertical opening and increasing asymmetry of the face with no history of trauma. Clinical examination revealed marked facial asymmetry, and radiographic imaging showed a large radiopaque lesion of the right mandibular condyle. CT scan confirmed a tumor of the right condyle head, and surgical removal of the enlarged condyle via preauricular approach was planned.

A preauricular incision was carried out, and the lesion including the condyle was removed successfully using a surgical bur. The entire specimen was decalcified in 10% formic acid and processed for histopathological examination. Histopathology showed chondrocytes of the cartilaginous cap arranged in clusters in parallel, oblong lacunar spaces, similar to that of the normal epiphyseal cartilage along with regular bony trabeculae. There was no evidence of cartilage islands in underlying bony trabeculae. These features were suggestive of osteochondroma, and the diagnosis of giant osteochondroma was made.
 
Postoperative recovery was uneventful, and periodic follow-up appointments were scheduled. During the latest visit of the patient, it was observed that his facial appearance and mouth opening had returned back to normal, and he had been symptom-free.

Discharge Condition:
The patient has successfully undergone surgical intervention for giant osteochondroma, and he has been discharged from the hospital with good postoperative recovery. The patient has been advised to continue follow-up appointments as scheduled.

Recommendations:
1. The patient is advised to follow up with his physician for periodic check-ups to monitor the condition and ensure a healthy recovery.
2. If any further complications or symptoms develop, the patient should seek medical attention immediately.
3. The patient is advised to continue maintaining healthy dietary habits and exercise regularly.
4. Surgery is not always the first treatment option, and any other treatment options should be discussed in detail with the patient and his caregiver.
5. Patients should be thoroughly educated on their diagnosis and the possible treatment options available to them.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:
Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]
Patient Name: [NAME]
DOB: [DOB]
Gender: Male

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital because one side of his face looked different from the other. He had trouble speaking and chewing because he could not move his lower jaw well. He also had pain on and off in the right jaw joint area. Over time, he could not open his mouth as wide, and his face became more uneven. He had no injuries that caused this. When doctors examined him, they saw his face was clearly uneven. X-rays showed a large growth on the right side of his lower jaw bone near the joint. A CT scan, which takes detailed pictures inside the body, confirmed a growth on the right jaw joint. Doctors planned surgery to remove the enlarged jaw joint through a cut made in front of the ear.

The doctors made a cut in front of the ear and removed the growth along with the jaw joint bone. They used a special dental drill to do this. The removed tissue was prepared and looked at under a microscope. The lab test showed the growth was made of cartilage and bone cells arranged in a normal pattern. There were no signs of cancer. The results showed the growth was a type of noncancerous bone tumor called a giant osteochondroma.

Recovery after surgery went smoothly with no problems. The patient was given regular follow-up visits. At his most recent visit, his face looked normal again and he could open his mouth normally. He had no more symptoms.

Discharge Condition:
The patient had successful surgery to remove a noncancerous bone growth. He has been sent home from the hospital and is recovering well.

Recommendations:
1. The patient should go to follow-up visits with his doctor as planned to check on healing and make sure he stays healthy.
2. If any new problems or symptoms come up, the patient should get medical help right away.
3. The patient should keep eating healthy foods and exercise regularly.
4. Surgery is not always the first choice for treatment. Other treatment options should be talked about fully with the patient and his family or caregiver.
5. Patients should learn all about their health condition and the different treatment choices available to them.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Sex: Male
Age: 39
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Diagnosis: Allergy to ketamine and morphine

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to our hospital with a diagnosis of chronic rhinosinusitis. He had a history of multiple anaphylactic reactions during surgical procedures and eye examinations performed under general anesthesia at various hospitals, since he was 11 years old. The patient and his relatives had been reluctant to come to the hospital due to extreme fear and discouragement for 10 years after his last episode of anaphylaxis during eye enucleation at the age of 29.

Following his evaluation by the allergist, skin testing was performed for morphine and ketamine, using standard concentrations according to the EAACI recommendation. The first test results were negative but due to his history of severe reactions, further testing was recommended 6 weeks later. The second round of skin tests was positive for ketamine and morphine. Hence, we provided a recommendation for the patient to avoid ketamine and morphine for future anesthetic procedures. The ADR card was updated accordingly, and alternative drugs were suggested, tested negative and were used for the patient's sinus surgery, which was performed without any adverse reactions.

The patient was advised to maintain close observation for allergic symptoms in the case of future anesthetic procedures with alternative drugs. The patient expressed full understanding of the recommendations and was discharged home in good condition. A comprehensive discharge report was provided to the patient and his carers.

Discharge Medications:

None recommended.

Follow-up:

The patient is advised to follow-up with the allergist as needed.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Sex: Male
Age: 39
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]
Diagnosis: Allergic to ketamine and morphine (two drugs used to make patients sleep during surgery)

Hospital Course:

The patient came to our hospital because of long-term sinus problems. Since the age of 11, the patient had several serious allergic reactions during surgeries and eye exams. These reactions happened when doctors gave medicine to make him sleep. The patient and his family were very scared to go to hospitals for 10 years. This fear started after his last serious allergic reaction at age 29 during eye removal surgery.

An allergy doctor checked the patient. The doctor did skin tests for morphine and ketamine using standard amounts. The first tests showed no allergy. But because the patient had such bad reactions before, doctors did more tests 6 weeks later. The second round of skin tests showed the patient is allergic to ketamine and morphine. We told the patient to never use ketamine or morphine for future surgeries. We updated the patient's drug allergy card. We tested other drugs that could be used instead. Those drugs did not cause a reaction. Doctors used these safe drugs for the patient's sinus surgery. The surgery went well with no allergic reactions.

The patient was told to watch carefully for allergy signs if given other sleep medicines in the future. The patient understood all the instructions. The patient went home feeling well. We gave the patient and his caregivers a full report about this hospital stay.

Discharge Medications:

None recommended.

Follow-up:

The patient should see the allergy doctor if needed.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient: 68-year-old female of Afghan descent.

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath, high-grade fever, and a cough productive of thick whitish sputum. The patient was diagnosed with acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia and was treated with methyl prednisolone. The patient was discharged after 10 days on oral prednisolone 40 mg daily.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia.

Condition at Discharge:

The patient was discharged in stable condition.

Disposition:

Discharged to home.

Follow-up:

Patient to follow up with neurosurgeon for correction of spinal stenosis.

Summary:

The 68-year-old female of Afghan descent was admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath, high-grade fever, and a cough productive of thick whitish sputum. The patient was diagnosed with acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia and was treated with methyl prednisolone. The patient was discharged after 10 days on oral prednisolone 40 mg daily. The patient was discharged in stable condition and advised to follow up with a neurosurgeon for correction of spinal stenosis.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 16-year-old boy who was admitted to the hospital due to fractures of the left tibia and the right forearm. The tibia fracture was managed with a circular frame, while the forearm fracture was fixed with intramedullary titanium elastic nails. The patient underwent surgery due to metalwork migration, during which the excess material was trimmed and impacted back into the bone. The nail exit holes had a cap of bone substitute applied.
 
Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with periprosthetic fracture of the right forearm and fracture of the left tibia.

Procedure:
The patient underwent corrective osteotomy, creation of an intramedullary canal using the combination drilling technique and insertion of titanium elastic nails. The half-pins were easily removed as they had not bonded to the bone. The wires proved to be more difficult to extract but were ultimately removed. 

Treatment:
The patient received treatment in the form of circular frame and intramedullary titanium elastic nails for fracture management. Surgical intervention was performed due to metalwork migration. 

Follow-up:
Over the course of 18 months, the titanium elastic nails began to migrate and cause some soft tissue irritation. The patient achieved full union and his function returned to his pre-injury state at 1-year follow-up. His range of motion was observed, with a maximum pronation of 25° and supination of 60°. According to the patient, this matched his pre-injury range of motion and he was satisfied with the overall outcome. No further follow-up was necessary.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 16-year-old boy who was admitted to the hospital because of broken bones in his left lower leg and right forearm. The lower leg break was treated with a ring-shaped metal frame attached around the leg. The forearm break was fixed with flexible metal rods placed inside the bone. The patient needed another surgery because the metal rods shifted out of place. During this surgery, the extra metal was trimmed and pushed back into the bone. The holes where the rods came out were covered with a bone-like filling material.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with a break near the metal hardware in his right forearm and a break in his left lower leg bone.

Procedure:
The patient had surgery to cut and reset the bone, create a channel inside the bone using a special drilling method, and insert flexible metal rods. The small metal pins were easy to remove because they had not attached to the bone. The metal wires were harder to take out but were removed in the end.

Treatment:
The patient received treatment using a ring-shaped metal frame and flexible metal rods inside the bone to fix his broken bones. Surgery was done because the metal rods moved out of place.

Follow-up:
Over 18 months, the flexible metal rods slowly shifted and caused some irritation to the nearby tissue. The patient's bones fully healed and he could do everything he did before the injury at his one-year checkup. His arm movement was checked. He could rotate his palm down 25 degrees and rotate his palm up 60 degrees. The patient said this matched how his arm moved before the injury and he was happy with the results. No more checkups were needed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Confidential

Gender: Male

Age: 52 years

Chief Complaint: Non-specific central chest pain

Diagnosis: Sternal Osteomyelitis

Admission Date: N/A

Discharge Date: N/A

Hospital Course: 

The patient presented with a two-year history of non-specific central chest pain. He had been diagnosed with costochondritis and was prescribed non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents. After two weeks, the erythematous changes over the sternum were observed with no improvement in his pain. A computed tomography scan of the chest revealed extensive destructive lesions in the sternal bone, suggestive of the diagnosis of sternal osteomyelitis. The biopsy revealed the growth of Mycobacterium tuberculosis. The patient was referred to the infectious disease team for management and commenced on anti-tubercular medications, including rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol, for 12 months.

Condition on Discharge: 

The patient showed improvement in his symptoms and completed the 12-month course of anti-tubercular medications. He had a complete resolution of his symptoms.

Plan:

The patient was discharged with instructions to follow-up with his primary care physician regarding his ongoing general health.

Discharge Medications: N/A

Follow-up Appointments: To be scheduled with the primary care physician.

Discharge Condition: Stable.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Confidential

Gender: Male

Age: 52 years

Chief Complaint: Unclear chest pain in the center of the chest

Diagnosis: Bone infection in the breastbone

Admission Date: N/A

Discharge Date: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient came in with chest pain in the center of his chest that had lasted two years. He was told he had swelling of the rib cartilage and was given pain relievers. After two weeks, the skin over his breastbone turned red, but his pain did not get better. A special X-ray scan of his chest showed serious damage to his breastbone. This pointed to a bone infection in the breastbone. A small tissue sample showed a germ called tuberculosis was causing the infection. The patient was sent to doctors who treat infections. He was started on four medicines to treat tuberculosis. These medicines were rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol. He took these medicines for 12 months.

Condition on Discharge:

The patient got better and finished all 12 months of his tuberculosis medicines. All of his symptoms went away completely.

Plan:

The patient was sent home with instructions to see his regular doctor for his overall health.

Discharge Medications: N/A

Follow-up Appointments: To be scheduled with the regular doctor.

Discharge Condition: Stable.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED] 
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED] 
Date of Admission: [REDACTED] 
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED] 

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 78-year-old male, was admitted to the emergency room with complaints of dry cough, fever and malaise persisting for 1 month. He was previously treated with amoxicillin with no improvement. His medical history included diabetes and arterial blood hypertension, for which he was on metformin and amlodipine for 7 years. The patient presented with mildly lethargic orientation, febrile with mild tachycardia, hypoxemia, and crackles in the mid- and lower-lung fields on physical examination. Laboratory tests revealed elevated WBC counts, NLR, ESR, and CRP. The chest radiograph showed diffuse bilateral interstitial infiltrates. The HRCT showed diffuse interstitial lung involvement with a ground glass pattern. Blood and sputum cultures were negative for bacteria, urine antigen tests for Legionella pneumonia and Streptococcus pneumoniae were negative. Serum immunology tests for autoimmune diseases such as antinuclear antibodies, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies were all negative. A bronchoscopy was then performed, BAL and TBLB were negative for malignancy, bacteria, nocardia, fungi, and mycobacteria. BAL increased cellularity and had a mixed cellularity pattern. In view of the above findings, a diagnosis of Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia (COP) was considered. Treatment with antibiotics was ceased, and the patient was started on prednisone 20mg daily. The patient showed immediate improvement, blood inflammatory markers decreased, and he was discharged after 5 days on prednisone. He was advised to taper off corticosteroids for a total duration of treatment of 6-12 months.

Medical Condition:
Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia (COP)

Treatment:
Prednisone 20mg daily

Follow-up:
The patient has had no recurrence of symptoms at follow-up visits after complete clinical and radiological resolution of his condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 78-year-old male, came to the emergency room with a dry cough, fever, and feeling unwell for 1 month. He was treated before with an antibiotic called amoxicillin, but it did not help. His health history included diabetes and high blood pressure. He had been taking metformin and amlodipine for these conditions for 7 years. When examined, the patient was slightly drowsy, had a fever, a slightly fast heartbeat, low oxygen levels, and crackling sounds in the middle and lower parts of his lungs. Blood tests showed signs of infection and swelling in the body. A chest x-ray showed cloudy patches in both lungs. A detailed lung scan showed widespread lung tissue damage with a hazy pattern. Tests of blood and mucus samples found no bacteria. Urine tests for two types of lung infection bacteria were negative. Blood tests for diseases where the body attacks itself were all negative. A camera was used to look inside the airways. Fluid and tissue samples from the lungs showed no cancer, bacteria, or fungus. The fluid samples did show more cells than normal with a mixed pattern. Based on all these results, the doctors believed the patient had a lung condition called Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia. This is a type of lung swelling with no known cause. The antibiotics were stopped. The patient was started on a steroid medicine called prednisone at 20mg once a day. The patient got better right away. The signs of swelling in the blood went down. The patient left the hospital after 5 days on prednisone. He was told to slowly lower his steroid dose over 6 to 12 months total.

Medical Condition:
Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia, a type of lung swelling with no known cause

Treatment:
Prednisone 20mg once a day

Follow-up:
The patient has had no return of symptoms at follow-up visits. His condition has fully cleared up based on exams and scans.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient presented with high-grade intermittent fever for three days with spontaneous gum bleeding and mild headache. He subsequently developed gradually worsening drowsiness, and was found to have thrombocytopenia with microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA). A diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenia purpura (TTP) was made. He was promptly commenced on therapeutic plasma exchange and 1 mg/kg of oral prednisolone. 

On the fifth day of illness, the patient developed two episodes of generalized tonic-clonic convulsions which progressed into a non-convulsive status epilepticus, requiring elective ventilation in the intensive care unit. On day 18, he developed flaccid paraparesis with sphincter dysfunction. Magnetic resonance imaging showed haemorrhage at multiple spinal levels.

Despite 16 plasma exchanges and high dose steroids, his platelet count remained persistently low, and he was investigated for alternative etiologies. Polymerase chain reaction of serum revealed clinically significant levels of cytomegalovirus (CMV). He was commenced on oral valganciclovir 450 mg/daily for 21 days. After about 6 days of valganciclovir treatment, his platelet count increased, and the MAHA resolved. 

The patient subsequently had regained normal haematological and biochemical parameters with a negative PCR quantification of CMV. He had regained ambulatory status with support and had normal sphincter function. The patient was transferred to a rehabilitation facility for further care.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came in with a high fever that came and went for three days. He also had bleeding gums and a mild headache. He then became more and more sleepy over time. Tests showed he had a very low platelet count and his red blood cells were breaking apart. The doctors found he had a rare blood disorder called TTP that causes these problems. He was quickly started on a treatment to replace his blood plasma. He also began taking a steroid medicine called prednisolone by mouth.

On the fifth day of being sick, the patient had two seizures where his whole body shook. These seizures then turned into ongoing brain activity problems. He needed to be put on a breathing machine in the intensive care unit. On day 18, he lost strength in both legs and could not control his bladder or bowels. A special scan showed bleeding at several spots along his spine.

The patient had 16 plasma replacement treatments and high doses of steroids. But his platelet count stayed very low. The doctors then looked for other causes. A blood test found high levels of a virus called CMV. He was started on an antiviral medicine called valganciclovir, 450 mg once a day for 21 days. About 6 days after starting this medicine, his platelet count went up. His red blood cells also stopped breaking apart.

The patient's blood tests later returned to normal. The virus was no longer found in his blood. He could walk again with some help. He could also control his bladder and bowels normally again. The patient was moved to a rehab center for more care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Name: [Name Redacted]
- Age: 65-year-old
- Gender: Female
- Diagnosis: Malignant melanoma of the urethra with positive surgical margins

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to our hospital with a 6-month history of difficulty in voiding associated with a small mass at the urethral meatus. Physical examination revealed a small tan-colored polypoid growth protruding from the external urethral meatus, mimicking a urethral caruncle, without any evidence of inguinal lymphadenopathy. Urethral carunclectomy was performed with uneventful postoperative recovery. The pathological examination of the specimen demonstrated malignant melanoma of the urethra with positive surgical margins. Further investigations revealed the presence of residual tumor in the last 0.5 cm of the urethra with no abnormal findings in either the proximal urethra or the urinary bladder. Thus, a radical urethrectomy with bladder preservation and a continent catheterizable stoma (Yang-Monti technique) was performed to achieve 2.5 cm margins. The patient tolerated the procedure well, and there were no complications postoperatively.

Outcome:
The final pathology report revealed a small focus (3mm) of a residual malignant melanoma with negative surgical margins. Currently, one year after the surgery, the patient is in complete clinical remission with no evidence of disease.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient will require long-term follow-up with regular monitoring and imaging tests to detect any signs of disease recurrence. The patient is instructed to perform self-intermittent catheterization and remain continent. Follow-up consultations with an oncologist and urologist are advised to monitor the patient's condition and ensure optimal management.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [Name Redacted]
Age: 65 years old
Gender: Female
Diagnosis: Skin cancer (melanoma) found in the urethra (the tube that carries urine out of the body). Some cancer cells were left at the edges after the first surgery.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to our hospital after having trouble urinating for 6 months. The patient also had a small growth at the opening where urine comes out. A physical exam showed a small tan growth sticking out from the urine opening. It looked like a common harmless bump. There were no signs of swollen glands in the groin area. Doctors removed the growth, and the patient recovered well from this surgery. Lab tests on the removed tissue showed it was skin cancer (melanoma) in the urethra. Some cancer cells were still at the edges of what was removed. More tests showed a small amount of cancer was still in the last half centimeter of the urethra. The upper part of the urethra and the bladder were normal. Doctors then did a second surgery to remove the entire urethra but kept the bladder. They created a new opening in the belly for the patient to empty the bladder using a thin tube. This surgery removed enough tissue to get 2.5 centimeters of healthy tissue around the cancer. The patient handled the surgery well and had no problems afterward.

Outcome:
The final lab report showed a very small spot (3 millimeters) of remaining skin cancer. This time, no cancer cells were found at the edges. Now, one year after surgery, the patient shows no signs of cancer.

Follow-up Recommendations:
The patient will need long-term checkups with regular tests and scans to watch for any return of the cancer. The patient must use a thin tube to empty the bladder on a regular schedule. The patient can control when to empty the bladder. The patient should see a cancer doctor and a bladder doctor for ongoing care and checkups.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Sex: Male
Age: 36 years
Date of Admission: October 2007

Medical History: The patient presented with a history of multiple exostoses and had undergone surgical interventions in the past. He was diagnosed with DSM-IV schizophrenia and had no family history of psychosis.

Hospital Course: The patient's treatment course was challenging, as he did not respond well to various neuroleptics and mood stabilizers. He underwent a combination of treatment strategies, including electroconvulsive therapy, which led to several hospitalizations.

Outcome: The patient showed a partial response to the combination of Olanzapine, Haloperidol, Lamotrigine, clonazepam and Zuclopenthixol. He was discharged on [Redacted].

Follow-up Care: The patient should be closely monitored for any psychiatric symptoms and comorbidities. The treatment plan should be reviewed regularly and modified if needed.

Summary: The patient was admitted with a diagnosis of schizophrenia and had a complicated hospital course due to medication-resistant symptoms. A combination of pharmacological and non-pharmacological approaches led to a partial response, and the patient was discharged with follow-up care.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Sex: Male
Age: 36 years
Date of Admission: October 2007

Medical History: The patient came in with a history of bony growths and had surgeries before. He was diagnosed with schizophrenia, a mental illness that affects thinking and behavior. No one in his family has had similar mental health problems.

Hospital Course: The patient's treatment was difficult. His symptoms did not get better with different medicines for mental illness and mood. He tried many treatment options, including a therapy that uses small electrical currents to the brain. This led to several hospital stays.

Outcome: The patient got somewhat better with a mix of five medicines: Olanzapine, Haloperidol, Lamotrigine, clonazepam, and Zuclopenthixol. He left the hospital on [Redacted].

Follow-up Care: The patient needs to be watched closely for any mental health symptoms and other health problems. The care team should check the treatment plan often and make changes if needed.

Summary: The patient came to the hospital with schizophrenia. His stay was difficult because his symptoms did not respond well to medicines. A mix of medicines and other treatments helped him get somewhat better. He left the hospital with a plan for follow-up care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: 64-year-old male
Reason for Admission: Implantation of a transcatheter leadless pacemaker
Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]

History of Present Illness:
The patient was admitted to Monaldi Hospital for implantation of a transcatheter leadless pacemaker. During the device implantation procedure, unacceptable electrical measurements and fluoroscopic findings indicated possible TPS dislodgement. An emergency computed tomography (CT) scan was requested and revealed that the TPS had migrated and was embolized in the pulmonary middle lobe artery.

Hospital Course:
Multi-detector CT (MDCT) angiography was performed and confirmed the dislodged device’s presence in the pulmonary middle lobe artery. Successful percutaneous retrieval of the dislodged device was readily performed through the proximal retrieval feature of the device.

Physical Examination:
The patient’s lungs were examined, and there were reduced blood flow and hyperlucent of the tributary lung territory due to the dislodged pacemaker.

Diagnostic Testing:
Computed tomography (CT) scan and Multi-detector CT (MDCT) angiography confirmed the presence of the dislodged pacemaker in the pulmonary middle lobe artery and pulmonary blood flow restrictions.

Course of Treatment:
Successful retrieval of the dislodged pacemaker was made through the proximal retrieval feature of the device. The pacemaker was then correctly reimplanted in the next hours.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient presented to Monaldi Hospital for implantation of a transcatheter leadless pacemaker. The procedure was terminated due to unacceptable electrical measurements and fluoroscopic findings that indicated the possible dislodgement of the device. A CT scan and MDCT angiography were performed, revealing that the TPS had migrated and was embolized in the pulmonary middle lobe artery. Successful percutaneous retrieval of the dislodged device was performed and correctly reimplanted.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged from the hospital in good condition and was advised to follow-up with his physician for further monitoring of the implanted pacemaker.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient was advised to follow up with his physician for further monitoring of the implanted pacemaker. No other follow-up recommendations were provided.

Discharge Medications:
No discharge medications were prescribed.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was advised to follow up with his physician for further monitoring of the implanted pacemaker. No other follow-up recommendations were provided.

Attending Physician:
[Physician Name and Signature]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: 64-year-old male
Reason for Admission: Placement of a small wireless pacemaker (a device that helps the heart beat regularly)
Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]

History of Present Illness:
The patient came to Monaldi Hospital to have a small wireless pacemaker put in. During the procedure, the readings and images showed the device may have moved out of place. Doctors ordered an emergency scan right away. The scan showed the pacemaker had moved and traveled into a blood vessel in the right lung.

Hospital Course:
A detailed scan of the blood vessels was done. It confirmed the pacemaker was stuck in a blood vessel in the middle part of the right lung. Doctors were able to remove the loose device using a built-in feature that allows it to be pulled back out.

Physical Examination:
The patient's lungs were checked. The area of the lung near the loose pacemaker had less blood flow. That part of the lung also looked lighter on images than normal.

Diagnostic Testing:
Scans of the chest and blood vessels confirmed the pacemaker was in a lung blood vessel. The scans also showed reduced blood flow in that area.

Course of Treatment:
Doctors successfully removed the loose pacemaker using its built-in removal feature. They then placed the pacemaker correctly in the following hours.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient came to Monaldi Hospital to have a small wireless pacemaker put in. The procedure was stopped because readings and images showed the device may have moved. Scans showed the pacemaker had traveled into a blood vessel in the right lung. Doctors removed the device successfully and placed it correctly afterward.

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital in good health. The patient was told to see a doctor for regular checks on the pacemaker.

Follow-up Instructions:
The patient should see a doctor for regular checks on the pacemaker. No other follow-up steps were given.

Discharge Medications:
No medications were prescribed at discharge.

Discharge Condition:
The patient should see a doctor for regular checks on the pacemaker. No other follow-up steps were given.

Attending Physician:
[Physician Name and Signature]

input:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:
Left renal calculus

Chief Complaint:
Poor effort tolerance

History of Presenting Illness:
A 60-year-old woman was admitted with a history of poor effort tolerance. On further evaluation, the patient was diagnosed with left renal calculus and scheduled for left percutaneous nephrolithotomy. During preanesthetic evaluation, the patient's irregularly irregular pulse and abnormal ECG raised concern for an asymptomatic sinus node dysfunction.

Past Medical History:
The patient reported a past medical history of ischemic heart disease with hypertension since 6 months and was on regular treatment. There was also evidence of an asymptomatic sinus node dysfunction, which was resistant to atropine.

Hospital Course:
The patient was given balanced general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation, induced with propofol and vecuronium, and secured under direct laryngoscopy with size 7.0 endotracheal tube. During the surgery, there were repeated episodes of continuous pacing by the temporary pacemaker, followed by a resumption of normal sinus rhythm. Other vital parameters were stable in the intraoperative period. Neuromuscular blockade was reversed with neostigmine and glycopyrrolate. The patient was shifted to the post anesthesia care unit with stable vitals.

Discharge Condition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition on the 5th postoperative day.

Follow-up Care:
The patient was advised to follow-up with their cardiologist for further evaluation and management of their asymptomatic sinus node dysfunction.

Medications on Discharge:
None

Instructions to Patient:
The patient was advised to rest and follow-up with their cardiologist for further evaluation and management of their asymptomatic sinus node dysfunction.
clean_ref:
Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:
Left kidney stone

Chief Complaint:
Difficulty doing physical activities

History of Presenting Illness:
A 60-year-old woman came to the hospital because she had trouble doing physical activities. After tests, doctors found a stone in her left kidney. The patient was scheduled for surgery to remove the stone through a small cut in the back. Before surgery, doctors noticed the patient had an uneven heartbeat and unusual heart test results. This pointed to a problem with the heart's natural pacemaker that was not causing symptoms.

Past Medical History:
The patient had a history of reduced blood flow to the heart and high blood pressure for 6 months. The patient was taking medicine for these conditions. The patient also had a problem with the heart's natural pacemaker that was not causing symptoms. This problem did not respond to a medicine called atropine.

Hospital Course:
The patient was put to sleep using general anesthesia. A breathing tube was placed using standard methods. Doctors used medicine to help the patient sleep and relax the muscles. During surgery, the heart's natural pacemaker stopped working several times. A temporary pacemaker kept the heart beating until the normal heartbeat returned. All other body functions stayed normal during surgery. Medicine was given to help the muscles work again after surgery. The patient was moved to the recovery room in stable condition.

Discharge Condition:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition 5 days after surgery.

Follow-up Care:
The patient was told to see their heart doctor. The heart doctor will check and treat the problem with the heart's natural pacemaker.

Medications on Discharge:
None

Instructions to Patient:
The patient was told to rest. The patient was also told to see their heart doctor for follow-up care of the heart's natural pacemaker problem.

input:
Discharge Summary:
Patient: 45-year-old female
Diagnosis: Multiple myeloma

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with multiple purpuric, ecchymotic lesions over periorbital area, cheeks, front and back of the neck with relative sparing of covered parts for the last 2 years, with a history of minor trauma precipitating these lesions. Skin was easily fragile in most areas. There was a history of multiple episodes of diarrhea and fever in the past 6 months. General examination showed mild pallor and bilateral pedal edema. Skin biopsy finding was suggestive of a bullous lesion with amorphous eosinophilic amyloid deposits. Serum protein electrophoresis and bone marrow aspiration confirmed it to be a case of multiple myeloma. However, radiological evaluations of skeleton were normal. 

Treatment:
The patient was referred to the department of clinical hematology where chemotherapy was started.

Prognosis:
The patient's prognosis will depend on the response to treatment and the stage of multiple myeloma. Close follow-up with clinical hematology is recommended. 

Follow-up:
It is recommended that the patient follow-up with clinical hematology regularly as well as adhere to any prescribed medication regimen.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:
Patient: 45-year-old female
Diagnosis: Multiple myeloma, a type of blood cancer that affects the bone marrow

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with multiple purple and dark bruise-like patches on the skin. These patches appeared around the eyes, cheeks, and the front and back of the neck. The areas normally covered by clothing were mostly clear. These skin problems started 2 years ago. Even small bumps or injuries caused these patches to appear. The skin was very easy to damage in most areas. The patient also had several bouts of loose stools and fever over the past 6 months. A physical exam showed the patient was slightly pale and had swelling in both feet. A small sample of skin was taken and tested. It showed a blister-like problem with an abnormal protein buildup called amyloid. Blood tests and a bone marrow test confirmed the diagnosis of multiple myeloma. X-rays and other images of the bones looked normal.

Treatment:
The patient was sent to the blood cancer specialists. Treatment with cancer-fighting drugs was started.

Prognosis:
How well the patient does will depend on how the cancer responds to treatment. It will also depend on how far the cancer has spread. The patient should stay in close contact with the blood cancer care team.

Follow-up:
The patient should see the blood cancer specialists on a regular basis. The patient should also take all medicines as directed.
