input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 68-year-old Japanese man, underwent resection surgery for a leiomyosarcoma at the mediastinum. Due to dissemination in the pleura, the patient received radiation therapy and a second operation. Ten months post-surgery, the patient developed pain and numbness in the left upper limb, and an enhanced solid lesion and a non-enhanced cystic lesion were observed in the apex of the left lung using contrast enhanced CT scan. Ablation using radiofrequency ablation (RFA) was performed after radiation and chemotherapy were deemed ineffective, and surgery was thought to carry a high risk of bleeding. The patient underwent RFA with a LeVeen electrode, with retractable hooks opened for ablation. A total of three ablations were performed, with the output started at 20 W and increased by 10 W every 2 minutes in each session. The procedure was performed under local anesthesia to monitor the appearance of sharp pain of the left upper limb. A maximum output of 80 W and total ablation time of 57 minutes and 14 seconds were achieved. Contrast enhanced CT performed two days after RFA showed that the enhanced solid lesion in the tumor was smaller in size compared to pre-RFA CT. The patient's pain disappeared for approximately 3 months, accompanied by an improvement in motor function disorder. However, the pain of the left upper limb reappeared due to growth of the tumor, three months after RFA.

Condition at Discharge:

The patient's pain had reappeared, and thus, he was discharged from the hospital with instructions to follow up with his healthcare provider.

Instructions:

The patient is advised to follow up with his healthcare provider regarding his condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 68-year-old Japanese man. He had surgery to remove a rare muscle cancer found in the chest area between the lungs. The cancer had spread to the lining of the lungs. Because of this, the patient had radiation treatment and a second surgery. Ten months after surgery, the patient started having pain and numbness in his left arm. A special scan using dye showed two growths at the top of the left lung. One growth was solid and the other was filled with fluid. Radiation and chemotherapy did not work well. Surgery was too risky because it could cause heavy bleeding. So doctors used a heat treatment called RFA to destroy the growths. The doctor inserted a special needle with small hooks into the tumor. The needle sent heat into the tumor to kill the cancer cells. The doctor did this treatment three times total. The heat was started low and slowly increased every 2 minutes each time. The patient was kept awake with numbing medicine only. This let doctors know right away if the patient felt sharp pain in his left arm. The highest heat used was 80 W. The total treatment time was 57 minutes and 14 seconds. A scan done two days later showed the solid growth had gotten smaller. The patient had no pain for about 3 months. His arm also moved better during this time. But the pain in his left arm came back after 3 months. This happened because the tumor started growing again.

Condition at Discharge:

The patient's pain had come back. He was sent home from the hospital. He was told to see his doctor for follow-up care.

Instructions:

The patient should see his doctor to check on his condition.

Attending Physician: [redacted]

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted for postoperative radiotherapy and chemotherapy after a total hysterectomy that was performed for cervical cancer. However, both treatments were cancelled due to urinary incontinence. An abdominopelvic CT scan was conducted to confirm the cause of the condition.

Summary of Findings: The CT scan confirmed that there were no residual disease findings in the abdominopelvic region. However, it also revealed the cause of urinary incontinence, which has made both radiotherapy and chemotherapy inappropriate treatment options.

Diagnosis: Urinary incontinence.

Treatment: None required.

Discharge Condition: The patient's discharge condition is stable.

Discharge Medications: None prescribed.

Follow-up Care: No follow-up care is required at this time.

Changes in Condition: No significant changes in the patient's condition were observed.

Prognosis: Prognosis is expected to be positive as no residual disease was found during the CT scan.

[End of Discharge Summary]

Patient Name: [redacted]
Date of Admission: [redacted]
Date of Discharge: [redacted]
Attending Physician: [redacted]

Hospital Course: The patient came to the hospital to receive radiation treatment and cancer-fighting drugs after surgery to remove the uterus. The surgery was done because of cervical cancer. However, both treatments were stopped because the patient could not control urine flow. A scan of the belly and lower body area was done to find out why this was happening.

Summary of Findings: The scan showed no signs of cancer left in the belly and lower body area. The scan also found the reason for the lack of bladder control. Because of this, radiation treatment and cancer-fighting drugs are not the right choice now.

Diagnosis: Lack of bladder control.

Discharge Condition: The patient is in stable condition.

Discharge Medications: No medicines were given.

Follow-up Care: No follow-up visits are needed at this time.

Changes in Condition: There were no major changes in how the patient was doing.

Prognosis: The outlook is expected to be good because the scan showed no cancer remaining.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 45 years
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: 7 days

Admitting Diagnosis:
Undifferentiated Spindle Cell Sarcoma Involving the Pancreas

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital urgently due to the acute onset of severe abdominal pain and collapse. Upon examination, she was found to be in class III haemorrhagic shock with distended and diffusely tender abdomen. She was immediately resuscitated with blood transfusions and intravenous crystalloid boluses. Hematological and basic investigations revealed haemoglobin level of 5g/dl and moderate amount of free fluid with mass around the proximal part of the pancreas, respectively. Following which, an emergency exploratory laparotomy was performed, and the source of bleeding was identified as the nidus, which was situated posterior to the body of the pancreas and had ruptured through the inferior border of the pancreas. The histopathological analysis of the biopsy revealed an infiltrating tumour composed of fascicles of spindle cells with oval, vesicular nuclei with scanty eosinophilic cytoplasm that was diagnosed as undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma, and the tumour was surgically excised.

Treatment:
The patient was managed in the intensive care unit for 4 days and received a course of Ifosfamide and Doxorubicin for 6 months as adjuvant chemotherapy. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography was performed before the commencement of chemotherapy, which showed a 7 × 4.8 × 4.9 cm mass lesion with a thin wall containing hypodense material, attached to the inferior border of the pancreas. There were no other metastatic lesions identified.

Outcome:
The patient recovered following surgery and was discharged 7 days after admission. She was referred to the oncologist and offered a pancreatectomy with excision of the tumour, which was refused. The patient received six months of chemotherapy and finally succumbed to the disease 18 months after the presentation.

Summary:
The patient was admitted to the hospital with undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma involving the pancreas that was treated with surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy. The patient was discharged following a 7-day hospitalization but eventually succumbed to the disease after 18 months.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 45 years
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Length of Stay: 7 days

Admitting Diagnosis:
A rare type of cancer made of long, thin cells affecting the pancreas

Hospital Course:
The patient was rushed to the hospital because of sudden, severe belly pain and collapse. Doctors found the patient had lost a large amount of blood and was in shock. The belly was swollen and painful to touch. The patient was given blood transfusions and fluids through a vein right away. Blood tests showed very low blood levels at 5g/dl. Imaging tests showed fluid in the belly and a growth near the upper part of the pancreas. Doctors then did emergency surgery to open the belly and find the bleeding. The bleeding came from a growth behind the pancreas that had burst through the lower edge of the pancreas. A tissue sample was taken and tested. The test showed a cancer made of bundles of long, thin cells. This cancer was diagnosed as a rare type called spindle cell sarcoma. The growth was removed during surgery.

Treatment:
The patient stayed in the intensive care unit for 4 days. The patient then received two cancer-fighting drugs called Ifosfamide and Doxorubicin for 6 months after surgery. Before starting these drugs, a special scan with dye was done. The scan showed a growth measuring about 7 by 4.8 by 4.9 centimeters with thin walls attached to the lower edge of the pancreas. The cancer had not spread to other parts of the body.

Outcome:
The patient got better after surgery and left the hospital 7 days after being admitted. The patient was sent to a cancer specialist. Doctors offered surgery to remove the pancreas and the growth. The patient said no to this surgery. The patient received six months of cancer-fighting drugs. The patient died from the disease 18 months after first coming to the hospital.

Summary:
The patient came to the hospital with a rare cancer affecting the pancreas. The cancer was treated with surgery and cancer-fighting drugs given after surgery. The patient left the hospital after staying for 7 days. The patient died from the disease 18 months later.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Name]

Date of Admission: [Date of Admission]

Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

Hospitalized for: Stenosis of Left Subclavian Artery

Brief Hospital Course: 

The patient, a 4-year-old boy, was admitted with an incidental detection of murmur. Clinical examination revealed low volume radial pulse in the left upper limb and lower BP in the same limb. The right radial pulse was normal. Anthropometrically, his height was 100 cm, and the left upper limb was 10 mm shorter than the right upper limb.  

Echocardiography showed structurally normal heart with RAA and aberrant LSA with flow turbulence. CT angiogram was done, which confirmed the diagnosis. A stenosis of 70-80% just beyond the origin of LSA was detected. The presence of limb length discrepancy justified treatment, and the patient was scheduled for transcatheter placement of stent. 

The procedure was performed successfully, and the patient tolerated it very well. Racer 5 mm × 18 mm stent was positioned across the stenosed segment. Post-procedure angiogram confirmed the stent's correct placement, and good flow was observed in LSA and left axillary artery. The patient was kept on overnight heparin infusion and discharged with oral aspirin.

Follow-up visits on 3 and 6 months showed near-normalization of radial pulse and blood pressure in the left upper limb, and significant improvement of limb length. USG of the left upper limb confirmed a laminar flow pattern in the LSA. The boy has been followed up for the last 4 years, with no remaining limb length discrepancy detected.

Discharge Diagnosis: Stenosis of Left Subclavian Artery.

Discharge Instructions: 

The patient will continue aspirin therapy (as directed by the physician). Follow-up visits should be scheduled as per the physician's instructions. Parents are advised to keep the patient's medical records updated and bring them to each visit. 

Signed, 

[Physician Name]
[Medical Institution]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Name]

Date of Admission: [Date of Admission]

Date of Discharge: [Date of Discharge]

Hospitalized for: Narrowing of the Left Arm Artery

Brief Hospital Course:

The patient, a 4-year-old boy, was admitted after doctors heard an unusual heart sound during a checkup. The doctor found that the pulse in the left arm was weak and blood pressure was low in that arm. The pulse in the right arm was normal. The patient was 100 cm tall. The left arm was 10 mm shorter than the right arm.

A heart ultrasound showed the heart structure was normal. However, it showed the main artery to the left arm was in an unusual position with uneven blood flow. A special scan of the blood vessels was done to confirm what was wrong. The scan showed the artery to the left arm was 70-80% blocked near where it starts. Because one arm was shorter than the other, treatment was needed. The patient was scheduled to have a small tube placed inside the artery to open it up.

The procedure went well, and the patient handled it without problems. A small metal tube was placed inside the narrowed part of the artery. Pictures taken after the procedure showed the tube was in the right place. Blood was flowing well through the left arm artery. The patient received blood-thinning medicine through an IV overnight. The patient went home with aspirin to take by mouth.

At check-up visits 3 and 6 months later, the pulse and blood pressure in the left arm were almost normal. The arm length had improved a lot. An ultrasound of the left arm showed smooth blood flow in the artery. The boy has been checked for 4 years now. There is no longer any difference in arm length.

Discharge Diagnosis: Narrowing of the Left Arm Artery.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient will keep taking aspirin as the doctor ordered. Follow-up visits should be scheduled as the doctor instructs. Parents should keep the patient's medical records up to date. Parents should bring these records to each visit.

Signed,

[Physician Name]
[Medical Institution]

Discharge Diagnosis: Narrowing of the Left Arm Artery.

Discharge Instructions:

The patient will keep taking aspirin as the doctor ordered. Follow-up visits should be scheduled as the doctor instructs. Parents should keep the patient's medical records up to date. Parents should bring these records to each visit.

Signed,

[Physician Name]
[Medical Institution]

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]

Medical Diagnosis:
The patient was admitted for acute limb ischemia and septic shock secondary to Streptococcus dysgalactiae bacteremia. A workup for infectious endocarditis was initiated and confirmed by transthoracic and transesophageal echocardiogram demonstrating aortic valve vegetations and severe aortic regurgitation.

Hospital Course:
The patient underwent emergent revascularization with left iliac thrombectomy and intravenous antibiotics with gentamicin for 10 days and ceftriaxone for six weeks. Cardiothoracic surgery was consulted, and the patient underwent aortic valve replacement surgery. Upon hemodynamic stabilization, he was discharged to the rehabilitation center one month after the initial hospitalization.

Summary of Admission:
A 56-year-old gentleman with a past medical history of untreated hepatitis C infection, osteomyelitis with right fourth toe amputation, and alcohol abuse presented with paresthesias and a pulseless left leg. Lab work demonstrated normocytic anemia, leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia, and creatinine elevation. Blood cultures were positive for Streptococcus dysgalactiae, and CT angiogram of the left leg demonstrated significant occlusions. The patient was treated for acute limb ischemia and septic shock and underwent an IE workup, which confirmed aortic valve vegetations.

Discharge Medications:
The patient's discharge medications were prescribed by the attending physician and are available in the patient's outpatient medical record.

Follow-up care:
The patient was advised to continue regular follow-up with his primary care physician and cardiothoracic surgeon. Further cardiac rehabilitation is recommended in coordination with the patient's medical team.

Discharge condition:
The patient was discharged with stable vital signs and improved clinical condition. The patient and family were advised to return to the hospital if any new or worsening symptoms persist.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
DOB: [redacted]
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]

Medical Diagnosis:
The patient was admitted because blood flow to the leg was suddenly blocked. The patient also had a serious body-wide infection caused by bacteria called Streptococcus dysgalactiae in the blood. Doctors checked for infection in the heart. Heart scans showed growths on the aortic valve and the valve was leaking badly.

Hospital Course:
The patient had emergency surgery to restore blood flow by removing a clot from the left hip area artery. The patient received antibiotics through a vein. Gentamicin was given for 10 days and ceftriaxone for six weeks. Heart surgeons were asked to help. The patient had surgery to replace the aortic valve. Once blood pressure and heart function became steady, the patient was sent to a rehab center. This was one month after first being admitted to the hospital.

Summary of Admission:
The patient is a 56-year-old man. His health history includes untreated hepatitis C liver infection and a bone infection that led to removal of his right fourth toe. He also has a history of alcohol abuse. He came to the hospital with tingling feelings and no pulse in his left leg. Blood tests showed low red blood cells, high white blood cells, low blood clotting cells, and poor kidney function. Blood tests found bacteria called Streptococcus dysgalactiae. A scan of the leg arteries showed major blockages. The patient was treated for blocked blood flow to the leg and serious body-wide infection. Testing confirmed growths on the heart valve.

Discharge Medications:
The patient's medications were ordered by the main doctor. They can be found in the patient's outpatient medical record.

Follow-up care:
The patient should keep seeing his regular doctor and heart surgeon. The patient should also do heart rehab exercises with help from his medical team.

Discharge condition:
The patient left the hospital with steady vital signs and better health. The patient and family were told to come back to the hospital if any new or worse symptoms continue.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission: 
A 65-year-old female with a history of Hashimoto's disease was admitted to the hospital for malaise and nausea of one-week duration without preceding infection. She had no psychiatric history. She presented with low serum levels of sodium on laboratory findings and inverted T waves on electrocardiogram (ECG).
 
Course: 
After admission, the patient's consciousness levels improved until she presented with paranoia and visual hallucinations four days later. She was delirious and was consulted by psychiatrists, who prescribed quetiapine for her delirium. Furthermore, on the same day, she complained of nausea and was pale and sweating. She was thought to be in a state of acute circulatory failure, and urgent coronary catherization was performed for possible percutaneous intervention. She was diagnosed with Takotsubo cardiomyopathy. Administration of hydrocortisone was initiated, and the hyponatremia and delirium improved dramatically in 3 days. An echocardiogram showed the left ventricular wall motion abnormalities including apical segment to be also markedly improved by 10 days after steroid administration.

Diagnosis: 
Endocrine studies showed decreases in serum cortisol and ACTH and reduced urinary secretion of free cortisol. It was found that the patient was suffering from isolated ACTH deficiency. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed no pituitary lesions or adjacent lesions.

Discharge: 
The patient was discharged in stable condition and instructed to continue taking oral hydrocortisone. She was advised to follow up with her primary care physician and endocrinologist to manage her isolated ACTH deficiency.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission:
A 65-year-old female with a history of thyroid disease was admitted to the hospital. She had been feeling tired and sick to her stomach for one week. She had no history of mental health problems. Her blood tests showed low sodium levels. Her heart test showed some unusual patterns.

Course:
After admission, the patient became more alert at first. Four days later, she started seeing things that were not there. She also became very suspicious of others. She was confused and restless. Mental health doctors were called to help. They gave her a medicine called quetiapine to help calm her confusion. On the same day, she felt sick to her stomach again. She became pale and started sweating. Doctors thought her heart might be failing. They did an emergency heart procedure to check her blood vessels. They found she had a heart condition called broken heart syndrome. This is a type of heart problem often caused by stress. Doctors gave her a steroid medicine called hydrocortisone. Within 3 days, her sodium levels and confusion got much better. A heart imaging test was done 10 days after starting the steroid. It showed her heart was pumping much better than before.

Diagnosis:
Blood tests showed her body was not making enough of certain stress hormones. These hormones are called cortisol and ACTH. Her urine tests also showed low levels of cortisol. Doctors found the patient had a rare condition. Her body could not make enough ACTH hormone. A brain scan was done to check the gland that makes this hormone. The scan showed no growths or problems in that area.

Discharge:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition. She was told to keep taking hydrocortisone by mouth. She was told to see her regular doctor for follow-up care. She was also told to see a hormone specialist to manage her ACTH hormone problem.

input:
Hospital Course:

47-year-old male was admitted following a car accident. The patient presented with forehead abrasion and confusion. A brain CT scan and cervical X-rays showed no hematoma or cerebral edema, but cervical instability at C6-7. The patient was immobilized with a neck collar and closely monitored. Two weeks after admission, the patient complained of severe posterior neck pain (VAS > 9) and bilateral upper extremity numbness. Subsequent surgical stabilization was performed. An anterior cervical plate was positioned, and pilot holes were tapped. The upper left pilot hole was stripped due to repeated drilling, and PMMA augmentation was performed, which resulted in a mass lesion at the spinal canal. C6 corpectomy was promptly performed to remove the mass. Anterior reconstruction with an anterior cervical plate and mesh cage from C5 through C7 was done. Postoperatively, the patient had left side weakness and impairment of the left 4th and 5th digit extension, but he improved without intervention.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Cervical injury with spinal cord compression leading to cervical corpectomy.

Hospital course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital following a car accident. During his hospital stay, he underwent surgical stabilization for cervical instability at C6-7. There were some complications during the procedure, which led to a mass lesion at the spinal canal and cervical corpectomy. The patient had some postoperative weakness and impairment of the left hand digits, but he improved without intervention. The patient was discharged in good condition with a follow-up appointment with a spinal specialist.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

A 47-year-old male was admitted after a car accident. The patient had a cut on the forehead and was confused. A brain scan and neck X-rays showed no bleeding or swelling in the brain. However, the images showed the neck bones were not stable at the lower neck area. The patient wore a neck brace and was watched closely. Two weeks after being admitted, the patient reported very bad pain in the back of the neck. The patient also had numbness in both arms and hands. Surgery was done to fix the unstable neck bones. During surgery, a metal plate was placed on the front of the neck. Holes were drilled to hold the plate in place. One hole in the upper left area was damaged from drilling too many times. Bone cement was used to fix this problem. This caused a lump to form in the space around the spinal cord. The surgical team quickly removed part of the damaged neck bone to take out the lump. The neck was rebuilt using a metal plate and a support cage from the fifth to seventh neck bones. After surgery, the patient had weakness on the left side. The patient also had trouble straightening the ring and pinky fingers on the left hand. These problems got better on their own without extra treatment.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Neck injury with pressure on the spinal cord. This led to surgery to remove part of a neck bone.

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the hospital after a car accident. During the hospital stay, the patient had surgery to fix unstable bones in the lower neck. There were some problems during the surgery. These problems caused a lump near the spinal cord. Part of a neck bone had to be removed. After surgery, the patient had some weakness and trouble moving fingers on the left hand. These problems got better without extra treatment. The patient left the hospital in good condition. The patient has a follow-up visit scheduled with a spine doctor.

input:
Hospital Course:

This 60-year-old male patient presented to our outpatient clinic with a 1-year history of generalized yellowish ovoid skin-colored papular eruption located on the trunk, groin, and extremities. The patient reported that he first noticed pruritic papules, and 2–3 months later many yellow and ovoid pruritic papules emerged. On skin physical examination, firm, well-defined ovoid skin-colored to yellow papules were seen on the distal and proximal of upper and lower extremities, trunk, groins, and abdomen.

Diagnostic laboratory tests such as full blood cell count with differential, peripheral blood smear, renal, and liver function tests all were normal. Histopathological examination of the skin biopsy from one of the lesion on the trunk revealed proliferation of mast cells with ovoid and spindle nuclei with distinct cytoplasm borders around capillaries, which was compatible with mastocytosis. Based on concurrent clinical and pathological features, we diagnosed our case as xanthelasmoid mastocytosis.

Treatment with H1 antihistamine, cetirizine 10 mg orally nightly and fexofenadine 120 mg for every morning was prescribed for pruritus control which was successful, but eruptions were persistent without any changes. Treatment with phototherapy also recommended to the patient, but despite a 1-year course, no significant improvement was seen in the lesions.

The patient refused further investigations such as bone marrow biopsy or alternative treatments and decided to end the hospital course. The patient was advised to monitor the lesions and seek medical attention if he noticed any changes or deterioration. The patient was discharged with no complications.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

This 60-year-old male patient came to our outpatient clinic with skin bumps that had lasted for 1 year. The bumps were yellow, oval-shaped, and skin-colored. They appeared on the chest, back, groin area, arms, and legs. The patient said he first noticed itchy bumps. Then 2 to 3 months later, many more yellow and oval itchy bumps appeared. During the skin exam, doctors found firm, clear-edged, oval bumps. These bumps were skin-colored to yellow. They were located on the upper and lower arms and legs, chest, back, groin area, and belly.

Lab tests checked the blood cells, kidney function, and liver function. All test results came back normal. Doctors took a small skin sample from one bump on the trunk. The sample showed too many mast cells, which are a type of immune cell. These cells had oval and long-shaped centers with clear edges. They were found around tiny blood vessels. This finding matched a condition called mastocytosis. Based on the skin exam and lab results together, doctors determined the patient had a rare type called xanthelasmoid mastocytosis.

The patient received two allergy medicines to control the itching. Cetirizine 10 mg was taken by mouth each night. Fexofenadine 120 mg was taken each morning. These medicines helped stop the itching. However, the skin bumps stayed the same without any changes. Doctors also suggested light therapy treatment. The patient tried this for 1 year, but the bumps did not get much better.

The patient chose not to have more tests like a bone marrow sample. The patient also chose not to try other treatments. The patient decided to end care at this time. The patient was told to watch the bumps and see a doctor if they change or get worse. The patient left the hospital with no problems.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age: 32

Gender: Male

Hospital: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]

Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Hepatic flexure mass

Primary Care Physician: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE

The patient was admitted to the hospital for an elective laparotomy to excise a hepatic flexure mass. The surgery was successful, and the patient was intubated with an 8.5 mm ETT on the first attempt. Hoarseness of voice was noted three hours after extubation.

An IDL revealed right vocal cord palsy, which resolved spontaneously after 40 days. The patient was closely monitored by the healthcare team during their hospital stay and received appropriate care and treatment.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS

The patient has been successfully discharged from the hospital and should follow up with their primary care physician for any further evaluation or treatment related to the hoarseness of voice. No other specific instructions or precautions are necessary at this time.

FOLLOW-UP

The patient is advised to follow up with their primary care physician for routine care, including any follow-up related to the hoarseness of voice. If the patient experiences any new or worsening symptoms, they should seek medical attention immediately.

SUMMARY

The patient was admitted to the hospital for an elective laparotomy to excise a hepatic flexure mass. During their hospital stay, the patient experienced hoarseness of voice, which resolved spontaneously after 40 days following IDL revealing right vocal cord palsy. The patient has been successfully discharged from the hospital and is advised to follow up with their primary care physician for routine care and further evaluation as needed.

Age: 32

Gender: Male

Hospital: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]

Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Growth near the liver area of the large intestine

Primary Care Physician: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE

The patient came to the hospital for a planned surgery. The surgery was done to remove a growth near the liver area of the large intestine. The surgery went well. During the surgery, a breathing tube was placed in the airway on the first try. Three hours after the breathing tube was removed, the patient had a hoarse voice.

A special camera was used to look at the throat. It showed that the right vocal cord was not moving properly. This problem went away on its own after 40 days. The care team watched the patient closely during the hospital stay. The patient received proper care and treatment.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS

The patient has left the hospital and is doing well. The patient should see their regular doctor for any further checkups or treatment for the hoarse voice. No other special instructions or safety steps are needed at this time.

FOLLOW-UP

The patient should see their regular doctor for normal care. This includes any checkups related to the hoarse voice. If the patient has any new symptoms or if symptoms get worse, they should get medical help right away.

SUMMARY

The patient should see their regular doctor for normal care and any further checkups as needed.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient: 4-month-old male intact Labrador

Hospital Course:

The patient presented with several small skin lacerations of the distal limbs, hock, and tail base. On physical examination, the dog was found to be clinically healthy with normal skeletal development. Several seroma-like swellings were noted in the regions of skin trauma (hocks, tail base). Aspirates taken from these swellings revealed moderate numbers of erythrocytes with fewer neutrophils and macrophages with proteinaceous debris. These findings were consistent with a seroma or hematoma formation, and the dog was treated with amoxycillin/clavulanic acid and firocoxib.

Two months after initial presentation, the patient was examined for ongoing seroma formation and joint abnormalities. Evidence of generalized joint hyperextensibility with a range of motion greater than 180° and skin hyperextensibility and fragility was noted. The dog had a skin extensibility test of 23.5% (normal < 14.5%). A complete blood count revealed mild anemia and serum biochemistry showed a mild hypoalbuminemia and hypoproteinemia. Other serum abnormalities included a moderate hyperphosphatemia, mild elevation in ALP, and decrease in creatinine that were all considered within normal limits for a young dog.

Discharge Diagnosis:

The patient was suspected to have a genetic collagen disease.

Treatment:

The patient was treated with amoxycillin/clavulanic acid and firocoxib. The owners elected to humanely euthanize the patient due to the ongoing joint hypermobility and skin lacerations.

Follow-up Plan:

None.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient: 4-month-old male unneutered Labrador

Hospital Course:

The patient came in with several small skin cuts on the lower legs, ankles, and base of the tail. During the exam, the dog appeared healthy with normal bone growth. Several fluid-filled bumps were found near the injured skin areas (ankles, tail base). Fluid samples from these bumps showed a moderate amount of red blood cells with some white blood cells and cell debris. These results suggested the bumps were pockets of blood or fluid, and the dog was given an antibiotic and a pain and swelling medication.

Two months later, the patient was seen again for ongoing fluid pockets and joint problems. The joints were found to bend too far, moving beyond the normal range. The skin also stretched too much and tore easily. A skin stretch test showed 23.5% stretch, which is higher than the normal limit of 14.5%. Blood tests showed slightly low red blood cells, low protein levels, and low albumin (a type of protein). Other blood results showed high phosphorus, slightly high liver enzyme levels, and low waste product levels. These other results were considered normal for a young dog.

Discharge Diagnosis:

The patient was believed to have an inherited disease affecting the body's connective tissue.

Treatment:

The patient received an antibiotic and a pain and swelling medication. The owners chose to humanely put the patient to sleep because of the ongoing joint looseness and skin cuts.

Follow-up Plan:

None.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the tertiary endometriosis centre with complaints of deep dyspareunia and abdominal pain. Physical examination, vaginal ultrasound, and MRI results indicated the presence of a deep endometriotic lesion at the top of the vagina. The patient underwent laparoscopic resection of the lesion using Sonicision. Post-procedure, the patient presented to the emergency department with vaginal pain and a bloody vaginal discharge after sexual intercourse. Examination revealed a vaginal cuff dehiscence, and a small loop of small intestine was visible in the upper vagina. The peritoneum was closed and the small bowel was repositioned. The patient returned for postoperative check-up and presented with a vaginal cuff dehiscence, which was found to be partly open, and the peritoneum was visible. A different technique was employed for a second repair, namely, to refresh the vaginal edges and perform a two-layer closure of the vagina with the subsequent employment of an omental flap. The omental flap was placed in the pelvis and was attached tension-free to the vaginal cuff closure by interrupted sutures.

Diagnosis:
- Deep endometriotic lesion at the top of the vagina with vaginal cuff dehiscence.

Treatment:
- Laparoscopic resection of the endometriotic lesion using Sonicision.
- Repositioning of the small bowel.
- Refreshment of the vaginal edges and two-layer closure of the vagina with the subsequent employment of an omental flap.

Condition at Discharge:
- Stable.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient was advised to attend follow-up care appointments, undergo regular check-ups, and adhere to the prescribed treatment plan.

Discharge Medications:
None prescribed.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was advised to avoid sexual intercourse for six weeks following the second repair. Additionally, the patient was urged to monitor for any signs of recurrent vaginal cuff dehiscence. The patient was advised to attend the scheduled follow-up appointments. In case of an emergency, the patient was instructed to seek medical attention immediately.

Primary Care Physician:
[REDACTED]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to a specialized center for a condition where tissue similar to the uterus lining grows in other places. The patient had pain during sex and belly pain. A physical exam, vaginal ultrasound, and MRI scan showed a growth deep in the tissue at the top of the vagina. The patient had surgery through small cuts in the belly to remove the growth using a special cutting tool. After the surgery, the patient came to the emergency room with vaginal pain and bloody discharge after having sex. The exam showed that the surgical cut at the top of the vagina had opened up. A small part of the intestine could be seen through the opening. Doctors closed the inner lining and put the intestine back in place. The patient came back for a check-up. The surgical cut at the top of the vagina had partly opened again. The inner lining could be seen through the opening. Doctors used a different method to fix it a second time. They trimmed the edges of the vaginal tissue to help it heal better. They closed the vagina in two layers. They also used a piece of fatty tissue from the belly to cover and protect the repair. This tissue was placed in the pelvis and attached loosely to the closure with stitches.

Diagnosis:
Deep tissue growth at the top of the vagina with opening of the surgical cut.

Treatment:
Surgery through small belly cuts to remove the tissue growth using a special cutting tool. Putting the intestine back in place. Trimming the vaginal edges and closing the vagina in two layers with a piece of fatty belly tissue to protect the repair.

Condition at Discharge:
Stable.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient was told to come to follow-up appointments, have regular check-ups, and follow the treatment plan.

Discharge Medications:
None prescribed.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was told not to have sex for six weeks after the second repair. The patient was told to watch for any signs that the surgical cut at the top of the vagina might open again. The patient was told to come to the scheduled follow-up appointments. If there is an emergency, the patient was told to get medical help right away.

input:
Discharge Summary:

This is a discharge summary for a 50-year-old male with a history of diabetes, hypertension and end-stage renal disease. He was admitted to the hospital in December 2020 with fever, decreased urine output, and right sub-costal pain. Initial investigations revealed a significant increase in white blood cell count and C-reactive protein levels. 

A CT scan of the abdomen showed multiple enlarged necrotic lymph nodes in the peripancreatic and portocaval areas with an increase in size compared to a previous scan in March 2020. The patient was referred for an EUS-guided biopsy of the peripancreatic lymph node mass. Histopathology revealed the presence of TB with multiple granulomas composed of epithelioid histiocytes and giant cells. MTB-PCR confirmed the diagnosis of TB. 

The patient was commenced on anti-tuberculosis treatment which significantly improved his condition. Repeat CT scans suggest interval reduction in the sizes of the centrally necrotic lymph nodes in peripancreatic and portocaval locations. Clinically, the patient had improved significantly with no further episodes of pyrexia or abdominal pain. Renal transplantation is planned for the patient once he completes treatment for TB. 

Overall, the patient was discharged with an improved condition and treated for TB. Further follow up is necessary to ensure full recovery.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

This is a discharge summary for a 50-year-old male with a history of diabetes, high blood pressure, and kidney failure. He was admitted to the hospital in December 2020 with fever, less urine than normal, and pain on the right side below the ribs. First tests showed a big increase in infection-fighting cells and swelling markers in the blood.

A scan of the belly showed several swollen lymph nodes with dead tissue near the pancreas and liver. These nodes had grown larger compared to an earlier scan in March 2020. The patient was sent for a test where doctors used a camera and needle to take a small tissue sample from the swollen lymph nodes near the pancreas. The tissue sample showed signs of tuberculosis with clusters of special cells that form when the body fights this infection. A lab test on the tissue confirmed the patient had tuberculosis.

The patient started taking medicine to treat tuberculosis and this helped him feel much better. Follow-up scans showed the swollen lymph nodes near the pancreas and liver had gotten smaller. The patient felt much better with no more fevers or belly pain. A kidney transplant is planned for the patient after he finishes his tuberculosis treatment.

Overall, the patient was discharged in better health after being treated for tuberculosis. More doctor visits are needed to make sure the patient fully recovers.

input:
Discharge Summary: Patient 2

Patient 2, a 45-year-old female, was admitted to hospital due to headache and vomiting in February 2016. Imaging examinations showed an intracranial mass, and the patient received neurosurgical resection of the space-occupying. Postoperative pathological examination indicated non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL). In March 2016, she received whole brain radiotherapy. Cranial MRI after radiotherapy revealed a decrease in the left parietal lobe, basal ganglia, and corpus callosum lesions. In May 2016, the patient underwent chemotherapy for 2 cycles. Two weeks after treatment, she experienced right weakness that gradually progressed to right hemiplegia with muscle strength at 0 level. Due to little symptomatic improvement from conventional therapy of mannitol and dexamethasone for dehydration, cranial MRI was performed, which indicated an increase in left parietal lobe edema.

In July 2016, the patient was started on oral apatinib, which showed remarkable improvement in right hemiplegia symptoms, and her muscle strength was restored to normal. Headache, nausea, and vomiting disappeared. Further details regarding the patient's medical history, clinical course, laboratory test results, medications, and imaging studies can be found in the patient's medical record.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary: Patient 2

Patient 2 is a 45-year-old woman. She came to the hospital in February 2016 with headaches and vomiting. Scans of her head showed a growth inside her brain. Doctors did surgery to remove the growth. Tests on the removed tissue showed a type of cancer called non-Hodgkin lymphoma. This is a cancer that affects the body's infection-fighting system.

In March 2016, the patient had radiation treatment to her whole brain. A brain scan after treatment showed the affected areas had gotten smaller. These areas were in the left side of her brain and in parts that connect the two sides.

In May 2016, the patient had two rounds of cancer-killing drug treatment. Two weeks later, the right side of her body became weak. This got worse until she could not move her right side at all. Doctors gave her medicines to reduce brain swelling. These medicines did not help much. A brain scan showed more swelling in the left side of her brain.

In July 2016, the patient started taking a pill called apatinib. Her right side weakness got much better. She could move normally again. Her headaches, upset stomach, and vomiting went away.

More information about the patient's health history, treatment, test results, medicines, and scans is in her medical record.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was a 50-year-old Chinese male who presented with cough and expectoration of black phlegm for about 2 weeks, sometimes accompanied by bloody sputum and breathlessness. A computed tomography (CT) scan of his chest revealed a rough-edged shadow measuring nearly 32×22×10 mm in the right upper lobe of his lung. The patient was diagnosed with a giant cell carcinoma-like tumor (GCCL) of the lung after undergoing a right-sided upper lobectomy and systematic mediastinal lymphadenectomy. Histopathologic examination of the tumor cells revealed a positive reaction for cytokeratin (CK) and vimentin, while immunostaining for chromogranin A (CgA) and synaptophysin (Syn) was negative.

The patient had several chronic disorders, including a 10-year history of bronchitis and emphysema, and was a smoker with a 30-pack-year history, but had no family history of lung cancer. He underwent combined chemotherapy using gemcitabine and cisplatin for four cycles and was followed up with whole-body radiography examinations every year in a local hospital. As of 7 years after surgery, he remained alive and free from disease progression.

The patient's hospital course was notable for his successful treatment of GCCL, good response to treatment, and his long-term follow-up. The patient was able to communicate clearly and effectively with the healthcare team during his stay. He was compliant with his treatment regimen and was actively involved in discharge planning. The healthcare team discussed the importance of smoking cessation and provided resources for smoking cessation for the patient.

Diagnoses:

1. Giant cell carcinoma-like tumor (GCCL) of the lung.

Discharge Medications:

None.

Follow-Up Care:

The patient is advised to continue yearly follow-up visits with a local hospital to monitor his condition. He is also encouraged to continue smoking cessation efforts.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was a 50-year-old Chinese male who came in with a cough and black mucus for about 2 weeks. Sometimes the patient also coughed up blood and had trouble breathing. A special X-ray scan of the chest showed a rough-edged mass about 32×22×10 mm in the upper part of the right lung. The patient had surgery to remove the upper part of the right lung and nearby lymph nodes. After surgery, doctors found a rare type of lung cancer called giant cell carcinoma-like tumor. Lab tests on the tumor cells showed positive results for certain markers called cytokeratin and vimentin. Tests for other markers called chromogranin A and synaptophysin were negative.

The patient had long-term health problems, including 10 years of swollen airways and damaged air sacs in the lungs. The patient smoked for many years, equal to one pack a day for 30 years. No one in the patient's family had lung cancer before. The patient received cancer-fighting drugs called gemcitabine and cisplatin for four rounds of treatment. The patient had full body scans every year at a local hospital. Seven years after surgery, the patient was still alive with no signs of the cancer coming back.

The patient did well during the hospital stay. The treatment worked well, and the patient was watched closely over many years. The patient talked clearly with the care team during the stay. The patient followed the treatment plan and helped plan for going home. The care team talked about the need to stop smoking and gave the patient information on how to quit.

Diagnoses:

1. Giant cell carcinoma-like tumor of the lung, a rare type of lung cancer.

Discharge Medications:

None.

Follow-Up Care:

The patient should keep going to yearly check-up visits at a local hospital to watch for any changes. The patient should also keep trying to stop smoking.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
- Name: [REDACTED]
- Age: 30 years
- Gender: Female
- Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
- Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
- Diagnosis: Acute T cell lymphoblastic leukemia

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the Department of Radiology at Ondokuz Mayis University Hospital due to palpable masses in both breasts. The mammograms revealed ill-defined, denser mass-like lesions in upper outer quadrants of both breasts and an ultrasonographic evaluation showed lobular-shaped and partly ill-defined hypoechoic masses. Both axilla indicated pathologically enlarged lymph nodes with skin thickening, increased echo texture in the breast parenchyma, and fine fluid between the fat lobules, which were consistent with edema of the breasts. A bone marrow biopsy confirmed a diagnosis of acute T cell lymphoblastic leukemia. The patient received chemotherapy which resulted in the disappearance of the breast lesions and decreased number and size of axillary lymph nodes.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient's condition significantly improved after receiving chemotherapy. The patient was deemed ready for discharge.

Follow-up:
The patient is advised to follow-up as scheduled with their oncologist for further management and evaluation. Appropriate imaging studies and laboratory tests may be repeated if clinically indicated. The patient is advised to maintain a healthy lifestyle, avoid exposure to sick individuals and engage in regular exercise. 

Disposition:
The patient was discharged to their residence with instructions on medication regimen and follow-up care.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 30 years
Gender: Female
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Diagnosis: A fast-growing blood cancer that starts in certain white blood cells called T cells

Hospital Course:
The patient came to Ondokuz Mayis University Hospital because of lumps that could be felt in both breasts. Breast x-rays showed areas that looked denser than normal in the upper outer parts of both breasts. An ultrasound test showed lumps with uneven edges. Both armpits had swollen lymph nodes, thicker skin, and signs of fluid buildup in the breast tissue. This pointed to swelling in the breasts. A test of the bone marrow showed the patient had a fast-growing blood cancer in certain white blood cells. The patient received cancer-fighting drugs. After treatment, the breast lumps went away. The swollen lymph nodes in the armpits also got smaller and fewer in number.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient got much better after receiving the cancer-fighting drugs. The patient was ready to go home.

Follow-up:
The patient should see their cancer doctor at the scheduled times for more care and checkups. The patient may need more imaging tests and blood tests if the doctor thinks they are needed. The patient should stay healthy, stay away from people who are sick, and exercise regularly.

Disposition:
The patient went home with instructions about how to take medicines and when to come back for checkups.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: XXX

Date of Admission: XX/XX/XXXX              Date of Discharge: XX/XX/XXXX

Admitting Diagnosis: Confusion

Hospital Course:

A 62-year-old African-American male with past medical history of schizophrenia and confusion was admitted to the emergency department. On physical examination, he was lethargic, hypotensive, hypoglycemic, and tachycardic. Initial laboratory studies revealed severe anemia, cytopenia, high serum ferritin level, and elevated soluble CD25. A bone marrow biopsy demonstrated hypercellular bone marrow with erythroid hyperplasia and rare hemophagocytic cells, raising the concern for HLH. A transthoracic echocardiogram was negative for vegetations and infectious workup was negative for viral respiratory PCR, HIV serology, acute infectious hepatitis, cytomegalovirus PCR, and herpes simplex virus (HSV).

The patient was treated with dexamethasone due to underlying severe liver dysfunction, leading to significant clinical improvement. The patient was extubated and steroids were gradually tapered per protocol. His liver enzymes, coagulation studies, platelet count, and ferritin level improved gradually. After 31 days of hospitalization, the patient was discharged home.

Follow-Up Plan:

The patient was advised to follow up with his primary care physician for further management and was provided with a list of his current medications. The patient was informed to schedule a follow-up visit with his primary care physician for a re-evaluation of his medical status, including the need for renal replacement therapy.

Instructions to the Patient:

The patient was advised to continue taking his medications as directed and to follow up with his primary care physician for regular check-ups. The patient was advised to adopt a healthy lifestyle and to maintain regular physical exercise to better manage his condition and prevent further complications.

Summary:

The patient was diagnosed with HLH and was treated with dexamethasone. After a 31-day hospitalization, the patient was discharged home with follow-up instructions for with his primary care physician. The patient was advised to continue his medications and to maintain a healthy lifestyle to manage his condition and prevent further complications.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: XXX

Date of Admission: XX/XX/XXXX              Date of Discharge: XX/XX/XXXX

Admitting Diagnosis: Confusion

Hospital Course:

A 62-year-old African-American male with a history of schizophrenia and confusion was brought to the emergency room. During the exam, he was very sleepy, had low blood pressure, low blood sugar, and a fast heartbeat. Blood tests showed very low red blood cell counts, low blood cell counts overall, high iron storage levels, and high levels of a protein called CD25. A test of the bone marrow showed too many cells and signs that the body was attacking its own blood cells. This raised concern for a condition called HLH, where the immune system attacks the body. A heart ultrasound showed no signs of infection on the heart valves. Tests for viruses, HIV, hepatitis, and other infections all came back negative.

The patient was given a steroid medicine called dexamethasone because his liver was not working well. This treatment helped him get much better. The patient was taken off the breathing machine. The steroid medicine was slowly reduced over time. His liver test results, blood clotting tests, platelet counts, and iron levels all got better over time. After 31 days in the hospital, the patient was sent home.

Follow-Up Plan:

The patient was told to see his regular doctor for ongoing care. He was given a list of his current medicines. The patient was told to schedule a visit with his regular doctor to check on his health. This includes checking if he needs treatment to help his kidneys work.

Instructions to the Patient:

The patient was told to keep taking his medicines as directed. He should see his regular doctor for routine check-ups. The patient was told to live a healthy lifestyle and exercise regularly. This will help manage his condition and prevent future problems.

Summary:

The patient was found to have HLH, a condition where the immune system attacks the body. He was treated with a steroid medicine called dexamethasone. After 31 days in the hospital, the patient was sent home. He was told to follow up with his regular doctor. The patient was told to keep taking his medicines and live a healthy lifestyle to manage his condition and prevent future problems.

input:
Hospital Course:

The ten-year-old female patient was admitted with complaints of difficulty in walking and stiffness of both lower limbs, for the past six months. On examination, she had cog-wheel rigidity, increased tone in all the four limbs, power weakness of 4/5 in the lower limbs, and postural tremor and dystonia. Her deep tendon reflexes were exaggerated, while the sensory system, cranial nerve examination, and cerebellar signs were normal. She was diagnosed with dopa-responsive dystonia (DRD) due to the typical diurnal variation of dystonia, and a therapeutic challenge with levodopa/carbidopa was tried, which resulted in a significant decrease in dystonia within two days. Hence, she was treated using a combination of Levodopa, Carbidopa, and Trihexyphenidyl.

Discharge Summary:

Ten-year-old female patient was admitted with complaints of difficulty walking and stiffness in both lower limbs that led to frequent falls. She had cog-wheel rigidity, increased tone, power weakness, postural tremor, and dystonia in all four limbs, with exaggerated deep tendon reflexes. Her sensory system, cranial nerve examination, and cerebellar examination were normal. The patient was diagnosed with DRD as she showed diurnal variation of dystonia and improved significantly after Levodopa/carbidopa therapy. The medication was continued, and the patient showed persistent improvement. The genetic study was planned but could not be conducted due to resource constraints. The patient was discharged in good condition with a plan for follow-up.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The ten-year-old female patient came to the hospital because she had trouble walking and stiffness in both legs for the past six months. During the physical exam, doctors found her muscles were stiff and jerky when moved. All four limbs had higher than normal muscle tightness. Her leg strength was slightly weak. She also had shaking when holding a position and muscle spasms that caused twisting movements. Her reflexes were stronger than normal when tested. Her ability to feel touch, her brain nerve function, and her balance were all normal. The patient was diagnosed with a movement disorder that responds to medicine called dopa-responsive dystonia. Doctors knew this because her symptoms got worse as the day went on. They gave her a test dose of levodopa and carbidopa medicine. Her muscle spasms got much better within two days. She was treated with three medicines called Levodopa, Carbidopa, and Trihexyphenidyl.

Discharge Summary:

The ten-year-old female patient came to the hospital with trouble walking and stiff legs that caused her to fall often. Her muscles were stiff and jerky when moved. She had increased muscle tightness, slight weakness, shaking when holding still, and twisting muscle spasms in all four limbs. Her reflexes were stronger than normal. Her ability to feel touch, brain nerve function, and balance were all normal. The patient was diagnosed with dopa-responsive dystonia. Her symptoms got worse as the day went on. She got much better after taking levodopa and carbidopa medicine. The medicine was continued and the patient kept getting better. A gene test was planned but could not be done because of limited resources. The patient left the hospital in good condition and will come back for a follow-up visit.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, an 86-year-old male, was admitted to our pain-clinic for chronic low back pain and left lower extremity pain for 2 years. The patient reported inadequate pain relief (<50%) and unwarranted side effects with oral analgesics. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the lumbar spine had revealed spinal stenosis, facet degenerative changes, and spondylosis. Per neurosurgery consult, no further surgery was indicated. 

Assessment and Plan:
Upon evaluation, patient was determined to have failed back surgery syndrome with left sacroiliac joint tenderness. Long-term pain relief was defined as pain relief >50% for at least 2 weeks post-injection, and at least 3 months post- Radiofrequency ablation (RFA). However, patient reported absence of any such pain relief. 

Disposition:
The patient was discharged with no further interventions or invasive procedures recommended. The patient was instructed to follow-up with his primary care physician for management of his chronic pain.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is an 86-year-old man. He came to our pain clinic for ongoing lower back pain and pain in his left leg. He has had this pain for 2 years. Pain medicines taken by mouth did not help enough. They also caused unwanted side effects. A special scan of his lower back showed narrowing of the spine, worn-out joints, and breakdown of the spine bones. A nerve and spine doctor said that surgery would not help.

Assessment and Plan:
The care team found that the patient still has pain after past back surgery. He also has soreness in the joint where the left hip meets the spine. Good pain relief would mean the pain is cut in half or more. This relief should last at least 2 weeks after a pain shot. It should last at least 3 months after a heat treatment to block pain nerves. The patient said he did not get this level of pain relief.

Disposition:
The patient was sent home. No more treatments or procedures were recommended. The patient was told to see his regular doctor to help manage his ongoing pain.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Identification:
The patient is a 38-year-old male who presented with a painful arthritis of the left wrist.

Admission and Hospital Course:
Upon admission, the patient's left wrist was examined and MSU crystals were observed upon aspiration. The patient had a past history of gout affecting the ankles and left knee and had a previous road traffic accident with a fracture of the left wrist. The initial treatment with Colchicine (2 mg/day) was not effective in controlling the gout symptoms. Anakinra was administered and the patient had a rapid clinical response. On follow-up at day 7 after the first injection, the left wrist was still slightly swollen and tender, but the ankle and knee were asymptomatic. The patient was subsequently started on allopurinol.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with gout.

Treatment:
The patient was treated with Anakinra and allopurinol.

Discharge Condition:
The patient's left wrist had improved, showing only slight swelling and tenderness upon discharge. The patient was advised to continue taking allopurinol and was instructed to maintain a follow-up appointment with the treating physician.

Follow-Up Care:
The patient was advised to consult with the treating physician regarding further management and treatment of his gout condition if needed.

Prognosis:
The patient's prognosis was considered to be favorable given the positive response to Anakinra and the asymptomatic ankles and left knee.

Other:
The patient's medical history was reviewed and is relevant to his current condition. The patient did not report any drug or food allergies. Medication reconciliation was carried out to ensure accuracy of prescribed medication upon discharge.

The patient was told to see his doctor about further care and treatment for his gout if needed.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 5-year-old Chinese woman, presented to the emergency department with complaints of cough, fever, headache, vomiting, and altered interactions with her parents. She was admitted to the pediatric intensive care unit and diagnosed with sepsis and pneumonia. Empiric antibiotic therapy, immunoglobulin supply, and restricted fluid administration were given. The patient's condition deteriorated, and she became comatose, hypotensive, and suffered from arrhythmia and hypoxia. Electrocardiogram confirmed prolonged Q–T interval while the thyroid test results were received, indicating untreated hypothyroidism secondary to autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's thyroiditis). Hormone replacement was immediately started, and the patient was administered levothyroxine via nasogastric tube. She also received vasopressor, mechanical ventilation, and continuous renal replacement treatment. Two days after starting levothyroxine, her consciousness was restored, and temperature returned to normal. She was extubated on the fifth day after the initial hormone administration. The dosage of oral levothyroxine was reduced, and she was discharged on a regimen of 3 μg/kg/d once daily.

Discharge Summary:
The patient, a 5-year-old Chinese woman, was admitted to the pediatric intensive care unit with symptoms of cough, fever, headache, vomiting, and altered interactions with her parents. She developed septicemia and pneumonia and was treated with empiric antibiotic therapy, immunoglobulin supply, and restricted fluid administration. She later deteriorated and became comatose, hypotensive, and suffered from arrhythmia and hypoxia. Her condition was diagnosed with untreated hypothyroidism secondary to autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's thyroiditis), and hormone replacement was immediately initiated. The patient received levothyroxine, vasopressor, mechanical ventilation, and continuous renal replacement treatment. After two days of hormone therapy, her consciousness was restored, and temperature returned to normal. She was extubated on the fifth day after the initial hormone administration and discharged on a regimen of 3 μg/kg/d once daily. The patient's family was recommended to undergo thyroid tests, and her father and grandmother were confirmed with Hashimoto's thyroiditis as well.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 5-year-old Chinese girl, came to the emergency room with cough, fever, headache, vomiting, and unusual behavior with her parents. She was placed in the children's intensive care unit. Doctors found she had a serious blood infection and a lung infection. She received antibiotics to fight infection, medicine to boost her immune system, and limited fluids. The patient got worse and fell into a deep sleep state where she could not wake up. Her blood pressure dropped too low. Her heart beat in an uneven pattern and her oxygen levels fell too low. A heart test showed her heart's electrical signals were too slow. Blood tests showed she had an underactive thyroid gland that was never treated. This was caused by her own immune system attacking her thyroid gland. Doctors quickly started giving her thyroid medicine called levothyroxine through a tube in her nose to her stomach. She also received medicine to raise her blood pressure, a machine to help her breathe, and a machine to help clean her blood like her kidneys would. Two days after starting the thyroid medicine, she woke up and her body temperature went back to normal. The breathing tube was removed on the fifth day after starting the thyroid medicine. Her thyroid medicine dose was lowered before she went home. She left the hospital taking thyroid medicine once a day at a dose of 3 micrograms per kilogram per day.

Discharge Summary:

Doctors found she had an underactive thyroid gland that was never treated. This was caused by her own immune system attacking her thyroid gland. Doctors quickly started giving her thyroid medicine. Two days after starting the thyroid medicine, she woke up and her body temperature went back to normal. The breathing tube was removed on the fifth day after starting the thyroid medicine. She left the hospital taking thyroid medicine once a day at a dose of 3 micrograms per kilogram per day. Doctors told the family to get their thyroid glands tested. Her father and grandmother were found to have the same thyroid condition.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Undisclosed

Date of Admission: Undisclosed

Date of Discharge: Undisclosed

Admission Diagnosis: Discharging sinuses over the lower anterior abdominal wall

Discharge Diagnosis: Dual infection caused by Mycobacterium szulgai and M. intermedium

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 46-year-old female with a history of laparoscopic appendectomy in 1998, presented with discharging sinuses over the lower anterior abdominal wall for the past two years. The initial incision wound did not heal properly and was later reopened after one month. Despite receiving treatment off and on with multiple injectable and oral antibiotics, there was no definitive microbiological diagnosis prior to admission. The patient did not have any history of local trauma/injury, immunosuppressive drugs/corticosteroids, or tuberculosis.

On admission, a previous surgical scar was noticed on the lower anterior abdominal wall in the midline with a large keloid formed over it. Six sinus openings were noted over the lower anterior abdominal wall with a single sinus discharging pus at the time of presentation. Tenderness was noted over and around the sinuses. Gram variable bacilli and numerous acid-fast bacilli were observed in the discharge. Following culture and antibiotic susceptibility testing, the patient was started on a combination of rifampicin, ethambutol, and clarithromycin.

After one month of therapy, there was no pus discharge from the sinus. The patient was advised to continue the therapy and report back to the hospital after another month. After two months of therapy, the patient was found to be completely all right with no such discharge.

Discharge Medications:

The patient was advised to continue taking rifampicin 600 mg once daily, ethambutol 600 mg once daily, and clarithromycin 500 mg twice daily.

Follow-Up:

The patient was advised to continue with periodic follow-up based upon clinical response and microbiological response.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Undisclosed

Date of Admission: Undisclosed

Date of Discharge: Undisclosed

Admission Diagnosis: Draining wounds on the lower front part of the belly

Discharge Diagnosis: Infection caused by two types of bacteria called Mycobacterium szulgai and M. intermedium

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 46-year-old woman. She had surgery to remove her appendix using small cuts in 1998. For the past two years, she had draining wounds on the lower front part of her belly. The cut from her surgery did not heal the right way. It opened up again after one month. She took many antibiotic medicines by mouth and by injection on and off. But doctors did not find the exact cause of the infection before this hospital stay. The patient had no history of injury to the area. She did not take medicines that weaken the body's defense system. She did not have tuberculosis.

When the patient came to the hospital, doctors saw an old surgery scar on the lower front part of her belly. A large raised scar had formed over it. There were six small tunnel openings on the lower front part of her belly. One of these tunnels was releasing thick fluid at the time. The area over and around these tunnels was sore to touch. Tests of the fluid showed bacteria. After testing which medicines would work best, doctors started the patient on three medicines called rifampicin, ethambutol, and clarithromycin.

After one month of treatment, the tunnel stopped releasing fluid. The patient was told to keep taking the medicines and come back in another month. After two months of treatment, the patient was doing well with no more fluid coming out.

Discharge Medications:

The patient was told to keep taking rifampicin 600 mg once a day, ethambutol 600 mg once a day, and clarithromycin 500 mg two times a day.

Follow-Up:

The patient was told to keep coming back for checkups. How often depends on how well she is healing and what tests show.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Date of Admission: February 2017

Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital: [Redacted]

Chief Complaint:
Recurrent tumor in the neck

History of Present Illness:
The patient, a 49-year-old female, was diagnosed with PTC in September 2009. She underwent four neck operations and received two sessions of RAI till May 2015. The patient was diagnosed with RAIR-DTC in August 2014, seven months before her last surgery. In February 2017, she was readmitted to the hospital for the surgical treatment of recurrent tumor in the neck. An enhanced CT scan revealed multiple recurrent lesions in the neck, extensive pulmonary metastases, and metastases behind the left scapula.

Hospital Course:
The patient participated in a phase II, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter clinical trial of anlotinib in RAIR-DTC (registration number: NCT02586337) from March to August 2017. The patient took placebo during the initial treatment period, which resulted in progression.

The patient subsequently underwent open label therapy with anlotinib in September 2017. Imaging evaluations were conducted in every two cycles for the first 12 cycles, and then every four cycles. The target tumors under the right anterior sternocleidomastoid muscle, under the right subclavian, and behind the left posterior scapula were treated, and other recurrent tumors in the neck and extensive lung metastases were not treated.

The patient received a total of 52 cycles of anlotinib. The target lesions were reduced by 93.79%, and no new lesions emerged. The patient experienced adverse events such as hypertension, tachycardia, twitching of the hand and foot, diarrhea, hand-foot syndrome, and headache during the clinical trial. Most were grade 1 or 2, and most were controlled by medication.

Diagnosis:
Recurrent thyroid cancer with pulmonary and scapular metastases

Treatment:
The patient underwent multiple neck operations and received two sessions of RAI before participating in a clinical trial of anlotinib.

Condition on Discharge:
N/A

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Follow-Up Plans:
N/A

N/A

N/A

Activity:

clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]

Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Date of Admission: February 2017

Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital: [Redacted]

Chief Complaint:
Cancer that came back in the neck

History of Present Illness:
The patient is a 49-year-old woman. She was told she had thyroid cancer in September 2009. Thyroid cancer starts in the thyroid gland in the neck. She had four neck surgeries and two radiation treatments by May 2015. In August 2014, doctors found that radiation treatment no longer worked for her cancer. In February 2017, she came back to the hospital because the cancer returned in her neck. A special scan showed the cancer had come back in several spots in her neck. The scan also showed the cancer had spread to her lungs and behind her left shoulder blade.

Hospital Course:
From March to August 2017, the patient joined a research study testing a drug called anlotinib. This study compared the drug to a sugar pill with no medicine in it. The patient got the sugar pill at first. Her cancer got worse during this time.

In September 2017, the patient started taking the real anlotinib drug. Doctors checked her scans every two treatment rounds for the first 12 rounds. After that, they checked every four rounds. The treated tumors were under the muscle on the right side of her neck and behind her left shoulder blade. Other tumors in her neck and lungs were not treated.

The patient took anlotinib for 52 treatment rounds in total. The treated tumors shrank by almost 94 percent. No new tumors appeared. The patient had some side effects during the study. These included high blood pressure, fast heartbeat, twitching in hands and feet, loose stools, skin problems on hands and feet, and headaches. Most side effects were mild to moderate. Most were controlled with medicine.

Diagnosis:
Thyroid cancer that came back, with spread to the lungs and shoulder blade area

Treatment:
The patient had several neck surgeries and two radiation treatments before joining the anlotinib research study.

N/A

Discharge Medications:

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a five-year-old female, presented with a swelling in the left lumbar region. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed a multinodular plexiform tumor within the subcutaneous planes of the lumbosacral region, extending from L1 to S1 vertebral levels. Surgical excision was performed. Histopathological examination revealed a multinodular tumor composed of spindle-shaped cells arranged in interlacing fascicles. The final pathologic diagnosis was plexiform schwannoma. The patient was asymptomatic and showed no sign of recurrence on one year clinical follow-up.

Discharge Summary:

The patient was diagnosed with plexiform schwannoma and underwent successful surgical excision. The patient was asymptomatic and showed no sign of recurrence during the one year clinical follow-up. Further follow-up is recommended as per standard guidelines. Family and patient education regarding necessary follow-up guidelines and potential adverse events has been provided. The patient was discharged in stable condition.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a five-year-old girl. She came to the hospital with a lump on her lower left back. A special scan called an MRI was done. The scan showed a growth with many small bumps under the skin. This growth was on her lower back, near the spine. Doctors did surgery to remove the growth. The removed tissue was looked at under a microscope. It showed a growth made of long, thin cells bundled together. The final diagnosis was plexiform schwannoma. This is a type of non-cancer nerve tumor. The patient had no symptoms after surgery. At her one-year checkup, the growth had not come back.

Discharge Summary:

The patient was diagnosed with plexiform schwannoma. This is a non-cancer nerve tumor. Surgery to remove it was successful. The patient had no symptoms after surgery. At the one-year checkup, the growth had not come back. More checkups are recommended based on standard guidelines. The family and patient were given information about future checkups. They were also told what warning signs to watch for. The patient left the hospital in stable condition.
