input:
Discharge Summary:

Medical History:
- 65-year-old male patient with a history of diabetes and chronic alcoholism.
- Presented in emergency with hematemesis and melena for the past 24 hours.
- Found to have hepatomegaly and signs of chronic liver disease with upper gastrointestinal (GI) bleed (variceal).
- Underwent endoscopic variceal ligation.

Hospital Course:
- Developed blackening of bilateral upper and lower limbs (UL and LL) which evolved rapidly into large necrotizing areas with fluid exudation.
- Discoloration was thought to be caused by terlipressin and was discontinued, after which proximal progression of the disease stopped.
- Symptomatic treatment in the form of vigorous dressing, antibiotics, and intravenous fluids was given.
- Patient refused amputation of bilateral toes and fingers despite the recommendation to prevent further progression of the disease and resultant sepsis.
- After 15 days, patient died of sepsis at home.

Follow-up:
- No follow-up available as patient went against medical advice.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Medical History:
The patient is a 65-year-old man with a history of diabetes and long-term heavy alcohol use. He came to the emergency room because he was vomiting blood and had black, tarry stools for the past 24 hours. Doctors found that his liver was enlarged and showed signs of long-term liver damage. He had bleeding in his stomach and food pipe from swollen veins. He had a procedure to tie off the bleeding veins using a flexible tube with a camera.

Hospital Course:
The skin on both arms and both legs started turning black. This spread quickly and became large areas of dying tissue that leaked fluid. Doctors believed a medicine called terlipressin caused this problem. They stopped giving this medicine, and the skin damage stopped spreading further up the limbs. The patient received treatment including bandage changes, medicines to fight infection, and fluids through a vein. Doctors told the patient he needed surgery to remove his toes and fingers to stop the damage from spreading and prevent a serious blood infection. The patient said no to this surgery. Fifteen days later, the patient died at home from a serious blood infection.

Follow-up:
There is no follow-up information because the patient left the hospital against medical advice.

input:
Discharge Summary

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 6-year-old female with mosaic Turner syndrome who was referred for cardiac evaluation secondary to her genetic condition. Physical exam revealed short stature, nevi on skin, and a broad-based gait. An echocardiogram was remarkable for severe apical and posterior wall left ventricular hypertrophy with a spongy appearance. The echocardiogram pictures raised concern for left ventricular noncompaction (LVNC), so a cardiac MRI was obtained. Cardiac MRI revealed increased trabeculations along the posterior apical aspect of left ventricle with noncompacted to compacted myocardial ratio >2.3 consistent with LVNC.

Medical Management:

The patient is being managed conservatively since she is clinically asymptomatic. Her follow-up visits with cardiology show preserved cardiac function (ejection fraction of 54% on the right side and 53% on the left side). She is receiving multidisciplinary care as an outpatient.

Plan:

The patient is recommended to receive continuous follow-up visits with cardiology to monitor her cardiac function. She is advised to continue with multidisciplinary care as an outpatient. 

Recommended Follow-up:

The patient is recommended to receive continuous follow-up visits with cardiology to monitor her cardiac function.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 6-year-old girl with a partial form of Turner syndrome. Turner syndrome is a genetic condition that affects girls. She was sent to a heart doctor because of her genetic condition. During the physical exam, doctors noticed she was shorter than average. She also had moles on her skin and walked with her feet wider apart than usual. A heart ultrasound showed that parts of her heart muscle were too thick. The lower left part of her heart had a spongy look. The heart ultrasound raised concern for a condition where the heart muscle does not form properly. Doctors ordered a special heart scan using magnets to get a better look. This scan showed extra finger-like tissue in parts of the lower left heart chamber. The results confirmed that the patient has a heart condition where the muscle did not fully develop before birth.

Medical Management:

The patient is being watched carefully without treatment because she has no symptoms. Her follow-up visits with the heart doctor show her heart is pumping well. The right side of her heart pumps out 54 percent of its blood with each beat. The left side pumps out 53 percent. She is seeing several different specialists as an outpatient.

Plan:

The patient should keep seeing her heart doctor regularly to check how well her heart is working. She should continue seeing her team of different specialists as an outpatient.

Recommended Follow-up:

The patient should keep seeing her heart doctor regularly to check how well her heart is working.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Left adnexal mass
Discharge Diagnosis: Benign endometrial-like tumor

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with a history of left ovarian cystectomy due to endometriosis. Ultrasonography revealed a huge left adnexal mass and abdominal computed tomography confirmed a solid mass with enhancement. The patient underwent laparoscopic left salpingo-oophorectomy to remove the 11-cm-sized tumor, which was found to be benign, most likely severe endometriosis, upon intraoperative frozen section examination. Other peritoneal foci were also resected. The patient did not exhibit any complications during the hospital stay and was discharged six days after the operation.

Follow-up:
The patient did not present residual disease at her follow-up six months after the operation.

Laboratory and Diagnostic Data:
The patient’s serum cancer antigen 125 level was elevated, while the CA 19-9 and carcinoembryonic antigen results were said to be normal. Ultrasonography revealed an 11-cm-sized, mixed-echoic, semi-solid mass in the left adnexa. Abdominopelvic computed tomography showed a solid mass with enhancement. Microscopic examination confirmed the left adnexal mass to be composed of endometrial-like tissue.

Specialists Consulted:
None

Discharge Medications:
None

Discharge Condition:
Stable, no complaints or complications at the time of discharge.

Primary Care Follow-up:
The patient will follow up with her primary care physician for further management.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Mass near the left ovary
Discharge Diagnosis: Non-cancerous growth made of tissue similar to the uterus lining

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted after having surgery in the past to remove a cyst from the left ovary caused by endometriosis. Endometriosis is when tissue like the uterus lining grows outside the uterus. An ultrasound showed a large mass near the left ovary. A CT scan confirmed a solid growth. The patient had surgery using small cuts and a camera to remove the left ovary and fallopian tube. The tumor was about 4 inches in size. A quick test during surgery showed the growth was not cancer. It was most likely caused by severe endometriosis. Other small areas of abnormal tissue in the belly lining were also removed. The patient had no problems during the hospital stay. The patient went home six days after surgery.

Follow-up:
The patient showed no signs of remaining disease at a check-up six months after surgery.

Laboratory and Diagnostic Data:
A blood test called CA-125 was higher than normal. This test can be elevated with endometriosis or other conditions. Two other blood tests called CA 19-9 and CEA were normal. An ultrasound showed an 4-inch mass near the left ovary that was part solid and part fluid-filled. A CT scan of the belly and pelvis showed a solid growth. Lab testing of the removed tissue confirmed it was made of tissue similar to the uterus lining.

Specialists Consulted:
None

Discharge Medications:
None

Discharge Condition:
Stable. The patient had no concerns or problems at the time of discharge.

Primary Care Follow-up:
The patient will see her regular doctor for ongoing care.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 70-year-old female with congestive heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and end-stage renal disease, was admitted to the general surgery clinic with a radiographic finding of a CCF. During workup for upper gastrointestinal bleeding, a CT enterography incidentally revealed a fistulous connection between her gallbladder and the proximal transverse colon. She reported right upper back pain that was unrelated to meals in the clinic. She had no abdominal tenderness and her liver function tests were within normal limits.

Medical Management:

The patient was hospitalized for surgical repair of the CCF with cholecystectomy, using the da Vinci Xi robotic system. The evaluation revealed an inflammatory connection between the gallbladder fundus and the proximal transverse colon, which was successfully separated. The gallbladder was dissected in a top-down fashion and a critical view of safety was obtained. Locking robotic clips were applied to the cystic duct and cystic artery, and the structures were transected. The colotomy was closed in a two-layer running fashion.

Outcome:

The patient recovered uneventfully and was discharged on the second postoperative day. Histopathological examination of the specimen revealed no evidence of malignancy, and the patient was seen in the clinic without postoperative complaints.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 70-year-old woman with heart failure, an irregular heartbeat, and kidney disease that needs dialysis. She came to the surgery clinic because a scan showed an abnormal tunnel between her gallbladder and part of her large intestine. Doctors found this tunnel by accident while checking for bleeding in her stomach area. She had pain in her upper right back that did not get worse after eating. Her belly did not hurt when pressed, and her liver tests came back normal.

Medical Management:

The patient stayed in the hospital to have surgery to fix the abnormal tunnel and remove her gallbladder. Doctors used a robotic surgery system for the operation. During surgery, they found swollen tissue connecting the bottom of the gallbladder to the large intestine. They carefully separated these two organs. The gallbladder was removed from top to bottom, and the surgeon made sure to clearly see all important parts. Small clips were placed on the tube and blood vessel connected to the gallbladder, and then these were cut. The small opening in the large intestine was closed with two layers of stitches.

Outcome:

The patient healed well with no problems. She went home two days after surgery. Lab tests on the removed tissue showed no cancer. The patient came back to the clinic for a follow-up visit and had no complaints or concerns.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

Patient Information:
The patient is a 48-year-old male who was admitted for treatment of renal cell carcinoma (RCC) with synchronous metastases to the liver. The patient was initially started on combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab and nivolumab, with Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT) to the primary tumor.

Medical History:
The patient's medical history was unremarkable except for a diagnosis of RCC.

Hospital Course:
During hospitalization, the patient experienced an elevation in creatinine (Cr) levels and decreased estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), which were managed with prednisone and mycophenolate. The patient had gradual improvement in kidney function over a period of six weeks. Imaging at that time showed a remarkable response, with shrinkage of all lesions. The patient ultimately elected to undergo consolidative nephrectomy.

Discharge Plan:
Discharge instructions were given to the patient, and he was advised to follow up with his urologist regularly. The patient is doing well without any evidence of disease. No further therapy is planned at this time.

Summary:
The patient was admitted for the treatment of RCC with synchronous liver metastases. The patient was treated with a combination of ipilimumab and nivolumab, with SBRT to the primary tumor. The patient developed a kidney dysfunction, which was managed with prednisone and mycophenolate. Imaging showed a remarkable response, and the patient underwent consolidative nephrectomy. The patient is doing well without any evidence of disease, and no further therapy is planned at this time.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admission Date: [insert date]
Discharge Date: [insert date]

Patient Information:
The patient is a 48-year-old male. He was admitted for treatment of kidney cancer that had spread to the liver. The patient was started on two medicines that help the immune system fight cancer, called ipilimumab and nivolumab. The patient also received targeted radiation treatment to the main tumor.

Medical History:
The patient had no other health problems before being diagnosed with kidney cancer.

Hospital Course:
During the hospital stay, the patient had signs that the kidneys were not working as well as they should. Blood tests showed higher waste levels and lower filtering ability. This was treated with two medicines called prednisone and mycophenolate. The patient's kidney function slowly got better over six weeks. Scans at that time showed very good results. All the tumors had gotten smaller. The patient chose to have surgery to remove the affected kidney.

Discharge Plan:
The patient received instructions before leaving the hospital. He was told to schedule regular visits with his kidney and bladder doctor. The patient is doing well with no signs of cancer. No more treatment is needed at this time.

Summary:
The patient was admitted for treatment of kidney cancer that had spread to the liver. The patient received two immune system medicines, ipilimumab and nivolumab, along with targeted radiation to the main tumor. The patient developed kidney problems, which were treated with prednisone and mycophenolate. Scans showed the tumors responded very well to treatment. The patient had surgery to remove the affected kidney. The patient is doing well with no signs of cancer. No more treatment is needed at this time.

input:
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:
Patient Name: [redacted]

Admission Date: [redacted]         Discharge Date: [redacted]
Length of Stay: [redacted] 

Admitting Diagnosis:
Degenerative scoliosis with malalignment of global sagittal and coronal balance with lumbar canal stenosis

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with back pain and claudication pain in both lower limbs. After evaluation, she was found to have degenerative scoliosis with malalignment of global sagittal and coronal balance with lumbar canal stenosis. Surgical intervention was warranted after the failure of an appropriate conservative trial. She underwent instrumentation from T10 to pelvis and interbody fusion was done at multiple levels using the standard posterior approach. Post-operatively, the patient was doing fine and was kept on regular follow-up.

During the six-year follow-up period, the patient presented with generalized back pain. Evaluation revealed breakage of both rods at the dorso-lumbar junction and breakage of the left side rod between the S1 screw and the S2-iliac screw. "Vacuum phenomenon" can be seen in the disc space corresponding with the site of breakage of the rod on CT scan. The solid bony union is appreciated in the rest of the construct. Conservative management was chosen, involving trigger point injections, analgesics, and physiotherapy. 

Outcome:
After five years of conservative management, the patient was completely asymptomatic. No further treatment was necessary, and the patient was discharged on [redacted] after a [redacted] day hospital stay with recommendations for regular follow-up care.
clean_ref:
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:
Patient Name: [redacted]

Admission Date: [redacted]         Discharge Date: [redacted]
Length of Stay: [redacted]

Admitting Diagnosis:
Curved spine due to wear and tear, with the spine out of line both front-to-back and side-to-side, along with narrowing of the spinal canal in the lower back

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with back pain and leg pain in both legs when walking. After testing, she was found to have a curved spine from wear and tear. Her spine was out of line both front-to-back and side-to-side. She also had narrowing of the spinal canal in the lower back. Surgery was needed because other treatments did not work. She had metal rods and screws placed from the middle back down to the pelvis. Bone was also fused at several levels through an opening in the back. After surgery, the patient was doing well and came back for regular check-ups.

During the six-year follow-up period, the patient came back with pain throughout her back. Testing showed that both metal rods had broken near where the middle and lower back meet. The left rod also broke between the screws near the base of the spine and pelvis. A CT scan showed gas bubbles in the disc space where the rod broke. The rest of the bone had healed well and fused together. The doctors chose to treat the patient without surgery. Treatment included pain shots into tight muscle areas, pain medicine, and physical therapy.

Outcome:
After five years of non-surgical treatment, the patient had no more symptoms. No further treatment was needed. The patient was discharged on [redacted] after a [redacted] day hospital stay. The patient should continue to come back for regular check-ups.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the emergency department with complaints of general weakness and poor oral intake. Her medical history includes diabetes, hypertension, severe osteoarthritis, and a recent episode of urinary tract infection treated with antibiotics.

Physical examination showed dehydration and hypotension. Laboratory results revealed elevated serum calcium levels, elevated creatinine levels, and decreased serum magnesium levels. The patient was diagnosed with UTI sepsis and admitted to the intensive care unit. Antibiotics and massive hydration were administered, leading to stabilization of her vital signs.

On the third hospital day, the patient’s serum calcium level decreased following the administration of saline and calcitonin. However, the level began to increase again from the third day of calcitonin administration. Further diagnostic workup, including contrast-enhanced computed tomography of chest and abdomen and whole body bone scintigraphy, ruled out malignancy.

Laboratory results revealed a suppressed PTH–vitamin D axis and secondary adrenal insufficiency may have contributed to her hypercalcemia.

Treatment with hydrocortisone (1.5 mg/kg) was initiated from the tenth hospital day and led to the normalization of serum calcium levels. Her general weakness and anorexia improved subsequently. The patient was lost for follow-up, and adequate monitoring for bone mineral density was recommended.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came to the emergency room feeling very weak and not eating well. Her health history includes diabetes, high blood pressure, severe joint pain from worn cartilage, and a recent bladder infection treated with antibiotics.

The physical exam showed she was low on body fluids and had low blood pressure. Blood tests showed high calcium levels, high waste product levels in the blood, and low magnesium levels. The patient was diagnosed with a serious blood infection from a bladder infection and moved to the intensive care unit. She received antibiotics and large amounts of fluids through an IV, which helped her vital signs become stable.

On the third day in the hospital, the patient's calcium level went down after receiving salt water fluids and a hormone medicine called calcitonin. However, the calcium level started going up again after three days of calcitonin treatment. More tests, including a special scan of the chest and belly and a full body bone scan, showed no cancer.

Blood tests showed that the systems controlling calcium and certain hormones were not working well, and low function of the adrenal glands may have caused her high calcium levels.

Treatment with a steroid medicine called hydrocortisone was started on the tenth day in the hospital and brought her calcium levels back to normal. Her weakness and poor appetite got better after that. The patient did not return for follow-up visits, and regular checks of bone strength were recommended.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: XXX
Gender: Male
Age: 62 years

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital for management of malignant ventricular tachyarrhythmia with underlying hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM). He had an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) inserted two months ago after his monozygotic twin brother suffered a fatal cardiac arrest. He presented with an unwitnessed syncopal episode preceded by pre-warning signs of dizziness while at work on a ladder.

Physical Examination:
The physical examination of the patient was unremarkable, with vital parameters within normal limits.

Diagnostic Imaging and Lab Studies:
An echocardiogram two months before the incident showed normal left ventricular size and systolic function, asymmetric septal hypertrophy of 2.8 cm, and no evidence of left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) obstruction. An urgent angiogram revealed no significant coronary artery disease. His previous 24-hour holter monitoring done three years ago showed no evidence of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (NSVT). 

Hospital Course and Treatment:
The patient was admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) with cardiac monitoring and was commenced on sotalol. There was no further ventricular tachyarrhythmias noted on telemetry and he was discharged on day 4 with outpatient follow-up. 

Follow-Up Plan:
The patient is scheduled for outpatient follow-up care to monitor his condition and ensure continued management of his HCM and underlying cardiac risk factors. 

Summary:
This is a 62-year-old male who presented with malignant ventricular tachyarrhythmia with underlying hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM). The patient had an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) inserted two months ago after his monozygotic twin brother suffered a fatal cardiac arrest. He was admitted to the ICU and commenced on sotalol. The patient was discharged after management of his condition and is scheduled for outpatient follow-up care.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: XXX
Gender: Male
Age: 62 years

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital for treatment of a dangerous fast heart rhythm. He also has a condition where the heart muscle is too thick. Two months ago, he had a small device put in his chest. This device can shock the heart back to a normal rhythm if needed. He got this device after his identical twin brother died from a sudden heart stoppage. The patient fainted without warning while at work on a ladder. Before fainting, he felt dizzy.

Physical Examination:
The physical exam showed nothing unusual. All vital signs like blood pressure and heart rate were normal.

Diagnostic Imaging and Lab Studies:
A heart ultrasound done two months before showed the heart was normal in size. The heart was pumping well. The wall between the heart chambers was too thick at 2.8 cm. There was no blockage of blood flow leaving the heart. An urgent test of the heart blood vessels showed no major blockages. A heart rhythm monitor worn three years ago showed no episodes of fast heart rhythm.

Hospital Course and Treatment:
The patient stayed in the intensive care unit with heart monitoring. He was started on a medicine called sotalol. No more dangerous fast heart rhythms were seen on the heart monitor. He went home on day 4 with a plan for follow-up visits.

Follow-Up Plan:
The patient has an appointment to see the doctor after leaving the hospital. The doctor will check his condition. The doctor will also keep managing his thick heart muscle disease and heart risk factors.

Summary:
This is a 62-year-old male who came in with a dangerous fast heart rhythm. He has a condition where the heart muscle is too thick. Two months ago, he had a small shocking device put in his chest. He got this device after his identical twin brother died from sudden heart stoppage. He stayed in the intensive care unit and was started on a medicine called sotalol. The patient went home after treatment. He has a follow-up appointment scheduled.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, an 89-year-old male, was admitted with complaints of cough and haemoptysis. The patient had a history of bronchiectasis in the right middle lobe bronchus for 30 years. One month before admission, the patient suffered a massive haemoptysis, which was controlled through bronchial artery embolization. However, haemoptysis recurred, and the patient was admitted to our hospital. Bronchoscopy performed on the patient showed active bleeding from the right middle lobe bronchus, which was temporarily controlled through haemostatic medications and cryotherapy.

Management:
On the next day, an 8 mm x 3 cm silicon stent was deployed using biopsy forceps after multiple attempts. Another customized stent with a diameter of about 6 mm was placed from the right intermediate bronchus to the right lower lobe bronchus, to avoid stent migration. Bleeding stopped promptly after the placement of stents. The patient received successive antibiotic therapy, and no obvious complication of obstructive pneumonia developed.

Outcome:
One month after the operation of stents implantation, the patient experienced complete atelectasis of the right middle and lower lobe with the complication of right pleural effusion. The patient received further surgery to remove the silicon stent inside the right intermediate bronchus. Thoracic puncture and drainage were done after stent extraction for relieving pleural effusion. Post-operation CT scan reviewed the patient's chest and revealed the right lower lobe recruitment. The patient did not experience any more haemoptysis during the three-month follow-up time and left the hospital ten days after stent extraction.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is an 89-year-old man. He came to the hospital because he was coughing and coughing up blood. The patient had a lung condition called widened airways in the right middle part of his lung for 30 years. One month before coming to the hospital, the patient coughed up a large amount of blood. Doctors blocked the bleeding blood vessel to stop it. But the patient started coughing up blood again and came to our hospital. Doctors used a small camera to look inside the patient's airways. They saw active bleeding from the right middle part of the lung. They used cold treatment and medicine to stop the bleeding for a short time.

Management:
The next day, doctors placed a small tube called a stent to keep the airway open. The stent was about 8 mm wide and 3 cm long. They used a gripping tool and tried several times to put it in place. They also placed another custom-made tube in a lower part of the right lung. This second tube helped keep the first one from moving out of place. The bleeding stopped right away after the tubes were placed. The patient took medicine to fight infection. The patient did not develop lung blockage or infection.

Outcome:
One month after the tubes were placed, the right middle and lower parts of the lung collapsed. Fluid also built up around the right lung. The patient had another procedure to remove the tube from the lower right airway. Doctors also drained the fluid from around the lung using a needle. A scan of the chest showed the right lower part of the lung opened back up. The patient did not cough up any more blood during the next three months. The patient left the hospital ten days after the tube was removed.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:

Residual bile duct tumor after endoscopic ampullectomy for ampullary adenoma with high grade dysplasia.

Hospital Course:

The patient underwent da Vinci robot-assisted laparoscopic pancreaticoduodenectomy (PD) due to residual bile duct tumor after unsuccessful endoscopic ampullectomy. The surgery was performed without any complications. The patient was stable throughout the hospitalization and was started on an oral diet on postoperative day 3. Postoperatively, the patient's pancreatic leak was managed conservatively, and the patient was discharged without any complications.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Tubular adenoma with focal high grade epithelial dysplasia involving the duodenum.

Discharge Plan:

The patient is instructed to follow a healthy lifestyle and diet. Postoperative follow-up will be discussed with the patient and scheduled as needed. The patient is prescribed pain medications and instructed to follow-up with the surgeon as needed. Patient education regarding self-care and instructions for wound care will be provided at discharge for continuity of care.

clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Date of Admission: [REDACTED]

Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Admission Diagnosis:

The patient had leftover tumor tissue in the bile duct. This happened after a prior procedure to remove a growth at the opening where the bile duct meets the small intestine. The growth had cells that were close to becoming cancer.

Hospital Course:

The patient had robot-assisted surgery to remove part of the pancreas and small intestine. This was needed because an earlier, less invasive procedure did not remove all of the bile duct tumor. The surgery went well with no problems. The patient stayed stable during the hospital stay. The patient started eating regular food by mouth on day 3 after surgery. After surgery, there was some leaking from the pancreas. This was treated without another surgery. The patient left the hospital without any problems.

Discharge Diagnosis:

The patient has a non-cancerous growth with some cells that look very abnormal. This growth is in the first part of the small intestine.

Discharge Plan:

The patient should eat well and live a healthy lifestyle. A follow-up visit after surgery will be set up with the patient as needed. The patient has been given pain medicine. The patient should see the surgeon again as needed. Before leaving, the patient will get information about how to care for the surgical wound and how to take care of themselves at home.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient presented with binocular vertical diplopia after an assault. Physical examination revealed restricted right ocular motion in temporal downward gaze with no scleral injection, bony step-off, enophthalmos, or proptosis. Head and orbital CT were negative for injury while the CT scan incidentally revealed a maxillary mucosal retention cyst. Ophthalmology was consulted and forced duction testing was performed, confirming gaze restriction. The patient was taken to the operating room where a trapdoor fracture with entrapment of the inferior rectus muscle was diagnosed and repaired. Retrospective review of the CT revealed soft tissue density with radiodensity characteristics consistent with herniation of orbital fat and entrapped muscle fibers.

Hospital Discharge Summary:

This 26-year-old male presented with binocular vertical diplopia following an assault. Upon physical examination, restricted right ocular motion was noted with no signs of injury. A maxillary mucosal retention cyst was incidentally detected on CT. Ophthalmology was consulted and confirmed the gaze restriction through forced duction testing. A trapdoor fracture with entrapped inferior rectus muscle fibers was diagnosed and repaired in the operating room. Retrospective CT evaluation led to the detection of herniation of orbital fat and entrapped muscle fibers. The patient was discharged without any further complications and with appropriate follow-up arranged.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient came in with double vision after being attacked. The double vision caused images to appear stacked on top of each other. A physical exam showed the right eye could not move well when looking down and to the side. There was no redness, bone damage, sunken eye, or bulging eye. A head and eye socket scan showed no injury. The scan did find a small fluid-filled sac in the sinus by chance. An eye doctor was called in. The doctor did a test where the eye was moved by hand to check for blockage. The test confirmed the eye could not move freely. The patient had surgery. During surgery, doctors found a type of bone break that had trapped a muscle that moves the eye up and down. The break was fixed. A second look at the scan showed that fat and muscle from the eye socket had pushed through the break.

Hospital Discharge Summary:

This 26-year-old male came in with double vision after being attacked. The double vision caused images to appear stacked on top of each other. A physical exam showed the right eye could not move well. There were no signs of injury. A scan found a small fluid-filled sac in the sinus by chance. An eye doctor was called in and did a test that confirmed the eye could not move freely. Doctors found a type of bone break that had trapped a muscle that moves the eye. The break was fixed during surgery. A second look at the scan showed that fat and muscle from the eye socket had pushed through the break. The patient left the hospital without any problems. Follow-up visits were scheduled.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient was admitted to the outpatient clinic at 34 weeks and 1 day gestational age for evaluation of a large vaginal mass and vulvar lesions. A transvaginal ultrasound confirmed the presence of varicosities within the vaginal wall. The patient underwent punch biopsy which showed squamous vaginal mucosa with reactive cellular changes. Her obstetrics history was significant for 2 prior C-sections, 1 ectopic pregnancy, and diet-controlled gestational diabetes. The patient complained of yellow vaginal discharge, dysuria, and discomfort made worse with walking. She had been treated unsuccessfully with fluconazole for this pain in the past. Fetal anatomy survey at 26 weeks and 2 days demonstrated mild bilateral hydronephrosis. 

On physical exam, clusters of soft varicosities were noted on the anterior and lateral vaginal walls, filling the vagina and protruding beyond the hymenal ring. A second opinion confirmed large vaginal varicosities by clinical examination. The patient was referred back to her original provider for continued prenatal care.

RECOMMENDATIONS:

The patient was advised a repeat C-section due to a history of previous C-sections and the potential for hemorrhage if the patient were to attempt vaginal delivery. The patient was also advised to follow up with her referring provider for continued prenatal care. 

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:

- Vaginal Varicosities

DISCHARGE PLAN:

The patient was discharged with the recommendation to continue prenatal care with her referring provider. No further diagnostic testing was performed for varicosities of the esophagus or a Doppler ultrasound of the extremities. As is typical with vulvar varicosities, our patient had complete resolution of her vaginal varicosities by her 6-week postpartum exam. 

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:

N/A

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:

The patient is advised to follow up with her referring provider for continued prenatal care. 

FOLLOW-UP:

The patient is advised to follow up with her referring provider for continued prenatal care.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient came to the clinic at 34 weeks and 1 day of pregnancy. She came to have a large growth in the vagina and sores on the outer genital area checked. An ultrasound done through the vagina showed swollen veins in the vaginal wall. The patient had a small tissue sample taken. The sample showed normal vaginal skin with some changes from irritation. Her pregnancy history included 2 past deliveries by surgery through the belly, 1 pregnancy that grew outside the womb, and pregnancy-related high blood sugar controlled by diet. The patient said she had yellow discharge from the vagina, pain when urinating, and discomfort that got worse when walking. She had tried a yeast infection medicine before, but it did not help her pain. A detailed check of the baby at 26 weeks and 2 days showed mild swelling in both of the baby's kidneys.

During the physical exam, groups of soft swollen veins were seen on the front and side walls of the vagina. These swollen veins filled the vagina and stuck out past the vaginal opening. Another doctor looked at the patient and agreed she had large swollen veins in the vagina. The patient was sent back to her regular doctor for ongoing pregnancy care.

RECOMMENDATIONS:

The patient was told she should have another surgical delivery through the belly. This was because of her past surgical deliveries and the risk of heavy bleeding if she tried to deliver through the vagina. The patient was also told to see her regular doctor for ongoing pregnancy care.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:

Swollen veins in the vagina

DISCHARGE PLAN:

The patient was sent home and told to continue pregnancy care with her regular doctor. No other tests were done to check for swollen veins in the food pipe or blood flow tests of the arms and legs. As often happens with swollen veins in the genital area, the patient's swollen vaginal veins went away completely by her checkup 6 weeks after giving birth.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:

None

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:

The patient is told to see her regular doctor for ongoing pregnancy care.

FOLLOW-UP:

The patient is told to see her regular doctor for ongoing pregnancy care.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]          Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Pain at left temporomandibular joint (TMJ) and numerous radiopaque masses around left TMJ

Discharge Diagnosis: Synovial chondromatosis

Medical History: None provided

Hospital Course: The patient, a 50-year-old male, presented at Dankook University Dental Hospital with complaints of pain and swelling at his left TMJ and numerous radiopaque masses around the joint on panoramic radiography. The patient showed spontaneous and opening pain and swelling at the left TMJ, but showed no tenderness or limitation of mouth opening. Through imaging tests, including panoramic imaging, CBCT, and MR imaging, numerous radiopaque loose bodies of various shapes and sizes surrounding the left condyle, and hypertrophy with sclerotic changes on the anterior and lateral sides of the temporal bone, the articular eminence, and the articular fossa were observed. A surgical excision under general anesthesia was performed to remove approximately 120 pieces of the loose bodies. Histopathologic examination confirmed the diagnosis of synovial chondromatosis. On a postoperative panoramic image, remaining flat hypertrophy of the articular eminence was observed, without any loose bodies. The patient's symptoms were relieved, and no signs of recurrence were observed at 1-month and 3-month follow-up visits.

Discharge Condition: The patient's condition has improved significantly, and he is discharged from the hospital in good condition.

Current Medications Upon Discharge: None provided

Follow-Up Care: The patient is advised to continue to attend follow-up visits to monitor his condition.

Instructions at Discharge: The patient is instructed to avoid any strenuous activities for a few weeks, avoid opening his mouth too wide, and not to eat hard or chewy foods for at least a week after the surgery. The patient is advised to continue oral hygiene practices, including brushing and flossing regularly.

Primary Care Physician: None provided

Attending Physician: [REDACTED]

Other contributors to care: None provided
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]          Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Pain at the left jaw joint and many small solid masses seen on X-ray around the left jaw joint

Discharge Diagnosis: Synovial chondromatosis (a condition where small, hard pieces of cartilage grow inside a joint)

Medical History: None provided

Hospital Course: The patient, a 50-year-old male, came to Dankook University Dental Hospital. He had pain and swelling at his left jaw joint. X-rays showed many small solid masses around the joint. The patient had pain on his own and when opening his mouth. He also had swelling at the left jaw joint. He did not have soreness when touched or trouble opening his mouth. Doctors used different imaging tests to look at the joint. These included regular X-rays, 3D X-rays, and MRI scans. The tests showed many loose pieces of different shapes and sizes around the lower jaw bone. The tests also showed thickened and hardened bone on the front and side of the skull bone near the joint. Doctors did surgery while the patient was fully asleep. They removed about 120 loose pieces from the joint. Lab tests on the removed pieces confirmed the diagnosis of synovial chondromatosis. An X-ray taken after surgery showed some flat, thickened bone still in the joint area. No loose pieces remained. The patient felt better after surgery. At check-up visits one month and three months later, there were no signs the condition had come back.

Discharge Condition: The patient has improved a lot. He is leaving the hospital in good condition.

Current Medications Upon Discharge: None provided

Follow-Up Care: The patient should keep going to check-up visits so doctors can watch his condition.

Instructions at Discharge: The patient should not do hard physical activities for a few weeks. The patient should not open his mouth too wide. The patient should not eat hard or chewy foods for at least one week after surgery. The patient should keep taking care of his teeth and gums by brushing and flossing every day.

Primary Care Physician: None provided

Attending Physician: [REDACTED]

Other contributors to care: None provided

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 68-year-old female, was admitted to the Department of General and Vascular Surgery and Angiology with chronic epigastric pain. An abdominal ultrasound revealed a fluid-filled tumor situated between the common iliac arteries. Further imaging studies were conducted, including a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis, which revealed a neoplasm in the presacral space. An arterial and venous Doppler ultrasound was performed, followed by a direct phlebography, revealing a large venous aneurysm of the right common iliac vein with an adhering thrombus and large collateral circulation. Anticoagulant treatment with rivaroxaban was administered, and the patient received this treatment for two years without any complications or aneurysm growth.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 68-year-old female, was admitted to the hospital with chronic epigastric pain lasting several years. Imaging studies revealed a fluid-filled tumor located between the common iliac arteries and a neoplasm in the presacral space. Further studies revealed a large venous aneurysm of the right common iliac vein with an adhering thrombus and large collateral circulation. The patient was treated with anticoagulant therapy with rivaroxaban. This treatment was administered for two years without any complications or aneurysm growth.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 68-year-old female, was admitted to the General and Blood Vessel Surgery Department with long-term stomach pain. A belly ultrasound showed a fluid-filled growth located between the main blood vessels in the lower belly. More imaging tests were done, including a CT scan of the belly and pelvis. These tests showed an abnormal growth in the space in front of the tailbone. Special ultrasound tests were done to check blood flow in the arteries and veins. A test using dye to see the veins was also done. These tests showed a large bulge in a major vein on the right side of the pelvis. There was also a blood clot attached to it and many extra blood vessels had formed nearby. The patient was given rivaroxaban, a blood-thinning medicine to prevent clots. The patient took this medicine for two years. There were no problems and the bulge in the vein did not get bigger.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient, a 68-year-old female, was admitted to the hospital with long-term stomach pain that had lasted several years. Imaging tests showed a fluid-filled growth located between the main blood vessels in the lower belly. The tests also showed an abnormal growth in the space in front of the tailbone. More tests showed a large bulge in a major vein on the right side of the pelvis. There was a blood clot attached to it and many extra blood vessels had formed nearby. The patient was treated with rivaroxaban, a blood-thinning medicine. This treatment was given for two years. There were no problems and the bulge in the vein did not get bigger.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient, an 18-year-old female, was referred from a private health facility to the renal ward of the University of Ilorin Teaching Hospital with complaints of recurrent facial and leg swelling, persistent vomiting, and reduction in urinary output. She had a history of hospital admission when she was 8-years-old with complaints of lower abdominal pain and urinary retention. The patient was small for age with a weight of 35 kg and a body mass index of 16.65 kg/m2. Initial laboratory results revealed persistent hyponatremia with azotaemia and grade IV bilateral hydronephrosis confirmed on ultrasonography. The patient underwent bilateral ureterostomy to relieve obstruction, which was unsuccessful in controlling the uremic symptoms. Due to financial constraints, the patient was unable to afford dialysis and was discharged at her own request on the sixteenth day of hospital admission.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was admitted to the renal ward of the University of Ilorin Teaching Hospital with recurrent facial and leg swelling, persistent vomiting, and reduction in urinary output. Initial laboratory results showed persistent hyponatremia with azotaemia, and grade IV bilateral hydronephrosis was confirmed on ultrasonography. The patient underwent bilateral ureterostomy to relieve obstruction, which was unsuccessful in controlling the uremic symptoms. Due to financial constraints, the patient was unable to afford dialysis and was discharged at her own request on the sixteenth day of hospital admission.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Course:

The patient is an 18-year-old female. She was sent from a private clinic to the kidney unit at the University of Ilorin Teaching Hospital. She came in with swelling in her face and legs that kept coming back. She also had ongoing vomiting and was making less urine than normal. When she was 8 years old, she was in the hospital for lower belly pain and not being able to pass urine. The patient was smaller than expected for her age. She weighed 35 kg and had a body mass index of 16.65 kg/m2. First tests showed she had ongoing low sodium levels in her blood and a buildup of waste products in her blood. An ultrasound scan showed severe swelling in both kidneys due to blocked urine flow. This was rated as grade IV, which is the most serious level. The patient had surgery to create openings in both tubes that carry urine from the kidneys. This was done to clear the blockage. The surgery did not work to control her symptoms from waste buildup in her blood. The patient could not pay for blood-filtering treatment. She asked to leave the hospital. She was sent home on the sixteenth day after being admitted.

Hospital Course Summary:

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient: 22-year-old male

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with bilateral muscular atrophy and tremor of the distal upper extremities with insidious onset 5 years ago. Cold exposure worsened the symptomatology. Clinical examination revealed symmetrical muscular atrophy of the hands and forearms and reduced muscular strength during flexion, extension, and abduction of the fingers and flexion, extension of the wrist, as well as reduced tendon reflexes from the affected neurotomes. Electroneurophysiology and electromyography showed signs of chronic motor neuron lesion, bilaterally. Magnetic resonance images (MRI) acquired in neutral position showed loss of cervical lordosis and focal cord atrophy at C5-C6 level. MRI acquired in flexion revealed widening of the posterior epidural space. Postcontrast images showed uniform enhancement of the epidural space. The enhancement was reduced in the neutral position. After considering all the findings, the diagnosis of HD was made, and the patient was treated conservatively.

Diagnosis:
Hirayama Disease (HD)

Summary:
The patient's symptoms were consistent with HD, and was diagnosed based on the MRI findings in neck flexion. The patient was treated conservatively and is being discharged at this time. Further monitoring and follow-up are recommended.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient: 22-year-old male

Hospital Course:
The patient came in with muscle loss and shaking in both hands and forearms. These problems started slowly about 5 years ago. Cold weather made the symptoms worse. The doctor's exam showed muscle loss in both hands and forearms that looked the same on each side. The patient had weaker grip and trouble moving fingers and wrists. Reflexes in the affected areas were also lower than normal. Nerve and muscle tests showed long-term damage to the nerves that control movement in both arms. Pictures of the spine taken while the neck was straight showed the neck curve was flatter than normal. These pictures also showed the spinal cord was smaller than normal at the middle of the neck. Pictures taken while the neck was bent forward showed a wider space behind the spinal cord. This space lit up on the scan when dye was used. The space lit up less when the neck was straight. After looking at all the test results, the doctors found the patient has Hirayama Disease. The patient was treated without surgery.

Diagnosis:
Hirayama Disease, a rare condition that causes muscle weakness and loss in the hands and forearms

Summary:
The patient's symptoms matched Hirayama Disease. The diagnosis was confirmed by spine pictures taken while bending the neck forward. The patient was treated without surgery and is now going home. The patient should continue to be watched by doctors and come back for check-up visits.

input:
Patient Information:
Name: [Patient's name]
Gender: Female
Age: 38 years

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient presented with painful, red raised lesions on the right forearm with fever, which gradually coalesced to form larger lesions. The patient had a prior history of upper respiratory tract infection and similar episodes three years back. On examination, a large erythematous plaque was present on the right upper extremity along with small erythematous plaques on both upper limbs, right side of the neck, and chest. Few lesions showed typical “relief of mountain range appearance”. Local temperature was raised in all the lesions. Superficial nerves were not thickened and sensations on the plaques were normal. Histopathology showed a diffuse infiltrate consisting predominantly of mature neutrophils located in the upper dermis without evidence of primary leukocytoclastic vasculitis.

Treatment:
The patient was initially treated with tablet dapsone 100 mg HS with dramatic early improvement, and relapse on discontinuation. The second episode was treated with a combination of dapsone and oral prednisolone started at 30 mg/day and gradually tapered over 4 weeks. The patient is now in remission and discharged. 

Follow-Up Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow-up with their primary care provider for further evaluation and management. 

Summary:
The patient presented with red raised, painful lesions and fever with a prior history of upper respiratory tract infection. Histopathology showed a diffuse infiltrate consisting predominantly of mature neutrophils located in the upper dermis without evidence of primary leukocytoclastic vasculitis. The patient was initially treated with tablet dapsone 100 mg HS with dramatic early improvement and relapse on discontinuation. The second episode was treated with a combination of dapsone and oral prednisolone started at 30 mg/day and gradually tapered over 4 weeks. The patient is now in remission and discharged with follow-up instructions to their primary care provider.
clean_ref:
Patient Information:
Name: [Patient's name]
Gender: Female
Age: 38 years

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient came in with painful, red, raised skin bumps on the right forearm along with fever. Over time, these bumps joined together to form larger patches. The patient had a cold before this happened and had similar skin problems three years ago. During the exam, doctors found a large red patch on the right upper arm. There were also smaller red patches on both arms, the right side of the neck, and the chest. Some patches had a bumpy surface that looked like a mountain range. All the patches felt warm to the touch. The nerves near the skin were normal and the patient could feel touch on the patches normally. A skin sample test showed a buildup of white blood cells called neutrophils in the upper layer of skin. There were no signs of blood vessel damage.

Treatment:
The patient first took a pill called dapsone 100 mg at bedtime. The skin got much better quickly. But the problem came back when the patient stopped taking the medicine. For the second round, the patient took dapsone along with a steroid pill called prednisolone. The prednisolone started at 30 mg per day. The dose was slowly lowered over 4 weeks. The patient's skin has now cleared up and the patient is going home.

Follow-Up Instructions:
The patient should see their regular doctor for more checkups and care.

Summary:
The patient came in with red, raised, painful skin bumps and fever after having a cold. A skin sample test showed a buildup of white blood cells in the upper layer of skin with no blood vessel damage. The patient first took dapsone 100 mg at bedtime and got better quickly. The problem came back when the medicine was stopped. For the second round, the patient took dapsone with prednisolone starting at 30 mg per day. The steroid dose was slowly lowered over 4 weeks. The patient's skin has now cleared up and the patient is going home. The patient should follow up with their regular doctor.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital for the management of a giant hepatic tumor. She underwent a right hepatectomy during which two well-differentiated hepatocellular carcinomas (HCCs) were removed. The patient recovered well after the surgery and was discharged on postoperative day 17.

Past Medical History:
The patient had no significant past medical history.

Hospital Course:
The patient's hospital course was significant for a sensation of abdominal distension and loss of appetite, at which point she underwent abdominal ultrasonography that discovered a giant hepatic mass in the right lobe. Physical examination was significant for hepatomegaly. Liver function tests showed evidence of nonicteric cholestasis with mild transaminase enzyme level elevation. Screening for viral hepatitis yielded negative results. Levels of the liver-specific tumor marker "protein induced by vitamin K absence-II" were highly elevated. Abdominal ultrasonography showed a giant hypoechoic mass measuring 139 × 120 mm in the right lobe of the liver. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) demonstrated a huge mass that occupied the right lobe of the liver and showed inhomogeneous hyperenhancement in the arterial phase, as well as uniform isoenhancement in the portal venous phase. A low-concentration area indicated the presence of necrosis in the tumor center, and a radiological diagnosis of HCC was therefore posited. The surgical team also discovered a congenital absence of the portal vein that indicated the presence of congenital absence of the portal vein type II. 

Disposition and Follow-up:
The patient was discharged on postoperative day 17 and successfully treated for a small recurrent HCC nodule detected 7 months after the operation with radiofrequency ablation. The patient has been on close follow-up for 6 months without any evidence of further recurrences.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital to treat a very large liver tumor. She had surgery to remove the right side of her liver. During surgery, doctors removed two slow-growing liver cancers. The patient healed well after surgery and went home 17 days later.

Past Medical History:
The patient had no major health problems before this.

Hospital Course:
The patient felt bloating in her belly and did not want to eat. Doctors did an ultrasound of her belly and found a very large mass in the right side of her liver. A physical exam showed her liver was larger than normal. Blood tests showed bile was backing up in her liver but she was not yellow. Her liver enzyme levels were slightly high. Tests for liver infections came back negative. A blood test for a liver cancer marker showed very high levels. An ultrasound showed a very large dark mass in the right side of the liver measuring about 139 by 120 millimeters. A special scan with dye showed a huge mass taking up the right side of the liver. The mass lit up unevenly at first and then evenly as blood flowed through. A dark area in the center showed dead tissue inside the tumor. Based on these images, doctors believed it was liver cancer. The surgery team also found that the patient was born without a certain blood vessel in her liver called the portal vein type two.

Disposition and Follow-up:
The patient went home 17 days after surgery. Seven months later, doctors found a small new liver cancer spot. They treated it using heat therapy to destroy the cancer cells. The patient has been checked regularly for 6 months and shows no signs of more cancer coming back.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Age: 22
Gender: Male

Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Length of Stay: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient presented with tenderness on the dorsoradial aspect of the scaphoid of the left wrist due to an unhealed proximal pole fracture and a broken headless screw. Physical examination showed an active range of motion from 30o extension to 40o flexion, 20o radial deviation, and 30o ulnar deviation. Plain X-ray and computed tomography confirmed the fracture and the broken screw. Surgery was performed by two certified orthopedic hand surgeons to remove the implant and replace it with a more antegrade central fixation carried out using a 2.8-mm headless screw. A pure cancellous autograft bone was harvested from the distal radius through a dorsal window just proximal to Lister’s tubercle, which was compressed in a syringe and packed into the cavity within the bone with a small impactor.

The patient was discharged after being placed in a removable long opponens splint for two weeks. The Colles cast was applied postoperatively. The tenderness of the wrist that originated from the non-healed scaphoid bone disappeared after three months, and wrist extension was reported as 60° and flexion as 40°. Radiographic signs of healing in the nonunion area without bone resorption were observed after three months. The patient returned to work five months after the last surgery with a good functional condition. The bridging trabecula was found on CT scans in two planes at nine months.

Diagnosis:

Unhealed proximal pole fracture and broken headless screw.

Treatment:

Surgery was performed by two certified orthopedic hand surgeons to remove the implant and replace it with a more antegrade central fixation carried out using a 2.8-mm headless screw. A pure cancellous autograft bone was harvested from the distal radius through a dorsal window just proximal to Lister’s tubercle, which was compressed in a syringe and packed into the cavity within the bone with a small impactor.

Follow-up:

The patient was followed for 14 months after surgery.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Age: 22
Gender: Male

Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Length of Stay: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient came in with pain on the thumb side of the left wrist. This was caused by a small wrist bone that did not heal properly and a broken metal screw inside. During the exam, the patient could move the wrist 30 degrees up and 40 degrees down. The patient could also tilt the wrist 20 degrees toward the thumb and 30 degrees toward the pinky. X-rays and a CT scan showed the broken bone and the broken screw. Two hand surgery specialists did the operation. They removed the old screw and put in a new one in a better position. The new screw was 2.8 millimeters wide. The surgeons also took a small piece of bone from the lower arm near the wrist. They packed this bone graft into the gap to help the bone heal.

The patient went home with a removable wrist and thumb splint to wear for two weeks. A hard cast was also put on after surgery. After three months, the wrist pain was gone. The patient could bend the wrist back 60 degrees and forward 40 degrees. X-rays at three months showed the bone was healing well with no bone loss. The patient went back to work five months after surgery and the wrist worked well. A CT scan at nine months showed new bone had grown across the break.

Diagnosis:

A small wrist bone that did not heal properly and a broken metal screw inside.

Treatment:

Two hand surgery specialists did the operation. They removed the old screw and put in a new one in a better position. The new screw was 2.8 millimeters wide. The surgeons also took a small piece of bone from the lower arm near the wrist. They packed this bone graft into the gap to help the bone heal.

Follow-up:

The patient was checked on for 14 months after surgery.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 53-year-old male nurse diagnosed with HCV infection 

Hospital Course:
In January 2011, the patient was evaluated at the Outpatient Infectious Diseases clinic for abnormal liver function tests. His HCV RNA was 660,000 IU/mL, genotype 3a. A liver biopsy showed chronic active hepatitis with Metavir A2 necroinflammatory grading and F2 fibrosis. The patient was considered eligible for antiviral treatment and started therapy with PEG-IFN-α-2a and ribavirin in March 2011. Treatment was well tolerated, and HCV RNA became negative by the fourth week and remained undetectable during the entire 24-week treatment regimen. ALT normalized within the first 3 weeks of treatment. The patient underwent post-treatment follow-up until September 2012, and a new biopsy revealed a mild reduction in Metavir grading and staging (A1 and F1, respectively).

Hospitalization:
Nine weeks after finishing the antiviral therapy, the patient developed symmetrical polyarthritis with joint pain and edema in the wrists, knees, shoulders, and metacarpophalangeal joints, associated with prolonged morning stiffness. MRI detected bone erosions with juxta-articular osteopenia in the wrist, knee, and hand joints, revealing a diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody testing were positive. 

Treatment:
The patient was treated with prednisone and NSAIDs for two months, followed by methotrexate and sulfasalazine with a quick and favorable response to treatment. 

Discharge:
The patient was discharged home in satisfactory condition with a prescription for methotrexate and sulfasalazine. Follow-up with Rheumatologist and Gastroenterologist was advised.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 53-year-old male nurse found to have hepatitis C virus infection

Hospital Course:
In January 2011, the patient was seen at the Outpatient Infectious Diseases clinic because of abnormal liver test results. His hepatitis C virus level was 660,000 units per milliliter, type 3a. A small sample of liver tissue showed ongoing liver swelling with moderate damage and mild to moderate scarring. The patient was a good fit for treatment and began taking two medicines called PEG-IFN-α-2a and ribavirin in March 2011. The patient handled the treatment well. The virus was no longer found in the blood by week four. It stayed gone during the full 24 weeks of treatment. Liver test results returned to normal within the first 3 weeks. The patient had follow-up visits until September 2012. A new liver tissue sample showed less damage and less scarring than before.

Hospitalization:
Nine weeks after finishing the antiviral treatment, the patient developed joint swelling on both sides of the body. The patient had pain and swelling in the wrists, knees, shoulders, and finger joints. The patient also had stiffness in the morning that lasted a long time. An MRI scan showed bone damage and bone thinning near the joints in the wrist, knee, and hand. These findings led to a diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis, a disease where the body attacks its own joints. Blood tests for rheumatoid factor and another marker for this disease were both positive.

Treatment:
The patient was treated with prednisone, a steroid, and pain relievers for two months. After that, the patient took methotrexate and sulfasalazine. The patient responded quickly and well to this treatment.

Discharge:
The patient went home in good condition with prescriptions for methotrexate and sulfasalazine. The patient should follow up with a joint specialist and a stomach and liver specialist.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 79-year-old male with advanced prostate cancer who presented with a mass on the tip of his urethral meatus. Biopsy confirmed squamous cell carcinoma, and the patient underwent partial penectomy and lymph node dissection which revealed positive right inguinal lymph nodes (three out of seven) with pathologic T2N2M0 disease. Adjuvant chemotherapy was administered with low dose carboplatin and paclitaxel, and radiation was delivered to the right inguinal region.

The patient developed disease recurrence with nodal involvement nine months later and was treated with additional chemoradiation with curative intent, including another round of low dose carboplatin and paclitaxel, and radiation to the left pelvic region. He responded well, but eventually developed disease progression and was considered for second-line therapy with the PD-L1 inhibitor atezolizumab.

After approximately two years of treatment with atezolizumab, the patient developed bullous pemphigoid, an immune-mediated toxicity of the skin commonly associated with those agents. Prednisone was administered and the patient had quick resolution of his blistering symptoms.

At the time of this report, the patient has shown near complete response and has been placed on treatment holiday. Follow-up appointments have been scheduled to monitor progress.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 79-year-old male with advanced prostate cancer who came to the hospital with a growth on the opening of his penis. A tissue sample showed a type of skin cancer called squamous cell carcinoma. The patient had surgery to remove part of his penis and nearby lymph nodes. Lymph nodes are small glands that help fight infection. The surgery found cancer in three out of seven lymph nodes on the right side of his groin. The cancer was staged as T2N2M0, meaning it had spread to nearby lymph nodes but not to other parts of the body. The patient received cancer-killing drugs called carboplatin and paclitaxel at low doses. He also received radiation treatment to the right groin area.

Nine months later, the cancer came back and spread to lymph nodes again. The patient received more cancer-killing drugs and radiation with the goal of curing the cancer. This included another round of low dose carboplatin and paclitaxel. Radiation was given to the left pelvic area. He responded well at first, but the cancer eventually grew again. The doctors then considered a different treatment called atezolizumab. This drug helps the body's defense system fight cancer.

After about two years on atezolizumab, the patient developed a skin condition called bullous pemphigoid. This causes blisters and is a known side effect of this type of drug. The patient was given a steroid medicine called prednisone. His blistering went away quickly.

At the time of this report, the cancer has almost completely gone away. The patient has stopped treatment for now. Follow-up appointments have been scheduled to check on his progress.
