input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age/Sex: 29/Female

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]

Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Achalasia

Hospital Course:

The patient was a 29-year-old female with a history of mild progressive dysphagia who presented with severe dysphagia. The patient had been diagnosed with achalasia three years ago but refused any treatment options. She was given ferrous sulfate capsules for iron therapy by her general practitioner about a month earlier, which led to the development of severe dysphagia after three weeks. She was referred to a gastroenterologist for further evaluation.

On admission, the patient had no odynophagia, nausea, or vomiting. Physical examination was unremarkable.

A barium swallow esophagography showed a dilated esophagus with a bird's beak appearance of the lower esophageal sphincter and a filling defect in the distal part of the esophagus. An EGD revealed a dilated esophagus and an elliptical-shaped, black-colored bezoar in the distal part of the esophagus. There were no ulcers or erosions in the esophagus. The lower esophageal sphincter was severely spastic.

Attempts were made to fragment and push the bezoar into the stomach, but it was discontinued due to high LES pressure and resistance and the risk of aspiration. Finally, the bezoar was retrieved with a basket.

An esophageal manometer was carried out and the results were compatible with the diagnosis of achalasia. In the next endoscopic session, achalasia balloon dilation (size 30 mm) was successfully applied.

The patient was asymptomatic during the 3-year follow-up.

Discharge Condition:

The patient's condition improved after treatment with achalasia balloon dilation. The patient was discharged in good condition and was advised to follow up with her gastroenterologist as scheduled.

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to follow up with her gastroenterologist as scheduled.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age/Sex: 29/Female

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]

Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Achalasia (a condition where the tube that carries food to the stomach does not work properly)

Hospital Course:

The patient was a 29-year-old female who had trouble swallowing that got worse over time. She came to the hospital because swallowing became very difficult. The patient was told she had achalasia three years ago but chose not to get treatment. About a month before coming to the hospital, her regular doctor gave her iron pills. After taking the pills for three weeks, her swallowing problems became much worse. She was sent to a stomach and digestive system doctor for more tests.

When the patient arrived, she had no pain when swallowing, no upset stomach, and no throwing up. The physical exam showed nothing unusual.

The patient had an X-ray test where she swallowed a special liquid. This showed a widened food tube with a narrowed bottom that looked like a bird's beak. It also showed something stuck in the lower part of the food tube. A camera test was done to look inside the food tube. This showed the food tube was widened. There was an oval-shaped, black mass stuck in the lower part of the food tube. The mass was made of the iron pills that had clumped together. There were no sores inside the food tube. The muscle at the bottom of the food tube was very tight.

The doctors tried to break up the mass and push it into the stomach. They had to stop because the tight muscle made it too risky. There was also a chance food could go into the lungs. The doctors used a small basket tool to pull the mass out instead.

A test was done to measure how well the food tube muscles work. The results matched the diagnosis of achalasia. In a later procedure, the doctors stretched the tight muscle using a balloon. The balloon was about 1.2 inches wide. The procedure worked well.

The patient had no symptoms during the 3 years of check-ups after treatment.

Discharge Condition:

The patient got better after the balloon stretching treatment. The patient left the hospital in good condition. The patient was told to see her stomach and digestive system doctor at her scheduled visits.

Discharge Medications:

None

Follow-up:

The patient was told to see her stomach and digestive system doctor at her scheduled visits.
perturbed:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Age/Sex: 29/Female

Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]

Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Admitting Diagnosis: Achalasia (a condition where the tube that carries food to the stomach does not work properly)

Hospital Course:

The patient was a 29-year-old female who had trouble swallowing that got worse over time. She came to the hospital because swallowing became very difficult. The patient was told she had achalasia three years ago but chose not to get treatment. About a month before coming to the hospital, her regular doctor gave her iron pills. After taking the pills for three weeks, her swallowing problems became much worse. She was sent to a stomach and digestive system doctor for more tests.

When the patient arrived, she had no pain when swallowing, no upset stomach, and no throwing up. The physical exam showed nothing unusual.

The patient had an X-ray test where she swallowed a special liquid. This showed a widened food tube with a narrowed bottom that looked like a bird's beak. It also showed something stuck in the lower part of the food tube. A camera test was done to look inside the food tube. This showed the food tube was widened. There was an oval-shaped, black mass stuck in the lower part of the food tube. The mass was made of the iron pills that had clumped together. There were no sores inside the food tube. The muscle at the bottom of the food tube was very tight.

The doctors tried to break up the mass and push it into the stomach. They had to stop because the tight muscle made it too risky. There was also a chance food could go into the lungs. The doctors used a small basket tool to pull the mass out instead.

A test was done to measure how well the food tube muscles work. The results matched the diagnosis of achalasia. In a later procedure, the doctors stretched the tight muscle using a balloon. The balloon was about 1.2 inches wide. The procedure worked well.

The patient had no symptoms during the 3 years of check-ups after treatment.

Discharge Condition:

The patient got better after the balloon stretching treatment. The patient left the hospital in good condition. The patient was told to see her stomach and digestive system doctor at her scheduled visits.

Discharge Medications:

None

Follow-up:

The patient was told to see her stomach and digestive system doctor at her scheduled visits.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 79-year-old male

Reason for Admission: Progressive dysphagia and weight loss

Medical History: None provided

Hospital Course:

Upon admission, the patient presented with symptoms including hematemesis, melena, fever, cough, and yellow sputum. Blood analysis revealed severe anemia, high leukocyte and neutrophil levels, and a low albumin level. A sputum smear indicated the presence of gram-positive cocci.

An endoscopy identified an elongated lump on 1 side of the vertical axis of the esophagus. The mass extended progressively for 15 cm along the esophageal longitudinal axis and invaded half of the esophageal circumference. Biopsy revealed the tumor to be a malignant melanoma, positive for S-100 and HMB-45, and negative for cytokeratin and carcinoembryonic antigen markers.

Computed tomography showed inflammation of the lungs and no evidence of distant metastasis. Additionally, a physical examination revealed no pigmented lesions at the skin, eyes, rectum, or other locations.

Due to the advanced age of the patient and comorbidities including hypoproteinemia, infection, and severe anemia, surgery, chemotherapy, or radiotherapy were not performed. The patient was discharged without receiving treatment.

Follow-up:

Approximately 1 month after discharge, the medical team was informed of the patient's death.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 79-year-old male

Reason for Admission: Trouble swallowing that was getting worse and weight loss

Medical History: None provided

Hospital Course:

When the patient came to the hospital, he had several symptoms. These included vomiting blood, dark or black stool, fever, cough, and yellow mucus. Blood tests showed he had very low red blood cell counts. His white blood cell counts were high, which can mean infection. He also had low protein levels in his blood. A test of his mucus showed a type of bacteria was present.

Doctors used a small camera to look inside the food pipe. They found a long growth on one side of the food pipe. The growth stretched about 15 centimeters along the food pipe. It covered half of the inside wall. A small tissue sample was taken and tested. The tests showed the growth was a serious type of skin cancer called melanoma. This cancer was growing inside the food pipe.

A scan of the body showed the patient had swelling in the lungs from infection. The scan showed the cancer had not spread to other parts of the body. Doctors also checked the skin, eyes, bottom, and other body areas. They found no dark spots or signs of cancer in those places.

The patient was 79 years old and had other health problems. These included low protein levels, infection, and very low red blood cell counts. Because of his age and these problems, doctors did not do surgery. They also did not give cancer-fighting drugs or radiation treatment. The patient left the hospital without receiving treatment for the cancer.

Follow-up:

About 1 month after leaving the hospital, the medical team learned that the patient had died.

input:
Hospital Course:

The patient, a 50-year-old male with a history of a pruritic plaque on his left hand, was admitted to the dermatology department for assessment. The plaque started as a small, erythematous papule that gradually increased in size over several months and became a larger, scaly plaque. The patient also experienced pruritus and a tingling sensation. Topical steroid treatment provided temporary relief; however, the plaque did not resolve. A biopsy was performed due to the unusual morphology of the plaque, revealing spongiotic dermatitis compatible with nummular dermatitis. Further treatment with potent topical steroids provided only temporary flattening of the plaque.

Several months later, the patient returned with ulceration of the center of the plaque and subcutaneous nodules in a sporotrichoid pattern extending from his left fifth digit superiorly to his elbow. Culturing a biopsy confirmed fish-tank granuloma, caused by M. marinum. The patient reported a history of having a salt-water fish tank and had lost several fish weeks after the onset of symptoms, although there was no necropsy.

Antimicrobial susceptibility testing showed sensitivity to amikacin, clarithromycin, doxycycline, ethambutol, moxifloxacin, rifabutin and rifampin, and resistance to ciprofloxacin and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Treatment with doxycycline and azithromycin provided no clinical improvement after 3 months of continuous therapy. Treatment was switched to moxifloxacin and clarithromycin, and the patient applied local heat to the affected skin for several hours per day using a reusable heat pack. The patient was referred to a plastic surgeon for excision of the subcutaneous nodules on his left hand, wrist, and arm. After 3 months of the new antibiotic regime and excision of the nodules, the patient's ulcerated plaque healed, and the excised subcutaneous nodules did not recur. 

The patient remained in hospital for 17 months before clinical resolution of the disease was confirmed. The patient was discharged following confirmation of resolution of the disease with no further medical follow-up required.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 50-year-old male with an itchy patch of skin on his left hand. He was admitted to the skin care department for evaluation. The patch started as a small, red bump. It slowly grew larger over several months and became a bigger, flaky patch. The patient also had itching and a tingling feeling. Skin cream with steroids helped for a short time, but the patch did not go away. A small tissue sample was taken because the patch looked unusual. The test showed a type of skin swelling called nummular dermatitis. Stronger steroid creams only made the patch flatten for a short time.

Several months later, the patient came back. The center of the patch had become an open sore. He also had lumps under the skin in a line from his left pinky finger up to his elbow. Testing a tissue sample found fish-tank granuloma, an infection caused by a germ called M. marinum. The patient said he had a salt-water fish tank at home. Several of his fish had died weeks after his skin problems started, but the fish were not tested.

Lab tests showed which medicines could kill the germ and which could not. The germ could be killed by amikacin, clarithromycin, doxycycline, ethambutol, moxifloxacin, rifabutin, and rifampin. The germ could not be killed by ciprofloxacin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Treatment with doxycycline and azithromycin did not help after 3 months. The medicines were changed to moxifloxacin and clarithromycin. The patient also put a reusable heat pack on the affected skin for several hours each day. The patient was sent to a surgeon to remove the lumps under the skin on his left hand, wrist, and arm. After 3 months on the new medicines and removal of the lumps, the open sore healed. The removed lumps did not come back.

The patient stayed in the hospital for 17 months until the disease was fully gone. The patient was sent home after tests showed the disease had cleared up. No further medical visits are needed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Date of Birth: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Reason for Admission: Shortness of breath

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the emergency department with shortness of breath and increasing dyspnea. She was found to have a large goiter that was compressing the trachea and causing respiratory distress. The patient was quickly transferred to the intensive care unit (ICU) where she was intubated using rapid sequence induction to relieve respiratory distress. Subsequent cerebral and thoracic computed tomography (CT) scans revealed a large goiter plunging into the upper mediastinal orifice, which was removed successfully by thyroidectomy 48 hours after admission. Prior to reversal of the neuromuscular blockage, the tube balloon was deflated, and the diagnosis of tracheomalacia was almost confirmed. The endotracheal tube was left in place for 24 hours, and then removed the next day without incident.

Course of Treatment:
The patient received corticotherapy (dexamethasone at 12 mg per day for two days) prior to thyroidectomy to accelerate fetal lung maturation. Surgery was completed without incident, as no intraoperative tracheostomy was necessary. Patient was treated with levothyroxin sodium tablets 75mcg/day post-surgery.

Follow-Up Plan:
Patient was discharged from the hospital on the 5th postoperative day without any complications. Pathology showed multinodular goiter with areas of atypical hyperplasia, but no sign of malignancy. Patient gave birth to a healthy newborn four weeks later.

Recommendations:
Patient should continue taking levothyroxin sodium tablets 75mcg/day as prescribed. She should follow up with her primary care physician for monitoring of her thyroid function and any potential complications related to the goiter removal.

clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Gender: Female
Date of Birth: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]
Reason for Admission: Shortness of breath

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the emergency room with shortness of breath that was getting worse. Doctors found a large growth on her thyroid gland. This growth was pressing on her windpipe and making it hard to breathe. The patient was moved quickly to the intensive care unit. There, doctors placed a breathing tube to help her breathe. Brain and chest scans showed the thyroid growth was very large and reaching into the upper chest area. Doctors removed the thyroid gland in surgery 48 hours after the patient arrived. Before waking the patient fully, doctors tested the windpipe. They found the windpipe walls were weak and floppy. The breathing tube stayed in place for 24 hours. It was removed the next day without any problems.

Course of Treatment:
The patient received a steroid medicine called dexamethasone before surgery. She took 12 mg per day for two days. This helped her baby's lungs develop faster. The surgery went well. Doctors did not need to make an opening in her windpipe. After surgery, the patient started taking thyroid hormone pills. The medicine is levothyroxin sodium tablets at 75 mcg per day.

Follow-Up Plan:
The patient left the hospital five days after surgery with no problems. Lab tests on the removed tissue showed a thyroid growth with many lumps. Some cells were growing in an unusual way. However, there was no cancer. The patient gave birth to a healthy baby four weeks later.

Recommendations:
The patient should keep taking levothyroxin sodium tablets at 75 mcg per day as directed. She should see her regular doctor for checkups. The doctor will monitor how well her thyroid medicine is working. The doctor will also watch for any problems from the thyroid removal surgery.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 29-year-old male of Syrian origin, was admitted for evaluation of a painless right-sided testicular swelling that had been present for 12 months. Upon clinical examination, he had a nontender enlarged right testis. Further evaluation with alpha-fetoprotein (α-FP) and beta human chorionic gonadotropin (β-HCG) tumor markers revealed negative results. An ultrasound scan of the scrotum showed a 7-cm hypoechoic, hypervascular, and cystic mass replacing the right testis. A right radical inguinal orchidectomy was performed, and a staging computed tomography scan was negative for lymphatic or metastatic disease.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was diagnosed with Sertoli cell sarcoma (SCS) with undifferentiated sarcoma confined to the testis. The tumor was composed of small, intermediate, and large epithelioid cells arranged in sheets with broad areas of necrosis. Microscopic features of these cells were consistent with SCS. Additionally, there were ill-defined areas of undifferentiated sarcoma composed of highly pleomorphic, epithelioid, and spindle-shaped cells with high mitotic activity invested in fibrous and fibromyxoid stroma. The tumor did not extend beyond the tunica albuginea. The spermatic cord and epididymis were uninvolved, and no lymphovascular invasion was identified.

Treatment:
The histological diagnosis was SCS with undifferentiated sarcoma confined to the testis. Further oncological treatment with adjuvant chemotherapy was strongly advised, but the patient declined and moved to a different locality.

Follow-up:
The patient should be advised to receive regular follow-up consultations for potential recurrence of the tumor, and to notify his healthcare provider if he develops any symptoms concerning for the same.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Medical Record Number: [Number]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 29-year-old man from Syria. He came to the hospital because of painless swelling in his right testicle. The swelling had been there for 12 months. During the exam, the right testicle was larger than normal but did not hurt when touched. Blood tests were done to check for cancer markers. These tests came back normal. An ultrasound of the scrotum showed a 7-centimeter mass in the right testicle. The mass had low echoes, many blood vessels, and fluid-filled areas. Surgery was done to remove the right testicle through a cut in the groin area. A full body scan showed no cancer spread to the lymph nodes or other parts of the body.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was found to have a rare type of testicular cancer called Sertoli cell sarcoma. There was also a fast-growing cancer that had no specific features. Both cancers were only in the testicle. The tumor had small, medium, and large cells grouped together in sheets. Large areas of dead tissue were also found. Some areas had oddly shaped cells that were dividing quickly. These cells were surrounded by tough, fiber-like tissue. The cancer did not grow through the outer covering of the testicle. The tube that carries sperm and the tissue next to the testicle were not affected. The cancer had not spread into blood vessels or lymph channels.

Treatment:
Tests confirmed the patient had Sertoli cell sarcoma with fast-growing cancer in the testicle only. Doctors strongly recommended additional cancer-killing drug treatment after surgery. However, the patient said no to this treatment and moved to a different area.

Follow-up:
The patient should see a doctor regularly to check if the cancer comes back. The patient should tell a doctor right away about any new symptoms that might be related to the cancer.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Clinical Course:
The 9-year-old male patient presented at the clinic with multiple papular lesions on his left cheek. The patient had a history of cutaneous leishmaniasis (CL) located in his nose two years ago that was successfully treated with intralesional meglumine antimonate injection. Dermatological examination on admission revealed multiple, coalescing, rough, slightly elevated, yellowish-brown papular lesions measuring 3-6 mm in diameter on the left cheek. Giemsa staining of the skin biopsy showed numerous leishmania parasites in their amastigote form. The patient was diagnosed with lupoid CL based on his medical history, clinical, and microscopy findings. The patient was started on weekly intralesional meglumine antimonate injection for four weeks, and significant improvement was observed in the patient's lesions.

Hospital Course:
The patient's hospital course was uneventful. The patient did not encounter any complications or adverse drug reactions during his hospital stay. The patient tolerated the treatment well and had no complaints of pain or discomfort.

Condition on Discharge:
On discharge, the patient's condition had improved significantly with no visible papular skin lesions on the left cheek.

Follow-Up Plan:
The patient was advised to maintain good hygiene practices and avoid exposure to sandflies. The patient was scheduled for a follow-up visit with the dermatology clinic in two months to monitor for recurrence of skin lesions or signs of CL.

Discharge Medications:
N/A

Indications for Follow-Up:
- Monitoring for potential recurrence of lupoid cutaneous leishmaniasis or any other skin lesions on the left cheek
- Review of the patient's clinical response to four weekly intralesional meglumine antimonate injection therapy.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

Clinical Course:
The 9-year-old male patient came to the clinic with several small raised bumps on his left cheek. Two years ago, the patient had a skin infection caused by a parasite on his nose. That infection was treated successfully with medicine injected into the skin. When doctors examined the skin on admission, they found several small, rough, slightly raised, yellowish-brown bumps. The bumps were about 3-6 mm wide and were joining together on the left cheek. A small piece of skin was removed and looked at under a microscope. The test showed many parasites inside the skin cells. Based on the patient's past illness, what the skin looked like, and the test results, doctors diagnosed a long-lasting form of this parasitic skin infection. The patient received medicine injected into the skin once a week for four weeks. The bumps on the patient's skin got much better after this treatment.

Hospital Course:
The patient's time in the hospital went smoothly. The patient did not have any problems or bad reactions to the medicine. The patient handled the treatment well and did not complain of pain or discomfort.

Condition on Discharge:
When the patient left the hospital, the skin had improved a lot. There were no visible raised bumps on the left cheek.

Follow-Up Plan:
The patient was told to keep the skin clean and stay away from sandflies. The patient has an appointment at the skin doctor's office in two months. This visit is to check if the skin bumps come back or if there are signs of the infection returning.

Discharge Medications:
None

Reasons for Follow-Up:
To watch for any return of the parasitic skin infection or new skin bumps on the left cheek.
To check how well the patient responded to the four weeks of medicine injected into the skin.

input:
Hospital Course: 

The patient, a 21-year-old male, presented to our hospital with right upper quadrant abdominal pain and distention. Upon examination, the patient showed signs of right upper quadrant tenderness and palpable liver. Initial investigations showed elevated cancer antigen (CA) 19-9 levels and MRI scans showed two lesions primarily located in the right lobe. The biopsy of the lesion showed undifferentiated embryonal sarcoma of the liver (UESL). The tumor was deemed to be unresectable, and the patient was started on chemotherapy. During his fifth cycle of therapy, an OLT was done and the patient responded well. The patient underwent regular outpatient department follow-ups for 18 months, and no lesions were found postoperatively. 

Discharge Diagnosis: 

Undifferentiated Embryonal Sarcoma of the Liver (UESL)

Hospital Course Summary: 

The patient was admitted to our hospital with right upper quadrant abdominal pain and distention. Initial investigations were within reference range except for elevated CA 19-9 levels. MRI scans revealed two lesions in the right lobe of the liver, and a biopsy of the lesion showed UESL. The tumor was deemed to be unresectable, and the patient was started on chemotherapy. During his fifth cycle of therapy, an OLT was done, and the patient responded well. The patient was followed up by the outpatient department for 18 months, and no lesions were found on the CT scan. 

Impressions: 

The patient was diagnosed with UESL, which was unresectable. The patient responded well to the chemotherapy and underwent an OLT. The patient had no postoperative complications and was followed up regularly by the outpatient department.

Plan: 

The patient should continue to be followed up by the outpatient department, with regular CT-scan-monitoring to detect any recurrence of the lesion.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

The patient is a 21-year-old male. He came to our hospital with pain and swelling in the upper right part of his belly. During the exam, the doctor found tenderness in that area and could feel that his liver was enlarged. Tests showed high levels of a substance called CA 19-9, which can be a sign of cancer. An MRI scan showed two growths in the right side of his liver. A small tissue sample was taken from the growth. It showed a rare type of liver cancer called undifferentiated embryonal sarcoma of the liver. The tumor could not be removed with surgery. The patient started treatment with cancer-fighting drugs. During his fifth round of treatment, the patient had a liver transplant. He responded well to this treatment. The patient came back for check-up visits for 18 months. No new growths were found after the surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis:

Undifferentiated Embryonal Sarcoma of the Liver, a rare type of liver cancer.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient came to our hospital with pain and swelling in the upper right part of his belly. Most test results were normal, except for high CA 19-9 levels. MRI scans showed two growths in the right side of his liver. A tissue sample showed a rare liver cancer. The tumor could not be removed with surgery. The patient started treatment with cancer-fighting drugs. During his fifth round of treatment, he had a liver transplant. He responded well. The patient came back for check-ups for 18 months. A CT scan showed no growths.

Impressions:

The patient had a rare liver cancer that could not be removed with surgery. He responded well to the cancer-fighting drugs and had a liver transplant. He had no problems after surgery. He came back for regular check-ups.

Plan:

The patient should keep coming back for check-up visits. He should have regular CT scans to check if the cancer has come back.

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient came to our hospital with pain and swelling in the upper right part of his belly. Most test results were normal, except for high CA 19-9 levels. MRI scans showed two growths in the right side of his liver. A tissue sample showed a rare liver cancer. The tumor could not be removed with surgery. The patient started treatment with cancer-fighting drugs. During his fifth round of treatment, he had a liver transplant. He responded well. The patient came back for check-ups for 18 months. A CT scan showed no growths.

Impressions:

The patient had a rare liver cancer that could not be removed with surgery. He responded well to the cancer-fighting drugs and had a liver transplant. He had no problems after surgery. He came back for regular check-ups.

Plan:

The patient should keep coming back for check-up visits. He should have regular CT scans to check if the cancer has come back.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: RA

Date of Admission: [DATE]

Date of Discharge: [DATE]

Reason for Admission: EEG Recording

Medical History: RA is a 31-year-old Italian monolingual female with profound unilateral congenital deafness caused by unilateral microtia. She has no history of neurological or psychiatric disorders or any intelligence deficits. She has corrected-to-normal vision and no history of difficulty in learning to speak or read.

Hospital Course: RA underwent an EEG recording and lateral preference assessment, which showed strong right-lateralization as to handedness, footedness, and eyedness while being strongly left-lateralized as to earedness. Eye dominance tests showed her right eye dominance. A reading comprehension test showed that she had no reading difficulties. Phonology and comprehension of the morphosyntactic relations were also assessed, with her responses fully within the normal range.

Summary: RA was admitted for an EEG recording which showed no abnormalities. She was found to have strong right-lateralization as to handedness, footedness, and eyedness while being left-lateralized as to earedness. She had no reading difficulties, and her responses to phonology and comprehension tests were within the normal range. RA has now been discharged.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: RA

Date of Admission: [DATE]

Date of Discharge: [DATE]

Reason for Admission: Brain wave recording test

Medical History: RA is a 31-year-old Italian woman who speaks only Italian. She was born completely deaf in one ear. This was caused by one ear not forming properly from birth. She has no history of brain or mental health problems. She has no learning difficulties. Her eyesight is normal with glasses or contacts. She had no trouble learning to speak or read.

Hospital Course: RA had a brain wave recording test and tests to check which side of her body she prefers to use. The tests showed she strongly prefers her right hand, right foot, and right eye. She strongly prefers her left ear. Eye tests showed her right eye is the stronger eye. A reading test showed she has no trouble reading. Tests of speech sounds and understanding sentence structure were also done. Her scores were completely normal.

Summary: RA was admitted for a brain wave recording test. The test showed no problems. She was found to strongly prefer her right hand, right foot, and right eye. She prefers her left ear. She had no reading problems. Her scores on speech sound and understanding tests were normal. RA has now been discharged.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient presented with abdominal pain and distention, elevated white blood cell count and eosinophilia, elevated serum IgE levels, and showed moderate ascites on imaging. Biopsies revealed eosinophilic infiltration of the gastrointestinal tract, suggestive of eosinophilic gastroenteritis. The patient was treated with oral prednisone, which resulted in symptomatic improvement and a complete resolution of ascites and gastrointestinal thickening. Upon completion of the steroids, the patient remained asymptomatic and has not required any further treatment. 

Hospital Course Summary:
A 28-year-old Saudi female with no significant past medical history presented with a two-week history of abdominal pain and distention. Upon physical examination, there was diffuse tenderness and moderate ascites. Blood work showed an elevated white blood count with eosinophilia and elevated serum IgE levels. Imaging studies revealed moderate ascites with gastrointestinal thickening and mesenteric lymphadenopathy. Biopsies from an esophagogastroduodenoscopy and colonoscopy confirmed eosinophilic infiltration of the gastrointestinal tract, consistent with eosinophilic gastroenteritis. 

Treatment was initiated with oral prednisone, resulting in symptomatic improvement and complete resolution of the ascites and gastrointestinal thickening. Upon completion of the steroid regimen, the patient remained asymptomatic without further treatment required. Follow-up peripheral blood count and imaging studies confirmed sustained improvement. The patient was discharged with instructions to follow up with their primary care physician as needed.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient came in with belly pain and swelling, a high number of white blood cells, and too many allergy-related cells in the blood. Blood tests also showed high levels of a protein linked to allergies. Scans showed a moderate amount of fluid buildup in the belly. Tissue samples showed too many allergy-related cells in the stomach and intestines. This pointed to a condition called eosinophilic gastroenteritis, where allergy cells cause gut inflammation. The patient was given a steroid pill called prednisone by mouth. This helped the symptoms go away. The fluid in the belly and the thickening of the gut walls also went away completely. After finishing the steroid treatment, the patient had no more symptoms. No other treatment was needed.

Hospital Course Summary:
A 28-year-old Saudi female with no major past health problems came in with two weeks of belly pain and swelling. During the physical exam, the doctor found tenderness all over the belly and a moderate amount of fluid buildup. Blood tests showed a high white blood cell count with too many allergy-related cells. A protein linked to allergies was also high. Scans showed moderate fluid in the belly, thickened gut walls, and swollen lymph nodes near the intestines. The patient had a scope test to look at the upper gut and another to look at the lower gut. Tissue samples from these tests confirmed too many allergy-related cells in the gut. This matched a diagnosis of eosinophilic gastroenteritis.

Treatment started with a steroid pill called prednisone by mouth. The patient's symptoms got better. The fluid in the belly and the thickened gut walls went away completely. After finishing the steroid treatment, the patient had no symptoms and did not need more treatment. Follow-up blood tests and scans showed the improvement lasted. The patient was sent home with instructions to see their regular doctor if needed.

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY
Patient Name: [REDACTED] 
Age: 84 years 
Sex: Male 
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
 
HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient presented to the otolaryngology clinic with a scalp lesion with a progressive enlargement over 2 months following the MMS surgery. The biopsy confirmed the presence of invasive squamous cell carcinoma in the setting of severe solar elastosis. A surgical extirpation was performed, creating a 5 × 7 cm defect (35 cm2) that extended down to the level of the galea. Reconstructive options were discussed, and the physician elected to use vCPM. vCPM was placed on the wound in direct contact with the galea, followed by Adaptic® dressing. The patient returned to the clinic on day 7 after the first vCPM application, and a second 5 × 5 cm vCPM graft was placed on the fully granulated wound. The patient returned on day 14, at which time the defect had reduced to approximately 3 × 5 cm (15 cm2) and substantial re-epithelialization was noted. Based on the appearance of the wound, a third application was not deemed necessary. At the day 21 follow-up, the patient's wound had completely closed with minimal scarring. Six months following treatment, the wound remained closed with continued improvement in the hyperemia. The patient experienced no complications and was very pleased with the outcome.
 
DIAGNOSIS:
Invasive squamous cell carcinoma in the setting of severe solar elastosis
 
DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient is advised to follow up with the physician for regular monitoring of the wound healing progress. Any signs of infection or delayed wound healing should be immediately reported to the physician.
 
FOLLOW-UP:
Follow-up appointments are scheduled with the physician for regular monitoring of the wound healing progress.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY
Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 84 years
Sex: Male
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came to the ear, nose, and throat clinic with a growth on the scalp. The growth had been getting bigger for 2 months after a skin cancer removal surgery. A tissue sample showed a type of skin cancer that had spread deeper into the skin. The skin also showed severe sun damage. The doctors removed the cancer through surgery. This left an open wound about 5 by 7 centimeters in size. The wound went down to the tough tissue covering the skull. The doctors talked about ways to help the wound heal. They chose to use a special skin graft material called vCPM. The vCPM was placed directly on the wound and covered with a non-stick bandage. The patient came back to the clinic 7 days after the first treatment. A second piece of vCPM was placed on the wound, which now had healthy new tissue growing. The patient returned on day 14. The wound had shrunk to about 3 by 5 centimeters. New skin was growing well over the wound. The doctors decided a third treatment was not needed. At the day 21 visit, the wound had fully closed with very little scarring. Six months after treatment, the wound stayed closed. The redness continued to fade. The patient had no problems and was very happy with the results.

DIAGNOSIS:
Skin cancer that spread deeper into the skin, with severe sun damage to the skin

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient should see the doctor for regular check-ups to watch how the wound heals. The patient should tell the doctor right away about any signs of infection or slow healing.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient has appointments scheduled with the doctor to check on wound healing.

input:
Hospital Course:

This 58-year-old female presented with sudden painless decreased vision in the left eye (LE) for 1 month. She had no history of any Valsalva maneuver or trauma. BCVA in the RE was 20/20 and LE was 20/200.

Assessment and Findings:

Fundus examination as well as OCT LE showed hemorrhagic PED at the fovea and sub-ILM hemorrhage at inferonasal macula. LE EDI-OCT confirmed the presence of pachychoroid in the BE. ICG did not reveal any hyperfluorescence in the LE due to masking by the hemorrhagic PED.

Treatment and Outcome:

With a provisional diagnosis of PCV, she was treated with LE IVB injection. At 1-month follow-up, her LE vision improved to 20/125. Fundus examination revealed the disappearance of sub-ILM hemorrhage with a reduction in the size of hemorrhagic PED which were confirmed on OCT. 

Conclusion:

The patient was discharged with improved vision after receiving LE IVB injection therapy. Follow-up visits and monitoring may be necessary to ensure that the patient’s condition is properly managed going forward.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:

This 58-year-old female came in with sudden painless vision loss in the left eye for 1 month. She had no history of straining or injury. Vision in the right eye was normal at 20/20 and the left eye was 20/200, which means much worse than normal.

Assessment and Findings:

Eye exams and imaging of the left eye showed bleeding under the retina at the center of vision. There was also bleeding under a thin layer of tissue in another part of the retina. Imaging confirmed that both eyes had thicker than normal blood vessel layers behind the retina. A special dye test could not show certain details in the left eye because the bleeding blocked the view.

Treatment and Outcome:

With a likely diagnosis of abnormal blood vessel growth in the eye, the patient was treated with a medicine injection into the left eye. At the 1-month follow-up visit, the left eye vision improved to 20/125. Eye exams showed that one area of bleeding had gone away and the other area of bleeding had gotten smaller. Imaging tests confirmed these improvements.

Conclusion:

The patient was discharged with improved vision after receiving the injection treatment in the left eye. Follow-up visits and monitoring may be necessary to make sure the patient's condition is properly managed going forward.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Diagnosis: N/A
Principal Diagnosis at Discharge: Cervical Adenocarcinoma

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient presented with abnormal uterine bleeding requiring transfusion. A large, firm, 6 cm mass in the cervix was found on examination, and a biopsy confirmed high-grade invasive cervical adenocarcinoma, which was an unusual variant of cervical mucinous adenocarcinoma. The patient underwent concurrent chemoradiation with weekly cisplatin followed by a brachytherapy boost. Significant tumor shrinkage was observed during brachytherapy. However, at a one-month follow-up, a 1.5 cm, firm, nodular mass within the cervical canal was found, confirmed by PET/CT, which was consistent with residual tumor. The patient underwent adjuvant hysterectomy in the form of an exploratory laparotomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, which revealed residual cervical tumor measuring up to 1.6 cm in greatest dimension.

CONDITION AT DISCHARGE:
The patient is without complaints and has maintained normal bowel and bladder function. At a 12-month follow-up, she continues to show no evidence of disease, and the patient feels well with no complaints.

FOLLOW-UP:
Follow-up appointments have been scheduled in accordance with the treating physician's recommendation.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
N/A

FOLLOW-UP MEDICATIONS:
N/A

FOLLOW-UP TESTS:
N/A

FOLLOW-UP DIAGNOSIS:
N/A

FOLLOW-UP TREATMENT:
N/A

FOLLOW-UP PLAN:
N/A

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Diagnosis: N/A
Principal Diagnosis at Discharge: Cancer of the cervix

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient came in with unusual bleeding from the uterus and needed a blood transfusion. A doctor found a large, hard growth about 2.4 inches wide on the cervix during an exam. A tissue sample showed the patient had a serious type of cervical cancer. This was a rare form of cancer that makes mucus. The patient received radiation and chemotherapy at the same time with a drug called cisplatin given weekly. After that, the patient received internal radiation therapy placed directly near the tumor. The tumor got much smaller during this internal radiation treatment. But at a checkup one month later, doctors found a small hard lump about half an inch wide in the cervical canal. A special scan confirmed this was leftover cancer. The patient then had surgery to remove more tissue. Surgeons opened the belly, removed the uterus, and removed both ovaries and fallopian tubes. The surgery showed leftover cancer in the cervix measuring up to 0.6 inches across.

CONDITION AT DISCHARGE:
The patient has no complaints and has normal bowel and bladder function. At a 12-month checkup, she still shows no signs of cancer. The patient feels well with no complaints.

FOLLOW-UP:
Follow-up appointments have been scheduled based on the treating doctor's advice.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: N/A
Patient ID: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Discharge Diagnosis: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML)

Hospital Course Summary:

A 56-year-old male patient presented to the hospital with a history of progressive shortness of breath. After initial blood work revealed abnormalities, further investigations identified AML. The patient’s bone marrow was infiltrated with blasts, confirming the diagnosis.

Treatment was initiated promptly, and the patient responded well to therapy. There were no significant complications during the patient’s admission, and they were closely monitored by a multidisciplinary team of healthcare professionals.

Throughout their stay, the patient was provided with appropriate supportive care and advice, including guidance regarding healthy lifestyle choices. The healthcare team worked collaboratively to develop a comprehensive and personalized treatment plan for the patient.

The patient also received counseling regarding their history of alcohol-dependence syndrome, as well as advice on how to manage their comorbid condition of chronic obstructive airways disease.

Upon discharge, the patient was provided with clear instructions regarding medication regimens and follow-up appointments. They were also advised to seek medical help immediately if they experienced any concerning symptoms.

In summary, the patient’s admission was characterized by a prompt diagnosis of AML and successful intervention. The healthcare team provided the patient with high-quality care, and the patient was able to safely return home with a comprehensive treatment plan and ongoing support.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: N/A
Patient ID: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Discharge Diagnosis: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), a type of blood cancer

Hospital Course Summary:

A 56-year-old male patient came to the hospital because he was having more and more trouble breathing. Blood tests showed something was wrong. More tests found that the patient has AML, a cancer of the blood. The patient's bone marrow had cancer cells in it, which proved the diagnosis.

Treatment started right away, and the patient did well with the treatment. The patient had no serious problems during the hospital stay. A team of doctors and nurses watched the patient closely.

During the stay, the patient received good care and helpful advice. This included tips about making healthy choices in daily life. The care team worked together to create a full treatment plan made just for the patient.

The patient also got help for a past problem with alcohol dependence. The care team gave advice on how to handle the patient's lung disease, called COPD, which makes breathing harder.

When leaving the hospital, the patient got clear directions about what medicines to take and when. The patient also learned when to come back for follow-up visits. The patient was told to get medical help right away if any worrying signs appeared.

In summary, the patient was quickly found to have AML and treatment worked well. The care team gave the patient very good care. The patient was able to go home safely with a full treatment plan and continued support.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 58-year-old man
Reason for Admission: Weight loss, jaundice, and abdominal pain
Diagnosis: Obstructive jaundice due to a well-differentiated pancreatic adenocarcinoma
Treatment: Three cycles of chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and infusional 5-fluorouracil.
Outcome: Significant reduction in local disease, leading to a curative Whipple procedure
Post-operative Status: Surgical margins free of disease

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with weight loss, jaundice, and abdominal pain that were consistent with obstructive jaundice. Laboratory findings were indicative of pancreatic adenocarcinoma. The patient underwent an endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration biopsy, which confirmed the diagnosis of a well-differentiated pancreatic adenocarcinoma. At the same time, a biliary stent was placed. 
The patient received three cycles of chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and infusional 5-fluorouracil, resulting in a significant reduction in local disease. Based on his favorable response, the patient underwent a Whipple procedure with curative intent, and histopathological analysis of the specimen demonstrated free surgical margins from disease. 

The patient made an uneventful recovery and was discharged from the hospital with a recommendation for a follow-up visit in the near future. The patient was advised to continue regular follow-up with his healthcare provider for further evaluation and management of his condition.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 58-year-old man
Reason for Admission: Weight loss, yellowing of the skin and eyes, and belly pain
Diagnosis: A blockage causing yellowing of the skin due to a slow-growing cancer in the pancreas
Treatment: Three rounds of cancer-fighting drugs, radiation therapy, and a cancer drug called 5-fluorouracil given through a vein.
Outcome: The cancer shrank a lot in the area where it started. This allowed doctors to do surgery to remove it.
Post-operative Status: The edges of the tissue removed during surgery showed no signs of cancer.

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital with weight loss, yellowing of the skin and eyes, and belly pain. These symptoms pointed to a blockage. Blood tests suggested cancer in the pancreas. The patient had a test where doctors used a thin needle guided by a special camera to take a small sample of tissue. This test confirmed a slow-growing cancer in the pancreas. At the same time, doctors placed a small tube to help drain bile.
The patient received three rounds of cancer-fighting drugs, radiation therapy, and a cancer drug given through a vein. The cancer shrank a lot in the area where it started. Because the treatment worked well, the patient had surgery to remove part of the pancreas and nearby organs. Tests on the removed tissue showed the edges were free of cancer.
The patient healed well without any problems and left the hospital. The patient was told to come back for a follow-up visit soon. The patient was also told to keep seeing the healthcare provider for ongoing check-ups and care.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: N/A (protected health information removed)
Gender: Male
Age: 33 years old
Admission Date: N/A (not specified)
Discharge Date: N/A (not specified)
Admitting Diagnosis: Papillary oral mass (right ventral surface of the tongue)
Final Diagnosis: Oral VX
Treatment Provided: Excisional biopsy

Hospital Course: 
A 33-year-old male patient was admitted to the oral pathology center with a non-tender, pink to yellowish mass with a papillary surface on the right ventral surface of the tongue. The lesion consistency was soft on palpation, and laboratory results were normal. An excisional biopsy was performed, and microscopic sections showed papillary hyperparakeratotic acanthotic epithelium with macrophages with foamy cytoplasm (xanthoma cells). The diagnosis of oral VX was confirmed based on clinical and histopathologic features. The patient was free of disease within 15 months following the operation.

Discharge Diagnosis: Oral VX
Follow-up: The patient is advised to follow up regularly with his primary care provider.

Discharge Instructions:
1. Protect the surgical area from trauma and infection.
2. Keep the surgical area clean and dry.
3. Do not consume hot or spicy foods.
4. If any complications arise, seek medical attention immediately.
5. Follow up regularly with your primary care provider to monitor any changes in your health.

Discharge Medications: None.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: N/A (protected health information removed)
Gender: Male
Age: 33 years old
Admission Date: N/A (not specified)
Discharge Date: N/A (not specified)
Admitting Diagnosis: A bumpy growth on the right underside of the tongue
Final Diagnosis: Oral verruciform xanthoma (a rare, harmless mouth growth)
Treatment Provided: Surgery to remove the growth for testing

Hospital Course:
A 33-year-old male patient came to the mouth disease center with a painless, pink to yellowish bumpy growth on the right underside of the tongue. The growth was soft to the touch. All lab tests came back normal. The doctors did surgery to remove the growth and look at it under a microscope. The tissue showed a bumpy outer layer with special fat-filled cells inside. Based on how the growth looked and what the tissue showed, the doctors confirmed it was a harmless mouth growth called oral verruciform xanthoma. The patient showed no signs of the growth coming back within 15 months after surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis: Oral verruciform xanthoma (a rare, harmless mouth growth)
Follow-up: The patient should see his regular doctor for checkups.

Discharge Instructions:
1. Protect the surgery site from injury and germs.
2. Keep the surgery site clean and dry.
3. Do not eat hot or spicy foods.
4. If any problems happen, get medical help right away.
5. See your regular doctor for checkups to watch for any changes in your health.

Discharge Medications: None.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 48 years
Gender: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospita Course:
The patient was admitted with a painful lump in the right iliac fossa region. The initial diagnostic impression was a query painful obstructed incisional hernia due to previous renal surgery. A CT scan was conducted, which showed a large retroperitoneal loculated collection. There was tracking of fluid into the anterior abdominal wall, with tiny gas pockets close to the anterior margin. The CT scan revealed that the patient had undergone bilateral autotransplanted kidneys in the iliac fossa region. Blood transfusion was given to the patient to optimize prior to the procedure.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient had a large retroperitoneal loculated collection.

Discharge Procedures:
The patient underwent a CT scan-abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast. Blood transfusions were optimized prior to the procedure.

Discharge Condition at Time of Discharge:
The patient’s condition was stable at the time of discharge.

Discharge Medications:
Medications are not required for discharge.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to follow up with the outpatient surgical clinic for further evaluation and management.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 48 years
Gender: Female
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

Hospital Stay:
The patient came to the hospital with a painful lump in the lower right belly area. At first, doctors thought it might be a bulge from a past kidney surgery that was blocked and causing pain. A scan of the belly was done. The scan showed a large pocket of trapped fluid deep in the belly behind the organs. Some of this fluid had spread into the belly wall. There were also tiny air bubbles near the front of the belly. The scan also showed that the patient had both kidneys moved to the lower belly area in a past surgery. The patient was given blood before any treatment to help prepare the body.

Discharge Diagnosis:
The patient had a large pocket of trapped fluid deep in the belly behind the organs.

Discharge Procedures:
The patient had a scan of the belly and pelvis area using dye put into a vein. Blood was given before the procedure to prepare the patient.

Condition at Time of Discharge:
The patient was in stable condition when leaving the hospital.

Discharge Medications:
No medications are needed at this time.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should schedule a visit with the surgery clinic for more tests and care.

input:
Hospital Course Summary:

Admitted for failure to thrive and extreme weight loss, a 56-year-old male presented with a history of refractory, sero-negative migratory arthritis and occasional episodes of abdominal pain and diarrhea. During initial testing, the patient exhibited neutrophilia, increased ESR, CRP levels, and abnormal WBC counts.

Over the course of several admissions, the patient's clinical condition continued to deteriorate, with thrombocytopenia and macrocytic anemia being added to his list of symptoms. Additionally, it was noted that he suffered from bilateral stenosis and total occlusion of the left internal carotid artery at the origin, among other issues.

Despite extensive testing and repeated hospital stays, the patient's condition remained mysterious, and no diagnosis was reached. However, he did test positive for Tropheryma whipplei PCR in the blood, which suggested the presence of Whipple's disease. Confirmation via small bowel biopsy was obtained, and the patient started a course of antibiotics for the disease.

As the patient's condition continued to decline, he was placed into hospice care, where he eventually passed away.

Overall, the patient's complex medical history and illness resisted easy diagnosis and treatment, despite a thorough course of testing and interventions.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was a 56-year-old man. He came to the hospital because he was not doing well and had lost a lot of weight. He had joint pain that moved around his body and did not respond to treatment. He also had belly pain and diarrhea from time to time. Early tests showed signs of infection and swelling in his body.

The patient came to the hospital several times. His health kept getting worse. He developed low levels of blood cells that help with clotting. He also had a type of blood problem where his red blood cells were too large. Doctors found that blood vessels in his neck were narrowed or blocked on both sides.

Doctors did many tests over several hospital stays. They could not figure out what was wrong for a long time. Then a blood test came back positive for a germ called Tropheryma whipplei. This pointed to a rare illness called Whipple's disease. Doctors took a small piece of tissue from his gut to confirm this. The patient then started taking medicine to fight the infection.

The patient's health kept getting worse even with treatment. He was moved to comfort care. He died while receiving this care.

The patient had many health problems that were hard to understand and treat. Doctors tried many tests and treatments but could not help him get better.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information: Mrs. X, 96-year-old female.

Chief Complaint: Dementia, physical restraints, and nasogastric tube.

Hospital Course:

Mrs. X was admitted to the hospital with a history of dementia, physical restraints and having nutrition via the nasogastric tube for the past three years. She was restrained with mittens as she attempted to remove the nasogastric tube. Her risk of aspiration pneumonia was high with a swallowing reflex time of 9.5 seconds. Repetitive saliva swallowing test also revealed the risk of aspiration.

Treatment and Intervention:

The patient received the extracts of traditional Chinese medicine Banxia Houpu Tang (BHT) through NGT for four weeks. This resulted in significant improvement in her swallowing reflex time which reduced to 3.5 seconds. The results of the repetitive saliva swallowing test also showed improvement. Oral feeding was subsequently initiated, starting from gelatinous foods to mixed ground foods of approximately 800 kcal. The nasogastric tube and physical restraints were later removed.

Outcome:

Mrs. X's oral intake improved significantly after receiving BHT extracts, and this facilitated the removal of her nasogastric tube and physical restraints. She was discharged in a stable condition, with instructions to continue her medication and follow-up with her primary care physician.

Follow-up:

Mrs. X was advised to continue her medication, eat a nourishing and balanced diet, follow-up with her primary care physician, and attend regular monitoring appointments for her swallowing reflex time.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information: Mrs. X, 96-year-old female.

Chief Complaint: Memory loss condition, physical restraints, and feeding tube through the nose.

Hospital Course:

Mrs. X was admitted to the hospital with a history of memory loss condition, physical restraints, and receiving nutrition through a feeding tube in her nose for the past three years. She wore mittens on her hands to stop her from pulling out the feeding tube. She had a high risk of food or liquids going into her lungs. Tests showed she took 9.5 seconds to swallow, which is too slow. Another swallowing test also showed she could choke easily.

Treatment and Intervention:

The patient received a traditional Chinese herbal medicine called Banxia Houpu Tang through her feeding tube for four weeks. Her swallowing got much better. Her swallowing time went down from 9.5 seconds to 3.5 seconds. The other swallowing test also showed improvement. The patient then started eating by mouth again. She began with soft jelly-like foods and moved to smooth blended foods of about 800 calories. The feeding tube and mittens were then removed.

Outcome:

Mrs. X was able to eat much better by mouth after receiving the herbal medicine. This allowed the care team to remove her feeding tube and physical restraints. She left the hospital in stable condition. She was told to keep taking her medicine and see her regular doctor.

Follow-up:

Mrs. X was told to keep taking her medicine and eat healthy, well-balanced meals. She should see her regular doctor and go to scheduled appointments to check her swallowing ability.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 33-year-old, Malaysian male, presented to the hospital following a nocturnal seizure. Prior to admission, the patient had flu-like symptoms, loss of appetite, and general myalgia. On admission, he was disoriented but had no lateralizing symptoms or signs. The patient experienced post-ictal periods of approximately 30 minutes and had a 21-second asystole during the third seizure. A permanent pacemaker was placed to address this complication.

The patient's neurological exam was unremarkable except for gaze-evoked nystagmus, thought to be a side effect of the administered phenytoin. Despite administration of anticonvulsant medication and subsequent optimization of phenytoin, the patient continued to have recurrent seizures. He developed additional symptoms such as right-sided headache, right ear pain, tenderness, and an unusual sound in the right ear which evolved into a hallucination of music. The patient also developed complex partial seizures, indicating the possibility of HSV encephalitis.

Investigations:
LPs performed on two separate occasions showed no abnormality other than a slightly elevated protein level. CT scans of the head with contrast were normal, and a SPECT scan showed a slight decrease in perfusion of the left side compared to the right. A repeat EEG showed nonspecific abnormality of the right temporal lobe.

Treatment:
The patient was started on a course of acyclovir, and over the first week of therapy, there was significant improvement. Seizures and headaches resolved, but the hallucination of music persisted for several days. The patient was discharged to complete a three-week course of acyclovir. While tapering the anticonvulsants, there were breakthrough seizures, so the patient remains on levetriacetam and phenytoin.

Summary:
The patient was diagnosed with possible HSV encephalitis, which was successfully managed with acyclovir therapy. The patient was discharged with instructions to continue the full course of medication and remain on anticonvulsants.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 33-year-old Malaysian male, came to the hospital after having a seizure during sleep. Before coming to the hospital, the patient had symptoms like the flu, no appetite, and muscle aches. When he arrived, he was confused but did not have weakness on one side of his body. After each seizure, the patient was confused for about 30 minutes. During the third seizure, his heart stopped beating for 21 seconds. Doctors put in a device called a pacemaker to keep his heart beating normally.

The patient's brain and nerve exam was mostly normal. He did have jerky eye movements, likely caused by a seizure medicine called phenytoin. Even after getting seizure medicine and adjusting the dose, the patient kept having seizures. He also started having pain on the right side of his head and right ear. He felt tenderness and heard a strange sound in his right ear. This later turned into hearing music that was not real. The patient also had a type of seizure that affects awareness and behavior. These signs pointed to a possible brain infection caused by the herpes virus.

Investigations:
Doctors took fluid from the spine two times. The tests were mostly normal but showed slightly high protein levels. Brain scans with dye were normal. A special scan showed slightly less blood flow on the left side of the brain than the right. A second brain wave test showed something slightly off in the right side of the brain near the ear.

Treatment:
The patient started taking a medicine called acyclovir to fight the virus. After one week, he got much better. The seizures and headaches stopped, but he still heard music that was not real for a few more days. The patient left the hospital to finish three weeks of acyclovir at home. When doctors tried to lower the seizure medicine, the seizures came back. So the patient is still taking two seizure medicines called levetiracetam and phenytoin.

Summary:
The patient was diagnosed with a possible brain infection caused by the herpes virus. The infection was treated well with acyclovir. The patient left the hospital with orders to finish all the medicine and keep taking the seizure medicines.

input:
Electronic Health Record - Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Attending Physician: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 4-month-old female, was admitted to the hospital due to poor eye contact. She was delivered at 35 weeks of gestation and had an unremarkable family history. The patient was diagnosed with Leber congenital amaurosis (LCA) due to the presence of esotropia and nystagmus, and the extinguished electroretinogram (ERG) in both eyes. Cycloplegic refractive errors were also noted, which were subsequently addressed. A slit lamp examination, fundus examination, and systemic evaluation for any metabolic and organ abnormalities were conducted, but no significant findings were observed except for the aforementioned ocular symptoms.

Diagnostic evaluations were performed, which included genetic testing for CRX, CRB1, GUCY2D, AIPL1, RDH12, RPE65, LRAT, RPGRIP1, and TULP1. However, the results were unremarkable. A brain MRI revealed the presence of a prominent enlargement in the subarachnoid space of both frontal convexities, and absence of the septum pellucidum. However, no focal lesions were found, and the size of the corpus callosum and white matter myelination were normal. Follow-up MRI scans showed the absence of the septum pellucidum, but with stable external hydrocephalus. A diagnosis of septo-optic dysplasia was considered, however, the normal appearance of the optic nerve and normal pituitary function left the diagnosis doubtful.

Medical History:
The patient did not have any notable medical history aside from the ocular and neuroimaging findings with LCA and the subarachnoid space abnormalities observed in the brain MRI scans.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient, a 4-month-old female, was admitted to the hospital for poor eye contact. She was diagnosed with LCA, and further diagnostic evaluations including genetic testing and a brain MRI were conducted. Follow-up MRI scans showed the absence of the septum pellucidum, but with stable external hydrocephalus. At the age of 3 years, the patient showed moderate developmental delay. No noteworthy medical history was obtained during her hospital course.
clean_ref:
Electronic Health Record - Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]
Attending Physician: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 4-month-old baby girl. She was brought to the hospital because she was not making eye contact. She was born 5 weeks early at 35 weeks of pregnancy. There were no known health problems in the family. The patient was found to have Leber congenital amaurosis, also called LCA. This is a rare eye condition that causes severe vision loss. The doctors found this because her eyes turned inward and moved back and forth on their own. A test that checks how the back of the eye responds to light showed no response in both eyes. The patient also had trouble focusing her eyes, which was treated. The doctors did several exams. They looked at the front and back of her eyes. They also checked her whole body for problems with her organs or body chemistry. These tests were normal except for the eye problems already mentioned.

The doctors did many tests to find the cause. They did genetic testing to look for changes in certain genes. These tests did not find any problems. A brain scan called an MRI was done. It showed extra fluid in the spaces around the front parts of the brain. It also showed that a thin wall in the middle of the brain was missing. This wall is called the septum pellucidum. No other brain problems were found. The main connection between the two sides of the brain looked normal. The protective covering around nerves in the brain was developing normally. Later brain scans still showed the missing wall in the middle of the brain. The extra fluid around the brain stayed the same and did not get worse. The doctors thought about a condition called septo-optic dysplasia. However, the eye nerves looked normal and the hormone gland at the base of the brain worked normally. This made the doctors unsure about that diagnosis.

Medical History:
The patient had no other health problems. Her only issues were the eye condition called LCA and the brain scan findings with extra fluid around the brain.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient is a 4-month-old baby girl who came to the hospital for poor eye contact. She was found to have LCA, a condition that causes severe vision loss. The doctors did genetic testing and brain scans. Later brain scans still showed the missing wall in the middle of the brain. The extra fluid around the brain stayed the same. By age 3, the patient showed some delays in learning and development. No other health problems were found during her time in the hospital.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 36-year-old female, presented to the hospital with a 4-year history of dull pain on the left back and a foreign body on the left side of the spine. She denied hematuria, dysuria, or frequency. The patient was admitted, and a computed tomography (CT) scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed moderate hydronephrosis of the left kidney. A surgical procedure was successfully performed to remove a 3-cm sewing needle that was found around the upper part of the left ureter. A double-J ureteral stent was placed to relieve the obstruction. After 6 months of follow-up, the patient's ureteral obstruction and hydronephrosis were significantly reduced, and the double-J ureteral stent was removed.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient presented to the hospital with a 4-year history of dull pain on the left back and a foreign body on the left side of the spine. She denied hematuria, dysuria, or frequency. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed moderate hydronephrosis of the left kidney. A surgical procedure was performed to remove a 3-cm sewing needle that was found around the upper part of the left ureter and a double-J ureteral stent was placed to relieve the obstruction. After 6 months of follow-up, the patient's ureteral obstruction and hydronephrosis were significantly reduced, and the double-J ureteral stent was removed.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 36-year-old woman. She came to the hospital with dull pain in her left back that had lasted 4 years. She also had a foreign object on the left side of her spine. She did not have blood in her urine, pain when urinating, or a need to urinate often. The patient was admitted to the hospital. A CT scan took pictures of her belly and lower body. The scan showed that her left kidney was swollen with fluid buildup. Doctors did surgery to remove a 3-cm sewing needle. The needle was found near the upper part of the tube that carries urine from the kidney to the bladder. A small tube called a stent was placed to help urine flow better. After 6 months of checkups, the blockage and swelling in the kidney were much better. The stent was then removed.

input:
Hospital Course: A 73-year-old male patient presented with severe postoperative hypertension following coronary artery bypass grafting. The patient had a medical history of type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, coronary heart disease, prostate cancer, and long-standing hypertension that had become harder to control during the past 2 years. The patient's family history was unremarkable, except for cases of sudden death among maternal relatives. A CT scan showed a small right adrenal mass, and biochemical testing revealed high levels of normetanephrines with no signs of familial syndromes. A meta-iodobenzylguanidine (MIBG) scan showed intense and moderate uptake of a 2 × 2 × 3 cm mass of the right adrenal gland with no distant metastases detected. The patient underwent adrenalectomy and was diagnosed with pheochromocytoma, confirmed by histology post-operatively. The patient has been discharged and will now continue with outpatient follow-up.

Summary of Hospital Stay: The patient was admitted due to severe postoperative hypertension that was difficult to manage despite medication. After further investigation, the diagnosis of Pheochromocytoma was confirmed by histology post-operative. The patient underwent adrenalectomy, and no complications were encountered during the procedure. The patient has been discharged and will be followed up in the outpatient clinic.

Diagnosis: Pheochromocytoma is a chromaffin cell tumor of the adrenal gland. It causes the excess production of catecholamines, including epinephrine, norepinephrine, and dopamine, resulting in variable symptoms of hypertension, palpitation, headache, sweating, and blurred vision.

Treatment: The patient underwent adrenalectomy to remove the tumor, after which there were no complications. The patient will continue to monitor and manage chronic conditions, including diabetes, dyslipidemia, and hypertension, with appropriate medication and lifestyle changes.

Prognosis: The prognosis is dependent on the size of the tumor, degree of malignancy, and any additional risk factors or comorbidities that the patient may have. In this case, the adrenal mass was small, and the Ki-67 proliferation index was less than one percent. Therefore, the patient's prognosis is positive.

Follow-up: The patient has been discharged and will return to the outpatient clinic for regular follow-up care to manage chronic conditions and monitor for recurrence or metastasis. Appropriate testing, including urine and plasma metanephrine levels, will be done to evaluate the patient's recovery.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course: A 73-year-old male patient came to the hospital with very high blood pressure after heart bypass surgery. The patient had a history of type 2 diabetes, high cholesterol, heart disease, prostate cancer, and high blood pressure that had been harder to control over the past 2 years. The patient's family history was normal, except for some sudden deaths among relatives on the mother's side. A CT scan showed a small growth on the right adrenal gland, which sits on top of the kidney. Blood tests showed high levels of certain hormones, with no signs of inherited conditions. A special imaging scan showed a small growth about 2 by 2 by 3 centimeters on the right adrenal gland with no spread to other parts of the body. The patient had surgery to remove the adrenal gland. Tests after surgery confirmed the growth was a pheochromocytoma, a type of tumor. The patient has been sent home and will continue care with regular doctor visits.

Summary of Hospital Stay: The patient was admitted because of very high blood pressure after surgery that was hard to control even with medicine. After more tests, doctors confirmed the patient had a pheochromocytoma, a type of tumor. The patient had surgery to remove the adrenal gland, and there were no problems during the procedure. The patient has been sent home and will see doctors at regular office visits.

Diagnosis: Pheochromocytoma is a tumor that grows in the adrenal gland. It makes the body produce too many stress hormones. This can cause high blood pressure, fast or pounding heartbeat, headaches, sweating, and blurry vision.

Treatment: The patient had surgery to remove the tumor, and there were no problems. The patient will keep taking medicine and making healthy lifestyle changes to manage ongoing health issues. These include diabetes, high cholesterol, and high blood pressure.

Prognosis: How well the patient does depends on the size of the tumor, whether it is cancerous, and other health problems. In this case, the growth was small. Lab tests showed the tumor cells were growing very slowly. Because of this, the patient's outlook is good.

Follow-up: The patient has been sent home and will return for regular doctor visits. These visits will help manage ongoing health issues and check if the tumor comes back or spreads. The patient will have blood and urine tests to check hormone levels and track recovery.
