input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 8 years old
Gender: Male
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Poor feeding, repeated upper respiratory infections, and inability to verbalize due to a wide cleft palate.

HOSPITAL COURSE:
An 8-year-old male child with Seckel syndrome and intrauterine growth retardation was admitted to our outpatient department with complaints of a wide cleft palate. After careful examination and investigation, it was decided to perform pushback palatoplasty under general anesthesia.

GA was administered using inhalational induction with sevoflurane in titrated doses. Anesthesia was maintained with oxygen and air along with titrated doses of sevoflurane and intermittent doses of atracurium. Pushback palatoplasty was done for incomplete cleft palate. The intraoperative period was uneventful, with no complications. The operation took approximately 75 minutes.

Postoperatively, the patient was monitored in the intensive care unit for any apneic spells, respiratory failure, or oral bleed. The postoperative period was uneventful, and the patient's vital signs remained stable. 

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
Wide cleft palate in a patient with Seckel syndrome and intrauterine growth retardation.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient was discharged home in stable condition. It is recommended to continue feeding the patient with soft and pureed foods that are easy to swallow. The patient should avoid foods that are hard, crunchy, or require significant chewing. Additionally, patients with Seckel syndrome should be monitored for potential developmental delays and co-morbidities. Follow-up with a pediatrician and a specialist for further evaluation and management is recommended.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient should schedule follow-up appointments with their pediatrician and specialists for further evaluation and management.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
None.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES AND PROCEDURES:
Wide cleft palate and pushback palatoplasty.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Age: 8 years old
Gender: Male
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]

Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

MAIN PROBLEM:
Poor feeding, repeated colds and lung infections, and trouble speaking due to a large opening in the roof of the mouth.

WHAT HAPPENED DURING THE HOSPITAL STAY:
An 8-year-old boy with Seckel syndrome and slow growth before birth was seen at our clinic. He had a large opening in the roof of his mouth. After a full checkup and tests, doctors decided to do surgery to close the opening. The patient was put to sleep using a breathing gas called sevoflurane. The patient stayed asleep with oxygen, air, sevoflurane, and a muscle relaxer called atracurium. The surgery closed the opening in the roof of the mouth. The surgery went well with no problems. It took about 75 minutes. After surgery, the patient was watched closely in the intensive care unit. Doctors checked for breathing pauses, breathing problems, or bleeding from the mouth. Recovery went well with no problems. The patient's heart rate, breathing, and other body signs stayed normal.

DIAGNOSIS AT DISCHARGE:
Large opening in the roof of the mouth in a patient with Seckel syndrome and slow growth before birth.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient went home in good condition. The patient should eat soft foods and foods mashed into a smooth paste. These foods are easy to swallow. The patient should not eat hard or crunchy foods. The patient should not eat foods that need a lot of chewing. Children with Seckel syndrome should be watched for slow development and other health problems. The patient should see a children's doctor and a specialist for more checkups and care.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient should make appointments with the children's doctor and specialists for more checkups and care.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
None.

DIAGNOSES AND PROCEDURES AT DISCHARGE:
Large opening in the roof of the mouth and surgery to close it.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient presented with intermittent abdominal fullness and nausea for 2 years, which improved after 10 minutes without any intervention. Endoscopic ultrasonography revealed a thickened gastric wall and an echo-poor submucosal layer of the gastric fundus. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography showed choledocholith, which was removed without any complications.

Diagnostic Assessment:
Gastroscopy revealed a protrusion lesion on the gastric fundus, and a lesion was removed during endoscopic submucosal dissection. An intraoperative cryopathological examination revealed disordered gastric mucosal glands and single-gland dilatation in the muscularis mucosa and submucosa, without malignancy.

Diagnostic Impression:
Based on the patient's clinical presentation and the diagnostic assessments, the patient was diagnosed with gastric fundus lesion with an eroded mucosal surface. Cholecystolithiasis and choledocholith were also identified.

Therapeutic Intervention:
Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography was performed to remove choledocholith, and endoscopic submucosal dissection was performed to remove the gastric fundus lesion.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient's condition significantly improved post-procedure, and the patient was discharged without any complications.

Follow-up:
The patient was advised to follow a low-fat diet and schedule a follow-up visit with the gastroenterologist to monitor for any symptoms and further treatment.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient came in with a feeling of fullness in the belly and nausea that had been happening on and off for 2 years. These symptoms got better after 10 minutes on their own. A special camera test using sound waves showed the stomach wall was thicker than normal. The inner layer of the upper part of the stomach looked different than expected. Another test that looks at the bile duct found a bile duct stone. The stone was removed without any problems.

Diagnostic Assessment:
A camera test looking inside the stomach found a bump in the upper part of the stomach. The bump was removed during a procedure that takes out tissue from under the stomach lining. A lab test done during the procedure showed the stomach tissue was not arranged normally. Some small tubes in the stomach lining were larger than usual. There were no signs of cancer.

Diagnostic Impression:
Based on the patient's symptoms and test results, the patient was found to have a growth in the upper stomach with a worn surface. The patient also had gallstones in the gallbladder and a stone in the bile duct.

Therapeutic Intervention:
A procedure was done to remove the bile duct stone. Another procedure was done to remove the growth from the upper part of the stomach.

Condition on Discharge:
The patient got much better after the procedures. The patient went home without any problems.

Follow-up:
The patient was told to eat foods low in fat. The patient should make an appointment with the stomach doctor. The doctor will check for any new symptoms and plan any treatment that may be needed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name], [Age], [Gender]

Admission date: [Date]
Discharge date: [Date]

Admission diagnosis: Abdominal pain 
Discharge diagnosis: Abscess of urachal remnants

Hospital course: 

The patient presented to the emergency department with severe abdominal pain associated with fever and nausea but without lower urinary tract symptoms. Physical examination revealed tenderness and focal rigidity over the umbilical area. Laboratory data showed leucocytosis with neutrophil predominance, and abdominal imaging confirmed the diagnosis of abscess of urachal remnants. 

The patient underwent medical-surgical treatment, which resulted in no postoperative complications. Pathological examination revealed non-malignant tissue, while a pus culture showed an Escherichia coli infection that was treated with ciprofloxacin. 

The patient remained symptom-free during the hospital course, and on follow-up examination two years later, was asymptomatic with no abnormalities of the abdominal wall.

Instructions at discharge: The patient was instructed on the administration of medications and wound care, as well as when to return for follow-up appointments. The patient was advised to contact the healthcare provider if there were any concerning symptoms or complications. 

Follow-up plan: 
-The patient was advised to return for follow-up appointments at regular intervals to monitor healing and ensure there were no recurrent abscesses.

Signed, [Your name], MD

Discharge Summary:

Patient: [Name], [Age], [Gender]

Admission date: [Date]
Discharge date: [Date]

Admission diagnosis: Belly pain
Discharge diagnosis: Infected pocket of pus in tissue left over from before birth near the belly button

Hospital course:

The patient came to the emergency room with bad belly pain along with fever and feeling sick to the stomach. The patient did not have problems with peeing. The physical exam showed pain and tightness when touching the belly button area. Blood tests showed a high number of white blood cells, which means the body was fighting an infection. Pictures of the belly confirmed an infected pocket of pus in tissue left over from before birth near the belly button.

The patient had treatment with medicine and surgery. There were no problems after the surgery. Tests on the removed tissue showed it was not cancer. The pus contained a common germ called E. coli. This infection was treated with an antibiotic called ciprofloxacin.

The patient had no symptoms during the rest of the hospital stay. At a check-up two years later, the patient felt fine and the belly area looked normal.

Instructions at discharge: The patient was told how to take medicines and care for the wound. The patient was also told when to come back for check-up visits. The patient was told to call the doctor if there were any worrying symptoms or problems.

Follow-up plan: The patient was told to come back for regular check-up visits. These visits are to make sure healing is going well and the infection does not come back.

Signed, [Your name], MD

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Mr C
Age: 60s
Sex: Male
Admission Date: [not provided]
Discharge Date: [not provided]

Hospital Course:

Mr C was admitted for psychological therapy due to symptoms of PTSD he developed after serving in the UK military police during the Northern Ireland peacekeeping operations in the 1960s. At the time of admission, he reported experiencing intrusive fragmented images of a trauma memory in his right peripheral field of vision, which he had been avoiding for years. He also reported experiencing fluctuating mood states that included detachment, overwhelming anxiety symptoms when confronted by a reminder of the event, and depression. Mr C had previously used alcohol to cope with his symptoms.

During his admission, Mr C was found to be avoidant in engaging in TF-CBT. He utilized several therapy sessions to discuss his concerns and attempted to use multiple grounding strategies in the therapy room. However, he either presented as matter of fact and detached from any emotion or was unable to tolerate 'in vivo' reliving of his trauma memory that involved closing his eyes and felt overwhelmed by physical panic symptoms leading to dissociation.

Mr C's PTSD symptoms were maintained as he avoided confronting his trauma memory. He did not gain access to his peri-traumatic cognitions in both reliving sessions.

Outcome:

Mr C was discharged after completing his psychological therapy. He was provided with some coping strategies that could help him manage his PTSD symptoms in case they re-emerged. No further medical intervention was recommended at the time of discharge.

Prognosis:

Prognosis for Mr C is considered fair. However, it is likely that his PTSD symptoms will return if he avoids confronting his trauma memory. It is recommended that he continues to practice coping strategies to manage his PTSD symptoms. Follow-up sessions were not recommended.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Mr C
Age: 60s
Sex: Male
Admission Date: not provided
Discharge Date: not provided

Hospital Course:

Mr C came to the hospital for talk therapy. He has post-traumatic stress disorder, also called PTSD. This is a mental health condition that can happen after a very upsetting event. Mr C developed PTSD after working as a military police officer in Northern Ireland in the 1960s. When he arrived, he said he kept seeing broken pieces of a scary memory in the corner of his right eye. He had been trying to avoid this memory for years. He also said his moods changed a lot. Sometimes he felt numb or cut off from his feelings. Other times he felt very worried and anxious when something reminded him of the event. He also felt sad and down. In the past, Mr C drank alcohol to help deal with these feelings.

During his stay, Mr C had trouble taking part in trauma-focused talk therapy. He used several sessions to talk about his worries. He tried different calming methods in the therapy room. But he either talked about things without showing any feelings or could not handle reliving his scary memory. When asked to close his eyes and go back to the memory, he felt too much panic in his body. This made him feel disconnected from what was happening around him.

Mr C's PTSD symptoms stayed the same because he kept avoiding his scary memory. In both sessions where he tried to relive the memory, he could not reach the thoughts and beliefs he had during the original event.

Outcome:

Mr C left the hospital after finishing his talk therapy. The care team gave him some ways to cope that could help him handle his PTSD symptoms if they come back. No other treatment was suggested when he left.

Prognosis:

The outlook for Mr C is fair. But his PTSD symptoms will likely come back if he keeps avoiding his scary memory. The care team suggests he keep using his coping methods to manage his PTSD symptoms. No follow-up visits were scheduled.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Pain in the left hand for 2 months

Hospital Course:

The patient presented with pain in the left hand that had been present for 2 months. He was referred to our hospital after undergoing a plain radiography at a local clinic which revealed a tumor in the second metacarpal bone. During the hospitalization, the patient underwent measurements of routine laboratory examinations, routine blood tests, routine urine tests, and blood biochemistry, which were within normal levels, and revealed no evidence of any underlying medical conditions.

Physical examination revealed stiffness and tenderness over the dorsum of the second metacarpal bone. Plain radiography, computed tomography, and MRI imaging revealed cortical expansion and well-demarcated lucency in the shaft of the second metacarpal bone with surrounding sclerosis and central calcification. The tumor exhibited a distinct margin on MRI with low signal intensity on T1-weighted images and high signal intensity on T2-weighted and short tau inversion recovery images. Axial contrast-enhanced images revealed only the tumor margin to be enhanced. No abnormal intensity was detected in the bone marrow or soft tissues around the lesion.

The patient was referred to an appropriate specialist for further diagnosis and treatment. We recommended that the patient follow up with the specialist on an outpatient basis.

Discharge Diagnosis: Inconclusive, requires further evaluation and treatment

Follow-up: Referral to a specialist to determine further diagnosis and treatment.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
Admitting Diagnosis: Pain in the left hand for 2 months

Hospital Course:

The patient came in with left hand pain that had lasted for 2 months. The patient was sent to our hospital after an X-ray at another clinic showed a growth in a hand bone. This bone is called the second metacarpal and is located below the pointer finger. During the hospital stay, the patient had many tests done. These included blood tests and urine tests. All test results came back normal. The tests showed no signs of other health problems.

A physical exam showed stiffness and soreness on top of the hand bone. The patient had X-rays, a CT scan, and an MRI done. These scans showed a growth in the hand bone. The bone wall had expanded and there was a clear dark spot inside. There was also some hardening around the growth and calcium buildup in the center. The MRI showed the growth had clear edges. The growth appeared dark on one type of image and bright on other types. When dye was used, only the edges of the growth showed up brighter. The bone and soft tissue around the growth looked normal.

The patient was sent to a doctor who specializes in this type of problem. We told the patient to see this specialist at their office for more care.

Discharge Diagnosis: Not yet known. The patient needs more tests and treatment.

Follow-up: The patient should see a specialist to find out what the growth is and get treatment.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

History of Presenting Illness:
The patient presented with stable angina pectoris lasting for two months. The patient had a past medical history of diabetes and high cholesterol. He was a smoker with no previous cardiac events. Coronary angiography was performed due to a positive treadmill exercise test, which revealed diffuse dissection that originated from the proximal bed of the second obtuse marginal-II branch and extended to its distal bed.

Hospital Course:
The patient received medical treatment with a beta-blocker and aspirin, as coronary angioplasty was not feasible due to technical difficulties and the narrowness of the ischemia-related artery. The patient remained stable throughout the hospital admission and was discharged with instructions to follow up with his primary care physician.

Summary:
The patient, a 52-year-old male, presented with stable angina pectoris, and a coronary angiogram revealed diffuse dissection originating from the second obtuse marginal-II branch. He was managed with a beta-blocker and aspirin, and due to the technical difficulties, coronary angioplasty was not feasible. The patient was discharged to follow up with his primary care physician.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
Admission Date: [REDACTED]
Discharge Date: [REDACTED]

History of Presenting Illness:
The patient came in with chest pain that had been happening for two months. This chest pain was steady and happened during activity. The patient had a history of diabetes and high cholesterol. He was a smoker with no past heart problems. A stress test on a treadmill showed a problem. A test using dye to look at the heart arteries was done. This test showed a tear in the wall of one of the small heart arteries. The tear started near the beginning of the artery and went to the end.

Hospital Course:
The patient was given medicine to slow the heart rate and aspirin to thin the blood. A procedure to open the artery was not possible. The artery was too narrow and hard to reach. The patient stayed stable during the hospital stay. The patient was sent home with instructions to see his regular doctor.

Summary:
The patient is a 52-year-old man who came in with steady chest pain. A test with dye showed a tear in the wall of a small heart artery. He was treated with medicine to slow the heart rate and aspirin. A procedure to open the artery could not be done because of the size and location of the artery. The patient was sent home to follow up with his regular doctor.

The patient was given medicine to slow the heart rate and aspirin to thin the blood. A procedure to open the artery was not possible. The artery was too narrow and hard to reach. The patient stayed stable during the hospital stay. The patient was sent home with instructions to see his regular doctor.

Summary:
The patient is a 52-year-old man who came in with steady chest pain. A test with dye showed a tear in the wall of a small heart artery. He was treated with medicine to slow the heart rate and aspirin. A procedure to open the artery could not be done because of the size and location of the artery. The patient was sent home to follow up with his regular doctor.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient is a 55-year-old male who presented to the emergency department with acute chest pain and a positive nasopharyngeal swab test for severe acute respiratory syndrome virus (SARS-CoV). The patient had a positive family history of myocardial infarction and hypertension, and was living in a home with two active home-isolated cases of COVID-19 in the immediate family. On physical examination, the patient had pericardial friction rub and ST-segment elevation in almost all of the leads. Echocardiographic examination also showed mild-to-moderate pericardial effusion. The patient was diagnosed with acute pericarditis secondary to COVID-19 illness.

Indomethacin and aspirin were prescribed, and pericardiocentesis was planned. The patient had not received any treatment before coming to the emergency department. The patient was discharged from the hospital with stable vital signs, and is advised to continue taking medications as prescribed and to follow up with their healthcare provider for ongoing management of hypertension. The patient is also advised to follow up with their healthcare provider for ongoing management of COVID-19 illness and to monitor symptoms for any potential complications.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

The patient is a 55-year-old man who came to the emergency room with sudden chest pain. A nose swab test showed the patient had COVID-19. The patient has family members who have had heart attacks and high blood pressure. The patient was also living at home with two family members who had COVID-19 at the time.

During the exam, the doctor heard a rubbing sound from the sac around the heart. Heart test readings showed signs of swelling in most areas. An ultrasound of the heart showed a small to medium amount of fluid around the heart. The patient was diagnosed with swelling of the sac around the heart caused by COVID-19.

The doctors gave the patient two medicines called indomethacin and aspirin. They also planned a procedure to drain fluid from around the heart. The patient had not received any treatment before coming to the emergency room. The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient should keep taking all medicines as directed. The patient should see their doctor to manage high blood pressure. The patient should also see their doctor to manage COVID-19 and watch for any warning signs of other problems.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The 16-year-old Bangladeshi girl presented to our hospital with hoarseness of voice, which had been gradually worsening over the last 4 months. She was treated with antibiotics by her local physician. Evaluation with a fiberoptic endoscopy revealed a polyp in the anterior 2/3 of the right vocal cord with impaired right vocal cord mobility. The patient underwent excision of the polyp, and subsequent histopathology showed well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. She was referred to the oncology clinic for further management.

The patient had no history of using tobacco or consuming alcohol, and no exposure to radiation/carcinogenic agents. There was no family history of malignancy. Computed tomography scan of the neck showed an irregularity of the right vocal cord, and no neck nodes were observed. Her biopsy slides confirmed the diagnosis of carcinoma, which was staged as T2N0M0 well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the larynx.

The patient was planned for radical radiotherapy. The treatment fields were as per the standard guidelines, as described previously. Neck nodes were not prophylactically treated, as the incidence of nodal positivity is <2% in T2 lesions. The patient was treated with radical radiotherapy using 6 MV photons in a linear accelerator. The patient tolerated the radiotherapy well and is on regular follow-up.

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Follow-Up Plans:

The patient is advised to continue 6-month follow-up visits for appropriate monitoring.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Medical Record Number: [Redacted]

Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The 16-year-old Bangladeshi girl came to our hospital because her voice had become hoarse. This problem had been getting worse over the past 4 months. Her local doctor had treated her with antibiotics. Doctors used a thin, flexible camera to look at her throat. They found a growth on the front part of her right voice box. Her right voice box was also not moving properly. The patient had surgery to remove the growth. Lab tests showed the growth was a type of cancer called squamous cell carcinoma. It was a slow-growing type. She was sent to the cancer clinic for more care.

The patient had never used tobacco or alcohol. She had not been around radiation or cancer-causing substances. No one in her family had cancer. A special scan of her neck showed a problem with her right voice box. No spread to the neck glands was found. The lab tests confirmed cancer. The cancer was at an early stage and had not spread to other parts of the body.

The patient was scheduled for radiation therapy to destroy the cancer. The treatment followed standard guidelines used before. The neck glands were not treated because this type of early cancer rarely spreads there. The patient received radiation therapy using a special machine. The patient handled the radiation treatment well. She is now coming back for regular check-ups.

Discharge Medications:

N/A

Follow-Up Plans:

The patient is advised to come back for check-up visits every 6 months so doctors can watch her progress.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name:
Gender: Male
Age: 38
Date of Admission: [Date of admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of discharge]
Admitting Diagnosis: Painless scrotal mass

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to [Hospital Name] with a chief complaint of a painless left scrotal mass. The patient had no significant past medical history and reported discovering the mass one month prior to admission. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm well-defined mass in the upper pole of the left testis with a firm consistency. Palpation of the right testis and lower pole of the left testis were normal. Routine blood tests were unremarkable. A bioassay of testicular tumor markers was ordered which revealed normal levels of Alpha-fetoprotein, human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). An extratesticular mass was observed on scrotal ultrasound, attached to the upper pole of the patient's testis. The mass was isoechoic to the testis parenchyma and poorly vascularized on Doppler. The patient underwent a radical inguinal orchiectomy. Histological examination of the operative specimen confirmed the presence of regular splenic tissue in the suspect mass without any signs of malignancy. The splenic proliferation had a preserved capsule, demarcating from the testis. Testicular pulp, the albuginea, and the tunica vaginalis had a preserved microscopic architecture. The patient was discharged on the second postoperative day.

Discharge Diagnosis: Painless scrotal mass

Summary:
A 38-year-old North African man presented with a painless left scrotal mass. A scrotal ultrasound showed an extratesticular mass, attached to the upper pole of his testis. The patient underwent a radical inguinal orchiectomy which confirmed the presence of splenic tissue in the suspect mass without any signs of malignancy. The patient was discharged on the second postoperative day and was examined again after 3 weeks and 2 months in our out-patient unit, where his spleen was found to be of habitual location with standard measurements and a regular aspect.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name:
Gender: Male
Age: 38
Date of Admission: [Date of admission]
Date of Discharge: [Date of discharge]
Admitting Diagnosis: Painless lump in the scrotum

Hospital Course:
The patient came to [Hospital Name] because of a painless lump on the left side of the scrotum. The patient had no major health problems in the past. The patient found the lump one month before coming to the hospital. A physical exam showed a 2 cm firm lump at the top of the left testicle. The lump had clear edges. The right testicle and the bottom of the left testicle felt normal. Blood tests came back normal. Tests for cancer markers in the blood were also normal. These included Alpha-fetoprotein, pregnancy hormone (hCG), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). An ultrasound of the scrotum showed a mass outside the testicle. The mass was attached to the top of the testicle. The mass looked similar to testicle tissue and had poor blood flow. The patient had surgery to remove the left testicle through a cut in the groin. Lab tests on the removed tissue showed the lump was made of normal spleen tissue. There were no signs of cancer. The spleen tissue had its own covering and was separate from the testicle. The testicle tissue and its outer layers looked normal under the microscope. The patient left the hospital two days after surgery.

Discharge Diagnosis: Painless lump in the scrotum

Summary:
A 38-year-old North African man came in with a painless lump on the left side of his scrotum. An ultrasound showed a mass outside the testicle, attached to the top of the testicle. The patient had surgery to remove the testicle through the groin. Lab tests showed the lump was made of spleen tissue with no signs of cancer. The patient left the hospital two days after surgery. The patient came back for checkups at 3 weeks and 2 months. At those visits, the spleen was in its normal place with normal size and appearance.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Not provided
Medical Record Number: Not provided

Hospital Course: The patient, a 50-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 16 years, was admitted to the hospital with neovascularization of the disc in her left eye and severe macular edema with subretinal fluid. Prior to admission, she had received an intravitreal injection of bevacizumab but with limited response. 

Treatment: The patient underwent vitrectomy, internal limiting membrane peeling, panretinal laser photocoagulation, and intraocular injection of dexamethasone implant (0.7 mg).

Outcome: The patient remained in the hospital for observation and follow-up. Her 12-month postoperative visual acuity remained at 20/63, and her central subfield macular thickness decreased from 712 μm to 280 μm. Her 12-month follow-up optical coherence tomography showed persistent loss of the ellipsoid zone.

Recommendations: The patient is recommended to continue to undergo follow-up examinations and regular ophthalmic care. 

Follow-up care: The patient is advised to continue any medication that was prescribed for her by her physician. She should also follow a healthy lifestyle to control her blood sugar and blood pressure. She is advised to seek medical treatment promptly if there is any worsening of the macular edema. 

Signed, 
[Physician's Name] 
[Date] 
[Electronic Signature]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Not provided
Medical Record Number: Not provided

Hospital Course: The patient is a 50-year-old woman who has had type 2 diabetes for 16 years. She was admitted to the hospital because of problems in her left eye. She had abnormal blood vessel growth on the nerve at the back of her eye. She also had swelling in the center of her vision with fluid buildup under the eye's light-sensing layer. Before coming to the hospital, she had received an eye injection of a medicine called bevacizumab. This treatment did not help much.

Treatment: The patient had eye surgery. The doctor removed the gel inside her eye. The doctor also removed a thin layer from the surface of the center of her vision. Laser treatment was applied to the back of her eye to stop abnormal blood vessel growth. A slow-release steroid medicine implant was placed inside her eye to reduce swelling.

Outcome: The patient stayed in the hospital so doctors could watch her recovery. After 12 months, her vision in that eye measured 20/63. The swelling in the center of her vision went down a lot. It went from 712 to 280 on the thickness measurement. A special eye scan at 12 months showed lasting damage to a layer of cells that sense light.

Recommendations: The patient should keep going to her follow-up eye exams. She should continue to get regular eye care.

Follow-up care: The patient should keep taking any medicines her doctor prescribed. She should follow a healthy lifestyle to control her blood sugar and blood pressure. She should get medical help right away if the swelling in her eye gets worse.

Signed,
[Physician's Name]
[Date]
[Electronic Signature]

input:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 44-year-old Hispanic male who was admitted for the evaluation of bilateral ocular inflammation in the setting of treatment with ipilimumab for metastatic cutaneous melanoma. He had received his third infusion two months prior to presentation, which led to systemic inflammatory side effects from therapy including rash, diarrhea, fever, and persistent rectal abscesses.

Physical Exam:
Upon presentation, the patient had central scotoma in the left eye, and both eyes showed pigment on the endothelium without keratic precipitates. Bilateral optic nerve edema with Paton’s lines and macular edema were noted on fundus examination. Cystic intraretinal and subfoveal fluid was also identified during Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT).

Treatment:
The patient was initially treated with topical therapy with prednisolone acetate and was referred to the uveitis service for further evaluation. Bilateral prednisolone acetate 1% was started four times a day with 0.2% brimonidine tartrate-0.5% timolol maleate ophthalmic solution twice a day and increased to 80 mg of oral prednisone daily. The patient was continually monitored and had his medication doses adjusted to promote healing.

Improvement and Discharge:
Over the course of following three weeks, his vision steadily improved with resolution of the optic nerve and retinal edema and subsequent development of a macular star. At the 2-month follow-up, his vision in both eyes improved to 20/25 and his anterior chamber reaction stabilized at 1+ flare and rare cell while taking prednisolone acetate four times a day. The patient was then lost to follow-up.
clean_ref:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Hospital Course:
The patient is a 44-year-old Hispanic male who was admitted for eye swelling in both eyes. This happened while he was being treated with a cancer drug called ipilimumab for skin cancer that had spread to other parts of his body. He had received his third treatment two months before coming to the hospital. The cancer treatment caused side effects throughout his body including a skin rash, loose stools, fever, and ongoing sores near his bottom.

Physical Exam:
When the patient arrived, he had a blind spot in the center of his left eye. Both eyes showed color deposits on the inner surface of the clear front layer of the eye. There were no clumps of cells stuck to this layer. The doctor looked at the back of both eyes and found swelling of the nerve that connects the eye to the brain. There were also curved lines around this nerve caused by the swelling. The central part of the back of the eye was also swollen. A special scan of the eye found small pockets of fluid in and under the center of the back of the eye.

Treatment:
The patient was first treated with steroid eye drops called prednisolone acetate. He was then sent to doctors who specialize in eye swelling for more care. He was given prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops for both eyes four times a day. He also received eye drops twice a day to lower eye pressure. These drops contained brimonidine tartrate 0.2% and timolol maleate 0.5%. His treatment was increased to include 80 mg of prednisone pills taken by mouth each day. The doctors kept checking on the patient and changed his medicine amounts to help him heal.

Improvement and Discharge:
Over the next three weeks, the patient's vision got steadily better. The swelling in the eye nerve and back of the eye went away. A star-shaped pattern then appeared in the central part of the back of the eye. At a check-up two months later, his vision in both eyes improved to nearly normal at 20/25. The swelling inside the front of his eyes became stable with only mild signs of irritation while using prednisolone acetate eye drops four times a day. The patient then stopped coming to his appointments.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: Louise
Age: 16
Gender: Female
Diagnosis: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)

Hospital Course:
Louise, a 16-year-old female, presented with ASD that was determined to function at the cognitive level of a 13-year-old child. Louise had been raised in a small rural village in Italy by her biological parents up until the age of nine when her parents divorced. She was then relocated to Canada by her father, where she continued her education and received psychological and psychiatric treatment for her underlying condition, as well as for comorbid conditions such as OCD.

Over the course of 5 years, while Louise remained in Canada, she connected with various mental health workers and attended school with special modifications to her programming. She was later ordered to be returned to Italy by the court as she was not deemed to meet grave significant harm criteria under her mother's care, although the psychiatric opinion concluded that Louise may suffer potential harm should she be returned to Italy given the disruption of her environment and reduced access to child and adolescent psychiatric services that would likely exacerbate her symptoms.

The psychiatric assessment also delved into Louise's mother's alleged emotional abuse toward her. During her stay in Italy, Louise was reportedly belittled and called derogatory words, which were translated to mean “retard” or “mongrel.” The nature of her underlying OCD was also explored and highlighted as a factor that may contribute to the potential harm that Louise would face should she be returned to Italy.

The discharge summary concludes that Louise's case is a complex one given her condition and history, and it is imperative that she receives continued medical attention and support in managing her condition. The psychiatric opinion stresses the need for access to child and adolescent psychiatric services and educational modifications, which had been provided during her stay in Canada, to be made available in her new environment. The discharge summary recommends close monitoring of her condition and progress, and regular follow-up with medical professionals for continued review and adjustment of her treatment plan as appropriate.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
Name: Louise
Age: 16
Gender: Female
Diagnosis: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD), a condition that affects how a person communicates and interacts with others

Hospital Course:
Louise is a 16-year-old girl with autism. Testing showed she thinks and learns at the level of a 13-year-old. Louise grew up in a small country village in Italy with both parents until age nine. Her parents then split up. Her father moved her to Canada. There she went to school and got help from mental health doctors. She also received treatment for a related condition called OCD. OCD causes unwanted repeated thoughts and behaviors.

Over 5 years in Canada, Louise worked with many mental health helpers. She attended school with special changes made to help her learn. Later, a court ordered her to go back to Italy to live with her mother. The court did not find that living with her mother would cause serious harm. However, the mental health doctors disagreed. They believed moving Louise back to Italy could hurt her. The change in her surroundings and less access to mental health care for young people would likely make her symptoms worse.

The mental health doctors also looked into claims that the mother emotionally mistreated Louise. During her time in Italy, Louise was reportedly put down and called hurtful names. The doctors also noted that her OCD could make the move to Italy even harder for her.

The doctors conclude that Louise has a complicated situation because of her condition and history. She needs ongoing medical care and support. The mental health doctors say it is important for Louise to have access to mental health services for young people. She also needs special changes at school to help her learn. These services were available to her in Canada. They should also be available wherever she lives now. The doctors suggest watching her condition closely. They recommend regular visits with medical professionals to check her progress and change her treatment plan if needed.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 61-year-old male with history of controlled type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia and past smoking presented with lumbar pain and weight loss.

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to Internal Medicine due to lumbar pain and weight loss. A CT scan revealed fractures and a mass in the left paravertebral region that was suspected to be tumoral in nature. Further imaging studies revealed a mass in the right hilum and another one in the left upper lobe, as well as inflammatory/infectious micronodules. A mass biopsy was performed and aspirated a purulent liquid that was positive for MT complex. The patient was started on quaternary antibacillary therapy with HRZE. The patient also received physiatry and orthopedic consultations, as well as rehabilitation physiotherapy. He was discharged after 25 days and forwarded to rehabilitation physiotherapy and Tuberculosis Differentiated Ambulatory Unit.

Follow-up: The patient has completed a two-month course of quaternary therapy and is currently under two-drug antibacillary therapy with HR. There was a complete disappearance of peripheric masses and a reduction in mass size was observed in the lumbar CT reevaluation.

Diagnosis: Chronic granulomatous inflammatory process consistent with tuberculosis.

Condition at discharge: Stable.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 61-year-old male with a history of controlled type 2 diabetes, high cholesterol, and past smoking. He came to the hospital with lower back pain and weight loss.

Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to Internal Medicine because of lower back pain and weight loss. A CT scan showed broken bones and a growth near the left side of the spine. Doctors thought it might be cancer. More imaging tests showed a growth near the right lung and another in the upper left lung. There were also small spots that could be from infection or swelling. Doctors took a sample from the growth and found pus. Tests showed it contained tuberculosis bacteria. The patient started treatment with four medicines to fight tuberculosis, called HRZE. The patient also saw specialists for physical medicine and bone care. He received physical therapy to help him recover. After 25 days, the patient left the hospital. He was sent to continue physical therapy and to visit a special clinic for tuberculosis care.

Follow-up: The patient finished two months of treatment with four medicines. He is now taking two medicines to fight tuberculosis, called HR. The growths outside the lungs have gone away completely. A new CT scan of the lower back showed the growth there has gotten smaller.

Diagnosis: Long-term infection with swelling caused by tuberculosis.

Condition at discharge: Stable.

Follow-up: The patient finished two months of treatment with four medicines. He is now taking two medicines to fight tuberculosis, called HR. The growths outside the lungs have gone away completely. A new CT scan of the lower back showed the growth there has gotten smaller.

Diagnosis: Long-term infection with swelling caused by tuberculosis.

Condition at discharge: Stable.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient is a 43-year-old previously healthy Caucasian male who was admitted due to persistent fever, abdominal pain, posterior chest pain, and earache. He was diagnosed with external otitis and discharged with oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and paracetamol. Laboratory tests at that time showed increased CRP and ALT levels. Following discharge, patient presented with hyperchromic urine, hypocolic faeces, and jaundice. Bilateral laterocervical lymph nodes were found, but no other alterations were detected in the physical examination, chest X-ray, or abdominal ultrasound.
The patient's blood tests revealed an increase in WBC, moderate increase in lipase, and increased bilirubin levels. So, he was given antibiotics, including Piperacillin-Tazobactam and Tigecycline, however, his condition worsened, leading to multiorgan failure and admission to the ICU. Autoimmune profile work-up, immunoglobulin values and lymphocytic typing were normal, while hepatic biopsy showed fragmented liver tissue with accentuated lobular architecture, portals enlarged for sclerosis and lymphocytic inflammatory infiltration, and granulocyte involvement. Subsequently, corticosteroids were initiated, which resulted in an improvement in the patient's condition, as well as a reduction in cholestasis parameters, acute phase reactants, and hepatic cytolysis.
The patient was discharged after 40 days of hospitalization, with tapering corticosteroid therapy. On the 47th day following discharge, the patient underwent a Coronary Computed Tomography Angiography, which was normal. Eighteen months later, the patient was reported as completely asymptomatic.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

The patient is a 43-year-old white male who was generally healthy before this illness. He was admitted to the hospital because of ongoing fever, belly pain, back chest pain, and ear pain. He was found to have an outer ear infection and sent home with amoxicillin-clavulanate and paracetamol, both taken by mouth. Blood tests at that time showed higher than normal levels of CRP, a marker of swelling in the body, and ALT, a liver enzyme.

After leaving the hospital, the patient came back with dark urine, pale stool, and yellow skin and eyes. Swollen lymph nodes were found on both sides of the neck. No other problems were found during the physical exam, chest X-ray, or belly ultrasound.

The patient's blood tests showed a rise in white blood cells, a mild rise in lipase, a digestive enzyme, and higher bilirubin levels. He was given two strong antibiotics called Piperacillin-Tazobactam and Tigecycline. However, his condition got worse. Several of his organs began to fail, and he was moved to the intensive care unit. Tests for immune system problems and immune cell levels came back normal. A liver tissue sample showed damaged liver tissue with changes in structure, scarring, and signs of swelling caused by certain white blood cells. The patient was then started on steroid medicines. This helped improve his condition. His liver function and signs of swelling in the body also got better.

The patient was sent home after 40 days in the hospital. He was given steroid medicine with the dose slowly going down over time. On day 47 after leaving the hospital, he had a heart scan using a special X-ray with dye. The results were normal. Eighteen months later, the patient had no symptoms at all.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Physician: N/A
Attending Physician: N/A

Hospital Course:

The 50-year-old male agriculturist was referred to our hospital for the management of acute crisis. The patient was admitted with a history of intermittent epigastric pain. He had undergone exploratory laparotomy for generalized peritonitis and duodenal perforation two days ago at a general hospital.

The patient had renal failure with hypokalemia and complained of severe abdominal pain and decreased urine output for the last 5 days at the time of admission in the general hospital. After surgery, the patient had puffiness of face and breathlessness, severe metabolic acidosis, hypotension, altered sensorium, and fever. The patient was put on ventilatory support and antibiotics (aztreonam, metronidazole, and intravenous fluids) considering bacterial peritonitis. Despite all these measures, the patient's condition deteriorated.

Peripheral blood examination showed normocytic normochromic anemia with elongated cells, stomatocytes and target cells. The results of laboratory examinations were as follows: Neutrophilic leucocytosis, thrombocytopenia, elevated ESR, elevated serum urea and creatinine levels, hypernatremia, hyperkalemia, elevated total bilirubin and conjugated bilirubin. Ultra-sonography revealed mild parenchymal renal disease, minimal perisplenic collection in the abdomen, and dilated intrahepatic biliary ducts. Urine microscopy showed 2-3 pus cells/HPF. Peritoneal fluid Gram stain smear showed occasional pus cells with few budding yeast cells and many barrel-shaped arthroconidia. Blood culture was sterile after 24 hours.

Peritoneal fluid culture was positive for Trichosporon beigelii. On the basis of these tests, the infection was treated as per standard medical protocol. Despite the treatment, the patient's condition continued to deteriorate, and hence, he was referred for the management of acute crisis.

Given the severity of the patient's condition, he will receive ongoing supportive care. Further treatment will be provided as necessary.

Discharge Diagnosis:

The patient was diagnosed with Trichosporon beigelii infection, which was treated during his hospital stay.

Follow-up Care:

The patient will receive ongoing follow-up care to ensure that he receives the necessary treatment. Any follow-up care instructions will be provided during subsequent visits.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A
Admitting Physician: N/A
Attending Physician: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 50-year-old male farmer who was sent to our hospital for emergency care. The patient came in with stomach pain that came and went. Two days before, he had surgery at another hospital to fix a hole in his small intestine and treat an infection that had spread in his belly.

When the patient first went to the other hospital, his kidneys were not working well. He had low potassium levels in his blood. He also had bad belly pain and was making very little urine for 5 days. After surgery, the patient had swelling in his face and trouble breathing. He also had too much acid in his blood, low blood pressure, confusion, and fever. The patient was put on a breathing machine and given medicines to fight infection. These medicines included aztreonam and metronidazole given through a vein. Even with all this care, the patient got worse.

Blood tests showed the patient had a low red blood cell count with some oddly shaped cells. Other test results showed the following. The patient had too many white blood cells and not enough cells that help blood clot. Tests that show swelling in the body were high. Kidney function tests showed the kidneys were not working well. Salt and potassium levels in the blood were too high. Liver tests were also high. An imaging test of the belly showed mild kidney disease and a small fluid buildup near the spleen. It also showed swollen tubes in the liver. A urine test showed a small number of cells that fight infection. A test of fluid from the belly showed some cells that fight infection along with yeast and fungus. A blood test for germs came back clean after 24 hours.

A test of belly fluid found a fungus called Trichosporon beigelii. Based on these tests, the infection was treated using standard medical care. Even with treatment, the patient kept getting worse. He was then sent here for emergency care.

Given how sick the patient is, he will keep getting care to support his body. More treatment will be given as needed.

Discharge Diagnosis:

The patient was found to have an infection caused by a fungus called Trichosporon beigelii. This was treated during his hospital stay.

Follow-up Care:

The patient will keep getting follow-up care to make sure he gets the treatment he needs. Instructions for follow-up care will be given at future visits.

input:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Not specified
Medical Record Number: Not specified

Admission Date: Not specified
Discharge Date: Not specified

Admitting Diagnosis: Exudative AMD with Subfoveal PED and an Associated MH

Hospital Course: The 73-year-old Caucasian female was admitted with decreased vision and metamorphopsia for 6 months in her left eye. Initial examination showed best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) of 20/80 left eye (LE) and 20/25 right eye (RE). Fundus biomicroscopy revealed the presence of exudative AMD with a subfoveal PED and an associated MH. Spectral domain optical coherence tomography (SD-OCT) confirmed a full-thickness MH with attached edges and a partially detached posterior hyaloid on top of the prominent PED. Intravitreal anti-VEGF-therapy with three loading doses of a monthly bevacizumab (1.25 mg)-injections was started. After 4 weeks, the first injection resulted in the resolution of the PED with an approximation of the MH edges. However, BCVA decreased to 20/160. In the follow-up, the second injection was administered after four weeks, and SD-OCT showed complete closure of the MH along with marked visual improvement to 20/50. During further follow-up, PED recurred, but no reopening of the MH was observed. Continued intravitreal drug therapy with bevacizumab according to Pro Re Nata regimen led to resolution of PED and visual stabilization.

Discharge Diagnosis: Exudative AMD with Subfoveal PED and an Associated MH

Discharge Education: The patient was counseled on the importance of continued ophthalmic checkups and adherence to medication regimen. The potential side effects of the medication and the importance of promptly reporting any new symptoms were also discussed.

Follow-Up Care: The patient will follow-up with her ophthalmologist in three months.

Signed,

[Physician Name]
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary

Patient Name: Not specified
Medical Record Number: Not specified

Admission Date: Not specified
Discharge Date: Not specified

Admitting Diagnosis: Wet age-related macular degeneration with a fluid-filled blister under the center of the retina and a related hole in the macula

Hospital Course: The 73-year-old Caucasian female was admitted with decreased vision and wavy or distorted vision for 6 months in her left eye. Initial examination showed the sharpest vision with glasses was 20/80 in the left eye and 20/25 in the right eye. A detailed eye exam showed wet age-related macular degeneration with a fluid-filled blister under the center of the retina and a related hole in the macula. A special imaging scan of the retina confirmed a hole going all the way through the macula with attached edges. It also showed a partly detached gel layer on top of the raised blister. Treatment with eye injections to stop abnormal blood vessel growth was started. The patient received three monthly injections of bevacizumab (1.25 mg) into the eye. After 4 weeks, the first injection made the fluid-filled blister go away and brought the edges of the hole closer together. However, vision got worse to 20/160. At the follow-up visit, the second injection was given after four weeks. The imaging scan showed the hole had closed completely and vision improved greatly to 20/50. During later follow-up visits, the fluid-filled blister came back, but the hole in the macula stayed closed. Ongoing eye injections with bevacizumab given as needed made the blister go away and kept vision stable.

Discharge Diagnosis: Wet age-related macular degeneration with a fluid-filled blister under the center of the retina and a related hole in the macula

Discharge Education: The patient was told about the importance of continued eye checkups and taking medication as directed. The possible side effects of the medication were explained. The patient was also told to report any new symptoms right away.

Follow-Up Care: The patient will follow-up with her eye doctor in three months.

Signed,

[Physician Name]

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient 1, a 94-year-old male with multiple facial basal cell carcinomas (BCC), presented to our institution and was treated with pharmacological intervention after refusing surgical ablation. Treatment with topical immunomodulators was successful in achieving complete response for all lesions; however, the nasal lesion relapsed after a year and a suspect keratoacanthoma appeared on the forehead.

A further course of imiquimod was administered, but the nasal BCC increased in size. Everolimus was initiated and gradually increased for a total treatment period of 12 months. Following an initial ulcerative phase, after eight months of everolimus treatment, the nasal BCC disappeared completely. The forehead lesion remained unchanged and was surgically removed, showing an invasive squamous cell carcinoma.

The complete response of the nasal BCC persisted for the last follow-up, eight months after discontinuation of everolimus. The patient subsequently died of natural causes.

The patient was thoroughly monitored and received exceptional medical attention during their stay at our institution. The medical team followed proper protocols and procedures for the patient's diagnosis and treatment. The patient was provided with adequate resources for their medical needs.

The patient's condition was managed effectively and the team was pleased to provide outstanding medical care.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient 1 is a 94-year-old man who had several skin cancers on his face. These were a common type called basal cell carcinoma. The patient came to our facility for treatment. The patient did not want surgery to remove the cancers. Instead, the patient was treated with medicine.

The patient used a skin cream that helps the body fight cancer cells. This treatment worked well and all the skin cancers went away. However, after one year, the cancer on the nose came back. A new growth also appeared on the forehead that looked like it might be a different type of skin problem.

The patient used the skin cream again, but the nose cancer got bigger. The patient then started taking a pill called everolimus. The dose was slowly increased over 12 months of treatment. At first, the nose cancer formed an open sore. After eight months on this medicine, the nose cancer went away completely. The growth on the forehead did not change with medicine. Doctors removed it with surgery. Tests showed it was a different type of skin cancer that had grown deeper into the skin.

The nose cancer stayed gone for eight months after the patient stopped taking everolimus. The patient later died of natural causes.

The patient was watched closely and received very good medical care at our facility. The medical team followed all proper steps for finding and treating the patient's condition. The patient was given everything needed for their medical care.

The patient's condition was handled well and the team was happy to provide excellent medical care.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
24-year-old Caucasian man, who came in with a comminuted posterior wall-posterior column right acetabular fracture, playing amateur rugby at a high standard.

History of Present Illness:
 The patient was tackled during play which caused him to land on his flexed right knee, resulting in immediate pain and an inability to bear weight. Imaging at the local hospital revealed a fracture, and the patient was transferred to our institution for definitive management.

Hospital Course:
 The patient underwent open reduction and internal fixation through a posterior Kocher-Langenbach approach, which involved elevating and supporting the articular surface with a local bone graft from his greater trochanter. Following the procedure, his recovery was uneventful, and he was discharged on the third postoperative day.

Treatment:
 The patient remained non-weight bearing on his right leg with crutches for six weeks before transitioning to full weight-bearing as he rehabilitated. Six months postoperatively, our patient was doing well, fully weight bearing, doing light gym work, and showing union on x-ray.

Follow-up Plan:
 As part of his long-term review, the patient will continue to be monitored.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Information:
24-year-old white man who came in with a broken hip socket on the right side. The bone was broken into several pieces in the back part of the hip socket. The patient plays amateur rugby at a high level.

History of Present Illness:
The patient was tackled during a game. He landed on his bent right knee. This caused immediate pain. He could not put weight on his leg. Scans at the local hospital showed a broken bone. The patient was moved to our hospital for treatment.

Hospital Course:
The patient had surgery to put the broken bones back in place. Metal hardware was used to hold the bones together. The surgeon went in through the back of the hip. Bone from the top of the thigh bone was used to support the joint surface. The patient healed well after surgery. He left the hospital three days after the operation.

Treatment:
The patient could not put weight on his right leg for six weeks. He used crutches during this time. After six weeks, he slowly started putting full weight on his leg as he did physical therapy. Six months after surgery, the patient was doing well. He could put full weight on his leg. He was doing light exercise at the gym. X-rays showed the bone had healed.

Follow-up Plan:
The patient will continue to have checkups over time.

input:
Hospital Course:
The patient, a 71-year-old Japanese woman, presented with a 10-year history of left hip pain and 1-year history of left leg edema. Physical examination revealed limitation of the range of motion (ROM) of her left hip, with 80° flexion, 20° abduction, 15° adduction, 45° external rotation, and 5° internal rotation. Plain radiographs revealed advanced osteoarthritis (OA) secondary to developmental dysplasia of the left hip. Computed tomography (CT) images and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed compression of the femoral vessels by a mass that was absent 8 years ago on a previous radiograph. The mass was diagnosed as a capital drop secondary to OA and total hip arthroplasty (THA) with resection of the mass was performed. Following surgery, the left leg edema immediately diminished and the postoperative course was uneventful. At the latest follow-up, that is, 3 years after the surgery, the patient was able to walk without an aid and was independent in all activities of daily living, with no evidence of recurrence. 

Diagnoses:
Advanced osteoarthritis secondary to developmental dysplasia of the left hip

Procedures performed:
Total hip arthroplasty (THA) with resection of the mass, using a short tapered wedge stem with a 32mm ceramic head and a 48mm cementless cup with a polyethylene liner

Histopathological findings:
Peripheral cartilage layer, with mature trabecular bone present in the central zone. No malignant cells were observed

Discharge condition:
Stable

Functional status at discharge:
The patient was able to walk independently and had no evidence of recurrence.
clean_ref:
Hospital Course:
The patient is a 71-year-old Japanese woman. She had left hip pain for 10 years and swelling in her left leg for 1 year. A physical exam showed she could not move her left hip normally. Her hip could only bend to 80 degrees and had limited movement in other directions. X-rays showed severe joint damage in her left hip. This was caused by a hip problem she was born with. CT scans and MRI scans showed that blood vessels in her leg were being pressed by a growth. This growth was not there 8 years ago on an earlier X-ray. Doctors found that the growth was a piece of bone that had broken off due to the joint damage. The patient had surgery to replace her hip and remove the growth. Right after surgery, the swelling in her left leg went down. Her recovery went smoothly with no problems. At her last check-up, 3 years after surgery, the patient could walk without help. She could do all daily activities on her own. The growth had not come back.

Diagnoses:
Severe joint damage in the left hip caused by a hip problem present since birth.

Procedures performed:
Full hip replacement surgery with removal of the growth. Doctors used a short stem that fits into the thigh bone, a ceramic ball measuring 32mm, and a 48mm socket that attaches without cement. The socket has a plastic lining.

Tissue study findings:
The outer layer was made of cartilage. The center had normal bone tissue. No cancer cells were found.

Discharge condition:
Stable.

Functional status at discharge:
The patient was able to walk on her own and the growth had not come back.

input:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Insert Name]

Medical Record Number: [Insert Number]

Admission Date: [Insert Date]

Discharge Date: [Insert Date]

Diagnosis:

- Congenital Left Diaphragmatic Hernia with Volvulus and Obstruction of Herniated Part of Stomach
- Acutely Torted Intrathoracic Spleen and Stomach
- Left Part of Transverse Colon, and Small Bowel Loops

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted with complaints of decreased appetite, dysphagia, epigastric pain, and shortness of breath. The chest X-ray and CT scan revealed a distended stomach and enlarged spleen with shift of mediastinal structures to the right side suggesting Bochdalek hernia. 

The patient underwent laparoscopic repair that was converted to laparotomy. During surgery, a large congenital left diaphragmatic hernia was observed with acutely torted intrathoracic spleen and stomach. The hernia was reduced successfully and a Gore-Tex mesh was applied to the diaphragmatic defect. 

The patient had an uneventful recovery and was discharged 10 days after the procedure. The patient was advised to follow-up with their primary care physician and continue treatment as prescribed. 

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient presented with typical symptoms of Bochdalek hernia and was diagnosed with the condition. A laparoscopic repair was initially attempted but was converted to laparotomy. The hernia was reduced successfully and a Gore-Tex mesh was applied. The patient recovered well and was discharged. The patient was advised to continue treatment as prescribed and follow-up with their primary care physician.
clean_ref:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Insert Name]

Medical Record Number: [Insert Number]

Admission Date: [Insert Date]

Discharge Date: [Insert Date]

Diagnosis:

Birth defect causing a hole in the left side of the muscle that separates the chest from the belly. This hole allowed the stomach, spleen, part of the large intestine, and part of the small intestine to move up into the chest. The stomach and spleen became twisted and blocked.

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with less hunger than usual, trouble swallowing, pain in the upper belly, and trouble breathing. A chest X-ray and CT scan showed a stretched stomach and enlarged spleen. These organs had pushed into the chest and shifted the heart and nearby parts to the right side. This pointed to a type of birth defect called a hole in the muscle below the lungs.

The patient had surgery. Doctors first tried to operate using small cuts and a camera. They had to switch to a larger cut to complete the repair. During surgery, doctors found a large hole in the muscle below the lungs on the left side. The spleen and stomach had moved into the chest and become twisted. Doctors moved these organs back to where they belong. They used a special patch to close the hole in the muscle.

The patient healed well with no problems. The patient left the hospital 10 days after surgery. The patient was told to see their regular doctor for follow-up care. The patient was told to keep taking medicine as directed.

Hospital Course Summary:

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Insert Name]

Diagnosis:

Birth defect causing a hole in the left side of the muscle that separates the chest from the belly. This hole allowed the stomach, spleen, part of the large intestine, and part of the small intestine to move up into the chest. The stomach and spleen became twisted and blocked.

Hospital Course:

The patient came to the hospital with less hunger than usual, trouble swallowing, pain in the upper belly, and trouble breathing. A chest X-ray and CT scan showed a stretched stomach and enlarged spleen. These organs had pushed into the chest and shifted the heart and nearby parts to the right side. This pointed to a type of birth defect called a hole in the muscle below the lungs.

The patient had surgery. Doctors first tried to operate using small cuts and a camera. They had to switch to a larger cut to complete the repair. During surgery, doctors found a large hole in the muscle below the lungs on the left side. The spleen and stomach had moved into the chest and become twisted. Doctors moved these organs back to where they belong. They used a special patch to close the hole in the muscle.

The patient healed well with no problems. The patient left the hospital 10 days after surgery. The patient was told to see their regular doctor for follow-up care. The patient was told to keep taking medicine as directed.
